Of particular interest is the term “disorder of affective contact,” which Kanner used to emphasize the defect present from very early stages of development in the area of affective language. This refers to the development of nonverbal communicative symbolizations, such as facial expressions, gestures, motor activities for social purposes, and the production and modulation of early sounds. 1 For this reason, we use the term “early autism” or simply “autism” instead of NDRC.

chunks.heading: 30 Infancy and Early Childhood
chunks.preview: 30 Infancy and Early Childhood Central to this issue is the question of how infants with autism communicate or fail to communicate. We can assume that from earliest infancy, children with autism are not devoid of the necessary, biologically determined, motivation for relationships and communication....
chunks.text: 30 Infancy and Early Childhood Central to this issue is the question of how infants with autism communicate or fail to communicate. We can assume that from earliest infancy, children with autism are not devoid of the necessary, biologically determined, motivation for relationships and communication. Instead, they lack the tools to communicate and interact in a typical way, thereby reducing the possibility that the environment will facilitate their sociocommunicative development. Social interaction and communication are thus not categorically absent but are qualitatively different. Such a vision of early disorders of relating and communicating complements the more traditional view, which emphasizes deficits and shortcomings rather than atypicalities in the social attitude of these infants and toddlers. Thus, the study of developmental trajectories leading to a diagnosis of ASD confronts us with the need to recognize atypical development within the relational framework that characterizes the child’s primary environment. If we assume that social interest is naturally present from birth, we may wonder how it can deviate from a typical path and become incomprehensible to others, and how the interpersonal world of these children is constructed. If we consider the deficit in affective contact as not only a core symptom of early autism but also its origin, we can try to specify the developmental skills that support any form of affective communication in the first years of life when the initial anomalies are visible. In the absence of such awareness by the caregivers, children with autism may suffer a prolonged exposure to interactive experiences that are not adapted to their needs. The more atypical and prolonged these experiences, the more a child’s development may be channeled toward an atypical outcome. The progressive atypical functional organization of the brain is congruent with an initially more nuanced clinical expression of autism that leaves space for its progressive definition in the second year of life, during the transition from simple to more complex social behaviors. For this reason, we begin to find clear symptoms of autism in the sociocommunicative area only after 12 months, when the child moves from primary intersubjectivity to more complex forms of secondary intersubjectivity. Social and Nonsocial Attention An atypical profile of competition between social and nonsocial interests emerges as the first pillar of those difficulties that affect, in subtle, fluctuating, gentle ways, the first interactions between self and environment in infants later diagnosed with ASD (Maestro et al., 2005). The atypical attention pattern (looking, smiling, vocalizing toward others) is characterized at first by an extremely early quantitative reduction in attention to social stimuli. In the first 6 months of life, all children pay increased attention to objects, with no notable differences between typically developing infants and those headed toward an ASD diagnosis. But the reduction in social attention for some children results in a clear preference for objects. This profile suggests that attention is not implicated as a primary function but only in its social component. During this period, mothers often perceive this deficit in social attention as they are faced with a child who captures their gaze less frequently or for as long as they expect. During the second half of the first year, infants with autism exhibit, alongside a significant increase in attention to social stimuli, an increase in attention to nonsocial stimuli, which, by the end of the first year of life, remains predominant. While

chunks.heading: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 31
chunks.preview: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 31 paying more attention to objects, children with autism still show some attention to the adult’s face and eyes, a pattern that increases throughout the first year of life. Their atypicality is not a question of the presence or absence of social skills, bu...
chunks.text: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 31 paying more attention to objects, children with autism still show some attention to the adult’s face and eyes, a pattern that increases throughout the first year of life. Their atypicality is not a question of the presence or absence of social skills, but rather of the amount and duration of their use. This imbalance appears as an early marker of NDRC because it is reliably absent in typically developing children. Some important implications of this atypical profile of social stimulus attention development include the following: (1) An observer should not be misled by the bias that a child who makes eye contact could not have an NDRC; (2) parents should pay attention to the duration of spontaneously initiated eye contact by the child and the amount of effort they make to capture and maintain eye contact with their child; and (3) early intervention can be directed toward recognizing, supporting, and enhancing a competence that is still present. Intersubjectivity What are the consequences of the atypical profile of social attention development on the frequency and quality of social interactions and the learning opportunities that can have cascading effects on the development of intersubjectivity? As hypothesized by Trevarthen (Trevarthen & Aitken, 2001), the primary interest of infants is of a social nature: Newborns clearly prefer human faces and actively seek contact with others through intentional movement and all sensory channels. This very early social interest of the newborn and infant is referred to as “primary intersubjectivity.” Only later, after 4–6 months, does the infant’s interest in objects in the physical world begin to compete with the protoconversational dyadic play typical of the first 3 months of life. This competition leads to the development of increasingly lively object-­ related play and, in the second half of the first year, to the rather sudden emergence of joint interest in both people and objects. Triadic sequences of shared attention are a sign, in typical development, of the child’s emerging ability to share the physical object within a playful relationship with others. This emerging ability is referred to as “secondary intersubjectivity,” which influences how adults interact with the child by introducing the object more frequently into the relationship, creating an alternation between dyadic and triadic interactions, with dyadic moments of intimacy becoming increasingly common. The atypical trajectory of social and nonsocial attention could underlie the peculiarities that characterize the development of intersubjective involvement in early autism. It has been observed in infants who will develop an NDRC (Chericoni et al., 2016) that babbling is not quantitatively less than in typically developing children, but it is much more frequently directed toward nonsocial entities. In atypical development, even babbling is captured by intense interest in objects and therefore does not contribute to the improvement of intersubjective exchange. While in typical development, the human face represents the preferred stimulus, and nonsocial attention gradually integrates into sequences of social interaction, in atypical development, it seems to happen the opposite way: Social attention gradually integrates into the interest in objects (which remains predominant). While in typical development, in the second half of the first year, the merging of interest in people and objects represents the process that allows the development of secondary intersubjectivity and sequences of shared attention (including declarative pointing), in NDRC, the ongoing and intense predominance of interest in objects over people creates a different

chunks.heading: 32 Infancy and Early Childhood
chunks.preview: 32 Infancy and Early Childhood development of the interpersonal world. The latter is dominated by the drive to understand how objects work, which takes precedence over interest in what is present and alive in people’s minds. The high salience of objects, besides being a possible early indicator of a...
chunks.text: 32 Infancy and Early Childhood development of the interpersonal world. The latter is dominated by the drive to understand how objects work, which takes precedence over interest in what is present and alive in people’s minds. The high salience of objects, besides being a possible early indicator of atypical development, could also underlie the unique ways in which autistic individuals interact with the physical world. These distinctive and atypical modes are typically described as belonging to the second diagnostic domain of ASD. Therefore, we could hypothesize a relationship between the trajectory of social development and the emergence of restricted, stereotyped, and repetitive interests. From this perspective, the two diagnostic domains would not be two separate and independent sets of symptoms. The early reduction in social attention would lead to a brain development in which areas dependent on social experience become more responsive to nonsocial stimuli. As a result, complex social processes, such as those related to secondary intersubjectivity, which typically emerge during the second year of life, cannot rely on simpler social processes that underlie them and are no longer enhanced by being the primary source of gratification in children with atypical development. Although this phenomenon is sometimes interpreted in terms of an attachment problem, it reflects instead the fact that the drive for knowledge (epistemophilia) of objects shapes the construction of the interpersonal world differently, affecting the relationship with caregivers. One of the most evident effects that the child’s early difficulties may have on interaction is a significant reduction in the duration of mutual interactions. An infant’s motor skills become incomprehensible to others, and the duration of episodes of “tuning” rapidly diminish. According to Feldman (2007), tuning is the ability to respond proportionately to the affective bodily stimulation of the other; that is, to show motor and bodily response appropriate to the soliciting emotional states of others. In atypical development, we often observe a reduction in the overall duration of sequences of interactions characterized by mutual attunement (Muratori & Apicella, 2022). Atypical infants seeking to respond to the request of the caregivers showed an inflexible motor apparatus that prevented them from sustaining interpersonal engagements. It is as if the social-­emotional impulse in the baby is not supported by its motor system; motoric capacity is like a cage instead of a fluid and variable apparatus that mediates the rhythms of enough long “protoconversation” with others. This observation led us to consider the hypothesis that in autism, the development of intersubjectivity is also disrupted by the specific difficulties of the motor system, and that body and motor skills play a central role in the genesis of the different interpersonal world in autism. In NDRC, there is indeed an impairment in interpersonal coordination during joint actions. Several key components of action coordination can be compromised, including cooperative behavior, temporal coordination (synchrony), motor planning, and, of course, early sensorimotor acquisitions. An early intervention aimed at enhancing the sensorimotor processes involved in the development of mutual action coordination could promote the social skills of these children. Imitation and Anticipation While clinicians agree that motor disorders are the rule rather than the exception, movement abnormalities are not usually considered a core element for the diagnosis of ASD. A growing body of scientific evidence supports the hypothesis that there are early indicators in the domain of motor development, such as hypoactivity, limited

chunks.heading: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 33
chunks.preview: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 33 motor repertoire, reduced social use of motor activity, and both static and dynamic asymmetry. The presence of these data, together with the absence of signs strictly related to the sociocommunicative domain in the first year of life, raises the hypothes...
chunks.text: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 33 motor repertoire, reduced social use of motor activity, and both static and dynamic asymmetry. The presence of these data, together with the absence of signs strictly related to the sociocommunicative domain in the first year of life, raises the hypothesis that motor atypicalities may be one of the most prominent precursors of abnormalities in social behavior and interests that consistently appear only from the age of 2. As with most developmental skills, what makes infants with autism atypical involves the social use of their behaviors, in this case their motor actions (Apicella et al., 2013). Infants with ASD often encounter difficulties in responding motorically to an adult’s attempts to capture their attention (e.g., engaging with the adult when prompted) and, more importantly, in initiating interaction sequences with the adult. In other words, these infants failed to organize movements that would intentionally position them in a responsive state to social stimuli from the environment. The impairment of the ability to use motor behaviors to engage in interactions with caregivers has significant repercussions on the development of reciprocal imitation. Imitating and being imitated constitute a fundamental intentional mechanism in every human interaction. Imitation requires continuous coupling between perception and action; in neurodevelopmental disorders, this capacity may be compromised by a defect in the organization of intentional, goal-­directed movement. Before assessing the presence or absence of imitation, it is important to pay attention to whether deferred imitation occurs spontaneously in the child. A child’s failure to respond adequately to a request for imitation is not necessarily due to reduced motivation or the absence of competence: It could indicate a difficulty in immediately accessing the motor repertoire necessary to produce imitation. The atypical development of early motor skills and the associated reduced motor initiative to respond to or provoke others can also underlie the deficit in anticipation that characterizes children with NDDs. The anticipation deficit is evident very early in the absence of certain spontaneous behaviors such as opening the mouth when a spoonful of food approaches or a delay in bringing the head into alignment during the pull-to-sit test, in which an adult approaches a supine 6-month-old infant and offers support with their hands to reach a sitting position. Early Motor Atypicalities The observation of reduced motor initiative, hypomobility and passivity, lack of proactive and provocative actions, imitation deficits, and lack of anticipatory movements is now considered fundamental in assessing neurodevelopment and the possible presence of NDRC. The first to describe a motor origin of these disorders was Teitelbaum (Teitelbaum, Teitelbaum, Nye, Fryman, & Maurer, 1998), who noted asymmetric postural alignment and the persistence of primitive reflexes in the first year of life. Subsequently, several longitudinal studies have reported the presence of a generalized delay in postural transitions (Bhat et al., 2012), a marked reduction in the rhythmic arm movements that normally accompany the emergence and development of babbling (Iverson & Wozniak, 2007); reduced movement variability, hypotonia, poor motor coordination, and hypoactivity (Flanagan, Landa, Bhat, & Bauman, 2012); a significant reduction in hand movements across the midline (Begum Ali, Charaman, Johnson, Jones, & BASIS/ STAARS Team, 2020); and a reduced quantity of postural transition movements, with reduced use of the hands for object manipulation (Mulligan & White, 2012).

chunks.heading: 40 Infancy and Early Childhood
chunks.preview: 40 Infancy and Early Childhood IEC02 Regulatory–Sensory Processing Disorders Regulatory–­sensory processing disorders (see Table 1.2) are constitutionally based maladaptive responses to sensory stimuli that may lead to difficult behaviors and complicate a child’s interactions with the environment. C...
chunks.text: 40 Infancy and Early Childhood IEC02 Regulatory–Sensory Processing Disorders Regulatory–­sensory processing disorders (see Table 1.2) are constitutionally based maladaptive responses to sensory stimuli that may lead to difficult behaviors and complicate a child’s interactions with the environment. Children vary in how they respond to different sensations (e.g., touch, sound), comprehend these sensations, and plan actions. How they gradually learn to perceive and organize information from multiple sensory modalities will determine the subsequent “regulatory–­sensory profile” that is unique for each child. Lester (1998) offers the “four A’s” (Arousal, Attention, Affect, and Action) as the core domains of behavioral regulation in young children. Some children, however, have processing differences extreme enough to interfere with daily functioning at home, in school, in other interactions with peers or adults, and with routine functions such as self-care, sleeping, and eating. Understanding individual differences in regulatory–­sensory processing can help therapists and families find ways to promote healthy emotional, social, and intellectual functioning in any child. The concept of regulatory–­sensory processing disorders arose in the 1980s and early 1990s from Greenspan’s concept of regulatory disorders (Greenspan et al., 1987) by integrating the occupational therapy literature with the developmental, individual-­ difference, relationship-­based (DIR) model of infant and early childhood mental health (Greenspan, 1992). A regulatory–­sensory processing disorder should be considered when a child’s motor and sensory patterns interfere with age-­expected emotional, social, language, cognitive (including attention), motor, or sensory functioning. There is now considerable evidence for the existence of regulatory–­sensory processing differences in children with a range of psychological, developmental, and learning difficulties. To understand how regulatory–­sensory processing differences affect children’s functioning, one must discriminate between research that includes attention to these processes as part of a comprehensive, developmental, biopsychosocial intervention program (Greenspan & Wieder, 2003) and studies that focus only on interventions for specific sensory processing dimensions. TABLE 1.2. IEC02 Regulatory–Sensory Processing Disorders IEC02 Regulatory–Sensory Processing Disorders (RSPD) IEC02.01 Hypersensitive or Overresponsive Disorder IEC02.01.1 Hypersensitive or Overresponsive: Fearful, Anxious Pattern IEC02.01.2 Hypersensitive or Overresponsive: Negative, Stubborn Pattern IEC02.02 Hyposensitive or Underresponsive, Self-Absorbed Pattern IEC02.02.1 Pattern A: Self-Absorbed and Difficult-to-Engage Type IEC02.02.2 Pattern B: Self-Absorbed and Creative Type IEC02.03 Active, Sensory-Seeking Pattern IEC02.04 Inattentive, Disorganized Pattern IEC02.05 Compromised School and/or Academic Performance Pattern IEC02.06 Mixed Regulatory–Sensory Processing Patterns

chunks.heading: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 41
chunks.preview: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 41 Diagnosis of Regulatory–Sensory Processing Disorders The diagnosis of regulatory–­sensory processing disorders requires both a distinct behavior pattern and a difficulty in sensory modulation, sensorimotor, sensory discrimination, or attentional processi...
chunks.text: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 41 Diagnosis of Regulatory–Sensory Processing Disorders The diagnosis of regulatory–­sensory processing disorders requires both a distinct behavior pattern and a difficulty in sensory modulation, sensorimotor, sensory discrimination, or attentional processing. When a behavioral and a sensory pattern are not both present, other diagnoses are more appropriate. For example, an infant who is irritable and withdrawn after being abandoned may be suffering from an attachment problem. An infant who is irritable and overly responsive to routine interpersonal experiences, in the absence of a clearly identified sensory, sensorimotor, or processing difficulty, may have an anxiety or mood disorder. The diagnosis of a sensory processing disorder implies that processing impairment interferes significantly with the child’s ability to manage calm, alert, emotionally positive states. Terms such as “overly sensitive,” “difficult temperament,” or “reactive” have commonly been used to describe infants and children with atypical motor and sensory processing. But clinicians have tended to use such terms without specifying the sensory system or motor functions involved. There is growing evidence that constitutional and early maturational patterns contribute to the difficulties of such infants by interfering with their ability to adapt and react to environmental solicitations. In these circumstances, the child may show behavioral patterns such as crying, rigidity in the face of change, avoidance or refusal of certain experiences, and more generally a delay in the maturation of self-­regulation systems (self-­soothing ability, adaptation to care routines, organization of biorhythms relating to sleep and feeding). It is also clear that early caregiving patterns can exert considerable influence on how constitutional–­maturational patterns develop and become part of a child’s evolving personality. The concept of “goodness of fit” offers a paradigm to illustrate how parents’ representations determine the expectations of the “social environment” that may “fit” or not with the constitutional characteristics of the child. Parental style in modulating interaction in response to a child’s communicative cues influences the child’s sensory integration, one of the biological roots of personality. As interest in these children increases, it is important to systematize descriptions of each child’s sensory, motor, and integrative patterns, as well as the caregiving patterns presumed to be involved. For a regulatory–­sensory processing disorder to be aptly diagnosed, the clinician must observe the way the child develops self-­regulation through the integration of the four domains of attention, arousal, affect, and action, and describe the resulting behavioral difficulties, such as a problem with sensory modulation, sensorimotor functioning, sensory discrimination, or attention. Other neurodevelopmental disorders, including ASDs or attention-­deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), which are diagnosed in part by atypia in sensory processing, must be taken into account in the differential diagnosis. Symptoms such as nightmares, withdrawal, phobias, anxiety, and sleeping or eating difficulties may have clinical features similar to sensory processing disorder without a maturational constitutional basis, or, alternatively, may be comorbid with it. The diagnostic process requires an accurate anamnesis of the pregnancy and the first months of the child’s life, with particular attention to behavioral aspects: participation in care routines, response to caregiver stimuli, and sensorial inputs. The anamnesis should conclude with an exploration of parental representations, expectations, desires, attributions, and interpretations of the child’s behavior. A careful observation of the child in different contexts is also necessary for this evaluation. To assess the child’s sensorimotor profile, the clinician can refer to Axis

chunks.heading: 42 Infancy and Early Childhood
chunks.preview: 42 Infancy and Early Childhood III (in this chapter), which describes the different features of sensory processing and provides some tools that may be useful in augmenting the clinician’s observations. Presenting Pattern Below we describe some patterns of regulatory–­sensory processing disorders, us...
chunks.text: 42 Infancy and Early Childhood III (in this chapter), which describes the different features of sensory processing and provides some tools that may be useful in augmenting the clinician’s observations. Presenting Pattern Below we describe some patterns of regulatory–­sensory processing disorders, using three dimensions: sensory processing impairments, behavior profiles, and caregiver attitudes. Although these disorders lie on a continuum and co-occur with many other conditions in early childhood, the diagnosis of a specific subtype may provide useful information for treatment and follow-­up. In arriving at a diagnosis, clinicians are advised to integrate their assessment with other tools (checklists, questionnaires, parents’ reports). Sensory Modulation Difficulties (Type I) Sensory Processing Impairments The first group of regulatory–­sensory processing disorders involves difficulties in sensory modulation, characterized by an inability to grade the degree, intensity, and nature of responses to sensory input. Often a child’s responses do not fit the demands of a situation. The child therefore has trouble achieving and maintaining an optimal range of performance and adaptation to ordinary challenges. There are three subtypes of sensory modulation problems: hypersensitive or overresponsive disorder (IEC02.01), hyposensitive or underresponsive, self-­absorbed pattern (IEC02.02), and active, sensory-­seeking pattern (IEC02.03). IEC02.01 Hypersensitive or Overresponsive Disorder Some children have responses to sensory stimuli that are more intense, quicker in onset, or longer-­lasting than those of most children facing the same conditions. Their responses are particularly pronounced when a stimulus is not anticipated. They may demonstrate overresponsivity in only one sensory system (e.g., “auditory defensiveness” or “tactile defensiveness”), or in multiple sensory systems. Overresponsivity to sensory stimuli in multiple modalities is often referred to as “sensory defensiveness.” Children with this problem may be particularly reactive to specific types of sensory stimuli (e.g., in the tactile domain, they respond defensively to light touch but not to deep pressure), rather than to all stimuli within a domain. Responses to sensory stimuli occur along a spectrum. Some children manage their tendency toward overresponsivity most of the time, while other children are overresponsive almost continually. Responses may appear inconsistent, because overresponsivity is highly dependent on context. Although children may attempt to avoid particular sensory experiences, sensitivities may vary throughout a given day and from one day to another. Because sensory input tends to have a cumulative effect, a child’s efforts to control responses to sensory stimuli may build up and result in a sudden behavioral disruption in response to a seemingly trivial stimulus. A child’s behavioral characteristics when faced with uncomfortable stimuli can fall within a broad range. At one end of that continuum, the child shows fearfulness and anxiety, often avoiding many sensory experiences. At the other, the child shows

chunks.heading: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 43
chunks.preview: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 43 negativity and stubbornness or obstinacy, exemplified by attempts to control the environment. This latter tendency is described as a separate type below. This range of reactions has been termed the “fight, flight, fright, or freeze” response and is attri...
chunks.text: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 43 negativity and stubbornness or obstinacy, exemplified by attempts to control the environment. This latter tendency is described as a separate type below. This range of reactions has been termed the “fight, flight, fright, or freeze” response and is attributed to sympathetic nervous system activation. Secondary behavioral characteristics include irritability, fussiness, poor adaptability, moodiness, inconsolability, and poor socialization. In general, children who are overresponsive to sensation have difficulty with transitions and unexpected changes. Sensory overresponsivity is often seen with other sensory reaction patterns. For example, children may be overresponsive to tactile stimuli while seeking proprioceptive stimuli. Sensory overresponsivity may coexist with sensory discrimination problems and dyspraxia. IEC02.01.1 Hypersensitive or Overresponsive: Fearful, Anxious Pattern Behavior Profiles Behavior profiles include excessive cautiousness, inhibition, and fearfulness. These children avoid sensation in an effort to control unexpected incoming stimuli. In early infancy, one sees a restricted range of exploration and assertiveness, dislike of changes in routine, and a tendency to be frightened and to cling. The behavior of toddlers and older preschoolers with sensory overresponsivity is characterized by excessive worrying and shyness in new situations, such as forming peer relationships or engaging with new adults. Occasionally a child behaves impulsively when overloaded or frightened, becoming easily upset, hard to soothe, and slow to recover from frustration or disappointment, especially in environments with multiple or intense sensory stimuli. The fearful, cautious child may have a fragmented rather than an integrated internal representational world, and may therefore be easily distracted by different stimuli. Caregiver Attitudes Caregiver attitudes characterized by soothing, regulating interactions that respect a child’s sensitivities and do not convey that the child has “bad behaviors,” are helpful. Supportive parents anticipate noxious environments and minimize them or prepare the child for them. Enhancing flexibility and assertiveness in fearful children requires empathy for the children’s sensory and affective experience. Caregivers who give lowkey support to explore new experiences and also set gentle but firm limits do well with these children. Inconsistent caregiver patterns intensify their difficulties, as when caregivers are overindulgent or overprotective some of the time and punitive or intrusive at other times. IEC02.01.2 Hypersensitive or Overresponsive: Negative, Stubborn Pattern Children with an overresponsive, negative, stubborn pattern may show evidence of the same physiological responses described for overresponsive, fearful, and anxious children. These children, however, seek to control their sensory environments and thus may prefer repetition and the absence of change (or, at most, change at a slow, predictable pace). Rather than becoming fearful, anxious, and cautious, they attempt to control their environments to minimize fear and anxiety.

chunks.heading: 44 Infancy and Early Childhood
chunks.preview: 44 Infancy and Early Childhood Behavior Profiles Behavior profiles may appear negative, stubborn, and controlling. A child can become aggressive and impulsive in response to sensory stimulation, often doing the opposite of what is requested or expected. Infants with this pattern tend to be fussy, di...
chunks.text: 44 Infancy and Early Childhood Behavior Profiles Behavior profiles may appear negative, stubborn, and controlling. A child can become aggressive and impulsive in response to sensory stimulation, often doing the opposite of what is requested or expected. Infants with this pattern tend to be fussy, difficult, and resistant to transitions and changes. Preschoolers with this pattern tend to be negative, angry, and stubborn, as well as compulsive and perfectionistic. These children can, however, display joyful, flexible behavior in certain situations. In contrast to the fearful or cautious child, the negative and stubborn child does not become fragmented but organizes an integrated sense of self around negative patterns. In contrast to the impulsive, sensation-­seeking child (described below), the negative and stubborn child is more controlling; tends to be avoidant of, or slow to engage in, new experiences rather than to crave them; and is not generally aggressive unless provoked. Caregiver Attitudes Caregiver attitudes that enhance flexibility, offer empathic support, approach change slowly, and avoid power struggles (e.g., by offering a child choices and opportunities for negotiation whenever possible) are helpful. Caregivers’ warmth, coupled with gentle, firm guidance and limits—­even in the face of negative or impulsive responses that may feel like rejection—­is critical to helping an oppositional child. Encouragement of symbolic representation of affective states (especially dependency, anger, and annoyance) also helps such children become more flexible. Caregiver patterns that are intrusive, excessively demanding, overstimulating, or punitive tend to intensify negative patterns. IEC02.02 Hyposensitive or Underresponsive: Self‐Absorbed Pattern Children who are hyposensitive or underresponsive to sensory stimuli are often quiet and passive. Because they have not registered the sensory input in their environment, they may appear withdrawn, difficult to engage, and/or self-­absorbed. The term “poor registration” is often used to describe their behavior, as they do not seem to detect or “register” incoming sensory information. They may also appear apathetic and lethargic, giving the impression of lacking the drive that most children have for socialization and motor exploration. They do not notice the possibilities for action that are around them. Their underresponsivity to tactile and proprioceptive inputs may lead to a poorly developed sense of their bodies, clumsiness, or poorly modulated movement. They may fail to respond to bumps, falls, cuts, scrapes, or objects that may burn or chill them, as they may not notice pain. This aspect of their sensory problem presents a significant danger. Children with sensory underresponsivity also may have sensory discrimination problems and dyspraxia. Underresponsive children are often deprived of the level of intensity in sensory input required for optimal maturation. Because they fail to make demands on people and things in the environment, children with this pattern may be overlooked as “good babies” or “easy children.” They often need high-­intensity and highly salient input in order to become actively involved in the environment, a task, or an interaction. Some children who are easily overloaded by sensory stimulation may appear underresponsive

chunks.heading: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 45
chunks.preview: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 45 when in fact they are extremely overresponsive. The observable behavior is one that suggests withdrawal and shutdown, perhaps as a defense mechanism. Some children with an underresponsive pattern are self-­absorbed, unaware, and disengaged; others, equal...
chunks.text: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 45 when in fact they are extremely overresponsive. The observable behavior is one that suggests withdrawal and shutdown, perhaps as a defense mechanism. Some children with an underresponsive pattern are self-­absorbed, unaware, and disengaged; others, equally self-­absorbed, are very creative and overly focused on their own fantasy lives. Accordingly, PDM-3 describes two subtypes of the underresponsive self-­absorbed pattern. IEC02.02.1 Pattern A: Self‐Absorbed and Difficult‐to‐Engage Type Behavior Profiles These children seem uninterested in exploring relationships or engaging with challenging games or objects. They may appear apathetic, easily exhausted, and remote. High affective tone and saliency are required to attract their attention and emotional engagement. Infants may appear delayed or depressed, lacking in motor exploration and social overtures. Preschoolers may evidence diminished verbal dialogue. Their behavior and play may present a limited range of ideas and fantasies. Sometimes these children seek out desired sensory input by engaging in repetitive sensory activities, such as spinning, swinging, or jumping up and down on a bed. They need the intensity or repetition of these activities in order to experience them fully. Caregiver Attitudes Caregiver styles that provide high-­energy interactive input help engage a child in activities and relationships and foster the child’s initiative. These involve energized wooing and robust responses to the child’s cues, however faint. In contrast, caregiver patterns that are low-key, “laid back,” or depressive in tone and rhythm tend to reinforce these children’s patterns of withdrawal. IEC02.02.2 Pattern B: Self‐Absorbed and Creative Type Behavior Profiles Self-­absorbed children of the creative type have a tendency to tune in to their own sensations, thoughts, and emotions rather than to attend to others’ communications. Infants may become interested in objects through solitary exploration rather than in the context of interaction. They may appear inattentive, easily distracted, or preoccupied, especially when they are not pulled into a task or interaction. Preschoolers with this pattern tend to escape into fantasy when faced with external challenges, such as a demanding activity or competition from a peer. They may prefer to play by themselves if others do not actively join in imaginative play as they have scripted it. In their fantasy lives, they may show enormous imagination and creativity. Caregiver Attitudes Caregiver attitudes that are beneficial include efforts to help a child engage in genuine dialogue—­that is, to “open and close circles of communication.” Caregivers should encourage a good balance between fantasy and reality, and help a child who is attempting to escape into fantasy to stay grounded in reality (e.g., by showing sensitivity to

chunks.heading: 46 Infancy and Early Childhood
chunks.preview: 46 Infancy and Early Childhood the child’s interests and feelings; by promoting discussion of daily events, feelings, and other real-world topics; and by making fantasy play a collaborative endeavor between parent and child rather than a solitary activity). Self-­absorption in caregivers may intensi...
chunks.text: 46 Infancy and Early Childhood the child’s interests and feelings; by promoting discussion of daily events, feelings, and other real-world topics; and by making fantasy play a collaborative endeavor between parent and child rather than a solitary activity). Self-­absorption in caregivers may intensify a child’s difficulties, as may any pattern of confusing family communications. IEC02.03 Active, Sensory‐Seeking Pattern Some children actively seek sensory stimulation. They seem to have an insatiable desire for sensory input. They energetically engage in activities geared toward increasing intensity. They tend to be constantly moving, crashing, bumping, and jumping; they may want to touch everything and have difficulty inhibiting this behavior; they may play music or television at loud volumes, fixate on visually stimulating objects, or seek out unusual smells and tastes. Their sensory experiences are more intense and longer-­ lasting than those of children with typical sensory responsivity. Atypical responses occur along a spectrum; some sensory-­seeking behavior is normal. Children at the sensation-­seeking end of the continuum prefer a higher level of arousal than adults usually find appropriate in a given situation. For those with reduced awareness of sensation, sensory seeking may be a means to increase their level of arousal. For children who are sensory seekers, the need for constant stimulation is difficult to fulfill and may be particularly problematic in environments (e.g., schools, performances, movies, libraries) where children are expected to sit quietly. When it is unstructured, the sought-­after sensory stimulation may increase a child’s overall state of arousal and result in disorganized behavior. If directed, however, it can have an organizing effect. When their sensory needs are not met, these children tend to become demanding and insistent. They may be impulsive, almost explosive, in their attempts to fill their quotas for sensation. Adjectives describing the secondary behavioral characteristics of these children include “overly active or aggressive,” “impulsive,” “intense,” “demanding,” “hard to calm,” “restless,” “overly affectionate,” and “attention-­craving.” Constant or extreme craving of sensory input can disrupt children’s ability to maintain attention and learn. Activities of daily living are frequently disrupted. Children with this pattern may have trouble in school because their drive for extra sensory stimulation interferes with their focus on tasks. Behavior Profiles Behavior profiles involve high activity and the seeking of physical contact and stimulation—­for example, through deep pressure and intense movement. The motorically disorganized child who craves stimulation is notable for disruptive behavior (e.g., breaking things, unprovoked hitting, intruding into other people’s physical space). Behavior that begins as a result of craving sensations may be interpreted by others as aggression rather than excitability. Once others react aggressively, the child’s own behavior may become aggressive in response. Infants in this group are most satisfied when provided with strong sensation in the form of movement, sound, touch, or visual stimulation. They may be content only when held or rocked. Toddlers may be very active. Preschoolers often show aggressive, intrusive behavior and a daredevil, risk-­ taking style, as well as a preoccupation with aggressive themes in pretend play. Young children who are anxious or unsure of themselves may use counterphobic behaviors, such as hitting another child first in anticipation of being hit, or repeating unacceptable

chunks.heading: 50 Infancy and Early Childhood
chunks.preview: 50 Infancy and Early Childhood patterns have been associated with not only internalizing problems (anxiety, depression, withdrawal), but also, to a lesser extent, with externalizing behaviors such as aggression and impulsivity. Appreciation of these subtypes can help clinicians formulate an accurate...
chunks.text: 50 Infancy and Early Childhood patterns have been associated with not only internalizing problems (anxiety, depression, withdrawal), but also, to a lesser extent, with externalizing behaviors such as aggression and impulsivity. Appreciation of these subtypes can help clinicians formulate an accurate description of a child’s behavioral problems and environmental responses in the area of regulation and sensory integration, as this area represents a crossroads between neurodevelopment, affective–­emotional development, caregiving patterns, and the relational and social context of the child’s upbringing. The richness of the items expands the clinician’s observational capacity and guarantees a complex and multidimensional approach. The variety of criteria creates large, flexible containers that can help the clinician in the diagnostic process and provide indicators of the child’s evolution during treatment. We note that, over the years, this taxonomy has proven to be of little use in collecting data on large and sufficiently homogeneous cases, making it difficult to evaluate the reliability of diagnostic criteria that can be shared within the scientific community. Accordingly, the latest edition of the DC:0–5 has proposed a simplification of these subtypes by removing the term “regulation” from their definition and referring to them as sensory processing disorders. Sensory processing disorders are clinically significant and impairing responses to sensory stimuli that are independent of other psychopathological and neurodevelopmental conditions. They do not include difficulties in motor coordination or depict the impact of these impairments on a child’s regulation system. DC:0–5 proposes three main subtypes: Sensory Overresponsivity Disorder, characterized by overresponsivity (e.g., increased intensity of reactions, quicker onset of responses, and prolonged adaptation or delayed recovery from sensory stimulation.); Sensory Underresponsivity Disorder, characterized by underresponsivity (e.g., diminished response intensity or delayed reaction time to sensory stimulation); and Other Sensory Processing Disorders or atypical responses to stimuli that may be characterized both by extended sensory exploration of stimuli (e.g., licking walls or doorknobs—­often not noticed) and by a mix of over- and underresponsivity. We suggest that the clinician temporarily retain the dual classification system in order to assess over time whether the simplification proposed by DC:0–5 results in a loss of clinical data relevant to the treatment and prognosis of these children. Developmental Pathways Some studies have investigated predictive associations between regulatory–­sensory processing disorders and children’s later difficulties. Kinnealey and Fuiek (1999) found hypersensitive patterns to be linked to social-­emotional problems in the internalizing domain (e.g., anxiety, depression, withdrawal). The association between sensory overresponsiveness and internalizing symptoms has also been documented in studies of typically developing individuals across the lifespan (Aron & Aron, 1997; Carter, Briggs-­Gowan, Jones, & Little, 2003; Goldsmith, Van Hulle, Arneson, Schreiber, & Gernsbacher, 2006; Kagan & Snidman, 1991), as well as in clinical populations, such as children with ASD (e.g., Pfeiffer, Kinnealey, Reed, & Herzberg, 2005). Although less commonly reported, hypersensitive patterns in typically developing toddlers have been modestly linked with externalizing behaviors such as aggression and activity/ impulsivity (Carter et al., 2003; Goldsmith et al., 2006).

chunks.heading: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 51
chunks.preview: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 51 Other studies have retrospectively associated regulatory–­sensory processing difficulties in school-­age children with impairments in academic skills and social participation (Stagnitti, Raison, & Ryan, 1999). Ben-­Sasson, Carter, and Briggs-­Gowan (2010...
chunks.text: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 51 Other studies have retrospectively associated regulatory–­sensory processing difficulties in school-­age children with impairments in academic skills and social participation (Stagnitti, Raison, & Ryan, 1999). Ben-­Sasson, Carter, and Briggs-­Gowan (2010) reported that sensory overresponsivity (SOR) toward tactile and auditory input can affect children’s participation in academic and social activities. The study investigated SOR in a representative sample of elementary school-­age children (n = 925, 50% boys, ages 7–11 years) who were followed from infancy. Sixteen percent of parents reported that at least four tactile or auditory sensations bothered their children. Certain sensations were common causes of irritation, while others were relatively rare. Parents of children both with and without elevated SOR in the school years reported higher frequencies of early and co-­occurring internalizing, externalizing, and dysregulation problems, and lower levels of concurrent adaptive social behaviors. Although the association with social-­emotional problems and competence has not been rigorously studied, the authors suggest that early identification of elevated SOR and assessment of concurrent social-­emotional status may minimize their impact on social adaptive behaviors at school age. These studies suggest that with entry into the more stimulating social environment of school, children may have less control over their abilities to process sensory information, manage environmental challenges, and carry out daily activities. Other evidence suggests that early symptoms of mixed regulatory–­sensory processing problems, such as difficulties with crying, sleeping, and feeding, are associated with parental stress (Calkins & House, 2004) and problematic infant–­parent interactions (Lindberg, Bohlin, Hagekull, & Palmerus, 1996). These factors may increase the risk of behavioral dysregulation across childhood by undermining the development of competent self-­regulation (Olson, Bates, Sandy, & Schilling, 2002). The childhood dysregulation syndrome (Althoff, Verhulst, Rettew, Hudziak, & van der Ende, 2010; Holtmann, Becker, Banaschewski, Rothenberger, & Roessner, 2011a; Holtmann et al., 2011b)—operationalized by summing anxious/depressed, impulsive/aggressive, and attentional problem scales from the Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL) (Holtmann et al., 2011b)—predicts negative outcomes in adolescence and adulthood, including anxiety, mood, and disruptive behavior disorders; drug abuse (Althoff et al., 2010); suicidality (Holtmann et al., 2011b); and personality disorders (Halperin, Rucklidge, Powers, Miller, & Newcorn, 2011). The childhood dysregulation profile appears to be stable throughout childhood at 7, 10, and 12 years (Boomsma et al., 2006; Hemmi, Wolke, & Schneider, 2011). The quality of early parent–­child relationships or family organization is one factor through which infant regulatory problems can cascade into later behavioral dysregulation (Kelly, Kelly, & Sacker, 2013; Sroufe, 1997; Wolke, Gray, & Mayer, 1994). One study (DeGangi, Breinbauer, Roosevelt, Porges, & Greenspan, 2000) revealed that 95% of children with moderate regulatory disorders subsequently developed motor, language, cognitive, and relationship problems. There is growing evidence that sensory processing disorders in children predict behavioral and emotional problems in childhood (Briggs-­Gowan, Carter, Bosson-­Heenan, Guyer, & Horwitz, 2006; DeGangi et al., 1993, 2000). In the follow-­up study by Maestro et al. (2012), children who had sensory processing regulatory disorders in early childhood had heterogeneous trajectories in school age: Approximately 40% showed no impairment; more than 20% had anxiety disorders, language disorders, and ADHD; and 35% had developed severe ASDs and intellectual disabilities. Retrospectively, this subgroup had worse scores on

chunks.heading: 52 Infancy and Early Childhood
chunks.preview: 52 Infancy and Early Childhood the CBCL, the Parenting Stress Index, and the parent–­infant relationship as assessed by Axis II of DC:0–3R. These different trajectories can be explained by a number of considerations, including the severity of the initial disorder and the impact of the early parent–­...
chunks.text: 52 Infancy and Early Childhood the CBCL, the Parenting Stress Index, and the parent–­infant relationship as assessed by Axis II of DC:0–3R. These different trajectories can be explained by a number of considerations, including the severity of the initial disorder and the impact of the early parent–­child relationship, which may mitigate or compensate for the initial vulnerability or exacerbate the child’s difficulties until they lead to emotional and behavioral dysregulation. Most studies have investigated predictive associations between regulatory–­ sensory processing disorders and behavioral disturbance assessed at only one time point; hence, the extent to which early disorders are developmental precursors of traitlike behavioral dysregulation over time is unclear. Some longitudinal studies confirm the persistence of sensory integration deficits and poor motor organization in more complex clinical pictures of hyperactivity, attentional disorders, and problems of emotional and behavioral regulation, such as depression, sleeping disorders, somatic complaints, and aggressive behaviors (Briggs-­Gowan et al., 2006; DeGangi et al., 1993, 2000). The cascade model of development proposed by Masten and Cicchetti (2010) offers insight into how early regulatory–­sensory processing disorders may culminate in different phenotypes at different ages (Shrout & Bolger, 2002). At this point, we need (1) a valid operational classification system to archive sufficiently homogeneous clinical populations; (2) single-­case studies to explore how constitutional impairments affect children’s trajectories as they progressively interfere with developmental tasks (e.g., a toddler who is easily overwhelmed by sensory stimulation may develop apparent separation anxiety when entering preschool); and (3) evidence on how environmental variables (early interactions, parenting style), including specific treatments, may correct or accentuate a child’s vulnerabilities. Therapeutic Implications Self-­regulation includes children’s abilities in relating to others, preverbal reciprocal affective communication, using symbols to represent emotional conflicts, and verbal competence to express subjectivity. Achieving self-­regulation generates self-­confidence and so may be considered a biological root of self-­esteem. A child’s biological endowment also has a role in shaping parental thoughts and beliefs, which, influenced by social and cultural contexts, will determine caregiving style. Treatments of regulatory–­ sensory processing disorders should be aimed at helping children cope with their vulnerabilities and increase their self-­confidence, and at supporting and shaping the social and nonsocial environments to enhance their adaptive skills. From a sensory integrative perspective, it is necessary (1) to help parents and caregivers understand sensory contributions to a child’s behavior; (2) to adjust environments to fit the child’s special needs; and (3) to provide, when necessary, specialized intervention designed to correct identified problems. The aims of treatment are to foster successful, satisfactory relationships between the child and significant others and to increase children’s perception of the impacts of their intentions. For an overview of general principles of treatment, see the comprehensive, developmental, biopsychosocial intervention program of Greenspan and Wieder (2003). The occupational therapy field has developed effective behavioral treatments that focus on specific sensory processing dimensions. The Sensory Therapies and Research (STAR) model (Miller, Coll, & Schoen, 2007) includes the following elements:

chunks.heading: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 53
chunks.preview: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 53 • An intensive model (three to five times a week) for a set, relatively short time (e.g., 20–40 sessions). • A strong emphasis on parent education (approximately one of every five or six sessions with parents only). • A multidisciplinary approach, involv...
chunks.text: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 53 • An intensive model (three to five times a week) for a set, relatively short time (e.g., 20–40 sessions). • A strong emphasis on parent education (approximately one of every five or six sessions with parents only). • A multidisciplinary approach, involving a pediatrician, psychologist, speech–­ language therapist, and occupational therapist. • An intervention model focused on arousal regulation, relationships and engagement, and sensory integration. In this approach, repairing sensory or motor functioning is a means to an end. Overall treatment goals include social participation, self-­regulation, and self-­ confidence. Early detection of regulatory–­ sensory processing disorders can prevent mismatches between a child’s constitutional response to sensory stimuli and parents’ possible maladaptive interactive patterns; a difficult child may undermine parents’ self-­confidence and thus increase their anxiety, making caregiving more difficult and intensifying the child’s regulation difficulties. To assess the role of sensory integration in interactive patterns, a practitioner should observe the child in different contexts, including with parents. Exploration of the parents’ representations and interpretations of the child’s behaviors and states is fundamental to effective interventions. Checklists and parents’ reports should be integrated with clinical observations to provide measures to evaluate outcome. Clinical Illustration Matthew is 2 years, 10 months old. At the beginning of the first year of kindergarten, the teacher had observed some “autistic traits” in his behavior: poor eye contact, repetitiveness in games, narrow interests, discontinuity and fluctuation in participation in the environment, aggressive reactions toward peers, inconsistency in responding to adult stimuli. These behaviors do not, however, manifest themselves in individual relationships with adults. Matthew was born from a second pregnancy with placental abruption in the second month, nausea, and maternal back pain throughout the pregnancy. He had normal motor development but a delay in speech. The mother reports that Matthew has started to talk in the last few months and is not yet potty trained. She notes that Matthew tends to be negative, angry, stubborn, and obsessive in his relationships. He is sometimes aggressive toward his sister. These behaviors are a source of concern for his mother, as they remind her of the problems she had in her childhood relationship with her younger brother. Matthew is a beautiful, chubby child with an open, sweet smile and a bright gaze. At the start of the consultation, he sits at the table waiting for something to happen, looks at his mother and the clinician, and smiles several times intensely. When asked to take toys, he says “no” and smiles. He looks a little inhibited and embarrassed. After some hesitation, he begins to explore the different toys, sometimes naming them. He also seems very attracted to the animal box. He makes two dinosaurs talk by making noises; he keeps pulling out other objects, Lego pieces, a little too quickly, but he is very smiley and interactive with the clinician. He also accepts the clinician’s suggestion to put something away. He asks how toys work, which he accompanies with onomatopoeic sounds; he takes the ambulance, moves it while simulating its sound, and

chunks.heading: 54 Infancy and Early Childhood
chunks.preview: 54 Infancy and Early Childhood puts a helicopter in it. Gradually, he seems to need a higher level of arousal: his voice becomes louder and more excited, as if he is seeking acoustic sensations. When the clinician calls him by name, he does not turn around; he is very absorbed in his game. The mothe...
chunks.text: 54 Infancy and Early Childhood puts a helicopter in it. Gradually, he seems to need a higher level of arousal: his voice becomes louder and more excited, as if he is seeking acoustic sensations. When the clinician calls him by name, he does not turn around; he is very absorbed in his game. The mother, evidently trapped in her internal representation of her son, continues to talk about the child’s autistic symptoms, while in fact he is open and interactive. On developmental testing, Matthew showed an age profile of 34 months, with a decline in language (20 months). In summary, Matthew has some features of sensory disorder, particularly the sensory-­seeking pattern. There is also a rather rigid maternal presentation, which interferes with the mother’s relationship with the child. IEC03 Anxiety Disorders Anxiety is a normal emotional state with an adaptive role in human development, serving the purpose of self-­protective action. Infants and young children display normative patterns of anxiety, as well as atypical excessive, misplaced, or persistent forms that can interfere with daily functioning and healthy development. Anxiety is closely related to evolutionarily based mammalian fear and can alert a child to threatening or dangerous situations. In typical development, fear of strangers and distress at separation from caregivers between ages 6 and 12 months are adaptive responses to potential dangers, such as unfamiliar adults or being alone. Such anxieties, which decrease for most children by about 30 months of age, reflect a child’s attachment to caregivers and ability to distinguish loved ones from strangers. Anxiety may become symptomatic when it limits developmentally adaptive behavior. In this case, it leads a child to develop specific fears or excessive worries, obsessions, or preoccupations, as well as atypical behaviors: excessive tantrums, compulsions, physical overactivity, or avoidance (including mutism, clinging, and poor self-­regulation). Children may also express anxiety by developing somatic symptoms, such as aches and pains or alterations in appetite. “Good-­enough” caregiving can help an infant to establish a secure attachment base from which to explore novel experiences. Insensitive relationships with primary caregivers may evolve into insecure relationships, difficulty in emotional regulation, and anxiety symptoms. Infants and children may be even less able than adults to identify the source(s) of their anxiety and may be quite distressed when unable to explore and seek new experiences because of disabling anxiety. It is well documented that up to 80% of anxious children have anxious parents, implicating both heredity and environment. Presenting Pattern Early in infancy, with a “good-­enough parent,” who can appreciate a child’s emotional life and reflect it to the child, typical development includes attachment to primary caregivers and the attainment of an awareness of self and other, a sense of connectedness to caregivers, and a wariness of others, expressed in normative separation anxiety. A 5- to 8-month-old will express distress when separated from a primary caregiver. Atypically, a child may express more generalized distress and be either unable to separate from a parent or inclined to separate too easily. Their less developed capacity for language and abstract reasoning may lead children to express distress through excessive tantrums, agitation, avoidance, and worries. Anxious behaviors in children

chunks.heading: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 55
chunks.preview: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 55 of all ages may be differentiated into “fight, flight, or freeze” reactions. As they grow, some children develop counterphobic reactions: To alleviate the anxiety they cannot control via mastery, they approach or provoke what they fear. The fight-or-­f l...
chunks.text: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 55 of all ages may be differentiated into “fight, flight, or freeze” reactions. As they grow, some children develop counterphobic reactions: To alleviate the anxiety they cannot control via mastery, they approach or provoke what they fear. The fight-or-­f light system activation may lead to a kind of hyperfocus or hypervigilance, which can cause even very young infants to be on near-­constant alert. Functioning at this heightened level can greatly impede interactions and experiences necessary for normal development. Children may present with various disruptions in their development and in their interactions with others, such as feeding and eating disorders, sleep difficulties, apprehensiveness about playing with peers, overly aggressive responses to perceived threats in play, school refusal, difficulty separating from parents to participate in activities, somatic preoccupations, and elimination disorders. Childhood anxiety disorders commonly present first as separation anxiety and specific phobias, then by the onset of social phobia. This may follow the development of insecure attachment patterns or follow strain that emerges when a highly anxious or depressed mother (or other primary caregiver) cannot sufficiently soothe or comfort the child, or when a temperamental mismatch leads to a caregiver’s poor understanding of the child’s needs. Because of the irrational, fantasy-­based nature of anxiety, parental attempts to respond with rational explanations to a child’s anxiety are generally fruitless. Often, they are also anxious and respond in a hypervigilant or hypersensitive fashion on the basis of their internal experiences of their children and their sense of themselves as “failing in parenting.” DC:0–5 describes the following key features for the most important anxiety disorders in early infancy: • Separation anxiety disorder. The child may worry to the point of being reluctant or refusing to separate from home or family, go to school, be alone, or sleep alone. The child may also experience repeated nightmares and may complain of physical symptoms such as headaches, stomachaches, nausea, or vomiting. • Social anxiety disorder (social phobia). The child exhibits marked and persistent fear of one or more social or performance situations involving unfamiliar people or possible scrutiny by others. • Generalized anxiety disorder. The child experiences excessive anxiety that is not linked to any one event or situation. He or she feels significantly distressed over any number of things—­from the health of family members to performance at school and future events. • Selective mutism. The child refuses to speak and communicate effectively in select social settings, such as school. Children with this disorder are able to speak and communicate in settings where they are comfortable, secure, and relaxed. Almost all children with selective mutism also have social phobia or social anxiety. As with mutism, research confirms a high co-­occurrence of these disorders. • Inhibition to novelty disorder. The child shows an overall and pervasive difficulty in approaching new situations, toys, activities, and persons. He or she exhibits fearful symptoms in the presence of novel/unfamiliar objects, people, and situations, with freezing, withdrawn, or pervasive negative affects. This disorder appears to increase risk for later emerging anxiety disorders.

chunks.heading: 56 Infancy and Early Childhood
chunks.preview: 56 Infancy and Early Childhood A clinician should first assess whether a processing impairment, such as sensory, motor, or other processing difficulties, interferes with a child’s ability to deal with unknown situations. When this is the case, an infant may be irritable, anxious, and overly responsi...
chunks.text: 56 Infancy and Early Childhood A clinician should first assess whether a processing impairment, such as sensory, motor, or other processing difficulties, interferes with a child’s ability to deal with unknown situations. When this is the case, an infant may be irritable, anxious, and overly responsive, but anxiety is primarily related to a regulatory–­sensory processing disorder. Second, the clinician should assess whether anxiety is primarily related to expected developmental transitions or to a task the child is having difficulty mastering (developmental anxiety disorder). Finally, anxiety related to the infant–­caregiver relationship should be considered. To achieve better accuracy in the clinical evaluation, one must consider not only symptoms but also impairments in emotional functioning and in the child’s subjective and interpersonal experience. Developmental Pathways A review of the main factors that may predispose or contribute to the onset and persistence of anxiety syndromes suggests multiple developmental pathways and complex transactions among biological, genetic, environmental, and relational factors. There is moderately strong evidence that a temperamentally inhibited infant or toddler tends to become a separation-­anxious or phobic child, who may evolve into a socially anxious child and then a generally anxious adult. The anxious temperament may reflect a biological predisposition to low-­threshold anxiety in response to environmental stressors, behaviorally expressed as a slow-to-warm-up, cautious, hypervigilant pattern, or, in a more extreme presentation, as behavioral inhibition. The expression of anxiety reflects the developmental stage of the child. The preverbal child whose experience of the world is less defined and comprehensible will present with irritability, tantrums, disruptions in eating or sleeping, and poor engagement in age-­appropriate activities. As the child matures and develops more complex verbal skills, he or she will be increasingly able to describe worries, fears, and phobias, and may become more avoidant and regressed. Parsing out contributing factors, both constitutional–­maturational and interactional–­situational, is critical in developing a diagnosis. A secure attachment to a primary caregiver has been found to build resiliency and provide infants and toddlers the resources to respond to stressful experiences. Conversely, insecure–­resistant attachment may be a risk factor for depressive and anxiety disorders in children and adolescents. Preschool children with insecure–­resistant attachment are tense, anxious, fearful, easily frustrated, passive, and oriented to adults. They are not able to make use of any comfort and reassurance they are offered, and they often show anger, emotional distress, and anxiety. Insecure attachment can intensify avoidant traits, especially in a sensitive child, who may avoid feared situations or react to frustration with oppositionality. There is considerable evidence for the increased risk of anxiety in a child whose mother is anxious. Caregivers whose overall attitude is not sufficiently attuned to their particular child or who, because of their own difficulties, cannot provide opportunities for their children to develop self-­regulatory capacities, who cannot create an environment that feels safe and secure to their children, will likely instill anxiety. A dysfunctional parenting style, characterized by preoccupation, intrusiveness, criticism, “catastrophism,” poor emotional warmth, negative affect, and a tendency to inhibit a child’s initiatives hinders emotional development toward autonomy and may lead to anxiety disorders.

chunks.heading: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 57
chunks.preview: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 57 Anxiety disorders represent a significant clinical risk for a psychopathological continuity in subsequent phases of development. Evidence for homotypic continuity was found for separation, social, and generalized anxiety in both genders, as well as for p...
chunks.text: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 57 Anxiety disorders represent a significant clinical risk for a psychopathological continuity in subsequent phases of development. Evidence for homotypic continuity was found for separation, social, and generalized anxiety in both genders, as well as for panic disorder in girls. Concerning heterotypic continuity, the presence of anxiety disorders during childhood is related to the development of depressive disorders during adolescence and adulthood (Copeland, Shanahan, Costello, & Angold, 2009). These disorders have a strong heterotypic continuity, especially among girls, even when researchers control for comorbidity between the two (Costello, Mustillo, Erkkanli, Keeler, & Angold, 2003; Cummings, Caporino, & Kendall, 2014). Research suggests that anxiety symptoms are an age-­dependent manifestation of the same pathological pattern found in depression (see Speranza, Liotti, Spoletini, & Fortunato, 2023). Therapeutic Implications Given that anxiety in later childhood and adulthood is often preceded by early childhood anxiety or temperamental predisposition to it, early identification and intervention may be protective. Given the role of the environment and the caregiver in helping a child to develop self-­regulatory capacities, there is a fine opportunity for therapeutic intervention. Anxious children benefit greatly from a safe, soothing environment—­ one that includes stable and reliable rituals such as waking up each morning to the same music, or going to sleep every evening to a familiar ritual such as bath, reading, and lights out. They can benefit from understanding where their fears come from and become better able to differentiate fantasy from reality. They also benefit from caregivers’ ability to help them with the separation process. Both through the relationship with a therapist, who can provide a new model for interacting, and through parent–­ child treatment, much symptom relief and restoration of a healthy developmental trajectory can be attained. Clinical Illustration Nina was a 2-year-old girl whose immigrant parents brought her to the hospital because she refused to eat. The parents described a normal pregnancy and delivery, followed by typical growth and development. Nina had nursed and made the transition to foods with no difficulties. She was described as a happy, bright child until the last few months. Her medical history was notable for a gastrointestinal infection 3 months earlier, during which time she had been vomiting and quite miserable for a few days. One week before admission, she became ill again with a viral illness and nausea. She stopped eating and expressed a fear that she would vomit again. Her social history was notable for her having become an older sister at about the time of the first illness. During her hospitalization, she continued to refuse to eat and ultimately was given a G-tube because of excessive weight loss. Nina seemed quite content to be fed by tube. On discharge, she began twice-­weekly therapy, coincident with weekly parental therapy. Because she was initially quite resistant to separating from her mother, the mother would leave the baby with a sitter and come in with Nina. As treatment progressed, it became clear that although she was unwilling to say so, Nina had a great deal of anger and resentment toward this new sibling and the parents who had brought her into their home. Through play, which was first aggressive and then more verbally expressive, she was able to express some of this anger. She became more able to leave her mother and participate in school and other activities, and she began to eat. The G-tube was removed.

chunks.heading: 58 Infancy and Early Childhood
chunks.preview: 58 Infancy and Early Childhood IEC04 Disorders of Emotional Range and Stability Infants experience and express emotion from birth (Brazelton, 1979; Oster, 2005), in the service of establishing a communicative, cooperative relationship with their caregivers (Trevarthen, 1993). Because they feel their...
chunks.text: 58 Infancy and Early Childhood IEC04 Disorders of Emotional Range and Stability Infants experience and express emotion from birth (Brazelton, 1979; Oster, 2005), in the service of establishing a communicative, cooperative relationship with their caregivers (Trevarthen, 1993). Because they feel their emotions intensely, infants and young children are reliant on their primary caregivers for help regulating states of both over- and underarousal (Fonagy, Gergely, Jurist, & Target, 2002; Hobson, 2002; Schore & Schore, 2007; Stern, 1985). From early infancy into the preschool years, children experience a gradual increase in their emotional range (differentiation) and stability. A 2- to 3-month-old infant shows global emotions—­joy, distress, rage, fear— and can shift rapidly from one state to another. A highly distressed baby may be quickly consoled by a sensitive parent, and will soon be smiling and babbling. Infants who feel a caregiver’s efforts to apprehend and scaffold their emotional experience and give it meaning grow into toddlers who can differentiate and express more nuanced emotions (Schore, 1994). A 16-month-old baby may evidence warmth, delight, curiosity, excitement, caution, fear, annoyance, assertiveness, sympathy, sadness, and other more subtle emotional states. The normally developing toddler experiences and expresses emotions in a relatively predictable and moderated manner in relationship to expectable experiences (e.g., happiness when a smiling parent appears, and anger when a favorite toy is put on the shelf because it is bedtime). A child’s capacity to experience the full range of age-­expected emotions and to regulate them in stable ways is an important foundation of that child’s future social and intellectual development (Lyons-Ruth, 1999). Because of their potential long-term consequences, it is important to recognize difficulties with age-­expected emotional range and stability. To determine whether a child has difficulty with age-­appropriate emotional range and stability, a clinician must have a road map of expected emotional development in infants and young children. Their emotional maturation can be observed in their body expressions, interactions with others, and age-­appropriate capacity to play (Hobson, 2002; Stern, 1985). Earliest expressions of emotion relate to bodily states (satiated, dry, warm, not tired) and social-­emotional experiences (security, pleasure, playfulness) with caregivers. Negative emotion in infancy appears to be largely responsive (e.g., to the pain of hunger or to feelings of not being safe when put down too quickly). More negative emotions emerge as children encounter limitations inherent in, or set by, the realities around them in relation to others and to their own capacities. They may feel anger, jealousy, rivalry, competition, aggression, and a wish for control and power over others. They may feel humiliation at their own lack of prowess and success. These “negative” emotions can challenge caregiver–­child interactions if the caregiver does not understand the emotional underpinnings of the child’s behaviors and if the relationship does not embrace the full range of human feelings (Woods & Pretorius, 2011). As a child masters reality testing, accommodating to compromise in line with possibilities, and reaches higher developmental levels, emotions expand to friendship, loyalty, justice, and morality. They become increasingly differentiated as a child is able to describe complex feelings, including ambivalence and conflicting emotions and the reasons for them. A 2-year-old boy may hit his baby sister. At 3, he may make a verbal claim on her toy. At 4, he may say that his mommy never spends time with him, that the baby always comes first, yet note his love for the baby. Between 5 and 6, he may be protective and helpful, but also able to talk about his jealousy. It is thus the lack of normal differentiation, range, and stability of emotions that characterizes disorders of emotional range and stability. In contrast to children with

chunks.heading: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 59
chunks.preview: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 59 developmental anxieties, children who are appropriately given this diagnosis present with a narrow range of affects or interests, and their experience may lack the fullblown intensity of anxiety reactions. Presenting Pattern Disorders in this category ma...
chunks.text: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 59 developmental anxieties, children who are appropriately given this diagnosis present with a narrow range of affects or interests, and their experience may lack the fullblown intensity of anxiety reactions. Presenting Pattern Disorders in this category may manifest themselves in a variety of ways.
