chunks.heading: 122 Infancy and Early Childhood
chunks.preview: 122 Infancy and Early Childhood pattern and progressively assessed through representational measurement of the internal working models), and quality of affect regulation associated with it. The standard tools for assessing attachment (the Strange Situation, the Attachment Q-Sort, narrative story ste...
chunks.text: 122 Infancy and Early Childhood pattern and progressively assessed through representational measurement of the internal working models), and quality of affect regulation associated with it. The standard tools for assessing attachment (the Strange Situation, the Attachment Q-Sort, narrative story stem techniques) provide diagnostic indicators of how the child regulates a sense of security and confidence in self and others, the quality of the child’s affective relationships and internal working models, and the ways these influence affect-­ regulation styles. As emphasized throughout this section of the chapter, the child–­caregiver relationship is characterized by many dimensions (intersubjectivity, intentional communication, play, learning, etc.). “Attachment” refers to the specific and circumscribed aspect of the relationship that is involved with making the child feel safe, secure, and protected. The child uses the caregiver as a secure base from which to explore and, when necessary, as a haven of safety and a source of comfort. Typically, the quality of attachment is largely determined by the caregiver’s response to the infant when the infant’s attachment system is “activated” by internal or external stimuli, such as physical or emotional threats. Attachment is one of the most powerful predictors of a child’s later social and emotional health. A wide range of research has shown that attachment is associated with mental capacities such as affective regulation, mentalization, reflective functioning, differentiation, relationship and intimacy, self-­esteem, internal experience, defensive functioning, and resiliency (all of which are included on the MA and MC Axes for adolescents and older children, respectively; see Chapters 7 and 3, pp. 413–467 and 151–211). Attachment Prototypes Even if a standardized assessment of attachment is not usually conducted in clinical settings, the clinician should undertake an attachment-­informed assessment— ­one that includes the history of the child’s attachment (available attachment figures, disruptions in care, abandonment, alternate caregivers, etc.) and information about the child’s attachment behaviors (help- or comfort-­seeking behaviors, discrimination of attachment figures, ability to use a caregiver for comfort, ability to explore and play in a new setting, quality of interaction with the caregiver, etc.). Below, patterns of attachment are described as assessed in the Strange Situation procedure (Ainsworth, Blehar, Waters, & Wall, 1978) for a 1-year-old, with some references to later behaviors and internal working models as assessed in reunion procedures and narrative story stems. The clinician may use a 5-point Likert-­t ype scale, with a higher score indicating a better match with the prototype. Secure A 1-year-old child who is securely attached to a parent (or other familiar caregiver) explores freely while the caregiver is present, typically engages with strangers, is often visibly upset when the caregiver departs, and seeks the caregiver upon reunion. Distress about separation often decreases immediately upon reunion, and active exploration starts again. Secure attachment is associated with sensitive caregiving. This attachment strategy is “organized,” because the child has a strategy for engaging with a sensitive caregiver: seeking proximity to and maintaining contact with the caregiver

chunks.heading: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 123
chunks.preview: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 123 until the child feels safe. The balance between attachment and exploration in the child is mirrored by the balance between protectiveness and encouragement of exploration in the caregiver. A 3-year-old securely attached child is able to explore, and to...
chunks.text: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 123 until the child feels safe. The balance between attachment and exploration in the child is mirrored by the balance between protectiveness and encouragement of exploration in the caregiver. A 3-year-old securely attached child is able to explore, and to use pretend play and symbolic communication to convey wishes and feelings. Following a longer separation, the child continues to show a comfortable and intimate relationship with the caregiver, often starting a warm and personal conversation with that person. At the representational level, the child tends to describe doll family interactions as positive, supportive, and warm, and the self as accepted and valued. Narratives are coherent, and attachment stories usually have a positive resolution: The child is looked after, taken care of, and helped. Positively contributing to a narrative story stem, a secure child is able to recognize the existence of a conflict by facing it, and never refers to self or the family as perfect and problem-­free. Insecure–Avoidant A 1-year-old child with the insecure–­avoidant attachment pattern shows little or no distress upon separation, avoids or ignores the caregiver’s efforts to attract attention or establish contact upon reunion, and shows little emotion when the caregiver departs or returns. This apparently unruffled behavior may represent an “organized shift of attention” away from the caregiver’s absence, but it is in fact a mask for distress—­a hypothesis supported by physiological studies demonstrating that while the child shows minimal distress on the outside, heart rate and cortisol level are elevated, indicating internal distress. Avoidance may be linked to a history of interactions in which the caregiver is rejecting of infant attachment signals. In this case, this “organized” strategy leads the child to avoid the caregiver when distressed and to minimize displays of negative emotion. A 3-year-old child with an avoidant pattern tends to be focused on play and seems indifferent to physical and psychological intimacy on reunion with the caregiver. In story stem assessments, the child has difficulty getting involved or interested in the stories, especially if they concern attachment issues. The child doll figure depicted in the narratives is often described as isolated and unloved, and as frequently punished. At other times, conflicts in the stories are simply not accepted; as a consequence, no action is taken toward possible solutions. Overall, for a child assessed as avoidant, issues related to attachment seem to raise emotional difficulties that activate defensive mechanisms. Insecure–Ambivalent/Resistant A 1-year-old child with an insecure–­ambivalent/resistant attachment pattern typically shows little exploration (in the Strange Situation) and is wary of strangers, even when the caregiver is present. The child might show intense distress and heightened emotionality during separation, then upon reunion seems unable to be settled by the caregiver. This attachment strategy is assumed to be a response to unpredictably responsive caregiving. Displays of anger or helplessness toward the caregiver on reunion can be regarded as a conditional (“organized”) strategy for maintaining the caregiver’s availability by preemptively taking control of the interaction: Extreme negative emotions draw the attention of an inconsistently responsive caregiver.

chunks.heading: 124 Infancy and Early Childhood
chunks.preview: 124 Infancy and Early Childhood A 3-year-old whose observed pattern is resistant/ambivalent attachment may show expressions of irritation or nervousness on reunion; the child may exaggerate the intimacy with the caregiver and seem dependent on the person, showing a relatively immature stance. Eviden...
chunks.text: 124 Infancy and Early Childhood A 3-year-old whose observed pattern is resistant/ambivalent attachment may show expressions of irritation or nervousness on reunion; the child may exaggerate the intimacy with the caregiver and seem dependent on the person, showing a relatively immature stance. Evidence of ambivalence may be observed in behavior that indicates hostility combined with apparent attempts at intimacy and dependence. Story stems produced by these children are characterized by lack of caregiver accessibility or excessive dependence, and may also suggest feelings of rejection and guilt in the child, who implements coercive actions to obtain support. Emotional expression may be exaggerated or inconsistent. Disorganized/Disoriented In the Strange Situation, a 1-year-old child might display disorganized and/or disoriented behaviors in the caregiver’s presence, suggesting a temporary collapse of behavioral strategies. The child might exhibit a mix of approach and avoidance, or “trance-­like” behaviors, especially upon reunion with the caregiver. This pattern may be linked to maltreatment or to frightening/frightened caregiver behaviors; the child displays behavior consistent with fear of the caregiver. A 3-year-old child may show role-­inverting or “disorganized/controlling” behavior with the caregiver, being either punitive (doing something to humiliate, embarrass, or refuse the caregiver) or caregiving/solicitous (being caring and protective of the caregiver, showing concern or care in a manner suggesting that the caregiver is dependent on the child), with the aim of directing the caregiver’s behaviors. The child might show moderate avoidance (e.g., talking to the caregiver with his or her back turned), as well as subtle or open hostility, and occasionally fear or sadness. Disorganized story stem responses often have hostile or negative themes. The child or a member of the family is described as involved in violent, hostile, or bizarre behaviors. Disorganized attachment in infancy is recognized as a powerful predictor for serious psychopathology and maladjustment in older children and adolescents. Summary of Relational Patterns and Disorders Level of relational patterns and disorders (Table 1.8), corresponding to the total score in Table 1.7, helps the clinician to capture the overall quality of significant relationships and to reflect on where the challenges are and from where they originate. The family history and functioning can be described in the clinical narrative, along with a clinical formulation of how they contribute to the current relationship. Relevant assessment tools are provided to help clinicians organize observations of the child’s relationships and to define a diagnostic profile of relational patterns and disorders. Most Relevant Assessment Tools This section includes rating scales of caregiver–­ child interactions and caregiving patterns, standardized procedures for assessing attachment, and tools for evaluating caregiver states of mind with respect to attachment, affect, and representational states.

chunks.heading: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 125
chunks.preview: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 125 TABLE.1.8. Level of Relational Patterns and Disorders R01. Healthy/Adapted Relational Patterns (range = 36–40) Parent–child relationship is well adapted and clearly promotes the growth of both child and parent. Interactions are enjoyable, and reciprocal...
chunks.text: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 125 TABLE.1.8. Level of Relational Patterns and Disorders R01. Healthy/Adapted Relational Patterns (range = 36–40) Parent–child relationship is well adapted and clearly promotes the growth of both child and parent. Interactions are enjoyable, and reciprocal and synchronous interactions can be observed. The parent is able to fully support the functional ability appropriate to the child’s age. Parental representations of the child are rich, detailed, and flexible, often marked by a moderate or high mentalizing capacity. R02. Adapted Relational Patterns with Some Areas of Difficulty (range = 29–35) Some areas of difficulty are evident, but overall, the parent–child relationship is adaptive and shows minor interference. At times, parent and child may be in conflict, but difficulties do not persist longer than a few days, and the relationship consistently shows adaptive flexibility (e.g., ability to negotiate conflict). The child may show some difficulties (e.g., in separating from the parents), or the parent may appear overprotective, expressing some concerns about the child’s well-being, behavior, or development. In some cases, the child has a mild constitutional difficulty that impairs the ability of the parent to maintain a suitable relationship. Despite these minor difficulties, the parent–child relationship is supportive of the child’s functioning. R03. Moderate Perturbation or Disturbance in Relational Patterns (range = 22–28) A perturbation or disturbance in parent–child relationship is notable but restricted to one domain of functioning (e.g., feeding, play, regulation), or the parent is unable to support two or more areas of the child’s functioning. Consistency is sometimes lacking between parent’s expressed attitudes toward the child and the quality of observed interactions (predictability, reciprocity, or both). Interactions are mildly stimulating and controlling or are lacking in vitality and mutual pleasure. The parent may be moderately insensitive and/or unresponsive to the cues of the child or does not adequately mirror the child’s internal affective states. Resolution of conflict is difficult and may lead to parent and/or child distress. R04. Significant Disturbance in Relational Patterns (range = 15–21) Most interactions between child and parent are conflictual and distressing for both partners. The parent often interferes with the child’s goals and desires or neglects the child’s physical and emotional needs. The parent has difficulty seeing the child as a separate person with individual needs; some domains of functioning (e.g., assertiveness or autonomy) may be thwarted. Conflicts may spread across multiple domains. The child’s range of affective expression may be notably constricted. Affective displays are flat, constricted, characterized by withdrawn and sadness, or interactions are tense, with little sense of relaxed enjoyment or mutuality. R05. Major Impairments in Relational Pattern or Relational Disorders (range = 8–14) These relationships show deeply rooted dysfunctional patterns. They are characterized by interactions that are harsh and abrupt, lacking in emotional reciprocity, and often hostile, angry, or frightening enough for the child to feel in danger. The child may show fearful, vigilant, and avoidant behaviors or may appear submissive, compliant, or defiant. These major impairments undermine the foundation of child’s healthy functioning. Tools for Assessing Caregiver–Infant/Child Interactions Parent–Infant Relationship Global Assessment Scale The Parent–­Infant Relationship Global Assessment Scale (PIR-GAS; Zero to Three, 2005) is a global assessment tool in DC:0–3R. Its aim is to evaluate the quality of the relationship between a parent (or other caregiver) and a child from 0 to 5 years old. Three components are assessed by the PIR-GAS: behavioral quality of parent–­infant interaction, affective tone, and psychological involvement. Ratings take into account the overall functioning of the dyad, including the level of dyadic distress, the adaptive flexibility of both caregiver and child, and the level of conflict and resolution. The

chunks.heading: 126 Infancy and Early Childhood
chunks.preview: 126 Infancy and Early Childhood evaluator assigns a global score on a 90-point scale, from grossly impaired (10) to well-­adapted (100). The scale is primarily a diagnostic tool rather than an evaluation measure. LoTTS Parent–Infant Interaction Coding System The LoTTS Parent–­I nfant Interaction Cod...
chunks.text: 126 Infancy and Early Childhood evaluator assigns a global score on a 90-point scale, from grossly impaired (10) to well-­adapted (100). The scale is primarily a diagnostic tool rather than an evaluation measure. LoTTS Parent–Infant Interaction Coding System The LoTTS Parent–­I nfant Interaction Coding System (LPICS; Beatty et al., 2011) was developed as a brief, simple, easy-to-teach observational measure of parent–­infant interactions during face-to-face free play. It allows for general impressions to be captured along a well-­defined continuum, and also for recording of the frequency of specific key behaviors. The LPICS includes three subjective/global scores that capture key elements of the interaction (responsiveness, sensitivity, and warmth) and four behavioral counts of key parent behaviors (looking, touching, talking to, and smiling at the infant). It was developed to require minimal training and to be maximally useful for clinical rather than research settings; although it is a simplified scale, it seems to be a reliable and valid tool. Crowell Procedure The Crowell procedure (Crowell & Feldman, 1988, 1991; Crowell, Feldman, & ­Ginsberg, 1988) is a method of observing caregiver–­child interactions in a clinical setting. It involves eight episodes designed to elicit behaviors that allow the clinician to focus on the relationship between a child and caregiver in a setting that is unstructured enough to allow for “real-life” or spontaneous interactions. The Crowell procedure requires 45–60 minutes to complete. The episodes include free play, cleanup, bubble-­blowing, four increasingly difficult problem-­solving tasks, and a separation–­ reunion. These episodes allow the clinician to see how comfortable and familiar the child and caregiver are with each other, how the dyad negotiates transitions, ability of members of the dyad to solve problems together, the use of shared affect (positive and negative) to communicate, and attachment behaviors. This procedure has been shown to be related to different types of caregivers’ representations (Aoki, Zeanah, Heller, & Bakshi, 2002; Crowell & Feldman, 1988), to distinguish between clinic-­referred and comparison toddlers (Crowell & Feldman, 1988), to distinguish between delayed and nondelayed toddlers (Crowell & Feldman, 1988), and to be specific for a given dyad (Aoki et al., 2002). Child–Adult Relationship Experimental Index The Child–Adult Relationship Experimental Index (CARE; Crittenden, 2003) is a rating scale used to code the quality of mother–­infant interactions, with a focus on maternal sensitivity and attachment behaviors. This coding procedure is suitable for infants from birth to 36 months of age. Usually 3–5 minutes of free-play interactions are video recorded, and the dyad is provided with toys but not forced to play with them. The CARE Index comprises 52 items organized around seven aspects of dyadic behavior: facial expression, verbal expression, position and body contact, affection, turn-­taking contingencies, control, and choice of activity. Each adult and infant are separately evaluated in these aspects of interactive behavior. For each one, there are three items describing the quality of adult behavior and four describing infant

chunks.heading: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 127
chunks.preview: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 127 behavior. For adults, one item describes sensitive behavior, one describes controlling behavior, and one describes unresponsive behavior. Four items describe the quality of the infant’s interactive behavior—­cooperative, compliant, difficult, passive. A...
chunks.text: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 127 behavior. For adults, one item describes sensitive behavior, one describes controlling behavior, and one describes unresponsive behavior. Four items describe the quality of the infant’s interactive behavior—­cooperative, compliant, difficult, passive. Although the adult is scored separately from the infant, mother and infant are each scored from the other’s perspective. Although developed for research purposes, the CARE Index can be applied to clinical contexts; video recording can be carried out in varied settings, such as laboratory, clinic, or home. Lausanne Trilogue Play The Lausanne Trilogue Play paradigm (Fivaz-­Depeursinge & Corboz-­Warnery, 1999) allows an assessment of coordination between two parents, coordination within the other two dyadic family subunits (mother–­child and father–­child), and the family’s coordination as a whole. The tool was developed to study families through the first year of life. The central element of the method is the Lausanne Trilogue Play situation. Parents and their babies are brought together to play in four configurations: mother–­ infant, father–­infant, mother–­father, and a “three-­together” configuration in which all play together. Tools and Procedures for Assessing Caregiving Patterns Ainsworth Maternal Sensitivity Scale The Ainsworth Maternal Sensitivity Scale (AMSS; Ainsworth, Bell, & Stayton, 1974) is a global measure of maternal sensitivity (i.e., a mother’s accuracy in perceiving and interpreting her infant’s cues, and her ability to react in a timely and appropriate manner). Four aspects of maternal behavior are evaluated: (1) the mother’s awareness of her baby’s signals, (2) accurate interpretation of them, (3) appropriate response, and (4) prompt response. Ratings are made on a 9-point scale of maternal sensitivity, with five anchor points: (1) highly insensitive (3) insensitive, (5) inconsistently sensitive (7) sensitive, (9) highly sensitive. The final score represents the degree to which all four of these components are observed during the interaction. To differentiate mothers of insecure–­avoidant infants from those of insecure–­ambivalent infants, Ainsworth and colleagues (1974) developed three additional scales (cooperation–­interference, acceptance–­rejection, and accessibility–­ignoring). No guidance on the optimal setting in which to observe and record interactions is provided. The AMSS can be applied to contexts of variable lengths and tasks, such as free play, feeding, or teaching interactions. Emotional Availability Scales: Infancy to Early Childhood Version The Emotional Availability Scales: Infancy to Early Childhood Version (EAS; ­Biringen, Robinson, & Emde, 1993, 2000) is a global rating system for assessing dyadic interactions. It measures the emotional availability of the parent to the child and of the child to the parent. The Infancy to Early Childhood Version is for ages 0–4 years. It is an emotion-­focused measure that assesses the overall affective quality of the relationship; it does not quantify distinct behaviors, but it analyzes the interactional style of the dyad. The scales comprises four parental dimensions (sensitivity, structuring,

chunks.heading: 128 Infancy and Early Childhood
chunks.preview: 128 Infancy and Early Childhood nonintrusiveness, and non-­hostility) and two infant dimensions (responsiveness and involvement). Data are collected with a video recording of at least 20 minutes of interaction, though the EAS has been used for shorter time periods. Each of the six dimensions is code...
chunks.text: 128 Infancy and Early Childhood nonintrusiveness, and non-­hostility) and two infant dimensions (responsiveness and involvement). Data are collected with a video recording of at least 20 minutes of interaction, though the EAS has been used for shorter time periods. Each of the six dimensions is coded on a 9-, 7-, or 5-point Likert-­t ype scale. Frightening/Frightened Coding System The Frightening/Frightened (FR) Coding System (Hesse & Main, 2006; Main & Hesse, 1992a, 1992b) is a scale intended to examine parental behaviors thought to be linked with a parent’s unresolved loss or trauma of the past. The coding system can be applied to the observation of either parent–­infant free-play interactions or structured interactions in different contexts, such as a laboratory or home setting. This coding system comprises six subcategories of FR behaviors. The first three subcategories are seen as the primary forms of FR behaviors, as experienced directly by the infant: frightening/threatening behaviors (e.g., looming over or assuming attack postures toward the child); frightened behaviors (e.g., frightened facial expressions, pulling or backing away from the infant); and dissociated behaviors (e.g., stilling; freezing; sudden changes in voice, mood, or state). The other three subcategories are considered secondary, as they are not seen as overtly frightening to the infant: timid/deferential behaviors (e.g., treating the child as an attachment figure, submissive behavior to the infant); sexualized behavior (e.g., extended kissing, sexualized or romantic caressing); and disorganized/disoriented behaviors (e.g., sudden change of affect, contradictory signals, limpness of movement). The level of FR behavior is scored for each occurrence of such a behavior on a 9-point scale, and an overall rating is given to the entire segment of observation, based on the highest score of all occurrences in that segment. Scores above 5 are classified as FR. Atypical Maternal Behavior Instrument for Assessment and Classification The Atypical Maternal Behavior Instrument for Assessment and Classification (AMBIANCE; Bronfman, Madigan, & Lyons-Ruth, 2008; Bronfman, Parsons, & LyonsRuth, 1993) is a coding scheme that expands on the FR Coding System of Main and Hesse (1992b) and is designed to assess atypical caregiver-­to-­infant behaviors that are linked with disorganized attachment in infancy. It was originally designed to assess atypical caregiver behaviors directed toward 2- to 18-month-olds, but it has since been used with caregivers of 4-month-old infants and 24-month-old toddlers. The AMBIANCE involves recording the number of atypical behaviors displayed by a caregiver during interactions with the infant on five dimensions (affective communication errors, role–­boundary confusion, disorganized/disoriented behaviors, negative/intrusive behavior, and withdrawal). Behaviors on each dimension are coded, and an overall score for the level of disruption on a 7-point scale is assigned. The caregiver’s behavior can be classified as disrupted or not disrupted in communication with the infant. Disconnected and Extremely Insensitive Parenting Measure The Disconnected and Extremely Insensitive Parenting Measure (DIP; Out, Bakermans-­ Kranenburg, & van IJzendoorn, 2009) is a coding system for maternal behavior. It encompasses two dimensions: disconnected behavior and extremely insensitive

chunks.heading: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 129
chunks.preview: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 129 behavior. The first dimension, disconnected parental behavior, consists of adapted and rearranged items from Main and Hesse’s (1992a, 1992b) coding instrument and includes five categories of parental behavior: frightening and threatening behavior; behav...
chunks.text: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 129 behavior. The first dimension, disconnected parental behavior, consists of adapted and rearranged items from Main and Hesse’s (1992a, 1992b) coding instrument and includes five categories of parental behavior: frightening and threatening behavior; behaviors indicating fear of the child; dissociative behaviors indicative of absorption or intrusion of an altered state of awareness; interacting with the child in a timid, submissive, and/or deferential manner; sexualized/romantic behaviors; and disoriented/disorganized behaviors. Each behavior is evaluated according to specific criteria that yield a score. The second dimension encompasses two forms of extreme parental insensitivity: parental withdrawal and neglect; and intrusive, negative, aggressive, or otherwise harsh parental behaviors. The evaluation is based on the duration, frequency, quality, and severity of the behaviors, and takes into account the context in which the behavior occurs. This dimension is an adaptation of a selection of items from the AMBIANCE. Discrete disconnected and extremely insensitive behaviors are coded on a 9-point scale every time they occur, and for both dimensions, a final score is assigned. Coparenting and Family Rating Scale The Coparenting and Family Rating Scale (CFRS; McHale, Kuersten-­ Hogan, & Lauretti, 2000) is a measure developed to assess seven dimensions of coparenting and family processes from video-­recorded family play sessions. The CFRS comprises, first, four dimensions of coparenting rated on a 5-point Likert-­t ype scale: competition, cooperation, verbal sparring, and coparental warmth. The competition dimension assesses the frequency with which parents interfere in each other’s interactions with the child (e.g., by overriding each other’s suggestions or distracting the child). The cooperation dimension measures the degree of support and collaboration between parents. The verbal sparring dimension evaluates the frequency and degree of parents’ sarcastic and/ or hostile remarks toward each other, ranging from mild teasing to explicit criticism or derogation. The coparental warmth dimension assesses the frequency and intensity of positive affect between parents (e.g., joking, warm glances, and verbal or physical affection). A fifth dimension indicates which family member predominantly shapes the direction of family play, ranging from (1) child-­centered to (5) parent-­centered; a sixth dimension measures the frequency and intensity of positive affect between each parent and the child, ranging from (1) low to (7) high parent–­child warmth. Finally, a dimension of parent–­child investment evaluates how active, engaged, interested, and attentive each parent appears with the child, ranging from complete disengagement (1) to overinvolvement (7). Procedures for Assessing Child Attachment Strange Situation The Strange Situation (Ainsworth et al., 1978) is a semistructured laboratory procedure designed to elicit and categorize attachment behaviors of children between ages 12 and 18 months toward a primary caregiver. The infant’s behavior is observed during different episodes involving separations and reunions with the caregiver, and during interaction with a friendly but unfamiliar female stranger. Each episode lasts 3 minutes. The infant’s behavior is observed in terms of separation anxiety, willingness to explore, stranger anxiety, and reunion behaviors. The infant’s reactions to the episodes can be placed into one of three organized attachment groups: secure

chunks.heading: 130 Infancy and Early Childhood
chunks.preview: 130 Infancy and Early Childhood (B), avoidant (A), or resistant (C). Later work by Main and Solomon (1986) defined a fourth group of disorganized (D) children, who showed inconsistent and confused behaviors. Attachment Q‐Sort The Attachment Q-Sort (AQS; Vaughn & Waters, 1990; Waters & Deane, 1985) i...
chunks.text: 130 Infancy and Early Childhood (B), avoidant (A), or resistant (C). Later work by Main and Solomon (1986) defined a fourth group of disorganized (D) children, who showed inconsistent and confused behaviors. Attachment Q‐Sort The Attachment Q-Sort (AQS; Vaughn & Waters, 1990; Waters & Deane, 1985) is an alternative measure to the Strange Situation, designed to assess the security of infants’ and toddlers’ attachment to their caregivers. It can be used with older children (between 12 and 48 months of age) and is not as intrusive and stressful as the Strange Situation. The AQS consists of a large number of cards (75, 90, or 100), on each of which a specific behavioral characteristic of children is described. After several hours of observation of a child and caregiver in the home setting, the observer sorts the cards into several piles (the number of piles and the number of cards that can be put in each pile are fixed), from “most descriptive of the subject” to “least descriptive of the subject.” At the end of this process, a score for attachment security can be derived. The AQS can be considered an adequate assessment tool of attachment because it appears to show sufficient validity. Narrative Story Stem Techniques Young children’s representations can be assessed with narrative story stem completion tasks. Protocols provide structured story stems depicting typical family situations and conflicts, with the aid of doll figures. Children are asked to respond to a set of narrative beginnings of stories, each with an inherent dilemma. Two protocols were originally developed by Bretherton and colleagues: the Attachment Story Completion Task (Bretherton, Ridgeway, & Cassidy, 1990b) and the MacArthur Story Stem Battery (Bretherton & Oppenheim, 2003; Bretherton, Oppenheim, Buchsbaum, Emde, & the MacArthur Narrative Group, 1990a). Many other researchers then developed other methods, such as the Story Stem Completion Task (Hodges, Hillman, & Steele, 2004) and the Manchester Child Attachment Task (Goldwyn, Stanley, Smith, & Green, 2000; Green, Stanley, Smith, & Goldwyn, 2000). All these measures allow for attachment classifications and provide a clinical picture of a child’s mental representations of self and relationships. Interviews for Assessing Caregiver Representations Adult Attachment Interview The Adult Attachment Interview (AAI; George, Kaplan, & Main, 1984, 1985, 1996) is a semistructured interview designed to capture an adult’s experience of attachment with childhood caregivers and current state of mind concerning attachment. The interview takes 1–2 hours to administer. Interviewees are asked to describe their early childhood relationship with their parents, to provide adjectives and examples to describe this relationship, and to recall early separations, losses, and means of comfort seeking. They are also asked to reflect on the reasons behind their parents’ behavior and to describe changes in the parental relationship over time. Category classification on the AAI is based on continuous 9-point scales ranging from low (1) to high (9). The scales relating to the inferred experiences are loving, rejecting, involving/reversing,

chunks.preview: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 131 pressure to achieve, and neglecting. The scales relating to state of mind with respect to attachment figures are idealization, anger, and derogation. Other scales concern overall states of mind relevant to attachment, such as overall derogation of attac...
chunks.text: Mental Health and Developmental Disorders 131 pressure to achieve, and neglecting. The scales relating to state of mind with respect to attachment figures are idealization, anger, and derogation. Other scales concern overall states of mind relevant to attachment, such as overall derogation of attachment, insistence on lack of recall, metacognitive monitoring, passivity of discourse, fear of loss, unresolved loss, unresolved trauma, coherence of mind, and coherence of transcript. After the scoring of the transcript, a rating of secure–­autonomous, insecure–­ dismissing, insecure–­preoccupied, unresolved with regard to past loss or trauma, or cannot classify is assigned. Working Model of the Child Interview The Working Model of the Child Interview (WMCI; Zeanah, Benoit, Hirshberg, Barton, & Regan, 1994) is a 1-hour semistructured interview developed to evaluate a parent’s perceptions and subjective experience of an infant’s individual characteristics and of the parent’s relationship with the infant. Initially developed for research purposes, it is now also used in clinical settings. The interview includes questions about the pregnancy, delivery, the infant’s development, the infant’s personality and behavior, and the parent’s relationship with the infant. The interview transcription can be rated on eight 5-point rating scales (richness in perception, openness to change, intensity of involvement, coherence, caregiving sensitivity, acceptance, infant difficulty, and fear for infant safety). From these ratings, parents’ representations can be classified as balanced, disengaged, or distorted. The affective tone of the parent’s responses can also be rated (i.e., joy, pride, anger, guilt, and other emotions). Parent Development Interview—Revised The Parent Development Interview—­Revised (PDI-R; Aber, Slade, Berger, Bresgi, & Kaplan, 1985; Slade, Aber, Bresgi, Berger, & Kaplan, 2004a) is a 45-item, semistructured clinical interview designed to elicit parents’ representations of their child, their perceptions of themselves as parents, and their experiences of their relationship. The interview evokes current experiences and memories. It can be used to explore the parent–­infant relationship and the parent’s mental representations of the relationship’s different aspects. In the interview, a parent is asked to portray the child’s behavior, thoughts, and feelings in various situations, and to provide examples. Parents are also asked to describe responses to the child in these situations, to describe themselves as parents, and to discuss the emotions they experience in the parental role. The interview takes approximately 90 minutes to administer. Training is required to be able to code the interview. Reflective Functioning in the Parent Development Interview “Reflective functioning” (RF) refers to individuals’ capacities to understand their own and others’ behavior in terms of the mental states underlying that behavior. It includes the capacity to understand the intersubjective nature of mental states. Slade, Bernbach, Grienenberger, Levy, and Locker (2004b) developed an addendum to the RF coding manual developed by Fonagy, Target, Steele, and Steele (1998) for use with the AAI. RF on the PDI is assessed in four broad categories (awareness of the nature of mental states, the explicit effort to tease out mental states underlying behavior, recognizing developmental aspects of mental states, and mental states in relation to the

chunks.heading: 132 Infancy and Early Childhood
chunks.preview: 132 Infancy and Early Childhood interviewer). It is an 11-point scale ranging from negative RF to full or exceptional RF. Scoring is based on a reading of verbatim transcripts from the PDI. Mind‐Mindedness Coding Scheme The Mind-­M indedness Coding Scheme (Meins & Fernyhough, 2010) was developed to...
chunks.text: 132 Infancy and Early Childhood interviewer). It is an 11-point scale ranging from negative RF to full or exceptional RF. Scoring is based on a reading of verbatim transcripts from the PDI. Mind‐Mindedness Coding Scheme The Mind-­M indedness Coding Scheme (Meins & Fernyhough, 2010) was developed to code mothers’ speech. It has been applied to mother–­infant play sessions transcribed verbatim and attempts to evaluate a mother’s capacity to view her infant as an intentional agent with autonomous thoughts, intentions, emotions, and desires. “Mind-­related” comments are defined as comments on mental states, such as knowledge, thoughts, desires, and interests; comments on mental processes; references to the infant’s level of emotional engagement; comments on the infant’s attempts to manipulate people’s beliefs; and the mother’s “putting words into the infant’s mouth,” so that her speech takes the form of a dialogue. Each mind-­related comment can be classified as an appropriate or not-­appropriate mind-­related comment. Axis V: Other Medical and Neurological Diagnoses Frequently children with mental health, developmental, regulatory–­sensory processing, language, and/or learning disorders show evidence of medical disorders that may contribute to their difficulties. For example, allergies may affect children’s behavior, not just their respiratory or dermatological reactions. Children with thyroid disorders may show over- or underresponsivity to their environments. Gastrointestinal disorders are not uncommon in children with pervasive developmental problems. Whether or not medical problems are clearly contributing, the clinician should make note of any such disorders. Careful listing of medical disorders facilitates their thorough investigation and the exploration of possible relationships with the child’s mental health, maturation, and learning challenges. chunks.heading: CHAPTER 2
chunks.preview: CHAPTER 2 From Infancy to Childhood C H A P T E R E D IT O R Anna Maria Speranza, PhD Development involves both major changes and continuity. Transitions from infancy to childhood, adolescence, and adulthood include transformations—­such as neurobiological changes; psychic reorganizations; emotional...
chunks.text: CHAPTER 2 From Infancy to Childhood C H A P T E R E D IT O R Anna Maria Speranza, PhD Development involves both major changes and continuity. Transitions from infancy to childhood, adolescence, and adulthood include transformations—­such as neurobiological changes; psychic reorganizations; emotional, relational, social, cognitive, and motor acquisitions; and developmental tasks specific to each stage—that affect the emerging personality and symptomatic conditions. When assessing psychopathology during development, it is important to consider the fluidity of diagnoses, changes in symptoms related to neurobiological factors, and clinical manifestations that are specific to developmental stages. One of the most diagnostically sensitive transitions is from infancy to childhood. This transition requires both a methodological and a developmental leap that must be taken into account so as not to minimize either the richness of the early years of life, when regulatory capacity, primary relationships, and maturing emotional competencies are consolidated and must be assessed, or the quality of older children’s mental functioning as their personalities emerge. From the beginning, the PDM has paid special attention to the 0–3 years period, which requires assessment of five different axes, while from age 4 onward—­despite the profound changes that occur in development and in the transition to adolescence—­the assessment includes mental functioning, emerging personality styles, and symptom patterns. With respect to primary diagnoses, symptoms and disorders may be defined somewhat differently, depending on the age when they appear (e.g., autism spectrum disorder may be diagnosed only after age 3; separation anxiety disorder may take on

chunks.heading: 144 Infancy and Early Childhood
chunks.preview: 144 Infancy and Early Childhood specific features after age 3, such as a persistent worry about the safety of one’s attachment figures that until then is not present). At the same time, some clinical pictures often diagnosed in infancy (e.g. regulatory–­sensory processing disorders) are not often di...
chunks.text: 144 Infancy and Early Childhood specific features after age 3, such as a persistent worry about the safety of one’s attachment figures that until then is not present). At the same time, some clinical pictures often diagnosed in infancy (e.g. regulatory–­sensory processing disorders) are not often diagnosed at later ages, while others (e.g., obsessive–­compulsive disorder or attention deficit/hyperactivity disorder) are not diagnosed until childhood. These changes may depend either on the emergence of the child’s new abilities or on the transformation of symptomatic manifestations that respond to the logic of heterotypic continuity. As we described in the Axis I section on developmental pathways, homotypic and heterotypic continuity can explain the stability or transformation of symptoms over time by recognizing an underlying continuity that connects different maladaptive behavioral manifestations underpinned by the same developmental or psychopathological processes. In the case of either homotypic or heterotypic continuity, it will not be difficult for the clinician to note symptoms that, according to descriptive psychiatric taxonomies, denote a disorder. In the PDM-3 approach, however, the clinician does not simply list the number and characteristics of a child’s symptoms but frames them within the functioning of that particular child. From this perspective, for a 2-year-old child, the clinician needs to understand the connotation of a specific symptom within the quality of primary relationships and in the context of the child’s developmental and regulatory abilities, while for an 8- to 9-year-old child, the clinician will try to understand the meaning of a symptom in the overall mental functioning and emerging personality. It is possible to trace a strong continuity and overlap between Axis I of the IEC (Infancy and Early Childhood; Primary Diagnoses) and the SC Axis, but it is more complicated to understand how Axes II (Functional Emotional Developmental Capacities), III (Regulatory–­Sensory Processing Capacities), and IV (Relational Patterns and Disorders) connect with the MC Axis. This chapter describes how the various IEC Axes contribute to defining and understanding the mental capacities of older children. We are hoping to help the clinician who needs to assess a child between ages 3 and 4 to decide which of the two diagnostic systems is more appropriate to the particular child. More importantly, we hope to provide insight into how the different mental capacities of a 5- to 6-year-old child may have been influenced by developmental impairments in earlier abilities. For practical reasons, the individual dimensions of the MC Axis are discussed separately, but clinicians need to maintain an overall perspective and be aware that many of these dimensions are intertwined (see Table 2.1). Capacity for Attention and Learning (Executive Functioning) child’s capacity for attention and learning is crucial for making sense of the world and the self. This is achieved through processing capabilities and executive functions that promote learning and growth. These abilities are strongly influenced by both regulatory–­sensory processing capacities (Axis III) and shared attention and regulation (Axis II, Level 1). In infancy and early childhood, the regulation of arousal (and of physiological states generally) is critical for successful adaptation to the environment and for learning self-­calming, attention, and emotional responsivity. The child’s regulatory–­sensory processing profile provides a vital means of understanding the development of the child’s self-­regulation. A child with sensory modulation difficulties,

Capacity for attention and learning (executive (Shared attention and regulation) functioning) • Axis III: Regulatory–sensory processing capacities • Axis II: Functional, emotional, developmental capacities 2. Capacity for affect regulation and expression (Engagement and relating; two-way purposeful emotional interactions) • Axis IV: Relational patterns and disorders (Quality of interactional patterns; affective tone of the caregiver– infant relationship; attachment patterns) • Axis II: Functional, emotional, developmental capacities 3. Capacity for differentiation and integration patterns) 6. Capacity to regulate self-esteem 7. Capacity for trust, empathy, and intimacy III. DEFENSE AND COPING • Axis III: Regulatory–sensory processing capacities 8. Capacity for impulse regulation • Axis IV: Relational patterns and disorders (Attachment 9. Defensive functioning patterns) 10. Capacity for adaptation and resilience IV. INSIGHT AND SELF-DIRECTION 11. Capacity to construct and use internal standards and ideals 12. Capacity to explore one’s inner life 13. Capacity for agency and purpose such as sensory hypersensitivity, may show fear and anxiety, and may avoid many sensory experiences or, alternatively, may show negativity and stubbornness in an attempt to control the environment. Such difficulties can impair children’s ability to regulate their responses to external and internal stimuli and to develop adequate attention and learning capacity. The capacity for shared attention and regulation also influences the functions of both attending to multisensory affective experiences and attaining mutual co-­regulation through appropriate responses from caregivers. Knowing how a child organized early states in the context of primary relationships helps a clinician to understand whether there are vulnerabilities in sensory processing or reciprocal regulation that may impair the child’s ability to plan and execute sequences of behavior and achieve age-­appropriate sensory integration.

chunks.heading: 146 Infancy and Early Childhood
chunks.preview: 146 Infancy and Early Childhood Capacity for Affect Regulation and Expression To understand how the child’s capacity for affect regulation and expression developed, a clinician may consider different levels of functional emotional developmental capacities (Axis II) and specific issues of relational...
chunks.text: 146 Infancy and Early Childhood Capacity for Affect Regulation and Expression To understand how the child’s capacity for affect regulation and expression developed, a clinician may consider different levels of functional emotional developmental capacities (Axis II) and specific issues of relational patterns and disorders, especially attachment patterns (Axis IV). With respect to emotional development, in the first year of life, engagement and mutual reciprocity allow for the integration of emotional aspects into dyadic interactions. Emerging positive and negative feelings become part of the forming relationship and, as the child grows, reciprocal, two-way, dialogic interactions provide the basis for the development of connections between emotional intent and purposeful action that are essential to affective regulation. A lack of reciprocal responses, such as in families in which a caregiver is self-­absorbed or anxiously overstimulating, can hinder the child’s achievement of adequate affective regulation, leading to difficulties in later years in identifying and communicating emotions. With respect to relational patterns and disorders (Axis IV), all relational dimensions are significant for affective regulation. The quality of interactional patterns and the affective tone of the caregiver–­child relationship, however, are particularly significant elements. “Emotional availability” refers to the capacity for mutual emotional awareness, perception, experience, and expression in a relationship. It denotes the dyad’s capacity for emotional connection and maintenance of a mutually satisfying, healthy interaction pattern. Caregiver depression, expressing itself through withdrawn and/ or intrusive styles and involving mainly the emotions of sadness and anger, can affect dyadic regulation and consequently the child’s capacity for affect tolerance. During the first year of life, emotional experiences in relationships are organized into internal working models of attachment. Patterns of attachment are strategies for affective regulation: “Attachment security” refers to a child’s confidence in a caregiver’s emotional availability to provide reassurance during distress and/or need, whereas “insecure attachment styles,” resulting from anxiety or avoidance, are associated with hyperactivating or deactivating emotional strategies, leading to different types of dysregulation. Capacity for Mentalization Closely related to the development of secure attachment, children’s ability to mentalize is strongly influenced by caregivers’ mentalizing capacities (assessed in Axis IV). Children’s capacity to reflect on mental states—­to comprehend their own and others’ behavior in terms of needs, feelings, intentions, and beliefs—­comes from experiences of having been held in a caregiver’s mind as a psychological agent. Because this ability is closely related to the development of a theory of mind, it is obvious that Levels 5 (creating symbols and ideas) and 6 (building logical bridges between ideas) are also of great importance. These organizational levels of experience, which can be readily observed in pretend play, affect the capacity to represent and symbolize experiences and to differentiate feelings, thoughts, and events that are internal from those that emanate from others.

chunks.heading: From Infancy to Childhood 147
chunks.preview: From Infancy to Childhood 147 Bodily Experiences and Representations Children’s regulatory–­sensory processing capacities (Axis III) contribute to the sense of body integrity and experience. A child’s motor and sensory patterns may interfere with age-­expected emotional, social, language, cognitive...
chunks.text: From Infancy to Childhood 147 Bodily Experiences and Representations Children’s regulatory–­sensory processing capacities (Axis III) contribute to the sense of body integrity and experience. A child’s motor and sensory patterns may interfere with age-­expected emotional, social, language, cognitive, motor, or sensory functioning, and thus with the ability to experience and represent one’s body in an integral, functional way. The child may show a marked disconnection or disorganization with respect to the body or may lack awareness of the body in space and with other people. Capacity for Impulse Regulation Children who have impairments in attention and perceptual motor abilities, as assessed in regulatory–­sensory processing capacities (Axis III), may be at risk for poor impulse control. The ability to regulate impulses is closely tied to the efficiency of executive functioning, which includes inhibiting dominant responses, activating subdominant responses, planning, and detecting errors. These processes rely on information processing that begins to stabilize during infancy and continues to develop throughout life. All other capacities of the MC Axis can be best understood by considering the quality of early relationships and attachment patterns. The development of the self is an interpersonal and intersubjective process: Defenses and capacities to regulate emotions are structured within primary relationships, and representations of self and others are constructed through these relationships. Attachment is a crucial predictor of a child’s future social and emotional wellbeing. It is linked to core mental capacities, including affective regulation, mentalization, differentiation, relationship and intimacy, self-­esteem, internal experience, defensive functioning, and resiliency. A secure attachment promotes a coherent and differentiated sense of self, in which the child feels respected and reassured, and in which trust in self and others emerges. The child creates a mental representation of the attachment figure based on the parent’s emotional and needs-­fulfilling responses. A self-­representation is formed that considers the child’s own abilities to achieve a sense of security. Internal working models guide children’s evaluation of experiences and attachment behavior, and enable predictions about affective experiences in meaningful relationships. Thus, children who receive appropriate and timely responses to their attempts to seek closeness and comfort with the attachment figure will develop internal working models of the caregiver as a helpful person and of themselves as worthy of care and effective in obtaining emotional support. This achievement allows a child to develop realistic and reliable self-­esteem, a capacity for empathy, and a sense of intimacy based on trust and the ability to manage states of anxiety or conflict through flexible use of defenses. Insecure attachment can lead to the development of internal working models of the other as an unavailable figure and of the self as unworthy of protection and care. Self-­esteem is greatly affected, as is confidence in relationships, often leading to difficulties in differentiation and integration. A lack of self-­efficacy and self-­control may result in rigid defenses that attempt to protect the self from excessive vulnerability. Clinicians thus need to pay attention to relational and attachment patterns, which are highly predictive of many aspects of eventual mental functioning.

chunks.heading: 148 Infancy and Early Childhood
chunks.preview: 148 Infancy and Early Childhood Case 1 Andrea is a 4-year-old boy with a speech delay who, when he cannot express himself clearly, has angry outbursts and cries. His parents are aware that these episodes are not just tantrums and are concerned about their inability to calm him down. Andrea is often...
chunks.text: 148 Infancy and Early Childhood Case 1 Andrea is a 4-year-old boy with a speech delay who, when he cannot express himself clearly, has angry outbursts and cries. His parents are aware that these episodes are not just tantrums and are concerned about their inability to calm him down. Andrea is often able to make his needs known, however, and appears communicative. During the session with the clinician, Andrea initially appears shy and clings to his father’s legs, but he eventually relaxes and begins playing with toy cars. In cases like this, clinicians should consider using the 0–3 IEC Axes instead of the 4–11 MC Axis. This child’s difficulties with verbal expression may make it challenging to understand his mental functioning fully. The 0–3 system is more useful in Andrea’s case, because it allows a systematic investigation by the clinician of the child’s functional, emotional, and developmental capacities, and also of the quality of relational patterns he has established with his parents and with the clinician. Case 2 Sally, a 31⁄2-year-old girl, has been brought to counseling in the context of her parents’ recent separation and disagreements over her upbringing. She seems highly anxious during times of separation, leading her parents to believe that she fears abandonment. During family observations, Sally shows age-­appropriate skills in emotional, relational, motor, cognitive, and language development. Her overall development appears to be harmonious, and she demonstrates her abilities through engaging in various activities. She is a resourceful, capable child who effectively uses symbolic communication, as evidenced by her narration of the experiences of the doll with which she plays. Sally is capable of employing appropriate defensive strategies. Both of her parents serve as important emotional reference points for her. In this case, the clinician can use the 4–11 MC Axis to assess the child’s emerging mental capacities through play and dialogue, which can aid in formulating a clinical profile.

chunks.heading: CHAPTER 3
chunks.preview: CHAPTER 3 Profile of Mental Functioning in Childhood MC Axis C H A P T E R E D IT O R S Norka Malberg, PsyD Anna Maria Speranza, PhD Alexandro Fortunato, PhD CONSULTANTS Jordan Bate, PhD Marianna Liotti, PhD Larry Rosenberg, PhD Stefanella Costa Cordella, PhD Eirini Lympinaki, PhD Catalina Sieverson...
chunks.text: CHAPTER 3 Profile of Mental Functioning in Childhood MC Axis C H A P T E R E D IT O R S Norka Malberg, PsyD Anna Maria Speranza, PhD Alexandro Fortunato, PhD CONSULTANTS Jordan Bate, PhD Marianna Liotti, PhD Larry Rosenberg, PhD Stefanella Costa Cordella, PhD Eirini Lympinaki, PhD Catalina Sieverson, PhD Mariagrazia Di Giuseppe, PhD Marcia Olhaberry Huber, PhD Introduction The assessment of children’s mental functioning is a fluid, dynamic, and complex process. In this chapter, we offer what we hope is a clinician-­friendly guide to assessment and formulation of a child’s overall mental functioning, one that takes into consideration the uniqueness of each child’s developmental process. The Mental Capacity (MC) Axis describes basic mental functions for children ages 4 through 11. Our goal is to provide clinicians with the tools necessary to describe clearly a child’s psychological state and place it within developmental context. The MC Axis integrates psychodynamic, cognitive, neuroscientific, and evolutionary models of development. Each aspect of overall functioning includes a general description, followed guidelines for normative developmental accomplishments in each area, followed by questions that may help clinicians in observation and formulation. The structure of the MC Axis offers clinicians the possibility of retracing the developmental path of emerging pathological manifestations in the context of maturation.

chunks.heading: 152 Childhood
chunks.preview: 152 Childhood Like the other M Axes, the MC Axis includes an assessment procedure whereby a clinician can indicate on a 5-point scale the level of attainment of each function. In conjunction with the PC Axis, the MC Axis allows clinicians to construct an individual profile that integrates the emergi...
chunks.text: 152 Childhood Like the other M Axes, the MC Axis includes an assessment procedure whereby a clinician can indicate on a 5-point scale the level of attainment of each function. In conjunction with the PC Axis, the MC Axis allows clinicians to construct an individual profile that integrates the emerging personality of the child with specific aspects of the child’s mental functioning. The Age 4–11 Developmental Period In the age range covered by this axis, rapid cognitive and social-­emotional changes take place. When development proceeds well, the foundations of a sense of self emerge. This sense of becomes increasingly coherent and consistent over time, becoming more evident in adolescence. A child’s sense of self, and the narrative that goes with it, are inherently fragile, however, and less well differentiated than those of either adolescents or adults. For children in the 4–11 age group, the influence of others is notable; how they view themselves is mainly a function of identification with parental figures and siblings. During this period, children become more differentiated and more exposed to influences outside the home, and their self-­representations become increasingly altered and solidified. Still, even young children have a sense of their likes and dislikes, and of the kinds of persons they are (e.g., “I am smart, pretty, helpful, naughty”). Their belief systems are typically taken from the adults around them. Particularly at the lower end of this age range, children are far less future-­oriented than their adolescent or adult counterparts. Their ability to reflect on the impact of their actions is neither fully developed nor far-­reaching. They continue to rely significantly on the adult figures in their lives for modeling, containment, and a basic sense of safety. In response to diverse constitutional and environmental factors, many children present with uneven developmental profiles. To obtain a more accurate and relationally informed diagnostic picture, clinicians should consider referring to the sections in this manual that explore earlier developmental functioning. Some 5-year-olds with feeding and sleeping difficulties, for example, may be better understood, and may become more understandable to parents and other caregivers, when a clinician consults the EC Axis for the larger developmental context. Dimensional Assessment in the Context of the MC‐Axis Capacities The assessment of children ages 4–11 is a challenging, painstaking enterprise, particularly when one aspires to a diagnostic profile that will guide clinical formulation and treatment planning. A thorough assessment cannot be accomplished in a single interview. As in other chapters of PDM-3, we recommend using a series of validated and nonvalidated tools to help bring together all the pieces of the puzzle of a child’s psychodynamic diagnostic profiling. Assessment and Parents Meeting with parents is essential. We use the term “parent” to refer to anyone who plays a primary caregiving role for the child. Clinicians should give careful consideration to whether it is appropriate or beneficial for a particular child to be present at the interview with parents. It may not be helpful, for example, for a child to hear information about family dramas or parent history that the child has not previously heard or

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 153
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 153 may be too young to comprehend (Malberg, Jurist, Bae, & Dangerfield, 2023). The presence of a parent may inhibit a child’s play or verbalization, and while this may be useful data in itself, it comes at the expense of other information. In addition to the interview...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 153 may be too young to comprehend (Malberg, Jurist, Bae, & Dangerfield, 2023). The presence of a parent may inhibit a child’s play or verbalization, and while this may be useful data in itself, it comes at the expense of other information. In addition to the interview, observational data should be collected as the child plays with the caregiver and as the clinician plays alone with the child. The observation of child– ­c aregiver interactions is an extremely valuable source of information (Agazzi, Soca Lozano, & Hernandez, 2023; Warren et al., 2023). Clinicians witnessing them can note how parents communicate their own thoughts and feelings (verbally and nonverbally), what signals they send to encourage the child’s expressions of thoughts and feelings, and how they support, contain, and encourage this process or, alternatively, avoid, respond inconsistently, and inhibit the communication and exploration of a child’s thoughts and feelings. In this context, clinicians should consider the parents’ own relational history and how it may be influencing their caregiving. It is vital, when trying to understand caregiver interactions with school-­age children, to be informed about relevant cultural values and expectations. The concept of ports of entry (Dugmore, 2014) is helpful here. A port of entry is an opportunity both to understand and to intervene. A caregiver’s nonverbal response to a child’s attempts to be calmed via bodily comfort, for example, is a port of entry, as is a child’s response to a parent’s attempt to help the child with a task. During assessment, noticing these ports of entry can inform both the clinical formulation and the treatment. Child and Caregivers: Normative Expectations Tables The Normative Expectations tables indicate the expected level of a child’s development and the type of environment—­particularly the caregiving environment—­that one can expect to find for each mental functioning capacity, in relation to developmental age between 4 and 11 years. These tables, referred to as Developmental Guidelines in the various sections, are included within each capacity and are intended to support clinicians in their assessments. The effects of caregivers in supporting or hindering the development of different capacities can be observed directly or through stories (Stern, 1995). Direct observation includes noting the parent’s interaction with the child during the interview or in an interactive activity requested of the pair (e.g., playing or drawing together). While observing, the clinician can identify the different aspects described in the boxes; for example, support for the child’s expression of ideas and emotions, the “scaffolding” function of the caregiver (provision of the right amount of help at the right time in the developmentally appropriate way), words of encouragement or invalidation, and so on (Riffin, Wolff, Estill, Prabhu, & Pillemer, 2020; Trude et al., 2021). To assess family routines and ordinary functioning, the clinician can ask parents to tell a story of a typical day for the child during the week and weekend, respectively, including how they participate. It may be valuable also to conduct interviews with third parties who witness the child’s home life, such as teachers who maintain regular contact with the family. Assessment in Other Contexts Clinical observations should be made across diverse contexts (school, home, and other settings). Informal reports should be obtained from other adults in regular contact with the child, such as grandparents and other relatives, pediatricians, camp counselors,

chunks.heading: 154 Childhood
chunks.preview: 154 Childhood and teachers. Clinicians may benefit from parent and teacher reports, child self-­report rating scales, and clinician-­rated scales to compare the perspectives of multiple raters (Quintigliano, Fortunato, Lauriola, & Speranza, 2023; Wurster & Biringen, 2023). These empirically based as...
chunks.text: 154 Childhood and teachers. Clinicians may benefit from parent and teacher reports, child self-­report rating scales, and clinician-­rated scales to compare the perspectives of multiple raters (Quintigliano, Fortunato, Lauriola, & Speranza, 2023; Wurster & Biringen, 2023). These empirically based assessments should be integrated with information obtained via child and parent interviews. These interviews should focus on the child’s current level of functioning, the child’s developmental history, and the family mental health history. When a fuller assessment is needed, a child should be referred for a comprehensive psychological test battery that includes cognitive, achievement, neuropsychological, projective, and self-­report data plus parent–­teacher report test measures. Such a battery will highlight how each component is affected by others and will help provide a well-­rounded picture of all aspects of the child’s current level of functioning. See Table 3.1 for a complete list of sources and settings that can provide insights into the child’s mental functioning. In their formulations, clinicians should consider culture, gender, and trauma (Kovess-­Masfety et al., 2021; Martin & Hadwin, 2022; Reiss, 2013). To obtain an accurate picture of children and their environment, they may need to ask difficult, uncomfortable questions. They should attend carefully to the subjective meanings implicit in behavioral and emotional expressions of children and caregivers. The following mental capacities (see Figure 3.1) are meant to help clinicians with observing, listening, and interacting, and with constructing a picture that captures who this child is as an emerging human being, not as an array of symptoms. The Mental Capacity Profile aims to provide, alongside the Emerging Personality Axis, a diagnostic formulation that speaks to the child’s subjective experience and helps clinicians communicate to adults in the child’s multiple contexts. We hope that it orients clinicians toward what the child needs to return to normal maturation. TABLE 3.1. Checklist of Sources of Data Informing a Child’s Mental Functioning Profile Consulting room (diagnostic observations) Primary caregivers (social and developmental history, checklists, interview) Pediatrician (medical history) Teacher (checklists, interview) Classroom observation Formal assessment (e.g., intelligence, achievement, neuropsychological, projective) Dyadic and triadic observations during diagnostic period Other sources of information (child care providers, grandparents/other extended family members, social services personnel)

chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 155 I. COGNITIVE AND AFFECTIVE PROCESSES 1. Capacity for Attention and Learning (Executive Functioning) 2. Capacity for Affect Regulation and Expression 3. Capacity for Mentalization II. IDENTITY AND RELATIONSHIPS 4. Bodily Experiences and Representations 5. Capacity f...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 155 I. COGNITIVE AND AFFECTIVE PROCESSES 1. Capacity for Attention and Learning (Executive Functioning) 2. Capacity for Affect Regulation and Expression 3. Capacity for Mentalization II. IDENTITY AND RELATIONSHIPS 4. Bodily Experiences and Representations 5. Capacity for Differentiation and Integration 6. Capacity to Regulate Self-Esteem 7. Capacity for Trust, Empathy, and Intimacy III. DEFENSE AND COPING 8. Capacity for Impulse Regulation 9. Defensive Functioning 10. Capacity for Adaptation and Resilience IV. INSIGHT AND SELF-DIRECTION 11. Capacity to Construct and Use Internal Standards and Ideals 12. Capacity to Explore One’s Inner Life 13. Capacity for Agency and Purpose FIGURE 3.1. Capacities that make up children’s mental functioning. 1. Capacity for Attention and Learning (Executive Functioning) The capacity for attention and learning helps the child make sense of the world and the self via processing capabilities and executive functions that promote learning and growth. It includes the following: • auditory processing and language • visual–­spatial processing • sensory modulation • related capacities for executive functioning (problem-­solving, sequencing, organizing and planning) • memory (working, declarative, and nondeclarative) • concentration and attention • intelligence • language development • organization and quality of thought processes • processing of affective and social cues Some of these capabilities are involved in more than one of the mental functions we depict here. High functioning in this capacity requires attaining capacities for affect regulation and expression, bodily experiences and representations, and impulse regulation, as described in what follows. Clinicians should assess their impact on the child’s executive functioning, cognitive skills, attention, and learning. The capacity should be evaluated considering the child’s developmental stage and their expected environment (see Table 3.2).
