chunks.heading: 160 Childhood
chunks.preview: 160 Childhood Important Considerations Clinicians should keep in mind the social and biological resources of each child, as well as the child’s overall self- ­regulation ability. The term “self-­regulation” refers to the capacity for sensory modulation (adaptation to sensory stimuli), emotional regu...
chunks.text: 160 Childhood Important Considerations Clinicians should keep in mind the social and biological resources of each child, as well as the child’s overall self- ­regulation ability. The term “self-­regulation” refers to the capacity for sensory modulation (adaptation to sensory stimuli), emotional regulation (management of feelings), and impulse control (control of actions). Problems in these areas can challenge children’s cognitive capacities, affecting their ability to learn (Newman, 2023; Wigfield, 2023). Clinicians should thus consider how the capacity for sensory modulation, executive functioning, and learning is connected with the capacity for affect regulation and expression and the capacity for impulse regulation, as in the following examples. 1. Social resources: Children who lack adequate schooling may be delayed in achieving age-­appropriate cognitive skills (e.g., language development, writing skills) (Zulauf-­McCurdy & Loomis, 2023). During early schooling, cultural beliefs about access to play or child care may affect the child’s capacity to develop the skills to function flexibly. 2. Biological resources: Children with neurodevelopmental differences may have difficulties concentrating and understanding social cues or school material. Neurodevelopmental disorders can also challenge sensory integration, language development, and/or thinking processes (Champ, Adamou, & Tolchard, 2023; Wilmot, Pizzey, Leitao, Hasking, & Boyes, 2023). Mild neurodevelopmental deficits may be compensated by other capacities, by environmental factors, and by early detection of deficit, leading to mitigation or avoidance of difficulty. 3. Self-­regulation issues: In a noisy class, children with auditory sensitivity may have trouble with sensory modulation and concentration (Romero-­Ayuso et al., 2023; Yochman & Pat-­Horenczyk, 2020). Children who become easily upset or have sudden mood swings may have restricted capacity for effortful control—­defined as the efficiency of executive functioning—­including the ability to inhibit a dominant response, activate a subdominant response, plan, and detect errors (Zhang et al., 2023)—and be unable to finish a complicated task. Those with hyperactivity may have difficulties with organizing and planning. The following questions are intended to guide the exploration and support the construction of a preliminary clinical formulation: • Does the child have age-­appropriate cognitive skills according to the relevant cultural context and school environment? • Can the child use age-­appropriate language to express thoughts and feelings? • Are there nontypical elements in the child’s thinking or use of language? • What is the quality of the child’s thinking? Are there peculiarities or elements of disorganization? • Does the child present learning difficulties and if so, what kind? • Are there intellectual restrictions? • Does the child react to sensory stimuli or have sensory integration issues? • Are there any physical impairments that interfere with perception or sensorimotor coordination? • Is the child unable to control actions or motility?

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 161
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 161 • Does the child’s emotional state influence interest in learning, capacity for concentration, or information processing? • Is the child capable of effortful control? Rating Scale At the end of this chapter, in Table 3.15a, we provide a 5-point rating scale to asse...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 161 • Does the child’s emotional state influence interest in learning, capacity for concentration, or information processing? • Is the child capable of effortful control? Rating Scale At the end of this chapter, in Table 3.15a, we provide a 5-point rating scale to assess each MC-Axis capacity, which can be used with the following anchor points for the Levels 5, 3, and 1. 5. The child can be focused, calm, organized, and able to learn most of the time, even under stress, in a developmentally expected fashion. There is age-­appropriate ability to plan and execute sequences of behavior and age-­appropriate sensory integration. Memory, attention, and executive function are all working in an integrated way at age-­expected levels. Language development and cognitive skills are age-­appropriate when the child has adequate support from the environment. 3. The child presents one or more of the following limitations: The child can be focused, organized, calm, and able to learn except when over- or understimulated (i.e., in a noisy, distracting, or very dull setting), when challenged to use a vulnerable skill (e.g., when a child with weak fine motor skills is asked to write rapidly), or when ill, anxious, or under stress. The child’s capacity to learn is sometimes restricted to short periods (concentration span is not consistent and is highly dependent on context and support); the child shows problems of language, motor, or visual processing that explain the uneven regulatory profile. The child seems to learn only when very interested or motivated. The child exhibits mild sensory issues. 1. The child has fleeting attention (a few seconds here or there), is very active or agitated, is mostly self-­absorbed, and/or is lethargic or passive. Learning capacity is severely limited by multiple processing difficulties. The child’s verbal expressions may be compromised, impoverished, or peculiar. The child seems easily overwhelmed by environmental stimuli (sounds, smells, bright lights). Thought processes lack coherence and/or stable connection with external reality. 2. Capacity for Affect Regulation and Expression The capacity for affect regulation and expression reflects the ability to experience, comprehend, modulate, and express affects (verbally or nonverbally) in a way that is appropriate to the situation and consistent with the expectations and norms of the cultural milieu (Troy et al., 2023). The clinician should evaluate the child’s ability identify emotions in self and others, including “reading” and interpreting others’ verbal and nonverbal affective cues. This capacity refers to the child’s ability to experience and identify an age-­appropriate range of emotions, including both positive and negative states, in varying degrees of intensity. Variability, richness, and depth of emotion should be observed, keeping in mind that during this age period (4–11 years), the range of specific affects is broad. For example, in the aggression domain, one finds gradations from assertive behavior to competitive behavior, to mildly aggressive behavior, to explosive, uncontrolled aggression. The same is true in the affection and caring domain, which ranges from

chunks.heading: 162 Childhood
chunks.preview: 162 Childhood uninhibited emotional hunger to mild affection, to a sincere sense of warmth, to compassion, to the developmentally advanced capacity for empathy. The prerequisite of the development of this ability, rooted in early infant–­caregiver interactions, is the capacity for mental representat...
chunks.text: 162 Childhood uninhibited emotional hunger to mild affection, to a sincere sense of warmth, to compassion, to the developmentally advanced capacity for empathy. The prerequisite of the development of this ability, rooted in early infant–­caregiver interactions, is the capacity for mental representations of affects; that is, the capacity for symbolization (Ghanbari, Vahidi, Behzadpoor, Goudarzi, & Ghabezi, 2023). Caregivers’ understanding and mirroring of the infant’s physical and emotional states gradually help the child build internal representations of bodily and affective states that promote identification and modulation of affective experiences (Masek et al., 2023). It is therefore important to observe parental reflective capacity, the parents’ ability to understand and express their own feelings and those of their child. Clinicians may assess this by observing the parent–­child dyad. Video feedback may produce vital data about caregivers’ capacities to identify, modulate, and express their own emotions, while helping their child to do the same. The clinician should also assess the child’s ability to express affective experience effectively and adaptively to others through both verbal (i.e., speaking one’s own feelings or the feelings of figures during play) and nonverbal modalities (i.e., somatic and facial gestures, tone of voice) (Schmitz et al., 2023; Trehub, 2023), noting the mode and quality of the child’s expression of emotion. Clinicians should also evaluate whether the expressed feeling is appropriate in quality and intensity to the context and should note the child’s capacity to regulate emotions when confronted with stressful or tense situations. High functioning in the domain of affect regulation and expression requires a developed capacity for symbolization (Gleason & White, 2023). It fosters capacities for mentalization and empathy. Low functioning in this capacity is linked with difficulties in symbolization and can lead to bottled-­up feelings and states of affect dysregulation such as the following: • Difficulties in identifying, differentiating, and communicating feelings that can lead to constricted thinking and even difficulty distinguishing between bodily sensations and emotional states (alexithymia). This becomes evident when children cannot talk about how they feel (Swerdlow, Pearlstein, Sandel, Mauss, & Johnson, 2020; Troy et al., 2023). For example, the clinician asks a girl, “How did you feel in that situation?” and she responds: “I don’t know. Normal,” or “I felt OK,” or “My ears were aching. Too much noise there!” Or the clinician asks a boy how he thinks his mother felt when he refused to stop playing video games for the day, and he responds: “I don’t know? I am not in her mind!” or “I don’t care! I want to play!” • Expression of affects via the body. Clinicians should attend to tension states, how they are manifested, and how the child deals with them (Bourke, Lingwood, Gallagher-­M itchell, & López-Pérez, 2023; Morgenstern, 2023). For example, a boy seems anxious and moves his leg repeatedly, but when asked about this, he cannot connect the movement with an affect. Or a girl seems relaxed during her parents’ divorce but develops a somatic symptom (e.g., stomachaches). • Expression of affects through behavior (Hajal & Paley, 2020); for example, a child withdraws from peers and becomes passive but is unable to identify a feeling of sadness; or a child yells and swears but denies feeling angry. The capacity should be evaluated considering the child’s developmental stage and their expected environment (see Table 3.3).

The following questions are intended to guide the exploration and support the construction of a preliminary clinical formulation: • Can the child establish and retain an emotional connection with the clinician? • Does the child express an age-­appropriate range of affects? • Can the child identify emotions? When asked, can the child make reasonable guesses about emotions in various situations? • Can the child understand emotional cues? • Can the child communicate emotions? Is the communication verbal or nonverbal? Is it appropriate to the child’s cultural milieu? • Are the child’s expressed feelings relevant to the situation being described, experienced, or enacted in play? Is the intensity appropriate, or is it more or less highly charged than one would expect? • Does the child experience dysphoric, painful emotions? If yes, are they chronic or situational, internalized or somehow expressed? • Does a child express specific states of mind through the body? • Can the child regulate emotional states? Is there evidence of dysregulation of specific feelings? • Can the child regain affective modulation with the help of a peer or an adult? • Can the child understand and empathize with other people’s emotions? • Can the child express congruent emotions verbally during pretend play? • Does the child need to use pretend play to convey emotional states? • Does the child talk about emotions only by imitating others (usually adults), or is there a genuine understanding and expression of emotional states? Rating Scale At the end of this chapter, in Table 3.15a, we provide a 5-point rating scale to assess each MC-Axis capacity, which can be used with the following anchor points for the Levels 5, 3, and 1.

chunks.heading: 166 Childhood
chunks.preview: 166 Childhood 5. The child can comprehend, regulate, and express an age-­ appropriate full range of emotions most of the time, even under stress. The child can convey emotions verbally and is capable of regulating intense affects. Regulation can be accomplished by the child, or by seeking help from...
chunks.text: 166 Childhood 5. The child can comprehend, regulate, and express an age-­ appropriate full range of emotions most of the time, even under stress. The child can convey emotions verbally and is capable of regulating intense affects. Regulation can be accomplished by the child, or by seeking help from others (caregivers, teachers, etc.), or symbolically (e.g., in play). The child has the self-­awareness to recognize internal emotional states, recognize needs for help from others, and seek support and care when necessary. There is an age-­appropriate capacity to understand affective cues and others’ affective state. 3. The child can communicate verbally or nonverbally a wide range of emotions, but may have difficulties comprehending and expressing certain emotions (i.e., anger, sadness) or occasional difficulties regulating emotions. At moments of dysregulation, the child may become fragmented, inhibited, or chaotic but is able to regulate emotions using others’ support. The child occasionally has difficulties in recognizing internal emotional states, seeking others for help. The child accurately reads and responds to a range of emotional signals, except in circumstances involving selected emotions, or when emotions are stressful. There are no cohesive, larger integrated emotional patterns. 1. Functioning at this level involves one or more of the following: (a) The child lacks awareness of internal emotional states and cannot recognize emotions or convey them verbally. When someone attempts to verbalize feelings for the child, the child becomes frustrated. (b) The child uses the body to channel feelings or acts them out in dysregulated behavioral patterns (e.g., outbursts or passivity). (c) The child misreads emotional cues and therefore feels suspicious, mistreated, unloved, or angry. 3. Capacity for Mentalization The capacity for mentalization (Fonagy & Bateman, 2006) concerns children’s ability to reflect on their own mental states (e.g., needs, feelings, desires, intentions, beliefs), as well as those of others, and to use this capacity personally (in reflection on mental states of the self) and interpersonally (in reflection on mental states of self and others in social interactions) (Midgley, Ensink, Lindqvist, Malberg, & Muller, 2017). It is an innate tendency of human beings to interpret the world around them and understand behaviors and actions of self and others. The capacity for mentalizing is cognitively rooted in what philosophers have called “theory of mind” (Ensink & Mayes, 2010). Its development is highly correlated with secure caregiver–­infant attachment and, more specifically, with the caregiver’s ability to imagine and interpret the child’s internal experience, giving it back to the child in a congruent, “marked” way. Via such mirroring from the caregiver, the infant starts organizing an experience of the self as a differentiated entity and as a psychological agent, whose actions are motivated by mental states (Gergely, 2018). Gradually the child is able to identify self-­states and differentiate them from those of other people. The development of the capacity to mentalize proceeds from a phase of “psychic equivalence”—when self and other’s mental states are perceived as identical—­toward acknowledging the existence of differentiated mental states and alternative perspectives. This maturation passes through a “pretend mode,” in which the child’s mental states are differentiated from both internal and external reality.

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 167
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 167 The capacity for mentalization is in constant interplay with the capacity for integration and differentiation (identity), and the capacity for affect regulation and expression. Increasing ability for self-­integration and differentiation allows the individual to ab...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 167 The capacity for mentalization is in constant interplay with the capacity for integration and differentiation (identity), and the capacity for affect regulation and expression. Increasing ability for self-­integration and differentiation allows the individual to abandon functioning in psychic equivalence and pretend modes, and promotes reflective capacities and self-­observation. Moreover, the capacity to identify and regulate emotions facilitates mentalization; to reflect on emotions (mentalized affectivity), the child must first be able to identify and regulate them (Jurist, 2018). The capacity to mentalize allows alternative perspectives that can support affect and impulse regulation. When assessing the capacity for mentalization, a clinician should observe a child’s reflective abilities in both direct and indirect ways. Description of interactions between the child and peers or family members, as well as reflection on play scenarios, can provide information on the child’s capacity to think about mental states and not just events and behaviors of self and others (Privizzini, 2017). The clinician can also pay attention to nonverbal affective shifts in both the clinician and the caregiver (leaning forward, frowning, smiling). The child’s ability to respond to nonverbal cues in ways appropriate to age and culture has a major effect on reflective functioning. For children with a history of chronic relational trauma (Luyten & Fonagy, 2019), the clinician should assess their capacity to mentalize in a containing, predictable environment. Observations should be made across observers (clinical and others) and settings. Early caregiver–­infant history is highly relevant to assessing functioning in this domain. It is also important observe the child’s consistent behavior toward others to see how it expresses the child’s understanding of others’ emotions (e.g. “My mother doesn’t love me because she didn’t buy me the toy I asked for”). The capacity should be evaluated considering the child’s developmental stage and their expected environment (see Table 3.4). The following questions are intended to guide the exploration and support the construction of a preliminary clinical formulation: • Can the child understand the mental states of self and others? • Does the child have the ability to appreciate different perspectives? • Is there evidence that the child can distinguish internal (mental) from external reality? • How does the child give meaning to the actions of self and others? • Is the child capable of exploring personal intentions, thoughts, and feelings, and differentiating them from those of others (e.g., “I feel sad, but that doesn’t mean my friends are sad”)? • Is the child operating at the level of “mental equation,” meaning that what the child feels is equated with what others feel (e.g., “I’m angry and I feel like I want to hurt someone. Therefore, I hurt others so they don’t hurt me”)? • When asked to imagine what a friend is thinking or feeling, can the child regulate emotions and accept the invitation to think about the other? • Does the child function in pretend mode (i.e., is the child’s inner reality cut off from what the child says or plays out)? • Does the child feel capable of understanding the feelings of others only if they act as the child desires? • Is there evidence of early attachment trauma? If yes, how well does the child mentalize when in a predictable and stable environment? • Does the child perceive and respond appropriately to nonverbal emotional cues?

chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 169 TABLE 3.4. (continued) Child Caregivers • Becomes capable of more complex • Promote conversations about acceptable “exceptions to the rules” mentalization abilities, such as the about speaking directly (e.g., not saying what one thinks because it understanding subtle social deceptions might hurt someone’s feelings). such as bluffs and white lies. • Comes to appreciate the opaque nature • Support curiosity about the mental states of others. of mental states (we really never know • Ask about the child’s internal states, noting that they cannot be what is in others’ minds), shows curiosity directly known. and interest in understanding others’ • Avoid categorical statements to others about the child’s mental perspectives. states (model opacity of mind). • Becomes able to interpret ambiguous • If needed, support the child’s understanding of interpersonal expressions (irony, sarcasm) and experiences requiring interpretation of ambiguous signals. can verbalize feelings and respond • Welcome feelings in response to nonverbal signals, support contingently to nonverbal cues. congruence between verbal and nonverbal expressions. Rating Scale At the end of this chapter, in Table 3.15a, we provide a 5-point rating scale to assess each MC-Axis capacity, which can be used with the following anchor points for Levels 5, 3, and 1. 5. The child demonstrates an age-­appropriate capacity to understand why people may be reacting in particular ways, can consider the intentions behind the behaviors of others in deciding how to respond to these behaviors, and can anticipate how people may react in specific social contexts. The child can mentalize even when emotionally activated, or may be able to do so after the activating event, especially when given support. The child can reflect on internal emotional reactions accurately, can think about them with others, and can assert needs and wishes, while showing interest in what the other thinks and feels. Children high in this capacity show curiosity, interest, or concern about why others do what they do, develop ideas about this, are curious about their own reactions, and can consider discrepancies between their actual and desired behaviors. 3. The child demonstrates a basic capacity to understand what others feel but struggles to anticipate how they may feel in specific circumstances, or how they may react in interpersonal contexts. This capacity fails when the child is confronted by more complex or difficult social and interpersonal interactions, or is emotionally aroused, or is faced with new challenges. Although there may be a basic capacity to identify personal affects and mental states, the process lacks complexity and nuance. Without outside scaffolding, the child cannot understand internal reactions. Although sometimes initially struggling in response to supportive invitations from mentalizing adults, the child is able eventually to think with the other, regain affect regulation, reflect on mental life, connect interpersonal events with inner states, and attempt to predict people’s actions. 1. The child either does not think about the feelings and intentions of others, showing no interest, or adopts an attitude of certainty while inaccurately attributing

chunks.heading: 170 Childhood
chunks.preview: 170 Childhood feelings and intentions to others. As a result, there may be frequent nonverbal miscuing and inappropriate reactions. Children low on this function seem unable to think about the impact of their behavior on others’ thoughts and feelings. Perspective-­taking abilities seem virtually abs...
chunks.text: 170 Childhood feelings and intentions to others. As a result, there may be frequent nonverbal miscuing and inappropriate reactions. Children low on this function seem unable to think about the impact of their behavior on others’ thoughts and feelings. Perspective-­taking abilities seem virtually absent across all contexts. Prementalizing modes of functioning prevail even when the child is not in stressful situations or is helped by an adult or peer capable of reflective function. 4. Bodily Experiences and Representations The area of bodily experiences and representations involves the links between perception, action, and subjectivity. The body is used to move and perform actions, it senses and perceives, and it is felt and experienced while also communicating. Several aspects of perception and representation make up body experience and representation. The “body schema” (Raimo et al., 2021) is the spatial–­mental representation of the body and the location of body parts and their surround. It is an internal model or mental map, created through visual and proprioceptive cues, that helps us navigate the world effectively. “Proprioception” (Chu, 2017) is the sensory process through which information is gathered from the joint and muscle receptors throughout the body, shaping our awareness of our body parts, posture, and movements. “Kinesthesia” refers to the sense of motion and change in position. “Interoception” refers to the sensations from receptors inside organs and the skin, which help to regulate hunger, thirst, digestion, temperature, and heart rate (Grist, Brown, Yu, & Clark, 2023). Awareness of one’s own body and the ability to imitate are present almost from birth. By age 2, children are typically able to recognize themselves in a mirror; if there is a red mark on their forehead or nose, they will touch it (Goslin & Koons-­B eauchamp, 2023; Lacroix, Smith, Husain, Orth, & von Ranson, 2023). The body schema develops slowly and gradually, however, over the first 10 years of life, and then further through adolescence, maturing in adulthood. Current neuroscience research (Walbrin, Almeida, & Koldewyn, 2023) suggests that in forming body representations, children rely more on visual cues than on proprioceptive ones. While both types of cues remain relevant over time, visual cues are less heavily weighted in adolescence and adulthood, when proprioceptive cues are integrated. Studies illuminating this process have used artificial body parts, so that visually, participants see their body in one position, while feeling it in a different position. Many researchers suggest that these representations are body-part centered. Because children’s bodies are growing, and their sizes and shapes are constantly changing, these body schemas need to be continually updated. The body schema is involved in motor planning, anticipation, and control (Hale, Colquhoun, Lancastle, Lewis, & Tyson, 2023). Similar processes are involved in imagining movements and in planning and ultimately executing them. Although a body schema develops early, these anticipatory functions mature more slowly. Development of body experience and representation, and coordination of posture and movement, tends to follow a long trajectory (Möllmann, Heinrichs, & Herwig, 2024). By ages 7–10, there are similarities between the activation involved in imagining and actually carrying out a movement, a correlation that becomes stronger in adolescence. The ability to adjust also becomes more refined in later childhood, adolescence, and adulthood. When doing a task that requires modifying their movements because of an environmental change, adults adjust almost immediately and automatically, while children make only some of the adjustments.

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 171
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 171 Through mirror neurons in the brain, body representation is linked to movements of others. Neuroscience research demonstrates that when one sees another person moving or making a facial expression, mirror neurons fire in similar patterns in one’s own brain, as if o...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 171 Through mirror neurons in the brain, body representation is linked to movements of others. Neuroscience research demonstrates that when one sees another person moving or making a facial expression, mirror neurons fire in similar patterns in one’s own brain, as if one were making the movement oneself. This neurobiological process is one component of empathy (Gallese, 2001). “Interoceptive awareness” (Grist et al., 2023) is the feeling of what is going on inside one’s body and the capacity to understand it and talk about it. Although its development is still underresearched, existing research suggests that the ability to sense hunger, thirst, and pain appears early in childhood and remains stable, while the ability to recognize the need to use the toilet and detect signs of illness appears to improve throughout the school-­age years. Children who have more physical activity tend to have better interoceptive awareness. “Body image” includes both perception of one’s own body and attitudes about it (Richburg & Stewart, 2024). Children develop ideas and feelings about their size, shape, and particular body (e.g., seeing it as attractive, potent, strong, weak, disfigured, disliked, masculine, feminine). Body image forms from the earliest moments in life, shaped by how caregivers touch, hold, and care for the child’s body. As Winnicott (1967) said, the mother is the first mirror for the baby, who internalizes what she reflects as a template for self-­understanding. Body image develops through both ongoing caregiving experiences and social and cultural messages about bodies, which children pick up on and internalize. Assessment of the capacity for bodily experience and representation involves observing the child’s physical motor coordination and ability to know and connect with what is felt in the child’s body (Cox, Heron, & Frederico, 2024; Mirabella et al., 2023; Engelhard & Sichel, 2023). It also involves assessing the pleasure or displeasure the child takes from their representation of their body and its functions. Clinicians need to appreciate how both social and biological resources shape the development of body experience and representation, affording opportunities as well as limitations. The capacity should be evaluated considering the child’s developmental stage and the expected environment (see Table 3.5). The following questions are intended to guide the exploration and support the construction of a preliminary clinical formulation: • Can the child comprehend body part names and name body parts? • Does the child have an accurate sense of where body parts are located? • Can the child draw pictures of people, and with how much accuracy? • Can the child imitate movements? • Can the child differentiate between right and left? • How does the child behave physically? How does the child take up space and move about the room? • Does the child have a body awareness in relation to the rest of the space? Is the child clumsy? • Can the child identify physical sensations such as temperature, pain, or discomfort? • Can the child identify physiological cues for bodily needs, such as hunger, sleep, elimination? • Can the child identify interoceptive cues, such as their heart beating fast or strongly, feeling out of breath, feeling dizzy? • Does the child trust the cues from their body?

chunks.heading: 172 Childhood
chunks.preview: 172 Childhood TABLE 3.5. Developmental Guidelines: Bodily Experiences and Representations Child Caregivers Ages 4–6 • Is increasingly good with gross motor skills such • Offer many opportunities for movements, including dance, as running, jumping, hopping, and throwing a ball outdoor play, climbing,...

Ages 7–8 • Can do most physical activities: running, jumping, • Provide opportunities for team sports or more complex hoping, throwing, with improved accuracy. physical activities. • Has greater motor coordination and is capable of • Engage children in helping with chores (e.g., sweeping, more engagement in sports. moving furniture, cleanup). • Has increased fluency with fine motor skills. • Engage in fun exercises, such as walking in various ways, • Begins to be able to link bodily sensations to push-ups, jumping jacks. feelings. • Model locating their own bodily expressions of feelings. • Is more aware of others’ evaluation of bodies. • Read age-appropriate books about the body. • Reflect on how they talk about their own bodies. Ages 9–11 • Has greater muscle strength. • Provide opportunities for more complex physical activities • Shows a growing awareness of how bodies that appeal to the child. mature, including ways that the child’s own body • Link emotions to your own bodily experiences and support and friends’ bodies are changing. the child in making such links. • Is involved in activities that use the body in more • Read books and talk about how bodies change in puberty. complex and skillful ways, such as playing sports. • Show acceptance of the child’s body, particularly as it • Becomes more skillful using tools and problem changes, and listen without judgment when the child talks solving. about bodily topics. • Has more capacity to notice bodily feelings. • Reflect on how they talk about their own bodies. • The child is increasingly exposed to societal and cultural messages about the body.

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 173
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 173 • Does the child express bodily worries or concerns? • Does the child express dislike of their body? • Does the child receive pleasure from the experience of their body? • Does the child compulsively self-­monitor their body? • Does the child seem indifferent to ap...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 173 • Does the child express bodily worries or concerns? • Does the child express dislike of their body? • Does the child receive pleasure from the experience of their body? • Does the child compulsively self-­monitor their body? • Does the child seem indifferent to appearance or to what others think? • Does the child neglect bodily needs? • Does the child use grooming products? • Does the child express a sense of disconnection from their body? • Can the child show bodily pride? • Can the child control bodily functions (e.g., toileting, fine and gross movement in getting dressed, bathing, or writing)? Rating Scale At the end of this chapter, in Table 3.15a, we provide a 5-point rating scale to assess each MC-Axis capacity, which can be used with the following anchor points for the Levels 5, 3, and 1. 5. The child experiences the body as part of the self. The child has a sense of bodily integrity and a desire to care for and protect the body, with an age-­appropriate ability to identify body parts and locations. The child can name and communicate physical sensations, make meaning of them, and give the impression of being able to listen to physical cues. There is age-­appropriate coordination and a sense of how to move one’s body and of how one’s own unique body moves and works. The body is a source of pride and enjoyment as the child explores motor skills and coordination. 3. The child has reached age-­appropriate levels of development in some areas but not others, or tends to regress when emotions are activated, or when the child is under stress. The child may struggle with feelings of body dissatisfaction or discomfort. There may be difficulty in identifying certain physiological indicators of emotions. Semantic understanding, including names and location of body parts, is underdeveloped. 1. The child displays a marked detachment, disconnection, or disorganization with respect to the body. The child appears not to register bodily sensations such as hunger, thirst, or the need to use the toilet, or to compulsively ignore these signals. The child may neglect the body, not seeming to care about it, or may be obsessive about physical appearance. The child may lack awareness of the body in space and with other people, and may be dysregulated, clumsy, or intrusive, or highly restricted and frozen. 5. Capacity for Differentiation and Integration The capacity for differentiation and integration reflects the ability to construct and maintain a differentiated, realistic, coherent, and complex representation of self (identity). This concept captures what the child experiences as their basic identity. A person’s identity comprises an integration of self-­representations, body characteristics and functions, wishes, emotions, and personality features. The experience of the body plays a pivotal role in the construction of the psychological self. The capacity for bodily experiences and representations is thus interconnected with this capacity. When assessing the capacity for differentiation and

chunks.heading: 174 Childhood
chunks.preview: 174 Childhood integration, however, the clinician should focus on features of the psychological self, keeping in mind that there is not a clear body–mind duality. During the gradual process of developing a sense of self (i.e., representations of one’s self) the child’s relationship with caregivers p...
chunks.text: 174 Childhood integration, however, the clinician should focus on features of the psychological self, keeping in mind that there is not a clear body–mind duality. During the gradual process of developing a sense of self (i.e., representations of one’s self) the child’s relationship with caregivers plays a critical role. The caregiver’s “mirroring” of the baby’s physical and affective states, when done with contingency, congruency, and markedness, allows the infant to internalize the caregiver’s responses. This internalization contributes to the gradual development of a sense of self that can be differentiated from that of others. In early development, this differentiation is partial; the child might need to identify with unrealistic representations to withstand the sense of inadequacy that the individuation process can evoke; eventually, the growing child can develop a realistic and coherent sense of a subjective psychological self. When rating this capacity, clinicians should pay attention to the child’s ability to distinguish (1) self from other, (2) fantasy from external reality, (3) internal images from external objects and circumstances, and (4) present from past and future mental states, making connections between these elements without confounding them. Important Considerations • Quality of identifications and early attachment relationships: The child acquires qualities perceived in others through a process of internalization or introjection. Perceived parts of the other slowly become part of the self. This process of identification occurs outside awareness (is unconscious) from the beginning of the child’s life within the context of relationship with caregivers. Healthy identifications foster differentiation and psychological growth. Identifications also help individuals integrate into their identity elements of social, cultural, and gender roles. The quality of a child’s attachment to caregivers is critical to this process. In a secure environment, children can feel safe to explore their own mind and the minds of others. This process promotes self-­integration, enhancing the child’s development of a coherent sense of self. Attachment trauma, parental abuse or neglect, parental mental illness, and other adverse conditions can affect the child’s identifications and promote the internalization of “bad” or “alien” parts in the child’s psychological self. These internalized parts usually have an aggressive quality toward self or others. A caregiver’s difficulties in self and other differentiation can lead to impingements upon the child’s sense of self. The outcome of these adversities can foster the development of a false self and/or serious deficiencies in self-­esteem, as the child is forced to accommodate to the caregiver’s agenda rather than the child’s personal needs and goals. • Evolving cognitive skills: Cognitive maturation enables the child to form more complex and accurate representations of self and others. • Parental projections: During the child’s growth, projections of parents onto the child influence the child’s sense of identity. For example, parents who project their own hostility onto the child, perceiving the child as hostile, may create in that child a representation of self as being mean and unlikable. Hence, during meetings with the child, the parents, or the family, the clinician needs to be attentive to attributions that may be projections. A child who is high-­functioning in this capacity can organize and manage various role demands (i.e., sibling, student, friend) that promote social adaptation. The child is open to novel experiences and can enjoy social life and personal achievements. A

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 175
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 175 clinician can infer the child’s sense of continuity from a capacity to integrate various affective states and also from the quality of the child’s stories and descriptions about self and family life (emergence of the capacity for autobiographical memory). Low funct...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 175 clinician can infer the child’s sense of continuity from a capacity to integrate various affective states and also from the quality of the child’s stories and descriptions about self and family life (emergence of the capacity for autobiographical memory). Low functioning in this capacity implies: • Constriction and oversimplification of experience. For example: (1) The child is not open to new experiences or feels threatened by other people’s differences (narcissistic vulnerability); (b) the child cannot reflect on the self or provide a description of the self (in age-­appropriate terms), or describe personal feelings; (c) the child cannot identify personal wishes or substantiate personal actions. • Confusion between self and others: Children with deficits in this capacity cannot differentiate their own feelings, personality features, needs, and desires from those of others. • Confusion between fantasy (internal states) and external reality. For example, the child attributes characteristics to self or others according to internal thoughts and fantasies, and not external reality. The outcome usually is a fragmented sense of self and others, and a deficiency in the capacity for reality testing. • Confusion between past, present, and future. For example, (a) An older child feels weak and inadequate in a situation that could in fact be managed. The feeling of a weak self might belong to earlier years of their development. (b) The child cannot feel any continuity between past, present, and future. An emotionally dysregulating situation experienced and described some days ago may be totally forgotten in the present. When asked about it, a boy with this deficit might say it happened a long time ago, that now he is fine and has no such worries anymore. Or even worse, he might completely alter the description of the story or the affect accompanying it, because the current emotional state is different. This is a form of dissociation that disrupts the sense of a coherent identity. In such cases the clinician notices that the child can keep in mind only the affective experience of the here-and-now and cannot work through emotions and experiences of the past, nor can the child imagine a change of emotions in the future (i.e., think of better days to come) when the situation at hand is emotionally dysregulating. The capacity should be evaluated considering the child’s developmental stage and their expected environment (see Table 3.6). The following questions are intended to guide the exploration and support the construction of a preliminary clinical formulation: • Can the child give an age-­appropriate description of personal characteristics? • Can the child keep self-­continuity across time (past, present, future) and different affective states? Or does the child appear to be fragmented? • Can the child describe self and experiences in age-­appropriate terms? • Are the child’s play scenarios coherent? • Have the child’s caregivers provided a stable, predictable upbringing? • Can caregivers differentiate their needs and aspirations from those of the child? Does the child seek to please them, adapting to their needs? • Can the child approach new experiences and relationships with confidence? Or is there non-age-­appropriate dependency and self-­constriction? • Does the child have good reality-­testing capacity when differentiating self from others?

chunks.preview: 176 Childhood TABLE 3.6. Rating Scale At the end of this chapter, in Table 3.15a, we provide a 5-point rating scale to assess each MC-Axis capacity, which can be used with the following anchor points for the Levels 5, 3, and 1. 5. The child has the capacity to integrate healthy identifications and can organize and differentiate experiences of self and others over time and across contexts. Play scenarios and descriptions of self and others reveal coherence and an age-­appropriate stability. The child seems open to experiences and has a spontaneous and flexible interpersonal style, which is increasingly shaped by the child’s sense of self in the world. 3. One or more of the following applies: (a) The child can differentiate and integrate self-and-other experience, but with some constriction, confusion, or rigidity, especially when strong emotions are present; stressful situations or conflicts can lead to temporary fragmentation or polarization of internal experience; (b) the child may confuse past, present, and future mental states of self and others, or seem more vulnerable and less open to new experiences; (c) the child may develop a false-self organization, adapting to others’ wishes at the expense of personal needs; (d) overall, while the child seems to seek a place in the world, the capacity to do so seems weak; others’ reflections are needed to integrate self-­characteristics and internal experiences. 1. Internal experience is fragmented most of the time or is rigidly compartmentalized and oversimplified; in extreme cases, internal experience may be detached from external context. Internalization of “alien” or “bad” parts disrupt the child’s self-­ coherence. The child seems developmentally stuck in an earlier state of dependency, and in a state in which self and other are undifferentiated.

chunks.heading: 178 Childhood
chunks.preview: 178 Childhood 6. Capacity to Regulate Self‐Esteem The capacity to regulate self-­esteem concerns the child’s level of confidence and estimation of self-value. It involves experiencing the self as an active agent with self-­efficacy and a capacity for self-­control (Deniz & Yildirim KurtuluŞ, 2025)....
chunks.text: 178 Childhood 6. Capacity to Regulate Self‐Esteem The capacity to regulate self-­esteem concerns the child’s level of confidence and estimation of self-value. It involves experiencing the self as an active agent with self-­efficacy and a capacity for self-­control (Deniz & Yildirim KurtuluŞ, 2025). It is connected with the child’s capacity to meet internal standards and come closer to an ego ideal. This capacity is rooted in, and is inextricably linked with, the capacity for differentiation and integration, but it focuses not on who the child feels to be (identity) but on how the child feels about that. Early relationships with caregivers affect the development of this capacity. Experiences of well-being, respect from caregivers, and realistic positive regard are gradually internalized by the developing child (Zhang, Wang, Liu, Feng, & Wei, 2023). A good-­ enough affective attunement of caregivers with the child results in building an internal sense of self as separate and as an active agent of change in relationships, and also the child’s internalizing a sense of him- or herself and his or her life are valued. Moments of rupture and repair (Scholtes, Lyons, & Skowron, 2021) form the child’s capacity to interact with the external world in a flexible, effective way, increasing the child’s self-­ esteem (e.g., “My mommy really loves me, and I love my mommy”). Physical deficiencies and cognitive difficulties (language, perceptual–­ motor, memory, or attention difficulties) can affect a child’s self-­esteem, as can a deformity or a problem in bladder or bowel control (Kaskens, Segers, Goei, van Luit, & Verhoeven, 2020). Both clinical and empirical data show that learning delays during the preschool years affect the experience of separation, autonomy, and self-­regulation. Parents’ self-­ esteem (inferable during clinical interviews) affects their capacity for reflective functioning and therefore their ability to model effective ways of coping with constitutional and environmental sources of stress that may affect the child’s emerging level of confidence. High functioning in this capacity involves a balanced self-­regard based on a realistic view of the child’s positive characteristics and achievements. Personal qualities and achievements are neither idealized nor denigrated unrealistically. Low functioning in this capacity can appear as follows: • Unrealistic sense of self-­regard that results in feeling either omnipotence and inflated self-worth, or a low estimation of self-value. • Experiences of disruptions in self-­coherence that result in difficulties in estimating self-worth. • Unbalanced and oscillating sense of self-worth depending on circumstances (deficiencies in self-­esteem regulation). The capacity should be evaluated considering the child’s developmental stage and their expected environment (see Table 3.7). The following questions are intended to guide the exploration and support the construction of a preliminary clinical formulation: • Is the child’s experienced self a source of pleasure or pain and conflict (e.g., “I’m a nice boy” vs. “No one likes me”)? What clues are in the child’s play? • How does the child feel about relationship experiences (e.g., “My friends want to play with me” or “Nobody wants me as their friend”)?

chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 179 • Are the child’s statements and achievements seen as realistically valuable to others? • How stable is the child’s self-­esteem in the face of failures or difficulties (in relationships, activities, learning)? Can the child accept mistakes and the feelings that accompany them? • Are there any peculiarities or deficits that affect the child’s self-­esteem (e.g., learning difficulties, language disorders, motor difficulties)? • Does the child have a realistically positive self-­perception, or is it unrealistically positive or negative? • Can the child accept weaknesses and difficulties, participating in activities without excessive shame or anxiety? • Is the child’s sense of self-worth stable or are there wide fluctuations in self-­ esteem? • Is the child able to regulate self-­esteem internally or is help from others (adults or peers) needed to do so? chunks.heading: 180 Childhood
chunks.preview: 180 Childhood Rating Scale At the end of this chapter, in Table 3.15a, we provide a 5-point rating scale to assess each MC-Axis capacity, which can be used with the following anchor points for the Levels 5, 3, and 1. 5. The child shows age-­appropriate capacity to maintain a stable sense of wellbein...
chunks.text: 180 Childhood Rating Scale At the end of this chapter, in Table 3.15a, we provide a 5-point rating scale to assess each MC-Axis capacity, which can be used with the following anchor points for the Levels 5, 3, and 1. 5. The child shows age-­appropriate capacity to maintain a stable sense of wellbeing, confidence, and realistic (not grandiose) self-­esteem (e.g., “I am good, but I am not better than everyone all the time”). Self-­esteem is balanced with, and appropriate to, internal and external situations. The child shows a developmentally adequate level of confidence in a capacity to deal with a broad array of tasks and challenges, including novel situations. These characteristics positively influence the way the child thinks, feels, and relates to others and the larger world. 3. The child’s sense of well-being, confidence, vitality, and self-­esteem is generally adequate but may be easily disrupted by strong internal emotions and stressful external situations, causing some fluctuations the child’s estimation of self-worth. The child often expresses (in words, play, behavior) feelings of vulnerability and inadequacy, with diminished confidence when in certain situations (e.g., peer activities), or when pursuing certain goals, or when trying to act effectively in the world (sense of agency). 1. The child shows feelings and behaviors indicating a deficient sense of self-­ regard (e.g., the figure in the dollhouse is lonely and has no friends, even though she is nice). Defensive maneuvers to manage feelings of low confidence are evident, as in the use of omnipotence and denial in fantasy (e.g., “I don’t want to play sports because I am good at all that stuff; the other kids are bad at it, and it’s frustrating”). There are notable inconsistencies in the child’s self-­evaluation; self-­regard fluctuates between being unrealistically high and unrealistically low. Such self-­esteem dysregulation may lead to a diminished capacity to cope with frustration and engage in age-­appropriate activities. 7. Capacity for Trust, Empathy, and Intimacy The capacity for trust, empathy, and intimacy reflects the quality of the child’s relationships with others, both external and internalized. It indicates the child’s ability to trust in a safe environment, feel empathy for others, and adjust interpersonal distance–­closeness as needed for different relationships, in line with cultural expectations (Fonagy & Allison, 2023). Trust in relationships is an evolving capacity that facilitates the child’s social learning. We learn from those we trust, and we internalize other people and qualities of relationships through practicing empathy and curiosity toward self and others. A child’s attachment style is linked to early experiences; if these have created basic trust, the child becomes open to learning new ways of relating with new people. The capacity should be evaluated considering the child’s developmental stage and their expected environment (see Table 3.8). Clinicians should pay attention to the following elements: 1. The quality of the child’s current relationships. Information from the child, from caregivers, and from other involved individuals (e.g., in school, sports, religious

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 181
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 181 communities) can reveal how the child relates or avoids relating to different people in the environment (family, teachers, peers, the therapist) (Dunn, 2004). Clinicians can reflect on their own relationship with the child, evaluating the child’s capacity to form r...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 181 communities) can reveal how the child relates or avoids relating to different people in the environment (family, teachers, peers, the therapist) (Dunn, 2004). Clinicians can reflect on their own relationship with the child, evaluating the child’s capacity to form relationships in an age-, context- and culture-­appropriate manner (with the degree of closeness or distance expected in each relationship), paying attention to any appeals for intimacy that are inappropriate to age or context (e.g., sexualized use of the body, gestures) (Benzi et al., 2023). How does the child react to meeting the clinician for the first time? Does the child seem too trusting or cautiously curious and connected with the clinician? Is the child able to ask for what he or she needs to manage the situation (e.g., call the caregiver into the room, get more assistance from the clinician in completing a task). Finally, does the child exhibits signs of appropriate epistemic trust in the context of meeting the clinician for the first time? 2 . The quality of the child’s internal representations of relationship. The relational patterns of children in the present are linked to their past experiences of relationship (Locati et al., 2023). Therefore, clinicians should evaluate the child’s internal representation of him- or herself in the context of relationships. What seem to be the child’s interactive expectations? The quality of a child’s attachment can be inferred from behavior with caregivers and with the therapist, and sometimes can be inferred from the child’s symptoms. For example, a child with attachment security can explore the world and engage in relationships in an age-­adequate manner that fosters the development of capacities for trust and intimacy. In contrast, a child with an insecure or disorganized attachment pattern may show intense separation anxiety, develop vigilance in relationships, or be unable to trust information conveyed by others (e.g., friends, family, teachers, the therapist). Clinicians should evaluate the child’s internal representations of significant others, considering the child’s transference and the clinician’s countertransference. “Transference” refers to unconscious infantile prototypes of relating that reemerge in the relationships with others, including the clinician (Steckley, 2023). The child unconsciously attributes to the clinician affects and intentions characteristic of past important relationships and anticipates emotional responses from the clinician that resemble the child’s internalized relational patterns in these early relationships. “Countertransference” is the clinician’s subjective response (physical, emotional, behavioral) to the child’s transference. Observation of the child’s transference and the clinician’s countertransference can provide vital information about the child’s capacity for trust and intimacy, and the influence of early relationships on the child’s sense of self. Some illustrations: (a) A child might appear to be enacting a parent’s attitude or behaving as he or she thinks the parent would wish (“overadaptation”); a child who has experienced abuse in the family can be excessively alert in relationships with others, keeping them at arm’s length as the child waits for—and sometimes even unconsciously provokes, to get it over with—a repetition of the abuse in their relationship with other people; or (b) such a child might completely withdraw from relationships or activities. 3. Capacity for empathy: Consider the child’s ability to understand social and emotional cues and respond accordingly (Heynen, Simon, van der Helm, Stams, & Assink, 2021). Understanding others’ emotions in social interactions allows the child to be empathic to others. Does the child seem to express interest in learning from others and seeing others as potential sources of connection and intimacy? Does the child express genuine interest in the other both verbally and nonverbally?

Toward the end of the 10th year, there is less reactivity to day-to-day peer fluctuations. The following questions are intended to guide the exploration and support the construction of a preliminary clinical formulation: • Does the child seek to engage with others? • Is the child withdrawn or avoidant? • Does the child’s relationship with caregivers suggest attachment security? • To whom does the child turn to enhance a sense of safety? • What or who makes the child feel afraid? • Does the child experience separation anxiety when away from parents? • How does the child relate to the clinician? Is it context- and age-­appropriate, or does the child demonstrate peculiar or unexpected relational patterns? • Does the child have friends? Are these close or superficial friendships? • In safe settings, is the child able to trust others and connect in an emotionally warm way, without vigilance? • Is the child trustworthy, or does the child engage in mischievous behaviors toward friends and family members? • Is the child’s relational focus on dyadic or triadic relationships? • What is the child’s level of investment in others? • What is the overall affective tone of the child’s relationships (e.g., is the child controlling or submissive)? • Does the child seek affection from others and show affection toward them? • Does the child seek assistance from others and, if so, from whom? To what degree? Appropriately, excessively?

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 185
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 185 • Does the child show evidence of non-age-­appropriate dependency? • Is there a history of relational trauma? • How does the child manage relationships in groups? Does the child become active, likable, competitive, or withdrawn? • Can the child respond appropriatel...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 185 • Does the child show evidence of non-age-­appropriate dependency? • Is there a history of relational trauma? • How does the child manage relationships in groups? Does the child become active, likable, competitive, or withdrawn? • Can the child respond appropriately to social cues? • Is there mutuality in the child’s relation to peers? • Is there evidence of concern, empathy, and age-­appropriate understanding toward others? Both adults and peers? • Can the child tolerate (when age-­appropriate) both likable and unlikable aspects of a close person? • Are elements of past relationships repeated in the child’s relations with friends or with the clinician? Does the child try to master traumatic experiences by reenacting them in new situations? • Do parents repeat past relational patterns with each other or with their child? Rating Scale At the end of this chapter, in Table 3.15a, we provide a 5-point rating scale to assess each MC-Axis capacity, which can be used with the following anchor points for Levels 5, 3, and 1. 5. The child can form and maintain relationships with adults and peers, showing an appropriate capacity in terms of age, culture, and context for intimacy and empathy, even when under stress and in the face of strong feelings. The child’s internalized relational patterns suggest security that allows trust and exploration. Inner images of relationships promote mutually gratifying connections. The child shows a developmentally normative desire and capacity to seek closeness in relationships. 3. The child can be described in one or more of the following ways: (a) The child is able to form relationships with adults and peers in a manner appropriate to age, culture, and context, but strong emotions (e.g., anger, fear, jealousy), conflicts, and stress can disrupt the capacity for intimacy and empathy; (b) internalized relational patterns suggest insecurity; the child sometimes shows reluctance to trust others; (c) the child shows an emotional dependency more typical of earlier developmental phases, sometimes resulting in overcompliance with the wishes of others. 1. The child can form only superficial, need-­oriented relationships distant from age-­expected norms. The child cannot read social cues and lacks the capacity for empathy. The child is either withdrawn from relationships, showing lack of interest and/or trust, or seeks closeness and intimacy in ways that are inappropriate to age, culture, and context. 8. Capacity for Impulse Regulation The capacity for impulse regulation refers to the child’s ability to restrain or control actions, when necessary, even when feelings are strong or when restriction of behavior conflicts with the child’s wishes (Lang & Perner, 2002). The ability to control impulses and modulate emotional expressions is the earliest and most ubiquitous demand that society places on children. Although impulse regulation can be considered a dimension

chunks.heading: 186 Childhood
chunks.preview: 186 Childhood of personality, children with difficulties in impulse control, particularly those with attention and perceptual–­motor impairments, are at risk for a diverse range of behavioral, social, emotional, and academic problems that may lead to severe psychopathology in later years. Impulsivit...
chunks.text: 186 Childhood of personality, children with difficulties in impulse control, particularly those with attention and perceptual–­motor impairments, are at risk for a diverse range of behavioral, social, emotional, and academic problems that may lead to severe psychopathology in later years. Impulsivity problems have been associated with numerous neuropsychiatric disorders, including addictions and conduct disorder/antisocial personality disorder (Nigg, Hinshaw, & Huang-­Pollock, 2015). Multiple psychological theories have considered impulsivity and the development of impulse control, inhibition, and self-­regulatory behaviors during childhood. Psychoanalytic theory posits that impulse control and self-­regulatory behaviors are outcomes of the individual’s defensive strategies as the ego negotiates conflicts between drives or wishes, moral values, and demands of the external world. Neuroimaging and neurophysiological studies (Lin et al., 2023; Probst & Van Eimeren, 2013) indicate that age-­related changes in brain network activities underlie the improvements in impulse control that characterize normal human maturation. Improvements in the control and regulation of impulses constitute the primary developmental achievement of children during the first decade of life. These gradual improvements are involved in controlling emotions, interacting positively with others, avoiding inappropriate aggression, and becoming an autonomous learner. Emerging empirical data on normative populations (as measured by self-­report measures, neuropsychological testing batteries, and behavioral observation) (Rohr, Bray, & Dewey, 2023; Yen, Lin, & Chiang, 2023) suggest that impulse control, self-­ regulation, and other impulsivity-­related phenomena follow different temporal trajectories; impulsivity decreases linearly over time, and sensation seeking and reward responsiveness follow an inverted U-­shaped trajectory across the lifespan. These different trajectories correlate with developmental brain changes, including early maturation of subcortical regions and later maturation of the frontal lobes, and may underlie the risk-­taking behavior often observed during adolescence. Many of the capacities involved in impulse regulation appear to have a basis in temperament. Temperamental self-­regulatory capacities have been termed “effortful control,” which includes shifting and focusing attention as needed, inhibiting inappropriate behavior, acting when strongly inclined to avoid, and carrying out some executive functioning skills involved in integrating information and planning. Effortful control plays a role in self-­regulation and also has a significant effect on personality functioning. It has been linked conceptually to the development and maintenance of children’s externalizing problems (e.g., aggression, defiance) (Heylen et al., 2024; Xu et al., 2023). It is believed to be involved in processing information and modulating emotion. In other words, a child who is having difficulty with executive functioning may also have difficulty tolerating negative feelings (e.g., sadness, anxiety, guilt) and will tend to manage those affects through externalizing behaviors (e.g., attributing blame to others), which can interfere with both learning and social-­ emotional development. Clinicians should observe the child’s capacity for effortful control during assessment and also obtain information from caregivers about the child’s behavior across various contexts. Deficiencies in this capacity may lead to unmodulated displays of aggressiveness and lack of age-­appropriate inhibition in one or more settings (home, school, play) (Diamant & Smet, 2024). Lack of adequate controls can lead to disruptions in learning and in relationships with peers and/or authorities. At the other end of this spectrum, children who excessively inhibit aggression and desire may become

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 187
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 187 rigid and overly controlled, also interfering with relationships and learning (Van Heel et al., 2020). High functioning in this capacity involves the ability to tolerate frustration when necessary, and to recognize and describe one’s impulses in the service of self...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 187 rigid and overly controlled, also interfering with relationships and learning (Van Heel et al., 2020). High functioning in this capacity involves the ability to tolerate frustration when necessary, and to recognize and describe one’s impulses in the service of self-­regulation. The capacity should be evaluated and consider the child’s developmental stage and their expected environment (see Table 3.9). The following questions are intended to guide the exploration and support the construction of a preliminary clinical formulation: • Does the child have a capacity for effortful control? • Does the child act impulsively, disregarding consequences? • Can the child cope with feelings and wishes in symbolic ways (e.g., via play) or does the child act out and/or use the body to discharge impulses? • Is the child hyperactive? • Can the child state wishes or object to other’s views when they are felt to be unfair, in ways appropriate to the social and cultural context? • Does the child seek immediate gratification of needs and desires, ignoring the rules and constraints of the environment? • Is the child excessively inhibited and overly accommodating to others? • Does the child have frequent temper tantrums and/or uncontrollable outbursts? • Does the child easily get into fights or engage in dangerous actions? • Is there evidence of self-­harming behaviors? • Is there any developmentally or culturally inappropriate sexual behavior? Rating Scale At the end of this chapter, in Table 3.15a, we provide a 5-point rating scale to assess each MC-Axis capacity, which can be used with the following anchor points for Levels 5, 3, and 1. 5. The child regularly and spontaneously considers the probable consequences of actions before acting. In the face of challenges or distress, the child can consider how past experience and current realities should inform behavior. The child can plan ahead and rely on self-­developed techniques to help regulate behavior. While being highly attuned to contextual and social norms that help to inform decisions, the child has an internalized sense of how to respond and has an internal locus of control. 3. The child is generally able to delay initial responses in consideration of the probable consequences of actions, including their impacts on others’ feelings. In the absence of a clear external authority, there may be more difficulty inhibiting responses and regulating behavior. In situations that are more challenging or frustrating, the child may be less able to regulate behavior and may respond in ways that have negative consequences for self or others. 1. The child’s inability to inhibit impulses or sustain attention in a developmentally appropriate way leads to behaviors that have negative effects on self and others. The child lacks the ability to consider the probable consequences of actions before acting. Initial reaction may be aggressive, disruptive, or uncooperative.
