Defensive Functioning The area of defensive functioning refers to a child’s ability to manage states of emotional arousal that are potentially overwhelming and inimical to development. Psychoanalytic theories of personality posit “ego defenses” as strategies to deal with anxiety and negotiate normal conflicts both internally (e.g., when one has conflicting aims, such as the desire to depend and the desire to be autonomous) and externally (e.g., when one’s wishes conflict with external demands) (Hoffman, Rice, & Prout, 2017). When a child’s defensive functioning fails, a clinician may witness moments of high emotional dysregulation and disorganization. Defensive style is central to the

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 189
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 189 organization of personality, follows specific lines of development, and is related to specific personality traits. A developmental view of defenses helps clinicians consider a particular defensive strategy in the context of the child’s current maturational stage (C...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 189 organization of personality, follows specific lines of development, and is related to specific personality traits. A developmental view of defenses helps clinicians consider a particular defensive strategy in the context of the child’s current maturational stage (Cramer, 2015). The period from ages 4–11 includes rapid maturational shifts that culminate in an autonomous sense of self in the context of relationships that may be sources of both support and psychic pain. Effective defenses allow children to navigate rough waters on their own, minimizing reality distortions and externalization of affects through behavior or use of the body. Defenses can address mental states stimulated from inside (e.g., guilt) and outside (e.g., fear) (Takyun & Ogwo, 2020). Anna Freud (1936) introduced the idea of a developmental hierarchy of defenses, a concept that has been supported by empirical research. “Primitive” defenses appear in early infancy, whereas “higher-­level” defenses arise after object constancy is established (when a child can keep a mental image of a caregiver that makes the child feel safe even when the caregiver is not present). “High-cost defenses” are primitive processes that continue to be used even when higher-­level defenses would be more adaptive. Defenses emerge at different moments of normal development during childhood with different intensity, depending on the stage of maturation and the psychological context. A developmental view of defenses allows for understanding the relational context in which they arise and provides a framework from which to understand observable defensive behaviors, affects, and ideas. Denial and projection are normal in early childhood but may indicate pathology later on. Repression and other higher-­level defenses emerge later in childhood. Altruism, humor, and sublimation are adaptive defenses that foster physical and mental health across the lifespan. We all use higher-­level as well as primitive or costly defenses. Optimal adaptation relies on the supremacy of higher-­level defenses. Overreliance on immature defenses, such as projective identification and splitting, is associated with greater symptom severity, suicide risk, personality disorder, insecure attachment, negative physical health outcome, and high levels of stress. Although defenses operate mainly unconsciously, their outcomes are discernible in behavior. Research (Nesse, 1990) has demonstrated that an individual can be partially or fully aware of using defenses higher in the hierarchy; for example, one can be aware of the presence of repression in one’s behavior and yet be unaware of the purposes, drives, and affects underlying its use. Or a person can be fully aware of using sublimation to fuel creativity or humor to deal with stress. For many years, the study of defenses in children has been limited to those considered observable at early stages of development. Scientists assumed that before cognitive functions mature, a child would not be able to use higher-­level defenses such as sublimation, whereas because of its simplicity, denial would be a preferred option. Recent studies, however, applying the DMRS theory for assessing the hierarchy of defenses, have demonstrated that young children can access the whole range of defenses (Kuftyak, 2020; Prout, Malone, Rice, & Hoffman, 2019). Because the primary function of defenses, both in childhood and adulthood, is to reduce stress from painful emotions, thoughts, and conflicts, one would expect that in moments of increased stress (disappointments, situations that challenge the child’s sense of control, threats to self-­esteem), the use of defenses would increase (Hoffman et al., 2017). Recourse to defenses would also be influenced by the type of stress, length of time experienced, level of stress, and effectiveness of the defense (its success at minimizing psychological disequilibrium). Stress-­inducing environmental factors

chunks.heading: 190 Childhood
chunks.preview: 190 Childhood and the possible patterns in response are essential to understanding use of defenses in childhood and to evaluating variables that facilitate or stunt development. When interviewing and observing parents and children, clinicians thus need to understand defenses in context, considering...
chunks.text: 190 Childhood and the possible patterns in response are essential to understanding use of defenses in childhood and to evaluating variables that facilitate or stunt development. When interviewing and observing parents and children, clinicians thus need to understand defenses in context, considering whether a child’s use of a defense is a way to adapt to cultural expectations and/or to a specific caregiving environment (Prout, Di Giuseppe, Alcha-Mano, Perry, & Conversano, 2022; Rice & Hoffman, 2014). Cultural considerations are highly relevant, as different cultures and subcultures foster different defensive strategies. In terms of the specific family situation, access to caregiving is so paramount for human beings that even when it is problematic, children are strongly motivated to maintain relationships with caregivers, regardless of the cost to their own development. In coping with a warped environment, a child’s adaptation will often be similarly warped, at the cost of healthy socialization, reality testing, impulse control, and overall psychological development. Defenses that involve action or major distortions of reality appear with higher frequency in physically abused children. The capacity should be evaluated considering the child’s developmental stage and his or her expected environment (see Table 3.10). The following questions are intended to guide the exploration and support the construction of a preliminary clinical formulation: • What are the main defenses that the child uses? • Do the child’s defenses massively distort reality or adapt to it? • What does the child defend against? Anxieties arising from the external world, anxieties originating internally, anxieties caused by conflict between the two? • How effective are the child’s usual defenses? • Are they age- and culture-­appropriate? • How rigid are they? • How do they affect the child’s relational functioning? • How dominant are immature defenses (e.g., splitting, projection, somatization)? • Is the child’s ego in a traumatized state of helplessness, and is there evidence of dissociation or withdrawal? • Is lack of effective defensive strategies causing emotional dysregulation? Rating Scale At the end of this chapter, in Table 3.15a, we provide a 5-point rating scale to assess each MC-Axis capacity, which can be used with the following anchor points for Levels 5, 3, and 1. 5. Highly adaptive defensive strategies predominate, allowing the child to experience a broad range of affects and to be aware of thoughts and feelings, and manage them in ways that permit continued growth. For example, the child can reflect upon relevant personal experiences, explore emotional reactions, and construct an accurate image of self and how that self may appear from another person’s perspective (self-­ observation). The child can describe emotionally conflictual situations and can channel dissatisfactions into creative activities (sublimation). The child may use neurotic-­ level obsessional defenses such as undoing; for example, in response to a feeling of guilt or shame, the child can make an act of reparation. When confronting emotionally

chunks.heading: 192 Childhood
chunks.preview: 192 Childhood charged topics, the child may use displacement to avoid addressing concerns directly (e.g., paying attention to someone dealing with a similar situation). Although the child may use immature defenses, they do not significantly impair functioning, as the child typically reverts to more...
chunks.text: 192 Childhood charged topics, the child may use displacement to avoid addressing concerns directly (e.g., paying attention to someone dealing with a similar situation). Although the child may use immature defenses, they do not significantly impair functioning, as the child typically reverts to more adaptive defenses when anxiety, conflict, and stress diminish. 3. The child uses mainly minor image-­distorting and disavowing defenses. When problematic feelings or wishes arise, the child gives some evidence of them (e.g., crying, appearing anxious, forgetting things, developing somatic symptoms with a symbolic relationship to the type of distress) but cannot clearly verbalize specific ideas associated with the emotional experience. In response to disappointment, shame, or injury to self-­esteem, the child may resort to devaluation, idealization, and omnipotence and may quickly dismiss the stressors. The child may view problems as external, and may minimize or ignore internal sources (developmental expectations are important in determining this). For example, a girl avoids acknowledging her own feelings or intentions by giving a plausible explanation that covers up her real motives (rationalization). There may be modest distortions of reality, beyond what would be developmentally expected. The child may seem defensive and may disavow aspects of experience through denial and projection (e.g., in play, the child may stop the action at a charged point, announcing, “The end!”, or abruptly stop talking about something). Overall, use of defenses is somewhat unpredictable and fluctuating, and sometimes relies on immature processes such as splitting and projective identification. 1. The child depends mostly on immature defenses and is easily overwhelmed by anxieties about psychological annihilation, physical harm, or death. These fears are not based in reality but in the child’s difficulties dealing with internal conflicts while coping with external demands. The child grossly distorts the external world but suffers poorly contained anxiety. Adaptation is highly impaired. Action and major image-­ distorting defenses predominate. In response to disappointment, disagreement, rejection, or internal anger or pain, the child may act rashly, without considering possible negative consequences, and may resort to impulsive escapes, such as reckless defiance of authority or hypomanic playfulness. The child’s self-­description is at times wholly negative; hatred of others may be similarly categorical, with no acknowledgment of the other’s redeeming features (splitting). Reliance on projective identification severely impairs the child’s capacity to function either in relationships or independently (e.g., when upset at someone in reality or in play, the child rages, loses control, and blames the other for the loss; still, the child may feel some guilt for losing control). 10. Capacity for Adaptation and Resilience The capacity for adaptation and resilience refers to the ability of a system to adapt successfully to challenges that threaten its function, survival, or future development. This definition is scalable across system levels and disciplines, and may be applied to an individual or family, or to a health care system, community, or economy. Contemporarily, resilience science is surging in the face of growing evidence about lifelong consequences of adverse childhood experiences. Robust findings (Fu, Pan, Zhao, Ji, & Peng, 2024; Gajwani & Minnis, 2023; Navarro-­Pérez et al., 2023; Zhang, Mersky, Gruber, & Kim, 2023) on resilience in childhood highlight the role of exposure dose (how intense and/or protracted were the adversities?), the value of an adaptive

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 193
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 193 perspective, the issue of timing within overall development, and the critical role of educators, health care professionals, and caregivers in nurturing resilience on the “front lines” of childhood experience. When assessing this capacity, a clinician should seek to...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 193 perspective, the issue of timing within overall development, and the critical role of educators, health care professionals, and caregivers in nurturing resilience on the “front lines” of childhood experience. When assessing this capacity, a clinician should seek to identify risk factors, as well as factor that protect the child’s resilience and overall well-being. We recommend a strength-­based lens in assessing children’s capacity to adapt and respond to challenges with behavioral and emotional flexibility. Childhood resiliency involves reduced vulnerability to adverse experiences, management of stress, and relatively good outcome despite adversity (Kisbu et al., 2023). It is inherently connected with the child’s use of defenses. High-level defenses (that do not distort reality, initiate impulsive actions, or express pain somatically) promote resiliency and adaptation. The capacity to mentalize others’ needs and feelings empathically, the ability to take perspective, and a tendency toward appropriate assertiveness can all be viewed as strengths. In an epigenetic process, just as the child influences the context, the context influences the child (Cecil, Neumann, & Walton, 2023; de Leeuw & Malcolm-­Smith, 2023; Tchombe, 2023). Observations of a child’s functioning and response to stress across settings, as reported by teachers, parents, and others, are extremely valuable when one evaluates a child’s resiliency (Boullier & Blair, 2018; Polat & Kröner, 2023). The diagnostic process itself is for many children a source of significant stress; observing how the child adapts to it is relevant here. Identifying potential or current protective and risk factors in the child’s relational environment is particularly important, especially for treatment planning. The capacity should be evaluated considering the child’s developmental stage and their expected environment (see Table 3.11). The following questions are intended to guide the exploration and support the construction of a preliminary clinical formulation: • What stresses affect the child? How does the child deal with stressful situations? • Can the child rely on inner strengths to cope with difficult situations? Or does the child seem extremely vulnerable even in less stressful situations? • What protective factors may help the child endure difficult situations?(Does the child use effective defense mechanisms? Can he or she seek help from people experienced as safe? Does the child have a good sense of self and self-­ confidence? Does he or she have a capacity for mentalization?) • What risk factors or deficiencies make the child vulnerable, reducing the capacity for adaptation? Rating Scale At the end of this chapter, in Table 3.15a, we provide a 5-point rating scale to assess each MC-Axis capacity, which can be used with the following anchor points for Levels 5, 3, and 1. 5. The child can adapt and respond flexibly to unexpected, stressful situations in age-­appropriate ways that do not significantly compromise overall functioning and ongoing development. The child seems aware of strengths and vulnerabilities, has a repertoire of flexible behavioral responses to stressful situations, and can ask

The child does not suffer states of disorganization or fragmentation, and leads with strengths (e.g., “I can use humor or fantasy to distract myself and cope with this painful experience”). 3. The child can adapt and respond flexibly to most unexpected, stressful situations in age-­appropriate ways. Sometimes, however, states of anxiety seem overwhelming, and the child uses maladaptive defenses that reduce feelings of competence and confidence. At other times, the child can return to a baseline capacity to think about feelings, regaining the capacity for self-­regulation. These experiences do not significantly restrict overall functioning and growth. Mild symptoms may be present. 1. Children at this level seem extremely vulnerable, at the mercy of the external world for their mental state and even physical survival. In unpredictable and stressful situations, their responses are maladaptive. They tend to regress to early modes and to try compulsively to regain some internal balance by exerting control on the external world in maladaptive ways. 11. Capacity to Construct and Use Internal Standards and Ideals The capacity to construct and use internal standards and ideals involves assessing the child’s superego (conscience and ego ideal) (Weiss, 2020). Clinicians need to observe the degree of the superego’s harshness (e.g., a harshly self-­critical child tears up work after completing it), rigidity (e.g., a rigid child erases and redoes work compulsively) or laxity (e.g., a child steals a pencil from a classmate but shows no guilt, shame, or remorse) and assess whether superego functioning is consistent and appropriate. Inconsistent superego functioning suggests that the child’s reactions depend on the meaning ascribed to a given circumstance. Clinicians should observe the balance, if it exists, between benign (e.g., “I can do it again”), punitive (e.g., “I always do everything

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 195
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 195 wrong”), and extremely lenient (e.g., “I’m not doing any of my homework”) superego manifestations. They should also note feelings of guilt, shame, and remorse, evaluating whether they are appropriate to the child’s age and circumstances (Deneault et al., 2023; Thom...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 195 wrong”), and extremely lenient (e.g., “I’m not doing any of my homework”) superego manifestations. They should also note feelings of guilt, shame, and remorse, evaluating whether they are appropriate to the child’s age and circumstances (Deneault et al., 2023; Thompson, 2019). The concept of the “ego ideal” refers to the internal standards we set for ourselves personally and in relationships with others (Jannah, Mulyanti, & Bayan, 2023, Jennah, Mazrur, & Rahmaniati, 2023). Extremely high and unrealistic standards express themselves in harsh judgments of self and others. Setting unrealistic standards can emanate from “wounds” or deficiencies in identity and self-­esteem. The clinician should note whether the child is terrified of being inadequate, or whether there is enough self-­esteem to support realistic motivations for improvement. A child’s superego evolves from the internalization of parental values through identification (Esmaeili, Tahmsebi, Mohammadi Arya, & Soltani, 2021). Caregiver approval or disapproval of their own or other people’s actions forms the child’s moral standards and aspirations. The capacity to construct and use internal standards and ideals is thus interlinked with the capacity for differentiation and integration. It is also centrally involved in the capacity to regulate self-­esteem. High functioning in the capacity to construct and use internal standards and ideals includes realistic ideals for the self and a balanced, benign superego that supports the child’s social adaptation and healthy moral development. Low functioning may take the form of (1) extreme demands to reach unrealistically high standards; (2) overadaptation and self-­restriction; (3) harsh, rigid, or excessively lax superego function; or (4) conspicuous fluctuations between very punitive and very lenient moral standards. The capacity should be evaluated considering the child’s developmental stage and their expected environment (see Table 3.12). The following questions are intended to guide the exploration and support the construction of a preliminary clinical formulation: • Is the child self-­punitive and excessively demanding (e.g., a boy tears up his painting because he doesn’t like it)? • Can the child tolerate imperfections when conducting a project or homework? Or are there signs of perfectionism and rigidity? • Can the child cope effectively with sexual and aggressive impulses? • Is the child overly shy, appearing to lack age-­expected spontaneity? • Is there age- and context-­appropriate guilt for actions or thoughts that contradict the child’s moral values, or the norms imposed by adults? • Does the child have a capacity for forgiveness? • Is the child indifferent to the consequences of personal actions? • Is the child overdemanding toward others? • Does the child engage in self-­punitive actions? Rating Scale At the end of this chapter, in Table 3.15a, we provide a 5-point rating scale to assess each MC-Axis capacity, which can be used with the following anchor points for Levels 5, 3, and 1. 5. The child’s internal standards are flexible and integrated, with a realistic sense of personal capacities and within the norms of the given social and cultural context.

Ages 9–11 • Has consolidated, through identification and internalization, a • Encourage verbalization of conflictual emotions capacity to direct, limit, punish, and reward the self. emerging from differentiation. • Has achieved a mental organization that includes a more • Promote a balanced self-image and encourage gendered self-representation and an internalized sense of self-observation from a balanced, reality-based right and wrong. perspective, preventing excessive harshness. • Shows a fully established capacity for guilt, and with it the need for reparative efforts. • Has stably internalized role-model behavior and positive regard. A more integrated, kinder “inner voice” replaces earlier authoritative messages. The child’s conscience is benign and appropriate to the child’s age and the given situation. Developmentally appropriate feelings of guilt act as signals for reappraising behavior. 3. The child shows one or more of the following patterns: (a) Internal standards and ideals are rigid and insufficiently sensitive to the child’s own capacities and social contexts, resulting in fears of inadequacy; (b) feelings of guilt are experienced more as self-­criticism, causing self-­restriction, than as a signal for reappraising behavior; (c) there is a mild oscillation between benign and more punitive superego manifestations or between appropriate prohibitions and less morally monitored states.

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 197
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 197 1. The child’s conscience seems unduly harsh, and self-­punitive. Or, in contrast, internal values seem to be missing. The child denies feelings of guilt or remorse for problematic actions; internal ideals and moral standards are either absent or unrealistic. 12. C...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 197 1. The child’s conscience seems unduly harsh, and self-­punitive. Or, in contrast, internal values seem to be missing. The child denies feelings of guilt or remorse for problematic actions; internal ideals and moral standards are either absent or unrealistic. 12. Capacity to Explore One’s Inner Life The capacity to explore one’s inner life refers to all processes of representing and observing internal mental states, fantasies, feelings, and past experiences, and their impact on one’s mental world. Until it attains some consolidation, this capacity undergoes many changes. Its evolution depends on the child’s particular cognitive and emotional capacities. From ages 4–11, transformations in how children explore their internal world are substantial. Children over age 10 have developed adequate cognitive and emotional capacities for observing self and others (Fabricius, 2023; Lockman & Tamis-­L eMonda, 2021). They can express their self-­exploration processes by using words to refer to the mental states of self and others. Younger children and less mature older children use fantasy and play to explore their thoughts, wishes, and emotions. In this process, they can identify with different roles, experiment with various alternative perspectives and outcomes, explore relational patterns, and represent their present and past experiences. It is thus through fantasy and play that children learn to explore their inner life. Their ability to use symbols is a central ingredient in forming fantasy scenarios and engaging in pretend play (Goldman, 2020). Capacity to symbolize is influenced by a host of factors: constitutional ones, such as cognitive functioning, and environmental ones, such as the quality of primary relationships. The development of the capacity for symbol formation entails the separation of meaning from things and of subject from object (i.e., child from caregiver). Symbols are representations of a person or a relationship that is currently unavailable to the child. In symbolic play, there is a relationship between the real element (e.g., a pen) and an element the child imagines (e.g., a syringe) and its fictional representation (that the pen is a syringe, and that the child is giving a doll an injection) (Bunce & Woolley, 2021; Colman, 2021). Use of symbols, fantasy, and play are all critical to developing other capacities, such as coping with hard realities and managing painful feelings. Children are not always aware that their play scenarios represent their inner world. They may have vivid imaginations and engage in rich symbolic play, but it might not always be easy to connect these aspects with their emotions and experiences (Normandin, Bate, Bégin, Fonagy, & Ensink, 2023). Children with high functioning in this capacity can sometimes make such connections. They can also use play to express and regulate intense affects or understand and cope with difficult experiences. At an older age, they can explore inner scenarios and imagine self- and others’ mental states in various situations and roles. In younger children, low functioning in this capacity would include very simple play scenarios (e.g., dolls wake up, eat, go to school, and then sleep again), or the absence of fantasy scenarios and symbolic play (e.g., preference for motor activities only, such as running and biking, or preference for turning the wheels of a car instead of rolling it as if on the road), or an inability to find or to accept connections between their play and their inner experiences, wishes, fears, feelings, impulses, or conflicts.

chunks.heading: 198 Childhood
chunks.preview: 198 Childhood The capacity should be evaluated, considering the child’s developmental stage and their expected environment (see Table 3.13). The following questions are intended to guide the exploration and support the construction of a preliminary clinical formulation: • What is the quality of the...
chunks.text: 198 Childhood The capacity should be evaluated, considering the child’s developmental stage and their expected environment (see Table 3.13). The following questions are intended to guide the exploration and support the construction of a preliminary clinical formulation: • What is the quality of the child’s play? Is it rigid and lonely, or is it creative, evolving, and collaborative? • Does the child seem able to reflect on the impact of personal actions on others? • Does the child use play to make sense of difficult situations and feelings? • Is the child able to stay with the play, and come out of it easily, in an age-­ appropriate manner, with some adult support? • Is the child’s capacity for symbolization affected by constitutional or environmental factors? • Is the child’s capacity to explore inner experiences age-­appropriate? Rating Scale At the end of this chapter, in Table 3.15a, we provide a 5-point rating scale to assess each MC-Axis capacity, which can be used with the following anchor points for Levels 5, 3, and 1. 5. The child has rich fantasy scenarios, creates stories, and engages in symbolic play. Inner experiences (emotions, wishes, and reflections upon experiences or relational patterns) can often be connected to the fantasy or play material. The child can use fantasy and play to understand others’ perspectives and regulate emotions. TABLE 3.13. Developmental Guidelines: Capacity to Explore One’s Inner Life Child Caregivers Ages 4–6 • Uses play to express difficult realities and feelings that the • Support the child’s attempts to develop child cannot understand or manage with the current level of narratives about experiences and the thoughts sociocognitive functioning. and feelings they stimulate. Ages 7–8 • Has differentiated enough from caregivers to have a more • Support the child’s play and its role in identifying reality-based exploration of inner experience. with ways of being in the world. • Begins to integrate positive and negative emotions more • Offer themselves as active partners in play, clearly in the context of relationships beyond the family. both for mutual enjoyment and as a way to • Engages in more complex play, linking inner and outer worlds. communicate, thus strengthening the bond • Uses more sophisticated symbolism and effective verbal between child and caregivers. ways of representing internal experiences. Ages 9–11 • Develops new ways of interacting outside the home and • Encourage self-observation in the child, exploring internal experience when away from caregivers, especially in the context of conflictual situations using increased capacity for verbal representation. (e.g., with peers or siblings). • Encourage the child’s self-observation, find opportunities to model observation of one’s own internal processes.

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 199
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 199 3. The child can play symbolically and create fantasy scenarios that seem less mature than the child’s age. Play and fantasy scenarios are mainly focused on actions rather than on the mental states of the imagined figures. Play scenarios seem connected to internal...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 199 3. The child can play symbolically and create fantasy scenarios that seem less mature than the child’s age. Play and fantasy scenarios are mainly focused on actions rather than on the mental states of the imagined figures. Play scenarios seem connected to internal experience, but the child either cannot recognize the connection or rejects it. The child may interrupt symbolic play when fantasy material stimulates unregulated affects or impulses, leaving the child feeling overwhelmed. 1. One or more of the following apply: (a) The child has little ability to create fantasy stories or initiate and sustain symbolic play; when invited to play symbolically, the child shows little or no interest in creating a scenario or exploring the mental states of imaginary figures or the self; (b) the child shows interest only in body activities, avoiding symbolic play or exploration of self and world via thoughts and fantasy; (c) the child may initiate symbolic play or fantasy scenarios but confuses reality with imagination; (d) the child observes and plays with toys in a peculiarly nonsymbolic manner. 13. Capacity for Agency and Purpose The capacity for agency and purpose refers to the ability to pursue aims that have individual value and to live a meaningful life. In childhood, agency is reflected in the ability to make personal choices and direct one’s own behavior in desired, personally relevant ways. This capacity is strongly linked with the child’s emergent sense of intentionality (Wolf & Tomasello, 2025; Tomasello, 2023). From birth on, children are oriented toward others; they gradually learn to think of themselves as active agents by exploring the responses elicited by their actions, thereby developing a sense of causal efficacy. Agency becomes fundamental to their identity construction and moral development (Arikan & Kumru, 2021; Jung, 2023; Trott, 2020). Around ages 4 to 5, brain development and improved motor skills lead children to be more self-­regulating, autonomous, and eager to explore. They become progressively more able to focus their efforts and tackle complex activities. It is crucial that caregivers encourage children’s exploration while also providing them with a secure base to return to when distressed or tired (Danilov & Mihailova, 2022). Children’s desire for autonomy gradually increases, becoming responsive to the growing importance of peer relationships. This capacity is linked to a sense of mutual sharing and enjoyment within significant relationships (i.e., to the capacity for relationships, empathy, and intimacy); when fully developed, it allows the child to make purposeful and personally valuable choices while taking into account the demands of the interpersonal context (Chen, Rouse, & Morrissey, 2024). The capacity should be evaluated, considering the child’s developmental stage and their expected environment (see Table 3.14). The following questions are intended to guide the exploration and support the construction of a preliminary clinical formulation: • Does the child seem motivated to try new ways of solving problems and adapting to stressful situations? • Does the child take initiative in trying new experiences and show behavioral flexibility when challenged?

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 201
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 201 • Does the child seem aware of the emotional impact of personal actions and words on another person? • What is the child’s level of awareness in self–other interactions? Does the child seem to be an active partner in relationships, showing intentionality and a desi...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 201 • Does the child seem aware of the emotional impact of personal actions and words on another person? • What is the child’s level of awareness in self–other interactions? Does the child seem to be an active partner in relationships, showing intentionality and a desire to collaborate and negotiate changes? • Is there evidence of the child’s wish to learn from others, as well to communicate thoughts and feelings in ways that may produce desired outcomes? Rating Scale At the end of this chapter, in Table 3.15a, we provide a 5-point rating scale to assess each MC-Axis capacity, which can be used with the following anchor points for Levels 5, 3, and 1. 5. The child demonstrates an adaptive, age-­appropriate ability to explore the environment and direct actions toward a desired outcome. The child seems curious, autonomous, and aware of personal preferences, needs, and desires. The child takes into account the expectations, viewpoints, and suggestions of others and is open to guidance from authority figures, such as parents and teachers. 3. The child shows some degree of agency and purposeful autonomous initiative; in moments of distress or conflict, however, the child may appear fearful, insecure, and overly dependent on caregivers, or oppositional, reckless, and inconsiderate of the opinions and needs of others in ways that are inappropriate to the child’s age. 1. The child has little or no capacity to organize behavior to pursue meaningful goals. The child is unable to consider the interplay between personal perspectives and those of others, or to perceive a sense of mutual sharing and connection. The child may passively and unquestioningly adhere to the belief systems, attitudes, or behaviors of others, or show excessive and maladaptive disregard for the advice, needs, and views of others, contrary to what might be expected according to the child’s age. Relevant Assessment Tools The following tools can be used during the assessment process. Although some of these instruments produce categorical findings, the ultimate aim of the PDM is to guide the construction of a dimensional profile that facilitates a common vision and understanding for all those who support the child’s development. Capacity for Attention and Learning (Executive Functioning) • Process Assessment of the Learner—­Second Edition (PAL-II): Diagnostics for Math (Berninger, 2007) • Naglieri Nonverbal Ability Test—­Second Edition (NNAT-2; Naglieri, 2008) • Battelle Developmental Inventory, Second Edition (BDI-2; Newborg, 2005) • Conners Parent–­Teacher Rating Scale—­Revised (Conners, 1997) • Multifactor Emotional Intelligence Scale—Short Version (MEIS; Mayer, Caruso, & Salovey, 1999)

chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 203 • Resiliency Scales for Children and Adolescents (RSCA; Prince-­Embury, 2005, 2006) • Strengths and Difficulties Questionnaire (SDQ; Goodman, 1997) • Penn Interactive Peer Play Scale (PIPPS; Fantuzzo et al., 1995) Summary of Basic Mental Functioning: MC Axis To obt...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 203 • Resiliency Scales for Children and Adolescents (RSCA; Prince-­Embury, 2005, 2006) • Strengths and Difficulties Questionnaire (SDQ; Goodman, 1997) • Penn Interactive Peer Play Scale (PIPPS; Fantuzzo et al., 1995) Summary of Basic Mental Functioning: MC Axis To obtain a rating of a child’s overall mental functioning, a clinician should add up the 1- to 5-point ratings assigned to each capacity (Table 3.15a), yielding a single numerical index of overall functioning ranging from 13 to 65. This total permits provisionally assigning a patient to one of the categories outlined in Table 3.15b, which provides brief qualitative descriptions of seven levels of mental functioning (MC). Schematically, healthy mental functioning corresponds to MC1; neurotic to MC2 and MC3; borderline to MC4, MC5, and MC6 (from high to low, from moderate impairments to significant defects); and psychotic to MC7. These categories reflect increasing levels of impairment in mental functioning, which serves as the foundation for assessing personality organization. The terms “neurotic,” “borderline,” and “psychotic” are not used as diagnostic labels but in reference to specific structural functioning. Given the wide range of ages covered in this chapter, in the guidelines provided in the chapter text, we have mentioned specific characteristics expected for subgroups. They reflect our own efforts to integrate contemporary clinical and empirical findings so as to guide a comprehensive, developmentally informed clinical assessment of children. TABLE 3.15A. chunks.text: 204 Childhood TABLE 3.15B. Levels of Mental Functioning Level/Range Heading Description Healthy MC 1; 58–65 Healthy/optimal mental The child shows optimal or very good functioning in all or most functioning capacities, with modest, expectable variations in flexibility and adaptation across contexts. Neurotic MC 2; 51–57 Good/appropriate mental The child shows an appropriate level of functioning, with some specific functioning with some areas of difficulty (e.g., in three or four mental capacities). These may areas of difficulty reflect challenges related to specific situations or events. MC 3; 43–50 Mild impairments in The child shows mild constrictions and areas of inflexibility in some mental functioning domains, such as self-esteem regulation, impulse and affect regulation, defensive functioning, and self-observing capacities. Borderline MC4; 36–42 Moderate impairments The child shows moderate constrictions and areas of inflexibility in most in mental functioning or almost all domains, affecting quality and stability of relationships, sense of identity, and range of affects tolerated. This level suggests significantly impaired adaptation. MC5; 22–35 Major impairments in The child shows major constrictions and alterations in almost all domains mental functioning (e.g., tendencies toward fragmentation and difficulties in self–object differentiation), along with limitations experiencing feelings and/or thoughts in major areas (i.e., love, school, play). MC6; 21–27 Significant defects in The child shows significant defects in most domains and has problems basic mental functions organizing and/or integrating and differentiating self and other. Psychotic MC 7; 13–20 Major/severe defects in The child shows major and severe defects in almost all domains, with basic mental functions impaired reality testing; fragmentation and/or difficulties in self–object differentiation; disturbances in perception, integration, and regulation of affect and thought; and defects in one or more basic mental functions. chunks.heading: CHAPTER 4
chunks.preview: CHAPTER 4 Levels of Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties in Childhood PC Axis C H A P T E R E D IT O R S Norka Malberg, PsyD Anna Maria Speranza, PhD Alexandro Fortunato, PhD CONSULTANTS Line Brotnow Decker, MsC Mariagrazia Di Giuseppe, PhD Larry Rosenberg, PhD Antonella Cirasola, PhD...
chunks.text: CHAPTER 4 Levels of Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties in Childhood PC Axis C H A P T E R E D IT O R S Norka Malberg, PsyD Anna Maria Speranza, PhD Alexandro Fortunato, PhD CONSULTANTS Line Brotnow Decker, MsC Mariagrazia Di Giuseppe, PhD Larry Rosenberg, PhD Antonella Cirasola, PhD Johanna Malone, PhD Mark Dangerfield, PhD Tracy Prout, PhD Introduction Personality consists of an interconnected set of relatively stable psychological traits that influence how an individual thinks, feels, behaves, and relates to others. It is shaped by temperamental, genetic, experiential, social, and cultural factors. While there is no consensus on what “relatively stable” means, research suggests that personality is fluid and dynamic, and can change somewhat throughout an individual’s life (Bleidorn et al., 2022; DeVries, Spengler, Frintrup, & Mussel, 2021). Young children do have personalities and individual tendencies that may persist over time and can best be assessed dimensionally (i.e., the extent to which a trait or theme or pattern is displayed), rather than in categorical terms. The stated reasons for most children’s referral to mental health services do not usually include personality problems (Hansen, Christoffersen, Telléus, & Lauritsen, 2021). Instead, children are most often referred for behavioral or learning difficulties. Personality variables are, however, often associated with behavioral and school-­related difficulties; they

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 213
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 213 influence children’s clinical presentations and clinicians’ intervention strategies. Some problems affecting learning and behavior are in fact personality issues for which there is no official diagnosis, indexed as other conditions. In turn, be...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 213 influence children’s clinical presentations and clinicians’ intervention strategies. Some problems affecting learning and behavior are in fact personality issues for which there is no official diagnosis, indexed as other conditions. In turn, behavioral, interpersonal, and school-­related problems shape internal representations of self, others, and relationships—­the main ingredients of the child’s emerging personality. Evidence in both clinical and empirical writings (De Clercq, Van Leeuwen, Van Den Noortgate, De Bolle, & De Fruyt, 2009; Hansen et al., 2021; Tackett et al., 2012) have shown that personality can be investigated during childhood, and that in school-­ age children certain personality traits may predict personality features in later stages of development (Hamlat, Young, & Hankin, 2020; Shiner, Masten, & Roberts, 2003). Thanks to a growing body of longitudinal studies on samples of children (Bleidorn et al., 2020, 2021; Morales-­Muñoz, Broome, & Marwaha, 2020, 2021), we now have a better understanding of the developmental nature of personality; these studies suggest that early assessment is an imperative preventive measure. Because children’s personalities are constantly evolving, we describe levels of personality organization as “emerging.” These levels (and the emerging personality patterns that can be inferred) depict children’s characteristic ways of engaging with others and with the opportunities and challenges presented by the environment. They include awareness of their strengths and vulnerabilities, and how such awareness affects their coping styles as these organizations begin to form in childhood and continue to develop and transform throughout the lifespan. Approaching Assessment of Emerging Personality This chapter provides a guide to the clinical assessment of children ages 4 through 11, with an emphasis on how such assessment can inform diagnosis, formulation, and treatment planning. By separating this period from both early childhood and adolescence, we recognize the uniqueness of each developmental stage and its impact on our understanding of symptoms in the context of a child’s emerging personality. Personality development involves ways of coping and relating to others that either enhance or impede a person’s success in life—the ability to work productively, to form satisfying interpersonal relationships, and to actualize innate creative potential. The complex question of how and why to consider levels of emerging personality organization in this age cohort has implications for the development of assessment processes, supervision, case formulation, and treatment. According to research in developmental psychopathology (Rief et al., 2023; Russoti et al., 2021; Stone, 2023), personality traits in early and middle childhood are not necessarily predictive of the later development of personality pathologies or specific disorder as defined by either the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, fifth edition, text revision (DSM-5-TR; American Psychiatric Association, 2022) or the International Classification of Diseases (ICD-11; World Health Organization [WHO], 2022). Young children experience rapid and wide-­ranging developmental changes (Caspi, Roberts, & Shiner, 2005). Although a child’s level of cognitive functioning, for example, can remain fairly stable over time, psychological assessments of children specify the child’s current level of intellectual functioning and do not presume to predict future intelligence quotient scores (Makharia et al., 2016). We apply this same consideration to diagnosing personality in children. Notwithstanding the issue of predictability, children with vulnerabilities and risk factors—­congenital, environmentally determined, or both—may have difficulty

chunks.heading: 214 Childhood
chunks.preview: 214 Childhood meeting developmental milestones or have increased vulnerability to psychopathology that would meet diagnostic criteria for lower-level personality pathology (Bleiberg, 2001; Cicchetti, 2006; Geiger & Crick, 2010; Price & Zwolinski, 2010; Slobodskaya, 2021Wright & Jackson, 2022). Hence...
chunks.text: 214 Childhood meeting developmental milestones or have increased vulnerability to psychopathology that would meet diagnostic criteria for lower-level personality pathology (Bleiberg, 2001; Cicchetti, 2006; Geiger & Crick, 2010; Price & Zwolinski, 2010; Slobodskaya, 2021Wright & Jackson, 2022). Hence, while certain factors may increase the chances of later psychological difficulty, all children with similar risk factors do not necessarily develop severe psychopathology (Gee, 2021; Kraemer, Stice, Kazdin, Offord, & Kupfer., 2001; Masten, Lucke, Nelson, & Stallworthy, 2021; Price & Zwolinski, 2010). Development is complicated and not amenable to reliable predictions. Yet despite this caveat, knowledge of a risk to a functional developmental process can guide therapeutic interventions. Such concerns are not oriented toward stigmatizing, labeling, or pathologizing children. They speak to the importance of using appropriate diagnostic tools to monitor and establish where each child is the maturational process. When evaluating children’s personality functioning, it is thus important to consider risk and protective factors, and the potential developmental trajectories of personality pathology. Looking beyond symptoms is critical—­that is, taking into account changes in the external world and how they affect the child’s psychological adjustment and overall emerging personality. Developmental challenges can strain the process of personality development. Milestones such as the birth of a sibling, the beginning of school, new cognitive and social challenges, physical maturation, and events that stimulate separation–­individuation issues (moving, losses of family members, death of pets) can be highly influential on personality development. The high level of heterogeneity during childhood makes the diagnostic task quite daunting. Some children typically respond to challenges with closure and rigidity, others with openness and flexibility. Some comply with direction from authorities; others react with defiance and an unwillingness to compromise. Some are perfectionistic and preoccupied with attaining the approval of parents and teachers; others seem indifferent. Some children, even before age 10, show a lack of empathy and a disregard for the feelings of others; others are so concerned with making others happy that they have difficulty expressing their own needs. Some children demonstrate attitudes of superiority and arrogance in their peer relationships; others show patterns of shyness, withdrawal from challenges, and difficulty engaging with peers. Some children, from very early and beyond what is age-­appropriate, show a tendency toward suspiciousness and blaming of others. These individual responses may be identified and may acquire continuity over time. They have meaning in the context of a child’s life experience, in combination with unique temperamental and developmental features. For example, defensiveness and rigidity may be associated with a “high-­reactive” temperament that predisposes a child to perceive ordinary life experiences as threats. Defiance of authority may emerge from “hard-to-read” children whose parents unwittingly persist in setting unrealistic expectations. Children who lack empathy may have neurodevelopmental difficulties with social engagement that interfere with learning about others’ affective experience in reciprocal exchanges. In any case, these levels of emerging personality organization, when unchecked and associated with repeated negative exchanges with adults and peers, may lead to increasingly severe difficulties in children’s social and emotional adjustment (Hajal & Pailey, 2020). Life circumstances and maturational stresses can substantially alter the nature of a child’s emerging personality, and the resulting behaviors may evoke varying responses from those caring for the child, which in turn further shape personality development. Children’s family constellations, including but not limited to siblings

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 215
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 215 and parents, have implications for the development of mental representations of self and others. A clinician should aim to understand the child’s emerging personality within the context of the family system and the larger sociocultural surround...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 215 and parents, have implications for the development of mental representations of self and others. A clinician should aim to understand the child’s emerging personality within the context of the family system and the larger sociocultural surround. Certain experiences increase the likelihood that personality difficulties or disorders will develop. Some of these risk factors (e.g., poverty, maltreatment, exposure to violence, trauma, parental substance misuse) might be loosely described as matters of “nurture,” exogenous factors that increase stress and may activate an existing vulnerability. Endogenous risk factors might include traits related to cognition, neurobiology, genetic factors, temperament, and so on. Any of these variables can serve as either protective factors or areas of vulnerability for children (Green et al., 2023; Luthar & Zigler, 1991; Panisch et al., 2020; Rutter, 1987). Like psychopathology, personality development should not be considered within the forced dichotomy of nature versus nurture, but rather as the evolving consequence of the epigenetic interaction of internal and external influences. Attention to the intersection of vulnerability, risk, and protective factors is the core of our developmental approach to personality in childhood (Cicchetti & Cohen, 2006; VanMeter & Cicchetti, 2020; Wilson & Olino, 2021). In our framework for assessing emerging personality, in keeping with the scholarly literature on the topic (O. F. Kernberg, 1975; P. Kernberg, Weiner, & Bardenstein, 2000; Lerner, 1991; McWilliams, 2011; Normandin, Weiner, & Ensink, 2023), we offer prototypes of children functioning at different levels of organization, ranging from healthy to psychotic. We ask clinicians to consider the current functioning of each child through multiple lenses. Our goal is to support the recognition of levels of emerging personality organization that illuminate children’s overall functioning and identify relevant treatment objectives and modalities. Our approach is designed to do the following: • Help clinicians appreciate children’s strengths and weaknesses • Be clinically useful for treatment planning and case formulations • Facilitate communication and mutual understanding between the child’s clinician and parents/caregivers • Maximize the likelihood of successful, effective treatment intervention We prioritize assisting parents in developing a clearer sense of who their child is and how they experience their own psychology, the psychologies of others, and the nature of the world. This insight can help parents/caregivers better tailor their responses to the child and foster the development of epistemic trust (i.e., the ability to view others as reliable sources of information), which is essential for the therapeutic experience to be effective. We assume that the ability to offer a comprehensive description of a child’s capacities will be useful for teachers, pediatricians, and others involved in the young person’s care. Developing a Profile of Emerging Personality: Levels of Organization Unlike PDM assessment of adults, which begins with the P Axis and continues with the M Axis, the PDM-3 approach to assessment in both childhood and adolescence (see Chapters 7 and 8) begins with the MC Axis, followed by the PC Axis. This sequence reflects the critical impact of developmental factors and the fluidity of personality in young people.

chunks.heading: 216 Childhood
chunks.preview: 216 Childhood In the following we describe how different factors shape personality in children. These factors include the following: 1. Interplay of genetic and environmental influences 2. Temperament 3. Neurocognitive functioning 4. Attachment pattern 5. Sociocultural influences 6. Physical health,...
chunks.text: 216 Childhood In the following we describe how different factors shape personality in children. These factors include the following: 1. Interplay of genetic and environmental influences 2. Temperament 3. Neurocognitive functioning 4. Attachment pattern 5. Sociocultural influences 6. Physical health, gender, and sexuality Factors Influencing Emerging Personality A growing empirical literature has highlighted the complex interplay of genetic and psychosocial factors that contribute to the internal organization and structure of children’s subjective experiences, coping styles, and relationship patterns. There follows a brief review of factors that should be taken into consideration when assessing a child’s emerging personality. Interplay of Genetic and Environmental Influences Both genetic and environmental factors have been found to influence personality development in meaningful ways (Kandler & Zapko-­Willmes, 2017). We know that human development is driven by genes but sculpted by experiences, especially those that take place during the sensitive and critical first few years of life (Teicher, Sampson, Anderson, & Ohashi, 2016). There is strong evidence that childhood adversities are extremely common experiences in those who develop severe mental disorders (Cutajar et al., 2010; Fuller-­T homson & Lewis, 2015; Sorensen et al., 2010; Varese et al., 2012), and that adverse childhood experiences related to abuse are the leading preventable risk factor for mental and substance use disorders. The data suggest that maltreatment alters trajectories of brain development to affect sensory systems, network architecture, and circuits involved in threat detection, emotional regulation, and reward anticipation (Teicher et al., 2016). People who have suffered adverse childhood experiences are often reluctant to reveal their stories of abuse, and health professionals are often reluctant to assess them (Read, Hammersley, Rudegeair, 2007; Read, Harper, & Tucker, 2018). The field of developmental psychopathology, nonetheless, has identified experiences that increase the incidence of later psychopathology. Many fall into the broad category of “stress,” events that in themselves are overwhelming or are overwhelming because the individual had inadequate support to deal with them. Both discrete traumatic events and repeated or chronic developmental trauma are much more frequently observed and reported among children and adults who struggle with personality difficulties. This seems particularly the case when trauma occurs in the context of close relationships, as in child abuse (physical, verbal, or sexual violence) or neglect. Since the 1980s, genetic and psychosocial risk factors for the development of personality difficulties are no longer studied in separate scientific and clinical contexts. The fields of behavioral genetics and epigenetics have demonstrated many pathways between genes and environmental factors that predispose an individual to personality difficulties. Genetic and environmental factors seem to have an additive effect; that is, the more of these that are present for an individual, the greater the likelihood of

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 217
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 217 poor functioning. The additive phenomenon may explain in part why some but not all individuals with a certain genetic load or exposure to a given traumatic event go on to have difficulties. The response to any stress depends on the presence of...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 217 poor functioning. The additive phenomenon may explain in part why some but not all individuals with a certain genetic load or exposure to a given traumatic event go on to have difficulties. The response to any stress depends on the presence of other influences, such as a protective relationship that buffers the effect, or the absence of help, which would exacerbate it. Beyond the apparent role of additivity, the field of contemporary behavioral genetics has identified numerous pathways by which genes and environment are influenced reciprocally. From the earliest moments in fetal development, environmental factors such as nutrition, exposure to toxins or drugs, and activation of stress hormones shape key biological processes. These factors can even switch gene activation (epigenetic mechanisms) on and off. In turn, both can have an impact on personality functioning. Conversely, a child’s having a specific genotype can influence the environment through behavior (child evocative gene–­environment interactions); in other words, how one behaves, based on constitutional tendencies, may elicit different reactions from others. Depending on a child’s genetic makeup and previous experiences, how such feedback is understood and integrated will vary, leading to endless iterations of gene–­environment interactions that become too complex to elucidate fully for any individual. In a clinical context, a number of lessons may be drawn from this ever-­evolving field of transdisciplinary research (Harold, Leve, & Sellers, 2017). One lesson is that human behavioral traits develop through such interwoven pathways that no single genetic or environmental factor can be isolated as the primary driving force. Working with children who struggle, and who may be challenging to their caregivers, can foster the development of complex and rich narratives instead of easy explanations (“He’s just like his dad” or “It’s all because of the bullying”). Such narratives appreciate the continuing, interactive evolution of both genetic and environmental influences, highlighting the never-­ending potential for “changing the story.” Clinical support of caregivers in maintaining high levels of warmth, responsiveness, sensitivity, and consistency has been shown to lead to more flexible regulation of stress physiology, which directly affects a child’s executive functioning and capacity for self-­regulation. This should be done with the understanding that a child’s genetically determined traits will influence the way the child is parented (Ge et al., 1996; Shewark et al., 2021). Encouraging parents to identify ways in which they are affected by their child (increasing reflective functioning) can help them develop the psychological strength necessary to buffer the potential environmental effects of risk factors influencing the child. Temperament Over the past decades, developmental psychology has strongly supported what most parents have always intuitively known (and what all child therapists quickly learn): Children begin life with very different temperaments. Temperament has been defined as “the disposition of a person to learn how to behave, react emotionally, and form attachments automatically by associative conditioning” (Cloninger, Cloninger, Zwir, & Keltikangas-­Järvinen, 2019, p. 14). Dispositional styles influence how children respond to their environment. Several decades of research have converged in descriptions of temperament along dimensions capturing responses to signals of punishment (e.g., levels of harm avoidance, shyness), novelty seeking (e.g., exploration, emotional irritability), reward (e.g., sociability) and reinforcement (e.g., levels of persistence). For an in-depth overview, see Cloninger et al. (2019). Measures of temperament in childhood seem to overlap with measures of adult temperament and personality traits; the

chunks.heading: 218 Childhood
chunks.preview: 218 Childhood fact that these correlations become steadily stronger over time may denote increased stability. Beyond descriptions of specific traits or elements of temperament, substantial empirical attention has been paid to the question of genetic and environmental influences on temperament. Altho...
chunks.text: 218 Childhood fact that these correlations become steadily stronger over time may denote increased stability. Beyond descriptions of specific traits or elements of temperament, substantial empirical attention has been paid to the question of genetic and environmental influences on temperament. Although a strong biological (or genetic) component has been assumed (partly given the early occurrence and relative stability of temperament), the biopsychosocial environment influences temperament from before birth, likely through the epigenetic processes mentioned earlier. In addition to teratogenic effects in utero based on nutrition, stress, or toxins (Gartstein & Skinner, 2018), environmental factors, especially those related to parenting, have been found to interact with temperament over time. Beyond the simple effects of parenting (where sensitivity and continuity are universally assumed to be a boon), children with more challenging temperaments may be particularly dependent on their caregivers’ functioning and may evoke less sensitive parenting than their peers (Perry, Dollar, Calkins, & Bell, 2018). These pathways are particularly striking, as some parents may be more likely than others to perceive their infants and young children as difficult, contributing to the negative developmental cascade leading to poor outcomes over time (see Taraban & Shaw, 2018). Research on temperament has had a positive impact on child psychotherapy and parent guidance. The knowledge that children can be “inherently difficult” can reduce parents’ guilt and allow for a richer (and more valid) understanding of the multiple factors that influence the development of a child’s personality. Assessing a child’s temperamental profile and helping parents to identify what the child brings out in them may be particularly protective in buffering negative effects of parent or child temperament—­that is, it may improve the “goodness of fit” between child and caregivers (Chess & Thomas, 1991). Clinicians can play a critical role in helping parents to understand and respond in constructive ways to their child’s temperament—­with a balance of supportive and limit-­setting practices that promote the child’s security and self-­confidence and reduce the danger of increasingly dysregulated, defiant, or withdrawn modes of thought, feeling, and behavior. Neurocognitive Functioning Children’s language, attention, memory, and executive functioning capacities (volition, planning, purposive action, and effective performance) evolve in tandem with their overall emotional and relational development and are deeply intertwined. Over time, a child’s growing facility with language and conceptual thought plays a significant role in establishing and articulating personal identity. It allows the child greater flexibility in the organization of self, greater continuity of experience, and an increased ability to take multiple perspectives on negative experiences that previously would have been experienced as irreconcilable. Executive functions promote preparedness and resilience; they permit greater adaptability and more flexible negotiation of adversity (Wu et al., 2021). The host of skills subsumed under executive functioning—­including, but not limited to, initiation, planning, attention, self-­monitoring, negotiating novelty, and relying on working memory—­allow children to generate an expanding array of problem-­solving strategies, along with the capacity to modify them in real time when necessary. When these changes are joined with enhanced conceptual language ability, a child is better positioned to take perspective on emotions, contextualize them,

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 219
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 219 and integrate them into judgments that reflect a greater tolerance for ambiguity and frustration. The presence or absence of these capacities influences a child’s internal representation of self and others, and the quality of relationships. Neu...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 219 and integrate them into judgments that reflect a greater tolerance for ambiguity and frustration. The presence or absence of these capacities influences a child’s internal representation of self and others, and the quality of relationships. Neurocognitive differences and difficulties have been found to predict psychopathology across diagnostic categories, including mood disorders (depression), attention-­deficit/hyperactivity disorder, personality disorders, and substance use disorders (Zelazo, 2020). Several causal mechanisms have been examined empirically, with a focus on difficulties relating to self-­regulation at the level of sensory sensitivity, attention, emotion, and behavior. The effects on personality functioning of being overwhelmed by sensory impressions, being unable to guide attention effectively, struggling to downregulate emotions through language or action or plan, and being unable to execute situationally acceptable behaviors are multifarious. Indeed, having pervasive difficulties in this domain may cause a child to appear disorganized, impulsive, oppositional, aggressive, dependent, or deeply withdrawn, often with immediate consequences for the child’s ability to adapt to social contexts (e.g., school) and interpersonal situations. At the level of identity/self-image, experiencing oneself as fundamentally “different” rather than neurotypical (because of specific deficiencies, e.g., learning disabilities or because of simply being far from the mean, as in the case of extreme intellectual giftedness) can have a deep and lasting impact. These experiences are often not verbalized, increasing the confusion and shame that may accompany them. Attachment Pattern Attachment theory posits that because of their urgent need for physical and psychological survival, children will develop strong relationships (attachments) to their primary caregivers. Depending on children’s features (e.g., their temperament) and those of their caregivers (e.g., their own attachment histories and current levels of stress), the nature of the attachment relationship will vary. Conventionally, attachment patterns in children are described in terms of their level of security (including secure attachment and several types of insecure attachment) and organization (organized/coherent or disorganized). Empirical and clinical research spanning many decades, typically conducted with toddlers, has illuminated personality-­based predictors and correlates of attachment patterns in children. Scientists have identified a plethora of associations between attachment pattern and personality development. Awareness of attachment and how it can influence emerging personality patterns can aid in the development of a clinical formulation. In the general population, a majority of children and adults (around 55%; Bakermans-­K ranenburg et al., 2025; Deneault et al., 2023) show secure attachment patterns or states of mind, associated with greater ego resilience, emotion regulation, and trust in others (Ward, Lee, & Polan, 2006; National Collaborating Centre for Mental Health, 2015). In many clinical populations, this picture is inverted. Although insecure attachment patterns in themselves are not considered pathological (i.e., not at odds with good health and functioning), insecure patterns are typically associated with higher levels of anxiety, maladaptive self-­reliance, hostility, and mistrust of others. Insecure attachment patterns are thought to reflect relationships with caregivers who were experienced as insufficiently attuned, sensitive, or available. Attachment disorganization (around 15–20% in the general population; Deneault et al., 2023)

chunks.heading: 220 Childhood
chunks.preview: 220 Childhood is thought to result from a greater vulnerability in the child (genetic load, prenatal conditions, temperament), in concert with significant psychosocial stressors, including social marginalization (poverty, minority status) and parental trauma (Cyr, Euser, Bakermans-­K ranenburg, & va...
chunks.text: 220 Childhood is thought to result from a greater vulnerability in the child (genetic load, prenatal conditions, temperament), in concert with significant psychosocial stressors, including social marginalization (poverty, minority status) and parental trauma (Cyr, Euser, Bakermans-­K ranenburg, & van IJzendoorn, 2010). It has been linked to frightening or frightened behavior on the part of the parent, behavior that traps the infant in a state that Main (1999) called “fright without solution”: the parent who should provide a safe haven becomes instead the source of fear. In longitudinal studies and meta-­analytic reviews, attachment disorganization has been associated with problems such as aggression in childhood (Forslund, Peltola, & Brocki, 2019; van IJzendoorn, Schuengel, & Bakermans-­K ranenburg, 1999), posttraumatic stress disorder in middle childhood (Macdonald et al., 2008), and dissociation in adolescence (Carlson, 1998; Obsuth, Hennighausen, Brumariu, & Lyons-Ruth, 2014). Attachment disorganization commonly occurs in the context of trauma, neglect, maltreatment, and loss. There is evidence from microanalytic studies of mother–­ infant communication that attachment disorganization is linked to parental failures to respond to infant emotional distress. Such failures include withdrawal from the child or denial of the child’s state—for example, laughing when the infant shows distress (Beebe et al., 2012). Such misattunement may contribute to difficulties in understanding and predicting emotional reactions in self and others, and to a feeling of not being sensed or known, especially when distressed. It may contribute over time to the contradictory interpersonal communications (Beebe & Steele, 2013) that are characteristic of borderline personality disorder. Strikingly, however, and highlighting again the importance of the child’s needs and perceptions (and personality) only a minority of individuals with these experiences in childhood develop significant mental health issues. Insofar as attachment patterns influence children’s perceptions and behaviors in close relationships, they quickly become relevant (and apparent) in the therapeutic relationship. How children position themselves during the assessment phase, when clinician and patient are getting to know each other, and whether they are willing and able to engage in a relational experience such as psychotherapy are influenced by the relational past that their attachment profile reflects. The nature of a child’s difficulties may be conveyed both by observable behavior and the feelings the child evokes in clinicians during assessment and intervention. A child’s level of proximity, for example, and the unpredictability of their emotional and behavioral reactions to “ordinary caregiver responses” may be powerfully diagnostic and may provide valuable guidance to clinicians. In summary, in assessing children, clinicians need to explore the quality of their relationships to primary caregivers, other adults, peers, and siblings. The relational histories of the parents, when available, can help professionals to understand their reactions to their child and infer how those reactions influence the adults’ internal representations of the child and the child’s representations of self in relationships. Sociocultural Influences Environmental influences on personality include specific aspects of family life and caregiving practices, as well as characteristics of the family’s cultural context. These may include ways in which affect is displayed, shared beliefs about childrearing, attitudes about gender and sexual expression, and views about violence. Culture is

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 221
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 221 transmitted in vivo through language and behavior, and is further enhanced by physical surroundings (architecture, art). As with many other influences described in this chapter, it is nearly impossible to identify the precise impact of cultural...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 221 transmitted in vivo through language and behavior, and is further enhanced by physical surroundings (architecture, art). As with many other influences described in this chapter, it is nearly impossible to identify the precise impact of cultural phenomena on personality development for an individual child. Yet in the context of assessment, culture (i.e., expectations of normal behavior) is always present as a context for understanding the child’s current functioning and for formulating treatment. A growing body of thinking in the psychodynamic tradition urges clinicians to consider the complex interaction of culture with clinical formulation and intervention (see, e.g., Akhtar, 2011; Altman, 2010; Tummala-­Narra, 2015). Ryder, Dere, Sun, and Chentsova-­Dutton (2014) emphasize that clinicians who, when assessing personality, are unaware of cultural expectations, or who fail to consider them, run the risk of misdiagnosing symptoms. They suggest that clinicians should consider the ways in which certain groups of people are exposed to increased environmental stressors (both past and present), and how cultural variations might occur with or without distress or impairment (Arshad & Chung, 2022; Cardona, Madigan, & Sauer-­Zavala, 2022). Clinicians need to be continually reflective about ways in which their own sociocultural backgrounds influence how they perceive and are perceived when working with children and their families. To ignore cultural influences on early child development leaves us subscribing to the dubious notion of the “universal child,” which, when we engage clinically with cultural others, runs the risk of cultural misattunement or, worse, cultural impingement. Shore (1996) notes that in the “universal child” assumption, “Culture is conceived as one of the contents of mind rather than as a defining attribute of mind” (p. 22). Clinicians need to be aware of inherent cultural biases in current theories of development and pathology. Asking about the cultural meaning of food, sleeping arrangements, toilet training, and other developmentally related matters during the diagnostic process opens the door to better communication with parents. It allows the exploration of issues such as unresolved loss, mourning, and shame in the parents’ lives that might be hampering their capacity to “see” their child as a separate individual. Discussing issues of culture openly with parents, teachers, and other involved professionals allows everyone involved to reconsider their assumptions about the child’s functioning. Cultural bias can either frame a culturally normative behavior as pathological or can blur our capacity to see a child’s behavioral pattern as problematic. Physical Health, Gender, and Sexuality Our experiences as human beings are inevitably embodied. Signals sent from and perceived by the body will over time influence the ways we perceive and interact with ourselves and the world around us. Being comfortable using the body to experience and act upon our surroundings facilitates exploration and learning and builds a sense of self-­esteem and safety. Pain, illness, or functional challenges can significantly affect this process both in terms of children’s ability to explore their surroundings and— over time—their perceptions of themselves as differentiated from the world and other people (Dixon-­Gordon, Conkey, & Whalen, 2018). Children who have to manage bodily pain, illness, or functional differences, whether acute or chronic, and whether sensory (e.g., deafness or blindness), or motor (e.g., an absent or differently functioning limb) may meet developmental milestones at a rate different from that of other children and may demonstrate transient or pervasive

chunks.heading: 222 Childhood
chunks.preview: 222 Childhood personality changes.
