Some children coping with physical problems become more withdrawn and fearful than they had been previously (or would have otherwise been), perhaps demonstrating more explicit help-­seeking behaviors or passivity and appearing more dependent than their peers. Other...
chunks.text: 222 Childhood personality changes. Some children coping with physical problems become more withdrawn and fearful than they had been previously (or would have otherwise been), perhaps demonstrating more explicit help-­seeking behaviors or passivity and appearing more dependent than their peers. Others may demonstrate higher levels of irritability, aggression, and oppositionality, demanding special consideration and expecting exceptions to be made for them. Both patterns may be adaptive or even normative in the context of their specific circumstances. Some children identify deeply with their physical condition (explicitly describing themselves as weak, damaged, different), while others appear unbothered (having perhaps received adequate support and having accepted their condition and adapted to it), and still others actively reject any notion that they are affected by it or have feelings about it. Depending on the specifics of the condition (acute or chronic, illness or functional difference, with limited or far-­reaching consequences) and the qualities of the caring environment, children’s experiences of pain, illness, or compromise of physical functioning may vary greatly. Clinically, we often find that experiences of physical suffering or difference are not verbalized, and that children’s thoughts about their own bodies have become wellkept secrets. Sometimes, a child is aware of problems that a physical condition has caused for family members, without its ever being explicitly addressed. Engaging the child and parents in an exploration of the meaning of a bodily condition, as well as any changes in personality that follow its appearance, may start to disentangle psychological and somatic aspects and initiate a richer understanding of the child’s inner world. Similarly, giving precise, idiosyncratic, and age-­adapted language to a bodily experience may allow others to empathize with the child and provide support. Such actions may have a lasting impact on the child’s personality development and perception of self and others. In recent years, children’s experiences and expressions of sexuality and gender identity have received increased scientific attention (Claahsen-­van der Grinten et al., 2021; Fortunato et al., 2020; Goodman et al., 2019; Wood et al., 2013). Undoubtedly influenced by physiological sensations and processes (including chromosomal variations and endocrine system functioning), both sexuality and gender identity are largely understood to be inborn and can be observed in children from a very young age. They become relevant in the context of assessing personality insofar as any perceived differences from cultural norms may trigger intolerant reactions or unwanted attention from others and cause atypical children to perceive themselves as different in a negative way and perhaps unsafe in the world (Goldhammer, Crall, & Keuroghlian, 2019). As in the case of other somatically related experiences, children may over time respond to reactions from their social environment with increased withdrawal, anxiety, or fearfulness, or with opposition or aggression. Well before adolescence, propelled perhaps in part by the earlier onset of puberty and increased social awareness, children can have concerns about their sexualities or gender identities and may already have deeply held beliefs about themselves, influenced in part by perceived or expected feedback from others. Because variations in sexual preferences or gender expression are normal, it is the reactions of the environment, and the potential of such responses to create personality difficulties, that may be cause for concern. Asking children and caregivers about how they experience their bodies, how they perceive their gender, how they experience attraction or sexual urges (if relevant for them), and what they think the world will think of them, can not only allow for the exploration of specific life experiences (of trauma or support) but also scaffold longer-­term personality development.

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 223
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 223 Assessment Tools for Emerging Personality in Childhood The clinical assessment of personality in childhood should address at least two diagnostic issues. The first is the identification of personality functioning, including levels of emerging p...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 223 Assessment Tools for Emerging Personality in Childhood The clinical assessment of personality in childhood should address at least two diagnostic issues. The first is the identification of personality functioning, including levels of emerging personality organization and any related difficulties that are interfering with adjustment. The second involves identification and amelioration of features that pose risks for developing future psychopathology, including personality disorder. Both issues can be approached from either categorical or dimensional perspectives, and from numerous theoretical vantage points. Both require careful consideration of a child’s developmental level and the severity of the child’s problematic traits. Dimensional conceptualizations provide a better opportunity to examine continuity between child and adult manifestations of personality disorders. There is general professional agreement that a multimethod approach to personality assessment is most desirable. For children, this would typically involve some combination of interview, self-­report measures, observer ratings, and performance-­based measures. Carrying out multimethod assessment for children presents challenges that are not encountered in older populations. The value of self-­report measures of personality, especially in children, may be limited because of a child’s (or parent’s) lack of insight and/or developmentally normal conflation between actual and ideal self. Other problems may arise because of issues in reading ability, language comprehension (e.g., understanding how to respond to “true” and “false” in negatively worded questions), other cognitive and emotional limitations, personality problems, and desires either to minimize problems defensively (e.g., the child is rigidly defended or seeks to externalize blame) or to exaggerate problematic personality (e.g., the child seeks to draw attention to problems that might not be seen as serious if presented more matter-­of-­factly). Although clinical and diagnostic interviews can be useful techniques, children may not be able to understand all the questions. Parents may have differing degrees of awareness of a child’s difficulties, depending upon whether problematic behaviors are occurring in the home or in school. They may have differing levels of understanding of their causes as well, depending upon the child’s ability and/or willingness to communicate feelings. Parents may be unaware of their child’s internal preoccupations, distress, or unusual thinking. Interview methods that rely less on insight and self-­ reflection, and focus more on evoking behaviors of interest thus have advantages for this age group. Assessments in clinical contexts often occur with children whose relationships with their parents have become strained, or with parents who appear to defensively deny problems or to exaggerate them in an effort to get help for their child (in either case, observer ratings are saturated with parent response bias). Clinical personality assessment sometimes involves children in foster care; foster parents may have little knowledge of the developmental trajectory of the child’s problems and may not be able to distinguish behaviors that are reactive accommodations to parental loss and placement from those indicating more long-­standing personality issues. Validity indices assessing parental desire to under- or overreport problematic personality features are thus critical for evaluating the results of observer ratings. The main tool completed by a parent is the Big Five Questionnaire for Children (BFQ-C; Barbaranelli, Caprara, Rabasca, & Pastorelli, 2003). Other instruments that assess personality in childhood, filled out by the parent, are relatively scarce. The few that exist have involved either downward extensions of adult personality measures or design of new instruments for children based on adult criteria. These include the following:

chunks.heading: 224 Childhood
chunks.preview: 224 Childhood • Millon Pre-­Adolescent Clinical Inventory (MPACI; Millon, Tringone, Millon, & Grossman, 2005). • Coolidge Personality and Neuropsychological Inventory for Children (CPNI; Coolidge, 2005). To overcome problems of adult reporting, scholars have recently introduced some clinician-­repor...
chunks.text: 224 Childhood • Millon Pre-­Adolescent Clinical Inventory (MPACI; Millon, Tringone, Millon, & Grossman, 2005). • Coolidge Personality and Neuropsychological Inventory for Children (CPNI; Coolidge, 2005). To overcome problems of adult reporting, scholars have recently introduced some clinician-­report tools for childhood personality assessment that demonstrate good psychometric properties. There are also some tools that were not created to evaluate personality but whose items can be used to evaluate some aspects of personality disorders. They include the following: • Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL; Achenbach & Rescorla, 2001). • Devereux Scales of Mental Disorders (DSMD; Naglieri, LeBuffe, & Pfeiffer, 1994). • Inventory of Callous and Unemotional Traits (ICU; Frick, Stickle, Dandreau, Farrell, & Kimonis, 2005; Frick & White, 2008). Although self-­report and observer ratings are important sources of information, they may frequently fail to capture data about developmental level, object relatedness, defensive structure, and personality attributes, as well as implicit cognitive, relational, and affective factors that may sustain maladaptive behaviors. For this reason, psychologists often employ projective measures. Meyer and Kurtz (2006), among others, have suggested that the terms “objective” and “projective” have misleading connotations and should be discarded to advance the field of personality assessment. Instruments that have typically been subsumed under the “projective” label might be better referred to as “performance-­based measures” or “constructive measures.” Even though such instruments can be used to assess the presence and severity of features of personality pathology in children, they are not capable alone of establishing a diagnosis. They may, however give valuable information on relevant constructs such as reality testing, thought disturbance, cognitive functioning, affects, and representations of self and others. The best-known projective tests are as follows: • Comprehensive System (Exner, 1974), or the Rorschach Performance Assessment System (Meyer, Viglione, Mihora, Erard, & Erdberg, 2011). • Thematic Apperception Test (TAT; Bellak, 1993; Murray, 1943). • Tell-Me-A-Story (TEMAS; Costantino, Malgady, & Rogler, 1988). • Roberts Apperception Test for Children (RATC-2; Roberts & Gruber, 2005). • Pictorial Personality Traits Questionnaire for Children (PPTQ-C; Mackiewic & Ciechiuch 2016). • Children’s Apperception Test (CAT; Bellak & Bellak, 1949). • Figure drawings: Draw-a-­Person test (DAP; Machover, 1949), House–Tree–­ Person technique (HTP; Buck, 1948), Draw-a-­Family test (Hulse, 1951), and Kinetic Family Drawing (Burns & Kaufman, 1970). Other instruments traditionally regarded as cognitive or neuropsychological in nature may assist with formulating a comprehensive understanding of how a child thinks, feels, and acts, contextualizing the findings obtained from “traditional” personality tests. Broadly conceived, abilities such as intelligence, executive functioning, and language processing influence the range of defenses and coping strategies available

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 225
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 225 to the child, mediate issues relevant to self-­esteem, and provide data relevant to treatment planning. Among the best-known are: • Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children—­Fifth Edition (WISC-V; Wechsler, 2014). • Wechsler Preschool and Prima...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 225 to the child, mediate issues relevant to self-­esteem, and provide data relevant to treatment planning. Among the best-known are: • Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children—­Fifth Edition (WISC-V; Wechsler, 2014). • Wechsler Preschool and Primary Scale of Intelligence—­Fourth Edition (WPPSIIV; Wechsler, 2012). • NEPSY—­Second Edition (Korkman, Kirk, & Kemp, 2007). • Clinical Evaluation of Language Functions—­Fifth Edition (CELF-5; Wiig, Semel, & Secord, 2013). Among possible informants on the child’s psychology, the clinician may be considered to be one of the most reliable. Accordingly, the Q-Sort method has been used to investigate adolescent and adult personality. The Shedler–­Westen Assessment Procedure–200 (Westen & Shedler, 1999a, 1999b; Westen, Shedler, Durrett, Glass, & Martens, 2003), is the most popular Q-Sort method to assess personality. Notably, the adolescent version of the SWAP-200-A (Westen et al., 2003; Westen, DeFife, Malone, & DiLallo, 2014) may be used to verify the presence of personality disorders in adolescence and to identify a specific, empirically derived taxonomy of emerging personality configurations. A Q-Sort tool has been created specifically for this age group that works similarly to the SWAP, not as an adaptation: • Childhood Personality Assessment Procedure Q-Sort (CPAP-Q; Fortunato, Tanzilli, Lingiardi, & Speranza, 2021, 2022). Levels of Emerging Personality Organization in Children Emerging personality organization in children exists on a continuum from healthy to highly compromised. According to O. Kernberg (1984) and others, the domains of severity include healthy, neurotic, borderline, and psychotic. In this chapter, rather than referring to personality “disorders,” we limit ourselves to providing useful indications for tracing developmental trajectories in terms of risk and protective factors. We use the terms “neurotic,” “borderline,” and “psychotic” as general diagnostic containers of the psychodynamic tradition and not as specific diagnostic labels. In line with Selzer, Koenigsberg, and Kernberg (1987) and P. Kernberg and her colleagues (2000), we outline here prototypical profiles of children who are functioning at each level of personality organization and describe core developmental features of their clinical presentations. When discussing levels of emerging personality organization (healthy, neurotic, borderline, psychotic), we refer to wide ranges of functioning in various areas. In childhood, any understanding of mental health must be put in the context of normal developmental processes. Please refer to Table 3.15b in Chapter 3 (MC Axis, p. 204) for descriptions of age-­appropriate development. Healthy Level of Emerging Personality Organization At this level, children with a healthy level of personality organization demonstrate a cohesive emergent personality configuration, in which their biological

chunks.heading: 226 Childhood
chunks.preview: 226 Childhood endowments—­including their temperamental vulnerabilities—­are managed adaptively within developmentally appropriate relationships with family members, peers, and others. They have developed a stable, positive, and integrated identity and an adequate capacity for self–other differentia...
chunks.text: 226 Childhood endowments—­including their temperamental vulnerabilities—­are managed adaptively within developmentally appropriate relationships with family members, peers, and others. They have developed a stable, positive, and integrated identity and an adequate capacity for self–other differentiation. They show overall positive self-­esteem, with a confident but balanced view of themselves. Their defenses and coping strategies are mostly mature, enabling them to manage aggressive impulses at an age-­appropriate level. If overwhelmed by stresses and/or strong emotions, they can be easily consoled and reregulated by caregivers. Their reality testing is intact; they can distinguish fantasy from reality and discriminate their own thoughts from those of others. They adapt flexibly to changes and tend to be resilient. IDENTIT Y Children at a healthy level of emerging personality organization have an organized, positive, and stable sense of self. They show age-­appropriate coping skills. Their view of self is reasonably positive without exaggerated biases in either a positive or negative direction. They can easily distinguish between themselves and others. At this level, children with a healthy level of personality organization do not show significant temperamental or neuropsychological vulnerabilities. They have a sense of bodily integrity, they feel pleasure in touching themselves and being touched by others, and they want to take care of themselves. Children with a healthy level of personality organization have age-­appropriate knowledge about the body and can distinguish internal from external stimuli. Such children have adequate fine and gross motor capacities and feel secure enough in their physical bodies to enjoy age-­appropriate school and play activities. Their ability to communicate verbally and nonverbally is commensurate with their age group. They are school-­ready: They can attend classes and concentrate in an age-­appropriate way. They can make sustained efforts; they remain calm and controlled enough to do so. They can follow instructions because (1) they tend not to be overly oppositional toward authority figures, and (2) they have the cognitive capacity to retain a sequence of instructions suitable for their age. These children have a full range of affects, which they can express at an age-­appropriate level. Their fears are manageable and do not interfere with their ability to form relationships, attend school, or participate in activities. Children at this level of emerging personality organization have a reasonably well-­ developed sense of self that does not change drastically over time or across settings. They can readily distinguish between what is coming from themselves and what is the other’s perspective. These children can rely on a stable, basic sense of self-­regard, with self-­esteem that is neither unrealistically positive nor extremely negative. They are generally hopeful and confident, and their sense of self is not easily or severely altered by criticism or disappointments. Furthermore, they have developed a healthy level of personality organization and age-­appropriate symbolic function and the capacity to play creatively. They have mastered adequately the ability to picture, remember, understand, and creatively replicate objects/events in their minds that are not immediately in front of them. For example, they can talk about or draw places they have visited, and can create new scenes and creatures from their imagination. They can also share meaning and engage with pretend play both alone and with others. Such children have an age-­appropriate sense of

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 227
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 227 causal efficacy and think of themselves as active agents. For example, they can explore and reflect upon the responses their actions elicit from the environment and other people. They have developed specific interests and/or preferences, and th...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 227 causal efficacy and think of themselves as active agents. For example, they can explore and reflect upon the responses their actions elicit from the environment and other people. They have developed specific interests and/or preferences, and they can make personal choices and direct their behavior in desired and personally relevant ways. OBJECT REL ATIONS Children at a healthy level of emerging personality organization can easily read social cues and can develop friendships even without adult assistance. They do not struggle with trust, and their view of others is generally positive, although they can tolerate the idea that people are neither all good nor all bad. Depending on their age and overall sociocognitive functioning, these children show an emerging or established theory of mind (i.e., they can mentalize others’ states of mind as separate from their own). There is evidence of age-­appropriate belief in self as an intentional agent (e.g., “I can negotiate with my friends; my behaviors have an impact on others”). These children show an emerging capacity for reflective functioning, characterized by an ability to consider the opaqueness of others’ states of mind (“What you see is not always what is true”). They can (1) use humor appropriately in the context of relationships, (2) rely on emerging behavioral flexibility (perspective taking), and (3) deal with overwhelming affective states by using symbols and words. In general, children with a healthy level of emerging personality organization make age-­appropriate use of interpersonal verbal and nonverbal cues; they tolerate experiences of rupture in relationships and can engage in repair. They have a sense of curiosity and interest in learning. They are in touch with their thoughts and feelings, and show curiosity about themselves and the impact of their own cognitions, affects, and actions on others and on the surrounding world. They tend to demonstrate a secure attachment pattern. Their caregivers are usually able to provide a safe and reliable base from which to explore. This is not to say that they have not experienced difficulties, but that, overall, given their own resilience and the available environmental supports, they have managed to cope with challenges in ways that are developmentally and socioculturally appropriate. Children at this level are able to form close, reciprocal friendships without adult assistance. They are empathic and sensitive to the motives and needs of others. Accordingly, they can understand social interactions and have a sense of belonging in social contexts. Their regard for others is relatively positive; they show evidence of appreciating the idea that people are neither all good nor all bad. They show genuine empathy and are increasingly able to understand how other people feel even when they do not feel similarly themselves. These children have developed an age-­appropriate capacity to trust others, generally and epistemically. As they get older, they are progressively able to (1) identify whom they can trust, (2) read social cues appropriately, and (3) track and evaluate the accuracy of the information they receive from others, and decide whose direction to follow. They can repair relational ruptures and restore trust if trust is justified. DEFENSE AND COPING Children with a healthy level of emerging personality organization employ mainly mature defenses (i.e., affiliation, altruism, self-­observation, sublimation, humor) that

chunks.heading: 228 Childhood
chunks.preview: 228 Childhood manage stress by integrating emotions and ideas without reality distortion. Their use of mature defenses can be partially or fully conscious and can potentially resolve the causes of distress. They are able to adapt flexibly to changes, and they tend to be resilient. Although they are...
chunks.text: 228 Childhood manage stress by integrating emotions and ideas without reality distortion. Their use of mature defenses can be partially or fully conscious and can potentially resolve the causes of distress. They are able to adapt flexibly to changes, and they tend to be resilient. Although they are in control of their aggressive impulses, children with a healthy level of emerging personality organization are not passive. They are constructively aggressive, capable of appropriate self-­assertion, competition, and ambition. They can tolerate anxiety and other negative affects without either behavioral or cognitive disorganization. When they temporarily lose their emotional equilibrium, they are responsive to caregivers’ attempts to soothe them. They reconstitute reasonably quickly and can reflect, either with support or independently, on disruptive experiences. These children are reasonably flexible in both thinking and ability to adjust to new or unanticipated situations. They have some degree of adaptability to adversity: They do not simply tolerate or survive adverse events but come away from them somewhat stronger. These children show the beginnings of a sense of morality. Their values are consistent with those of their immediate and larger surrounding cultures. They are interested in fairness to others as well as to themselves. They can play cooperatively by the rules of a game and may feel guilt and remorse for cheating or for inappropriate or hurtful behavior. They are not overly harsh with either themselves or others; they understand that all children misbehave at times. Hence, they have the capacity to forgive both themselves and others. RE ALIT Y TESTING Children at a healthy level of emerging personality organization have intact reality testing and can distinguish fantasy from reality, and their own thoughts from those of others. These children with a healthy level of personality organization have established adequate reality testing. They can distinguish reality from fantasy and can differentiate their own thoughts and feelings from those of others. These children see the external world in ways that generally align with the perceptions of the children around them. They use defenses that distort reality to only a minor degree. They are able to adapt flexibly to changes and tend to be resilient. Neurotic Level of Emerging Personality Organization (Mild Dysfunction) At this level, children are logical and thoughtful. They tend to adapt well to new and challenging circumstances. When they do become upset or dysregulated, they can regain equilibrium, sometimes via their internal resources for self-­soothing, and sometimes with help from others. They can be impulsive, oppositional, or disruptive at times, but when this behavior is addressed in a sensitive manner, they can regain control. Children at a neurotic level of emerging personality organization have typically adopted the values of their caregivers and the surrounding culture without undue questioning. Their defenses, which are internalizing as well as externalizing, operate

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 229
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 229 effectively to maintain reality testing. They have a pretty good sense of who they are and can make reasonable assessments of others. These children can see good and bad in people simultaneously. While they can be demanding or self-­centered, t...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 229 effectively to maintain reality testing. They have a pretty good sense of who they are and can make reasonable assessments of others. These children can see good and bad in people simultaneously. While they can be demanding or self-­centered, they are capable of empathy and are sensitive to the motives and needs of others. They tend to have close relationships with peers, caregivers, and authority figures. Their interpersonal boundaries are reasonable. Relational difficulties that arise are usually resolved without undue effort. Their affects include a wide range of feelings and are appropriate to context. IDENTIT Y Children at a neurotic level of emerging personality organization have a reasonably well-­developed sense of self that does not change radically over time and across situations. Yet they can be excessively self-­conscious; their self-­esteem and view of themselves can easily worsen in challenging or stressful situations. Except under stress or while experiencing intense emotions, they can usually distinguish adequately between themselves and others. These children may experience declines in their attention and learning capacity caused by rituals and obsessions, performance anxiety, or worries about others’ reactions. Their sleep and their food intake is generally regular. When not anxious, they show age-­appropriate verbal skills. A neurotic level of personality organization is not associated with any cognitive, motor, or language deficits or problems. Most of the time, children at this level feel and express a wide range of affects congruent with their developmental stage. They can, however, experience intense feelings and struggle to communicate them effectively, especially if they perceive them as negative. This struggle can sometimes affect their cognitive and/or social capacities such that they appear excessively inhibited. They show an emerging or established theory of mind and have developed an adequate capacity for reflective functioning. They can, however, occasionally fail to recognize how their actions can affect others, especially family members. These children have developed an overall age-­appropriate sense of curiosity and interest in themselves and others, but at times, especially when managing high levels of emotional impact, they may show little curiosity about the surrounding world or about the impact of their own feelings, thoughts, and actions on others. These children overall show an age-­appropriate sense of morality and a value system in line with their family culture. They can tell right from wrong and play cooperatively by the rules of a game. Their moral and ethical standards can sometimes be excessively rigid, however. They may readily feel guilt and/or shame for wrongdoing and may not forgive themselves or others easily. Overall, these children have developed an age-­ appropriate symbolic function and capacity to play symbolically and creatively. At times, especially under stress or strong emotions, their thinking and play can be rigid. While they can be creative with their language and play, they sometimes struggle to share meaning with others and/ or engage in pretend play, especially when led by others rather than initiated by themselves. Their involvement in creative and parented play with others may, for example, be mainly of a passive sort. Generally, children functioning at this level have age-­appropriate interests, preferences, and values, and can think of themselves as active agents. Yet they may be more

chunks.heading: 230 Childhood
chunks.preview: 230 Childhood passive and dependent than children with a healthy level of personality organization. Under stress or strong emotions, they may struggle to (1) explore and reflect upon the responses their actions elicit and (2) recognize the existence of a causal relationship between different mental...
chunks.text: 230 Childhood passive and dependent than children with a healthy level of personality organization. Under stress or strong emotions, they may struggle to (1) explore and reflect upon the responses their actions elicit and (2) recognize the existence of a causal relationship between different mental states and actions. OBJECT REL ATIONS Children at a neurotic level of emerging personality organization are able to read social cues, have friends, and enjoy being part of a group. Their shame and fear of intimacy can create issues in relationships, however, causing them to appear too dependent on caregivers and to struggle in trusting others. At times, they may find it hard to tolerate the notion that people are neither all good nor all bad. At this level of emerging personality organization, children have a reasonably well-­developed sense of self. They can distinguish between self and others but are not always entirely comfortable with themselves. Although their self-­esteem tends to be positive overall, it is not as stable and/or positive as that of children with an emerging healthy personality organization. Their view of themselves can easily diminish in stressful circumstances such as academic and competitive settings or during significant transitions. They can have friendships and enjoy being part of a group but may suffer in certain social situations because of shame and related fears of intimacy. Despite reading social cues accurately, they may feel apprehensive socially. They may struggle to maintain the idea that people are neither all good nor all bad. In their family, they may be jealous of siblings and negative toward parents, on whom they may also be overly dependent. They are generally capable of empathy and sensitive to the motives and needs of others, but they can also be demanding or self-­centered. They have developed age-­appropriate epistemic trustworthiness. This means that, except in times of high anxiety, they are able to recognize who is trustworthy and what information they should consider. However, under stress, their epistemic trust capacity goes through distortions. This may lead them to interpret social cues more negatively and have a greater need for reassurance in order to interact with others or, conversely, to overtly and indiscriminately trust others and have a greater need for support to differentiate the reliability of the information they receive. In addition, these children may have difficulty assessing a person’s trustworthiness over time and adjusting their level of trust accordingly. When faced with relational difficulties, they may struggle to calibrate trust based on new information. DEFENSE AND COPING Children at a neurotic level of emerging personality organization mainly employ neurotic defenses (i.e., repression, displacement, undoing, intellectualization), reflecting the partial ability to cope with stress by handling either the emotional or the cognitive aspect of a problem. The avoided element of the conflict can be repressed, distorted, or denied. Although children at a neurotic level of emerging personality organization have generally developed some capacity for impulse control, it can be fragile. They may

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 231
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 231 struggle to tolerate anxiety and other negative feelings, or may be passive or excessively inhibited. They tend to be reasonably flexible and able to cope with new situations. Compared with children at a healthy level of emerging personality or...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 231 struggle to tolerate anxiety and other negative feelings, or may be passive or excessively inhibited. They tend to be reasonably flexible and able to cope with new situations. Compared with children at a healthy level of emerging personality organization, these children may need more help from others to regulate themselves in the face of stressors and/or changes. These children often have a family history of anxiety and depression, especially in caregivers. Although their temperament may be characterized by irritability, anxiety, stubbornness, avoidance, hypervigilance, and high arousal, they do not suffer from neuropsychological or neurocognitive deficit. They often show an anxious attachment pattern and tend to be overly concerned about their relationship with their caregivers, who are often anxious and/or critical, and may struggle to cope with their children’s intense affects. Difficulties may emerge or be exacerbated in these children during sudden changes or stressors in their lives. RE ALIT Y TESTING Children at a neurotic level of emerging personality organization have good reality testing except when under significant stress. These children can differentiate reality from fantasy and their own thoughts and feelings from those of others. They employ mature defenses that distort reality to only a minor degree. How Children in This Category May Be Experienced Clinically There is usually a sense of mutuality in play and conversation in children at a neurotic level of emerging personality organization. Because they can develop trust in the therapist, they can form a positive alliance, characterized by a mutual feeling that child and therapist are on the same team, working toward the same goals. They typically experience therapy as helpful. Such children are interested in the therapist; they are curious, want to know the clinician better, and may ask about the therapist’s thoughts. Depending on their stage of development and capacity to represent emotions, their play tends to be representational/symbolic. Unless under stress or feeling intense emotions, they are creative and tend to welcome the therapist in co-­creating stories, whether in conversation or in play. These children are forgiving. They may have negative emotions, based on their internal representations of others, but if the therapeutic environment has fostered the consolidation of a sense of trust toward each other, they are able to repair ruptures. It is easy for clinicians to empathize with these children, who are similarly able to empathize with the therapist. They show a capacity for guilt and a sense of fairness. Because they are easy to engage, they make a therapist feel confident and effective, or at least like a nice, helpful person. Their defenses make them accessible to therapists, as they tend to enjoy sublimation and displacement as ways of expressing and exploring emotions and thoughts. They play out or talk about themes that are fairly understandable, providing a therapist with a vivid sense of the child’s experience of self and important others. Even when they portray relationships as troubled or punitive, they do not typically view others as sadistic or cruel, nor do they view the therapist this way.

chunks.heading: 232 Childhood
chunks.preview: 232 Childhood Borderline Level of Emerging Personality Organization (Moderate Dysfunction) Children functioning at the borderline level of emerging personality organization tend to be rigid and inflexible in their thinking. Taking another person’s point of view is hard for them. They generally fail...
chunks.text: 232 Childhood Borderline Level of Emerging Personality Organization (Moderate Dysfunction) Children functioning at the borderline level of emerging personality organization tend to be rigid and inflexible in their thinking. Taking another person’s point of view is hard for them. They generally fail to see nuances and instead perceive events, people, and interactions in all-good and all-bad terms. They may show evidence of thought disturbance (e.g., loosening of associations, tangentiality, concrete thinking) and may find it difficult to differentiate what is coming from within themselves from what is coming from outside. Under stress, these children become easily disorganized, sometimes to the point of experiencing short breaks with reality. They can reconstitute with help, however, or when a distraction is provided. Once they become dysregulated, they are difficult to soothe. Tantrums, rage, or sobbing may persist for extended periods of time, seemingly regardless of attempts by caregivers to be empathic, supportive, and comforting. Furthermore, they show less than age-­appropriate capacity for self-­reflection. At this level of emerging personality organization, children have great difficulty differentiating what is in their own minds from what is in the minds of others. They tend to show intense shifts in emotion, which is more often acted out than expressed verbally. They have little curiosity about themselves and may find it difficult to maintain a consistent sense of who they are. They may relate to others in terms of the function they serve (in psychoanalytic terms, part–­object relating), but they show little appreciation or understanding of who others are or what motivates them. Rapid shifts in how they perceive and relate to other people are not uncommon; they can be truly empathic in one moment or context and strikingly callous in the next. Histories of maltreatment, including neglect and abuse, are common among children functioning at this level. Consequently, many children show a disorganized attachment pattern. IDENTIT Y Children at a borderline level of emerging personality organization have a fragmented sense of self. They view themselves and others inconsistently over time and across situations. Their representations of self and others shift between positive and negative poles. Their self-­esteem may be unstable and/or negative. They show poor self-­observation and self-­awareness. Subjectively, they struggle to distinguish what comes from the self from what comes from others. Like adolescents or adults who function at the borderline level, children in this range may express emotions through the body, in the form of dysregulation, lack of care, or self-harm. They may show sudden regressions in body management and acquired functions, especially in highly emotional and/or stressful moments. The physical body can be a source of great tension or confusion to them. These children usually have normal cognitive and motor development. Their intellectual functioning and learning capacity can be compromised, however, because of (1) attention difficulties, (2) intense emotions, and/or (3) external stressors. They often have problems in sleep regulation and/or food intake but are less likely to have sensory or motor issues. When anxious, they may become vague or abstract in their thinking; they have trouble remaining connected to reality when their emotional arousal is high.

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 233
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 233 These children tend to convey intense and rapidly changing emotions, which they may struggle to communicate in an age-­appropriate way. They may experience mood instability and anhedonia. They may appear needy and dependent, and often display r...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 233 These children tend to convey intense and rapidly changing emotions, which they may struggle to communicate in an age-­appropriate way. They may experience mood instability and anhedonia. They may appear needy and dependent, and often display regressive tendencies and low frustration tolerance. At the same time, they may show a strong aversion to turning to others for help in regulating their emotions. When their capacity for affect regulation is compromised, their cognitive and/or social ability declines as well. They lack a well-­integrated or clearly defined sense of self. Their self is fragile, sometimes concealed by grandiosity. They can be theatrical, arrogant, and envious. They may have difficulty establishing what comes from oneself versus what comes from others. Their body image is unstable. Self-image tends to be unstable and/or negative; the fragmented nature of their internal experience contributes to their difficulty regulating self-­esteem. Especially when under stress, these children struggle to think of themselves as active agents. While they may have developed some specific interests and preferences, they do not have an adequate sense of causal efficacy and agency. They struggle to explore and reflect upon the responses that their actions elicit in others. Similarly, they find it difficult to recognize causal connections between mental states and actions, especially when feeling strong emotions. OBJECT REL ATIONS Children at a borderline level of emerging personality organization tend to have difficulties in relationships with both adults and peers. They may struggle to trust others and often appear suspicious and difficult to approach. In relationships, they may be hypervigilant and controlling, and may have trouble developing a true sense of intimacy. They may experience a deep sense of loneliness and may feel a need to be admired by others. They tend to view people as all good or all bad. Children functioning at a borderline level of emerging personality organization tend to struggle to read social cues. They have not yet developed an age-­appropriate sense of curiosity about themselves, others, and the surrounding world. They often display poor self-­observation skills and may have little, if any, self-­awareness. These children may have difficulties in relationships with both adults and peers. They often feel a deep sense of loneliness, coupled with suspicion and hostility toward others. In relationships, they are often controlling, and they may struggle to develop a genuine intimacy. They can also be manipulative, jealous, and dissatisfied, and often crave admiration from others. They may show little empathy overall or may be truly empathic one moment (or in one setting or with a specific person) but insensitive the next. They experience chronic or frequent failures in their capacity to trust others. They may show exaggerated vigilance toward incoming information, especially information that is communicated personally. They read even neutral social cues negatively and often interpret communication differently from how speakers expect their words to be taken. However, at other times, they may accept others’ information uncritically and indiscriminately. Such oscillations seem to reflect the dynamics of functioning in which the other is alternately perceived as threatening and manipulative or idealized, becoming an unchallenging source of truth. As a result, they tend to (1) expect others to have negative intentions or conversely, indiscriminately positive ones; (2) respond

chunks.heading: 234 Childhood
chunks.preview: 234 Childhood more negatively to moderate levels of interpersonal frustration, and (3) have little capacity to repair relational ruptures when these arise. At a borderline level of emerging personality organization, children tend to be uninhibited. They may hit others, for example, without thinking...
chunks.text: 234 Childhood more negatively to moderate levels of interpersonal frustration, and (3) have little capacity to repair relational ruptures when these arise. At a borderline level of emerging personality organization, children tend to be uninhibited. They may hit others, for example, without thinking about the consequences. Their moral and ethical standards may be weak. Children functioning at the lower level of the borderline range may show behavior associated with conduct disorders; for example, being cruel to animals or tending to damage things. Such children may show little remorse for their disruptive behaviors. While they tend to have adequate language capacity, their symbolic function and capacity to play creatively can be compromised, especially under stress and in social situations. For example, they can create mental images of objects or other people, but they may struggle to store them mentally in their absence, or they may do so in a rigid rather than a creative way. Under stress, they may find it difficult to engage in pretend play, especially when led by others. DEFENSE AND COPING Children at a borderline level of emerging personality organization employ mainly immature defense mechanisms (i.e., acting out, passive–­aggressive behavior, splitting, projective identification) that reflect their inability to contain emotional distress. Impulsive behaviors, aggressive withdrawal, and significant image distortion are predominant at this level of defensive functioning. These children may have poor impulse control and often display disruptive and aggressive behavior. They tend to be easily dysregulated, and they struggle to reregulate. They struggle also to reflect on their actions and disruptive experiences, even with help from others. Externalizing defenses are predominant; they often have difficulty coping with stress, change, and anxiety. Dissociation and somatization may also be present. These children have trouble coping with new situations. They show little resilience and have usually developed only a limited capacity for compassion. RE ALIT Y TESTING Children at a borderline level of emerging personality organization generally have intact reality testing, but it can be lost under conditions of stress in ways that are not age appropriate. When they are experiencing stress or intense emotions, the reality testing of children at a borderline level of emerging personality organization can be compromised. They may become disorganized, sometimes to the point of displaying short breaks with reality. With support from caregivers, however, they may have the capacity to reconstitute. How Children in This Category May Be Experienced Clinically Children at a borderline level of emerging personality organization can be exhausting. Developing a therapeutic alliance with them is challenging. Life with them is a roller-­ coaster ride of intense emotions, with rapid and unanticipated shifts in the nature of the relationship. A therapist is called upon to be a container of those intense emotions,

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 235
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 235 to experience deeply a child’s intense mental state while outwardly remaining on an even keel. Reactions to the therapist are frequently at extremes of love and hate, dependence and rejection. These children’s affective displays are intense, an...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 235 to experience deeply a child’s intense mental state while outwardly remaining on an even keel. Reactions to the therapist are frequently at extremes of love and hate, dependence and rejection. These children’s affective displays are intense, and they tend to be unforgiving. They can make therapists feel incompetent. Because of their self-­esteem issues and their reliance on primitive defenses, they tend to communicate their state of mind not by words but by projection of internal content onto others. There are moments when a therapist feels it is impossible to “get it right,” and other moments of feeling as if one is the best therapist who ever lived. Whatever state of affairs exists in the room with a child at a borderline level at any time is tenuous at best. Characters in play are often portrayed by such children as unempathic, cold, abusive, or outright sadistic, as manipulating others for their own purposes, or as targets of such attitudes. Children with these themes can treat a therapist in extremely hurtful ways, showing little empathy or concern, and even being gleeful about the therapist’s suffering. Maintaining boundaries with children in the borderline range can be challenging. They tend to push limits of physical distance, sexuality, and aggression. They may ask inappropriate questions about the therapist or show no interest at all. Because of either misperception or lack of empathy, they find it difficult to appreciate others’ perspectives. As noted earlier, they tend to be more about acting than thinking—­either about themselves or about what might be in the mind of another. Psychotic Level of Emerging Personality Organization (Severely Dysfunctional) Children at a psychotic level of emerging personality organization often have an anxious, hypervigilant temperament and may have a disorganized attachment pattern. These children are also characterized by difficulty in adequately examining reality. They may have experienced caregivers who were humiliating, sadistic, negligent, and/ or violent, and they often have suffered trauma and/or early adversity. A psychotic level of emerging personality organization in children is marked by verbal productions that are odd, difficult to follow, and sometimes unrelated to the conversation or situation at hand. Changes in the level of cognitive functioning are frequent. Breaks with reality occur consistently and are not subject to correction. Delusions and hallucinatory experiences may be present. Somatization and bodily concerns are not uncommon. Affect is inappropriate to content. Affective reactions, behaviors, and verbal productions may reflect a child’s response to internal rather than external stimuli. At this level, children may laugh or become frightened for no discernible reason. Affect may be flat or blunted, or at times extremely intense; in either case, it is out of proportion with circumstances. These children have difficulty discriminating internal from external stimuli and may display confusion about what distinguishes one person from another. Others may, without apparent justification, be experienced as threatening. Peers tend to experience these children as “weird” or “crazy.” They are frequently bullied or avoided. Their play tends to be idiosyncratic, with strange, rapidly changing, and hard-to-­follow themes. They may lose the boundary between self and other, and between play and reality. There is little capacity for, or interest in, self-­reflection. Attempts to enter the world of these children, or to know them better, may be seen as intrusive or motivated by hostility, or may be met with indifference. The families of these children frequently have significant histories of mental illness.

chunks.heading: 236 Childhood
chunks.preview: 236 Childhood IDENTIT Y Children at a psychotic level of emerging personality organization lack an age-­ appropriate and clear sense of themselves and the difference between themselves and others. They tend to be passive, rigid, and withdrawn. Although they may be aware of being “different,” they ha...
chunks.text: 236 Childhood IDENTIT Y Children at a psychotic level of emerging personality organization lack an age-­ appropriate and clear sense of themselves and the difference between themselves and others. They tend to be passive, rigid, and withdrawn. Although they may be aware of being “different,” they have few, if any, self-­observation skills. At a psychotic level of emerging personality organization, children tend to experience problems in attention and learning. Their thinking, speech, and behavior may seem bizarre or peculiar to others. They often experience learning difficulties. They may also experience difficulties with sleep patterns, food intake, and/or grooming and self-care, which can appear peculiar and not age-­appropriate. They tend to show a limited range of feelings and are easily dysregulated emotionally. They tend to be jealous, suspicious, cold, aloof, and overly self-­reliant. They struggle to communicate emotions in an age-­appropriate way. Often the affect they display is not appropriate to the content and/or the situation. Children at this level of emerging personality organization may not distinguish internal from external stimuli, may experience or treat the body as if it were not theirs, and may be uncoordinated and disorganized in motor patterns. They may be unable to read internal stimuli (interoception) and may show fear or discomfort about being touched. They may have stereotypies and bizarre movements. They tend to have low self-­esteem and/or perceptions of self that are simultaneously diminished (e.g., guilt, paranoia) and elevated (e.g., grandiosity). They have very little capacity for self-­observation but are often aware of being considered “different,” even if they do not understand why. They lack a stable and integrated sense of self and tend to be passive, rigid, and withdrawn. These children have not developed an age-­appropriate sense of causality and agency. They struggle to find meaning and pleasure in their activities. Their actions do not seem to be guided by specific, personally relevant choices, interests, and/or values. They struggle to recognize the existence of a causal relationship between different mental states and actions, and they are unable to explore and reflect upon the responses their actions elicit from the environment and from others. OBJECT REL ATIONS Children at a psychotic level of emerging personality organization have not developed an age-­appropriate capacity to foster and maintain genuine and reciprocal interpersonal relationships. They have little interest in relationships and take no pleasure in being in social situations. They struggle with trust, are hypersensitive, and often fear betrayal or humiliation. In these children, reflective capacity tends to be severely impaired. They appear to have limited, if any, interest in trying to understand others. They lack an age-­ appropriate sense of curiosity and interest in themselves, others, and the surrounding world. They have little, if any, self-­observation skills and self-­awareness. They have not developed an age-­appropriate capacity to foster and maintain genuine and reciprocal interpersonal relationships, especially beyond close family members. They take no pleasure in being in social situations or in the company of others. They are hypersensitive to the possibility of betrayal and humiliation. Their empathy

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 237
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 237 and understanding of other people’s emotions are minimal at best. Epistemic trust can suffer significant distortions. Information from external sources may be perceived as intrusive, confusing, or threatening, leading the child to appear disint...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 237 and understanding of other people’s emotions are minimal at best. Epistemic trust can suffer significant distortions. Information from external sources may be perceived as intrusive, confusing, or threatening, leading the child to appear disinterested or impervious to others’ views. Conversely, in other cases, indiscriminate and uncritical receptivity may be observed, leading the child to rely recklessly on the information received and to develop a marked dependence on the views of others. They respond negatively to moderate levels of interpersonal frustration and do not seem to be interested in, or capable of, repairing relational ruptures. Accordingly, they have difficulty updating their beliefs based on new information, including sensory–­ affective experiences. Their tendency to interpret ostensive communication differently from how speakers expect often results in confusion or misunderstanding. They tend to be both uninhibited and very rigid morally. Although such children may have developed an age-­appropriate language capacity, their ability to share meaning and play symbolically and creatively tends to be severely compromised. Any symbolic play they engage in tends to be rigid and repetitive, and is sometimes difficult to understand. They are unable to create a make-­believe scene and to engage in play organized around a theme or plan, especially if led by others. DEFENSE AND COPING Children at a psychotic level of emerging personality organization mainly employ primitive defense mechanisms (e.g., delusional projection, psychotic denial, distortion, autistic withdrawal) that distort reality and fail in defensive regulation. These children tend to be uninhibited; they do not adequately filter what they say or do. They also tend to be easily dysregulated, and they struggle to reregulate, even when helped by caregivers. Immature defenses are predominant. They tend to be inflexible and unaware. They struggle to cope with new situations and show little resilience. RE ALIT Y TESTING Children at a psychotic level of emerging personality organization have a highly compromised reality-­testing capacity. Breaks with reality occur repeatedly and are not subject to correction. These children may describe delusions and hallucinatory experiences. Their thoughts and behaviors are often considered inappropriate to their age and/or culture, and they can often experience impairment or loss of their sense of reality. How Children In This Category May Be Experienced Clinically When therapists who are relating to children find themselves unable to make logical sense of what they are talking about, their experience may be an important diagnostic indicator. Connecting with these children and developing an alliance can feel like an impossible task. Children at a psychotic level of emerging personality organization have great difficulty communicating their thoughts. There are moments when they seem to disappear from the room, having gone to some other place in their minds, attending to voices other than their therapist’s or their own. Their reactions may seem

chunks.heading: 238 Childhood
chunks.preview: 238 Childhood entirely inconsistent with what has just been said or done, possibly following from their inability to process information adequately or because they are responding to internal stimuli rather than to what is coming from the therapist. A clinician can often feel internally the extreme a...
chunks.text: 238 Childhood entirely inconsistent with what has just been said or done, possibly following from their inability to process information adequately or because they are responding to internal stimuli rather than to what is coming from the therapist. A clinician can often feel internally the extreme anxiety that grips these children—­a terror of extraordinary intensity. At times, one gets the sense of a world entirely out of control, unpredictable, incomprehensible. At a psychotic level of emerging personality organization, children can be hypervigilant. They may have peculiar interests, see the world in unique ways, and easily and often lose track of what is and is not real. Working with such children can be confusing, intense, and frightening, but also rewarding. They are sometimes open to being helped. Because they feel themselves to be different and find themselves rejected by peers, they may turn to their therapists for assistance. This bid for help can be key to building the therapeutic alliance. Emerging Personality Patterns in Children: A Clinical Proposal After assessing a child’s level of emerging personality organization, clinicians could evaluate emerging personality patterns, assessing carefully the associated risk factors and protective factors. Because personality is in constant evolution—­not just in childhood—­a knowledge of developmental trajectories should guide clinical interventions that are tailored to the individual child. This section provides general descriptions of common emerging personality patterns—­a taxonomy of children rather than symptoms. They should not be considered as categorical or fixed but as an aid in pattern recognition; that is, clinicians should ask whether a patient “fits” a particular description. Like all the P Axes of PDM-3, the classification system of the PC Axis reflects an integration of clinical knowledge, theoretical reflection, and empirical research. The first edition of the PDM (PDM Task Force, 2006) included emerging personality patterns but put childhood and adolescence in the same section. This led to an imbalance in pattern descriptions, often toward adolescence, and failure to recognize the specificities of childhood in all its nuances. The writers of the childhood section of the second edition, in contrast, in response to a controversy in the literature on the subject, focused solely on level of organization, omitting the question of personality pattern. In this edition of the manual, we repropose the idea of emergent personality patterns already present in the first edition of the PDM, rethought in light of the more recent literature on child personality (for a review, see Fortunato & Speranza, 2018) and some preliminary empirical work (Fortunato, Tanzilli, Lingiardi, & Speranza, 2021, 2022). Clinicians have reported that representations of these personality patterns in childhood help them develop case formulations and treatment plans that are more accurate and more sensitive to the specific characteristics of their patients (see Figure 4.1). Clinicians should note that a particular child may show no similarity to any pattern here, or may show a moderate similarity to one or more patterns, or may match a prototype very closely. Emerging High‐Functioning Personality Pattern Children with an emerging high-­functioning personality tend to be dynamic and expansive, conscientious, responsible, creative, and empathetic. They have internalized

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 239
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 239 • Emerging high-functioning personality pattern • Emerging inhibited/withdrawn personality pattern • Emerging depressive personality pattern • Emerging obsessive personality pattern • Emerging dysregulated personality pattern • Emerging impulsi...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 239 • Emerging high-functioning personality pattern • Emerging inhibited/withdrawn personality pattern • Emerging depressive personality pattern • Emerging obsessive personality pattern • Emerging dysregulated personality pattern • Emerging impulsive personality pattern • Emerging suspicious personality pattern • Emerging schizoid personality pattern FIGURE 4.1. Emerging personality patterns in childhood. clear moral and ethical standards. They demonstrate high levels of cognitive and affective functioning, and have mastered processes such as regulation, attention, learning, movement, and language. They typically use age-­appropriate language; appreciate and respond to humor, and use talents, skills, and energy in effective and productive ways. These children can express, comprehend, and communicate emotions that, in both quality and intensity, tend to be appropriate to the situation. They show good adaptation, a cohesive sense of identity, and an intact ability to test reality. In relationships, they are capable of intimacy and reciprocity. They form healthy, mutually gratifying friendships. When hurt or disappointed, they can let others, including their parents, console them. They manage their self-­esteem well. They use defenses and coping strategies that handle aggressive impulses without becoming passive. They have normal ambitions and can compete in a healthy way, managing anxiety and negative emotions without disorganizing. They tend to enjoy challenges and respond to stress and conflict with appropriate feelings. These children are also adaptable, flexible, and resilient. Unless they encounter significant traumas or stresses, they are well positioned to handle life’s demands and inconveniences. If children with this emerging personality pattern develop a clinical condition, they can usually overcome the difficulty with adequate support. Emerging Inhibited/Withdrawn Personality Pattern Children with an emerging inhibited/withdrawn personality suffer from excessive insecurity, self-­criticism, and general inhibition. They are easily embarrassed or shamed. They worry about being criticized, disapproved of, rejected, or abandoned. Consequently, they tend to be passive, anxious, depressed, dejected, reticent, shy, and withdrawn. They may find little or no pleasure, satisfaction, or enjoyment in everyday activities and may have difficulty feeling strong positive emotions. They may struggle to express any feelings, particularly negative emotions, which they tend to deny. They may have trouble expressing disagreement or hostility, and they may be characterized by pessimism and a lack of self-­confidence. Their feelings of inadequacy, especially of not measuring up to peers, lead them to avoid social situations and to become overly dependent on their caregivers. Anxieties about being hurt underlie their desire for protection from others. They fear separation, either recurrently or pervasively, and typically act in submissive, clingy ways. They are

chunks.heading: 240 Childhood
chunks.preview: 240 Childhood often anxious in new situations; they shrink from taking risks or trying new behaviors. Such avoidance can hinder their school and social functioning, and prevent them from reaching developmental milestones. They tend to have poor social skills in general, and to be highly sensitive to...
chunks.text: 240 Childhood often anxious in new situations; they shrink from taking risks or trying new behaviors. Such avoidance can hinder their school and social functioning, and prevent them from reaching developmental milestones. They tend to have poor social skills in general, and to be highly sensitive to criticism, especially at school. While children with this pattern of emerging personality can be highly functional, those who exemplify its more severe forms can be seriously compromised. Difficulty with independent action and assertion often reflects a problem in mastering aggressive impulses and wishes, based on either a constitutional impairment or conflicts over rage and destructiveness. Because their withdrawal is not perceived as developmentally dangerous or interpersonally aggressive, teachers and other observers may not be concerned about children with this emerging personality pattern. They often come from dysfunctional families with dominant, untrustworthy, and intrusive caregivers who discourage autonomy and reward dependence. Growing up is implicitly seen as a betrayal, and being dependent is the only way to maintain the bond with parents. These children may have an insecure–­ambivalent attachment pattern and may avoid school, extracurricular activities, and relationships with peers. In friendships, they may seek unconditional acceptance. This pattern may coexist with depression or anxiety, social inhibition, low self-­ esteem, isolation, fear of strangers or new situations, anger, and low impulsivity. Emerging Depressive Personality Pattern Children with an emerging depressive personality suffer intermittent or chronic dysphoric affect. Whereas the previous pattern is organized around fear and avoidance, the depressive pattern is characterized by guilt and self-­criticism. This pattern can be expressed through passivity, somatization, difficulty expressing emotions, and self-­recrimination. They tend to express aggressiveness in passive and indirect ways. Like inhibited children, they may be needy, overdependent, and excessively anxious, and they may feel inadequate, inferior, or incompetent. They tend to feel powerless, weak, or at the mercy of forces beyond their control. In stressful situations or in the face of strong emotions, they tend to show a significant worsening of their usual level of functioning, so much so that they may lose skills that they have previously acquired. Because they have an irrationally negative estimation of themselves, children with this emerging pattern are highly reactive to loss and rejection, for which they may blame themselves. They tend to hold themselves to scrupulous standards and chronically feel they fall short. They may struggle to interact with peers and may experience self-­devaluation and fear of judgment at school. Rather than blaming others, they attribute their suffering to their own badness. Taking on this responsibility allows them a sense of control, leaving them with the feeling that they have the power to change that which feels unchangeable. Depressively inclined children work hard to be “nice” and “good.” In treatment, it is vital to elicit their buried, defended-­against, negative feelings, including normal criticism of the clinician. They typically idealize the therapist, try to be good patients, and assume that therapist’s noncritical acceptance indicates that they have not noticed how bad they really are. It is critical that they come to understand that their internal representation of badness is a misplaced effort to protect themselves from other dangers, such as the fact that one’s life is not always under one’s control. Children with this emerging pattern may also experience neuropsychological,

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 241
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 241 motor, and scholastic difficulties, as well as dysregulation issues. In general, their development may present delays or difficulties, such as poor verbal articulation, learning and memory problems, and poor motor coordination that leaves them...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 241 motor, and scholastic difficulties, as well as dysregulation issues. In general, their development may present delays or difficulties, such as poor verbal articulation, learning and memory problems, and poor motor coordination that leaves them clumsy and awkward. At school, they may have difficulties because they tend to not initiate or carry out tasks alone because of their fear of being laughed at or mocked. They tend to develop somatic symptoms; they may complain and be whiny in response to stress or conflict; and they may use their own medical or psychological problems as an excuse to miss school or fail to meet responsibilities. Although this is often a medium to high-­functioning personality pattern, children with severe versions of this pattern can suffer extensively from concomitant emotional dysregulation, which, if present, is associated with lower functioning. Depressive patterns may continue into adolescence and adulthood, as they present a homotypic continuity. Emerging Obsessive Personality Pattern The emerging obsessive personality is characterized by excessive attention to detail, strict adherence to rules, a preference for productive activities, and ruminative thoughts not typical of the child’s age. Children with this emerging pattern may lack empathy, seem a bit bizarre, have difficulty with intimacy, and struggle to express negative emotions They may be anxious, worried, hypervigilant, and controlling. They are rigid and stubborn in their thinking, often engaging in compulsive rituals related to precise ideas about how things should be done. They are excessively worried about rules, practices, order, organization, tidiness, and cleanliness. Fear of dirt is common in these children, whose parents are often overprotective and anxious, or the opposite—­that is, negligent, such that the child has had to impose a strict order on a chaotic experience. Because they worry about criticism and disapproval, obsessive children are polite, conscientious, and responsible. At the same time, despite being competitive, they are self-­critical, inhibited, and afraid of getting angry. They may automatically submit to authority. Consequently, they tend to express aggression in passive and indirect ways, and they tend to swing from being very dependent and needy to being counterdependent and avoidant. Because they do not feel the need for social interaction, these children may have few friends. Their school performance can suffer because of their need to follow exhausting rituals. The obsessive personality pattern can have both positive and negative impacts on school performance, because educational systems tend to reward obsessive–­ compulsive traits such as cleanliness, precision, competitiveness, self-­ criticism, and self-­control. Children who fit this pattern may maintain a sense of control to the point of losing the main purpose of an activity. Morally and ethically, they may be overly conscientious, scrupulous, and uncompromising. They may be incapable of throwing away objects that are consumed or valueless, even those that have no affective meaning. In these children, emotional expression is compromised, often being repressed or avoided at all costs. They have difficulty with empathy and recognition of others’ points of view and may be awkward in relationships generally. Excessive control, inhibition of aggression, magical thinking, concern with rules, and sensitivity to criticism can be present in healthy development, but obsessive children are hypersensitive in these areas. Fussiness, order, punctuality, intolerance of

chunks.heading: 242 Childhood
chunks.preview: 242 Childhood confusion and mess, and attention to detail take inordinate amounts of time and interfere with their ordinary activities. Obsessive–­ compulsive patterns can be construed as on a continuum. At the healthy end are obedient children who follow rules and are adaptively diligent. At the mo...
chunks.text: 242 Childhood confusion and mess, and attention to detail take inordinate amounts of time and interfere with their ordinary activities. Obsessive–­ compulsive patterns can be construed as on a continuum. At the healthy end are obedient children who follow rules and are adaptively diligent. At the most disturbed end are obsessive children who cannot defend themselves from overwhelming anxiety, regression, or loss of reality testing or impulse control. Their need for control has a stubborn quality that can be oppressive to their family and their environment. Obsessive children handle anguish with defenses such as the isolation of affect, compartmentalization, and intellectualization. They may manage anxiety through compulsive perfectionist and repetitive behaviors that conceal aggressive fantasies. This emerging pattern runs in families: Genetic factors may predispose the child to obsessive traits, and caregiving patterns often model obsessive and compulsive preoccupations. Obsessive traits tend to persist from childhood into adulthood, suggesting a homotypic continuity. The obsessive aspect is central; the compulsive aspect may not always be present in childhood. Emerging Dysregulated Personality Pattern An emerging dysregulated personality is characterized by mood instability, impulsivity, anger, identity disorders, and primitive defenses. Children with this emerging pattern tend to spiral out of control and subsequently to experience extreme anxiety, unhappiness, anger, or excitement. Their sudden mood swings or emotional shifts lead them to express emotions theatrically, extravagantly, dramatically, or through somatization. They tend to feel angry and conflicted, and to be easily frustrated and impulsive. Children with this emerging personality struggle with unstable and intense but superficial relationships with peers and adults. Although dependent on others for attention, they often deny their need for it. They may behave seductively, lack empathy, and act out. Their sense of self is fragile, often shifting from all-bad to all-good attributions. Their vulnerability to sudden regression compromises their level of ego function, accounting for their cognitive deficits, attention and memory problems, and delays in verbal and motor development. They often fail to control their impulses and behave immaturely for their age in other ways. These children tend to arouse strong feelings and reactions in others. Their relationships are characterized by intrusive and controlling behaviors, hostility, detachment, role reversal, and manipulation, all of which reflect a deep fear of being rejected. Exquisitely sensitive to their environment, they may make desperate efforts to avoid real or imaginary abandonment. Sometimes they seem not to need anyone; other times, to achieve a sense of identity, they try to control someone else or submit to another person. They can be afflicted by chronic feelings of emptiness. Emotional dysregulation is characterized by sudden, intense, and unpredictable changes. It is context dependent, especially in relation to the caregiving relationship.
