Emotional dysregulation, rather than impulsivity, is a strong predictor of borderline personality disorder symptoms in adulthood. Affective dysfunction, disinhibition (in the form of sensation seeking), self-­regulation, and emotion regulation deficits also have a role in emerging dysregulated personality in childhood. The etiology of dysregulated emerging personality may be lie in specific traumas, genetic and temperamental predispositions, and environmental experiences, such as dysfunctional, abusive, and neglectful parent–­child interactions. A disorganized

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 243
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 243 attachment pattern is often evident in both caregivers and children. Difficulties in emotional regulation seem to overlap with those found in maltreated children subject to states of dissociation. Children with an emerging dysregulated pattern...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 243 attachment pattern is often evident in both caregivers and children. Difficulties in emotional regulation seem to overlap with those found in maltreated children subject to states of dissociation. Children with an emerging dysregulated pattern may enact an insatiable search for attention that is always frustrated. This frustration has the paradoxical effect of increasing rather than extinguishing their attention-­seeking behavior. Caregivers of children with this pattern are often negligent; they may show more care when their child is sick or injured. Such parents sometimes convey the idea that they are the only source of support, rewarding their children for being dependent and passive. Some engage in inappropriate and sexualized ways of relating. A notable feature of these children is their sensitivity to their caregivers’ moods and unexpressed thoughts. Children with this kind of emerging personality often have clinical conditions related to feeding, sleep, behavior, and regulation. Their school functioning may be severely compromised. In general, this is a developmentally impaired personality pattern. Emerging Impulsive Personality Pattern Children with an emerging impulsive personality pattern have difficulty regulating self-­esteem, feel excessive anger (and fail to feel more vulnerable affects; e.g., sadness), and rely on the defenses of splitting and devaluation. They tend not to consider the consequences of their actions; they may use others as objects, manipulating them for personal gain. They may be easily frustrated, distracted, inflexible, stubborn, sulky, irritable, and disobedient. They may seem precociously self-­sufficient. Because they are often aggressive, provocative, and difficult, they tend to get into power struggles with adults. Caregivers can be exhausted and fearful of their behavior. Impulsive children are often brought to treatment for behavior disorders and/or neuropsychological, executive, and attention deficits. Theirs is a rather compromised kind of personality. On the adaptive end of the spectrum, such children may achieve success at the expense of meaningful, mutually enhancing relationships. At the maladaptive end, criminality is a danger. Problems in narcissistic development are noteworthy. At the healthier end of the narcissistic spectrum, children are highly focused on realistic pride in their achievements. At the more dysfunctional pole, children with narcissistic motivations may display pervasive patterns of grandiose fantasies and/or behavior. They may behave with arrogance, entitlement, a need for admiration, and a lack of empathy toward others. Or they may appear depressed, irritated, and full of complaints, behind which lie frustrated grandiose aspirations. Although they may seem “spoiled” and entitled, most are clearly defending themselves against feelings of low self-­esteem and are trying to avoid shame and humiliation. The heterogeneity of these issues, their early onset, and the related risk factors suggest at least two subgroups of precursor signals: too-high or too-low levels of arousal and parenting interactions. Children with early impulsivity can differ depending on the presence or absence of callous–­unemotional traits: In the first group, temperamental difficulties in processing emotional stimuli and responding to punishments can interfere with the normal development of empathy, guilt, and other aspects of conscience; in the second, cognitive deficits and ineffective socialization lead to behavioral and emotional dysregulation. Impulsive children are trapped in a pattern of discontinuous experience. Because they lack reflective function, they project unreflective images on the environment.

chunks.heading: 244 Childhood
chunks.preview: 244 Childhood They look to find what is wrong with the world and may evoke behavior in others that confirms their expectations. For these children, reflective functioning increases rather than decreases their psychological vulnerability. Consequently, they may enter autohypnotic states to escape pai...
chunks.text: 244 Childhood They look to find what is wrong with the world and may evoke behavior in others that confirms their expectations. For these children, reflective functioning increases rather than decreases their psychological vulnerability. Consequently, they may enter autohypnotic states to escape pain, terror, and impotence. Early dysfunctional interactions with caregivers can be an important predictor. Parents may behave in aggressive ways because they are frightened by their children’s vulnerabilities, which they find unbearable in themselves. Their children then suffer from a twofold deficit: parental failure to meet their attachment needs, and parental attack for vulnerabilities that their caregivers have denied in themselves and projected onto them. Environmental, genetic, temperamental, peer-­relationship, and social-­skill factors all play a role in the development of this emerging personality pattern. A critical connection has been identified between self-­regulation and impulsivity. During childhood, success in emotional self-­regulation depends largely on parental support and responsiveness. Preschool children show aggressive, opposing, and impulsive behavior when their parents are not good resources for emotional care and education. These children have disharmonious parent–­child relationships characterized by low levels of affectivity, positive involvement, and support. Impulsive children may lack friends. Because of their resistance to involvement, their disrespect for others and for rules, and their sensitivity to criticism, they may struggle in school. They tend to resort to violence or intimidation to control someone deemed important, to have difficulty maintaining friendships, and to blame others for their own failings or flaws. They may lie or cheat and be unreliable and irresponsible. At school, peers typically provide support for the development of regulatory functions, but the anger, aggression, and impulsivity of these children sabotages that possibility. They can be coercive and controlling, and may deceive, exploit, and abandon their friends as if they had never mattered to them. Peers’ refusals may then have negative consequences for their development. Rejection, victimization, and exclusion are related to subsequent aggressive behaviors and high levels of delinquency. Such children need special assistance via appropriate limits, empathetic responsiveness, and help with affect regulation and behavioral containment. A therapist can help them associate self-­esteem with self-­control. Emerging Suspicious Personality Pattern Suspicious children may be mistrustful, jealous, hostile, rancorous, and vindictive. They tend to interpret reality as malevolent. They are hypervigilant, hypersensitive, and controlling, and easily feel easily embarrassed and ashamed. They do not like to share—­either objects or emotions. Distrust of others, whose motives they interpret as malevolent, can be mild, moderate, or pervasive. Shame and humiliation are important determinants of this emerging personality pattern. Children with an emerging suspicious personality have patterns of behavior and thoughts that are greatly compromised. There is limited research on this type of emerging personality, but clinical experience suggests that adults with diagnosable paranoid personality disorder often developed this pattern during childhood, when they seemed to be suspicious, afraid to get close to others, diffident, lacking in humor, unable to express positive emotions, serious, detached, moralistic, and envious. These children tend also to be shy and easily frightened. In interactions with peers and adults, they are poised to receive shame and humiliation, an attitude that can severely compromise both social and school functioning.

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 245
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 245 To cope with this expectation, they project, avoid, and preemptively attack others in words and/or actions. They feel no pleasure in being with others and often feel persecuted and victimized. They tend to be more self-­sufficient than is typic...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Organization and Difficulties 245 To cope with this expectation, they project, avoid, and preemptively attack others in words and/or actions. They feel no pleasure in being with others and often feel persecuted and victimized. They tend to be more self-­sufficient than is typical for their age, because they cannot accept being dependent. These children use defenses that include splitting, projection, projective identification, denial, and omnipotent control. They have two distinct inner selves, an idealized one and a persecutory one, and they experience others in these categories as well. The persecutory image is projected onto external objects, whereas the idealized image is introjected, producing an all-good self-image (megalomania) and an all-bad representation of others (persecution). Their use of projective identification leads to attitudes and behaviors that provoke others into aggressiveness and hostility, thereby confirming their expectations. They use the utmost control to maintain this expectation of malevolence, so that they can protect themselves against it. They tend to be hypervigilant and to avoid social interactions, and they often carry resentment and anger about past experiences of being humiliated or victimized. Such experiences, along with their innate sensitivity and other temperamental inclinations, make it difficult for them to develop trust in others. This personality pattern is associated strongly with childhood trauma, including experiences of being overwhelmed and humiliated, teased and taunted, and uncomforted during times of loss. Sadistic behavior and/or violence in caregivers, and bullying and/or victimization by peers are among the traumatic events that influence the development of this personality pattern. Emerging Schizoid Personality Pattern The emerging schizoid personality pattern is characterized by peculiar thoughts, expressions, and behaviors. Children with emerging schizoid psychologies tend either to have a very limited range of affect or to be so overwhelmed by emotion that they automatically retreat to an internal safe place. They may be shy, timid, and withdrawn, and may have few or no close relationships. They may have difficulty relating to others, be socially avoidant, and lack pleasure in others’ company. Children with the emerging schizoid personality pattern are often anxious and passive, and may seem bizarre. Their school performance may be poor. Because of their immersion in their inner world, their reality testing may be compromised. They may have poor social skills; their peers may ignore, neglect, and avoid them. They resort to magical thinking, fantasy, and ideas that seem strange to others. They may behave in unusual ways, think in concrete terms or in images, and describe experiences in general terms. Their speech may be tortuous, vague, and disconnected, verging on inarticulate. Some children with this emerging personality pattern learn to walk and/or talk later than other children and are somewhat uncoordinated, clumsy, and awkward. They have problems with concentration and are easily frustrated. They may play in immature ways and have set rituals linked to urinating or defecating, going to bed, or eating. Schizoid psychology is dominated by a chronic dilemma about relationships: Children with this pattern feel easily overwhelmed by relationships and keep their distance, but they may have fantasies about perfect closeness. Either because of severe inhibition or because they lack social skills, children with this pattern often cannot spontaneously interact with others appropriately. When asked about their withdrawal from others, they may assert that others do a variety of harms to them. Clinical experience

chunks.heading: 246 Childhood
chunks.preview: 246 Childhood suggests that they have learned to fear the negative consequences of their need for love. This can be a highly compromised personality pattern, similar to the adult one. The fantasies of these children are often associated with early relational traumas that seem to justify their fears...
chunks.text: 246 Childhood suggests that they have learned to fear the negative consequences of their need for love. This can be a highly compromised personality pattern, similar to the adult one. The fantasies of these children are often associated with early relational traumas that seem to justify their fears of others. Temperament plays a role in their psychology, but failures of the primary environment are critical. Children with an emerging schizoid pattern use defenses that include splitting, projection, projective identification, and retreating into fantasy, which may lead to confusion between their inner world and external reality. While they may appear emotionally cold and detached, they often build a private subjective world of omnipotence, aggression, and idealized attachment. Their avoidance of expressing emotions creates the impression of a general aloofness, detachment, and severely flat affect. This pattern is heterogeneous and can change over time, with some children showing improvements, while others may develop more serious disorders such as schizotypal or psychotic symptoms. Schizoid personality may have a genetic link to schizophrenia and is more common in children with parents who have an affective disorder. Early relational traumas may also play a role in the development of this personality pattern. BIBLIOGRAPHY General Bibliography Bellak, L., & Bellak, S. S. (1949). Children’s ­Apperception Test. chunks.heading: CHAPTER 5
chunks.preview: CHAPTER 5 Child Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience SC Axis C H A P T E R E D IT O R S Anna Maria Speranza, PhD Norka Malberg, PsyD Alexandro Fortunato, PhD CONSULTANTS Flaminia Alimonti, MD Pia Rita Fragomeni, PhD Mireia Plech Sazatornil, BSc Kenneth Barish, PhD Ilenia Gori, MD Maria Quinti...
chunks.text: CHAPTER 5 Child Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience SC Axis C H A P T E R E D IT O R S Anna Maria Speranza, PhD Norka Malberg, PsyD Alexandro Fortunato, PhD CONSULTANTS Flaminia Alimonti, MD Pia Rita Fragomeni, PhD Mireia Plech Sazatornil, BSc Kenneth Barish, PhD Ilenia Gori, MD Maria Quintigliano, PhD Thomas Barrett, PhD Alexandra Harrison, MD Larry Rosenberg, PhD Israel Bobadilla Gonzalez, MD Leon Hoffman, MD Miriam Steele, PhD Francine Conway, PhD Athanassia Kotroni, MSc Federico Tonioni, MD Lea de Setton, PhD Eirini Lympinaki, PhD Steve Tuber, PhD Maria Dimitropoulou, MSc Johanna Malone, PhD Karin Ensink, PhD Sofia Papakonstantinou, MSc Introduction Assessment of childhood psychopathology can be challenging for mental health professionals, especially when we have to understand whether a child’s symptomatic pattern is due to a temporary difficulty or to an overt disorder. Our problem is partly a consequence of the ever-­changing nature of children’s development and the potentially transient nature of their difficulties as part of their maturation. Each child has a unique pace and/or pattern of neurocognitive and emotional development. It is not uncommon for a 4-year-old to function in some areas as a younger child or for an 11-year-old to have fully entered puberty. Psychological disorders, despite some continuity, may be manifested differently depending on a child’s age. Assessment of risk

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 257
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 257 factors is critical to supporting children’s growth with effective interventions at both relational and developmental levels. Mental health problems are not determined solely by a child’s temperament, or solely by the relational environment (Cicchetti, 1984, 2016; Cicchet...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 257 factors is critical to supporting children’s growth with effective interventions at both relational and developmental levels. Mental health problems are not determined solely by a child’s temperament, or solely by the relational environment (Cicchetti, 1984, 2016; Cicchetti & Cohen, 1995; Sroufe, 1997). Rather, they emerge from a dynamic interaction: Children are endowed with constitutional characteristics that interact with the environment into which they are born. Sometimes the outcome of this interaction is more or less favorable; other times there is an imbalance in the complex dynamic interaction between child and caregiver(s) that results in a difficulty. Looking at both the individual child and the familial and cultural context, including assessing relationships with caregivers in a timely manner, is vital to a balanced and flexible clinical formulation and the development of a treatment plan. The period from age 4 to age 11 brings a gradual, intersubjectively determined transition. Each year brings new developmental challenges, and cognitive and emotional capacities. Around age 4, children start to develop bodily autonomy in most basic functions, along with new skills, such as full language development, theory of mind (Baron-Cohen, Tager-­Flusberg, & Cohen, 2000; Dennet, 1978; Ensink & Mayes, 2010; Tomasello, 1999), and the capacity for self-­awareness and emotional reflection. Additionally, they are more actively involved in social life with peers and other nonfamilial adults (especially in school settings). From this age on, any symptoms may have a strong impact on the subjective experience of children, affecting their relationships, thoughts, feelings, and sense of bodily self. This SC Axis does not define particular disorders (for which one can refer to the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (5th ed., text rev. [DSM5-TR]; American Psychiatric Association, 2022), the International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems (11th ed.; ICD-11; WHO, 2021), or the more specific Diagnostic Classification of Mental Health and Developmental Disorders of Infancy and Early Childhood (DC:0–5TM; Zero to Three, 2016). Instead, we offer a psychodynamically oriented description of the child’s subjective experience of the symptoms connected with particular diagnostic categories (Table 5.1). We also provide information, when known, about the continuity of symptomatic patterns. We examine possible clinical pictures in infancy that predispose a young patient to a given disorder during childhood, and we look at how certain disorders have a higher risk of evolving into long-term psychopathology. At the end of each description, we provide therapeutic indications and clinical illustrations to support clinicians in their therapeutic work with children affected by different psychopathologies. SC0 Healthy Responses SC0.1 Developmental Crises and Situational Crises Development is a fluid but disharmonious process. Traditionally, child psychoanalysis conceptualized children’s mental health in terms of a harmonious interaction among the ego, superego, and id, among the different subsystems of each, and between these agencies and outside influences. Such harmony could be achieved only if the inner agencies have reached and can maintain comparable levels of development, and if the external influences on them reflect an “average expectable environment” (Hartmann, 1939). The internal agencies are always in latent dispute once adequate internal organization has occurred. One indicator of the level of such structuralization is the nature

(continued) SC13 Elimination Disorders SC13.1 Encopresis SC13.2 Enuresis SC14 Event- and Stressor-Related Disorders SC14.1 Adjustment disorders (other than developmental) SC14.2 Posttraumatic stress disorder SC14.3 Complex posttraumatic stress disorder SC14.4 P...

(continued) SC13 Elimination Disorders SC13.1 Encopresis SC13.2 Enuresis SC14 Event- and Stressor-Related Disorders SC14.1 Adjustment disorders (other than developmental) SC14.2 Posttraumatic stress disorder SC14.3 Complex posttraumatic stress disorder SC14.4 Prolonged mourning/grief reaction SC15 Attachment Disorders SC16 Impulse Control and Disruptive Behavior Disorders SC16.1 Oppositional defiant disorder SC16.2 Conduct disorder SC16.3 Intermittent explosive disorder SC16.4 Web-mediated psychopathology: Internet and videogaming addiction and social withdrawal SC16.5 Substance-related disorders SC16.6 Kleptomania SC17 Clinical Signs of Psychotic Functioning in Childhood That Require Attention of the defenses a child uses to manage anxiety (see the MC Axis). For children, even the most normal developmental shifts may not feel harmonious. Every move forward from a phase that is familiar, safe, and gratifying can feel uncertain and even dangerous to a child. Anna Freud’s (1965) concept of “developmental lines” highlights the fact that development is a back-and-forth process. In a healthy response to a developmental challenge, maturational progress is interrupted, with no obvious external precipitant, by an arrest or regression to a prior phase. Such reactions follow a stable period of adjustment and are time-­limited. Developmental crises are normal aspects of maturation and vary in how visible they are to others. In efforts to resolve earlier unresolved crises before moving on, children typically regress to the psychological issues associated with earlier phases. In a situational crisis, a child’s development is interrupted by a precipitant that may look relatively minor. The child’s reaction follows a stable period of adjustment and is time-­limited. Examples of precipitants of normal situational crises include a mother’s pregnancy, the arrival of a sibling, the first day of day care, the loss of a transitional object, illness, a medical procedure, separation at the beginning of school, a caregiver’s withdrawal following the death of a relative, and other stresses that fall short of constituting obvious traumas. Subjective Experience of Developmental Crises in Childhood Affective States Affective states include shame and/or blame of self or others for painful feelings. Anger, sadness, and shame may be conveyed in outbursts. A child’s mood may be subdued or highly charged, inappropriate or euphoric. Acute distress may be expressed by

chunks.heading: 260 Childhood
chunks.preview: 260 Childhood crying, rage, or destructive behavior. The child may understand what has occurred but be upset anyway, or may refuse to acknowledge the meaning of the event, yet come to terms with it over time. Thoughts and Fantasies Thoughts and fantasies may include the child’s puzzlement at the des...
chunks.text: 260 Childhood crying, rage, or destructive behavior. The child may understand what has occurred but be upset anyway, or may refuse to acknowledge the meaning of the event, yet come to terms with it over time. Thoughts and Fantasies Thoughts and fantasies may include the child’s puzzlement at the destabilization of a previously unproblematic sense of self. The child may deny or disavow the existence of a problem, or project and displace feelings onto others. Somatic States Sleep patterns may be disturbed. The child may complain of not feeling well. Appetite may either increase or be suppressed. Relational Patterns The quality of the primary relationships that support the child’s development is of great importance during these times. A caregiver’s capacity to note changes in a child’s behavior and see them as purposeful and meaningful is critical to developmental restoration. Children’s behavioral and affective expressions are highly variable, depending on their age, their resilience, the nature of the challenge, the support from caregivers, and other factors. In a healthy response to a developmental challenge, a child maintains the capacity for age-­appropriate relationships but may become clingier and more demanding while struggling with the imbalance created by the crisis. There may be bouts of resentment and anger at caregivers seen as unhelpful. Siblings may become targets of displaced anger and hostility. Continuity Both developmental and situational crises do not have a specific continuity. Their course may depend on the general state of development, the extent of the developmental challenge, the stability of the environment, and nature of the crisis event. Therapeutic Indications If these crises do not lead to substantial problems, they do not necessarily require clinical intervention. When intervention does seem advisable, work with the child or with the parents alone may be indicated. Clinical Illustration Jeremy, age 4, was referred for chronic constipation. He had twin brothers, born when he was 3 years old. Jeremy came across as shy and well-­mannered. Although small for his age, he seemed keen to show off his physical abilities (e.g., jumping and throwing a ball). Jeremy’s symptoms had begun 6 months, after his brothers were born. His parents understood the behavior as a response to the birth of the twins. A year later, however, he continued to suffer from chronic constipation and refused to use the toilet,

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 261
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 261 defecating only in a diaper. Further evaluation showed that he struggled with new places and situations, and that he was extremely concerned about his mother’s wellbeing (she had been very ill during the second pregnancy). Parent sessions were critical in this case. Jerem...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 261 defecating only in a diaper. Further evaluation showed that he struggled with new places and situations, and that he was extremely concerned about his mother’s wellbeing (she had been very ill during the second pregnancy). Parent sessions were critical in this case. Jeremy’s mother and father were able to discuss ways of encouraging him to verbalize his feelings and to promote age-­appropriate management of his body. They were invited to think about the meaning behind his behaviors and to take a less rigid approach to toileting. Jeremy attended once-­weekly child therapy sessions, the aim of which was to help him use his capacity for symbolic play to express his frustration and anger over the twins’ birth and to explore his fears about his mother’s health. After few months of treatment, he began to use the toilet, declaring that his siblings now needed to do so “and not do disgusting poos in their pants.” His development had returned to a normal developmental trajectory. He remained a somewhat rigid child, but he was now able to use the help of his caregivers in facing new challenges. SC1 Autism Spectrum Disorders Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is a complex neurodevelopmental condition that persists throughout life, affecting many aspects of development including sociocommunicative skills, reciprocity, sensory processing, and motor behavior. There are diverse phenotypes, influenced by cognitive functions and neuropsychological profiles, irrespective of cultural, racial, ethnic, or socioeconomic backgrounds, and there is substantial heterogeneity with respect to genetic susceptibility, neural mechanisms, clinical presentation, comorbidities, response to treatment, and developmental trajectories. Prevalence is estimated to be as high as 1 in 44 children (Maenner, 2023) and is considerably higher among those assigned male at birth. Across the spectrum, proportion is roughly 4:1 male to female, but in individuals without intellectual disability, it increases to 10:1. Explanations for these differences are still being debated. Factors such as differences in phenotypic expression by gender, more frequent misdiagnosis in females, varying compensatory strategies, and biological factors (genetic and epigenetic) are all being considered (Autism and Developmental Disabilities Monitoring Network; Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2023). Cross-­cultural factors must also be taken into consideration. These are particularly important because cultural perspectives on appropriate behaviors and typical development may influence parental/caregiver reports and the diagnosis itself. Although difficulties in social interaction differentiate males with autism from children with developmental delays and from females with autism, these challenges are not as distinguishing for females (Lai, Lombardo, & Baron-Cohen, 2014; Prosperi et al., 2021). Higher-­functioning females with autism may experience delays in diagnosis compared to males, or they may go undiagnosed entirely. Generally, the female phenotype has been associated with a greater ability to mask symptoms. Young girls with autism may possess specific traits that parents may overlook, such as enhanced nonverbal communication, peripheral socialization skills, ability to engage in social interactions in pairs, or a strong desire for social interaction. Some of these characteristics have also been observed in girls with autism who have intellectual disabilities. Females are often more cognizant of feelings of inadequacy and face greater social pressures and expectations related to gender socialization, resulting in increased stigma if they behave atypically or lack empathy. From a clinical perspective, comorbidities such as selective mutism,

chunks.heading: 262 Childhood
chunks.preview: 262 Childhood anxiety, shyness, and even obsessive–­compulsive disorder may mask autistic traits in females in this age group. In recent years, there has been growing interest in the co-­occurrence of gender nonconformity and autistic spectrum disorder in both young people and adults. This associati...
chunks.text: 262 Childhood anxiety, shyness, and even obsessive–­compulsive disorder may mask autistic traits in females in this age group. In recent years, there has been growing interest in the co-­occurrence of gender nonconformity and autistic spectrum disorder in both young people and adults. This association has implications for managing both conditions clinically (Strang et al., 2018). Recent research (Mazzoli et al., 2022) suggests that autistic traits may be an epiphenomenon of gender nonconformity, stemming from social deficits and minority stress in noncomforming individuals, rather than being intrinsic to autism. Scientists are investigating etiological factors, particularly in genetics, epigenetics, neuroanatomy, and neurofunctionality. Families with one autistic child have a 10% risk of autism in another (Sandin et al., 2014) and a 20–25% risk of other neurodevelopmental disorders in siblings (Ozonoff et al., 2011). Family studies raise questions about genetic factors and the uncertain etiology of autistic spectrum disorder. These profiles are all characterized by atypical neural development and altered cerebral connectivity, including an excess of local connectivity neurons, unmodulated processing, and limited coordination. Clinicians have long recognized that autism often coexists with other challenges. Over 95% of individuals diagnosed with autistic spectrum disorder meet criteria for at least one other disorder, and many have multiple co-­occurring symptoms that span both somatic and psychiatric domains (Lord et al., 2020). The complexity of autism significantly affects outcomes for children with average intelligence or intellectual disability. Given symptom overlap and atypical symptom presentation, the assessment, diagnosis, and treatment of conditions co-­occurring with autism is challenging. Many children with autism also encounter difficulties in accessing medical care (Fuentes, Hervás, & Howlin, 2021). About 31% of individuals on the autistic spectrum show intellectual disability (tested IQ of less than 70), with 25% falling within the borderline range (IQ of 71–85), and 44% with scores in the average to above-­average range (IQ higher than 85) (Wolff, Stroth, Kamp-­B ecker, Roepke, & Roesser, 2022). At least 96% of individuals diagnosed with autism report hypersensitivities and hyposensitivities across multiple domains (Lefebvre et al., 2023). Sensory behavioral differences vary from mild to severe and may persist into adulthood. Individuals on the autistic spectrum are more prone than their neurotypical peers to coexisting internalizing and externalizing disorders, such as depression, anxiety, oppositional defiant disorder, and bipolar disorder. Anxiety and depression are particularly prevalent, or at least more observable, in verbally fluent individuals (Gordon-­ Lipkin, Marvin, Law, & Lipkin, 2018; Kirsch et al., 2020). In conditions such as attention-­deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), in contrast, anxiety disorders (generalized anxiety, social anxiety, separation anxiety, and phobias), obsessive–­compulsive disorder, irritability, and disruptive behaviors become more apparent during the school years. Childhood (ages 6–9) is marked by higher rates of internalizing disorders, while ADHD and oppositional and conduct disorders increase during preadolescence (ages 9–12 years) (Nasca, Lopata, Donnelly, Rodgers, & Thomeer, 2020). The comorbidity with sleep, eating, conduct disorders, and, in fewer cases, psychotic disorders may be linked to core symptoms such as deficits in sensory processing, cognitive flexibility, or other medical–­genetic conditions. Irritability and aggression are more common in children on the autistic spectrum (25%) than in those with other developmental disorders (e.g., idiopathic intellectual disability); nearly 28% of 8-year-olds with autism engage in self-­injurious behaviors (Simonoff et al., 2008).

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 263
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 263 Average age of identification of the disorder remains at 4–5 years old (Lord et al., 2020). Despite advances in early detection of autism, most diagnoses are confirmed in late childhood. Assessment should involve a multidisciplinary approach using input from multiple info...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 263 Average age of identification of the disorder remains at 4–5 years old (Lord et al., 2020). Despite advances in early detection of autism, most diagnoses are confirmed in late childhood. Assessment should involve a multidisciplinary approach using input from multiple informants (e.g., caregivers, schoolteachers, siblings). Early detection is crucial for effective intervention (Fuentes et al., 2021; Lord, Elsabbagh, Baird, & Veenstra-­ Vanderweele, 2018; Lord et al., 2020). Clinicians working with parents of children on the spectrum should try to facilitate their acceptance of the diagnosis. They should also encourage insight that fosters positive and reciprocal nonverbal relational experiences and enhances responsive and attuned communications between caregiver and child. This approach can help parents avoid the sense of navigating an incomprehensible and “unmentalized ASD world” (Slade, 2009). The objective is to recognize the intimate connection between dyadic function and specific parenting abilities in shaping the psychological structure and self-­regulatory abilities of the child with autism. DSM-5 (confirmed in DSM-5-TR and ICD-11) has instigated changes in classification and diagnostic criteria. They include the introduction of autism as a behaviorally defined neurodevelopmental disorder, encompassing a single spectrum based on two domains: social communication, and restricted, repetitive, or unusual sensorimotor behaviors. Currently, children on the spectrum with preserved cognitive function and language are categorized as having high-­functioning autism rather than the prior diagnosis of Asperger’s syndrome, although this differentiation remains somewhat controversial. There is a current preference for describing children with autism by their varying levels of need for support in different environments. This approach takes into account not only the severity of their symptoms but also how those symptoms may affect their independence and functioning. It underscores the child’s reliance on adult support systems and the development of “agency,” defined as the ability to regulate emotions and actions. A sense of agency is crucial for mentalization functions; it is influenced by factors such as IQ, age, and possibly medical, genetic, or environmental factors. Drawing from new psychoanalytic, developmental, and neurobiological research, three distinct autistic profiles have been outlined, depicting a spectrum of severities with or without intellectual disability. These align with the need to evaluate the presence or absence of intellectual disability and language difficulties, as suggested in DSM-5-TR. The neuropsychological abilities of individuals with autism vary significantly, running from disability to exceptional talent, with each person possessing distinct strengths and weaknesses. Autistic experience is characterized by specific alterations in cognitive processes, affecting attention, memory, executive functions, language, and social cognition. Studies suggest the development of sensorimotor intentionality in ASD, which correlates with memory and planning abilities (Trevarthen & Delafield-­ Butt, 2013). Deficits in social cognition are closely tied to joint attention and can influence language acquisition and spatial information processing, potentially disrupting social cognition (Mundy, Sullivan, & Mastergeorge, 2009; Sano et al., 2021; Vivanti, Fanning, Hocking, Sievers, & Dissanayake, 2017). Children with autism diagnoses face several challenges. First, they must focus on understanding their own internal world to establish self-­coherence and develop their identity and sense of agency. Second, they need to work on emotion regulation, which is crucial for improving cognitive flexibility and nurturing symbolic and expressive communication skills. Enhanced abilities in embodied narrative facilitate the creation

chunks.heading: 264 Childhood
chunks.preview: 264 Childhood of shared meaning through autobiographical memory organization, while mentalization skills enable children to learn strategies for better social communication and more satisfying interactions in diverse contexts. The third challenge, particularly for children ages 8–11, involves the de...
chunks.text: 264 Childhood of shared meaning through autobiographical memory organization, while mentalization skills enable children to learn strategies for better social communication and more satisfying interactions in diverse contexts. The third challenge, particularly for children ages 8–11, involves the development of intimacy. Children with autism must navigate physical changes and psychosocial demands essential for their development, while dealing with the emergence of new interests during puberty. They have a harder time with intimacy than their neurotypical peers do, as they may find it difficult to cope with new sexual feelings and interests. Navigating “intimacy”—defined as the sharing of emotional, cognitive, and physical aspects of oneself with another—­often brings challenges related to their rigidity and need for repetition. Their sensitivity to physical contact and difficulty tolerating the internal sensations it creates can cause great anxiety. The inability of the child with autism to interpret the thoughts, feelings, or expressed sensations of a partner can result in miscommunication, emotionally or physically painful experiences, and feelings of shame and guilt. SC1.1 Children with Severe Autism with/without Intellectual Disability Children with severe autism and/or intellectual disability (IQ <70) typically behave with dependency, limited attentional capacity, and minimal engagement in reciprocal interactions. They often appear absorbed in their own world. They may experience regression in skills and encounter a higher prevalence of associated neurological difficulties. Language deficits represent a significant challenge, ranging from complete absence of verbal communication to limited verbal and nonverbal language skills, along with poor recognition of others, leading to apparent unresponsiveness. Expressive language difficulties hinder their ability to communicate needs and engage in shared narratives with others. Impaired autonomy and adaptive functioning are common, accompanied by deficiencies in self-care. Because of their limited social and language abilities, they tend to engage more with parents or adults than with peers. The presence of numerous associated neurological or medical issues further impedes their progress, which is already notably slow. Consequently, the long-term prognosis for these children tends to be unfavorable. Subjective Experience of Severe Autism with/without Intellectual Disability in Childhood Affective States The poor language skills of children with autism affect their ability to express emotions; they may show flat affect and limited understanding of basic feelings. And yet they may also have more complex emotions such as guilt or shame. Emotional dysregulation is common, often linked to difficulties in controlling sensory–­perceptual inputs. Managing aggression and rage can be difficult, leading to episodes of selfharm or aggression toward others. Under stress, they may increase their repetitive and stereotyped movements, in an effort to communicate feeling or regulate it through kinesthetic means such as balancing. They may struggle with object permanence or experience anxiety when parts of themselves feel lost. Insecurity and vulnerability in academic and social skills are common. Internalizing symptoms such as depression or anxiety can arise as direct

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 265
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 265 consequences of these challenges, with intensity influenced by the response of the caregiving system. Their self-­esteem and regulation may improve when they are grounded in a safe and predictable environment with supportive relationships. Thoughts and Fantasies For these...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 265 consequences of these challenges, with intensity influenced by the response of the caregiving system. Their self-­esteem and regulation may improve when they are grounded in a safe and predictable environment with supportive relationships. Thoughts and Fantasies For these children, all learning processes are compromised: Attention and engagement skills are diminished, and they are drawn toward unusual aspects of their environment that are related to their sensory interests. They often continue to explore using their mouths. Imitation becomes a crucial strategy for skills improvement. They have few strategies for resilience and show extreme rigidity when faced with changes in routines, often being strongly tied to affective states or behavioral stereotypes. Although they be able to use ideas or repeat words and short phrases, their repetition tends to be more rote than meaningful, and they often have a greater capacity for receptive language than for expressive language. They show high cognitive rigidity and low flexibility; they often lack imaginative creativity and rely heavily on repetition, in proportion to their degree of expressive language impairment. Because of their poor symbolization, they struggle with distinguishing between reality and fantasy, and their ability to organize their thoughts may be unreliable. Consequently, their capacity for self-­reflection is often limited. Their capacity to recognize basic feelings and express intentions often relies on interpretation by caregivers. Somatic States These children have poor expression and understanding of gestures. They often struggle to differentiate sensory–­perceptual inputs; they may express affective and mental states instead through bodily movements, which can serve as signals of internal states and may have a function of kinesthetic self-­regulation. Stereotyped or repetitive motor movements, as well as the repetitive use of objects or speech (e.g., simple motor stereotyping, lining up toys, or turning objects), may persist for an extended period. Relational Patterns They may have poor interpersonal skills, hindered by their repetitive, stereotyped, and uncreative play. They often prefer one-on-one relationships, typically with adults rather than peers. Children with severe autism often show limited curiosity about their environment and may use toys inappropriately, because they do not understand symbolic play. They typically show minimal interest in drawing and tend to persist in their interests, often favoring physical play despite their potential clumsiness. They may develop greater adaptive skills and autonomy in a predictable environment. Moral development may be evident in feelings of shame and guilt, but they may struggle to internalize abstract parental values. Parents may suffer conflicting emotions and stress in response to the complexity of a child’s psychopathology and comorbidities. Their stress level can vary based on their socioeconomic status, family support networks, and other factors that affect their ability to accept their child’s diagnosis. Unresolved negative feelings toward the child increase the likelihood of insecure or ambivalent attachment.

chunks.heading: 266 Childhood
chunks.preview: 266 Childhood SC1.2 Children with Intermediate Severity Autism with/without Borderline Intellectual Functioning Children with IQs ranging from 71 to 85 typically focus on solidifying their identity, differentiating themselves from others, and engaging in social reciprocity, often with more spontaneo...
chunks.text: 266 Childhood SC1.2 Children with Intermediate Severity Autism with/without Borderline Intellectual Functioning Children with IQs ranging from 71 to 85 typically focus on solidifying their identity, differentiating themselves from others, and engaging in social reciprocity, often with more spontaneous peer interactions. Between ages 4 and 6, they may struggle to use gaze, express themselves, or communicate. They may not engage in gestures of affection and rarely interact with others. Any disruption to their daily routine is poorly tolerated and can lead to rage and frustration. They often develop unusual attachments to routines without significant meaning or function, and they may persistently fixate on objects lacking affective value. Maintaining eye contact may be challenging, and they may not respond to smiling or other facial expressions from parents. They may not look at objects or events that parents point to, or point to objects or events themselves. Their joint attention tends to be directed more toward nonsocial stimuli—­visual sensory stimuli such as spinning objects or repetitive movements such as somersaulting. During this period, some motor signs may be exacerbated, including clumsiness in walking and repetitive hand movements resembling flapping. Language and communication skills can vary, with some children showing impairments typical of autism, such as neologisms, stereotyped sentences, echolalia, or difficulties with phonetics, syntax, and pragmatics. Their linguistic abilities lag behind those of their peers, and they may struggle to understand pronouns and the meaning of words, often using combinations of words without coherent meaning. Between ages 6 and 8, their language delay becomes more apparent as they struggle to maintain reciprocal conversations. Children with autism and borderline intellectual capacity may also struggle to understand gestures and abstract concepts, rarely asking questions. With progressive acquisition of routines, they can become more autonomous. They focus on recognizing their own and others’ emotions to regulate them, developing intersubjective and mentalizing skills, empathy, and basic social skills. They may have the ability to modulate, discriminate, and integrate sensory information from their bodies and their environment. Subjective Experience of Children with Intermediate Severity Autism with/without Borderline Intellectual Functioning Affective States Children on the autistic spectrum struggle with recognizing and regulating basic emotions. While this development is age-­dependent, they may gradually integrate and understand even complex emotions such as shame and guilt, and may even acquire empathic abilities. They have trouble tolerating any disruption to their daily routine, however, and so are frequently frustrated. Because of their difficulties reading social contexts, they may suffer emotional dysregulation in peer relationships, often erupting in anger and aggression. Such behaviors can be moderated by adults. They are notably prone to oppositional behavior problems and may experience negative affective states and internalizing symptoms. Affective regulation often relies on sensory processes, such as hypersensitivity to noise or light in environments (e.g., classrooms and public areas). Repetitive and stereotyped movements, such as hand flapping or jumping, may serve as a form of emotional regulation and expression.

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 267
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 267 Thoughts and Fantasies Learning processes proceed slowly and are often aided by visual tools to facilitate access to symbolism. Linguistic development may be delayed and may involve specific alterations. These children frequently experience attention difficulties, often r...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 267 Thoughts and Fantasies Learning processes proceed slowly and are often aided by visual tools to facilitate access to symbolism. Linguistic development may be delayed and may involve specific alterations. These children frequently experience attention difficulties, often requiring longer breaks and assistance from adults both at home and in school. Over time, through behavioral adaptations, they may gradually acquire autonomy in daily routines. Cognitive rigidity can decrease over time, particularly with adult interest and involvement, thereby fostering resilience. Transitions between activities require extra time and should be anticipated and predicted to minimize disruption. Children with autism may develop a strong sense of self, dependent on sensory processing and integration, and are capable of distinguishing themselves from others. Their ability for self-­observation remains mediated by caregivers, however, even though they may engage appropriately in simple activities. Their sense of identity is fragile, with slow and limited autobiographical skills. Cognitive inflexibility can be gradually influenced via their interests and motivations, if understood by caregivers and others. They may access symbolism through more embodied learning processes and engage in symbolic and even creative play within their restricted interests. Play often involves partial and nonfunctional use of objects, in variable sequences. They may develop only minimal capacity to express desires and ideas, and may continue to struggle with second-­order beliefs related to their cognitive patterns. They may continue to encounter difficulties differentiating fantasy from reality and, under stress, may withdraw into their own world. These children often seem to be in prementalizing states of mind, such as pretend mode and psychic equivalence. Somatic States Their learning primarily occurs through their bodies, and repetitive behaviors may persist for extended periods. Various motor signs, both gross and fine, may still be evident; they may show clumsiness, frequently walking on tiptoe and engaging in repetitive, often flapping hand movements. Problems with coordination and with distinguishing proprioceptive sensations often impair their ability to perform activities. They may be hyperactive. They often develop an unusual attachment to routines that lack significant meaning or function, and to objects that lack emotional value. Their difficulties in sensory integration and mutual attention lead to interpersonal challenges. Communication of their inner states, including emotional and purely physical aspects, is challenging to them. Somatic complaints are common, and they are prone to sleep and eating disorders, as well as to related medical conditions such as bronchitis, gastroenteritis, atopic skin conditions, and allergies. Relational Patterns Relationships are influenced by the sociocommunicative difficulties of these children. They may develop an interest in peers, often preferring gender-­related activities within their restricted interests. They may struggle to regulate their strength, leading to clumsiness and rough behavior. They tend to feel isolated and to select their “best friend” carefully. Their ability to engage in prolonged interactions is facilitated by a strong sense of agency, and they typically avoid competitive behavior.

chunks.heading: 268 Childhood
chunks.preview: 268 Childhood Parental support plays a crucial role in their relational development, but parents may experience distress, seem anxiously or avoidantly attached, and lack mentalization skills. They may therefore require consistent empathic support, with easy access to services that promote secure att...
chunks.text: 268 Childhood Parental support plays a crucial role in their relational development, but parents may experience distress, seem anxiously or avoidantly attached, and lack mentalization skills. They may therefore require consistent empathic support, with easy access to services that promote secure attachment and help parents understand their children throughout their lives. All co-­occurring conditions, not just core symptoms, should be carefully considered. SC1.3 Children with Low-Severity Autism with/without High Cognitive Function Children with low-severity autism who function well—a group that includes those previously diagnosed with Asperger syndrome—­typically have an IQ exceeding 85. Although their cognitive abilities are well preserved and often high, their neuropsychological profile shows autistic patterns. Their symptoms are more modulated, and gender differences more pronounced, than children at the lower end of the autistic spectrum. They are often seen as “strange” or “eccentric.” These children frequently experience attentional difficulties related to a focus on details, which can affect their interactions in the areas of reciprocity and learning. They may have difficulty understanding the feelings or intentions of others. They tend to use formal and technical language regardless of context, struggling to use terms they understand verbatim. Pragmatic language alterations are evident in variations of voice volume and tone. Despite having fairly good communication skills, they may behave with hyperactivity and/or emotional dysregulation in specific situations. Children between ages 4 and 6 may, with adult assistance, develop adaptive skills and daily routines, and gradually become more autonomous. Children at this age often have behavioral problems and ADHD, and require support during their transition to school with specific curricula and adaptations. Between ages 8 and 11, they may become more aware of their differences from peers, making them vulnerable to internalizing symptoms. Subjective Experience of Children with Low‐Severity Autism and High‐Functioning Autism Affective States These children may show disturbances in emotional regulation in response to both external and internal stimuli. Hypersensitivity to noise, light, or unexpected change may cause them extreme discomfort. Their responses may include disconnection from reality or feelings of distress. As they struggle to recognize their own emotional states and to read those of others, their cognitive and language abilities may expedite their development of empathy, a process that requires continuous effort. They often report a sense of disorientation or isolation, and they may feel intense sadness and anxiety as a result of feeling misunderstood. Emotional dysregulation is common, and they may have trouble controlling anger. Thoughts and Fantasies These children have intact high intelligence; their verbal IQ is often higher than their performance IQ. Such a disparity resembles that of some individuals with nonverbal learning difficulties. Their executive functions may be impaired. They may have a

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 269
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 269 strong interest in details, leading to repetitive, inflexible, and persistent behaviors. This attention to detail can be advantageous in certain academic subjects, such as mathematics, in which they may possess extensive knowledge about their restricted interests. Communi...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 269 strong interest in details, leading to repetitive, inflexible, and persistent behaviors. This attention to detail can be advantageous in certain academic subjects, such as mathematics, in which they may possess extensive knowledge about their restricted interests. Communication difficulties are more specific, involving prosody, tone, and missing subtleties when compared to neurotypical agemates. They may struggle with conversation patterns, including topic management, gaze management, intonation, and reciprocity. In the area of identity, their functioning may be lower than that of their peers, with a greater susceptibility to confusion between fantasy and reality, and difficulties in autobiographical skills that hinder their self-­observation abilities. They may adhere inflexibly to routines or ritualized patterns of verbal and nonverbal behaviors, and experience extreme distress in response to small changes, transitions, or challenges to their rigid thinking patterns. High-­functioning children with autism often struggle with false beliefs and the expression of wishes and intentions. They find it challenging to comprehend complex mental states and feel more at ease with less ambiguous, socially, or emotionally charged content. While they can attribute characteristics to themselves or others based on their thoughts and fantasies, their attributions may not always align with external reality. This tendency can contribute to a fragmented sense of self and others, and to difficulties distinguishing reality from fantasy. Responsive and attentive caregiving can help with this vulnerability. Their play tends to be more symbolic and shared within their interests but remains rigid. Somatic States These children are often hyper- or hyporeactive to sensory stimuli, and may have unusual reactions to them (e.g., apparent indifference to pain or temperature, or adverse responses to specific sounds or textures). With age, they may improve in self-­ understanding and self-­regulation. Their restricted and repetitive patterns of behavior, interests, or activities, including motor movements, use of objects, or speech (e.g., simple motor stereotypes, lining up toys, or flipping objects) may affect their development. They often show hyperactive behavior and fine motor deficits. With external assistance, they can recognize internal feelings and perception inputs such as hunger or pain. Relational Patterns These children are often seen as “odd” or “quirky.” While they may possess a sense of agency and purpose, their development remains challenging. They struggle to maintain and understand relationships, finding it difficult to adapt their behavior to various social contexts or share fantasy games and make friends. They have deficits in socioaffective reciprocity such as abnormal social approaches, failure in normal long-­distance conversation, and reduced sharing of interests and feelings. They may have friends and relate well to adults but still miss many social cues, including nonverbal signals essential to reciprocal relating. Continuity ASD seems to have significant homotypic continuity, but further investigation—­ research that transitions from a depiction of illness to a focus on development—­is

chunks.heading: 270 Childhood
chunks.preview: 270 Childhood needed to understand its trajectories better. Recognizing the diverse clinical and nonclinical phenotypes within individuals diagnosable with autism contributes to a holistic understanding of the condition as part of the human experience. There has recently been a notable shift away fr...
chunks.text: 270 Childhood needed to understand its trajectories better. Recognizing the diverse clinical and nonclinical phenotypes within individuals diagnosable with autism contributes to a holistic understanding of the condition as part of the human experience. There has recently been a notable shift away from mere symptom reduction and toward prioritizing the overall well-being of children with autism. Studies on the quality of life of adults on the spectrum demonstrate the value of considering both objective and subjective measures when assessing outcomes (Lord et al., 2020). Such studies have helped us to recognize autism earlier and to tailor interventions accordingly. They have shown that social integration difficulties persist into adolescence and adulthood, influencing economic attainment, vocational training, and mental health. Positive experiences in midchildhood, such as friendships and peer engagement, correlate with better adult outcomes, including both symptom reduction and overall independence. Follow-­up studies suggest a decrease in symptom severity over time, with many individuals showing improved social and communication skills. Language development and average nonverbal IQ emerge as strong predictors of positive outcomes. But even individuals who no longer meet diagnostic criteria may still have subtle some difficulties (Fuentes et al., 2021). Outcomes vary across domains such as socialization, communication, intellectual functioning, and adaptive social skills (Fenton et al., 2003; Kanne et al., 2011; Szatmari et al., 2015; Tomanik et al., 2007). Recent studies underscore the heterogeneous nature of developmental pathways in autism, suggesting the possibility of a “post-­autism” psychopathology, a topic that warrants further investigation (Di Renzo et al., 2020). Therapeutic Indications Childhood is a crucial period for addressing autism. Assessment requires the involvement of children, their families, and the community. Sequenced interventions tailored to developmental stage, strengths, needs, and comorbid conditions are necessary for each individual. Attention should be paid to sociocultural barriers that influence outcomes. The heterogeneity and variability within this population justify individualized approaches and underscore the poverty of any one-size-fits-all treatment approach. Most interventions for this population focus on behavior management and modification, with applied behavior analysis having the most basis in empirical evidence. Recently, however, there has been a shift toward more naturalistic, child-led treatments such as the Early Start Denver Model. General guidelines emphasize the importance of reliable assessments, functional behavioral analysis, strengths-­based approaches, collaboration with families and educators, autism-­friendly environments, respect for individual rights, and empirically supported interventions (Fuentes et al., 2021; Lord et al., 2022). Childhood developmental treatments target various areas such as executive functions, social cognition, peer interaction, communication, motivation, language, and reading. They also address anxiety, stress, depression, ADHD, and sleep issues. Interventions across clinics, schools, and families aim to mitigate negative impacts on developmental trajectories, reduce the risk of bullying and co-­occurring conditions, and improve quality of life. Family involvement is essential, particularly in the early years, although it may decrease during school years and vary in adulthood. Therapeutic and educational approaches focus on enhancing brain plasticity by creating networks, forming new connections, and fostering growing connectivity (Fuentes et al., 2021; Hyman, Levy, Myers, & Council on Children with Disabilities, 2020).

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 271
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 271 Specific psychodynamic and developmental interventions, such as the Joint Attention Symbolic Play Engagement and Regulation program (JASPER) and the Preschool Autism Communication Trial (PACT), have shown efficacy in enhancing social communication skills. Other models, in...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 271 Specific psychodynamic and developmental interventions, such as the Joint Attention Symbolic Play Engagement and Regulation program (JASPER) and the Preschool Autism Communication Trial (PACT), have shown efficacy in enhancing social communication skills. Other models, including mentalization-­based treatment for children (MBT-C), exchange and development therapy (EDT), developmental, emotional regulation, and body-based intervention (DERBBI), and sensorimotor attunement in therapy, offer additional ways to support socialization and communication skills. The challenge of providing psychosocial and group interventions for children on the autistic spectrum is significant. There are more manualized programs available for infants and toddlers, and for adolescents than for children ages 5–12. Interventions for younger patients tend to be individualized, while those for high-­functioning autistic children over age 12 often involve group-based approaches. Clinical Illustration Cristina, a 9-year-old girl, was described as struggling with peer interaction. She showed reluctance to engage, though she tolerated play and occasional sharing. There were no reported feeding issues, and she slept with two dolls over which she exerted considerable control. With adult support, she managed transitions adequately. She denied having rituals, and her interests mainly revolved around animals and dolls. Although she remained connected to her environment, she was responsive to her name, was cooperative, and her eye contact was inconsistent. Cristina spoke fluently, responded coherently to questions, and was able to participate creatively in storytelling and drawing. She shared her interests with her parents, although her mother’s responses appeared emotionally detached. Despite signs of restlessness and attention difficulties, Cristina did not exhibit oppositional behavior, motor stereotypes, or emotional dysregulation. She engaged without difficulty in doll play, drawing, and interviews. Her intellectual functioning was in the low-­average range, with an IQ score of 80 and a verbal comprehension score of 85. Her verbal concept formation, reasoning, and expressive language skills were notably limited, though her inductive and abstract reasoning were stronger than her quantitative reasoning and lexical knowledge. Despite the absence of hyperactivity and emotional dysregulation, Cristina and her parents were deemed suitable for weekly group therapy. Initially, she required adult guidance to manage her social anxiety but gradually improved her social interaction skills, reducing her dependence on such support. She continued to struggle with prosody and communication nuances, but these areas improved over time with parallel parental work. At school, Cristina required help to maintain focus. She often escaped into fantasy but showed interest in physical activities and collaborative tasks. Educational interventions focused on fostering independent learning and gradually reducing the level of support. Although family members were initially hesitant to acknowledge Cristina’s developmental challenges, they eventually recognized her progress across multiple areas in response to the emphasis on functional enhancement and individualized support. SC2 Attention‐Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorders DSM-5-TR offers three criteria for a diagnosis of ADHD: inattention, impulsivity, and hyperactivity. Depending on how these features are manifested clinically, the diagnosis

chunks.heading: 272 Childhood
chunks.preview: 272 Childhood can fall into three subtypes: combined presentation, predominantly inattentive presentation, and predominantly hyperactive–­impulsive presentation. Among the three subtypes of ADHD, the predominantly hyperactive–­impulsive subtype is associated with a level of activity higher than that...
chunks.text: 272 Childhood can fall into three subtypes: combined presentation, predominantly inattentive presentation, and predominantly hyperactive–­impulsive presentation. Among the three subtypes of ADHD, the predominantly hyperactive–­impulsive subtype is associated with a level of activity higher than that of a child’s peers and is typically diagnosed early. The predominantly inattentive subtype is likely to be diagnosed after the early grades of elementary school when academic assignments become more demanding and a child’s effort does not always lead to adequate performance. The combined subtype recognizes both inattentiveness and impulsivity. It is likely to be more noticeable with the onset of the school years, when difficulties with sustaining attention, completing tasks, and not interrupting others become more noticeable. ADHD is not a uniform diagnostic entity. It includes considerable individual and subgroup variations whose diverse etiologies range from neurological abnormalities to social–­environmental insults. Prenatal risk factors include ADHD in a parent or sibling, conditions that cause premature birth or low birthweight, parental use of drugs or alcohol, and exposure to environmental toxins (Martin, Pick, & Hay, 2006). Postnatal risk factors include chronic adverse experiences (complex trauma), emotional conflict, neglect, departure from a culturally informed judgment of appropriate childhood behaviors, mourning, depression, and growing up with a severely depressed or absent caregiver (Conway, Oster, & Szymanski, 2011). The expression of ADHD varies, depending on context and setting. Symptoms may be minimal or absent when a child is in a novel setting, or under close supervision, or engaged in highly absorbing activities such as electronic games. They may increase when the child is in a large-group situation and lacks interest in the group’s activity. When the child is required to focus on academic instruction, it is not unusual to see an increase in impulsivity, hyperactivity, and inattentiveness. The impact of attention deficits and hyperactivity on the child’s life, whatever the specifics of the individual picture, includes functional and relational impairments. Children with ADHD have deficient self-­regulation and organization skills, leading to poor academic performance and conflictual social relationships. Intellectual deficits, school-­related problems, and peer neglect tend to be more closely associated with elevated symptoms of inattention and impulsivity. Peer rejection and, to a lesser extent, accidental injury are more closely associated with hyperactivity. On average, individuals with ADHD obtain less schooling and have poorer vocational achievement than their peers. In its more severe form, ADHD can markedly impair social, familial, educational, and/or occupational adjustment. Regardless of the etiology, ADHD adversely impacts psychological experiences with the self and others. Core difficulties include problems such as perspective taking, reality testing, impulse control, and self-­regulation. The child’s divided attention contributes to difficulty carrying the emotional cargo needed to manage the insults that children encounter in their relational world and interferes with their developmental achievements. Subjective Experience of Children with ADHD Affective States The inner lives of children with attention deficits and hyperactivity tend to be inextricable from their ADHD symptoms and behavioral problems. Consequently, their affective states vary widely, depending on subtype and context. When impulsivity

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 273
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 273 and agitated mood combine, they may appear overactive and on the brink of losing control. The difficulty they have in relying on secondary-­process functions to organize their thoughts and make sense of what is happening can leave them unable to contain intense emotions a...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 273 and agitated mood combine, they may appear overactive and on the brink of losing control. The difficulty they have in relying on secondary-­process functions to organize their thoughts and make sense of what is happening can leave them unable to contain intense emotions and hence more prone to act out. Their inconsistent, inadequate application to tasks that require sustained effort often masks an underlying anxiety over task demands. Others often interpret their attitude as laziness, irresponsibility, and failure to cooperate. Internalized negative perceptions of themselves contribute to their seeming lack of motivation and even to their impulsive reactions as they find it hard to contain wishes and intense affects. Thoughts and Fantasies The thoughts of children with ADHD are focused mainly on overcoming or avoiding experiences of failure, and on the consequences of their profound lack of competence. They may have compensatory fantasies of omnipotence or be preoccupied with themes of punitive aggression. When they attempt to express thoughts about others, their ideas are often egocentric; taking others’ perspectives can be difficult for them. Children who are diagnosable with ADHD may have trouble understanding their own thoughts and feelings, particularly in social interactions. As a result, their expressed ideas can appear disorganized. Their modes of engagement may reflect states of mind that are disorganized and overexcited. They often can sustain attention if an object or activity is sufficiently stimulating, but in general, inattention shields them from painful or confusing thoughts and feelings. Their impulsive and hyperactive behaviors—­moving rapidly from topic to topic or activity to activity with no apparent connective thread—­may function to soothe themselves or avoid difficult emotions. Their sudden shifts or random statements may seem triggered by excitement or impulsivity that belies an underlying anxiety or agitation that they cannot contain, reflect on, or express in words. Somatic States Somatic states may include feelings such as “I have to move” or “My muscles are exploding.” Children with ADHD may sense that the body has its own imperatives, in the face of which they are helpless (“My legs just do what they need to”). They frequently recruit physical movement to manage and regulate emotions. Socially and interpersonally, they often have trouble understanding appropriate space and physical boundaries, with the result that they violate personal space expectations. Relational Patterns A necessary foundational element of relationships is the capacity to interpret one’s own and other’s actions implicitly and explicitly in meaningful ways. In children with attention deficits and hyperactivity, this capacity to take the perspective of others and reflect on one’s own behavior is underdeveloped. As a result, their ability to regulate emotions that arise naturally and expectably during relational conflicts breaks down. Others are not always aware that their ensuing behavioral and emotional responses arise from internal emotional states. In response to a child with ADHD, even adults

chunks.heading: 274 Childhood
chunks.preview: 274 Childhood are likely to experience intense reactions and often fail to consider what internal experiences a child’s behavior might be communicating. The modes of engagement of these children are inconsistent, making sustained interactions with others difficult. Their problems in reading social c...
chunks.text: 274 Childhood are likely to experience intense reactions and often fail to consider what internal experiences a child’s behavior might be communicating. The modes of engagement of these children are inconsistent, making sustained interactions with others difficult. Their problems in reading social cues and organizing a fitting response provoke negative responses at home and at school. Their interactions often include peer rejection, neglect, and teasing. Their impulsive, restless demeanor and apparent lack of consideration of others may cause family discord. Aggravating the problem, their difficulty using verbal symbols to communicate internal state may exacerbate conflicts. The fragility of their emotional regulation capabilities, along with their harshly critical internalizations, lead to dysregulated, disruptive, and disorganized interpersonal experiences. Continuity Diagnosed ADHD has a heterogeneous trajectory with varying levels of inattention and hyperactivity–­impulsivity across the life cycle. Core symptoms during childhood do not sufficiently predict their persistence during adolescence or even adulthood. For example, while hyperactivity and impulsivity may be present during the early years, peak around middle school, and then decline, impulsivity may continue throughout the child’s life. The degree to which ADHD symptoms persist, decline, or escalate hinges on several factors, including aggression, emotion dysregulation, social isolation, life stressors, and disciplinary practices. Children with high levels of ADHD symptoms during late adolescence are more likely than others to engage in antisocial behaviors, resulting in arrests, school dropouts, and lower participation in the workforce. Therapeutic Indications Medication (stimulants) may be helpful to children with ADHD symptoms but are not the sole or primary treatment. Functional abilities that are compromised warrant clinical attention. Children with attentional and hyperactivity problems have difficulty with the developmental tasks that lead to success at school and in a career. A child’s ability to pay attention is a critical component of learning, with inevitable emotional repercussions, and interpersonal relationships are marred by inattention and impulsivity. Children with ADHD experience poor relationships with peers, parents, and teachers, and may have difficulties even late in life with authority figures. Their sibling relationships may be strained, and they may have difficulty relating to peers or maintaining friendships. Impulsive and hyperactive behaviors that disrupt their class in school may provoke strained relationships with teachers. Interactions with children who have ADHD symptoms can range from endearing to strained and conflictual. Some parents and teachers perceive a boy with ADHD as intellectually gifted, curious, and charming, while others see him as oppositional and defiant, intentionally ignoring instructions, lying, lazy, or lacking motivation.
