On their side, children with ADHD may experience others as impatient and unfair. They may doubt their sincerity and hence respond angrily. Children with attentional problems and hyperactivity are often punished for their behaviors and too often suffer abuse and maltreatment. Clinical intervention involves both child and caregivers. Educating both about ADHD, identifying strategies that increase the child’s ability to pay attention, and improving capacities to organize, plan, and control impulses are worthwhile aims. But

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 275
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 275 given the challenges that their symptoms pose for children’s relational world, a clinical focus on strengthening a child’s relationships is also an important goal. Specifically, given that the capacity to take the view of others is underdeveloped in children with ADHD, he...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 275 given the challenges that their symptoms pose for children’s relational world, a clinical focus on strengthening a child’s relationships is also an important goal. Specifically, given that the capacity to take the view of others is underdeveloped in children with ADHD, helping the child and parents to understand each other’s perspective will increase empathy, reduce conflict, and help parents understand a child’s behavior more empathically (Conway, 2014). “Mentalization,” the capacity to attribute mental states to others, allows both child and caregivers to interpret their own and each other’s actions in meaningful ways. As mentalization develops, the child’s ability to self-­regulate improves. In both children and parents, curiosity and imagination are the key components to building mentalization and reflective capacity. Clinicians should encourage children with ADHD symptoms to be curious about their thoughts (mind in mind) and the thoughts of others (other’s mind in mind) and invite their parents and teachers to be curious about their own thoughts and the thoughts of the child. All parties need support in developing representations of self and others, including but not limited to the experience of ADHD. Clinical Illustration Eduard, a 9-year-old boy whose pediatrician had diagnosed ADHD and prescribed Ritalin, was referred to a therapist by his mother, who reported that he had nightmares, difficulty with separation, and oppositional behaviors, especially around morning and bedtime routines. His emotions alternated between intense reactions and seeming indifference to others. Eduard and his mother had frequent power struggles that required the father’s intervention. Although he seemed content to play by himself, he also seemed to need others to be available and willing to play at the level of his interests. Eduard had always seemed somewhat slow to react and would spend many hours watching cartoons. He was very close to his mother, and although he had many playdates, he never seemed to have a close friend. His language appeared delayed. His school troubles began in first grade and had continued since. His teachers complained that he would not listen, was easily distracted, had problems concentrating, neglected to complete assignments, and failed at tasks requiring sustained attention. Although his distractibility and restlessness subsided after he was prescribed Ritalin, he still had difficulty completing his assignments and seemed more withdrawn and apathetic. Getting him to accept taking the pill each morning was a struggle, and the parents reported prolonged agitation in the evening when the effects of the medication wore off. Completing homework was also a challenge. Eduard would express his frustration in self-­deprecating statements such as “I hate myself” and “I can’t do this.” Eduard expressed confusion about what was happening between him and his mother. He felt generally uncomfortable in the school setting but could not articulate why. He seemed to have little curiosity about his experiences and emotions, and no capacity to link internal states and external demands. He would become fidgety and switch topics or activities, as if what was happening was of no interest to him. He seemed responsive, however, to the therapist’s efforts to point out disjunctive moments in their sessions and to empathize with his difficulty tolerating or understanding what he had experienced. The therapist expressed curiosity about Eduard’s mind and experiences, and engaged him in imagining what his mother, teacher, and friends were thinking and

chunks.heading: 276 Childhood
chunks.preview: 276 Childhood why they behaved as they did. When she was able to join him in creating a narrative that explained how his and others’ inner experiences are connected to behaviors, he felt understood and recognized. The boy’s attitude visibly changed, and he became more engaged in the therapeutic rela...
chunks.text: 276 Childhood why they behaved as they did. When she was able to join him in creating a narrative that explained how his and others’ inner experiences are connected to behaviors, he felt understood and recognized. The boy’s attitude visibly changed, and he became more engaged in the therapeutic relationship. SC3 Neuropsychological Disorders Some neuropsychological issues that cannot be considered psychopathological disorders in their own right have transdiagnostic relevance. One such condition is visual–­ spatial processing disorders, which compromise the use of visual–­spatial information for perception, analysis, synthesis, and problem solving. Children with this problem have difficulties with visual–­spatial analysis, visual–­constructive skills, and attention. When these disorders primarily impair academic skills, such as reading or mathematics, they may prompt an additional diagnosis of a learning disorder. Undiagnosed visual–­spatial processing disorders can create difficulties for children in traditional educational settings. Disorders related to social perception are not part of the diagnosis of problems in visual–­spatial processing. In such cases, it may be appropriate to consider the diagnosis of ASD. Another transdiagnostic issue is memory impairment. The capacity to recall information plays a key role in academic, social, and daily life. When memory issues become distressing, a diagnostic evaluation is advisable. Memory problems accompany many different conditions, including learning disorders, ADHD, brain injury, ASD, cognitive deficits, trauma, and mood symptoms. Each of these has distinct manifestations. These challenges are particularly evident in academic settings, but they also affect social interactions and daily routines. When diagnosing memory deficits, one needs to consider the possibility of other disorders. For example, the impairment of working memory in ADHD may be attributed to difficulties in attention. Executive function deficits are also transdiagnostic. Prefrontal cortex development is rapid in the preschool years, enabling processes such as working memory, inhibitory control, and cognitive flexibility. Parenting has a major impact on the development of these capacities. Executive function is crucial for academic and social success, social interaction, and self-­regulation. Deficits in it are evident in various conditions, including neurological disorders, obsessive–­compulsive disorder, Tourette syndrome, ADHD, ASD, and other neuropsychological conditions. Although children with ADHD frequently have reduced executive functions, not all such difficulties indicate ADHD. Learning disabilities, emotional disturbances, stress, bipolar disorder, traumatic experiences, and neglect have also been linked to impairment of executive function. Transdiagnostic disorders can appear across various mental health conditions, often emerging in childhood as the initial detectable difficulty. Because they are more salient in childhood, it is important to identify and treat them appropriately, as they may precede and potentiate secondary disorders later on. SC3.1 Intellectual Disabilities Intellectual disabilities (former mental retardation, general learning disability) denote the level of cognitive functioning demonstrated by specific children. They are characterized by significant limitations in intellectual functioning and adaptive behavior. The latter is expressed through conceptual, social, and practical adaptive skills. An

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 277
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 277 intellectual disability is defined by an IQ below 70 and by deficits in adaptive behavior or daily living skills, such as eating, dressing, communication, and participation in group activities. The diagnosis can be assigned at any age up to 18 years. Problems with intelle...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 277 intellectual disability is defined by an IQ below 70 and by deficits in adaptive behavior or daily living skills, such as eating, dressing, communication, and participation in group activities. The diagnosis can be assigned at any age up to 18 years. Problems with intellectual or adaptive functioning that appear after this developmental period should be diagnosed as neurocognitive disorder. Intellectual deficits should be assessed by standardized, culturally appropriate IQ tests. A score of approximately two standard deviations below average, which occur in about 2.5% of the population, represents a significant cognitive deficit (Schalock, Luckasson, & Tassé, 2021). Deficits in intellectual functioning include mental abilities such as reasoning, problem solving, planning, abstract thinking, judgment, and academic learning (ability to learn in school via traditional teaching methods). Deficits in adaptive functioning are also measured using standardized, culturally appropriate tests. There are four categories of intellectual disability: (1) mild (IQ: 55–69), (2) moderate (IQ: 54–36), (3) severe (IQ: 20–35), and (4) profound (IQ <20). Apart from IQ tests, a comprehensive assessment often includes interviews with the individual and others (family, teachers, etc.) who have observed a child’s adaptive functioning (i.e., conceptual, emotional, social, and practical functioning). Other additional tests may be needed, such as general medical, neurological, psychological, special education, hearing, speech, and vision tests, and physical therapy evaluations. Approximately 70% of individuals with severe intellectual disability and 50% with mild intellectual disability have an organic or biological basis for their disorder (Shree & Shukla, 2016). Some children’s cognitive deficits may simply reflect the lower end of the normal IQ distribution. In such cases, cognitive functioning represents an interaction of both genetic and environmental factors. Poverty, neglect, abuse, limited stimulation, and poor parent–­child interactions are only some of the factors that have been found to influence intellectual functioning (Shree & Shukla, 2016). Research suggests that multiple or chronic exposure to one or more forms of developmentally adverse interpersonal trauma (abandonment, neglect, physical or sexual assaults, threats to bodily integrity, coercive practices, abuse, witnessing violence and death) can lead to disturbance in the child’s developing capacity to relate to self and others. Clinicians should therefore explore environmental conditions and their psychological effects when a child diagnosed with intellectual disability comes for assessment or treatment. Psychoanalytic theories suggest that, in some cases, intellectual disability emerges as a defense against recalling traumatic memories (i.e., physical or sexual abuse) (­Cottis, 2019). It can also be a defense against the trauma of living with the psychological and emotional pain of another primary pathology, such as autism. Subjective Experience of Intellectual Disabilities in Childhood Affective States Children with intellectual disabilities vary in personality, disposition, and beliefs. They suffer emotional challenges that contribute to academic and social failures. Consequences of these disorders include lowered self-­esteem, pervasive feelings of shame, profound insecurity about academic skills, and a deep sense of vulnerability. Symptoms of depression and anxiety are sometimes direct consequences. Difficulties in acquiring object constancy and undergoing the process of individuation make separation anxiety common among these children.

chunks.heading: 278 Childhood
chunks.preview: 278 Childhood Thoughts and Fantasies Learning, memory, and attention capacities of children with intellectual disabilities are significantly below average in comparison to peers without disabilities. Depending on the severity of the disability and the cognitive deficits, the capacity for organizatio...
chunks.text: 278 Childhood Thoughts and Fantasies Learning, memory, and attention capacities of children with intellectual disabilities are significantly below average in comparison to peers without disabilities. Depending on the severity of the disability and the cognitive deficits, the capacity for organization of thoughts may vary. In many cases, children with intellectual disabilities can recognize and express their feelings. They often function in a psychic equivalent or pretend mode. Children with mild intellectual disabilities who have received adequate training and support become able to organize their thoughts and express their fantasies during symbolic play. The context of their thoughts and fantasies, although limited, is similar to that of their peers. Somatic States As is the case with other health problems, children with intellectual disability may have coexisting conditions such as physical, motor, orthopedic, visual, and auditory impairments. Somatic states may vary depending on the physical and emotional capacities of each child. Self-­regulation capacity may be limited by the lack or underdevelopment of metacognitive processes. Being in control, and able to mediate or regulate one’s own affects and impulses are two tasks that are usually particularly challenging. Relational Patterns In many cases, children with intellectual disabilities are stuck in the early stages of separation–­individuation; that is, differentiation, practicing, and early rapprochement. As a result, they became very dependent on caregivers. Their ability to form relationships varies depending on the severity of their disability. Children with mild learning disabilities have a limited repertoire of social skills but seek to interact and play with peers. Because of their psychosocial limitations and emotional dependency, they usually form dyadic relationships. They typically have a superficial, needs-­oriented capacity for relationship, distant from age-­expected norms. Such limitations usually evoke rejection from their peers. In the best cases, they relate well with younger children. Families of children with disabilities develop a range of reactions from extremely anxious and trauma-­based to positive and life-­affirming. Parental responses to a child with intellectual disability often reflect ambivalence or emotional conflict. These vary with time, the nature of social, emotional, and economic experiences, and parental psychology. Current research suggests that brothers and sisters of children with disabilities have developmental trajectories that are unique in some ways but typical in others. These siblings experience numerous stressors (i.e., negative emotions, sense of neglect from their parents) that shape their emotional development and their relationship with the intellectually disabled child. Continuity Children with intellectual deficits typically have a slower pace of development, often evident from birth. They may reach milestones, such as sitting up, crawling, walking, or talking, later than other children. As toddlers, they may show motor and speech delays, have difficulty understanding concepts such as time or how to use a phone,

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 279
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 279 struggle with social skills, or have difficulty with regular personal care tasks such as eating or getting dressed. Emotional development also progresses at a slower pace. Mastering object permanence and object constancy poses significant challenges for children with inte...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 279 struggle with social skills, or have difficulty with regular personal care tasks such as eating or getting dressed. Emotional development also progresses at a slower pace. Mastering object permanence and object constancy poses significant challenges for children with intellectual disabilities, making it difficult for them to reach the individuation stage of emotional development. In some cases, dependency on others for everyday living persists into adulthood. These challenges typically continue to some degree throughout adolescence and adulthood. Therapeutic Indications Intellectual disability is a lifelong condition. Although there is currently no cure, most children can learn to improve their functioning over time. Receiving early and ongoing interventions can often enhance functioning, thereby allowing them to thrive. Most treatment plans for intellectual disability focus on the person’s strengths, needs, and the support required to function. Many services exist to assist children and their families, including school, home, and community options. Diagnosis may help determine the type of supportive services that the child may need. These include early interventions designed to identify intellectual disability in infants and toddlers; special education; academic support, such as individual education plans, transition services, and vocational programs; psychoeducation or psychotherapy (including individual, group, and family therapy); and parent counseling. Most of these enable individuals with intellectual disability to function normally in society. Clinicians need to be aware of the varieties of intellectual disability, understand their impact on the child’s developing mental capacities, and be able to facilitate insight and awareness. Clinical Illustration Nick, a 10-year-old boy, was referred for psychological and educational reevaluation by his parents. At the ages of 3 and 5, respectively, Nick had been diagnosed with language delays and specific language disorder. However, his developmental history indicated delays of at least 2 years in all normal milestones. Although he had reached an independent level of self-care, he continued to need support in all other areas. His self-­esteem was low, he had trouble following his peers’ play, and he preferred to play with younger children. At times, he was subject to affective dysregulation and aggressive outbursts. The psychological and educational reevaluation, which employed psychological, educational, and adaptive tests, as well as reports from school, indicated the presence of moderate intellectual disability. The suggested therapeutic plan included special education services, participation in small-group play and athletic activities, individual psychotherapy, parental counseling, and family therapy. SC3.2 Motor Skills Disorders Motor skills disorders in children have been variously labeled as “problems with motor planning,” “vestibular disorders,” “developmental apraxia,” “perceptual–­motor dysfunction,” and “coordination disorders,” among several other terms. DSM-5-TR puts “developmental coordination disorder” in the category of “neurodevelopmental disorders,” which may be the best general term. Developmental coordination disorder

chunks.heading: 280 Childhood
chunks.preview: 280 Childhood is a motor development problem defined by inadequate motor performance. It affects individuals’ daily living activities and academic performance, and cannot be explained by intellectual disability or any congenital or acquired neurological condition. In the course of development, child...
chunks.text: 280 Childhood is a motor development problem defined by inadequate motor performance. It affects individuals’ daily living activities and academic performance, and cannot be explained by intellectual disability or any congenital or acquired neurological condition. In the course of development, children with this disorder show diverse motor signs, including fine and gross motor problems, impaired postural control and balance, and difficulties with sensorimotor coordination or motor learning. Developmental coordination disorder is diagnosed more commonly in children; it is more likely to be detected in boys than in girls, at ratios ranging from 2:1 to 7:1. Prevalence ranges from 1.8% to 8% (Biotteau, Albaret, & Chaix, 2020). The multiplicity of terms created to describe gross and fine motor problems in children suggests a lack of specificity in this area, as well as a lack of knowledge about etiology. The main hypotheses that attempt to account for developmental coordination disorder involve deficits in (1) visual–­spatial functions, (2) procedural learning, (3) internal modeling, and (4) executive functions (Ke et al., 2023). In this disorder, different brain areas are affected at the neuroanatomical and neurofunctional levels. Early milestones, such as crawling, walking, feeding, and dressing are delayed. Drawing, writing, and sports performance also usually lag behind what is expected for a child’s age. Signs of the disorder are present early, but because children vary greatly in their speed of maturation, a definitive diagnosis is usually not made until a child is 5 years old. Children diagnosed with this problem are significantly restricted from participation in ordinary activities. Such limitations increase the risk of difficulties with attention, social skills, reading, and spelling. Some children have no comorbid conditions, but most who are diagnosable with developmental coordination disorder have other problems in maturation as well. Clinicians must take into account that there is a large overlap between this difficulty and ADHD, and also with ASD, learning disabilities, and language disorders. Coordination problems affect children’s self-­esteem and motivation, making them easily bullied, resulting in a series of experiences that hinder their normal development. Therapeutic interventions with children with this problem have been found to foster increased functional connectivity in networks associated with the regulation of self, emotion, and attention. The diagnostic process should include a specific assessment for motor skills (gross and fine). This may be done by an occupational therapist, physiotherapist, pediatrician, or neurologist, depending on the child’s country and the services available to the family. Because problems with coordination affect children socially, emotionally, and behaviorally, parents must deal with different health care and educational professionals. Children’s adaptations vary widely and are heavily influenced by familial and cultural values. Subjective Experience of Motor Skills Disorders in Childhood Affective States Children diagnosable with developmental coordination disorder are prone to both internalizing and externalizing symptoms. They may show emotional immaturity, low self-­esteem, and high anxiety, which impair their ability to take pleasure in group play activities. It is challenging for children with this problem to feel confident and masterful. In more severe cases, they may experience significant insecurity of their bodies in space, causing them to bump into objects or fall, and consequently to feel excessively

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 281
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 281 vulnerable to injury and loss. Frustration with their symptoms often creates behavioral problems. Thoughts and Fantasies The cognitive abilities of young children are typically concrete and body-­focused. Even if cognitive abilities and verbal communication skills remain...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 281 vulnerable to injury and loss. Frustration with their symptoms often creates behavioral problems. Thoughts and Fantasies The cognitive abilities of young children are typically concrete and body-­focused. Even if cognitive abilities and verbal communication skills remain unimpaired, the inability to function independently from family members can impair one’s sense of efficacy. Children with this problem often find it hard to concentrate for more than a few minutes. They also have difficulty following instructions and copying information, have poor capacity to organize and complete tasks, and are slow to acquire new skills. To facilitate their learning, these children need encouragement and repetition. In school, they may be better off in a one-to-one situation rather than in a group. Somatic States Developmental coordination disorder is sometimes identified in infants whose sitting, crawling, or walking is delayed but is often not diagnosed until a child enters school and begins to have trouble keeping up with peers. Children with this disorder may have difficulty with activities that require hopping, jumping, running, catching or kicking a ball, climbing and descending stairs, writing, drawing and using scissors. Their writing and drawing may appear scrawled and less developed than those of agemates. They may also have difficulty with dressing, buttoning, tying shoelaces, and standing still. They may swing or move their arms and legs a lot. They may appear clumsy and awkward, bump into objects, drop things, and fall frequently. Reactive symptoms such as generalized pain, dizziness, fatigue, headache, nausea, and stomach pain may accompany this problem. Relational Patterns Children with developmental coordination disorder are often clumsy and awkward in their physical approach to their peers, with resulting problems in relationships. Especially in the early school years, status among peers, especially for boys, may depend on agility, strength, and speed. Children who lack competence in motor skills are at risk of withdrawing from athletic activities and peer interactions; they may avoid taking part in team games and may be bullied for being “different” or clumsy. Avoidance of activities that are hard for them may increase their relative weakness and further undermine their self-­esteem. They may revert to depending on adults, showing an anxious–­insecure pattern of attachment. Parents and teachers can help to foster the gradual achievement of personal autonomy in different tasks. Continuity The vast majority of children with coordination problems require long-term assistance. Without intervention, their motor issues can hinder their growth and development and persist from childhood into adulthood. Although the physical coordination of children with developmental coordination disorder may remain below average, their deficits in this area often become less problematic as they age.

chunks.heading: 282 Childhood
chunks.preview: 282 Childhood Treatment Indications The treatment of developmental coordination disorder needs to be individualized. It typically involves collaboration among multiple health care professionals. Goals include not only enhancing motor skills but also improving overall quality of life. The work often...
chunks.text: 282 Childhood Treatment Indications The treatment of developmental coordination disorder needs to be individualized. It typically involves collaboration among multiple health care professionals. Goals include not only enhancing motor skills but also improving overall quality of life. The work often focuses on the capacity to organize materials and engage in drawing and writing, physical activities that advance the child’s social-­emotional wellness. Improvements are sought in visual–­perceptual and visual–­spatial analysis, visual–­constructive skills, praxis organization, and motor control. Efforts at rehabilitation and reeducation include both “bottom-­up” approaches (e.g., sensory integration therapy, perceptual–­motor training), which focus on sensory and cognitive processes that provide experiences necessary for selective neural networks, and “top-down” approaches, which consolidate connections (goal-­directed cognitive processes) and emphasize the overall movement pattern. Although developmental coordination disorder does not affect a child’s intelligence, it can make learning more challenging. Difficulties in school, particularly in producing written papers, may become more apparent and require extra assistance from parents and teachers. In the short term, interventions targeting specific motor skills can enhance motor competence and performance, with positive psychological consequences. In the long term, treatment plans that include additional support at school can help children manage many of their physical difficulties, improve their overall self-­esteem, and transition into confident adults. Clinical Illustration Jack, a 7-year-old boy, was consistently the last child to leave the classroom when it was time for recess. He struggled with zipping his jacket, had difficulty putting on his boots, and frequently could not locate his hat. Even in summer, when outerwear was unnecessary, he lagged behind because he found it hard to disengage from his current activity and transition to a new one. Once on the playground, he took time to survey the scene before trying to join a group of boys. If they were engaged in group play, he often appeared to “crash the party” by physically squashing himself into the cluster, which irritated the others. They typically responded by jumping up and running away, leaving Jack behind as he attempted to follow but soon fell behind and lost the group. He was unable to master playground equipment such as monkey bars or swings. Although he rarely spoke about these experiences, on one occasion he sadly told his mother that he was the slowest runner in the class and that this was the reason he didn’t have any friends. SC4 Specific Learning Disorders Specific learning disorders involve neurodevelopmental impairment of the ability to learn or use specific academic skills that are foundational for further academic learning, such as reading, writing, and math. The three most common types are as follows: 1. Impairment in reading (dyslexia): includes difficulties in word-­reading accuracy, reading rate and fluency, and reading comprehension. 2. Impairment in written expression (dysorthography and dysgraphia): includes

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 283
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 283 difficulties in spelling, grammar, punctuation, and clarity, and organization of writing; or in motor processes that result in in difficulties and slowness in writing. 3. Impairment in mathematics (dyscalculia): includes difficulties in number sense, memorization of arith...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 283 difficulties in spelling, grammar, punctuation, and clarity, and organization of writing; or in motor processes that result in in difficulties and slowness in writing. 3. Impairment in mathematics (dyscalculia): includes difficulties in number sense, memorization of arithmetic facts, calculation, and math reasoning. Specific learning problems result from cognitive deficits in attention, memorization, and speed of processing knowledge. They arise from disorders in specific neurological, biological, and congenital psychological processes. These are recognizable across cultures. They are chronic conditions that develop in childhood and typically persists in adulthood, albeit with some cultural and developmental differences. A specific learning disorder is diagnosed through individual standardized achievement measures and comprehensive clinical assessment. To make the diagnosis, one must rule out several other disorders, including intellectual disabilities, uncorrected auditory or visual acuity problems, other mental or neurological disorders, and adverse conditions (e.g., psychological trauma, lack of language proficiency, inadequate instruction). Although the IQ–­achievement discrepancy had been the main diagnostic criterion for decades, it was abandoned in DSM-5 (American Psychiatric Association, 2013). Diagnosis typically requires that an individual exhibit core features characteristic of a specific learning disorder, that these features significantly impact functioning, that the difficulties first become evident during this developmental age, and that other potential conditions be ruled out as explanations for these difficulties. Clinicians encounter children with serious learning disabilities that resemble a specific learning disorder but seem to result from emotional issues related to attachment trauma or inadequate caregiving (e.g., lack of parental reflective function, parents who obstruct their child’s self-­differentiation and individuation, negligent or punitive parenting) (Papanastasiou, 2017). Learning is integrally connected to the capacity for epistemic trust. Adverse relational situations can reinforce epistemic hypervigilance and impose threats and/or intense stress on the developing child. Dysfunctional coping mechanisms can result in cognitive depletions that hinder a child’s capacity to learn. It is difficult for a clinician to determine whether such issues coexist with a specific learning disorder, whether they have exacerbated the learning difficulties, or whether they originally activated them. These considerations are central to formulation and planning. Subjective Experience of Specific Learning Disorders in Childhood Affective States Children with learning disorders have reported feelings of embarrassment, anxiety, frustration, anger, guilt, and melancholy. Almost daily, they experience some social isolation that may seriously deplete a school-­age child’s confidence, self-image, and self-­esteem. These children often lack the motivation to learn. Positive feedback from their environment is usually absent because of their low performance. When learning disabilities coexist with hyperactivity, students often face criticism for both their academic performance and general behavior. Relentless negative appraisals undermine both the academic performance and the personality formation of the child. Behavior problems resulting from negative experiences are not uncommon in youth with learning disorders. The strain and frustration of consistent underachievement can

chunks.heading: 284 Childhood
chunks.preview: 284 Childhood cause them to be reluctant to go to school, throw temper tantrums before school, or become truant. Thoughts and Fantasies The thoughts of children with specific learning disorders are quite organized. Especially when highly motivated, they can concentrate on stimuli and focus on play a...
chunks.text: 284 Childhood cause them to be reluctant to go to school, throw temper tantrums before school, or become truant. Thoughts and Fantasies The thoughts of children with specific learning disorders are quite organized. Especially when highly motivated, they can concentrate on stimuli and focus on play activities and tasks. In their symbolic play, aspects of their subjective experience and affective states may appear. They often express the belief that they are stupid or hopelessly disabled. Somatic States Because of the anxiety that children with specific learning disorders tend to feel in classroom situations, they may complain of physical pains (headache, stomachache, or chest pain) or describe other anxiety-­based somatic symptoms. Their difficulty attending to stimuli for long time spans may cause fatigue and distraction. Such symptoms tend to resolve on weekends and during vacations, when the child does not have to be in school. Relational Patterns Given their low self-­esteem and negative self-image, children with specific learning disorders may have difficulties relating to other children. Embarrassment over their inability to perform at the same level as other children may interfere with peer relationships. Shame is pervasive in these types of learning disorders. Children who experience chronic negative feedback often view the world as threatening, demanding, judgmental, and punitive. A specific learning disorder may have a persistent impact on family relationships. Parental distress and difficult parent–­child interactions are common. Caregivers often develop a negative perception of their child when a son or daughter is diagnosed as having “special needs.” They may feel helpless and disheartened, and become pessimistic about their child’s academic and social future. It is not uncommon for parents to report feelings of guilt for passing on a genetic contribution to dyslexia. Continuity Early indicators of a specific learning disorder are delay or difficulties in language, motor and auditory perception, working memory, and speed of processing. Children start showing these problems when they enter school and suffer delays in acquiring reading, writing, and mathematical skills. Children with above-­average intelligence and verbal comprehension may show signs of these difficulties in the later grades of elementary school. A thorough academic, intellectual, and psychological assessment will determine whether a specific learning disorder exists. Therapeutic Indications Early identification and intervention are crucial for the child to achieve the academic skills necessary to proceed in school and beyond. A team of teachers, special educators, school or clinical psychologists, and other specialists need to cooperate in assessment

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 285
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 285 and treatment formulation. The psychoeducational plan must consider both the academic and emotional aspects of a specific learning disorder. Because caregiver attitudes toward a child’s learning issues are critical to a positive treatment outcome, parental counseling is a...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 285 and treatment formulation. The psychoeducational plan must consider both the academic and emotional aspects of a specific learning disorder. Because caregiver attitudes toward a child’s learning issues are critical to a positive treatment outcome, parental counseling is a necessary part of the overall treatment plan. Where emotional issues are intense, the child may need individual psychotherapy to support the psychoeducational program. Clinical Illustration George was a well-­developed third grader. His parents and teachers requested a psychoeducational assessment of his reading and writing problems. They also noted his difficulties with focusing on school tasks that require concentration (e.g., homework). His parents mentioned additionally that he often seemed anxious and unhappy. The psychoeducational assessment determined that George’s mathematical skills were at an average level. His reading and writing skills, however, were two standard deviations below average. His writing and letter formation skills were also poor, and he had not yet reached decoding or word identification. In the intelligence test, he performed moderately well, with average or above average verbal comprehension, visual–­ spatial perception, and reasoning skills. But the subscale scores in working memory and processing speed revealed significantly below-­average performance. Based on the discrepancy between his intellectual and achievement performance, a diagnosis of specific learning disorder was made. In addition to his academic difficulties, George was experiencing high levels of separation and performance anxiety. His limited capacity for effective defenses against anxiety interfered with his capacity for affective expression. The stress often resulted in impulsive outbursts, leading to fights with other children, or was manifested physically in concentration problems and psychomotor restlessness. Although he had the capacity to form relationships, he often perceived others as judgmental or diminishing. His capacity for self-­regulation was prone to disruption, and his self-­esteem and sense of self-­efficacy seemed to decline under intense stress. Feelings of inadequacy and weakness were evident. SC5 Communication Disorders SC5.1 Language and Auditory Processing Disorders Language and auditory processing disorders have traditionally been divided into two broad categories, described as common linguistic characteristics of “developmental language disorder,” also known as “specific language impairment.” The latest versions of DSM-5-TR and ICD-11, however, feature differing nomenclature and subtypes. Whereas DSM-5-TR uses the term “language disorder,” ICD-11 employs “developmental language disorder.” ICD-11 includes three subtypes; DSM-5-TR does not distinguish subtypes but includes a diagnostic category called “social (pragmatic) communication disorder,” corresponding to the subtype “developmental language disorder with impairment mainly in pragmatics” in ICD-11. In DSM-5-TR, “language disorders” are categorized under neurodevelopmental disorders. The distinction between expressive and mixed types of language impairment has been eliminated, as has the differentiation between verbal and nonverbal intellectual skills. Both DSM-5-TR and ICD-11 characterize these difficulties as persistent deficits in language acquisition,

chunks.heading: 286 Childhood
chunks.preview: 286 Childhood understanding, production, or use, occurring during the developmental period, typically in early childhood, and causing significant limitations in communication skills, with language abilities below the expected level for chronological age and impacting the child’s global functioning....
chunks.text: 286 Childhood understanding, production, or use, occurring during the developmental period, typically in early childhood, and causing significant limitations in communication skills, with language abilities below the expected level for chronological age and impacting the child’s global functioning. For these diagnoses, language deficits cannot be explained by another neurodevelopmental disorder, sensory impairment, or neurological condition, such as hearing impairment or intellectual disability. Resolving the debate surrounding language disorders is challenging because of inconsistencies in terminology and diagnostic criteria, lack of reliable and culturally valid assessment tools supported by adequate normative data, and limited comparison of communication profiles across different neurodevelopmental disorders. Language disorders coincide with language acquisition, which typically emerges between 15 and 18 months of age. Delayed attainment of language milestones, particularly in expressive language, is common, so diagnosis is often deferred until the child reaches age 5 (Sansavini et al., 2021). Prevalence rates range between 2.0 and 7.0% (Plug et al., 2021). Given the essential role of communicative competence in social discourse, a disorder of expressive and/or receptive language may contribute to relationship problems. Without early intervention, the disorder may become increasingly problematic as the child matures. The symptoms of developmental language disorders include difficulties that create obstacles to communication or learning in everyday life, difficulties that are unlikely to resolve on their own, or that have not been resolved by age 5, and are not associated with a known biomedical condition. The heterogeneity of associated factors and different neurological profiles implies different neurobiological correlates. Recent research indicates comparable numbers of boys and girls with this problem, with male-to-­female ratios ranging from 1.33 to 1.22 (Nitin et al., 2022). Previous studies had shown different, more gender-­discrepant prevalence rates, a disparity that may reflect changes in terminology and conceptualization; this disorder has received numerous diagnostic labels (Bishop, 2014). The presence of a developmental language disorder does not exclude the possibility of concurrent neurodevelopmental disorders such as ASD, ADHD, motor problems (including developmental coordination disorder or developmental dyspraxia), dyslexia, disorders of executive functions, and behavioral or emotional disorders. Children with specific language impairment may show deficits in auditory attention and its control (auditory processing disorders). These differences are related to auditory and visual functioning, cognition, language, reading, physiological, and neuroimaging measures. It is still being debated whether auditory processing disorders should be regarded as deficits specific to the auditory modality or as a multimodal deficit. Developmental language disorders are heterogeneous; symptoms can be expressive, receptive, or both. Recent evidence suggests that children with this diagnosis also have deficient pragmatic skills, a non-core feature of language. Georgiou and Spanoudis (2021) have posited a subtype involving reduced pragmatic abilities. A specific assessment that considers various aspects of language functioning is necessary for diagnosis. The American Speech–­Language–­Hearing Association defines “language disorder” as an impairment in comprehension and/or use of spoken, written, or other symbol systems, encompassing deficits in language form, content, and function. Both lexical deficit disorder and pragmatic–­lexical deficit disorder are described as central integration process deficit disorders. Clinicians should consider form, content, and function of language, considering that these profiles can present in a mixed form during development. For a clear clinical description, it is still common to characterize these children as follows:

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 287
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 287 1. Children with expressive language disorders may have difficulties across various domains such as overall language functioning, grammar, semantics, pragmatics, and discourse. 2. In receptive language disorders, verbal fluency remains intact, with minimal or no deficits...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 287 1. Children with expressive language disorders may have difficulties across various domains such as overall language functioning, grammar, semantics, pragmatics, and discourse. 2. In receptive language disorders, verbal fluency remains intact, with minimal or no deficits in speech production. A diagnosis of receptive language disorder is established when tasks such as picture naming, word repetition, and language comprehension, assessed through individually administered standardized tests of language functioning, fall significantly below the expected performance of children of the same age or grade. These children may also encounter challenges in one or more of the following areas: overall language functioning (comprehension), grammar (understanding complex sentences), semantics (limited vocabulary, difficulty in understanding conversations), pragmatics (difficulty in distilling, inferring, and generalizing ideas from conversations—­not exclusively associated with ASD), and discourse (comprehension of complex narrative information). 3. Mixed language disorder (phonological syntactic deficit) is the most common variant in children. It is termed “developmental dysphasia” on the basis of its agrammatical nature: short and grammatically incorrect utterances, deficient phonology, and reduced vocabulary, while comprehension remains intact. This disorder can affect both receptive and expressive communication in degrees ranging from mild to severe. The impairment is not attributed to sensory deficits, nonverbal intellectual deficits, neurological conditions, environmental deprivation, or psychiatric impairments. 4. Another mixed disorder is auditory verbal agnosia, characterized by a severe deficit in comprehension and phonological decoding, resulting in profound expressive deficits. Subjective Experience of Language and Auditory Disorders in Childhood Affective States Children with language and auditory disorders are emotionally expressive. They may be in negative affective states, including frustration, anger, and aggression, which are particularly prevalent in the school environment and in relationships with peers. They may also have internalizing problems, such as sadness, low self-­esteem, anticipatory anxiety, or school phobia, if the underlying disorder is not recognized. Thoughts and Fantasies These children struggle to formulate and express ideas. They appear increasingly disorganized and confused in the classroom, seeming to “zone out” and simply shut down during group instruction. Difficulties in the organization of thinking comes with greater severity and may instigate withdrawal into fantasy and impairment of attention control. Somatic States Expression of somatic and internal states is sometimes difficult for children with these problems. They may show psychomotor restlessness, isolation, or hyperactivity in distress situations, such as in the classroom or in extracurricular and collective situations.

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 289
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 289 Stuttering often appears between ages 2 and 5, ultimately affecting 4–5% of children. About 1 child in 30 goes through a period of stuttering, but by adolescence, the prevalence drops to 1%. Although the ratio of males to females who begin stammering between ages 2 and 4...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 289 Stuttering often appears between ages 2 and 5, ultimately affecting 4–5% of children. About 1 child in 30 goes through a period of stuttering, but by adolescence, the prevalence drops to 1%. Although the ratio of males to females who begin stammering between ages 2 and 4 years is approximately equal, males are more likely than females to have co-­occurring speech disorders, especially in articulation and phonology. Recovery usually occurs from 6 to 36 months after onset; females are more likely to recover without assistance. In a percentage of children who stutter, the symptom persists into adolescence or even adulthood, even if it is limited to certain circumstances. By adolescence, the male-to-­female ratio for persisting stuttering is approximately 3:1 (Yairi & Seery, 2021). Most contemporary researchers believe that stuttering has a multiplicity of converging causes that include biological vulnerability, language delay, environmental demands and expectations, and temperamental characteristics of the speaker (Smith & Weber, 2017). The mechanism that produces stuttering seems to be a failure of the central nervous system to generate patterns of motor commands necessary for fluent speech to continue. Thus, lack of fluency is defined as a neurodevelopmental disorder. Measures of neural activity while children process the phonological properties of words have revealed neurodevelopmental differences between fluent children and those who stutter. But as many clinicians have noted, stuttering can also emerge after an extended stressful period or traumatic event. Psychogenic stuttering can be diagnosed when organic and/or physiological causes are excluded. A number of psychoanalytic hypotheses have been put forth to explain the phenomenon. These include the idea that it represents a developmental conflict in which unconscious hostile and anxious affects interfere with spontaneity. Some analysts have formulated stuttering as a manifestation of a conflict between separation from and merger with a parent. Subjective Experience of Fluency Disorder in Childhood Affective States Children who stutter are often introverted, anxious, and compliant. From the age of 5, they can be aware of their speech problem and the emotional burden it entails. They often feel embarrassed, ashamed, afraid, frustrated, and sometimes helpless. Some children invent coping mechanisms to reduce the symptom, avoiding uttering words that aggravate their stammering and limiting communication to avoid other children’s teasing. They try to hide or minimize their problem, showing annoyance when others refer to their stuttering. When symptoms are intense and/or frequent, the child experiences emotions that may be difficult to manage, such as sadness, fear, excitement, disruption, or anger. Thoughts and Fantasies Apart from sports and the arts, there are not many areas in which stuttering children can demonstrate their capacities without relying heavily on speech (and even these areas depend on speech to a certain extent). Stuttering is hard not to notice, causing its sufferers to think of themselves as problematic or sick. Stuttering children may denigrate themselves, thinking they are less adequate in comparison to their peers, and they may fear they disappoint people close to them. Fantasies of failure, rejection, and being ludicrous are common.

chunks.heading: 290 Childhood
chunks.preview: 290 Childhood Somatic States Children with stuttering react to their symptoms with tension and struggle. They may emit unusual facial and body movements associated with the effort to speak (facial grimacing, tremors of muscles involved in speech, eyeblinks). In some cases, these movements have a ste...
chunks.text: 290 Childhood Somatic States Children with stuttering react to their symptoms with tension and struggle. They may emit unusual facial and body movements associated with the effort to speak (facial grimacing, tremors of muscles involved in speech, eyeblinks). In some cases, these movements have a stereotypical character and may appear as a tic. Researchers have found that many stuttering children react more slowly than their peers to external stimuli; they move at a slower pace and with more frequent errors and irregularities, and their rate of speech is slower. Relationship Patterns School is usually the most stressful environment for the stuttering child. Because peer interaction requires verbal communication, the child who stutters may avoid speaking and tends to be reluctant to initiate interactions with other children. There is fear of being bullied for stuttering and a tendency to withdraw from groups of peers. The more stressed a child feels, the more difficult speech becomes; the problem can inhibit communication even with people who are not that close. A child who manages to establish a close friendship may adopt a compliant stance toward the other child’s wishes. When stuttering is intense, others may feel the stressful effort of the child who tries to communicate. Parents feeling this stress can react by either being impatient as their child speaks or talking on their child’s behalf. Therapeutic Indications In neurodevelopmental stuttering, speech therapy is used both to shape fluent speech and to help the afflicted children to speak with less tension, avoiding interruption the flow of communication. Clinicians should explore the child’s life situation at the time of onset of the symptom. When there is evidence that stuttering is psychogenic, speech therapy on its own can prove ineffective. The child’s emotional experience needs to be assessed, especially when there are frequent disruptions in the child’s capacity to regulate affects and impulses. To plan effective treatments, clinicians need to address the causes of problems in fluency. Relational issues, deficiencies in mentalized affectivity, harsh superego standards, disruptions in the child’s capacity for identity, inability to identify and express aggressive feelings, and defenses that are ineffective in resolving internal conflicts can reinforce the symptom. To enhance chances for recovery, speech therapy can be combined with family interventions or child psychotherapy with parallel parental work. Clinical Illustration James, a 7-year-old boy, was referred by his speech therapist for psychological evaluation after he had stuttered for over 1 year. He had already attended 9 months of speech therapy with no evident improvement. His stuttering began 1 month after the family moved from a small village to the city because of financial problems. His mother had been a full-time homemaker, but when the family moved, she started working fulltime and had only a few hours a day available for her child on weekdays. His father was overwhelmed with his new, demanding job and became impatient with his son, often shouting or punishing him for being clumsy and slow in doing his homework.

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 291
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 291 Stuttering had provoked bullying from other children at school, and James had become reluctant to speak in class. He spoke only when the teacher asked him to and kept himself in the periphery of his peer group. Psychological evaluation revealed his anxiety and sadness abo...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 291 Stuttering had provoked bullying from other children at school, and James had become reluctant to speak in class. He spoke only when the teacher asked him to and kept himself in the periphery of his peer group. Psychological evaluation revealed his anxiety and sadness about the radical changes in his life. His father’s outbursts filled him with anger and with fears he could not identify and express verbally. These feelings, along with loneliness and helplessness, were evident in his play, which focused entirely around missing his old neighborhood and his walks with his mother. SC5.3 Social (Pragmatic) Communication Disorder DSM-5 introduced a new diagnosis, social (pragmatic) communication disorder, for children with communication and pragmatic language impairments who do not meet criteria for ASD (typically the primary diagnosis applying to deficits in social communication). Symptoms of social communication disorder are similar to those of autism but less severe. The key differentiator involves restricted–­repetitive patterns of behavior, interests, or activities, which are characteristic of autism but not of social communication disorders. DSM advises diagnosing social communication disorders only when this symptom has never been in the clinical picture, because individuals on the autistic spectrum may show such patterns only during their early development. Given the similarities between the two conditions, however, and the paucity of research evidence that the diagnostic criteria identify a coherent and persistent clinical condition, we consider social communication and pragmatic language impairments as symptoms rather than as a diagnostic entity. Children may have impairments in any of three areas: word knowledge, grammar, and discourse. Discourse includes narrative and conversational exchange. Children with more specific forms of language disorder have variable social interaction and communication difficulties relative to developmentally typical peers. Social communication disorder can co-occur with other DSM-5 communication disorders (language disorder, speech sound disorder, childhood-­onset fluency disorder, and unspecified communication disorder), and may include difficulties establishing social relationships, poorer quality friendships, difficulties with peer negotiation and conflict, and poorer social cognition. Diagnosis of social (pragmatic) communication disorder is currently challenged by a lack of culturally valid assessment tools. This condition is characterized by a primary deficit in both nonverbal and verbal social communication. It involves difficulties in using language effectively for social interactions. Affected individuals struggle to adjust their communication to different social contexts, follow conversational norms, interpret figurative language, and coordinate speech with nonverbal cues. Because language skills must be sufficiently developed before these higher-­order pragmatic deficits can be detected, a diagnosis of social (pragmatic) communication disorder is not to be made until children are 4–5 years old. This requirement seems to assume that social communication and pragmatic language skills are manifestations of the same underlying cognitive process(es). Many studies have framed the problem with pragmatics as symptomatic of other conditions, mainly ASDs and developmental learning disorders, but also ADHD, fragile X syndrome, and intellectual disability. Variations in the profile of pragmatic difficulties may thus imply the existence of different subtypes. The question of whether pragmatic disorders can be observed in the absence of other disorders needs to be answered through reliable and objective diagnoses in future research.

chunks.heading: 292 Childhood
chunks.preview: 292 Childhood Pragmatic disorders are less precisely defined than other communication disorders, mainly because their symptoms are not observed within objectifiable and measurable areas such as phonology or syntax; instead, they become evident within the heterogeneity, breadth, and subjectivity of s...
chunks.text: 292 Childhood Pragmatic disorders are less precisely defined than other communication disorders, mainly because their symptoms are not observed within objectifiable and measurable areas such as phonology or syntax; instead, they become evident within the heterogeneity, breadth, and subjectivity of social interaction. Researchers and clinicians are continuing to discuss the diagnostic distinction between symptoms or syndrome; we await the establishment of a profile that identifies social pragmatic deficits in the absence of autism or other disorders. The development of robust assessment tools that use consistent, reliable diagnostic criteria is urgently needed, as is research on the family histories and developmental trajectories of children with social communication disorders. Subjective Experience of Social (Pragmatic) Communication Disorder in Childhood Affective States Children with this problem understand the world of emotions intellectually rather than emotionally; feelings are like a foreign language. Only when they reach a threshold of explosive intensity can they experience their own affective states. Most often, these are frustration, rage, and terror. Thoughts and Fantasies These children seem to have a diminished capacity to develop a theory of mind. They seem unaware that others have beliefs, desires, and intentions. Consequently, they appear to disregard others’ thoughts and feelings. Their capacity for pretense, whether imaginative play or intentional deception, is greatly limited. Somatic States There are no typical somatic states that characterize these children. They appear immature or inappropriate and are socially disconnected when with others. Their behavior may become dysfunctional, as they become argumentative, disruptive, or disrespectful. Relational Patterns These children have difficulties interacting with peers in groups. They fail to “read” other people’s body language, facial expressions, and vocal intonations, and are therefore socially inept. They are unable to be with other children even for brief periods of time without great distress; consequently, they rarely make friends, though they may interact reasonably well with adults. While they have many of the social features of children with nonverbal learning disabilities, they do not have the same cognitive or academic deficits. Their early development typically appears unimpaired; it is only when they begin to interact with others that their problems emerge. Their self-help skills development is not comparable to that of other children their age. Because they do not seem to know how to play with others, they are eventually excluded from social groups. They try to memorize rules that would explain how people are supposed to behave, but then are mystified as to when these rules apply and when they do not.

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 293
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 293 They long for contact with others, yet when they occasionally make efforts to befriend someone, they are disappointed at being met with indifference. Their consequent isolation deprives them further of opportunities to develop socially and thus compounds their difficultie...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 293 They long for contact with others, yet when they occasionally make efforts to befriend someone, they are disappointed at being met with indifference. Their consequent isolation deprives them further of opportunities to develop socially and thus compounds their difficulties. Although they may have one or two friends, by adolescence, they are typically rejected by peers and excluded from group activities. They are not usually successful in maintaining friendships and may be rejected, teased, or bullied by peers. Continuity Data on continuity do not exist. There is a considerable interest among researchers in clarifying the points of distinction among social communication disorders, developmental language disorders, and ASDs. A recent longitudinal epidemiological study based on DSM-5 criteria came to discrepant results: Social (pragmatic) communication disorder was much more common in children with a history of developmental learning disorders than in those without them; having had a language disorder in kindergarten was a significant risk factor for a social communication disorder in adolescence; other results showed that social communication disorders were equally distributed across children with and without a history of developmental language disorders. Therapeutic Indications Psychologists and/or speech therapists can work on conversational skills, language processing, and pragmatic skills. Social communication disorders are linked to other language disorders and need comprehensive treatment. Speech–­language intervention for children with pragmatic language impairment is burdened by the lack of evidence to support the practice. These treatments employ social skills strategies (e.g., social stories, peer-­ mediated interventions, and technology-­ enhanced instruction), self-­management/self-­regulation strategies, and video modeling (viewing a recorded interaction, discussing, and rehearsing and repeating the interaction to learn the social skills it exemplifies). Intensive interaction may involve individualized, mutually engaging games and activities, during which the therapist promotes optimal attention and arousal, attributes intention to student actions, and responds contingently (following the student’s lead). Therapists can model conversational behaviors such as topic initiation, continuation, and management, using questions to keep the conversation moving and providing validating comments. Generalization is pursued by the therapist’s encouragement to try new skills at home with family or in class with friends and discuss the outcome with the clinician. If more help is needed, the clinician can discreetly “sit in” on an interaction in which the student uses the skill with a peer and give feedback. Clinical Illustration David was a 9-year-old child in an upper-­middle-­class family. His older brother had no communication problems. Verbal and nonverbal assessments gave him a profile of language strengths and weaknesses consistent with a diagnosis of pragmatic language impairment or social communication disorder. His parents reported that he had been a good baby who did not demand much attention. His general development was normal. Although his early communicative maturation appeared normal to his parents, they noticed that his language production declined radically sometime during his second

chunks.heading: 294 Childhood
chunks.preview: 294 Childhood year, and he also did not seem to respond to language. His hearing was within normal limits, and he had good self-­adaptive skills. His nonverbal intelligence was below average, with problems understanding body language. Parents and teachers noticed that he had difficulty in starting a...
chunks.text: 294 Childhood year, and he also did not seem to respond to language. His hearing was within normal limits, and he had good self-­adaptive skills. His nonverbal intelligence was below average, with problems understanding body language. Parents and teachers noticed that he had difficulty in starting and maintaining a conversation, leading to isolation, and that he lacked an adequate narrative. He had trouble keeping friends and could not follow the rules of a mutual game. He had episodes of emotional dysregulation and anger, which he could not relate to his difficulties. As he struggled to formulate and express his ideas, he appeared increasingly disorganized and confused in the classroom, seeming to “zone out.” During group instruction, he simply shut down. SC6 Sensory Processing Regulation Disorders Every child has a unique pattern of regulating emotion and processing sensory stimuli. There is a broad continuum of functional variability that reflects individual differences in children’s healthy social, emotional, and intellectual functioning. Nevertheless, some children show response and regulation patterns that are so peculiar that they significantly interfere with their functioning at home, in school, and in relationships. Regulatory–­sensory processing disorders are generally recognizable in infancy or early childhood and may continue into latency or adolescence. They have been described by the major infancy and childhood classifications (DC:0–3R, Zero to Three, 2005; Interdisciplinary Council on Developmental and Learning Disorders, 2005; DC:0–5TM , Zero to Three, 2016), according to which the diagnosis requires both a distinct pattern of behavior and a difficulty in sensory modulation, sensorimotor processing, sensory discrimination, or attentional processing. Identifying a child as having a particular subtype is difficult because clinicians often encounter children with mixed features. For an in-depth discussion of regulatory–­sensory processing disorders, see the Infancy and Early Childhood (IEC) section on pages 19 to 148. Variations in sensory processing can extend into the school years, affecting school performance. Latency-­age children with regulatory disorders may be hypersensitive to sounds, sight, and touch; seem easily distracted; and have problems with emotional regulation/modulation. Descriptions by parents and caregivers aid in the diagnosis of regulatory disorders, and standardized assessments may yield signs of gross and fine motor skills difficulties. Sensory tolerance varies widely even across subtypes and variations. Long-term difficulties with social interactions and behavior problems may occur. Such disorders are thought to originate from multiple factors (e.g., endowment, temperament, and parent–­infant “fit”); physiological, parasympathetic, and neurological indicators of differences between typically developing children and those with regulatory disorders have been noted (e.g., in electrodermal activity, vagal tone, and white matter microstructure). Although studies in these areas have identified correlations, causation, including clear evidence of genetic links, remains undetermined. Children with prenatal exposure to teratogens (including drugs and alcohol), children with postnatal histories of trauma, and even some children with neurotic defensive inhibitions or ego restrictions may also show similar symptoms. They are also seen in children diagnosed with separation anxiety, ADHD, oppositional defiant disorder, and ASD. Over the past two decades, there has been a growing interest in sensory processing disorders, with an emphasis on the importance of behavioral and sensory regulation for adjustment and well-being. There is still a paucity of literature, however, on

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 295
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 295 the continuity of dysregulation disorders. The limited available data have provided intriguing suggestions. Symptoms of dysregulation in various areas remain stable from early childhood to preadolescence. And there is mounting evidence that sensory processing disorders in...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 295 the continuity of dysregulation disorders. The limited available data have provided intriguing suggestions. Symptoms of dysregulation in various areas remain stable from early childhood to preadolescence. And there is mounting evidence that sensory processing disorders in infancy predict behavioral and emotional problems in childhood in the internalizing domain (e.g., anxiety, depression, withdrawal). Children with dysregulation disorders may have a low sense of self-­efficacy and difficulties in developing a clear sense of identity. They are more at risk of developing both externalizing and internalizing disorders. SC7 Mood Disorders SC7.1 Depressive Disorders Clinical depression in children lies on a spectrum ranging from subclinical to syndromal. Children can present with sadness, irritability, temper tantrums, low frustration tolerance, somatic complaints, social withdrawal, and/or anhedonia. Depressed children have more symptoms of separation anxiety and phobias than do adolescents. Even in infancy, children can experience a depressive disorder that is very similar to depression in later years. Parents and teachers are less able to detect depressed feelings in children than in adolescents, especially in children who also have more externalizing difficulties. Children themselves are often not capable of seeking help or support for their depressive symptoms and feelings; they can convey a sense of lethargy, discouragement, and unhappiness. School performance suffers, and learning is inhibited; a precipitous drop in grades can stem from poor concentration and an inner preoccupation. Epidemiologically, depression is more common in adolescents than in prepubertal children, perhaps reflecting the diagnostic problems discussed earlier. In childhood depression, there are no significant differences between males and females. The prevalence of depression in young children appears to be increasing, and age of onset is becoming younger. Clinicians need to be able to recognize the symptoms, and often masked presentations of depression, to be aware of the distinction between child and adult forms, to make sense of symptoms in a developmental context, to distinguish comorbid disorders, and to identify protective and risk factors. There are major conceptual issues in understanding depression in children. The role of gender and age, the impact of genetic–­biological–­hormonal differences, and psychological processes are all important. The symptoms and problems involved in depressive spectrum disorders occur frequently in other disorders of childhood. For example, impulsivity, lack of concentration, and oppositionality appear in ADHD and oppositional defiant disorder. Anxiety disorders often accompany depression in children. It is important to determine whether depression is primary or secondary to another disorder. For example, obsessive symptoms may be a feature of a depressive disorder, or depression may develop secondary to an obsessive–­compulsive disorder. Children with depressive symptoms often show other psychopathology and meet criteria for two or more comorbid diagnoses—­most commonly anxiety disorders, followed by disruptive behavior disorders and ADHD. Depression increases risks for obesity, problematic family and peer relationships, and educational underachievement. Depression in children likely results from a complex interaction among psychological and biological vulnerabilities and environmental factors. Although studies indicate

chunks.heading: 298 Childhood
chunks.preview: 298 Childhood Therapeutic Indications Distinguishing between the acute and nonacute phases is essential, as interventions need to be tailored according to the specific moment. Clinicians need to take into account the chronic nature of the disorder as well. Psychotherapy may be important not only for...
chunks.text: 298 Childhood Therapeutic Indications Distinguishing between the acute and nonacute phases is essential, as interventions need to be tailored according to the specific moment. Clinicians need to take into account the chronic nature of the disorder as well. Psychotherapy may be important not only for the child but also for the parents. Studies indicate that psychodynamic psychotherapy has comparable outcomes to other psychological treatments (e.g., cognitive-­behavioral, family systems therapy). The psychodynamic treatment of depressive disorders in childhood prioritizes understanding and addressing the underlying emotional and interpersonal dynamics that contribute to the child’s symptoms. Treatment goals include facilitating emotional expression, exploring unresolved conflicts, and promoting insight into the origins of depressive feelings within the child’s developmental context. In a safe, supportive therapeutic environment, therapy can help children identify and process difficult emotions, develop healthier coping skills, and enhance their self-­awareness and self-­ esteem. Interventions seek to strengthen the child’s emotional regulation, improve interpersonal relationships, and foster resilience in the face of adversity. Aims include alleviation of symptoms, improvement in overall functioning, and promotion of longterm psychological well-being by addressing the underlying psychological factors contributing to the child’s distress. Clinical Illustration Charles, a sturdy-­looking 8-year-old boy, was referred for long-­standing difficulty with emotional regulation and out-of-­control behavior. Previous testing had placed him in the superior range of intellectual functioning. Despite being placed part-time in a classroom for emotionally impaired children, he skipped from second to fourth grade; teachers reported he performed well above grade level. His sensory–­perceptual skills were normal, and his executive functioning was solid. He was removed from general education classes because of outbursts of verbal and physical aggression toward peers and staff. When he resisted rules, teachers tried to accommodate him. The mother complained that everyone “walked on eggshells” around him. He had previously been diagnosed with generalized anxiety disorder and oppositional defiant disorder, and there was a suspected ASD. His brother, 2 years older, suffered from congenital abnormalities and consequent atypical development. Both sides of the family had histories of anxiety and depression. Labor and delivery had been unremarkable, but Charles seemed serious from birth on; his mother worried that he would never smile. His outbursts dated from about age 4, when it appears he was painfully neglected at home, especially compared to his needier brother. He held grudges and resented the attention his brother got at his expense. Over time, he was put on behavioral programs both at home and at school. Charles related tensely to the therapist, refusing contact but becoming more enlivened while playing ball with him. He would typically become hypercompetitive, get enraged both at himself and the therapist, and melt down into a whimpering state of hopeless, helpless despair. He showed over-the-top aggressive reactions to frustration and seemed to feel ashamed of his loss of control. In states of quiescence, such as when building an impenetrable bank with Legos, he would be quiet, a bit sullen, and capable of unusual concentration and focus. Interactive games provoked the need to win at all

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 299
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 299 costs. His drawings revealed a more depressive state. He seldom experienced fun or excitement and appeared strikingly anhedonic. Eventually, he was able to bond with his therapist via humor, teasing him about being a Yankees fan. He began to take pleasure in his knowledge...
