chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 299 costs. His drawings revealed a more depressive state. He seldom experienced fun or excitement and appeared strikingly anhedonic. Eventually, he was able to bond with his therapist via humor, teasing him about being a Yankees fan. He began to take pleasure in his knowledge of sports trivia and began asking the therapist how he did and whether he was clever. A “middle ground” began to open between his lacerating competitiveness and his encapsulated, somber states. His intense anger masked a fairly severe depression. His need for control tended to alienate others and provoked attempts to mediate his behavior through consequences and rewards but had evoked little empathy or recognition of his deep unhappiness. SC7.2 Disruptive Mood and Dysregulation Disorder “Irritability,” defined as a high propensity to anger, can be a normal expression of children’s emotional development. Its manifestations can range from normative, appropriate expressions of negative affect to chronic irritable mood and aggressive outbursts. Children with severe emotional and behavioral dysregulation represent a diagnostic challenge to clinicians and researchers. Although they share some symptoms with sufferers of mood and disruptive behavior disorders, these children do not fit neatly into existing DSM-5-TR categories. Chronic irritability is one of the few crosscutting symptoms (restlessness and lack of concentration are the others) of externalizing, internalizing, and neurodevelopmental disorders. Clinical and empirical findings highlight the need to diagnose children with pediatric bipolar symptomatology more adequately and have directed research toward an operational definition of chronic irritability. Current diagnostic systems show some apparent differences in the interpretation of research findings. Severe mood dysregulation syndrome is characterized by abnormal mood (anger or sadness), hyperarousal (insomnia, agitation, distractibility, racing thoughts or flight of ideas, pressured speech, and intrusiveness), and markedly increased reactivity to negative emotional stimuli. Studies have shown that chronic irritability is distinct from episodic irritability. We need to have a useful diagnostic category to identify children with chronic nonepisodic irritability. The introduction of “disruptive mood and dysregulation disorder” among DSM-5-TR mood disorders stems from the need to reduce the risk of overdiagnosis of pediatric disorder; children with nonepisodic irritability are often misdiagnosed as bipolar disorder. The central feature of disruptive mood and dysregulation disorder is severe chronic irritability that is more frequent, intense, and constant than a normal developmental emotional manifestation. The diagnosis was formulated on the basis of the severe mood dysregulation syndrome. In DC:0–5TM, the diagnosis of disruptive anger and aggression dysregulation disorder has been added. This category identifies manifestations of emotional and behavioral dysregulation in terms of significant anger and dysregulated temperament, refusal to cooperate and comply with rules, and the presence of reactive and proactive aggression. ICD-11 instead frames disruptive mood and dysregulation disorder as a diagnostic specifier of oppositional defiant disorder (with chronic irritability–­rage). This decision seems to give more credence to studies that recognize the greater import of the emotional aspects of behavioral disorders and suggests that oppositional defiant disorder and disruptive mood and dysregulation disorder be conceptualized along the same continuum of severity as emotion regulation disorders. Some research has in

chunks.heading: 300 Childhood
chunks.preview: 300 Childhood fact identified irritability as a major subdimension of oppositional defiant disorder symptoms and has shown that irritability can be significantly associated with the later development of anxiety and depression. Subjective Experience of Disruptive Mood and Dysregulation Disorder in Ch...
chunks.text: 300 Childhood fact identified irritability as a major subdimension of oppositional defiant disorder symptoms and has shown that irritability can be significantly associated with the later development of anxiety and depression. Subjective Experience of Disruptive Mood and Dysregulation Disorder in Childhood Affective States These children often show hyperlabile and dysregulated emotions and poor skill at regulating them. They tend to be touchy and chronically prone to irritation and anger. They often find themselves stuck in states of heightened susceptibility to anger, frustration, and distress. Their inadequate emotional regulation skills make them susceptible to frequent mood swings; they may react with verbal outbursts that are disproportionate to the situation. Mild frustrations often trigger emotional and behavioral dysregulation, resulting in frequent verbal and angry outbursts. Thoughts and Fantasies The cognitive focus of these children often gravitates toward the threatening aspects of their experiences. They may interpret ambiguous stimuli as potentially persecutory. They may have reduced cognitive flexibility, attention difficulties, and distractibility. All these characteristics contribute to their heightened risk of high levels of anger and aggression. Somatic States These children are prone to difficulties with physiological and behavioral modulation, often leading to states of behavioral dysregulation. Emotional dysregulation, especially intense anger, is often accompanied by a loss of control over behavior, typically resulting in recurrent outbursts of aggression (e.g., throwing objects, hitting) that are often inappropriate to the situation and the child’s developmental level. In addition to anger reactions, regulatory difficulties are evidenced by more general regulatory problems, such as sleep disturbances. Relationship Patterns These children suffer higher levels of social deficits across various domains. They are markedly impaired in multiple areas of social and relational functioning. In their interactions with family members and teachers, they struggle with processing social cues related to emotional and behavioral dysregulation reactions. Factors such as low parental support, marital dissatisfaction, and inconsistent parenting may exacerbate difficulties in modulating angry emotions and contribute to the children’s overall irritability. Continuity Children diagnosed with disruptive mood and dysregulation disorder face greater impairment and are at a high risk for a range of negative outcomes, including affective,

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 301
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 301 behavioral, and functional problems. Most children diagnosed with this problem have been given at least one other diagnosis. Chronic irritability, a key clinical feature of oppositional defiant disorder and other problems (intermittent explosive disorder, major and persis...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 301 behavioral, and functional problems. Most children diagnosed with this problem have been given at least one other diagnosis. Chronic irritability, a key clinical feature of oppositional defiant disorder and other problems (intermittent explosive disorder, major and persistent depressive disorder, and ADHD), is also found in generalized anxiety disorder, social anxiety disorder, pediatric bipolar disorder, and pediatric posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD). Chronic irritability does not specifically predict bipolar disorder, but it is significantly associated with PTSD, oppositional defiant disorder, conduct disorders, and ADHD, as well as with anxiety and depression. Episodic irritability in bipolar disorder is linked to anxiety and mania. Children with disruptive mood and dysregulation disorder often continue to be significantly impaired in relational and social functioning into adulthood. They have higher rates of being single or divorced and have fewer children. Therapeutic Indications In addition to providing general guidance on setting boundaries and limits, which is crucial for these children, therapeutic interventions should focus on regulating and processing dysregulated emotional and behavioral states. For example, it could be beneficial to address the child’s reflective functions, which are often deficient and oriented toward perceiving external reality as threatening and hostile. Given the relational implications of the problem, involving parents in the treatment program is essential for effective intervention. Clinical Illustration Andy, an 8-year-old boy in first grade, lived with his parents and his 4-year-old brother. He was described as affectionate and not vindictive but frequently irritable. His language skills were considered adequate, though he showed signs of restlessness and impulsivity both at home and school. He often engaged in motor activity such as running and climbing at inappropriate times. He avoided tasks that required sustained mental effort and frequently interrupted others while they were speaking. At school, he was distractible, forgot his homework, and had difficulty following directions. He struggled to connect with peers. In both home and school environments, Andy was highly sensitive to perceived slights, often reacting with intense outbursts of anger that sometimes resulted in selfharm or aggression toward others. These reactions included crying, screaming, throwing objects, and self-­directed behaviors such as head banging. Following these episodes, he typically experienced sadness and remorse and attempted to apologize. His symptoms included chronic irritability and frequent verbal and physical outbursts in response to minor frustrations. These behaviors significantly impaired his social functioning and emotional regulation. SC7.3 Bipolar Disorder For several years, in the context of controversy about possible overdiagnosis and conflation with disruptive and mood dysregulation disorder, there has been an increase in research on bipolar disorder in children. The absence of a more sophisticated developmental perspective is evident in studies that group children and adolescents together under the misnomer “pediatric bipolar disorder.” Nonetheless, although it was once

chunks.heading: 302 Childhood
chunks.preview: 302 Childhood considered primarily an adult disorder, bipolar disorder diagnosed in children has increased over the years and is found equally in males and females. Children with bipolar disorder suffer enormously and are difficult to soothe and comfort. Even in infancy, there can be sensory sensiti...
chunks.text: 302 Childhood considered primarily an adult disorder, bipolar disorder diagnosed in children has increased over the years and is found equally in males and females. Children with bipolar disorder suffer enormously and are difficult to soothe and comfort. Even in infancy, there can be sensory sensitivities, tantrums, sleep disturbance, and separation difficulties. There is a consistency in the disorder’s symptomatic presentation and diagnostic comorbidity. Periods of increased energy and grandiosity are often reported, involving fluctuating, inflated self-­esteem beyond what is expectable for their age. Because the capacity to fantasize is part of developmentally appropriate imaginativeness, clinically significant grandiosity in young children may be hard to see. Bipolar disorder is increasingly being construed as a spectrum disorder comprising different levels of mania, intensity, periodicity, and cycling. Developmentally inappropriate episodes of elevated mood and irritability are considered cardinal symptoms. Irritability appears to be the complement in children to elated mood in adults. Pivotal to the diagnosis are deviations from typical behavior in the form of episodes and the presence of elevated and expansive mood. Aggression is often the main presenting problem and the most disruptive to adjustment. Aggression that is markedly episodic and co-­occurs with other symptoms of mania indicates the need for careful assessment, including identification of any family history of mood disorder. Along with a decreased need for sleep, another symptom may be “hypersexuality,” especially with mania and in the absence of sexual abuse. Clinicians must distinguish between children’s normal sexual behavior, excessive sexual activities, and interests in an at-risk population. For example, normal preschool children show interest in the genitals and breasts of familiar adults and children. But surreptitious touching of adults and forcefully demanding to touch or be touched are signals for clinical concern. For young school-­age children, playing games related to sexuality is common, whereas coercing others to play is a pathological sign. Hypersexuality is characterized not only by its frequency in some bipolar children but also by features of precocity, boundary intrusion, compulsiveness, and aggression. There is considerable evidence for a strong genetic component in this disorder. Bipolar illness runs in families and is one of the most heritable of all psychiatric disorders. There is a four- to sixfold increased risk of the disorder in first-­degree relatives of affected individuals, and the degree of family connection is higher with early onset. Premorbid problems are common with early onset (e.g., preschool), especially disruptive behavior, irritability, and dysregulation. Clarifying the characteristic features of bipolar disorder in children is a critical clinical concern because of its confusion with other disorders (ADHD, anxiety, depression, conduct disorder), its strong association with aggression and suicide, the trend toward earlier onset and earlier suicide, and often a longer and more severe course of the condition. It is important to distinguish bipolar disorder from disruptive and mood dysregulation disorder (DSM-5-TR) and from anger and aggression dysregulation disorder of childhood (DC:0–5TM). These disorders have irritability, aggression, and angry outbursts in common, but they occur periodically, while in bipolar patterns these symptoms are persistent. Bipolar disorder can be comorbid with several common and well-­ established children’s disorders: ADHD, obsessive–­compulsive disorders, depressive disorders, conduct disorders, and anxiety-­related disorders, including panic disorders. Bipolar disorder and ADHD share inattentiveness, impulsivity, motoric overactivity, talkativeness, and disorganized thinking.

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 303
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 303 The bandwidth for diagnostic criteria and the continuity of child bipolar disorder with the adult version are critical issues. Some evidence suggests that an overly broad definition of bipolar disorder in children can lead to overdiagnosis and to premature, overly aggress...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 303 The bandwidth for diagnostic criteria and the continuity of child bipolar disorder with the adult version are critical issues. Some evidence suggests that an overly broad definition of bipolar disorder in children can lead to overdiagnosis and to premature, overly aggressive use of medication. The validity of diagnosing bipolar disorder in preschool children has not been well established. What are euphemistically labeled “mood-­stabilizing” medications are essentially “antimanic” pharmaceuticals, which have adverse side effects that have not been adequately studied longitudinally in young children. Like many adults with bipolar disorder, children with bipolar disorder can be resistant to taking medication; they want not to need it. Given these concerns, early and careful diagnosis of child bipolar disorder is essential. The risk factors of family genetics, comorbidity, and predictable life course favor early diagnosis, which in turn can lead to earlier treatment on individual, family, and educational levels. Subjective Experience of Bipolar Disorder in Childhood Affective States Bipolar disorder is a severe affective disorder featuring fluctuating states of mood and emotion, with extreme feelings that occur without pleasure or a sense of well-being. Bipolar children suffer as they are continually bombarded with affect that they are unable to regulate or understand. Features of the disorder include grandiosity, negative self-­appraisals, being overly self-­referential and insensitive to others, and being overly reactive to sensory stimuli. These children tend to be driven by impulse, hypersexuality, and poor social judgment. Thoughts and Fantasies Youth with bipolar disorder are more easily characterized through their actions and intense feelings than through the fantasies and thoughts that accompany their mood swings. Thinking and social judgment can be disrupted and distorted by episodic bursts of energy. Grandiose self-­images and expansive thoughts can alternate with self-­hatred, feelings of hopelessness, and lack of control. For children with bipolar disorder, grandiose fantasies are too readily converted into unrealistic, inappropriate actions and coercive attempts to enlist others to comply. Somatic States Many children with bipolar disorder appear to be restless, irritable, and uncomfortable in their own bodies. Sleep and appetite can be disrupted. They can be injury-­prone and overly reactive to accidents and normative medical conditions of childhood. These children have immense difficulty in regulating both their feelings and their bodies. Relational Patterns Because of the heritability of the disorder, families are at risk for being unable to deal with unpredictable and prolonged episodes of rage, irritability, and despair. Children with bipolar disorder have recurring problems with parents, siblings, peers, and teachers. They experience less maternal warmth, more tension in their relations with their parents, and more impaired peer relationships. Greater family, social, and school

chunks.heading: 304 Childhood
chunks.preview: 304 Childhood impairment is also reported. These children display greater novelty seeking, consistent with reports of greater creativity in adults with bipolar disorder; however, they also feel less pleasure, have lower resilience and self-­direction, and have less capacity to cooperate in relations...
chunks.text: 304 Childhood impairment is also reported. These children display greater novelty seeking, consistent with reports of greater creativity in adults with bipolar disorder; however, they also feel less pleasure, have lower resilience and self-­direction, and have less capacity to cooperate in relationships than other children. Continuity Like other mood disorders, accurately diagnosed bipolar disorder shows a homotypic continuity pattern. It can also lead to numerous negative consequences. Children with bipolar disorder are at significant risk for adverse outcomes, including suicide, substance abuse, psychotic postpartum depression, and borderline personality disorder. Therapeutic Indications The risk of misdiagnosis and inappropriate treatment is notable. Poorer course and outcomes are associated with early age of onset, low socioeconomic status, higher frequency of mixed and rapid-­cycling episodes, exposure to negative life events, and family history of psychopathology. Early identification, accurate diagnosis, and ongoing treatment are all protective factors. In cases of correctly diagnosed bipolar disorder (and for individuals at high risk of these disorders before symptomatic manifestation), integrated child–­parent treatment is most suitable, particularly for early-onset cases. Clinical Illustration Frederick, an intense, wiry 5-year-old, was seen for psychotherapy during his parents’ high-­conflict divorce and custody dispute. Problems included aggression, cruelty to animals, episodic preoccupations with death and killing, and hypersexuality. There were allegations of sexual abuse and a history of disruptive behavior in preschool, leading to expulsion—­an ominous sign in a child his age. Frederick and his 8-year-old brother had normal birth histories and a developmental context featuring parental acrimony punctuated by numerous moves. There was substance abuse and bipolar illness on the father’s side, and depression on the mother’s side. In contrast to his brother, Frederick was described from birth as hyperactive. He already carried an ADHD diagnosis, and the parents fought about whether to medicate him. Frederick frequently tried to coerce his brother into aggressive sexual acts. Much of their play was unsupervised. He made many references to pornography. After considerable clinical work with both parents, the child began treatment. From the outset, he appeared out of control, hyperactive, and in continual need of limit setting and restraint. His immature speech and expressive language complicated the situation. Receptive language was also a concern; he appeared not to understand what was said to him, but rather reacted to internal cues, or to what he quickly guessed was being asked. The therapist was struck by his efforts to control all aspects of every interaction. He would dart out of the office to check on the person waiting for him, demand water and food, and ask to take toys home. When told that the toys needed to stay in the office, he tried to steal them. When the therapist prevented the theft, Frederick called him “mean,” said he hated him and planned to kill him, and threatened to leave. Most early sessions devolved into battles for control. Frederick had almost no frustration tolerance. The slightest mistake in drawing or building led to meltdowns and tantrums; he would throw things, curse, and crumple

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 305
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 305 papers. He seemed inconsolable. If solutions did not come quickly, he gave up angrily and hopelessly. Many behaviors were sexualized, he would masturbate and would suck the tail of a toy dinosaur, putting it into a heating vent with great frenzy. Themes of killing and tor...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 305 papers. He seemed inconsolable. If solutions did not come quickly, he gave up angrily and hopelessly. Many behaviors were sexualized, he would masturbate and would suck the tail of a toy dinosaur, putting it into a heating vent with great frenzy. Themes of killing and torturing animals permeated his play and speech. He seemed terribly uncomfortable in his own body, constantly stimulated himself, and (in his own way) demanded limits, support, and nurture. His mother felt that his behavior in sessions was typical of his behavior at her home, but his father tended to minimize its occurrence at his home and did not find it especially problematic. Both parents said Frederick was accident-­prone and needed constant supervision. He would often lie in the middle of a rural road when frustrated, threatening to kill himself. SC7.4 Suicidality Suicide among young children is likely to be underreported. It is seldom officially noted as a cause of death, and figures on suicide in children under age 10 years are not reported. A key rationale for underreporting is the general belief that children lack a sufficient cognitive understanding of death to contemplate suicide. Research indicates, however, that children do think about and discuss death, and gradually understand the concept. Experiences related to death are common and important in childhood, and children begin at an early age to try to understand it. Four components of the biological–­scientific concept of death have been identified: universality, irreversibility, nonfunctionally, and causality. When assessing suicidality in children, clinicians should keep developmental norms in mind. The consensus of scientists is that children under age 5 do not recognize death as final but think of it as reversible. Children between ages 5 and 9 years may continue to consider death temporary, but by age 7, they are thought to be cognitively capable of seeing death as irreversible and permanent. Age is not the only or even the central factor in how death is conceived of by children. Clinical reports indicate that some children as young as age 4 or 5 realize the finality of death. Some younger children can recognize the irreversibility of death but are relatively unable to verbalize or bear the associated pain for more than brief periods. Children can conceive of death, and young children can better accept the finality of death, if provided with empathically imparted honest information. Suicidal thoughts are not uncommon in children. They affect boys and girls equally. A major distinction is made between “suicide attempts,” which reflect the clear intention of dying, and “suicide gestures,” understood as communication without the intent to kill oneself. Except for children between ages 4 and 11, gender differences have been found in rates of suicide attempts, gestures, and deliberate self-harm. Suicide attempts after age 11 occur predominantly in males, while suicide gestures and deliberate self-harm are seen mostly in females. Suicide attempts early in life are particularly ominous, because they significantly lower the threshold for, and increase the probability of, subsequent attempts later in life. Research indicates that suicidal behavior runs in families, and that the risk of such behavior is transmitted across generations. Youth with predominantly fearful or preoccupied attachments are more likely to harbor suicidal thoughts than those with more secure or dismissing attachment styles. Serious adversities during childhood, such as physical and sexual abuse, neglect, family violence, physical illness, and financial strain, significantly increase the risk and persistence of suicidality from childhood through adulthood. Such adversities have been linked to suicidal ideation, suicide

chunks.heading: 306 Childhood
chunks.preview: 306 Childhood attempts, and suicide gestures in children under 18. Sexual abuse or repeated traumatic events increases greatly the probability of a suicide attempt in children. Extrapolations to children from findings in adults suggest that school difficulties, presence of mania, and diagnosed psych...
chunks.text: 306 Childhood attempts, and suicide gestures in children under 18. Sexual abuse or repeated traumatic events increases greatly the probability of a suicide attempt in children. Extrapolations to children from findings in adults suggest that school difficulties, presence of mania, and diagnosed psychiatric disorders (especially those with affective, impulsive, and aggressive symptoms, e.g., depressive episodes, conduct disorder, and antisocial personality disorder) increase the risk of later suicide attempts. Subjective Experience of Suicidality in Childhood Affective States Children who express intense feelings of rage, sadness, and shame should alert clinicians to the possibility of suicidal feelings. Such children have a lower tolerance for affect and a lower threshold for distress than others. They frequently explain their actions in terms of wanting to stop unbearable feelings or escape from painful situations. Risk factors include hopelessness, anhedonia, impulsiveness, and high emotional reactivity. These children can struggle with feelings of omnipotence, manifested by a need for power and control of others, as well as its opposite: profound feelings of hopelessness and helplessness. An attitude of “I don’t care” or “It doesn’t matter” can barely conceal underlying feelings of rage and dysphoria. These states often vary in relationship to feeling cared about; it is important to inquire into a child’s feelings about death, as well as specific thoughts of suicide. Children’s tolerance for pain is strongly defended against and limited. Parents of a youngster with suicidal ideation may behave with considerable denial about the seriousness of their child’s struggle with suicidal feelings and intentions. Thoughts and Fantasies Thoughts and fantasies of children struggling with suicide are intimately tied to tolerating and managing painful feelings. Unremitting, hopeless ruminations about painful relationships with family and peers are common. Suicidal children have often been victims of bullying, exclusion, and shaming. They may see themselves as a burden, uncared for and unwanted. Fantasies that follow a loss can center on themes of reunion, restoration, and remorse. The act of killing oneself is inherently aggressive; it often has the quality of retaliation and revenge toward family members, authorities, and peers. Anticipated acts of suicide invariably carry particular meanings, including thoughts about how others will feel at the funeral. In-depth case histories frequently reveal that a suicidal child was curious about the death of a person and/or animal, and asked questions about what happens after death. Some suicidal children are remarkably accident-­prone, often to a point where parents either deny injury or become emotionally blunted to the suicidal intentions in these “accidents.” Somatic States The presence of abuse and/or an atypical or incipient mood disorder suggests that somatic states in some suicidal children can be linked to mood and to the physiological responses associated with physical intrusion, fear, rage, and despair. Many children with sensory processing disorders feel there is “something wrong” with their bodies, as they suffer from allergic reactions, sleep disturbance, and eating difficulties. One

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 307
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 307 senses that “nothing is quite right” for these children, and that suicide can be a desperate way to “end it all.” Relationship Patterns Perceptions of family and social support, as well as the experience of parental warmth, have been empirically established as protective...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 307 senses that “nothing is quite right” for these children, and that suicide can be a desperate way to “end it all.” Relationship Patterns Perceptions of family and social support, as well as the experience of parental warmth, have been empirically established as protective factors against suicide attempts. Conversely, a child’s sense of rejection can often serve as a risk or precipitating factor. Suicidality always takes place within the context of relationships. Numerous clinical studies of the sequence of events leading to suicide attempts implicate early, developmentally formative connections, especially the mother–­child relationship. Core conflicts revolve around issues of soothing/containment, separation, the pleasure economy, and pathological modes of relating and communicating. The documented risk factors for suicide, including attachment style, transgenerational transmission of suicidality, comorbid diagnoses, abuse, and lack of parental warmth, all bear significantly on relationships. The pervasive cultural denial of suicide makes it difficult for both parents and professionals to discuss it openly and truthfully. Few schools acknowledge suicide as the cause of a student’s death, in stark contrast to protocols for addressing children’s deaths from other causes. Clinically, it is important to discuss suicide and death with both parents and children in an empathic, developmentally informed, and unvaryingly truthful way. This is especially the case when there is a family history of suicide, which has often become a family secret. Therapeutic Indications Treatment should encompass both the acute management of suicidal attempts and the treatment of associated mental disorders. Hospitalization may be necessary, and clinicians should anticipate that these children may express persistent thoughts of death and endure prolonged suffering. Consequently, treatment should persist until their mental state or level of suicidality stabilizes. Psychotherapy should address the mental disorders associated with suicidal attempts, with a focus on the family as a whole. Clinicians may also require support themselves to manage the distress that such situations can evoke. Clinical Illustration Heather, a 10-year-old girl, was brought to treatment by her mother after a potentially lethal suicide attempt involving aspirin. Despite Heather’s preoccupations with death, paralyzing anxiety, and episodes of depression, her mother had not recognized that her daughter had either the wish or the capacity to take her own life. Her naïveté was striking. Both parents reported that Heather had been difficult to soothe as an infant, intolerant of environmental changes, and prone to sensory sensitivities. She had been colicky and displayed sudden, unpredictable movements, beginning “serious tantrums” at 14 months. In response, her parents attempted to appease her demands, establishing a pattern of accommodation that led to homeschooling, excessive dependency on caregivers, and repeated short-lived attempts at therapy. The parent–­child relationship became inverted, with Heather “calling the shots” and her parents complying. Her 14-year-old brother faded into the background.

chunks.heading: 308 Childhood
chunks.preview: 308 Childhood Heather had been breastfed until age 3, then abruptly weaned due to her mother’s thyroid condition. On formula, she experienced severe pain and vomiting, which seemed to set a precedent for overwhelming reactions to later changes. Until recently, she had wet her bed once or twice a wee...
chunks.text: 308 Childhood Heather had been breastfed until age 3, then abruptly weaned due to her mother’s thyroid condition. On formula, she experienced severe pain and vomiting, which seemed to set a precedent for overwhelming reactions to later changes. Until recently, she had wet her bed once or twice a week. In preschool, interactions with peers were anxiety provoking, and birthday parties became tolerable only when her mother managed all the planning. She frequently felt “left out” and reacted strongly to perceived slights. Despite her difficulties, Heather showed considerable strengths. She was intellectually gifted, perfectionistic, and academically successful. She demonstrated empathy and read others well. She described herself as shy and intensely lonely around others. Her sad, waif-like appearance and poor eye contact underscored her vulnerability. She admitted, “I’ve threatened to kill myself. No, I tried to kill myself, not just threatened.” She had reported severe anxiety since early childhood and described her depression as beginning shortly after the end of fifth grade. When it “set in,” she felt unable to cope and determined to end her life. Assessment revealed a child born with an insecure temperament, which overwhelmed her caregivers. Her parents surrendered authority to her intense emotions, giving in to her outbursts in ways that provided short-term relief but failed to foster resilience. Heather met criteria for generalized anxiety disorder, major depressive disorder, emerging dependent personality disorder, and, most significantly, suicidality. Her suicide attempt was neither impulsive nor situational. It reflected a lifelong experience of severe emotional pain and alienation from her family and body. The act served as a means of ending her suffering and punishing those she felt had failed her. Afterward, she appeared emboldened, deriving a sense of power from her ability to impact others. Risk factors included multiple comorbid diagnoses, a serious suicide attempt, and early-onset sensory sensitivity and depression. Protective factors included high intelligence, a well-­educated and caring family, and access to residential treatment. SC8 Disorders Related Primarily to Anxiety Fear and anxiety are universal human emotions that have evolved as signals of danger and uncertainty about our ability to control major events and to maintain bonds with important others. Emotion theorists often distinguish between fear and anxiety: Fear motivates immediate escape or avoidance, whereas anxiety concerns unavoidable events (e.g., an examination). Panksepp and Biven (2012) locate fear and anxiety in different areas of the brain, mediated by different neurotransmitters. The former (the fear system) concerns external stimuli seen as dangerous, the latter (the panic–grief system) concerns separation from a needed attachment figure. Psychoanalysts have called the former “annihilation anxiety” or “paranoid anxiety,” and the latter “separation anxiety.” For both children and adults, the experience of anxiety depends, decisively, on an appraisal of coping potential; that is, whether an individual feels able to master the threat or challenge. In its most adaptive function, fear motivates caution and preparedness. Mild to moderate anxiety often stimulates constructive problem solving. Severe anxiety, however, typically disrupts concentration and other cognitive processes, including perspective taking and long-term planning. Like all emotions, anxiety has characteristic cognitive, affective, physiological, and behavioral components.

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 309
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 309 It involves increased autonomic arousal, a narrowing of attention, increased vigilance, and an inhibition of exploratory behavior. Chronic anxiety is likely to have significant consequences for all aspects of a child’s life and is a risk factor for many other emotional an...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 309 It involves increased autonomic arousal, a narrowing of attention, increased vigilance, and an inhibition of exploratory behavior. Chronic anxiety is likely to have significant consequences for all aspects of a child’s life and is a risk factor for many other emotional and behavioral disorders. Anxiety has a central role in the history of psychoanalytic theory and remains a fundamental principle of a psychodynamic understanding of both symptoms and personality. Some degree of anxiety is present in almost all humans. Anxiety is more than a symptom; it is a dynamic process, a feeling state that sets in motion both adaptive coping responses and maladaptive actions. Throughout life, how well we cope with anxiety determines, to a large extent, our emotional health. Biological risk factors for developing anxiety disorders in childhood, evident in children’s temperament early in life, have been reliably established. There is a strong genetic influence on anxiety disorders; scientists are investigating specific genes and gene–­environment interactions that may predispose children to anxiety. Neuroscience research has mapped brain regions and neural pathways associated with fear and anxiety (Beesdo et al., 2009; Jovanovic, Nylocks, & Gamwell, 2013). Family risk factors have also been studied. Insecure attachment, marital conflict, and high levels of parental criticism and overcontrol are associated with increased risk of childhood anxiety disorders (Knappe et al., 2009). Less often studied, but of equal importance, are experiences outside the home—­especially the impact of learning disorders, peer rejection, and bullying. Anxiety is a daily experience in the lives of children, as they anticipate interactions with their parents, teachers, and peers. Children may be anxious in any new situation (e.g., the first day of school or summer camp). They are anxious about possible criticism and punishment; about separation from attachment figures; about possible illness, injury, pain, and death; and about experiences that evoke feelings of shame (e.g., social rejection or academic failure). An anxiety disorder is diagnosed when a children’s experience of anxiety causes significant distress over an extended period of time and/or interferes with the child’s ability to participate in normal activities. Six principal anxiety disorders have been identified (general anxiety disorder, separation anxiety disorder, social anxiety disorder, specific phobias, panic disorder, and selective mutism), with diagnostic criteria for each. Current research indicates a high co-­occurrence of these disorders in individual children. But these symptom patterns occur separately with sufficient frequency to justify their identification as distinct syndromes. They must be distinguished from typical developmental anxieties and fears. The assessment of any anxious child (or any child who, for extended periods of time, avoids normal, age-­appropriate activities) should include an exploration of the situations or demands in the child’s current life that may be contributing to anxiety or avoidance. The difficulty of diagnosing these disorders is often connected with parents’ inability to understand that children need others to regulate themselves. Many hold the mistaken belief that maturation will resolve their children’s anxieties. Caregivers may unwittingly encourage children’s avoidance by changing life around them. To make the diagnosis, adults must make an inference about the emotional states of children. Inconsolable crying, clinging, tantrums, attempts to hide, or avoidance suggest anxiety. These manifestations are common to children of all ages and to all types of anxiety disorders. In addition to avoidance and inhibition, children make use of defenses (e.g., denial, repression, displacement, selective inattention, and dissociation) that minimize

chunks.heading: 310 Childhood
chunks.preview: 310 Childhood their experience of anxiety. Children’s anxiety may also be masked by oppositional behavior or an attitude of indifference. Anxiety is often comorbid with other mood disorders. SC8.1 Generalized Anxiety Disorder Despite their differences, generalized anxiety disorder, separation anxiet...
chunks.text: 310 Childhood their experience of anxiety. Children’s anxiety may also be masked by oppositional behavior or an attitude of indifference. Anxiety is often comorbid with other mood disorders. SC8.1 Generalized Anxiety Disorder Despite their differences, generalized anxiety disorder, separation anxiety disorder, and social anxiety disorder often occur together. In the following we note their symptomatologic distinctions, but we cover their common general characteristics only once. Factors influencing these disorders include temperament, family issues, and adverse experiences. To diagnose them, clinicians must consider the presence of depression, PTSD, delirium, and medical or drug-­induced conditions; all these can induce secondary anxiety states. Comorbidities include other anxiety disorders, depression, and behavioral disorders. Generalized anxiety disorder is characterized by excessive and persistent anxiety and worry. Concerns may relate to future events, the appropriateness of past behaviors, and concerns about competence in one or more domains. Anxieties are manifested in multiple situations, relationships, and activities, and are not specific and may change focus. Children with this problem are unable to regulate their anxiety on their own and may continually require caregiver intervention. There is hyperarousal and avoidance of worrisome situations, but the lack of connection with a specific situation or event is the main feature of this disorder. Girls are twice as likely to suffer from anxiety as boys. Subjective Experience of Generalized Anxiety Disorder in Childhood Affective States A state of anguish and constant alertness torments these children. In their attempts to avoid situations that make them anxious, they may make urgent, insistent demands. An anxious child is often described as clingy, controlling, defiant, or stubborn. Thoughts and Fantasies When children are chronically anxious, or when their anxiety is easily evoked, their thoughts, imagination, behavior, and relationships are all drawn into the orbit of their anxious mood. Because anxious children expect or imagine that bad things will happen, they may avoid or withdraw from common activities. or run away to restore their feeling of safety. Somatic States Anxiety may be more varied in its expression than any other emotion. Agitation, fatigue, difficulty concentrating, irritability, muscle tension, eating and sleep disorders, general arousal hyperactivation, somatic complaints, anger, dysregulated behavior, frequent nightmares, sleeping disorders, and regressive behaviors are all common expressions of anxiety in children. They often deny that they are anxious, but instead complain of physical symptoms (e.g., stomachaches, headaches, nausea, or frequent urination).

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 311
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 311 Relationship Patterns When parents (or clinicians) regard a child’s behavior as demanding or controlling (rather than as anxious), they often respond with anger, criticism, or punishment. This common misunderstanding then sets in motion a vicious cycle of negative interac...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 311 Relationship Patterns When parents (or clinicians) regard a child’s behavior as demanding or controlling (rather than as anxious), they often respond with anger, criticism, or punishment. This common misunderstanding then sets in motion a vicious cycle of negative interactions, leading to both continued anxiety and escalating family conflict. Continuity Anxiety disorders show homotypic continuity with the same disorder at later ages. Those who suffer it may change the content of their worries but remain highly anxious. The more typical childhood anxiety disorders such as separation anxiety have notable heterotypic continuity. Anxiety disorders can lead to depression and conduct disorders. Therapeutic Indications It is wise to consider all possible determinants of a child’s anxiety as avenues for exploration and therapeutic intervention. The evidence suggests that even relatively shortterm psychotherapy can reduce anxiety symptoms for a considerable period. Longer-­ term therapy may foster more lasting improvement, especially when other disorders are present. Clinical Illustration Hanna, a bright 9-year-old girl, excelled academically and had many friends but experienced frequent tantrums. During homework time, she often became overwhelmed, finding excuses to delay, such as needing a different pencil or seat. Small mistakes triggered dramatic emotional responses. She also had meltdowns over minor frustrations, such as being told to stop using her iPad. She protested intensely, saying things like, “No! I have to . . . ” or “I need to. . . . ” On school mornings, she struggled to choose what to wear and was often late. Every Sunday night, she had a tantrum. She had difficulty falling asleep and often seemed tired. On weekends, she was hard to wake up and sometimes missed activities she normally enjoyed, like skiing. Hanna’s behavior created ongoing stress within the family. Her parents had become frustrated—­first with her, then with each other. While they understood that her difficult behavior stemmed from anxiety dysregulation, they also viewed her as spoiled, demanding, and controlling. Treatment involved individual therapy for Hanna and family counseling. The focus was on helping Hanna tolerate anxiety and regulate emotions, and on guiding her parents to respond in more supportive ways, reversing destructive family interaction cycles. SC8.2 Separation Anxiety Disorder The general features of separation anxiety disorder in children are similar to those of generalized anxiety disorder. These children show excessive, developmentally atypical levels of anxiety related to separation from home or caregivers. Reassurances are of no use in regulating their anxiety. It is excessive and recurring even in anticipation of separation, with persistent worries, refusal of activities that lead outside the home,

chunks.heading: 312 Childhood
chunks.preview: 312 Childhood fears of staying home alone, and sleep problems. Every separation is accompanied by physical and psychic symptoms. Such children are often preoccupied with thoughts that something bad will happen to their attachment figures in their absence, or that they will suddenly leave. They appea...
chunks.text: 312 Childhood fears of staying home alone, and sleep problems. Every separation is accompanied by physical and psychic symptoms. Such children are often preoccupied with thoughts that something bad will happen to their attachment figures in their absence, or that they will suddenly leave. They appear clingy, like shadows of their caregivers, with no urge to move away from home even for pleasant things (e.g., being with friends). When separated from their caregivers, they can withdraw into apathy or sadness and may seek constant contact. Separation anxiety can be accompanied by varied fears, homesickness, and aggression. It is the most common anxiety disorder in children, and its prevalence decreases with age. In clinical samples, there are no differences between males and females, while in the general population, females suffer more. Other mood or anxiety disorders may be present. Family factors can be particularly significant. The hyperprotectiveness, intrusiveness, or neglect of caregivers can favor the presence of this disorder, which effectively prevents the drive for autonomy and growth. A vicious cycle begins between children and parents from which it can be very hard to escape, making therapeutic intervention necessary. The fear that outside the home there is only pain and despair makes these children feel trapped, but even their caregivers may feel they have no escape. Adverse events in the family, such as the loss of a parent, separation and/or divorce, illness, and accidents may influence the problem. SC8.3 Social Anxiety Disorder The general features of social anxiety disorder in children are also similar to those of generalized anxiety disorder. Sufferers of social anxiety disorder fear one or more social or performance situations that involve exposure to unfamiliar people or possible judgment from others. These children are withdrawn, shy, submissive, and physically rigid, frequently subject to conversion symptoms. Fear and anxiety are greatly disproportionate to their age and situation. Exposure to feared social situations may cause panic, crying, screaming, tantrums, freezing, clinging, shrinking, or the inability to speak, and consequently, to the avoidance of feared situations. Social anxiety is expressed not only toward adults, whom a child may fear for various reasons, but also and especially toward peers. Children with social anxiety fear negative evaluation, and fear that their anxiety itself will make them the object of ridicule. In situations they do not construe as dangerous, these children behave in completely normal ways. It is therefore risky to remove them from all social situations. Their discomfort will no longer be perceived, but it will also not be overcome, and their autonomy and movement through developmental milestones may be thwarted. Mistreatment is a risk factor for this disorder, which shows homotypic continuity. If left untreated, some children with social anxiety disorder engage in a massive withdrawal in adolescence and become vulnerable to addiction to the internet, to videogaming, or to substances. SC8.4 Specific Phobias A phobia can develop early in life. Although many children are excessively fearful at some time during childhood, a severe or prolonged phobia may lead to serious complications in a child’s overall emotional adjustment. Risk factors for a child’s development of a phobia include an anxious temperament, family psychopathology (especially an anxiety disorder in a first-­degree relative), and early developmental difficulties. Specific phobias may develop following a traumatic event (e.g., being attacked by an animal or trapped in an elevator).

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 313
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 313 From a psychodynamic perspective, a phobia can be understood as symbolizing unconscious anxieties about separation, injury, or death. Anxiety about these basic dangers is displaced onto an object that is less threatening, either because it is inherently less dangerous or...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 313 From a psychodynamic perspective, a phobia can be understood as symbolizing unconscious anxieties about separation, injury, or death. Anxiety about these basic dangers is displaced onto an object that is less threatening, either because it is inherently less dangerous or because of its decreased proximity. The process by which the phobic object becomes associated with a more general danger is usually not accessible; children are rarely able to explain how they became afraid of a benign object. A phobia resolves when a child is able to master the original anxiety, making the displacement no longer necessary. A child’s phobia is usually limited to a specific situation (e.g., flying, heights, animals, infections, the sight of blood). In some cases, however, the child’s anxiety may spread from one object to many. The course of children’s phobias is extremely variable, lasting from days to years. Children with phobias may cry, have tantrums, freeze, or cling. These reactions are not transitory and always appear in the presence of the phobic stimulus. Their abject fear, disproportionate to the object or their age, often triggers urgent preoccupations with avoidance. Especially in young children, this avoidance is not always detectable. Subjective Experience of Specific Phobias in Childhood Affective States If there is fear or intense anxiety in one or more situations in which a child feels exposed, a sense of rejection or humiliation may affect schoolwork and disable the child in other areas. As long as a child is able to avoid the phobic stimulus, there is minimal anxiety. When the feared object or situation becomes unavoidable, however, the child characteristically feels severe anxiety that can escalate into panic. Thoughts and Fantasies The phobic object and strategies to elude it may dominate the thoughts and fantasies of afflicted children, or they may avoid thinking about the topic entirely. Overall, the ideational lives of children with phobias may be constricted with respect to certain age-­expected emotional themes, such as competition and aggression, and intensified with respect to other themes, such as dependency relationships. Somatic States Children with phobias may express their intense fear by crying or having tantrums that may persist for long periods. Some are hypervigilant about their environment, even in the absence of phobic objects. They often develop somatic symptoms associated with the anticipation of exposure to the phobic object: tightening in the chest, stomachaches or a hollow feeling in the stomach, rapid heartbeat, loss of appetite, frequent urges to urinate or defecate, nausea, and vomiting. The intensity of these physical complaints depends on the severity of the fear. The feelings dissipate if the phobic object is removed or the situation is avoided. Relationship Patterns These children often approach relationships with caution. They may assimilate new situations and people only gradually. Their capacity for attachment relationships,

chunks.heading: 314 Childhood
chunks.preview: 314 Childhood however, tends to be unimpaired; they are often quite dependent on their caregivers, despite the fact that no amount of parental reassurance diminishes their fright in the presence of the phobic object. Continuity Phobic disorders are often accompanied by other anxiety disorders, which...
chunks.text: 314 Childhood however, tends to be unimpaired; they are often quite dependent on their caregivers, despite the fact that no amount of parental reassurance diminishes their fright in the presence of the phobic object. Continuity Phobic disorders are often accompanied by other anxiety disorders, which may persist even when the phobia fades. If left untreated, phobias generally show a homotypic continuity. Therapeutic Indications Treatment of phobias follows different paths depending on the therapeutic approach. Psychodynamic therapists work both emotionally and cognitively on the areas in which they believe the phobia represents a displacement. The phobia is not the center of the treatment in this case, only the starting point. Cognitive-­behavioral therapists focus more on the symptom, using exposure therapies to reduce the child’s phobic reactions. Clinical Illustration Mary, a 4-year-old girl, was hospitalized with a life-­threatening illness. Her parents told her she had to fight the “little bugs” that were making her ill. During her recovery, she had to stay in the hospital for several months and at home for several more. In this period, she developed a phobia of all bugs (insects, arachnids), for which she had a successful 8-month treatment with psychodynamic psychotherapy. Her favorite game was to imagine that she was Princess Rapunzel, trapped in a tower by an evil witch who kept her away from her parents. Through this game, she was able to symbolically express and process her feelings of anger and frustration toward her mother (and her therapist), the “evil witch” who kept her confined. She talked about her fear of being killed by bugs associated with her illness, and her fears of retaliation from her parents because of her anger. SC8.5 Panic Disorder Although panic disorder is quite rare in children, older patients often report that in childhood they had “fearful spells” (attacks of anxiety that do not quite meet the criteria for a panic attack). Panic disorder consists of several sudden attacks of intense fear or discomfort, with strong physical arousal, reaching a peak in a few moments. Adolescents and adults describe fears of dying, fears that the panic attack indicates a serious illness, and fears about the frequency of attacks. In children, in contrast, panic attacks are more associated with severe separation anxiety and particular events. They are not unexpected or “out of the blue.” Noncatastrophic interpretations of the symptoms of panic are more typical. SC8.6 Selective Mutism Selective mutism, formerly called “elective mutism,” because of the assumption that the child unconsciously chooses (elects) not to talk, is characterized by the absence of speech in nonfamilial or social settings where speaking is expected. The child typically communicates normally with family members or close friends but is silent in the

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 315
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 315 presence of other children, adults, or teachers and classmates. Diagnosis requires the mutism to have persisted more than 1 month and not to be attributable to lack of language proficiency or another disorder (e.g., communication disorder, ASD, psychotic disorder). The on...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 315 presence of other children, adults, or teachers and classmates. Diagnosis requires the mutism to have persisted more than 1 month and not to be attributable to lack of language proficiency or another disorder (e.g., communication disorder, ASD, psychotic disorder). The onset of selective mutism is typically in early childhood, often before age 5, and it becomes clinically apparent when the child begins school and refrains from speaking in class. Its prevalence varies between 0.3 and 1.9% (Muris & Ollendick, 2021), depending on the samples in different studies. Some research finds a slightly higher female-­to-male ratio (Monzo, Micotti, & Rashid, 2015). Evidence suggests that symptoms are sometimes transient, resolving spontaneously after a short period. In many cases, however, they persist for much longer, disrupting the child’s social life and academic development. Selective mutism is recognized as a heterogeneous disorder that may involve various psychopathological and developmental factors. Many studies of children with this problem find a high co-­occurrence of anxiety disorders, particularly social anxiety (Viana, Beidel, & Rabian, 2009). Anxiety, social phobia, and selective mutism have been observed to run in families. Acculturation anxiety has been linked to the condition, as it appears more frequently among immigrants who are required to speak the language of their new country. Some clinicians view mutism as a compensatory strategy or reaction formation intended to alleviate anxiety in social situations or to manage internal relational conflicts. Selective mutism has been proposed as a developmental subtype of social anxiety, even though social anxiety typically emerges around the age of 10, because the two conditions have high comorbidity and involve similar personality traits. Consequently, DSM-5 and DSM-5-TR categorized selective mutism under anxiety disorders. Despite extensive research, however, specific etiological factors underlying mutism remain unclear. Previously, experts associated it with early emotional trauma or abuse, as well as with oppositional defiant disorder. The trauma hypothesis stemmed from cases in which it had begun after a traumatic event, particularly sexual abuse. Recent epidemiological studies have cast doubt on the trauma etiology theory. And although selectively mute children may behave in contrarian ways, it is unclear whether oppositional defiant disorder is causally related to mutism. For children, withholding words can convey a sense of omnipotence and control over the environment, and their anxiety and inability to speak may lead to disruptive behavior. Although clinicians and other adults encountering the child’s mutism may perceive the silence as an aggressive or rebellious act, it is not under the child’s conscious control. Consequently, the interpretation of mutism has evolved over time. Rather than being seen as an oppositional behavior, it is now viewed as a panic-like, freezing reaction—­the main reason for the shift from “elective” to “selective.” Contemporary theories suggest that mutism may stem from a shy, inhibited temperament accompanied by neuropsychological delays. Selective mutism often coexists with other problems, such as obsessive–­compulsive disorder, language delays, and elimination disorders. While it may share features with the ASDs, DSM-5-TR excludes an autistic diagnosis as a criterion for diagnosing selective mutism. Existing speech and language issues in selectively mute children may become more noticeable when they are exposed at school to the language skills of peers. Some theorists propose that language difficulties can provoke anxiety, leading the child to be hesitant to speak and potentially become mute. The coexistence of enuresis and encopresis in children with selective mutism has prompted speculation that

chunks.heading: 316 Childhood
chunks.preview: 316 Childhood a neurodevelopmental immaturity underlies both disorders (Sharkey & McNicholas, 2008). An alternative interpretation may lie in the connection between sphincter control and language development, both of which occur during the same period in a child’s life. From a psychoanalytic perspec...
chunks.text: 316 Childhood a neurodevelopmental immaturity underlies both disorders (Sharkey & McNicholas, 2008). An alternative interpretation may lie in the connection between sphincter control and language development, both of which occur during the same period in a child’s life. From a psychoanalytic perspective, mutism could be viewed as a form of verbal constipation, in which the child withholds words to suppress aggressive feelings toward a person, thereby preserving their relationship. The psychoanalytic literature contains additional etiological hypotheses, not mutually exclusive and possibly synergistic, for the onset or persistence of mutism, primarily derived from therapies of selectively mute patients. One theory connects the condition with issues of separation from a symbiotic bond with the mother. The child’s dependency needs may lead to a desire to regress to a stage when words were not needed for communication. Or it may arise when speaking becomes entangled in conflict; the child unconsciously perceives speaking as a hostile act and remains silent as a defense against aggressive impulses. Such impulses may be partially satisfied, as silence itself can also carry a hostile quality. Family dynamics also play a role in the development and persistence of selective mutism. Marital dissatisfaction in parents, for instance, may contribute to a dependency relationship with the child, reinforcing its symptoms (Monzo et al., 2015). Parents of selectively mute children are often shy, socially anxious, and prone to schizotypal behaviors, suggesting a familial predisposition to the condition (Monzo et al., 2015; Viana et al., 2009). Despite various etiological hypotheses, the exact mechanisms underlying selective mutism remain elusive. Further research is needed to elucidate the complex interplay of factors contributing to the onset and persistence of this disorder. Subjective Experience of Selective Mutism in Childhood Affective States Children with selective mutism often are shy and easily embarrassed in social situations. They tend to fear new experiences and interactions outside their close family circle, leading them to avoid such situations. When they perceive an environment as unsafe, they feel a strong mixture of anxiety and aggression. Thoughts and Fantasies The selectively mute child appears comfortable speaking with family members, but ceases talking when encountering strangers, adopting a “freeze reaction” apparently as a safety measure. The child seems to feel an overwhelming sense of danger, finding solace in silence as a means of self-­protection. Mutism can serve as a defense that shields children from interactions that feel threatening, while allowing them to regain a sense of control and even omnipotence. Reliance on family members for autonomy and independence appears to undermine their sense of efficacy, as their presence is seen as essential for survival. Such fantasies may lead to the development of separation anxiety disorder or school phobia. Somatic States The somatic experiences of a child with selective mutism closely resemble those of children with anxiety disorders. Heightened activation of the sympathetic nervous system

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 317
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 317 can result in a frozen reaction, eventually leading to behavioral and vocal withdrawal, such as standing still and remaining silent. Somatic complaints are common as well. Relationship Patterns Lack of speech in the presence of peers has a significant impact on a child’s...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 317 can result in a frozen reaction, eventually leading to behavioral and vocal withdrawal, such as standing still and remaining silent. Somatic complaints are common as well. Relationship Patterns Lack of speech in the presence of peers has a significant impact on a child’s social interactions. Selectively mute children often avoid social engagements, preferring nonverbal play with their classmates, particularly in school settings, where they are generally accepted by peers. They typically remain on the periphery of groups, however, and their inability to speak may occasionally disrupt their social connections, leading to feelings of isolation. Their reliance on family members, with whom they often maintain a strong symbiotic bond, exacerbates their dependence on others for communication. The parent–­ child relationship is suffused with ambivalence, which reinforces the child’s emotional challenges when separated. This dynamic expresses a combination of helplessness, anger, and need for control within the relationship. Caregivers are frequently described as overprotective and overinvolved, further perpetuating the child’s reliance on them. When this occurs, the relationship with the other parent tends to be distant, with that parent assuming a peripheral and passive role. Overall, these family dynamics often reflect an insecure attachment pattern that hinders the child’s ability to develop trust in relationships. Continuity Usually, the symptom endures for more than 5 years. In some cases, it remains relatively stable and then suddenly disappears during adolescence or young adulthood, while in others, it gradually diminishes until it ceases altogether. Some clinicians suggest that the symptom does persist into adolescence but is manifested differently, as prepsychotic or psychotic behavior (Monzo et al., 2015). Others argue that communication difficulties may persist into adulthood or develop into social phobia during adolescence or adulthood. Longitudinal studies and investigations of adults who experienced the disorder in childhood indicate remission rates ranging from 39 to 100% (Sharkey & McNicholas, 2008). In adulthood, individuals affected by mutism often describe themselves as having low self-­confidence, lack of independence, and diminished motivation to achieve academically. Therapeutic Indications Selective mutism is considered one of the most resistant symptoms to therapeutic intervention. Early identification of the issue can facilitate prompt intervention and potentially prevent or reduce the social and educational challenges the child may face. Recent literature increasingly supports cognitive-­behavioral and pharmacological treatments, moving away from previous suggestions for psychodynamic therapies. Symptoms can significantly impede a child’s academic progress and social life, particularly during crucial developmental periods. Therefore, therapists should be prepared to recommend comprehensive treatment strategies aimed at reducing symptom severity, while enhancing the child’s overall mental and social well-being. The multitude of factors contributing to the development of this disorder, and the fact of its frequent comorbidities (social phobia, generalized anxiety, obsessive–­compulsive disorder, separation anxiety, elimination disorders, adverse family conditions, trauma), suggest that it stems

chunks.heading: 318 Childhood
chunks.preview: 318 Childhood from various neurodevelopmental, psychological, and social challenges. Consequently, when determining the best approach, clinicians should not focus solely on observing the symptom; they should also assess the child’s personality, mental functioning, and family dynamics, and understand...
chunks.text: 318 Childhood from various neurodevelopmental, psychological, and social challenges. Consequently, when determining the best approach, clinicians should not focus solely on observing the symptom; they should also assess the child’s personality, mental functioning, and family dynamics, and understand their interactions with the child’s mental capacities. This holistic approach can guide clinicians toward the most suitable treatments, which may include individual psychotherapy, parent–­child psychotherapy, family therapy, language and speech therapy, and medical interventions, among others. Clinical Illustration Layan was 6 years old when she was referred for psychotherapy. She was unable to speak to anyone outside of her extended family and a neighborhood friend. Initially, her parents attributed her silence to excessive shyness, but once she began school, her selective mutism became clear. She used gestures to communicate with teachers and peers, and relied on her childhood friend, also a classmate, as a communication intermediary. Although she played with peers and showed preference for some of them, she did not speak to them. Interestingly, she participated in a school chorus and sang normally during group performances. Her maternal grandmother had been diagnosed with schizophrenia during Layan’s mother’s childhood. The mother had experienced a difficult upbringing but remained emotionally close to her mother. This closeness extended to Layan, whom she breastfed for 2.5 years. When faced with Layan’s mutism, the mother became even more fearful of having passed on her mother’s illness—­a fear she kept secret from her daughter. Layan’s father worked long hours and remained emotionally distant from the mother–­daughter relationship. The couple’s marital difficulties were ongoing but unspoken. At the beginning of therapy, Layan appeared tense and nearly motionless, with wide eyes and a frightened demeanor. The therapist used drawing and Play-Doh to engage her. Layan gradually responded and began to enjoy their shared play. She eventually participated in singing softly with the therapist during sessions. Over time, she began to speak in a predictable pattern: babbling at the beginning of the session, speaking like a toddler midway through, and using age-­appropriate speech by the end. This process appeared to allow her to transition into a differentiated, communicative state. Her play frequently included stories about princesses trapped in towers and knights attempting to rescue them—­symbolic representations of her feelings of entrapment and her unconscious wish to remain close to her mother. By the end of treatment, Layan was able to speak with more of her peers and her teacher. However, speaking with strangers or outside of school remained a persistent challenge. SC9 Obsessive–Compulsive and Related Disorders SC9.1 Obsessive–Compulsive Disorder Obsessive–­compulsive disorder often appears during childhood and can become a chronic and highly disabling condition if left untreated. Its effects extend beyond the patients themselves, affecting their families and school environments. With a prevalence ranging from 1.8 to 5.5% (Nazeer, Latif, Mondal, Azeem, & Greydanus, 2020),

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 319
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 319 the condition is highly comorbid, particularly with anxiety, mood, and motor disorders such as tics. While obsessive–­compulsive disorder has some similarities across age ranges, its manifestations and most appropriate treatments are distinct. “Obsessions” (defined as int...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 319 the condition is highly comorbid, particularly with anxiety, mood, and motor disorders such as tics. While obsessive–­compulsive disorder has some similarities across age ranges, its manifestations and most appropriate treatments are distinct. “Obsessions” (defined as intrusive and nonsensical thoughts), and “compulsions” (repetitive behaviors aimed at alleviating anxiety), may not always align clearly in pediatric cases. Often, obsessions may be perceived as ego-­syntonic, and compulsions may not have an obvious connection to the obsessive thoughts. These behaviors can sometimes be mistaken for addictive habits or innocuous rituals, but appreciation of their emotional and functional impact is crucial for accurate diagnosis. Children may use a pretend mode as a defense against these mental states. In younger patients, defining the purpose of behaviors or mental acts can be challenging, leading to a higher frequency of compulsions than obsessions. Cognitive immaturity may prevent children from recognizing obsessive thoughts as irrational (Garcia et al., 2010). Distinguishing obsessive–­compulsive disorder from restrictive interests or stereotypies of children on the autistic spectrum can be subtle. The critical distinction lies in how these behaviors are manifested: In autism, they indicate difficulties in integrating mental states, and they aid in emotional regulation, whereas in obsessive–­compulsive disorder, there is typically clearer recognition of more alien obsessive symptoms and compulsions (Piras et al., 2022). Fortunately, this condition is one of the few mental health disorders for which, consistent with its well-­ established neurobiological basis, both psychological and pharmacological treatments have significant effectiveness (MacMaster & Rosenberg, 2021). Subjective Experience of Obsessive–Compulsive Disorder in Childhood Affective States Anxiety typically dominates the emotional landscape, fueled by the intrusive nature of incoherent thoughts that sometimes prompt the development of compulsive behaviors to alleviate them. This process profoundly affects the well-being of afflicted children. The distress they express about these thoughts or behaviors often comes with a sense of being unable to escape them, leading to feelings of helplessness, frustration, or shame. This emotional situation can escalate to panic attacks or secondary depressive states. Additionally, the content of obsessions can have a significant emotional impact, such as fear for the health of caregivers or oneself, or embarrassment at verbalizing these thoughts and imagining the impact on others. Many children with obsessions and compulsions recognize that their fears are irrational but still feel compelled to act on them. Initially, most seek help actively, but some are immobilized by shame, exacerbating their anxiety. Thoughts and Fantasies Obsessions dominate the thought process, often permeating a child’s entire mental life to the extent that it can be hard to distinguish them from delusional or psychotic thoughts. Assessing them as absurd and noting their limited correlation with disorganized or inappropriate behaviors can aid in differential diagnosis.

chunks.heading: 320 Childhood
chunks.preview: 320 Childhood Common obsessions involve fear of contamination, fear of harming self or others, intrusive sexual or violent thoughts, excessive focus on religious or moral ideas, fear of losing possessions, and preoccupation with order and symmetry. In childhood, catastrophic thoughts, dread of death...
chunks.text: 320 Childhood Common obsessions involve fear of contamination, fear of harming self or others, intrusive sexual or violent thoughts, excessive focus on religious or moral ideas, fear of losing possessions, and preoccupation with order and symmetry. In childhood, catastrophic thoughts, dread of death, hypochondriacal ideas, or concerns about specific body parts are more prevalent. Thinking tends to be concrete, with difficulty accessing fantasy and creativity, often reflected in structured, rigid, and emotionally contained drawings and games. Developing mentalizing skills is crucial in dealing with intrusive thoughts and behaviors. Somatic States Sensory overresponsivity (extreme behavioral responses to sensory stimuli) is relatively common among children with obsessive–­compulsive disorder. Tactile, visual–­auditory, and gustatory–­olfactory hypersensitivity may occur. This overresponsivity seems more common in younger children. In some cases, there is evidence of hyperawareness of particular bodily processes, urges, or sensations, such as a focus on breathing and tongue movement. Relational Patterns Children with obsessive–­compulsive disorder typically do not have inherent relationship issues, but the intensity of their symptoms and associated behaviors can lead to isolation or peer rejection. The nature of the obsessive thoughts and compulsive behaviors often dictate the social and relational impact. When not consumed by their fears, these children may function well socially, actively engaging in life and forming close relationships. But when prevented from engaging in compulsive behaviors, they experience severe anxiety or panic, leading to rigid control over situations and relationships. This behavior may be misinterpreted as controlling rather than as stemming from an effort to manage severe anxiety. Avoidance behaviors and disproportionate fears may result in children avoiding contact with others, including primary caregivers. While there is no specific attachment pattern, anxious, avoidant, and rigid aspects influence their relationship with parents. The attachment style becomes crucial in seeking help or managing symptoms of the disorder.
