Continuity The disorder has two peaks of incidence, in childhood and adulthood, respectively, but does not always persist from childhood into adulthood. Its high comorbidity with anxiety and affective disorders suggests that we consider its impact on other developmental stages. Therapeutic Indications A foundation of trust in the therapeutic relationship is necessary to prevent the obsessive ideas and ritualistic behaviors from attaching themselves to, or reinvigorating themselves within, the therapeutic setting. Sessions can be challenging for these children, who see the free expression of emotions as threatening and equate expressiveness with being “out of control.” The therapeutic relationship can thus become a

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 321
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 321 battleground between the obsessive need to break free from emotional isolation and the anxiety surrounding intimacy. Therapy should focus on not only exploring the patient’s inner world but also fostering a mutual understanding that facilitates growth. These children requ...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 321 battleground between the obsessive need to break free from emotional isolation and the anxiety surrounding intimacy. Therapy should focus on not only exploring the patient’s inner world but also fostering a mutual understanding that facilitates growth. These children require a clinician who can acknowledge their limitations without judgment, and encourage them to explore and evaluate their internal and external realities freely. A therapist who shows patience, courage, and empathy, even in the face of mistakes, can help the child reconnect fully with life. Countertransferences often provide valuable clues for navigating this emotional territory. Treatment for obsessive–­compulsive disorders in children should focus on not only reducing symptoms but also addressing underlying psychological factors and promoting lasting change. Although psychodynamic therapies may require longer-­term engagement than most cognitive-­behavioral treatments, they offer the potential for both enduring symptom relief and psychological resilience by addressing the underlying dynamics of the disorder. Goals include gaining insight into unconscious conflicts, resolving unresolved emotions, and understanding the origins of symptoms within the child’s developmental context. Through exploration and interpretation, therapy aims to empower children toward healthier ways to cope and a more adaptive relationship with their inner life. By differentiating between fantasy, obsessive thoughts, and reality, therapy helps alleviate the fear of losing control that is often associated with this condition. It provides a supportive environment for exploration and growth. Addressing associated comorbidities such as depression, tics, or anxiety disorders may be essential for effective intervention. Clinical Illustration Phillip, a 10-year-old boy, was referred to a mental health center due to anxiety related to isolation and obsessive thoughts about death. He experienced recurrent, intrusive thoughts that his parents would die, which caused intense anxiety and disrupted his ability to attend school. In response, he developed a strong need to remain physically close to his parents. They observed him arranging his toys by color and size for hours, particularly during periods of heightened anxiety. He frequently asked them if they had been to the doctor or if they were sick. Despite their consistent reassurances—­reassurances he appeared to understand—­he continued to ask the same questions repeatedly. Phillip had always been a quiet and cautious child, more orderly and less spontaneous than his siblings. He had no difficulty making friends or keeping up academically. He was acutely aware of the emotional toll his obsessive thoughts took on him. He often cried inconsolably out of fear for his parents’ safety, while acknowledging that he knew his fears were irrational, saying, “I can’t get this idea out of my head.” SC9.2 Tic Disorders and Tourette Syndrome Tics are brief, stereotyped, involuntary movements and/or vocalizations. They are common in childhood: About 5% of all children have transient tics that resolve within 12 months. A smaller number have a diagnosable motor or vocal tic disorder, and an even smaller number have Tourette syndrome, which involves both motor and vocal tics. Epidemiological studies find a prevalence between 1 and 3% in young people ages 5–18 (Set & Warner, 2021). These disorders have a high comorbidity, especially

chunks.heading: 322 Childhood
chunks.preview: 322 Childhood with ADHD, anxiety disorders, obsessive–­compulsive disorders, and behavioral disturbances. In ASDs, it is important to differentiate between motor stereotypies (Stern, 2018). Subjective Experience of Tic Disorders and Tourette Syndrome in Childhood Affective States The main symptom is...
chunks.text: 322 Childhood with ADHD, anxiety disorders, obsessive–­compulsive disorders, and behavioral disturbances. In ASDs, it is important to differentiate between motor stereotypies (Stern, 2018). Subjective Experience of Tic Disorders and Tourette Syndrome in Childhood Affective States The main symptom is the anxiety generated by the tics, and this anxiety can trigger or worsen the symptom. The child’s inability to hide it and the painfulness of its social impact at this age have a direct impact on mood and may set off secondary depressive symptoms. Irritability and aggressiveness sometimes arise also from frustration over lack of control. These difficulties lead to feelings of guilt and shame that worsen a child’s affective state. Thoughts and Fantasies Thoughts may involve fears of loss of control, especially of aggressive and sexual feelings and impulses. Children with tic disorders may come to see themselves as different, defective, or odd. Their self-­concepts may be influenced by the reactions of family, teachers, and peers, who may not understand te involuntary nature of the behavior, and who may thereby aggravate the child’s anxiety and pain. Somatic States In addition to the movement disorders themselves, there may be secondary somatic symptoms created by the anxiety that they generate, with frequent somatizations that would allow the child to avoid painful situations and divert the focus of their problems. Relational Patterns Although these children do not usually have social difficulties, the intensity of their symptoms can evoke rejection or ridicule from peers, stimulating social isolation and making school a torture to be avoided. With their families, afflicted children may behave in regressive ways or channel their frustration and depressive feelings into problematic actions. Therapeutic Indications Both psychological and pharmacological treatment may be essential, both to treat the specific symptoms and to address associated problems. Psychoeducation of parents and other authorities is essential to explain the involuntariness of the tics, to avoid behavioral disorders, and especially to prevent bullying, secondary school dropout, and social isolation. Clinical Illustration Andrew, an 8-year-old boy, had experienced a gradual onset of involuntary movements—­such as jumping on one foot—and episodes of acute screaming since

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 323
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 323 the age of 7. He frequently complained of headaches and stomachaches, which had no medical explanation but prevented him from attending school regularly. At home, he had been an affectionate and somewhat dependent child until his symptoms worsened, at which point he becam...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 323 the age of 7. He frequently complained of headaches and stomachaches, which had no medical explanation but prevented him from attending school regularly. At home, he had been an affectionate and somewhat dependent child until his symptoms worsened, at which point he became aggressive and rebellious. He attributed his hostile behavior to his illness. Andrew had few friendships, limited mainly to his extended family. He avoided going out for fear that his tics would appear in public and that others would make fun of him. His increased physical and emotional symptoms seemed to isolate him further and contributed to a growing sense of frustration and social withdrawal. SC9.3 Trichotillomania This disorder is characterized by a compelling need to pull out hair from different parts of the body, especially the head. This behavior lacks an aesthetic rationale but rather has obsessive or anxiolytic motivations. Generally, trichotillomania starts just before or after puberty. At any given time, about 1–2% of people suffer from it (Thomson, Farhat, Olfson, Levine, & Bloch, 2022). Continuity over time is not constant but may recur in states of high anxiety. The behavior is fairly automatic in some children, whereas others are more conscious of their actions. The act of removing the hair is not driven by obsessions or concerns about appearance (as in body dysmorphic disorder) but may be preceded by a feeling of tension or anxiety that is relieved by it, usually causing a pleasurable sensation. To make a useful intervention, the differential diagnosis and associated anxious symptoms are highly relevant. SC9.4 Skin‐Picking Disorder Skin-­picking disorder, also called dermatillomania or excoriation disorder, consists of not being able to stop picking at the skin, generating dermatological lesions and high secondary anxiety. It usually begins in adolescence but has been seen at earlier ages (Lochner, Roos, & Stein, 2017). Skin-­picking disorder is similar to obsessive–­ compulsive disorder in terms of the compulsivity of the action, but it does not usually involve discomfort or previous obsessive thinking and may even be associated with feelings of pleasure or secondary relaxation. Sometimes the severity of excoriations requires dermatological intervention. The condition may have a profound impact on socialization, since the feelings of guilt and secondary shame that the symptom creates may spur efforts to hide from disapproving eyes. Early diagnosis and treatment are critical to treating the disorder and any associated problems. SC10 Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders Children come to mental health professionals with a variety of combined psychological and physical issues: from psychological problems resulting from adjusting to illness, which can sometimes exacerbate a condition, to somatic symptom and functional neurological disorders (conversion). Differentiating among categories of somatic symptom and related disorders can be difficult, as individuals may have more than one psychosomatic condition. Somatic symptoms and related disorders in childhood mainly involve somatization of psychological distress. There may be preexisting biological vulnerabilities of specific organs or systems, however, that make children susceptible to dysfunction

chunks.heading: 324 Childhood
chunks.preview: 324 Childhood under psychological stress. Recent studies on functional neurological disorders have challenged either–­or formulations of organic versus psychological etiology. Current assessment and treatment approaches therefore suggest a combination of psychological and physical interventions. A c...
chunks.text: 324 Childhood under psychological stress. Recent studies on functional neurological disorders have challenged either–­or formulations of organic versus psychological etiology. Current assessment and treatment approaches therefore suggest a combination of psychological and physical interventions. A child’s somatic complaints may revolve around one or more bodily symptoms. The child’s reaction to these symptoms and to the treatment approach selected by parents, doctors, and mental health clinicians often depends on the caregivers’ stance and alliance. At the onset of persistent somatic complaints, if symptoms are mild (e.g., limb pains or stomachaches), parents commonly react by downplaying the seriousness of the problem or attempting to comfort the child by seeking its cause. Sometimes, they begin to suspect the child of feigning illness to gain extra attention or avoid demands. They may try to provide psychological or “magical” relief (e.g., a rub or kiss), or analgesic medication. But when a symptom is severe (e.g., paralysis), they may fear a serious physical illness, leading them to request multiple medical examinations and alternative opinions. A psychiatric diagnosis may cause confusion, mistrust, worry, and guilt, or it may provide some relief to caregivers. SC10.1 Somatic Symptom Disorder Somatic symptom disorder, known as “somatization disorder” in DSM-IV, involves one or more bodily symptoms that distress a patient or significantly disrupt daily life, along with health concerns and excessive thoughts, feelings, or behaviors related to the symptom. In children, recurrent abdominal pains, headaches, limb pains, fatigue, and nausea are common symptoms. Somatic symptom disorder in childhood usually involves a single prominent symptom. While young children may have somatic complaints, they rarely worry about “illness” in general before adolescence. Caregiver response to the symptom is a crucial determinant of the level of associated distress. It is parents who interpret the problem and make decisions about school absence and/or medical help. Despite its increasing prevalence among children, research on this disorder is relatively scarce. While somatic symptoms affect 25–75% of young people, only 5–7% meet the full criteria for somatic symptom disorder (Gershfeld-­Litvin et al., 2022). Gender differences have not been found among children ages 4–11. Various biological, psychological, and social factors contribute to its development, including high sensitivity to pain, early childhood self-­regulation difficulties, ineffective coping skills, perfectionism, alexithymia, history of childhood illness or physical injuries, high rates of negative life events, and excessive parental concern about the child’s health. Psychoanalytic theories posit that somatization can occur when a child’s emotional experience is not processed into symbolic representations. Emotional conflicts may channel expression through the body when not adequately managed by words and symbols. Subjective Experience of Somatic Symptom Disorder in Childhood Affective States The somatic symptom invades everyday life, causing anxiety, neediness, and feelings of loneliness. When children are distressed or in pain, they may perceive themselves as

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 325
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 325 in serious danger. A child whose caregivers doubt the reality of the illness is likely to protest loudly, feel invalidated, and become enraged. Thoughts and Fantasies The child’s thoughts and attention are fixed on the recurrence of the symptom and how to cope with it. So...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 325 in serious danger. A child whose caregivers doubt the reality of the illness is likely to protest loudly, feel invalidated, and become enraged. Thoughts and Fantasies The child’s thoughts and attention are fixed on the recurrence of the symptom and how to cope with it. Somatic problems or complaints arise in anxiety-­arousing situations; somatic manifestations substitute for mental representations of mental conflict, indicating repression of disturbing ideas and difficulties in symbolization. Somatic States Children with somatic symptom disorder may report a range of bodily symptoms without identifiable medical causes, including stomachaches, headaches, back or limb pain, nausea, and other physical complaints. While these symptoms typically arise in situations that provoke anxiety, children often fail to recognize or acknowledge that their physical symptoms are linked to stressful life events. Relationship Patterns Somatization tends to run in families that struggle to integrate physical sensations, emotions, and thoughts. The somatic complaint or preoccupation can become the primary method through which the child expresses emotional states, conveys needs for connection and intimacy, and vents hostility or ambivalence. In some instances, the somatic complaint or symptom becomes a means to control or punish caregivers, leading situations in which to family life revolves around the child’s symptom. The child may also experience anxiety when separated from caregivers, developing an age-­ inappropriate dependent relationship and withdrawing from peers. School absences and the abandonment of leisure and sport activities can further disrupt the child’s social life. Continuity The incidence of somatic symptoms tends to increase with age. Somatic symptom disorder may persist into adulthood; from adolescence onward, it is strongly associated with anxiety and depression. Therapeutic Indications Children with somatic symptoms disorders often navigate the health care system for extended periods without receiving adequate psychological support, often resorting to medication instead. This can lead to a cycle of disability, frustration, and missed opportunities for both families and health care providers. Symptoms may become chronic, resulting in significant impairments in children’s social and emotional lives, such as increased school absences, loss of peer relationships, social isolation, and heightened risk for emotional disorders such as anxiety or depression. Early psychotherapeutic interventions, such as cognitive-­behavioral or psychodynamic psychotherapy, accompanied by family therapy or parental counseling, have shown positive outcomes in cases of selective symptom disorders.

chunks.heading: 326 Childhood
chunks.preview: 326 Childhood Clinical Illustration Elena, an 8-year-old girl, was referred to psychotherapy by her general practitioner after suffering from medically inexplicable nausea and stomachaches for 6 months. Her symptoms first appeared after she had gastroenteritis. The doctor noted that they occurred ei...
chunks.text: 326 Childhood Clinical Illustration Elena, an 8-year-old girl, was referred to psychotherapy by her general practitioner after suffering from medically inexplicable nausea and stomachaches for 6 months. Her symptoms first appeared after she had gastroenteritis. The doctor noted that they occurred either in the morning before school or in the evening before bedtime. Symptoms would often disappear after a reassuring medical appointment but then would reappear a few weeks later. During Elena’s initial meeting with the psychotherapist, her parents discussed an event from her infancy when she was 6 months old and had been hospitalized for bronchiolitis for 3 weeks. Elena’s mother became emotional, expressing feelings of guilt for not insisting on staying overnight in the hospital with her daughter at the time, while her father recounted trying to remain strong during the difficult period. When Elena was alone with the therapist, she expressed concerns about problems at school. She feared that a classmate might take away her best friend. She admitted to disliking the classmate but was unsure how to handle the situation. Additionally, she mentioned her desire to protect her mischievous 6-year-old brother both at school and at home. Elena began weekly individual psychodynamic therapy sessions, while her parents engaged in parallel parental work every 2 months with another therapist. During her appointments, Elena primarily engaged in drawing and playing out everyday scenarios with a concrete quality, such as family outings and meal preparation. Initially, these scenarios lacked emotional depth. Yet as therapy progressed, Elena began imagining conflictual situations, allowing her to explore her desires and negative thoughts and feelings toward her classmates, brother, parents, and therapist. After 8 months of therapy, her somatic symptoms disappeared, but she developed a phobia of horse riding, despite its having previously been her favorite sport. Her internal conflicts were no longer manifested physically but rather through psychological symptoms, motivating her to explore her mental states further. It became apparent that Elena required additional assistance to improve her ability to regulate and express her feelings, manage her impulses, and develop more effective ways to cope with internal and external conflicts. By the end of her treatment, both her somatic and phobic symptoms were gone. SC10.2 Conversion Disorder (Functional Neurological Symptom Disorder) Conversion disorder involves motor or sensory symptoms that cannot be explained by a neurological or medical condition. Typical symptoms include motor weakness, abnormal movements, swallowing difficulties, loss of speech, sensory disturbances, and nonepileptic seizures. Many professionals prefer “functional neurological symptom disorder” to “conversion disorder,” because the latter term implies only a psychological etiology. Current neuroscience research suggests that the symptom is a mind–body condition influenced by physical, emotional, cognitive, and environmental factors (Espay et al., 2018; Watson, Sivaswamy, Du, & Agarwal, 2019) whose interplay, particularly interactions between emotional and motor brain regions, contributes to the generation of symptoms. The etiology of this disorder seems to involve a complex combination of predisposing factors, including emotional trauma and physical injuries. Children with conversion disorder often have at-risk patterns of attachment and histories involving unresolved loss and trauma (Perez et al., 2021). It typically begins after the age of 9,

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 327
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 327 although there are rare cases with onset as early as age 3 or 4 years. Symptoms often appear suddenly, overnight, and can be transient or persistent. Factors associated with a better prognosis include early onset, short duration of symptoms, healthy premorbid functioning,...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 327 although there are rare cases with onset as early as age 3 or 4 years. Symptoms often appear suddenly, overnight, and can be transient or persistent. Factors associated with a better prognosis include early onset, short duration of symptoms, healthy premorbid functioning, absence of coexisting organic pathology, and identifiable stressors (Mink, 2013). Psychoanalysts have long studied conversion phenomena, with early theories ascribing functional neurological conditions, often termed “hysteria,” to defenses such as repression, condensation, and displacement. These were thought to be directed toward intense instinctual wishes and conflicts, particularly of a sexual nature, which the patient was keeping out of consciousness. Contemporary psychoanalytic theories suggest that conversion symptoms may serve as a motor discharge, a calming maneuver, and/or a call for help. Subjective Experience of Conversion Disorder in Childhood Affective States The child’s experience of symptoms often leads to catastrophic outcomes, fostering feelings of helplessness and a sense of unpredictability. To manage worries and painful emotions, they frequently suppress them, leading to denial or repression of distress and sadness. Children with this problem commonly show disruptions in emotional identification, expression, and regulation. Thoughts and Fantasies Functional neurological symptom disorders compromise reflective and self-­ observational capacities, resulting in constrained psychological thinking and limited insight. Somatic States Children with the disorder often fixate on their symptoms, worrying about their severity or potential deterioration. Research indicates that their autonomic system is overactivated. Relational Patterns Children with conversion disorders have been found to have non-­normative patterns of attachment, suggesting a persistent disruption the caregiving environment, which is not reliably secure and nurturing. Family conflicts, significant losses, trauma, and sexual abuse or overstimulation appear to be associated with the development of this condition. Conversion disorders disrupt a child’s daily routines and family life. Because of their severity, children with these symptoms often withdraw for extended periods from school and other activities, including sports and performance arts such as dance, gymnastics, or figure skating, leading to social isolation. The sudden onset of a severe neurological condition can be terrifying for both child and parents, prompting them to seek explanations. The diagnosis itself may evoke disbelief or anger, especially if medical professionals are dismissive or fail to note the need for psychological intervention as part of the child’s treatment.

chunks.heading: 328 Childhood
chunks.preview: 328 Childhood Continuity The majority of children with these disorders experience a positive outcome. When mood or anxiety disorders accompany the problem, however, they often continue after the resolution of conversion symptoms. Longitudinal studies of lifelong outcomes of childhood-­onset function...
chunks.text: 328 Childhood Continuity The majority of children with these disorders experience a positive outcome. When mood or anxiety disorders accompany the problem, however, they often continue after the resolution of conversion symptoms. Longitudinal studies of lifelong outcomes of childhood-­onset functional neurological symptom disorder are lacking in available data (Kaplan, 2014). Therapeutic Indications The most effective treatment approach integrates medical care with psychological and physical therapies (e.g., physiotherapy, speech therapy, or occupational therapy). Clinicians should prioritize building a trusting relationship and a strong therapeutic alliance with both the child and the parents rather than solely focusing on symptom management. They need to convey information about the disorder in a manner that ensures both parents and child feel heard and respected. Using age-­appropriate language can aid the child in understanding how stress impacts the mind, leading to somatic symptoms. Collaborating with parents is essential in treatment, as strengthening their reflective function capacity enables them to provide emotional support to their child and reduce the impact of any secondary gains the child receives from the illness role. In individual psychotherapy sessions, therapists should attune themselves to the narrative expressed by the child’s body. Using body-based language to describe anxious or depressive feelings may be particularly effective initially, while creating a safe environment in which both bodily sensations and mental states can be explored. The primary objective is to assist the patient in managing the impact of the condition on daily life. As therapy progresses, enhancing the child’s ability to regulate emotions and engage in reflective thinking can facilitate the processing of thoughts, feelings, and memories of past unpleasant or traumatic events that may contribute to the development or persistence of conversion symptoms. Clinical Illustration Joseph, a 10-year-old boy, had lost significant weight in the 2 months prior to referral. He reported having no appetite, prompting parental concern about a medical illness or the onset of anorexia. The pediatrician found no physical explanation and referred him to a psychiatrist. Joseph was a well-­behaved, anxious boy with a history of separation anxiety and fear of the dark. These early issues had resolved without treatment but left behind a temperament prone to worry. He was especially anxious about maintaining good grades and peer relationships. During evaluation, Joseph explained that he felt something was stuck in his throat—­ specifically, a thumbtack—­which made swallowing difficult. This belief originated from an incident at school where classmates had been tossing thumbtacks near him while he was eating a sandwich. He became convinced that one had entered his food. The resulting fear of swallowing caused him to eat very little, leading to weight loss. Medical exams confirmed there was no physical obstruction. He was diagnosed with functional neurological symptom disorder and obsessive–­compulsive disorder. Psychotherapy focused on addressing his obsessive fears. After several months, Joseph

chunks.heading: 330 Childhood
chunks.preview: 330 Childhood relationships with parents, mealtimes may be seen as restrictive. There may be numerous fantasies surrounding certain foods, often characterized by specific attributes such as texture, color, or smell. Traumatic experiences may be associated with these fantasies. When the restrictive b...
chunks.text: 330 Childhood relationships with parents, mealtimes may be seen as restrictive. There may be numerous fantasies surrounding certain foods, often characterized by specific attributes such as texture, color, or smell. Traumatic experiences may be associated with these fantasies. When the restrictive behavior is more related to discomfort with one’s body shape, underlying fantasies may concern control over bodily maturation processes. In picky eating disorders, the expressed disgust toward certain foods may indicate an anally preoccupied dimension of character, reflecting “expulsive” fantasies. Somatic States Somatic symptoms associated with undereating disorders (stomach pain, headaches, vomiting, nausea) serve primarily, whether consciously or unconsciously, to avoid meals. In undereating syndromes, the body increasingly assumes a central role as children mature. Fantasies revolving around controlling the body—both its need for food and its physical transformations—­often become intense and pivotal. These fantasies may be linked to primal drives that remain unprocessed during latency. They may also turn into self-­directed aggression, reflecting an attack on internalized inner objects. Relational Patterns Relationships can vary significantly depending on the root cause of the eating disorder. When it stems from relational challenges with caregivers, family relationships are likely to be affected during mealtimes and beyond. Patterns characterized by domination, coercion, insensitivity, and a lack of empathic understanding may emerge. Children may withdraw from relationships or become oppositional. If the symptomatology is more linked to body image issues, relational difficulties may extend beyond the family sphere and become particularly pronounced in eating-­ related contexts. As children mature, the impact of the disorder on their relational life intensifies, negatively affecting relationships with both peers and adults. SC11.2 Overeating Disorder When feeding behavior leans toward the “over” type, children typically consume larger quantities of food than befits their age and/or body size. This condition necessitates thorough medical evaluation, especially considering its frequent association with hormonal pathologies. It presents significant challenges in caregiver relationships, as food can become a source of conflict and may give rise to pathological dynamics such as power struggles. Subjective Experience of Overeating Disorder in Childhood Affective States In younger children, excessive food consumption and concerns about food often persist over the long term, significantly affecting their daily lives. They may become highly agitated if their eating behavior is obstructed. Older children may continue overeating for various reasons. They may turn to food to cope with painful emotions, feelings of

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 331
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 331 emptiness, or intense anxiety. They may engage in overeating because of difficulties regulating impulses that they struggle to manage in any other way. Difficulty articulating emotions, lack of emotional awareness, and suppression of emotional expression appear to be risk...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 331 emptiness, or intense anxiety. They may engage in overeating because of difficulties regulating impulses that they struggle to manage in any other way. Difficulty articulating emotions, lack of emotional awareness, and suppression of emotional expression appear to be risk factors for maladaptive eating behaviors. Thoughts and Fantasies Children with these disorders often think, talk, and play about food and eating. Unconsciously, they may use food to conceal aggressive impulses. Their aggression may be directed not only outward toward others but also inward toward the self. Somatic States Overeating behaviors pose a risk factor for increased body weight and the development of numerous chronic diseases, including cardiovascular diseases, musculoskeletal complications, diabetes, and cancer. The overweight body often assumes a central role that can be distressing for children. Changes in body size and image, particularly evident in older children, significantly influences their self-­esteem and overall selfimage. Relational Patterns Eating habits tend to be transmitted from one generation to the next, with attachment relationships serving as key agents of transmission, especially during childhood and adolescence. The family context can play a central role in this disorder. Peer relationships can also profoundly influence children’s experiences with it. Overweight children are at increased risk of bullying: Family attitudes toward the child serve as a major protective factor. Continuity Feeding and eating disorders in childhood often have continuity with previous undereating disorders during infancy. Anorexia in infancy is a risk factor for other disorders such as anxiety, depression, or behavioral disturbances, indicating the presence of patterns of heterotypic continuity. Research on feeding and eating disorders in childhood has identified patterns of homotypic continuity from this age to adolescence. Specifically, undereating disorders in childhood are more frequently associated with anorexia nervosa in adolescence and adulthood, particularly among females. Picky eating behaviors are often linked to subsequent risk of anorexia nervosa in both females and males. Childhood overeating is a significant risk factor binge eating disorder in adolescence. Therapeutic Indications Involving parents in the therapeutic process is essential. Eating symptomatology not only profoundly affects the family system, which must develop alternative coping strategies, but also tends to arise within dysfunctional familial interactional dynamics. Given that eating disorders are closely linked to difficulties in emotional regulation, a significant portion of therapeutic effort should be directed at helping children

chunks.heading: 332 Childhood
chunks.preview: 332 Childhood express, process, and attribute new meanings to their emotions. This process enables them to find healthier outlets for emotional expression beyond dysfunctional eating behaviors. SC12 Sleep Disorders Sleep-­related difficulties are highly prevalent during childhood. They may arise eit...
chunks.text: 332 Childhood express, process, and attribute new meanings to their emotions. This process enables them to find healthier outlets for emotional expression beyond dysfunctional eating behaviors. SC12 Sleep Disorders Sleep-­related difficulties are highly prevalent during childhood. They may arise either temporarily or persistently and may be part of other psychopathological conditions or stand-alone issues with significant repercussions on the well-being of the child and family system. Sleep patterns can be influenced by medical and neurological factors, stressful life events, trauma, sensory overresponsivity, and self-­regulation problems. Sleep disturbances can reflect attachment and separation problems with caregivers, while psychosocial factors such as family dynamics and cultural attitudes toward sleep further contribute to sleep problems. Three commonly observed sleep disturbances are insomnia, nightmares, and night terrors. Insomnia can take various forms, including difficulty falling asleep, difficulty maintaining sleep, and early morning awakenings, without the ability to return to sleep. The duration and impact on daytime functioning, influenced by family culture, are essential factors in diagnosing insomnia. Nightmares involve intensely felt dreams typically featuring threats to survival or physical integrity; upon waking, the child quickly becomes alert. Night terrors, characterized by sudden screaming, distress, and apparent disorientation, may include physical reactions such as elevated heart rate and sweating. Although frightening for families, night terrors are generally harmless and often are not remembered by the child upon waking. These events, considered arousal disorders, involve a temporary overactivation of the nervous system in response to significant stimuli. Although sleep difficulties often coexist with other childhood problems, they may not initially be apparent. They are typically revealed during clinical interviews with parents or child. Sleep disorders commonly lead to impaired functioning across various domains and are associated with poor daytime behavior, anxiety, and depression. Subjective Experience of Sleep Disorders in Childhood Affective States Children suffering from insomnia may display varying degrees of agitation during the night, often seeking comfort by either going to their parents’ bedroom to resume sleep or requesting to sleep in their parents’ bed. Children experiencing frequent nightmares often require parental comfort to return to sleep, although psychomotor and physiological agitation may make such comfort challenging. Night terrors, distinct from nightmares, are typically not remembered by the child. It may be difficult to awaken the child from them. While they can distress families because of the child’s terrified state, they are generally not harmful. Factors such as stressful life events, sleep deprivation, fever, and medications may contribute to their occurrence. Bedtime routines can become problematic in these situations, causing difficulties for both child and caregivers. Children’s sleep difficulties often significantly reduce the quality of their parents’ sleep, leading to agitation, conflict, and tensions.

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 333
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 333 Thoughts and Fantasies Children with sleeping disorders experience a variety of thoughts and fantasies that can disrupt their ability to fall asleep. These include fearful thoughts about bedtime, worries and concerns, excitement about upcoming events, engagement in imagin...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 333 Thoughts and Fantasies Children with sleeping disorders experience a variety of thoughts and fantasies that can disrupt their ability to fall asleep. These include fearful thoughts about bedtime, worries and concerns, excitement about upcoming events, engagement in imaginary scenarios, and experience of intrusive thoughts. Somatic States When it is persistent and enduring, sleep deprivation frequently results in the child’s experiencing fatigue and physical exhaustion throughout the day. A sleep-­deprived child may suffer from frequent headaches and moments of drowsiness during waking hours. Absence of restful sleep can impair the capacity to engage in school activities by diminishing the ability to concentrate. It can also hinder participation in leisure activities such as sports. Relationship Patterns These conditions can also affect relationship dynamics. Parents may be inclined to allow the child to sleep with them to prevent disturbances during the night. This practice may worsen separation issues and may also escalate any conflicts within the parental relationship. Continuity Sleeping difficulties, classified as dysregulation disorders, have been observed to have a homotypic pattern, persisting steadily from early childhood through preadolescence. SC13 Elimination Disorders Elimination disorders encompass the intentional or involuntary inappropriate elimination of urine (enuresis) in children older than 5 years, or feces (encopresis) in those older than 4 years. This group of disorders is heterogeneous, requiring clinicians to consider various aspects and distinctions. First, there is a differentiation between primary and secondary enuresis and encopresis. Primary enuresis involves incontinence present since birth, whereas secondary enuresis occurs after a period of bowel and bladder control. In some cases, children may experience both enuresis and encopresis, while in others, they are distinct symptoms, often involving daytime enuresis and/or retentive encopresis. Bladder and bowel incontinence that result from medical conditions are not classified as elimination disorders. Research has shown associations in children and adolescents between elimination disorders and other psychiatric conditions, such as antisocial or defiant disorder, ADHD, anxiety, and depression (Joinson, Grzeda, von Gontard, & Heron, 2019a, 2019b). It remains unclear, however, whether elimination disorders are causes or consequences of these conditions. Enuresis and encopresis have also been linked to adverse life conditions, with stressful events such as abuse, disruptions in parental care, exposure to trauma, and

chunks.heading: 334 Childhood
chunks.preview: 334 Childhood chaotic family environments often found in the personal histories of affected children (Joinson, Sullivan, von Gontard, & Heron, 2016). These disorders can significantly disrupt a child’s everyday life, intermittently or chronically, affecting bodily experiences, sense of control, self...
chunks.text: 334 Childhood chaotic family environments often found in the personal histories of affected children (Joinson, Sullivan, von Gontard, & Heron, 2016). These disorders can significantly disrupt a child’s everyday life, intermittently or chronically, affecting bodily experiences, sense of control, self-­esteem, peer relationships, family dynamics, and participation in daily activities (e.g., school, sports, social gatherings). Their persistence into late childhood and early adolescence imposes a greater burden on overall mental functioning and social adjustment. SC13.1 Encopresis Encopresis is characterized by the repeated and persistent passing of feces in inappropriate places, starting around age 4 or a similar developmental stage. Although prevalence rates fluctuate across studies and age brackets, they generally hover around 1.5% for children over age 7. Males are afflicted at a three times higher rate than females (Martin, Volkmar, & Lewis, 2007). Encopresis has two distinct subtypes: one involving constipation and overflow incontinence, the other lacking these features. While involuntary soiling predominates in most cases, intentional occurrences have also been documented. Despite extensive research, the precise etiology of encopresis remains elusive. Studies have identified a heightened incidence of bowel incontinence in traumatized children and have established as well a significant association between encopresis and behavioral issues such as oppositional behavior, antisocial activities (e.g., aggression toward people and animals, property destruction), diminished self-­esteem, anxiety, obsessions and compulsions, developmental delays, depression, ADHD, and academic underperformance. The frequency of soiling episodes correlates with the severity of these concurrent difficulties. Subjective Experience of Encopresis in Childhood Affective States Encopresis can evoke intense emotions in children grappling with bowel incontinence. The acquisition of sphincter control represents a milestone in body management and impulse regulation, imbuing the child with a sense of pride and mastery. Children with encopresis confront the disheartening reality of struggling to command their bodily functions, leading to feelings of shame. As the child grows older and incidents of soiling escalate, the emotional toll intensifies. Shame often intertwines with apprehensions of rejection and abandonment, sparking bouts of rage or sadness. Thoughts and Fantasies Coping with these distressing emotions can prompt the child to use dysfunctional defenses such as denial, projection of aggression, or identification with the aggressor. The defense of reaction formation is observable in the apparent indifference displayed by many children with encopresis toward the impact of their symptoms on those around them. Often, they identify as “the unwanted child,” provoking rejection through provocative behaviors or adopting aggressive tendencies as a means of projection or identification.

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 335
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 335 There may be a profound internal conflict between the yearning for emotional connection and intimacy on one hand, and fear and ambivalence harbored toward others on the other. This conflict is often an issue in a child’s excessive desire for omnipotent control over signif...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 335 There may be a profound internal conflict between the yearning for emotional connection and intimacy on one hand, and fear and ambivalence harbored toward others on the other. This conflict is often an issue in a child’s excessive desire for omnipotent control over significant individuals. In cases of retentive encopresis, the child’s entire emotional and psychological world may revolve around the symptom, further complicating interpersonal relationships and emotional well-being. Somatic States In encopresis, the body serves as a conduit for the expression of impulses and affective states. Bodily experiences and representations are significantly compromised. Among other channels (e.g., play, behavior), emotions such as ambivalence, rage, and despair find an outlet through bowel movements. It is not uncommon for children experiencing bowel incontinence to notice they are passing gas without realizing that they are also defecating. This stark incongruity between bodily actions and their perception adds to their distress. Children with encopresis often seem to detach from their bodily functions and show a limited capacity for embodied mentalization. They may lose control over their elimination yet remain unaware, lacking a metacognitive understanding of their defecation through tactile and olfactory senses. Negative remarks and displays of disgust from peers and family members can profoundly impair the child’s body image and self-­esteem. In cases of retentive encopresis, constipation frequently results in pain during bowel movements. This enduring discomfort draws attention from both the child and the caregivers. Medical interventions such as laxatives, suppositories, and enemas, while aimed at alleviating bowel issues, are often perceived as intrusive by the child, leading to a mix of relief and a sense of diminished body control. In some children, encopresis can even serve as a substitute for masturbatory activities. Relational Patterns Encopresis exerts detrimental effects on a child’s relationships and social adaptation, causing shame and anxiety in parents confronted with their child’s distressing “accidents.” Parents often misinterpret their child’s soiling as a deliberate choice rather than an involuntary act, leading to expressions of anger and frustration. In response, children with encopresis may become aggressive or antisocial in their behavior, directing their hostility toward both adults and other children. As they age, difficulties in relationships tend to worsen, with siblings and peers becoming either victims or perpetrators of bullying. Maternal depression and anxiety, particularly during the child’s toilet training, are linked to encopresis. Feces may symbolize “gifts” or “products of aggression” to the mother, fostering ambivalence in children, especially amid strained relationships. The withholding and release of feces can serve as a means for children to assert control over others and master their own autonomy. Both overly strict, rigid toilet training and excessively lax and inconsistent training may have negative effects on a child’s relationship with caregivers and capacity for bladder and bowel continence. Subconsciously, the child’s symptoms may serve to maintain distance in relationships, providing a sense of illusory control, as the expected outcome of rejection aligns with the symptom or associated provocative behaviors.

chunks.heading: 336 Childhood
chunks.preview: 336 Childhood Continuity Encopresis is associated with various forms of psychopathology in later life. Childhood vulnerabilities manifested through soiling may resurface during adolescence, potentially in the form of depression or substance misuse. Moreover, encopresis during childhood can shape per...
chunks.text: 336 Childhood Continuity Encopresis is associated with various forms of psychopathology in later life. Childhood vulnerabilities manifested through soiling may resurface during adolescence, potentially in the form of depression or substance misuse. Moreover, encopresis during childhood can shape personality characteristics in later developmental stages. The humiliation endured during formative years may spur compensatory behaviors, such as the development of defensive narcissistic pride and the persistence of sadistic tendencies well into adulthood. Therapeutic Indications The psychological treatment of encopresis poses considerable challenges. Children with infrequent soiling or incontinence stemming from constipation typically do not undergo psychological evaluation or therapy; instead, caregivers often seek medical solutions such as medication or dietary adjustments. In cases of retentive encopresis, addressing constipation can sometimes alleviate or eliminate bowel incontinence. Behavioral and educational interventions, when combined with medication, have shown positive outcomes. The extent to which a child’s emotional well-being and sense of self improves as a result of such interventions remains unclear, however. Higher frequency of incontinence episodes and inadequate compliance from the child are both associated with poor outcomes. Psychoanalytic clinicians argue that symptoms of encopresis often reveal significant vulnerabilities in a child’s mental capacities and overall psychological makeup. Focusing solely on eliminating the symptom may address only a fraction of the child’s emotional, developmental, and relational challenges. Clinicians need to interpret what the child conveys through the symptom to the environment, as limited symbolic capacities may be impelling the expression of emotions through the body. They should adopt a comprehensive therapeutic approach, addressing present and past relationship difficulties, noting dysfunctional defenses, and enhancing the child’s ability for symbolization and emotional regulation. Concurrent engagement of parents in therapeutic work is essential for improving the child’s symptom and overall mental well-being. Parents need support in understanding their child’s lack of physical control in encopresis. Therapeutic efforts should focus on managing parents’ feelings of shame and helplessness, while enhancing their reflective capacities to respond sensitively to their child’s emotional needs, avoiding expressions of anger, denigration, or punishment. Clinical Illustration Thomas, a 9-year-old middle child, was referred for encopresis and intense bullying directed at his younger brother. His mother, who was primarily responsible for childrearing, juggled the care of three children alongside part-time work, contending with emotional distance and fatigue. His father was a strict, rigid man who approached matters with a problem-­solving mindset, often avoiding emotional nuances. Thomas’s struggles with soiling began early, with his mother’s unsuccessful attempt at toilet training him at 21⁄2 years old, coinciding with the birth of his younger brother. Overwhelmed by the care demands of the newborn, she withdrew some of the attention he had received, exacerbating Thomas’s feelings of neglect and jealousy. Initially, his parents attributed his soiling to deliberate acts stemming from jealousy of his brother.

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 337
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 337 As Thomas entered primary school with persistent incidents of soiling, psychoanalytic psychotherapy was initiated, though it met with resistance from his parents, who seemed not to take seriously their role in his recovery. Their involvement in therapy was marked by aggre...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 337 As Thomas entered primary school with persistent incidents of soiling, psychoanalytic psychotherapy was initiated, though it met with resistance from his parents, who seemed not to take seriously their role in his recovery. Their involvement in therapy was marked by aggression and helplessness toward clinicians. Thomas’s relationship with his therapist mirrored this affective tone. He was verbally aggressive and disdainful as soon as he entered the therapy room. His symbolic play was replete with themes of oral and anal aggression. Despite some progress over 6 months in building rapport with the therapist, his encopresis persisted. Frustrated by this, Thomas’s father reverted to the belief that his son was deliberately soiling himself and withdrew abruptly from treatment. In the end, the parents allowed the therapist only one final farewell session with Thomas. This unfortunate outcome highlights the complexities and challenges inherent in treating encopresis within the broader familial and psychological context. SC13.2 Enuresis Enuresis is a common disorder among young children, decreasing in frequency with age. Genetic factors play a significant role: There is a correlation between a child’s enuresis and a parent’s enuresis in childhood, influencing the prognosis for symptom persistence. The prevalence ratio between males and females is not definitively established. Enuresis is not a uniform disorder. Subjective experiences of enuretic children and their parents are related to its particular qualities and its frequency. There are three primary types: nocturnal (bedwetting), diurnal (wetting during the day), and a combination of both. Frequency enuretic incidents compound the practical, emotional, and social burdens on both the child and their caregivers. Nocturnal enuresis, the most common type, has garnered the most research attention. It is often construed as a developmental and “biobehavioral” issue, attributed to a multitude of maturational, biological, genetic, and psychosocial factors. Children with nocturnal enuresis may face risks of internalizing problems such as depression or social anxiety. Early temperament and behavioral issues are considered potential precursors to bedwetting during school age. Daytime wetting and combined day–night wetting are associated with a higher incidence of psychiatric disturbances, particularly externalizing problems such as attention deficits, oppositional behavior, and conduct issues. Stressful family situations, maternal postnatal depression, and familial conflicts are also risk factors for enuresis (Joinson et al., 2019b). From a psychodynamic perspective, enuresis can be interpreted as a discharge of sexual and/or aggressive impulses, or as an outlet for anxious tensions. Secondary enuresis may signify a child’s struggle with a loss, such as the withdrawal of maternal emotional investment by a depressed mother or a mother preoccupied with the birth of a sibling. Regression ensues as a way to attract caregiver attention and restore parent–­ child intimacy. Subjective Experience of Enuresis in Childhood Affective States Children with enuresis commonly feel acute shame about their symptom, which at times becomes so overwhelming that they use the defense of reaction formation to cope with it, adopting a seemingly blasé attitude. At other times, they may grapple

chunks.heading: 338 Childhood
chunks.preview: 338 Childhood with embarrassment, guilt, anger, and diminished self-­esteem. Particularly during later childhood years, when self-­esteem plays a crucial role in personality development, the inability to remain dry can precipitate feelings of depression or passive compliance. Children with enuresis...
chunks.text: 338 Childhood with embarrassment, guilt, anger, and diminished self-­esteem. Particularly during later childhood years, when self-­esteem plays a crucial role in personality development, the inability to remain dry can precipitate feelings of depression or passive compliance. Children with enuresis often avoid comments from others about their symptom and may respond with hostility or opposition, reflecting a mix of frustration, anger, and a desire for control to compensate for the loss of bodily mastery. These reactions serve as outlets for their emotional turmoil as they struggle to manage the psychological and social implications of their condition. Thoughts and Fantasies Children with enuresis often perceive themselves as “faulty” and fantasize that their bodies are damaged and incapable of retaining urine. They may internalize blame for their symptoms, conceptualizing themselves as “the bad-­wetter.” Because of their symptoms, they feel painfully different from their peers. Enuresis has been linked to poor school performance, possibly stemming from night awakenings that disrupt concentration and learning. It is also possible that the notion of having insufficient or damaged genital or body parts becomes displaced to the mind, contributing to psychological distress and academic difficulties. Somatic States Mastering bodily functions and impulses is a critical accomplishment in a child’s development. Bladder incontinence contradicts this developmental achievement, leaving children with enuresis with a sense of deficient body control and an inability to regulate impulses effectively. Enuresis, particularly daytime bladder incontinence, appears to be associated with attention problems and heightened motor activity in childhood. The act of urination and the sensation of warmth from urine on the body can intertwine with the child’s sexual impulses, leading to feelings of excitation and pleasure. Additionally, a vulnerable body part or an unstable bodily function such as bladder continence can act as a conduit for suppressed emotions or uncontrolled impulses that cannot be identified and managed in a mentalized manner. Therefore, during periods of heightened stress, enuretic symptoms may intensify in frequency, only to subside when stress levels decrease. Relational Patterns The type of enuresis matters significantly for the child’s relationships with family and peers. Daytime enuresis tends to have a more detrimental effect on both child and parents because of its social implications and associated embarrassment. Parents often hold a more negative view of their child, attributing daytime wetting to laziness or disobedience. Derogatory remarks from peers or siblings can exacerbate hostile behaviors, leading to poor social adjustment and feelings of isolation. Unlike daytime enuresis, bedwetting is typically kept private within families, as the child’s behavior is not outwardly evident in social settings. Children with nocturnal enuresis often avoid sleepovers and camps, however, for fear of experiencing an “accident” and being subjected to bullying. Parents’ attitudes toward the symptom are heavily influenced by the frequency of incontinence incidents and can greatly impact the child’s emotional responses.

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 339
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 339 Continuity Wetting typically emerges between ages 5 and 7. This period has the highest prevalence of the symptom, which predominantly occurs during sleep. Bedwetting tends to decrease during adolescence and further diminishes in adulthood (Martin et al., 2007). The persis...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 339 Continuity Wetting typically emerges between ages 5 and 7. This period has the highest prevalence of the symptom, which predominantly occurs during sleep. Bedwetting tends to decrease during adolescence and further diminishes in adulthood (Martin et al., 2007). The persistence of enuresis exacerbates the emotional and social challenges associated with the symptom. Many adolescents grappling with enuresis express feelings of helplessness and pessimism about their future because of their inability to control their bladder. The development of genital sexuality during adolescence can intersect with past or present experiences of bladder incontinence. Ejaculation, for example, may act as a reminder of the unpleasant sensation of losing mastery over bodily functions, potentially intensifying feelings of distress and embarrassment. Therapeutic Indications Some children with enuresis experience spontaneous remission of their symptoms as they progress through childhood. Bedwetting, in particular, spontaneously remits at a rate of approximately 15% per year. It is common for parents to overlook mentioning nocturnal enuresis when seeking psychological assessment for other presenting psychopathologies. Diurnal or combined day and night enuresis may warrant medical and/or psychological referral, however, especially for older children. Many parents, having experienced enuresis themselves during childhood, perceive it as a transient symptom that will naturally resolve as their child matures. Consequently, a significant number of children with enuresis do not undergo assessment or treatment for their condition. While psychotherapy can assist in managing the emotional and behavioral challenges associated with enuresis, its effectiveness in stopping primary enuresis has not been conclusively demonstrated. But children with secondary enuresis, particularly those in whom it was preceded by traumatic events, may benefit greatly from therapeutic interventions. Regardless of the specific approach, parental involvement is essential. Children with enuresis require their parents’ support and positive reinforcement to bolster their self-­esteem. The two primary modalities for treating enuresis involve behavioral techniques (e.g., alarm signaling and bladder biofeedback) and psychopharmacological methods (e.g., imipramine and desmopressin). These approaches appear to be particularly effective for addressing nocturnal enuresis; children with daytime wetting often experience relapses after treatment cessation. In cases in which enuresis is secondary to constipation, addressing the underlying constipation typically resolves the incontinence episodes. Clinical Illustration Patrick, age 81⁄2, was referred for assessment of bedwetting that occurred three to four times weekly and for recurrent aggressive outbursts both at home and in school that were directed toward adults and peers. Patrick was born 1 year after his stillborn s­ibling, a loss that devastated both parents, who were grieving and overwhelmed. During her pregnancy with Patrick and after his birth, his mother suffered significant anxiety and depression and feared another tragic loss. The family had avoided

chunks.heading: 340 Childhood
chunks.preview: 340 Childhood discussing the loss of Patrick’s sibling, and the parents admitted to delaying their mourning process. At the time of referral, Patrick had not yet achieved nocturnal bladder control, although his parents were more concerned about his social behavioral issues than his bedwetting. His f...
chunks.text: 340 Childhood discussing the loss of Patrick’s sibling, and the parents admitted to delaying their mourning process. At the time of referral, Patrick had not yet achieved nocturnal bladder control, although his parents were more concerned about his social behavioral issues than his bedwetting. His father had a history of enuresis until age 11. Cognitive assessment revealed superior nonverbal intellectual capacity but linguistic difficulties, including poor vocabulary for his age and difficulty understanding abstract words. Psychotherapeutic evaluation revealed problems in affect regulation, an underdeveloped sense of self, impulse control issues affecting relationships, hyperactivity, severe difficulties with symbolization (fantasy and play), and reliance on primitive defenses. Establishing a therapeutic rapport proved challenging, as Patrick transferred his aggression to the therapist from the outset, with a hypervigilant and defensive stance that resembled a scared animal. Patrick struggled with maintaining a coherent sense of identity, often fusing his identity with roles suggested during play, leading to affect dysregulation and attacks on the therapist. Over time, therapy focused on reducing hypervigilance and creating a secure base through emotionally bearable pretend play and concise verbal interventions. As treatment progressed, Patrick gradually expressed his need for admiration and love, alongside aggressive fantasies of retaliation, stemming from feeling rejected due to perceived inadequacies. After 18 months of intensive psychodynamic treatment, Patrick’s parents disclosed the story of his lost sibling, prompting a cathartic release of emotion from Patrick. His outbursts at school became rare. He occasionally aimed his aggression toward his parents but would quickly seek repair of the relationship once they helped him restore his affect regulation. He also began to display genuine empathic feelings toward others, both people and animals. Nocturnal enuresis persisted until age 11, but frequency of incidents significantly decreased. SC14 Event- and Stressor‐Related Disorders SC14.1 Adjustment Disorders (Other Than Developmental) Sudden stressors can impinge upon children’s progressive development, posing additional strain on their sense of safety and well-being. Exposures to such stressors may induce states of disharmony characterized by sudden regressions or temporary inhibitions of aspects of the children’s ordinary functioning (e.g., peer relationships). Subjective Experience of Adjustment Disorders in Childhood No single pattern characterizes adjustment disorders, as children’s affective states, thoughts and fantasies, somatic states, and relational patterns vary widely depending upon the particular stressor. They may feel anxious or depressed, angry or impulsive, or bereft and defeated. Thoughts and fantasies reflect whatever emotional pattern is triggered by the loss, illness, family problem, or school problem to which the child is reacting. The child may become more dependent and clingy or more distant and aloof. Children with adjustment disorders may also experience a variety of somatic discomforts, sleeping problems, and eating difficulties. Subjective states accompanying adjustment disorders are similar to those in other childhood disorders, but in

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 341
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 341 adjustment disorders, the reaction is temporary and related to a specific situation or event. Helping a child understand the subjective reaction in relationship to the current challenge, along with providing extra support and opportunities for mastery, can favorably influ...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 341 adjustment disorders, the reaction is temporary and related to a specific situation or event. Helping a child understand the subjective reaction in relationship to the current challenge, along with providing extra support and opportunities for mastery, can favorably influence the child’s subjective state. Helping the parent or caregiver understand the context of the child’s experience will benefit the parent and subsequently the child. Clinical Illustration Luca, an 8-year-old boy, was brought for evaluation by his parents, who were concerned about his severe nightmares, which had started 9 months previously. They described his needing a parent to be with him while he fell asleep and his waking in the middle of the night to crawl into their bed, too frightened to sleep alone. These symptoms had begun when a fire alarm salesman had come to their apartment to demonstrate his product and showed a graphic video of a devastating fire. The boy was transfixed. About a week later, he developed the sleep disorder. Upon further exploration with the boy, the therapist became aware of an unresolved separation problem that had been reactivated by the incident. SC14.2 Posttraumatic Stress Disorder Childhood trauma is defined by a child’s experience in reaction to an event or series of events perceived to pose catastrophic danger to life or self. In young children, a perceived threat to a primary caregiver can be felt to pose such a threat. Such experiences produce overwhelming affect that leaves a child feeling helpless, paralyzed, unable to organize thoughts, and unable to cope. Normal defenses fail, and dissociative responses are typical. Researchers have increasingly found that continuous, cumulative, aversive events (i.e., exposure to domestic violence, sexual abuse, physical abuse, neglect, maternal depression, chronic affective misattunement) can have significant effects on both neuropsychological and ego development. Taken individually, such events may or may not lead to symptoms consistent with the diagnosis of PTSD, and may or may not lead to a child’s becoming overwhelmed or developmentally disrupted by the affective intensity associated with these events. Scholarly writing has depicted overwhelming events in various terms, including “strain trauma,” “cumulative trauma,” “relational trauma,” “complex trauma,” and “developmental trauma.” These early and persistent aversive experiences are risk factors for defensively determined personality traits, including severe personality disorders, emergent in adolescents and fixed in adults. Traumatogenic events can even function as organizers of personality and identity. Thorough history taking is essential for understanding PTSD symptoms and for planning treatment. “Episodic trauma” refers to a single event that leads to symptoms consistent with the PTSD. The full array of posttraumatic symptoms may or may not be instantaneously felt by the child or observed by others. But traumatogenic events are by definition overwhelming, leaving children unable to manage the intensity of affect they generate, and often interfering with their capacity to continue to develop, make use of symbolic capacities, or mentalize. Heightened professional sensitivity to the effects of aversive events has revived a long-­standing caution not to overuse the term “trauma,” thereby diminishing our appreciation of the profound intensity of suffering of victims of true psychic trauma.

chunks.heading: 342 Childhood
chunks.preview: 342 Childhood Scholars and clinicians have observed that too often, events are categorized as “traumatic” without consideration of their actual effects on a child. An event can only be considered traumatic if a person reacts to it with a trauma response. Aversive events present a risk for trauma but...
chunks.text: 342 Childhood Scholars and clinicians have observed that too often, events are categorized as “traumatic” without consideration of their actual effects on a child. An event can only be considered traumatic if a person reacts to it with a trauma response. Aversive events present a risk for trauma but do not equate with it. Not all children who are exposed to seemingly identical stressors (e.g., death of a parent, earthquake, domestic violence, physical or sexual abuse, painful medical treatments) are traumatized by the experience. They may be greatly distressed, grief stricken, or temporarily disrupted, but not traumatized. A child’s cognitive and emotional capacities, prior risk factors (e.g., previous traumatization, insecure attachment) and protective factors (e.g., family support, community resources, inherent resiliency) and are mediating variables. A child’s ability to avoid or reduce the duration of PTSD depends centrally on support from caregivers. The benefit derived from a parent who is able to tolerate hearing what has occurred and how it has affected the child, and to be empathic and comforting in response, cannot be overstated. When a parent is the victim of trauma, or suffers from other mental health issues, the capacity to provide such support may be compromised. Helping the child to avert or attenuate traumatic symptoms involves helping both child and parent recognize the impact of a traumatogenic event and facilitating communication between the child and parent about that event and its consequences. Therapy may involve empathically addressing impediments to the parent’s ability to respond helpfully to the child’s needs. Single-­episode psychological trauma in children can create disturbances similar to those found in adults. There is some evidence that females are more vulnerable to posttraumatic stress syndromes, but that males are more disabled by their symptoms. Regressive responses to trauma are particularly visible in childhood, especially in younger children. For example, a previously confident child may cling to a caregiver, or a child who was able to sleep alone may stop being able to do so. Older children may withdraw from social contact, appear hyperactive, or show other behavioral difficulties. Traumatized children may also show hyperalertness and an increased startle response. Increased volatility, poor concentration, poorer attention and distractibility, and more labile affect are all possible consequences of a child’s having been traumatized. So are disturbances in eating and sleeping (including disturbing dreams), which may affect learning and memory. Other common responses include the defensive/adaptive use of superheroes in efforts to avoid stimuli associated with the traumatic events or gain a sense of mastery over them. Some children develop ritualistic behaviors, or label events as omens and predictors of future adversity. Subjective Experience of Posttraumatic Stress Disorder in Childhood Affective States Affective response patterns emanate in part from the sense of loss of control, power, and identity that results from a traumatic experience. Responses may range from anxiety and panic to obsessive preoccupation and fear, which may appear most vividly in nightmares. Some children become irritable and/or angry as a way of turning passive into active responding, and in an unconscious effort to ward off feelings of helplessness and despair. Others may be overtaken by exhaustion, sadness, and/or shame. Traumatized children may have a level of emotional lability out of keeping with their premorbid condition.

chunks.heading: Child Symptom Patterns 343
chunks.preview: Child Symptom Patterns 343 Thoughts and Fantasies Thoughts and fantasies may involve the aforementioned preoccupations, compulsive ruminations, fragmented thinking, “all-or-­nothing” thinking, and escapist fantasies (including a degree of magical thinking beyond what is age-­appropriate). If the na...
chunks.text: Child Symptom Patterns 343 Thoughts and Fantasies Thoughts and fantasies may involve the aforementioned preoccupations, compulsive ruminations, fragmented thinking, “all-or-­nothing” thinking, and escapist fantasies (including a degree of magical thinking beyond what is age-­appropriate). If the nature of the traumatic event is interpersonal, children may come to think of themselves as bad, dirty, or unlovable. A newfound belief in omens may serve the defensive function of allowing a child the sense of knowing when danger is to be anticipated rather than contending with the unpredictability of life. In some children, denial is a dominant defense in response to the experienced threat. In the play of children who have been traumatized, the drama may be compulsively reenacted, often in a distorted form that seeks to prevent the return of traumatic memories and help the children gain mastery over the event. At the same time, traumatized children may develop difficulty maintaining the boundary between fantasy and reality—­something that can be observed in their play and in other manifestations of their extreme anxiety. Somatic States Somatic states follow affective patterns, most notably those associated with anxiety, such as rapid heart rate, tense muscles, gastrointestinal symptoms, headaches, and muscle aches. If the trauma involved physical pain, this may be reexperienced in dissociative ways. Sexual abuse, molestation, and exposure to sexualized material are particularly likely to trigger both somatic symptoms and hypersexualized behaviors. Avoidant or phobic behaviors may often be accompanied by somatic worries and complaints. Relational Patterns Relationships may become needy and dependent, as the child seeks relief from traumatic anxiety. But some children become withdrawn and avoid even close friends, and evade contact with strangers, who come to be seen as potentially harmful. Any stimulus (sounds, smells, etc.) that reminds a child of a traumatizing event can trigger an anxious, aggressive, or avoidant reaction. In children whose somatic complaints become the vehicles for attaining closeness and support, relationships may come to have a manipulative cast. Continuity The continuity of trauma-­related disorders can vary significantly depending on the type, intensity, and specificity of the traumas experienced. These disorders often take the form of anxiety or depressive disorders, and they can also lead to various personality problems, including histrionic, borderline, narcissistic, paranoid, and antisocial personality disorders. Therapeutic Indications Children who have experienced trauma can benefit greatly from psychodynamic therapies, which have shown effectiveness when compared to alternative treatments. It is crucial for clinicians to allow children to explore their traumatic experiences at

chunks.heading: 344 Childhood
chunks.preview: 344 Childhood their own pace, avoiding pressure to discuss them before they are ready. Play is an especially important avenue for children’s nonverbal expression of what happened. It allows them to gradually elaborate on their traumatic experiences in a safe, supportive therapeutic environment. Clin...
chunks.text: 344 Childhood their own pace, avoiding pressure to discuss them before they are ready. Play is an especially important avenue for children’s nonverbal expression of what happened. It allows them to gradually elaborate on their traumatic experiences in a safe, supportive therapeutic environment. Clinical Illustration Alex, a 6-year-old boy, was brought to treatment due to uncontrollable behavior. He was thin, with angular features and wide eyes, and appeared disheveled and emotionally dysregulated. His mother was intensely emotional—­either overinvolved or appropriately engaged, depending on perspective. She was unmodulated, oscillating between anger and despair. In contrast, Alex’s father was passive and emotionally withdrawn. His older sister also appeared disheveled but was more regulated than Alex. At school, Alex had angry outbursts when asked to comply with requests. He was hypersensitive to perceived aggression and had no close friends. He appeared indifferent to social relationships. Alex had been diagnosed with a rare abdominal cancer at age 4. He underwent surgeries, painful procedures, chemotherapy, and isolation. Though these saved his life, they severely disrupted his development. During treatment, his mother remained physically close, often sharing a bed with him. She battled doctors over procedures and with her son to ensure compliance.
