There may be anxiety, depression, and a spectrum of intense emotions such as fear, worry, and frustration. Manifestations of anxiety and depression can vary greatly among children. Positive emotional states, in contrast, such as feelings of empowerment, security, hope, trust, and optimism can be nurtured through personal and parental coping processes, resilience, and attachment styles. When children are overwhelmed by fear, they may defensively dissociate, leading to the formation of contingent selves as a protective measure against fragmentation.

chunks.heading: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 383
chunks.preview: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 383 Trauma can instigate distinct self-­ states. This process can result in fragmented, trauma-­related representations that allow other aspects of the self to engage with a less distressing reality. Thoughts and Fantasies Children’s cog...
chunks.text: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 383 Trauma can instigate distinct self-­ states. This process can result in fragmented, trauma-­related representations that allow other aspects of the self to engage with a less distressing reality. Thoughts and Fantasies Children’s cognitive abilities are particularly vulnerable to adverse experiences due to their ongoing development. Research shows that executive functions—­such as working memory and inhibitory control—­are especially affected by stress, trauma, and depression. Children exposed to war or traumatic events often suppress certain cognitive functions as a defense mechanism, which can impair daily functioning. During the COVID-19 pandemic, social restrictions, increased screen time, and online education contributed to a rise in neuropsychological issues among children, with reduced social interaction posing a significant risk to cognitive development. Fear of contagion also led to more frequent compulsive cleaning behaviors and obsessive thoughts around hygiene. Regarding climate change anxiety, while research is still limited, some evidence suggests the presence of obsessive-­like thoughts and intense worries about the environment, along with other cognitive patterns typically linked to anxiety and panic. Somatic States Children’s subjective perception of their physical state may vary based on the severity and duration of exposure to adverse experiences. For instance, children in war-torn regions may sustain severe injuries, resulting in prolonged hospitalization, physical deformities, or permanent disabilities. The aftermath of such events can damage a child’s sense of physical self in the short or long term, depending on the gravity of the harm. The injuries may serve as constant reminders of the traumatic incident, impeding the psychological process of trauma resolution (Green & Kocijan-­Hercigonja, 1998). Adverse experiences arising from unpredictable environments can elevate anxiety or even lead to PTSD. Somatic issues (e.g., sleep disturbances, bedwetting, complaints of bodily discomfort) may accompany these mental states, altering a child’s perception of his or her physical self. During the COVID-19 pandemic and subsequent lockdowns, online studying expanded children’s screen time, often resulting in complaints of headaches. Confinement in small apartments and restrictions on regular school and outdoor activities fostered sedentary lifestyles, exacerbating weight problems for children already grappling with obesity. High stress levels and changes in dietary habits and daily routines disrupted children’s sleep patterns, contributing to increased fatigue. There is some evidence that climate change and air pollution detrimentally impact children’s physical health from an early age. These somatic conditions can persist over time, intersecting with psychological well-being and influencing a child’s self-image. Anxiety may be directly related to symptoms (e.g., respiratory issues caused by pollution). Clinicians need to consider all these factors when treating a child who has suffered adversity. Relational Patterns In times of crisis and amid unpredictable conditions, parental support is critical to children’s well-being. Exposure to fearsome and life-­threatening situations activates

chunks.heading: 384 Childhood
chunks.preview: 384 Childhood a person’s attachment system, prompting individuals to seek protection. In a secure attachment relationship, children rely on caregivers to provide safety and comfort in times of distress. Young children, in particular, tend to cling and hesitate to separate from their parents, who ser...
chunks.text: 384 Childhood a person’s attachment system, prompting individuals to seek protection. In a secure attachment relationship, children rely on caregivers to provide safety and comfort in times of distress. Young children, in particular, tend to cling and hesitate to separate from their parents, who serve as their secure base. Because of cognitive limitations stemming from their young age, they may struggle to fully comprehend the situations they face, relying heavily on their caregivers’ perspectives and responses to gauge safety. Although older children may have more capacity to grasp the complexities of events, they still require parental guidance to clarify uncertainties and validate their emotions. The psychological response of caregivers to unpredictable circumstances can act as either a risk or protective factor for children. In the wake of natural disasters or armed conflicts, parents may experience extreme anxiety, PTSD, grief, or depression, rendering them unable to provide the necessary emotional support for their children. When caregivers are incapable of containing their own emotions or creating a supportive environment for their children, there may be long-term damage to mental health for both child and caregiver. In war-torn regions, families endure prolonged suffering. Loss of a parent or separation from caregivers exacerbates children’s vulnerability to psychological distress. Children may develop relational or dyadic PTSD symptoms, shaped by their interactions with traumatized caregivers. Researchers and therapists have increasingly noted the significance of these perspectives in understanding children’s responses to parental trauma, identifying potential pathological developmental trajectories set in motion during childhood. Parental responses to climate change can significantly influence children’s reactions. Caregivers play a critical role in their children’s affect regulation by not only shielding them from alarming climate-­related news but also sensitively addressing their emotions without downplaying them or becoming overwhelmed by them. Positive outcomes may emerge in children’s relationships with peers, fostering prosocial behaviors and altruistic acts. The COVID-19 pandemic has complicated social interactions and family dynamics. Children have had to adapt to new norms, such as wearing masks and limiting physical contact, even with close friends and significant others. Confinement to homes has placed immense pressure on parents, who must juggle tele-­schooling, indoor activities, employment demands, and emotional support for their children. The increase in domestic violence and child abuse seen during lockdowns is symptomatic of the emotional turmoil experienced by parents in these unprecedented circumstances. Therapeutic Indications Exceptional demands fall on caregivers and on mental health professionals when children face challenging and/or unpredictable environments. Clinicians should identify both protective and risk factors influencing a child’s ability to process and manage large-scale traumatic events such as climate change, war, and pandemic outbreaks. Preexisting psychopathology may exacerbate symptoms or other mental health issues in children exposed to unpredictable circumstances. Strengthening protective factors and mitigating the impact of risk factors can enhance the resilience of both children and caregivers. Interventions with parents may help them to recognize and regulate their emotions. By doing so, they can better mentalize their children and attend to their emotional needs.

chunks.heading: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 385
chunks.preview: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 385 Enabling children to “speak the unspeakable” through various means is often a promising clinical avenue. Children who struggle to articulate their difficulties verbally may find alternative forms of expression through play or art. Pr...
chunks.text: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 385 Enabling children to “speak the unspeakable” through various means is often a promising clinical avenue. Children who struggle to articulate their difficulties verbally may find alternative forms of expression through play or art. Providing a safe clinical environment that encourages the expression of painful emotions can empower the child, fostering a sense of agency that facilitates the gradual processing of traumatic events. Clinical Illustration Eight-year-old Daphne was referred for psychotherapy at age 7 after being diagnosed with generalized anxiety disorder. She harbored persistent worries about unforeseen events that could affect her or her parents, fearing injury or death. These intense feelings of fear and helplessness often expressed themselves physically, in stomachaches and a frequent urge to urinate. Daphne withdrew from activities and social engagements with friends, was reluctant to leave her home and parents, and sometimes refused to attend school. After 9 months of psychotherapy, Daphne’s anxiety and somatic symptoms had diminished, enabling her to relish her social interactions and outdoor pursuits again. During summer vacation, she eagerly accepted an invitation to her grandparents’ country retreat, successfully spending a week away from her parents. Her stay was disrupted, however, by a forest fire in the vicinity that required a swift evacuation. Witnessing her cherished dolls being left behind and fearing for their destruction left Daphne distraught. Although the fire was extinguished just short of the house, she returned to her parents feeling deeply unsettled. She was preoccupied with the risk of conflagration, frequently questioned her parents about fire hazards, and sometimes wondered if she smelled something burning. After the summer break, Daphne resumed therapy. September brought heavy rains and devastating floods across the country, saturating television news coverage with catastrophic images. Daphne’s fears escalated, particularly her dread of drowning in floodwaters. She vigilantly monitored the sky for clouds and felt panic every time it rained, often refusing to venture outdoors, even for school. The ordeal of extreme weather conditions significantly disturbed her still fragile mental well-being, undermining her sense of security and precipitating a temporary relapse characterized by heightened anxiety symptoms. 6.2. Minoritized Demographic Groups (Ethnic, Religious, Linguistic, Racial) A cultural approach is essential to evaluating children from all backgrounds. Cultural factors affect household composition, food preferences, levels of acculturation if caregivers are from abroad, and the moral/religious/spiritual outlooks of members in a family (Causadias, 2013). Cultural factors also affect the age at which children accomplish key psychological tasks such as social learning, cooperating and collaborating with others, and developing a theory of the mind (Nielsen & Haun, 2016). Any cultural assessment must account for cross-­cultural differences in the ages at which children meet developmental milestones, the age at which children are expected to behave autonomously in terms of dressing, sleeping, and eating behaviors, and parenting and disciplining styles (Rousseau, Measham, & Bathiche-­Suidan, 2008).

chunks.heading: 386 Childhood
chunks.preview: 386 Childhood Children who are socially excluded on the basis of their minority status may, by late childhood, show negative mental health outcomes, including a diminished selfimage and feelings of inadequacy, especially in social environments. Young people who belong to minoritized religious groups...
chunks.text: 386 Childhood Children who are socially excluded on the basis of their minority status may, by late childhood, show negative mental health outcomes, including a diminished selfimage and feelings of inadequacy, especially in social environments. Young people who belong to minoritized religious groups may be at elevated risk for experiencing bias, discrimination, and a range of adverse emotional, health, and behavioral problems, including painful attacks on their faith at school. Parents who are members of ethnic and racial minorities play a critical role in their children’s psychological wellbeing, depending on how they navigate racial, ethnic, and cultural diversity. Research suggests that despite some externalizing difficulties, parental socialization is associated with positive self-­perceptions, higher quality of interpersonal relationships, and internalizing behaviors (Wang, Henry, Smith, Huguley, & Guo, 2020). Subjective Experience of Minoritized Groups in Childhood Individuals whose race, ethnicity, or religious affiliation differs from the majority of their peers may face overt stressors in various settings. These include victimization and harassment, which create anticipatory anxiety and hypervigilance about further minority stressors such as rejection and discrimination. Exposure to discrimination and victimization can have enduring psychological effects, including depressive symptoms, heightened negative emotions (especially anxiety, anger, and shame), lowered self-­esteem, and avoidance of situations perceived as stigmatizing. Instances of discrimination within educational settings have been linked longitudinally to feelings of victimization, vulnerability, social isolation, rejection, and concealment behaviors, such as passing as a member of the majority to avoid future victimization. Minority stress may foster the internalization of prejudice against one’s group, darkening one’s self-­concept. Many children encounter microaggressions based on their social identities. These are brief, seemingly insignificant verbal, behavioral, or environmental offenses—­ intentional or unintentional—­that convey hostile, derogatory, or negative messages to the target individual or group. For example, adopted children whose appearance differs from that of family members may face discomfiting inquiries or remarks about their adoptive status that implicitly demean their racial or ethnic inheritance or their caregivers. Early and middle childhood, when caregivers are the primary shapers of their children’s mental health, are critical stages in psychosocial and emotional development. The family plays a pivotal role at this time in supporting children in marginalized social groups. Therapeutic Indications Clinicians should carefully consider and respect the cultural origins of children and the various cultural systems in which they participate. These include the family, with its beliefs and practices; the community, which encompasses the characteristics of the neighborhood in terms of resources, culture, safety, and other specific conditions; and relevant children’s groups, including prevailing activities and peer group culture. They should recognize the interplay between cultural factors and ongoing development, which is a key part of a cultural assessment that differs from treating adult patients who come into care at more stable life phases. Moreover, clinicians should recognize the critical role that cultural, ethnic, and economic factors play in treatment.

chunks.heading: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 387
chunks.preview: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 387 6.3. Gender Nonconformity Gender nonconformity is the extent to which an individual’s behavior, appearance, and/or identity deviate from culturally prescribed gender norms associated with the child’s assigned sex at birth. Gender non...
chunks.text: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 387 6.3. Gender Nonconformity Gender nonconformity is the extent to which an individual’s behavior, appearance, and/or identity deviate from culturally prescribed gender norms associated with the child’s assigned sex at birth. Gender nonconformity differs categorically from gender incongruence, which entails marked and persistent distress resulting from dissonance between one’s experienced or expressed gender and one’s assigned gender at birth. It may look different depending on whether it emerges in early childhood or during adolescence or adulthood. Awareness of gender typically begins in early childhood, with children as young as age 2 beginning to label themselves as male or female. It is during this developmental stage that children may also express gender identities that diverge from their assigned gender. Gender nonconforming behavior in childhood does not always correspond to a transgender identity or gender incongruence later in life. Only around 15% of cases of gender incongruence in childhood persist into adolescence or adulthood. In many cases, the incongruence desists during puberty, with individuals identifying with their birth-­assigned gender but developing non-­heterosexual interests. It is vital to acknowledge the psychological and cultural developmental context of behaviors and experiences that diverge from gender expectations (e.g., “sissy” and “tomboy” children). Gender nonconformity, in which a child’s gender identity, role, or expression deviates from the cultural norms associated with that child’s birth-­assigned gender, differs significantly from genuine gender incongruence. Referrals to specialized gender identity services suggest that approximately 1.3 to 2.7% of schoolchildren self-­identify as transgender or gender-­nonconforming (Shields et al., 2013). Early indications of cross-­gender identity formation can be observed indirectly through stereotypically gender-­related behaviors, such as a preference for cross-­dressing and refusal to wear clothing typical of one’s assigned gender; a tendency to personify roles of the other gender in make-­believe games, selection of playmates of the other gender; and a preference for toys, games, or activities stereotypically associated with that gender. In some cases, these divergent behaviors and desires are accompanied by a strong, pervasive aversion to one’s primary sexual characteristics and a desire to have the sexual, primary, and/or secondary characteristics of the experienced gender. Research and clinical experience suggest that gender diversity in childhood is often more fluid than in adolescence, when gender identity tends to be more stable. Consequently, there are no reliable means of predicting an individual child’s gender evolution, and the gender-­related needs of a particular child may vary over the course of childhood. We should note, however, that gender nonconformity in children may lead to clinically significant distress or impairment in social situations, school, and other important areas. Designations of gender incongruence are controversial, particularly in childhood. While there is nearly universal support among clinical experts for maintaining diagnoses in adolescence and adulthood that facilitate access to health care, opinions diverge regarding the retention of diagnoses in childhood. Some clinicians contend that diagnosing gender incongruence in childhood is unnecessary and stigmatizing, and serves no medical purpose, whereas others argue for retaining childhood diagnoses, because despite the fact that gender-­incongruent children may not require medical intervention, they and their families often need substantial psychosocial support from mental health professionals.

chunks.heading: 388 Childhood
chunks.preview: 388 Childhood Clinical approaches to prepubescent children with gender incongruence are topics of significant controversy. They can vary with the protocols of particular clinics and the preferences of parents. One traditional approach, now generally considered outdated, involves collaborating with t...
chunks.text: 388 Childhood Clinical approaches to prepubescent children with gender incongruence are topics of significant controversy. They can vary with the protocols of particular clinics and the preferences of parents. One traditional approach, now generally considered outdated, involves collaborating with the child and caregivers to alleviate dysphoria related to gender incongruence and to diminish cross-­gender behaviors and identification. This approach assumes that reducing dysphoria will decrease the likelihood that the incongruence will persist into adolescence and develop into a transgender identity, which is perceived as an undesirable outcome on the grounds of social stigma and potential risks, and costs of hormonal and surgical procedures. Critics of this approach argue that there is no empirical evidence demonstrating that such treatment reduces the rate of persistence and prevents the development of a transgender identity, particularly considering that many children’s gender incongruence naturally resolves without intervention. An alternative approach, pioneered by the Dutch follows a watchful-­waiting model and refrains from actively trying to diminish gender incongruence or gender-­ atypical behaviors. Instead, it allows the natural unfolding of the developmental trajectory of gender identity, without anyone pushing or encouraging a specific outcome. Yet another approach, known as the gender-­affirmative model, validates a child’s cross-­gender identification through social rather than medical transitioning to a cross-­ gender role. This involves the child living publicly, either in all situations or in certain contexts, in alignment with the gender identity the child affirms. This approach avoids diagnoses, referring to these children simply as gender-­variant. Advocates of this approach believe that children who undergo this transition can return to their original assigned gender if their incongruence desists, as the transition is purely social and does not involve medical intervention. Critics argue that supporting gender transition in childhood increases the likelihood of persistence, risks a lifetime of medical treatment, and overlooks the complexities of reverting to the birth-­assigned gender for those children whose incongruence diminishes. Still, social transition process may provide a protective function for some prepubescent children and may thereby contribute to positive mental wellness. Regardless of the approach taken, there is widespread consensus that a multidisciplinary model offers the most effective way to provide gender-­affirming care to pediatric populations. This model should encompass medical, mental health, and social service professionals with expertise in gender-­related issues. Such a comprehensive approach promotes high-­quality medical and mental health care, as well as social and emotional well-being within family units, school environments, peer groups, and communities. Because gender is a social construct whose cultural expectations vary across societies, a cultural assessment of gender-­nonconforming youth should attempt to understand expectations from caregivers, peers in the child’s network, and the child directly. Subjective Experience of Gender Incongruence in Childhood Affective States Affective states may encompass both anxious and depressive moods. Gender-­ incongruent children may show disproportionate anger outbursts, tantrums, oppositional behavior, and frustration, particularly concerning their gender expression. When allowed to express their perceived gender, whether privately or publicly, these children typically report feelings of satisfaction and pleasure.

chunks.heading: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 389
chunks.preview: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 389 Thoughts and Fantasies Cognitive patterns may involve intense, obsessive thoughts and selective interests in gender-­related topics, such as fixations on specific gendered activities or behaviors, preoccupation with clothing, and con...
chunks.text: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 389 Thoughts and Fantasies Cognitive patterns may involve intense, obsessive thoughts and selective interests in gender-­related topics, such as fixations on specific gendered activities or behaviors, preoccupation with clothing, and concerns about the body and its physical characteristics. Somatic States Discomfort, shame, and a sense of detachment from the body are common. Only a small minority of young children openly express discomfort about their secondary sexual characteristics or express a desire to change their gender or body parts. But they may suffer fears and worries about their body, particularly in relation to growth. The inability to express their experienced gender may engender anger toward the child’s own body or toward external factors or individuals perceived as hindering gender expression. Frustration of one’s attempts to play or identify with one’s perceived gender may lead to self-­harming behaviors. Relational Patterns Gender-­incongruent children often face challenges in peer relationships and social interactions. They may gravitate toward playmates of the other gender, feel a sense of difference, and endure social isolation and withdrawal. These experiences can lead to low self-­esteem, fear of bullying, victimization, and struggles to feel accepted. They may attempt to navigate between their own perceived gender and the gender expectations of their peers. Clinical Illustration Tom, a 4-year-old assigned male at birth, healthy, and with no disorders of sexual development, had been trying on his mother’s jewelry and shoes since age 2. He often wore a towel as a skirt and colored his fingernails with crayons. When told not to do so, he would cry and become withdrawn. He preferred playing with girls and avoided roughhousing with boys. The father discouraged his cross-­gender interests; the mother saw them as a harmless, passing phase. She thought he might grow up to be gay, which was not a problem for her. After Tom kept insisting on sitting while urinating and hiding his penis, tensions between the parents about the “proper” response increased. They researched the various clinical approaches online and discussed them with their pediatrician. Neither wished to have the child make the social transition to being female (which would involve a name change and require using the girls’ bathroom at school). Although the father would have preferred to go to a clinic that discouraged cross-­gender behaviors, he acquiesced to his wife’s wish to follow a more permissive path that would help the couple come to terms with the child’s cross-­gender interests without a social transition. 6.4. Child Abuse Child abuse is any physical, sexual, or psychological mistreatment, deliberate harm, or neglect inflicted upon a child by a caregiver or other adult. It encompasses any action or failure to act that could lead to actual or potential harm for the child. Abuse can

chunks.heading: 390 Childhood
chunks.preview: 390 Childhood occur in various settings, including the child’s home, within familial or social circles, at school, within organizations, or within any community of which the child is a part. In addition to the direct experience of abuse, children often encounter myriad additional challenges and depr...
chunks.text: 390 Childhood occur in various settings, including the child’s home, within familial or social circles, at school, within organizations, or within any community of which the child is a part. In addition to the direct experience of abuse, children often encounter myriad additional challenges and deprivations. These may include physical ailments or disabilities, exposure to domestic violence, low family income, and a range of parental issues (e.g., parental mental illness), a history of parental abuse, or parental substance misuse. These difficulties can further jeopardize the child’s mental well-being and overall welfare. After experiences of abuse, not all children show overt symptoms or disorders. There are protective factors that foster a child’s resilience and ability to cope with adversity. But there is also evidence suggesting that childhood abuse can lead to a broad spectrum of emotional, physical, behavioral, social, and cognitive problems that may persist throughout an individual’s lifetime. Subjective Experience of Child Abuse Affective States Feelings of fear, helplessness, shame, anxiety, discomfort, and low self-­esteem are prevalent in abused children. In cases of sexual abuse, self-blame and self-hate can dominate the child’s inner mental world. Emotional turmoil can escalate to depressive reactions and self-­destructive behaviors. Poor impulse control affects various aspects of mental functioning, including cognitive capacities, the sense of agency, moral development, capacity for reflective function, sense of self, and ability to form relationships, indicating significant impairments in the use of effective defenses. Children often resort to primitive defenses such as denial, dissociation, projection, splitting, somatization, or avoidance of thinking to shield their minds from overwhelming painful emotions. These defenses serve to protect them from the terrifying realization that those they depend on for protection are the ones who harm them. Acknowledging the malevolence of caregivers would evoke intolerable rage, vulnerability, and helplessness. Thoughts and Fantasies Abused children often experience inner fragmentation, overstimulation, a heightened instinctual charge, and dissociation. Their inability to achieve a cohesive sense of self hinders their capacity to think coherently about their own mental states and those of others. Reliance on maladaptive defenses can impede the development of language, cognition, and motor skills, leading to learning difficulties and academic underachievement. Splitting processes may be used to preserve the illusion of having a nurturing parent, sometimes resulting in the child’s showing affection and attachment to the abusive caregiver. The negative qualities of the abusive caregiver are either projected onto others or internalized, creating a “bad” or “alien” aspect of the self. Consequently, the child continually seeks to externalize or project this aspect onto others, as internalizing it may lead to self-­directed aggression. Somatic States Physically abused children suffer a high prevalence of neurological abnormalities. These may stem from injuries sustained during the abuse, such as direct head traumas or “shaking injuries,” which can lead to brain damage, resulting in intellectual

chunks.heading: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 391
chunks.preview: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 391 disabilities or physical deformities. Abused children frequently experience an exacerbation of somatic complaints and symptoms, sleep disturbances leading to fatigue, functional neurological symptoms, enuresis and/or encopresis, and...
chunks.text: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 391 disabilities or physical deformities. Abused children frequently experience an exacerbation of somatic complaints and symptoms, sleep disturbances leading to fatigue, functional neurological symptoms, enuresis and/or encopresis, and heightened excitability, often in the form of hyperactivity, stemming from hypervigilance. Relational Patterns Abuse is often associated with attachment issues, principally a disorganized attachment style. When abuse occurs within the family, it creates an ongoing sense of insecurity and instability. The child’s resulting perception of the environment as potentially hostile may hinder the development of trust in relationships and interfere with attaining object constancy. Symptoms such as enuresis, encopresis, stealing, conduct disorders, and emotional instability or depression may lead the child to withdraw from peers or face rejection. Emotional neglect is a type of abuse that often instills feelings of deprivation and “emotional hunger.” Neglected children may have superficial relationships or be inclined toward aggressive outbursts, excessive demands, or antisocial behavior. Abused children may unconsciously replicate problematic relational patterns in new relationships with adults and peers, using primitive defenses such as projective identification and dissociation. The compulsion to repeat traumatic experiences arises from an unconscious desire to gain control over the trauma. There is considerable evidence of intergenerational transmission of the adverse effects of violence and abuse within families (Widom & Wilson, 2014). Parents who experienced maltreatment as children are more likely to perpetrate abuse against their own children; their identification with the aggressor is a maladaptive defense to cope with abuse. Continuity Childhood abuse has profound and enduring impacts, associated with a broad spectrum of lifelong challenges. These include depression, disruptive–­impulse control–­ conduct disorders, eating and sleep disturbances, functional neurological disorders, dissociation, and increased vulnerability to revictimization through subsequent abusive relationships in adolescence and adulthood. Childhood abuse has been implicated in the development of psychosis and of personality disorders, particularly borderline personality disorder, in adulthood. Experiences of abuse have also been linked to physical illnesses, including heart disease, cancer, and diabetes. Therapeutic Indications When child abuse is suspected, an interdisciplinary team must collaborate. Psychiatrists, psychologists, and social workers need to work alongside physicians, other therapists (e.g., speech and occupational therapists), schoolteachers, legal advisors, and district attorneys. This collaboration is necessary to protect the child, assess family dynamics, and identify protective factors. It is essential to evaluate the family’s ability to acknowledge the impact of abuse on the child’s behavior and the family’s readiness to make necessary changes. Interventions should address both the child and the family environment. If the assessment reveals risks to the child’s physical and mental well-being, temporary removal from caregivers may be necessary. Specialized units for abused children can

chunks.heading: 392 Childhood
chunks.preview: 392 Childhood provide emotional support in a therapeutic environment while legal decisions are made regarding foster care, reunification with a parent, or adoption. Mental health professionals play a vital role in supporting both child and parents, while remaining mindful of legal processes that aff...
chunks.text: 392 Childhood provide emotional support in a therapeutic environment while legal decisions are made regarding foster care, reunification with a parent, or adoption. Mental health professionals play a vital role in supporting both child and parents, while remaining mindful of legal processes that affect the family. When disrupted mental functioning or symptomatic development occurs, individual child psychotherapy may be necessary. Abused children often struggle with trust in relationships and are therefore slow to trust the therapist. Clinicians must navigate safety concerns while gradually addressing the traumatic experience of abuse. Observing the child’s body language during sessions can guide therapeutic interventions and help the clinician explore the child’s emotional state. 6.5. Adopted Children “Adoption” may be defined as the permanent, legal placement of an abandoned, relinquished, or orphaned child within a family of relatives (kinship adoption) or within an unrelated family (non-­kinship adoption). Many countries allow adoptions by not only heterosexual couples but also gay and lesbian partners. Adoptions occur through different pathways: foster care, kinship care, private domestic adoption, and international adoption. Adoption is associated with many rewards and significant challenges. On the positive side, it offers the prospect of stability, loving care, security, and lifetime family connections for children whose biological parents are unable to raise them. For children who would otherwise remain in neglectful or abusive homes, in long-term foster care, in orphanages, or with parents who are unwilling or unprepared to care for them, adoption can be a successful social intervention that fosters a remarkable remediation, particularly in the areas of physical growth, attachment, and cognitive development. Yet adoption poses many challenges and complications. Adopted children are overrepresented in mental health settings; compared with nonadopted peers, they show higher levels of externalizing and internalizing symptoms and learning problems. They do not show more serious self-­esteem problems or significant differences in IQ (Juffer & van IJzendoorn, 2007). Although adoptees do not achieve as well as nonadopted schoolmates, on average, they show better school performance than children who remain institutionalized. The question remains as to whether social stigma surrounding adoption, including low expectations for adjustment among adoptees, is associated with the depressive symptoms and behavioral difficulties frequently reported in clinical studies of adopted individuals. Societal attitudes about adoption (e.g., adoption stigma) play important roles in influencing adoptees’ development, perhaps particularly in cases of international and transracial placements (Farr & Grotevant, 2019). Indiscriminate friendliness (i.e., difficulty in understanding and maintaining appropriate social boundaries with strangers, lack of wariness and social inhibition around unfamiliar others, and even willingness to go off with a stranger) occurs disproportionately among children adopted internationally who were institutionalized prior to adoption. This behavior may persist for years following successful adoptive placement, despite rapid and nearly complete gains in other areas. Thus, early interventions for children experiencing disruptions or deprivation in care are particularly important across all types of adoptions. We note that it is not adoption status per se that leads to the more serious adjustment difficulties of these children, but rather adverse preadoptive circumstances such

chunks.heading: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 393
chunks.preview: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 393 as prenatal substance exposure, malnutrition, maltreatment, neglect, parental psychopathology, and an adverse genetic legacy. Higher age at the time of placement correlates with more difficulties, but this is often a proxy for length...
chunks.text: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 393 as prenatal substance exposure, malnutrition, maltreatment, neglect, parental psychopathology, and an adverse genetic legacy. Higher age at the time of placement correlates with more difficulties, but this is often a proxy for length of institutionalized care; that is, the association between age at adoption and outcomes can be explained by the presence of preadoption adversities. Individual and family dynamics related to adoption can have further effects on the child’s identity and parent–­child relationships, often leading to adjustment problems. Critical challenges for parents involve how and when to inform their children about their adoption and how to help them toward a healthy understanding of the meaning and implications of being adopted. Although many parents refer to their children’s origins throughout the first few years (if they disclose their adoptive status), active attempts to impart meaningful information to children typically begin in the preschool years. Most parents start with very basic information about their children’s backgrounds and gradually elaborate their adoption narratives as they get older. Children may interpret the information in varying ways that change over time; their evolving curiosity and knowledge may affect their adjustment, self-­esteem, and identity. The increase in school-­age children’s capacity for problem solving may lead them to reject, or at least challenge, their parents’ simple explanations of the circumstances of their adoption. By 6–8 years of age, children begin to appreciate the significance of family members’ biological connections. For many adopted children, this new knowledge raises questions about the nature and even authenticity of their belonging in the family (Brodzinsky, 2011). In middle childhood, logical thought emerges, enabling children to recognize that having gained a new family through adoption also means having been separated from a previous one. This insight sensitizes them to the reality of their loss, a core issue in the emotional adjustment of adopted individuals and one that helps explain the emergence of increased adjustment difficulties during this period. In attempting to understand the birth parent’s decision to give them up, some school-­age children begin to question whether they were ever wanted by the birth parents, a worry that can undermine their views of themselves. Young children’s curiosity about their adoption is not, however, linked with behavior problems. During middle childhood, the ability to understand another person’s perspective and to feel empathy for another’s plight grows significantly, helping adopted children imagine the problems faced by their birth parents. School-­age children often begin to wonder whether their birth parents are thinking of them and, if so, whether they feel sadness or even regret about their decision. In this context, it is normal for children to feel confused, anxious, or sad. As they develop a more realistic understanding of adoption, they begin to examine what it means to them as well as to others. Subjective Experience of Adopted Children Affective States Adoption is inherently connected to loss. Some adoption-­related losses tend to be felt in middle childhood; others, not until adolescence. The first and clearest loss experienced by adopted children is the separation from birth parents. For youngsters placed as babies, the sense of loss emerges slowly, usually around age 6 to 7, as they begin to take in the meaning and implications of being adopted. Because they have never formed attachments to the birth parents, these children seldom experience their loss as

chunks.heading: 394 Childhood
chunks.preview: 394 Childhood traumatic, nor do they feel a painful loss of biological siblings, grandparents, uncles, aunts, or cousins. For those adopted at older ages, however, the loss of birth parents, siblings, and/or extended family is likely to be felt more acutely because the adoption severed known relatio...
chunks.text: 394 Childhood traumatic, nor do they feel a painful loss of biological siblings, grandparents, uncles, aunts, or cousins. For those adopted at older ages, however, the loss of birth parents, siblings, and/or extended family is likely to be felt more acutely because the adoption severed known relationships, some of which may have provided considerable emotional security. Eventually, awareness of the primary loss may lead to a search for the birth parents and family, to supply the “missing pieces” of one’s identity. Adopted children also experience adoption-­related loss when they recognize that their peers may have negative attitudes about adoption, or about them because they are adopted. This type of loss accentuates feelings of difference that can undermine children’s self-­esteem and identity. So, too, can the fact of being physically, temperamentally, or behaviorally dissimilar to other family members. Because of the lack of a genetic link to their adoptive parents, it is common for adopted children to recognize that they do not look like others in the family or that their interests, personality traits, temperament, talents, skills, and/or behaviors are not particularly similar to those of their parents and siblings. Adoptees may experience this dissimilarity in varied ways. For some, it is just a matter of being different, with no inherent value attached to that fact. For others, especially for transracially placed children, the differences are deeply unsettling, arousing a feeling of not fitting into the family. Thoughts and Fantasies A belief that one was unwanted, and related ideas of defectiveness and unworthiness, can arise from the knowledge that one was relinquished by one’s biological parents. As adopted children mature cognitively and emotionally, they begin to fantasize about their birth parents, wondering who they are, what they are like, and what they may be doing with their lives. Nonadopted children often imagine themselves to have been adopted by their mundane parents from exceptional, powerful progenitors (the “family romance”). Such fantasies deal with issues that include omnipotence, anxiety, separation, control, inclusion–­exclusion, revenge, and fury; in other words, the fantasy of having been adopted may resolve various conflicts. For the adopted child, it is adoption itself that has produced psychic distress: The adoptee may wish to deny adoption, establish a fantasized blood tie to the adoptive parents, and thereby erase the humiliation associated with rejection by the biological parents. The family romances of adopted children are constructed from bits of knowledge infused with fantasies of self and others. Social services may provide adoptive families with snippets of information about the birth family, but these are highly dependent on what is deemed “appropriate” and are influenced by the fantasies of the professionals involved. The adoptive parents have their own fantasies, which they unconsciously relay to the child as they pass on the information. While all family romances are fantastical, the narratives of the adopted child are mixed with layer upon layer of limited fact, fantasy, and memory; children not adopted as newborns have memories of a life before adoption. For adopted children, the family romance is a powerful narrative in which feelings of abandonment, rejection, rescue, loss, vulnerability, disappointment, and insistent hope are complexly integrated. Somatic States For adopted children from deprived backgrounds, the first area to remediate is physical growth, the foundation of any psychological development. Malnutrition and neglect in

chunks.heading: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 395
chunks.preview: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 395 the first few months or years after birth may have impaired growth in height, weight, and head circumference. Adoption may not fully reverse this lag in development or be equally successful in each of those three areas. The first few...
chunks.text: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 395 the first few months or years after birth may have impaired growth in height, weight, and head circumference. Adoption may not fully reverse this lag in development or be equally successful in each of those three areas. The first few years after birth may be a more sensitive period for brain growth than for development; for damage caused by malnutrition, it is more difficult to recover from neural damage than from delayed physical (or psychological) growth. Brain growth may be hampered by not only food deprivation but also the stresses of institutional care. When adoptions take place without contexts of deprivation, there are no specific somatic states associated with them. Relational Patterns For children who were separated from their birth parents through relinquishment, abandonment, or death, basic trust is a key developmental issue. When they arrive in their adoptive homes, these children may show indiscriminate friendliness, reactive attachment disorders, or disorganized and insecure attachments. In early childhood (as in infancy) young adopted children’s attachment systems are capable of reorganizing and flexibly adapting to new caregivers after earlier relationships are disrupted. Most children who are placed in adoptive or foster homes appear to consolidate attachments to their new caregivers within a few months. They show substantial recovery in attachment quality; the prevalence of attachment security is considerably higher among adopted children than among those in maltreating homes or institutions. But relative to nonadopted children, those with preadoptive adversity are still at risk for forming insecure attachments to their adoptive parents. In middle childhood, the consequences of early maltreatment or institutionalization vary across different aspects of the attachment system. Such experiences appear to have long-term implications for children’s thoughts and feelings about close relationships in general. Adopted individuals with histories of adversity are more likely than nonadopted individuals to develop insecure internal working models. By contrast, there is only limited evidence that early maltreatment or institutionalization histories have long-term effects on adoptive individuals’ attachment security within the adoptive parent–­child relationship. Age of adoption has a key impact on the attachment pattern. Late-­placed children (i.e., those adopted after 12 months of age who have suffered at least one ruptured relationship with an attachment figure) usually have experienced a wide range of difficulties with their primary caregivers, ranging from neglect, rejection, and role reversal to physical, psychological, and sexual abuse. Because of such experiences, the attachment models of late-­placed children tend to be insecure or disorganized, leading to defiant and contradictory behaviors toward their adoptive parents and threatening the opportunity to create affectional bonds with them. Adoption of late-­placed children has often been described as a “natural experiment” that could alter the life courses of traumatized children. Being adopted offers them the chance to build new relationships that could help them revise their early insecure and/or disorganized internal working models toward more secure models, thus breaking the intergenerational transmission of the “cycle of abuse” and reorienting their development pathways. Therapeutic Indications Clinical work must take into account a child’s level of knowledge and understanding of adoption. In middle childhood, therapists need to emphasize the normality of

chunks.heading: 396 Childhood
chunks.preview: 396 Childhood children’s curiosity about their origins, to welcome their emotional responses to a better understanding of their families, and to help adoptive parents to validate and support their children’s efforts toward more knowledge about, and more connections with, their past. Clinicians worki...
chunks.text: 396 Childhood children’s curiosity about their origins, to welcome their emotional responses to a better understanding of their families, and to help adoptive parents to validate and support their children’s efforts toward more knowledge about, and more connections with, their past. Clinicians working with adopted children need to acknowledge and value the variety of their experiences while also recognizing their grief. Although one may point out, explicitly or implicitly, what a child has gained through adoption, if the therapist fails to acknowledge the pain of loss, the adopted child may feel ignored, misunderstood and unsupported. If adopted children feel understood and accepted, even in the midst of their adoption-­related confusion, sadness, and anger, they will eventually find a way to integrate this aspect of their lives into a healthy and secure sense of self. A common dynamic among adopted children is to project and displace their anger toward their biological and adoptive parents onto the therapist. They may transferentially view their therapists as angry and rejecting, reflecting their own projected self-­states or their expectations that the therapist will be like the biological or adoptive parents. In addition to these negative transferences, an adoptee may enact a number of idealizing transferences. Some adopted children attempt to maintain an internalized positive image of their biological parents to avoid the greater pain of discovering their limitations. They may come to see the therapist as a perfect, idealized object. They may try to protect the clinician, claim responsibility for the clinician’s inevitable mistakes during treatment, or deny that they are ever hurt by the therapist’s mistakes. The adopted child’s ability to distinguish reality from fantasy is often compromised by the fact that anyone from that child’s life could be the biological parent, an element that is crucial to take into account in transference. “I’ve always been aware that the adoption agency could have sold me to any family, and that it was purely random that I ended up with this mother and father,” one of our patients once said. The chronic fear of abandonment and the compromised sense of security resulting from losing one’s biological parents can lead to seeing the therapist as unreliable and therefore not acceptable as a reassuring object. As loving and dependent responses emerge during treatment and the therapist becomes a more emotionally valued figure, adopted children’s fears of abandonment and other hazards can be expected to intensify. A primary goal of therapy with adopted children is to facilitate a mourning process in which they progressively modify the conviction that they unequivocally belong with adoptive parents and understand that they have reached a new attachment relationship after a long, painful, sometimes dangerous journey. The clinician needs to avoid the risk of a catastrophic collapse of the adoptive bond, which is threatened by both the adopted child’s negative transference toward the new family and the adoptive parents’ inability to cope with that reality, and with their own reactive feelings. Therapists must help parents to distinguish their own dynamics from their child’s projected hostility. In other words, the adoptive parents need to deal with their children’s ambivalence without externalizing and projecting their own ambivalence (although they love the adopted child, that child may be seen as representing their failure to be biological parents). When adopted children are disturbed, difficult to care for, and seemingly unresponsive to the adoptive parents’ loving care, any parental fantasies of deficiency tend to be reinforced. The clinician may be initially idealized as a creative agent who can bring a wounded child to psychic life. When a relationship between a therapist and adoptive parents falters, the problem may involve a reenactment of the

chunks.heading: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 397
chunks.preview: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 397 parents’ unconscious experience of not having been able to conceive. Their experience of failure may then be projected into the therapist. Because of increasing rates of adoption across racial, ethnic, and linguistic lines, clinician...
chunks.text: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 397 parents’ unconscious experience of not having been able to conceive. Their experience of failure may then be projected into the therapist. Because of increasing rates of adoption across racial, ethnic, and linguistic lines, clinicians should inquire about the identities of each member in the family (Aggarwal, 2010). Rather than assume a patient’s cultural identity, clinicians can ask youth and caregivers how they self-­identify, paying attention to differences in identifications. Clinicians should ask the child and caregivers about other biological and adoptive siblings. They should also ask caregivers whether there are differing role expectations and responsibilities within the family based on biological differences, while asking children if they perceive such differences. Clinical Illustration Mara, age 8, had been given up by a mother who lived in a poor country and could not afford to keep her. Mara had never known her father, who died when she was a few years old. She had been neglected and had suffered sexual abuse by male friends of her mother; she had also been abused in at least one of her prior foster homes. She was referred because of phobias, anxieties, and learning problems. In psychotherapy, her assertiveness and self-­assurance grew over time. One game was particularly important. She and her young female therapist had to (pretend to) dress up in sparkling gowns and twirl around in front of Prince Charming. The prince would then announce who was the more beautiful, who would accompany him to the ball. This game was repeated incessantly. Week after week, with increasing confidence, decreasing anxiety, and not a little healthy sadism, Mara relegated the therapist to second place. The clinician saw this as a positive development. Mara had been told that her birth mother had been too poor to care for her. She seemed to believe she could not compete with such a damaged mother for fear of hurting her further. But she gradually came to experience her therapist as a robust helper who could not only tolerate but also actively enjoy her competition. As therapy progressed, her parents reported that her phobias were diminishing, her memory and learning skills improving, and her general mood brightening. Eventually Mara gave up the sparkling dresses game, having evidently used it to work through something. Her relationship with the clinician began evolving: She began to see the therapist as a parental figure who could contain her rivalry and aggression without retaliation, seeing her less as a rival, on an equal footing. She began to play new games with the baby doll. In earlier sessions, this baby had always been stolen in the night or given away to a granny to be looked after; now she began to care for the baby herself, tenderly and imaginatively. 6.6. Children Born through Assisted Reproduction For individuals who are physically unable to conceive (biological infertility) or for those without a partner to conceive with (social infertility), the use of assisted reproductive technologies (ART) is an increasingly popular option for creating a family. These technologies include both homologous techniques (using eggs and sperm of the intended parents) and reproductive donation (using donated gametes or surrogacy). Along with technological advancements, shifts in societal attitudes and consequent legislation have permitted families of varying characteristics, depending on the

chunks.heading: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 401
chunks.preview: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 401 misunderstand it as a sign of repudiation, thus reinforcing their fears that a conniving kidnapper (the donor or the surrogate) might displace them in their child’s heart. Only with a foundation of trust and safety can a child feel a...
chunks.text: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 401 misunderstand it as a sign of repudiation, thus reinforcing their fears that a conniving kidnapper (the donor or the surrogate) might displace them in their child’s heart. Only with a foundation of trust and safety can a child feel adventurous enough to wander beyond the confines of the family, even in fantasy. Without that safety, the space for the family romance can easily collapse. As children move through latency to adolescence and into emerging adulthood, the family romance can facilitate the construction of their identity and protect against identity confusion. Subjective Experience of Having Been Born through Assisted Reproduction Affective States The emotional states of ART children depend on the amount of information they have about their origin and their level of understanding of it. Affective states may include feeling different, special, indifferent, or confused. These children’s feelings do not depend so much on their family structure (i.e., whether they have two mothers, a mother and a father, two fathers, or a single mother); rather, they depend on the age and circumstances of disclosure. Children who are told in their teen years or later and/ or who discover the circumstances of their birth accidentally, tend to feel anger and resentment toward their parents. Thoughts and Fantasies To make sense of their genetic and gestational roots, ART children may need to create in fantasy a donor or a surrogate who wants them, rather than someone who callously gives gametes away or loans out a womb and then walks away. As they try to find their place in the family and feel challenged by too many players, they may, like parents, feel a need to expel the birth other from the family circle and transform that person into an “evil other” (Ehrensaft, 2005) to sustain a traditional triangular membership group, to which only intended parents need apply. In the traditional oedipal triangle, a child grows anxious about fantasies of stealing one parent from the other, fearing retribution, punishment, even genital mutilation. But in ART oedipal narratives, it is the child, not the parent, who may be stolen away—a fantasy that may be held by both parents and child. For the child, the fear may be not so much one of fantasized annihilation, but of being spirited away, leaving no parents with whom one can fall in love or learn to be a third. Children facing this fantasy may dispense with the conniving kidnapper to get on with the tasks of individuation, identity, and belonging with their parents. Alternatively, the child may bring the donor or surrogate into another mental configuration. As the child grows older, a donor or surrogate can be transformed once again from the generous person of the early stories to the conniving kidnapper, to the king or queen who will bring the child fame and fortune. The child traverses the oedipal circle and lands on the birth other as the key player in the latency-­age family romance, as the child dreams of an idealized family into which now it is the parents who need not apply to belong. Somatic States There are no specific somatic states associated with being conceived through ART. Somatic states in these children reflect their individual symptomatic patterns.

chunks.heading: 402 Childhood
chunks.preview: 402 Childhood Relational Patterns ART children’s primary attachments and identifications are with their parents, the people who nurture them, whether genetically connected or not. These very attachments and identifications are the source of their fears of hurting or even losing their parents if they...
chunks.text: 402 Childhood Relational Patterns ART children’s primary attachments and identifications are with their parents, the people who nurture them, whether genetically connected or not. These very attachments and identifications are the source of their fears of hurting or even losing their parents if they think about, fantasize about, or wish to find their donor or surrogate. Parents sometimes respond to their children’s curiosity about, or involvement with, the birth other with subtle or overt discomfort or dread, reverting to early fears of the birth other as kidnapper. As they see their children develop deeper understandings of their origins, or more elaborated ideas about the birth other and to the birth other’s placement in the family circle, they may suffer. As children negotiate the tasks of “Am I different?”, “To whom do I belong?”, and “Who am I?”, the parent–­child relationship is critical in facilitating resolutions of the child’s dilemmas. Good outcomes depend on the extent to which the child feels parentally supported and free to weave together a narrative that includes all the participants in their family. Parents will discover that their child’s bond to them is solid and that the family they have built with the aid of ART is highly resilient. Therapeutic Indications Therapists working with ART children should be mindful that despite their fantasies, children may very well want what they have, or want not to lose what they have, and not necessarily want what to therapists (or others) is seen as missing. When we automatically assume a deficit (e.g., a child does not have a father, just a sperm donor) we may be projecting our own anxieties about these new ways of making babies, anxieties that stem from discomfort, prejudices, or un-­thought-­out criticisms of the child, parents, and donor or surrogate. For ART children and their parents, the therapy room should be a psychic sphere in which they can explore freely together their psychic interpolations about the birth other, the birth other’s place in the family life, and each of the parents’ and children’s connections to one another (whether blood ties or affective ones). Encouraging a family reverie allows the child’s sense of belonging to their parents to be consolidated. In such a space, children can keep images of the donor or surrogate in mind and come to the parents with those images; the parents can respond with thoughts about this person who was instrumental in bringing their child to life. ART parents will benefit from learning that the family romance is not a bond-­breaker; it is a private affair in which the child can begin to individuate and explore a world apart from parents, using the birth other as a foil. Parents can be reassured to learn that the family romance dovetails nicely with the child’s identity tasks: Through fantasies about the birth other, the child can compensate for potential genealogical bewilderment and engage in internal mirroring and self-­discovery. In contrast to the family reverie, the parent’s role in the family romance is to stay out rather than step in. If the child shares these fantasies, parents need to receive the child’s internal musings as self-­explorations rather than repudiate them as threats to attachment. Within this circular process, the therapist plays a key role. By normalizing fantasies about the birth other and bringing them to the surface, parents will be spared potential guilt, shame, or resentment; the fantasies will shrink to a manageable size rather than expand to delusional proportions. Clinicians can soothe any tensions between partners and pave the way for a more realistic role of the birth other in the family’s life. Therapists need to monitor and process their own experiences with

chunks.heading: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 403
chunks.preview: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 403 parenthood and (in)fertility, and their ideas about how families should be formed and how children should be conceived, lest they lead to biased clinical evaluations and treatments. Similarly, when working with children born to paren...
chunks.text: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 403 parenthood and (in)fertility, and their ideas about how families should be formed and how children should be conceived, lest they lead to biased clinical evaluations and treatments. Similarly, when working with children born to parents of minoritized sexual identities, clinicians need to be mindful of their assumptions about motherhood and fatherhood and about gender-­biased caregiving activities. In cases in which the therapist is informed by parents about the assisted conception and the child is ignorant of that reality, the transference–­countertransference dynamic may involve the therapist’s leaking the “unthought known” to the child in unconscious or subtle ways. There is no baby without all those who intended to have the baby and all those who participated in its procreation. There will be no healthy child in the absence of awareness of the tapestry woven by threads from each of the participants as they relate to the child and to each other. When children go in search of their birth other, which may occur in adolescence or later, attention to the family tapestry from conception forward will have prepared parents for that search. It will help them see that the accomplishment of all three developmental tasks—self-­concept, belonging, and identity—­may be facilitated by contact with the birth other, not as an idealized or “true” parent but as a person responsible for either the genes or the prenatal growth of the child, a person who may be holding that child in mind. Inviting prospective parents to begin thinking about all these matters before their baby is even a blip on the sonogram screen may be the best insurance policy for their child’s healthy development. Clinical Illustrations Gina, 9 years old, was referred by her parents Lucilla and Marek for severe anxiety, especially at bedtime, when she required both parents to pat her cheeks five times and hold her hands until she fell asleep and “the crawling dragon disappears from my bed.” She had had visits from this intrusive image every night for 6 months. Other symptoms included a compulsion to rearrange items on her desk and a fear of contamination by germs, including dust balls she could see in the light. Early in therapy, she did a lot of artwork and enjoyed being messy, but she needed permission to express “ugly feelings” such as anger and disgust. Parallel work with her parents was critical. Gina had been conceived through anonymous sperm donation. Her parents had ruled out disclosing her origins to their daughter. Marek was too frightened that he would lose her love once she knew he was not her genetic father. Only when he felt his that bond with Gina was strong enough did he propose to Lucilla that they reveal to their daughter the truth of her conception. In response, Lucilla expressed her conviction that withholding the information from Gina was the right thing to do, that it would be much better if she did not know—not now, not ever. Marek had his doubts about continuing to conceal this information; he worried about being dishonest with his daughter, especially now that he felt ready to raise the topic. The tension between Lucilla and Marek—to disclose or not to disclose—­made the therapist curious, as it is typically the nongenetic parent who is more squeamish about telling the truth to a child. Why was Lucilla so reluctant, after Marek elaborated upon his nongenetic fatherhood, when he expressed uneasiness about keeping the secret? Lucilla expressed worry that if they were to tell their daughter, Gina would turn the insemination information into a sexual issue: “You mean you did it with someone else, Mommy?” In a parent session that Marek could not attend, Lucilla became more specific about the circumstances of Gina’s conception. When she described the sperm donor’s

chunks.heading: 404 Childhood
chunks.preview: 404 Childhood involvement, her voice lowered, and her face took on a dreamy look as she reported with pride that Gina’s donor was a pediatric surgeon from Sweden. It was as if she were describing the most erotic of intimate rendezvous rather than a medical procedure. At the end of the session, it wa...
chunks.text: 404 Childhood involvement, her voice lowered, and her face took on a dreamy look as she reported with pride that Gina’s donor was a pediatric surgeon from Sweden. It was as if she were describing the most erotic of intimate rendezvous rather than a medical procedure. At the end of the session, it was clear that in her deepest private fantasies, she had been unfaithful to her husband with an attractive, powerful doctor. She seemed to have projected her sexual fantasies about the donor into her worry about Gina’s potential questions about the insemination. Her desire to keep Gina’s origins secret betrayed her desire to keep secret her fantasy of adultery. The therapist hypothesized that Lucilla’s sexual fantasies had been transmitted to Gina unconsciously, that the girl had absorbed and enacted them as ideas of contamination and invasion from “the crawling dragon” that disappeared once her parents were close to her bed. When Lucilla talked with Marek in therapy about her fantasies about the donor, it became possible to normalize them and alleviate her guilt over the fantasized adultery. Gina’s anxiety and need for bedtime rituals diminished. While her parents were still dealing with the issue of disclosure—­how and when to tell Gina about her sperm donor insemination—­their daughter was gradually becoming less anxious and less focused with order and control. 6.7. Children with Giftedness, High Cognitive Potential, and Talent Individuals are labeled as “gifted” if they demonstrate, or have the potential to demonstrate, exceptional abilities in areas considered significant within their cultural context (Keating, 2009; Pfeiffer, 2013; Sternberg, Jarvin, & Grigorenko, 2010). Although an IQ of 130 or more is commonly used as the threshold for giftedness, this criterion is not universally accepted (Carman, 2013). According to this definition, approximately 2.2% of the population would be classified as gifted. If the threshold is lowered to include individuals with an IQ above 120, often referred to as having “high cognitive potential,” the proportion of the population classified as gifted would increase to approximately 6.7%. Giftedness is often assessed through IQ evaluations but is not solely defined by a numerical score. Because factors such as motivation and drive to excel often outweigh static abilities, giftedness is better depicted in terms of a functional profile. Giftedness arises from numerous internal and external sources. Recently, scholarly focus has shifted from merely recognizing intellectual ability or academic achievement to identifying other talents such as creativity, leadership, and artistic aptitude. Understanding how these talents develop into strengths involves examining social, cultural, environmental, psychological, and educational factors, recognizing that different abilities follow different developmental paths. Giftedness encompasses a range of cognitive, behavioral, and personality factors. Gifted individuals are unique, but they share some characteristics: early and advanced language skill, problem-­solving abilities, exceptional memory, strong curiosity and drive to learn, high energy levels, intense emotional reactions to stimuli, heightened emotional sensitivity, perfectionism, struggles with emotion regulation, and a strong sense of justice. These traits may not always coexist and can be manifested differently, even among children with similar IQ scores. They may vary throughout an individual’s life or in different environments. Several studies suggest that certain traits associated with giftedness make gifted persons more susceptible to stressors (Pfeiffer & Stocking, 2000). Their sensitivity

chunks.heading: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 405
chunks.preview: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 405 may lead to intense emotional reactions to adverse events and circumstances, while perfectionism and excessively high expectations of themselves and others are risk factors and potential sources of distress. These traits align with t...
chunks.text: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 405 may lead to intense emotional reactions to adverse events and circumstances, while perfectionism and excessively high expectations of themselves and others are risk factors and potential sources of distress. These traits align with the concept of “overexcitability” (Dabrowski, 1964), which can appear across psychomotor, sensory, intellectual, imaginative, and emotional domains. Another notable feature of gifted individuals is asynchronous development across domains (Pfeiffer & Stocking, 2000; Webb et al., 2005). Social, emotional, physical, and/or relational skills may lag behind early and robust cognitive development. Asynchrony does not denote psychopathology and does not usually create significant difficulties if caregivers and others recognize the child’s needs. When disparities among intellectual, emotional, social, and motor development do impede the daily life of a gifted individual, leading to potential psychological problems as the child’s distress escalates, the child is described as having desynchrony. Psychometrically, developmental asynchrony appears as a verbal–­performance discrepancy in Wechsler IQ tests, which quantify the cognitive disparity between verbal abstraction skills and nonverbal reasoning. Gifted children have distinctive ways of seeing the world that are often misunderstood. Their excitability may be misconstrued as excessive and categorized as hyper­ active disorder; their critical attitude may be interpreted as defiance of authority; their imagination and propensity for divergent thinking may be seen as inattentiveness; their intense emotionality, as immaturity; and their creativity and self-­absorption, as signs of oppositional defiant disorder (Gross, 1998). Gifted children are frequently unrecognized as such and are often referred to clinicians for problems arising from phenomena that, if solely interpreted as symptoms, can exacerbate the child’s challenges (Di Renzo, Palladino, Bianchi Di Castelbianco, & Racinaro, 2017). Symptoms-­ oriented diagnoses and patterned therapeutic interventions may miss the underlying distress of these children and increase the risk of psychopathological outcomes. Misdiagnosis may stem from insufficient knowledge and training in the area of giftedness (Webb et al., 2005). The most prevalent misdiagnoses include attention-­deficit/hyperactivity disorder, oppositional defiant disorder, obsessive–­compulsive disorder, mood disorders, and autism spectrum disorders. Clinically referred children with high intellectual potential typically have internalizing problems, such as self-focus issues stemming from excessive emotional and behavioral control. Those classified as “highly” gifted (IQ ≥ 145) and those showing developmental asynchrony via significant discrepancy between verbal and performance IQ scores, are more likely to experience behavioral disturbances (Guénolé et al., 2013). When giftedness is accurately diagnosed, cognitive potential should always be factored into the treatment plan. Subjective Experience of Giftedness in Childhood Affective States Gifted children may feel things intensely across various domains—­psychomotor, intellectual, emotional, sensory, and imaginative—­and may become easily overstimulated. While their heightened sensitivity can lead to a deeper engagement with life’s experiences, it can make it hard for them to maintain emotional equilibrium. They require stimuli that match their level of knowledge, cognitive potential, and zone of proximal development.

chunks.heading: 406 Childhood
chunks.preview: 406 Childhood Their intense and passionate approach to activities can be trying for those around them, as they may be demanding and exhausting to manage. Their vivid imagination, intuition, thirst for knowledge and complexity, creativity, perfectionism, and boundless energy may be impressive, but th...
