Social relationships, primarily with peers, play a key role in regulating adolescent self-­esteem. In what can become a vicious cycle, adolescents’ assessment of their own worth influences their emotional and behavioral responses to social feedback, which in turn contributes to their ability to regulate self-­esteem. Adolescents have varying levels of self-­esteem, influenced by different factors, especially age. Overall, self-­esteem tends to stabilize or even increase during middle and late adolescence and into early adulthood. Individuals whose self-­esteem is high in childhood are likely to become adolescents with high self-­esteem. Higher levels of self-­esteem are related to relationships with attuned primary caregivers who convey to infants that they are worthwhile people, that their lives are meaningful, that they will be valued by others. These messages enhance self-­perception and positive

chunks.heading: 432 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 432 Adolescence behaviors, and equip the child to face diverse tasks and challenges, including new situations. Adolescents with high self-­esteem tend to have healthy mental functioning, good interpersonal interactions, higher academic performance, and effective coping strategies. Low self-­esteem i...
chunks.text: 432 Adolescence behaviors, and equip the child to face diverse tasks and challenges, including new situations. Adolescents with high self-­esteem tend to have healthy mental functioning, good interpersonal interactions, higher academic performance, and effective coping strategies. Low self-­esteem involves negative attitudes toward the self and a poor ability to cope with daily tasks and developmental challenges. It may diminish adaptation and resilience, and is associated with peer victimization and mental problems, including depression, anorexia nervosa, delinquency, self-harm, and even suicide. Adolescents with low self-­esteem frequently make poor decisions as a result of their lack of confidence and sense of worth. Given all the physical, mental, and social changes occurring in adolescence, self-­esteem can be fragile and subject to fluctuations. DEVELOPMENTAL GUIDELINES: Capacity to Regulate Self-­Esteem in Adolescence Early Adolescence (ages 12–14) Young adolescents face major bodily, psychological, and social changes that threaten their self-­esteem regulation. Their difficulty in managing these changes may be intense and may challenge their prior certainties, leading to a sense of low self-­efficacy and poor agency. At this phase, they are particularly vulnerable to others’ judgments, especially those coming from peers. They struggle to keep their self-­ esteem in balance. These vulnerabilities may evolve into psychological problems or symptomatic expressions. Middle Adolescence (ages 15–16) During this stage, body transformation is slow in girls but ongoing in boys. Self-­ perception as a sexual body begins to stabilize. Relationships with peers may remain difficult and poorly defined, as increasingly complex environmental demands do not yet foster adequate security and stability in the evaluation of oneself as effective, adequate, and valuable. Self-­esteem tends to fluctuate; as adolescents address developmental challenges, they may feel powerful and effective at some points and powerless and inadequate at others. Late Adolescence (ages 17–19) The experience acquired during the earlier stages of adolescence may by now have fostered a stronger sense of identity, personal resources, relational abilities, sexual self-­awareness, and of other pivotal dimensions. This new awareness drives adolescents to make sense of their thoughts, affects, needs, and behaviors and has a marked impact on their evaluation of their own worth. Although there is still uncertainty about the future, most older adolescents are able to imagine themselves as effective individuals who can meet the challenges posed by further developmental tasks. Rating Scale 5. At this level, adolescents are able to maintain a stable, realistic sense of wellbeing, confidence, vitality, and self-­esteem, both across contexts and when under

chunks.heading: Mental Functioning in Adolescence 433
chunks.preview: Mental Functioning in Adolescence 433 stress. Self-­esteem seems appropriate to both internal and external and internal circumstances. They may show realistic confidence in their ability to cope with a wide range of complex tasks and situations, including novel and unexpected ones. These adaptive c...
chunks.text: Mental Functioning in Adolescence 433 stress. Self-­esteem seems appropriate to both internal and external and internal circumstances. They may show realistic confidence in their ability to cope with a wide range of complex tasks and situations, including novel and unexpected ones. These adaptive characteristics positively influence the ways in which adolescents think, feel, and relate to others and the world at large. 3. At this level, feelings of well-being, confidence, vitality, and self-­esteem are generally adequate but can easily be compromised by strong emotions and stressful situations. Feelings of vulnerability and inadequacy may result in adolescents’ loss of confidence in their ability to cope with certain tasks (at school and elsewhere), to achieve desired goals, or to act in effectively in the world. When this happens, they may seek external support from peers or valued authorities to sustain a fragile sense of being “good enough.” 1. Adolescents at this level feel empty and incomplete, and appear very concerned about their sense of their own worth. Self-­esteem is unstable; it may swing abruptly between extremes. There may be inconsistencies between internal experience and manifest behavior (e.g., arrogant behaviors that compensate for inner feelings of vulnerability) and between implicit and explicit self-­esteem. In both cases, there is reduced agency, adaptation, and resilience. 7. Capacity for Trust, Empathy, and Intimacy The capacity for trust, empathy, and intimacy denotes an adolescent’s ability to adjust relational distance and closeness in response to interpersonal and intrapsychic circumstances, to commit to others, to seek out mutual and collaborative exchanges, to communicate adaptively, and to rely on relationships as a source of relevant and generalizable knowledge, worth integrating into one’s life and mental organization. This last facet has been recently conceptualized as epistemic trust (Fonagy, Luyten, Allison, & Campbell, 2019). This capacity allows for mentalization, the ability to be aware of, try to comprehend, and respond to others’ subjective states with imaginative inference about what it would be like to be in that person’s situation. This process permits empathy, that is, vicariously experiencing another person’s emotional states. Finally, it refers to the ability to engage in stable, reciprocally satisfying relationships with adults and peers, friends, and romantic partners. This capacity is ultimately dependent on the attachment patterns developed in early childhood and the coherence of the resulting internal working models. Secure attachment patterns nurture the ability to establish intimate relationships in which one both accepts care from others and provides care to them. Primary caregivers play an essential role in the development of this capacity. Caregiver–­infant interactions that promote a secure attachment bond also foster the flexible integration of experiences and the ability to manage stress, interpersonal conflicts, and ambiguity. Through ostensive cues and marked, contingent mirroring (e.g., Fonagy & Allison, 2014), they convey that significant others are reliable, attentive, and well-­intentioned communicators, and that relational exchanges are vital for learning and adaptation. Such interactions help children lower their level of epistemic vigilance and open the channel of trust, learning about their interpersonal and cultural world and using others’ feedback to modify behaviors and mental representations adaptively throughout the lifespan.

chunks.heading: 434 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 434 Adolescence During adolescence, individuation and intimacy processes are complementary rather than mutually exclusive. This capacity is intertwined with identity development, as well as with the capacity for agency and purpose. Adolescents who are able to form intimate, empathic, and healthy rel...
chunks.text: 434 Adolescence During adolescence, individuation and intimacy processes are complementary rather than mutually exclusive. This capacity is intertwined with identity development, as well as with the capacity for agency and purpose. Adolescents who are able to form intimate, empathic, and healthy relationships—­behaving in a trustworthy way and trusting others to do the same—­experience such relationships as an integral part of their emerging identity and as a source of vital information. This process significantly influences, and is influenced by, the ability to reason about mental states of others and assume their perspective, to perceive one’s own and another person’s affective states, to respond to in a balanced and effective manner, and to function well defensively. Mature intimacy also entails ease with autonomy and independence, which are fundamental aims of adolescence. In the teen years, attachment relationships with external and internalized caregivers undergo significant changes. Brain development and growing cognitive and emotional abilities foster a reevaluation of past and present exchanges with parents. A negotiation, often a trying one, begins between autonomous exploration and dependence. During this phase, there is a gradual change in the people who mostly activate the attachment system, from caregivers to peers. In the peer group, relationships require cooperation and reciprocity and the ability both to offer and to ask for care. Such changes may lead the varied functions of the attachment system to operate less harmoniously, as adolescents compensate for the loss of intimacy with early caregivers. The area of intimate peer relationships is one in which teenagers can experiment with attitudes and behaviors, learn different ways to cope with difficulties, test cultural and social expectations, and develop and validate new perspectives on the world and the self. The reframing and reorganization of mental representations of attachment figures (supported by growing mentalization skills and the emergence of a new epistemic space in which to reexamine past experiences), the ongoing changes in relationships with parents, and the increasing importance of friends and partners as a source of support add up to challenging developmental tasks in this domain. Adolescents with the need to have their attachment needs met have to balance their natural propensity to rely on the peer group more than on adults, discover how to negotiate closeness and distance in different forms of rapport, and find out from whom it is most useful and safe to learn. To establish what sources of knowledge are reliable, they must find an equilibrium between epistemic vigilance and trust among different sources of social information, a task that may be complicated by the drive to be accepted within the peer group. Difficulties in mentalizing and emotional regulation may arise in ambiguous or conflicting situations, and the progressively more intense need for autonomy of thought and behavior may make adolescents excessively mistrustful of specific figures, and/or overly credulous toward others. Traumatic experiences in childhood may distort this process, leading some adolescents to increased epistemic vigilance self-­ protectively or, in contrast, to be easily manipulated and exploited (i.e., blind trust or credulity). Complex trauma may result in a profound confusion about who can be trusted and who cannot, and about how to interpret the behavior of others. The processes subsumed in this mental capacity include the adolescent’s emerging sexual behaviors and orientations. Integration of sexuality into one’s self-­conception is a notable task of this life phase. A mature capacity for relationships and intimacy allows for the adolescent’s exploration of sexuality and for the integration of sexuality within emotional intimacy. The formation of healthy and intimate relationships

chunks.heading: Mental Functioning in Adolescence 435
chunks.preview: Mental Functioning in Adolescence 435 is empirically associated with greater self-­esteem and psychological well-being, while problems in trust and empathy may represent significant risk factors for the development and maintenance of psychopathology. DEVELOPMENTAL GUIDELINES: Capacity for Trust, Em...
chunks.text: Mental Functioning in Adolescence 435 is empirically associated with greater self-­esteem and psychological well-being, while problems in trust and empathy may represent significant risk factors for the development and maintenance of psychopathology. DEVELOPMENTAL GUIDELINES: Capacity for Trust, Empathy, and Intimacy in Adolescence Early Adolescence (ages 12–14) At this age, a notable shift occurs in the interests and interactions of adolescents. They become more concerned with privacy and with exchanges with peers perceived as similar. They more frequently feel and express social emotions (e.g., blushing in reaction to real or perceived evaluation). Their desire for a close friend becomes particularly strong, though relationships within the peer group fluctuate rapidly and are based primarily on concrete similarities (e.g., personal taste and preferences, favored activities). Females tend to be ahead of males in their the ability to form connections based on support, self-­affirmation, and mutual understanding. The physiological changes accompanying this stage induce explorations of one’s body, including masturbation. Concerns about normality and acceptance may arise. While young adolescents increasingly value peer relationships, they continue to rely on parents and other adults, who remain important sources of new knowledge in times of vulnerability, danger, or separation. Skepticism or mistrust toward authority figures may also emerge. Middle Adolescence (ages 15–16) Middle adolescents increasingly value mutuality and interdependence over control and conformity. Their relationships become characterized by more open discussions of personal matters (again, gender difference may be present). They develop a greater sense of reciprocal responsibility and increasingly direct attachment needs to peers, who replace caregivers as sources of intimacy and relevant new knowledge. They may feel both tenderness and fear in the area of sexuality; concerns about sexual attractiveness become prominent. Sexual orientation and activity become central preoccupations. They begin dating, typically in brief and casual ways that serve various purposes (e.g., to alleviate loneliness, validate one’s value and attractiveness, attain social status, and explore). Perceived social norms play a significant role in shaping the intimate relationships of middle adolescents. Their sexual preferences and behaviors, for example, may be affected by anticipated approval or disapproval from others. Relationships within the peer group hold considerable power over their thoughts, values, feelings, and behaviors, while they strive to separate themselves from parental figures. Late Adolescence (ages 17–19) At this stage, adolescents are increasingly concerned with the formation of stable and exclusive relationships, particularly with romantic partners. They begin to show a clear sexual identity and are able to balance their own needs with those of others. They recognize intimacy and relatedness as desirable because they not

chunks.heading: 436 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 436 Adolescence only elicit pleasant feelings but also promote the achievement of common goals. Their tendency to control intimate others or to become excessively entangled in relationships tends to decrease. Dating becomes more intentional, often focused on partner selection, and romantic relations...
chunks.text: 436 Adolescence only elicit pleasant feelings but also promote the achievement of common goals. Their tendency to control intimate others or to become excessively entangled in relationships tends to decrease. Dating becomes more intentional, often focused on partner selection, and romantic relationships begin to last longer. Dating may begin to interfere with friendships; late adolescents tend to spend more time with their partner and less with the peer group, even though they strive to maintain broader social connections. Both friends and romantic partners may function as attachment figures, while relationships with caregivers are characterized by a more autonomous stance. This phase marks a crucial period of growth in adolescents’ ability to evaluate information received from others. They become increasingly capable of discerning reliable sources of knowledge and incorporating multiple perspectives into their understanding of the world. Rating Scale 5. At this level, adolescents show an age-­appropriate capacity for trust, empathy, and intimacy, even when distressed, across contexts (e.g., with family, peers, romantic partners). They seek closeness in relationships and can flexibly adjust to changing social-­emotional demands at both an inter- and intrapersonal level. They can balance their desires for intimacy and autonomy, negotiating and integrating their own and others’ needs and perspectives even when in disagreement. They associate sexuality with the establishment of an emotional bond, and they engage in behaviors that fit their role and context. They seek emotionally supportive relationships, are able to express concern, can talk about private matters, and can both ask for and provide support to others. They value communication and discussion of affective-­centered themes and can resolve interpersonal conflicts. Finally, they are able to judiciously determine who and what they can trust, basing their level of epistemic vigilance on multiple factors (context, information content, personal needs, other people’s competence, etc.). 3. At this level, adolescents show a sufficiently adequate capacity for trust, empathy, and intimacy, but it may be disrupted by strong emotions such as anger, shame, or separation anxiety. In challenging situations, they may withdraw, act out, or become overly needy and dependent. They may show conformity and acquiescence among friends beyond what is appropriate for their age or be defiant and aggressive, expressing an excessive oppositionality toward authorities. In distressing situations, they may either misread others’ intentions as useless or malevolent, appearing wary and unable to change perspective or behavior, or they may uncritically accept interpersonally transmitted knowledge, adopting attitudes or beliefs passively and compliantly. Sexuality may be avoided or dissociated from emotional intimacy. Significant relationships may be represented in stereotypic rather than nuanced ways. At this level of functioning, teenagers may find it difficult to adjust to situational demands and may show deficits in either the ability to accept support from others or the capacity to provide others with support. 1. These adolescents show a superficial, need-­oriented capacity for affection; their relationships lack mutuality and empathy, and are usually rudimentary and undifferentiated. They are often indifferent to the needs of others and may appear withdrawn, socially isolated, and detached. They may react to others’ efforts to establish closeness with anger, confusion, or fear. Their intimate communication skills are

chunks.heading: Mental Functioning in Adolescence 437
chunks.preview: Mental Functioning in Adolescence 437 scarce or absent, and loving or caring manifestations toward others are infrequent. They prefer to discuss concrete and contingent topics or self-­related themes, without being able to develop a sense of closeness or rapport. Sexuality is often unexpressed or n...
chunks.text: Mental Functioning in Adolescence 437 scarce or absent, and loving or caring manifestations toward others are infrequent. They prefer to discuss concrete and contingent topics or self-­related themes, without being able to develop a sense of closeness or rapport. Sexuality is often unexpressed or nonreciprocal. Interactions tend to be one-way, lacking mutuality, and characterized by marked deficits in the ability to support others or to accept support from them. These adolescents are unable to rely on interpersonal exchanges to learn and adapt; they appear either pervasively inflexible and mistrustful or blindly accepting of what others convey and consequently prone to manipulation and exploitation, or both in different circumstances. 8. Capacity for Impulse Regulation The capacity for impulse regulation concerns the ability of adolescents to modulate impulses and express them in age-­appropriate, adaptive ways that align with cultural expectations. A growing body of evidence shows that from childhood to adulthood there is a gradual decline in levels of impulsivity associated with an increase in individual self-­regulation skills. Significant changes occur during adolescence, however, when there may be a spike in risky behaviors. This phenomenon represents the interaction of biological, psychological, and social factors. The capacity to regulate impulses is closely related to neurophysiological mechanisms that characterize adolescence. At this stage, an imbalance arises between patterns of behavior controlled by different brain regions: the prefrontal cortex, associated with inhibition, has not reached maturity, while the limbic system, associated with reward seeking and immediate gratification, has matured significantly. Specific neurotransmitters (dopamine and serotonin) and sex hormones, which increase considerably from puberty onward, increase the risk of impulsive behaviors (e.g., use of substances; see also SA Axis, Chapter 9, pp. 523–626). Some adolescents may experience a severe deficit in self-­control owing to an excessive imbalance between sensation seeking and executive control. Structural and functional changes in the adolescent brain are more likely to result in dangerous conduct if they are combined with other features, particularly temperamental factors such as high levels of sensation seeking. Thrill seeking, attraction to novelty and adventure, disinhibition, and disposition toward boredom may incite risky actions in young people, above and beyond the brain imbalance factors noted earlier. Optimal functioning of the capacity to regulate impulse is rooted in good primary relationships that, throughout childhood, contributed to the organization of the child’s self-­regulatory strategies. Episodes of maltreatment, trauma, abuse or witnessing of abuse, and lack of a stable, reliable, attuned caregiving environment may have a serious negative impact on adolescents’ functioning in this domain. Deficits in this capacity can result in overdependence on external support (provided primarily by the peer group) or counterphobic or hypomanic attitudes (cf. emerging personality styles/ disorders of the PA Axis of the manual, Chapter 8). In the tension between the strong activation of reward and gratification circuits associated with dangerous conduct and the weaker response of inhibitory control, sociocultural factors also play a critical role. Decision-­making or action-­transition processes in adolescents are greatly influenced by context, especially by the peer group or other affectively significant relationships. In assessing this function, clinicians

chunks.heading: 438 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 438 Adolescence should consider the specific developmental stage the adolescent is in, as well as proximal and distal social-­emotional influences that may shape this capacity’s trajectory. Impairment in this domain may result in unmodulated expression of impulses (impulsivity) or rigid overcontrol...
chunks.text: 438 Adolescence should consider the specific developmental stage the adolescent is in, as well as proximal and distal social-­emotional influences that may shape this capacity’s trajectory. Impairment in this domain may result in unmodulated expression of impulses (impulsivity) or rigid overcontrol (inhibition), often combined with some degree of emotional coercion. Optimal functioning involves tolerating the frustrating fact that many needs and desires cannot be immediately gratified, being patient with delays of gratification, and being able to recognize impulses and manage them through effective regulation strategies. DEVELOPMENTAL GUIDELINES: Capacity for Impulse Regulation in Adolescence Early and Middle Adolescence (ages 12–16) At this stage, adolescents tend to engage in impulsive behaviors. Typical motivations to achieve greater autonomy, confront limits, explore new contexts and ways of being, seek pleasurable emotions, and have rewarding experiences can lead them in dangerous directions. Cognitive immaturity, including relatively concrete and/ or action-­oriented thinking, and weaknesses in inhibitory control of dysfunctional behaviors create some hazards for early to midteens. They have a greater susceptibility to developing addictions, to seeking attention and confirmation from the peer group at any cost, to deficits in self-­esteem and a still unstable self-­representation, and to intensified conflicts with parents, all of which may increase the risk of maladaptive behavior. Late Adolescence (ages 17–19) At this stage, impulsivity and risk taking tend to stabilize or decline as compared with earlier years. There is considerable improvement in control of needs and desires and a greater ability to postpone gratification. As all mental functions keep maturing, these adolescents gain greater consistency and stability in their sense of identity, improved efficiency of executive functioning and the behavioral control system, improved logical and rational thinking in decision making and problem solving, and more sophisticated and complex moral reasoning. All these developments tend toward a reduction in maladaptive behaviors and an increase in actions indicating higher levels of awareness and orientation toward more focused goals. Rating Scale 5. At this level of functioning, adolescents are able to manage their impulses in ways that are appropriate to their age and their varying contexts. Impulse control is flexible and effective. Needs and desires are expressed in a balanced and adaptive manner that contributes to the strengthening of interpersonal bonds. 3. At this level, adolescents are reasonably able to control and regulate impulses, but they may have difficulty in highly affectively charged or conflictual situations (e.g., with a partner, a teacher, or peers, or when facing frustration). They may show tendencies toward hypercontrol (rigidity) or a lack of control (dysregulation) in different situations or circumstances.

chunks.heading: Mental Functioning in Adolescence 439
chunks.preview: Mental Functioning in Adolescence 439 1. Adolescents at this level seem unable to control or modulate impulses appro­ priately, with serious consequences for familial and social relationships. Impulse ­control may be so deficient that they are unable to modulate anger or other intense affects; alte...
chunks.text: Mental Functioning in Adolescence 439 1. Adolescents at this level seem unable to control or modulate impulses appro­ priately, with serious consequences for familial and social relationships. Impulse ­control may be so deficient that they are unable to modulate anger or other intense affects; alternatively, it may be so rigid that it does not allow them any form of expression. 9. Defensive Functioning Defensive functioning refers to the use of unconscious, automatic psychological processes through which an adolescent tries to manage desires, affects, and internal experience. It involves the ability to modulate anxiety and other painful emotional states that may arise from internal conflicts, external challenges, or threatening experiences, without resorting to excessive acting out, somatization, extreme withdrawal, or distortions in self-­perception or reality testing. This capacity is critical during adolescence in light of the extensive physical, emotional, cognitive, and interpersonal transformations of this life stage and given the peer demands, social pressures, and continuing expectations from caregivers that accompany the teen years. Defensive functioning in adolescence should be understood developmentally. We know empirically (e.g., Cramer, 2012) that different defenses tend to emerge at different life stages and play a vital role in each individual’s maturation. In general, while primitive or costly defenses appear in the earliest years (as normative reactions to frustrations that exceed infants’ abilities to face them), the most adaptive and complex defenses emerge in adolescence and young adulthood. Defenses develop with age and progressively organize themselves hierarchically. Typically, the defenses that scholars view as immature, those that significantly distort reality and create problems in adapting to it, tend to decrease during the course of development, while there is an increase in mature defenses, which maximize awareness of internal and external stressors, modulate the expression and gratification of wishes and needs, and promote flexible adaptation to stressful circumstances. In the transition from early to late adolescence, although there is a progressive reduction in the use of immature and neurotic defenses (e.g., denial, projection, splitting, repression) and an increased use of mature defenses (e.g., self-­assertion, altruism, humor, sublimation), the maturation of defensive functioning in adolescents does not follow a universal pattern. The shift to more adaptive defenses occurs gradually over the course of adolescent cognitive maturation (especially via the consolidation of formal operational thinking), in concert with developmental changes in the individuation pathway and the refinement of mentalization skills. Defensive functioning in adolescence may also differ by their gender. Significant differences have been found between girls and boys on the prevalence of specific defenses. Many boys resort to obsessional defenses, probably in an effort to avoid and control their strong impulses, threatening feelings, or unacceptable desires and wishes, while remaining aware of the cognitive component of their experience. Other boys use disavowal and action defenses more than girls do, refusing to acknowledge some intolerable aspects of their internal world and/or external reality, and acting on their impulses without considering potential negative consequences. Girls show greater reliance on neurotic defenses, a difference that may indicate an effort to protect themselves from awareness of threatening wishes, thoughts, or motives and maintaining the

chunks.heading: 440 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 440 Adolescence emotional elements of a conflictual experience. Like adults, adolescents tend to “specialize” in the use of certain defenses that characterize their personal style. One thing that clearly varies is their degree of insight into their defenses. The greater the level of insight, the hig...
chunks.text: 440 Adolescence emotional elements of a conflictual experience. Like adults, adolescents tend to “specialize” in the use of certain defenses that characterize their personal style. One thing that clearly varies is their degree of insight into their defenses. The greater the level of insight, the higher the individual’s level of adaptation and well-being. DEVELOPMENTAL GUIDELINES: Defensive Functioning in Adolescence Early and Middle Adolescence (ages 12–16) Early adolescence is characterized by a quantum leap in cognitive competence (development of formal operational thinking and abstract reasoning), which fosters a softening of the psychic structure. However, pressures related to physical, psychological, and social changes make early adolescents vulnerable to shifting and volatile states of mind, and to a sense of self-­fragmentation. Immature defenses such as disavowal, denial, projection, and splitting are still common at this age. These upheavals cause early adolescents to be self-­centered and sensitive to the external supports on which they rely. During midadolescence, when conflicts with parents increase and peer pressures exacerbate feelings of vulnerability and inadequacy, the use of narcissistic defenses, often associated with denial and acting out, may intensify. Faced with social-­emotional conflicts, adolescents tend to use idealization, devaluation, and omnipotence to regulate their self-­esteem and protect themselves from feelings of helplessness, ineptitude, and fragility. Late Adolescence (ages 17–19) In the transition from middle to late adolescence, an integration of self occurs. The consolidation of formal operational thinking, along with an increased sense of stability, allows high-­functioning older adolescents to rely more on internal supports. This greater sense of a firm foundation allows them to use more mature defenses such as sublimation, humor, affiliation, self-­assertion, and intellectualization. High-­ functioning older teenagers are more empathic and emotionally engaged with others, including family members. They are much more in control of their feelings and actions. Rating Scale 5. At this level, adolescents use mostly adaptive defenses appropriate to their age. These permit them to experience a wide range of affects and to manage thoughts and feelings in ways that allow for continued growth and conscious exploration of relationships and the environment. Transient reliance on neurotic defenses (e.g., affective isolation, reaction formation, repression, displacement) does not result in impaired functioning, as adolescents generally revert to using more mature defenses (e.g., humor, sublimation) as soon as anxiety, conflict, and stress decrease. 3. These adolescents may react to stressors (internal or external) by locating the cause of problems outside the self and minimizing their internal motives. As a result, they may partially distort reality, especially in situations involving threat or anxiety. They may ignore aspects of their subjective experiences through denial or projection; alternatively, they may dismiss the impact of negative experiences (e.g., shame,

chunks.heading: Mental Functioning in Adolescence 441
chunks.preview: Mental Functioning in Adolescence 441 helplessness, disappointment) by devaluing or idealizing themselves or others, or by maintaining an illusion of omnipotence. Defensive functioning may appear fluctuating and unpredictable, and often involves immature defenses (e.g., splitting, projection, actin...
chunks.text: Mental Functioning in Adolescence 441 helplessness, disappointment) by devaluing or idealizing themselves or others, or by maintaining an illusion of omnipotence. Defensive functioning may appear fluctuating and unpredictable, and often involves immature defenses (e.g., splitting, projection, acting out), especially in situations causing severe distress. 1. At this level, adolescents appear overwhelmed by anxieties about dissolution or annihilation of the self. They may fear physical harm or death, with such fears stemming not from impaired reality-­testing but from a severe inability to manage internal conflicts or cope with external demands. Consensual reality may be severely distorted, anxiety states fail to be contained, and level of adaptation is severely impaired. The most commonly employed defenses are splitting, delusional projection, psychotic denial, and dissociation. Functioning at the individual and relational levels may be severely impaired or restricted. 10. Capacity for Adaptation and Resiliency The capacity for adaptation and resiliency refers to the adolescent’s ability to engage with age-­specific problems and to convert unexpected, challenging, or stressful situations into experiences of personal growth. “Adaptation” in this capacity implies a critical adjustment, and not necessarily conformity, to specific contexts. It does not restrict psychic functioning but results in a constructive elaboration of lived experience. It enables an adolescent to transcend obstacles and turn setbacks into opportunities for growth. “Resiliency” denotes a complex interaction among internal, interpersonal, and contextual risk factors and protective mechanisms, which can redirect the effects of even adverse life events in a positive direction. This ability includes the internal modulation of thoughts, affects, needs, and desires in order to meet the demands of the internal and external world and cope with negative events adaptively. It allows the young person to face life experiences with an active, curious, creative attitude and to use even painful experiences in the service of growth. Neurobiological factors may influence adolescents in both their susceptibility and their responses to stress. Whatever their individual temperaments, experiences of adverse events can modify their neuropsychology in the direction of adaptation and resiliency. There is growing recognition that protective factors may operate at different times and on different trajectories throughout life. In adolescence, the capacity for adaptation and resiliency allows the subjective rethinking of bodily changes, relationships, developmental challenges, and painful life events. It results in an assertive, self-­ conscious engagement with reality. Adolescents with a high capacity for adaptation and resiliency can approach difficult experiences as events from which they can learn ways to cope with life’s adversities. Even some instances of trauma can become positive turning points that profoundly mark a young person’s personal, relational, and professional choices. Adolescents with poor adaptation and resiliency skills may feel trapped when faced with internal or external stressors, which they experience as insurmountable and inevitably harmful. These young people tend to react to negative experiences with a passive, self-­deprecating attitude, feelings of sadness and upset, and a pervasive sense of helplessness, often accompanied by intense psychological distress. However, the capacity for adaptation and resiliency tends to increase during adolescence along with the maturation of other mental capacities.

chunks.heading: 442 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 442 Adolescence DEVELOPMENTAL GUIDELINES: Capacity for Adaptation and Resiliency in Adolescence Early Adolescence (ages 12–14) Young adolescents tend to perceive internal and external events as entirely beyond their control. Their stress level when facing developmental tasks is high and not moderate...
chunks.text: 442 Adolescence DEVELOPMENTAL GUIDELINES: Capacity for Adaptation and Resiliency in Adolescence Early Adolescence (ages 12–14) Young adolescents tend to perceive internal and external events as entirely beyond their control. Their stress level when facing developmental tasks is high and not moderated by sufficient awareness of their potential skills. They manage stress without much creativity or flexibility and may use defensive withdrawal to escape from painful realities. Adolescents at this stage may often feel helpless, unhappy, ashamed, and reluctant to ask for help. Middle Adolescence (ages 15–16) At this stage, adolescents’ capacity to cope with life’s adversities gradually begins to improve. They can manage some expectations and pressures (internal and external) in a more personal, creative way, making them feel more self-aware and agentic. New relationships with peers help them learn about new aspects of self and the world, leading to greater personal adaptation and satisfaction. They may deal with adverse events more actively, though feelings of worry, discomfort, and anxiety may persist in particularly difficult situations. They may be able to reorganize their emotional and cognitive responses. Late Adolescence (ages 17–19) Late adolescents continue to make improvements managing stress and thinking of possible responses to adversities. They face most developmental challenges with openness, curiosity, and self-­ reflection, experiencing them as opportunities for growth. Perceived difficulties mainly concern specific situations in which they continue to feel insecure or worried, yet they can address these actively by taking advantage of personal and social resources. Rating Scale 5. At this level, adolescents can adapt and respond flexibly to unexpected or stressful situations in ways appropriate to their age that do not compromise their overall functioning or individual development. They seem aware of their own strengths and vulnerabilities, show a repertoire of adaptive responses, and are capable of asking for help. They can handle a wide range of emotional experiences and novel situations, even when ambiguous, without a decline in cognitive processes or an impairment in interpersonal functioning. 3. Adolescents at this level can adapt and respond flexibly and age-­appropriately to most unexpected or stressful situations. They can use their own resources in the service of their overall functioning. In complex social and emotional contexts or novel situations, however, they may resort to less adaptive strategies that temporarily interfere with emotional, cognitive, and behavioral regulation, and they may become mildly symptomatic. In general, these issues do not significantly impair their individual functioning and development. 1. At this level, adolescents are extremely vulnerable. They depend on the outside

chunks.heading: Mental Functioning in Adolescence 443
chunks.preview: Mental Functioning in Adolescence 443 world to maintain their emotional and behavioral balance. Their self-­ regulatory mechanisms are severely deficient, causing highly maladaptive patterns of response to adverse events and a tendency in unexpected or stressful situations to regress to primitive m...
chunks.text: Mental Functioning in Adolescence 443 world to maintain their emotional and behavioral balance. Their self-­ regulatory mechanisms are severely deficient, causing highly maladaptive patterns of response to adverse events and a tendency in unexpected or stressful situations to regress to primitive modes of functioning (avoidance, overcontrol, dissociation). They are generally unable to adapt to complex social and emotional contexts, and may develop severe symptoms and significant impairments in their functioning. 11. Capacity to Construct and Use Internal Standards and Ideals This category concerns the adolescent’s sense of morality. The construction of values, standards, and ideals involves a stable self-­representation within one’s cultural context and the ability to make decisions based on a coherent, flexible, integrated set of moral principles. Values and ideals, which are internalized in the early stages of development during interactions with parents and significant others, are reworked in adolescence in the context of peers, teachers, mentors, and other influences. The intensification of sexual and aggressive urges makes adolescence a critical time for the elaboration of a mature superego. During this phase, emotions of guilt and shame can play a central role in moral development. Their impact consists of two interacting aspects: (1) the constructive use of such emotions to develop a personal sense of morality and to guide behaviors that foster self-­esteem and positive self-­perception, and (2) a more problematic outcome in which the limitations of adolescents in making sense of their complex internal experiences can generate or exacerbate a deep sense of inadequacy, self-blame, and self-­loathing. As moral values are learned within social contexts, the maturation of moral development in adolescence implies the capacity to manage relationships and protect oneself from negative peer influence. This capacity is an outgrowth and integration of other mental functions. In adolescence, empathy and perspective taking play essential roles in moral development and ethical decision making. DEVELOPMENTAL GUIDELINES: Capacity to Construct and Use Internal Standards and Ideals in Adolescence Early Adolescence (ages 12–14) At this age, although adolescents have usually developed a sufficient capacity for abstract reasoning, they are still at a preconventional level of moral reasoning. They start to test rules and limits as a means of self-­discovery, but they tend to judge the morality of an action mainly by its consequences (rewarding, unrewarding, or punitive). At this age, teenagers may have notable difficulties using flexible, integrated, and realistic internal standards and fully integrating external moral standards into their emerging sense of self and personal ethics. Middle Adolescence (ages 15–16) This phase is marked by a growing interest among adolescents in choosing role models and developing personal ideals. They show more consistent evidence of self-­ consciousness and the capacity to set goals according to internal standards. Morality is still dictated by outside forces, however, often depending on others’ beliefs

chunks.heading: 444 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 444 Adolescence about right and wrong. In the midadolescent years, teens are highly sensitive to peer pressure; their use of internal standards often reflects heightened sensitivity to others’ approval or disapproval. Late Adolescence (ages 17–19) With their increasingly mature introspection, mental...
chunks.text: 444 Adolescence about right and wrong. In the midadolescent years, teens are highly sensitive to peer pressure; their use of internal standards often reflects heightened sensitivity to others’ approval or disapproval. Late Adolescence (ages 17–19) With their increasingly mature introspection, mentalization, self-­ esteem, sense of agency, and orientation toward meaning, and with more capacity to set goals and follow through on them, late adolescents become capable of formulating and applying values that reflect a thoughtful consideration of self within their interpersonal and cultural context. They make progress in formulating a personal philosophy and a refined moral sense. Their internalization of values synthesizes external influences, personal experiences, and early superego functions. They go beyond conformity to societal norms to construct a personal “moral compass” aligned with their evolving sense of identity and greater capacity to cope with the emotions of guilt and shame, which they use as guides to action and signals for self-­reflection. Rating Scale 5. At this level, adolescents have internal standards and ideals that seem flexible and integrated with a realistic sense of their capacities. Their moral sense seems congruent with their age and social context. Their values support their personal efforts and promote feelings of authenticity and self-­esteem. They use guilt feelings as a stimulus to reconsider their behavior. They judge others with compassion and empathy as well as a balanced critical capacity. 3. At this level, adolescents may have standards and ideals that at times appear rigid and inflexible or vary according to context. They respond to contingent needs, desires, and feelings, and adhere to external standards more than to an internally coherent set of moral rules. They seem not to have fully integrated a realistic perception of their own abilities and values or their social context. Their self-­esteem is overly influenced by peers. Feelings of guilt may take the form of excessive self-­criticism or, conversely, may not be processed in a fully conscious way. 1. These adolescents have standards and ideals that are notable either for their absence or for their extreme strictness, concreteness, and inflexibility. They lack realistic perception of their abilities and ideals, which seem unintegrated with the norms of their sociocultural context. They may be unaware of guilt feelings or turn them into indiscriminate blame toward self or others, whom they tend to see as all-good or allbad. Their judgments are not softened by compassion or balance. In interpreting their own and others’ behavior, morality may play either an insignificant or authoritarian role. Nuanced standards and ideals are mostly absent. 12. Capacity to Explore One’s Inner Life The capacity to explore one’s inner life allows adolescents to identify and elaborate the psychological processes underlying their mental states, both conscious and less conscious. They can appreciate associations between emotions, thoughts, intentions,

chunks.heading: Mental Functioning in Adolescence 445
chunks.preview: Mental Functioning in Adolescence 445 motives, and behaviors in self and others. They develop narratives that weave together the mental content of present and past experiences with future expectations, constructing interpretations aimed at increasing their self-­awareness and understanding the deep...
chunks.text: Mental Functioning in Adolescence 445 motives, and behaviors in self and others. They develop narratives that weave together the mental content of present and past experiences with future expectations, constructing interpretations aimed at increasing their self-­awareness and understanding the deeper meanings of their inner life. This capacity implies the ability to pursue self-­ understanding with a self-­reflective, spontaneity and curiosity. It overlaps with psychological mindedness, the capacity to observe one’s inner world realistically, and in the service of better adaptation to external reality, it integrates understanding of one’s own and others’ desires, needs, motives, and intentions (McCallum & Piper, 1997). This ability includes attention to fantasies, slips, creations of one’s imagination, myths, witticisms, and, especially, dreams, as an expression of one’s unconscious life. Adolescents’ ability to question how their own and others’ minds work, and to achieve greater self-­awareness, enables them to organize their own subjective and intersubjective experiences, including appreciating the nature of problems, anxieties, ambivalences, conflicts, and any aspect of functioning that may compromise their sense of well-being and the quality of their lives. In adolescents, the achievement of an optimal level of the capacity to explore one’s inner life is the result of a complex process. It requires good affective and (meta)cognitive skills, high levels of mentalization, improved capacity for abstraction, access to symbolic thinking, good narrative skills, and the ability to take the other’s perspective. At its core is the ability to read the actions of others in terms of their mental states, understanding the web of perspectives that connect an individual with others within the social context. This mental function not only promotes greater self-­awareness and better adaptation of internal states to external reality but also fosters the development of deeper, more intimate, reciprocal relationships. A poor level of functioning in this capacity implies lack of emotional awareness, impoverishment of internal psychological experience, little interest in the world of dreams or unconscious fantasies, concrete thinking, and a style of behavior centered primarily on external reality. DEVELOPMENTAL GUIDELINES: Capacity to Explore One’s Inner Life in Adolescence Early Adolescence (ages 12–14) At this stage, teenagers begin to show curiosity about the intrapsychic processes underlying their mental life. They try to reflect on their own mental states, grasping possible connections between various affective, cognitive, motivational, and behavioral aspects of their subjective and intersubjective experience. Nevertheless, they need support from adult figures to process and understand the complexity and depth of certain psychological dynamics. Their comparatively low levels of psychological mindedness, associated with limited abilities in mentalization, executive functioning, affective regulation, and impulse management, must be understood in light of the profound changes that teenagers undergo with puberty. Middle Adolescence (ages 15–16) In these years, adolescents experience a remarkable developmental shift in this capacity. This improvement is partly related to brain remodeling processes that promote increased functional specialization of the prefrontal cortex and better efficiency of its integrative functions. They show greater introspective and meaning-­making

chunks.heading: 446 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 446 Adolescence abilities toward their own and others’ thoughts, emotions, and behaviors, and can make meaning of their experiences. Their levels of self-­awareness increase, though at times, midadolescents still have difficulties interpreting and integrating con­ flicting or contradictory aspects o...
chunks.text: 446 Adolescence abilities toward their own and others’ thoughts, emotions, and behaviors, and can make meaning of their experiences. Their levels of self-­awareness increase, though at times, midadolescents still have difficulties interpreting and integrating con­ flicting or contradictory aspects of internal and external reality. Adaptation to social reality improves, but cognitive and affective skills clearly need to increase further. Late Adolescence (ages 17–19) At this stage, there is a consolidation of the capacity to explore one’s inner life, which appears closely intertwined with the strengthening of mentalization, emotional regulation, cognitive functioning, and behavioral control. Adolescents show a more mature ability to introspect, and their interpretations of their inner world and social reality are more insightful. Their higher levels of psychological mindedness promote the achievement of deeper self-­awareness and better adaptation to reality. Rating Scale 5. At this level, adolescents are strongly motivated to learn about themselves in relation to others. They reflect on a wide range of their own and others’ needs, motives, and affective states in a manner that is self-aware and appropriate to their age. Thanks to a stable view of themselves and their values and goals, they can reflect on the present and the past, and can grasp complex relationships between feelings, thoughts, and actions. They become notably interested in dreams and unconscious fantasies, and they try to deepen their understanding in these realms. 3. These adolescents may be motivated and able to recognize and reflect on the underlying meanings of words and actions, have some insight into their own and others’ motivations and needs, and be able to grasp connections between past and present. Under stress, however, their awareness and ability to reflect on their own thoughts, feelings, intentions, and behaviors, as well as those of others, may falter or decline significantly. In stressful situations, adolescents fail to use such information effectively or may succeed only partially. 1. These adolescents seem poorly motivated or unable to reflect spontaneously on feelings, thoughts, and actions in the present and past. They may be unable to understand psychological constructs. Their self-­ awareness is limited to a reduced understanding of affective states, often polarized in nature, or to simple emotional reactions. Their ability to reflect on their own and others’ subjective experience thus appears deficient, and they have a tendency toward fragmentation. When confronted with intense affects, these adolescents are mostly unable to process their emotional experiences. They may resort to primitive defenses, acting impulsively, or entering dissociative states. 13. Capacity for Agency and Purpose The capacity for agency and purpose is closely related to identity construction and moral development, and to a realistic acknowledgment of personal strengths and limitations. It is influenced by the maturation of mentalizing capacities, which allow

chunks.heading: Mental Functioning in Adolescence 447
chunks.preview: Mental Functioning in Adolescence 447 adolescents to be sensitive to the attitudes, values, thoughts, and emotions of others, and to experience a sense of belonging and participation. During adolescence, the ability to see oneself as the author of one’s behaviors and to assert decision-­making powe...
chunks.text: Mental Functioning in Adolescence 447 adolescents to be sensitive to the attitudes, values, thoughts, and emotions of others, and to experience a sense of belonging and participation. During adolescence, the ability to see oneself as the author of one’s behaviors and to assert decision-­making power in various contexts significantly increases, influenced by the reorganization of attachment experiences, the progressive acquisition of autonomy, the development of abstract thinking, and the acquisition of greater self-­regulation. This developmental phase is crucial for cultivating a sense of purpose, direction, and personal meaning. The literature has extensively shown that the development of this capacity is related to better psychological adjustment, greater self-­esteem, and overall well-being. In addition, a strong sense of self-­direction often serves as a catalyst for creativity and affiliation. Delayed maturation of this capacity is associated with inhibition, self-­sabotage, or an uncritical adherence to ideologies through parental or peer influence. DEVELOPMENTAL GUIDELINES: Capacity for Agency and Purpose in Adolescence Early Adolescence (ages 12–14) During this phase, adolescents begin to show an interest in gaining a basic understanding of their own choices and actions. They begin to reflect on their level of control over their behavior. A desire to exert independence and agency emerges. Nonetheless, young teenagers can encounter significant challenges in articulating a cohesive self-­narrative and in integrating different perspectives or broader, more abstract concepts of self and others into their decision making. They continue to look to adults as a primary source of meaning and value. Middle Adolescence (ages 15–16) Middle adolescents are centrally focused on establishing feelings of connection with peers, a priority that significantly affects their decision making. Their growing desire for independence may take the form of their exploring various interests and hobbies autonomously. This phase often sees the formulation of new personal objectives and reevaluation of previous aims. Their beliefs and evaluations of self and others become increasingly differentiated across varying situations, relationships, and roles. Their ongoing reliance on caregivers or other authorities can cause them anger or ambivalence. Late Adolescence (ages 17–19) At this age, adolescents show a growing tendency to organize and reorganize their present and past experiences so as to form a coherent sense of personal mastery and establish a feeling of self-­determination. They have an increasing ability to conceptualize values, beliefs, and subsequent objectives independently. They begin to find meaning in more abstract and complex constructs related to self-­identity and overarching life philosophies. Late teenagers start to pursue meaning through balanced connections with others and to engage with ideas that require long-term effort for actualization, reflecting a deeper understanding of themselves within a broader sociocultural context.

chunks.heading: 448 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 448 Adolescence Rating Scale 5. At this level, adolescents show a clear, unwavering sense of purpose and meaning, and an intrinsic sense of agency and self-­direction. They can look beyond themselves and transcend immediate situational concerns. Subjectivity and intersubjectivity play major roles in...
chunks.text: 448 Adolescence Rating Scale 5. At this level, adolescents show a clear, unwavering sense of purpose and meaning, and an intrinsic sense of agency and self-­direction. They can look beyond themselves and transcend immediate situational concerns. Subjectivity and intersubjectivity play major roles in the lives of these adolescents, who are able to involve themselves fully in individual and shared projects. 3. Adolescents at this level show some sense of purpose and meaning, along with periods of uncertainty and doubt. They are able to look outside the self with effort, and have some sense of connectedness with other people and the world. They can grasp the broader implications of attitudes and beliefs but tend to accept alternative perspectives only in less emotionally charged or conflict-­free domains. 1. These adolescents show a lack of direction, aimlessness, and little or no sense of purpose. When asked, they appear unable to articulate a cohesive personal philosophy or set of life goals. Subjectivity and intersubjectivity have little or no importance to them. They may be unaware of this lack of direction, and may experience and express a pervasive sense of isolation and meaninglessness, while being uncritically involved in projects and ideologies. Empirically Grounded Assessment of MA‐Axis Capacities The assessment of adolescents’ mental functioning presents some special issues. To make a nuanced evaluation, clinicians should consider several sources of information, each of which can provide meaningful insights. They can draw relevant data from adolescents and their parents, as well as from teachers or peers, through interviews, self-­report, or performance-­based measures. While adolescents are the most knowledgeable informants about their internal experience, parents, teachers, or peers can help in identify relational difficulties that arise in teens’ interactions with others. An accurate, comprehensive assessment integrates the different “puzzle pieces” of an adolescent’s clinical diagnostic profile into a useful psychodynamic formulation. The following section lists the main tools for assessing the thirteen mental abilities of the MA Axis, with the aim of proposing different evaluation strategies designed to integrate different sources and methods of information gathering. Moreover, we refer readers to the instrument directly derived from the PDM-3: the Psychodiagnostic Chart–3 in the adolescent version (PDC-3-A). Most Relevant Assessment Tools 1. Capacity for Attenton and Learning (Executive Functioning) Many tools are available to assess adolescents’ attention and learning. Self-­ questionnaires and informant-­based checklists give critical information about this capacity. Assessment of complex cognitive processes, however, may require neuropsychological tests. Here are measures that may be useful in assessing intelligence, attention, executive functions, and learning. • Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children—­Fourth Edition (WISC-IV; Wechsler, 2014) • Leiter International Performance Scale—­Revised (Leiter-­R; Roid & Miller, 1997)

chunks.heading: Mental Functioning in Adolescence 449
chunks.preview: Mental Functioning in Adolescence 449 • Comprehensive Executive Function Inventory (CEFI; Naglieri & Goldstein, 2013) • Behavior Rating Inventory of Executive Function (BRIEF; Gioia, Isquith, Guy, & Kenworthy, 2000) • Brown Attention-­Deficit Disorder Scale for Adolescents (BADDS-A; Brown, 2001) •...
chunks.text: Mental Functioning in Adolescence 449 • Comprehensive Executive Function Inventory (CEFI; Naglieri & Goldstein, 2013) • Behavior Rating Inventory of Executive Function (BRIEF; Gioia, Isquith, Guy, & Kenworthy, 2000) • Brown Attention-­Deficit Disorder Scale for Adolescents (BADDS-A; Brown, 2001) • Conners–­Wells Adolescent Self-­Report Scale (CASS; Conners et al., 1997) • Multidimensional Adolescent Functioning Scale (MAFS; Wardenaar et al., 2013) 2. Capacity for Affect Regulation and Expression • Thematic Apperception Test (TAT; Murray, 1943) • Shedler–­Westen Assessment Procedure for Adolescents, Version II (SWAP-II-A; Westen, DeFife, Malone, & DiLallo, 2014) • Behavioral and Emotional Rating Scale (BERS; Epstein & Sharma, 1998) • Multifactor Emotional Intelligence Scale, Adolescent Version (MEIS-AV; Mayer, Salovey, & Caruso, 1997) • Brief-­Mentalized Affectivity Scale for Adolescents (B-MAS-A; Liotti et al., 2023) • Emotional Quotient Inventory, Youth Version (EQ-i:YV; Bar-On & Parker, 2000) • Affect Intensity and Reactivity Scale for Youth (AIR-Y; Jones, Leen-­ Feldner, ­Olatunji, Reardon, & Hawks, 2009) • Affect Intensity Measure (AIM; Larsen & Diener, 1987) • Emotional Intensity Scale (EIS; Bachorowski & Braaten, 1994) • Emotional Reactivity Scale (ERS; Nock, Wedig, Holmberg, & Hooley, 2008) • Levels of Emotional Awareness Scale (LEAS; Lane, Quinlan, Schwartz, & Walker, 1990; Nandrino et al., 2013) • Positive and Negative Affect Scale—­Expanded Version (PANAS-X; Watson & Clark, 1994) • Emotion Regulation Questionnaire for Children and Adolescents (ERQ-CA; ­Gullone & Taffe, 2012) • Cognitive Emotion Regulation Questionnaire (CERQ; Garnefski, Kraaij, & ­Spinhoven, 2001, 2002) • Difficulties in Emotion Regulation Scale (DERS; Gratz & Roemer, 2004; Weinberg & Klonsky, 2009) 3. Capacity for Mentalization Existing measures to assess mentalization in adolescents include, among others, advanced theory-­of-mind measures, self-­reports, vignette-­based measures, and scales derived from projective assessments. To approximate the demands of everyday life on social cognition, advanced tests using video footage have been developed. Examples of each type follow: • Reflective Function Questionnaire for Youths (RFQY; Sharp et al., 2009) • Reflective Functioning Questionnaire, Short Version (Badoud, Luyten, Fonagy, Eliez, & Debbané, 2015; Luyten, Fonagy, Lowyck, & Vermote, 2012) • Social Cognition and Object Relations Scale (SCORS; Westen, Lohr, Silk, & ­Kerber, 1985)

chunks.preview: 450 Adolescence • Mentalization Stories for Adolescents (MSA-v.2; Rutherford et al., 2012; Vrouva & Fonagy, 2009) • Adolescent Levels of Emotional Awareness Scale (ALEAS; Pratt, 2006) • Movie for Assessment of Social Cognition (MASC; Dziobek et al., 2006) • Meta- ­Cognitions Questionnaire—­Adolescen...
chunks.text: 450 Adolescence • Mentalization Stories for Adolescents (MSA-v.2; Rutherford et al., 2012; Vrouva & Fonagy, 2009) • Adolescent Levels of Emotional Awareness Scale (ALEAS; Pratt, 2006) • Movie for Assessment of Social Cognition (MASC; Dziobek et al., 2006) • Meta- ­Cognitions Questionnaire—­Adolescent (MCQ-A; Cartwright-­Hatton et al., 2004) 4. Bodily Experiences and Representations Listed below are some of the most commonly used tools for evaluating this function. Some are not specific to adolescents, but we mention them to advance research in this area of inquiry, to promote the adaptation and validation of these measures for adolescents. • Appearance Evaluation (AE) and Body Areas Satisfaction Scale (BASS) of the Multidimensional Body Relations Questionnaire (MBSRQ; Brown, Cash, & Mikulka, 1990; Cash, 2000) • Body Appreciation Scale (BAS and BAS-2; Avalos, Tylka, & Wood-­Barcalow, 2005; Tylka & Wood-­Barcalow, 2015) • Body Dissatisfaction subscale (BD) of the Eating Disorder Inventory–3 (EDI-3; Garner, 2004) • Body Esteem Scale for Adolescents and Adults (BESAA; Mendelson, Mendelson, & White, 2001) • Body Image Scale for Youth (BISY; Jalali-­Farahani, Amiri, Zarani, Zayeri, & Azizi, 2022) • Body Shape Questionnaire (BSQ; Cooper, Taylor, Cooper, & Fairburn, 1987) • Body Uneasyness Test (BUT; Cuzzolaro, Vetrone, Marano, & Garfinkel, 2006) 5. Capacity for Differentiation and Integration • Assessment of Identity Development in Adolescence (AIDA; Goth, Foelsch, Schlüter-Müller, & Schmeck, 2012) • Social Cognition and Object Relations Scale—­Global Rating Method (SCORS-G; Stein, Hilsenroth, Slavin-­Mulford, & Pinsker, 2011) • Identity Disturbance Questionnaire for Adolescents (IDQ-A; Westen, Betan, & DeFife, 2011) • Depressive Experiences Questionnaire—­ Adolescent Version (DEQ-A; Blatt, D’Afflitti, & Quinlan, 1976; Blatt, Schaffer, Bers, & Quinlan, 1992) • Shedler–­Westen Assessment Procedure for Adolescents, Version II (SWAP-II-A; Westen et al., 2014) 6. Capacity to Regulate Self‐Esteem • Shedler–­Westen Assessment Procedure for Adolescents, Version II (SWAP-II-A; Westen et al., 2014) • Rosenberg Self-­Esteem Scale (RSES; Rosenberg, 1965) • Scales of Psychological Capacities (Greenfield et al., 2013)

Capacity for Trust, Empathy, and Intimacy • Epistemic Trust, Mistrust, and Credulity Questionnaire (ETMCQ; Campbell et al., 2021; Liotti et al., 2023; Milesi et al., 2024) • Basic Empathy Scale (BES; Jolliffe & Farrington, 2006) • Network of Relationships Inventory (NRI; Furman & Buhrmester, 1985) • Experiences in Close Relationships—­Revised (Fraley, Waller, & Brennan, 2000) • Inventory of Interpersonal Problems—­Circumplex—­Short Form (IIP-C-SF; Soldz, Budman, Demby, & Merry, 1995) • Interview of Personality Organization Processes in Adolescence (IPOP-A; Ammaniti, Fontana, Kernberg, Clarkin, & Clarkin, 2011; Ammaniti, Fontana, & Nicolais, 2015) • Shedler–­Westen Assessment Procedure for Adolescents, Version II (SWAP-II-A; Westen et al., 2014) • Social Cognition and Object Relations Scale—­Global Rating Method (SCORS-G; Stein, Hilsenroth, Slavin-­Mulford, & Pinsker, 2011) 8. Capacity for Impulse Regulation • Barratt Impulsiveness Scale—­Version 11—Adolescent (BIS-11-A; Fossati, Barratt, Acquarini, & Di Ceglie, 2002) • Sensitivity to Punishment and Sensitivity to Reward Questionnaire (SPSRQ; Franken & Muris, 2006; Torrubia, Avila, Molto, & Caseras, 2001) • Urgency, Premeditation, Perseverance, and Sensation-­Seeking (UPPS) Impulsive Behavior Scale (Whiteside & Lynam, 2001) • Brief Self-­Control Scale (BSCS; Tangney, Baumeister, & Boone, 2004) • Behavioral Inhibition System and Behavioral Activation System Scales (BIS/BAS; Carver & White, 1994) 9. Defensive Functioning • Shedler–­Westen Assessment Procedure for Adolescents, Version II (SWAP-II-A; Westen et al., 2014) • Defense Style Questionnaire (DSQ; Andrews, Singh, & Bond, 1983) for Adolescents (Muris, Winands, & Horselenberg, 2003) • Defense Mechanism Rating Scales and Defense Mechanism Rating Scales Q-Sort (DMRS; Perry, 1990) and the DMRS Q-Sort (Di Giuseppe, Perry, Petraglia, Janzen, & Lingiardi, 2014) • Response Evaluation Measure–71 (REM-71; Araujo, Ryst, & Steiner, 1999; Prunas, Preti, Huemer, Shaw, & Steiner, 2014; Steiner, Araujo, & Koopman, 2001)

Capacity to Explore One’s Inner Life • Brief-­Mentalized Affectivity Scale for Adolescent Version (B-MAS-A; Liotti et al., 2023) • Toronto Alexithymia Scale–20 (TAS-20; Bagby, Parker, & Taylor, 1994; Parker et al., 2010) • Psychological Mindedness Scale (PMS; Conte, Ratto, & Karasu, 1996) • Shedler–­Westen Assessment Procedure for Adolescents, Version II (SWAP-II-A; Westen et al., 2014) 13. Capacity for Agency and Purpose • Shedler–­Westen Assessment Procedure for Adolescents, Version II (SWAP-II-A; Westen et al., 2014)

chunks.heading: Mental Functioning in Adolescence 453
chunks.preview: Mental Functioning in Adolescence 453 • Dispositional Flow Scale–2 (DFS-2; Jackson & Ecklund, 2002; Wang, Liu, & Khoo, 2009) • Future Orientation Scale (FOS; Steinberg et al., 2009) • Junior Temperament and Character Inventory (JTCI; Andriola et al., 2012; Luby, Svrakic, McCallum, Przybeck, & Cloni...
chunks.text: Mental Functioning in Adolescence 453 • Dispositional Flow Scale–2 (DFS-2; Jackson & Ecklund, 2002; Wang, Liu, & Khoo, 2009) • Future Orientation Scale (FOS; Steinberg et al., 2009) • Junior Temperament and Character Inventory (JTCI; Andriola et al., 2012; Luby, Svrakic, McCallum, Przybeck, & Cloninger, 1999) • Qualitative and Structural Dimensions of Self-­Descriptions (QSDSD; Blatt, Bers, & Schaffer, 1993) • Adolescent Life Goal Profile Scale (ALGPS; Gabrielsen, Ulleberg, & Watten, 2012) Summary of Basic Mental Functioning To obtain a rating of an adolescent patient’s overall mental functioning, a clinician should add up the 1- to 5-point ratings assigned to each capacity (Table 7.1a), yielding a single numerical index of overall functioning, ranging from 13 to 65. This global index permits provisionally assigning a patient to one of the categories outlined in Table 7.1b, which provides short qualitative descriptions of seven levels of mental functioning. Schematically, healthy mental functioning corresponds to MA1; neurotic to MA2 and MA3; borderline to MA4, MA5, and MA6; and psychotic to MA7. TABLE 7.1A. MA-Axis Functioning: Total Score MC-Axis Capacities Rating scale 1. Capacity for attention and learning (executive functioning) 5 4 3 2 1 2. Capacity for affect regulation and expression 5 4 3 2 1 3. Capacity for mentalization 5 4 3 2 1 4. Bodily experiences and representations 5 4 3 2 1 5. Capacity for differentiation and integration 5 4 3 2 1 6. Capacity to regulate self-esteem 5 4 3 2 1 7. Capacity for trust, empathy, and intimacy 5 4 3 2 1 8. Capacity for impulse regulation 5 4 3 2 1 9. Defensive functioning 5 4 3 2 1 10. Capacity for adaptation and resilience 5 4 3 2 1 11. Capacity to construct and use internal standards and ideals 5 4 3 2 1 12. Capacity to explore one’s inner life 5 4 3 2 1 13. Capacity for agency and purpose 5 4 3 2 1 Total score =

Neurot...
chunks.text: 454 Adolescence TABLE 7.1B. Levels of Mental Functioning Level; range Heading Description Healthy MA 1; 58–65 Healthy/optimal mental Optimal or very good functioning in all or most mental capacities, with functioning modest, expectable variations in flexibility and adaptation across contexts. Neurotic MA 2; 51–57 Good/appropriate Appropriate level of mental functioning, with some specific areas of mental functioning with difficulty (e.g., in three or four mental capacities) that reflect conflicts or some areas of difficulty challenges related to specific life situations or events. MA 3; 43–50 Mild impairments in Mild constrictions and areas of inflexibility in some domains of mental mental functioning functioning, implying impairments in areas such as self-esteem regulation, impulse and affect regulation, defensive functioning, and inner life exploration capacities. Borderline MA4; 36–42 Moderate impairments Moderate constrictions and inflexibility in most or almost all domains of in mental functioning mental functioning, affecting quality and stability of relationships, sense of identity, and range of affects tolerated. Functioning begins to reflect the significantly impaired adaptations that are described as “borderlinelevel” in many psychodynamic writings, and that are found, in increasing severity, in the next two levels. MA5; 22–35 Major impairments in Major constrictions and alterations in almost all domains of mental mental functioning functioning (e.g., tendencies toward fragmentation and difficulties in self–object differentiation), along with limited experience of feelings and/or thoughts in major life areas (i.e., love, school or work, play). MA6; 21–27 Significant defects in Significant defects in most domains of mental functioning, along with basic mental functions problems in the organization and/or integration–differentiation of self and objects. Psychotic MA 7; 13–20 Major/severe defects in Major and severe defects in most domains of mental functioning, with basic mental functions fragmentation; impairment in reality testing or self–object differentiation; disturbances in perception, integration, and regulation of affect and thought; and defects in one or more basic mental functions (e.g., perception, integration, motor, memory, regulation, judgment).

chunks.heading: CHAPTER 8
chunks.preview: CHAPTER 8 Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders in Adolescence PA Axis C H A P T E R E D IT O R S Annalisa Tanzilli, PhD Carla Sharp, PhD Introduction Personality is observable during childhood and continues to develop throughout the lifespan. Most adolescents can be described as having a relati...
chunks.text: CHAPTER 8 Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders in Adolescence PA Axis C H A P T E R E D IT O R S Annalisa Tanzilli, PhD Carla Sharp, PhD Introduction Personality is observable during childhood and continues to develop throughout the lifespan. Most adolescents can be described as having a relatively organized emerging personality style1 that structures their thoughts, feelings, behaviors, and ways of being. Notably, in response to numerous variables (including biological and temperamental predispositions, cognitive and socioaffective abilities, relationships with peers, family type and family dynamics, broader societal and cultural influences, and life events), an adolescent’s emerging personality contains elements of both strength and vulnerability and may change over time. Assessing personality functioning in adolescence is challenging. Many issues related to personality development, especially factors that either increase risk or protect against pathology, were discussed in Chapter 4 (the PC Axis, pp. 212–255). Many personality dynamics and processes in adolescence have continuity with those of adult personality (for a more in-depth discussion, see Chapter 12 on the P Axis, pp. 651– 708). In this chapter, we focus on the specific characteristics of adolescent personality. As in the sections on personality in childhood, adulthood, and later life, the major organizing principles for the PA Axis are (1) level of personality organization and 1 In this context, the term “style” refers to an adolescent’s emerging personality configuration, which shows relatively adaptive characteristics. In their more extreme, rigid and dysfunctional forms, personality styles become diagnosable as personality disorders.

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 469
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 469 (2) personality style. The former is an index of global functioning (more or less severe) that applies to all the emerging personality styles or disorders. This aspect of personality is potentially responsive to treatment and other influences, and is su...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 469 (2) personality style. The former is an index of global functioning (more or less severe) that applies to all the emerging personality styles or disorders. This aspect of personality is potentially responsive to treatment and other influences, and is subject to change over time. The latter, in contrast, represents individual ways of thinking, feeling, behaving, and relating. We begin with an overview of contributions from both clinical observation and empirical research, which increasingly demonstrates the importance of assessing adolescent personality. Next, we discuss the unique aspects of the adolescent stage of development and the attendant clinical issues that should be considered when assessing adolescent personality. We then review the most valid and reliable instruments that clinicians and researchers can use with young people in this age range. Next, we detail an approach to diagnosing level of personality organization and emerging personality style or disorder in adolescents. Finally, we provide a general description of common emerging personality styles/disorders, including expectable transference and countertransference responses and indications for treatment.
