Emerging Personality of Adolescents A growing body of evidence suggests that patterns of personality pathologies emerging in adolescence or earlier are both highly prevalent and persistent (Chanen, 2015; Chanen & Kaess, 2012; Grilo & McGlashan, 1998; Hutsebaut, Clarke, & Chanen, 2023; Kongerslev, Chanen, & Simonsen, 2015; Sharp, 2017; Sharp & Fonagy, 2015; Westen, DeFife, Malone, & DiLallo, 2014). And researchers have found that personality disorder traits tend to be as stable as in adulthood (Bornovalova, Hicks, Iacono, & McGue, 2009; Ferguson, 2010). Accordingly, both the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (most recently DSM-5 and DSM-5-TR; American Psychiatric Association, 2013, 2022) and the International Classification of Diseases (currently ICD-11; World Health Organization, 2019) permit the diagnosis of personality disorder in adolescence. Long-­standing myths about the perceived instability of personality in young people are not based on empirical evidence. They may reflect efforts both to distinguish characteristics normative to a developmental stage characterized by “storm and stress” from persistent pathology (Casey, Jones, & Levita, 2010), and to protect youth from social stigma (Catthoor, Feenstra, Hutsebaut, Schrijvers, & Sabbe, 2015; Chanen, Jovev, & McGorry, 2008; Laurenssen, Hutsebaut, Feenstra, Van Busschbach, & Luyten, 2013). Clinical and empirical research, however, indicate that adolescent personality pathology is distinct from both normal development and other psychopathology (Chanen, Sharp, Nicol, & Kaess, 2022). This finding has critical relevance to developing treatments for adolescent patients. Parsing out the distinction between developmental stage and pathology, Kernberg (1998) differentiates adolescents who face an identity crisis from those with identity diffusion (see also Erikson, 1956). Identity crisis is a normal part of adolescence, reflecting issues relating to individuation from the family and management of conflicting feelings about sexual urges. But identity diffusion—­evidenced by a lack of integration of internal representations of both self and others—­indicates personality pathology (Chen, Cohen, Johnson, Kasen, & Crawford, 2004; Johnson, Chen, & Cohen, 2004; Wiley & Berman, 2013). Adolescents with identity diffusion lack a normative sense of who they are and have trouble understanding and relating to other people without distortions. The distinction between adolescent personality pathology and

chunks.heading: 470 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 470 Adolescence “normal adolescent upheaval” is further supported by longitudinal studies of personality development showing that emerging personality pathology co-­occurs with other forms of impairment and predicts not only adult personality disorders but also problematic interpersonal functioning...
chunks.text: 470 Adolescence “normal adolescent upheaval” is further supported by longitudinal studies of personality development showing that emerging personality pathology co-­occurs with other forms of impairment and predicts not only adult personality disorders but also problematic interpersonal functioning and a range of other negative outcomes in the years following adolescence (Daley, Copeland, Wright, Roalfe, & Wales, 2006; Feenstra, Van Busschbach, Verheul, & Hutsebaut, 2011; Hastrup, Jennum, Ibsen, Kjellberg, & Simonsen, 2022; Kasen et al., 2007; Vanwoerden et al., 2021; Wertz et al., 2020; Winsper et al., 2020). Attending to personality pathology in adolescents is important for several reasons. First, by assessing adolescent personality disorders, clinicians may more effectively shape treatments of psychiatric disorders. Personality functioning is known to influence the presentation and severity of internalizing disorders, externalizing disorders, and eating pathology (Freeman & Reinecke, 2007; Johnson et al., 2000). Second, comorbid patterns of psychiatric psychopathology can be tied to common personality features (Allertz & van Voorst, 2007; Chen, Cohen, Kasen, & Johnson, 2006; Lavan & Johnson, 2002). Third, there is increasing evidence that high levels of maladaptive personality traits that can be observed prior to adolescence remain relatively stable into adulthood (Chanen et al., 2004, 2022; Cohen, Crawford, Johnson, & Kasen, 2005). For example, childhood diagnoses of disruptive, impulse-control, and conduct disorders with callous–­unemotional characteristics are now thought to persist as a form of psychopathy or adult antisocial personality disorder (Cohen et al., 2005; Rey, Morris-­Yates, Singh, Andrews, & Stewart, 1995). These characteristics are not only stable but also comorbid with other conditions, including substance use disorders, which are diagnosable in adolescence (Biederman et al., 2008; Chanen et al., 2004; Cohen et al., 2005; DiLallo, Jones, & Westen, 2009; Frick, Stickle, Dandreaux, Farrell, & Kimonis, 2005; Jones & Westen, 2010; Rey, Morris-­Yates, Singh, Andrews, & Stewart, 1995). Such findings speak to the need to provide early, targeted interventions to treat emerging personality pathology. At the same time, we should note that the developmental trajectories of adolescents are not linear or predetermined. Concepts from Developmental Psychopathology such as equifinality and multifinality suggest that the outcomes of a personality disorder may depend on several concomitant variables, and that risk factors for personality pathology do not always lead to true disorder diagnoses. Personality assessment in adolescents can therefore be more complex than in adults. Therapists are often hesitant to diagnose personality disorders with young patients, partly because of concern about assigning labels that may stigmatize adolescents. Nevertheless, young people do have personalities, which can include both adaptive and maladaptive features. Some experts (e.g., Elvins & Kaess, 2022) have warned that efforts to protect young people by avoiding the diagnosis of personality disorder may unintentionally collude with and reinforce discriminatory beliefs, thereby creating further harm and jeopardizing opportunities for early intervention (Chanen, 2021; Hutsebaut, Clarke, & Chanen, 2023). Despite pleas from both patients and their families (e.g., Baltzersen, 2021), young people with personality disorders and those who care for them suffer greater stigma than people with other mental disorders (Catthoor et al., 2015) and struggle to be respected and taken seriously (Chanen, 2021). Lack of training and a “translational research gap” may thus be driving mistaken beliefs and negative attitudes, with the consequence that young people with personality disorders are less likely to be offered care and more likely to suffer early withdrawal of mental health services (Francey, Jovev, Phassouliotis, Cotton, & Chanen,

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 471
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 471 2018). Clinicians working with adolescent patients should take into account their age and developmental stage—13- and 19-year-olds are, of course, significantly different. They should be sensitive to family and environmental variables that may have cont...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 471 2018). Clinicians working with adolescent patients should take into account their age and developmental stage—13- and 19-year-olds are, of course, significantly different. They should be sensitive to family and environmental variables that may have contributed to shaping the adolescent’s emerging personality. Deciding whether a young person’s personality style are sufficiently problematic to be considered disorder depends on the clinician’s assessment of these elements. Moreover, especially if there is not even convincing evidence of impairment in the 13 capacities of mental functioning outlined in Axis MA, the clinician may consider a relatively stable personality style as the unique and personal synthesis of the adolescent’s strengths and vulnerabilities (just as one would with an adult). Taking into account the development and personality characteristics of the adolescent, the clinician should assess the characteristics that emerge from the mutual interaction of many factors. Some of these mechanisms have been discussed in more detail in Chapter 4 devoted to the PC Axis. What follows is only a brief review: • An individual is born with certain biological dispositions, such as activity level and ability to be soothed (i.e., temperament). The infant’s unique neuropsychological state develops over time in response to interactions with the environment. • Within the activities of daily life, unique caregiver–­infant patterns develop that ideally allow the child to master, modulate, and regulate affective responses. With maturation, the field of core relationships widens. By adolescence, there may be a tendency to repeat early patterns of interaction (i.e., attachment patterns). • There is a gradual development of a pattern of inner experience and behavior that continues to affect and be affected by interactions with the people in the adolescent’s expanding environment—­an environment that the young person has increasing power to shape. The adolescent’s self-­esteem develops as a result of the conscious and unconscious meanings attached to these experiences. • During development, adaptive and defensive operations emerge to master both unusual or traumatic experiences and the normal frustrations of growing up. Young people with high levels of aggression and irritability are often in chronic conflict with parents, teachers, and peers. In contrast, adolescents with more internalizing pathology and with behavioral inhibition may elicit overprotective responses or be overlooked, resulting in increased inhibition and consequent feelings of inferiority. Developmental Aspects of Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders in Adolescence Puberty ushers in a period of expansion and potential inner turmoil. Most adolescents manage to navigate this transition successfully (i.e., without undue storminess). But some show temporary difficulties that will not develop into disorders during adulthood, and others may experience persistent pathological functioning. Over the course of adolescence, a young person has to accomplish three major tasks: (1) adapting to hormonal and other bodily changes; (2) beginning the process of transferring attachment from the family of origin to other love objects; and (3) constructing a more nuanced and competent capacity to frame and evaluate experiences realistically in the context of an emerging, increasingly complex sense of self.

chunks.heading: 472 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 472 Adolescence Early Adolescence Early adolescence (approximately ages 12–14 years) is characterized by notable physical changes (growth in body hair, gains in height and weight, changes in voice), among which the most important is sexual maturation (onset of menstruation in girls, testicular and p...
chunks.text: 472 Adolescence Early Adolescence Early adolescence (approximately ages 12–14 years) is characterized by notable physical changes (growth in body hair, gains in height and weight, changes in voice), among which the most important is sexual maturation (onset of menstruation in girls, testicular and penile growth and seminal emissions in boys). Body issues may arise, ranging from mild to extreme, as adolescents integrate physical changes. In response to gender stereotypes and other influences, some adolescent girls hide their bodies in baggy outfits, while others wear overly revealing clothing. Some boys become focused on demonstrating stereotypical masculinity, whereas others withdraw into a more cerebral stance. Ways of coping with physical changes vary enormously. Many adolescents also struggle with their sense of identity, feel awkward about themselves and their bodies, and worry intensely about being “normal” and fitting in. With hormonal changes and increased sexual feelings, it is normative for both boys and girls to engage in masturbation, which may include eroticized fantasy. As their fantasies develop, they may become more aware of sexual preferences (Adelson, 2012; Lyons, 2022). Gender identity and sexual orientation may thus become more articulated with the onset of secondary sex characteristics. While most adolescents may have some gender-­nonconforming behaviors, some begin to express dissonance between their assigned gender and phenotypic sex characteristics. Cognitively, young adolescents show an increasing capacity for abstract thinking and self-­reflection. Through pruning and myelination of synaptic connections, exceptional brain development occurs during adolescence. Neural networks undergo a complex restructuring as they differentiate into more specialized brain regions with better interconnections. Although these processes gradually promote greater integrative abilities, at this stage, adolescents’ attention is still generally focused on the present rather than the future. Some are moody and, when stressed, tend to return to more child-like behaviors. Adolescents may test rules and limits and engage in risky behaviors that may reflect both individual difficulties with impulse control and the generally slower maturation of the prefrontal cortex, which plays an important inhibitory role. Middle Adolescence During middle adolescence (approximately ages 15–16 years), physical growth slows for girls and continues for boys. Self-­reflection increases, permitting adolescents to explore and reflect about inner experience. They become interested in moral reasoning and thinking about the meaning of life. There is an increase in conflicts with parents and more focus on peer interactions. During this period, adolescents are especially vulnerable to peer pressure; in the absence of a clear sense of self, they look to agemates for guidance. In moving away from family members as the principal objects of affection and in diminishing identifications with parents, adolescents may show a variety of patterns: identifications with (and imitation of) a range of peers and other adults, sexual experimentation and/or a hypermoralistic stance, obsessive involvement with idealistic or intellectual pursuits, and/or egocentric hedonism. An adolescent may deny vulnerability and mortality with counterphobic, impulsive acts or may withdraw from action in the world. Empirical studies show that risk taking, novelty seeking, and hunger for stimulation increase during adolescence and are present in about the 50% of the adolescent population (Irwin, 1989; Kelley, Schochet,

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 473
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 473 & Landry, 2004). With respect to attachment pattern, dismissive youth may seek to distance themselves from parents as soon as possible, whereas preoccupied youth may be unwilling or unable to embrace parental demands for greater autonomy. Although famil...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 473 & Landry, 2004). With respect to attachment pattern, dismissive youth may seek to distance themselves from parents as soon as possible, whereas preoccupied youth may be unwilling or unable to embrace parental demands for greater autonomy. Although families may experience an increase in conflict and a decline in overt warmth, such changes may represent a new and temporary wave of insecurity rather than an overall worsening of relationships. As adolescents become more aware of sexual desires and more sexually experienced, they usually develop increased certainty about aspects of sexual identity and orientation. Development of sexual identity in adolescents is influenced by a range of sociodemographic factors (e.g., age, culture), as well as sexual experience (D’Augelli & Patterson, 2001; Mustanski, Kuper, & Greene, 2014). Gender identity may become more clearly developed; some transgender youth may wish to find ways to have more conformity between their gender identity and their biological sex. Like other minority groups, adolescent minorities of gender and sexual orientation may be more vulnerable to bullying and exclusion in certain social and cultural contexts (for lesbian, gay, bisexual, and transgender issues). Family dynamics play a critical role in adolescent personality development: Parent–­ child relationships require realignment during adolescence, a time when midlife reevaluation may compromise parents’ abilities to meet their adolescents’ needs to negotiate increasing autonomy. Parental socialization may influence adolescents’ development of cognitive attributions and emotion regulation, affecting how they face the maturational tasks of this period. Given the importance of both family and peer relationships, parent training, school services, and peer programs all have significant value during adolescence, both therapeutically and preventively. Late Adolescence In late adolescence (approximately ages 17–19 years, though some issues may extend into the early 20s), young women are generally fully developed, while young men continue to gain weight, height, and strength. In this last phase, adolescents become increasingly able to delay gratification and attain insight, with a firmer sense of identity, increased emotional stability, and continued interest in moral reasoning. In late adolescence, youth ideally continue to develop a more nuanced view of their sexualities and gender identities. Significant Contextual Factors in Adolescents’ Personality Development In addition to considering the developmental sequence of adolescence, clinicians should attend to a complicated array of contextual factors. A brief overview follows. Family Relationships Given the vast evidence for the impact of family stress on personality development, clinicians should evaluate how family relationships contribute to the emergence and/or maintenance of personality pathology. Risk factors include negative parenting behaviors (e.g., abuse, neglect, low parental affection, lack of care) (Keinänen, Johnson, Richards, & Courtney, 2012; Stepp, Whalen, Pilkonis, Hipwell, & Levine, 2012);

chunks.heading: 474 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 474 Adolescence parental conflict; parental psychiatric disorders (Cohen et al., 2005); early separation from parents (Malone, Wester, & Levendosky, 2014), particularly before the age of 5 (Lahti, Pesonen, Räikkönen, & Erikson, 2012); parental suicide attempts or completion; parental incarceration h...
chunks.text: 474 Adolescence parental conflict; parental psychiatric disorders (Cohen et al., 2005); early separation from parents (Malone, Wester, & Levendosky, 2014), particularly before the age of 5 (Lahti, Pesonen, Räikkönen, & Erikson, 2012); parental suicide attempts or completion; parental incarceration history; and domestic violence (Afifi et al., 2011). Personality problems in adolescence (e.g., interpersonal oversensitivity, emotional dysregulation, self-harm) are empirically correlated with negative parenting (higher levels of conflict, negative affect, lack of support for managing emotions, and invalidation), while caregivers’ positive affective behaviors are associated with a faster rate of decline in borderline personality severity scores across time (Adrian, Zeman, Erdley, Lisa, & Sim, 2011; Crowell, Beauchaine, & Lenzenweger, 2008; Fatimah et al., 2020; Keenan et al., 2010; Steele, Townsend, & Grenyer, 2019; Vanwoerden, Kalpakci, & Sharp, 2017; Vanwoerden et al., 2021; Yap et al., 2011; Zalewski et al., 2014). When the family environment fails to help adolescents manage negative emotions—­that is, when parents create an insensitive, punitive, chaotic, or hostile environment—­their negative emotionality tends to increase over time (Bates, Schermerhorn, & Petersen, 2014; Lengua & Wachs, 2012). High and increasing levels of family conflict from early to late adolescence are associated with higher levels of antisocial behavior (Fosco & LoBraico, 2019; Hofer et al., 2013; Lansford et al., 2009). Parenting styles may affect adolescents’ development of emotion regulation in different ways. Authoritarian parenting, characterized by high levels of parental control and demandingness, and low levels of warmth and acceptance may inhibit the development of self-­regulation by creating emotional hyperarousal and suppression of negative emotions in adolescents (Byrd et al., 2020; Whalen et al., 2014). With permissive/ indulgent parenting, marked by high levels of warmth and acceptance, and very low levels of behavioral control (Baumrind, 1991; Goagoses et al., 2022), teens are unable to learn how or when to self-­regulate and are more likely to engage in impulsive, dysregulated behaviors. To facilitate diagnosis and inform therapy, clinicians should involve an adolescent patient’s parents in treatment. This practice helps in constructing a more three-­ dimensional picture of the young person’s emerging personality and helps parents to understand how family dynamics contribute to the patient’s personality style and to the perseverance of personality pathology. A clinician’s work with parents may increase parental capacity to respond in a supportive way to a child, especially during times of conflict and stress (Sharp & Rossouw, 2019; Stepp et al., 2012). This is particularly crucial for adolescents at risk for development of borderline pathology given the link between the formation of a cohesive sense of identity and feelings of positive interpersonal contact and connectedness (Sharp & Oldham, 2023; Stanley & Siever, 2009). Involving parents may help them shift from coercive, nonmentalizing cycles of engagement to mentalizing discussions that promote trust, security, attachment, and effective communication (Bleiberg, 2004; Fonagy, Luyten, Allison, & Campbell, 2017). A clinician is thus assessing not only the young person but also the capacity of the caregiving system to engage in and support the therapeutic process. Peer Relationships As adolescents recognize that their parents are not infallible, they increasingly question and resist parents’ attempts to influence and guide them. A relational vacuum can result, which tends to be filled by peers. Although parental influence does not disappear over the adolescent years, it may decline relative to that of agemates. Peer influence

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 475
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 475 may be protective (Roach, 2018). For example, preadolescents who have friends and attain friendships of higher quality show fewer depressive symptoms, have higher self-­ esteem, and experience fewer internalized problems than adolescents whose friendshi...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 475 may be protective (Roach, 2018). For example, preadolescents who have friends and attain friendships of higher quality show fewer depressive symptoms, have higher self-­ esteem, and experience fewer internalized problems than adolescents whose friendships are of lower quality (Prinstein & Giletta, 2016; Rubin et al., 2004; Updegraff & Obeidallah, 1999). Positive relationships with peers can moderate the association between negative parenting and externalizing behavior problems (­Lansford, Criss, Pettit, Dodge, & Bates, 2003; Telzer, Fuligni, Lieberman, ­M iernicki, & Galván, 2015; Victor, Hipwell, Stepp, & Scott, 2019); buffer children from destructive effects on their self-­esteem of chronic maltreatment in the family (Bolger, ­Patterson, & Kupersmidt, 1998; Yoon, Yoon, Pei, & Ploss, 2021); and, in economically disadvantaged families, protect adolescents from negative consequences of economic stress on their life satisfaction (Raboteg-Šarić, Šakić, & Brajša-Žganec, 2009; Wang & Wang, 2024). Similarly, the school environment can affect the emergence and continuation of personality problems; in schools with a strong focus on learning, students show declines in borderline, narcissistic, antisocial, and histrionic personality features (Aldridge & McChesney, 2018; Kasen et al., 2007). But peer relationships can also put adolescents at risk for maladaptive personality development. Risky behaviors tend to occur within a peer context (Wolfe, Jaffe, & Crooks, 2006); within their friendship groups, teens experience a host of interactions that shape their attitudes about such behaviors (Dishion, Spracklen, Andrews, & Patterson, 1996; Patterson, Dishion, & Yoerger, 2000). Peers tend to develop similar attitudes and behaviors over time in the areas of substance use (Ivaniushina & ­Titkova, 2021; Urberg, Değirmencioğlu, & Pilgrim, 1997, general delinquency (Kiesner, Cadinu, Poulin, & Bucci, 2002), dropping out of school (Cairns, Cairns, & Neckerman, 1989), and unsafe sexual practices (Henry, Schoeny, Deptula, & Slavick, 2007; van de Bongardt, Reitz, Sandfort, & Deković, 2015). Peer pressure has been identified as a risk factor for substance use and deviant behavior (Claesen, Brown, & Eicher, 1986; Giletta et al., 2021; Santor, Messervey, & Kusumakar, 2000). During evaluations, clinicians should consider adolescents’ social climates; those characterized by high conflict and shallow interpersonal relationships predict the emergence of personality problems (Kasen, Cohen, Chen, Johnson, & Crawford, 2009). Socioeconomic and Cultural Factors Low familial socioeconomic status has been identified as a factor in several personality disorders, even after researchers have controlled for the effects of trauma, stressful life events, dysfunctional parenting, and comorbid symptoms (Cohen et al., 2005; Johnson, Cohen, Dohrenwend, Link, & Brook, 1999). Neighborhood characteristics may influence personality problems as well (Hart & Marmorstein, 2009). There is also evidence of cultural influences on the prevalence and presentation of personality patterns; the prominence of various symptoms within a given diagnostic category may vary by cultural background (Newhill, Eack, & Conner, 2009; Winsper et al., 2020). Cultural factors may account for the fact that antisocial and borderline personalities are more common in Western cultures (Mulder, 2018). Clinicians need to be aware of how cultural values and beliefs may influence what is seen as normative adolescent behavior within a specific cultural group, especially while gathering parental reports and exploring issues such as separation–­individuation. Clinicians should approach cultural issues from a mentalizing stance of genuine curiosity and inquisitiveness, fostering an atmosphere of respect, trust, and safety that will facilitate exploration of

chunks.heading: 476 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 476 Adolescence how cultural dynamics might be affecting young persons and their families (see also Chapter 9 on the SA Axis). Clinical Issues Arising in the Treatment of Adolescents The physical, emotional, and cognitive develpmental changes outlined earlier make working with adolescent patients di...
chunks.text: 476 Adolescence how cultural dynamics might be affecting young persons and their families (see also Chapter 9 on the SA Axis). Clinical Issues Arising in the Treatment of Adolescents The physical, emotional, and cognitive develpmental changes outlined earlier make working with adolescent patients different from working with either children or adults (for a review of working psychodynamically with adolescents, see Loughran, 2004). Fonagy, Gergely, Jurist, and Target (2002) emphasize two critical developmental challenges: (1) the adolescent patient’s recent development of formal operations, which leads to the intensification of interpersonal understanding—­a potentially overwhelming process in which the adolescent can be flooded by new affective experiences of self and other; (2) separation from both the caregiver and the internal representation of the caregiver. In this individuation process, some adolescents have difficulties resulting from early issues that were largely concealed until this stage. Current attachment relationships should be more directly addressed with adolescents than with adult patients. This may mean considering whether, in addition to individual sessions, there should be family meetings, consultations with teachers, and separate communication or meetings with parents without the adolescent. When caregivers are involved, the clinician should focus on helping them become more reflective and consistent in how they think about their child and the child’s personality. When deciding which family member(s) to meet with, rather than having predetermined rules, a clinician should consider the nature of the presenting problem and how the meeting may influence the family system and treatment alliance. Issues of confidentiality are particularly critical in this age group. The clinician should discuss with the adolescent and the family the limits of confidentiality and consider how to handle communications about risky sexual activity, drug/alcohol use, and other potentially self-­harming behaviors. In listening to an adolescent, a clinician should consider all modes of communication. This includes paying close attention to nonverbal cues of affect, body language, and clothing/physical appearance. The clinician should think about how the adolescent is adapting to relevant cultural surroundings at many levels (home, school, friend groups, neighborhood, culture of origin, religious background, etc.). It may be useful to think about ways that the adolescent perceives self and others in each of these settings, and to notice when things feel either congruent or in conflict. For example, are there different expectations about identity and relationships from one setting to another? If so, how does the adolescent make sense of those disparities and navigate this challenge? To facilitate the adolescent’s growth, the clinician needs to consider the strengths of the adolescent patient, the family, and the community. An engaged, affirming stance is necessary for forming a treatment alliance and fostering an adolescent’s curiosity about self and others. A clinician’s treatment approach should be guided by an assessment of the adolescent’s personality organization and style. For example, transference–­countertransference dynamics and effective interventions with a patient with an emerging depressive personality style and a neurotic level of organization may differ dramatically from those with an adolescent with an emerging antisocial personality disorder and a psychotic level of personality organization (Tanzilli et al., 2020). This difference is critical for treatment even when both patients meet criteria for a DSM diagnosis of major depressive disorder.

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 477
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 477 Clinicians need to consider personality strengths as well as protective factors in the family and wider social systems. The term “mentalizing capacities” has been applied to protective factors that may differentiate between adolescents who go through a...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 477 Clinicians need to consider personality strengths as well as protective factors in the family and wider social systems. The term “mentalizing capacities” has been applied to protective factors that may differentiate between adolescents who go through a tumultuous period but do not develop a personality disorder and those who behave similarly and then develop a personality disorder. Clinicians should consider three key protective factors: (1) reflective functioning (the capacity and willingness to recognize, experience, and reflect on one’s thoughts, feelings, and motives); (2) agency (the sense of oneself as being effective and responsible for one’s actions); and (3) relatedness (valuing of relationship, with efforts to engage with others, and openness to their perspectives (Bleiberg, 2004; Hauser, Allen, & Golden, 2006; Sharp & Rossouw, 2019). Mentalizing may enable adolescents to perceive and label emotions stemming from difficult life experiences and to engage in a reflective process that may reduce their negative impact (Fonagy, Target, Gergely, Allen, & Bateman, 2003). Mentalizing capacities appear to protect against the development of psychopathology in individuals who have suffered early abuse and neglect; in male adolescents, they also moderate the link between psychopathy and aggressive behavior (Taubner, White, Zimmermann, Fonagy, & Nolte, 2012). For a more detailed description of this aspect of development, see Chapter 7 (pp. 413–467) on the MA Axis. Other factors may provide additional information for assessment, clinical formulation, and treatment planning. Higher intelligence quotient (IQ), talents, and positive self-­perceptions, for example, are associated with positive outcomes among children who have overcome diverse disadvantages and adversities (Sapienza & Masten, 2011). Contextual factors that have been identified as promoting resilience in youth include positive parenting, effective schools, and connections to prosocial organizations such as places of worship (Masten, 2007). Competent functioning in any of the central tasks of adolescence (school and peer relationships) may be yet another source of resilience (Skodol et al., 2007). Personality Assessment Tools in Adolescence Clinical tests may provide valuable data that can guide diagnosis and treatment planning. They can also document changes associated with treatment. The use of standardized instruments raises a number of issues, however, one of which is the choice of informants. Research on interrater reliability of clinical assessments in both childhood and adolescence has consistently shown only low to moderate correlations among informants (e.g., De Los Reyes & Kazdin, 2005). The nature of personality pathology (Wall, Ahmed, & Sharp, 2019), and factors such as the child’s age, setting, and type of problem influence the level of agreement. In adolescence, agreement among youth, parents, and other informants (usually teachers) is lower than that in childhood (see Smith, 2007, for a clinically based review). In light of these complexities, we emphasize the importance of gathering information about adolescent patients from multiple perspectives. Clinically, attention to the level of agreement among adolescents, parents, and other observers can enrich case formulation. The attainment of agreement about the adolescent’s difficulties can also function as a treatment outcome measure. In the service of a multimethod approach, we list some useful tools for personality assessment in adolescence. Because the Shedler–­Westen Assessment Procedure–­I I for adolescents (SWAP-II-A; Westen et al., 2014) is a measure of choice for this purpose, we provide a brief description of this instrument.

chunks.heading: 478 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 478 Adolescence Self‐Report Measures of Personality Functioning in Adolescence • Personality Assessment Inventory—­Adolescent (Morey, 2007; Rios & Morey, 2013) • Minnesota Multiphasic Personality Inventory—­Adolescent (Butcher et al., 1992; Sellbom & Jarrett, 2014) • Big Five Inventory—­Adolescent (...
chunks.text: 478 Adolescence Self‐Report Measures of Personality Functioning in Adolescence • Personality Assessment Inventory—­Adolescent (Morey, 2007; Rios & Morey, 2013) • Minnesota Multiphasic Personality Inventory—­Adolescent (Butcher et al., 1992; Sellbom & Jarrett, 2014) • Big Five Inventory—­Adolescent (John, Caspi, Robins, Moffit, & Stouthamer-­ Loeber, 1994; John, Donahue, & Kentle, 1991) • Millon Adolescent Clinical Inventory (Millon & Davis, 1993; Millon, Millon, Davis, & Grossman, 2006) • Levels of Personality Functioning Questionnaire (LoPF-Q 12–18; Goth, Birkhölzer, & Schmeck, 2018) Interview‐Based or Clinician‐Reported Measures • Adolescent to Adult Personality Functioning Assessment (AD-APFA; Naughton, Oppenheim, & Hill, 1996) • Shedler–­Westen Assessment Procedure– ­II—­Adolescent (SWAP-II-A 2; Westen et al., 2014). The SWAP-II-A is a clinician-­report personality and personality pathology measure for adolescents ages 14–18. It includes 200 statements about diverse aspects of young people’s personalities, worded in jargon-­free language common to clinicians across theoretical orientations. Each statement is sorted by the clinician or rater into a number of categories based on their applicability to the patient (Q-sort methodology). The sorting takes place following in-depth clinical interviewing or longitudinal knowledge of the patient. Previous research indicates that experienced clinicians need 6 hours of contact to produce adequate ratings (Shedler & Westen, 2007). In an attempt to increase clinical relevance, items were drawn from multiple sources (including the DSM) and have been revised based on clinician feedback. The score profiles scale provides (1) dimensional scores for DSM-5 and DSM-5-TR (section II) personality disorder diagnoses (PD Scales), (2) dimensional scores for an alternative set of personality styles/disorders identified empirically through SWAP research (Q-­Factors), and (3) dimensional trait scores derived via factor analysis of the SWAP item set. These different SWAP scores are accompanied by suggested cutoffs to yield DSM-­ compatible diagnostic categories. Top-rated items can be grouped to form the basis of a narrative case formulation, by using the descriptions on the sorting cards to create the outline of a written evaluation (Shedler & Westen, 2007). Empirical studies support the validity of both the adult (Blagov, Bi, Shedler, & Westen, 2012) and adolescent versions of the SWAP-II (DeFife, Malone, DiLallo, & Westen, 2013; see also Westen, Dutra, & Shedler, 2005). • Structured Interview of Personality Organization—­Adolescent Version (STIPO; Ammaniti et al., 2012) • Interview of Personality Organization Processes in Adolescence (IPOP-A; Ammaniti, Fontana, Kernberg, Clarkin, & Clarkin, 2011) • Identity Disturbance Questionnaire (IDQ-A; Westen, Betan, & DeFife, 2011) 2 Unlike the SWAP for adults, whose electronic version on CD is available in different translations, the SWAP-II (adults) and the SWAP-II-A (adolescents) are not currently available.

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 479
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 479 Performance‐Based Methods • Rorschach Inkblot Test, interpreted by either Exner’s Comprehensive System (Exner, 2003) or the Rorschach Performance Assessment System (Meyer, Viglione, Mihura, Erard, & Erdberg, 2011) Storytelling Techniques • Thematic Appe...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 479 Performance‐Based Methods • Rorschach Inkblot Test, interpreted by either Exner’s Comprehensive System (Exner, 2003) or the Rorschach Performance Assessment System (Meyer, Viglione, Mihura, Erard, & Erdberg, 2011) Storytelling Techniques • Thematic Apperception Test (TAT; Bellak, 1993; Murray, 1943, 1971) • Tell-Me-A-Story (TEMAS; Costantino, Malgady, & Rogler, 1988) • Roberts Apperception Test for Children–2 (Roberts-2; Roberts, 2005) • Social Cognition and Object Relations Scale (SCORS; Stein, Hilsenroth, Slavin-­ Mulford, & Pinsker, 2011; Westen, Lohr, Silk, Gold, & Kerber, 1985) The PA‐Axis Diagnostic Approach Below, we outline a two-step process of evaluating level of personality organization and then adolescent’s emerging personality style or disorder. Level of Personality Organization We construe personality organization as on a continuum from healthy through neurotic, borderline, and psychotic levels. Based on Kernberg’s (1984) model, these levels can be identified and differentiated according to specific criteria: degree of identity diffusion versus integration, prevalence of primitive versus mature defense mechanisms, and adequacy of reality testing. Quality of object relations is also considered in the PDM. Consistent with the PC and POA Axes, levels of personality organization can also be assessed by taking into account the evaluation of the 13 capacities of mental functioning in the M Axes.3 These capacities are as follows: • Cognitive and Affective Processes 1. Capacity for Attention and Learning (Executive Functioning) 2. Capacity for Affect Regulation and Expression 3. Capacity for Mentalization • Identity and Relationships 4. Bodily Experiences and Representations 5. Capacity for Differentiation and Integration 6. Capacity to Regulate Self-­Esteem 7. Capacity for Trust, Empathy, and Intimacy • Defense and Coping 8. Capacity for Impulse Regulation 9. Defensive Functioning 10. Capacity for Adaptation and Resilience 3 Otto Kernberg has added some criteria for the identification of personality organizations that are consistent with the architecture of the M Axes of the PDM (Kernberg, 1984). Among these criteria are level of integration of the superego; presence of primitive aggression; and ego strength (ability to tolerate fears and anxieties, control particularly intense emotions, and sublimate impulses).

chunks.heading: 480 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 480 Adolescence • Insight and Self-­Direction 11. Capacity to Construct and Use Internal Standards and Ideals 12. Capacity to Explore One’s Inner Life 13. Capacity for Agency and Purpose At the healthy end of the personality organization, it is typical that all mental capacities work together in an...
chunks.text: 480 Adolescence • Insight and Self-­Direction 11. Capacity to Construct and Use Internal Standards and Ideals 12. Capacity to Explore One’s Inner Life 13. Capacity for Agency and Purpose At the healthy end of the personality organization, it is typical that all mental capacities work together in an adaptive, flexible, age-­expected manner that supports good functioning. At the other pole, limited interpersonal skills, poor emotional regulation strategies, nuanced cognition, poor mentalization, and other immature processes can be found. Below we describe four common patterns of personality organization. These can be construed as general ways of engaging the world, arranged from most to least adaptive. Healthy Personality These adolescents demonstrate cohesive personality organization. They manage their biological endowments, including their temperamental vulnerabilities, adaptively within developmentally appropriate relationships with families, peers, and others. In relation to their stage of development, they have an increasingly organized sense of self, including age-­appropriate coping skills and empathic, conscientious ways of dealing with feelings about self and others. Barring unforeseen, unmanageable adversities, such adolescents are likely to grow into a rich array of healthy characters. Neurotic Level of Personality Organization (Mild Dysfunction) These adolescents demonstrate less cohesive personality organizations. They manage their biological endowments, including their temperamental vulnerabilities, less adaptively. Early in life, their primary caregivers may have had trouble helping them manage their constitutional dispositions. Thus, their relationships with families, peers, and others are more fraught. Their senses of self and of reality are relatively coherent. As development proceeds, their adaptations may involve moderately rigid defensive patterns, and their reactions to adversities may be somewhat dysfunctional. Borderline Level of Personality Organization (Moderate Dysfunction) These adolescents demonstrate vulnerabilities in reality testing and sense of self. Such problems express themselves in maladaptive ways of dealing with feelings about self and others. They may resort to defenses that involve distortion of reality and indicate a low level of mentalization (e.g., their own feelings may be perceived in others, rather than in themselves; the intentions of others may be misperceived, and so on).4 4 Several studies associate identity diffusion (Sharp et al., 2022) and incoherent narrative identity (Lind et al., 2022) with personality dysfunction. Like maladaptive personality traits, identity diffusion shows a normative increase in adolescents which tracks closely with diagnosed borderline features (Sharp et al., 2021). There is also emerging evidence supporting the relationship between pre-­adolescent maladaptive traits and later development of problems in self-­f unction and identity (Vanwoerden, Franssens, Sharp, & De Clercq, 2022).

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 481
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 481 Psychotic Level of Personality Organization (Severe Dysfunction) These adolescents show significant deficits in their capacity for reality testing and in forming a sense of self, manifested by consistently maladaptive ways of dealing with feelings about...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 481 Psychotic Level of Personality Organization (Severe Dysfunction) These adolescents show significant deficits in their capacity for reality testing and in forming a sense of self, manifested by consistently maladaptive ways of dealing with feelings about self and others. Their defenses interfere with their basic capacities to relate to others and to separate their own feelings and wishes from those of others. Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders in Adolescence In addition to assessing level of personality organization, clinicians need to get a sense of their adolescent patients’ emerging personality styles and/or disorders. Rather than thinking of these configurations categorically, it is useful to take a dimensional approach, assessing the degree to which each young patient’s patterns match the clinical description or prototype of each personality (see DeFife et al., 2015a, 2015b; Haggerty et al., 2016; Westen, Shedler, & Bradley, 2006; Westen, Shedler, Bradley, & DeFife, 2012). No adolescent perfectly matches a single prototype, of course; mixtures of characteristics and overlaps of traits are common, reflecting the complexity of personality diagnoses in adolescence. Consistent with all P Axes in PDM-3, the PA Axis classification system derives from an attempt to systematize and integrate the body of knowledge drawn from the theoretical literature and clinical observation with the more established data from empirical research. As we discuss in Chapter 12 on adult personality assessment, emerging personality styles and/or disorders can be organized into empirically grounded superordinate categories or spectra (i.e., internalizing and externalizing; Markon, Krueger, & Watson, 2005; Westen et al., 2012). Research with adolescent samples has broadened these spectra, identifying the “internalizing,” “externalizing,” and “borderline-­ dysregulated” spectra, to which an obsessive style may be added (Westen et al., 2014). Adolescents with internalizing pathologies are self-­critical and chronically prone to painful dysphoric feelings (especially depression, anxiety, guilt, shame, and embarrassment), while those on the externalizing spectrum blame others for their difficulties and are inclined toward anger and hostility (with expressed contempt and aggressive, provocative, or deviant conduct). Youth with emerging personalities included in the emotionally dysregulated spectrum differ significantly from adolescents with stably internalizing or externalizing characteristics. Their clinical pictures are much more complex and are characterized by impaired capacities to regulate impulses and emotions, abrupt and intense mood shifts, deficits in the perception of self and others, leading them to have intense and chaotic relationships and unstable reality testing. Thus, the classification of emerging personality styles and/or disorders below reflects, in part, the configuration of personality pathologies that emerged from empirical studies by Westen and colleagues (2014). These diagnostic categories have been revised in light of recent contributions from the clinical and empirical literature on personality assessment in both developmental years and adulthood. While we are aware of the specificities that distinguish the affective, cognitive, motivational, and behavioral functioning of the adolescent from that of the child or adult, we have created in the PA Axis a systematic framework for the organization and assessment of personality disorders that is in dialogue with the PC Axis and, especially, with the P Axis. This Axis offers a comprehensive overview of the spectra and emerging personality styles/disorders as reported below:

chunks.heading: 482 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 482 Adolescence Primarily internalizing spectrum • Emerging depressive (and hypomanic) personalities • Emerging anxious–­avoidant and phobic (and counterphobic) personalities • Emerging schizoid personalities • Emerging somatizing personalities • Emerging dependent and dependent–­victimized personal...
chunks.text: 482 Adolescence Primarily internalizing spectrum • Emerging depressive (and hypomanic) personalities • Emerging anxious–­avoidant and phobic (and counterphobic) personalities • Emerging schizoid personalities • Emerging somatizing personalities • Emerging dependent and dependent–­victimized personalities • Emerging obsessive–­compulsive personalities Primarily externalizing spectrum • Emerging antisocial–­psychopathic personalities • Emerging narcissistic personalities • Emerging paranoid personalities • Emerging hysterical and impulsive–­histrionic personalities Emerging Emotionally Dysregulated Personalities These styles and disorders (and their prototypical descriptions) can help clinicians develop more accurate case formulations and plan treatments oriented toward the specific personalities of adolescent patients. In the following, we provide a brief overview of extant theory and research on each style/disorder. For those derived from research on SWAP-II-A, we offer diagnostic prototypes of adolescent patients with these respective personality styles/disorders (for further discussion, see Westen et al., 2014). Moreover, we included three clinically founded descriptions to provide a narrative formulation of some emerging personality styles and disorders (e.g., emerging hypomanic personalities) that have been derived from clinical and empirical contributions in the field. Finally, we have included a correspondence to prototype scale, so that clinicians can assess the extent to which a given patient conforms to a prototype (see DeFife, Goldberg, & Westen, 2015a; Westen et al., 2006, 2012). Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders of the PA Axis5 Primarily Internalizing Spectrum Emerging Depressive Personalities Adolescents with emerging depressive personalities are not necessarily chronically clinically depressed, but they may have symptoms in the realms of affect (e.g., sadness, anhedonia, irritability), cognition (e.g., alterations in form and content of thought, slowing or accelerating speech), psychophysiology (e.g., chronic pain, sleep disturbance, hypo- or hypersexuality), and behavior (psychomotor problems, self-­abnegation) (cf. SA Axis, Chapter 9, pp. 523–626). Such deficits may cause significant impairments in the adolescent’s mental functioning for periods of time. Depressive periods may occur recursively and intensify when youth are exposed to new situations or face age-­specific developmental tasks. Adolescents with emerging depressive personalities show a relatively stable configuration of persistently dysphoric mood with pervasive feelings of melancholy, loneliness, inadequacy, sadness, and guilt. They are characterized by an impoverishment of a sense of their own worth and a tendency to turn anger against themselves in the form 5 The PA Axis, like the PC Axis, lacks codes for each personality syndrome. Because child and adolescent personality development is a work in progress, we prefer not to reify personalities with codes.

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 483
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 483 of self-blame. These features may have roots in premature or traumatic experiences of loss, separation, or bereavement of significant persons who were loved in childhood in a deeply ambivalent way. They have responded to such losses by idealizing the ot...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 483 of self-blame. These features may have roots in premature or traumatic experiences of loss, separation, or bereavement of significant persons who were loved in childhood in a deeply ambivalent way. They have responded to such losses by idealizing the other and turning the (unconscious) negative side of their ambivalence against the self. Thus, blame and other hostile affects once felt toward the other are directed inward. Unable to cope with the overwhelming sense of helplessness associated with the loss, or to accept the separation, these children come to believe that they are responsible for the painful separation they have suffered. This solution preserves an unconscious sense of omnipotence but maintains an internal hostile dependency on the lost person. The internalization of deep unconscious conflict derived from these early, painful experiences has a significant impact on the relationships of these adolescents. They tend to be compliant. They do not express disappointment or disagreement explicitly but may do so indirectly and in a passive–­aggressive manner. They frequently use reaction formation to avoid the shame and guilt associated with unacceptable feelings, especially hostile ones. They may also use denial, idealization of others, and devaluation of self. Such strategies may set them up for disappointment and frustration when others cannot meet their expectations. Adolescents with emerging depressive personalities may develop depressive disorders characterized by clinical symptoms (especially depressed or irritable mood, sleep or appetite disturbances, a general sense of fatigue, and lack of energy). Clinicians need to distinguish these diagnostic configurations accurately to plan effective interventions. Blatt (2004) delineated two major forms of depressive personality: the introjective and the anaclitic. Introjective depression is characterized by a focus on internal defectiveness, low self-worth, and self-­critical tendencies, and a pervasive sense of internal badness, evil, or irreversible damage. Adolescents with an introjective depressive personality are primarily concerned with problems of self-­definition or identity; they strive to develop and maintain a sense of autonomy, power, independence, and control. In interpersonal relationships, they tend to be perfectionistic, judgmental, and critical. They are often introverted, distrustful, and distant, reflecting a fearful–­avoidant attachment (Blatt & Homann, 1992; Levy & Blatt, 1999). Anaclitic depression is characterized by issues of relatedness, with themes of dependency, fear of abandonment, and feelings of emptiness, loneliness, and helplessness. Adolescents with an anaclitic depressive personality seek closeness and have difficulty maintaining clear boundaries between self and other. Some research shows an association with a preoccupied attachment (Levy & Blatt, 1999). Anaclitic and introjective depressive configurations are not mutually exclusive; rather, they represent closely interrelated modes of impairment that occur in response to severe disruptions in the normal dialectical development of self-­definition and interpersonal relationships (Blatt, 2008). The intrapsychic and interpersonal dynamics of adolescents with introjective depressive personalities resemble those with narcissistic styles, whereas those with anaclitic depressive dynamics resemble adolescents with dependent styles. Differentiating between these emerging personality disorders is important in adolescence. Individuals who are predominantly narcissistic (especially those with the vulnerable/fragile pattern; see p. 501) are often depressed, but they differ significantly from adolescents with depressive personalities in that they massively distort self and others. They harbor underlying or overt feelings of grandiosity that coexist with, and defend against, feelings of helplessness, inadequacy, and failure. The distinction between depressive

chunks.heading: 484 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 484 Adolescence and dependent personalities is less clear-cut, but in general, adolescents with emerging dependent personalities cope with depressive feelings by attaching themselves to others, from whom they seek excessive reassurance and support, whereas adolescents with anaclitic depressive perso...
chunks.text: 484 Adolescence and dependent personalities is less clear-cut, but in general, adolescents with emerging dependent personalities cope with depressive feelings by attaching themselves to others, from whom they seek excessive reassurance and support, whereas adolescents with anaclitic depressive personalities keep their attachment longings private. Transference: Because of their intense feelings of helplessness and lack of agency, patients with emerging depressive personalities may become highly dependent on a therapist. To counteract their negative self-­evaluations, they typically idealize the clinician. Idealization may then collapse under the weight of excessive expectations, uncovering more aggressive or hostile feelings. Some adolescents may become progressively paranoid, angry, or distrustful, especially when a therapist tries to refute or question negative beliefs (e.g., “There is something essentially bad or inadequate about me,” “Someone or something necessary for my well-being has been irretrievably lost”). These adolescents may read clinicians’ positive regard for them as “false,” or as indicative of a lack of competence. Countertransference: Early in treatment, therapists tend to feel parental and protective toward adolescent patients with depressive personalities who are hence vulnerable to enactments that can reinforce patients’ pathogenic beliefs about the self. They may feel compelled to “rescue” them by attempting to convince them of their positive aspects, rather than helping them to explore and understand their self-­devaluation and pathogenic beliefs. As they try to help them develop more multifaceted and balanced representations of self and others, therapists may be put off by some patients’ rigid adherence to negative beliefs and problematic convictions about reality. Painful feelings of helplessness, worry, or confusion may emerge, especially with the suicidal tendencies that can arise when a severe clinical depression reinforces depressive personality characteristics. BOX 8.1. Empirical Prototype: Emerging Depressive Personalities in Adolescents Adolescents with emerging depressive personalities may be unhappy, dpressed, or despondent. They may feel inadequate, inferior, worthless, or like “failures.” They tend to be self-­critical, to set unrealistic standards for themselves, and to experience high levels of guilt. It may be difficult for them to acknowledge or express anger or criticism toward others. They may complain of fatigue and low energy, describe a sense of emptiness, and believe that life has no meaning. They may find little satisfaction or enjoyment in, and be conflicted about, pleasurable emotions and achievement. They may deny or squelch realistic dreams to protect themselves from disappointment. Some have a deep sense of inner badness, for which they may unconsciously punish themselves or deny themselves pleasure. They may be preoccupied with thoughts of death and dying. In relationships, they may fear rejection or abandonment. 5 very good match (patient exemplifies this disorder; prototypical case) Diagnosis 4 good match (patient has this disorder; diagnosis applies) 3 moderate match (patient has significant features of this disorder) Features 2 slight match (patient has minor features of this disorder) 1 no match (description does not apply)

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 485
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 485 Indications for treatment: Blatt’s pioneering studies and subsequent empirical data on efficacy over the past decades suggest that anaclitic and introjective depressive personality subtypes respond differently to different forms of treatment. Adolescent...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 485 Indications for treatment: Blatt’s pioneering studies and subsequent empirical data on efficacy over the past decades suggest that anaclitic and introjective depressive personality subtypes respond differently to different forms of treatment. Adolescents with introjective depressive personalities may benefit more from interpretive interventions that foster insight and increase understanding of their inner experiences. Conversely, adolescents with anaclitic depressive personalities may benefit from supportive interventions that normalize their dependency needs and their sensitivities to loss in the context of a reliable, safe therapeutic relationship. Emerging Hypomanic Personalities Mania is a state of mind in which depressive tendencies are counteracted by the defense of denial; it is the mirror image of depression. When denial pervades all aspects of someone’s experience, we consider that person to have a hypomanic personality. Adolescents with emerging hypomanic personalities are excited, optimistic, energetic, hyperconfident, impulsive, extraverted, and hypersexual. They may also be irritable, irresponsible, rude, and reckless (Akhtar, 1988; Akiskal & Akiskal, 1992). Instability of mood, excitement, distractibility, irritability, and an exaggerated but superficial sociability are notable among young people with hypomanic tendencies. The perpetual mobility of these adolescents suggests profound anxieties about self-­fragmentation. To ward off feelings of helplessness, weakness, and fragility, and to maintain their self-­esteem, they avoid pain at all costs. If they encounter failure, suffer a loss, or experience a painful separation, their manic “armor” may lead them in the direction of suicide attempts or psychotic breakdown. The backgrounds of these adolescents tend to include traumatic experiences (abuse, abandonment, mistreatment, neglect), to which they have responded by reliance on the defenses of denial, omnipotence, and acting out. Because these are primitive defenses, these emerging personalities are usually found at the borderline level of personality organization and have considerable overlap with emotionally dysregulated personalities. But dysregulated adolescents have more pronounced identity disturbance, greater impulsivity, higher levels of emotional dependency, more aggressiveness and hostility, and more intense feelings of shame and guilt than do hypomanic adolescents (Bornstein, 2023). Unlike symptoms of bipolar disorders, which are more time-­limited and intense, hypomanic personality styles and disorders are relatively stable. Some scholars (e.g., Judd & Akiskal, 2003), however, consider this personality configuration to be a subclinical condition of the bipolar disorder spectrum, noting the dysregulated mood, cognition, behavior, and sense of self in hypomanic individuals. In their view, the relationship between hypomanic personality and severe bipolar disorders is similar to that between schizotypal personality and the schizophrenia spectrum disorders; the differences lie mainly in the severity of the psychopathological features of the respective clinical conditions. Evidence has shown that young people with more pervasive hypomanic traits (high energy/enthusiasm, irritability, dysphoria, flight of ideas, mild grandiose beliefs, risky behaviors, and variability of affect) are at high risk of developing bipolar, and depressive disorders (Angst, Gamma, Sellaro, Lavori, & Zhang, 2003; Hoertel, Le Strat, Angst, & Dubertret, 2013; Kwapil et al., 2000) and psychotic disorders (­M iettunen et al., 2011). Hypomanic personality traits predict drug and alcohol abuse problems, probably because of poor impulse control, high sensation-­seeking, and general tendency to seek an elated state of mind (Krumm-­Merabet & Meyer, 2005).

chunks.heading: 486 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 486 Adolescence Some research indicates that hypomanic personality is a dysfunction of the behavioral activation system (BAS)—the motivational system providing the motoric/affective contribution to active engagement with the environment—­in response to anticipated reward (Johnson et al., 2000). Adol...
chunks.text: 486 Adolescence Some research indicates that hypomanic personality is a dysfunction of the behavioral activation system (BAS)—the motivational system providing the motoric/affective contribution to active engagement with the environment—­in response to anticipated reward (Johnson et al., 2000). Adolescents with hypomanic tendencies may thus have a deficit in the ability to regulate motivation and affect after the BAS has been triggered. Because of this deficit, they may get into potentially harmful situations or engage in dangerous conduct. A better understanding of hypomanic personality during adolescence may promote more accurate assessment and preventive interventions, aimed at improving personality functioning and avoiding the onset of bipolar disorder. Transference: These patients initially come across as interesting, energetic, and intellectually stimulating. Over time, however, their inability to establish deep attachments emerges clearly in the therapeutic relationship. Their unconscious fear of developing emotional bonds becomes apparent, probably because attachment raises the specter of traumatic past relationships or losses. They fear their own wishes for closeness. To protect themselves from another loss that would be devastating, they have a tendency to flee treatment when they begin to feel deeply connected with the therapist. Countertransference: Clinicians may initially be fascinated by these adolescents, underestimating the intensity and severity of their psychopathology. As treatment ­progresses, they often experience a sense of helplessness, annoyance, and irritation in the face of patients’ excessively high levels of arousal and entertaining interpersonal style, which make it difficult to get in touch with their painful and more authentic feelings. BOX 8.2. Clinically Derived Description: Emerging Hypomanic Personalities in Adolescents Adolescents with emerging hypomanic personalities often appear excitable, energetic, and overly confident. Their optimism and impulsiveness drive them to take risks and frequently engage in impulsive or reckless behavior. They tend to be extroverted, seeking attention and social engagement, but their sociability tends to be superficial and exaggerated. Their moods are unstable, swinging between excitement and irritability, making it difficult for them to focus or maintain calm. These young people tend to struggle with profound inner anxiety, using their energy to avoid feelings of helplessness or fragility. They often guard themselves against vulnerability, trying to protect their self-­esteem by denying any pain or failure. If faced with emotional setbacks, such as loss or separation, their hypomanic traits might push them toward extreme reactions, even leading to self-­destructive behaviors such as suicide attempts or substance abuse. 5 very good match (patient exemplifies this disorder; prototypical case) Diagnosis 4 good match (patient has this disorder; diagnosis applies) 3 moderate match (patient has significant features of this disorder) Features 2 slight match (patient has minor features of this disorder) 1 no match (description does not apply)

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 487
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 487 Indications for treatment: These adolescent patients may benefit from psychodynamic treatments. Therapeutic work with adolescents with emerging hypomanic personalities needs to be eventually focused on denial of pain and negative feelings. Therapists ne...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 487 Indications for treatment: These adolescent patients may benefit from psychodynamic treatments. Therapeutic work with adolescents with emerging hypomanic personalities needs to be eventually focused on denial of pain and negative feelings. Therapists need to proceed cautiously to prevent both flight and breakdown; prognosis depends on the level of intensity and severity. Very troubled patients, those who are at a borderline or psychotic level of organization and who cannot tolerate a focus on grief and loss, may require cognitive-­behavioral interventions and/or integrated treatment, including medication. Emerging Anxious–Avoidant and Phobic Personalities Adolescents with emerging anxious–­avoidant and phobic personalities show a pervasive pattern of interpersonal anxiety, social inhibition, feelings of inadequacy, shame and guilt, low self-­esteem, and hypersensitivity to negative evaluation from others. The roots of emerging anxious–­avoidant and phobic personalities are intimately linked to temperamental traits and early experiences with primary caregivers. Shyness, fearfulness, exaggerated startle response, tearfulness, and social withdrawal are temperamental precursors to this emerging personality style/disorder. Not surprisingly, these adolescents have often grown up in contexts where one or both parents had a similar constellation of anxious–­avoidant and/or phobic traits. Children with this pattern have been described as “slow to warm up” (Thomas, Chess, & Birch, 1968) and may have lived in a family environment notable for efforts (1) to contain and manage interpersonal anxiety and inhibitions shared by infant and caregiver, (2) to create distance from persons who are not family members, (3) to limit exposure to social awkwardness or fear-­inducing interactions, and (4) to manage concerns about separation and individuation. Early in life, a child begins to develop “inner working models” (Bowlby, 1969, 1988) or “internalized relationships” (Loewald, 1962) that not only affect the child’s sense of self, others, and self-with-­others but also influence the child’s developmental trajectory. These inner working models predict (1) the degree to which emotional closeness is tolerable, (2) whether others are experienced as overwhelming and frightening or safe and calming, (3) the extent of any internalized sense of deficiency or incompetence, and (4) whether the individual avoids social interactions for fear of embarrassment. Inner working models affect how the individual is perceived by others and may affect the degree to which the self is seen as an object of persecution or ridicule. Adolescents with anxious–­avoidant and phobic personalities desire relationships with others but are terrified of being criticized, humiliated, and rejected. Their pervasive anxiety (over shame, feelings of impotence, fear, social isolation, and ineffectiveness) can lead to their use of maladaptive defenses intended to keep their anxiety from becoming completely overwhelming. When these defenses fail, they feel the “free-­f loating” anxiety that forms the core of this personality structure. Fears of annihilation, cues signaling interpersonal danger, and separation anxiety (the terror of losing a needed person) dominate this emerging personality. Many adolescents also fear internal fragmentation and report a sense of falling apart. Often, they have been reared by parents who, unable to manage their own anxiety, have inadequately contained their own emotions and have failed to provide a secure base for their child’s development. Their offspring then lack adequate coping capacities and suffer severe deficits in affective regulation and mentalization. They may have recurrent somatic complaints. Under stress, they may show symptoms of panic, hypochondriacal

chunks.heading: 488 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 488 Adolescence distress, or physical malaise—­characteristics that may lead to neglect by peers, intensifying their social inhibition and at times culminating in school refusal. Over the course of their development, these adolescents may become increasingly avoidant of people, until they are essent...
chunks.text: 488 Adolescence distress, or physical malaise—­characteristics that may lead to neglect by peers, intensifying their social inhibition and at times culminating in school refusal. Over the course of their development, these adolescents may become increasingly avoidant of people, until they are essentially isolated. A vicious cycle ensues: Socially avoidant behaviors become negatively reinforcing, ensuring more avoidance and fewer opportunities to form new relationships and representations. Because an adolescent with an emerging anxious-avoidant and phobic personality has little opportunity to disconfirm the sense of self as incompetent and fragile, and the world as dangerous and unsafe, social terror and anxiety become the organizing components of the character structure. At the adaptive end of the continuum, adolescents with this emerging personality may be highly sensitive, sweet, empathic, thoughtful, and creative, with a few close friends and a capacity to pursue interests in depth. At the maladaptive end, they may limit themselves emotionally and intellectually so severely that they avoid or retreat from many age-­expected peer and school activities. Transference: Initially, adolescents with emerging anxious–­avoidant and phobic personalities may feel embarrassed, ashamed, or otherwise uncomfortable around therapists. The intimate nature of the therapeutic relationship scares them. Once they feel confident enough that they will not be rejected, they may idealize their clinicians, seeing them as sources of safety or as “saviors” on whom they need to depend. When separations are imminent (e.g., vacations or short breaks in treatment), they may suffer high levels of anxiety. Countertransference: Early in treatment, clinicians may experience warm parental–­protective feelings toward these adolescents. They may attempt to “rescue” BOX 8.3. Empirical Prototype: Emerging Anxious–­Avoidant and Phobic Personalities in Adolescents Adolescents with emerging anxious–­avoidant personalities experience high levels of anxiety and tend to be shy and self-­conscious. Fears of humiliation and embarrassment cause them to avoid social situations; they are more focused on eluding what they fear than on pursuing what they desire. To manage their anxiety, some adhere to rigid routines. Separations from parents or caregivers may be particularly challenging. With authorities, they tend to be passive, unassertive, and compliant. They may lack social skills and appear awkward or inappropriate. They may inhibit the expression of wishes or impulses and have trouble making decisions. Some ruminate obsessively. Adolescents matching this prototype may have panic attacks, develop somatic symptoms in response to stress, or suffer exaggerated fears about contracting medical illnesses. Some are unduly frightened by sexuality, associating it with danger. 5 very good match (patient exemplifies this disorder; prototypical case) Diagnosis 4 good match (patient has this disorder; diagnosis applies) 3 moderate match (patient has significant features of this disorder) Features 2 slight match (patient has minor features of this disorder) 1 no match (description does not apply)

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 489
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 489 them by trying to convince them that the extent of their fear and anxiety is disproportionate to actual life events. But when therapists try to counteract patients’ distorted thought processes associated with severe fears or anxieties, they may unintent...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 489 them by trying to convince them that the extent of their fear and anxiety is disproportionate to actual life events. But when therapists try to counteract patients’ distorted thought processes associated with severe fears or anxieties, they may unintentionally be drawn into actions that lead patients to become (or perceive themselves as being) more embarrassed, ashamed, anxious, or frustrated. Indications for treatment: Adolescents with emerging anxious–­ avoidant and phobic personalities may benefit from both psychodynamic and cognitive-­behavioral interventions. The former might involve exploring unconscious dynamics that interfere with interactions with others, such as separation anxieties, conflicts about anger, inhibition of aggressive impulses, and embarrassment over sexual desires or fantasies. The latter might include social skills training, exposure to frightening situations, development of alternative coping strategies, training in anxiety management, and other means of increasing competence. Emerging Counterphobic Personalities Adolescents with emerging counterphobic personalities respond to potential states of high anxiety by actively seeking out what they fear rather than avoidance of frightening situations. They may have a higher threshold for stimulation (probably related to a temperamental variation of “novelty seeking” or a reaction to affective deprivation) and tend to deny any apprehension. During adolescence, they can expose themselves to great dangers—­driving recklessly, taking drugs indiscriminately, and engaging in high-risk sexual behavior (PDM Task Force, 2006). Mild counterphobia is a common attitude in adolescents as they seek to overcome various developmental challenges and test their own limits. The activities of young people with emerging counterphobic personalities, however, are excessively risky and reflect their intolerance of any sense of vulnerability. Their need to act, without considering the consequences, seems to preserve their sense of power and control. Acting out has the function of immediately discharging feelings of fear and dread that cannot be processed because they would cause excessive levels of emotional tension. Adolescents at the more adaptive end of the continuum of counterphobic personalities may become involved in dangerous activities but not to a degree that completely jeopardizes their safety. Those at the maladaptive extreme may behave in ways that are potentially lethal. Transference: Adolescents with emerging counterphobic personalities tend to treat therapists with an avoidant/dismissing attitude. They try to avoid meaningful connection with, or dependence on, the therapist and behave with detachment and indifference. Countertransference: Clinicians tend to feel anxious and irritated with these young people. Through projective identification, they may become imbued with the fear and distress that these patients deny. The narcissistic sense of invulnerability and omnipotence that these patients convey, despite their dangerous conduct, can also create irritation and apprehension. Indications for treatment: Therapeutic interventions (psychodynamic, humanistic, and cognitive-­behavioral) focusing on these emerging personalities are difficult to

chunks.heading: 490 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 490 Adolescence BOX 8.4. Clinically Derived Description: Emerging Counterphobic Personalities in Adolescents Adolescents with emerging counterphobic personalities tend to confront, rather than avoid, feared or dangerous situations. They actively seek out anxiety-­inducing experiences, such as reckle...
chunks.text: 490 Adolescence BOX 8.4.
