Clinically Derived Description: Emerging Counterphobic Personalities in Adolescents Adolescents with emerging counterphobic personalities tend to confront, rather than avoid, feared or dangerous situations. They actively seek out anxiety-­inducing experiences, such as reckless driving, drug use, or high-risk sexual behavior, as a way to manage their worries and apprehensions and demonstrate their fearlessness. Their heightened stimulation threshold, possibly linked to sensory craving or emotional deprivation, leads them to deny their anxiety. While some engage in risky behavior to test boundaries and overcome developmental challenges, others tend to act out in extreme ways, seeking to maintain a sense of control and power. 5 very good match (patient exemplifies this disorder; prototypical case) Diagnosis 4 good match (patient has this disorder; diagnosis applies) 3 moderate match (patient has significant features of this disorder) Features 2 slight match (patient has minor features of this disorder) 1 no match (description does not apply) implement. Counterphobic dynamics must be approached with extreme caution and clinical sensitivity, especially if the defenses put in place to nullify fears, fears, and anxieties have traumatic roots. Emerging Schizoid Personalities Adolescents with emerging schizoid personalities have a pervasive pattern of detachment from social relationships and withdrawal into fantasy. Their affect may seem flat and their cognition impaired (sometimes including deficits in mentalization). They show a restricted range of emotion expression and seem to have little interest in intimacy; because of their avoidance of social interactions, they are seen by others as “loners.” In contrast to adolescents with anxious–­avoidant and phobic personalities, they may have few or no friends. Because of severe constriction and lack of social skills, many youth with schizoid personalities cannot interact spontaneously with others and tend to remain isolated and emotionally distant. Temperamentally sensitive, easily overstimulated, and hyperreactive (McWilliams, 2011), adolescents with emerging schizoid personalities tend to manage overstimulation by withdrawing into their minds and creating their own worlds. They may become lost in fantasies that deny external reality. These young people may also be isolated by the uniqueness of their ideas and perceptions. Given their preference for solitude and apparent indifference to relationships, many observers have assumed that they lack the desire and need for close attachments. Contemporary scholarship, however, suggests that some young people with emerging schizoid personalities long for intimacy and social connection as much as other individuals do (Shedler & Westen, 2004). Clinical literature suggests two possible etiologies of a schizoid pattern: either a low-­warmth, unemotional, detached parenting style or an overbearing, smothering style. Either may lead to a powerful need to distance oneself from others. The

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 491
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 491 intrapsychic dynamics of adolescents whose schizoid personality reflects one or the other of these parenting styles may differ. Whereas the child of an overbearing caregiver may struggle with fears of engulfment, impingement, or control, those whose car...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 491 intrapsychic dynamics of adolescents whose schizoid personality reflects one or the other of these parenting styles may differ. Whereas the child of an overbearing caregiver may struggle with fears of engulfment, impingement, or control, those whose caregivers were detached may be most concerned to avoid their own longing for intimate care. The deep rejection of unmet dependency needs that characterizes adolescents with emerging schizoid psychologies fosters defensive strategies such as splitting and projection, which allow them to place strong feelings outside themselves. When frightened or overwhelmed, they take refuge in the world of their imagination and/or seek physical solitude (McWilliams, 2011). Schizoid personalities exist along a continuum from a mildly quirky to severely troubled. At the neurotic level, adolescents with emerging schizoid personalities appear emotionally detached and largely uninterested in building and maintaining relationships. Their capacity to mentalize may be questionable, and they may have difficulty with social interaction. Toward the psychotic level, they have more schizotypal traits, and their detachment from the social world derives less from indifference and more from their active fantasy lives. Loss of touch with reality, bizarre beliefs, magical thinking, unusual perceptual experiences, and odd behaviors are notable. These adolescents, potentially at risk for psychosis, may show cognitive deficits, language abnormalities (ideas of reference, vague and tangential speech), epistemic mistrust and credulity, depersonalization and other dissociative symptoms, and sometimes suicidal ideation. They may lack the capacity to maintain a feeling of integrity and cohesion, as if there were no boundary between themselves and others, no protection against being overwhelmed by external stimuli. When identity is so diffuse, closeness with others can be disorganizing, as one copes with threats of feeling vulnerable, invaded, annihilated. Along the entire spectrum, aloneness and withdrawal into fantasy are primary defenses that allow an individual to project outward the unbearable sensations of “object hunger” or “love made hungry” (Fairbairn, 1952; McWilliams, 2011) onto others “out there.” Schizoid personality traits can be difficult to distinguish from autism spectrum disorder. Given that both pathologies are characterized by social differences, odd behaviors, isolation, and apparent lack of interest in interpersonal relationships, adolescents with emerging patterns of schizoid personality may not behave or appear drastically different from high-­functioning peers with autism. The main difference is that individuals with autism are genuinely unable to read social cues, whereas schizoid people with schizoid personalities read the cues but reject relationships for dynamic reasons. Transference: Patients with emerging schizoid personalities may have difficulty making sense of clinicians’ behaviors, as well as their own. Because they do not know how to relate in therapy, they may “test” therapists to see if they will react to their eccentric qualities as others in their lives do. They often hope that clinicians will not lose interest and stop helping them, despite their bizarre, unpredictable, and unusual qualities. They may need a long time to learn how to communicate with therapists and, especially in the early stages of therapy, may feel afraid, lost, empty, and out of touch (McWilliams, 2011). Countertransference: When treating adolescents with schizoid personalities, clinicians may find it difficult to attain the balance between closeness and distance that is crucial for developing a good-­enough therapeutic alliance. This goal is hindered

chunks.heading: 492 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 492 Adolescence by the activation or exacerbation of patients’ anxieties about invasion and control. Patients with emerging schizoid personalities can evoke reactions of ineffectiveness, helplessness, and frustration that may be difficult for therapists to manage (Tanzilli, Gualco, Baiocco, & Lingia...
chunks.text: 492 Adolescence by the activation or exacerbation of patients’ anxieties about invasion and control. Patients with emerging schizoid personalities can evoke reactions of ineffectiveness, helplessness, and frustration that may be difficult for therapists to manage (Tanzilli, Gualco, Baiocco, & Lingiardi, 2020). They may attempt too quickly to contain and allay patients’ deep-­seated fears and may urge these young people to “open up” and let them into their world. Such encouragement is risky and even harmful if it reinforces patients’ fantasies of aggressive intrusion. Sometimes, given the peculiar personality functioning of these adolescents, therapists may unwittingly (and harmfully) view them more as specimens of an “interesting species” than as human beings (McWilliams, 2011, p. 238). Indications for treatment: Many authors (e.g., Gabbard, 2005; Kernberg, Weiner, & Bardenstein, 2000) recommend individual and/or group therapy for adolescents with emerging schizoid personalities. Adolescents with a rigid, idiosyncratic identity organized around defensive independence from others, who are affectively restricted, deficient in relatedness, socially inhibited, eccentric, and often hypersensitive, need to be supported in learning social skills. Such skills may avert a gradual decline in personality functioning and prevent the development of more serious psychopathology, especially psychosis. Early in therapy, clinicians should prioritize supportive interventions over expressive ones. They should avoid interpretations with patients who fear being invaded, intruded upon, engulfed, controlled, and trapped; these will usually incite defensive withdrawal. Exploration of the inner world of the adolescent with emerging schizoid personality requires slowly building a therapeutic alliance, one in which the relationship with the clinician provides a secure base. Cognitive interventions that focus on dysfunctional pathogenic beliefs underlying interpersonal withdrawal or impaired thinking processes may be useful, especially if schizotypal traits are present. BOX 8.5. Empirical Prototype: Emerging Schizoid Personalities in Adolescents Adolescents with emerging schizoid personalities may be socially awkward. They may have few or no friendships and may be ignored or avoided by peers. They may have peculiarities of appearance or manner, show only a limited range of emotions, and be emotionally detached from or indifferent to other people. They may feel like outcasts or outsiders, and may be bullied or teased. Such adolescents may seem childish for their age. They may think in overly concrete terms. Their descriptions of important others may seem two dimensional, and they may have difficulty making sense of the behavior of others. Some may use reasoning processes that seem odd or idiosyncratic (e.g., arbitrary inferences), and some may elicit boredom in others (e.g., by talking incessantly, without feeling, or about inconsequential matters). 5 very good match (patient exemplifies this disorder; prototypical case) Diagnosis 4 good match (patient has this disorder; diagnosis applies) 3 moderate match (patient has significant features of this disorder) Features 2 slight match (patient has minor features of this disorder) 1 no match (description does not apply)

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 493
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 493 Emerging Somatizing Personalities Adolescents with emerging somatizing personalities have difficulty identifying, processing, and communicating emotions verbally, and tend to develop physical symptoms in response to stressful situations. In adolescence,...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 493 Emerging Somatizing Personalities Adolescents with emerging somatizing personalities have difficulty identifying, processing, and communicating emotions verbally, and tend to develop physical symptoms in response to stressful situations. In adolescence, the body changes significantly. In young people with somatizing tendencies, it becomes the center of affective and relational conflicts. Distress in such adolescents is manifested by physical preoccupations; in painful or emotionally charged situations, they complain of bodily pain. Although their physical distress is real and often debilitating (e.g., migraines, gastrointestinal problems, dermatitis, breathing difficulties), they often cannot be traced back to a specific medical condition. In individuals who tend to somatize, emotionally unpleasant or painful experiences are converted into physical discomfort. Somatization enables these adolescents to protect themselves from awareness of psychological conflicts by expressing them in a dissociated, bodily-­invested forms. Young people with emerging somatizing personalities are often alexithymic (lacking words for affect) and tend to resort to concrete or operational thought processes (Sifneos, 1973). They show low levels of mentalized affectivity; that is, they have severe difficulties making sense of their emotions in light of their autobiographical memory and history (Jurist, 2018; Liotti et al., 2023). They are unable to identify emotions and name them, let alone process, regulate, and express them with a sense of agency. Their quality of cognition is poor; they do not know how to access symbolic, representational, or imaginative thinking. Because of their poor reflective function, they cannot interpret their own and others’ actions and reactions in terms of underlying mental states. Transference: Adolescent patients with emerging somatizing personalities are self-­ absorbed and focused on their physical complaints. They tend not to seek psychological treatment, as they believe their problems to be simply physical. When they do come to therapy, they may be reluctant to engage and suspicious of the clinician, fearing that he or she will try to persuade them that their suffering is not “real.” When they are in pain or debilitated, however, they may seek the therapist’s reassurance about their somatic concerns. Countertransference: Because these patients focus so tenaciously on bodily complaints, clinicians treating adolescents with somatizing personalities tend to feel helpless, impatient, and frustrated. Countertransference reactions may include boredom, withdrawal, and disengagement. Therapists may try to persuade patients prematurely that their problems have a psychological origin—­an enactment that can reinforce patients’ fear that clinicians will not take their suffering seriously and will accuse them of malingering. Indications for treatment: Adolescents with emerging somatizing personalities may benefit from exploratory psychodynamic psychotherapy, which helps them reconvert bodily sensations and malaises into emotions. In these young people, emotions are not so much repressed or denied as never having been formulated; consequently, therapy requires clinicians to focus patiently on helping them to name unformulated affects (Stern, 2009). Treatment can be difficult because of the resistance of these adolescents to accepting and processing the psychological meaning of their symptoms, and

chunks.heading: 494 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 494 Adolescence BOX 8.6. Clinically Derived Description: Emerging Somatizing Personalities in Adolescents Adolescents with emerging somatizing personalities often struggle to identify, process, and verbally communicate their affects, and tend to develop physical symptoms in response to stressful or...
chunks.text: 494 Adolescence BOX 8.6. Clinically Derived Description: Emerging Somatizing Personalities in Adolescents Adolescents with emerging somatizing personalities often struggle to identify, process, and verbally communicate their affects, and tend to develop physical symptoms in response to stressful or emotionally charged situations. They frequently experience physical pain, particularly when confronted with threatening feelings or psychologically complex conditions. In these adolescents, emotional distress tends to be “converted” into physical suffering, allowing them to protect themselves from facing their conflicts more directly. These young people often have difficulty naming their emotions, as if they lack the words to describe them. Their thinking tends to be concrete and operational rather than abstract or reflective. Furthermore, they struggle to regulate or express their feelings, failing to make sense of their own actions or those of others due to their difficulty grasping underlying mental states. 5 very good match (patient exemplifies this disorder; prototypical case) Diagnosis 4 good match (patient has this disorder; diagnosis applies) 3 moderate match (patient has significant features of this disorder) Features 2 slight match (patient has minor features of this disorder) 1 no match (description does not apply) to tracing their problems to the (often traumatic) affective and relational experiences from which they originated. Emerging Dependent and Dependent–Victimized Personalities Adolescents with emerging dependent personalities feel a compelling need for care. They seek constant reassurance and support, and experience difficulty making decisions and choices independently. Typically, they remain passive, inhibit the expression of their ideas (especially if they are at odds with those of others), and rely heavily on others. They struggle to express anger and often seem fearful, shy, and modest. They are not resourceful; they may fail to start projects without help and ongoing support. Feelings of self-­direction and self-­efficacy are severely compromised. They fear greater autonomy more or less consciously, lest they lose dependent relationships and become lonely. Clinical and empirical literature on personality pathology in adolescence (cf. Westen et al., 2014) suggests that the range of dependent personalities is wide, extending into the borderline/psychotic level of organization, where dependent characteristics may be expressed in toxic, dangerous, or abusive relationships. Adolescents with emerging dependent–­victimized personalities have a notably fragile sense of identity and low trust in themselves: They seek safety, care, and support as they try to sustain an unstable sense of self and regulate their poor self-­esteem. They fear separation and are terrified of abandonment; to avoid being alone, they may tolerate unpleasant situations or become victims of emotionally or physically abusive others. These young people suffer dysphoric affects such as anxiety, shame, guilt, and humiliation, along with significant levels of emotional dysregulation. Because new situations make them highly

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 495
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 495 anxious, they shrink from them. Temperamentally, they may have low levels of novelty seeking, and higher levels of harm avoidance and reward dependence that culminate in their “heroically” enduring suffering. These adolescents have little sense of agenc...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 495 anxious, they shrink from them. Temperamentally, they may have low levels of novelty seeking, and higher levels of harm avoidance and reward dependence that culminate in their “heroically” enduring suffering. These adolescents have little sense of agency; they repeatedly ask for advice or help and often delegate to others the decisions that they could make themselves. In relationships dominated by pathological dependency, the use of borderline and narcissistic defenses is common (Di Giuseppe & Perry, 2021; Perry, 1990). Devalued, despised representations of self may coexist with unrealistically positive or even omnipotent images of others. These young people appear closed and inhibited; to avoid conflicts, they withhold (or have no awareness of) their own opinions. They strongly inhibit anger and hostility but may express aggressive feelings in passive and indirect ways and/or turn such feelings inward in “malignant self-­ regard” (Huprich, 2014; cf. Huprich & Malone, 2022). Unconsciously attracted to the self-­punishment inherent in painful situations, they may avoid pleasant experiences in the service of perpetuating relationships that cause suffering. In adolescents with dependent–­victimized personalities one can see features of relational masochism, connected with the belief that all relationships involve sacrifice, pain, and victimization. Adolescence is a time when young people typically seek experiences outside their families when they face new rules governing social relationships. These experiences can be particularly challenging for young people growing up in the following family types: 1. Families with high levels of orientation-­to-the-other (vs. orientation-­to-self). In such systems, adolescents have learned to attend constantly to the needs of others and to expect others to behave the same way in return. In social contexts that do not function by these norms, they tend to seek care and support from others and suffer when their expectations of reciprocity are disconfirmed. 2. Entangled families, in which pathological dependency is strongly reinforced and attempts at individuation are discouraged. These environments are characterized by pervasive parental control; intrusive, overinvolved, or coercive caregivers reward obedience and reject drives for independence. In these contexts, adolescents give up autonomy and develop intense bonds in which they rely heavily on authorities. 3. Family systems that create anxious–­ambivalent or disorganized attachment patterns. Family contexts that foster anxious–­ambivalent attachment patterns include caregivers who respond to the child’s needs with overly contingent mirroring, resulting in inconsistent and confusing responses to the baby’s bids for intimacy and reassurance. In such families, the child’s early explorations of the world are treated by parents with emotional detachment. The roots of disorganized attachment involve more dysfunctional contexts, in which severe traumatic events, significant loss, or mistreatment characterized early caregiving, resulting in a lack of consistency, predictability, and overall security. These attachment styles can greatly inhibit the autonomy-­seeking process in adolescence. Many adolescents with emerging dependent–­victimized personalities have histories of childhood mistreatment and humiliation. Reactions of anger and resentment, over which such children feel ashamed and/or guilty, may lead them to perpetrate toxic relational situations in adolescence. When ridiculed or bullied by peers, they may react passively, or they may become socially alienated, lonely, and withdrawn, resulting in poor academic performance and potential dropout from school. To avoid the painful

chunks.heading: 496 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 496 Adolescence sense of exclusion, some enact compensatory actions aimed at seeking social acceptance at any cost, including engaging in risky behaviors to earn admiration from peers. This kind of acting out, which may expose them to serious harm, defends against states of demoralization, passivity...
chunks.text: 496 Adolescence sense of exclusion, some enact compensatory actions aimed at seeking social acceptance at any cost, including engaging in risky behaviors to earn admiration from peers. This kind of acting out, which may expose them to serious harm, defends against states of demoralization, passivity, or social isolation. Adolescents with extreme versions of this emerging personality style may be severely self-­injurious and suicidal. Transference: Initially, these patients idealize their therapists and seem preoccupied with receiving reassurance and approval from clinicians they see as powerful rescuers. When their intense dependency needs are not gratified, however, they may react with resentment, anger, shame, or humiliation. These adolescents then tend to perceive their clinicians as emotionally detached or rejecting, leading to their reliving in the therapeutic relationship the traumatic experiences of abandonment and rejection that characterized their primary caregiving relationships. Sometimes, adolescents with dependent–­victimized personalities may imperil themselves by unconsciously reenacting a childhood scenario in which they could obtain parental attention only if they were in peril or in pain. Countertransference: Early in treatment, therapists of adolescents with dependent personalities tend to have mostly positive feelings, including compassion, protectiveness, and a generous willingness to provide care and reassurance. Over time, however, more negative countertransference feelings may appear, reflecting the emotional burden of these patients’ incessant demands for attention, approval, and support. Therapists working with adolescents with emerging dependent–­victimized personalities may BOX 8.7. Empirical Prototype: Emerging Dependent–­Victimized Personalities in Adolescents Adolescents with emerging dependent–­victimized personalities may have trouble acknowledging negative feelings toward others and instead may become depressed, self-­critical, and self-­ punitive. They may express anger and criticism in passive and indirect ways. With peers, they tend to be ingratiating or submissive. They may be passive and unassertive, needy, and fearful of rejection and abandonment. They may not be able to bear solitude. They tend to be easily influenced and are insufficiently concerned with meeting their own needs. They complain that they are inauthentic with others. Many tend to seek out opportunities to save, rescue, help, or protect others. They tend to idealize others and may fantasize about ideal love. Yet they tend to be interested in people who are unavailable or who seem like realistically inappropriate partners. They are vulnerable to being drawn into emotionally or physically abusive relationships, and they may put themselves needlessly in dangerous situations. These adolescents tend to feel powerless and at the mercy of forces beyond their control. 5 very good match (patient exemplifies this disorder; prototypical case) Diagnosis 4 good match (patient has this disorder; diagnosis applies) 3 moderate match (patient has significant features of this disorder) Features 2 slight match (patient has minor features of this disorder) 1 no match (description does not apply)

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 497
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 497 feel helpless, desperate, and tempted to retreat defensively, disinvesting in the clinical relationship. Sadistic fantasies may also emerge, especially when efforts to help are devalued, thwarted, or “neutralized”; these adolescents may frustrate their...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 497 feel helpless, desperate, and tempted to retreat defensively, disinvesting in the clinical relationship. Sadistic fantasies may also emerge, especially when efforts to help are devalued, thwarted, or “neutralized”; these adolescents may frustrate their therapists with the defense of help-­rejecting complaining (Perry, 1990). Indications for treatment: Overall, adolescent patients with emerging dependent personalities may benefit from psychodynamic or cognitive-­ behavioral treatments aimed at promoting the acquisition of greater autonomy and independence. Emerging Obsessive–Compulsive Personalities Adolescents with emerging obsessive–­compulsive personalities are preoccupied with orderliness, perfectionism, and mental and interpersonal control, at the expense of flexibility, openness, and efficiency. They suffer a degree of emotional coercion, including allowing themselves to feel few pleasant emotions, and they tend to use the defenses of intellectualization and affective isolation to reduce or neutralize the emotional charge of any conflict or stress. They have difficulty acknowledging anger, except when they see it as “legitimate” or righteous. Reaction formation allows them to avoid the shame and guilt they would feel if they acknowledged or expressed negative and hostile affects. Their conscientiousness, meticulousness, and diligence have been interpreted as reaction formations against wishes to be irresponsible, disorderly, or rebellious. These adolescents suffer considerable guilt; psychoanalytic language describes them as having a harsh superego. In contrast to many of their peers, obsessive adolescents defer to authorities and have a deeply internalized moral center of gravity. In adolescence, young people tend to experience considerable pressure to achieve, academically and in other areas. High expectations about their educational paths and future life projects may create strong anxiety and considerable distress. In response to demands for performance, young people with mild-to-­moderate obsessive traits may resort to highly organized modes of behavior and defense that are designed to support their fragile self-­esteem. They may emotionally (but not cognitively) detach, striving for a persona that is strong, rational, and devoid of weaknesses. Adolescents who are more rigidly obsessional may feel a crushing sense of duty and be unduly severe with themselves. They may have greater difficulties and be unable to ward off primitive anxieties. To exert control over uncertain, anxiety-­provoking circumstances in which “failure” entails serious negative consequences, they feel increasingly anxious and develop the misperception that even the most mundane activities come with high stakes (e.g., losing the love of others or losing a social position achieved through sacrifice). They may become so determined to make a “perfect” choice that they become paralyzed, unable to make any decision. Ego-­dystonic symptoms may then appear, including ruminations, obsessions, and compulsions. A thorough assessment requires clinicians to note any clinical symptomatology. Transference: Clinicians may be perceived as controlling and judgmental. Because they are anxious about being controlled, adolescent patients may become angry or hostile and view the treatment as a threat to their struggle for freedom and autonomy, though at the conscious level, they see themselves as trying to be compliant with the therapist.

chunks.heading: 498 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 498 Adolescence BOX 8.8. Empirical Prototype: Emerging Obsessive–­Compulsive Personalities in Adolescents Adolescents with emerging obsessive–­compulsive personalities strive for perfection. They tend to be overly concerned with rules, procedures, order, and organization. They may adhere rigidly to...
chunks.text: 498 Adolescence BOX 8.8. Empirical Prototype: Emerging Obsessive–­Compulsive Personalities in Adolescents Adolescents with emerging obsessive–­compulsive personalities strive for perfection. They tend to be overly concerned with rules, procedures, order, and organization. They may adhere rigidly to routines and be excessively devoted to school or productivity to the detriment of fun, pleasure, or friendships. They may see themselves as logical, rational, and uninfluenced by emotion, and they tend to think in abstract, intellectualized terms. Emotionally, they are constricted and inhibited. They tend to become absorbed in details to the point of missing the big picture. They may be self-­critical and self-­righteous, with unrealistically high standards. They tend to deny or disavow a need for nurturance, care, or comfort, and are invested in seeing themselves as emotionally strong and untroubled. In their relationships, they may be competitive, controlling, stingy, or withholding. 5 very good match (patient exemplifies this disorder; prototypical case) Diagnosis 4 good match (patient has this disorder; diagnosis applies) 3 moderate match (patient has significant features of this disorder) Features 2 slight match (patient has minor features of this disorder) 1 no match (description does not apply) Countertransference: Adolescents with emerging obsessive personalities are usually conscientious, responsible patients who try hard to cooperate. Nevertheless, clinicians may experience feelings of frustration and of being under their control, especially when they become critical, argumentative, or unconsciously oppositional. These qualities can generate fantasies of retaliation. Because of these patients’ intellectualization, clinicians may feel unengaged in sessions and find themselves feeling annoyed, bored, and distracted. Indications for treatment: Adolescent patients with emerging obsessive personalities may be helped by psychodynamic, humanistic, or cognitive-­behavioral psychotherapy, both individually and in groups. Since they avoid intense emotions and try to think rather than feel, the focus of treatment should be on helping them make contact with their feelings. Primarily Externalizing Spectrum Emerging Antisocial–Psychopathic Personalities Adolescents with emerging antisocial–­psychopathic personalities have a pervasive disregard for the rights of others. They may be impulsive, irritable, aggressive, deceitful, and lacking in empathy, remorse, and guilt, but they can also be charming and socially successful. In our view, DSM-5 and DSM-5-TR (American Psychiatric Association, 2013, 2022) underemphasize core personality traits and overemphasize externally observable antisocial characteristics such as noncompliance with social norms and engagement in unlawful acts, a configuration that overlaps with conduct disorder

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 499
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 499 (i.e., arrest, repeated lying, repeated fights or assaults). To identify this emerging personality accurately in adolescence, clinicians need to go beyond observable antisocial acts and attend to inner qualities that include untrustworthiness, pathologi...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 499 (i.e., arrest, repeated lying, repeated fights or assaults). To identify this emerging personality accurately in adolescence, clinicians need to go beyond observable antisocial acts and attend to inner qualities that include untrustworthiness, pathological self-­ centeredness, manipulativeness, callousness, deceptiveness, impulsivity, and pleasure in omnipotent control—­tendencies that are more closely aligned with the construct of psychopathy (Cleckley, 1941; Hare, 1970). A biopsychosocial model posits that these emerging personalities result from complex interactions among temperamental, psychological, and sociocultural factors. There is solid evidence (e.g., Odgers et al., 2008) that excessive aggressiveness and oppositionality, observable from infancy on, predispose children to developing psychopathy and increase their risk of acting antisocially. Adolescents with this personality configuration are characterized by higher levels of novelty seeking and lower levels of dependence on conventional rewards. An unusually high need for sensory stimulation seems to underlie their constant search for exciting and dangerous experiences. Some antisocial behaviors have been empirically associated with deficits in emotion processing, changes in brain structures and functions, alterations in cortisol secretion, and atypical psychological traits such as increased callous–­unemotional features (Fairchild, van Goozen, Calder, & Goodyer, 2013). Links between early trauma and antisocial conduct have also been established. Many adolescents with emerging antisocial–­psychopathic personalities have histories of abandonments, loss of primary figures, deprivation, neglect, or abuse (Moreira, Moreira, Barbosa, Sousa-Gomes, & Fávero, 2022). Their childhood context was marked by insecurity and instability. Some clinicians have noted a confusing combination of punitiveness and overindulgence in the backgrounds of young people with these personalities. In chaotic or frightening environments, they could not experience love, trust, protection, and coherence. Such deficits in caregiving are similar to, but more severe than, those associated with the origins of narcissistic personality, with which there is considerable overlap: These children have received material indulgence but not parental devotion. Given their inability to express and communicate their feelings, it is difficult to describe the affective world of youth with antisocial–­psychopathic personalities. In general, they appear emotionally cold, insensitive, oppositional, and sometimes violent; they are manipulative, unempathic, and careless of the well-being of others; they experience mostly feelings of anger, envy, and hatred, and seem unable to feel affection, gratitude, or remorse. They may derive satisfaction from reprehensible behaviors, which buoy their hypertrophied self-­esteem and reinforce feelings of grandiosity and narcissistic invulnerability. Young people with severe versions of this personality may take pleasure in sadism (Kernberg, 1984). Feelings of helplessness and weakness, rooted in poor experiences of caregiving, may have reinforced the grandiosity of adolescents with this emerging personality style. Omnipotent control, associated with splitting, projective identification, and acting out, is their primary defense (McWilliams, 2011). They feel most powerful when implementing exploitive strategies that gain advantage over others. They are devaluing, dismissive, and disparaging toward others, with whom they are unable to develop authentic relationships. Because these behaviors may appear as early as school age, it is critical for clinicians to determine the extent to which lack of empathy and consideration pervades the personality. The primary identifier of this personality is an inability to develop attachment bonds and a consequent orientation to others as objects rather

chunks.heading: 500 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 500 Adolescence than subjects. Bad behavior is not enough to make this diagnosis; some misbehaving young people are deeply attached to peers in criminal subcultures; others may be reenacting a specific dissociated trauma. Two main variants have been identified in these emerging personality configura...
chunks.text: 500 Adolescence than subjects. Bad behavior is not enough to make this diagnosis; some misbehaving young people are deeply attached to peers in criminal subcultures; others may be reenacting a specific dissociated trauma. Two main variants have been identified in these emerging personality configurations. Those with the passive–parasitic subtype pursue their exploitative intentions through manipulative and less overtly angry strategies, while those with the aggressive subtype are characterized by anger and violence. Like other emerging personality configurations, antisocial–­psychopathic personalities exist on a continuum. At the neurotic level, adolescents with these features may hide their cruel, coercive intentions while using their intelligence to outwit and manipulate others. Often, these young people can build transactional relationships and achieve considerable success in academic, social, and work spheres. Lower-­functioning adolescents with this emerging personality show thinking problems, poor reality testing, lack of internal integration, and a greater propensity for dangerous, aggressive, and criminal behaviors. Transference: These patients tend to project their predatory needs onto therapists, attributing to them the intention to act in the service of personal, selfish ends. To gain advantages, they may be highly effective in manipulating clinicians. Not having had experiences of love and empathy, they aggressively devalue empathic or generous attitudes from therapists, and can only make a therapeutic alliance via a sense of respect for the clinician’s power and intelligence. Countertransference: Initially, therapists may to be charmed and fascinated these adolescents. They may be drawn into enactments that involve treating them as “exceptions” or breaking rules for them, including violating the usual boundaries of the BOX 8.9. Empirical Prototype: Emerging Antisocial–­Psychopathic Personalities in Adolescents Adolescents with emerging antisocial–­psychopathic personality disorder may behave with reckless disregard for the rights, property, or safety of others. When they cause harm, they show little remorse. They routinely take advantage of others. Toward authority figures, they tend to be rebellious and defiant, though they can charm them if it is in their interest to do so. They can be deceitful and manipulative, incapable of empathy, and unable or unwilling to respond to others’ feelings. They may be impulsive, unreliable, and blaming. They tend to be thrill seeking, to engage in antisocial behavior, and to abuse substances. Some are prone to violence and may gain pleasure from being aggressive and from being seen as “bad” or “tough.” They may dominate and intimidate partners. Despite protestations of commitment to change, but typically revert to maladaptive behavior. 5 very good match (patient exemplifies this disorder; prototypical case) Diagnosis 4 good match (patient has this disorder; diagnosis applies) 3 moderate match (patient has significant features of this disorder) Features 2 slight match (patient has minor features of this disorder) 1 no match (description does not apply)

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 501
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 501 therapeutic setting. When they realize they are being manipulated, they tend to feel powerless, inadequate, and frightened. Contempt, moral indignation, hostility, and hatred may arise when therapists recognize these patients’ lack of integrity and sens...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 501 therapeutic setting. When they realize they are being manipulated, they tend to feel powerless, inadequate, and frightened. Contempt, moral indignation, hostility, and hatred may arise when therapists recognize these patients’ lack of integrity and sensitivity. A strong affective disinvestment from the therapeutic relationship is a risk, mirroring the inability of these patients to feel interest in and empathy for others. Indications for treatment: Historically, it was widely believed that because they cannot attach deeply, including to a therapist, people with psychopathy are untreatable. But a growing body of evidence now shows that some treatment approaches can mitigate their relationship difficulties, lack of empathy, and problematic behaviors (Meloy & Yakeley, 2014). In a review of research on treatment for antisocial personality, Salekin, Worley, and Grimes (2010) found that outcome for younger patients was sometimes positive. Bateman and Fonagy (2004, 2006, 2008, 2013) and their colleagues have been investigating the effectiveness of Mentalization-­Based Therapy. They assume that adolescents with emerging antisocial–­psychopathic personalities cannot understand their own internal worlds and do not emotionally grasp the internal states of others. They misperceive perceive certain emotions (most disturbingly, fear). They can often identify others’ emotional states cognitively, however, and use this knowledge to coerce or manipulate. Mentalization-­Based Treatment for antisocial personalities seeks to promote reflective function and capacity for empathy. This approach has promise for modifying the primitive emotional experiences of adolescents with this personality functioning. Emerging Narcissistic Personalities Adolescents with pathologically emerging narcissistic personalities are highly heterogenous. Some are grandiose and self-­centered, crave admiration and attention, exploit others, and lack empathy. Immersed in fantasies of unlimited power and success, they may set high goals yet feel recurrently dissatisfied. They often fail to take pleasure in daily activities. These adolescents may also be charming and seductive. They are unable to develop deep, intimate bonds, and they seem insensitive to the needs and feelings of others, but they also expect special treatment. These adolescents fit the “grandiose” narcissistic pattern (cf., Ogrodniczuk, 2013), also called “overt” (Cooper, 1998), “oblivious” (Gabbard, 1989) and “thick-­skinned” (Rosenfeld, 1987). This clinical presentation roughly corresponds to the DSM description of narcissistic personality disorder (American Psychiatric Association, 2013, 2022). A rich clinical and empirical literature (see Gabbard & Crisp, 2018) attests to a vulnerable version of narcissism as well—also labeled “covert,” “hypervigilant,” and “thin-­skinned” which is not included in the DSM or International Classification of Diseases (ICD). Adolescents with this narcissistic pattern have the same grandiosity behind a brooding, competitive, envious stance. They are more inhibited, affectively labile, and distressed by strong feelings of inferiority and inadequacy. They fear criticism and seek constant validation. The more vulnerable adolescents with emerging narcissistic personalities may be shy, outwardly self-­effacing, and hypersensitive; they tend to feel ashamed and humiliated when they fail to receive approval from others. Envy and shame undermine their fragile sense of self-­esteem. A growing body of evidence supports the existence of a healthier group of individuals with narcissistic personality, described as “high-­functioning/exhibitionist” (e.g.,

chunks.heading: 502 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 502 Adolescence Caligor, Levy, & Yeomans, 2015; Russ, Shedler, Bradley, & Westen, 2008; Tanzilli & Gualco, 2020). These adolescents tend to be egocentric and self-­absorbed, with attention-­seeking behaviors and seductive or defiant attitudes, but they also have significant psychological resources a...
chunks.text: 502 Adolescence Caligor, Levy, & Yeomans, 2015; Russ, Shedler, Bradley, & Westen, 2008; Tanzilli & Gualco, 2020). These adolescents tend to be egocentric and self-­absorbed, with attention-­seeking behaviors and seductive or defiant attitudes, but they also have significant psychological resources and adaptive strengths. Adolescence is a critical period for the development of normal and pathological narcissism, as young people face crucial developmental challenges, including defining the self and regulating self-­esteem (Kernberg, 1975). Mass culture and social media can exacerbate narcissistic preoccupations, but most scholars attribute the origins of narcissistic pathology to early development, when the child’s nuclear self is constituted within caring relationships (Kohut, 1971). In families that support healthy growth, parents attune emotionally to their child’s inner world and respond empathetically. Through loving acceptance, pride, and mirroring of their children’s genuine attributes, they help them develop and maintain a cohesive, vital sense of self. Parental empathic failure seems to underlie narcissistic problems. In the context of cold or misattuned parenting, a child can become locked into an infantile attitude of grandiosity (Kohut & Wolf, 1978). This can occur not only when one is excessively criticized, but also when parents praise children excessively and protect them from the “optimal frustration” (Kohut, 1977) required for developing a realistic sense of limits. These adolescents’ insatiable need for reassurance and confirmation of their worth has often been associated with childhood experiences in which their caregivers treated them as narcissistic extensions (McWilliams, 2011). In other words, through either criticism or excessive praise, parents have sent the message that their children’s primary function is to make the parents look good to others. In confused and entangled family contexts, adolescent grandiosity defends against the painful reality of not being seen as a separate, unique subject. Winnicott (1965) notes that such experiences create a “false self,” an identity not based on authentic feelings, but constructed in response to what others expect. Fearing they are impostors, adolescents who have been idealized by parents can become caricatures of themselves. In response to parental pandering, they may feel entitled to considerable privileges, but at the same time, they worry that they are not worthy of this inflated admiration (which may vanish if parental expectations are not met). Anxiety about not being “perfect” becomes the axis around which narcissistic personalities revolve. Adolescents with emerging narcissistic personalities have a painful underlying sense of deficiency, for which they compensate by exaggerated pride, vanity, and putative self-­sufficiency. Kernberg (1975) construes the grandiosity of narcissistic patients as a defense, powered by envy, against feelings of normal gratitude and realistic dependence on others. Needing someone is unacceptable, because it exposes the fact that one is not already perfect and complete. Omnipotence, idealization of self, devaluation of others, splitting, and projection are the defensive pillars on which the emerging adolescent narcissistic personality is built. To maintain this grandiose structure, adolescents with emerging narcissistic personalities relentlessly compare themselves with others, basing their self-­esteem on how much better (or worse) they are. An external barometer substitutes for internal sources of self-­esteem. If outside affirmation is unavailable, an overwhelming sense of internal emptiness and depletion follows. When defensive fantasies of perfection and projection of one’s deficiencies onto others fail, powerful emotions such as envy, rage, and intense shame arise. These states occur when the adolescent experiences ridicule, insult, or exposure. Adolescents with these emerging personalities are in a constant balancing act in which their self-­esteem is at stake.

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 503
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 503 All three subtypes of narcissistic personality can exist at different levels of severity. Adolescents at the healthier pole are highly focused on realistic pride in accomplishment. At the more dysfunctional end, young people may show pervasive patterns...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 503 All three subtypes of narcissistic personality can exist at different levels of severity. Adolescents at the healthier pole are highly focused on realistic pride in accomplishment. At the more dysfunctional end, young people may show pervasive patterns of grandiose fantasy and/or behavior, combined with arrogance, need for adulation, and lack of empathy. Or they may seem depressed, vulnerable to criticism and humiliation, chronically envious, and full of complaints, behind which are frustrated grandiose aspirations. Transference: Therapists tend to be alternately idealized and devalued. They may be recipients of powerful projections split-off parts of the adolescent’s self; projections of patients with emerging narcissistic personalities have thus been construed as “self–­ object” transferences (Kohut, 1971) as opposed to projections of full human beings. Varied self–­object transferences can occur over time: mirroring transferences (based on needs for admiration and reassuring mirroring), idealizing transferences (based on needs to merge with an idealized image, to which one attributes omnipotence), devaluing transferences (based on defensive needs to feel superior), and twinship transferences (based on the need for reassuring similarity). Countertransference: Countertransference reactions to adolescent patients with emerging narcissistic personalities are heterogenous (Tanzilli & Gualco, 2020). When treating grandiose adolescent patients, clinicians may have negative emotional responses that include irritation, disengagement, and hopelessness. They may have an intense sense of being unappreciated, criticized, and belittled, and find themselves angry and resentful at being dismissed and devalued. They may resent their patients’ manipulative and defiant behaviors (Gabbard & Crisp, 2018). They may also experience boredom, emotional distraction, frustration, and feelings of invisibility. It is hard not to be affected by the indifference of grandiose adolescents and to focus on building a safe, cooperative therapeutic relationship. Therapists may feel manipulated when they realize they are being used only to provide infusions of affirmation of their patients’ importance and worth. When treating adolescent patients of the fragile subtype, clinicians tended to feel concerned and may become overinvolved, and find themselves taking special precautions to protect their patients’ feelings because these young people are so prone to shame and humiliation (Ronningstam, 2009). Unlike grandiose adolescents, narcissistically fragile young people are unhappy, depressed, and sensitive to failure and criticism; in therapy, they may feel exposed to the clinician’s scrutiny, which can make them feel vulnerable, dependent, and thus humiliated. Adolescent patients of the high-­functioning/exhibitionistic subtype tend to elicit more positive countertransference reactions. Therapist responses are characterized by good-­enough engagement and less irritation, frustration, withdrawal, and helplessness. Indications for treatment: Narcissistic personality disorders are among the most challenging to treat in psychotherapy, especially in adolescents. Young people with grandiose narcissism are particularly difficult to engage because of their self-­ centeredness and illusory self-­sufficiency. While they may initially appear charming, during treatment they tend to become hostile and may sabotage all therapist interventions through devaluation, manipulation, and omnipotent control. Countertransference reactions may be intense but, if properly noted and managed, can be a valuable resource for improving clinical work, reducing ineffective or harmful interventions and bridging impasses. These adolescents can benefit from psychodynamic treatments,

chunks.heading: 504 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 504 Adolescence BOX 8.10. Empirical Prototype: Emerging Narcissistic Personalities in Adolescents Adolescents with emerging narcissistic personalities of the grandiose type appear to feel privileged and to expect preferential treatment. They have an exaggerated sense of self-­importance and tend to...
chunks.text: 504 Adolescence BOX 8.10. Empirical Prototype: Emerging Narcissistic Personalities in Adolescents Adolescents with emerging narcissistic personalities of the grandiose type appear to feel privileged and to expect preferential treatment. They have an exaggerated sense of self-­importance and tend to be dismissive, arrogant, and controlling. These individuals seek to be the center of attention and treat others as witnesses to their own importance. They believe they should be associated with people they see as having higher status. They tend to be critical, manipulative, and lacking in empathy. They are competitive and envious of others. Striving for an illusory perfection, they inevitably feel inadequate or inferior, despite portraying themselves as emotionally strong and untroubled. 5 very good match (patient exemplifies this disorder; prototypical case) Diagnosis 4 good match (patient has this disorder; diagnosis applies) 3 moderate match (patient has significant features of this disorder) Features 2 slight match (patient has minor features of this disorder) 1 no match (description does not apply) such as Transference-­Focused Psychotherapy (TFP) for personality disorders in adolescence (Normandin, Ensink, & Kernberg, 2015) and Mentalization-­Based Therapy (MBT; Bateman & Fonagy, 2006). They may also do well in Dialectical Behavior Therapy (DBT; Linehan et al., 1999; Rathus & Miller, 2002). Treatment that foregrounds empathy and attunement to the patient’s self–­object needs may be useful for adolescents with vulnerable narcissism, who need interventions that acknowledge pain, insecurity, and vulnerability (Kohut, 1977). In such therapies, they may learn to tolerate feelings of fragility and dependence without resorting to defensive omnipotence or devaluation. Adolescents with a high-­functioning narcissistic personality may benefit from an interpretive, insight-­oriented approach that may increase awareness about their narcissistic defenses against meaningful attachments (Russ et al., 2008). Emerging Paranoid Personalities A pervasive and rigid pattern of suspiciousness and hostility is relatively uncommon in childhood; in adolescence, it is less rare and is often related to emerging antisocial–­ psychopathic or borderline personality disorders, or other worrisome clinical conditions. Adolescents with emerging paranoid personalities are aggressive and mistrustful. They infer malevolent intentions in others’ behaviors. They are hypervigilant as they try to control reality, while fearing that others will attack, betray, deceive, or manipulate them. Projection, splitting, and denial are their main defenses. These young people disavow unacceptable feelings and disturbing aspects of themselves, and project them onto others and onto the social world, which becomes seen as a dangerous, frightening place from which they must protect themselves. Adolescents with these personality configurations have a constantly guarded attitude and seek to maintain power

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 505
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 505 and control. Devoting themselves to protection against imminent threats, they become socially isolated. Their internal reality is dominated by anger, resentment, envy, shame, contempt, and desires for revenge. They suffer an overwhelming degree of fear....
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 505 and control. Devoting themselves to protection against imminent threats, they become socially isolated. Their internal reality is dominated by anger, resentment, envy, shame, contempt, and desires for revenge. They suffer an overwhelming degree of fear. Although some of their affective states resemble those of adolescents with narcissistic personalities, who worry about exposure of their limitations, adolescents with emerging paranoid personality style are unable to get in touch with their own weaknesses. Projection enables them to locate the source of their difficulties outside themselves; therefore, chronic distrust, circumspection, constant scrutiny of the outside world, and efforts to control others are ego-­syntonic. Because they misinterpret the underlying motives behind others’ behavior, they are deficient in mentalization and reflective function and may lack empathy. As with adolescents with schizotypal personality, young people with emerging paranoid personalities may have mild and transient thought disorders. Paranoid personalities have their roots in early childhood experiences characterized by overwhelming emotional experiences, fear, shame, and humiliation. Some adolescents with paranoid personalities grew up in families where warmth and stability were confusingly combined with rigidity, aggressiveness, and sarcasm. Others suffered ridicule, sadistic teasing, demeaning criticism, specious punishment, reproach, mortification, and other forms of maltreatment. Bullying by peers is common in the histories of paranoid youth. In some cases, parents with strong paranoid traits have passed on magical, unrealistic beliefs about the world, conveying messages of fear, danger, and threat. These recurrent and systematic distortions of reality undermine epistemic trust (Fonagy & Campbell, 2017; see also the MA Axis, Chapter 7, pp. 413–467). In some caregiving environments, children who become paranoid have experienced insensitive interactions with deeply unstable, intrusive, and manipulative caregivers; in these unpredictable relational contexts, they have developed epistemic mistrust. When unable to protect oneself from information that is misleading, incorrect, useless, or harmful, a child may construct a reality in which all relationships involve threat and danger, generalizing to all contexts the information acquired from their parents. They learn to attribute malicious intentions to others, based on the assumption that they are all untrustworthy and false. In such adolescents, this feeling of deep mistrust is evident in a dismissive attitude toward any form of dependence and the development of interpersonal avoidance strategies (Liotti et al., 2023). They tend to engage in power struggles with authority figures. Litigiousness and provocativeness may arise from the need to challenge and triumph over the oppression, injustice, and humiliation they have suffered. Adolescence is a developmental stage when early symptoms of psychotic disorders may arise. Therefore, when treating adolescents with significant paranoia, especially those with a family history of psychosis, clinicians should try to distinguish paranoid qualities that are attributable to personality from prodromal or attenuated manifestations of a psychotic disorder. It is crucial to assess the degree of inflexibility of paranoid thinking; if it is impervious to challenge, it may be connected with a developing delusion. Transference: Anger and hostility are often projected onto therapists, with the result that the patient feels fear and distrust. As a result, establishing a therapeutic alliance based on genuine bonding is difficult. Therapists may be suspected of manipulative designs, ulterior motives, and capricious reactions. This climate can be

chunks.heading: 506 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 506 Adolescence wearing for both patients and therapists, increasing feelings of helplessness, anxiety, and frustration. Countertransference: When working with adolescent patients with emerging paranoid personalities, therapists may feel impelled to try to convince them of the unrealistic nature of...
chunks.text: 506 Adolescence wearing for both patients and therapists, increasing feelings of helplessness, anxiety, and frustration. Countertransference: When working with adolescent patients with emerging paranoid personalities, therapists may feel impelled to try to convince them of the unrealistic nature of the dangers by which they feel threatened. The pervasiveness of their distrust will frustrate that aim. Clinicians may then feel angry, irritated, and helpless (Tanzilli et al., 2020). They may also feel criticized, devalued, and unappreciated. These reactions mirror the strong negative feelings of anger, fear, hostility, humiliation, and threat that these patients inevitably reactualize in the clinical relationship through denial, splitting, projection, and projective identification. Feelings of helplessness may be the hardest countertransferences to overcome when working with paranoid adolescents. Indications for treatment: These patients’ suspiciousness, mistrust, and fears of emotional intimacy make them particularly challenging. We recommend individual psychodynamic therapies that integrate supportive interventions with more expressive techniques (Gabbard, 2014; Gabbard, Litowitz, & Williams, 2024). The clinician’s primary goal should be to build a sufficiently solid alliance. Early in treatment, a respectful, nondefensive stance is more likely to defuse malign spirals of escalating paranoia than explicit reassurance. Adolescent patients with emerging paranoid personalities need clinicians’ empathy for their distorted feelings, fantasies, and perceptions, and need to trust that the therapist will not act out in response to projected anger and fear. A respectful, open attitude offers these young people a chance to experience a sense of safety in relationship. The goal of treatment should be to help the adolescent BOX 8.11. Empirical Prototype: Emerging Paranoid Personalities in Adolescents Adolescents with emerging paranoid personalities may be prone to intense anger that is disproportionate to the situation at hand. They tend to blame their own problems on others, hold grudges, and have extreme reactions to slights and criticism. They are often hostile and suspicious, assuming that others will harm, deceive, or betray them. They may lack close friendships and may feel misunderstood, disliked, and/or ignored by others. They tend to devalue and, therefore, disavow their own feelings and impulses, which they then attribute to others, often eliciting in them the feelings they have projected. They have difficulty seeing positive and negative qualities in the same person, especially when upset. Strong emotions may lead them to be especially irrational, and sometimes their perception of reality seems impaired. These adolescents often lack psychological insight and have difficulty making sense of their own behavior and that of others. They may ruminate, dwell on problems, and be preoccupied by aggressive fantasies. 5 very good match (patient exemplifies this disorder; prototypical case) Diagnosis 4 good match (patient has this disorder; diagnosis applies) 3 moderate match (patient has significant features of this disorder) Features 2 slight match (patient has minor features of this disorder) 1 no match (description does not apply)

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 507
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 507 make contact with and normalize feelings that are disowned and projected. These patients need to own their deepest anxieties and dreads and have them matter-­of-­factly accepted. Some clinicians recommend Mentalization-­Based Treatment for adolescents w...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 507 make contact with and normalize feelings that are disowned and projected. These patients need to own their deepest anxieties and dreads and have them matter-­of-­factly accepted. Some clinicians recommend Mentalization-­Based Treatment for adolescents with emerging paranoid personalities (Bateman & Fonagy, 2006), emphasizing that improved mentalization may allow the patient to access alternative interpretations of dynamics they typically understand as malevolent. Emerging Hysterical and Impulsive–Histrionic Personalities Adolescents with emerging hysterical personalities are characterized by attention-­ seeking, emotional lability, and rich imaginative life. They have an impressionistic cognitive style aimed at grasping the global, generic, and superficial aspects of reality: they pay little attention to details while being captured by more obvious, macroscopic features of reality. These young people prefer activities that require intuitive or creative rather than cognitive skills. They may appear self-­referential, self-­centered, ambitious, and competitive. Despite unconscious conflicts about sexuality, they may be seductive and attractive. Although their affect may appear superficial, their relationships with others tend not to be marked by severely maladaptive processes. Kernberg (1984) places hysterical personalities mostly at a neurotic level of personality organization; he construes more severe and primitive variants of histrionic (or impulsive–­histrionic) personalities at a borderline level. Youth with impulsive–­ histrionic personalities show excessive and florid emotionality, exaggerated exhibitionism, self-­dramatization, impulsivity, and provocation. These adolescents have a more diffuse sense of identity, greater emotional lability, and poorer ability to tolerate frustration than those with neurotic-­level hysterical tendencies. They express their feelings theatrically and may appear frivolous and disinhibited; they may behave in explicitly sexual and sometimes dangerous ways. Although highly dependent, they tend to have unstable and superficial relationships, largely because they unconsciously use emotionality and sexuality to control others. This behavior has the defensive aim of increasing self-­esteem and agency by achieving greater social visibility or popularity. Many adolescents with emerging hysterical or impulsive–­histrionic personalities come from families that failed to mirror or meet their needs, in which they felt devalued or even humiliated, often for their gender. Sometimes they have histories of sexual abuse. The crude exhibitionism and sexuality often represent counterdepressive and counterphobic strategies. By enacting eroticized, exaggerated, or impulsive behaviors, they actually seek appreciation, acceptance, and reassurance from others. In adolescents with impulsive–­histrionic personalities, seducing others to satisfy such needs then becomes self-­defeating. To the extent that they act rashly, without considering consequences, they paradoxically evoke attention to the problems for which they are trying to compensate. Receiving the attention they so deeply desire may also reinforce the belief that only by exaggerating and exposing themselves to danger will they be able to feel seen, loved, and appreciated. When others react negatively to their seductive or manipulative behaviors, they may become bitter and resentful. These characteristics may appear narcissistic, but in contrast to the global defensive grandiosity of adolescents with narcissistic personalities, the exhibitionism and need for confirmation of adolescents with emerging impulsive–­histrionic personalities is mostly confined to the areas of sexuality and gender. In adolescence, individuals become more impulsive than they were in childhood or will be later. This spike in impulsivity results from complex biological, psychological,

chunks.heading: 508 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 508 Adolescence neurophysiological, and sociocultural factors. Some theorists (e.g., Cloninger, Svrakic, & Przybeck, 1993) posit a temperamental etiology of impulsive–­histrionic personality based on a constitutional disposition to seek excitement (novelty seeking), which at this developmental stage...
chunks.text: 508 Adolescence neurophysiological, and sociocultural factors. Some theorists (e.g., Cloninger, Svrakic, & Przybeck, 1993) posit a temperamental etiology of impulsive–­histrionic personality based on a constitutional disposition to seek excitement (novelty seeking), which at this developmental stage may lead to extreme or dangerous behavior. Other theories (see Weiser & Reynolds, 2011) focus on critical changes in the adolescent brain, related to synaptic pruning and neuronal myelination affecting the prefrontal cortex. This brain region, in concert with some areas of the limbic system, handles automatic reward processes and deals with impulse control. It is the last brain area to develop fully; to manage impulsive behavior, the prefrontal cortex must compete with the limbic system, which has already reached maturity.6 This comparative cortical weakness slows the acquisition of developmental capacities related to self-­control that begin in childhood, such as temporal or delay discounting,7 ability to predict consequences, sensation seeking, impatience, and immediacy. Thus, immaturity of key brain regions contributes significantly to risky behaviors (e.g., with sex or drugs) that may have negative long-term consequences. From a psychological and social perspective, the sensation seeking and impulsivity characteristic of this age lead youth toward new experiences. An inclination to challenge oneself and confront one’s limits plays a key role in the construction of adolescents’ identity: It pushes them toward greater independence. As they grow up, young people must find new solutions to interpersonal dilemmas and learn to negotiate complex intrapsychic and interpersonal relationships, including with attachment figures and peers. They must also respond to sociocultural influences that support some behaviors and discourage others. Adolescents who develop emerging impulsive–­ histrionic personalities may endanger themselves, violate boundaries and social norms, and transgress rules, sometimes with serious academic, social, occupational, medical, or even legal consequences. Transference: Early in treatment, clinicians are often the recipients of hysterical behavior as adolescents with impulsive–­histrionic personality patterns search for attention and approval. They may be perceived as rewarding figures, capable of providing validation, acceptance, and affection. Sharp fluctuations between idealizations and angry devaluations may follow. These patients may sexualize the therapeutic relationship (Kernberg et al., 2000), but it is essential to distinguish the more neurotic-­level erotic transferences of young people with hysterical personalities from the grossly erotized transferences of those with histrionic or impulsive–­histrionic personalities at the borderline or even psychotic level. When testing boundaries by exhibitionistic and impulsive behavior, these young people may deploy strategies that 6 In adolescence, certain neurotransmitter systems are involved in the competition between self-­ control, supported by the prefrontal cortex, and impulse, associated with areas of the limbic system. Dopamine release in the limbic system, linked to feelings of novelty and pleasure, promotes reward/sensation seeking and other immediately gratifying behaviors, while serotonin acts in cooperation with the prefrontal cortex for the purpose of inhibiting impulsivity. Because the dopaminergic and serotonergic systems mature on different schedules, the inhibitory influence of serotonin is less powerful than the dopamine system, which is already active during adolescence. Impulsive behavior is also influenced by sex hormone levels that increase dramatically in this phase. These phenomena increase the risk of a pattern of addiction for impulsive–­histrionic teens. 7 Temporal or delay discounting refers to the phenomenon in which the subjective value of some rewards lose power when they are delayed. In other terms, it is the preference for a smaller, immediate reward over one that is larger but for which one must wait (Weiser & Reynolds, 2011).

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 509
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 509 frustrate clinicians’ attempts to help. Adolescents with emerging impulsive–­histrionic personalities seek care and affection through seductive and self-­destructive modes; if their therapists are unable to decode the emotional needs underlying their be...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 509 frustrate clinicians’ attempts to help. Adolescents with emerging impulsive–­histrionic personalities seek care and affection through seductive and self-­destructive modes; if their therapists are unable to decode the emotional needs underlying their behaviors, they cannot feel safe enough to process the deep-­seated anxieties underlying their intense transferences. Countertransference: Initially, clinicians may feel admired or fascinated and may find themselves colluding with patients’ demands for approval and affirmation. Or they may respond to erotized transferences with disorientation, irritation, distress, confusion, or attraction. They may feel used or manipulated (Kernberg et al., 2000; Tanzilli et al., 2020). As treatment progresses, if countertransference reactions are not monitored and processed, therapists risk either enacting them or enacting defenses against them, such as defensive withdrawal and disinvestment from the therapeutic relationship. Clinicians can become tired and exasperated by the high levels of commitment demanded by adolescents with emerging impulsive–­histrionic personalities. Their intensity can be challenging. Countertransference feelings of overwhelm, helplessness, frustration, and disorganization are usually related to their powerful sexualized transferences. Indications for treatment: Psychodynamic psychotherapy is the most appropriate form of treatment for adolescent patients with emerging hysterical and BOX 8.12. Empirical Prototype: Emerging Impulsive–­Histrionic Personalities in Adolescents Adolescents with emerging impulsive–­histrionic personality disorder often find themselves in relationships that are unstable and chaotic. They may be seductive or provocative and more sexually promiscuous than others their age, often without genuine sexual pleasure. They often choose partners who are realistically bad matches in terms of age, background, or status. They may get drawn into relationships in which they are emotionally or physically abused or endangered. They may act impulsively, seek high levels of stimulation, and be prone to substance use disorders. They may attach quickly or intensely to people who are emotionally unavailable, develop feelings and expectations that are unwarranted by the history or context of the relationship, or get drawn into romantic triangles. Some fantasize about ideal or perfect love. Their emotions may change rapidly and unpredictably, may seem exaggerated or theatrical to others, and may spiral out of control. They may try to override feelings of fear or anxiety by rushing headlong into feared situations. They may surround themselves with troubled peers or may draw peers into problematic scenarios (e.g., being uncharacteristically insensitive or cruel, or feeling like the only person in the world who can help). They lack a clear sense of who they are and seem untroubled by contradictory feelings or beliefs. 5 very good match (patient exemplifies this disorder; prototypical case) Diagnosis 4 good match (patient has this disorder; diagnosis applies) 3 moderate match (patient has significant features of this disorder) Features 2 slight match (patient has minor features of this disorder) 1 no match (description does not apply)

chunks.heading: 510 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 510 Adolescence impulsive–­histrionic personalities. Interpretive techniques that focus on the therapeutic relationship may be particularly effective. An interpretive focus may promote greater awareness of the unconscious motivations behind these adolescents’ behaviors; increase insight into previou...
chunks.text: 510 Adolescence impulsive–­histrionic personalities. Interpretive techniques that focus on the therapeutic relationship may be particularly effective. An interpretive focus may promote greater awareness of the unconscious motivations behind these adolescents’ behaviors; increase insight into previously unacceptable emotions, thoughts, and memories; and promote trust and reciprocity in relationships. They may also benefit from group treatments, where the need to share attention with others can modify habits of intense self-­ centeredness (cf. Gabbard, 2014). Emerging Emotionally Dysregulated Personalities Adolescents with emerging emotionally dysregulated personalities show a pervasive, severe pattern of identity disturbance, affective instability, impulsivity, chaotic and intense interpersonal relationships, dissociative symptoms, transient and stress-­related paranoid ideation, and suicidal behaviors. In DSM terms, they have borderline personality disorder; in the PDM’s dimensional terms, they are at the borderline level of personality organization. The sense of identity of these adolescents is diffuse, unintegrated, and immature. They may appear disoriented with respect to self and body image, insecure about their own worth, uncertain about values and ideals, or confused about their sexual orientation or gender. Self-­representations are poorly differentiated from representations of others, who are perceived in equally fragmented ways in alternating extremes of idealization and devaluation. Other people are not viewed as separate and independent individuals, and there is insufficient autonomy and separateness in interpersonal relationships, which are unstable, chaotic and emotionally charged. These young people oscillate between deep dependency needs on the one hand, expressed in an anguished fear of being abandoned or rejected, and intense hostility, anger, or despair on the other, arising from the terror of being overwhelmed by excessive closeness, control, and loss of boundaries.
