In situations of high distress or frustration, they have exaggerated emotional reactions, characterized by outbursts of anger, excessive anxiety, and despair; their severe affective dysregulation and poor impulse control lead them to engage in reckless and often self-­destructive actions. They are accident-­prone and may engage in self-­injury, risky sexual behavior, substance use, or suicide attempts, acts that are often precipitated by felt threats of separation or rejection from others (Sharp & Fonagy, 2015; Vanwoerden, Kalpakc, & Sharp, 2017). These adolescents suffer a pervasive and chronic feeling of inner emptiness, closely associated with the lack of a well-­defined identity. When their inner confusion and disorganization become unbearable, their sense of loneliness is exacerbated, and they respond by seeking attention and reassurance. They may behave manipulatively and seductively, trying to hold others close for their own purposes; using projective identification, they may unconsciously use others to regulate their out-of-­control emotional states. Anger outbursts and impulsive attacks eventually push others away, however, reinforcing their abandonment anxieties. When intensely emotional or conflicted, they may experience symptoms of dissociation (especially derealization and depersonalization), paranoia, and even transient psychosis (hallucinations, ideas of reference, delusions). The emotionally dysregulated personality pattern has a complex etiology. Recent evidence implicates genetic–­biological factors and/or neurophysiological vulnerabilities, mostly related to maladaptive functional and structural changes in the brain

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 511
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 511 occurring in adolescence (Goodman et al., 2014).8 The emotional dysregulation, aggressiveness, inability to tolerate frustration, and impulsivity that characterize these clinical conditions could be considered as markers of constitutional–­maturational...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 511 occurring in adolescence (Goodman et al., 2014).8 The emotional dysregulation, aggressiveness, inability to tolerate frustration, and impulsivity that characterize these clinical conditions could be considered as markers of constitutional–­maturational deficits. Reciprocal interaction of these temperamental vulnerabilities with childhood adversity increases the risk that an adolescent will develop an emotionally dysregulated pattern. Some psychoanalytic theories trace the difficulties of these adolescents to their early years, identifying an alteration in the child’s early separation–­individuation process that is reactivated by the “second individuation” of adolescence (Blos, 1967). Otto Kernberg (1975, 1984) and Paulina Kernberg et al. (2000) posit that severe impoverishment of a child’s sense of self may result from the combination of innate aggression and a traumatic environment, which promotes the use of immature defenses such as splitting, projective identification, idealization–­devaluation, denial, and acting out. In adolescence, under the impetus of growth-­related changes, these defenses prevent the reconciliation of good and bad representations of self, others, and self–other relationships, and the negotiation of needs for both attachment and separateness. Masterson and Rinsley (1975) implicate ambivalent caregiving, suggesting that mothers of adolescents with borderline personality have taught their children that pursuing autonomy will forfeit maternal love. These adolescents appear to be victims of an unresolvable dilemma: When close to another person, they feel panic about overcontrol and impingement, but when alone, they experience abandonment despair. According to other psychoanalytic perspectives (e.g., Gunderson & Lyons-Ruth, 2008), interpersonal hypersensitivity, affective instability, and impulsivity can result from experiences of childhood maltreatment and abuse. These children have adapted to pathogenic interactions with inadequate caregivers by becoming excessively reactive to danger. Some researchers influenced by attachment research (e.g., Fonagy & Luyten, 2009; Fonagy et al., 2003; Liotti, 2004), ascribe borderline psychology to the disorganized attachment pattern that results from primary traumatic relationships—­an etiology that accounts for these patients’ deficits in mentalizing (including hypermentalizing; Sharp, 2014; Somma et al., 2019) and frequent recourse to dissociation. In adolescents with emotionally dysregulated personalities, relationships are permeated with a sense of vulnerability and imminent danger. To protect themselves and restore a sense of security, they may maintain a state of epistemic hypervigilance (Fonagy & Luyten, 2016), which prevents them from experiencing trust and establishing healthy intimate relationships. Transference: In treatment, adolescents with this emerging personality may relate with hostility, oppositionality, a sense of entitlement, anxiety, seductiveness, and 8 Although further studies are needed to assess which mechanisms are activated and at the expense of which areas of the central and autonomic nervous system, scholars note that significant alterations in serotonin release underlie impulsive or suicidal behavior in patients with this personality disorder (Goodman, Mascitelli, & Triebwasser, 2013). Other studies have implicated volume reduction in the frontolimbic network, including the orbitofrontal cortex (e.g., Brunner et al., 2010) and anterior cingulate cortex (Goodman et al., 2013). Functional impairments in white matter pathways involved in emotion regulation have also been identified (Maier-Hein et al., 2014). Hyperactivity of the amygdala and changes in its volume appear to be relevant risk factors in the development of this personality disorder (e.g., Monk et al., 2003), but research findings are contradictory and difficult to interpret due to the co-­occurrence of borderline disorders and trauma-­related diagnoses.

chunks.heading: 512 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 512 Adolescence intensity. Affective lability and mood swings, combined with a propensity for acting out, may create recurrent ruptures of the therapeutic alliance that undermine relational safety. These young people tend to reactivate with clinicians the chaotic interpersonal dynamics that characte...
chunks.text: 512 Adolescence intensity. Affective lability and mood swings, combined with a propensity for acting out, may create recurrent ruptures of the therapeutic alliance that undermine relational safety. These young people tend to reactivate with clinicians the chaotic interpersonal dynamics that characterize their significant relationships (with parents, friends, teachers, and others). Their sudden swings from exaggerated needs for closeness (idealization) to anger and resentful withdrawal (devaluation) can be difficult. They often feel overwhelmed by anxieties about real or imagined abandonment (Tanzilli et al., 2023). Countertransference: When treating adolescent patients with emotionally dysregulated personalities, clinicians may experience intense countertransference reactions of helplessness, fear, confusion, worry, anxiety, and anger (Tanzilli et al., 2020). They may also feel profound wishes to rescue and reparent. Their reactions probably reflect the adolescents’ severe difficulties in maintaining stable representations of self, others, and self-with-­others, in regulating emotions and behaviors, and in establishing reliable intimate relationships. With adolescents who are at high risk of self-harm (e.g., suicide attempts, self-­injury, substance use), clinicians may feel dread and hopelessness. They may also become overinvolved and have problems maintaining a well-­defined therapeutic frame. These reactions need to be carefully monitored and managed. Although these patients may respond to limits with anger and protest, they need stable boundaries and safe containment that allow for their expression of unmodulated affects and impulses. BOX 8.13. Empirical Prototype: Emerging Emotionally Dysregulated Personalities in Adolescents Adolescents with emerging emotionally dysregulated personalities feel emotions that spiral out of control and change rapidly and unpredictably. Prone to painful feelings of emptiness, they cannot soothe themselves without another person’s help. When feeling intense emotions, they may become irrational. They may engage in acts of self-­mutilation and may make repeated suicidal threats or gestures, which are often more in the nature of cries for help than efforts to die. Nevertheless, such behavior can be potentially lethal. And some adolescents with borderline personalities struggle with genuine wishes to kill themselves. Relationships for these young people tend to be unstable and chaotic. They become attached quickly, but when upset, they cannot see both positive and negative qualities in another person. They can be simultaneously rejecting and needy, with fears of being alone and abandoned. These adolescents lack a stable sense of who they are and may have a deep feeling of inner badness. They tend to feel unhappy, depressed, and misunderstood, and may believe that life has no meaning. When they are distressed, their sense of reality may become impaired, and they may enter altered or dissociative states. 5 very good match (patient exemplifies this disorder; prototypical case) Diagnosis 4 good match (patient has this disorder; diagnosis applies) 3 moderate match (patient has significant features of this disorder) Features 2 slight match (patient has minor features of this disorder) 1 no match (description does not apply)

chunks.heading: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 513
chunks.preview: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 513 Indications for treatment: Psychoanalytic/psychodynamic and cognitive-­ behavioral therapies can be effective in treating adolescent patients with emotionally dysregulated personalities. Through expressive interventions (aimed primarily at what happens...
chunks.text: Emerging Personality Styles and Disorders 513 Indications for treatment: Psychoanalytic/psychodynamic and cognitive-­ behavioral therapies can be effective in treating adolescent patients with emotionally dysregulated personalities. Through expressive interventions (aimed primarily at what happens in the here-and-now of the therapeutic process) and a well-­structured setting, TFP (Clarkin, Yeomans, & Kernberg, 1999; Normandin, Ensink, Weiner, & Kernberg, 2021) aims to reduce the use of primitive defenses and self-­destructive behaviors, fostering greater integration of representations of self and others, and facilitating separation and individuation. Strengthening adolescent patients’ individuality and sense of autonomy promotes affect regulation and impulse tolerance, and builds well-­differentiated, satisfying relationships. MBT (Bateman & Fonagy, 2004) fosters regaining and strengthening the capacity for mentalization by promoting the development of reflective processes that restore coherence to the narrative of traumatic or aversive events of childhood. DBT (Linehan, 1993; Linehan et al., 1999) helps patients manage and modulate their emotionality, limits dysfunctional behaviors by correcting cognitive distortions, reduces the intensity of emotional distress, and reinforces resources. chunks.heading: CHAPTER 9
chunks.preview: CHAPTER 9 Adolescent Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience SA Axis C H A P T E R E D IT O R S Annalisa Tanzilli, PhD Mario Speranza, MD, PhD CONSULTANTS Massimo Ammaniti, MD Andrea Fontana, PhD Marta Mirabella, PhD Tommaso Boldrini, PhD Marianna Liotti, PhD Barnaby Nelson, PhD Flavia Fiorentin...
chunks.text: CHAPTER 9 Adolescent Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience SA Axis C H A P T E R E D IT O R S Annalisa Tanzilli, PhD Mario Speranza, MD, PhD CONSULTANTS Massimo Ammaniti, MD Andrea Fontana, PhD Marta Mirabella, PhD Tommaso Boldrini, PhD Marianna Liotti, PhD Barnaby Nelson, PhD Flavia Fiorentino, PhD Gabriele Lo Buglio, PhD Andrea Polari, MD Alice Fiorini Bincoletto, PhD Nick Midgley, MD, PhD Riccardo Williams, PhD Introduction This chapter focuses on the subjective experience of adolescents with symptom patterns described in the major international classifications of mental disorders: the latest editions of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM-5 and DSM-5-TR; American Psychiatric Association, 2013, 2022) and the International Classification of Diseases (ICD-11; World Health Organization [WHO], 2019). Adolescents whose symptoms fall into the same diagnostic category may vary widely in their experience (Fonagy & Target, 2002). Exploring adolescents’ subjective world is therefore essential for accurate diagnostic assessment. Since the response to treatments is associated with subjective experiences (e.g., Blatt, 2008), our person-­centered approach has important implications for planning focused and effective interventions. Symptom patterns in adolescents are best viewed within a developmental, dynamic context that integrates temperamental (biological), psychological, social–­ environmental, and cultural determinants. Clinicians working with adolescents are continually reminded of the many factors that contribute to different symptom patterns and to the unique developmental pathways that lead to them.

chunks.heading: 524 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 524 Adolescence Adolescence poses multiple challenges that test individuals’ capacities for adaptation and overall emotional and behavioral flexibility (autonomy, peer relationships, sexuality, social roles, etc.). Young people develop rapidly and experience relatively sudden changes in their feelin...
chunks.text: 524 Adolescence Adolescence poses multiple challenges that test individuals’ capacities for adaptation and overall emotional and behavioral flexibility (autonomy, peer relationships, sexuality, social roles, etc.). Young people develop rapidly and experience relatively sudden changes in their feelings, thoughts, fantasies, actions, and symptom patterns. The meaning of a symptom may thus be quite distinct at different developmental stages. The maturation of mental capacities and personality in adolescence may influence symptom expression—­especially in the areas of identity, relational capacities, mentalization, self-­esteem regulation, and defensive functioning. Clinicians must take into account the unstable expression of symptom patterns at this time of life and favor observations over extended periods before considering a specific diagnosis. For purposes of accurate assessment, we stress in this section the relevance of the clinician’s subjectivity when treating adolescents. Although therapists’ responses to patients’ clinical conditions are largely associated with their personality characteristics (see Chapter 8 on the PA Axis), some common reactions recursively occur in therapists in relation to specific symptomatic patterns in adolescence. Therapy with adolescents may evoke more complex and confounding countertransference responses than therapy with adults, reactions that arise from not only the dynamics of the clinical relationship with the young patient but also interactions with parents, other caregivers, the family system, and the personal characteristics and history of the therapist. Countertransference feelings and reactions that are not recognized by clinicians can have a significant negative impact on their judgment and behavior (e.g., Satir, Thompson-­Brenner, ­Boisseau, & Crisafulli, 2009; Tanzilli, Gualco, Baiocco, & Lingiardi, 2020). In this chapter, we discuss the most common symptom patterns in adolescence (see Table 9.1). We invite clinicians to read the corresponding children’s and adults’ Axes of PDM-3 (Chapters 5 and 14, pp. 256–380 and 760–874, respectively), and in some cases to refer to the symptom descriptions of DSM-5 and DSM-5-TR and ICD-11. More than in the PDM-2, we stress the developmental perspective, highlighting the specifics of different clinical configurations in adolescence and enriching the descriptions of subjective experiences of symptom patterns in young people (especially in terms of affective states, cognitive patterns, somatic states, and relational patterns). We have also expanded the descriptions of the subjective experience of therapists, emphasizing the value that countertransference reactions to adolescent patients have in informing the diagnostic and therapeutic process. Finally, based on the clinical literature, international guidelines (when present), and empirical studies of efficacy/ effectiveness, we offer indications for the treatment of clinical presentations that are currently most typically found in youth populations. Table 9.1 provides an overview of the SA Axis, listing the sections and subsections of the various conditions we addressed. SA0 Healthy Responses SA01 Developmental and Situational Crises Adolescence is a difficult period, characterized by major transformations at the physical, cognitive, and socioemotional levels. Adolescents have to construct their identity and seek their place in the world. Since these goals are never achieved without difficulty, they may have both progressive and regressive responses to this developmental phase.

chunks.heading: 526 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 526 Adolescence For most clinicians, the concept of “crisis” is intrinsic to adolescent growth. A crisis can be considered a temporary phase of imbalance, a destabilizing moment in the adolescent’s life. It can be activated in a developmental sense (when a teen is confronted with a need for increase...
chunks.text: 526 Adolescence For most clinicians, the concept of “crisis” is intrinsic to adolescent growth. A crisis can be considered a temporary phase of imbalance, a destabilizing moment in the adolescent’s life. It can be activated in a developmental sense (when a teen is confronted with a need for increased autonomy, or new cognitive demands, or different types of emotional relationships), or it can be the result of individual difficulties or relational conflicts that are complicated to manage. Or an external event (bereavement, trauma, separation) may disrupt the individual’s psychic life. Crisis does not mean disorder. Certain crises that characterize adolescence are healthy processes, necessary for the construction of identity. Sometimes, in fact, it is the absence of crisis that is pathological. A crisis can have a favorable or unfavorable outcome, depending on both internal and external factors. A favorable solution may allow the adolescent to access a more evolved level of functioning, characterized by acquiring new potentials and resources. An unfavorable solution may create a psychological breakdown or blockage, accompanied by childish behaviors. Outcomes of crises depend on not only on the adolescent’s ability to develop stable psychic functioning but also the resources that the young person and the relational environment can mobilize. When a system can adapt to the changes that characterize an adolescent’s development, anxiety is contained. When attempts to find new solutions fail, and the adolescent resorts to rigid control mechanisms or disorganized and chaotic reactions, we often see the acute onset of a pathological symptom or behavior. A crisis can eventually turn into an emergency. Regardless of its origin (e.g., fragmentation, depersonalization, loss, separation), anxiety, the element common to every critical situation in adolescence, invades the psychic functioning of adolescents and those close to them. Acting out sometimes seems to them the only available strategy to express feelings that are intolerably intense and to get rid of unbearable anxiety. Enactments then take the place of mental representations. They may involve a transformative moment, insofar as something that could not be registered at the psychic level is now inscribed in reality. Almost all adolescent psychopathology challenges the containment and reassurance functions of the family and the social surround. But the behaviors that lead to its emergence are not pathological per se; they represent only the tip of a larger iceberg: the family and social system. We do not minimize the presence of actual psychopathology in the adolescent, but we do emphasize the role of the environment in the onset and maintenance of symptomatic manifestations. For therapists, offering appropriate responses to the suffering of adolescents and their families is a crucial step. The clinician’s intervention must not only reduce anxiety and contain out-of-­control emotional reactions but also open a space for reflection and processing that the developmental crisis requires. The phase following a problematic enactment, for example, can paradoxically be a foundational time to reestablish less pathological communication and ask questions about the individual and relational dynamics that led to the difficult situation. An adequate response to moments of crisis requires working with those in a relationship with the adolescent. Family involvement is vital to a good prognosis (Tompson, Borger, & Asarnow, 2012). Failure to rely on a support network irremediably compromises a healthy adolescent growth process. SA1 Neurodevelopmental Disorders In this section, we discuss adolescents’ common subjective experiences of the symptom patterns of three disorders that usually emerge in infancy and/or childhood: autism

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 527
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 527 spectrum disorder, attention-­deficit/hyperactivity disorder, and specific learning disorders. In DSM-5, all these are classified as neurodevelopmental disorders—­conditions with early onset, reflecting developmental deficits that create impairments of personal, soci...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 527 spectrum disorder, attention-­deficit/hyperactivity disorder, and specific learning disorders. In DSM-5, all these are classified as neurodevelopmental disorders—­conditions with early onset, reflecting developmental deficits that create impairments of personal, social, academic, or occupational functioning. Developmental deficits range from specific limitations of learning or control of executive functions to global impairments in social skills or intelligence. Neurodevelopmental disorders often co-occur and have enduring patterns, with serious consequences that go well beyond childhood. We describe these disorders in depth because of their high prevalence, and because they involve specific challenges in adolescence. Neurodevelopmental disorders are covered in Chapter 5, pages 256–380, on the SC Axis. We refer readers to this section for a more complete description of each. SA11 Autism Spectrum Disorder Autism spectrum disorder is a complex neurodevelopmental condition characterized by qualitative impairment in social interaction and in verbal and nonverbal communication, and restricted interests, repetitive stereotyped behaviors, and atypical responses to sensory experiences. These symptoms are present from early childhood and cause severe limitations in several key areas. Manifestations vary greatly depending on the severity of cardinal symptoms, language, and intellectual ability, age and pattern at onset, and concurrent genetic/medical or environmental/acquired conditions. DSM-5 explicitly recognizes the “spectrum” nature of autism, subsuming under that rubric the subgroups of DSM-IV’s “pervasive developmental disorder”: Asperger’s disorder, Rett’s disorder, pervasive developmental disorder not otherwise specified, and childhood disintegrative disorder. DSM-5 has thus highlighted the dimensional nature of the cardinal behavioral domains of autistic psychologies and emphasized their heterogeneity. The introduction of the notion of diagnostic specifiers speaks for the value of an individualized assessment of needs in planning therapeutic interventions. The developmental trajectory for individuals with autism spectrum disorder is neither uniform nor linear. These individuals have varying patterns of improvement in adolescence and early adulthood, despite continuing to show behavioral characteristics of autism. Adolescents with this disorder show improvements in communication and a decrease in stereotyped movements and behaviors. However, impairments in social communication tend to persist. Developmental trajectories depend on the severity of the disorder, language and intellectual levels, comorbid disorders, and the history of treatment interventions. Subjective Experience of Autism Spectrum Disorder in Adolescents Children diagnosable as on the autistic spectrum are confronted with difficult challenges as they enter adolescence. Separation from the family, construction of a personal identity, opening to social relationships, access to sexuality, and vocational orientation are particularly taxing to them. Because of their difficulties in identifying and expressing their needs in a sufficiently comprehensible way, adolescents with autism spectrum disorder continually receive invalidating responses from the environment, which increases their experience of being misunderstood and may amplify their problematic behaviors or self-­protective withdrawal.

chunks.heading: 528 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 528 Adolescence Affective States Adolescents on the autistic spectrum may experience a range of anxiety symptoms, including physiological arousal, separation anxiety, panic, and social anxiety. Anxiety is thought to increase during adolescence as these teens, especially those without intellectual di...
chunks.text: 528 Adolescence Affective States Adolescents on the autistic spectrum may experience a range of anxiety symptoms, including physiological arousal, separation anxiety, panic, and social anxiety. Anxiety is thought to increase during adolescence as these teens, especially those without intellectual disabilities, become more aware of their differences from peers. Because adolescents with autistic patterns may not be able to recognize and describe how they feel, anxiety can be difficult to diagnose. Parents and care providers need to recognize diverse signs of anxiety, including changes in behaviors such as sleep patterns and eating habits, worry or rumination, withdrawal from normal daily activities, and increases in repetitive or destructive behaviors. Once an anxiety response is triggered, individuals with autism spectrum disorder can have difficulty controlling it. Anxiety can be debilitating, affecting individuals across environmental contexts. Relationships between social skills deficits and social anxiety can be reciprocal: Adolescents with poor social skills are likely to experience negative peer interactions, which may then lead to fear and distress about subsequent social interactions, resulting in avoidance and withdrawal. This behavior then limits opportunities to acquire social skills through exposure to social situations, thereby leading to social skills deficits. Depression is also common among adolescents diagnosed with autism spectrum disorder. Studies of parent reports show rates of depressed mood as high as 50% (Schall & McDonough, 2010). Emerging research indicates an increased risk for suicidal thoughts and tendencies among teens on the spectrum. As with anxiety, recognizing depression in autistic adolescents can be challenging, as some characteristics of autism (e.g., decreased interactions with peers and reduced emotional expression) resemble signs of depression. Depression may appear as increased moodiness (including anger, irritability, and sadness), reduction of interest in prior favorite activities, and changes in eating and sleep habits. Anxiety and depressive symptoms have been correlated with the bullying behaviors or ostracism frequently experienced by adolescents with autism spectrum disorder in school and social contexts, despite their efforts to adapt to social demands (Livingston, Shah, & Happé, 2019). This is particularly true of adolescent girls with this disorder, who tend to overinvest in social relationships by using costly and ineffective camouflaging behaviors. These may delay others from recognizing their difficulties and providing support (Cook, Hull, Crane, & Mandy, 2021). Cognitive States Adolescents with autism spectrum disorder, even those whose functioning is relatively good, have deficits in social cognition. They frequently fail to understand the subtleties of interpersonal relationships and are unable to take the point of view of others. They may struggle to understand irony, sarcasm, implicit messages, and other nonliteral forms of language. They may be unable to make inferences about information that is not directly presented and to use nonverbal means to understand or convey a message. These cognitive problems may hamper the development of social relations and increase their tendency to restrict their interests. Somatic States These adolescents may show different somatic signs of anxiety, including trembling hands, breathing difficulties, or stomachaches, especially when confronted with social

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 533
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 533 lessons. At home, he had problems with homework organization and time management. He was feeling exhausted and thinking of giving up on school. SA13 Specific Learning Disorders Specific learning disorders are also discussed in Chapter 5 (see pp. 256–380). In DSM-5, t...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 533 lessons. At home, he had problems with homework organization and time management. He was feeling exhausted and thinking of giving up on school. SA13 Specific Learning Disorders Specific learning disorders are also discussed in Chapter 5 (see pp. 256–380). In DSM-5, this diagnostic category is defined by certain deficits in the basic cognitive mechanisms related to understanding mathematics or using written and/or spoken language. As a result of these deficits, individuals have persistent and significant difficulty learning foundational academic skills, including listening, written expression, basic reading skills, mathematical calculation, and mathematical reasoning. Performance of the affected academic skills is well below average, or acceptable performance levels are achieved only with extraordinary effort. The learning difficulties are considered “specific” because they are not attributable to intellectual disabilities, to sensory or neurological problems, to psychosocial adversity, or to inadequate educational instruction. Specific learning disorders are believed to arise from the interaction of genetic, epigenetic, and environmental factors that affect the brain’s ability to perceive or process information efficiently and accurately. The current literature provides no conclusive evidence on the biological markers associated with these conditions. Neurocognitive, neuroimaging, or genetic testing is not useful for the diagnosis, which is instead based on performance tests. The learning difficulties are usually readily apparent in the early school years. In some children, however, they may not be fully emerge until the later school years, by which time learning demands have increased and exceed the person’s limited capacities. Specific learning disorders can produce lifelong impairments in activities that require the skills that are problematic and can interfere with later occupational performance. Adolescents with these deficits may perform daily activities in unconventional ways; they may not fully conform with ordinary classroom environments. They frequently encounter difficulties during their school years and report lower self-­esteem, less emotional support, and more problems with academic and emotional adjustment than peers without these disorders. They may also feel profound social isolation. Longitudinal studies have shown that emotional and behavioral disorders appear 2–3 years after the emergence of a specific learning disorder, suggesting a possible causal link between learning disorders and emotional problems (Ingesson, 2007). This link is probably mediated by the quality of the self-image that an adolescent develops over time. Subjective Experience of Specific Learning Disorders in Adolescents Affective States and Cognitive Patterns According to Harter’s (1999) model of self-­development, an essential feature of the learning process is the feeling of mastery. The cognitive jubilation that an individual feels when achieving success, and the narcissistic rewards that go with such experiences, are fundamental to the motivation to learn. The perception of one’s own competence deeply affects one’s interest in an activity and one’s efforts to attain competence in it. Because of repetitive failures, adolescents with specific learning disorders develop a negative evaluation of their competence. Avoidance of learning activities, for

chunks.heading: 534 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 534 Adolescence fear of failure and criticism, limits training opportunities and reduces the potential for improving one’s competence. This may have a strong impact on self-­development, beyond the specific domain of competence. Adolescents with specific learning disorders have inefficiencies in the...
chunks.text: 534 Adolescence fear of failure and criticism, limits training opportunities and reduces the potential for improving one’s competence. This may have a strong impact on self-­development, beyond the specific domain of competence. Adolescents with specific learning disorders have inefficiencies in their learning procedures that oblige them to exert constant vigilance, for which they pay a high price in fatigue. When there are additional attentional difficulties, performance further deteriorates, leading to a negative perception of the learning activity and potential refusal of all knowledge. But different adolescents react differently to their condition. There are two basic response groups: those who comply with the unwritten school “contract,” and those who reject it. In the first case, suffering is intense. These teens maintain the goal of academic success and value knowledge, but they feel keenly the impact of their disability. The feeling of responsibility for their failures dominates their inner lives. An internal logic captures those who identify themselves with failure and, as in the model of learned helplessness, they anticipate future failures. The devaluation and demoralization often result in anxiety and depressive symptoms, a sense of paralysis, or a general inability to engage in learning activities. These responses may take the form of inhibition or passivity. For adolescents who refuse the school contract, the suffering is less evident and is experienced more by the environment. These individuals can adopt passive oppositional strategies, among which slowness is one of the most glaring. They can sometimes adopt a more provocative stance as a reaction to feelings of incomprehension and rejection—­an attitude that may be amplified by conflicts around school activities. In other cases, the oppositional/provocative attitudes are more severe, with an outright refusal of the educational system and the emergence of a conduct disorder. In the first type of response, the emotional and behavioral disorders are linked to progressive school failure and the low self-­esteem associated with the learning disorders. In the second, disruptive behaviors, which frequently follow early problems with attention and impulsivity, interfere with the subsequent acquisition of learning skills (Trzesniewski, Moffitt, Caspi, Taylor, & Maughan, 2006). There are some key periods for the development of a child’s “learning identity” (i.e., self-­perception as an agent of the process of learning academic skills). Self-­esteem is especially fragile in the first years of school in children with specific learning disorders, especially before the diagnosis, when feelings of inadequacy and helplessness are paramount. Self-­esteem shows a general trend of improvement in adolescence, probably related to better understanding and acceptance of the disorder, adoption of more functional strategies (e.g., proactive attitudes and compartmentalization of the disabilities), and more adaptive school and professional choices (Ingesson, 2007). Adolescents in an informed, supportive environment (at school or at home) show a more positive self-image (Al-Yagon, 2007; Humphrey, 2002). Several factors have been empirically associated with better outcomes: self-­awareness/understanding of disorders, self-­advocacy/proactive attitudes, and presence and use of support systems (peer support and mentorship) (Raskind, Goldberg, Higgins, & Herman, 1999). Somatic States Adolescents with specific learning disorders have high levels of anxiety, expressed in performance anxiety, school refusal, or somatic complaints (e.g., chronic fatigue and sleep difficulties).

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 535
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 535 Relational Patterns These teens frequently struggle with dependency issues. As children, they may have had parents who were very involved in supporting them to achieve. In striving toward autonomy, they can become intolerant of parents’ and teachers’ help, although t...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 535 Relational Patterns These teens frequently struggle with dependency issues. As children, they may have had parents who were very involved in supporting them to achieve. In striving toward autonomy, they can become intolerant of parents’ and teachers’ help, although they may continue to need supervision. They may seek support instead among peers. The choice of peers, along with the positive or negative role they eventually play, depends on an adolescent’s self-­confidence and personal resources. In some cases, an adolescent with a learning disorder may develop a strong intimate relationship with a partner that mimics past experiences with parents. Subjective Experience of the Therapist In treating adolescent patients with specific learning disorders, clinicians may experience feelings ranging from guilt or compassion for their fragile condition, to irritation, helplessness, and anger associated with an overly passive and dependent attitude. Or they may find themselves wanting to minimize the disability via defensive denial, avoidance, or withdrawal. Clinical Illustration Drew, a 15-year-old boy, was admitted after a suicide attempt following an argument with his mother. Conflicts between Drew and his parents about school had been increasing. He had a highly symbiotic relationship with a girlfriend, about which he was very defensive. Drew had a history of severe developmental dyslexia. He devalued himself and the school, and he had growing difficulties paying attention. He could easily talk about sadness, anger, and exhaustion connected with his learning disorder. He smoked cannabis to relax from school pressure. SA2 Predominantly Psychotic Disorders Psychotic disorders are also discussed in Chapter 14 on the S Axis (see pp. 756–870). Psychosis is defined as a severe disruption of thought and behavior, resulting in the loss of reality testing. The diagnosis of psychosis is based on overt changes in a person’s behavior and functioning, with formal and/or content thought disorders and perceptual-­like experiences. Adolescents suffering from psychosis create challenging diagnostic and treatment dilemmas for clinicians. Psychotic-­like experiences are relatively frequent phenomena in teens. They lie on a continuum from developmentally appropriate, nonpathological phenomena, to indicators of a vulnerability to develop a psychotic disorder in the near future (i.e., risk factor), to psychotic spectrum disorders. Appropriate evaluation and diagnosis are crucial to creating an effective, risk-­minimizing, and recovery-­oriented treatment plan. Despite the lack of precise epidemiological data, it is generally agreed that the prevalence of psychotic disorders increases in adolescence. The onset of schizophrenia spectrum disorders occurs then and in young adulthood. Only 4% of adults diagnosed with schizophrenia report onset before age 14, with half reporting onset before 25 and a peak age of onset of 20.5 (Solmi et al., 2022). Once the full disorder is present, the

chunks.heading: 536 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 536 Adolescence clinical picture of adolescent psychoses is similar to what is observed in adults. Psychotic disorders severely disrupt an adolescent’s ability to function normally in daily activities and are often quite debilitating and disabling. Two revolutionary findings have recently influenced...
chunks.text: 536 Adolescence clinical picture of adolescent psychoses is similar to what is observed in adults. Psychotic disorders severely disrupt an adolescent’s ability to function normally in daily activities and are often quite debilitating and disabling. Two revolutionary findings have recently influenced the clinical management of psychotic disorders, however: (1) Longer durations of untreated psychotic symptoms are associated with worse treatment responses and outcomes; and (2) a significant portion patients with first-­episode psychosis can achieve symptom remission and recovery if they receive effective treatment promptly. These discoveries have led to clinical strategies aimed at reducing the time between patients’ onset of symptoms and their diagnosis and treatment, and at promoting their engagement in treatment to foster recovery and prevent relapses. There is increasing evidence that early recognition and treatment of psychotic disorders can lead to improvements in recovery and overall functioning (Lieberman, Small, & Girgis, 2019). First-­episode psychosis studies have prompted researchers to extend early intervention to people with an increased risk of developing a psychotic disorder in the near future. Therefore, over the last several decades, there has been a drive to identify psychotic disorders at their earliest stages or even before their onset. By helping adolescents who may be at increased risk for psychosis, we may be able to prevent the condition from occurring or at least to ameliorate its severity and/or impact. The clinical pictures of the different psychotic disorders are well described in the S Axis (see Chapter 14). We focus here on early detection and intervention, addressing two main questions: the clinical outcomes of brief psychotic disorder (i.e., first-­episode psychosis) and the prodromal phase of adolescent-­onset schizophrenia. We note that most studies in this field have focused on “youth mental health,” using an age range somewhat more encompassing than adolescence (~12–25 years). When possible, we provide information specific to adolescents, referring readers to Chapter 5 for psychotic disorders in childhood (see SC Axis). SA21 Brief Psychotic Disorder As in adults, the essential feature of a brief psychotic disorder in adolescence is the onset of at least one of four core symptoms of schizophrenia (negative symptoms are not included): delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, and grossly disorganized or catatonic behavior (American Psychiatric Association, 2022). In adolescents, these symptoms are frequently associated with phenomena of depersonalization and/or derealization, extreme mood lability, and a dream state of consciousness. The consistent presence of somatic signs (insomnia, motor agitation) makes this disorder a true emergency situation requiring a protective setting. The clinical picture has an acute, rich, polymorphous presentation and a relatively brief and self-­limited clinical course. The change from a nonpsychotic to a clearly psychotic state may occur within a short time period (less than 2 weeks, but sometimes within hours). Psychosocial stressors often precipitate the episode. Brief psychotic disorder may occur more frequently in adolescents and young adults with a fragile personality organization, such as those that Chapters 8 and 12 considered as at the psychotic level on the PA and P Axes, respectively. Precipitants may include positive states, such as falling in love or being touched by an aesthetic experience (as has been described for the “Stendhal syndrome,” recalling the psychotic-­like experience of the romantic French writer during his initial journey in Italy). Adolescents with heightened sensibilities and fragile personalities characterized by strong

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 537
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 537 dependent features can experience the loss of ego boundaries when faced with intense emotions. The symptoms are transient by definition. An episode usually lasts less than 1 month, with a full return to the premorbid level of functioning. When the symptoms persist be...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 537 dependent features can experience the loss of ego boundaries when faced with intense emotions. The symptoms are transient by definition. An episode usually lasts less than 1 month, with a full return to the premorbid level of functioning. When the symptoms persist beyond 1 month, a different diagnosis should be given. Brief psychotic disorder remains a divisive and shifting nosographic concept even in the current classification of mental disorders (see the S Axis, Chapter 14, pp. 756– 870). Some scholars argue that when it occurs in adolescence, it should not even be considered a disease entity. The paradigm of clinical high-risk states of psychosis (see the later section “At-Risk Mental States for Psychosis”) has operationalized the brief psychotic episode not as a frank psychotic disorder but as a state of increased risk for developing persistent psychotic conditions. “Brief limited intermittent psychotic symptoms” (BLIPS) (Yung et al., 2005) are defined as lasting less than 7 days, whereas “brief intermittent psychotic symptoms” (BIPS) exclude individuals with seriously disorganizing and dangerous features (Miller et al., 2003) and last up to 3 months. Both BLIPS and BIPS are currently used as criteria for identifying young people at risk of developing a more severe psychotic disorder in the near future (Fusar-Poli et al., 2020). This approach is grounded in epidemiological data on the prognosis of brief psychotic disorders. Meta-­analytic evidence has shown that the risk of recurrence for a brief psychotic episode increases from 15% at 6 months, to 33% at ≥36 months follow­up, with no consistent factor predicting clinical outcome. The prospective diagnostic stability (average follow-­up 4 years) was 49%; the proportion of diagnostic change to schizophrenia spectrum psychoses was 19%; to affective spectrum psychoses, 5%; to other psychotic disorders, 7%; and to other (nonpsychotic) mental disorders, 14% (Provenzani, de Pablo, Arribas, Pillmann, & Fusar-Poli, 2021). Yet other evidence about first-­episode psychosis (with slightly different operationalization) showed that half the patients were in symptomatic remission around 4 years after the first-­episode psychosis, whereas about one-third showed recovery after 5.5 years (Catalan et al., 2021a). Beyond possible psychiatric outcomes, the major consequences of a brief psychotic disorder in adolescence may include the secondary reorganization of the personality toward the avoidance of intimate relationships of any kind, the development of a constrained personality, and a diminished sense of self (notably, the latter may also occur even years before the first psychotic episode). A psychotic episode, even if it goes into full remission, as in the case of brief psychotic disorder, can itself be a highly traumatic experience. Psychotherapy treatments should be aimed at supporting young patients in a process of integrating the experience of the psychotic episode into a coherent and continuous autobiographical perspective over time. Clinical Illustration Paul is a 15-year-old boy with no known psychiatric history. His parents, who were alarmed about his recent behavior, brought him to the emergency service. For a few days, he had mentioned odd ideas; for example, he thought a stranger who offered him some food two days earlier had drugged him. These ideas appeared suddenly after a short period of excitement and endless existential questioning. In the weeks before intake, he had smoked a lot of hashish with his band of “marginal” friends. Shortly before this episode, Paul had had several moments of anxiety accompanied by concerns about his sexual orientation (fear of being homosexual). He reported moderate insomnia and had lost 11 pounds in a month.

chunks.heading: 540 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 540 Adolescence before the psychosis onset (Shah et al., 2017). The prodromal syndrome usually becomes apparent in adolescence or young adulthood; it is characterized by subclinical manifestations of positive and negative symptoms, as well as by nonspecific symptoms associated with a decline in inte...
chunks.text: 540 Adolescence before the psychosis onset (Shah et al., 2017). The prodromal syndrome usually becomes apparent in adolescence or young adulthood; it is characterized by subclinical manifestations of positive and negative symptoms, as well as by nonspecific symptoms associated with a decline in interpersonal and academic functioning. Nonspecific symptoms in adolescents in the prodromal phase may include odd beliefs (unusual/ extraordinarily intense new ideas, strange new feelings); thought preservation (i.e., obsessive-­like irrelevant thoughts or mental images); thought pressure (i.e., a sense of “chaos” associated with unrelated thoughts); mistrust of others; decreased ability to discriminate between ideas and perceptions, or between fantasy and true memories; and unusual perceptual experiences (increased sensitivity to sounds or sights, mistaking noises for voices) (Schultze-­Lutter, Addington, Ruhrmann, & Kosterkötter, 2007). See below for details on different sets of criteria (and related symptoms) that inform early detection strategies provided in specialized clinics. Depersonalization and derealization are frequently reported during or prior to these periods and are associated with a flickering sense of reality. Progressive changes in the subjective experiences of the self, the world, and relationships are frequently associated with a negative emotionality (e.g., dysphoric mood and inappropriate affects) and with behavioral changes such as odd behaviors, social withdrawal, a dramatic drop in academic performance, or a marked decline in personal hygiene or selfcare. The idea that the prodromal phase offers a critical opportunity for the prevention of schizophrenia was first introduced by Harry Stack Sullivan a century ago (1927); over the past 30 years, it has been rediscovered and has become the subject of an intensive research program (for a review, see Fusar-Poli et al., 2020). Whether certain constellations of experiences represent the prodromal phase of a severe psychotic disorder is difficult to determine prospectively. The progression from prodromes to disease is not linear. Because in some cases, the “prodromal” phase decreases in intensity and does not lead to overt psychosis, the prodrome has been considered as a syndrome that confers an increased vulnerability to psychosis but does not inevitably predict it. Thus, the prodrome is a risk factor for psychosis and not a precursor. In fact, because the term implies that psychosis is inevitable, its use could be considered misleading; we note that the concept of the prodromal syndrome is a retrospective one. For these reasons, the term “at-risk mental state” has been introduced, and researchers have developed sets of operationalized criteria, including the “ultra-high risk” (Yung et al., 2005) and the “basic symptoms” (Schultze-­Lutter et al., 2007; see later discussion). The term “clinical high risk for psychosis” (Fusar-Poli et al., 2020) is also used to refer to both this preventive approach and to individuals at risk of psychosis. Such terms and criteria, despite variation across research centers, are prospective concepts introduced to identify youth for whom intervention at an early stage, before the onset of frank psychosis, may be justified to postpone, ameliorate, or prevent a psychotic disorder, and to address comorbidities (e.g., depressive, anxiety, and personality disorders, which are highly prevalent in this population; Lo Buglio et al., 2024; Solmi et al., 2023). Notably, criteria for states at risk for psychosis were not developed to be applied to the general population. Psychotic-­like experiences (i.e., mild hallucinations and/or delusions) are common (7.5% among adolescents; Kelleher et al., 2012), and in most cases, they remit spontaneously. Assessing an at-risk state by considering only symptoms would thus result in a high false-­positive rate and poor sensitivity. To maximize the possibility of accurately identifying those truly at increased risk of developing a full-blown psychotic disorder, attention to age (adolescence to young adulthood, when

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 541
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 541 the incidence of psychotic disorder is highest; Solmi et al., 2022), and clinical need for care (the young person must be seeking help, or be identified by someone, such as a parent or teacher, as needing help) are considered mandatory for satisfying at-risk for psyc...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 541 the incidence of psychotic disorder is highest; Solmi et al., 2022), and clinical need for care (the young person must be seeking help, or be identified by someone, such as a parent or teacher, as needing help) are considered mandatory for satisfying at-risk for psychosis criteria. According to the ultra-high-risk inclusion criteria, young patients are considered at risk for psychosis if they have one or more of the following characteristics: 1. Attenuated psychotic symptoms: patients who have had subthreshold positive psychotic symptoms (e.g., bizarre ideas, perceptual disturbances) during the past year. 2. Brief (limited) intermittent psychotic symptoms: patients who have had frank psychotic symptoms that have spontaneously remitted in less than a week without antipsychotic treatment. 3. Vulnerability group: patients who have been diagnosed with a schizotypal personality disorder or who have a first-­degree relative with a psychotic disorder and whose functioning has significantly decreased during the previous year. The main measures based on these criteria are the Comprehensive Assessment of At-Risk Mental State (CAARMS; Yung et al., 2005) and the Structured Interview for Psychosis-­R isk Syndromes (SIPS; Miller et al., 2003). These tools have some similarities but also some differences. Another approach to identifying psychosis risk is the basic symptoms approach emerging from European psychopathological research. Basic symptoms are subtle, subclinical disturbances in several domains (e.g., perception, language, stress tolerance, affect, and thinking). Individuals who experience them immediately identify them as disturbances in their mental processes (Schultze-­Lutter et al., 2007). Basic symptoms may identify people at an earlier stage of risk compared with the prodromal phase targeted by the ultra-high-risk criteria. Measures to assess them include the Schizophrenia Proneness Instrument, Adult version (SPI-A; Schultze-­Lutter et al., 2007) and Child and Youth version (SPI-CY; Schultze-­Lutter, Marshall, & Koch, 2012). The ultra-high-risk criteria have strong predictive validity for psychotic disorders when applied to a help-­seeking population: The transition rates to a first-­episode psychosis for at-risk adolescents range from 10.4% at 6 months, to 20% at 12 months, 23% at 24 months, and 23.3% at ≥36 months (Catalan et al., 2021b). These magnitudes are several thousand times greater than the expected incidence rate in the general population (Nelson & McGorry, 2020). Numerous variables have been identified that predict those who transition to psychosis among youth who have already been identified as being at risk (Coutts, Koutsouleris, & McGuire, 2023). Positive and negative symptoms, thought disorder, poor baseline social functioning, decline in social functioning, and longer duration of symptoms before clinic entry have been found to be the most predictive clinical variables. Of environmental risk factors, a history of childhood trauma and cannabis abuse has been noted most consistently. Finally, cognitive deficits in executive function and verbal memory and several neuroimaging factors have been associated with conversion to psychosis. We urge caution when interpreting these data given contradictory findings and the limited range of variables assessed to date. A suspected at-risk mental state should be assessed by a trained mental health specialist (NICE, 2013). Needs-based and psychotherapy treatments with or without family intervention are the main treatment options. Early intervention strategies should aim at preventing not only the onset of a psychotic disorder but also the development

chunks.heading: 542 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 542 Adolescence or persistence of social, educational, or vocational problems. There is a general consensus that psychotherapy represents the first treatment option, as it presents the best benefit–­risk ratio. If psychological treatments are ineffective and there are severe and progressive attenuat...
chunks.text: 542 Adolescence or persistence of social, educational, or vocational problems. There is a general consensus that psychotherapy represents the first treatment option, as it presents the best benefit–­risk ratio. If psychological treatments are ineffective and there are severe and progressive attenuated psychotic symptoms, or if the attenuated psychotic symptoms are associated with the risk to self or others, then a low-dose second-­generation antipsychotic could be considered. Overall, pharmacological interventions should not be seen as the first order of business, except to treat any (and frequent) comorbid disorders. After treatment, if symptoms, poor functioning, or distress continue, regular monitoring for up to 3 years using structured and validated assessment tools is needed. Efforts to avoid dropout and to foster a therapeutic alliance and treatment adherence are crucial. Communication of the diagnosis should be provided optimistically (most at-risk youth will not later develop a psychotic disorder) and weighed against the negative impact it may have on the expectations and acceptance of others in the adolescent’s environment. Subjective Experience of Psychotic Disorders in Adolescents Affective States Common subjective experiences of adolescents with psychotic illness may include a pervasive sense of inner void, perplexity, anonymity, feelings of nonexistence or lack of identity, feeling significantly different from other people, and not being the subject of their own perceptions, thoughts, actions, and emotions (Henriksen, Raballo, & Nordgaard, 2021). These persisting experiences (i.e., “self-­disorders” or “anomalous self-­ experiences”), which emerge in both childhood and adolescence (often years before the first psychotic episode), suggest a disturbance at the basic level of selfhood and have been viewed as the pathogenetic core of psychotic spectrum disorders (Henriksen et al., 2021; Parnas et al., 2005). The prodromal phase is often characterized by perplexity, alienation, and a sense that something salient is going to occur. The first psychotic episode may bring some feelings of momentary relief, conferred by delusional explanations of phenomena unfolding in an altered world, as well as a sense of dissolution and devitalization (Fusar-Poli et al., 2022). Intense anxiety or even terror, with psychomotor agitation, may accompany hallucinations, delusions, and the sense of being controlled by external forces. Classical clinical concepts such as “nameless dread” (Bion, 1967) and “ontological insecurity” (Laing, 1965) capture a level of anxiety that is qualitatively different from that found in other mental disorders. Depersonalization and derealization phenomena may be particularly disturbing. Depression is common in adolescents with schizophrenia at every phase of the illness. It may take the form of blunted or inappropriate affect or of an inability to feel pleasure (anhedonia). Depressive symptoms may also appear once positive symptoms are reduced by medications; they are frequently associated with progressive insight into the illness. These periods, however, put an adolescent at great risk for self-harm and suicide attempts. Cognitive Patterns The thought processes of adolescents may be impaired by hallucinations and delusions. Hearing voices, for example, can interfere with the ability to pay attention to

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 543
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 543 external stimuli, memorize and process information, prioritize tasks, make decisions, and plan future actions. Adolescents may subject their own thoughts, behaviors, feelings, and perceptions to intense scrutiny, and may excessively monitor their inner life during in...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 543 external stimuli, memorize and process information, prioritize tasks, make decisions, and plan future actions. Adolescents may subject their own thoughts, behaviors, feelings, and perceptions to intense scrutiny, and may excessively monitor their inner life during interactions with the environment (i.e., “hyperreflexivity”; Parnas et al., 2005). They may find it hard to suppress recurrent or persistent thoughts; their inner speech or thoughts may obtain acoustic qualities and experience their mental processes spatially (Parnas et al., 2005). Both prodromal and first-­episode psychosis stages may involve the feeling that events are directed toward the self or even revolve around the self. In the first episode, the confusion associated with thought disorganization can be overwhelming (Fusar-Poli et al., 2022). Adolescents experiencing first-­episode psychosis may have trouble organizing their thoughts, as they doubt their perceptions and feelings, and may have difficulty thought content. They may suddenly perceive the environment as incomprehensible. Some youth feel intense anxiety or shame about these uncontrollable changes; they may not want to report the symptoms to parents or professionals for fear of being seen as “crazy.” Somatic States Adolescents diagnosed with schizophrenia may report unusual body experiences that may be related to synesthesia or to somatic delusions (loss of control of the body, infestations by insects or worms, emitting a foul odor, and conviction of bodily defects). Because these can be terrifying, they may engender ritualistic behaviors (endless verifications, gazing into the mirror) and self-­injuring acts. Catatonic phenomena (body rigidity and unresponsiveness to outside stimuli) may also be observed in adolescents. They may feel numbness, pressure inside or on the surface of the body, pain in specific areas, migrating bodily sensations, a sense of being electrified, and vestibular sensations. When looking in a mirror, they may perceive their face as changing (e.g., their face may appear deformed), or they may search for such changes obsessively (Parnas et al., 2005). Before the prodromal stage, adolescents may report a sense of disembodiment (e.g., lack of ownership of their own body). Then, as the first episode occurs, they may feel that external forces are violating their body boundaries and that parts of their body are located outside their own ego boundaries (Fusar-Poli et al., 2022). Changes in sleeping and eating are common in adolescents with schizophrenia and may be amplified by medication. Although antipsychotic drugs can have a positive effect on psychotic symptoms, they also have several negative effects. Adolescents often complain about emotional numbing that reduces their capacity to interact with others. Weight gain is a common side effect; the resulting transformation of their self-image may pose major problems for adolescents and is a common reason for their discontinuing treatment. Relational Patterns Qualitative studies exploring the social adjustment of adolescents diagnosed with schizophrenia have shown that they have great difficulties with interpersonal relationships and social adaptation. There are several possible reasons for social withdrawal and reduced peer engagement in adolescents suffering psychosis: their blunted emotional expression and lack of interest in the outside world, their odd and suspicious

chunks.heading: 544 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 544 Adolescence behaviors, and their difficulties in self-care. As in adults, specific deficits in mentalizing and integrating contextual information may impair these adolescents’ understanding of the social world. Such difficulties may directly contribute to the development and perpetuation of unus...
chunks.text: 544 Adolescence behaviors, and their difficulties in self-care. As in adults, specific deficits in mentalizing and integrating contextual information may impair these adolescents’ understanding of the social world. Such difficulties may directly contribute to the development and perpetuation of unusual psychological experiences and may increase their reluctance to communicate them to others. In addition, fear of stigma may lead to their concealing their difficulties and delaying their seeking help. For adolescents with psychotic functioning, improvements in social understanding and communication (including the expression of their unusual experiences) may be key to improving their emotional well-being and fostering their recovery. The individual may feel profoundly different from others, have a sense of being somebody else (e.g., one’s own mother) and experience intense feelings of anxiety when touched by other people or even when physically close to others (Parnas et al., 2005). Clinicians influenced by interpersonal, object relations, and self-­psychological theories have referred to boundary confusion between inside and outside experience, between the self and others. The prodromal stage may include difficulties in disclosing to others one’s own unusual experiences, a general inability to communicate with others, feelings of being excluded, and difficulties understanding social norms. The outbreak of psychosis may be accompanied by a withdrawal from both family and friends, isolation, loss of trust toward others (often associated with persecutory delusions), and a sense of helplessness (Fusar-Poli et al., 2022). Subjective Experience of the Therapist A clinician working with an adolescent with a psychotic disorder may feel many different experiences, ranging from alienation to subjective omnipotence (Garrett, 2019). Contrary to many unfounded misconceptions about clinical work with individuals with psychotic disorders, many clinicians report positive outcomes when treating patients with psychosis. Youth with psychotic disorders are often both dependent and idealizing. When they express appreciation for the therapist’s effort to reframe and normalize their concerns, the therapist may feel benevolent and strong, even omnipotent. Countertransferences may resemble the feelings of caregivers with young children: The wonder of attachment coexists with dread about responsibility for their urgent needs. On the other pole of the empowerment continuum, many therapists (especially beginners working with patients with acute symptoms) may fail to grasp the patient’s communications and conclude that they are unable to establish a therapeutic alliance. They may feel alone or identified with the patient’s sense of hopelessness. They may feel unable to offer psychological help—the clinician may even suffer a sense of alienation that resembles that of the patient. These reactions can be shifting and confusing. In therapy with adolescents at risk for psychosis, establishing a therapeutic alliance may be challenging, as they may have difficulty trusting others. To avoid miscommunication—­a potential risk in therapy with these patients (Ridenour, Knauss, & Neal, 2021)—clinicians should adopt a “not-­knowing stance,” leaving the events of sessions open to discussion. Concerns that the patient’s condition is worsening may lead to worry, omnipotent fantasies (“I will save you!”), and paternalistic attitudes. Taking an open and honest attitude, communicating the diagnosis matter-­of-­factly, and involving the patient in the clinical decision-­making process can mitigate these risks. For clinicians in private settings, keeping contact with specialized clinics for

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 545
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 545 early intervention in psychosis, and referring patients to them when necessary, may also alleviate such troubling emotional states, in addition to being of benefit to the patients themselves. SA3 Mood Disorders In this section, we describe the most common mood disord...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 545 early intervention in psychosis, and referring patients to them when necessary, may also alleviate such troubling emotional states, in addition to being of benefit to the patients themselves. SA3 Mood Disorders In this section, we describe the most common mood disorders in adolescence, emphasizing specific developmental features of youth who suffer them. Unlike DSM-5 and DSM-5-TR (American Psychiatric Association, 2013, 2022), but in accordance with the ICD-11 (WHO, 2019), we include in this diagnostic grouping the symptoms of depressive and bipolar disorders. This section also encompasses suicidality and nonsuicidal self-­injury, two conditions recently introduced within the DSM-5-TR chapter “Other Conditions That May Be a Focus of Clinical Attention” (American Psychiatric Association, 2022). Although suicidal behavior and nonsuicidal self-­injury are considered transnosographic behaviors, we discuss them here because they are frequently associated with mood disorders, especially depressive conditions. However, clinicians should explore these patterns carefully in the context of any clinical syndrome. SA32 Depressive Disorders Depression is among the leading causes of illness and disability in individuals ages 15–19, and is a major risk factor for suicide. Paying attention to the burden of depressive disorders in adolescence is crucial, given its huge cost to societies and mental health services. Prevalence estimates currently available are alarming: 8% for major depressive disorder, 4% for persistent depressive disorder, and 34% for major depressive symptomatology (Shorey, Ng, & Wong, 2022). Moreover, from age 12 on, a gender gap emerges, with females having a higher risk of developing these disorders. The prevalence rate of depression among girls is approximately double that observed among boys (Beauchaine & Hinshaw, 2017). Depression tends to remain stable over time. Depressive syndromes in adolescence often have their psychopathological roots in childhood and represent a significant risk factor not only for later depressive disorders, but also for a broad range of comorbid psychopathologies (especially substance abuse, anxiety, and personality disorders) in adulthood. Depression and anxiety often co-occur, with shared roots and interconnected symptoms; they show tendencies toward heterotypic continuity (Speranza, Liotti, Spoletini, & Fortunato, 2023). Most adolescents with diagnosed depression have been exposed to stressors and adverse life events in childhood (see SC Axis, Chapter 5, pp. 256–380). During adolescence, variations in symptom severity and stability have been examined, revealing a trend for depressive symptoms to grow in both intensity and resistance to reduction (Morken, Viddal, Ranum & Wichstrøm, 2021). Making an accurate diagnosis of depressive disorders is more difficult in adolescence than in adulthood. Depressive pathologies in this developmental phase have been found to represent a broader, more heterogeneous diagnostic grouping. Alarmingly, many cases of adolescent depression go unrecognized, are not properly assessed, and fail to receive timely and effective intervention. Despite the clear need to understand depressive symptoms within a developmental perspective, DSM-5 and DSM5-TR have not introduced more sensitive diagnostic criteria for depressive disorders in children and adolescents. Currently, DSM includes only predominant irritability

chunks.heading: 546 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 546 Adolescence (instead of only depressed mood) in the diagnosis of major depressive disorder, and the temporal criterion of 1 year instead of 2 in persistent depressive disorders. Notably, although irritability may often precede the onset of a depressive disorder, some studies suggest that irritab...
chunks.text: 546 Adolescence (instead of only depressed mood) in the diagnosis of major depressive disorder, and the temporal criterion of 1 year instead of 2 in persistent depressive disorders. Notably, although irritability may often precede the onset of a depressive disorder, some studies suggest that irritable mood is not a core symptom of youth depression, nor does it accurately describe the subjective experience of depressed young people (Midgley et al., 2015, 2017). DSM diagnostic criteria for depressive disorders thus remain more applicable to adults than to adolescents. In adolescents, depressive manifestations are heterogeneous. Studies have identified social isolation, loneliness, hopelessness, helplessness, hostility toward self and others, pessimism, despair, and withdrawal from the world (e.g., Midgley et al., 2015), suggesting the need for clinically sensitive diagnoses in the youth population. Loneliness is strongly associated with sadness, feelings of worthlessness, and anhedonia in early adolescence (Grygiel, Dolata, Humenny, & Muszyński, 2023). Research supports contributions from interpersonal psychopathology (Lachal et al., 2012) and Blatt’s conceptualization (Blatt & Zuroff, 1992) of distinct experiences of depression, suggesting the need to formulate more clinically sensitive diagnoses with young people. Subjective Experience of Depressive Disorders in Adolescents Depressive dynamics are hard to diagnose (and treat) in adolescence, mainly because of difficulties in differentiating what belongs to depressive disorders from aspects of normal maturation. Blatt and his colleagues (e.g., Blatt & Homann, 1992) have argued that depression is more than a clinical disorder; it is an affective state that varies from normal to pathological—­from mild, transient, appropriate dysphoric reactions to painful life events to severe, disabling, persistent, and intense reactions with little justification in current life circumstances. Clinical observations and empirical evidence support a dimensional approach and emphasize the value of taking into account subthreshold depressive manifestations in youths that are associated with an increased risk of developing a frank depressive syndrome. Classical psychoanalytic theories of adult depression focused mainly on issues related to loss of a significant other and a sense of responsibility for having attacked and damaged an ambivalently loved or lost object. Individuals who experience what has been called “guilty depression” thus see themselves as mean, bad, potentially destructive. Their concerns about hurting and offending others contribute to fear and anxiety about losing their attachment relationships. Thus, depressive experiences connected with interpersonal events, especially abandonments and separations, also referred to as “borderline depression” or “abandonment despair” (Masterson, 1972), are characterized by feelings of loss, loneliness, and emptiness. Psychoanalytic scholars who focus on the development of the self have conceptualized a “narcissistic depression” characterized by a marked vulnerability, expressed as a fragile sense of self-­definition and self-­esteem. The main issues of these patients concern the domain of identity and are associated with feelings of failure, culpability, and lack of self-­confidence. Research has given support for the position that these two formulations may capture different subtypes of depression, each describing a group of depressed individuals with distinct presentations and fragilities (Blatt, 1998). According to Blatt and Zuroff (1992), these two depressive experiences correspond to different psychological configurations derived from discrete developmental lines, which are thought to be in

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 547
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 547 synergistic interaction: the anaclitic (or dependent) and the introjective (self-­critical) developmental processes. In adolescence, the anaclitic subtype of depression manifests itself in interpersonal issues related to dependency, as this period raises questions ab...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 547 synergistic interaction: the anaclitic (or dependent) and the introjective (self-­critical) developmental processes. In adolescence, the anaclitic subtype of depression manifests itself in interpersonal issues related to dependency, as this period raises questions about autonomy and the consolidation of personal independence. Adolescents with the introjective (self-­critical) subtype are absorbed with identity development and self-­efficacy. Research has established that these respective psychopathological pictures are associated with potentially distinct responses to various types of psychotherapy. Studies of adult patients have shown that individuals with introjective depression are more responsive to longer-­ term, intensive psychodynamic psychotherapy, while those with anaclitic depression benefit from brief supportive therapies or treatments informed by other theoretical orientations (Blatt, 1998). Interpersonal psychotherapy (adapted for adolescents) and cognitive-­behavioral therapy (CBT) have been the most recommended interventions for depression in adolescence (American Psychological Association, 2019; NICE, 2019). Short-term psychodynamic therapy (Cregeen et al., 2017; Goodyer et al., 2017), brief psychosocial interventions, and psychodynamic internet-­based therapy have shown effectiveness in this population. So far, among pharmacological interventions, only fluoxetine, in combination with psychological treatments, has been demonstrated to be effective for moderate–­severe or nonresponsive depression. Affective States The core symptoms described by depressed adolescents are persistent overwhelming sadness and despair and a sense of “spiraling down and within” (Dundon, 2006). In the introjective subtype, feelings of failure, worthlessness, guilt, and a sense of loss of control dominate. Central concerns involve problems of self-­definition and self-­esteem, strongly associated with feelings of emptiness, self-­criticism, and lack of autonomy and self-worth. These adolescents are imbued with a strong desire for perfection and can often be ambitious, but they are plagued by doubts about their worth and can be deeply wounded by criticism from both self and others. These young people report a pervasively negative outlook on life (Twivy, Kirkham, & Cooper., 2022), reaching high levels of hopelessness and risk for suicide attempts.
