Adolescents with the anaclitic subtype feel highly vulnerable to disruptions of gratifying interpersonal relationships and typically describe a sense of loss and loneliness. Intense feelings of shame, humiliation, and low self-­esteem can lead to chronic feelings of insecurity and fears of being abandoned and left unprotected. These teens have a strong desire for comfort and care from others; their depressive episodes are often precipitated by rejections, separations, breakdowns, and grief. Severely depressed adolescents may explicitly express their inner experience of emotional emptiness (Lachal et al., 2012). Cognitive Patterns Adolescents with depressive disorders often begin to slide academically as a result of mental slowing and decreased concentration. They show significant cognitive impairments in certain functions, including inhibition capacity, phonemic verbal fluency, sustained attention, verbal memory, and planning. Self-­scrutiny and criticism overwhelm

chunks.heading: 548 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 548 Adolescence them with thoughts of hopelessness (included among the symptoms of depressive episodes in ICD-11), leading to loss of the desire to live and sometimes to the determination to die. Suicide risk is thus high in severe cases. Psychotic manifestations may sometimes be present, and not on...
chunks.text: 548 Adolescence them with thoughts of hopelessness (included among the symptoms of depressive episodes in ICD-11), leading to loss of the desire to live and sometimes to the determination to die. Suicide risk is thus high in severe cases. Psychotic manifestations may sometimes be present, and not only in the most impaired adolescents. This clinical evidence reinforces the DSM-5 and DSM-5-TR guidance to indicate the presence of psychotic features irrespective of the level of depressive severity. Somatic States Qualitative studies depict a constant experience of “being weighed down.” Somatic complaints, particularly feelings of fatigue coexisting with a lack of self-care, are often the first symptoms of depression that adolescents perceive and report. The presence of neurovegetative symptoms, particularly sleep disturbances, weight problems, and loss of energy, appears to be critical in identifying depressive disorders in youth compared with adults. Relational Patterns Persistent feelings of depression may alienate adolescents from relatives, peers, and school personnel, especially if they commit impulsive acts and convey unrelenting anger toward parents, friends, or teachers. Qualitative studies of depressed adolescents highlight the centrality of loneliness and emotional distance, which can incite a vicious cycle (Achterbergh et al., 2020). A tendency to withdraw from the world can accompany different intrapsychic and interpersonal dynamics. While some adolescents experience a sense of relief in being alone, others want to communicate their feelings openly. Some teenagers with anaclitic depressions rely excessively on others and feel threatened and desperate in the face of separations or rejections; those with introjective depressions may depend on external approval to counteract low self-­esteem, feelings of unworthiness, and fear of being hurt by criticism or mistreatment (Tanzilli et al., 2021). Research has shown that introjective depressive experiences are often related to histories of parental rejection and authoritarianism that have created fearful attachment styles early in life, whereas anaclitic depressive experiences are mostly associated with “enmeshed” families and histories of parental overcontrol, in which the child’s strivings for independence and separateness have been frustrated. Subjective Experience of the Therapist Clinicians’ subjective experience and their ability to monitor countertransference reactions are invaluable for both making good diagnoses and increasing their therapeutic impact. Therapists treating adolescents with depressive disorders report mixed feelings: of inadequacy and disengagement but also trust and self-­confidence. Undoubtedly, the patient’s personality and level of severity of problems can influence the variety and intensity of the therapist’s emotional responses. Therapists’ positive responses are empirically related to the quality of the therapeutic alliance: The early establishment of an alliance is significantly associated with the subsequent reduction of depressive symptomatology, regardless of its initial severity. Treatment has been associated with reduced symptoms in adolescents with depressive disorders, along with better coping strategies and improved relationships within the family and social environment.

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 549
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 549 Clinical Illustration Fanny, a 15-year-old girl, came with her parents to consult a psychiatrist because her teachers noticed her increasingly frequent outbursts of anger and crying. This behavior had started at home and then extended to school a few weeks later. Her...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 549 Clinical Illustration Fanny, a 15-year-old girl, came with her parents to consult a psychiatrist because her teachers noticed her increasingly frequent outbursts of anger and crying. This behavior had started at home and then extended to school a few weeks later. Her only complaint was of sleep disturbances; her parents described a recent tendency toward isolation and mistrust. Their daughter had also dropped out of singing lessons and was convinced she would never be able to succeed in her preparation for the choir concert at the end of the year. She told the clinician that he was wasting his time talking to her because nobody could help her. The psychiatrist noted her sad tone and felt for the distress of his patient and her parents. He was rapidly diagnosed an introjective depression and advised treatment with psychotherapy. SA34 Bipolar Disorders Bipolar disorders are described in more detail in Chapter 14 on the S Axis (pp. 756– 870) and in Chapter 5 on the SC Axis (pp. 256–380). Diagnosis of these conditions is based on unusual shifts in a person’s mood, energy, and ability to function that are different from ordinary ups and downs. In the most common form of bipolar disorder, long known as manic–­depressive illness, a person experiences episodes of abnormally elevated mood alternating cyclically with depressive episodes or symptoms. Adolescents may present with acute and heterogeneous symptomatic manifestations involving a shift from periods of predominantly depressed mood to phases characterized by expanded, euphoric, or irritable mood states of varying intensity. Young people may be diagnosed with bipolar disorder I (characterized by alternating depressive, manic, and hypomanic episodes) or II (in which depressive episodes occur with hypomanic ones); it is also possible to detect the presence of cyclothymic disorder, which represents a mild form of bipolar disorder. Yet many adolescents who experience bipolar-­like phenomena with clinically significant distress and impairment do not meet the criteria for one of these disorders; these youth may receive a diagnosis of other specified or unspecified bipolar and related disorders. Diagnosis of bipolar disorders in adolescence is controversial (Malhi, Jadidi, & Bell, 2023). In the United States, the prevalence of diagnosed bipolar disorders in children and adolescents has risen rapidly, especially between 1996 and 2004; discharges of adolescents with a bipolar diagnosis increased fourfold (see Blader & Carlson, 2007). The concomitantly increasing prescription of antipsychotic medication for children and adolescents has concerned both scientists and clinicians. Several researchers have questioned this trend, noting that it might have been influenced by certain methodological assumptions, and especially by the criteria used to identify bipolar disorders in these populations. Additionally, research evidence on prevalence rates presents inconsistent findings, often attributable to theoretical, taxonomic, methodological, and assessment-­related challenges (Malhi et al., 2023). As Carlson and Klein (2014) stated, one core disagreement was whether mania in adolescents should be diagnosed according to a narrow and conservative viewpoint (with discrete episodes representing a clear departure from prior functioning, and with symptoms equivalent to those of adults), or whether it should be diagnosed more broadly (including the onset of chronic emotion dysregulation in early childhood, usually superimposed on ADHD and oppositional defiant symptoms with multiple other comorbidities). This broader phenotype includes severe irritability and intense,

chunks.heading: 550 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 550 Adolescence protracted rage outbursts. But the debate about diagnosing this condition in childhood and adolescence continues in the scientific community (e.g., Malhi et al., 2023). The DSM-5 Work Group on Bipolar Disorders eventually decided to limit the diagnosis of bipolar disorders in young p...
chunks.text: 550 Adolescence protracted rage outbursts. But the debate about diagnosing this condition in childhood and adolescence continues in the scientific community (e.g., Malhi et al., 2023). The DSM-5 Work Group on Bipolar Disorders eventually decided to limit the diagnosis of bipolar disorders in young people to a narrow phenotype resembling that of the adult. This choice was based on longitudinal studies showing that nonepisodic, chronic irritability predicts unipolar depressive and anxiety disorders but not bipolar disorders. These experts proposed a new diagnostic category, “disruptive mood dysregulation disorder,” for the broader phenotype that follows in the next section. Notwithstanding these controversies, from a clinical perspective, symptoms of bipolar disorders in young people differ from those of adults in several significant ways. Unlike adult-onset disease, in which mania appears in elevated moods or euphoria, mania in adolescence is more likely indicated by heightened irritability, extreme sensitivity to negative reactions from others, destructive outbursts, and highly disruptive and/or aggressive behaviors, with significant difficulty in interpersonal relationships (see also Beauchaine & Hinshaw, 2017). Affective lability is considered one of the strongest predictors of later bipolar diagnoses (de Back et al., 2023). This clinical presentation may complicate the differential diagnosis between bipolar disorders and emerging borderline (or emotionally dysregulated) personality disorder. Recent research supports prioritizing activity symptoms over cognitive and emotional symptoms when assessing mania in youth given the challenge of distinguishing them from normative variations (Malhi et al., 2023). Indicators such as sleep disturbances and psychomotor agitation are valuable for identifying mania during adolescence (see also Asarnow & Mirchandaney, 2021). As late adolescence marks the development of cognitive and emotional regulation abilities, these aspects may gain increasing importance then. The scholarly literature underscores the importance of acknowledging distinct developmental stages, implying that a formal diagnosis of bipolar disorder may be controversial prior to the onset of puberty. After that, it becomes possible to make a tentative diagnosis of bipolar disorder, using a considerably higher diagnostic threshold than that used for adults, and preferably assessing symptoms through separate evaluations conducted over a span of time (Malhi et al., 2023). Youth who develop bipolar disorder tend to seek help first for a depressive episode. They are more likely than adults to experience mixed symptoms, as well as symptoms that cycle more relentlessly and rapidly between depressive and manic states. In contrast, adults may have distinct periods of depression and mania that last for weeks or months. Delusional symptoms during manic episodes, which may be misdiagnosed as schizophreniform disorder, are also more frequent in adolescents than in adults. Recent network analysis suggests that irritability and mood lability should be carefully addressed (Weintraub, Schneck, & Miklowitz, 2020). These symptoms seem related to the switch from a major depressive disorder to a diagnosis of bipolar disorder I or II, and to the onset and maintenance of mixed features. As we previously noted, adolescents with bipolar disorders have high rates of comorbid disorders (e.g., depression, ADHD, disruptive behavior problems, substance use disorders) that may complicate the differentiation of truly bipolar symptoms from symptoms of other disorders. Clinicians may have difficulty distinguishing bipolar disorders from specific emerging personality syndromes (in particular, borderline, antisocial, and narcissistic disorders). Patients with bipolar disorders tend to have greater mood elevation and more predictable temporal variations in mood states, sometimes

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 551
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 551 called “episodicity” (cf., Bornstein, 2023). Thus, a comprehensive history of the course of the illness, with a clear definition of episodes, may help disentangle bipolar disorders from other problems. Epidemiological evidence yield inconclusive results with respect...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 551 called “episodicity” (cf., Bornstein, 2023). Thus, a comprehensive history of the course of the illness, with a clear definition of episodes, may help disentangle bipolar disorders from other problems. Epidemiological evidence yield inconclusive results with respect to prevalence rates across genders: Whereas certain studies suggest similar rates, others indicate a higher incidence among boys, potentially linked to the elevated presence of comorbid ADHD, which is more frequently observed among males. Adolescent-­onset bipolar disorder involves heightened instances of recurring hospitalizations, substance misuse, suicide attempts, and completed suicides, along with diminished responsiveness to treatment and impaired interepisode functioning when compared to adult-onset bipolar disorder. Risk factors for adverse outcomes include early onset, presence of psychotic features, mixed manic and depressive symptoms, history of maltreatment, and limited socioeconomic resources (Beauchaine & Hinshaw, 2017). Recent research has attempted to develop staging models of bipolar disorders (e.g., Berk et al., 2017; Duffy, Gooday, Keown-­Stoneman, & Grof, 2019; Post, 2015), suggesting the possibility of identifying individuals at risk for bipolar I or II disorder. Overall, there is strong evidence for diagnostic familiarity among close relatives, accompanied by the presence of notable clinical manifestations. The latter include symptoms strongly related to bipolar syndromes (e.g., depressive symptoms, mood lability, subthreshold mania [homotypic precursors]) or other psychopathological conditions (e.g., anxiety disorders, ADHD [heterotypic precursors]; see also the report of the International Society of Bipolar Disorders Task Force; Faedda et al., 2019). Some studies have reported that up to 23.4% of individuals assessed as being at high clinical risk for bipolar disorders are likely to develop bipolar I or II disorder after 2 years (Salazar de Pablo et al., 2023). This line of research is still underexplored, and further studies are needed. A systematic overview of clinical guidelines for pediatric bipolar disorder has been recently developed (Fang et al., 2019). The efficacy of pharmacotherapy as a firstline intervention in adolescence—­especially for the management of acute mania—has been established. Nevertheless, prescribing medications to young people is not without hazard and requires an accurate clinical evaluation. Many psychotherapeutic interventions have also been recommended, ranging from psychoeducation to cognitive-­ behavioral and interpersonal treatments. Discrepancies among guidelines indicate the need for further research. Therapeutic and psychosocial interventions appear to be correlated with better psychological functioning, increased treatment adherence, lower relapse rates, and management of other psychopathological symptoms. Subthreshold bipolar syndromes call for specific clinical attention; functional impairment, psychiatric comorbidities, and suicide risk partially overlap with those of full-­threshold bipolar disorders. Subjective Experience of Bipolar Disorders in Adolescents Bipolar disorders are difficult to identify in adolescence, because this developmental period is notable for “affect storms,” affective dysregulation and impulsivity, agitation, irritability, and hyperactivity. Classic psychoanalytic theories (e.g., Freud, 1921) view manic episodes as a defense against intense negative affective states, especially depression; they involve a pursuit of the ideal self and a denial of superego prescriptions. From this perspective, individuals manage the feelings of inferiority that are common in adolescence through manic defenses, including the development of a grandiose self

chunks.heading: 552 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 552 Adolescence and a massive use of the defenses of denial and omnipotent control. These allow adolescents to refuse to acknowledge crucial issues associated with a faltering sense of their own worth and to develop an exaggerated sense of their value, combined with the fantasy that they are in full...
chunks.text: 552 Adolescence and a massive use of the defenses of denial and omnipotent control. These allow adolescents to refuse to acknowledge crucial issues associated with a faltering sense of their own worth and to develop an exaggerated sense of their value, combined with the fantasy that they are in full control of the world and are too powerful to be sabotaged by any experience of anxiety or depression. Object relations theorists understand mania in bipolar adolescents as a denial of separation from a lost other. Given that adolescence represents a second separation–­ individuation phase, the (real) separation from the family environment can reactivate intrapsychic experiences of loss and abandonment from early childhood that interfered with the person’s growth toward genuine psychic autonomy. The activation of manic dynamics allows the adolescent to deny dependence on early and deep attachment bonds, and to develop an idealization of self that is based on an illusory sense of self-­ sufficiency. Clinicians should be sensitive to the complexity of the inner experience of adolescents with bipolar disorder, recognizing both symptoms and underlying intrapsychic and intersubjective dynamics. Affective States and Cognitive Patterns The emotional states of young people with bipolar disorders are unusually intense and may change dramatically. Extreme highs and lows of mood are accompanied by marked changes in energy, activity, sleep, and behavior. These shifts are usually unpredictable and uncontrollable. Most of the time, adolescents experience mixed states. During manic episodes, they feel a great surge of energy, with intense pleasure and a decreased need for sleep. They may feel unrealistically self-­confident and powerful, and may see themselves as exceptional or invincible. This state can be associated with poor inhibition and risk-­taking behaviors, such as misuse of alcohol and drugs, reckless driving, and sexual carelessness. At the same time, adolescents with bipolar disorder may experience intense irritability—­a feeling of internal tension and hypersensitivity, especially to criticism, which may lead to extreme temper outbursts. Their thoughts are often confused and illogical, and they seem unable to concentrate on a topic. The internal experience is of being out of control, with an unstable self that oscillates between weakness and strength, competence and incompetence. Even in the euthymic phase, adolescents with bipolar disorder show significant cognitive impairment in several domains, such as working memory, verbal learning, verbal memory, visual learning, and visual memory. Somatic States The biological rhythms of adolescents with bipolar disorder are highly disrupted in all phases of the illness. Somatic states include increased energy, restlessness, and sleeplessness. However, adolescents with bipolar conditions also experience states of hyperarousal, frequently associated with irritability and anxiety. Relational Patterns Adolescents with bipolar disorders frequently show unpredictable, chaotic, and impulsive relationship patterns. They experience immense difficulty in interpersonal functioning with peers. Family relationships are highly disturbed. Difficulties in the

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 553
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 553 interpretation of social signals emerge, which may explain such maladaptive interpersonal functioning. Increased suicide ideation seems to be associated with highly impaired relationships. Subjective Experience of the Therapist Developing a therapeutic relationship w...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 553 interpretation of social signals emerge, which may explain such maladaptive interpersonal functioning. Increased suicide ideation seems to be associated with highly impaired relationships. Subjective Experience of the Therapist Developing a therapeutic relationship with adolescents with bipolar disorder can be exceedingly challenging. Their mood swings, low compliance, and risk for adverse outcomes (including suicide, substance misuse, and risky sexual conduct) may undermine the therapeutic alliance and evoke strong negative responses in clinicians. SA36 Disruptive Mood Dysregulation Disorder Disruptive mood dysregulation disorder is characterized by chronic, severe, persistent irritability. This irritability has two prominent manifestations: (1) severe, frequent temper outbursts that seem disproportionate to the situation and inconsistent with the adolescent’s maturational level, and (2) chronic, persistently irritable or angry mood between the outbursts. Copland, Angold, Costello, and Egger (2013) found that the prevalence of the disorder ranges from 0.8 to 3.3%. However, there are some discrepancies across studies (e.g., Tapia & John, 2018). Some symptoms of disruptive mood dysregulation are present in other child or adolescent psychiatric disorders, such as depression, bipolar disorders, and oppositional defiant disorder. However, mood dysregulation disorder is characterized by irritable mood with a nonepisodic character, and its severity in young people depends considerably on the frequency and duration of temper outbursts. Many adolescents who are diagnosable as having this disorder also meet the criteria for ADHD and anxiety disorders. Notably, these adolescents show more symptoms of hyperarousal, impulsivity, and disorganized attention. Noteworthy, the diagnosis of disruptive mood dysregulation disorder is no longer included in the ICD-11. In light of its high rate of comorbidity with other psychopathologies such as ADHD and oppositional defiant disorder, the WHO Task Force does not recognize the clinical utility of the diagnosis (see also Lochman et al., 2015). Subjective Experience of Disruptive Mood Dysregulation in Adolescents Affective States The affective experience of adolescents with this psychopathological pattern includes chronic irritability with angry mood, expressed in frequent temper outbursts. Adolescents’ difficulty modulating emotions may impel them toward risky behaviors, suicide attempts, and severe aggression, which may lead to psychiatric hospitalizations. Cognitive Patterns The internal experience of these adolescents may include being out of control of their frequent temper outbursts. This subjective dyscontrol may be associated with a negative and unstable sense of self. They may have limited insight into their behaviors and often experience the setting of limits as a painful injustice.

chunks.heading: 554 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 554 Adolescence Somatic States Although hyperarousal is not among DSM-5 criteria for disruptive mood dysregulation disorder, adolescents with this clinical profile frequently suffer symptoms of hyperarousal, such as insomnia or distractibility. Relational Patterns The chronic, severe irritability of...
chunks.text: 554 Adolescence Somatic States Although hyperarousal is not among DSM-5 criteria for disruptive mood dysregulation disorder, adolescents with this clinical profile frequently suffer symptoms of hyperarousal, such as insomnia or distractibility. Relational Patterns The chronic, severe irritability of patients with this diagnosis creates marked disruption in adolescents’ family relationships, peer relationships, and school performance. Due to their extremely low frustration tolerance, these adolescents often struggle to succeed in school and may be unable to take part in activities typically enjoyed by their peers; their family lives are severely disrupted by their outbursts and irritability; and they have trouble initiating or sustaining friendships. Levels of dysfunction in adolescents with bipolar disorders and disruptive mood dysregulation are generally comparable. Subjective Experience of the Therapist Clinicians have reported difficulties in setting boundaries with adolescents with this diagnosis and have expressed feelings of being overwhelmed, disorganized, and frustrated about their patients’ refusals of their interventions. Adolescents with disruptive dysregulated mood disorder may appear distant from their feelings, deny their own problems, maintain an attitude of superiority, and try to exert control over the clinician. Therefore, building a therapeutic alliance may be challenging. The practitioner’s flexibility throughout the whole treatment process may foster the therapeutic relationship. SA37 Suicidality Suicidal ideation and behavior, a severe public health problem worldwide, has its peak incidence in adolescence. It is one of the leading causes of death among people between the age of14 and 29, with girls at higher risk of suicidal behaviors and boys suffering more lethality (Miranda-­Mendizabal et al., 2019; Pompili, 2019). For the WHO (2021), reducing suicide is now a primary objective. The main manifestation of suicidal behavior disorder is a “suicide attempt,” defined as a behavior that the individual has undertaken with at least some intent to die. A suicidal attempt does not necessarily have serious consequences for the individual’s life. The crucial aspect is the intentionality with which the suicidal conduct is enacted. It is very difficult to determine the degree of determination that drove the subject to want to procure death. Some evidence has conceived of suicide as the final stage of a decision-­making process sustained by specific motivations (Williams et al., 2024). Therefore, the pathway starting from a volitional stage may evolve to the actual decision to die, which subsequently precipitates into planning and action. In this vein, the individual’s mental state at the time of the action represents a crucial risk marker. Notably, the suicidal act differs substantially from nonsuicidal self-­injury; the latter involves direct injuries to the body undertaken to induce relief from painful affective states (see SA38, Chapter 9, pp. 558–561). ICD-11, which does not include a diagnostic category for suicidal behaviors, also invites clinicians to indicate whether

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 555
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 555 the person has engaged in intentional self-­harming behaviors and, whenever applicable, to note whether the individual intended to die. Overall, attempted suicide is a more statistically widespread phenomenon than actual suicide, with rates of prevalence of 8–10% wor...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 555 the person has engaged in intentional self-­harming behaviors and, whenever applicable, to note whether the individual intended to die. Overall, attempted suicide is a more statistically widespread phenomenon than actual suicide, with rates of prevalence of 8–10% worldwide; the highest reported attempted suicide rate is among those ages 15–24. Only a small percentage of the 17% of adolescents reporting suicidal ideation actually attempt suicide. Psychodynamic psychotherapy, dialectical behavior therapy, mentalization based therapy, and family therapies all seem promising interventions for this clinical population (Bahji et al., 2021; Briggs et al., 2019; Itzhaky et al., 2022). Because suicidal patients are highly heterogenous, however, clinicians need to personalize their interventions. Subjective Experience of Suicidality in Adolescents There are several crucial aspects of the movement toward suicidality that directly involve adolescent development. The overall psychological and neurophysiological rearrangement that occurs from preadolescence through adolescence may enhance suicidal risk because of increased impulsivity, emotional lability, affective dysregulation, and sensation seeking. In general, difficulties in identity formation and in establishing new affective and social relationships may increase vulnerability and contribute to what has been described as psychache (Pompili, 2019), an unbearable anguish that prompts adolescents to consider internal and external possibilities for modulating or ending their distress. Most contemporary scholars agree that suicide represents the outcome of a process that begins before actual suicidal behavior occurs and even before suicidal ideation becomes conscious (Barzilay & Apter, 2014). Suicide results from a sense of entrapment, hopelessness, guilt, and personal distrust in modulating the experience of anguish. The narrowing of prospects for a possible solution further increases the state of internal distress and mental confusion that precipitates the actual suicidal crisis. This trajectory involves a crucial motivational phase in which the initial idea of self-death gradually transforms into more stable suicidal ideation that can lead to the intent and then the plan for suicide (Moselli, Frattini, Williams, & Ronningstam, 2021). Because many adolescents report that they have cultivated suicidal ideation and even suicidal intention without resorting to suicidal behavior, many authors believe that the staging model of suicide should consider a volitional stage subsequent to the initial motivational stage. From this perspective, suicidality is regarded as the product of several interacting risk factors, including distal or long-­course factors, and proximal or precipitating factors. Common distal risk factors include genetic predisposition to emotional distress, familiarity with suicide, and membership in a minority or socially disadvantaged group. Precipitating factors include events such as illness, bereavement, suicide by family members or peers, and a history of physical or sexual abuse or bullying, especially for issues related to gender and sexual orientation (see also Chapter 10). In adolescence, suicide risk has been associated with poor school performance, sexual promiscuity, substance abuse, and other clinical conditions. The presence of a psychiatric disorder is considered the primary source of vulnerability to psychache and to the use of poor coping strategies that precipitate the suicidal crisis, with an increased suicidal risk in nonclinical populations. Observational evidence and retrospective studies show that 96% of adolescents who have attempted

chunks.heading: 556 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 556 Adolescence suicide would meet criteria for psychiatric diagnoses, especially juvenile affective disorders and schizophrenia, eating disorders and, particularly in early adolescence, ADHD and other externalizing disorders. It is well documented that nonsuicidal self-­ injury and hospitalization...
chunks.text: 556 Adolescence suicide would meet criteria for psychiatric diagnoses, especially juvenile affective disorders and schizophrenia, eating disorders and, particularly in early adolescence, ADHD and other externalizing disorders. It is well documented that nonsuicidal self-­ injury and hospitalization for previous suicide attempts are important predictors of future suicidal behaviors in adolescence. Finally, diagnosis of personality disorders in adolescence has been associated with 19–23% of suicide attempts, and 30–42% of completed suicides. Several contributors have suggested a synergistic interaction between external stressors, developmental adaptation tasks, and personality disorders, which may work together as an activating factor, increasing the risk of suicidal behavior. Emerging personality syndromes may also play a facilitating role in the transformation of suicidal ideation into suicidal behavior, by interfering with the effective psychological processing of negative emotions and psychache. Among personality disorders, Cluster B personality disorders are the most significant predictors of suicide, although recent evidence has implicated emerging narcissistic and antisocial patterns (Moselli, Casini, Frattini, & Williams, 2023; Ronningstam, Weinberg, Goldblatt, Schechter, & Herbstman, 2018; Williams, Casini, Moselli, Frattini, & Ronningstam, 2021). The degree of severity of personality pathology (as assessed by the DSM-5 Alternative Model of Personality Disorders [AMPD]; American Psychiatric Association, 2013) has also been associated with an increased likelihood of suicide attempts. An overall assessment of all these factors, although it may be incomplete and difficult to achieve in the clinical context of adolescence, is required for the clinical management of suicidal risk. Two considerations are notable: First, not all psychiatric patients sharing the same diagnosis eventually commit suicide; second, not all suicidal adolescents (or adults) actually meet the criteria for a specific diagnostic category. In adolescence, in particular, many suicide attempts seem to occur in the absence of known risk factors. Although constant monitoring of distal and proximal risk factors is the strategy of choice for suicidal risk management in adolescence, researchers and clinicians have gone further to account for the imponderability of suicidal outcomes (Franklin et al., 2017). The accurate evaluation of motivations underlying suicidal behaviors seems a promising complementary approach for researchers as well as clinicians working with adolescents. Focusing on motivations is consistent with a staging model of suicide, considered not as a unitary construct but as a decisional process that can eventually culminate in a suicide attempt. According to this model, in some individuals, suicide ideation turns into the intention to die, and is followed by concrete action planning and actual suicidal behavior. Because mental functioning and personality patterns shape the individual’s motivation to die, clinicians should attend to them carefully (see also Ronningstam et al., 2018). Suicidal mental states and underlying motives vary among adolescents. Exploration of adolescents’ feelings before and after suicide attempts, and of the expectations and meanings they connect to this action, is vital for developing adequate preventive and therapeutic plans. Affective States and Cognitive Patterns Three themes emerge frequently in the narratives of suicidal adolescents: sadness, negative attitudes toward the self, and need for control over one’s life (Lachal, Orri, Sibeoni, Moro, & Revah-Levy, 2015). The condition of psychache in adolescents

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 557
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 557 often follows failures in school, sports, friendships, and romantic interactions that are immediately saturated with intense shame and humiliation, leading to self-­sabotage, helplessness, a sense of defeat, and a lack of control. Adolescents and young adults further...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 557 often follows failures in school, sports, friendships, and romantic interactions that are immediately saturated with intense shame and humiliation, leading to self-­sabotage, helplessness, a sense of defeat, and a lack of control. Adolescents and young adults further describe sadness, mental pain, despair, detachment, anger, and irritability. They depict a devalued self, defined by failure, and feelings of uselessness, incompetence, and worthlessness. They sometimes mention self-­hatred. Many have the impression that they have lost control of their existence and can no longer deal with problems and make decisions. The inner experience is of loss of the meaning of life and entrapment in endless suffering, with no possible better future. Because the suicidal act appears as an escape from an overwhelming, unmanageable life situation, it may be associated with positive emotions. Suicide attempts seem to give devastated adolescents a feeling of mastery over their bodies and the lives they feel they have already lost. Failure of the suicidal act may be experienced as yet another demonstration of ineptitude and thus may reinforce an adolescent’s negative self-­perception. Some teens report having attempted suicide with a total lack of consideration of the potentially lethal consequences of their act, with an ensuing absence of fear and a dissociative detachment from themselves and others. Somatic States Suicidal adolescents often experience high levels of anxiety, frequently expressed in somatic sensations, fear, shame, or rejection of parts of their body that have specific emotional meanings. Anxiety can be a proximal risk factor for suicidal acts and should be monitored attentively in at-risk adolescents. In some teens, the depersonalized states accompanying their suicidal conduct cause them to experience their own body or part of their body as alien. Recurrent nonsuicidal self-­injury or past suicide attempts may decrease adolescents’ sensitivity to physical pain and fear and habituate them to suicidal behavior. Relational Patterns Adolescents frequently describe problems and impasses with family members that seem to parallel their inner negative emotions. Family dynamics are perceived as rigid and overwhelming, without acceptance or trust. Conflicts, separations, losses, and absences can all contribute to the decision to act. Relationships with friends or partners, frequently characterized by high dependency, fail to compensate for the family difficulties, thereby increasing feelings of loneliness. The sense of being unheard and misunderstood by significant figures is central to the suicidal process: before the suicide attempt, in precipitating factors, and while the adolescent is in treatment. Another feeling commonly expressed involves being seen as different and being rejected on that basis. Suicidal adolescents find themselves marginalized, isolated, and rejected, based on elements of reality (school, sexuality, religion, origins, etc.). The fear of being judged by others amplifies feelings of painful solitude. In relationships, adolescents contemplating suicide may oscillate between the sense of being a burden to others and the sense of not belonging anywhere. These feelings are elaborated into specific suicidal motivations that include dispensing with relationships of dependence that are felt as intrusive or humiliating; freeing others from one’s burdensome presence; claiming one’s unique, original, and isolated existence;

chunks.heading: 558 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 558 Adolescence merging with a lost person; triumphing over disdainful others and exacting revenge against them; provoking guilt; manipulating and punishing others; and making a bid for help. As we have emphasized, in adolescents’ suicide attempts, communication issues are fundamental. Teenage suici...
chunks.text: 558 Adolescence merging with a lost person; triumphing over disdainful others and exacting revenge against them; provoking guilt; manipulating and punishing others; and making a bid for help. As we have emphasized, in adolescents’ suicide attempts, communication issues are fundamental. Teenage suicidal behavior seems primarily an interpersonal phenomenon; it may be seen as the only option to express pain, once every other communicative possibility has failed (Orri et al., 2014). Adolescents who recover from suicidal crises often describe a gradual reopening of communication with others—­a process that lays the groundwork for changes in family relationships. Getting better requires that those around these adolescents, including their clinicians, express their understanding and empathy. Although engaging with a suicidal adolescent can be challenging, current evidence supports the importance of empathic attunement to psychache, suicidal thoughts, and the desire to die as a key strategy for mitigating risk in both pre- and post-­suicidal crises. Mental health professionals and other adults may be reluctant to probe for suicidal ideation and its underlying emotional states, both for fear of encouraging self-harm and because it may feel like a violation of an adolescent’s privacy. These worries are baseless. The invitation to adolescents to disclose this deep part of their experience is now considered a key protective factor in managing suicidal processes. Adolescents’ experience of suicide is influenced by how their peers and others respond to their distress. How a society, including mass media, understands suicidal acts or stigmatizes the suicidal person has an impact on attempts. Self-­esteem is based on internalized standards and values (hard work, academic success, integrity, beauty, and other ideals) within familial, religious, and sociocultural contexts. Failure to achieve these standards results in shame and a sense of stigma, guilt, or anger toward oneself and others. Suicidal adolescents report problems in feeling they belong to a group, leading to their isolation and withdrawal, eventually creating a vicious cycle that reinforces their sinking into an abyss of loneliness and incommunicability. Subjective Experience of the Therapist Suicidal patients elicit strong and adverse countertransference reactions. Clinicians report feelings of being overwhelmed, anxious, confused, angry, manipulated, or defied (Michaud, Greenway, Corbeil, Bourquin, & Richard-­Devantoy, 2021). These emotions may mirror the inner world of suicidal patients, especially their intense pain and hopelessness, which can be contagious. Empirical evidence indicates that therapists may also feel emotions of fulfillment and engagement, which may represent a way of empathizing with these patients and sustaining the therapeutic work. SA38 Nonsuicidal Self‐lnjury In nonsuicidal self-­injury, adolescents repeatedly inflict shallow yet painful injuries on the surface of their body. Gillies et al. (2018) report a 22.9% lifetime prevalence of this behavior in young people, with girls more likely to engage in it. Also, sexual minority youth—­especially those who are transgender and bisexual—­have shown a greater vulnerability compared to heterosexual/cisgender peers. Mean age of onset is estimated at 12 to 13 years. Prevalence of nonsuicidal self-harm peaks in middle adolescence and decreases in later years.

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 559
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 559 In the DSM-5 and DSM-5-TR, this behavior is distinguished from suicidality. Viewing it as a standalone diagnostic entity calls for additional research. In DSM 5-TR, nonsuicidal self-­injury is also considered a diagnostic criterion of borderline personality disorder....
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 559 In the DSM-5 and DSM-5-TR, this behavior is distinguished from suicidality. Viewing it as a standalone diagnostic entity calls for additional research. In DSM 5-TR, nonsuicidal self-­injury is also considered a diagnostic criterion of borderline personality disorder. In ICD-11, it is given a separate code among symptoms and signs of psychopathological condition, but is not classified as a separate diagnostic entity; it is defined as “intentional self-­inflicted injury to the body, most commonly cutting, scraping, burning, biting, or hitting, with the expectation that the injury will lead to only minor physical harm” (WHO, 2021). Although nonsuicidal self-­harming acts should be differentiated from suicidal behaviors, they often coexist with suicidal ideation and conduct. In fact, engaging in these acts is a prime risk factor for suicide ideation and attempts. The harm may occur episodically or may turn into a recurrent behavior, on a weekly or daily basis. The frequency of occurrence and severity of self-­ injuries, along with the stereotypic, compulsive, or impulsive quality underlying them, need to be analyzed to infer their psychopathological meaning. Self-­harming behaviors may have both intrapsychic and interpersonal functions. The former is more frequently reported by adolescents (Taylor et al., 2018); the most common motive they give for self-­injury is to reduce negative emotions, such as tension, anxiety, psychic pain, shame, self-­hatred, or loneliness. They often mention an immediate sensation of relief and well-being brought about by self-harm, and a sense of urgency and craving for that result, such that the outcome of repetitive self-­injury comes to resemble the activation of the brain’s reward system in addictive behavior. Less frequently, self-harm is reported to have an interpersonal meaning, which may include seeking attention, asking for help, or trying to punish others—­an indirect expression of rage within abusive and traumatic contexts. In a meta-­synthesis, Stänicke, Haavind, and Gullestad (2018) found that achieving release, exerting control over heightened negative feelings, exhibiting unaccepted emotions and impulses, and making a connection with others are the most frequent themes expressed by adolescents who injure themselves. More than half of those with nonsuicidal self-­harming patterns are diagnosed with a dissociative disorder. The debate continues as to whether these acts are performed to avoid painful feelings connected to the outbreak of dissociative states, or, conversely, to induce a dissociative state in order to reduce the psychic pain of emotional dysregulation. Until recently, nonsuicidal self-harm was primarily associated with depression, anxiety, eating disorders, substance abuse, personality disorder (especially borderline personality disorder), and other serious psychopathology. Additional risk factors include a history of past or recent trauma and sexual abuse. Current scientific literature suggests that self-­injurious behaviors are a transdiagnostic symptom (Benteley, Cassiello-­Robbins, Vittorio, Sauer-­Zavala, & Barlow, 2015); that is, they are found in teens with a range of disorders and also in some who have no psychiatric diagnosis. The emotional dysregulation and lability common in adolescent development may account for this. Several strategies have been suggested to reduce self-­injurious behaviors in adolescents. Foremost is helping them improve their affective language and other communicative skills. Involving the family in the support and treatment of self-­harming teens is a critical part of therapeutic intervention, given that poor communication among family members has been associated with both nonsuicidal self-­injury and suicidal behavior in teens. Improving the family members’ understanding of the adolescent’s selfharm can help to decrease conflicts. Learning deescalation strategies and expanding

chunks.heading: 560 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 560 Adolescence listening and communication skills can be also beneficial. Dialectical behavior therapy, mentalization-­based therapy, CBT, and other interventions developed specifically for self-­harming behaviors seem promising but require further study (Bahji et al., 2021; Calvo et al., 2022; Coo...
chunks.text: 560 Adolescence listening and communication skills can be also beneficial. Dialectical behavior therapy, mentalization-­based therapy, CBT, and other interventions developed specifically for self-­harming behaviors seem promising but require further study (Bahji et al., 2021; Calvo et al., 2022; Cook & Gorraiz, 2016). Subjective Experience of Nonsuicidal Self‐Injury in Adolescents Affective States Robust evidence indicates that emotional dysregulation is a risk factor for self-­injurious acts. Most commonly, these behaviors temporarily alleviate overwhelming negative emotions such as anxiety, depression, anger, frustration, guilt, shame, or internal deadness. Self-­harming acts may help adolescents to regain a sense of control, avoid distressing or suicidal thoughts, or interrupt dissociative experiences. Self-­injury can also be a form of anger or punishment directed against the self. Once it becomes recurrent, some adolescents report that they crave such behaviors to obtain relief, making them eventually compulsive. Cognitive Patterns In the time immediately preceding self-­injurious conduct, adolescents experience a range of negative thoughts, sometimes including suicidal ideas. Self-­harming teens are frequently self-­critical and unable to find an external target for their rage, even when anger at others seems justified. Victimization, for example, instead of engendering rage and revenge, may evoke powerful feelings of shame and humiliation that lead the adolescents to self-harm. Prior to their acts, self-­injurious adolescents often ruminate for a period about the intended behavior; they may think continually about self-­injuring even when they do not act on the impulse. Some report hurting themselves to stop suicidal ideation or to stop themselves from attempting suicide. Some seem to share the same attraction toward death and repulsion toward life that suicidal adolescents report. Others do not report any intention to die. The relationship between nonsuicidal self-harm and suicide attempts thus remains controversial and probably complex. Somatic States Adolescents who deliberately injure themselves describe high levels of reactivity, affective intensity, and physiological arousal, especially under stress, and often report other somatic symptoms of anxiety. Self-­harming behaviors may induce a state of psychic numbing that helps them reduce physiological and emotional arousal. Some describe how the physical pain from self-­injuries gives them relief by substituting for psychic pain. In repetitive, compulsive patterns of self-harm, there is progressive desensitization to pain. Relational Patterns Self-harm may serve interpersonal functions for adolescents. While many youth engage in self-­injury in private and do not disclose it to others, some learn that their

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 561
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 561 behaviors elicit positive reinforcement through increased attention and concern from those around them. Some report having wanted to protect others by directing intense feelings of anger toward themselves. Self-­injury may also help a teen to avoid difficult relation...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 561 behaviors elicit positive reinforcement through increased attention and concern from those around them. Some report having wanted to protect others by directing intense feelings of anger toward themselves. Self-­injury may also help a teen to avoid difficult relational situations. Family and peer conflicts may play a significant role in adolescents’ self-­injurious conduct and may mediate the role of emotion regulation difficulties. Sometimes the threat of self-harm causes others to decrease pressure (e.g., to stop attempting to get the teenager to complete homework, chores, or other tasks). In developmental contexts characterized by trauma and neglect, self-­injurious acts may be effective as a bid for help and connection or as a way of indirectly blaming or attacking others. Subjective Experience of the Therapist The emotional consequences of nonsuicidal self-­harming acts may pose problems for building an alliance. Clinicians report a range of negative reactions to teens who injure themselves. They may see them as playing a game, being manipulative, or seeking attention. It may help to remember that self-harm generally stems from emotional pain, not malevolent intent, and that the adolescent needs skills to replace self-­injury as a coping strategy. Clinicians need to consider the intrapsychic and relational functions of the behavior. Therapy should take into account the meanings of the teen’s self-harm, so that the emotional needs behind such aggression against the self can be handled in better ways and the therapeutic alliance can be developed and reinforced. To gain an adolescent’s trust and establish a spirit of collaboration, therapists should adopt an attitude of respectful curiosity and emotional validation. The effort to achieve a mentalizing stance can slowly strengthen the adolescent’s sense of identity and reduce the psychological suffering from which the self-­harming behaviors emerged. SA4 Disorders Related Primarily to Anxiety Anxiety disorders are also described in Chapter 14 on the S Axis and Chapter 5 on the SC Axis, pages 756–870 and 256–380, respectively. The DSM-5-TR identifies several patterns of anxiety, including separation anxiety disorder, specific phobia, social anxiety disorder, panic disorder, agoraphobia, and generalized anxiety disorder. ICD11 categorizes these as “anxiety or fear-­related disorders.” Both DSM-5 and ICD11 have introduced a separate category for obsessive–­compulsive disorder and body dysmorphic disorder (dysmorphophobia), that is, “obsessive–­compulsive and related disorders.” During adolescence, anxiety symptoms may accompany different conditions, complicating the diagnostic process. Accurate, comprehensive assessment requires considering the duration, intensity, and functional impairment associated with the symptoms. The dynamic nature of adolescence, with its complex interplay of biological, psychological, and social factors, contributes to this overlapping symptomatology. Onset of these disorders varies significantly, with general anxiety disorder, agoraphobia, panic disorder, obsessive–­compulsive disorder, and body dysmorphic disorder often emerging in adolescence. Epidemiological evidence consistently identify anxiety disorders as the most prevalent psychiatric disorders among children and youth. The numerous transitions of

chunks.heading: 562 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 562 Adolescence adolescence increase the risk of developing maladaptive patterns for regulating and expressing anxiety. Anxiety disorders are diagnosed more frequently in girls than in boys; this gender difference becomes more pronounced as individuals progress through development, with prevalence r...
chunks.text: 562 Adolescence adolescence increase the risk of developing maladaptive patterns for regulating and expressing anxiety. Anxiety disorders are diagnosed more frequently in girls than in boys; this gender difference becomes more pronounced as individuals progress through development, with prevalence ratios reaching 2:1 or 3:1 by adolescence. SA41 Anxiety Disorders Manifestations of anxiety in adolescence encompass a wide range of emotional and behavioral patterns. Adolescents with anxiety disorders frequently experience overwhelming fear and worry, often accompanied by somatic symptoms. They may suffer states of panic: sudden, intense surges of fear or physical discomfort and a strong desire to escape or avoid the triggering situation. Anxiety can accompany school-­ related problems, such as school refusal; adolescents with an anxiety disorder may find it hard to attend school regularly and may worry excessively about academic performance, social interactions, or specific situations within the school environment. Such concerns frequently lead to high levels of distress and avoidance. When assessing these disorders in adolescence, clinicians should keep in mind that this phase is marked by many new experiences that can provoke anxiety. While it is natural for adolescents to feel some anxiety in unfamiliar situations (e.g., separating from parents, starting school, forming friendships), anxiety disorders burden them with persistent and excessive affective states that interfere with age-­appropriate activities, hampering the achievement of developmental milestones. Anxiety functions as a signal of potential danger, although what is perceived as threatening can vary widely from person to person. The source of perceived danger can be conscious or unconscious, real or imagined. During adolescence, it often revolves around basic safety concerns and relationship issues (e.g., fear of illness or injury, fear of loss of or harm to caregivers or other love objects, concerns about social evaluations, or even the imagined consequences of success). Although anxiety can be observed from early childhood on, the capacity to regulate and metabolize it improves and evolves. As children grow older, they typically develop greater cognitive and emotional resources to manage it. In some instances, however, dysregulation persists and may even intensify during adolescence, affecting various aspects of teenagers’ lives and significantly shaping their thoughts and behaviors. The course of an adolescent anxiety disorder varies greatly depending on the teen’s history and personal characteristics and on the broader context. Family dynamics—­including attachment experiences in childhood, parental examples of how to cope with anxiety, and the availability of emotional support—­can affect an adolescent’s experience of anxiety. Especially in the case of school phobia, peer relationships and the school environment may also contribute to its regulation and expression. The onset and persistence of anxiety disorders in adolescence are influenced by many variables. Some literature emphasizes the role of neurobiological factors, particularly at the neurotransmitter level (e.g., dysfunction in the dopaminergic, serotoninergic, and GABA[gamma-­aminobutyric acid]ergic systems). Other studies confirm the role of abnormal brain activity patterns, including dysfunctional prefrontal cortex activity, altered connectivity of the amygdala, and impaired connectivity between frontal and limbic areas. Temperament, along with its interplay with the environment (e.g., parental responses and peer interactions), also plays a role. Traits such as behavioral inhibition, heightened sensitivity to threat, and negative affectivity

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 563
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 563 have been implicated. Family conflict and stressful life events that affect adolescents’ bodily well-being increase the likelihood of their developing anxiety sensitivity in this phase of life. These vulnerability factors may predispose adolescents to an anxious resp...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 563 have been implicated. Family conflict and stressful life events that affect adolescents’ bodily well-being increase the likelihood of their developing anxiety sensitivity in this phase of life. These vulnerability factors may predispose adolescents to an anxious response when faced with stress or developmental challenges. The central tasks of adolescence (exploration of personal identity, establishment of autonomy and independence, and negotiation of peer relationships) expose teens to new and potentially anxiety-­provoking situations, creating fertile ground for the emergence of maladaptive responses that can precipitate psychopathology. The escalating pressures in adolescence to conform to societal expectations, meet academic demands, and adhere to social norms can further contribute to the development of anxiety disorders. The revival of attachment experiences with caregivers also plays a key role. Insecure and disorganized attachment styles, which may lead to difficulties in managing separation, effectively seeking reassurance, and developing a sense of self-worth, have consistently been linked to the development of anxiety disorders in adolescence. The interplay between individual vulnerability factors and the developmental challenges of adolescence highlights the multifaceted nature of anxiety disorders during this period. This underlines the importance of early detection, appropriate intervention, and an environment that fosters secure attachment relationships, encourages autonomy while providing guidance, and promotes healthy coping strategies. Symptoms of anxiety may vary depending on the adolescent’s age and stage of development. In some instances, they may be transient and episodic, while in others they can be so persistent and severe that they drastically impair daily functioning. Younger adolescents may show more externalizing behaviors, such as irritability, restlessness, or conduct difficulties, while older teens may suffer more internalizing symptoms, such as excessive worry, perfectionism, and depressed mood. The clinical interview is fundamental to the assessment of anxiety disorders in adolescents. It allows clinicians to gather essential information and gain a comprehensive understanding of the teenager’s symptoms, experiences, and functioning. Multiple sources of data are ideal. Because teenagers have unique insights into their internal experiences, self-­report questionnaires and rating scales are valuable tools. These facilitate adolescents’ reporting of symptoms they may not have disclosed in an interview because of shame or difficulties articulating their experience. Caregivers, teachers, and other involved adults can provide insights into an adolescent’s behavior and emotional patterns in different settings. Parents’ input is especially vital with early adolescents, who may have trouble recognizing and communicating their affective states. Finally, clinicians should explore symptoms that overlap with other psychiatric conditions that may interact with anxiety. They should inquire about depressed mood, current or past suicidal thoughts, and nonsuicidal self-­injury. International clinical guidelines recommend the use of evidence-­based approaches, particularly cognitive-­ behavioral therapy, as primary psychological interventions. Exposure techniques can desensitize patients to anxiety triggers, particularly in instances of phobias and obsessive–­compulsive disorders. Medication, such as selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) or serotonin–­norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (SNRIs), should be considered for severe cases and/or when psychotherapy alone is not yielding positive results. Although benzodiazepines effectively reduce anxiety, prescribing them is discouraged because of their significant side effects, notably their potential for creating dependency.

chunks.heading: 564 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 564 Adolescence Below, we offer a detailed discussion of each anxiety disorder, the subjective experience of adolescent patients suffering from these symptom patterns, and the countertransference dynamics that therapists have reported. Despite some specificities of the different clinical conditions...
chunks.text: 564 Adolescence Below, we offer a detailed discussion of each anxiety disorder, the subjective experience of adolescent patients suffering from these symptom patterns, and the countertransference dynamics that therapists have reported. Despite some specificities of the different clinical conditions in this diagnostic grouping, many aspects—­related primarily to the regulation and expression of anxiety—­are shared among the various disorder categories. SA41.1 Specific Phobias Specific phobias are described in more detail in Chapter 14 (pp. 796–797) and in Chapter 5 (pp. 312–314). They are characterized by an identifiable and persistent fear that is excessive or unreasonable, triggered by the presence (or anticipation) of a specific object or situation. Adolescents with one or more phobias consistently experience anxiety when exposed to a particular object or situation. The fear, anxiety, or avoidance behavior is immediately induced by the phobic situation to a degree that is persistent and out of proportion to its actual risk. Common phobias include fear of animals, insects, blood, heights, closed spaces, and flying. In adolescents, the affective state that characterizes a specific phobia must persist for a long time; otherwise, if it lasts less than 6 months, the fear is considered transient. A significant number of teens meet criteria for multiple phobia types, indicating that phobias often extend their influence across a diverse territory rather than confining themselves to a particular domain. Although there is currently very limited knowledge about how to prevent specific phobias in adolescents, early detection and intervention can reduce the severity of symptoms, promote adaptive growth and development, and improve adolescents’ overall quality of life. Research has shown that adolescents diagnosed with specific phobias tend to have comorbid conditions, particularly depressive and somatoform disorders. SA41.2 Social Anxiety Disorder Social anxiety disorder (“social phobia” before DSM-5) is described in more detail in Chapter 14, p. 797). This condition is defined as an intense fear of social interactions and situations involving the possibility of being scrutinized or judged by others. Adolescents with social anxiety disorder experience significant distress during such situations and may go to great lengths to avoid social encounters. Their fear and avoidance frequently revolve around the possibility of initiating conversations, participating in group activities, giving talks, or sometimes even eating or drinking in public. This problem is common in adolescents and young adults, with prevalence rates reported as high as 28%. It often emerges during early adolescence, a period characterized by increased self-­awareness and significant social and emotional changes, all leading to heightened attention to social evaluation. Transient social anxiety or avoidance is common during the early teen years (e.g., young teenage girls may refrain for a short period from drinking and/or eating in front of others) and does not necessarily have clinical significance. When anxiety is persistent, disproportionate, and impairing, however, it can have a profound impact on development, academic performance, relationships, and overall quality of life. Teens with social anxiety struggle with participation in class discussions, giving presentations, and social activities. Their avoidance can lead to reduced self-­confidence and limited social support networks.

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 565
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 565 Interpersonal stressors have been identified as predictors of both depressive and social anxiety symptoms. Peer victimization, including bullying and social rejection, can contribute to the development of social anxiety and can have powerful effects on an adolescent’...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 565 Interpersonal stressors have been identified as predictors of both depressive and social anxiety symptoms. Peer victimization, including bullying and social rejection, can contribute to the development of social anxiety and can have powerful effects on an adolescent’s self-­esteem and social functioning. Although there is an association between low peer acceptance and social anxiety in both boys and girls, there are distinct risk factors that primarily apply to girls, including having a limited number of close relationships, suffering negative experiences within the peer group, and being subjected to relational victimization. For both males and females, emotional maltreatment within the family (e.g., harsh criticism or excessive control) can contribute to feelings of inadequacy and social anxiety. Onset of social anxiety is typically gradual, with symptoms intensifying over time as individuals contend with the mounting social demands and pressures of this critical life phase. In this context, it is relevant to consider hikikomori, a phenomenon that shares some similarities with severe social anxiety. Hikikomori (the term is derived from two Japanese verbs: hiku, “to pull,” and komoru, “to shut oneself in one’s room” or “to hide away”) refers to a state of extreme social isolation, often lasting months or even years. While social anxiety disorder may lead to avoidance of specific social situations, hikikomori represents a more severe form of disengagement, in which adolescents or young adults cut themselves off completely from the outside world, often remaining confined to a single room. This condition, often linked to excessive internet use, can be seen as a response to overwhelming pressures from family, school, and societal expectations, particularly in an environment perceived as hostile or unsupportive. From a psychodynamic perspective, hikikomori can be understood as a form of “psychic retreat” (Steiner, 1993) a defense against relational difficulties and unresolved internal conflicts. These adolescents construct an internal fortress, shielding themselves from the intolerable realities of interpersonal life. Teenagers suffering from both hikikomori and social anxiety may have failed to establish a stable internal sense of self, one capable of tolerating the demands of social life and permitting the development of a healthy sense of autonomy. Social isolation thus becomes a way to maintain a fragile sense of control. Subjective Experience of Social Anxiety Disorder in Adolescents Affective Patterns The affective experience of this disorder in adolescence is characterized by pervasive anxiety and shame, which may disrupt the development of a cohesive sense of self, leading to fragile self-­esteem and excessive sensitivity to social feedback. Adolescents with social anxiety disorder often experience a profound sense of uneasiness during interpersonal exchanges. Underlying feelings of inadequacy and worthlessness fuel their fears of negative evaluation by others. The anticipation of social interactions can trigger intense anxiety, in the form of panic or dread; even minor exposure to social contexts can evoke acute embarrassment and a sense of danger. This chronic state of hyperalertness to social cues and fear of potential criticism contributes to pervasive feelings of vulnerability and emotional discomfort that can feel overwhelming, leading to withdrawal and isolation. Adolescents with social anxiety may experience a constant, even persecutory anxiety of negative evaluation, projecting their fears onto the social world, thus experiencing it as hostile and/or threatening.

chunks.heading: 566 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 566 Adolescence Cognitive Patterns Teenagers with social anxiety disorder show pronounced negative self-­perception and cognitive distortions. They tend to overestimate the likelihood of negative outcomes during interpersonal exchanges and interpret ambiguous social cues as signs of rejection or rid...
chunks.text: 566 Adolescence Cognitive Patterns Teenagers with social anxiety disorder show pronounced negative self-­perception and cognitive distortions. They tend to overestimate the likelihood of negative outcomes during interpersonal exchanges and interpret ambiguous social cues as signs of rejection or ridicule. These adolescents often suffer anticipatory anxiety, worrying obsessively about upcoming social interactions and ruminating over past encounters. Cognitive distortions such as catastrophizing and erroneous attributions regarding others’ intentions and mental states are common, leading teenagers to believe that others are constantly scrutinizing and judging them negatively. Rumination also frequently concerns the adolescent’s own behaviors and somatic reactions that can reveal anxiety (e.g., blushing) during social interactions. These are deemed highly visible to others and seen as potential causes of derision or ostracism. Distorted cognitive patterns can perpetuate anxiety and patterns of avoidance, preventing teenagers from developing a more balanced and realistic view of self and others. Somatic States Somatic manifestations of social anxiety disorder in adolescence vary and are often severe. Adolescents may experience symptoms such as sweating, trembling, blushing, and gastrointestinal distress in anticipation of or during social interactions. These bodily responses are not only experienced as particularly distressing but also reinforce the cycle of anxiety and avoidance, as the fear of displaying these symptoms in public becomes a significant source of anxiety. Muscle tension, headaches, and fatigue are also common, reflecting the chronic stress and hyperarousal associated with sustained social anxiety. Relational Patterns The relationship patterns of adolescents with social anxiety disorder are profoundly influenced by their difficulties in managing emotions, particularly anxiety and shame. These teenagers often struggle with dependency and autonomy, as their fear of negative evaluation can lead to excessive dependence on close family members for emotional support and social decision-­making. This can hamper their ability to achieve a sense of autonomy and confidence in social contexts. Attempts to assert independence may be thwarted by overwhelming anxiety and fear of social failure, complicating the separation–­individuation process. Teenagers with social anxiety also often report heightened sensitivity to social rejection and criticism. This can result in avoidant behavior, including withdrawal from social interactions to protect against perceived threats of humiliation and rejection. Or it may be expressed in becoming overly clingy and seeking constant reassurance from trusted individuals. In close relationships, internalized anger and frustration at perceived social limitations can be expressed as irritability or conflict. Subjective Experience of the Therapist Due to their feelings of inadequacy and fears of rejection, teenagers with social anxiety disorder may appear uncomfortable, ashamed, guarded, noncompliant, or withdrawn from the therapeutic relationship, especially during the early phases of treatment. As therapy progresses and trust is built, there may be a shift to excessive dependency,

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 567
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 567 with the adolescent viewing the therapist as a crucial source of validation and support. These patterns can elicit a range of countertransference responses, from protective and nurturing instincts to feelings of frustration and helplessness. SA41.3 Agoraphobia Both a...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 567 with the adolescent viewing the therapist as a crucial source of validation and support. These patterns can elicit a range of countertransference responses, from protective and nurturing instincts to feelings of frustration and helplessness. SA41.3 Agoraphobia Both agoraphobia and panic disorder are described in more detail in Chapter 14 (pp. 797–798). The essential feature of agoraphobia is excessive anxiety about being in (or anticipating being in) situations from which escape may be difficult or in which help may not be available. It involves panic attacks (i.e., abrupt surges of intense fear or discomfort, accompanied by physical and/or cognitive symptoms) in response to an unexpected or typically feared object or situation. Often, the adolescent has the thought that something dire is about to happen. The symptoms occur in multiple contexts, such as using public transportation, being in open or enclosed spaces, being in a crowd, or being alone outside the home. Adolescents may believe that escape would be difficult or that help would be unavailable in the event of panic or incapacitating symptoms. Their anxiety can vary depending on proximity to the feared environment, occurring during both anticipation and actual exposure. Teenagers may actively avoid these situations, seek the presence of a companion, or feel terror if they do confront them. Severe agoraphobia can lead to complete home confinement, resulting in excessive dependence on caregivers. This isolation may contribute to feelings of demoralization, depressive symptoms, and the misuse of substances like alcohol and prescription drugs. Agoraphobia is not uncommon in adolescence. It is often associated with longterm psychiatric problems (Creswell, Waite, & Cooper, 2014). In DSM-IV, agoraphobia was considered a qualifier of panic disorder. With the introduction of DSM-5, criteria for agoraphobia were revised to make it a separate and codable diagnosis, adding the requirement of fear in multiple situations. This modification was made to prevent overdiagnosis based on exaggerated perceptions of danger or occasional fears, which may be common in the teenage years. One study found that one-­fourth of adolescents who met the DSM-IV criteria for agoraphobia no longer met DSM-5 criteria. Nevertheless, these individuals showed substantial symptomatology and functional impairment across various areas, much like those meeting the updated criteria (Cornacchio, Chou, Sacks, Pincus, & Comer, 2015). These teens also reported high levels of anxiety sensitivity and internalizing psychopathology compared with those diagnosed with agoraphobia under DSM-5 criteria. Therefore, during this critical life phase, it is essential to address anxiety sensitivity and related functional impairments to ensure that young individuals showing agoraphobic symptoms receive appropriate and effective psychotherapeutic and pharmacological treatments. SA41.4 Panic Disorder Panic disorder is a debilitating psychiatric condition characterized by recurrent and unexpected panic attacks, that is, by abrupt surges of intense fear or discomfort that reach a peak within minutes. These experiences are often accompanied by physical and cognitive symptoms, followed by a persistent concern about additional attacks (or their consequences). Such fears may impel significant behavioral changes. Panic attacks can occur in any state of calmness or anxiety when specific situations or stimuli (external or internal) trigger them. They can also arise without any apparent cause. Adolescents may have difficulty identifying the antecedents of states of panic, stating

chunks.heading: 568 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 568 Adolescence that their anxiety can arise at any time, without any discernible association with external conditions or internal sensations. Research suggests that panic disorder often appears in the teen years, with mean age of onset in late adolescence. Fantasies surrounding separation and indep...
chunks.text: 568 Adolescence that their anxiety can arise at any time, without any discernible association with external conditions or internal sensations. Research suggests that panic disorder often appears in the teen years, with mean age of onset in late adolescence. Fantasies surrounding separation and independence are common areas of conflict for patients with panic disorder, especially teenagers. Difficulties in expressing and modulating anger are frequent concomitants of anxiety as well. Panic among adolescents can function as a way to express a need for care or as a form of self-­punishment over guilt about dependency needs. Even more than adults, teenagers diagnosed with panic disorder tend to be avoidant (e.g., of bars, restaurants, crowded places, small and large rooms, elevators, parks, and stores).
