Adolescents diagnosed with anxiety disorders without agoraphobia may experience fewer disruptions in their daily lives compared to those who also present with agoraphobic symptoms. Furthermore, when compared to adolescents with other anxiety disorders, those suffering from panic disorder (regardless of the presence of agoraphobia) tend to exhibit higher levels of depressive symptoms. These factors contribute to greater distress and anxiety associated with panic attacks, often limiting the ability of adolescents to participate in their usual activities. Comorbid psychiatric conditions, such as substance use disorders, are common, further complicating the clinical presentation and treatment approach. At all ages, panic attacks occur across various mental health diagnoses, including generalized anxiety disorder, social anxiety disorder, and posttraumatic stress disorder. Recognizing this frequency across various conditions, the DSM-5 introduced a panic attack specifier applicable to all diagnoses. During adolescence, the presence of panic attacks represents a clinically significant indicator of greater disorder severity and increased comorbidity risk. Up to 90% of adolescents diagnosed with panic disorder have co-­occurring psychiatric conditions. Panic in adolescents is thus unlikely to be an isolated symptom. Research has identified four distinct personality subtypes in both adolescents and adults suffering from panic disorder: a high-­functioning group (healthy personality structure), an emotionally dysregulated group (with characteristics associated with borderline personality disorder), an avoidant group (characterized by feelings of inadequacy, shame, and guilt), and a somatizing group (characterized by chronic anxiety, hypochondria, and obsessive tendencies; see also Chapter 8). Patients who closely align with one or another of these diagnostic prototypes differ significantly in terms of etiology, comorbidity, global functioning, and prognosis. This finding has vital clinical implications: Brief panic-­focused treatments may be suitable for high-­functioning adolescents whose primary concern is panic. Such treatments also show promise in ameliorating panic symptoms in avoidant patients. They may prove insufficient, however, for emotionally dysregulated adolescents, and they fail to address personality pathology within any of the subgroups. Incorporating a focus on personality in the treatment of panic disorder, along with considering specific areas of mental functioning that show impairment, may yield more positive outcomes for adolescents who have not responded well to primarily symptom-­based approaches. Subjective Experience of Panic Disorder in Adolescence Affective Patterns Adolescents with panic disorder experience emotional states that largely overlap with those suffering from other conditions primarily related to anxiety, as described earlier in this chapter. Their deep apprehension about the possible recurrence of the attacks

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 569
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 569 can make it difficult for them to enjoy activities or concentrate on tasks. The persistent nature of these affective states perpetuates the cycle of panic and reinforces emotional distress. In addition to concerns about panic attacks and their aftermath, adolescents...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 569 can make it difficult for them to enjoy activities or concentrate on tasks. The persistent nature of these affective states perpetuates the cycle of panic and reinforces emotional distress. In addition to concerns about panic attacks and their aftermath, adolescents frequently experience ongoing or intermittent anxiety related to broader issues of health and well-being. For example, they may anticipate severe consequences arising from seemingly minor physical symptoms or potential side effects of medication. They may worry excessively about trivial aches or interpret harmless bodily sensations as signs of imminent danger. Feelings of helplessness and vulnerability are also prevalent. The unpredictable nature of panic attacks can leave teens feeling powerless and out of control during a period when reliance on caregivers can evoke conflicting feelings. They may struggle with guilt and shame, particularly because the physical signs of panic attacks (e.g., sweating, shaking, or rapid breathing) are often noticeable to those around them. Heightened self-­consciousness can lead to feelings of embarrassment, as these adolescents worry about being judged or misunderstood by peers. Previously enjoyed interpersonal activities can become restricted, impairing social development, academic performance, and overall quality of life. Cognitive Patterns Early adolescents tend to report panic-­related cognitive symptoms (e.g., fear of going crazy, losing control, dying) less frequently than somatic ones, which tend to be the main focus of their distress. By midadolescence, however, teens have developed the cognitive skills (e.g., abstract reasoning) that can lead them to make errors in understanding their distressing experiences: The cognitive component of panic disorder may therefore become more apparent. They often misinterpret somatic sensations as proof of an imminent catastrophe or threat, an interpretation that intensifies their anxiety. During a panic attack, they may think that their rapid heartbeat signifies imminent heart failure, that their dizziness means they will faint and never regain consciousness, or that their physical sensations will invite humiliation, making them unable to face others again. Nevertheless, adolescents typically have trouble recognizing these patterns, believing that their attacks of panic occur unpredictably and without any systematic relationship to environmental situations, internal sensations, and/or thoughts. Somatic States The most common somatic states associated with panic include palpitations, nausea, abdominal discomfort, alternating hot and cold sensations, profuse sweating, trembling, shaking, and shortness of breath. Adolescents often see these acutely uncomfortable symptoms as proof that a threat is present. They often describe feeling trapped, with panic attacks acting as a constrictive force that separates them from the flow of everyday life. Some also experience numbness, a sense of detachment from their body, or a feeling of unreality, though these symptoms are less commonly reported by children and adolescents than by adults with panic disorder. Panic symptoms in adults may also take longer to reach their peak intensity. Relational Patterns Similar to those with other disorders characterized by anxiety, adolescents with panic disorder frequently show heightened dependence on caregivers and close family

chunks.heading: 570 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 570 Adolescence members. This bid for reassurance and support often arises from their fears of having a panic attack in situations where they feel unsafe. An adolescent’s requests can burden familial relationships, however, as caregivers may oscillate between providing support and encouraging indepe...
chunks.text: 570 Adolescence members. This bid for reassurance and support often arises from their fears of having a panic attack in situations where they feel unsafe. An adolescent’s requests can burden familial relationships, however, as caregivers may oscillate between providing support and encouraging independence. These adolescents often feel disconnected and isolated, believing that others cannot truly comprehend their attacks. Any perceived lack of understanding, dismissal, or minimization of the adolescent’s distress evokes frustration and anger. But when others respond to a panic attack with too much intensity, the teenager feels additional distress. These patterns can perpetuate a cycle of withdrawal and avoidance (often rationalized as a measure to avoid disappointment), further isolating the adolescent from social interactions. Subjective Experience of the Therapist Therapists may grapple with countertransference reactions that vary in intensity depending on the severity of their patient’s symptoms. When faced with patients with a debilitating and severe panic condition, clinicians may experience feelings of insecurity, fear, and heightened anxiety, often inclining them toward an overly caring and/ or overly directive stance. This reaction can manifest itself in the therapist’s offering excessive advice to adolescent patients and/or trying to exert some degree of control over their decisions, and/or doubting their ability to manage their condition independently. Clinical Illustration Paula, a 13-year-old girl, was brought for assessment for recent severe panic attacks. A psychiatrist had prescribed an antidepressant and an anxiolytic, and recommended attention to the dynamics of the anxiety. This girl was a straight-­A student, popular among peers, and active in sports. The onset of the anxiety seemed related to her concern about weight gain and attractiveness to boys. Although she did not have a weight problem, she had made several unsuccessful attempts at dieting. Her mother was concerned about her worries, but her father teased her for being “fat.” Paula felt enraged at her father and alienated from her mother, who did not protect her from the teasing. At first, even though their daughter was extremely distressed, the parents did not take her attacks seriously. In time, they escalated in intensity, at which point her parents became overly concerned, and the daughter felt vindicated in having convinced them of her distress. In therapy, she focused increasingly on issues of independence and autonomy. When the attacks diminished in intensity and became more manageable, Paula was able to move on developmentally. SA41.5 Generalized Anxiety Disorder Generalized anxiety disorder is described in more detail in Chapter 14 (pp. 760–874). In adolescence, its key feature is persistent and excessive anxiety across multiple domains, perceived as difficult to control and extending beyond specific situations. These concerns encompass fears about poor academic performance, personal failings and mistakes, a loved one’s becoming ill or passing away, parental divorce, personal health crises, global catastrophes, and natural disasters. Teens struggling with generalized anxiety often experience concomitant somatic symptoms, including restlessness, heightened vigilance, debilitating fatigue, compromised concentration or cognitive

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 571
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 571 clarity, irritability, muscular tension, and disturbances in sleep patterns. They consistently report experiencing an overwhelming sense of apprehension and hypervigilance in their daily activities. When general anxiety emerges during adolescence, it frequently persi...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 571 clarity, irritability, muscular tension, and disturbances in sleep patterns. They consistently report experiencing an overwhelming sense of apprehension and hypervigilance in their daily activities. When general anxiety emerges during adolescence, it frequently persists into adulthood, posing a significant risk for the subsequent development of psychiatric conditions, including mood and substance use disorders. Empirical evidence has supported the efficacy of both pharmacological and psychotherapeutic treatments for the disorder during the teen years. The strongest evidence points to the separate or combined use of SSRIs and cognitive-­behavioral therapy. Early detection and treatment of childhood anxiety disorders can prevent substantial impairment and functional disability. The concerns of teenagers diagnosable with generalized anxiety disorder frequently revolve around their performance and competence levels in academic and athletic endeavors, even in the absence of external evaluation. They often describe perfectionism and excessive self-doubt. They tend to seek others’ approval and reassurance about their performance and other areas of concern. The diagnosis of generalized anxiety disorder in adolescents requires careful differentiation from other anxiety disorders. The symptoms may overlap with those of specific phobias, social anxiety disorder, and separation anxiety disorder. Affective, cognitive, somatic, and interpersonal responses resemble those of other anxiety conditions. Subjective Experience of Generalized Anxiety Disorder in Adolescents Affective States In adolescents, the subjective experience of generalized anxiety disorder includes fear, a pervasive sense of danger, and a heightened state of alertness. When particularly intense, the anxiety may be accompanied by a sense of impending doom. In milder cases, especially in middle and late adolescence, teens often describe a vague sense of worry and restlessness or use terms like “stress” and “tension” to articulate their feelings. In all phases of adolescence, anxiety can result in increased irritability and frustration, even in the face of small triggers; teenagers’ emotional responses may feel excessive and magnified, negatively affecting their relationships with peers, family, and authority figures. Feelings of helplessness and hopelessness may arise, accompanied by a sense of being overwhelmed. This experience can exacerbate emotional distress and have a negative effect on mood, ability to feel positive emotions, and quality of life. Excessive anxiety can hinder communication and lead to feelings of shame, fear of misunderstanding, and to consequent reluctance to share experiences. This can further erode confidence, concentration, and optimism about the future. Cognitive Patterns Moderate to severe anxiety can impair performance and thinking processes in general: Rumination and cognitive distortions (similar to those observed in adults) are common features of anxiety syndromes, including generalized anxiety disorder. Adolescents with this condition are more likely than individuals in other age groups to interpret ambiguous or neutral stimuli as threatening or dangerous, even when there is no supporting evidence. They can overestimate the likelihood of negative outcomes, perceiving threats where few exist and then engaging in avoidance behaviors, which

chunks.heading: 572 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 572 Adolescence in turn reinforce emotional dysregulation in anxiety-­inducing situations and reduce any sense of mastery. A diminished ability to cope with emotions and anxiety-­related somatic states may lead to seeing them as aversive or harmful or as evidence of an imminent catastrophe. This seq...
chunks.text: 572 Adolescence in turn reinforce emotional dysregulation in anxiety-­inducing situations and reduce any sense of mastery. A diminished ability to cope with emotions and anxiety-­related somatic states may lead to seeing them as aversive or harmful or as evidence of an imminent catastrophe. This sequence intensifies symptoms and sets in motion a cycle of emotional dysregulation and distress. Selective attention toward anxiety-­provoking stimuli can result in a narrow focus on perceived dangers (internal and external) that, combined with still developing executive functioning abilities, can make it challenging for teenagers to shift attention from threatening stimuli to neutral and positive aspects of their surroundings. Somatic States Generalized anxiety disorder has numerous physical manifestations, including alterations in motor activity and changes in posture. Some individuals engage in increased gross motor activity to discharge their anxious state, a behavior that provides temporary relief but does not contribute to the development of a sense of mastery; it may even maintain and amplify an overall sense of discomfort. Physiological reactions include accelerated heart rate, increased or decreased breathing, palpitation, sweating, trembling, and increase in galvanic skin responses. Muscle tension (e.g., tightness in the neck, shoulders, or jaw) is common and can lead to local discomfort or headaches. Prolonged or chronic anxiety may aggravate somatic problems such as eczema and neurodermatitis. Unmanaged anxiety can affect gastrointestinal functioning and evacuation. Behaviors such as nail-­biting, picking at scabs and sores, and hair pulling are also common among adolescents. They sometimes report dizziness or lightheadedness and feelings of being “unsteady” or close to fainting, particularly in situations in which they fear losing control or being negatively judged. Sometimes they describe heightened sensitivity to noise, light, touch, or other sensory inputs. Relational Patterns Adolescents with generalized anxiety disorder often struggle to balance their need for dependency with their desire for autonomy. They may rely heavily on caregivers for emotional support and decision making, hindering their ability to develop a sense of self-­efficacy. They may shrink from making independent choices or taking responsibility for themselves and may rely excessively on others to manage anxiety and provide reassurance. Teenagers with generalized anxiety disorder may fear the loss of parental approval and support as they strive for independence, resulting in ambivalence toward the separation–­individuation process itself. Anxiety may originate from repressed aggressive impulses or unresolved conflicts with parents or other significant figures; when this is the case, it may manifest through expressions of aggression such as irritability or defiance, further complicating adolescents’ relationships. Fear of abandonment or rejection is common and can lead to clinginess, excessive need for support and care, or avoidance of intimacy to protect against potential emotional pain. Subjective Experience of the Therapist During the initial phase of therapy, adolescents with generalized anxiety disorder may appear difficult to reach. They can be guarded, reluctant to open up, and resistant to connection. As treatment progresses, they can become excessively dependent on, and

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 573
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 573 demanding of, their therapist, who can be idealized and viewed as the sole provider of safety. This adulation may foster unrealistic expectations of the clinician’s constant availability and reassurance. Consequently, for the adolescent, any perceived rejection or de...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 573 demanding of, their therapist, who can be idealized and viewed as the sole provider of safety. This adulation may foster unrealistic expectations of the clinician’s constant availability and reassurance. Consequently, for the adolescent, any perceived rejection or detachment can elicit strong negative reactions, including feelings of abandonment, anger, or deepened anxiety. In the therapist, such patterns can evoke feelings of frustration, overwhelm, or constraint. SA5 Obsessive–Compulsive and Related Disorders SA51 Obsessive–Compulsive Disorder Obsessive– ­compulsive disorder is described in more detail in Chapter 14 (pp. 760– 874). In adolescence, it is an impairing condition characterized by specific distressing symptoms, including repetitive, intrusive thoughts (obsessions) and time-­consuming rituals (compulsions). Although symptoms can appear in children as young as age 4, they tend to emerge between ages 6 and 9. New or recurrent onset is possible in early adolescence and young adulthood, likely in response to developmental challenges. Once considered rare in youth, obsessive–compulsive disorder has been found to have an estimated prevalence of 0.25 to 4% in children and adolescents. As in childhood, these conditions in adolescence frequently co-occur with other psychopathology, including tics, anxiety disorders, and depression. Although this disorder may fluctuate in severity, it usually follows a chronic course, leading to significant impairment across home, school, and social settings. Academic performance may suffer because of time-­consuming rituals and intrusive thoughts, leading to difficulties in concentrating, completing assignments, and participating in classroom activities. The etiology of pediatric obsessions and compulsions remains poorly understood. While genetic factors clearly influence their expression, the effects of environmental factors remain in need of further investigation. Onset is often associated with stressful life events such as the loss of a loved one, relocation, parental divorce, changes in educational settings, or school-­related difficulties. Such events can trigger intense anxiety in adolescents, leading them to adopt compensatory strategies aimed at reducing arousal to more manageable levels. Obsessive–compulsive phenomena may stem at least in part from fears of criticism and an exaggerated sense of personal responsibility. This hypersensitivity to criticism may reflect an intense concern about social acceptance. Adolescents may engage in checking compulsions, for example, to preempt disapproval. The content of obsessions often centers on behavioral potentials (e.g., sexual and aggressive impulses) that teens perceive would evoke criticism and rejection from others if acted upon. Multiple obsessions and rituals can coexist. Adolescents may use the defenses of undoing, reaction formation, and isolation of affect to cope with distressing thoughts, urges, or actions. The ruminations of teenagers suffering from obsessive–compulsive disorder often concern potential harm or loss to themselves or their loved ones. During adolescence, the syndrome is marked by an increased prevalence of sexual obsessions (e.g., extreme worry about sexual orientation or sexual acts with family members), indicating a relationship between intrusive thoughts and the social and moral challenges of this stage of life. Cognitive-­behavioral therapy has been found to be the most effective form of treatment in alleviating obsessive and compulsive symptoms. Specific techniques such

chunks.heading: 574 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 574 Adolescence as exposure and response prevention are particularly effective for adolescents of higher cognitive maturity whose symptoms are of short duration. Pharmacological treatment (e.g., SSRIs) can be considered for severe cases or when nonpharmacological interventions are insufficient. Choi...
chunks.text: 574 Adolescence as exposure and response prevention are particularly effective for adolescents of higher cognitive maturity whose symptoms are of short duration. Pharmacological treatment (e.g., SSRIs) can be considered for severe cases or when nonpharmacological interventions are insufficient. Choice of treatment should be influenced by symptom duration, severity, cognitive maturity and level of motivation, family dynamics, and previous psychotherapeutic experiences. Subjective Experience of Obsessive–Compulsive Disorder in Adolescents Affective States As with the other disorders in this chapter, the primary affective experience of individuals with obsessive–­compulsive disorder is pervasive anxiety that seems disproportionate to the threat with which they are preoccupied. In adolescents with this disorder, its source is usually not clear. Both parents and teenagers tend to focus on the anxiety itself rather than its origins. Yet the characteristic patterns of symptom onset suggest that the sources of many obsessions and compulsions lie in the developmental challenges posed by this life phase. For example, anger and fears of loss of control often underlie the anxiety of adolescents with obsessions and compulsions. These emotions can intensify the symptoms, further amplifying the adolescent’s distress. Adolescents with obsessions and compulsions may also experience other aversive emotions, including fear, guilt, and disgust about their recursive thoughts, urges, or images. These emotions can be intense, sometimes more so than their anxious feelings. Cognitive Patterns Thoughts and fantasies include a preoccupation with obsessional ideas or compulsive rituals in the service of defending against underlying anxiety. Especially in early adolescence, teens may have limited insight into their obsessions and compulsions, often equating thoughts with actions and perceiving them as dangerous and in need of immediate suppression. At all ages, obsessive–­compulsive teenagers may overestimate the potential for a catastrophic outcome, at the same time underestimating their ability to and misjudging their degree of responsibility for real or imagined disasters. All these patterns, in turn, heighten the need to resume their compulsions. Cognitive inflexibility is common in adolescents with this disorder, who may have an unrealistic need for certainty and reassurance. Somatic States Headaches, nausea, cramps, and stomachaches are frequent concomitants of obsessive and compulsive symptoms. Adolescents with contamination obsessions often have skin health problems (e.g., cracked, bleeding skin, or infections from compulsive sanitizing). Sleep disturbances are common. Teenagers with obsessions and compulsions often have a heightened sensitivity to, and hyperawareness of, bodily functions and physical sensations, and often have concerns about bodily integrity. They may fixate on the pace of their breath or the rate of their blinking or heartbeat. They may monitor all physical cues of sexual excitement and experience unwanted sexual arousal in response to obsessive thoughts or situations. This “groinal response” can trigger intense shame, guilt, and confusion, further exacerbating their distress.

chunks.heading: 576 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 576 Adolescence SA52 Body Dysmorphic Disorder (Dysmorphophobia) Body dysmorphic disorder is described in more detail in Chapter 14 (pp. 760–874). In DSM-5, the syndrome is included among obsessive–­compulsive disorders, with the possible specifier of “muscle dysmorphia” (i.e., the preoccupation or b...
chunks.text: 576 Adolescence SA52 Body Dysmorphic Disorder (Dysmorphophobia) Body dysmorphic disorder is described in more detail in Chapter 14 (pp. 760–874). In DSM-5, the syndrome is included among obsessive–­compulsive disorders, with the possible specifier of “muscle dysmorphia” (i.e., the preoccupation or belief related to the perception that one’s physique is too small, lean, or lacks sufficient muscle mass). In ICD-11, body dysmorphic disorder is placed within “obsessive–­compulsive or related disorders” whose degree of insight may be specified as “with fair to good insight” or “with poor to absent insight.” Body dysmorphic disorder is a debilitating psychological condition characterized by preoccupations with perceived bodily flaws and physical appearance. Although actual flaws are often minimal or nonexistent (Windheim, Veale, & Anson, 2011), adolescents diagnosed with this problem have a genuine, heartfelt belief in their physical ugliness, which provokes overwhelming anxieties about deformity, inadequacy, and judgment by others (Phillips, 2004). The central theme of the disorder is a delusional obsession centered on physical appearance. Adolescents with this preoccupation may describe themselves as unattractive and deformed. Although the face is frequently the primary source of concern, they may be worried about any region of the body (Windheim et al., 2011). The delusional preoccupations in body dysmorphic disorder appear to be relatively difficult to inhibit or control. Teenagers may spend several hours a day focusing on or attempting to modify their appearance. Although onset is typically in early adolescence, it can develop in middle to late childhood (Phillips, 2004). Symptoms tend to be consistent with several other psychological disorders and conditions, including those of depression, social phobia, obsessive–­compulsive disorder, eating disorders, and several personality disorders (Buhlmann & Winter, 2011). These similarities can interfere with the recognition of body dysmorphic disorder in adolescents, further complicating diagnosis. This disorder has major implications for social development, adjustment, and transitioning into adulthood (Phillips, 2004). Unfortunately, the problem often goes unnoticed and unrecognized by teachers, friends, family, and health care professionals (Buhlmann & Winter, 2011). Studies from community samples suggest a general prevalence rate of 0.7–2.9%, while those in clinical samples are significantly higher. Body dysmorphic disorder tends to be more prevalent in females than in male adolescents. The disorder compromises the psychosocial functioning of all affected individuals, especially adolescents (Brewster, 2011). Although research remains relatively limited, the integration of psychotropic medication and CBT appears to provide the best outcome for individuals with this diagnosis (Phillips, 2004). Given its adverse effects on adolescents, understanding the mechanisms of the disorder and promoting awareness of it are paramount in improving acceptance, decreasing stigma, and increasing access to care. Subjective Experience of Body Dysmorphic Disorder in Adolescents Affective States A negative or alien conception of one’s body in adolescence can cause extreme distress, shame, and anxiety. Body image is a central concern at that time, often a foundational element of self-­esteem. Fear of negative evaluation by peers, intensified by social media platforms where teens compare their appearance, can be paralyzing. Adolescents with

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 577
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 577 body dysmorphia may become angry if reassurances are not forthcoming, or if caregivers deny desired cosmetic interventions, or if cosmetic solutions do not have the anticipated outcome. Suicidal thoughts and delusional somatic beliefs are common among afflicted adole...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 577 body dysmorphia may become angry if reassurances are not forthcoming, or if caregivers deny desired cosmetic interventions, or if cosmetic solutions do not have the anticipated outcome. Suicidal thoughts and delusional somatic beliefs are common among afflicted adolescents, more so than among adults. Preoccupations about one’s body parts reflect an unstable, unintegrated sense of self and may substitute for thinking about emotions. Symbolic expression and creativity may be diminished when emotions are expressed only through physical complaints. Cognitive Patterns The range of reflective function in patients diagnosed with body dysmorphic disorders, as for those diagnosed with obsessive–­compulsive disorders, is broad. Some adolescents can acknowledge that their body-­oriented preoccupations may be exaggerated, while others have virtually no capacity to see their condition objectively. In those with poor insight, there may be delusional beliefs or repetitive psychotic thoughts; they may be in complete denial about the irrationality of their bodily concerns. They may be unable to see that these beliefs have a psychological origin or that they are related to the perception of the other’s gaze. Fixations on body parts may come with clinically relevant obsessive–­compulsive symptoms, such as incessant grooming, mirror checking, or trichotillomania. A persistent, anxious focus on real or perceived body defects may lead to cognitive problems such as poor attention, difficulties in focusing on other matters, and impairment in school, work, and psychosocial functioning. Somatic States Somatic states in adolescents with body dysmorphia may include hyperarousal linked to intense somatic anxieties and complaints about their body or bodily sensations. Relational Patterns Because they tend to avoid social situations for fear of being humiliated for their perceived flaws, adolescents with body dysmorphia may miss many aspects of normal social interaction. Leaving the house may feel impossible to them. Staying away from school, work, sports, and social events may make it hard for them to make and keep friendships and romantic connections. The extrafamilial relationships these adolescents maintain may be damaged by avoidance and repeated demands for reassurance concerning their physical appearance. Subjective Experience of the Therapist In treating patients with body dysmorphia, typical countertransference reactions include detachment, dissatisfaction, and feelings of inadequacy caused by a sense of powerlessness. These feelings may reflect the difficulties therapists encounter in engaging these adolescent patients in feeling and processing their emotions. Therapists should not dismiss concerns about patients’ appearance as unimportant or delusional and instead should empathize with their patients’ suffering. Psychotherapy can reestablish a connection between words and emotions, mitigating the self-­hatred and shame about the perceived body defect.

chunks.heading: 578 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 578 Adolescence SA6 Somatic Symptom Disorder and Related Disorders Disorders of somatization (described in Chapter 14, pp. 760–874) are defined by an intense focus (disproportionate thoughts, feelings, and behaviors) on physical phenomena that cause significant distress and/or interfere with daily f...
chunks.text: 578 Adolescence SA6 Somatic Symptom Disorder and Related Disorders Disorders of somatization (described in Chapter 14, pp. 760–874) are defined by an intense focus (disproportionate thoughts, feelings, and behaviors) on physical phenomena that cause significant distress and/or interfere with daily functioning. Under this heading, DSM-5 includes somatic symptom disorder, illness anxiety disorder (“hypochondriasis” in DSM-IV), functional neurological symptom disorder (“conversion disorder” in DSM-IV), and factitious disorders. ICD-11’s “disorders of bodily distress or bodily experience” include these conditions, plus bodily distress disorder and body integrity dysphoria. SA61 Somatic Symptom Disorder Somatic symptom disorder is characterized by distressing bodily symptoms that psychosocial factors are considered to play a role in initiating, maintaining, or exacerbating. In DSM-5, DSM-5-TR, and ICD-11 (whose prior “somatoform disorders” has been replaced by “bodily distress disorder”), the diagnosis is based on the combination of somatic symptoms and excessive attention directed toward those symptoms, rather than on the absence of a medical explanation for somatic problems. Adolescents with this disorder may feel fear and distress in response to persistent or recurrent bodily symptoms, and may have problematic thoughts, feelings, and behaviors related to these health concerns. The patients’ suffering is not illusory and is often symptomatic of diagnosed medical pathology. However, the level of attention they devote to their illness often appears disproportionate to its actual nature and severity. Adolescents with somatic symptom disorder experience their medical pathology in intense or dysfunctional ways. They may complain about physical problems: stomachaches, headaches, urinary or bowel urgency, and other problems. Although an adolescent usually does not associate the symptoms with particular stressors, they appear to be related to situations that evoke anxiety. Symptoms often begin spontaneously or after a minor illness; teen patients are typically convinced that they represent a purely physical problem. A pattern ensues in which the young person progressively uses the symptoms in the service of avoidance. This pattern may generalize to the point that the adolescent appears debilitated by the symptoms. Somatization processes underlying these clinical manifestations are typically related to a lack of insight, low mentalization, and poor emotional awareness. In adolescence, the body undergoes striking changes that can place it at the center of affective and relational conflicts and turn it into the main vehicle for expressing distress. The capacities for identifying one’s own emotions, processing and expressing them, and recognizing one’s own and others’ mental states are beginning to develop (cf. MA Axis). The ability to understand, regulate, and express affects is strongly influenced by an adolescent’s ability to construct sufficiently articulated and complex mental representations of self (including body image) and others. Some authors have offered the concept of “embodied mentalization” to define the capacity to see the body as the seat of emotions, and to reflect on one’s physical experiences and sensations (Luyten, van Houdenhove, Lemma, Target, & Fonagy, 2012). Difficulties with embodied mentalization may be related to interpersonal situations involving high arousal or stress. Research by Chudleigh et al. (2019) found that youth diagnosed with somatic symptom disorder experience a heightened state of physiological arousal and encounter challenges in regulating their bodily state.

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 579
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 579 When treating adolescents with somatic disorders, it may therefore be useful to focus on promoting mentalization skills. These should improve their ability to modulate the intensity of emotional states and prevent their anxieties about bodily changes from turning int...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 579 When treating adolescents with somatic disorders, it may therefore be useful to focus on promoting mentalization skills. These should improve their ability to modulate the intensity of emotional states and prevent their anxieties about bodily changes from turning into somatic symptoms. In other words, by helping adolescents develop a deeper understanding of the complex interplay between thoughts, emotions, and mental representations (involving bodily needs and urges), therapists can enable them to cope more effectively with their emotional and social lives, thus promoting their psychological well-being and their maturation. Subjective Experience of Somatic Symptom Disorder in Adolescents Affective States Adolescents with somatic symptom disorder are terrified about illness and usually appear to be in great pain. Their distress is compounded if they feel that caregivers question the reality of their illness. Adolescents who feel disbelieved may feel injured, protest loudly, and become enraged. They are often genuinely unable to access other emotions, including those that may contribute to their symptoms; the terms “alexithymia” (Sifneos, 1972) and “affective agnosia” (Lane, Weihs, Herring, Hishaw, & Smith, 2015) have been coined to describe this inability to find words for affects. Cognitive Patterns The adolescent’s thoughts often focus on the particular somatic symptoms, and there is often a narrowing of age-­expected interests. Some adolescents with this pattern report fear more characteristic of younger children. Patients’ conviction about the gravity of the illness leads to concerns about dire physical consequences, which may be either vague and ill-­defined or specific and elaborate. They may believe they are seriously ill and in danger of dying. Somatic States With these patients, there is always the possibility of a serious medical condition going undiagnosed due to physicians’ assumptions that the adolescent is overreacting to a minor issue. Whatever its ultimate physiological status, the physical suffering of adolescents with this disorder is real and often debilitating. Relational Patterns Depending on the dynamics that trigger the somatic symptoms, relationships may be reasonably intact or may have severe regressive features. In the latter case, the adolescent may become excessively fearful of separating from caregivers or may become withdrawn and isolated. Relationships may be characterized by massive dependency (which prevents the attainment of greater autonomy), and new relationships may be avoided. Subjective Experience of the Therapist Treating adolescents with somatic symptom disorder is difficult. Therapists often find that developing a shared understanding of the symptoms is a time-­consuming and

chunks.heading: 580 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 580 Adolescence arduous process. These young patients focus attention on concrete concerns about bodily illness and fail to access a symbolic representation of the body. For clinicians, the sense that the therapy is going much too slowly may lead to feelings of helplessness and worry. They may becom...
chunks.text: 580 Adolescence arduous process. These young patients focus attention on concrete concerns about bodily illness and fail to access a symbolic representation of the body. For clinicians, the sense that the therapy is going much too slowly may lead to feelings of helplessness and worry. They may become frustrated by repetitive discussions of the organic causes of the symptoms and have significant difficulty helping the adolescent find psychological factors behind the somatic complaints. Feelings of boredom are common. The alexithymia of somatizing patients may evoke a disengaged reaction. Countertransference reactions thus include impatience, helplessness, excessive expectations, anger, rejection, insecurity, an impulse to act, or a feeling of becoming insignificant to the patient. Whether or not a physical origin of the adolescent’s symptoms can be found, taking their pain and anxiety seriously may prevent an escalation of those complaints, which are unconsciously designed to demonstrate just how bad the suffering is. When patients and therapists are able to work together to identify psychological factors related to physical symptoms, clinicians may find that working with these patients becomes rewarding. Clinical Illustrations Anastasia, a 12-year-old daughter of immigrants, became unable to walk or move. Neurological tests failed to explain her paralysis. In therapy, she revealed an intense conflict about whether to identify with her “new” culture (implying a move away from her parents) or to stay in the “old” one with her parents (and avoid anxieties about new challenges). After 6 months in treatment, she began to move around the consultation room, and 2 years later, she was back in school functioning normally. Ben, a 14-year-old boy, had trouble swallowing. He could not get his breakfast down or take medicine, but in the therapist’s office, he had no trouble downing a hamburger. He had ongoing conflicts with his mother, a strict disciplinarian, with whom he would provoke fights. His therapist saw his symptom as expressing his difficulty “swallowing” what his mother was “feeding” him. It remitted once he expressed his anger about her harsh discipline. SA62 Illness Anxiety Disorder (Hypochondriasis) Illness anxiety disorder (described in more detail in Chapter 14, pp. 760–874) is a preoccupation with, and unrealistic fear of, having or developing a serious disease in the absence of medical evidence of an illness. The term “hypochondriasis,” a label that many now see as pejorative, was previously applied to this syndrome. ICD-11 retains this label and places the condition within the group of obsessive–­compulsive and related disorders, based on the perseverative cognitions and behaviors characteristic of these clinical conditions. Adolescents with illness anxiety disorder ruminate constantly about their physical health. Their fears may derive from misinterpreting nonpathological physical symptoms or normal bodily functions (e.g., abdominal bloating and crampy discomfort, awareness of heartbeat, sweating). They make frequent appeals to family members, peers, or healthcare professionals for reassurance, which provides only temporary relief before anxiety returns. Symptoms are often vague and may change. Some adolescents recognize that their fear is unreasonable or unfounded. Transient hypochondriacal states frequently arise during periods of developmental change; thus, adolescence is a favorable time for the appearance of anxiety about

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 581
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 581 illness. Adolescents need time and experience to integrate their physical changes into their overall mental representation of their body. Even when no medical abnormalities are found, adolescents with this disorder may suffer from vivid images and relentless worries...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 581 illness. Adolescents need time and experience to integrate their physical changes into their overall mental representation of their body. Even when no medical abnormalities are found, adolescents with this disorder may suffer from vivid images and relentless worries that something specific is wrong with their bodies. Some may, out of embarrassment, hide their concerns. This concealment can lead to further anxiety and to obsessive and compulsive behaviors. Treatment of illness anxiety disorder includes establishing a consistent, supportive physician–­ patient relationship; cognitive-­ behavioral therapy has been found to be helpful (Sørensen, Birket-­Smith, Wattar, Buemann, & Salkovskis, 2011; Wicksell, Melin, Lekander, & Olsson, 2009). Subjective Experience of Illness Anxiety Disorder in Adolescents Affective States Adolescents with hypochondriacal concerns often have symptoms of depression and anxiety. They tend to see mild somatic problems as signs of grave illness. Their constant anxious preoccupation with the threat of illness impels them to seek recurrent reassurances. Cognitive Patterns and Somatic States Adolescents with illness anxiety disorder are absorbed by recursive negative thoughts and ruminations, including alarm about the putative malfunctioning of a body part or system and an overall sense of the precariousness and vulnerability of their physical integrity and health. Actual bodily sensations are misinterpreted, often as confirmation of an organic disorder. In severe cases, there are obsessive thoughts, delusional ideas, and catastrophic fantasies about one’s own body, which can severely hamper psychological and social functioning. Relationship Patterns Adolescents with illness anxiety often feel alone, rejected, or misunderstood, especially if they perceive that their somatic complaints fall on deaf ears. They may feel that being “sick” is the only way they can be cared for, seen, and heard. Some seem to have an unconscious wish to control others through their hypochondriacal behavior, which may, at least initially, elicit solicitous and responsive concern and keep them alive in the mind of the other. When these adolescents do not feel acknowledged in their suffering, their overall psychological functioning may deteriorate. It is very difficult to reassure them. Subjective Experience of the Therapist Therapists treating patients with illness anxiety disorder may be troubled by their tendency to be uncooperative and demanding. They may feel manipulated as well. These adolescents typically feel frustrated or angry if their disproportionate somatic concerns are “downsized” by the therapist. Because illness has become crucial to their identity, trying to reduce their worry can reinforce deep-­seated anxieties about neglect. Clinicians may find themselves thinking about their own health and mortality. It is a challenge to be nonjudgmental and to embrace the sense of helplessness and

chunks.heading: 582 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 582 Adolescence vulnerability felt by these patients, while remaining objective about their exaggerated and persecutory somatic concerns. SA63 Factitious Disorders Factitious disorders are described in more detail in Chapter 14 (pp. 760–874). Following DSM-5, PDM-3 places this group under “Somatic S...
chunks.text: 582 Adolescence vulnerability felt by these patients, while remaining objective about their exaggerated and persecutory somatic concerns. SA63 Factitious Disorders Factitious disorders are described in more detail in Chapter 14 (pp. 760–874). Following DSM-5, PDM-3 places this group under “Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders”; ICD-11 includes it in a separate, dedicated category. “Malingering,” defined as faking illness when the individual has a clear, conscious motive (e.g., to benefit financially or to avoid school or work), is considered a separate condition. A growing body of research indicates the presence of factitious disorders within younger populations, but further studies are needed to increase our knowledge of these conditions in adolescence. In the factitious disorder generally seen in adulthood, once called “Munchausen’s syndrome,” a person intentionally produces or feigns physical or psychological symptoms. In childhood and adolescence, it is more common to see “Munchausen’s syndrome by proxy,” in which a parent (or sometimes a nurse or other caregiver) repeatedly causes illness or physical harm to another person. The perpetrator denies playing any role and comes to medical personnel expressing great distress over the health crisis of the person harmed. In contrast to malingering, there is no obvious secondary gain, though there may be some psychological reward in the attention received during medical dramas created by factitious crises. There are few studies of the etiology, family patterns, or predisposing factors, but parents with this diagnosis typically have a prior history of early (and often traumatizing) medical treatment. Adolescents with factitious disorders share many of the clinical characteristics seen in adults, with the notable exception that they are more inclined to admit to their fabrications and accept psychotherapy. Despite the difficulty in formulating accurate diagnoses in this archipelago of heterogeneous clinical conditions, when confronted with adolescents reporting unexplained symptoms or implausible medical histories, therapists should include factitious disorders in their differential diagnoses. Early identification of these syndromes is crucial to avoid counterproductive and detrimental referrals, especially considering that adolescents may injure themselves, ingest substances, or require invasive medical procedures to suggest the presence of a specific illness or induce a medical outcome. At this time, there are no official guidelines for the treatment of adolescent factitious disorders. An interdisciplinary effort by specialized teams with professionals from different backgrounds would be helpful in framing the problem and planning more timely interventions tailored to these patients. Subjective Experience of Factitious Disorder in Adolescents Affective States Adolescents with fictitious disorders may report anxiety and dysphoria. If challenged about the illness they claim to have developed, they may react with irritation, hostility, and anger. In adolescence, self-harm that is associated with deception can be closely intertwined with emotional instability and identity diffusion. There may be high impulsivity, lack of remorse, and little regard for the consequences of one’s behavior for oneself and others.

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 583
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 583 Cognitive Patterns These adolescents are absorbed in thoughts related to the simulation of medical or psychological signs and symptoms. They may use different strategies of faking or inventing physical problems, reporting bodily abnormalities, complaining of difficul...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 583 Cognitive Patterns These adolescents are absorbed in thoughts related to the simulation of medical or psychological signs and symptoms. They may use different strategies of faking or inventing physical problems, reporting bodily abnormalities, complaining of difficulties, or procuring harm for the purpose of presenting themselves as sick or injured. They appear obsessed with persuading themselves and others of the reality of their illness and suffering. In the most extreme cases, or at times of severe decline in general functioning, they may show perceptual abnormalities (including hallucinations) and disturbances of thought process or content. Somatic States On a somatic level, these adolescents are in a state of chronic tension. To obtain attention, emotional support, and medical care, they may use substances, induce illness in themselves or others, and cause severe and even irreversible harm. They may disregard the risk of medical procedures, even invasive ones, in the service of concealing and falsifying their health conditions and exaggerating the severity of their illness to family, friends, or doctors. Relational Patterns It is usually very difficult to connect with adolescents with factitious disorders. If others confirm the seriousness of their condition, they appear complacent, but if they perceive disbelief, lack of attention, or confrontation about their role in injuring or sickening themselves, they may become hostile and aggressive. It is typical for others to feel deceived, betrayed, and manipulated by these teens, and frustrated by their withdrawal from connection when they are not believed. The relationships they do manage to establish are characterized by pathological dependency, superficiality, and lack of reciprocity. Subjective Experience of the Therapist Adolescents with factitious disorders may elicit intense negative countertransferences, including irritation and hostility about their deceptions. These reactions may lead to ignoring or underestimating the patient’s somatic complaints. Clinicians may also feel helpless in the face of their inability to assess the reality of a patient’s medical situation. They may feel intensely frustrated when young patients are disinclined to admit to their fabrications and are uninterested in exploring possible motives behind their symptoms. SA7 Feeding and Eating Disorders Feeding and eating disorders (also discussed in Chapter 14, pp. 760–874; hereafter shortened to “eating disorders”) include several persistent disturbances of eating or eating-­related behavior, resulting in altered food consumption or absorption and significant impairment in health or psychosocial functioning. We focus here on anorexia

chunks.heading: 584 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 584 Adolescence nervosa, bulimia nervosa, and binge-­eating disorder, which frequently have onset in adolescence. Pica, rumination, and avoidant/restrictive food intake disorders, also included in the DSM-5-TR category of feeding and eating disorders, tend to occur earlier, in infancy and childhood,...
chunks.text: 584 Adolescence nervosa, bulimia nervosa, and binge-­eating disorder, which frequently have onset in adolescence. Pica, rumination, and avoidant/restrictive food intake disorders, also included in the DSM-5-TR category of feeding and eating disorders, tend to occur earlier, in infancy and childhood, and these conditions are often related to other developmental disorders. The frequent onset of eating disorders in adolescence is related to the physiological and psychological changes that occur at this time. Puberty induces hormonal variations, body composition changes, and food intake regulation. This complicates young individuals’ adjustment to their bodily changes and maturing sexuality as puberty proceeds. During adolescent individuation and empowerment, many transient and deviant food choices or dysfunctional eating behaviors may occur, including bingeing, craving, nibbling, and other temporary deviations from normative eating habits. Restrictive behaviors, weight loss, and dietary deficiencies may delay the process of puberty and interfere with the development of secondary sex characteristics. Thus, anorexia, bulimia, binge eating, and related behaviors may have dramatic repercussions for puberty and growth, along with an increased risk of osteoporosis, gastrointestinal complications, and nutritional abnormalities. Anorexia nervosa is the most commonly diagnosed eating disorder in clinical populations during the peripubertal period (70–80%), with frequency peaks at about the ages of 12–13 and 17–19. Early-onset anorexia (ages 12–13) may have long-­lasting effects on critical developmental processes. However, younger patients typically show less body image distortion, self-­induced vomiting, and purging. In addition to the usual food avoidance, they also tend to restrict fluid intake. Late-onset anorexia may involve severe body dissatisfaction and more purging behaviors and psychiatric comorbidities. In addition to restricting their food intake, adolescents with anorexia may engage in excessive exercise, self-­induced vomiting, and improper use of diuretics and laxatives, all of which contribute to maintaining underweight status or frank malnutrition. In Western populations, the prevalence of diagnosed anorexia among adolescents has remained relatively stable over the past few decades, with higher cases in females than in males. Epidemiological data show increasing prevalence in non-­Western areas, particularly in eastern Asia. Adolescents with bulimia nervosa represent approximately 16.4–36.5% of teens with eating disorders. Their attempts to control their eating and weight may be interrupted by episodes of bingeing and subsequent compensatory behaviors. Whereas anorexia has a peak age of onset in early to middle adolescence, bulimia typically emerges during or after late adolescence. Teens with bulimia have reported higher rates of suicidality than adults with that diagnosis, and higher rates than youth with other eating disorders. More than half endorsed suicidal ideation, and many of these patients had a plan or a prior attempt at suicide. Bingeing episodes in adolescence may be hard to differentiate from nonclinical behaviors, as youth may consume food copiously during growth spurts. Some argue that the best criterion for binge-­eating disorder in adolescence, especially early adolescence, is a feeling of loss of control over eating. Although the onset of the disorder is commonly placed in late adolescence and young adulthood, some researchers have found an early reported onset (median of 12.6 years) in community samples of adolescents ages 13–18; in a sample of teens seeking treatment for obesity, researchers found the reported onset of the first episode of bingeing to be 10.8 years. Binge eating disorder prevalence rates are between 1 and 3%, with about twice as many cisgender females as males reporting this problem.

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 585
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 585 Eating disorders, especially in adolescence, need to be approached from biological, psychological, familial, and sociocultural perspectives. Standard classifications should not be limited to somatic or nutritional criteria (e.g., weight changes or vomiting) but shoul...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 585 Eating disorders, especially in adolescence, need to be approached from biological, psychological, familial, and sociocultural perspectives. Standard classifications should not be limited to somatic or nutritional criteria (e.g., weight changes or vomiting) but should include psychological criteria (e.g., fear of being fat, disturbance in the experience of body weight or shape, horror of weight gain and obesity), and behavioral criteria (e.g., intake restriction, binge eating, purging, compulsive exercising, muscle building, laxative use). Variations in these criteria across cultures should be noted. Cisgender adolescents assigned female sex at birth constitute the vast majority of reported cases, whereas in younger patients with restrictive eating disorders, epidemiological data suggest a reduced female-­to-male ratio. Among adolescents with nonheterosexual identities, especially males, there is more dieting, bingeing, purging, and ingestion of diet pills. Several studies have found evidence that in gender-­diverse adolescents the onset of puberty and the associated bodily modifications represent a significative risk for developing eating disorders. The most recent studies and guidelines for treating adolescents with eating disorders recommend multidisciplinary care, including regular somatic, nutritional, psychosocial, and psychiatric interventions. Treatments should be aimed not only at restoring weight, altering disordered eating attitudes, and treating any medical complications, but also enhancing autonomy, facilitating identity formation, and increasing self-­esteem. The participation of the family is essential for adolescents with problematic eating. Like adults, adolescents with eating disorders display diverse patterns of functioning, including obsessive–­compulsive, narcissistic, and borderline traits. It is also arguable that severely anorexic patients, who believe they must restrict eating even while at realistic risk of starvation, represent inherently a condition at the psychotic level of personality functioning (see Chapters 12 and 8 on the P and PA Axes, pp. 468–522 and pp. 651–708, respectively). Patients with bulimia and anorexia with purging tend to be at a borderline level of functioning. Personality traits and disorders may contribute to the persistence of symptoms from adolescence into later developmental stages, and the persistence of an eating disorder over the years may exacerbate certain pathological personality traits. Some studies have found higher impulsive/dysregulated traits in adolescents with bulimia compared to those with other eating disorders. Youth with anorexia show more trait anxiety, emotional inhibition, and social isolation than young people with other eating disorders or those whose eating is not pathological. Three personality profiles have emerged in samples of adolescents with eating disorders: (1) high-­functioning/perfectionistic, distinguished by not only severe perfectionism and self-­criticism but also several psychological strengths; (2) dysregulated, with emotional dysregulation, impulsivity, and a tendency to manipulate; and (3) avoidant/ depressed, characterized by feeling worthless, needy, helpless, inadequate, depressed, and unassertive. Like adults, adolescents with eating disorders of all kinds show migrations among diagnostic categories and subtypes. Most with an initial diagnosis of anorexia experience diagnostic crossover over time, either from one to another subtype or from anorexia to bulimia. Premorbid problems with weight, or with early onset of the binge-­eating–­purging subtype, are risk factors for long-term bulimic disorders. Fewer patients with bulimia develop anorexia nervosa, a condition that seems much more resistant to change. Psychiatric comorbidity, temperament, familial factors, and anthropometric–­symptomatic factors seem to predict crossover and evolution of eating symptomatology.

chunks.heading: 586 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 586 Adolescence Subjective Experience of Eating Disorders in Adolescents Affective States Adolescents with eating disorders typically show a chronic difficulty recognizing, identifying, and managing emotions. They experience feelings as uncontrollable, rapidly oscillating, intense, painful, and thre...
chunks.text: 586 Adolescence Subjective Experience of Eating Disorders in Adolescents Affective States Adolescents with eating disorders typically show a chronic difficulty recognizing, identifying, and managing emotions. They experience feelings as uncontrollable, rapidly oscillating, intense, painful, and threatening to a vulnerable self. Such impairments in affective functioning sometimes arise from a primary relationship with caregivers who failed to provide adequate mirroring and created an environment that was unresponsive or demanding, resulting in less room for the elaboration of emotional experiences. Dysfunctional eating behaviors may represent strategies to regulate and deal with such situations. Common affective states may include self-­ depreciation, low self-­ esteem and empathy, and difficulty in self-­affirmation. Alexithymia is common, as is avoidance in coping with conflicts. Food may then offer the primary or only means of emotional self-­expression. Anxious and depressive mood is also common, often associated with self-­harming methods of coping with distress. Shame and embarrassment may be activated in social contexts: Adolescents with anorexia tend to be ashamed of their body weight and shape, whereas adolescents with bulimia experience intense emotions of shame and guilt, related to their tendency to hide their bulimic behavior, which may extend beyond food to self-harm and addiction. Dependency and self-­criticism are often part of the clinical picture—­not surprisingly, given that a major problem of adolescents with eating disorders is a struggle for autonomy and self-­ definition that is compromised by incomplete separation–­ individuation during early childhood. Adolescents with anorexia show high dependency features and fear abandonment. Self-­critical (introjective) depression seems a more specific feature of patients with bulimia and may reflect the fragility of their identity and their difficulties regulating self-­esteem. Individuals who binge-eat may eat compulsively to try to escape negative aspects of self-­awareness and to control the dysphoric states associated with borderline-­level personality organization. Cognitive Patterns Adolescents with eating disorders may exhibit significant cognitive rigidity, with heightened attention to details, expressed in maladaptive eating routines and rituals such as calorie-­counting and body-­checking (e.g., fixating on the size of a specific body part or on the presence of a space between thighs). Control over emotions and actions can take the form of obsessive symptoms that go beyond food themes and may be exerted in a tyrannical way that neglects basic bodily needs. Any failure in control may then trigger intense shame and guilt. The fantasy lives of these patients tend to be impoverished, and their thoughts tend to be concrete. Preoccupations with being undervalued, inadequate, or unappreciated by caregivers or peers may lead to defensive strategies such as omnipotent control and intellectualization. Dysfunctional eating in adolescents seems to preserve a small degree of ownership of body and mind, providing a sense of safety and protection at several levels, fostering a sense of personal agency and allowing them to regain self-­control and stability. Cognitive patterns may include negative attitudes about transitioning from childhood to adulthood, leading to avoidant behaviors (e.g., maintaining a child-like physical appearance by reducing weight that affects endocrine stimulation, height, and

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 587
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 587 secondary sex characteristics) or counterphobic acts (e.g., high-risk sexual behaviors). An adolescent may depict this paradox in terms of having a split personality: a façade that is rational and an anorexic–­bulimic self that is completely irrational. The concretiz...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 587 secondary sex characteristics) or counterphobic acts (e.g., high-risk sexual behaviors). An adolescent may depict this paradox in terms of having a split personality: a façade that is rational and an anorexic–­bulimic self that is completely irrational. The concretization of this split may take the form of inner voices guiding and controlling the adolescent’s behavior. Some adolescents with eating disorders perceive their condition as part of their identity and consider any modification of their eating as imperiling their core sense of self. Somatic States Adolescent patients with eating disorders may deny certain physiological sensations. They frequently note vague physical complaints or perceive intense states such as pain, disgust, and guilt when eating. Sensation that their bodies are suddenly “bulging,” “swelling,” and “huge” after meals are common. Concerns about body weight, shape, and size are ubiquitous, often expressing themselves in an excessive tendency to check the mirror and to measure body parts that are perceived as indicators of self-­esteem and self-worth. Like adults with eating disorders, adolescents may have trouble distinguishing between mental and somatic states and may have a weak interoceptive awareness, that is, a difficulty in perceiving or interpreting stimuli arising from the body. Patients may develop rigid, repetitive, and automatic behavioral patterns—­such as the hyperactivity often seen in individuals with anorexia or the binge–purge cycles typical of bulimia—­which temporarily alleviate distress and help restore a sense of inner equilibrium. In the long term, such practices may activate the brain’s reward centers in ways that parallel those observed in addictive disorders involving substances. Relational Patterns Adolescents with eating disorders often find social contexts threatening because of their deep feelings of insecurity, lack of confidence, and sense of inadequacy. They may suffer from bullying, victimization, social exclusion, and rumor-­mongering from peers, often related to their weight and/or appearance, which may worsen their dissatisfaction with their body and their disordered eating. They may subordinate personal relationships to performance, viewing social environments as contexts of evaluation or criticism rather than as sources of relationship or care. As a consequence, they may adopt strategies of withdrawal, isolation, or angry opposition, seeing the other as despicable or as an antagonist to be defeated. Relations with peers and love objects are characterized by desires for dependence and emotional exclusivity, and by difficulty in managing boundaries. Relationships with parents are sometimes reversed; adolescents with eating disorders may try to exert control over their parents’ behaviors or feelings. They may avoid separations, because they trigger their eating disorders. This social impairment might be the counterpart of overattachment to parents. Current theories consider disordered eating to reflect an arrest in the separation–­individuation process, often traceable to a caregiver’s failure to provide essential functions during development. Symptoms may represent attempts to cope with needs that stem from this incomplete maturation. However, social pressures to be thin and physically “perfect” also play a major role in eating disorders.

chunks.heading: 588 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 588 Adolescence Subjective Experience of the Therapist Adolescents with eating disorders can evoke intense countertransferences that may vary with the type of disorder and/or personality. Those who are alexithymic and controlling can arouse feelings of frustration, hopelessness, and helplessness. Ad...
chunks.text: 588 Adolescence Subjective Experience of the Therapist Adolescents with eating disorders can evoke intense countertransferences that may vary with the type of disorder and/or personality. Those who are alexithymic and controlling can arouse feelings of frustration, hopelessness, and helplessness. Adolescents with bulimia who are more impulsive and emotionally unstable can provoke feelings of coldness and a sense of incompetence. Adolescents with eating disorders with trauma histories involving parental abuse can evoke therapists’ compassionate overinvolvement. This wide spectrum of reactions can affect the treatment process and outcome. When working with adolescents with eating disorders, clinicians need to be attuned to their countertransferences and manage them with sensitivity. Clinical Illustration Anita, a 14-year-old girl with no history of childhood obesity, had begun to lose weight after menarche. She was an excellent student, described by her parents as a rigorous perfectionist. She had no best friend or boyfriend and shunned peers in favor of schoolwork and running competitions. Her beloved older brother had left for college the preceding year. Her restrictive eating had begun then, reportedly because she was feeling fat and uncomfortable with pubertal body changes. Initially, she had avoided sugar, fat, and snacks, and had to fight against hunger. She started controlling her food and trying to control her parents’ eating and cooking habits. The parents suspected laxative abuse and noted some obsessive–­compulsive symptoms. More recently, she was refusing family meals and insisting on eating alone with her mother, whom she called continually throughout the day. She told her physician she was doing well; she simply felt too fat. She was angry with him for prohibiting her physical exercise because of her dangerously low weight. Because of the severity of her weight loss, massive denial, anorexic cognitions, physical hyperactivity, purgative behaviors, obsessive–­compulsive practices, and fused relationship with her mother, hospitalization was deemed necessary. SA8 Event‐ and Stressor‐Related Disorders The event- and stressor-­related disorders are clinical conditions in which exposure to a traumatic or stressful event has been an explicit diagnostic criterion. These include adjustment disorders, acute stress disorder, PTSD, complex PTSD, dissociative disorders, and conversion disorders. In DSM-IV, disorders related to traumatic and stressful events fell under anxiety disorders. In DSM-5 and 5-TR they are considered separate diagnostic categories because some patients with these symptoms do not seem anxious; instead, they may complain of anhedonia or dysphoria, anger, aggression, dissociation, or other painful mental state. In ICD-11, these conditions are categorized as “disorders specifically associated with stress.” For the entire SA8 group of symptom patterns, we offer more information in Chapter 14 of this manual (pp. 760–874). SA81 Trauma- and Stressor‐Related Disorders SA81.1 Adjustment Disorders In the DSM-5-TR, adjustment disorders are included in the new category of traumaand stressor-­related disorders. Their core feature is emotional or behavioral symptoms

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 589
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 589 in response to an identifiable stressor. Events such as relocation, family discord, conflicts with peers, change of school, separation from a loved one, loss of a pet, termination of a romantic relationship, parental divorce, or harassment can trigger and sustain mal...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 589 in response to an identifiable stressor. Events such as relocation, family discord, conflicts with peers, change of school, separation from a loved one, loss of a pet, termination of a romantic relationship, parental divorce, or harassment can trigger and sustain maladaptive reactions. In all cases, the individual’s experience of distress seems out of proportion to the severity of the stressor and produces impairments in several areas of functioning. Maladaptive reactions to the stressor emerge within 3 months of the event and can be marked by anxiety, depressed mood, or disturbances of conduct (either in isolation or in combination). Adjustment disorders in adolescents require special attention, as their long-term outcomes may be more severe than those in adults. Adapting treatment to the maturational stage and unique needs of each adolescent is essential for fostering resilience and promoting lasting positive outcomes. Treating adolescents with these problems requires a multifaceted approach that takes into account both their emotional and cognitive responses to the stressor(s) and their developmental stage. The empirical literature suggests integrating methods that have been effective for other psychological conditions, such as anxiety and depression (O’Connor & Cartwright, 2012). These include many approaches, including therapies from cognitive-­behavioral (including newer techniques from third-wave CBT), gestalt, humanistic, emotion-­focused, and psychodynamic sources. The goal of treatment is to enhance the adolescent’s coping skills, alleviate psychological distress, and restore more adaptive functioning. Prognosis is favorable, typically resulting in complete alleviation of symptoms. There are, however, several factors that can significantly interfere with recovery from adjustment disorders, including personality functioning, multiple psychological stressors, socioeconomic status, and absence of an adequate support network. Subjective Experience of Adjustment Disorders in Adolescents Affective States As mentioned earlier, adjustment disorders can involve anxious or depressive affects, alone or in combination. These are focused on an identifiable stressor whose nature, along with the individual’s subjective perception of it, influences the patterns of emotional response. During adolescence, reactions to adverse events can vary widely, from attempts to increase self-­mastery to using regressive defenses. Often these responses coexist, resulting in affective and behavioral patterns that differ from those typically observed in adults. The internal experience of loss, sadness, or threat can be difficult for adolescents to perceive, express, and modulate. Anger can intensify in response to feelings of anguish, fear, and being overwhelmed, resulting in disturbances in behavior. This life stage includes greater emotional oscillations, which in turn may lead to unpredictable alternations between withdrawing and seeking social support. Adolescents tend to make decisions based on feelings. They may misuse substances, especially during middle and late adolescence, to cope with sad or anxious thoughts. Aggressive and/or risk-­taking actions, suicidality, self-harm, and provocative behaviors are common ways for adolescents with adjustment disorders to express their emotions. Such actions can also be used to conceal depressive and anxious states. Cognitive Patterns Experiencing little or no control over events may lead teenagers to adopt a cognitive style characterized by hopelessness, helplessness, self-­criticism, and/or self-­deprecation,

chunks.heading: 590 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 590 Adolescence especially when multiple stressors are present. Cognitive patterns may be centered around a sense of loss and/or injustice (usually associated with depressed mood and problematic conduct), or around a sense of danger and vulnerability (usually associated with anxiety). Excessive rumi...
chunks.text: 590 Adolescence especially when multiple stressors are present. Cognitive patterns may be centered around a sense of loss and/or injustice (usually associated with depressed mood and problematic conduct), or around a sense of danger and vulnerability (usually associated with anxiety). Excessive rumination may arise as a maladaptive coping strategy. Heteroaggressive (more common in males) and self-­attacking behaviors (more common in females) may be interpreted as specific ways to escape from adolescents’ perception of passivity and lack of agency. Somatic States Adolescents with adjustment disorders (especially younger ones) may experience physical symptoms such as headaches, palpitations, stomachaches, or changes in eating or sleeping habits. Psychomotor restlessness may be present. Relational Patterns Relational patterns in adolescents with adjustment disorders reflect their struggles to cope with significant life changes or stressors. Teenagers may exhibit heightened sensitivity to perceived rejection or criticism, leading to problematic interactions with peers, family members, and authority figures. Difficulties in forming and maintaining close relationships can emerge, as adolescents with adjustment disorders may exhibit withdrawal, irritability, avoidance. Their emotional responses may be intense and unpredictable, further damaging their ability to connect with others.
