Avoidance of social situations is particularly frequent when the clinical picture involves anxious states, whereas teenagers experiencing depressed mood can appear emotionless, distant, and scarcely engaged in interpersonal interactions. Subjective Experience of the Therapist When working with adolescents with adjustment disorders, therapists may feel frustrated or helpless in the face of an adolescent’s fluctuating emotional states or resistance to change. They may also have warm, empathic responses to the adolescent’s distress. The complexities arising from comorbid conditions, such as personality disorders, or when the clinical presentation is characterized by multiple risk factors, can lead to countertransferences that are more challenging to manage, as the interaction between the adjustment disorder and other issues can amplify emotional dynamics within the therapeutic relationship. Clinical Illustration Carlo, a 14-year-old boy with no prior antisocial acts, committed repeated robberies at school. His presentation was fierce and aggressive; he refused to speak to the psychiatrist assigned to him. His parents spoke of the declining health of his younger brother, with whom he had been highly competitive before his diagnosis of leukemia. Carlo refused to speak about his brother. A week later, he was expelled from school for insulting a teacher. Suddenly, after his fourth consultation, he came into the office in tears, describing a dream in which he saw his brother dying despite his own efforts to help him. His dream seemed to have lowered his sense of culpability and allowed him to speak about his deep sadness and anxiety. After several consultations (both with

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 591
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 591 and without his parents), his depressed mood lifted, and his aggressive and antisocial behaviors stopped. SA81.2 Acute and Posttraumatic Stress Disorders In DSM-5, acute stress disorder and posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) are distinct diagnostic categories. Both...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 591 and without his parents), his depressed mood lifted, and his aggressive and antisocial behaviors stopped. SA81.2 Acute and Posttraumatic Stress Disorders In DSM-5, acute stress disorder and posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) are distinct diagnostic categories. Both describe maladaptive responses to a traumatic event involving reliving the danger or reacting through modes associated with it. Acute stress disorder is a short-term condition that typically occurs in the aftermath of the event and lasts up to 1 month. It can progress to PTSD, or it may be a transient stress response that remits after the traumatic exposure. Because approximately half of those who develop PTSD are initially diagnosable with an acute stress disorder, that diagnosis may help to identify adolescents who are at risk for long-term maladaptive responses to a traumatic event and who may thus benefit from early intervention. We note that a significant proportion of adolescents who develop PTSD do not at first meet the diagnostic criteria for acute stress disorder. Symptoms can worsen after the first month, often because of ongoing life stressors or further traumatic events. Current research supports using adult criteria for diagnosing PTSD in adolescents (i.e., persistent recurrence of intrusive symptoms concerning the traumatic event or related stimuli; persistent avoidance of these situations; marked alterations of thoughts and emotions associated with the traumatic event(s) or increasing arousal and reactivity; possible presence of dissociative symptoms). Nonetheless, clinicians should consider the unique aspects of this developmental stage. Teenagers face notable challenges, including constructing a coherent sense of self, learning to recognize and regulate emotions of increasing complexity, forming supportive relationships with peers, and gaining independence from parental figures. These transitions can increase the likelihood of maladaptive reactions to traumatic events. Given the immaturity of their cognitive, social, and emotional abilities and the relative instability of social networks in adolescence, teenagers’ capacity for adaptive coping in the face of adversities may be diminished. Avoidance, emotional reactivity, and risk taking are often part of normal adolescence; after a traumatic event, they can be exacerbated, significantly aggravating the clinical picture. Neurobiological changes that occur during this life phase make teenagers (particularly during middle and late adolescence) more receptive to the rewards of sensation seeking and other impulsive and affective-­based behaviors, while simultaneously being less sensitive to their potential costs. These factors not only heighten teenagers’ risk of developing PTSD after a traumatic event but also make psychotherapeutic intervention more complex, especially in the presence of multiple risk factors. The potential effects of co-­occurring traumatic experiences and the significant interplay between trauma and loss have been confirmed empirically. Particularly in the case of high-risk populations, repeated adverse events are quite common: A significant percentage of teens report having experienced six or more traumatic events from early to late adolescence. This can lead to having more symptoms, and more severe symptoms, of PTSD. The influence of trauma on the developing self may be difficult to interpret purely in terms of psychopathology, particularly when dissociative responses appear, or when the initial traumatic event is unknown. The experience of one or more life-­threatening events can influence attitudes, expectations, personal choices, and the ability to construct healthy and supportive relationships with peers. It may also interfere with neurobiological development, negatively affecting cognitive and emotional abilities, mood,

chunks.heading: 592 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 592 Adolescence self-­esteem, coping styles, and behaviors. Trauma can thus exert a profound and disruptive effect on an adolescent’s development, potentially determining long-term negative outcomes and heightening the likelihood of a plethora of mental disorders. If PTSD symptoms are detected durin...
chunks.text: 592 Adolescence self-­esteem, coping styles, and behaviors. Trauma can thus exert a profound and disruptive effect on an adolescent’s development, potentially determining long-term negative outcomes and heightening the likelihood of a plethora of mental disorders. If PTSD symptoms are detected during the assessment process, we recommend that, if possible, the clinician conduct a formal evaluation involving the teenager’s caregivers. This is critical to ensure that the PTSD is not missed or misdiagnosed. In adolescents, PTSD can resemble other conditions, such as ADHD, anxiety disorders, oppositional defiant disorder, or depressive disorders; differential diagnosis is thus essential. Once the adolescent is diagnosed with PTSD, clinical guidelines suggest adopting a holistic and comprehensive treatment approach that takes comorbid psychiatric disorders into account. Trauma-­focused therapies should be the first line of treatment. These guidelines emphasize addressing the symptoms directly, engaging parents in the therapeutic process where appropriate, and going beyond symptom reduction to foster adolescents’ resilience, empowerment, and sense of safety. This approach increases the chances of restoring normal developmental pathways. Treatment decisions may vary, depending on severity, from solely providing psychotherapy to combining psychotherapy with medication. Subjective Experience of Acute and Posttraumatic Stress Disorders in Adolescents Affective States Most affective states that characterize adolescent acute or posttraumatic stress resemble those experienced in adulthood. Some are reinforced, however, by the uniqueness of this life phase, which can affect the ability to identify, regulate, and communicate emotions. After a trauma, adolescents are particularly vulnerable to shock, confusion, anhedonia, asthenia, and emotional numbness. They may find it hard to access positive emotions and may feel endlessly sad, frightened, anxious, guilty, and/or ashamed. These states can be hard for traumatized teenagers to recognize and express, and all can be maintained in a vicious cycle by the cognitive alterations produced by trauma. Feelings of guilt, shame, and humiliation—­more pronounced when the traumatic event has an interpersonal nature—­can lead to heightened anger and aggressiveness (directed at both oneself and others), and to a desire for retaliation. Posttraumatic shame is a risk factor for the onset and maintenance of psychological difficulties, and a significant predictor of the severity of PTSD symptoms. It is a common reason for not seeking help and for reluctance to disclose and discuss traumatic experiences with others. This is particularly relevant during adolescence. Teenagers are deeply concerned about how they are evaluated by other people; shame is a key affective experience in this phase, even when the individual’s growth is not affected by trauma. The persistence and pervasiveness of feelings of shame, fear, anxiety, and guilt (all common reactions to an adverse event) can be interpreted by traumatized adolescents as proof of their incapacity to elicit positive reactions in others or to adapt to their social environment. They may also be afraid of losing control over their emotions or of having their traumatic memories resurface in an overwhelming way. These fears may generate or exacerbate a desire to recoil from social interactions, increasing the likelihood of enduring negative mood and generating the risk of academic difficulties or school

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 593
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 593 withdrawal. In the attempt to regulate their feelings, teenagers may be more susceptible to adopting maladaptive strategies such as substance use or eating disturbances. Feelings of being valueless, unlovable, or otherwise subject to rejection or dislike can elicit f...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 593 withdrawal. In the attempt to regulate their feelings, teenagers may be more susceptible to adopting maladaptive strategies such as substance use or eating disturbances. Feelings of being valueless, unlovable, or otherwise subject to rejection or dislike can elicit further fear, perceived humiliation, and helplessness, which in turn create heightened anger, resentment, and aggressive behaviors, directed either outward or inward. Self-harm and suicidal ideation are common in traumatized adolescents, particularly when the traumatic event is associated with shame, as in the case of sexual abuse or sexual or psychological harassment. Feelings of body incompetence and dissatisfaction with body image are also frequent and of notable clinical relevance in these cases. Cognitive Patterns Cognitive patterns may include reexperiencing the traumatic event through flashbacks, ruminations, or nightmares. Such reexperiencing can be spontaneous or triggered by reminders of the event(s). Adolescents suffering severe or multiple adversities may develop negative expectations about the future. Pessimistic beliefs may arise, centered around the perception of being ill-fated, worthless, or incapable of returning to a normal life. Other people may be seen as distant, incapable of understanding, or unable to offer adequate support. Fantasies of revenge, reparation, or suicide may accompany such ideas. Persistent concerns about one’s own and others’ personal safety may emerge. Heightened perceived threat and increased cognitive misappraisal following trauma represent significant risk factors for the onset of acute stress disorder or PTSD. Cognitive patterns are characterized by traumatic memories that are usually sensory-­laden, fragmented, and temporally disorganized. Such memories may arise suddenly, despite efforts to suppress them, and may engender significant distress, especially in early adolescence. Rumination on aspects of the trauma is a frequent response to the intrusions of thoughts or memories related to it; this process probably reflects an attempt to regain some sense of control. When PTSD symptoms are pervasive and persistent, autobiographical memories, which enable individuals to construct a sense of identity and continuity, can become overgeneralized, creating a risk factor for developing other psychopathological conditions. Memory and attentional biases for negative information may be responsible for cognitive errors or reduced speed and accuracy of processing, which in turn may contribute to impaired judgment, difficulties in decision making, decreased interest or pleasure in activities, and lowered academic performance. Problems concentrating are common at any age. Somatic States Somatic states are similar to those in adults and are particularly frequent in individuals with high experiential avoidance. Sleep disorders are common, as are reactions such as stomach pain, headaches, tachycardia or tachypnea, muscle tension, accelerated heartbeat, inexplicable sensations of cold or heat, heavy limbs, or general fatigue. During adolescence, somatization can be understood as a complex phenomenon encompassing both psychological and physiological components. Bodily symptoms such as unexplained physical pain or discomfort may serve to communicate distress to others and obtain support and care from attachment figures. They may also indicate the presence of dissociation, by which psychological distress is translated into physical discomfort, allowing the individual to cope with overwhelming emotions by externalizing them as bodily complaints.

chunks.heading: 594 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 594 Adolescence Relational Patterns Adolescents with PTSD often have intricate relational patterns that reflect the profound impact of traumatic experiences on their functioning. The aftermath of trauma can lead to a cascade of complex behavioral changes, influencing how these adolescents engage wit...
chunks.text: 594 Adolescence Relational Patterns Adolescents with PTSD often have intricate relational patterns that reflect the profound impact of traumatic experiences on their functioning. The aftermath of trauma can lead to a cascade of complex behavioral changes, influencing how these adolescents engage with others. Loss of friends may occur as traumatized adolescents grapple with navigating the complexities of their emotions and the experiences they have endured. This struggle may lead them to gravitate toward figures within the peer group who are perceived as sharing similar experiences, behaviors, and attitudes (e.g., a sense of victimization). This can provide a sense of understanding and solidarity but may also inadvertently reinforce negative coping mechanisms. Traumatized adolescents’ emotional turmoil—­marked by irritability, fear, and suspicion—­can lead toward bullying others, inadvertently pushing away potential healthy connections with peers, or toward withdrawing altogether from relational exchanges. Traumatized adolescents often experience fluctuations in self-­esteem, sense of control, and desire to rely on others; they may struggle to establish healthy intimate relationships. Compulsive repetitions of traumatic behaviors often complicate their interactions, leading to behaviors that might puzzle or alarm others. Teenagers suffering from PTSD may unconsciously seek out situations that evoke their past trauma, either to regain a sense of control or (especially in the case of repeated traumatic events) because there is a sense of familiarity in the trauma. These self-­destructive behaviors can result in a cycle of harmful choices and emotional difficulties, often exacerbating relational problems. The distinctive characteristics of this developmental phase introduce additional relational complications. Adolescents, already facing challenges in areas such as identity formation and autonomy, may find it difficult to place trust in adults. A heightened sense of vulnerability and mistrust toward others can lead to a reluctance to establish intimate, meaningful relationships with adults or to discuss their adverse experiences with them. This can further isolate traumatized adolescents, exiling them from crucial support and guidance. Adolescents with PTSD may oscillate between the desire to distance themselves from others, seeking solace in self-­imposed isolation, and a tendency toward hypervigilant expectation of harm from others. The impetus toward self-­punishment (because of the intense feelings described in the section “Affective Patterns”) may lead them to provoke violent confrontations or attract abuse as a means of feeling something other than emotional numbness or as an escape from dissociative states. This complex interplay of behaviors and emotions underscores the complicated and often bewildering relational patterns of adolescents contending with PTSD, highlighting the need for sensitive and well-­informed support on their journey toward healing and healthier attachments. Subjective Experience of the Therapist Countertransference responses to adolescents with PTSD range from physical sensations (e.g., rapid heartbeat, restlessness) to intense positive or negative feelings toward the patient. The client’s traumatic narratives can elicit anxiety, repulsion, and sorrow, as well as anger and a desire for retaliation. Therapists may find themselves feeling inadequate, ashamed, and helpless, and criticize themselves as failures. When the

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 595
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 595 patient describes adverse experiences, they may also feel fear, tension, and rigidity, or a sense of being overwhelmed. With sexually provocative or hostile adolescents, or with those whose PTSD symptoms include maladaptive behaviors such as self-harm or suicide atte...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 595 patient describes adverse experiences, they may also feel fear, tension, and rigidity, or a sense of being overwhelmed. With sexually provocative or hostile adolescents, or with those whose PTSD symptoms include maladaptive behaviors such as self-harm or suicide attempts, it becomes challenging to establish healthy boundaries. Clinicians may find it difficult to set necessary limits—­or may fail to do so—­because their own anxieties about dangerous behaviors lead them to overlook the adolescent’s psychological defenses, for example by pressuring the teen to disclose painful experiences before they are ready. To cope with these countertransferences, some therapists have described emotionally detaching from patients with PTSD, or using defenses such as denial or minimization of the traumatic event(s). Others have described reacting with overinvolvement, enmeshment, and too much emphasis on the traumatic events, inadvertently promoting excessive dependency. SA81.3 Complex Posttraumatic Stress Disorder Complex posttraumatic stress disorder is described in more detail in Chapter 14 (pp. 760–874). Although many traumatic events are time-­limited, some adolescents experience trauma that continues or recurs for months or years. DSM-5-TR’s PTSD diagnosis does not fully capture the severe psychological harm that results from chronic forms of trauma, but the recent edition of ICD-11 includes this category in the context of “disorders specifically associated with stress.” The diagnosis of complex PTSD has been proposed to identify this pattern of symptoms associated with prolonged neglect, abuse, and/or severe mistreatment, often perpetrated by caregivers, that continues throughout development (National Center for PTSD, 2016). Adolescents react to chronic relational trauma in various ways, many of which are not fully captured by the current DSM’s diagnostic criteria for PTSD. Recognizing and evaluating the impact of complex trauma is thus a demanding process, requiring attention to numerous factors. Further complicating the issue is the fact that adolescents may not respond to early adverse experiences until later in development; their reactions may evolve as they mature and may be greatly influenced by their developmental stage. Complex trauma often has a cumulative effect that cascades over time. A relevant concept is that of deferred action (Nachträglichkeit). Freud (1895) described how a traumatic event may not have an immediate impact on an individual’s psyche: Its true significance might only become understood when juxtaposed with subsequent events or circumstances, or when developmental process makes the individual better able to appreciate the traumatic potential of previous events. For example, a child may not know that sex play initiated by a parent is a destructive exploitation; the child has grown up to normalize it. But the child’s sexual maturation eventually casts a harsh and different light on what happened. Adolescence involves substantial transformations and transitions, during which unresolved issues from earlier in life can resurface, acquire new meaning, and have a different or greater impact on functioning. In individuals with a history of complex trauma, such resurfacing may increase the risk of developing psychopathology or may aggravate preexisting symptoms. Development is an epigenetic process in which each stage is influenced by the outcomes of previous ones. The acquisition of adaptive capacities in one phase lays the groundwork for effectively navigating subsequent phases, leading to increased

chunks.heading: 596 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 596 Adolescence complexity and organization of functioning. Complex trauma destroys the ability to use internal or external resources appropriately; it can distort the individual’s cognitive, emotional, and interpersonal maturation, and it often disrupts brain development, creating enduring function...
chunks.text: 596 Adolescence complexity and organization of functioning. Complex trauma destroys the ability to use internal or external resources appropriately; it can distort the individual’s cognitive, emotional, and interpersonal maturation, and it often disrupts brain development, creating enduring functional deficits (e.g., impaired executive functioning stemming from altered maturation of prefrontal cortical regions, dysregulation of the hypothalamic–­pituitary–­adrenal axis; abnormal cortisol levels, alteration in synaptic pruning and myelination processes, altered brain structure of crucial regions such as the hippocampus and the amygdala, functional alterations in the developing right brain). Complex trauma may damage the adolescent’s sense of self and self-­ esteem, capacity to self-­regulate and integrate information, capacity to trust, and ability to establish intimate relationships. In the face of developmental trauma, dissociation may be a child’s only survival strategy, often at the long-term cost of depersonalization, derealization, and discontinuities of self and personal experience. The beginning of adolescence, with its inherent pressures toward intimacy and sexuality and its reorganization of past experiences with significant others, is challenging for all young people, but in individuals with a history of trauma, it poses greater risks of dissociative episodes when sexual experiences and close connections can trigger memories associated with past abuse. Guidelines for the treatment of complex PTSD in adolescence emphasize the need for a holistic, comprehensive approach. Clinicians need first to identify all symptoms and problems that require special attention, including attachment disturbances, dissociative symptoms, problems in cognition, disturbances in sense of self, comorbid conditions, and difficulties in behavioral and emotional regulation. Therapeutic exposure techniques must be balanced with attention to the adolescent’s affect regulation skills and level of stabilization. Treatment effectiveness may increase by using a combination of approaches, tailored to individual symptomatology and derived from varied traditions (e.g., cognitive, behavioral, humanistic, psychodynamic). A strong therapeutic alliance that builds the patient’s sense of safety and trust is critical, as is adaptation to the adolescent’s cultural and developmental contexts. Advocacy and other interventions at the systemic level may become necessary. These encompass several domains, including family dynamics, legal considerations, and educational aspects. Subjective Experience of Complex PTSD in Adolescents Affective States Adolescents who have suffered developmental trauma often have difficulties perceiving, regulating, and expressing their emotions. They may convey feelings of inadequacy, guilt, shame, despair, worthlessness, and hopelessness. They may also feel empty, emotionally detached, numb, spaced out, and/or disoriented. They may be insensitive to others’ emotions or may struggle to understand and contextualize them. They may appear hostile and aloof or oppositional and defiant; frequent outbursts of rage are not uncommon. These teenagers may distance themselves from negative emotional states and their consequences over extended periods of time. Many of them endure persistent anxiety and episodes of clinical depression. All emotional states, including those typically associated with positive experiences, pose a significant problem for these adolescents. Because they fear they will not

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 597
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 597 be able to tolerate intense affects, they work hard to compartmentalize negative emotions. Unacknowledged shame and self-­hatred, often residing in dissociated self-­states, may trigger rage and aggression, which may be directed either outward or inward and be diffic...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 597 be able to tolerate intense affects, they work hard to compartmentalize negative emotions. Unacknowledged shame and self-­hatred, often residing in dissociated self-­states, may trigger rage and aggression, which may be directed either outward or inward and be difficult to contain. These affective states may lead to a lack of hygiene and general self-care. Emotionally dysregulated adolescents may exhibit suicidal tendencies or panic symptoms or may resort to risky, violent, or self-­harming behaviors to cope with their inner turmoil, thereby exacerbating their emotional dysregulation. They may reenact their traumatic histories, as either aggressor or victim, or they may find themselves in “frozen avoidance reactions” (van der Kolk, 2005). Adolescents with complex PTSD often repress emotions or numb them through substance abuse, medication misuse, or high-risk activities (e.g., dangerous sex, recklessness, rule violation, self-­mutilation, provoking others’ aggression). To escape from facing their feelings, they may try to distract themselves and others. They may us a combination of strategies. When angry, for example, a teenage boy might simultaneously withdraw from social interactions and distract himself. Experiences with attachment figures have taught these young people that intense and distressing affective states are so overwhelming that, to survive, they need to avoid emotions altogether. Their mind becomes organized around the principle of dissociation from affect (e.g., Levy & Lemma, 2004). Dissociation becomes a sort of affect phobia, originating in early trauma with caregivers and persisting as an automatic response to any experiences that may evoke feelings of terror, humiliation, disgust, or rejection. This compartmentalization then prevents subsequent integration. Even the most well-­intentioned efforts to establish emotional intimacy can trigger dissociative responses in these teens, reinforcing their avoidance. Affective states may be so thoroughly disowned as to be entirely outside of awareness. As we have mentioned, adolescents with complex PTSD develop automatic responses that predispose them to expect the traumatic events to recur. Consequently, they may respond to unfamiliar situations (even benevolent ones) with hostility, violence, feelings of defeat, detachment, or hyperengagement. In social environments, they may remain in a state of hypervigilance and arousal, often misinterpreting interpersonal signals that are ambiguous or neutral and ascribing malevolent intentions to others. These teens adopt a range of maladaptive relational patterns, including compulsive isolation or commitment to relationships that prove to be psychologically or physically harmful, increasing the risk of revictimization and polyvictimization. When entering relationships, developmentally traumatized adolescents carry the expectation of betrayal and victimization, at the same time often showing a confused understanding of boundaries and oscillating between withdrawing and clinging. Cognitive Patterns During adolescence, individuals acquire additional cognitive skills, expand their social knowledge, and enhance their overall abilities. This includes improvements in attentional capacities, mental organization, and integration. In adolescents with complex PTSD, this developmental process can be significantly disrupted, leading to biased information processing. Cognitive patterns may become inflexible, concrete, and resistant to new evidence. Adolescents with cumulative experiences of being overwhelmed and out of control show notably distorted perceptions of self, others, and the future. They are more likely to form maladaptive self-­evaluations; make negative judgments about both real

chunks.heading: 598 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 598 Adolescence and hypothetical life events; underestimate their abilities; set unrealistic and perfectionistic goals; perceive their efforts as futile; and develop pessimistic, cynical, and/ or hopeless expectations about the future. Such beliefs may become ingrained in their sense of self, signif...
chunks.text: 598 Adolescence and hypothetical life events; underestimate their abilities; set unrealistic and perfectionistic goals; perceive their efforts as futile; and develop pessimistic, cynical, and/ or hopeless expectations about the future. Such beliefs may become ingrained in their sense of self, significantly influencing their behavior and development. They may identify themselves with their trauma, that is, as a victim, an aggressor, a permanently damaged person, a loner, a “loser,” or a “slut.” They may have an attentional bias toward threatening stimuli and show selective attention to negative events. Disturbances in information processing are common; they can be associated with attention or memory impairments, distorted interpretation processes, and failures in integration. Imaginative process may be inhibited; confusions of thought and problems concentrating are common. These young people may also have trouble reading others’ mental states. Such patterns can influence how they process information related to interpersonal interactions, leading to negative expectations and perceptions of others, who are often seen as incapable of meeting their needs or providing care and assistance, and/or as their only source of support and guidance. They may have distorted beliefs about the perpetrator(s). Adolescents with complex PTSD may believe that others want to hurt, mistreat, manipulate, or humiliate them; that misfortunes or catastrophes are likely to occur at any time; and/or that people they care about will ultimately abandon them. They may internalize the conviction that they are fundamentally defective and/or different, or that they must repress or ignore their emotional needs. They may question their ability to make judgments or decisions or to respond to environmental challenges. A lack of sense of self and self-­agency is quite common. As teenagers transition from early to middle adolescence, they may lose their previous beliefs. Cognitive schemas are often state-­dependent and segregated from one another in the adolescent’s mind. Multiple, incoherent, unintegrated patterns of thought about self and others are therefore common. Trauma-­induced alterations in cognitive processing contribute to posttraumatic symptoms such as anxiety, depression, aggression, and dissociation. Somatic States Younger teenagers with complex PTSD often have difficulty regulating bodily functions. All adolescent sufferers may have chronic sleep and eating disturbances, and wildly varying responses to pain and injury from repeated traumas, which may have damaged their ability to interpret physical sensations. Somatoform dissociation (i.e., disruption of normal integration of thoughts, emotions, and physical sensations, leading to disconnection from immediate bodily experiences) is a common symptom of complex PTSD. In addition to problems in initiating and maintaining sleep, young individuals may have sleep-­related conditions such as crying, walking, or talking during sleep. These disturbances can give rise to additional issues, including irritability, diminished concentration, fatigue, and other physical effects of sleep deprivation. Confrontation with emotionally salient stimuli may provoke loss of bodily function in middle and late adolescents, an impact of complex PTSD that can have far-­reaching effects on overall well-being. Developmentally traumatized teenagers frequently report physical discomfort, distress, and symptoms of illness that may be persistent, chronic, or episodic, including migraines, dermatitis, asthma, and stress-­induced spontaneous vomiting, among others. Fatigue and asthenia are common, especially following highly arousing situations. Hypersensitivity to stimuli (e.g., sounds) may also be present.

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 599
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 599 Relational Patterns Adolescents with complex PTSD have relational patterns similar to those described in the PTSD section but exacerbated by the profound and pervasive impact of their experiences. The repeated relational trauma they have endured becomes the foundatio...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 599 Relational Patterns Adolescents with complex PTSD have relational patterns similar to those described in the PTSD section but exacerbated by the profound and pervasive impact of their experiences. The repeated relational trauma they have endured becomes the foundation upon which their perception of the world and self–other concepts are built, resulting in a range of maladaptive attitudes, expectations, and behaviors. When entering relationships, traumatized adolescents frequently anticipate betrayal and victimization and grapple with confusion about emotional and physical boundaries. Personal interactions may set off a sense of vulnerability and impending danger, further compounded by unresolved attachment issues that may resurface when the adolescent with complex PTSD attempts to forge an intimate bond. Complex trauma results in marked mistrust. Adolescents with complex PTSD may have severe difficulties discerning kindness, care, and trustworthiness in others; they often have an implicit conviction that people are inherently ill-­intentioned, motivated by self-­interest and exploitive agendas. To achieve a sense of safety, they may retain a state of hypervigilance to prevent their desire for closeness from causing a repetition of their childhood anguish, or they may resort to behaviors that interfere with relying on others and establishing healthy relationships. These adolescents may be unusually rigid, with a hypertrophic fear of losing their autonomy that causes them to react defensively when they perceive any outside influence or control. Traumatized teenagers may view others as potential aggressors, even in neutral or benign situations (e.g., when someone is making a request or expressing a different opinion). Feelings of helplessness, humiliation, and/or violation may follow, to which they may respond with aggressive, dysregulated behaviors, or with isolation and social withdrawal. Traumatized adolescents are at risk of repeatedly engaging in psychologically or physically harmful relationships. Despite their distrust in many situations, they can be naïvely overtrusting, with a blind desire to rely on a significant other, making them vulnerable to further exploitation and abuse. Their difficulty discerning who can be trusted and who cannot incline them to respond compliantly, with behavior that appears masochistic or, when the compliance coexists with hostility, passive–­ aggressive. They may often feel dissatisfied, misunderstood, and unappreciated. They may take on conflicting and shifting roles (e.g., from deference to defiance) and adopt unpredictable behaviors that provoke others. Subjective Experience of the Therapist Countertransference reactions toward adolescents with complex PTSD are similar to those that clinicians report toward patients with less complicated posttraumatic disorders. They range from feelings of helplessness, being overwhelmed, overinvolvement, shame, and guilt, to defensive reactions of detachment and withdrawal (for a more comprehensive description, see the previous section on therapists’ subjective experience of PTSD cases). When working with adolescents with a history of developmental trauma—­whose inner world is often organized around polarized, unintegrated experiences and who oscillate between idealization and devaluation—­therapists often find themselves splitting. They may unconsciously align with young patients, tuning in to their pain and coming to perceive the caregivers as “bad objects” worthy of their anger and disdain. This dynamic can reinforce the adolescent’s own splitting processes, perpetuating patterns that hinder emotional integration and growth.

chunks.heading: 600 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 600 Adolescence SA82 Dissociative Disorders Dissociative disorders are described in more detail in Chapter 14 (pp. 760–874). According to DSM-5-TR, dissociative disorders encompass symptoms that involve disruptions of and/or discontinuities in the normal integration of consciousness, memory, identit...
chunks.text: 600 Adolescence SA82 Dissociative Disorders Dissociative disorders are described in more detail in Chapter 14 (pp. 760–874). According to DSM-5-TR, dissociative disorders encompass symptoms that involve disruptions of and/or discontinuities in the normal integration of consciousness, memory, identity, emotion, perception, body representation, motor control, and behavior. Dissociative symptoms can disrupt every area of psychological functioning. Adolescents may report a range of dissociative symptoms that indicate a lack of coherence in mental functioning (International Society for the Study of Dissociation, 2004). These may include (1) fluctuations in consciousness, evident in variable levels of attention and in trance states; (2) lack of autobiographical memories and fluctuations in the ability to access knowledge, indicating incoherence in developmental memory processes; (3) fluctuating moods and behavior, including episodes of rage and regression, reflecting difficulties in self-­regulation; (4) beliefs in the presence of alternative selves or parts of self that exert control over one’s behavior, reflecting disruptions in the development of a cohesive identity; and (5) experiences of depersonalization and derealization, reflecting a subjective sense of dissociation from normal bodily sensations, perceptions, or sense of self. Dissociation in adolescence is a malleable developmental phenomenon that can accompany a range of clinical pictures. Dissociative symptoms can be observed in adolescents with other disorders, including PTSD, obsessive–­compulsive disorders, reactive attachment disorders, eating disorders, and borderline personality disorder, as well as among adolescents with delinquent behavior, and traumatized and hospitalized adolescents, without a diagnosis of psychopathology. Dissociative symptoms in the adolescent years have been found to correlate with a history of significant sexual and/or physical abuse, as well as with neglectful, rejecting, and inconsistent parenting styles. Attachment disorganization caused by traumatic experiences within the caregiving system can lead to psychopathological vulnerabilities such as dissociation. Individual differences in susceptibility to dissociative symptoms may also be related to temperament and personality traits. As children transition into the teen years, dissociative states may become more durable and rigid, and symptom patterns may begin to resemble those observed in adults. Adolescents differ significantly from children not only in their capacity to recognize disruptions in their behaviors and be aware of fragmented states, but also in their subjective distress related to such inconsistencies. Dissociative symptoms may thus have different implications in different phases of development. All of them disrupt the creation of a coherent, developmentally appropriate sense of self. As Ogawa and colleagues (1997) note: “When salient experience must be unnoticed, disallowed, unacknowledged, or forgotten, the result is incoherence in the self-­structure. Interconnections among experiences cannot be made, and the resulting gaps in personal history compromise both the complexity and the integrity of the self. Important meanings are lost” (pp. 871–872). Under dissociative disorders, DSM-5-TR includes depersonalization–­derealization disorder (experiences of unreality or detachment from one’s mind, self, or body and/or experiences of unreality or detachment from one’s surroundings), dissociative amnesia (inability to recall autobiographical information), and dissociative identity disorder (the presence of two or more distinct personality states or an experience of possession and recurrent episodes of amnesia). Among the most common symptoms in youth are depersonalization and derealization, amnesia, trance states, hallucinations, self-­ destructive behavior, profound identity fluctuations or a sense of divided identity, and

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 601
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 601 processes that reflect and/or precipitate disintegration across biological, emotional, cognitive, and behavioral systems (Putnam, 1997). Adolescents may lack memory of behaviors that are inconsistent with their usual pattern (e.g., outbursts of rage, overeating). Sym...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 601 processes that reflect and/or precipitate disintegration across biological, emotional, cognitive, and behavioral systems (Putnam, 1997). Adolescents may lack memory of behaviors that are inconsistent with their usual pattern (e.g., outbursts of rage, overeating). Symptoms may be triggered by substance use (e.g., cannabis). The affective states of adolescents with dissociative tendencies may shift rapidly; they may suffer periods of disorientation and disrupted sleep patterns (excessive sleepiness or lack of sleep). They may suffer intrusive self-­critical thoughts experienced as internal voices. Abrupt onset of academic difficulties, such as school-­ related anxiety, avoidance, or a decline in academic performance, are possible indicators of dissociative conflicts between different self-­states. Dissociating teenagers (especially during early adolescence) may appear to be daydreaming or in a daze, seemingly out of touch with reality. They may become absorbed in activities such as watching TV or listening to music, apparently unaware of what is happening around and inside them, or they may abruptly go into a trance-­like state. They may suddenly act like a much younger child. Adolescents with dissociative disorder can also show rapid, striking changes in cognitive or behavioral skills, style, and personal preferences, and inconsistencies in speech (e.g., tone of voice, accent) and behavior. They are also vulnerable to somatoform dissociation, including, for example, sensory losses, loss of motor control, general paralysis, and alterations of vision, hearing, taste, and smell (see SA83, Conversion Disorder). In diagnosing dissociative disorders in adolescents, clinicians should carefully assess disruptions in identity, consciousness, memory, and other areas suggesting disturbances in typical functioning and development. The assessment process should consider a range of symptoms, such as transient trance states, amnesia, imaginary friends, alterations in self-­perception and self-­representations, state shifts, self-­injury behaviors, and posttraumatic and somatic symptoms. We advise against leading questions and recommend open-­minded inquiry. Treatment should focus on achieving self-­ understanding and self-­ cohesion, enhancing motivation for growth, promoting self-­acceptance, resolving conflicting feelings, desensitizing traumatic memories, and fostering healthy attachment bonds. Family therapy plays a crucial role here; when appropriate, treatment should involve family members. They can work with the main clinician, or a separate therapist may work with them in a closely coordinated way. Clinicians should carefully evaluate whether parental mental health issues are impeding the teenager’s therapy, in which case they should be referred for individual therapy. Treatment of complex cases often requires the involvement of teachers, pediatricians, and other third parties. Adjunctive treatments such as hypnotherapy, pharmacotherapy, art therapy, and group therapy can sometimes be beneficial. Severe cases may require inpatient or residential treatment that focuses on stabilizing behavior and ensuring safety before starting psychotherapeutic work. Throughout the treatment process, the clinician should aim to empower the adolescent, enhance self-­regulation skills, and promote whatever level of integration is possible. SA83 Conversion Disorder Conversion disorder is described in more detail in Chapter 14 (pp. 760–874). In conversion, symptoms and deficits in voluntary motor function suggest a neurological or physical condition, but no such condition can be found. In many cases, symptoms appear abruptly after a minor illness or injury (Leary, 2003). Symptoms, which are

chunks.heading: 602 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 602 Adolescence experienced as involuntary, can take many forms: motor weakness or abnormalities, loss of sensory functions such as touch, sight, taste, or hearing, or positive symptoms such as nonepileptic seizures. Conversion disorders are part of the broader category of functional somatic symptom...
chunks.text: 602 Adolescence experienced as involuntary, can take many forms: motor weakness or abnormalities, loss of sensory functions such as touch, sight, taste, or hearing, or positive symptoms such as nonepileptic seizures. Conversion disorders are part of the broader category of functional somatic symptoms in DSM-5-TR and frequently coexist with medically unexplained pain, nonspecific somatic symptoms, sensory disturbances, anxiety, and depression. This disorder arises most commonly in the preadolescent years or early adolescence, roughly twice as often in girls as in boys. Most studies have found that onset occurs around age 12, earlier in the case of psychogenic nonepileptic seizures. These cases place heavy demands on consulting time and diagnostic resources. Predisposing factors include rigidly obsessional personality traits, anxiety and depression, low emotionality and/or low threshold of responsiveness, and a history of sexual abuse. Environmental factors such as relational stress, feelings of parental rejection, poor intrafamilial communication, medical diseases in relatives, unresolved grief, and school-­related difficulties also contribute to the development of conversion disorders in adolescence. Conversion disorder is more frequently diagnosed among teens in lower socioeconomic groups or among those whose families lack knowledge about medical diseases. Clinical experience suggests that many patients with conversion symptoms are reared in families in which emotional concerns are disregarded, while physical complaints receive attention. Factors associated with a favorable prognosis are early onset, sensory rather than motor symptoms, less severe symptoms at the onset, absence of any concomitant organic disease, and absence of other major psychiatric disorders. The etiology of conversion symptoms remains unknown, but various mechanisms have been postulated. Freud used the term “conversion” to describe a psychological phenomenon resulting from a failure of drive energy to connect with a mental representation (Breuer & Freud, 1895); instead, the energy is converted into a physical symptom that symbolizes an unconscious mental conflict. Engel (1968) described it as “a psychic mechanism whereby an idea, fantasy, or wish is expressed in bodily rather than verbal terms and is experienced by the patient as a physical rather than a mental symptom.” Janet (1919) proposed that conversion symptoms have a neurocognitive component, conceptualizing them as disorders of memory processing that arise after trauma. Notably, difficulties that children and adolescents with conversion disorders have in integrating autobiographical information within attachment narratives. Neurobiological models have emphasized deficits in memory and attention, particularly during stressful conditions. It has also been hypothesized that individuals with conversion disorder may have enhanced functional connectivity between the brain regions involved in emotion processing, movement, and sensory representation, leading, under stress, to the manifestation of unusual sensorimotor symptoms. Symptoms such as nonepileptic seizures or tremors have been thought to be secondary to hyperventilation or fear-­induced bradycardia, resulting in reduced cerebral blood flow/oxygen availability in the context of perceived physical or psychological threat. Research has shown that conversion symptoms often emerge when individuals face present-­day life stressors that resonate with earlier traumatic experiences. This finding supports Freud’s and Janet’s original traumatogenic hypotheses and highlights childhood maltreatment as a strong predictor of the onset of conversion symptoms. A definitive diagnosis of conversion disorder is frequently delayed for fear of misdiagnosis. Excessive medical procedures may ensue. In the vast majority of cases, diagnosis can be achieved through a detailed physical examination and the identification of clinical incongruities. A delay in the diagnostic process can also be related, however,

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 603
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 603 to the refusal of parents or adolescents to accept the absence of an organic disease. Such refusal often is associated with denial of familial problems and anger toward medical personnel. Treatment should begin with an acknowledgment that the young person has a real...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 603 to the refusal of parents or adolescents to accept the absence of an organic disease. Such refusal often is associated with denial of familial problems and anger toward medical personnel. Treatment should begin with an acknowledgment that the young person has a real illness that has seriously disrupted normal patterns of living. The disability should be regarded as a symbolic form of communication expressing unconscious conflict and/ or traumatic experience. One effective strategy is a graduated physiotherapy program linked to rewards, directed by an empathic physiotherapist. This can be provided in a children’s hospital ward under the supervision of pediatricians. Good results have also been achieved with behavior therapy (using both positive and negative reinforcement), psychodynamic therapy, and hypnosis. SA9 Disruptive Behavior Disorders Disruptive behavior disorders are problems in controlling one’s emotions and behaviors at a cost to others. DSM-5 introduced a category of disruptive, impulse control, and conduct disorders, combining several problems with self-­control previously included among disorders usually first diagnosed in infancy, childhood, or adolescence—­notably, oppositional defiant disorder and conduct disorder. In ICD-11, these disorders are classified in two separate categories: “impulse control disorders” and “disruptive behavior or dissocial disorders.” The underlying causes of problems in controlling emotions and behaviors vary greatly across disorders and individuals with these diagnoses. Nature (including neurodevelopmental disorders, e.g., ADHD) and nurture both play significant roles in pathogenesis. Disruptive behavior disorders are heterogeneous, associated with different neuropsychiatric problems, neuropsychological profiles, and social–­ familial difficulties. They are best understood by taking into account the developmental trajectories of the youngsters diagnosed with them. There are some gender differences in these trajectories. For girls, externalizing problems are negatively correlated with other symptoms, whereas for boys, they constitute a risk factor for other problems (Liu, Thompson, Watson, Montena, & Warren, 2023). Boys show more co-­occurring conduct problems in the transition from early to middle adolescence (Murray et al., 2022). Early externalizing problems increase an individual’s susceptibility to other disorders, across domains (Healy et al., 2022). In recent years, growing evidence (e.g., Boldrini, Ghiandoni, Maninelli, Salcuni, & Solmi, 2023) attests to the effectiveness of psychosocial interventions for disruptive behavior disorders. These are considered the gold standard for treating adolescents diagnosed with oppositional defiant disorder or conduct disorder. These combinations of psychotherapy, counseling, and training aim at reducing psychological distress, limiting dysfunctional behaviors, and improving adaptive functioning. NICE guidelines (2013) recommend psychosocial treatments rather than pharmacological interventions for adolescents with disruptive patterns and emphasize the goal of understanding dysfunctional behaviors and related emotional, cognitive, and relational processes, so that one can influence the dynamics of the family system. When working with adolescents with these problems, clinicians need to focus on behaviors that consistently expose them to danger and to enhance protective factors that can reinforce and consolidate better adaptive skills. Training programs for parents and families are most effective when they take into consideration family members’ potential expectation that

chunks.heading: 604 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 604 Adolescence help will be shaming, judgmental, and stigmatizing. Positive outcomes are more likely when professionals are perceived as nonjudgmental, normalizing, and sensitive to the broader family context, including the emotional needs of parents. SA91 Conduct Disorder Conduct disorder (also di...
chunks.text: 604 Adolescence help will be shaming, judgmental, and stigmatizing. Positive outcomes are more likely when professionals are perceived as nonjudgmental, normalizing, and sensitive to the broader family context, including the emotional needs of parents. SA91 Conduct Disorder Conduct disorder (also discussed in Chapter 5, pp. 256–308), denotes a pattern of behavior in which an adolescent violates the basic rights of others or defies age-­ appropriate societal norms. DSM-5 groups such behaviors into aggressive conduct that causes or threatens physical harm to other people or animals, nonaggressive conduct causing property loss or damage, deceitfulness, and theft and serious rule violations. ICD-11 criteria are similar. Teens with conduct disorder have major impairments in social, academic, and occupational functioning. Research has identified two subtypes of antisocial behavior. The first is evident in early childhood and persists throughout life; the second emerges in adolescence and reflects peer processes such as imitating antisocial peers, and remits in early adulthood. DSM-5 further postulates a subtype with limited prosocial emotions and high callous–­unemotional traits including flat affect, lack of empathy, and absence of remorse. This subtype is associated with more severe, varied, and persistent antisocial behavior and aggression. Many children with conduct disorders have coexisting symptoms, such as mood disorders, anxiety, posttraumatic syndromes, problems with attention and hyperactivity, learning problems, substance misuse, and thought disorders. The persistence of the pattern into adolescence is a strong predictor of antisocial personality disorder, especially among boys from families with high callous–­unemotional traits. Adolescents with conduct disorder are at high risk of substance use disorders, school dropout, unwanted pregnancies, conviction and incarceration for illegal acts, and increased injuries and mortality. Disruptive behavior disorders are the most common reason for referral to child and adolescent mental health services. An estimated 6% of all children and adolescents, most often boys, have some problems with conduct. Girls are more likely to develop these disorders in adolescence, whereas onset in boys is usually in childhood and involves more aggression. Many factors may contribute to etiology, including brain damage, child abuse, genetic vulnerability, school failure, inadequate relationships, and traumatic experiences. Oppositional behaviors and violence may reflect specific relational dynamics between a youngster and the social and familial environment. Problematic behaviors may occur in situations of mutual misunderstanding in which rigid attitudes provoke an escalation of aggression (e.g., in the absence of mediation for generational and cultural conflicts, with failure of any solutions other than direct confrontation). They may also be the young person’s reaction to real or perceived familial or social rejection. Parental attitudes may play a role in children’s behavior problems. Rigid or chaotic educational attitudes, emotional neglect, or harsh, insensitive parenting may underlie the insecure or disorganized attachment patterns behind these problems. Some researchers (e.g., Frick & Viding, 2009) have concluded that enduring antisocial behavior is a neurodevelopmental disorder emerging from interactions between individual vulnerabilities and environmental adversity. Empirical findings also suggest that severe antisocial behaviors arising in adolescence frequently have a negative prognosis and are rarely limited to the teen years (Fairchild, van Goozen, Calder, & Goodyer, 2013). Recurrent antisocial acts are associated with emotion-­processing

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 605
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 605 deficits, altered brain structure and function, changes in cortisol secretion, and atypical personality patterns (e.g., increased callous–­unemotional traits). Subjective Experience of Conduct Disorder in Adolescents Affective States Adolescents diagnosed with conduc...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 605 deficits, altered brain structure and function, changes in cortisol secretion, and atypical personality patterns (e.g., increased callous–­unemotional traits). Subjective Experience of Conduct Disorder in Adolescents Affective States Adolescents diagnosed with conduct disorders are heterogeneous. Those who exhibit high callous–­unemotional, psychopathic traits seem unaware of their own feelings and unresponsive to others’ feelings. They appear cold, oppositional, and oriented solely to their own desires. These teens are unempathic, opportunistic, incapable of affection, and unable to bond with others. They tend to have lower levels of anxiety and to be more thrill seeking. Young people with conduct disorders may not consider the consequences of their actions and may feel a sense of omnipotent invulnerability, strongly associated with their violent and aggressive and criminal behaviors. Feelings of remorse and guilt are generally absent. Other adolescents with this diagnosis have labile affects that they cannot regulate; their behavior shows a mix of impulsivity, low frustration tolerance, and negative affectivity. They may be unable to bear even small delays of gratification. These teens may see themselves as inadequate; their feelings of helplessness to achieve desired outcomes may set off anger, rash behavior, and aggression toward others. They may be attracted to delinquent acts and abuse of substances. They can sometimes acknowledge feelings of fear and may also admit pain and resentment about mistreatment or neglect by others. They commonly express an attitude of having given up on people. Clinical observation and empirical research (cf. DiLallo, Jones, & Westen, 2009; Moffit, Caspi, Dickson, Silva, & Stanton, 1996) have identified another group of adolescents diagnosable with conduct disorders. In these young people, there is emotional constriction, negative affectivity, and a tendency toward isolation and withdrawal. They report feelings of inadequacy or inferiority, expressed in a sense of being outsiders or outcasts. Anxiety, depression, timidity, and inability to understand and adhere to social norms and rules are their specific characteristics. Cognitive Patterns Adolescents with conduct disorders may adopt a defensive sense of omnipotence and may regard others with indifference. Their goals usually include material gain and power. Thoughts about any other aspect of personal relationship may be notably absent. Their thoughts and fantasies often center on the belief that they have been wronged. Young people with higher psychopathy and callous–­unemotional traits have abnormalities in processing punishment cues, tend to engage in more deviant conduct, and lack empathy (see Frick, Ray, Thornton, & Kahn, 2014). Poor levels of mentalization, lack of insight, and little capacity for perspective taking characterizes even the more inhibited adolescents diagnosed with conduct disorders. Somatic States Somatic states most often seen in these teens include arousal and hypervigilance. There may also be bodily symptoms associated with the core psychopathology or as a consequence of the substance use.

chunks.heading: 606 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 606 Adolescence Relationship Patterns Adolescents with conduct disorders may be irresponsible, impulsive, deceitful, grandiose, and remorseless; they take advantage of others, externalize blame, and show a reckless disregard for others’ rights. Since they see others as objects to manipulate in the s...
chunks.text: 606 Adolescence Relationship Patterns Adolescents with conduct disorders may be irresponsible, impulsive, deceitful, grandiose, and remorseless; they take advantage of others, externalize blame, and show a reckless disregard for others’ rights. Since they see others as objects to manipulate in the service of power, excitement, or material benefits, their relationships tend to be superficial and short-lived. Some adolescents with this diagnosis are more aggressive, openly hostile, contemptuous, domineering, and arrogant, whereas others are characterized by poor social skills and appear interpersonally inappropriate. Their peers tend to reject teens in this latter group, and their lack of close friendships, combined with their inability to understand social norms and rules, may account for much of their problematic behavior (cf. DiLallo et al., 2009). Subjective Experience of the Therapist Dealing with adolescents with conduct disorders can evoke frustration, apprehension, and anger that challenge clinicians’ equilibrium. Therapists can feel helpless and overwhelmed, and may fear being dominated, exploited, belittled, and controlled. These countertransferences reflect the interpersonal functioning of these young people who, lacking a capacity for love and empathy, may act callously toward the clinician, express hostility, or lie and manipulate. Feelings of unsympathetic detachment and difficulties establishing a cooperative therapeutic relationship are typical of therapists’ responses to adolescents with conduct disorders. It is essential that clinicians avoid reacting aggressively to patients’ provocative and severely maladaptive behaviors. To contain these adolescents’ attacks and keep mutual aggression and impulsivity under control, they need to maintain firm, well-­defined boundaries. Clinical Illustration Bobby, a 16-year-old boy who had failed eighth grade, had been stealing cars since age 12, and was intimidating peers. Eventually, he was arrested and sent to a detention center, where he was described as angry and entitled, with little regard for others. He tried to intimidate the staff—a significant problem, given his size and strength. An intuitive officer began talking sympathetically with him, often about his history of being treated unfairly, while also setting limits on his intimidation by giving him “time outs” in the county jail. After a year, he began to comply with the rules of the setting; within 2 years, he had become one of its best students. SA92 Oppositional Defiant Disorder Oppositional defiant disorder is also discussed in Chapter 5 (see pp. 256–380). In adolescence, it is characterized by argumentativeness, negativity, hostility, and defiance of authority. Although all adolescents can be oppositional in the service of self-­ definition, openly provocative, uncooperative, hostile behavior is concerning mainly when it becomes persistent, is markedly more pervasive and rigid than other peer behaviors, and impedes adaptation in central areas of the adolescent’s life. Outbursts of rage, violent quarrels with adults, ongoing defiance of rules, attempts to harass or upset others, contemptuous behavior toward others, tendencies to be touchy and easily irritated, and frequent expressions of resentment and anger are features of this

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 607
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 607 picture. Young people diagnosed with oppositional defiant disorder may face all of the developmental challenges of adolescence, only with difficulty, showing severe problems controlling their emotions in school, at home, and in social interactions. In DSM-5, the symp...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 607 picture. Young people diagnosed with oppositional defiant disorder may face all of the developmental challenges of adolescence, only with difficulty, showing severe problems controlling their emotions in school, at home, and in social interactions. In DSM-5, the symptoms of this disorder fall into three groups: angry/irritable mood, argumentative/defiant behavior, and vindictiveness. The chronic irritability in these adolescents suggests links between oppositionality and mood disorders, and reflects a deficit in emotion regulation behind a propensity toward explosive outbursts and disruptive, self-­defeating behaviors. In ICD-11 the condition is placed under “disruptive behavior or dissocial disorders” and can be diagnosed with or without chronic irritability/anger. Oppositional and defiant behaviors are among the most common reasons for referral to mental health services in childhood and adolescence. Symptoms are relatively stable during childhood and adolescence. They commonly occur alongside other conditions such as anxiety, depression, and ADHD, and they may ultimately develop into diagnosable conduct disorders. Evidence indicates that oppositional defiant disorder is a precursor to antisocial behaviors and diagnosed conduct disorder, and also predicts mood disorders and anxiety. Although the causes of such behavior are not fully known, its symptoms often parallel problematic early patterns. It may be hard to disentangle the respective contributions of individual characteristics and problematic family interactions. Fonagy (see Bateman & Fonagy, 2012) proposes a developmental model of disruptive behavior disorders that takes into account the interplay between a child’s temperamental difficulties and parents’ inability, because of their own difficulties, to attune to the child’s needs. Insensitive parental treatment amplifies the child’s reactions, triggering cyclical mutual misunderstanding. The environment does not contain the child’s impulsiveness, hyperactivity, and irritability, which generate unsatisfactory relational exchanges that deprive the child of positive experiences needed to develop a consistent self-­representation and confidence in others. Oppositional behaviors then may protect a fragile identity from feelings of dependence. The child develops compelling interactive strategies that later permeate other relational contexts. Subjective Experience of Oppositional Defiant Disorder in Adolescents Affective States Adolescents with oppositional and defiant patterns often have low self-­esteem and feel they are not getting the attention they deserve. Beneath their overt disruptiveness lie feelings of demoralization, self-doubt, resentment, and self-­hatred. Such adolescents almost always feel poorly understood. Their affect is marked by a dramatic and persistent state of hyperalertness, oriented toward guarding their self-worth. They are poised to feel put-upon and righteously indignant. Their efforts to maintain their self-­esteem may look more stimulating and euphoric than anxious. They often have problems with self-­regulation. Cognitive States ost adolescents with this diagnosis have weak insight into their problems. They typically do not regard themselves as angry, oppositional, or defiant. Instead, they justify their behavior as a response to unreasonable circumstances and attribute the problem

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 609
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 609 Substance use during adolescence is common worldwide. Data from the 2022 Monitoring the Future Survey estimated that in the United States, the percentage of adolescents reporting substance abuse remained broadly stable, after significantly declining between 2020 and...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 609 Substance use during adolescence is common worldwide. Data from the 2022 Monitoring the Future Survey estimated that in the United States, the percentage of adolescents reporting substance abuse remained broadly stable, after significantly declining between 2020 and 2021, during the COVID-19 pandemic. In 2022, use of illicit substances remained at or significantly below prepandemic levels for all grades, involving 11% of eighth graders, 21.5% of 10th graders, and 32.6% of 12th graders. Substance use in adolescence often co-­occurs with psychological distress and psychiatric illness. Adolescents with psychiatric disorders are at increased risk for problematic substance use, and high rates of psychiatric illness are seen in adolescents with substance use disorders. This co-­occurrence complicates treatment and worsens prognosis. Psychological dysregulation in childhood may precede adolescent substance use and promote problems that are both externalizing (e.g., aggression) and internalizing (e.g., anxiety) in the teen years. Young people who externalize appear to be more responsive to substances that activate the brain’s reward system, whereas those who internalize may use substances that control a hyperresponsive fear–­anxiety system. Both share a self-­regulatory deficit with cognitive, behavioral, and emotional difficulties, leading to executive function impairment, impulsivity, affective lability, and a tendency to act out. Several environmental influences increase the risk of accelerated and problematic use of alcohol and other drugs, and the development of adolescent substance use disorders, including child maltreatment and other traumatic events, parental influences (negligent or abusive caregiving and substance use by parents), and peer influences. Some of these are also empirically associated with diagnosed manifestations of psychological dysregulation, such as conduct disorders, ADHD, anxiety-­related problems, and depressive conditions. During its development, the brain is vulnerable to negative events. If an adolescent experiences trauma or uses substances, brain development can be disturbed, leading to reduced cognitive control over behavior and increased dominance of dysregulated responses. In adolescence, substance abuse can also increase the risk of being exposed to traumatic events and decrease the ability to cope adaptively with new stresses. Young people with a psychiatric disorder and a comorbid substance use disorder need an integrated, multimodal therapeutic response, including psychotherapy, drug treatment, pharmacological treatments, family involvement, and collaboration with social workers. Subjective Experience of Substance‐Related Disorders in Adolescents Social pressures and curiosity are the motives adolescents most frequently cite for initiating drug or alcohol use. They may start using substances to imitate peers and be accepted by them or to enhance social activities; they may also fear that if they refuse, their friends will exclude them. Substance use may come to be a coping strategy to deal with stress, to forget unpleasant experiences, to feel numb or indifferent to life challenges, and to erase painful feelings such as anxiety, emptiness, depression, embarrassment, humiliation, and rage. Reasons for initiating drug or alcohol use are likely distinct from those that contribute to the progression from initial use to regular use and ultimately to a diagnosis of a substance use disorder. The majority of adolescents using substances report that the drugs make them feel good, help them cope with difficulties, and enhance their sense of confidence and self-­esteem.

chunks.heading: 610 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 610 Adolescence Affective States The differing affective states of adolescents who abuse substances need to be understood within each person’s individual psychological and interpersonal functioning. Research on the relationship between substance use and personality has identified one cluster of adol...
chunks.text: 610 Adolescence Affective States The differing affective states of adolescents who abuse substances need to be understood within each person’s individual psychological and interpersonal functioning. Research on the relationship between substance use and personality has identified one cluster of adolescents with severe emotional dysregulation, anxious and depressive states, and feelings of self-­devaluation, inadequacy, and unworthiness. These young people exhibit abrupt mood swings and intense emotions that they are unable to manage; they can only calm down within a highly dependent relationship or by resorting to substances. A second group includes personalities characterized by hostility, impulsivity, disregard for the consequences of actions, unreliability, sensation seeking, and fascination with the dangerous situations associated with drug use. Finally, some adolescents with substance use disorders present with social withdrawal and intense feelings of shame, embarrassment, and humiliation, which are indicative of low self-­esteem. Their use of illicit substances becomes a kind of self-care to reduce painful affective states. Cognitive Patterns When confronted with their problematic substance consumption, adolescents frequently respond with rationalization and denial. Their cognitions are often related to the main emotions that led to substance use. Once an adolescent regularly uses drugs, the resulting cognitive patterns reflect the effect of the substance(s) on the individual’s brain: This may include fragmented and paranoid thinking, fantasies of specialness and brilliance, or wish-­fulfilling convictions of desirability, potency, and invulnerability. Somatic States There are somatic aspects of both the affects described earlier and the substances used. Teenagers can be creative and inventive in their efforts to maximize intoxication, which can put them at risk for complications in many organ systems, with both immediate and long-term consequences. Somatic states may vary from arousal and agitation to numbness and tranquility. Relationship Patterns Adolescents with substance-­related disorders show a marked need for guidance and approval from others; they may perceive themselves as powerless and ineffective and may seek incessant support and reassurance. Relationship patterns may involve intense feelings of neediness, alternating with protests that they have no such needs. Relationships may be described as shaky, untrustworthy, or disappointing, and may range from active involvement in a social group where the substance misuse is ritualized to self-­absorbed states of solitary withdrawal. Once a person is involved in regular substance misuse, relationships tend to be valued as a means of obtaining the desired substance and thus take on a manipulative quality. Subjective Experience of the Therapist Countertransferences relative to adolescents with substance use disorder may be intense and difficult to manage, and are generally marked by helplessness and frustration.

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 611
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 611 Although we need further research on the topic, we note that clinicians’ reactions may depend in part on the emotional, cognitive, and relational characteristics of the patient. For example, when treating severely dysregulated, troubled adolescents who fear rejection...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 611 Although we need further research on the topic, we note that clinicians’ reactions may depend in part on the emotional, cognitive, and relational characteristics of the patient. For example, when treating severely dysregulated, troubled adolescents who fear rejection or abandonment, therapists may feel overwhelmed by their intense emotional demands, find themselves disorganized or overinvolved, and have trouble maintaining adequate therapeutic boundaries. In contrast, with those with more externalizing characteristics, who appear aggressive, angry, deceitful, manipulative, unresponsive, and unempathic, they may feel powerless, rejected, criticized, or devalued. In severe cases, a sense of panic, threat, fear, or distrust may lead them to withdraw from the therapeutic relationship. Less compromised adolescents, who are capable of greater commitment to the treatment process, evoke less intense countertransferences. Clinical Illustration Donny, an 18-year-old male, was brought for therapy by his mother for substance use that was interfering with his school performance. His father opposed the treatment. The parents had separated when Donny was age 4 after his father, who was heavily involved in drugs, had overdosed, attempted suicide, been hospitalized, and lost his driver’s license. At 14, the boy began using marijuana occasionally, escalating quickly to daily use. During a 4-year course of therapy that was only partially successful, it became apparent that his marijuana use expressed an effort to identify with his father. His escalation during high school also reflected his determination to distract himself from emerging homosexual longings. SA102 Internet Addiction Disorder Internet addiction disorder (also referred to as “problematic internet use” or “compulsive internet use”) refers to such extensive use of the internet that it interferes with daily life.
