The diagnosis is often subdivided by activity, such as gaming, social networking, blogging, email checking, excessive or overwhelming internet pornography use, or internet shopping (shopping addiction). These habits are troubling only when they interfere with normal life and have cognitive and behavioral outcomes such as progressive loss of control, tolerance, and withdrawal symptoms, analogous to the symptoms of substance use disorders. Adolescents who are rejection-­sensitive and vulnerable narcissistically seem especially prone to problematic internet use (Fontana, Benzi, Ghezzi, Cianfanelli, & Sideli, 2023; Fontana, Callea, Casini, & Curti, 2018). Notwithstanding these similarities with substance use disorders, internet addiction disorder has not been included in the DSM-5 chapter on substance-­related and addictive disorders. Currently, there is insufficient scientific evidence to establish the diagnostic criteria and course descriptions needed to identify these behaviors as mental disorders. One problem that has been considered as a provisional diagnosis in DSM-5, however, is internet gaming disorder, which has been included in the section of the manual addressing conditions on which future research is encouraged. ICD-11 also fails to include internet addiction disorder, but it does list “gambling disorder, predominantly online,” and “gaming disorder, predominantly online.” The key feature of the latter is persistent and recurrent participation in computer gaming, typically group games, for many hours (generally more than 8–10 per day). These games involve competition between groups of players, who participate in complex structured activities that include a significant element of social interaction.

chunks.heading: 612 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 612 Adolescence Comparable to those with substance use disorders, individuals with internet gaming disorders pursue their addiction at the expense of other potential interests. They strongly resist attempts to direct them toward schoolwork, activities, or family obligations. If prevented from using...
chunks.text: 612 Adolescence Comparable to those with substance use disorders, individuals with internet gaming disorders pursue their addiction at the expense of other potential interests. They strongly resist attempts to direct them toward schoolwork, activities, or family obligations. If prevented from using a computer and returning to the game, they become agitated and angry. An emerging consensus suggests that this syndrome is commonly defined by (1) withdrawal, (2) loss of control, and (3) conflict (King & Delfabbro, 2014b). Because money is not at risk, this condition is discriminated from internet gambling disorder. The main reason individuals give for excessive computer use is avoiding boredom. King and Delfabbro (2014a) identified four underlying cognitive factors in internet gaming disorder: beliefs about game reward value and tangibility; maladaptive and inflexible rules about gaming behaviors; overreliance on gaming to meet self-­esteem needs; and gaming as a method of gaining social acceptance. Much of the literature on diagnosed internet gaming disorder comes from Asian countries and centers on young males, with a point prevalence of 8.4 and 4.5% for male and female adolescents between ages 15 and 19, respectively (Lam, 2014). A survey of a large European adolescent sample using the proposed DSM-5 criteria found that 1.6% of adolescents met the full criteria, with a further 5.1% at risk, meeting up to four criteria (Müller et al., 2015). The disorder was closely associated with psychopathological symptoms, especially aggressive and rule-­breaking behavior and social problems. In other studies, the problem has been associated with psychiatric diagnoses such as social anxiety, depression, ADHD, and obsessive–­compulsive disorder, as well as with maladaptive personality traits (Şalvarlı & Griffiths, 2021). This growing literature demonstrates that problematic internet gaming is common among adolescents and is related to psychosocial and psychiatric problems. Although the classification of the phenomenon is still under debate, there is a growing awareness of the risk of excessive use of online computer games, which can lead to functional impairment and intense distress in adolescents and young adults. There is an urgent need for more methodologically sound assessment procedures and youth-­specific prevention and treatment (Petry et al., 2014). Subjective Experience of Internet Addiction Disorder in Adolescents Affective States Adolescents who are addicted to the internet may experience depressive and anxious affective states. For some, internet gaming is a way to cope with these affects, but it orients them toward living an “online” life at the expense of the real, relational one. “Alexithymia,” a difficulty in identifying and verbalizing emotions, has been found to be a significant predictor of addictive internet use. Teens with these patterns may find it hard to implement emotion regulation strategies and may have difficulty controlling their impulsive responses to negative affective and interpersonal experiences. Cognitive States Patients with this problem may have limited self-­control, a tendency to feel nervous, and an inclination to worry about various domains. Their executive functioning, especially with respect to attention shifting and inhibitory capacities, may be hindered.

chunks.heading: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 613
chunks.preview: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 613 Somatic States When individuals are involved in internet games, certain brain pathways are triggered in the same direct and intense way that a drug affects an addicted brain. The gaming prompts a neurological response that produces feelings of pleasure and reward, an...
chunks.text: Adolescent Symptom Patterns 613 Somatic States When individuals are involved in internet games, certain brain pathways are triggered in the same direct and intense way that a drug affects an addicted brain. The gaming prompts a neurological response that produces feelings of pleasure and reward, and the result, in the extreme, is an addictive behavior. Relational Patterns Adolescents’ family functioning seems to play a critical role in the development of internet addiction. Risk factors include high levels of family conflict and poor quality of parent–­child relationship. Parents of adolescents with internet addiction tend to behave with low emotional availability and responsiveness. Given this painful family reality, the adolescent may look to escape into an “alternative” reality characterized by virtual friendships. Subjective Experience of the Therapist Countertransference reactions evoked by adolescents with internet addiction may include anger, irritation, boredom, withdrawal, a sense of loss, and helplessness. These young patients often seem unreachable. The clinician feels frustrated by the difficulty of getting in touch with the adolescent’s inner world, in the face of the patient’s immersion in a virtual reality over which the gamer has full control. Actual reality exposes these adolescents to danger, because it seems unrewarding, unpredictable, painful, limited, and therefore terrifying. Some clinicians have described a sense of loneliness and emptiness that seems to preclude the possibility of their establishing a human, intimate, and reciprocal bond. chunks.heading: CHAPTER 10
chunks.preview: CHAPTER 10 Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention in Adolescence C H A P T E R E D IT O R S Annalisa Tanzilli, PhD Guido Giovanardi, PhD CONSULTANTS Neil Krishan Aggarwal, MD Jack Drescher, MD Vittorio Lingiardi, MD 10.1. Climate Change, War, and Pandemic: Living in a Nonpredi...
chunks.text: CHAPTER 10 Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention in Adolescence C H A P T E R E D IT O R S Annalisa Tanzilli, PhD Guido Giovanardi, PhD CONSULTANTS Neil Krishan Aggarwal, MD Jack Drescher, MD Vittorio Lingiardi, MD 10.1. Climate Change, War, and Pandemic: Living in a Nonpredictable Environment In adolescence, a stable, predictable environment on which one can depend is a prerequisite for exploring the myriad potential configurations of one’s developing adult identity. Repeated catastrophes and deprived living conditions during adolescence compound the already arduous process of individualization. In optimal conditions, parting ways with a secure base facilitates the relinquishment of the familiar world of childhood and the exploration of the unknown geography of adulthood. Over the past decade, the uncertain future looming for humanity, the manifold losses and restrictions imposed by the COVID-19 pandemic, the consequent economic crises, and the nearness of frightening, technologically advanced wars have converged into a significant risk factor for the psychological well-being of adolescents. An international study of more than 11,000 individuals ages 13–19 revealed that for 1 in 4 teenagers, climate change is the most feared issue of our time (Cambridge International, 2020). Anxiety and depression have become disturbingly prevalent among youth. Recent research shows a drastic deterioration in the quality of life linked to distress induced by vicarious traumatization, extreme climatic events, and the pandemic (Brophy, Olson, & Paul, 2023; Clayton, Pihkala, Wray, & Marks, 2023; Lass-­Hennemann et al., 2024).

chunks.heading: Psychological Experiences That May Require Attention 629
chunks.preview: Psychological Experiences That May Require Attention 629 disease, there can be intense fear, concern, a sense of overwhelm, frustration, and preoccupations about the future. We should note that positive affective states, including hope, trust, and proactiveness, can be prevalent as well. Optimism a...
chunks.text: Psychological Experiences That May Require Attention 629 disease, there can be intense fear, concern, a sense of overwhelm, frustration, and preoccupations about the future. We should note that positive affective states, including hope, trust, and proactiveness, can be prevalent as well. Optimism and indifference are more frequently observed in boys. Young individuals often view older generations with distrust and suspicion, but given current challenges to sustainability, peace, economic security, and health, those feelings can turn into resentment and animosity, especially when they perceive older people as neglecting or minimizing environmental disaster. Cognitive Patterns Together with the fatalistic thoughts that may accompany what has been called the eco-­paralysis, proactive cognitive patterns emerge that push proenvironmental behaviors and motivate involvement in the diverse causes of social injustice. Some adolescents experience a profound existential crisis which leads to obsessive thinking and a state of learned helplessness. Internet and smartphone addiction, substance abuse to cope with emotional distress, and difficulty concentrating are common by-­products of anxiety in young people. Somatic States Sleep disorders have increased alarmingly since the pandemic. Food insecurities and pollution may trigger eating disorders such as binge eating and orthorexia nervosa (obsessions with eating certain foods and avoiding others seen as harmful). Direct exposure to natural disasters and catastrophic events creates posttraumatic stress and difficulties in adaptation. In light of ecoanxiety and apprehensions related to wars and pandemics, research documents an increase in hypochondriacal concerns and somatization (e.g., palpitations, irritable bowel syndrome, panic attacks). Relational Patterns The forced confinement of the pandemic period has led to emotional distress and frustration. Modern technologies have allowed the creation of virtual communities, which, in some cases, have replaced face-to-face interactions. Social withdrawal is spreading. Rather than deal with the uncertainty and complexity of the world, increasing numbers of adolescents take refuge in a definitive withdrawal from society. Aggression and violence become more frequent and are often enacted online. Conversely, concerns about contemporary risks and instabilities often find a significant relational outlet in activism. Subjective Experience of the Therapist Communication within the therapeutic dyad can be put to the test when the anxiety brought by the patient is linked to the climate crisis. Older clinicians may feel defensive and exasperated when treated with hostility and suspicion based on their own generation’s failures to avert ecological devastation. They may need to find a genuine sense of respect for the experience of adolescent patients, whose apocalyptic fears are more complex and deeply felt. Disclosure by clinicians of their own perspectives and concerns may favor and support the working alliance.

chunks.heading: 630 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 630 Adolescence 10.2. Minoritized Demographic Groups (Ethnic, Cultural, Linguistic, Religious, Political) Understanding an individual’s inner functioning necessarily implies integrating individual and cultural dimensions. von Bertalanffy (1968) considers culture and the self as systems, conceiving t...
chunks.text: 630 Adolescence 10.2. Minoritized Demographic Groups (Ethnic, Cultural, Linguistic, Religious, Political) Understanding an individual’s inner functioning necessarily implies integrating individual and cultural dimensions. von Bertalanffy (1968) considers culture and the self as systems, conceiving them as a “set of elements standing in interrelation.” “Culture” is defined as an open and dynamic system of interconnected knowledge, concepts, rules, and practices affected by individual and social changes over time. This perspective allows investigation into how selves and cultures influence each other and are continuously involved in mutual interactive exchanges. Human development involves many aspects: physical, intellectual, economic, artistic, spiritual, emotional, social, and moral. Adolescence provides windows of opportunity for the expression and development of different individual attributes. During this long process, adolescents receive multiple cultural messages about what is expected from them, often starting with their gender. The empirical and clinical literature has tended to focus on individual (e.g., temperament), interactional (e.g., parenting behaviors), and relational (e.g., attachment, friendship) influences on development but until recently has largely overlooked the role played by cultural norms and beliefs in interpreting the appropriateness of individual characteristics and the kinds of interactions and relationships that are considered possible or culturally acceptable. Social categories such as culture, subculture, ethnicity, and religion influence children’s and parents’ functioning by shaping the other ecological systems according to their unique, developmentally relevant characteristics. Matsumoto (1997) defines culture as the “attitudes, values, beliefs, and behaviors shared by a group of people, communicated from one generation to the next” (p. 5). Cultural systems include predominant values and beliefs, economic and social resources, and available opportunities for personal growth. Clinicians should consider all cultural systems in which an adolescent is involved: family (with its cultural and spiritual beliefs and practices), community (the nature of the neighborhood in terms of resources and culture, as well as safety and other conditions), and peer relationships (predominant activities with peers, formal and informal relations, the nature of peer culture). Encouragement of multicultural sensitivity in psychotherapy training programs should increase the effectiveness of psychotherapy, as competence in managing the intersection of race and immigration has been found to be relevant to the therapeutic relationship (Hayes, Owen, & Bieschke, 2015; Tummala-­Narra, 2015, 2020a), especially in transference and countertransference dynamics (cf. Tumarra-­ Narra, 2020b). Scholarship in these areas suggests that clinicians should do the following: • Recognize that specific racial, ethnic, and cultural factors influence the treatment process. • Try to understand and respect each adolescent’s cultural surround and commit to a multilevel understanding of the person that encompasses family, community, helping systems, and culture. • Consider symptoms from both a cultural and clinical perspective. • Consider that an adolescent may be caught between multiple cultures—­the values of the family and those of the surrounding society. This may be of special relevance in identity formation, as the adolescent tries to integrate disparate cultural traditions.

chunks.heading: Psychological Experiences That May Require Attention 631
chunks.preview: Psychological Experiences That May Require Attention 631 • Incorporate relevant cultural, racial, and economic factors into treatment planning. • Appreciate that an adolescent’s “family” may differ from the nuclear family; in many cultures, it includes several generations and extended family relati...
chunks.text: Psychological Experiences That May Require Attention 631 • Incorporate relevant cultural, racial, and economic factors into treatment planning. • Appreciate that an adolescent’s “family” may differ from the nuclear family; in many cultures, it includes several generations and extended family relationships. Clinicians should avoid the following: • Failing to recognize the cultural dimension or minimizing its relevance, as in beliefs that “one developmental theory fits all” and that “everybody is the same.” • Considering single dimensions as separate from others (biological, emotional, familial, community, cultural). • Devaluing spirituality and family belief systems. Spirituality may be foundational support for teens in minoritized populations; statements that devalue spirituality or other cultural beliefs may alienate adolescents and risk their withdrawal from treatment. Ecklund (2012) suggests applying intersectionality theory to patients with multiple cultural group identities. “Intersectionality of identity” refers to how an individual embodies multiple group identities; a child may possess multiple intersecting identities, and the child’s family members may each represent intersectionality of identity within the family unit (Mahalingam, Balan, & Haritatos, 2008). Such a perspective allows a therapist to understand simultaneous and multiple influences of the diverse cultural group values, norms, and expectations that contribute to a complex individual identity (Narváez, Meyer, Kertzner, Ouellette, & Gordon, 2009; Shih & Sanchez, 2009). Cultural psychiatrists suggest that clinicians conduct cultural assessments with all adolescents, not just those who are perceived to be from minoritized backgrounds, because identity, social relationships, and age-­related responsibilities are in flux during this developmental phase (Aggarwal, 2010). Some questions providing a cultural lens to clinical work should focus on cultural identity and cultural explanations of illness, cultural factors related to the psychosocial environment, and levels of functioning and those related to the patient–­clinician relationship. Clinical Illustration A 16-year-old Pakistani Muslim boy was referred to a psychologist because of frequent school absences. He said he felt apathetic and did not want to leave home; in fact, he wanted to stay out of school permanently. Some boys had begun to tease him at lunch because his religion prohibited his eating certain foods. Although he had support from close friends, he felt the conflict between his religious values and the school context. The therapist tried to help him feel safe to express feelings, fears, needs, desires, and conflicts about the process of reaching an integrated and comfortably differentiated identity. 10.3. Lesbian, Gay, and Bisexual Populations As noted in the corresponding section of Chapter 15 (pp. 875–896), lesbian, gay, and bisexual (LGB) adolescents who live in stigmatizing environments are routinely subject to minority stress, including internalized homophobia, that can undermine

chunks.heading: 632 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 632 Adolescence psychological and relational well-being (for a recent review, see de Lange et al., 2022). Minority stress can affect the development of sexual identity in young people because of its impact on self-­acceptance and the coming-­out process. LGB adolescents are often subjected to stigma...
chunks.text: 632 Adolescence psychological and relational well-being (for a recent review, see de Lange et al., 2022). Minority stress can affect the development of sexual identity in young people because of its impact on self-­acceptance and the coming-­out process. LGB adolescents are often subjected to stigmatization, victimization, bullying, and cyberbullying. Their early attractions may be associated with beliefs that they are “sick,” feelings of being different from peers, and fear of disappointing parents, teachers, or friends. In some individuals, antipathy toward one’s own same-­gender feelings may be externalized by committing violence against peers perceived as lesbian, gay, or “nonconforming” to gender expectations. Given the pervasiveness of heteronormativity and sexism, development in children who grow up to be LGB is often characterized by “gender confusion” and “gender stress” (Drescher, 1998). In gender confusion, individuals interpret their first same-­ gender attractions in terms of gender stereotypes. For example, a girl may think, “Since I’m attracted to women, then I’m a man,” or a boy may think, “Since I’m attracted to men, then I’m a woman.” Self-­stigmatizing responses such as “I am depraved,” “I will be alone,” or “I am a sinner” may also be present. Gender stress can occur over a protracted period, as early same-­gender attractions become linked to gender-­ nonconforming interests, and as individuals come to realize they are failing to meet the cultural and social expectations of their assigned gender. LGB adults, whether gender-­conforming or nonconforming as children, have described gender stress in their attempts to integrate their early homoerotic feelings into their gender identity. “Homonegativity” is pervasive in many contexts, and most young people are exposed to its effects. In school environments, homonegative bullying ranges from ridicule to disapproval to violent attacks on LGB people. Because gender and sexuality are essential parts of identity, homophobic bullying is more destructive than other bullying (Berg, Munthe-­Kaas, & Ross, 2016; Lingiardi & Nardelli, 2014; Rivers, 2011). Compared with heterosexual peers, sexual minority youth are at increased risk of being bullied (Earnshaw, Bogart, Poteat, Reisner, & Schuster, 2016; Evans & Chapman, 2014). The internet and social media bring a high risk of cyberbullying, which is often more insidious and more difficult to control, as it often relies on anonymity and has a far wider reach among bystanders (Abreu & Kenny, 2017; Cooper & Blumenfeld, 2012). For adolescents, as for adults (see Chapter 15), attention should be paid to the microaggressions that gender and sexual minorities suffer daily. Forms of microaggressions for LGB teenagers include explicitly denigrating and offensive messages, more subtle criticisms, and disrespect in social environments (school, sports, etc.). Microaggressions can give rise to a wide range of effects on physical and psychological well-being (for a review, see Nadal, Whitman, Davis, Erazo, & Davidoff., 2016). A critical developmental task for LGB adolescents is that of “coming out.” The term is short for “coming out of the closet,” an expression that means “revealing one’s orientation to others.” Coming out can have great relevance to a young person’s psychological well-being. Being able to communicate one’s homoerotic fantasies or desires to others results in greater recognition of these feelings and allows homoerotic attractions and related feelings to be integrated into one’s identity (Guittar & Rayburn, 2016; Sand, 2015). Managing one’s disclosures is a crucial but far from simple task. It is a communicative act that, while it may foster authenticity in a relationship, may elicit hostile reactions. From the adolescent’s point of view, a central fear about coming out is of disappointing or displeasing parents, and thus compromising the serenity of the family. Family rejection can interfere with developmental pathways and lead to negative health outcomes, including suicidal behaviors (de Lange et al., 2022; Ryan,

chunks.heading: 638 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 638 Adolescence factors for adolescents with nonbinary identities. Recent research demonstrates that compared to binary transgender adolescents, those with nonbinary identities tend to have lower family and friendship support and higher risk of anxiety, depression, low self-­esteem, substance use, s...
chunks.text: 638 Adolescence factors for adolescents with nonbinary identities. Recent research demonstrates that compared to binary transgender adolescents, those with nonbinary identities tend to have lower family and friendship support and higher risk of anxiety, depression, low self-­esteem, substance use, self-harm, and eating disorders (Chew et al., 2020). The ways the environment has responded to gender-­variant behavior can range from accepting and supporting to rejecting and stigmatizing. Parents often have a hard time tolerating ambivalence and want to know as soon as possible what their child’s gender identity will be. They then present themselves to specialist services or to clinicians with this question. To help them tolerate the ambiguity, it may be preferable to tell families (and youngsters) that it is too early to know which way development will unfold, rather than referring to gender-­atypical behaviors and feelings as a “phase”—a response that may feel disapproving or minimizing. It may also be useful to explore what the child’s gender-­atypical presentation means (Adelson & AACAP CQI, 2012), and to educate youth and families about the possibilities and limitations of different treatment approaches. Feeling accepted and free to talk can bring considerable relief to everyone. An interdisciplinary approach is strongly recommended. Mental health professionals should cooperate with a pediatric endocrinologist when reassignment treatment is needed (Coleman et al., 2022). Subjective Experience of Gender Incongruence in Adolescents Affective States Affective states include anxious and depressed moods. Especially with the onset of secondary sexual characteristics, there may be feelings of hopelessness and thoughts of suicide. In response to minority stress and internalized transphobia, gender-­ incongruent adolescents may suffer guilt toward their parents, contempt toward themselves, and anger directed inward or at other people, based on the belief that they are sick. Clinicians may help youth with gender incongruence to work through three levels of gender dysphoria: psychological, physical, and social. These forms of dysphoria are conceptually interrelated: The first refers to the discomfort one may feel toward oneself as a noncisgender person or as a person who is “not like other people.” The second refers to feelings of distress and inadequacy about different parts of one’s body (e.g., primary and secondary sexual characters). The third is experienced when one has to be in social and relational contexts. The micro- and macroaggressions most associated with social dysphoria involve victimization and misgendering. Discomfort may be relieved when an adolescent is permitted to express the experienced gender (e.g., wearing clothing associated with it). Cognitive Patterns Cognitive patterns include preoccupations with gender, which usually disappear if and when transition services are available and provided. Change of first name and pronouns may be desired. Some transgender individuals are diagnosable as being on the autistic spectrum. These adolescents require a differential diagnosis, because gender-­ nonconforming identity may also be part of the rigid convictions that are typical for autism. Although specific cognitive characteristics of some adolescents with gender incongruence (rigidity, inflexible beliefs) have led some researchers to hypothesize an overlap with autistic spectrum functioning (see Øien, Cicchetti, & Nordahl-­Hansen,

chunks.heading: Psychological Experiences That May Require Attention 639
chunks.preview: Psychological Experiences That May Require Attention 639 2018), this hypothesis has not been further confirmed (Turban & van Schalkwyk, 2018). It may be useful, however, for clinicians to pay attention to this possibility, especially when a teen has developed “autistic” defenses to cope with dyspho...
chunks.text: Psychological Experiences That May Require Attention 639 2018), this hypothesis has not been further confirmed (Turban & van Schalkwyk, 2018). It may be useful, however, for clinicians to pay attention to this possibility, especially when a teen has developed “autistic” defenses to cope with dysphoria. Somatic States Somatic states may include extreme discomfort with one’s own physical sexual features. Actual and anticipated body morphology may evoke dismay. Attempts to hide one’s sexual characteristics are common: Assigned females at birth (AFAB) may bind their breasts, and assigned males at birth (AMAB) may bind their genitals to make erections less visible. If sexually active, they may exclude their primary sex characteristics from lovemaking. It can be hard for cisgender clinicians to fully understand the experiences of gender-­incongruent adolescents during this period. Breast development, for example, may be perceived as foreign or dangerous, and can be highly traumatic. It can become impossible to go to school or other places outside the home, except by concealing the body in loose clothing or binding it up. This increased level of body dysphoria, along with the social gender intensification involved in adolescence, can result in social withdrawal accompanied by a strong sense of isolation and worthlessness. Relational Patterns Relational patterns vary. Adolescents may encounter social difficulties, often within school contexts, with gender-­atypical interests, leading to their social isolation. They may also be the object of various kinds of discrimination (e.g., both transphobic and homophobic bullying). Sometimes their own family members victimize them. Increasingly, progressive communities are creating safe spaces for these teens. As is true for other gender and sexual minorities, friendships can play a crucial role in mitigating minority stress for adolescents with gender incongruence. The sexuality of many adolescents with gender incongruence may be significantly inhibited until they are given the freedom to experience and express openly their true gender identity. Subjective Experience of the Therapist Clinician countertransferences may range from lack of acceptance of gender incongruence, including rejection of an adolescent’s wish to transition, to uncritical acceptance of the stated gender preference, without considering alternative explanations (differential diagnosis). It is common for cisgender therapists to feel intense emotions when faced with the possibilities or realities of medical and surgical interventions for an adolescent patient. Such reactions can include high levels of anxiety or concern, even perceptions of mutilation (Hansbury, 2017). It is important for therapists to fully understand and process these feelings so that they do not translate into excessive anxiety that undermines the adolescent’s trust in the therapist and thus the therapeutic alliance. Therapists need to tolerate the anxiety and uncertainty about outcomes that are typical of these stages. In this way, they can provide gender-­incongruent adolescents and their families with room to explore their experience with their own bodies, gender, social roles, and relationships. In such a space, individuals can feel recognized in their incongruence and understand its meaning in light of the biopsychosocial complexity that characterizes gender variance.

chunks.heading: 640 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 640 Adolescence Clinical Illustration Lily, 15 years old, AMAB, has shown a preference for feminine toys and clothing since early childhood. Her parents have consistently downplayed these gender-­incongruent behaviors, believing that they would fade as their son grew older. An isolated adolescent, s...
chunks.text: 640 Adolescence Clinical Illustration Lily, 15 years old, AMAB, has shown a preference for feminine toys and clothing since early childhood. Her parents have consistently downplayed these gender-­incongruent behaviors, believing that they would fade as their son grew older. An isolated adolescent, she claimed to have only one friend, in another city, with whom she communicated exclusively through chat. Lily would often retreat into her own world of comic book reading and drawing. Around age 12, she developed eating problems with a bulimic pattern, and her social isolation increased. At 14, she began cutting her arms and then her face. She refused to go to school until she had meticulously plucked each facial hair from her cheeks, often causing wounds and bruises. Her parents began to make some concessions but remained strongly opposed to any transition process. After various negotiations, driven by her escalating suicide threats, for which they sought the help of a psychologist, they allowed her to dye her hair various colors and wear nail polish. Currently, Lily wishes to begin hormone therapy, although she is not well-­ informed about it. She desires facial plastic surgery as well, including cheek augmentation, jawline lightening, and nose reconstruction. She follows many girls on Instagram who have undergone similar procedures. chunks.heading: CHAPTER 11
chunks.preview: CHAPTER 11 Psychodynamic Diagnostic Assessment in the Early Adult Years C H A P T E R E D IT O R Annalisa Tanzilli, PhD It may surprise contemporary readers that only a few generations ago, the concept of “adolescence” itself was groundbreaking. The word had existed previously but had not been in ge...
chunks.text: CHAPTER 11 Psychodynamic Diagnostic Assessment in the Early Adult Years C H A P T E R E D IT O R Annalisa Tanzilli, PhD It may surprise contemporary readers that only a few generations ago, the concept of “adolescence” itself was groundbreaking. The word had existed previously but had not been in general use until changes in the industrially developed world essentially created this developmental phase. In 1904, G. Stanley Hall, the first president of the American Psychological Association, published the highly influential Adolescence: Its Psychology and Its Relations to Physiology, Anthropology, Sociology, Sex, Crime, Religion, and Education, defining adolescence as the period between an individual’s physical maturation into the capacity for parenthood and the age at which the surrounding community deemed him or her a fully grown-up member. Preliterate and preindustrial social groups, in contrast to those of colonizers, had no waiting-­for-­adulthood phase in which teenagers had to learn skills and knowledge necessary to take on roles that demanded extensive expertise. Instead, young people in such tribes would undergo rites of passage that marked their immediate transformation from child into adult. Hall contrasted modern societies, describing the difficulties of adolescence in terms of mood disruptions, conflict with parents, and risky behaviors. Ever since that conceptual window opened between childhood and adulthood, we have tended increasingly to subdivide adolescence. The more complex life becomes, the more choices young people face, the more their fully adult status is postponed, and the more they inhabit a limbo between childhood and acknowledgment as adults. This liminal space offers not only extensive opportunities but also many hazards and frustrations. “Emerging adulthood,” generally conceptualized as ages 18–25 years, encompasses the transition from adolescent to adult identity (e.g., Arnett, 2000; Munsey, 2006). It is characterized by complex transformations in several domains of psychological

chunks.heading: Psychodynamic Diagnostic Assessment 645
chunks.preview: Psychodynamic Diagnostic Assessment 645 functioning. This phase has only recently been recognized (Arnett, 2000, 2007) as a specific developmental stage, distinct from others and notable for exploring various life choices, perspectives, and directions. Given the major shifts related to social role...
chunks.text: Psychodynamic Diagnostic Assessment 645 functioning. This phase has only recently been recognized (Arnett, 2000, 2007) as a specific developmental stage, distinct from others and notable for exploring various life choices, perspectives, and directions. Given the major shifts related to social role expectations, relationships, living arrangements, and vocational engagement, it is an important but stressful time. In this period, individuals face developmental tasks that involve the gradual consolidation of their senses of identity, the construction of more stable, intimate, and secure significant extrafamilial relationships, and the attainment of greater psychological and socioeconomic independence (cf. Schulenberg, Sameroff, & Cicchetti, 2004; Shulman, 2017). Individuals in this developmental cohort report high levels of psychological distress (GBD 2019 Mental Disorder Collaborators, 2022). During and after the COVID-19 pandemic, while facing many obstacles to normal maturational goals, younger adults reported unprecedented levels of anxiety and intense feelings of insecurity and helplessness, leading to increased worries about their future choices and sometimes to avoidance of interpersonal situations (e.g., Hawes, Szenczy, Klein, Hajcak, & Nelson, 2022). Unlike other phases, emerging adulthood is not conceptualized as a period of life with universal and immutable features. It is culturally constructed (Arnett, 2000, p. 470), rooted in the profound changes in Western societies over the past 50 years (Arnett, 2015). Longer educational demands facing many in this group, prolonged transitions to stable employment, and delays in taking on commitments and responsibilities (e.g., marriage or parenthood) associated with significant changes in traditional gender roles, have required a new conceptualization of this phase of life (Arnett, 2000, 2015). In other words, emerging adulthood is, like adolescence itself, the result of processes connected to industrial and postindustrial progress and to the growing globalization of the world economy. The construct thus applies to certain cultures and specific times in which entry into adult roles is postponed. Because not all models of psychological maturation have considered emerging adulthood as a separate developmental phase, we mention some scholarship that may help clinicians address the specific issues of this developmental period. For example, Peter Blos (1979) divided the adolescent years into preadolescence, early adolescence, adolescence, late adolescence, and postadolescence, noting that in normative development, the last stage involves a “gradual extinction” of childhood and adolescent psychological conflicts, and the consolidation of a coherent, stable identity. An implication of Blos’s model is that any stagnation at this stage may represent a roadblock in the journey to adulthood and is inherently psychopathological. Erik Erikson’s (1950, 1968) theory of development originally conceptualized eight stages, each representing a potential crisis involving a qualitative change in functioning. According to this perspective, the stage of “identity versus role confusion” that connoted adolescence was followed by the stage of “intimacy versus isolation” in young adulthood (which roughly spanned the 20s and 30s). In some cases, however, Erikson envisioned a “physiological” extension of adolescence or a kind of “psychosocial moratorium,” that is, a period devoted to self-­exploration and role experimentation. If it did not go on too long, this phase was necessary to establish the stable identity essential for young adults to find their place in society. Kenneth Kenniston (e.g., 1960) made comparable observations in the 1960s, when a “youth culture” was first observed in Western societies. James Marcia (1966) similarly described an identity moratorium, a period of exploration without commitment. According to these authors, at this postadolescent stage, the individual freely explores different personal and vocational opportunities without feeling bound by

chunks.heading: 646 Adolescence
chunks.preview: 646 Adolescence rigid social obligations. From this perspective, emerging adulthood represents an opportunity more than a pathological condition. Recent years have seen a growing consensus that child and adolescent development does not proceed through a predictable series of stages that culminate in...
chunks.text: 646 Adolescence rigid social obligations. From this perspective, emerging adulthood represents an opportunity more than a pathological condition. Recent years have seen a growing consensus that child and adolescent development does not proceed through a predictable series of stages that culminate in the construction of a solid identity in late adolescence or early adulthood (Schwartz, Côté, & Arnett, 2005). Jeffrey Arnett (2015), who introduced the concept of emerging adulthood, noted its key characteristics: 1. Identity explorations: Emerging adults explore various options for their lives, especially in romantic relationships and careers. Unlike in adolescence, traditionally associated with identity formation, exploration during this phase helps develop a higher level of self-­awareness. 2. Instability: Identity explorations and changing options in emotional ties and work contexts make this period notably unstable. Individuals often change residences, jobs, and relationships, trying to find balance and a reliable future direction. Ongoing revision of decisions contributes to personal growth, even though it may involve insecurity. 3. Self-focus: Emerging adulthood is a period when individuals focus primarily on themselves, making autonomous decisions, developing skills, and gaining deeper self-­understanding. 4. Feeling in-­between: In this phase, not only do individuals often not define themselves as adolescents but they also do not consider themselves fully mature. Emergence from this in-­between state occurs through the gradual consolidation of three qualities that define individuals as adults: accepting responsibility for themselves, making decisions independently, and becoming economically autonomous (cf. Arnett, 2018). 5. Possibilities/optimism: Optimism and a sense of possibility are notable in emerging adulthood. Young adults still have many opportunities available, and this period offers the chance to alter the direction of their lives. Especially for those from difficult backgrounds, it allows them to redefine themselves and determine their future. According to these perspectives, emerging adulthood represents a distinct phase of human development that prepares postadolescents to face the commitments of adult life with greater self-­awareness and better-­articulated aspirations. These “young adults” navigate life in heterogeneous ways based on reciprocal interactions between individual factors (e.g., cultural beliefs, social class, life events, personal resources) and contextual ones (e.g., sociocultural and economic pressures). Numerous trajectories are possible in education, work, personal belief, self-­growth, and relationships (Arnett, ukauskiené, & Sugimura, 2014; cf. Schechter, Herbstman, Ronningstam, & Goldblatt, 2018). Given its complexity, emerging adulthood may also see the onset of various psychopathologies (Solmi et al., 2022), though in many cases, mental problems at this age have roots in childhood or adolescence and may become more fixed in this phase through homotypic or heterotypic continuities (cf. Copeland, Shanahan, Costello, & Angold, 2009; Speranza, Liotti, Spolentini, & Fortunato, 2023). For individuals going through this phase, a psychodynamically grounded diagnosis offers a complex, in-depth perspective for understanding challenges related to identity definition, the search for autonomy, and the formation of significant relationships. Accurate assessment of the emerging adult’s intrapersonal processes and interpersonal

chunks.heading: Psychodynamic Diagnostic Assessment 647
chunks.preview: Psychodynamic Diagnostic Assessment 647 dynamics is vital for planning early interventions and “developmentally informed” preventive programs that target this specific population (Arnett et al., 2014; cf. Nelson, 2021). Our developmental orientation in this edition of the PDM emphasizes understandi...
chunks.text: Psychodynamic Diagnostic Assessment 647 dynamics is vital for planning early interventions and “developmentally informed” preventive programs that target this specific population (Arnett et al., 2014; cf. Nelson, 2021). Our developmental orientation in this edition of the PDM emphasizes understanding and interpreting maturational needs and tasks specific to this phase. We note and frame the conflicts and crises of the emerging adult in the context of early adult development, attempting to recognize positive potentials, while identifying some challenges and possible difficulties of this inevitably intricate transition. Diagnostic assessment in emerging adulthood can be difficult. To prepare mental health professionals for working with individuals in this life phase, and to equip them with the tools necessary to develop accurate case formulations and personalized interventions, we advise an approach that is multidimensional and sensitive to the unique characteristics of this age-­specific group. We recommend a combination of tools and techniques, including clinical interviews (with family, friends, teachers, or colleagues), questionnaires and standardized self-­report tools (to evaluate psychological functioning and identify areas of suffering, particularly anxiety and depression), and clinician-­ report measures. We suggest that clinicians use the PDM’s Psychodiagnostic Chart for adults (PDC), though in individual cases involving later psychological maturation, the PDC for adolescents (PDC-A) may be more appropriate. Our multiaxial approach respects the heterogeneity of emerging personality styles, mental functioning, and symptom patterns in emerging adulthood. In fact, inherent in this stage of growth is the risk of personality disorders that significantly interfere with young adults’ adaptations to their emotional, interpersonal, and social contexts. In the transition from adolescence to adulthood, the prevalence rate of these pathologies has been put at about 20.0%, with borderline diagnoses being especially frequent (D’Huart et al., 2022). Such findings may reflect problems in the identity formation process characteristic of this stage (Arnett et al., 2014). With respect to mental functioning, clinicians who work with emerging adults need to pay attention to this, because their clients are repeatedly reorganizing and negotiating intrapsychic and interpersonal relationships between self and environment. With respect to symptom patterns, diagnosable disorders may arise or manifest themselves more clearly in emerging adulthood (Solmi et al., 2022). Depressive conditions are common, with a prevalence of 4% in people ages 20–24 (WHO, 2022), and are increasing rapidly, especially in women (cf. Thapar, Collishaw, Pine, & Thapar, 2012). According to the World Health Organization (2022), anxiety disorders have a similar prevalence rate of 4.7% in this population. Because both depression and anxiety can derail development, preventive interventions are essential to support young people in this often-­fraught period. In summary, clinical work with emerging adults requires a sensitive, developmentally oriented diagnostic approach that takes into account the complexity of their experiences and the challenges involved in their transitions to full adulthood. chunks.heading: CHAPTER 12
chunks.preview: CHAPTER 12 Personality Styles and Disorders P Axis C H A P T E R E D IT O R S Nancy McWilliams, PhD, ABPP Jonathan Shedler, PhD CONSULTANTS Allan Abbass, MD Robert Feinstein, MD Steven K. Huprich, PhD John F. Clarkin, PhD Glen O. Gabbard, MD Robert Michels, MD Ferhan Dereboy, MD Brin Grenyer, PhD Fr...
chunks.text: CHAPTER 12 Personality Styles and Disorders P Axis C H A P T E R E D IT O R S Nancy McWilliams, PhD, ABPP Jonathan Shedler, PhD CONSULTANTS Allan Abbass, MD Robert Feinstein, MD Steven K. Huprich, PhD John F. Clarkin, PhD Glen O. Gabbard, MD Robert Michels, MD Ferhan Dereboy, MD Brin Grenyer, PhD Frank Yeomans, MD, PhD Introduction A model of personality is ultimately a form of map. Its purpose is to help users find their bearings, orient themselves with respect to recognizable landmarks, and navigate to their desired destination. Different maps serve different purposes. Motorists need road maps and mountaineers needs topographical maps of elevations and terrain contours. It is not sufficient for a map to be accurate; it must also serve the purpose for which it will be used. The explicit purpose of the PDM-3 P Axis is to help clinical practitioners better understand the psychological makeup of their patients, anticipate clinical challenges, and navigate the terrain of psychotherapy. Other personality maps, such as those provided by the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (currently DSM-5-TR; American Psychiatric Association, 2022) and International Classification of Diseases (currently ICD-11; World Health Organization, 2019), do not adequately serve this purpose. They have advantages and disadvantages, but clinical practitioners generally report that they do not help them understand their patients more deeply or offer information to guide the work of psychotherapy (e.g., Spitzer, First, Shedler, Westen, & Skodol, 2008).

chunks.heading: 652 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 652 Adulthood One important difference between DSM and ICD maps and the P Axis is that the former taxonomies emphasize “disordered” personality types. In contrast, the P Axis emphasizes that all people, whether healthy or troubled, have personalities and personality styles. Understanding personality...
chunks.text: 652 Adulthood One important difference between DSM and ICD maps and the P Axis is that the former taxonomies emphasize “disordered” personality types. In contrast, the P Axis emphasizes that all people, whether healthy or troubled, have personalities and personality styles. Understanding personality dynamics is crucial to clinical case formulation—­identifying underlying psychological factors that give rise to a person’s symptoms and presenting problems, and contribute to perpetuating them, or both. These underlying psychological processes are the focus of psychotherapies aimed at meaningful and lasting psychological change. An appreciation of them may also be useful to clinicians working with patients who are not in such therapies (e.g., patients in rehabilitation settings, coping with acute trauma and grief, or noncompliant with other treatment regimens). Although the concepts we present here may be most familiar to clinicians with psychodynamic backgrounds, our goal is to provide information to deepen understanding and enhance the clinical effectiveness of clinicians of any theoretical orientation (Lingiardi & McWilliams, 2015). We emphasize both our respect for other therapy models and our intention to be helpful to therapists of all therapeutic traditions. Major Organizing Principles: Personality Style and Level of Organization The two major organizing principles of the P Axis are (1) level of personality organization and (2) personality style. Level of organization is a spectrum of functioning that ranges from healthy and high functioning to extremely impaired. In keeping with historical clinical usage, we use the terms healthy, neurotic, borderline, and psychotic to describe levels of personality organization. Level of organization is also discussed in the clinical literature as the severity dimension. We use these terms interchangeably. Personality style refers to clinically familiar personality configurations (narcissistic, obsessive–­compulsive, dependent, paranoid, etc.) that practitioners have come to recognize over generations of clinical experience (e.g., MacKinnon, Michels, & Buckley, 2006; McWilliams, 2011; Shapiro 1965). In recent decades, the major personality styles have been confirmed via statistical clustering methods and their core features identified via empirical research (e.g., Shedler, 2022b; Westen & Shedler, 1999a, 1999b; Westen, Shedler, Bradley, & DeFife, 2012). The diagnostic system we offer here is therefore (1) evidence-­based and (2) reflective of clinical knowledge and understanding accrued over generations of clinical practice. Level of personality organization crosscuts personality style. Any personality style can, in principle, exist at any level of personality organization. For example, we may see a person with a narcissistic style organized at a healthy, neurotic, borderline, or psychotic level. A person with a narcissistic style at a high level of personality organization may be a leader or visionary, and may achieve extraordinary successes in life. A person with a narcissistic style at a low level of organization may be dysfunctional in many or all life domains. When clinicians locate an individual with respect to both personality style and level of organization, they have already developed the broad strokes of a clinical case formulation that can provide a road map to effective treatment for that individual (Shedler, 2022a). All people have a personality; it is a mistake to conflate personality styles with “disorders.” Unfortunately, the DSM, from 1980 on, has led many clinicians to make

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 653
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 653 just this mistake. To include personality styles in a taxonomy of “disorders,” the framers of DSM-III (American Psychiatric Association, 1980) described personality styles in their most extreme and pathological forms (i.e., at a low level of personality organiza...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 653 just this mistake. To include personality styles in a taxonomy of “disorders,” the framers of DSM-III (American Psychiatric Association, 1980) described personality styles in their most extreme and pathological forms (i.e., at a low level of personality organization). As a result, many clinicians learned to ignore personality entirely, unless a patient meets criteria for a specific DSM-­defined personality disorder. Some have come to equate any clinical reference to personality with pathology. As a result, clinical understanding of personality has generally devolved rather than evolved, in ways that serve neither mental health professionals nor the individuals we treat. Conceptualizing Personality Personality is more about who one is as a person than what disorder one has. It comprises much more than what is outwardly observable via behavior or measured traits; it subsumes a range of internal psychological processes, including temperament, motives, fantasy life, inner conflicts, characteristic patterns of thinking and feeling, ways of experiencing self and others, ways of attaching and relating, and ways of coping and defending (the historical term was “character”; we use “personality” and “character” interchangeably). Irrespective of overt presenting problems, many patients come to realize, as they participate in therapy, that their difficulties are inextricably tied up with who they are. They need their clinicians to comprehend something psychologically systemic that helps them understand why they are repeatedly vulnerable to certain kinds of suffering. Psychological problems are often complexly intertwined with personality, may be the flip side of a person’s strengths, and need to be appreciated in the context of the whole person, as well as the larger relational and cultural contexts in which they are embedded. If the goal of mental health treatment is meaningful psychological change, understanding a person’s overall psychological makeup and developmental trajectories may ultimately be more important than diagnosing symptoms or using disorder-­specific techniques and interventions (American Psychological Association, 2012; Li et al., 2024; Luyten & Fonagy, 2021; Norcross & Wampold, 2018; Sharp et al., 2015; Sharp & Wall, 2021; Shedler, 2022a). Consequently, a skilled therapist conducting a clinical interview not only assesses a patient’s current symptoms, mental status, and sociocultural context but the patient’s personality functioning, including strengths, weaknesses, and major organizing themes (see Eels, 2022; Peebles-­K leiger, 2014). In psychotherapy outcome studies, investigators are starting to attend to the role of personality differences in both therapists and patients (e.g., Blatt, 2004, 2008; Duncan, Miller, Wampold, & Hubble, 2010; Gabbard, 2009a, 2009b; Heinonen & Nissen-­Lie, 2020; Høglend et al., 2008; Li et al., 2024; Lingiardi, Muzi, Tanzilli, & Carone, 2018; Miller & Moyers, 2021; Norcross & Wampold, 2018; Roth & Fonagy, 2005; Westen, Novotny, & Thompson-­Brenner, 2004). Personality Styles versus Personality Disorders There is no clear demarcation between a personality style and a personality disorder. All people have personality styles or blends of them. The term “disorder” is a linguistic convenience for clinicians, denoting a degree of extremity or rigidity that causes significant dysfunction, limitation, or suffering to self or others (Feinstein, 2022). As noted earlier, one can have a narcissistic personality style without having a narcissistic

chunks.heading: 654 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 654 Adulthood personality disorder (or a depressive style, dependent style, paranoid style, obsessive–­ compulsive style, and so on). Another consideration in applying the term “disorder” is that the personality patterns are of long-­standing duration and so integral to the person’s experience that...
chunks.text: 654 Adulthood personality disorder (or a depressive style, dependent style, paranoid style, obsessive–­ compulsive style, and so on). Another consideration in applying the term “disorder” is that the personality patterns are of long-­standing duration and so integral to the person’s experience that they cannot remember or easily imagine being different. The personality patterns may be as invisible to the person as water to a fish. Some patients are entirely oblivious to their dysfunctional personality patterns and come to treatment only at the urging of others. When they arrive for a clinical consultation, they are likely to tell the clinician they have come at someone else’s behest and may have considerable trouble articulating difficulties or problem areas for which they themselves would like help. Others come of their own accord, typically seeking treatment not for personality patterns but for a more specific, circumscribed complaint such as anxiety, depression, an eating disorder, somatic symptoms, addictions, phobias, self-harm, trauma, or relationship problems. It is important to differentiate personality from symptom constellations, organic brain syndromes, neurodivergence, and psychotic spectrum disorders. Ritualized behavior, for example, may indicate an obsessive–­compulsive disorder unrelated to personality, or may be one piece of evidence for an obsessive–­compulsive personality style, or may express a psychotic delusion, or may be the result of an organic brain syndrome, or may reflect repetitive behavior associated with the autistic spectrum. It is also important to evaluate whether what appears to be personality is a response to ongoing situational stress. For example, a man who had no notable psychological problems before leaving his homeland, who has moved into a society whose language he does not speak, may appear paranoid, dependent, or otherwise disturbed in personality functioning (Akhtar, 2010; Najjarkakhaki & Ghane, 2023). Under sufficient strain or trauma, any of us can look borderline or even psychotic. It is therefore not possible to identify a personality style or disorder without considering the range of other possibilities that could explain the patient’s experience and behavior. Finally, individuals from cultures unfamiliar to the interviewer can be misunderstood as having personality disturbance. The concepts of normality and pathology are not fixed and universal; they vary, depending on cultural systems (Alarcón & Foulks, 1995a, 1995b; Clauss-­Ehlers, Chiriboga, Hunter, Roysircar, & Tummala-­ Narra, 2019; Kakar, 2008; Kitayama & Markus, 1999; Lewis-Fernández et al., 2014; Najjarkakhaki & Ghane, 2023; Tummala-­Narra, 2016). Part 1: Level of Personality Organization (Severity) The Continuum of Personality Health The recognition that personality health–­disturbance is a dimension or continuum has evolved over decades of clinical observation and empirical research (Caligor, Clarkin, & Sowislo, 2022; Hopwood et al., 2018). At the healthy end of the spectrum are people who show good functioning in the domains of identity, relationship, defenses, management of aggressive impulses, moral functioning, and relation to reality (Yeomans, Diamond & Caligor, 2024), who would be assessed as high on the PDM-3 M-Axis functions. They usually can engage in satisfying relationships, experience and understand a relatively full range of developmentally expectable feelings and thoughts, function relatively flexibly when stressed by external events or internal conflict, maintain a relatively coherent sense of personal identity, express or regulate impulses according to the situation, conduct themselves in accordance with internalized moral values, and

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 655
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 655 neither suffer undue distress nor impose it on others. At the severely disturbed end are people who respond to distress in rigidly inflexible ways (e.g., by relying on a restricted range of costly defenses) or have major and severe deficits in many of the domain...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 655 neither suffer undue distress nor impose it on others. At the severely disturbed end are people who respond to distress in rigidly inflexible ways (e.g., by relying on a restricted range of costly defenses) or have major and severe deficits in many of the domains described in the Summary of Basic Mental Functioning: M Axis (e.g., executive functioning, affect regulation, capacity to mentalize). Historical Context of Levels of Personality Organization By the end of the 19th century, psychiatric classification distinguished between two general types of psychiatric problems: (1) “neurosis,” a term that may refer to either minor or major psychopathology. in which the capacity to perceive reality is intact, and (2) “psychosis,” in which there is significant impairment in reality testing. In the ensuing decades, as clinicians slowly appreciated that many people suffer not from isolated symptoms but from issues that pervade their lives more comprehensively, they also began to distinguish between neurotic symptoms and neurotic character. The latter is roughly equivalent to the DSM concept of “personality disorder.” In the mid-20th century, clinical writers began describing individuals who seemed too disturbed to be considered neurotic yet too anchored in reality to be considered psychotic. Slowly, a “borderline” group was identified (Knight, 1953; Main, 1957; Stern, 1938). The concept of disturbance on the border between psychosis and neurosis was subsequently investigated empirically (e.g., Grinker, Werble, & Drye, 1968; Gunderson & Singer, 1975) and elaborated theoretically (e.g., Hartocollis, 1977; Kernberg, 1975, 1984; Masterson, 1976; Stone, 1980, 1986). Patients construed as psychologically organized on that border often fared badly in the kinds of treatments that were usually helpful to healthier patients. They would unexpectedly develop intense, problematic, and often rapidly shifting feelings toward their therapists. Some who did not show psychotic tendencies outside therapy developed intractable “psychotic transferences.” For example, they might experience their clinicians as omnipotently good or malevolently evil, or exactly like a person from their past, and could not be persuaded otherwise. Note that the term “borderline,” used by psychodynamic clinicians to denote a level of personality organization, has a different meaning from the term “borderline” in the DSM. The DSM description of “borderline personality disorder” is, in fact, a conflation of borderline level of personality organization and histrionic personality style. Our use of the term “borderline” as a level of organization is far broader than DSM usage and more consistent with the clinical observation and theory (e.g., ­Kernberg, 1975) that gave rise to the term—and led to its widespread clinical adoption to denote patients with identity diffusion, predominance of primitive defenses, and fragile but intact reality testing (Kernberg, 1984). It is also consistent with contemporary ideas about how a history of complex trauma can become structured into personality (see, e.g., Chefetz, 2015; Lewis, Caputi, & Grenyer, 2012; Lewis & Grenyer, 2009). Although each personality style can, in principle, exist at any level of organization, some personality styles are more likely to be found at the higher-­functioning (e.g., healthy or neurotic) end of the severity spectrum and others at the more disturbed (e.g., borderline or psychotic) end. For example, patients with hysterical or obsessional personality styles are more likely to be organized at a healthy or neurotic level of severity; those with paranoid or psychopathic styles are more likely to be organized at a borderline or psychotic level.

chunks.heading: 656 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 656 Adulthood The distinction between level of organization and personality style permits, for example, the identification of “quiet borderline” patients (Sherwood & Cohen, 1994), such as schizoid individuals who function at a borderline level of organization but have little resemblance to the emoti...
chunks.text: 656 Adulthood The distinction between level of organization and personality style permits, for example, the identification of “quiet borderline” patients (Sherwood & Cohen, 1994), such as schizoid individuals who function at a borderline level of organization but have little resemblance to the emotionally and interpersonally chaotic individuals who meet DSM criteria for borderline personality disorder. It also sheds light on the psychology of personality patterns such as severe paranoid, narcissistic, sadistic, and psychopathic styles, all of which can be understood as functioning at a borderline or psychotic level of organization. The DSM diagnosis “borderline personality disorder” confusingly conflates a personality style (histrionic) with a level of organization, and renders the categories of borderline personality disorder and histrionic personality disorder essentially indistinguishable empirically (Shedler & Westen, 2004). Despite this conceptual confusion in the DSM, we will refer to research and theory based on the DSM definition of borderline personality disorder given the extensive empirical literature on that diagnosis that has accrued since its inclusion in DSM-III in 1980 (see, e.g., Leichsenring et al., 2023). Kernberg (e.g., 2006) addresses the problem of discrepant uses of the term “borderline” by distinguishing between borderline personality disorder (the DSM concept) and borderline personality organization (the psychoanalytic concept). In PDM-2, we adopted his solution by describing a borderline level of personality organization and also including borderline personality as a diagnosable P-Axis personality style. This was an imperfect and inelegant solution that was inconsistent with our overall conceptual framework, but given the extensive literature on the DSM construct of borderline personality disorder and its widespread clinical acceptance, we believed it necessary to include it in the PDM. In this third edition, we have attempted to improve on this awkward solution and avoid some confusion by referring to the DSM-­defined condition as “emotionally dysregulated personality disorder.” Domains of Functioning Relevant to Assessing Level of Personality Organization Overall level of personality organization should be assessed as the first step of P-Axis diagnosis after considering the 13 psychological capacities included in the M-Axis profile of mental functioning. The levels of organization described in the next sections can be considered a general synthesis of these psychological capacities. Past generations of psychoanalytic theorists sometimes viewed level of personality organization in terms of “fixation” at, or regression to, a particular developmental challenge or stage, but this view now holds little currency. Contemporary theorists understand personality pathology as reflecting a confluence of factors including genetic heritability, temperament, early life experiences (e.g., trauma, neglect), and early attachments, among others (for a discussion of “biopsychosocial” models of personality, see, e.g., Leichsenring, Leibing, Kruse, New, & Leweke, 2011). When tested for attachment, individuals with more severe levels of organization typically show one of the insecure styles (Ainsworth, Blehar, Waters, & Wall, 1978). Evidence indicates that problems at the borderline and psychotic levels of organization may have a genetic component. Twin studies suggest that in both men and women, genetic influences account for about 40% of the variance in the severity of borderline traits (Distel et al., 2008; Torgersen et al., 2008). Genetic interpersonal hypersensitivity has been proposed as an endophenotype marker (Gunderson, 2007). Neuroimaging studies have revealed the functional neuroanatomy (including amygdala dysfunction) of characteristics associated with borderline personality, including

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 657
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 657 the hypersensitivity, intolerance for aloneness, and attachment fears typical of patients in this broad diagnostic group (Buchheim et al., 2008; Degasperi, Cristea, DiRosa, Costa, & Gentili (2021); Fertuck et al., 2009; King-Casas et al., 2008; Schulze, Schmahl,...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 657 the hypersensitivity, intolerance for aloneness, and attachment fears typical of patients in this broad diagnostic group (Buchheim et al., 2008; Degasperi, Cristea, DiRosa, Costa, & Gentili (2021); Fertuck et al., 2009; King-Casas et al., 2008; Schulze, Schmahl, & Niedtfeld, 2016). Healthy Level of Organization At the healthy level of personality organization, most of the psychological capacities described in the M Axis are well developed and generally reliably present. We say “generally” because, under sufficient stress, any capacity or combination of capacities can falter. Mental health at any point in time necessarily reflects an interaction of individual psychology and stressors, current and chronic. Some people who become symptomatic under stress are correctly described as functioning at a healthy level of personality organization based on their enduring psychological capacities. They may have certain characteristic patterns of personality functioning and favored ways of defending and coping, but they often blend characteristics of more than one personality style, and they have enough flexibility to accommodate adequately to challenging realities (though not necessarily to severe trauma, which can damage even people who have previously been quite resilient; see Boulanger, 2007; van der Kolk, 2014). Neurotic Level of Organization Despite having many capacities at the high end of the M Axis, individuals with neurotic-­ level personality organizations are notable for their relative rigidity (Caligor, Kernberg, Clarkin, & Yeomans, 2018; Shapiro, 1965, 1981); that is, they tend to respond to certain stresses with a comparatively restricted range of responses, defenses, and coping strategies. Common personality styles and disorders in this area on the severity continuum are depressive personality, obsessive–­compulsive personality, phobic personality, and hysteric personality (historically, “hysteric” or “hysterical” has been used to describe this personality style at higher levels of personality organization, and “histrionic” to describe it at borderline and psychotic levels). The emotional difficulties of people whose psychology is organized at the neurotic level tend to be restricted to specific psychological domains such as loss, rejection, and self-­punitiveness in depressive personality; issues of gender, sexuality, and power in hysteric personality; or preoccupations with authority and control in obsessive–­ compulsive personality. Individuals at the neurotic level of organization frequently experience their problems as involving inner discord or conflict. For example, they may feel sexual temptation, experienced as a desire in conflict with an inner prohibition, or they may find themselves recurrently angry while simultaneously feeling their anger is unjustified or excessive. Maladaptive defensive patterns of individuals in the neurotic range may be limited to the area of their specific psychological difficulty. For example, in contrast to patients organized at a borderline level, who use defenses that tend to distort reality more globally (e.g., denial, splitting, projective identification, omnipotent control, severe dissociation), the rigid or problematic defenses of neurotic-­level patients are more likely to concern one area—­problems with authority, for example, rather than problems in all relationships. Outside their areas of psychological difficulty, persons at the neurotic level may have a satisfactory work history, maintain good relations with others, enjoy a satisfying sexual life, tolerate dysphoric affects without taking

chunks.heading: 658 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 658 Adulthood impulsive or ill-­considered actions, and be able and ready to collaborate in a therapy relationship. Neurotic-­level patients often have some perspective on their recurrent difficulties and can imagine how they would like to change. They usually form adequate working alliances with th...
chunks.text: 658 Adulthood impulsive or ill-­considered actions, and be able and ready to collaborate in a therapy relationship. Neurotic-­level patients often have some perspective on their recurrent difficulties and can imagine how they would like to change. They usually form adequate working alliances with their clinicians. In initial interviews, therapists tend to react to them comfortably, respectfully, sympathetically, and with the expectation of a collaborative partnership. Borderline Level of Organization The most salient observable feature of people in the borderline range of personality organization is emotional dysregulation. They are vulnerable to extremes of overwhelming and rapidly shifting affect, including episodes of intense rage, deep depression, anxiety, and panic. They may have chaotic relationships, severe problems with intimacy, struggles with work, and difficulties with impulse regulation (including vulnerability to substance abuse and other compulsive behaviors; for example, gambling, shoplifting, binge eating, or addictions to sex, video games, or the internet). In periods of distress which are typically precipitated by disruption or threat to an important attachment, they may be at risk of self-harm, which can include self-­ mutilation, sexual risk taking, reckless spending, or other self-­destructive behavior (Caligor et al., 2018, 2022; Yeomans, Clarkin, & Kernberg, 2015). This behavior may reflect desperate efforts to regulate unbearable emotion, failures in impulse control, or both. As a general rule, people with personality dysfunction severe enough to warrant a DSM personality disorder diagnosis function at a borderline level of personality organization, irrespective of the specific DSM personality diagnosis. Borderline personality organization can be divided into higher levels (toward the border with the neuroses), describing patients whose overall personality presentation is closer to a neurotic personality organization (Caligor, Kernberg, & Clarkin, 2007), and lower levels (toward the border with the psychoses), describing patients with more severe disturbance (Clarkin et al., 2001). Patients at higher and lower levels of borderline organization may require different therapeutic approaches. A number of experienced clinicians suggest more exploratory (i.e., interpretive, insight-­oriented) treatments for higher-­level borderline patients and more supportive, capacity-­building treatments for lower-level borderline patients. Clinicians who described their patients using the Shedler–­Westen Assessment Procedure (SWAP; Lingiardi, Shedler, & Gazzillo, 2006; Shedler, 2022a, 2022b; Westen & Shedler, 1999a, 1999b, 2007; Westen et al., 2012) emphasized the affect and impulse regulation problems of individuals with borderline personality organization, noted the extremity and rawness of their emotions, and their reliance on defenses clinicians regard as costly, immature, or “primitive” (Cramer, 2015; Di Giuseppe & Lingiardi, 2023; Di Giuseppe & Perry, 2021; Kernberg, 1984; Kramer, de Roten, Perry, Presniak, & Olson, 2013; Vaillant, Bond, & Vaillant, 1986). Given that neurotic-­level individuals may also use primitive defenses under stress, it is arguable that what distinguishes borderline from neurotic organization may have more to do with the relative absence of mature defenses under stress than the existence of costly ones. The most noted costly defenses are splitting, projection, and projective identification. “Splitting” refers to compartmentalization (dissociation) of positive and negative perceptions and feelings, with the result that the person views self and others in black

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 659
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 659 and white, either–­or, and idealized or devalued categories (e.g., heroes and rescuers, villains and abusers, coldly indifferent enablers of abuse). Splitting may involve perceiving some people as “all good” and others as “all bad,” or alternating, contradictory...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 659 and white, either–­or, and idealized or devalued categories (e.g., heroes and rescuers, villains and abusers, coldly indifferent enablers of abuse). Splitting may involve perceiving some people as “all good” and others as “all bad,” or alternating, contradictory perceptions of the same person (Gairdner, 2002; Klein, 1946; Main, 1957). Splitting is linked to identity diffusion, or the inability to integrate disparate aspects of identity (self-­representations) into a coherent whole, or “stand in the spaces” between different self-­states (Bromberg, 1998), especially under stress.
