Consequently, patients whose personalities are organized at a borderline level show identity diffusion: Their attitudes, values, goals, and feelings about themselves tend to be unstable and changing, and their self-­perceptions can oscillate between contradictory extremes. Such patients may look quite different on different occasions, as different fragments of identity emerge. When feeling good, they may appear blithely indifferent to the fact that they were recently suicidal; when depressed, they may appear to have no access to any positive feelings or self-­perceptions whatever. Projection operates hand in hand with splitting and occurs when dissociated, disavowed aspects of self are attributed to someone else. A defining feature of borderline-­ level projection (in contrast to projection at higher levels of functioning) is its certainty; the person regards it as fact that their projected thoughts and feelings belong to the other person and is unwilling to consider other possibilities. Projective identification takes the defense of splitting a step further: in addition to projecting a disturbing aspect of their own personality onto someone else (e.g., the clinician), they proceed to treat that person in ways that elicit the very feelings and attitudes they have projected with such vehemence. A familiar example is a person who projects their own dissociated hatred onto another person, then treats that person so badly that the other person does indeed become enraged and hateful. This is then taken as absolute confirmation that all the badness and hate does indeed reside in the other person, not in themselves. Clinicians on the receiving end of such projective identifications may feel intense pressure to conform to the projection. Some describe being unable to think their own thoughts or feel their own feelings, as if their minds have been colonized by something alien (Gabbard & Wilkinson, 1994). Under such circumstances, effective intervention begins with internal work on the part of the clinician to reclaim the use of their own mind and work their way back from thinking the patient’s projected thoughts to thinking their own. Other defenses characteristic of persons in the borderline range include denial (ignoring or disregarding things that are disturbing, as if they did not exist); withdrawal (from reality into fantasy); indiscriminate forms of introjection (the wholesale “taking in” of someone else’s characteristics, attitudes, and even mannerisms—­ sometimes called “introjective identification”); omnipotent control (treating another as an extension of oneself and insisting that the other person thinks the thoughts assigned to them instead of their own); acting out (repetitively living out an internal drama that is not remembered, felt, or understood); severe dissociation (incongruous disconnections between different aspects of experience, or shifts of self-­states without a sense of the continuity of experience); extreme idealization (seeing another as all-good and larger than life, as a small child might see an admired adult); and extreme devaluation (seeing another as completely worthless, with no redeeming qualities whatever). Vaillant’s (1992) research adds hypochondriacal concerns and passive aggression to the list of psychologically costly defenses. (For further discussion of mature and costly defenses, see, e.g., Cramer, 2015; Di Giuseppe & Perry, 2021; Di Giuseppe, Perry,

chunks.heading: 660 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 660 Adulthood Petraglia, Janzen, & Lingiardi, 2014; Lingiardi, Lonati, Fossati, Vanzulli, & Maffei, 1999; Perry, 1990, 2021; Porcerelli, Cogan, Kamoo, & Miller, 2010; Porcerelli & Hibbard, 2004; Prunas, Di Pierro, Huemer, & Tagini, 2019). Somatization is also ubiquitous at the borderline level of fu...
chunks.text: 660 Adulthood Petraglia, Janzen, & Lingiardi, 2014; Lingiardi, Lonati, Fossati, Vanzulli, & Maffei, 1999; Perry, 1990, 2021; Porcerelli, Cogan, Kamoo, & Miller, 2010; Porcerelli & Hibbard, 2004; Prunas, Di Pierro, Huemer, & Tagini, 2019). Somatization is also ubiquitous at the borderline level of functioning. Abbass and his colleagues note how often patients they describe as “highly resistant and fragile” (roughly corresponding to those at the lower levels of our continuum) come to medical professionals with somatic complaints (see, e.g., Abbass, 2016; Abbass et al., 2020). Their research has identified three pathways for unbearable anxiety: the voluntary muscle system (e.g., pain, headaches, tics), the smooth muscle system (e.g., irritable bowel syndrome, migraine, blood pressure problems), and cognitive perceptual disruption (visual, auditory, sensory, and motor symptoms). In contrast to the benign compassion patients with neurotic-­level personality organization tend to elicit in clinicians, patients with borderline organization, especially at lower levels, evoke intense emotions that clinicians struggle to manage and contain. These are often negative, such as fear, confusion, helplessness, punitiveness, or rage. However, clinicians can also experience powerful rescue fantasies, sexual desire, and fantasies of curing the patient through love (Betan, Heim, Zittel Conklin, & Westen, 2005; Bhola & Mehrotra, 2021; Bourke & Grenyer, 2010, 2013; Colli, Tanzilli, Dimaggio, & Lingiardi, 2014; Dahl, Røssberg, Bøgwald, Gabbard, & Høglend, 2012; Lingiardi, Tanzilli, & Colli, 2015; Røssberg, Karterud, Pedersen, & Friis, 2007). Patients with borderline personality organization tend to exert a strong pressure on therapists to act, often in ways incongruent with their professional roles. This dynamic accounts for some cases of boundary violations by therapists. Under the influence of projective identifications, clinicians may feel a pull to behave in uncharacteristically punitive ways, or alternatively, to cross professional boundaries in misguided efforts to “save” the patient (Guthiel, 2005). Psychotic Level of Organization The traditional conceptualization of psychosis involves a break with reality. Some patients who have never had a diagnosed psychotic illness, or who have had episodes of psychosis from which they have recovered, may have psychotic features such as overgeneralized, concrete, or bizarre thinking; socially odd and inappropriate behaviors; pervasive and severe annihilation anxiety; self-­referential interpretations of their experience; and the unshakable conviction that their own attributions about someone are correct, regardless of anything the other person may say or do. People with the most severe personality disorders may impute their own thoughts and feelings to others, become convinced of the rightness of their most erroneous perceptions, and act on that basis. In response to terrifying anxieties, they may use primitive defenses such as psychotic denial, autistic withdrawal, distortion, delusional projection, fragmentation, and concretization (Berney, de Roten, Beretta, Kramer, & Despland, 2014). A psychotic level of personality organization implies identity diffusion, poor differentiation between representations of self and others, poor discrimination between fantasy and reality, reliance on primitive defenses, and marked deficits in reality testing. An example of a person with psychotic personality organization would be a man who stalks his love object in the conviction that this person “really” loves him, despite all the person’s protestations to the contrary. Such behavior demonstrates both a deficit in reality testing and a lack of differentiation between self and other.

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 661
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 661 Seasoned therapists have long reported that they understand their most disturbed patients as organized at a psychotic level, even when they have never had a diagnosed psychotic illness. Clinical explorations that assume a psychotic range of functioning include t...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 661 Seasoned therapists have long reported that they understand their most disturbed patients as organized at a psychotic level, even when they have never had a diagnosed psychotic illness. Clinical explorations that assume a psychotic range of functioning include the writings of Arieti (1974), Bion (1967), Garrett (2019), Kernberg (1984), McWilliams (2015), Rosenfeld (1987), Steiner (1993), and others. In contrast, researchers have tended to think about psychosis in the framework of a medical model, as a discrete illness. Since the first PDM was published, the clinical utility of theorizing a psychotic range of personality functioning has gained research support (see Lingiardi, McWilliams, Bornstein, Gazzillo, & Gordon, 2015; Sharp & Wall, 2021; Luyten & Fonagy, 2021). We note in this connection that DSM-5 categorizes schizotypal personality disorder under both “personality disorders” and “schizophrenia spectrum and other psychotic disorders,” suggesting that a similar clinical presentation may reflect a personality type or a psychotic illness, or some combination of both. Examples of persons who may be usefully understood as functioning in the psychotic range include anorexic patients who are dangerously close to starvation but insist they are overweight; people with extreme compulsions (e.g., severe hoarding) who cling to their rituals and may experience annihilation anxiety when asked to consider not acting on their compulsions; somatizing patients who regard their own bodies as sadistic persecutors; dissociative patients who cannot distinguish past trauma from present reality; patients with severe factitious disorders; and individuals who suffer from recurrent and severe paranoid reactions, even in the absence of hallucinations or frank delusions. In all these instances, the patient’s fixed ideas or “extreme overvalued beliefs” are so absolute as to approach delusional proportions (Meloy & Rahman, 2020; Rahman, Meloy, & Bauer, 2019). Attempts to question these beliefs often lead patients to react with anxiety and hostility or to experience their clinicians as dangerous. Clinical Implications of Level of Personality Organization For seasoned therapists, a patient’s level of personality organization has crucial implications for treatment focus, level of therapist activity, explicitness of limit setting, frequency of sessions, and other aspects of intervention. Sometimes this adaptation is intuitive; other times it reflects the therapist’s understanding that there is a continuum from supportive to exploratory interventions that corresponds roughly to level of personality organization. Sometimes it reflects the clinician’s training in one of the evidence-­based approaches to more severe personality disorders (e.g., transference-­focused psychotherapy [Caligor et al., 2018; Green & Yeomans, 2022; Yeomans et al., 2015], dialectical behavior therapy [Crowell, Kaliush, Vlisides-­Henry, & Molina, 2022; Linehan, 1993], mentalization-­based therapy [Bateman & Fonagy, 2004; Drozek & Henry, 2022], schema therapy [Schaich, Fassbinder, & Arntz, 2022; Young, Klosko, & Weishaar, 2003], dynamic deconstructive therapy [Gregory & Remen, 2008; Gregory & Sachdeva, 2016] Gunderson’s good psychiatric management [Gunderson & Links, 2014] or Meares’s [2012] conversational model). Certain treatment principles follow from an evaluation of level of personality organization. Before discussing them, we remind readers of relevant caveats: We offer general recommendations for which many exceptions exist; they are not “rules.” The levels of personality organization lack clearly demarcated boundaries. Even psychologically healthy individuals can look borderline or psychotic when gravely misunderstood

chunks.heading: 662 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 662 Adulthood or severely distressed, as many clinical writers have noted. Ignorance of cultural differences can lead therapists to pathologize individuals with unfamiliar behavior that is congruent with their own cultural norms. Finally, the unique qualities of a given patient may obviate common cl...
chunks.text: 662 Adulthood or severely distressed, as many clinical writers have noted. Ignorance of cultural differences can lead therapists to pathologize individuals with unfamiliar behavior that is congruent with their own cultural norms. Finally, the unique qualities of a given patient may obviate common clinical wisdom. Individuals who are psychologically organized in the neurotic-­to-­healthy range tend to be helped by psychodynamically-­ informed therapies that emphasize self-­ understanding and insight (Grenyer, 2012; Yeomans et al., 2015; Zanarini, 2009). Because their capacity to form a therapeutic alliance improves prognosis in any treatment, they can be helped by other approaches as well. Their relative psychological health will increase the likelihood of taking medication as prescribed when receiving pharmacological treatment or doing homework assignments in cognitive-­behavioral treatment (CBT), or willingness to experience and express the feelings evoked in emotion-­focused therapy. A therapist can ordinarily develop a reliable working alliance rather quickly with a neurotic-­level patient and feel a sense of partnership with the person’s self-­observing capacity. Regressive responses tend to be contained within the therapy hour; that is, the patients may become aware of powerful and primitive feelings toward others, including their therapists, but at the end of a session, they can usually pull themselves together and resume normal functioning. For patients in the borderline range of personality organization (i.e., most patients who meet diagnostic criteria for any DSM personality disorder), long-term or openended therapies can provide significant help (Fonagy, Luyten, & Bateman, 2017; Levy, Johnson, & Notsu, 2022; Yeomans et al., 2015). Short-term, highly focused psychodynamic treatments can be beneficial as well (e.g., Abbass, 2015, 2016; Gregory & Remen, 2008; Gregory & Sachdeva, 2016; Town, Abbass, & Hardy, 2011), especially when patients have had prior long-term psychotherapy (Grenyer, Deane, & Lewis, 2008). Transferences at higher levels of personality organization tend to coexist with a capacity to consider that reactions to the therapist are transferential (influenced by experiences of past relationships). Thus, they can usually be addressed by the clinician without risking intolerable damage to the patient’s self-­esteem. For example, an anxious woman experiences her therapist as if the clinician were her mother; when the therapist points out the similarity, she feels more interested than hurt or shamed. Countertransference with higher-­functioning patients tends to fall within the comfort range of seasoned therapists and is usually experienced as more interesting than emotionally disruptive (Gazzillo et al., 2015). Because such patients generally have adequate tolerance of anxiety and ambiguity, open-ended exploration tends to facilitate discovery, mastery, and growth, supporting their sense of agency and counteracting any chagrin about what they discover. Investigation of historical antecedents of their current feelings and behavior usually contributes to therapeutic engagement and change. Patients in the borderline range need therapeutic relationships that take into account their intense reactivity, extreme anxiety, potential for disorganizing regression, lack of self- and object constancy, and profound fear of attachment and simultaneous deep need for it. They are helped by clear limits and structure, and they usually need clinicians to be relatively active and forthcoming, so that they can develop a sense of them as “real persons” (hence neither the psychoanalytic couch nor long silences are usually helpful). A focus on both love (relationships) and work (meaningful activity) increases the likelihood of improvement (Grenyer, Townsend, Lewis, & Day, 2022).

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 663
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 663 Because patients with borderline personality organization suffer painfully, clinicians often feel pulled to respond to their neediness with increased availability and special accommodations. However, efforts by therapists to extend themselves or make special exc...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 663 Because patients with borderline personality organization suffer painfully, clinicians often feel pulled to respond to their neediness with increased availability and special accommodations. However, efforts by therapists to extend themselves or make special exceptions to an established treatment frame are usually counterproductive. Commonly observed exceptions include frame deviations like extending sessions or frequently adding extra sessions between scheduled meetings, offering appointments outside of normal work hours, and being “on call” for the patient at all hours. Deviations from the therapy frame can induce unmanageable regressions and painful levels of emotional disorganization in patients. They often result in therapists becoming depleted, overwhelmed, or resentful. In short, the boundaries of the treatment frame provide structure, safety, and security needed for both parties to engage fully in the work of therapy. Whatever treatment frame (in terms of session frequency, cost, length, etc.) is adopted by the two parties, the therapist should maintain it consistently. Patients in the borderline range often strain against the frame and may experience therapeutic boundaries as arbitrary, callous, punitive, or depriving. The therapist’s most useful response is to help patients explore and express their anger and hurt about the therapeutic boundaries, not dispense with them. This brings these emotional experiences—­ which the patient experiences routinely in daily life—into the therapy relationship where they can be recognized, explored, and understood. This recommendation is not an ironclad directive, because good clinical work calls for flexibility. But when frame deviations become the rule rather than the exception, the treatment is in trouble. Many clinicians employ specific contractual agreements about self-­destructive behavior (e.g., keeping self-­harming behaviors under control), with continuation of treatment contingent on the patient’s adherence to the contract. The clinician must also be capable of sustaining a commitment to treatment over the time required. Patients with borderline organization may be exquisitely sensitive to shifts in the therapist’s vocal tone and nonverbal behavior, and may become terrified they will be abandoned. Their most disturbing symptoms may come back at times of separation from their clinicians—­and, ironically, during treatment crises caused by their beginning to change for the better and starting to feel trust and hope in the therapeutic relationship (Grotstein, 2009; Rosenfeld, 1987). The transferences of patients at the borderline level of organization tend to be intense and difficult for the patient to recognize as transferential. For example, a man sees his therapist as alternately negligent and controlling. When the clinician suggests that he is reacting as if the clinician had qualities like those of his father, he may conclude, “Yes, it’s terrible that you are exactly like him.” The patient’s nonverbal behavior and the therapist’s countertransference are both important channels of communication which can provide information more useful than the patient’s words. Discussion of historical antecedents of current behavior may not foster change; in fact, it may be used to rationalize not changing (e.g., “How could I possibly be different given my background?!”). Treatment should thus generally focus on the here-and-now of the therapy relationship and how the patient is experiencing the clinician (Grenyer, 2012; Stern et al., 1998). Therapies that are helpful to patients with borderline organization tend to be active and structured, and to include affective expressiveness from both patients and clinicians (McCarthy, Mergenthaler, Schneider, & Grenyer, 2011). Because such patients often know that they provoke strong reactions in other people, and because they tend to interpret neutral stimuli with a negative attributional bias (Daros, Zakzanis, &

chunks.heading: 664 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 664 Adulthood Ruocco, 2013), therapists who try to appear nonreactive can be experienced as insincere or dangerously out of touch. As we have noted, a number of clinical theorists have developed manualized, evidence-­based therapies specifically for patients with borderline personality organization,...
chunks.text: 664 Adulthood Ruocco, 2013), therapists who try to appear nonreactive can be experienced as insincere or dangerously out of touch. As we have noted, a number of clinical theorists have developed manualized, evidence-­based therapies specifically for patients with borderline personality organization, including those who meet criteria for DSM’s borderline personality disorder. Other theorists have written more from the perspective of their clinical experience, for example, Giovanni Liotti (Liotti, Cortina, & Farina, 2008) and Clara Mucci (2018). Although their specific recommendations differ, they all advise giving primary attention to the therapeutic relationship in the “here-and-now,” contracting against self-­ destructive behaviors, clarifying and maintaining boundaries, educating patients about issues such as trauma and emotional regulation, keeping the focus on the present, and developing self-­reflective capacities. These theorists are sensitive to the special vulnerability of people in this group to issues of separation and abandonment. All note the need for therapists to tolerate strong countertransferences (especially feeling helpless and overwhelmed) and emphasize the importance of ongoing clinical consultation and support. There is general recognition that therapists who treat patients in the borderline range of functioning enter turbulent emotional waters and are foolish to go it alone. Patients at the psychotic level of personality organization need clinicians to be especially respectful, conversational, and down-to-earth. Patience is also critical: For patients in this range, language may be impoverished, and narrative history reporting may be impaired (Carter & Grenyer, 2012). In traditional psychoanalytic thinking, individuals whose personalities were conceptualized at a psychotic level of organization were seen as needing supportive rather than exploratory therapy—­that is, treatments supporting ego functions and defenses rather than seeking to loosen defenses (Federn, 1952). But realistically, there are supportive elements in exploratory clinical work and exploratory elements in supportive work; in the clinical literature, efforts to demarcate the differences between supportive and exploratory therapies have been problematic (Appelbaum, 2008; Hellerstein, Pinsker, Rosenthal, & Klee, 1994; Pinsker, 1997; Rockland, 1989). We have observed that clinicians experienced in working with patients with psychotic tendencies tend to answer patients’ questions directly (rather than trying to explore underlying fantasies), to give concrete advice when necessary, to contextualize and help patients make sense of distressing symptoms and experiences, to identify triggers of their worst states of mind, and to educate them about elements of emotion, cognition, and behavior about which they may be confused. They may disclose information about themselves that may make patients feel more fully understood and less crazy. They take pains to be experienced as “real persons” who are concerned, nonhumiliating, plain-­spoken, and sympathetic (Arieti, 1974; Atwood, 2011; Garrett, 2019; Karon & VandenBos, 1981; Lotterman, 2015; Marcus, 2017; Silver, 2003; Sullivan, 1962). Therapists who work effectively with psychotic-­level patients are also attuned to the anxiety these patients may suffer (Porges, 2011). They may need to monitor medication compliance and find ways of reinforcing basic self-care. They identify their patients’ strengths and build on them rather than encouraging an opening-­up, uncovering attitude that may terrify a person whose sense of self is already tenuous. Their overall stance is authoritative enough that patients struggling to keep their sanity feel safe in their care and reassured that their thoughts and feelings will not harm or destroy the therapist. At the same time, effective clinicians convey profound respect

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 665
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 665 and a deeply egalitarian attitude, thereby minimizing patients’ humiliation about their symptoms or even about simply needing help. Practitioners treating people in the psychotic range emphasize safety and focus on the issues of greatest concern to their patient...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 665 and a deeply egalitarian attitude, thereby minimizing patients’ humiliation about their symptoms or even about simply needing help. Practitioners treating people in the psychotic range emphasize safety and focus on the issues of greatest concern to their patients, which are not necessarily those the therapists themselves find most troubling (Garrett, 2012). Part 2: Personality Styles There are many ways to conceptualize and describe psychological differences among people. Any system of description necessarily entails oversimplification. Clinicians who come to know a patient intimately find that over time, that person no longer seems to fit a clear-cut diagnostic construct; the person’s individuality eventually becomes more salient. This is especially true for patients at higher levels of personality organization, who commonly show mixes of personality styles. “Purer” examples of personality styles are generally seen at lower levels of personality organization (lowerlevel neurotic, borderline, and psychotic). Nevertheless, it is clinically valuable to consider the predominant personality style or styles that best characterize a person. Such an understanding can contextualize presenting complaints and symptoms and provide a preliminary map of the major terrain features of the person’s internal landscape. As noted previously, any personality style can in principle exist at any level of personality organization (the major exception being emotionally dysregulated personality, which is necessarily linked to a borderline level of organization), but some styles are more commonly found in the healthier ranges of personality organization and others in the more severe ranges. For example, psychopathic individuals, whose defensive operations include omnipotent control, projection, denial, and splitting (Meloy, 1997), are found mainly at the borderline and psychotic levels of personality organization (Gacono & Meloy, 1994). Personality styles characterized by less costly defenses (e.g., intellectualization in obsessive–­compulsive personality) are more commonly found in the neurotic range. Consistent Personality Themes, Seemingly Inconsistent Behavior Specific unconscious themes underlie each personality style (e.g., Silberschatz, 2005). These themes can produce behaviors that may appear inconsistent on the surface but amount to different expressions of the same underlying theme. For example, an obsessive–­compulsive man, unconsciously preoccupied with submission versus defiance, may be either compulsively prompt or regularly late, fastidiously neat in most areas yet improbably messy in some, ingratiatingly compliant or stubbornly oppositional—­ depending on how, in the moment, he tries to solve the problem of feeling controlled. A person with hysteric–­histrionic personality dynamics, implicitly preoccupied with sexuality and gender, and their relation to power, may be either sexually promiscuous or inhibited, or both on different occasions. A person with a paranoid personality may alternate between feeling moved to attack others and being terrified of attack by others. Surface traits (like punctuality or sexual provocativeness in the earlier examples) often coexist with their seeming opposites. Personality is better understood in terms of consistency in unconscious organizing themes than consistency of overt behaviors, which can represent different expressions of the same underlying preoccupation or conflict (McWilliams, 2012).

chunks.heading: 666 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 666 Adulthood Diagnostic Prototypes To facilitate practical personality diagnosis in day-to-day clinical practice, we provide diagnostic prototypes for the 10 major personality styles most often encountered in clinical practice. A “diagnostic prototype” is an empirically derived, paragraph-­length d...
chunks.text: 666 Adulthood Diagnostic Prototypes To facilitate practical personality diagnosis in day-to-day clinical practice, we provide diagnostic prototypes for the 10 major personality styles most often encountered in clinical practice. A “diagnostic prototype” is an empirically derived, paragraph-­length description of a personality style in its “ideal” or pure form. Diagnosis is made by rating the overall resemblance or “match” between an individual and the diagnostic prototype on a 0- to 5-point scale (0 = no match, 5 = very good match). The diagnostic prototypes are reproduced from Westen et al. (2012). They were derived empirically in a clinical sample of N = 1,201 patients described by their clinicians using the Shedler-­Westen Assessment Procedure (SWAP; Shedler, 2022a, 2022b). The SWAP is a comprehensive personality assessment instrument completed entirely by the clinician, not by the patient. Clinicians who wish to conduct a complete assessment with the SWAP instrument and obtain diagnostic scores for the personality styles included in the P Axis, as well as a range of other personality constructs, can access the instrument online at https://swapassessment.org. A computer-­generated Clinical Interpretive Report provides diagnoses, case formulations, and treatment recommendations. Note that this approach does not treat personality styles as discrete categories to which a person does or does not “belong.” Rather, they are “fuzzy sets” lacking clear-cut boundaries, to which an individual may belong to a greater or lesser extent (depending on their match with a diagnostic prototype). The 0- to 5-point diagnostic rating scale reflects this understanding and allows clinicians to diagnose personality styles on a continuum. This dimensional diagnostic approach contrasts with the DSM approach, which treats personality configurations as discrete categories and forces clinicians to make artificial present–­absent diagnostic decisions. Shedler, Westen and their associates have described the conceptual and empirical rationale for dimensional personality diagnosis based on prototype matching (Westen, Shedler, & Bradley, 2006; Shedler, 2022a, 2022b). This approach also conceptualizes personality styles as complex patterns of interrelated psychological processes that must be considered as a whole. Because it is the pattern that is of clinical interest, not the presence or absence of specific features, personality styles cannot be deconstructed or disassembled into separate constituent elements. Much as recognizing a face depends on pattern recognition rather than tabulating separate features, recognizing a personality style is fundamentally a matter of pattern recognition. Thus, diagnostic prototypes are presented in paragraphs meant to be considered as a whole, not broken into separate criteria for tabulation. This approach has the advantage of combining a configural or pattern-­based understanding of personality with dimensional (versus categorial) diagnosis. Some of the personality styles we describe in this section did not emerge empirically in research with the SWAP instrument, possibly due to their infrequency in the clinical populations studied. Yet long clinical experience attests to the value of considering them in our discussion of personality styles. Where no empirically derived prototype exists, we instead offer descriptions based on clinical experience and synthesis of the relevant clinical literature; these descriptions are labeled as “clinically derived descriptions” rather than empirically-­derived prototypes. Internalizing and Externalizing Spectra Various schemes exist to organize psychological patterns and symptoms into superordinate constructs (e.g., the DSM groups personality disorders into “Clusters” A, B, and

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 667
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 667 C). Based on both clinical and empirical wisdom, we list the personality prototypes (with the exception of emotionally dysregulated personality) in an order consistent with two empirically identified superordinate groupings or spectra: “internalizing” and “exter...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 667 C). Based on both clinical and empirical wisdom, we list the personality prototypes (with the exception of emotionally dysregulated personality) in an order consistent with two empirically identified superordinate groupings or spectra: “internalizing” and “externalizing” (Shedler, 2022a; Westen et al., 2012). We first consider personality styles that are more internalizing (depressive, dependent, anxious–­avoidant and phobic, obsessive–­compulsive, somatizing, and schizoid) and then those that are more externalizing (hysteric–­histrionic, narcissistic, paranoid, psychopathic, and sadistic). Generally speaking, patients with internalizing-­spectrum personality pathology suffer inwardly. They tend to blame themselves for their difficulties and are chronically vulnerable to depression or anxiety. Patients with externalizing spectrum personality pathology are more likely to impose suffering on others (although some, notably those with hysteric–­histrionic and narcissistic personalities, may experience deep inner suffering as well). They tend to blame others for their difficulties and are more prone to outward expression of anger and aggression. Individuals whose personalities are emotionally dysregulated (called “borderline personality disorder” in PDM-2) cannot be readily subsumed under either spectrum. In contrast to patients with either stable internalizing or stable externalizing psychologies, they may be better described as “stably unstable” (Schmideberg, 1959), often oscillating between internalizing and externalizing modes of functioning. Primarily Internalizing Personalities Depressive (and Hypomanic) Personalities Despite its absence from the DSM, depressive personality may be the most common personality style seen in clinical practice (Shedler & Westen, 2004). Individuals with depressive personalities are chronically vulnerable to painful affect, especially depression, guilt, shame, and feelings of inadequacy. They appear conflicted about experiencing pleasure and may squelch or inhibit positive feelings such as joy, excitement, and pride. The two predominant psychological themes in depressive personality styles center on (1) internally arising attacks on the self (e.g., aggression turned inward) and (2) rejection and loss. Individuals with depressive personalities tend to be highly self-­critical or self-­ punitive, holding themselves to unrealistic standards and blaming themselves when things go wrong (Huprich and his associates have aptly described such depressive styles as disorders of “malignant self-­regard;” Malone & Huprich, 2023). They may fear rejection or abandonment, feel alone even in the presence of others, or have a pervasive sense that someone or something necessary for their well-being has been lost forever. They are often unaware of their underlying anger and hostility. Depression, self-­criticism, or self-­punitiveness often defend against awareness of underlying anger and aggression (for further description, see Shedler, 2022a). Individuals with depressive personalities may or may not experience depressive mood states that reach the intensity of a diagnosable mood disorder, and not all individuals with mood disorders have depressive personalities organized around the motifs described earlier. Finally, many personality styles (not just depressive personality) can be pathways to clinical depression as a mood disorder (Shedler, 2022a; Huprich, DeFife, & Westen, 2014). Some investigators who study affective disorders view the phenomena we discuss as depressive personality in terms of chronic mood disorder (e.g., recurring

chunks.heading: 668 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 668 Adulthood major depression and/or chronic subsyndromal depression; e.g., Gwirtsman, Blehar, McCullough, & Kocsis, 1997). Although mood disorders and depressive personality commonly co-occur, depressive personality can be differentiated from mood disorder. Personality is enduring, whereas mood di...
chunks.text: 668 Adulthood major depression and/or chronic subsyndromal depression; e.g., Gwirtsman, Blehar, McCullough, & Kocsis, 1997). Although mood disorders and depressive personality commonly co-occur, depressive personality can be differentiated from mood disorder. Personality is enduring, whereas mood disorders tend to be episodic, with acute periods and remissions. In the former, a clinician is struck by ingrained and repetitive personality themes that intensify under stress and persist irrespective of presence or absence of acute depressive symptoms. In the latter, the clinician is more impressed by vegetative symptoms (e.g., psychomotor retardation, appetite change, sleep disturbances, decreased sexual interest), intensity of dysphoric affect, and relative incongruence between the severity of mood disturbance and baseline psychological functioning. Whereas antidepressant medications may help alleviate depressive symptoms in some patients with mood disorders per se, they do little to ameliorate the self-­punitiveness or rejection sensitivity of an individual with a depressive personality style. An individual with a depressive personality style who responds to antidepressant medication may feel better, but they will still have a depressive personality style. Blatt (2004, 2008) distinguished empirically between two kinds of depressive affect, which he termed introjective and anaclitic. Introjective depression is characterized by self-­criticism, self-­punitiveness, and guilt (i.e., aggression directed against the self). Anaclitic depression is characterized by sensitivity to loss and rejection, with corresponding feelings of emptiness, inadequacy, and shame. Introjectively depressive individuals berate themselves for real or imagined shortcomings and blame themselves when bad things happen (cognitive researchers have discussed this in terms of “attributional style”; e.g., Peterson & Seligman, 1984). This readiness to blame themselves may be a residue of the familiar tendency of abused or neglected children to deny that their caretakers are negligent or dangerous (ideas that are too frightening) and instead blame their mistreatment on their own “badness”—something they can try to control. Thus, people with introjective personality dynamics strive to be “good,” but rarely feel good enough. Anaclitically depressive individuals suffer distress and disorganization in the face of loss and separation. Their psychologies are organized around the need for relationship, affection, trust, intimacy, and warmth, and vulnerability to withdrawal of those emotional supplies. They feel empty, lonely, incomplete, helpless, and weak rather than perfectionistic or excessively self-­critical. They may complain of existential despair and view life as hollow and meaningless. Empirical evidence indicates that introjective and anaclitic features can coexist. Clinician countertransference to patients with depressive personalities, especially those at higher levels of organization, tends to be positive. Therapy sessions typically feel cooperative and collaborative, and patient and therapist often develop warm feelings toward one another. Clinicians report feeling good about themselves and the work, and they tend to view depressive patients as “good patients.” The clinical challenge is recognizing that their positive feelings may stem from the very patterns that must change if the treatment is to have lasting benefit. Specifically, the therapist may feel good about the patient and the work because the patient is recreating a pattern of subordinating their needs to those of others (in this case, the therapist), accepting responsibility and blame for the disappointments and frustrations that arise inevitably in therapy, and defending against awareness of dissatisfaction or anger toward the therapist. Treatment implications. In treating someone with a depressive personality, it is vital for the clinician to recognize and welcome the patient’s disavowed feelings,

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 669
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 669 especially anger, hostility, and criticism. Where self-­critical and self-­punitive (introjective) themes are salient, patients benefit from insight into the ways they defend against angry and critical feelings toward others and instead direct them against thems...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 669 especially anger, hostility, and criticism. Where self-­critical and self-­punitive (introjective) themes are salient, patients benefit from insight into the ways they defend against angry and critical feelings toward others and instead direct them against themselves in the form of self-­criticism and self-­punitiveness. Where preoccupations with rejection and loss (anaclitic themes) predominate, patients benefit from the experience of having their perceived “badness” and inadequacies recognized and accepted within the emotional intimacy of the therapy relationship, allowing them to gradually internalize the clinician’s reliable presence and more accepting attitudes. Where preoccupation with loss is salient, patients may need the clinicians’ help to mourn what they have lost before they can invest emotionally in what life can offer in the present. Patients with depressive personalities thus benefit from both the interpretive and relational aspects of psychotherapy. Hypomanic Manifestations The clinical literature also describes a phenomenon that has traditionally been termed “hypomanic personality,” in which driven, obligatory energy and optimism defend against underlying depressive themes (Eckblad & Chapman, 1986). The term “hypomanic” can easily cause confusion because it has been used in the mental health BOX 12.1. Depressive Personality Prototype Summary statement: Individuals with depressive personality are prone to feelings of depression and inadequacy, tend to be self-­critical or self-­punitive, and may be preoccupied with concerns about abandonment or loss. Individuals who match this prototype tend to feel depressed or despondent and to feel inadequate, inferior, or a failure. They tend to find little pleasure or satisfaction in life’s activities and to feel that life has no meaning. They are insufficiently concerned with meeting their own needs, disavowing or squelching their hopes and desires to protect against disappointment. They appear conflicted about experiencing pleasure, inhibiting feelings of excitement, joy, or pride. They may likewise be conflicted or inhibited about achievement or success (e.g., failing to reach their potential or sabotaging themselves when success is at hand). Individuals who match this prototype are generally self-­critical, holding themselves to unrealistic standards and feeling guilty and blaming themselves for bad things that happen. They appear to want to “punish” themselves by creating situations that lead to unhappiness or avoiding opportunities for pleasure and gratification. They have trouble acknowledging or expressing anger and instead become depressed, self-­critical, or self-­punitive. They often fear that they will be rejected or abandoned, are prone to painful feeling of emptiness, and may feel bereft or abjectly alone even in the presence of others. They may have a pervasive sense that someone or something necessary for happiness has been lost forever (e.g., a relationship, youth, beauty, success). 5 very good match (patient exemplifies this disorder; prototypical case) Diagnosis 4 good match (patient has this disorder; diagnosis applies) 3 moderate match (patient has significant features of this disorder) Features 2 slight match (patient has minor features of this disorder) 1 no match (description does not apply)

chunks.heading: 670 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 670 Adulthood professions to refer to two different things: a defensive style (in the psychoanalytic tradition) and one pole of bipolar mood disorder (in descriptive psychiatry). Some research measures also appear to have conflated the phenomena (Parker, Fletcher, McCraw, & Hong, 2014). The hypomani...
chunks.text: 670 Adulthood professions to refer to two different things: a defensive style (in the psychoanalytic tradition) and one pole of bipolar mood disorder (in descriptive psychiatry). Some research measures also appear to have conflated the phenomena (Parker, Fletcher, McCraw, & Hong, 2014). The hypomanic personality, as traditionally conceived, is characterized by mild mood inflation, high energy, conspicuous absence of guilt, inflated self-­esteem, vulnerability to substance abuse, hypersexuality, and interpersonal wit and charm in the context of rather superficial relationships (Akhtar, 1992). The fact that these qualities appear to be the polar opposites of depressed mood has led some to propose that such a clinical presentation necessarily reflects a bipolar diathesis, but we suggest that careful assessment may point to a bipolar etiology for some patients and to a personality style for others. Where the behavior reflects a defensive style and not a subclinical bipolar mood disorder, interpretation leading to insight into its defensive function (e.g., “It seems like you use a lot of energy trying to avoid feeling sadness”) may pave the way to new solutions to old psychological problems. If it is instead an expression of a bipolar mood disorder, such interventions will accomplish little in the absence of effective pharmacological treatment. Therapists may initially find patients with hypomanic personalities captivating, but they may soon feel confused, overstimulated, irritatingly “entertained,” and distanced. Individuals with hypomanic personalities tend to be resistant to exploratory psychotherapy. They are hard to keep in treatment because they tend to bolt from relationships that elicit feelings of dependency. They become unconsciously terrified of being abandoned and may seek to master that fear by abandoning the other first. Depending on other psychological factors and circumstances, hypomanic defenses can be extremely costly, highly adaptive, or both. Treatment implications. Many therapists, after diagnosing a hypomanic personality style in a patient, call to the person’s attention the lifetime pattern of abrupt flight from relationships that usually pervades their history. They then try to preempt a similar flight from therapy by negotiating an agreement that the client will keep coming for a given number of sessions after any abrupt, unilateral decision by the patient to stop treatment. Therapy tends to be both slow and fraught with outbreaks of intense negative affect and worrisome enactments. Masochistic (Self‐Defeating) Personalities The psychoanalytic literature is rich in accounts of patients who behave in ways that seem strikingly antithetical to their own well-being, and appear unconsciously drawn to pain or suffering (“masochism”). On the surface, because of the salience of emotional pain in the clinical presentation, masochistic personality dynamics may resemble depressive personality dynamics. However, masochistic and depressive dynamics reflect different underlying themes that call for distinct treatment approaches. Like all psychological phenomena that gradually become problematic, masochistic patterns arise from adaptive efforts. Self-­sacrifice for one’s family or community is evolutionarily adaptive; hence, masochistic or self-­defeating personality can be seen as an exaggeration or rigidification of innate motivations to prioritize others’ welfare. Masochism is not a unitary construct; it may be better conceptualized as a many-­ headed hydra. The term “masochism” has been used to refer to a range of phenomena with different psychological origins that serve different psychological functions (Kernberg, 1988), with the common denominator of an apparent (unconscious) attachment

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 671
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 671 to suffering. There is some controversy about whether a masochistic pattern should be conceived as a personality style or as a trait. We have concluded that although masochistic patterns can be associated with a range of personality styles (Huprich & Nelson, 201...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 671 to suffering. There is some controversy about whether a masochistic pattern should be conceived as a personality style or as a trait. We have concluded that although masochistic patterns can be associated with a range of personality styles (Huprich & Nelson, 2014), some patients are best described as organized characterologically along masochistic lines (Glick & Berger, 2022). Based on their frequent involvements (with intimates and with clinicians) in alternating dominance–­submission or abuser–­abused interactions, some theorists (e.g., Brenner, 1959) link masochistic personalities with sadistic dynamics. Others have connected them with narcissistic (e.g., Cooper, 1988; Glickauf-­Hughes, 1997), dependent (Bornstein, 1993; Waska, 1997), and paranoid (Bak, 1946; Nydes, 1963) themes. Below, we briefly describe versions of masochistic dynamics associated with dependent personality themes, narcissistic themes, and paranoid themes, respectively. All may involve disavowed sadistic elements. Relational Masochism The more dependent version of masochistic personality dynamics, sometimes called “relational masochism” (McWilliams, 2011), applies to people who subordinate their own needs to those of others and appear to view deprivation and suffering as requirements for maintaining a needed attachment. The relationship may be experienced as desperately needed; fear of losing it may override concerns for the person’s own safety and welfare. Beneath overt submissiveness, there is often an undercurrent of disavowed aggression, expressed in passive–­aggressive ways that tend to trigger or provoke mistreatment from others. At lower levels of personality organization, masochistic patients may rely on projective identification to defend against awareness of their own sadistic aggression—­while simultaneously inducing the other person to enact it. For empirical evidence for this subtype of masochistic or self-­defeating personality pattern, see the description in the next session of the “dependent-­victimized personality” style derived from empirical research with the SWAP instrument (Westen et al., 2012). Moral Masochism The more narcissistic version of masochistic personality dynamics, sometimes called “moral masochism” (Reik, 1941), applies to people who equate self-­renunciation and suffering with virtue. Self-­esteem is tied to deprivation and suffering: The greater the deprivation, the greater the sense of virtue. Such people hold the conviction, often unconscious, that their suffering makes them morally superior, and they may seek to demonstrate this to others in self-­righteous ways. Some morally masochistic individuals adopt an aggrieved, martyred tone that can initially induce sympathy or guilt in others but ultimately tends to alienate them. Moral masochism is relatively prevalent in the mental health and other helping professions. These professions offer ample opportunities for self-­sacrifice, for example, by attending to others’ needs at the expense of one’s own. Disavowed sadism is often revealed through intolerance, harshly critical attitudes, or outright aggression toward others who fail the moral masochist’s purity tests. Thus, moral superiority, self-­righteousness, self-­sacrifice, and sadistic cruelty can coexist. A variant of moral masochism, often referred to as “victim entitlement,” applies to people who appear to hold the (unconscious) conviction that the world owes them compensation in proportion to their suffering. To the extent their suffering or victimization secures imagined compensation, they may be deeply invested in defeating their

chunks.heading: 672 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 672 Adulthood clinicians’ efforts to help (cf. the “aggrieved pattern” of Millon & Grossman, 2007, or the “help-­rejecting complainer” of Frank et al., 1952). Relinquishing suffering is tantamount to relinquishing an entry ticket to a paradise where all past wrongs will be set right. It also means g...
chunks.text: 672 Adulthood clinicians’ efforts to help (cf. the “aggrieved pattern” of Millon & Grossman, 2007, or the “help-­rejecting complainer” of Frank et al., 1952). Relinquishing suffering is tantamount to relinquishing an entry ticket to a paradise where all past wrongs will be set right. It also means giving up the unconscious pleasure in tormenting the therapist. Victim entitlement (“I’ve had a terrible life, so the world owes me”) can evoke irritated, judgmental, even sadistic countertransferences. Therapists can be helpful to the extent that they can help the patient grieve past wrongs while keeping a focus on their capacity to take responsibility for current choices, irrespective of their difficult histories. In the context of a solid alliance, a clinician can help a patient recognize the high personal costs of insistent efforts to demonstrate to the world that they have been wronged and extract compensation for their grievances. Paranoid (Harm‐Seeking) Masochistic Patterns The more actively harm-­seeking version of masochistic dynamics involves the (disavowed and projected) expectation of aggression from others. The connection with paranoia was made by Nydes (1963), who noted that both paranoid and masochistic psychologies are preoccupied with threat, but with different solutions (the paranoid person essentially enacting the scenario “I’ll hit you before you hit me” and the masochistic person enacting “I’ll hit myself before you hit me, so you won’t”). Some individuals oscillate between these solutions. Patients who experienced a childhood caregiver as envious and retaliatory may become paralyzed with anxiety, expecting to be attacked for any personal success or satisfaction. Unconsciously, they may seek to “get it over with” by provoking the attack they anticipate as inevitable. This pattern is identifiable by the relief that follows the self-­destructive enactment. Because a person with this psychology can only slowly tolerate the anxiety behind the urge toward self-­destructive action, it needs to be recognized, understood, and worked through patiently in treatment. Treatment implications. In contrast to patients with primarily depressive personality dynamics, who generally benefit from their clinicians’ sympathetic care and concern, those with prominent masochistic dynamics may respond badly to a therapist’s generosity and sympathy. Explicit compassion may reinforce the patient’s conviction that suffering is the best or only route to care, or it may provoke self-­destructive enactments in a patient who experiences guilt for accepting care that feels undeserved. Instead, tactful confrontation about the patient’s own contributions to recurrent difficulties can stimulate curiosity about the meaning of their self-­defeating patterns. Patients with masochistic personality dynamics may hold the conviction, conscious or unconscious, that the clinician is interested in them only because they are suffering. Such beliefs can pose a powerful barrier to getting better. A clinician can help self-­defeating patients become aware of such unconscious expectations, as well as help them recognize and feel that the clinician’s (and others’) interest and attention are not contingent on their suffering or continued victimization. Dependent and Dependent–Victimized Personalities Individuals with dependent personalities maintain a sense of well-being via relational connection, to the detriment of personal agency and development. They tend to subordinate themselves to others, to define themselves mainly in relation to others, and to seek security and satisfaction predominantly in interpersonal contexts (e.g., “I am fine

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 673
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 673 as long as everything is OK with my husband”). Symptoms may appear when something goes wrong in a primary attachment relationship. Individuals with dependent personalities may feel inadequate when left to themselves. They tend to regard others as powerful and ef...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 673 as long as everything is OK with my husband”). Symptoms may appear when something goes wrong in a primary attachment relationship. Individuals with dependent personalities may feel inadequate when left to themselves. They tend to regard others as powerful and effective. Organizing their lives with a view to maintaining nurturing relationships in which they are submissive, they may feel contented when they have successfully developed such relationships and acutely distressed when they have not. Emotional preoccupations include performance anxiety and fears of criticism and abandonment. They tend to feel weak and powerless, to be passive and nonassertive, and to be easily influenced by others. They often appear naïve. They may feel worthless and have difficulties expressing anger or negotiating with others to achieve a mutually satisfying compromise. In therapy, which they may enter readily, they may be compliant to a fault. They tend to idealize their therapists, ask for advice, and seek reassurances that they are “good patients.” In attempts to become important to the therapist, they may try to “read” their therapists and meet the therapists’ perceived needs. Some insist, even after being informed about professional boundaries, on offering favors and bringing gifts. Research using the SWAP instrument (Westen et al., 2012) has identified a “dependent–­victimized” version of dependent personality. Individuals with dependent–­ victimized personality dynamics are drawn to relationships in which they are mistreated or they are repeatedly put “in harm’s way.” They are prone to express unconscious anger and resentment in passive–­aggressive ways which can elicit aggression and cruelty from others. Individuals who are characterologically overdependent are found at all levels of personality organization. At the neurotic level, people with dependent personalities may seek treatment in midlife or later, after a bereavement or divorce, or when retirement has deprived them of clearly defined relational roles with established rules and expectations. At the borderline and psychotic levels, dependent patients may become dysregulated when they must rely on their own inner resources and may rely on costly defenses, such as somatization and acting out, in desperate bids to elicit care. Bornstein (1993, 2005; Bornstein & Natoli, 2022) has conducted the only comprehensive empirical examinations of problematic dependency known to us. His findings suggest that it may arise from any or all of the following: overprotective and/ or authoritarian parenting, gender role socialization, and cultural attitudes about achievement versus relatedness. Participants in his studies demonstrated “relationship-­ facilitating self-­presentation strategies” such as ingratiation, supplication, exemplification, self-­promotion, and intimidation. Although popular prejudice assumes that female patients are more likely than males to be excessively dependent, Bornstein notes that women may simply be more willing to express dependent longings overtly. Feminist psychoanalytic scholars (e.g., Benjamin, 1988; Chodorow, 1994, 1999; Dimen & Goldner, 2012; Gilligan, 1982, 2023) have criticized Freudian theories about female sexuality for normalizing the devaluation of femininity and the relational orientation of most women. They argue that this masculinist bias has fueled cultural prejudice toward women and reinforced gender stereotypes. In addition to being sensitive to this issue, clinicians should recognize that different cultures have different conceptions of how much dependency is “too much.” A diagnosis of dependent personality disorder should always take account of the cultural context. Treatment implications. Clinicians’ countertransference with excessively dependent patients, especially those who are notably self-­defeating, is typically benign at first, then increasingly characterized by irritation and a sense of burden. Dependent

chunks.heading: 674 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 674 Adulthood patients may devise unconscious tests (Bugas & Silberschatz, 2000) to see whether the clinician supports nascent strivings toward autonomy or, alternatively, basks in the patient’s invitation to take on the role of expert and advisor. Clinicians need to resist seduction into the role o...
chunks.text: 674 Adulthood patients may devise unconscious tests (Bugas & Silberschatz, 2000) to see whether the clinician supports nascent strivings toward autonomy or, alternatively, basks in the patient’s invitation to take on the role of expert and advisor. Clinicians need to resist seduction into the role of expert authority, encourage the patient toward autonomous functioning, and contain the patient’s anxieties that arise in the process. Although clinicians treating patients with dependent personalities may be tempted to collude in avoiding negative affect, if they invite the patients’ anger and other more aggressive feelings, they may facilitate the person’s ultimate sense of personal agency and pride in accomplishment. Passive–Aggressive Personalities An important variant of dependent psychology is a passive–­aggressive pattern in which the patient’s relationships are characterized by hostile dependency (Kantor, 2017). Passive–­ aggressive individuals define themselves in relation to others, but with a negative valence (e.g., “I’m the husband of that bitch”). Because they locate themselves psychologically in opposition to others, it is hard for them to conceive of and pursue their own autonomous goals. Like paranoid patients, they may attack to preempt expected attacks by others, but they do so indirectly. Their enacted aggression often triggers mistreatment from others, perpetuating a vicious cycle: underlying anger and resentment lead to passive–­aggressive behavior, triggering aggression and mistreatment from others, which in turn fuel more anger and resentment. This self-­ perpetuating pattern reinforces their core conviction that anger must not be experienced or expressed directly. Treatment implications. It is challenging to connect with a person who responds passive aggressively to efforts to connect. The clinician needs a sense of humor as a counterpoise to the feelings of impatience and exasperation the patient is likely to evoke. At the same time, the clinician must not collude with the patient’s denial of aggression. Negative feelings arise quickly in treatment, and they are valuable cues that the clinician may be on the receiving end of a sadistic enactment that must be made conscious through words. Power struggles need to be side-­stepped. Sometimes stunningly naïve about the hostility they exude, passive–­aggressive patients need help naming their angry and hostile feelings, and differentiating thoughts and feelings from behavior. To avoid activating their oppositionality, which may take the form of sabotaging any outcome the clinician seems to desire, therapists should take care not to seem too highly invested in their progress. Instead, they need to take their provocations in stride, help them become consciously aware of the anger and aggression underlying their behavior, and keep a sharp focus on the price they pay for it. Counterdependent Personalities Finally, there is a defensively pseudo-­independent variant of dependent psychology. Bornstein (1993) describes a continuum from maladaptive dependency (submissiveness) through healthy interdependency (connectedness) to inflexible independence (unconnected detachment) or counterdependency. Some individuals at the inflexibly independent end of that spectrum have powerful dependent longings that they keep out of awareness via denial, reaction formation, and dissociation. They thus have what amount to dependent personality dynamics masked by disavowal and

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 675
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 675 pseudo-­independence. In their relationships, they may define themselves as the ones on whom others depend, and they may pride themselves on being able to take care of themselves. Counterdependent individuals may look askance at expressions of need and may regar...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 675 pseudo-­independence. In their relationships, they may define themselves as the ones on whom others depend, and they may pride themselves on being able to take care of themselves. Counterdependent individuals may look askance at expressions of need and may regard evidence of emotional vulnerability in themselves or others with scorn. It is likely their childhood attachment style would have been classified as avoidant. Often, they have some secret area of dependency (on a substance, a partner, a mentor, an ideology); some have a tendency toward illness or injury that gives them a “legitimate” reason to be cared for by others. Because they resist acknowledging need, counterdependent individuals seldom seek psychotherapy but may be pushed into it by partners who feel starved for genuine emotional intimacy. Therapeutic implications. In treatment, counterdependent individuals need help to accept their dependent needs as normal aspects of being human before they can develop a healthy balance between connectedness and separateness. Therapists who tolerate their defensive protestations about their independence long enough to develop a therapeutic alliance report that when the counterdependent defenses are given up, a period of mourning for early and unmet dependent needs then ensues, followed by more genuine autonomy. BOX 12.2. Dependent–­Victimized Personality Prototype Summary statement: Individuals with dependent–victimized personality are highly dependent and fearful of being alone, tend to show insufficient concern for their own well-being to the point of jeopardizing their welfare or safety, and have difficulty expressing anger directly. Individuals who match this prototype tend to be needy and dependent, fear being alone, and fear rejection or abandonment. They tend to be ingratiating or submissive, often consenting to things they find objectionable in an effort to maintain support or approval. They tend to be passive and unassertive and to feel helpless and powerless. They tend to be indecisive, suggestible or easily influenced, and naïve or innocent, seeming to know less about the ways of the world than would be expected. They tend to become attached to people who are emotionally unavailable, and to create relationships in which they are in the role of caring for or rescuing the other person. Individuals who match this prototype tend to get drawn into or remain in relationships in which they are emotionally or physically abused, or needlessly put themselves in dangerous situations (e.g., walking alone or agreeing to meet strangers in unsafe places). They are insufficiently concerned with meeting their own needs and tend to feel unworthy or undeserving. Individuals who match this prototype have trouble acknowledging or expressing anger and instead become depressed, self-­critical, or self-­punitive. They tend to express anger in passive and indirect ways (e.g., making mistakes, procrastinating, forgetting) that may provoke or trigger anger or mistreatment from others. 5 very good match (patient exemplifies this disorder; prototypical case) Diagnosis 4 good match (patient has this disorder; diagnosis applies) 3 moderate match (patient has significant features of this disorder) Features 2 slight match (patient has minor features of this disorder) 1 no match (description does not apply)

chunks.heading: 676 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 676 Adulthood Anxious–Avoidant and Phobic Personalities Although avoidant personality disorder has been included in the DSM since its third edition, research indicates a broader spectrum of related personality styles in which anxiety is a central organizing theme (Shedler & Westen 2004; Westen et al...
chunks.text: 676 Adulthood Anxious–Avoidant and Phobic Personalities Although avoidant personality disorder has been included in the DSM since its third edition, research indicates a broader spectrum of related personality styles in which anxiety is a central organizing theme (Shedler & Westen 2004; Westen et al., 2012). We discuss anxious, avoidant, and phobic personalities in this section to emphasize their overall continuity. Anxious personality styles are easily recognized in the neurotic through the borderline ranges. At a psychotic level or organization, individuals with anxiety-­driven psychologies become so filled with dread that they depend on primitive externalizing defenses, and their overall psychologies may be better understood as overlapping with paranoia. In the higher functioning ranges, patients with an anxious personality structure may appear at first to have either obsessional or hysterical styles (hence the old diagnosis of “anxiety hysteria”), depending on how they attempt to deal with their pervasive fear. Unlike individuals with obsessional or hysterical psychologies, however, they are chronically aware of their anxiety because their defenses fail to keep it outside conscious awareness. Phobic and avoidant patients attach their anxieties to specific objects or situation which they attempt to avoid. Other characterologically anxious individuals experience a free-­floating, global sense of anxiety, with no idea what frightens them. Leigh McCullough’s research team (McCullough et al., 2003) coined the useful term “affect phobia” for people terrified of their own emotional life. Phobic and avoidant patients can be shy and reserved; tend to feel inferior, inadequate, indecisive, and inhibited; and may have difficulty recognizing and describing their feelings. Contemporary cognitive theorists emphasize how such patients show difficulties in identifying their anxiety-­charged thoughts, connecting them to their environmental triggers, mastering them, and assuming a “decentered” perspective on the anxiety-­inducing situations. Long clinical experience has identified several different kinds of anxiety that seem universal among human beings, including “separation anxiety” (fear of losing an attachment object), “castration anxiety” (fear of damage to the body, including sexual mutilation), “moral anxiety” (dread of violating one’s core values), “posttraumatic anxiety” (expectation of retraumatization), and “annihilation anxiety.” Annihilation anxiety can involve either a terror of fragmentation and consequent loss of the sense of self (cf. Kohut’s [1977] “disintegration anxiety”) or a terror of destruction based on prior traumatic experience. Panksepp’s research (see Panksepp & Davis, 2018) has established that annihilation anxiety (the FEAR system) originates in a different part of the brain from separation anxiety (the PANIC/GRIEF system) and is mediated by different neural pathways. In contrast to patients in whom one of these types of anxiety tends to predominate, these different subjective experiences of anxiety may all be discernible in patients whose anxiety is incapacitating enough to warrant a DSM personality disorder diagnosis. In general, the lower the level of personality organization, the more likely it is that annihilation anxiety dominates the clinical picture. Research confirms that these characterological anxieties have a developmental trajectory, impede treatment progress, and, most important, require a deeper form of therapy than the traditional exposure therapies that are often cited as “evidence-­based” approaches to treating anxiety (Hurvich, 2018; Kirsten, Grenyer, Wagner, & Manicavasagar, 2008). The proximal source of characterological anxiety may lie in affective dysregulation (Mucci, 2018; Schore, 2003), reflecting insufficient development of coping

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 677
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 677 strategies or defenses to mitigate normative developmental fears. Individuals with anxious personalities often report having had primary caregivers who, because of the caregivers’ own anxiety, could not adequately comfort them, convey a sense of security, or sup...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 677 strategies or defenses to mitigate normative developmental fears. Individuals with anxious personalities often report having had primary caregivers who, because of the caregivers’ own anxiety, could not adequately comfort them, convey a sense of security, or support a sense of agency. Mikulincer and Shaver (2016) note that most people with characterological anxiety would probably have been identifiable in early childhood as having an anxious attachment style. Their research suggests that despite the continuity of attachment style across the lifespan, insecure attachment styles may slowly change toward more secure attachment in the context of a long, devoted relationship, particularly intensive psychotherapy (cf. Sroufe, 2005). Countertransference with chronically anxious patients may include a responsive anxiety, including, with those at the borderline or psychotic level, a degree of annihilation anxiety intense enough to cause the therapist to feel overwhelmed and hence impelled to step outside the therapeutic role to “do” something to relieve the patient’s anxiety. Preliminary research findings suggest that clinicians working with anxious patients tend to develop both parental and disengaged feelings, while specifically phobic patients tend to elicit mainly parental feelings (Colli et al., 2014; Gordon et al., 2016). In therapy, anxious patients may connect in a submissive, apprehensive way, seeking rescue from their panicky feelings. Because of the unbearable nature of anxious affect, they often come to treatment already dependent on antianxiety drugs. Although clinicians may be tempted to offer chemical relief, the risk of addiction suggests that for people with characterological anxiety, one should prescribe anxiolytic medication with caution. Especially in phobic patients who believe they will be safe so long as they keep away from certain dangers, there may be some magical thinking that the therapist will have a formula to resolve the anxiety without their having to face and explore it. When alone, anxious patients tend to feel small, inadequate, and threatened; they respond by trying to elicit protection from those they see as powerful. It is vital that their clinicians do not act out a rescue fantasy, but instead encourage them toward graduated exposure to what is feared (Weinberger, 2014). Treatment implications. A therapist should maintain confidence in a patient’s ability to develop their own capacities to tolerate and reduce anxiety. It is important to try to help the patient give words to previously inchoate states of feeling (Stern, 2018). Many anxious patients are both verbally and behaviorally avoidant, changing the subject whenever anything disturbing enters their consciousness. When they make sweeping proclamations of danger, they should be pressed for details (“And then what would happen?”) and asked for specific fantasies. Once there is a secure therapeutic alliance, phobic patients need to be helped to face what they fear, whether the feared stimuli are external or internal. Exposure and response prevention treatments, as well as education in mindfulness and meditative disciplines, may be useful adjuncts to understanding, naming, and mastering previously unformulated emotional states. Counterphobic Personalities Characterologically counterphobic patients seek therapy relatively infrequently. Their rarity in clinical caseloads is probably due to their avoidance of vulnerable acts such as asking for help. They may, however, come for treatment when urged by a partner or in the aftermath of traumatic events. Counterphobic individuals are psychologically organized around defenses against their fears. They may seek out dangerous

chunks.heading: 678 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 678 Adulthood BOX 12.3. Anxious Avoidant Personality Prototype Summary statement: Individuals with anxious–­avoidant personality are chronically prone to anxiety, are socially anxious and avoidant, and attempt to manage anxiety in ways that limit and constrict their lives. Individuals who match this...
chunks.text: 678 Adulthood BOX 12.3. Anxious Avoidant Personality Prototype Summary statement: Individuals with anxious–­avoidant personality are chronically prone to anxiety, are socially anxious and avoidant, and attempt to manage anxiety in ways that limit and constrict their lives. Individuals who match this prototype are chronically anxious. They tend to ruminate, dwelling on problems or replaying conversations in their minds. They are more concerned with avoiding harm than pursuing desires, and their choices and actions are unduly influenced by efforts to avoid perceived dangers. They are prone to feelings of shame and embarrassment. Individuals who match this prototype tend to be shy and self-­conscious in social situations and to feel like an outcast or outsider. They are often socially awkward and tend to avoid social situations because of fear of embarrassment or humiliation. They tend to be inhibited and constricted and to have difficulty acknowledging or expressing desires. They may adhere rigidly to daily routines, have trouble making decisions, or vacillate when faced with choices. Their anxiety may find expression through a variety of channels, including panic attacks, hypochondriacal concerns (e.g., excessive worry about normal aches and pains), or somatic symptoms in response to stress (e.g., headache, backache, abdominal pain, asthma). 5 very good match (patient exemplifies this disorder; prototypical case) Diagnosis 4 good match (patient has this disorder; diagnosis applies) 3 moderate match (patient has significant features of this disorder) Features 2 slight match (patient has minor features of this disorder) 1 no match (description does not apply) situations, thrive on risk, and have a reputation for unnerving calm in the face of peril. Having disavowed and projected their own anxieties, they evoke anxious countertransferences in therapists, who see the realistic dangers in their risk-­taking and become alarmed on their behalf. Treatment implications. It is critical to work slowly with counterphobic patients and to tolerate their bravado for some time before beginning to push them toward acknowledging even normal human fears, much less neurotic anxiety.
