In all patients for whom anxiety is pervasive, there may be a period in treatment in which they suffer a depressive reaction to giving up some of the magical ideas that accompany their anxiety-­driven psychology and behavior. Obsessive–Compulsive Personalities Individuals with obsessive–­compulsive personalities are emotionally constricted and regimented. They prefer to operate as if emotions were irrelevant or weak, and to defend against emotional life and desires through rigidity, regimentation, and intellectualization. They tend to be excessively concerned with and devoted to rules, procedures, order, organization, schedules, and so on, and may be excessively devoted to work and “productivity” to the detriment of leisure and relationships. They rely on intellectualization as a defense and tend to see themselves as logical and rational,

chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 679 uninfluenced by emotion. Asked how they feel, they are apt to describe what they think. They tend to think in either experience-­distant abstractions or become occupied with detail, or both. While these ways of thinking may seem like opposites, they are both way...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 679 uninfluenced by emotion. Asked how they feel, they are apt to describe what they think. They tend to think in either experience-­distant abstractions or become occupied with detail, or both. While these ways of thinking may seem like opposites, they are both ways of avoiding the personal and the emotional. Beneath an “orderly,” regimented exterior, they are preoccupied with underlying issues of control, and caught in an unconscious conflict between the feeling that they must submit to others’ demands (at the cost of rage and shame) on the one hand, and the desire to rebel against them and defy them (at the cost of anxiety and fear of punishment) on the other. Rigidity, order, and intellectualization defend against awareness of the underlying conflict around control and the emotions that accompany it. Conflict around control may originate in early struggles with caregivers and authority figures. Freud (1913) became impressed with the similarity of the stubborn, punctilious, and hoarding tendencies of the obsessive–­compulsive adult to a resistant child’s response to toilet training—­a leap that captured the imagination of his contemporaries and gave us the word “anal” as a descriptor of this psychology. He associated a tendency toward this character style with high levels of temperamental aggressiveness, which may make toilet training a struggle, but which also would intensify any situation in which a child is prematurely required to exert control over impulses and desires (e.g., eating, sexuality, general obedience). Gabbard (2005) attributes the workaholic tendencies of obsessive–­compulsive individuals to an unconscious wish to be found acceptable, at long last, by childhood authorities. Parents with controlling tendencies may contribute to this character style (Playfair, 2022) and to its characteristic, related conflicts: giving versus withholding, neatness versus messiness, and compliant submission versus oppositional defiance. In a different route to this personality style, clinicians have described patients reared in chaotic situations who became obsessive–­compulsive in response to their childhood need for some sense of order in the absence of needed stability and predictability. People with obsessive–­compulsive personalities, evocatively described by Reich (1933/1972) as “living machines,” seem to have identified with caregivers who expected them to be more grown-up than was possible at their age. They regard expression of most emotion as “immature,” overvalue rationality, and suffer humiliation when they feel they have acted emotionally and childishly. They may feel deep shame about normal human emotional experience. Only when an emotion is logically defensible or morally justified—­for example, righteous anger—do they find it acceptable. Early psychoanalytic scholarship (Fisher & Greenberg, 1985; Salzman, 1980; Shapiro, 1965) suggested that people with obsessive–­compulsive personality styles fear that their impulses, especially their aggressive urges, will get out of control. Most obsessive thoughts and compulsive actions involve efforts to undo or counteract impulses toward destructiveness, greed, and messiness. Because guilt over unacceptable wishes is severe, the conscience of an obsessive–­compulsive person is famously rigid and punitive. Self-­criticism can be harsh; such individuals hold themselves (and others) to ideal standards. They follow rules literally, get lost in detail, and postpone decisions because they want to make the perfect one. They are scrupulous to a fault but may have trouble relaxing, joking, playing, and enjoying intimacy. Although obsessive and compulsive traits express similar unconscious preoccupations and hence appear together, some people show obsessional features with little compulsivity, while others have compulsive features with little obsessionality. Obsessive people live “in their heads”: thinking, reasoning, judging, doubting. Compulsive

chunks.heading: 680 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 680 Adulthood souls are chronically “doing and undoing”: cleaning, collecting, perfecting. Obsessive patients are ruminative and cerebral; their self-­esteem depends on thinking. Compulsive individuals tend to be busy, meticulous, perfectionistic; their self-­esteem depends on doing. Treatment impli...
chunks.text: 680 Adulthood souls are chronically “doing and undoing”: cleaning, collecting, perfecting. Obsessive patients are ruminative and cerebral; their self-­esteem depends on thinking. Compulsive individuals tend to be busy, meticulous, perfectionistic; their self-­esteem depends on doing. Treatment implications. In therapy, individuals with obsessive–­compulsive personalities may try hard to be cooperative but covertly resist the therapist’s efforts to explore emotional life (de Reus & Emmelkamp, 2012). They may become subtly oppositional, expressing unconscious protest by coming late, forgetting to pay, and prefacing responses to the therapist’s comments with “Yes, but . . . ” They may interrupt and talk over the therapist. To the clinician, the relationship may feel like a subtle (or not so subtle) power struggle. As the patient insists on tendentious argument rather than more authentic emotional engagement, the therapist may become impatient and exasperated. Effective therapy requires sustained exploration of those aspects of personality that individuals with obsessive–­ compulsive psychologies otherwise spend inordinate energy trying to subdue. Clinicians may be tempted to try to find ways BOX 12.4. Obsessive–­Compulsive Personality Prototype Summary statement: Individuals with Obsessional Personality are intellectualized and overly “rational” in their approach to life, are emotionally constricted and rigid, are critical of themselves and others, and are conflicted about anger, aggression, and authority. Individuals who match this prototype tend to see themselves as logical and rational, uninfluenced by emotion. They tend to think in abstract and intellectualized terms, to become absorbed in details (often to the point of missing what is important), and prefer to operate as if emotions were irrelevant or inconsequential. They tend to be excessively devoted to work and productivity to the detriment of leisure and relationships. Individuals who match this prototype tend to be inhibited and constricted, and have difficulty acknowledging or expressing wishes, impulses, or anger. They are invested in seeing and portraying themselves as emotionally strong, untroubled, and in control, despite evidence of underlying insecurity, anxiety, or distress. They tend to deny or disavow their need for nurturance or comfort, often regarding such needs as weakness. They tend to adhere rigidly to daily routines, becoming anxious or uncomfortable when they are altered, and to be overly concerned with rules, procedures, order, organization, schedules, and so on. They may be preoccupied with concerns about dirt, cleanliness, or contamination. Rationality and regimentation generally mask underlying feelings of anxiety or anger. Individuals who match this prototype tend to be conflicted about anger, aggression, and authority. They tend to be self-­critical, expecting themselves to be “perfect,” and to be equally critical of others, whether overtly or covertly. They tend to be controlling, oppositional, and self-­righteous or moralistic. They are prone to being stingy and withholding (e.g., of time, money, affection). They are often conflicted about authority, struggling with contradictory impulses to submit versus defy. 5 very good match (patient exemplifies this disorder; prototypical case) Diagnosis 4 good match (patient has this disorder; diagnosis applies) 3 moderate match (patient has significant features of this disorder) Features 2 slight match (patient has minor features of this disorder) 1 no match (description does not apply)

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 681
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 681 around their patients’ defenses to help them express their “real” feelings, but this is generally unproductive. Instead, clinicians are well-­advised to address the defenses themselves—­to call attention to them as they arise in the here-and-now of the clinical...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 681 around their patients’ defenses to help them express their “real” feelings, but this is generally unproductive. Instead, clinicians are well-­advised to address the defenses themselves—­to call attention to them as they arise in the here-and-now of the clinical interaction, and invite the patient’s curiosity about their psychological function. A helpful metaphor is that if the patient is building a wall, the goal is not to get over or through it, but to take an interest in the wall itself. How is it being constructed, and why just here and just now? Schizoid Personalities The word “schizoid” is among the most confusing in the clinical literature, because the same term has been used to describe markedly different psychologies. Individuals who receive a DSM diagnosis of schizoid personality disorder are most often characterized by severe deficits in basic psychological resources and capacities. Some may have subsyndromal psychotic spectrum disorders in which negative symptoms predominate (e.g., affective flattening, impoverished speech and thought). There is evidence that the more schizotypal the patient’s presentation, the graver the level of severity (e.g., Lenzenweger, Clarkin, Kernberg, & Foelsch, 2001). Research with the SWAP instrument (Westen et al., 2012) identified a cluster of patients labeled “schizoid–­schizotypal,” characterized by “pervasive impoverishments, and peculiarities, in interpersonal relationships, emotional experience, and thought processes” (p. 280). The empirically derived schizoid–­schizotypal personality prototype identified through this research is reproduced in Box 5. Although this deficit-­based version of “schizoid” may be more familiar to clinicians trained in DSM diagnosis, psychoanalytic writers have observed and described a different, higher-­functioning psychology to which they have also applied the term “schizoid” (Guntrip, 1969; Khan, 1974; McWilliams, 2022; Seinfeld, 1991). Individuals with this version of a schizoid personality style are not characterized by the kind of inner depletion of thought and feeling typically associated with the DSM diagnosis, and their psychological makeup may be better understood in terms of intrapsychic (inner) conflict rather than solely in terms of deficits in capacities. Individuals with this higher-­functioning version of schizoid personality easily feel in danger of being engulfed, enmeshed, controlled, intruded upon, overstimulated, and traumatized—­dangers they associate with involvement with other people (Klein, 1946). They may appear notably detached, or they may behave in a socially appropriate way while privately attending more to their inner world than to the surrounding social world of other humans (Fairbairn, 1952). Some schizoid individuals withdraw into solitary environments and even hermit-­like reclusiveness; others retreat from social engagement in more psychological ways, to the fantasy life of their minds (Winnicott, 1971). Although seriously schizoid individuals, especially those with schizotypy, may appear indifferent to social acceptance or rejection, to the extent of having eccentricities that serve to put others off, this seeming indifference may have more to do with establishing a tolerable level of space between themselves and others than lack of awareness or understanding of social expectations; in this way, they differ significantly from individuals on the autism spectrum (Ridenour, 2014). The clinical literature is mixed about whether to view schizoid psychology from the perspective of conflict (between closeness and distance needs) or of deficit (developmental arrest, or genetic predisposition that limit the capacity for interpersonal relatedness).

chunks.heading: 682 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 682 Adulthood BOX 12.5. Schizoid Schizotypal (Deficit-­Based) Personality Prototype Summary statement: Individuals with Schizoid–Schizotypal Personality are characterized by pervasive impoverishment of, and peculiarities in, interpersonal relationships, emotional experience, and thought processes. I...
chunks.text: 682 Adulthood BOX 12.5. Schizoid Schizotypal (Deficit-­Based) Personality Prototype Summary statement: Individuals with Schizoid–Schizotypal Personality are characterized by pervasive impoverishment of, and peculiarities in, interpersonal relationships, emotional experience, and thought processes. Individuals who match this prototype lack close relationships and appear to have little need for human company or contact, often seeming detached or indifferent. They lack social skills and tend to be socially awkward or inappropriate. Their appearance or manner may be odd or peculiar (e.g., their grooming, posture, eye contact, or speech rhythms may seem strange or “off”), and their verbal statements may be incongruous with their accompanying emotion or nonverbal behavior. They have difficulty making sense of others’ behavior and appear unable to describe important others in a way that conveys a sense of who they are as people. They likewise have little insight into their own motives and behavior, and have difficulty giving a coherent account of their lives. Individuals who match this prototype appear to have a limited or constricted range of emotions and tend to think in concrete terms, showing limited ability to appreciate metaphor, analogy, or nuance. Consequently, they tend to elicit boredom in others. Despite their apparent emotional detachment, they often suffer emotionally: They find little satisfaction or enjoyment in life’s activities, tend to feel life has no meaning, and feel like outcasts or outsiders. A subset of individuals who match this prototype show substantial peculiarities in their thinking and perception. Their speech and thought processes may be circumstantial, rambling, or digressive; their reasoning processes or perceptual experiences may seem odd and idiosyncratic; and they may be suspicious of others, reading malevolent intent into others’ words and actions. 5 very good match (patient exemplifies this disorder; prototypical case) Diagnosis 4 good match (patient has this disorder; diagnosis applies) 3 moderate match (patient has significant features of this disorder) Features 2 slight match (patient has minor features of this disorder) 1 no match (description does not apply) Schizoid individuals are often seen by themselves and others as loners; they tend to be more comfortable by themselves than with other people. At the same time, they may feel a deep yearning for closeness that is concealed by defensive avoidance and withdrawal (Shedler & Westen, 2004), and they may harbor intense fantasies about emotional and sexual intimacy (Doidge, 2001; Seinfeld, 1991). Because they depend on withdrawal rather than more distorting defenses, individuals with higher-­level (more conflict based) schizoid personalities can sometimes be startlingly aware of features of their inner lives that tend to be unconscious in individuals with other psychologies, so they may be perplexed to learn that others are unaware of aspects of themselves that, to them, seem obvious. The DSM assertion that schizoid people rarely experience strong emotions is not supported by clinical experience or research (Shedler & Westen, 2004). Some schizoid individuals feel pain of an intensity so excruciating as to require their defensive detachment to endure it. They may seldom feel strong pleasurable emotions, however. Moreover, if they have a low-level borderline or psychotic personality organization, they may show signs of thought disorder, persecutory anxieties, and concrete thinking.

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 683
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 683 Treatment implications. Individuals with the higher functioning, more conflict-­ based version of schizoid personality may do well in treatments that both offer possibilities for emotional intimacy and respect their need for sufficient interpersonal space (McWil...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 683 Treatment implications. Individuals with the higher functioning, more conflict-­ based version of schizoid personality may do well in treatments that both offer possibilities for emotional intimacy and respect their need for sufficient interpersonal space (McWilliams, 2011; Ridenour, 2014). They may communicate their concerns most intimately and comfortably via metaphor, or emotionally meaningful references to literature, the arts, and areas in which they have a passionate interest, whether in abstract realms such as theology or more prosaic areas such as video games. In some cases, particularly those in which their schizoid qualities seem to express a deficit in relating rather than a conflict around relating, they may not be consciously aware of the psychological meaning of these communications. In contrast, people with lower level, more deficit-­based versions of schizoid psychology—­in particular, those at a borderline or psychotic level of personality organization—­may evince severe deficits in making sense of their own and other people’s behaviors (Kernberg, 2021). They benefit from a supportive approach aimed at strengthening ego functions (executive function), and developing ways of compensating for deficits in psychological capacities. Somatizing Personalities Psychiatrists and other medically trained clinicians are more likely than other therapists to have recurrently somatizing patients in their caseloads. A cluster of individuals whose problems are chronically expressed in bodily difficulties did not emerge from research using the SWAP instrument, perhaps because it was conducted mainly by sampling from the caseloads of psychologists and psychiatrists (from professionals working with patients who have identified their difficulties as mental rather than simply medical). In the first PDM, we posited a somatizing personality disorder, and we continue to see value in this construct—­but, as with depressive psychologies, some scholars see somatizing presentations as outside the personality spectrum. However we label the phenomenon, for many patients, psychosomatic pathologies are a major source of concern. Their bodies are the “preferential” means through which they express their painful experiences. Somatization and somatic preoccupations are common throughout the severity dimension but become particularly difficult to treat in the borderline range, where illness may interfere gravely with the continuity of treatment, and in the psychotic range, where bodily preoccupations may be expressed as somatic delusions. There is scant empirical literature on characterological somatization. There are, however, many studies showing relief of pervasive somatic symptoms via psychotherapy (e.g., Abbass, 2015, 2016; Abbass et al., 2020; Luyten & Fonagy, 2016). Investigation of the psychological aspects of a person’s chronic and medically confounding bodily complaints is complicated by the likelihood that some individuals diagnosed as defensively somatizing have an undiagnosed illness, such as an autoimmune or infectious disease, that explains their bodily preoccupations. Clinically, it can be hard to tell whether the self-­involved, complaining style common among patients with inexplicable, intractable ailments represents preexisting personality characteristics or the psychological consequences of chronic physical discomfort and medical runarounds (MacKinnon et al., 2006). Notwithstanding this problem, clinical experience suggests the existence of a personality tilt toward expressing dysphoria through the body (McDougall, 1989). Some somatizing patients may present a confusing combination of hypochondriacal preoccupations, diagnosable physical illness known to be stress-­related, and bodily

chunks.heading: 684 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 684 Adulthood symptoms that express ideas and affects too painful to put into words (e.g., conversion symptoms). Often a somatizing patient consults a psychotherapist reluctantly and in desperation, at the urging of a series of defeated medical specialists. The patient “sent” to therapy in this way...
chunks.text: 684 Adulthood symptoms that express ideas and affects too painful to put into words (e.g., conversion symptoms). Often a somatizing patient consults a psychotherapist reluctantly and in desperation, at the urging of a series of defeated medical specialists. The patient “sent” to therapy in this way may arrive in a resentful, defensive state of mind, expecting to be accused of malingering. Addiction to prescribed pain medications is a common complication. Although there is some overlap between the two kinds of suffering, individuals who somatize should be differentiated from those with hypochondriasis—­a more severe condition characterized by exaggerated fears of physical illness, a low threshold of physical discomfort, rituals related to bodily concerns, and the substitution of a preoccupation with the body for meaningful, in-depth relationships with other individuals. In intensive psychotherapy, patients with hypochondriacal tendencies may become paranoid when the therapist tries to explore transference reactions. Somatizing patients are often notable for their “alexithymia” (Sifneos, 1973) or “affective agnosia” (Lane, Weihs, Herring, Hishaw, & Smith, 2015), terms that refer to an inability to express emotions verbally. Although the connection between somatization and difficulty putting feelings into words is not necessarily present in all somatizing patients, clinicians have reported this observation frequently. It is also inferable from research with toddlers and young children. Greenspan (1992), for example, found that children who cannot verbalize feelings tend either to act out or to get sick. Marty and M’Uzan (1963) described somatizing patients as using la pensée opératoire or “operational thinking,” meaning that they were strikingly devoid of fantasy, incapable of symbolic expression, and invested more in “things” than in products of the imagination. Their preoccupations tend to be concrete and repetitive (Joyce, Fujiwara, Cristall, Ruddy, & Ogrodniczuk, 2013), and they lack what Jurist (2005) has called “mentalized affectivity.” Presumably, early caregivers of somatizing patients failed to foster a capacity to represent feelings verbally, leaving their bodies to convey what their minds could not. Their unformulated affect makes talk therapy difficult but also vital for their improvement (Jurist, 2018). Although they may have once received some secondary gain from the sick role, the pain they suffer is real and debilitating, and in adulthood, unless they are invested in maintaining a legally disabled status, there is little that is rewarding about their experience of physical discomfort and compromise. The sense of self in chronically somatizing patients tends to be fragile; they may feel unentitled and powerless. They often report that they feel repeatedly unheard—no doubt, partly because listeners tune out defensively as their efforts to help are frustrated, but possibly also because of early experiences with caregivers who failed to respond to their communications. With professionals, they may act both helpless and oppositional. The unvoiced hatred that interviewers often feel from somatizing patients, especially those in the borderline range, may result at least partly from their repeated experience of being treated as annoying complainers and given the message “It’s all in your head.” In addition, their inner working model of relatedness may require them to be ill as a condition of being cared about by someone important to them. Common countertransferences to somatizing patients may include feelings of futility, impatience, and irritation. Boredom, inner deadness, and disengagement are also not uncommon. Treatment implications. Treatment of individuals with somatizing tendencies is difficult and requires patience with their inarticulateness and negativity. Empathic

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 685
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 685 BOX 12.6. Clinically Derived Description: Somatizing Personalities Individuals with a somatizing personality style or disorder tend to express emotional suffering on a bodily level. Worries, complaints, and physical discomfort represent the “preferred” means thr...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 685 BOX 12.6. Clinically Derived Description: Somatizing Personalities Individuals with a somatizing personality style or disorder tend to express emotional suffering on a bodily level. Worries, complaints, and physical discomfort represent the “preferred” means through which painful experiences and dysphoria are manifested. Physical symptoms are the results of feelings and ideas, often negative, that are too intense to be expressed in words. Their bodily problems may include both conversion reactions and stress-­related physical damage and malfunction. They show alexithymic characteristics; that is, they are unable to communicate their emotions verbally. Their thinking is often characterized by a striking lack of imagination, an inability to express themselves symbolically, and a greater investment in “things” than in the products of the imagination. Their concerns tend to be concrete and repetitive. Their sense of self is fragile; they often feel powerless, weak, and at the mercy of forces beyond their control. Some individuals who chronically somatize express painful, recurrent experiences to the degree that others do not hear their suffering or take it seriously. 5 very good match (patient exemplifies this disorder; prototypical case) Diagnosis 4 good match (patient has this disorder; diagnosis applies) 3 moderate match (patient has significant features of this disorder) Features 2 slight match (patient has minor features of this disorder) 1 no match (description does not apply) observation that their suffering is real is critical to therapeutic progress; without that explicit acknowledgment, they may feel accused of manufacturing or exaggerating their pain. Because any movement toward emotional expression is stressful for them, they frequently become ill and cancel appointments just when their therapists begin to see progress. Central to their improvement is their therapists’ tactful encouragement to feel, name, and accept their emotional states. Primarily Externalizing Personalities Hysteric–Histrionic Personalities Because the terms “hysteric” and “histrionic” are redolent of patriarchy and gender inequality, they are understandably offensive to many. There is, however, a difference between the term and the phenomenon. Regardless of terminology, a personality style exists and is seen commonly in clinical practice (and not simply in the cisgender women with whom it has been most often associated; see Gabbard, 2014; Magid & Fox, 2022). It has been described repeatedly in the clinical literature and has emerged reliably in empirical research using statistical clustering methods. Objections to terminology should not blind us to the clinical phenomenon. On the surface, people with hysteric–­histrionic personality styles exemplify gender stereotypes. They may present as stereotypically feminine or masculine, like a leading lady or leading man in a stylized Hollywood movie. Beneath the surface, they are preoccupied with gender and sexuality, and their relation to power. They attribute weakness, defect, or inferiority to people of their own gender and see those of the other gender as powerful, exciting, frightening, and enviable (Horowitz, 1997).

chunks.heading: 686 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 686 Adulthood Unconsciously, whatever their intellectual understanding of gender, they tend to have binary images of gender. With respect to outward behavior, in Western cultures, they generally come across as flamboyant and seductive, though a subset may, seemingly paradoxically, strike clinicians...
chunks.text: 686 Adulthood Unconsciously, whatever their intellectual understanding of gender, they tend to have binary images of gender. With respect to outward behavior, in Western cultures, they generally come across as flamboyant and seductive, though a subset may, seemingly paradoxically, strike clinicians as curiously inhibited, conventional, and modest. Before DSM-III introduced the diagnosis of histrionic personality disorder, most psychoanalytic thinkers used the term “hysterical” for neurotically organized people with these personality dynamics and “histrionic” or “hysteroid” for those in the borderline and psychotic ranges. The DSM diagnosis of borderline personality disorder essentially depicts a hysteric–­ histrionic personality style organized at a borderline level of severity (cf. Zetzel, 1968). This unfortunate conflation of a personality style with a level of personality organization has led to considerable clinical confusion. Individuals with hysteric–­histrionic personalities tend to seek power via seductiveness toward persons they see as dominant (“pseudohypersexuality”). Their use of their sexuality thus has a defensive function, serving to ward off feelings of weakness, defectiveness, or fearfulness, and to gain a sense of power or conquest over the exciting (but envied and frightening) overvalued other. Sexual intimacy may arouse internal conflicts because of unconscious shame about one’s gendered body and fears of being damaged by the more powerful opposite gender. Some people with hysteric–­histrionic psychologies flaunt their sexuality in an exhibitionistic way, in an unconscious effort to counteract unconscious shame and fear, while others, especially those in societies that value virginity and modesty in women, are sexually avoidant or unresponsive. Many observers have noted that overtly hysteric–­histrionic psychologies are more evident in cultures with strict, hierarchical gender roles. Cultural factors influence the expression of hysterical personality dynamics. The DSM depiction of self-­dramatizing histrionic traits may apply more to people in Western cultures than to individuals in societies that emphasize modesty and control of sexuality—­as did the Viennese culture in which Freud worked with “hysterics” who were often described as reserved and shy. Clinical experience suggests factors that can give rise to hysteric–­histrionic personality dynamics include a seductive parent, being reared in a family in which boundaries are violated, or feeling rejected by caregivers based on one’s gender. Patients with hysteric–­ histrionic personalities fear overstimulation, much as schizoid patients do, but from inside rather than from outside. Their own feelings and desires evoke anxiety. The dread of being overwhelmed by affect may be expressed in a self-­dramatizing way, as if emotion is being unconsciously derided through exaggeration. Cognitive style may be impressionistic (Shapiro, 1965), as these individuals prefer not to look too closely at details for fear of seeing and knowing something frightening. Disavowed intrapsychic conflict may be expressed through medically inexplicable physical symptoms (conversion). Behavior, especially sexual behavior, may be impulsive or driven, yet disconnected from identifiable internal states. Like narcissistic individuals, patients with hysteric–­histrionic personalities may compete for attention that reassures them of their value, but their exhibitionistic and competitive qualities are limited to the realm of sexuality and gender. Outside that area, they may be capable of warm and stable attachments. Power dynamics experienced in the therapeutic relationship are likely to parallel the gendered power constellations in the patient’s family of origin. As treatment progresses, the clinicians’ own conflicts and discomfort around issues of gender, power, and sexuality may emerge. Therapists whose gender is devalued by the patient may feel irritated and demeaned; those whose gender is overvalued may initially feel

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 687
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 687 narcissistically inflated but may later come to feel manipulated or “played.” At the borderline level, histrionic patients tend to evoke fear and exasperation, as their intense unconscious anxiety impels them to express disavowed emotion in action rather than wo...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 687 narcissistically inflated but may later come to feel manipulated or “played.” At the borderline level, histrionic patients tend to evoke fear and exasperation, as their intense unconscious anxiety impels them to express disavowed emotion in action rather than words (technically, “acting out”). With a therapist they see as powerful, they may be flagrantly seductive in ways therapists experience as alarming and disturbing. Treatment implications. A patient with hysteric–­histrionic personality dynamics can benefit from both the relational and interpretive (exploratory) aspects of psychotherapy (Horowitz, 1997). Psychotherapy constitutes a new and different kind of relationship, with a professional who is neither seductive nor seducible, and who is neither competitive with the patient nor powerless and ineffectual. The dependability and emotional availability of the therapist, along with the safety and stability of the therapeutic frame, provide a context for self-­examination in which the patient can gain insight into internal conflicts. Neurotically organized patients with hysteric–­ histrionic personality styles respond well to the insight-­oriented aspects of therapy; BOX 12.7. Hysteric–­Histrionic Personality Prototype Summary statement: Individuals with hysteric–histrionic personality are emotionally dramatic and cognitively impressionistic; sexually provocative and interpersonally suggestible; idealizing of admired others; and paradoxically, both intensely and superficially attached. Individuals who match this prototype are emotionally dramatic and prone to express emotion in exaggerated and theatrical ways. Their reactions tend to be based on emotion rather than reflection, and their cognitive style tends to be glib, global, and impressionistic (e.g., missing details, glossing over inconsistencies, mispronouncing names). Their beliefs and expectations seem clichéd or stereotypic, as if taken from storybooks or movies, and they seem naïve or innocent, seeming to know less about the ways of the world than would be expected. Individuals who match this prototype tend to be sexually seductive or provocative. They use their physical attractiveness to an excessive degree to gain attention and notice, and they behave in ways that seem to epitomize gender stereotypes. They may be flirtatious, preoccupied with sexual conquest, prone to lead people on, or promiscuous. They tend to become involved in romantic or sexual “triangles” and may be drawn to people who are already attached or sought by someone else. They appear to have difficulty directing both tender feelings and sexual feelings toward the same person, tending to view others as either virtuous or sexy, but not both. Individuals who match this prototype tend to be suggestible or easily influenced, and to idealize and identify with admired others to the point of taking on their attitudes or mannerisms. They fantasize about ideal, perfect love, yet tend to choose sexual or romantic partners who are emotionally unavailable, or who seem inappropriate (e.g., in terms of age or social or economic status). They may become attached quickly and intensely. Beneath the surface, they often fear being alone, rejected, or abandoned. 5 very good match (patient exemplifies this disorder; prototypical case) Diagnosis 4 good match (patient has this disorder; diagnosis applies) 3 moderate match (patient has significant features of this disorder) Features 2 slight match (patient has minor features of this disorder) 1 no match (description does not apply)

chunks.heading: 688 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 688 Adulthood patients organized at the borderline level may require more deliberate and proactive attention to boundary issues, confrontation about destructive enactments, and explicit psychoeducation. Histrionic patients at the psychotic level may be virtually impossible to help because of their t...
chunks.text: 688 Adulthood patients organized at the borderline level may require more deliberate and proactive attention to boundary issues, confrontation about destructive enactments, and explicit psychoeducation. Histrionic patients at the psychotic level may be virtually impossible to help because of their tendency to create one crisis after another, leaving little room for thoughtful reflection amid the drama of their lives. Narcissistic Personalities Individuals with narcissistic personality styles exist all along the continuum of severity. Toward the healthier neurotic end of the spectrum, they may be socially appropriate, personally successful, and interpersonally charming. Although somewhat deficient in the capacity for intimacy, they may be well adapted to their family circumstances, work, and interests. In contrast, people with narcissistic personalities at lower levels of personality organization, whether personally successful or not, suffer from identity diffusion (often concealed by a grandiose self-­presentation), lack an inner-­directed morality, and may behave in ways that are highly destructive and toxic to others. Kernberg (1984) described the most severely disturbed version of narcissistic personality as “malignant narcissism”—narcissism suffused with sadistic aggression—­and placed it on a continuum with frankly psychopathic personality. The characteristic subjective experience of narcissistic individuals is a sense of inner emptiness and meaninglessness that requires recurrent infusions of external affirmation of their importance and value. Those who succeed in extracting such affirmation in the form of status, admiration, wealth, or success may feel an internal elation, behave in a grandiose and arrogant manner, evince a sense of entitlement, and treat others, especially those perceived as lower status, with dismissive contempt. When the environment fails to provide such infusions of affirmation, narcissistic individuals may feel depressed, ashamed, and envious of those who succeed in attaining the status that they lack. They fantasize about unlimited success, beauty, wealth, glory, and power, and their lack of real pleasure in either work or love can be painful to witness. The DSM criteria for diagnosing narcissistic personality disorder describe the grandiose version of narcissistic personality (first described by Reich, 1933/1972, as the “phallic narcissistic character”), but without addressing the inner experiences of emptiness and inadequacy that fuel the grandiosity. The DSM construct of narcissism also omits entirely the many persons who come to therapists feeling shy and ashamed, avoiding relationships, and describing a sense of emptiness and failure. Although they may present as depleted and beaten down by life rather than overtly entitled or grandiose, they are internally preoccupied with self-­referential fantasies centering on their rightful success and status that the world has denied them. Many have identified subtypes of narcissism. Rosenfeld (1987) distinguished between the thick-­skinned and thin-­skinned narcissist; Akhtar (1989) between the overt and covert (shy) narcissistic patient; Gabbard (1989) between oblivious and hypervigilant types; Masterson (1993) between exhibitionistic and closet variants; and Pincus and colleagues (Pincus, Cain, & Wright, 2014) between grandiose and vulnerable clients. Russ, Shedler, Bradley, and Westen (2008) identified three subtypes of narcissistic patients empirically, which they labeled grandiose/malignant, fragile, and high-­functioning/exhibitionistic; they described the last subtype as notable for significant psychological strengths, along with attention seeking, self-­ importance, and seductive charm. Patients with narcissistic concerns who are not in a position to act arrogantly may demand that the therapist teach them how to be “normal” or

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 689
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 689 “popular,” complaining that they want what more fortunate people have. Narcissistic individuals often have hypochondriacal preoccupations and somatic complaints. In recent years, Diamond, Yeomans, Stern, and Kernberg (2021) have studied attachment and mentalizat...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 689 “popular,” complaining that they want what more fortunate people have. Narcissistic individuals often have hypochondriacal preoccupations and somatic complaints. In recent years, Diamond, Yeomans, Stern, and Kernberg (2021) have studied attachment and mentalization in patients who meet DSM criteria for comorbid narcissistic and borderline personality disorders. Their research suggests that narcissistic people have insecure attachment styles (both attachment anxiety and avoidance) that may result from early relationships that were confusing, unpredictable, and full of hidden agendas (cf. Fossati, Feeney, Pincus, Borroni, & Maffei, 2015; Kealy, Ogrodniczuk, Joyce, Steinberg, & Piper, 2013). Under such circumstances, children may respond by divesting themselves of meaningful emotional investment in others and becoming preoccupied instead with their sense of self and their bodily integrity. This tendency makes it notably difficult for therapists to engage them in therapy as adults. Individuals with narcissistic personalities spend considerable energy evaluating their status relative to that of others. They tend to defend their wounded self-­esteem through a combination of idealizing and devaluing other people (Bradley, Heim, & Westen, 2005). When they idealize someone, they feel more special or important by virtue of their association with that person; when they devalue someone, they feel superior. Therapists who work with such individuals tend to feel unreasonably idealized or unreasonably devalued, or simply disregarded. Effects on the therapists may include boredom, detachment, distraction (daydreaming, inability to focus attention or to track therapeutic dialogue), mild irritation, impatience, and a feeling of invisibility (Colli et al., 2014). Therapists may also, especially if the wounds to these patients’ self-­esteem are apparent, have parental feelings. Gabbard (2009b) noted that a narcissistic patient, especially one of the grandiose and overt type, can be experienced “as speaking ‘at’ instead of ‘to’ the therapist, leaving the clinician unable to invest emotionally in the therapeutic relationship and be a real ‘participant observer.’ ” (p. 132). Treatment implications. The clinical literature on narcissistic personality disorder includes diverse speculations about etiology and consequently different treatment recommendations. The two most seminal theorists have been Heinz Kohut and Otto Kernberg. Kohut (1971, 1977) emphasized empathic attunement and exploration of the therapist’s inevitable empathic failures, and described periods in treatment when the patient is in a state of idealization, treating the analyst as a perfect or all-­powerful parent figure (an “idealizing transference”). He felt that during these times, the primary challenge for the therapist is to resist the temptation to confront this pattern too quickly. Kernberg and his colleagues (Kernberg, 1975, 1984; Diamond et al., 2021), on the other hand, have recommended the tactful but systematic exposure of defenses against shame, envy, and healthy dependency. It is relevant to their differences in technical recommendations that Kohut tended to be treating more idealizing narcissistic patients (candidates in psychoanalytic institutes), whereas Kernberg was working with more devaluing patients (individuals hospitalized for severe personality disorders, who devalued others to defend against their feelings of emptiness and failure). Contemporary practitioners are more likely to adopt an integrated approach to working with narcissistic individuals—­confronting defenses when they are salient, and attuning empathically to underlying hurt and vulnerability when those feelings are accessible (Gabbard & Crisp, 2018). Narcissistic envy can be an impediment to progress in therapy, since progress would make explicit the patient’s need for the therapist’s help. Idealization can put pressure on the therapist to be brilliant (but not so brilliant as to eclipse the patient’s

chunks.heading: 690 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 690 Adulthood BOX 12.8. Narcissistic Personality Prototype Summary statement: Individuals with narcissistic personality are grandiose and entitled, dismissive and critical of others, and often show underlying signs of vulnerability beneath a grandiose façade. Individuals who match this prototype hav...
chunks.text: 690 Adulthood BOX 12.8. Narcissistic Personality Prototype Summary statement: Individuals with narcissistic personality are grandiose and entitled, dismissive and critical of others, and often show underlying signs of vulnerability beneath a grandiose façade. Individuals who match this prototype have an exaggerated sense of self-­importance. They feel privileged and entitled, expect preferential treatment, and seek to be the center of attention. They have fantasies of unlimited success, power, beauty, or talent, and tend to treat others primarily as an audience to witness their importance or brilliance. They tend to believe they can only be appreciated by, or should only associate with, people who are high-­status, superior, or “special.” They have little empathy and seem unable to understand or respond to others’ needs and feelings unless they coincide with their own. Individuals who match this prototype tend to be dismissive, haughty, and arrogant. They tend to be critical, envious, competitive with others, and prone to get into power struggles. They attempt to avoid feeling helpless or depressed by becoming angry instead, and tend to react to perceived slights or criticism with rage and humiliation. Their overt grandiosity may mask underlying vulnerability: Individuals who match this prototype are invested in seeing and portraying themselves as emotionally strong, untroubled, and emotionally in control, often despite clear evidence of underlying insecurity or distress. A substantial subset of narcissistic individuals tend to feel inadequate or inferior, to feel that life has no meaning, and to be self-­critical and intolerant of their own human defects, holding themselves to unrealistic standards of perfection. 5 very good match (patient exemplifies this disorder; prototypical case) Diagnosis 4 good match (patient has this disorder; diagnosis applies) 3 moderate match (patient has significant features of this disorder) Features 2 slight match (patient has minor features of this disorder) 1 no match (description does not apply) brilliance). Progress may be slow, but any improvement for a patient with a narcissistic psychology is valuable for both the patient and those in the patient’s life. Narcissistic people may be easier to help in therapy in midlife or later, when their fantasies of limitless fame, wealth, beauty, or power have failed to materialize and life’s realistic limits have become undeniable. Paranoid Personalities Individuals who are so recurrently or chronically paranoid that they have a diagnosable personality disorder are found mainly in the borderline and psychotic ranges. Paranoid psychologies are characterized by unbearable affects, impulses, and ideas that are disavowed and projected onto others, who are then viewed with fear, rage, and other intense affects experienced as reactive to the other (Lee, 2017). Projected feelings may include hostility, as in the common paranoid conviction that one is being persecuted by hostile others; dependency, as in the sense of being deliberately rendered humiliatingly dependent by others; and attraction, as in the belief that others have sexual designs on the self or on the people to whom one is attached (e.g., in the common phenomenon of paranoid jealousy or the syndrome of

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 691
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 691 erotomania). Other painful affects, such as hatred, envy, shame, contempt, disgust, and fear, may also be disowned and projected. Although paranoia is described in somewhat one-­dimensional ways in other international taxonomies, persons with paranoid psychologi...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 691 erotomania). Other painful affects, such as hatred, envy, shame, contempt, disgust, and fear, may also be disowned and projected. Although paranoia is described in somewhat one-­dimensional ways in other international taxonomies, persons with paranoid psychologies may have complex subjective experiences organized around their terror to trust. Because pathologically paranoid individuals tend to have histories marked by felt shame and humiliation (Gilbert, Boxall, Cheung, & Irons, 2005; Meissner, 1978), they expect to be humiliated by others and may attack first to spare themselves the agony of waiting for the inevitable attack from outside. Their expectation of mistreatment creates the suspiciousness and hypervigilance for which they are noted—­attitudes that tend to evoke the very hostility they fear. Their personality is defensively organized around projection of their own unacceptable impulses, and themes of danger and power (either the persecutory power of others or the megalomanic power of the self). Paranoid patients tend to have some degree of thought disorder, and trouble conceiving that thoughts are different from actions—­a belief that may stem from childhoods in which they were (or felt) criticized for their real or presumed attitudes, as if thoughts and feelings are equivalent to actions. Some clinical reports (e.g., Bonime, 1979) suggest that they have experienced a parent as seductive or manipulative and are consequently alert to the danger of being seduced and exploited by the therapist and others. They may exist in anxious conflict between feeling panicky when alone (afraid that they will be damaged by an unexpected attack, or afraid that their destructive fantasies will damage or have already damaged others) and anxious in relationships BOX 12.9. Paranoid Personality Prototype Summary statement: Individuals with paranoid personality are chronically suspicious, angry and hostile, and may show disturbed thinking. Individuals who match this prototype are chronically suspicious, expecting that others will harm, deceive, conspire against, or betray them. They tend to blame their problems on other people or circumstances, and to attribute their difficulties to external factors. Rather than recognizing their own role in interpersonal conflicts, they tend to feel misunderstood, mistreated, or victimized. Individuals who match this prototype tend to be angry or hostile and prone to rage episodes. They tend to see their own unacceptable impulses in other people instead of in themselves and are therefore prone to misattribute hostility to other people. They tend to be controlling, oppositional, contrary, or quick to disagree, and to hold grudges. They tend to elicit dislike or animosity and to lack close friendships and relationships. Individuals who match this prototype tend to show disturbances in their thinking, above and beyond paranoid ideas. Their perceptions and reasoning can be odd and idiosyncratic, and they may become irrational when strong emotions are stirred up, to the point of seeming delusional. 5 very good match (patient exemplifies this disorder; prototypical case) Diagnosis 4 good match (patient has this disorder; diagnosis applies) 3 moderate match (patient has significant features of this disorder) Features 2 slight match (patient has minor features of this disorder) 1 no match (description does not apply)

chunks.heading: 692 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 692 Adulthood (afraid that they will be used and destroyed by the others’ agendas). Finally, paranoid people have difficulties putting themselves in others’ shoes and considering their perspective; that is, they have problems in “cognitive decentration” (Dimaggio & Semerari, 2004) or “mentalization”...
chunks.text: 692 Adulthood (afraid that they will be used and destroyed by the others’ agendas). Finally, paranoid people have difficulties putting themselves in others’ shoes and considering their perspective; that is, they have problems in “cognitive decentration” (Dimaggio & Semerari, 2004) or “mentalization” (Bateman & Fonagy, 2004; Fonagy, Gergeley, Jurist, & Target, 2002; Jurist, 2018). Clinical experience attests to the rigidity of the pathologically paranoid person (Shapiro, 1981). Therapist countertransference tends to be strong, mirroring feelings that the paranoid patient disowns and projects, such as fear, helplessness, and anger. Clinicians may also feel intensely criticized when the patient expresses only suspicion and anger, and disavows any feelings of need or vulnerability. These reactions are not limited to clinicians; patients with paranoid personalities commonly elicit such feelings in others with whom they interact. Treatment implications. The clinical literature emphasizes the importance of maintaining a patient, matter-­of-­factly respectful attitude; the communication of a sense of strength (lest such patients worry unconsciously that their negative feelings could destroy the therapist); a willingness to answer questions (lest the patient feel evaded or toyed with); and attending to the patient’s private conviction that aggression, dependency, and sexual desire—­and the verbal expressions of any of these strivings—­ are inherently dangerous. It is best not to be too warm and solicitous, as such attitudes may stimulate a terror of regression, and amplify paranoid suspicions about the therapist’s ulterior motives and why they are “really” being so nice. Psychopathic Personalities We prefer the earlier term “psychopathic” (Cleckley, 1941; Hare, 1991; Meloy, 1988, 1997) to the current “antisocial.” Many people with psychopathic personalities are not obviously antisocial; that is, they are not observably at odds with social norms. In fact, many people with psychopathic personalities pursue their self-­promoting agendas with social approval and even admiration. In some occupations, psychopathic behavior is rewarded. Although some psychopathic individuals run into trouble with authorities, others are quite adept at evading accountability for the harm they do to others. Once referred to as “moral insanity” (Prichard, 1835), psychopathy and psychopathic personalities are more commonly found in the borderline to psychotic range of severity (Gacano & Meloy, 1994) because of the primitive defenses (projection, devaluation, denial, and acting out) underlying this pattern (Porcerelli et al., 2010). The characteristic motivation of psychopathic people is seeking power for its own sake. They take pleasure in duping, manipulating, and controlling others (Bursten, 1973) and prioritize “winning” over whatever it is that is actually won. Although the stereotype of antisocial personality involves aggression and violence, clinical writings since Henderson’s (1939) work have also noted more non-­violent, parasitic versions of psychopathy, like the person who operates a scam or Ponzi scheme. The Dynamic Model of Psychopathy (Gullhaugen, Heinze, & Kornev, 2021) usefully examines ways that psychopathic individuals enhance the sense of self: through relationships with others, disaffiliation, controlling a situation, and refraining from activity. The authors confirm prior findings that psychopathic traits are associated with severe abuse during childhood, with consequent disorganized attachment (e.g., Craig, Gray, & Snowden, 2013; Schimmenti et al., 2015). There appear to be genetic contributions to psychopathy (e.g., Gabbard, 2022; Viding & McCrory, 2012).

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 693
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 693 Psychopathic people feel anxiety less frequently and intensely than others (­Ogloff & Wong, 1990; Zuckerman, 1999). People with diagnosed antisocial personality disorder have a higher-­than-­normal craving for stimulation and may seek it addictively (Raine, Vena...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 693 Psychopathic people feel anxiety less frequently and intensely than others (­Ogloff & Wong, 1990; Zuckerman, 1999). People with diagnosed antisocial personality disorder have a higher-­than-­normal craving for stimulation and may seek it addictively (Raine, Venables, & Williams, 1990; Vitacco & Rogers, 2001). Psychopathic individuals lack the empathy and the moral center of gravity that, in people of other personality types, tame strivings for power or direct it toward more socially beneficial purposes. There is, however, evidence that unconscious shame can play a role in the generation of psychopathic behavior (Gullhaugen et al., 2021). Psychopathic individuals may be charming and charismatic, and they may read others’ emotional states with stunning accuracy (Dolan & Fullam, 2004). They may be hyper-aware of their surroundings, even as they think and act from a self-­referential stance and for self-­serving purposes. Their emotional life tends to be impoverished, and their expressed affect is often insincere and manipulative. Affective connection to others is minimal; they typically lose interest when they see people as no longer useful. They cannot describe their emotional life with any depth or nuance. Their indifference to the feelings and needs of others, including their characteristic lack of remorse after harming others, may reflect a grave disorder of early attachment. Neglect, abuse, addiction, chaotic undependability in caregivers, and profoundly bad fits between a child’s temperament and the temperaments of responsible adults have been associated BOX 12.10. Psychopathic (Antisocial) Personality Prototype Summary statement: Individuals with psychopathic–antisocial personality exploit others, experience little remorse for harm or injury caused to others, and have poor impulse control. Individuals who match this prototype take advantage of others and tend to lie or deceive and be manipulative. They show a reckless disregard for the rights, property, or safety of others. They lack empathy for other people’s needs and feelings. Individuals who match this prototype experience little remorse for harm or injury they cause. They appear impervious to consequences and seem unable or unwilling to modify their behavior in response to threats or consequences. They generally lack psychological insight and blame their difficulties on other people or circumstances. They often appear to gain pleasure by being sadistic or aggressive toward others, and they may attempt to dominate significant others through intimidation or violence. Individuals who match this prototype tend to be impulsive; to seek thrills, novelty, and excitement; and to require high levels of stimulation. They tend to be unreliable and irresponsible, and may fail to meet work obligations or honor financial commitments. They may engage in antisocial behavior, including unlawful activities, substance abuse, or interpersonal violence. They may repeatedly convince others of their commitment to change, leading others to think “this time is really different,” only to revert to their previous maladaptive behavior. 5 very good match (patient exemplifies this disorder; prototypical case) Diagnosis 4 good match (patient has this disorder; diagnosis applies) 3 moderate match (patient has significant features of this disorder) Features 2 slight match (patient has minor features of this disorder) 1 no match (description does not apply)

chunks.heading: 694 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 694 Adulthood with later psychopathy, but there also appear to be temperamental contributing factors (­Gabbard, 2022). Therapists working with psychopathic individuals may find themselves feeling initially charmed and then deeply disturbed. They miss the usual sense of emotional connection and may f...
chunks.text: 694 Adulthood with later psychopathy, but there also appear to be temperamental contributing factors (­Gabbard, 2022). Therapists working with psychopathic individuals may find themselves feeling initially charmed and then deeply disturbed. They miss the usual sense of emotional connection and may find themselves feeling uncharacteristically apprehensive, jittery, or even “under the thumb” of psychopathic patients—­all countertransference responses that are highly informative. Recent empirical studies have identified clinicians’ emotional reactions of being criticized and overwhelmed while working with psychopathic patients (Colli et al., 2014). When such countertransferences coexist with any known history of violence in a patient, they should impel clinicians to give first consideration to their own safety. Treatment implications. Prognosis is poor unless other inclinations coexist with psychopathic traits; there has been debate in the clinical literature about whether truly psychopathic patients can benefit from psychotherapy at all. Treatments in which therapists try to reach out sympathetically are destined to come to grief because psychopathic patients are likely to regard kindness as a sign of weakness to exploit. Clinicians may, however, have a therapeutic influence on some psychopathic individuals by operating from a position of power and dominance (something few therapists are comfortable doing), behaving with scrupulous integrity, and recognizing that therapeutic leverage comes from appealing to the patient’s naked self-­interest, not from intrinsic desire to grow psychologically or become a better person. The prospects for any therapeutic influence are better if a psychopathic individual has reached midlife or later and has thus felt a decline in physical strength and encountered limits to omnipotent strivings. Sadistic Personalities Sadistic personality style is found mainly at the borderline or psychotic level and is organized around the theme of domination. Internally, the sadistic person may feel deadness and a sense of emotional sterility, which are relieved by inflicting pain and humiliation in fantasy and often in reality. The diagnosis of sadistic personality disorder was listed as provisional in DSM-III-TR but disappeared in DSM-IV; yet, as Meloy (1997, p. 631) noted, “burning the map does not eliminate the territory.” Although the reasons for the removal of this personality disorder from DSM are not clear, they may have included a concern that there is considerable overlap between sadistic and antisocial psychologies. Sadism and psychopathy are highly related (Holt, Meloy, & Strack, 1999; Westen et al., 2012); however, they are not identical. Except for studies of criminal sexual sadism, there has been little empirical research on sadistic personality patterns or disorders. Millon (1996) offers one of the few comprehensive accounts in the literature. Sadistic individuals are seen mainly in forensic settings, in which professionals may confront numerous people whose overriding motivation involves controlling, subjugating, and forcing pain and humiliation on others. Sadistic personality is readily recognizable. Meloy (1997) cites the man who smiles broadly and shamelessly while recounting battering his wife, and the child “who does not angrily kick a pet, but instead tortures animals with detached pleasure” (p. 632). In the search for total control over another—­a project Fromm (1973, p. 323) called the turning of “impotence into omnipotence”—the sadistic person

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 695
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 695 always chooses as targets those who are subordinate, weaker, and comparatively powerless (Shapiro, 1981). Only a fraction of those who abuse others are characterologically sadistic. Although many people strike out when provoked or attacked, sadistic people tend...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 695 always chooses as targets those who are subordinate, weaker, and comparatively powerless (Shapiro, 1981). Only a fraction of those who abuse others are characterologically sadistic. Although many people strike out when provoked or attacked, sadistic people tend to inflict their tortures with a dispassionate calm (perhaps originally a defense against overwhelming rage). Thus, forensic scientists distinguish between “affective” (catathymic) and “predatory” violence (e.g., Serin, 1991). The hallmark of sadistic personality is the emotional detachment or guiltless enthusiasm with which domination and control are pursued. This detachment, which may include the systematic, step-by-step preparation of a sadistic scenario, has the effect (and probably the intent) of dehumanizing the other. Although it is likely that all individuals with sadistic personality disorder are sadistic in their preferred expressions of sexuality, many people whose sexuality is organized around sadistic fantasies or activities are not sadistic generally and do not have a sadistic personality style or disorder. Professionals interviewing sadistic individuals report feelings of visceral disturbance, vague unease, intimidation, “creepiness,” and being overwhelmed by negative feelings (Genova & Gazzillo, 2018). Meloy (1997) mentions goose bumps, the sense of one’s hair standing on end, and other atavistic reactions to a predator–­prey situation. Sadistic individuals are mendacious (Stone, 2009); they may enjoy tormenting their interviewers by lying, by withholding information, or by elaborating on descriptions of their sadistic preoccupations. Countertransferential torment may be an indication of underlying sadism. A therapist should take seriously any disturbing reactions of this sort; they indicate the need for more thorough assessment and a treatment plan that takes into account the person’s possible dangerousness. Therapeutic implications. We know of no reports of successful therapy for characterological sadism. Stone (2009, 2018), who carefully analyzed biographical accounts of murderers, considered the sadistic individuals he studied to be beyond the reach of therapy. The attachment deficit inherent in treating other beings as objects to toy with may preclude developing a mutual working alliance. Additionally, the pleasure BOX 12.11. Clinically Derived Description: Sadistic Personalities Individuals with a sadistic personality style or disorder may experience an internal sense of deadness and emotional sterility, from which they derive relief by inflicting pain and humiliation—­in fantasy and often also in reality. The motivations of sadistic people involve controlling, subju­gating, mortifying, and torturing other people in a calm, cool manner. They show a kind of detached enjoyment and ruthless determination to seek dominance and control. This attitude, which may include the systematic, step-by-step preparation of a sadistic scenario, has the effect (and probably also expresses the intent) of dehumanizing the other. Although individuals who are not characterologically sadistic may develop sexually sadistic preferences, individuals with this personality style or disorder are typically also sadistic in their sexual behavior. Their capacity for attachment may be severely impaired, in that they tend to treat other human beings as objects for their amusement rather than as subjects to be respected. They may lie and take pleasure in deceiving others, including keeping the fact of their sadistic preoccupations completely private.

chunks.heading: 696 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 696 Adulthood in sadistic acts, especially orgiastic pleasure in sexual sadism, may be so reinforcing that efforts at change are futile. Still, accurate understanding of sadistic personality has significant implications for making recommendations to judicial officers, limiting opportunities for harm...
chunks.text: 696 Adulthood in sadistic acts, especially orgiastic pleasure in sexual sadism, may be so reinforcing that efforts at change are futile. Still, accurate understanding of sadistic personality has significant implications for making recommendations to judicial officers, limiting opportunities for harm, helping people affected by sadistic persons, allocating resources realistically, and protecting oneself from becoming a target of a patient’s sadistic enactment. Emotionally Dysregulated Personalities Since the first edition of the PDM in 2006, we have struggled with how best to handle the dual meaning of the term “borderline.” In PDM-2, we adopted Kernberg’s solution of distinguishing between borderline personality organization and DSM-­defined borderline personality disorder, but received feedback that readers, especially newer clinicians, found the double meaning confusing. In this edition of the PDM, in an effort to avoid confusion, we reserve the term borderline for a level of personality organization and refer to the DSM concept of borderline personality disorder as “emotionally dysregulated personality.” Efforts to understand emotionally dysregulated personality span decades. Scholars have viewed this personality configuration in terms of characteristic defenses, notably splitting, projection, and projective identification; through the lens of psychiatric management problems (Gunderson & Singer, 1975); as a deficit in the capacity to mentalize (to recognize internal states that underlie or motivate behavior in self and others) and regulate affect (Fonagy et al., 2002; Mucci, 2018); in terms of disorganized attachment (Fonagy et al., 2000; Holmes, 2004; Liotti, 2004); and as inability to experience continuity of self and others (Chefetz, 2015; Meares, 2012). With respect to etiology, there is evidence for a genetic vulnerability (Kernberg & Caligor, 2005; Siever & Davis, 1991; Stone, 1980; Torgersen, 2000), for origins in an early attachment disorder (Guidano & Liotti, 1983), for developmental arrest (­Bateman & Fonagy, 2004; Masterson, 1976), and for effects of relational trauma (Meares, 2012). Neuroscientific research (Fertuck, Lenzenweger, Hoermann, & ­Stanley, 2006; van der Kolk, 2003) indicates that early trauma can damage capacities for executive control and affect regulation. The relative weight of these factors certainly varies from person to person. In attachment research, scholars have identified a relevant disorganized/disoriented or “type D” insecure attachment style (e.g., Fonagy, Target, & Gergely, 2000; Liotti, 2004; Liotti, Pasquini, & Cirricone, 2000; Main & Solomon, 1986). This pattern is characterized by chronic, long-term difficulties in tolerating and regulating affect, and involves regarding attachment figures (such as therapists) as both objects of safety and objects of fear, causing them to be treated with confusing combinations of desperate clinging, hostile attack, and states of detachment. Emotionally dysregulated personality is specifically linked to the borderline level of personality organization. All patients with emotionally dysregulated personality or DSM borderline personality disorder function at a borderline level of personality organization. The reverse is not true: not all patients who function at a borderline level of personality organization have emotionally dysregulated personality style. Historically, emotionally dysregulated individuals have been widely considered difficult to treat, partly because they may challenge ordinary therapeutic boundaries and evoke intense countertransferences, and partly because they require special

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 697
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 697 training for the therapist that diverges from the treatment models in which many clinicians are trained. Patients with emotionally dysregulated psychologies experience intense emotions that spiral out of control and compromise their functioning. They tend to cat...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 697 training for the therapist that diverges from the treatment models in which many clinicians are trained. Patients with emotionally dysregulated psychologies experience intense emotions that spiral out of control and compromise their functioning. They tend to catastrophize, may be unable to self-­soothe, and consequently require the presence of another person to help regulate affect. As relationships become closer, they are faced with an impossible contradiction: they experience a deep fear of rejection and abandonment (Masterson & Klein, 1995), become hypervigilant for signs of rejection and abandonment, and tend to misread benign behavior as rejecting or abandoning. At the same time. they feel easily controlled or engulfed, and so need to distance or escape. Patients with emotionally dysregulated personality tend to have difficulties connecting their own actions and other’s actions with internal experience (i.e., perceptions, thoughts, feelings, motives). They have trouble mentalizing, or understanding other people’s behaviors, intentions, desires, and emotions, often misinterpreting them via projection, or taking it for granted that other people’s thoughts and feelings mirror their own. They have difficulties putting themselves in other people’s shoes and taking their perspective. When distressed, they tend to see other people in both binary (all-good or all-bad) and self-­referential ways. They may be naive, with a tendency to develop stereotypical explanations of their own and other people’s behaviors, intentions, and desires; or they may be unduly suspicious, assuming harmful intent, and developing convoluted and paranoid interpretations of ordinary events and behavior. Because they lack a coherent and cohesive sense of identity, they have difficulties feeling a sense of continuity in their own experience. They may shift from one strong emotion to another and from one self-state to another, without noticing the inconsistencies in their reactions. As a result, they may feel disoriented by their own reactions, and they may leave others who interact with them feeling confused and disoriented as well. They tend to interact with others in ways that evoke emotions like those they themselves are experiencing, or those they are dissociating and disavowing (projective identification). They tend to feel an inner “void” (Miller, Townsend, & Grenyer, 2021) and may enter dissociated or trance-­like states of consciousness. They may engage in self-­ harming or self-­mutilating behavior to soothe themselves or to counteract dissociative experiences; many report that self-­harming acts make them feel alive or reconnected with their bodies. They may make suicidal threats or gestures for the same reason (whether consciously or unconsciously) or, alternatively, with the aim of eliciting care or controlling others’ behavior. They may also make real suicide attempts out of deep hopelessness and despair. Thus, it is critical to evaluate any instance of suicidality, especially in patients with diagnosable mood disorders, as its meaning may shift over time. Dysregulated patients may behave sexually or aggressively in response to intense attachment needs. They are often impulsive, and they tend to have trouble making and maintaining long-­lasting, gratifying close relationships and stable, satisfying work lives. Treatment implications. The general recommendations we have made for working with individuals with borderline personality organization apply to those with characterological emotional instability. To our knowledge, all approaches to helping emotionally dysregulated individuals emphasize the need for a clearly defined treatment

chunks.heading: 698 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 698 Adulthood frame; the centrality of the working alliance and the work of processing reactions to the inevitable disruptions to it; the critical role of therapeutic boundaries, and the therapist’s willingness to tolerate the patient’s rage and hurt when boundaries are maintained; the discouragemen...
chunks.text: 698 Adulthood frame; the centrality of the working alliance and the work of processing reactions to the inevitable disruptions to it; the critical role of therapeutic boundaries, and the therapist’s willingness to tolerate the patient’s rage and hurt when boundaries are maintained; the discouragement of regression; the expectation of intensity; the inevitability of either–­or dilemmas; the importance of the patient’s sense of the therapist as an emotionally genuine person; and the development of capacities for self-­reflection and mentalization. They also emphasize the need for ongoing clinical supervision and consultation (Levy et al., 2022). Interestingly, psychoanalytic theorists who have written about the treatment of such individuals emphasize how their techniques diverge from those of standard psychoanalytic treatments (e.g., Bateman & Fonagy, 2004; ­Yeomans et al., 2015), and cognitive-­behavioral therapists emphasize how their treatments diverge from the practice of standard CBT (e.g., Linehan, 1993; Young et al., 2003). BOX 12.12. Emotionally Dysregulated Personality Prototype Summary Statement: Individuals with borderline–­dysregulated personality have impaired ability to regulate their emotions, have unstable perceptions of self and others that lead to intense and chaotic relationships, and are prone to act on impulses, including self-­destructive impulses. Individuals who match this prototype have emotions that can change rapidly and spiral out of control, leading to extremes of sadness, anxiety, and rage. They tend to “catastrophize,” seeing problems as disastrous or unsolvable, and are often unable to soothe or comfort themselves without the help of another person. They tend to become irrational when strong emotions are stirred up, showing a significant decline from their usual level of functioning.
