Individuals who match this prototype lack a stable sense of self: Their attitudes, values, goals, and feelings about themselves may seem unstable or ever-­changing, and they are prone to painful feelings of emptiness. They similarly have difficulty maintaining stable, balanced views of others: when upset, they have trouble perceiving positive and negative qualities in the same person at the same time, seeing others in extreme, black-or-white terms. Consequently, their relationships tend to be unstable, chaotic, and rapidly changing. They fear rejection and abandonment, fear being alone, and tend to become attached quickly and intensely. They are prone to feeling misunderstood, mistreated, or victimized. They often elicit intense emotions in other people and may draw them into roles or “scripts” that feel alien and unfamiliar (e.g., being uncharacteristically cruel, or making “heroic” efforts to rescue them). They may likewise stir up conflict or animosity between other people. Individuals who match this prototype tend to act impulsively. Their work life or living arrangements may be chaotic and unstable. They may act on self-­destructive impulses, including self-­mutilating behavior, suicidal threats or gestures, and genuine suicidality, especially when an attachment relationship is disrupted or threatened. 5 very good match (patient exemplifies this disorder; prototypical case) Diagnosis 4 good match (patient has this disorder; diagnosis applies) 3 moderate match (patient has significant features of this disorder) Features 2 slight match (patient has minor features of this disorder) 1 no match (description does not apply)

chunks.text: CHAPTER 13 Profile of Mental Functioning M Axis C H A P T E R E D IT O R S Vittorio Lingiardi, MD Laura Muzi, PhD Robert F. Bornstein, PhD CONSULTANTS Chloe Campbell, PhD Elliot Jurist, PhD J. Christopher Perry, MPH, MD Nicola Carone, PhD Alessandra Lemma, MA, MPhil, John H. Porcerelli, PhD Christopher Christian, PhD DClinPsych Caleb Siefert, PhD Mariagrazia Di Giuseppe, PhD Marta Mirabella, PhD Frank Yeomans, MD, PhD Peter Fonagy, PhD, FBA John S. Ogrodniczuk, PhD Sigal Zilcha‐Mano, PhD Introduction This chapter describes 13 categories of basic mental functions that can help clinicians capture the complexity and individuality of each patient. We provide descriptions of different levels of psychological functioning and adaptation, ranging from more compromised to more functional, along with descriptions that allow a clinician to rate patients in each capacity. Clinical utility demands that both adaptive and maladaptive functioning be taken into account in order to plan the most effective therapeutic interventions, anticipate challenges that may arise during treatment, delineate therapeutic goals, and evaluate treatment progress. While the P Axis encourages thinking about both overall functioning and specific personality patterns–­disorders, the M Axis encourages consideration and assessment of particular areas of mental functioning. In the P Axis, we ask clinicians to make a preliminary evaluation of some mental capacities (e.g., per Kernberg’s [1984] model) to consider identity integration, quality of object/interpersonal relations, and defensive functioning, in order to assess a patient’s level of personality organization within

chunks.heading: 710 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 710 Adulthood the healthy–­neurotic–­borderline–­psychotic continuum. The P Axis thus estimates the person’s level of personality functioning and best match with a prototypic personality style–­disorder; the M Axis complementarily fleshes out the specific functions that contribute to the patient’s p...
chunks.text: 710 Adulthood the healthy–­neurotic–­borderline–­psychotic continuum. The P Axis thus estimates the person’s level of personality functioning and best match with a prototypic personality style–­disorder; the M Axis complementarily fleshes out the specific functions that contribute to the patient’s personality. Consider, for example, two patients, both of whom have borderline personality organizations. One may be understood as borderline on the basis of cognitive schemas and/or frequent use of immature defenses, whereas the other is seen as borderline because of impaired interpersonal functioning, poor self-­integration, and use of midlevel defenses. Even though both have an ostensibly similar personality organization, the M Axis helps the clinician characterize the capacities and limitations that shape the overarching organization for each patient according to more specific issues in adjustment, functioning, and adaptation. The M-Axis domains reflect a wide range of psychodynamic, cognitive, and developmental theory-­driven concepts and observations, as well as empirical research related to these frameworks. The M Axis explicitly conceptualizes mental functioning as resulting from the integration of nature (temperament, genetic and epigenetic predispositions, basic underlying traits) and nurture (learning, experience, cultural and social context). The inclusion of multiple domains allows for an in-depth approach to personality, clinical symptoms, and the therapeutic process. Although Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM) and International Classification of Diseases (ICD) diagnoses have been useful for research purposes, and provide a common vocabulary for mental health professionals, they do not encourage further thinking about an individual’s overall personality functioning or underlying psychological dynamics. Thus, they are somewhat limited for purposes of case formulation, for predicting the quality of the therapeutic relationship, or for anticipating the therapeutic process. To sum up, the M Axis provides more nuanced, treatment-­oriented descriptions of functions that are close to the observable level and hence useful in considering treatment interventions with individuals, thus complementing the assessment of their levels of personality organization and patterns of personality style or disorder. It helps clinicians to appreciate and assess relevant psychological processes that underlie both symptom patterns and the subjective experiences of symptoms as described in the S Axis. Thus, the M Axis bridges the P and S Axes. The M Axis in the PDM-3 The M-Axis capacities have been partially reformulated from previous PDM editions. Some labels have been revised and updated: The previous capacity for regulation, attention, and learning is now listed as capacity for attention and learning (executive functioning); the previous capacity for relationship and intimacy has been reformulated as capacity for trust, empathy, and intimacy; and the previous self-­observing capacity (psychological mindedness) has been reformulated and renamed as the capacity to explore one’s inner life. In addition, in response to the growing clinical and empirical literature supporting the relevance of body-­related dimensions and experiences in psychological functioning, we have added a new mental function: bodily experiences and representations. Even though these 13 domains should not be considered as completely independent and distinct categories, the M-Axis capacities have been grouped into four domains (see below). We have tried to write descriptions of mental functioning in an empirically

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 711
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 711 grounded, assessment-­relevant, clinician-­friendly way. Each mental function includes an assessment procedure (see Tables 13.1a and 13.1b at the end of the chapter) whereby a clinician can indicate on a 5-point scale the level at which it operates in a patient. De...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 711 grounded, assessment-­relevant, clinician-­friendly way. Each mental function includes an assessment procedure (see Tables 13.1a and 13.1b at the end of the chapter) whereby a clinician can indicate on a 5-point scale the level at which it operates in a patient. Descriptors of key characteristics of levels of functioning (5 = healthy/optimal functioning, 3 = mild/moderate impairments, and 1 = major/severe impairments) are provided for each function. In PDM-2, the M-Axis chapter included a comprehensive list of the “Most Relevant Assessment Tools” for each mental capacity. For diagnostic formulations, clinical reports, treatment planning, and research, mental health professionals should be familiar with a range of measures for evaluating both adaptation and psychopathology. Given the frequency with which new assessment tools are published, however, and because their psychometric properties are continually updated, we have opted in this edition to make the M Axis more practical and user-­friendly, leaving the clinician or researcher to choose the most appropriate, validated, and clinically useful measure for the specific domain of mental functioning under consideration and for the patient being assessed. We have thus omitted a comprehensive reference list of assessment measures for each mental capacity. Nevertheless, clinicians can find at the end of this chapter a concise depiction of well-known, widely used, empirically validated and clinically useful tools that may enhance their evaluations of all the M-Axis mental capacities (see “Empirically Grounded Assessment of M-Axis Capacities”). The 13 M-Axis capacities are conceptualized with two questions in mind: (1) Which psychological processes are being assessed in each capacity? (2) How can these processes be systematically and practically assessed to provide meaningful information that will facilitate psychotherapy? In addition, although the PDM-3 M Axis articulates mental functioning in 13 distinct capacities, we ask readers to recognize that although conceptually distinct, these capacities are not entirely separate. Additionally, the level of functioning in a given capacity may partially alter the nature of others. Thus, mental functioning should be conceptualized as an integrated set of processes that work together—­a totality, a unique individual synthesis—­that operationally can be divided into contiguous categories, in the Aristotelian sense. As the ensuing definitions illustrate, these 13 capacities cover a broad range of areas and psychological processes. Despite the inherent overlap among certain capacities, each category highlights at least one critical and unique feature of mental functioning that cannot be easily and completely accounted for by other capacities. Although no profile or set of scores can capture the full richness of each person’s mental life—as Virginia Woolf (1922) wryly observed, “It is no use trying to sum people up”—the following 13 areas represent our best effort to cover the relevant territory. We group them into four domains: Cognitive and Affective Processes 1. Capacity for Attention and Learning (Executive Functioning) 2. Capacity for Affect Regulation and Expression 3. Capacity for Mentalization Identity and Relationships 4. Bodily Experiences and Representations 5. Capacity for Differentiation and Integration 6. Capacity to Regulate Self-­Esteem 7. Capacity for Trust, Empathy, and Intimacy

chunks.heading: 712 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 712 Adulthood Defense and Coping 8. Capacity for Impulse Regulation 9. Defensive Functioning 10. Capacity for Adaptation and Resilience Insight and Self‐Direction 11. Capacity to Construct and Use Internal Standards and Ideals 12. Capacity to Explore One’s Inner Life 13. Capacity for Agency and Purp...
chunks.text: 712 Adulthood Defense and Coping 8. Capacity for Impulse Regulation 9. Defensive Functioning 10. Capacity for Adaptation and Resilience Insight and Self‐Direction 11. Capacity to Construct and Use Internal Standards and Ideals 12. Capacity to Explore One’s Inner Life 13. Capacity for Agency and Purpose An Overview of Mental Functioning Capacities Cognitive and Affective Processes The capacity for attention and learning (executive functioning) is a multidimensional domain encompassing the range of constitutional and maturational contributions to an individual’s overall psychological functioning. It includes higher-­order cognitive processes that enable planning and provide goal-­directed control over thoughts, emotions, and actions. This capacity allows the individual to respond to nonroutine or changing circumstances in an effective, adaptable manner. The capacity for affect regulation and expression, an index of the individual’s affective functioning, involves the ability to identify, experience, modulate, comprehend, reflect on, express, and respond to affects in ways that are appropriate to a particular situation and consistent with one’s cultural milieu. The capacity for mentalization concerns individuals’ abilities to infer and reflect on their own mental states (e.g., needs, desires, feelings, beliefs, goals, intentions, and motivations) as well as those of others, and to use this capacity in personal and social interactions. Identity and Relationships The bodily experiences and representations capacity involves both conscious and unconscious perceptions, sensations, feelings, thoughts, and attitudes about one’s body, as well as the implicit awareness of one’s own body as a complex entity with defined, temporally consistent boundaries that allow individuals to distinguish the self from the environment. The capacity for differentiation and integration involves the ability to distinguish self from other, fantasy from reality, internal objects and representations from external objects and circumstances, and present from past and future, and to delineate connections between these elements without confounding them. The capacity to regulate self-­esteem reflects the level of confidence in and regard for an individual’s relationship to self, other people, and the larger world. It also refers to one’s overall sense of personal value, with self-­esteem that is neither unrealistically high nor unrealistically low, and self-­confidence that varies appropriately from one situation to another. The capacity for trust, empathy, and intimacy is a multidimensional domain describing the quality of an individual’s relatedness and interpersonal functioning. “Basic trust” can be defined as the ability to explore the environment and face threatening or conflictual situations with a balance between hypervigilance and credulity

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 713
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 713 toward others, as is appropriate to the context. “Empathy” involves both cognitive and emotional ability to understand and respond sensitively to the feelings of others, whereas “intimacy” involves the ability to adjust interpersonal distance and closeness in respo...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 713 toward others, as is appropriate to the context. “Empathy” involves both cognitive and emotional ability to understand and respond sensitively to the feelings of others, whereas “intimacy” involves the ability to adjust interpersonal distance and closeness in response to situational demands, based on each relationship and consistent with cultural expectations. Defense and Coping The capacity for impulse regulation involves an individual’s ability to modulate and regulate impulses and to express needs, motives, and urges in flexible, adaptive, ­culturally appropriate ways. Deficiencies in this capacity appear as either unmodulated impulsivity or rigid overcontrol of impulses, with concomitant affective constriction. Defensive functioning involves the way an individual characteristically manages motives, affects, urges, conflicts, memories, thoughts, and other potentially anxiety-­ producing inner experiences. It includes the individual’s response to anxiety, shame, grief, and other painful states resulting from external challenges and stressors or from threats to the self. The capacity for adaptation and resiliency involves the ability to adjust to unexpected events and changing circumstances, along with the ability to cope effectively and creatively when confronted with uncertainty, loss, stress, and challenge. “Adaptation” refers to the ability to cope with changing and uncertain circumstances, whereas “resiliency” denotes the ability to endure and thrive when faced with adverse (even potentially traumatic) events. Insight and Self‐Direction The capacity to construct and use internal standards and ideals involves the individual’s personal sense of morality, including beliefs, emotions, motives, and ideals that guide the person’s chosen endeavors. The capacity to formulate internal values and ideals reflects a consideration of self in the context of one’s culture and the ability to make mindful decisions based on a set of coherent, internally consistent underlying moral principles. The capacity to explore one’s inner life denotes the ability to reflect upon and understand the sources of one’s mental states. It involves grasping implicit connections between emotions, motives, thoughts, and behavior, both past and present, including one’s interpretations of others and one’s relationships with others. It includes the concept of “psychological mindedness,” that is, the capacity to observe one’s inner world mindfully and realistically (cf. the concept of “observing ego”). The capacity for agency and purpose involves the ability to master one’s own urges, feelings, and thoughts, and then to construct a narrative that gives cohesion and meaning to personal experiences and choices. It implies the feeling of “authorship,” internal control, and self-­efficacy at different levels of consciousness. Definitions and Assessment of the 13 M‐Axis Capacities 1. Capacity for Attention and Learning (Executive Functioning) The capacity for attention and learning (executive functioning) refers to a complex set of higher-­order cognitive processes that underlie the ability to plan and exert goal-­ directed control over thoughts, emotions, and actions, and the ability to respond to changing circumstances in an effective, adaptive manner. As a multidimensional

chunks.heading: 714 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 714 Adulthood capacity, it involves several domains, including inhibition (i.e., the ability to control attention, thoughts, emotions, and actions in order to avoid impulsiveness or to suppress irrelevant stimuli), working memory, attentional focus on both consciously and unconsciously perceived inf...
chunks.text: 714 Adulthood capacity, it involves several domains, including inhibition (i.e., the ability to control attention, thoughts, emotions, and actions in order to avoid impulsiveness or to suppress irrelevant stimuli), working memory, attentional focus on both consciously and unconsciously perceived information, and cognitive flexibility (i.e., the ability to switch selectively among mental processes, behavioral responses, and strategies). This capacity comprises several subcomponents including, but not limited to, set shifting, action planning, organizing, processing of affective and social cues, inhibiting of undesired responses, effortful control, and memory in its various forms (short- and long-term, semantic, autobiographical/episodic, and implicit/nondeclarative). Other processes that contribute to this area of functioning are auditory processing and language, visual–­ spatial processing, sensory modulation, intelligence, and attention. Taken together, these skills enable us to attend to and process internal and external information, regulate the experience of self and the environment, filter extraneous input from consciousness when appropriate, and learn from our experiences to enhance goal-­ directed action, and thereby to adapt and cope. This capacity differs somewhat from others on the M Axis, in that ratings of a patient’s functioning in this domain are strengthened by (and in some cases require) external corroboration (e.g., by neuropsychological assessment tools, such as the Wechsler Adult Intelligence Scale). A clinician’s initial conclusions about a patient’s capacity for attention and learning may be based primarily on self-­report and referral data, but when difficulties are also documented in the person’s file, in life history records, or by knowledgeable informants (e.g., a sibling or partner), the clinician can have greater confidence in an assessment. Clinicians evaluating this capacity should note that beginning with early child–­ caregiver interactions and continuing throughout development, variations in genetic predisposition, temperament, and neurobiological substrates of executive functioning (i.e., a network of frontal and corticolimbic neural regions) can be modified by a person’s social and emotional context. From diverse influences, we develop characteristic predispositions and biases in executive functioning, which become evident in how we later interact with others and with our environment. Because this capacity can be influenced by momentary affect states (e.g., anxiety, distress, fear, shame, anger), performance varies across situations and settings. Executive functioning may be affected by factors such as illness, temporary states of mind (e.g., fatigue), or medications (e.g., stimulants), with effects on an individual’s affective–­emotional, motivational, and interpersonal functioning. Rating Scale At the end of this chapter, Table 13.1 provides a rating scale for this and all the M-Axis capacities, which can be assessed on a scale from 5 (high functioning) to 1 (lower functioning). For each capacity, we offer descriptions of anchor points for levels 5, 3, and 1. 5 The individual is appropriately focused, well organized, and able to adapt and learn from experience, even under stress. There is a good ability to express thoughts, affects, and other inner experiences, both verbally and nonverbally. Memory, attention, goal-­oriented action planning, cognitive flexibility, problem solving, and inhibitory control are all functioning at a high level, and appear to be well integrated. 3. The individual is generally focused, organized, and able to adapt and learn when motivated and/or under specific circumstances. When not fully engaged, or

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 715
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 715 when under stress, the person may show a mild to moderate decline from customary functioning, with some difficulties in expressing thoughts, affects, and other inner experiences, both verbally and nonverbally. There is some difficulty in shifting the attentional fo...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 715 when under stress, the person may show a mild to moderate decline from customary functioning, with some difficulties in expressing thoughts, affects, and other inner experiences, both verbally and nonverbally. There is some difficulty in shifting the attentional focus, planning action sequences, and processing information in a goal-­ directed way. The individual at this level can attend and focus only for relatively short periods and to a limited degree, thus showing only moderate cognitive flexibility. 1. The individual’s attention is fleeting, with severe difficulties in shifting attention away from threat or inhibiting irrelevant information. Individuals at this level typically show cognitive rigidity and have an impaired capacity to remain focused, organized, and attentive in order to carry out goal-­oriented behaviors. The person may have difficulty adapting to the environment, learning from experience, and planning action sequences, and may be self-­absorbed, ruminative, or, in the most severe cases, lethargic. Learning capacity is severely limited by one or more processing or attentional difficulties. Both verbal and nonverbal expressions of thoughts, affects, and other inner experiences may be compromised, impoverished, or peculiar. 2. Capacity for Affect Regulation and Expression The second capacity involves the ability to identify, experience, modulate, comprehend, reflect on, express, and respond to affects in ways that are appropriate to a particular situation and consistent with one’s cultural milieu. This domain represents an index of the individual’s affective functioning, a multidimensional construct with several subcomponents. Affective regulation indicates all explicit and implicit processes involved in monitoring, evaluating, altering, and modulating affective experiences. This capacity comprises the ability to experience a wide range of emotions, positive as well as negative, in varying degrees of intensity. Impaired functioning in this domain may be evidenced by rapid and unpredictable onset of unmodulated, intense feelings (usually negative emotions ; e.g., anger, anxiety, or irritability), an inability to regulate behaviors while experiencing specific affects (e.g., falling apart in the face of sadness; becoming violent or excessively aggressive in the face of anger), and/or notable difficulty modulating affect to navigate situational demands adaptively (e.g., laughing inappropriately at a funeral; weeping uncontrollably at a wedding). Individuals differ in the extent to which they can tolerate affective experience, that is, in their level of comfort with feelings and their capacity to be conscious of them. Affective expression is the ability to communicate feelings to other people effectively. Adaptive affective expression involves verbal and nonverbal communications that promote social connection, pursuit of one’s goals, self-­respect, and self-­understanding. Individuals able to label their emotions, link internal emotional responses to triggers, and understand the social nature of emotional expression are more likely to experience and express affect adaptively. In rating affective expression, clinicians should consider the quality of expression, whether it is intentional or unintentional (e.g., acting out an emotion), and whether it fits the individual’s aims and social/cultural context (i.e., whether the expression of affects is appropriate in quality and intensity to the situation at hand; whether the affects are particularly variable or labile). A person’s unique patterns can be captured in a narrative characterizing the individual’s affect-­related behaviors, such as changes in facial behavior or posture. Clinicians assessing this capacity should note that because our affective experience is interpersonally shared, shaped, and regulated, it may be influenced by biologically based predispositions (e.g., affective temperament), cultural backgrounds and norms, and

chunks.heading: 716 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 716 Adulthood early infant–­caregiver interactions. Jurist’s (2005) “mentalized affectivity” (the ability to infer and reflect on thoughts, feelings, and other intentional mental states that inform our understanding of our emotions and help us anticipate future situations) is a bridge concept betwee...
chunks.text: 716 Adulthood early infant–­caregiver interactions. Jurist’s (2005) “mentalized affectivity” (the ability to infer and reflect on thoughts, feelings, and other intentional mental states that inform our understanding of our emotions and help us anticipate future situations) is a bridge concept between this capacity and the ability to mentalize, described below. Rating Scale 5. Individuals at this level are able to identify, modulate, express, communicate, and understand a wide range of affective states, subtle as well as overt, and positive as well as negative. They can integrate positive and negative feelings and can be conscious of affective experiences, tolerating them even if upsetting or painful. They can decipher and respond to most emotional signals flexibly and accurately, even under stress (e.g., safety vs. danger, approval vs. disapproval, acceptance vs. rejection, respect vs. disdain). Affective expression seems appropriate and congruent, in quality and intensity, to the situation in the moment, and to the content and affective valence of communications. 3. Individuals at this level experience a constricted range of emotional states, and/or show difficulty in identifying, experiencing, and modulating specific affects (e.g., anger, despair, regret, guilt, shame, envy). They show a limited ability to tolerate both pleasant and unpleasant emotions, and they are unable to acknowledge some affects consciously. When challenged or distressed, or when a particular feeling is intense, they may not be able to see other people’s perspectives or decipher others’ emotional states, and they may then respond to emotional signals in a dysregulated or asynchronous way. Certain emotions may be expressed in ways that are inadequate or disproportionate to situations, social expectations, and the cultural milieu. 1. Some individuals at this level show mostly fragmented, severely dysregulated, overwhelming, and/or chaotic emotional expression. They may convey little emotion at all (e.g., be unable to show facial expression, modulate vocal tone, or maintain body posture appropriate to the situation). They may have severe difficulties identifying, differentiating, modulating, and communicating feelings; they lack imagination and have a constricted, externally oriented thinking style (i.e., alexithymic tendencies). Their affective expression may seem odd or incongruent with their verbalizations and they may misread others’ emotional signals (e.g., therefore feeling suspicious, mistreated, unloved, or angry) and have difficulty identifying feelings and distinguishing them from bodily sensations. 3. Capacity for Mentalization “Mentalization”—the ability to infer and reflect on one’s own and others’ subjective states—is a form of mental activity considered to be both imaginative (when we mentalize, we are “imagining” what others think or feel), and reality-­based (because the capacity to mentalize with reasonable accuracy is crucial for effective interpersonal functioning). Mentalization is primarily preconscious, occurring outside our focus of attention; it aims at understanding and interpreting the behavior of self and others in terms of mental states (needs, desires, feelings, beliefs, goals, intentions, and motivations). This capacity enables us to use ideas to experience, describe, and express our internal life; to regulate our affects and maintain a coherent sense of self; to make accurate inferences about the mental states of others; and to make corrections if our inferences lead to mistaken conclusions. It allows us to distinguish between internal

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 717
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 717 and external reality, and between mental and emotional processes and real-world interactions. It is basic to the ability to consider alternative explanations rather than impulsively embracing the first thought that comes to mind to explain our own behavior or the b...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 717 and external reality, and between mental and emotional processes and real-world interactions. It is basic to the ability to consider alternative explanations rather than impulsively embracing the first thought that comes to mind to explain our own behavior or the behavior of others. Mentalization is supported by several cognitive skills, including understanding of emotional states and the ability to think implicitly about others’ states of mind. Allen, Fonagy, and Bateman (2008, p. 3) have described it as a means by which we “keep in mind the mind,” “understand misunderstandings,” “see [ourselves] from the outside and the other from the inside,” and “[attribute] a quality of mind to things or develop a mental perspective.” The construct of reflective functioning operationalizes the capacity to mentalize—­in other words, it is how we measure mentalization. Clinicians assessing this capacity should consider that mentalization involves the ability to symbolize affectively meaningful experience (i.e., to organize experience via mental representation rather than somatically or behaviorally), and to use it effectively both interpersonally and for self-­regulation. This ability may be apparent in verbal behavior but can also be manifested in more subtle and indirect ways, nonverbally, via one’s tone, and via the body. The capacity to represent experience enables us to use ideas to experience, describe, and express our internal life, and to make accurate inferences about the mental states of others. It influences our willingness to trust in the authenticity and personal relevance of interpersonally transmitted information, with the expectation that others will communicate information consistent with their own mental states; that is, it is vital to the concept of epistemic trust, which should be assessed within the “Capacity for Trust, Empathy, and Intimacy” (see below). The foundation for mentalization is laid early in life, as dyadic interactions with caregivers are encoded and internalized. The “psychological self” develops as an infant assimilates a caregiver’s attitude that they are an individual with their own mental states. Thus, the capacity to develop a mentalizing stance depends greatly on parental mentalizing capacities. Caregivers must be able to take the child’s perspective and, appreciating the inherent separateness of minds with different contents, treat the child as a “psychological agent” whose actions are motivated by mental states. This capacity includes the ability to make sense of the infant’s powerful affect states and other aspects of the baby’s nonverbally expressed internal world. Parents who cannot reflect on and respond empathically to the inner experience of their child deprive the child of a nuclear psychological experience necessary to build a cohesive sense of self. Maladaptive caregiving (e.g., by a mother or father lacking in the capacity for empathy or low in the capacity for mentalization themselves) can foster severe distortions in mentalizing ability, including hypermentalization (overinterpretive mental state reasoning) or vulnerability to breakdowns in mentalizing capacity, especially under stress. Attuned caregiving, in contrast, fosters the elaboration, differentiation, and integration of affective self-­states, setting the stage for the capacity to experience one’s own and others’ subjectivity. Awareness that other people accurately attend to and respond to one’s experience gradually builds in the infant the ability to reflect on these experiences through language (i.e., private and shared symbols), and ultimately increases the capacity for mentalization. Rating Scale 5. Individuals at this level have an adequate ability to form relatively accurate models of the minds of self and others, fostering self-­regulation and adaptive social

chunks.heading: 718 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 718 Adulthood relationships. They can comprehend and reflect on internal mental states (e.g., emotions, thoughts, desires, and needs) and understand, even when challenged or distressed, subjective experiences that underlie others’ actions and reactions. They are capable of distinguishing inner from...
chunks.text: 718 Adulthood relationships. They can comprehend and reflect on internal mental states (e.g., emotions, thoughts, desires, and needs) and understand, even when challenged or distressed, subjective experiences that underlie others’ actions and reactions. They are capable of distinguishing inner from outer reality; have insight into their own motives, affects, and behaviors; and can think about self and others in nuanced and sophisticated ways. They can construct representations of internal mental states using both overt and subtle cues (e.g., arousal, behavior, context). A healthy level of functioning in this capacity involves care toward others and humility about what can be fully known (i.e., the person demonstrates some confidence in mentalizing, while at the same time showing awareness of the inherent limits on knowledge of self and other). The individual uses internal representations to modulate and inhibit impulses and to express them adaptively and appropriately. 3. Individuals at this level are generally able to understand and reflect on their own actions and reactions in terms of mental states (e.g., needs, desires, feelings), to infer others’ internal experiences from behavioral and contextual cues, and to construct and use internal representations to experience a cohesive sense of self and others. In situations of conflict, distress, or intense arousal, however, there is a notable decline from usual levels of awareness of mental states of self and others, limited ability to elaborate on a feeling in its own right, a tendency toward external justifications, and a proclivity to act out or somatize wishes and feelings. Some such individuals can become aware of their limitations in understanding themselves and others (e.g., when reassured by a safe interpersonal context). 1. Individuals at this level are unable to infer, understand, or reflect on their own affective experiences or to symbolize others’ mental states. They have severe difficulties distinguishing between internal and external reality, and between mental and emotional processes and real-world interactions. They may conclude that what exists in the mind must exist “out there,” and vice versa, reflecting an inability to consider complex models of one’s own mind and/or those of others. They may develop inaccurate models of the mind of self and others, as reflected in a tendency toward giving long, overly detailed accounts with little relationship to observable reality. These individuals often misunderstand, misinterpret, or are confused by others’ actions and reactions, and are generally unable to represent emotions as they are being experienced. They are also unlikely to be willing to revise their beliefs. Thus, they tend to remain embedded in an affect, unable to express it in words or to regulate it effectively. When functioning in this capacity is extremely low, there is no experience of connection between internal experiences and possibilities for self-­observation. Especially under stress, the self may be experienced as incoherent, depleted, or altogether absent. 4. Bodily Experiences and Representations This capacity involves conscious and unconscious perceptions, sensations, feelings, and thoughts about one’s body, as well as the implicit awareness of one’s body as a complex entity with defined boundaries that allow individuals to distinguish the self from both physical and interpersonal environments. In accordance with Freud’s (1922) observation that “The ego is first and foremost a bodily ego,” clinicians rating this domain should take into account both implicit and explicit bodily experiences. Implicit ones include the physical signals that continuously inform us about the general state of our own organism and also contribute to a sense of identity, integrity,

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 719
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 719 and self-­continuity. Explicit bodily experiences include the somatic resonance of emotions, with more active involvement of the body, which can become the focus of our attention whenever something physically relevant occurs. This capacity involves perception, inte...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 719 and self-­continuity. Explicit bodily experiences include the somatic resonance of emotions, with more active involvement of the body, which can become the focus of our attention whenever something physically relevant occurs. This capacity involves perception, interpretation, monitoring, and modulation of stimuli originating inside our body (interoception) and outside it (exteroception) that may represent an early, prementalized form of self-­awareness. Bodily representations, which include (but are not limited to) body image and its components (e.g., body satisfaction/dissatisfaction), are grounded in the interaction between current multisensory inputs, such as visual and tactile awareness, and internal models or expectations about the form or structure of one’s own body. Bodily experiences and representations are thought to emerge from constitutional factors, repeated and consistent experiencing of sensorimotor actions, and contingent proprioceptive signals during infancy, resulting in a sense of an embodied self. The infant’s first experiences of being seen, touched, and held by a caregiver trigger the formation of a psychic space inhabited by mental representations. If an infant’s bodily experience can be represented in the mind of the caregivers, the infant will be able to experience a coherent sense of self rooted in the body. Before they are progressively differentiated into psychic states, affects and other inner experiences are felt as bodily sensations. The caregiver’s ability to make sense of the infant’s nonverbally expressed internal world (i.e., to “keep in mind” or mentalize the body; Shai & Belsky, 2011) plays a key role in laying the foundations for an embodied sense of self. By contrast, developmental interferences, such as early traumatic experiences, may result in segregated embodied meanings that cannot be expressed in fantasies and verbal expressions, and in an impaired capacity for mind–body differentiation. The body, especially the skin, may become a primary venue for self-­expression and the main way to handle unverbalizable feelings. Self-­destructive attacks on the body (e.g., self-­cutting), body-­related concerns (e.g., psychosomatic complaints and/or illness anxieties), major modifications of one’s physiognomy (e.g., cosmetic surgeries), dissociative experiences involving the body, conversion symptoms, dysmorphic representations of one’s body, and body image disturbances (e.g., those in feeding and eating disorders) are clinical examples of low functioning in this capacity. For individuals with gender incongruence (see Chapter 15, pp. 875–896), the assessment of this capacity should not focus on the issue of incongruence per se, but should address the presence and degree to which the individual may experience dysphoric and/or altered feelings, thoughts, or attitudes at a bodily level. Rating Scale 5. At this level of functioning, there is both prereflective and conscious awareness of one’s own body as a complex entity with defined boundaries between self and environment, enhancing self-­integrity. Bodily representations are adequately integrated into overall self-­experience, and the individual can differentiate inner experiences (e.g., emotions) from internal bodily states, even when under stress or conflicted. Bodily sensations are accurately perceived, and stimuli arising from inside and outside the body, respectively, can be identified. An ability to reflect upon and to express implicit and explicit bodily experiences (including those related to gender incongruence), allows the person to make mindful, adaptive decisions, especially in social and relational contexts.

chunks.heading: 720 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 720 Adulthood 3. At this level, individuals show a notable deficit when in stressful or conflictual situations in their ability to differentiate bodily states from psychic inner experiences and to integrate self-­experience. There may be a perceived sense of separation from one’s body and a diminish...
chunks.text: 720 Adulthood 3. At this level, individuals show a notable deficit when in stressful or conflictual situations in their ability to differentiate bodily states from psychic inner experiences and to integrate self-­experience. There may be a perceived sense of separation from one’s body and a diminished capacity to experience body ownership, along with mild to moderate difficulties in the ability to mentalize one’s physical experience and regulate intense affects (e.g., rage, frustration, despair). Whenever something occurs that is threatening or unprocessed, the body may become the focus of attention, self-­ expression, or concern (e.g., with body shape and weight or physical health). 1. Individuals at this level have severe difficulties in perceiving self–body unity and a sense of body ownership, and often cannot differentiate bodily experiences from mental states. Somatic features appear to the person as disturbing or degrading, such that one’s body is perceived as unfamiliar, alien, or disintegrating (not to be considered for individuals with gender incongruence). The person may have difficulty being aware of bodily sensations, and the mind–body split can lead to disembodiment and altered self-­perceptions in which individuals may feel like outside observers of their own mental processes and their body. Out-of-body experiences may result from abnormal multisensory integration. When there is difficulty distinguishing between stimuli inside and outside the body, the body may become the object of delusions and/or hallucinations. Individuals at this level may use the body as their primary means of expressing unprocessed inner experiences and modulating overwhelming emotions, leading to self-­injurious and self-­destructive behaviors. 5. Capacity for Differentiation and Integration The capacity for differentiation and integration involves the ability to distinguish self from other, fantasy from reality, internal objects and representations from external objects and circumstances, and present from past and future, and to delineate connections between these elements without confounding them. It includes the ability to construct and maintain a differentiated, consolidated, realistic, coherent, integrated, and nuanced representation of self (identity) and other people, and to connect these internalized representations in a way that facilitates adaptation and functioning, especially in relationships. A core feature of this capacity is the ability to construct and maintain a realistic and coherent identity. This domain implies a sense of personal continuity over time and includes both the conscious sense of individuality and unconscious strivings for continuity of self-­experience. High levels of this capacity imply that a person can organize and manage complex role demands and affects, and typically results in openness to novel experience and the ability to shift flexibly or adapt to a complex, ambiguous environment. Low levels of functioning imply constriction and oversimplification of experience, or confusion between self and others, fantasy and reality, and past, present, and future. Identity disturbance may involve a painful sense of incoherence, striking inconsistencies between beliefs and behaviors, overidentification with groups or roles, and, to a lesser extent, difficulties with commitment to personal values and goals. Some individuals have some capacity for differentiation and individuation, but the quality of their representations results in identities that create recurrent conflict and limit adaptation. Erikson (1968) postulated the concept of “negative identity,” in which one’s unique sense of differentiation involves excessive levels of social distrust, dichotomous beliefs, and cynicism, resulting in disruptions in self-­regulation and in relationships.

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 721
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 721 Rating Scale 5. Individuals at this level can appreciate the separateness and relatedness of different affect states, motives, and wishes of self and others, even when these are nuanced and ambiguous, and can organize experience over time and across contexts that c...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 721 Rating Scale 5. Individuals at this level can appreciate the separateness and relatedness of different affect states, motives, and wishes of self and others, even when these are nuanced and ambiguous, and can organize experience over time and across contexts that contain contrasting role demands (e.g., relating to a spouse vs. a parent). They can interact with an increasingly complex and demanding environment while maintaining a coherent and integrated self-­experience and identity, including personal values, interests, and ideals that fit their interpersonal, social, and cultural background. Perceptions and descriptions of significant others are differentiated, realistic, and cognizant of complexity. 3. Individuals at this level can differentiate and integrate experience, but with some constriction and oversimplification, especially when under stress. Strong emotions or wishes, both general and context-­specific, can lead to the temporary fragmentation or polarization (splitting) of internal experience. In some individuals, an apparently integrated but polarized representation of the self is accompanied by difficulties in developing an integrated view of significant others (e.g., in narcissistic personalities); in others, there may be a lack of integration of their self-­experience, yet a subtle representation of significant others (e.g., in schizoid personalities). Capacities for differentiation and integration may be more adequate in relationships and contexts that are more superficial and less intimate. 1. At this level, internal experience is either fragmented or rigidly compartmentalized and oversimplified most of the time. In extreme cases, it may be detached from external context, and self and other may be confused, with extremely rigid, polarized thinking (e.g., extremely positive or extremely negative). Individuals may have a severe sense of internal emptiness that they may attempt to escape through dysfunctional behaviors and defenses. There may be a lack of internalized personal values or chaotic and contradictory attitudes toward such values. Descriptions and experiences of self and others are confused, incongruent, and unstable, with concomitant impairment in reality testing. 6. Capacity to Regulate Self‐Esteem This capacity involves one’s overall sense of self-worth. It includes a comprehensive, nuanced appraisal of one’s positive and negatives attributes. Rosenberg (1989, p. 31) notes that good self-­esteem regulation “expresses the feeling that one is ‘good enough.’ The individual simply feels that he is a person of worth. . . . He does not necessarily consider himself superior to others.” This domain includes the degree to which a person has a sense of internal control, self-­efficacy, and self-­compassion even in instances of perceived failure, inadequacy, or distress—­in other words, people high in this capacity have a nonjudgmental, compassionate sensitivity toward their own pain and see suffering as a shared human experience. Self-­esteem comes originally via relationships with primary caregivers. Infants internalize experiences of well-being, self-­respect, vitality, and realistic self-­esteem when parents’ affectively attuned interactions cause their baby to feel lovable, with a meaningful life that is valued by others. When caregivers are abusive or negligent, their children may feel deeply rejected, unworthy, insufficient, and unlovable. Optimal regulation in this capacity involves balance, with self-­esteem that is neither unrealistically high nor unrealistically low, and self-­confidence that varies

chunks.heading: 722 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 722 Adulthood appropriately from one situation to another. Individuals with good self-­esteem regulation can balance their desire for approval with the demands of reality and the needs and rights of others. Those who are low in this capacity may suffer severe fluctuations in self-­esteem. Some indiv...
chunks.text: 722 Adulthood appropriately from one situation to another. Individuals with good self-­esteem regulation can balance their desire for approval with the demands of reality and the needs and rights of others. Those who are low in this capacity may suffer severe fluctuations in self-­esteem. Some individuals show primarily a pattern of exaggerated self-­importance, self-­centeredness, entitlement, and arrogance. Others feel mostly inadequacy, failure, shame, and hypersensitivity to others’ evaluations. Conscious self-­approval sometimes defends against unconscious negative feelings; for example, a grandiose self-image may disguise excessively critical unconscious evaluations. Conversely, conscious negative self-­appraisals may mask less conscious grandiose expectations. Difficulties in self-­esteem, sometimes framed as falling short of one’s ego ideal, may generate envy and a desperate need for others’ admiration. They may appear as excessive self-­criticism, exaggerated emphasis on one’s negative qualities, and a driving preoccupation with visible achievements. Inflated self-­esteem with ego-­syntonic aggression and suspiciousness (e.g., Kernberg’s [1984] malignant narcissism) is an extreme version of impaired functioning in this capacity; with patients who show this pattern, clinicians should also carefully evaluate the capacity to construct and use internal standards and ideals. Rating Scale 5. Individuals at this level are able to maintain a stable, realistic sense of wellbeing, self-­confidence, and vitality in varying contexts, even when under stress or in conflictual situations. Their self-­confidence is appropriate to internal and external reality. They show a balanced evaluation of themselves, with nuanced and flexible appreciation of both positive and negative elements. They react to perceived failures, instances of inadequacy, and personal suffering with kindness and nonjudgmental acceptance of self and others. 3. At this level, the person’s sense of well-being, confidence, vitality, and self-­ esteem is generally adequate but may be disrupted by strong emotions and stressful situations. Self-­esteem and confidence may be compromised by feelings of envy, entitlement, rage, and a tendency to blame one’s own shortcomings or failures on others, or on circumstances; or it may be diminished by shame, vulnerability, and a sense of failure and inadequacy. Individuals at this level may experience notable oscillations in their overall sense of personal value, self-­acceptance, and self-­confidence, with self-­critical attitudes or harsh judgments that serve in part to restore their fragile self-­esteem. 1. Individuals at this level may show a pervasive self-­centeredness, excessive and unrealistic ambitions, and certainty of being superior, special, or envied, often accompanied by hatred and envy toward others. Or they may have intense feelings of inadequacy, unworthiness, and shame. Their self-­esteem is either unrealistically low or irrationally inflated and may oscillate between these poles. Severe feelings of self-­depletion and emptiness may be apparent. There may be discrepancies between internal experience and external behavior (e.g., overcompensations for underlying feelings of vulnerability), and between implicit and explicit self-­esteem; both dynamics are associated with diminished adaptation. Constant blaming and devaluing of others, as well as harsh self-­scrutiny and overly critical evaluations of one’s own behavior, lead to a pervasive inability to derive satisfaction from personal accomplishments. The individual handles perceived failures or criticisms with hostility and rage toward others or ruthless self-­denigration.

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 723
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 723 7. Capacity for Trust, Empathy, and Intimacy This capacity reflects key relevant areas in relating adaptively to others. First, it involves the depth, range, and consistency (i.e., stability) of one’s interpersonal relationships. Healthy relatedness reflects not on...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 723 7. Capacity for Trust, Empathy, and Intimacy This capacity reflects key relevant areas in relating adaptively to others. First, it involves the depth, range, and consistency (i.e., stability) of one’s interpersonal relationships. Healthy relatedness reflects not only the degree to which an individual has stable, mutually satisfying relationships but also the quality of internalized object relations—­ mental representations of self, others, and self–other interactions. A good capacity for intimacy is found in relationships that are mature, affectively positive, and capable of evolving over time. Impairment in this domain is evidenced by relationships that are primitive (i.e., lacking in nuance), negatively toned, and rigid (i.e., incapable of changing in response to new experiences). Basic trust allows us to explore our environment and face situations of threat or conflict with a balance between vigilance and credulity appropriate to the interpersonal context. Individuals with a foundation in trust can rely on significant others to meet their need without harming them. Interpersonal trust implies accepting risk and vulnerability based upon positive expectations of others’ intentions or behaviors. Clinicians rating this capacity should consider the person’s ability to trust in the authenticity and relevance of interpersonally transmitted information—­that is, the concept of epistemic trust (Fonagy & Allison, 2014; Fonagy, Luyten, Allison, & Campbell, 2017b). Epistemic trust is influenced by developmentally based expectations about the competence and benevolence of others. It assumes that the knowledge they convey is significant, relevant to the self, and generalizable, and it includes implicit expectations that others will convey information consistent with one’s own’s mental states. Another component of this capacity is “empathy,” the cognitive and affective ability to understand and respond to others’ emotions. Cognitive empathy is the capability to take the others’ perspective, inferring both cognitive and emotional content. Affective empathy includes understanding others’ feelings affectively and responding to their emotional states in sensitive, prosocial ways. “Intimacy” denotes a person’s ability to adjust interpersonal distance and closeness responsively, based on each relationship and consistent with cultural expectations. When we function well in this area, our relationships are both stable and spontaneous, allowing us to adjust interpersonal bonds flexibly yet reliably, in accordance with inter- and intrapersonal circumstances. Individuals with adaptive functioning in this area are capable of reciprocity and mutuality, particularly in long-term relationships. They can accept support from others and provide support to them as situations demand. This capacity includes sexuality: awareness of one’s desires and emotions, freedom to engage in pleasurable sexual fantasies and activities, and ability to combine sexuality and emotional intimacy. Depth of relatedness varies across relationships. Adaptive functioning in adulthood involves navigating and facilitating connections that differ in degree of intimacy (e.g., casual acquaintances, professional contacts, close friendships, romantic relationships). Despite individual differences in desires and needs, adaptive intimate functioning typically entails depth and stability in core relationships, cultivation of relational networks of varying levels of closeness, and adaptive interpersonal responsiveness, including limit setting. Clinicians evaluating this capacity should consider how individuals’ trust, empathy, and intimacy contribute to their overall interpersonal functioning. When patients may function somewhat differently in these three domains, clinicians should prioritize the most relevant and representative dimension(s) of this category.

chunks.heading: 724 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 724 Adulthood Rating Scale 5. Individuals at this level have a deep, emotionally rich capacity for intimacy, caring, and empathy, even when their feelings are intense or when they are under stress. They can adjust to changing social-­emotional demands as circumstances evolve, provide support to othe...
chunks.text: 724 Adulthood Rating Scale 5. Individuals at this level have a deep, emotionally rich capacity for intimacy, caring, and empathy, even when their feelings are intense or when they are under stress. They can adjust to changing social-­emotional demands as circumstances evolve, provide support to others, and accept support as needed. They are selectively and appropriately trusting in the interpersonal context and can adapt to different levels of interpersonal distance and closeness. They can both extend and withhold trust. Their ability to experience feelings of trust, empathy, and intimacy makes their relationships mutually satisfying, a source of pleasure, comfort, nurture, and security. 3. At this level, the person has a capacity for intimacy, caring, and empathy that may be disrupted by strong emotions such as anger, shame, or separation anxiety. Under stress, the person may withdraw, act out, or become overly clingy and dependent. Individuals at this level find it difficult to adapt to some situations because of deficits in either the ability to accept support from others or the ability to provide it. There may be insufficient emotional and/or cognitive responsiveness to others’ experiences, excessive emotional contagion (sensitivity to other people’s feelings may become overwhelming), or moderate mistrust or lack of discrimination in receiving and using social knowledge. Problems in feeling trust, empathy, and intimacy may compromise relationships, which, in stressful circumstances, become confusing, painful for self and other, too distant, or too adhesive. 1. Individuals at this level appear superficial, self-­focused, and needs-­oriented, lacking in intimacy and empathy. They are indifferent to the needs of others, and appear aloof, withdrawn, socially isolated, and detached. Interactions tend to be unbalanced and lacking in mutuality, characterized by marked deficits in the ability to support others or accept support from them. Affective and cognitive empathy may be absent, entirely self-­oriented, and/or intended to deceive and manipulate others for self-­benefit. Some individuals with severe deficits in this capacity are deeply mistrustful, treating any source of information as unreliable or ill-­intentioned and seeking to avoid being influenced by others; some are overly credulous, with a pronounced lack of vigilance and discrimination, signaling a lack of clarity about their own position and putting themselves at risk of exploitation. 8. Capacity for Impulse Regulation This capacity involves the ability to modulate and regulate impulses and express needs, motives, and urges in flexible, adaptive, culturally appropriate ways. Deficiencies in this capacity appear as unmodulated impulsivity or rigid overcontrol of impulses, with concomitant affective constriction. “Impulsivity” has been defined variously as affective lability or instability (e.g., difficulties in anger management), the tendency to behave rashly when distressed or emotionally overwhelmed, difficulty in premeditation and planning (failure to think about consequences of behavior before acting), lack of perseverance (failure to persist in tasks or obligations), and difficulty tolerating delay of gratification. These features may be accompanied by self-­destructive actions or impulsive aggression toward others, often related to an attempt to express, externalize, or control anger, anxiety, and psychic pain that cannot be expressed verbally. Overcontrol of impulses can include rigid restraint of affects, denial of aggressive urges and feelings, inflexibility, or a harsh need to control self and others.

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 725
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 725 Diverse personality styles and disorders are notable for compromises of this capacity. They range from borderline and histrionic pathology at the uncontrolled/dysregulated end of the spectrum to obsessive traits and obsessive–­compulsive personality disorder at the...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 725 Diverse personality styles and disorders are notable for compromises of this capacity. They range from borderline and histrionic pathology at the uncontrolled/dysregulated end of the spectrum to obsessive traits and obsessive–­compulsive personality disorder at the overcontrolled end. Some clinical disorders (e.g., eating pathologies, addictive disorders, problems with explosivity) are also rooted deficits in this capacity. Inhibitory control is a function of the orbitoprefrontal region, primarily in the right hemisphere of the brain. The emotion-­generating and -processing “right brain” develops within the crucible of the primary caregiver’s moment-­by-­moment affectively attuned interactions with the infant. Early encoding of arousal modulation by the baby’s caregiver becomes the foundation for the adult’s capacity to regulate impulses. Trauma, neglect, or inability to form a stable infant–­caregiver bond may lead to difficulties in self-­concept, attachment, and development of inner working models, impairing the ability to function in this domain. Rating Scale 5. At this level, the person can manage and express impulses appropriately, even under stress, in a manner congruent with the situation at hand and the cultural milieu. Impulse control is flexible and effective; urges and affects are expressed in a modulated and adaptive manner, in a way that strengthens relationships. When needed, individuals at this level are able to give up or postpone the expression of an underlying urge or impulse, showing a good capacity for effortful control. 3. At this level, the person is somewhat able to control and regulate impulses but may encounter difficulties in certain affect- and conflict-­laden situations (e.g., with a romantic partner, a supervisor at work, or family members), or when experiencing some emotional states, such as anger, sadness, or fear of rejection. Across many situations, a pattern of overcontrol (rigidity) or undercontrol (dysregulation) is evident. 1. The person is unable to control or regulate impulses appropriately, leading to profound difficulties in social, sexual, and work relationships. Impulse control is so weak that the person is incapable of modulating anger and other strong affects, or it may be so rigid that impulses remain almost completely unexpressed. Individuals with severe difficulties in this area may oscillate between undercontrol and overcontrol of needs and impulses (e.g., needs and desires are disavowed and permitted virtually no expression in most situations but are then expressed impulsively, with little regard for consequences). In severe cases, the person may act without any regard for consequences and engage in destructive behaviors (e.g., suicidal attempts, physical aggression). 9. Defensive Functioning The capacity for defensive functioning involves the ways we attempt to manage anxiety coming from motives, affects, urges, wishes, memories, thoughts, internal conflicts, or external challenges. Individuals who function well in this area are able to use defenses effectively, integrating both emotional and cognitive components of a challenging experience with no distortion or minimal distortion in reality testing; those with worse defensive functioning use less adaptive defenses that include greater reality distortion. As extensively discussed in Chapter 12 on the P Axis (pp. 657–660, “defenses” are psychological responses to internal or external stressors and emotional conflicts.

chunks.heading: 726 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 726 Adulthood They protect us against excessive anxiety, manage conflictual wishes, affects, ideas, memories, and other inner experiences, and help us adapt to the external world. They occur partly or wholly out of awareness. People vary in the degree to which they have insight about their defenses,...
chunks.text: 726 Adulthood They protect us against excessive anxiety, manage conflictual wishes, affects, ideas, memories, and other inner experiences, and help us adapt to the external world. They occur partly or wholly out of awareness. People vary in the degree to which they have insight about their defenses, with more psychologically minded people having greater insight. Although individuals tend to use similar defenses to cope with different stressors, defenses may vary as a function of the motives or conflicts active at the time and as a function of the person’s mood, anxiety, or physical level. Under severe stress, even stable defensive functioning can deteriorate. Defenses differ in overall adaptiveness; they evolve during development from more primitive processes (e.g., splitting, denial, projective identification, withdrawal, somatization, acting out, extreme dissociation, omnipotent control) through neurotic-­ level processes (e.g., repression, reaction formation, rationalization, isolation of affect, reversal, intellectualization, displacement) to more mature adaptations (suppression, affiliation, anticipation, identification, humor, sublimation, altruism). Defenses can help the individual integrate distressing feelings and ideas, cope with emotional or cognitive aspects of stressful topics, alter representations of self and other, avoid being overwhelmed by painful experiences, and express intolerable aggressive feelings impulsively toward the self or the external world. More mature and adaptive defenses may overlap with conscious coping strategies. Rating Scale 5. Adaptive defenses predominate, along with some neurotic-­ level defenses, allowing the individual to experience a broad range of affects, to be aware of salient ideas and motives, and to interact with others and the world in adaptive and creative ways. When neurotic-­level defenses are used by someone functioning at this level, the person may deal with either the emotional or the cognitive elements of internal conflicts or external stressors, which can be handled one at a time. These defenses do not significantly impair functioning; the individual will typically revert to higher-­ order defenses when anxiety, conflict, and stress diminish. Adaptive defenses foster the awareness and integration of feelings, ideas, desires, and thoughts associated with an internal conflict or external stressful situation; they help the person in resolving the internal or external cause of distress. 3. Individuals at this level, when under stress, tend to see problems as external, and/or to minimize or ignore internal sources. As a result, they may distort reality to some degree, especially when feeling anxious or threatened. They may disavow aspects of internal experience through episodic recourse to denial, rationalization, or projection. They may mask negative experiences (e.g., powerlessness, disappointment, shame) by employing splitting-­based defenses, such as devaluing or idealizing self or others, or by maintaining an illusion of omnipotence. They may use some neurotic defenses but at times of greatest stress revert to using more immature processes. These defensive strategies may obscure awareness of both emotional and cognitive aspects of internal conflicts or external stressors. 1. The individual at this level is often overwhelmed, for no evident reason, by fears of dissolution or annihilation of the self, physical harm, or death. External reality may be grossly distorted, anxiety uncontained, and adaptation impaired. Common defenses include delusional projection, distortion, psychotic denial, fragmentation, concretization, and apathetic withdrawal. Individuals at this level of functioning

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 727
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 727 may deal with internal or external stressors by blindly refusing to acknowledge some aspects of their internal experience and of external reality (e.g., a physical object, a life event), or treat an element of their inner life as part of external reality. These str...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 727 may deal with internal or external stressors by blindly refusing to acknowledge some aspects of their internal experience and of external reality (e.g., a physical object, a life event), or treat an element of their inner life as part of external reality. These strategies may either repel others or induce them to take control to help the individual contain affect and manage anxiety. At times of low conflict and minimal stress, individuals at this level may employ splitting-­based defenses that fall short of full impairment in reality testing, but they ultimately revert to projective identification and lower-level defenses when conflict and stress increase. 10. Capacity for Adaptation and Resiliency This capacity involves the ability to adjust to unexpected events and changing circumstances, and to cope effectively and creatively when confronted with uncertainty, loss, stress, and challenge. “Adaptation” denotes the ability to cope with changing and uncertain circumstances, whereas “resiliency” refers to the ability to thrive when faced with adverse (even traumatic) events, and to self-­ regulate dynamically and appropriately under changing and challenging circumstances. Both adaptation and resiliency inform how we respond to and cope with circumstances that are beyond our immediate control. Because positive mental health is more than the absence of symptoms and/or disorders, this capacity can also be defined as a state of subjective well-being in which individuals may realize their own abilities, effectively cope with stressful and conflictual situations, use their talents, abilities, and energy productively and fruitfully, and make a contribution to their community. They take pleasure in accomplishing things that have personal relevance. Clinicians should keep in mind social and cultural differences in adaptation and resiliency when evaluating this capacity. Clinicians rating this capacity should note that the ability to adapt does not reflect uncritical or conformist adaptation to social expectations, but instead may involve unusual and creative ways of dealing with challenges. Similarly, acceptance of circumstances beyond one’s control does not equate with “giving up.” For example, patients diagnosed with terminal illness may be able to accept the reality of the diagnosis while simultaneously focusing on what they can control (e.g., strengthening family connections; enjoying what is still possible, exemplifying both hope and realistic resignation in the face of challenge). This capacity may include individuals’ domain-­specific strengths, such as sensitivity to others’ needs and feelings, and the ability to recognize alternative viewpoints and to be appropriately assertive. An individual with optimal adaptation and resiliency can transcend obstacles and turn setbacks into opportunities for growth and positive change. Adaptation to a complex social-­emotional environment and accommodation to one’s dynamically shifting internal states are based in part on unconscious affective and conscious cognitive processes. They include the capacity to manage a wide range of emotional experiences and situations that are novel, inherently ambiguous, and/or fluid in nature. Rating Scale 5. High levels of resiliency and adaptation imply the ability to experience a personal sense of well-being, manage positive and negative affect states, and use one’s

chunks.heading: 728 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 728 Adulthood talents and strengths effectively, even in the face of difficult situations. When this occurs, painful and stressful circumstances do not hinder adaptive responses, which may impel individuals to experience intense feelings of sadness or anxiety without resorting to reactive, performat...
chunks.text: 728 Adulthood talents and strengths effectively, even in the face of difficult situations. When this occurs, painful and stressful circumstances do not hinder adaptive responses, which may impel individuals to experience intense feelings of sadness or anxiety without resorting to reactive, performative, or desperate reactions. Individuals functioning at this level can adapt to complex social-­emotional contexts and novel situations, and can cope with changing, uncertain, or even frightening circumstances without losing clarity of thought or compromising interpersonal functioning. They can assimilate, use, and benefit from information that is emotionally threatening (i.e., that challenges cherished beliefs, perceptions, and self-­perceptions). 3. Individuals at this level may be generally able to experience a personal sense of well-being and are somewhat able to adapt to complex social-­emotional contexts and novel situations, but when under stress, they experience some impairment in thought processes and interpersonal functioning. When confronted with uncertainty, loss, stress, challenges, and painful experiences, there may be a notable decline in their overall functioning, with moderate impairments in their ability to use their talents and psychological strengths effectively, come to terms with threatening situations and information, recognize alternative viewpoints, or find satisfaction in life. These individuals have difficulty accepting that challenges, adversity, and uncertain events are intrinsic aspects of life; as a result, their moods and attitudes may be defiant, pessimistic, passive, or angry. 1. Individuals functioning at this level are unable to manage complex and uncertain social-­emotional situations. They experience uncontrolled, vacillating emotional states that significantly impair both their cognition and their interpersonal functioning. They lack a subjective sense of well-being, even in the absence of stressful and conflictual situations; instead, they report a prevalence of unpleasant and negative emotions, along with feelings of hopelessness, emptiness, and internal disorganization. There are severe impairments in their ability to cope with unexpected, challenging, and painful circumstances, and an absence of the capacity to process and gain benefit from them. 11. Capacity to Construct and Use Internal Standards and Ideals This capacity involves one’s personal sense of morality, including moral and ethical beliefs, and associated emotions and motives. The capacity to formulate internal values and ideals reflects a consideration of self in the context of one’s culture and the ability to make mindful decisions based on a set of coherent, internally consistent underlying moral principles. This capacity is related theoretically to the psychoanalytic construct of the superego, the mental agency concerned with internalized norms and values that confers self-­approval and disapproval based on whether we approximate our ego ideal. It involves numerous related processes: internalization of a set of values that serve as standards for behavior, use of these values to regulate actions and expressions, and the capacity to monitor behaviors and expressions to detect discrepancies between one’s actions and values. Such discrepancies may produce feelings of guilt and/or shame. Ideally, however, these emotions are experienced at levels that promote reflection and serve to make future actions more consistent with one’s values. Thus, in these cases, feelings of guilt and shame may be adaptive. Emotional interactions with caregivers influence the internalization of ego ideals and consequently the development of moral competencies, ethical actions, and empathy. Internal standards may arise from early shame experiences, but when shame

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 729
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 729 turns into humiliation because of a caregiver’s lack of attunement or inability to repair disruptions, a child cannot easily construct flexible, integrated, and realistic internal standards.
