Neuroscientific studies, including research on mirror neurons, have demons...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 729 turns into humiliation because of a caregiver’s lack of attunement or inability to repair disruptions, a child cannot easily construct flexible, integrated, and realistic internal standards. Neuroscientific studies, including research on mirror neurons, have demonstrated the importance of early relational experiences to moral development. The neural network underlying moral decisions appears to overlap with the network representing others’ intentions (i.e., theory of mind), as well as the network representing others’ vicariously experienced cognitive and emotional states. Standards and ideals are internalized early in life through interactions and identifications with caregivers. They are also later elaborated and refined in interactions with peers and role models (e.g., teachers, mentors), and through immersion in the values, norms, and expectations of one’s culture. Clinicians evaluating this capacity should consider the flexibility with which individuals interpret their own and others’ behaviors. High functioning requires that an individual’s moral reasoning be based on not only a set of cohesive principles but also awareness of the impact of one’s decisions on others. Low functioning may be indicated by an overly conscientious effort to adhere to unreasonably strict or concrete norms, or by the absence of an inner-­directed morality, with lack of remorse or guilt over harm to others. Rating Scale 5. At this level, the individual’s internal standards seem flexible and integrated with a realistic sense of values, ideals, and norms. Internal moral standards support meaningful striving, feelings of authenticity and self-­esteem, and membership in a culture. Feelings of remorse, guilt, and shame are tempered by self-­compassion and a tolerance of one’s human defects and limits, and so are used as signals for reappraising behavior. The self and others are viewed with a balance of compassion, tolerance, and constructive criticism. 3. At this level, internal standards may be rigid and concrete. Or the person’s ideals may appear variable and inconsistent across contexts, driven more by immediate needs, desires, and feelings than by a coherent set of moral guidelines. In some individuals, internal standards are not fully integrated with a realistic sense of values, ideals, and norms rooted in interpersonal, social, and cultural contexts. Their moral behavior may oscillate, especially in stressful and conflictual situations, and they may experience guilt, shame, and remorse more as paralyzing self-­criticism than as signals for reappraising their behavior. Or they may deny such feelings, thinking and acting from a primarily egocentric, self-­focused stance that prioritizes power, self-­protection, and personal gain. 1. At this level, internal standards and ideals seem either overly rigid or absent. Moral concerns have either a tyrannical or an insignificant or role in organizing self-­ experience and interpreting the behavior of self and others. Individuals at this level lack a realistic sense of their values, which appear unintegrated or grossly dissonant with their social and cultural milieu. Any feelings of guilt and remorse are not offset by objective evaluation; they often view self and others as irredeemably bad or totally good. Those with excessively harsh standards may feel unable to pursue goals, take risks, engage in unfamiliar activities, or build relationships of depth. They may expect criticism or rejection and be certain of being disliked, shamed or ridiculed. Or they may have an aggressive certainty about their moral superiority. Individuals who lack inner norms are oriented toward seeking power for their own advantage. They show a

chunks.heading: 730 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 730 Adulthood striking absence of empathy or remorse, may glibly normalize destructive actions, and may take pleasure in duping and manipulating others. 12. Capacity to Explore One’s Inner Life This capacity involves one’s ability to understand mental states and processes. It includes grasping impli...
chunks.text: 730 Adulthood striking absence of empathy or remorse, may glibly normalize destructive actions, and may take pleasure in duping and manipulating others. 12. Capacity to Explore One’s Inner Life This capacity involves one’s ability to understand mental states and processes. It includes grasping implicit connections between emotions, motives, thoughts, and behavior—­both past and present—­including one’s interpretations of and relations with other people. It implies both specific insights (i.e., exploring and understanding the dynamics underlying particular thoughts, feelings, choices, interpersonal patterns, and behavioral reactions), and a more general “insightfulness,” a capacity for self-­ understanding. The inclination to explore our inner lives reflects the degree to which we are motivated to introspect more deeply. It includes “psychological mindedness,” a term that denotes our ability and proclivity to observe our subjective worlds mindfully and realistically, and to infer accurately the needs and motives of others. It involves discerning patterns among these concepts to make sense of inner experience and using this information effectively in relationships. These domains play a vital role in organizing emotional experiences. They enable us to pause and reflect, even when an experience is intense or painful. This mental function is not a purely intellectual capacity. It has a key emotional dimension (i.e., feelings, curiosity, motives, desires, thoughts) and the connections between them may be explored and acknowledged but at the same time experienced affectively (see also the concept of “observing ego”). Clinicians can begin to evaluate this capacity on the basis of relevant therapeutic phenomena. Exploring and mapping individuals’ implicit associative networks (i.e., free associations) may help one to understand how they think, fantasize, feel and/or act under various internal and external circumstances. Dreaming is another element of this capacity. Manifest dreams, as well as the individual’s ability to be aware of and reflect on dream material, are a source of relevant information, because they express fears, preoccupations, conflicts, anxieties, wishes, and conceptions of the self and the world—that is, the individual’s inner life. Rating Scale 5. Individuals at this level are able to observe and understand themselves, and to reflect on the full range of their own and others’ experiences and feelings (including implicit connections between emotions, motives, thoughts, and behavior), even under stress. They understand psychological constructs. In this range, a person can reflect on present and past experiences, with a long-term view of self, and can appreciate complex patterns and relationships among feelings, thoughts, and behaviors and the dynamics underlying them. Such individuals are generally open to sharing and reflecting upon fantasies and dreams, which often include discernible narrative, metaphorical, and symbolic elements. 3. At this level, individuals seem somewhat able to recognize and reflect on meanings that underlie observable and conscious thoughts, choices, interpersonal patterns, and actions. They have some insight into their own motives and intentions, and can make some links between their inner world and external reality. Under stress, however, these abilities may fluctuate or deteriorate, with a notable decline in their capacity to reflect upon and understand themselves. The contents of their dreams are often “concrete,” with a reduced capacity for symbolic elaboration; in some cases, their

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 731
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 731 dream life may be confused with external reality, especially if linked to traumatic, stressful, or painful experiences. 1. Individuals at this level seem unwilling or unable to reflect on relationships among feelings, thoughts, and actions, either present or past....
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 731 dream life may be confused with external reality, especially if linked to traumatic, stressful, or painful experiences. 1. Individuals at this level seem unwilling or unable to reflect on relationships among feelings, thoughts, and actions, either present or past. They may not conceptualize the existence of an inner “reality.” Their capacity for insight is severely impaired or lacking. Even when called upon to do so, they cannot reflect on specific subjective experiences or affect states, and in the face of intense affect, they may use primitive defenses, act out impulsively, or dissociate. Their dream life is characterized by a pervasive dream–­reality confusion, and its content (when verbalizable) can be bizarre, concrete, grossly violent, and preoccupied with death. The seem to have no ability for symbolic elaboration. 13. Capacity for Agency and Purpose The final capacity reflects the experience of mastering our own actions, feelings, and thoughts, and constructing a narrative that gives cohesion and meaning to personal choices. It implies the feeling of personal “authorship,” internal control, and self-­ efficacy at different levels of consciousness and awareness. Clinicians should consider both implicit and explicit manifestations of the sense of agency: The former refers to prereflective feelings of initiating and controlling one’s actions, whereas the latter denotes higher-­level, conscious attributions of agency (e.g., “that was me”). Explicit agency may include experiences of intending to act, choosing one particular action rather than another, and initiating the action. This domain includes a sense of individuation (i.e., separateness from others), concern for succeeding generations, capacity for psychological growth, and spirituality or internal systems of ideals (not necessarily traditionally religious) that imbue one’s life with direction and purpose. It involves the ability to think beyond immediate concerns and grasp the broader implications of one’s attitudes, beliefs, values, and behaviors. Individuals functioning at a high level of this capacity show a strong sense of internal control, self-­efficacy, direction, and purpose; a coherent personal philosophy that guides decisions; comfort with personal choices, including those with unexpected outcomes; openness to alternative viewpoints; and the ability to transcend immediate concerns and grasp “the big picture.” They are capable of curiosity, wonder, and freshness of perspective. Individuals with low functioning in this capacity may describe externally generated stimuli as self-­produced (i.e., illusory self-­agency) or, conversely, attribute self-­generated outcomes to an external cause (i.e., illusory other-­agency). They may see their own life as meaningless, as driven primarily by external forces. Some may find meaning through adherence to fundamentalist belief systems or cults. Rating Scale 5. Individuals at this level show an integrated, intrinsic sense of agency, self-­ efficacy, and control over inner and outer experiences, and an unwavering sense of purpose and meaning. They can look beyond the self and transcend immediate situational concerns. They value subjectivity and intersubjectivity, approaching new situations and relationships with an open, creative perspective. They may find a sense of personal meaning in belonging and contributing to a community (e.g., organization, neighborhood). They have integrated both pleasant and painful experiences from the past into a personal narrative that features growth and self-­actualization.

chunks.heading: 732 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 732 Adulthood 3. At this level, individuals generally show a sense of agency and self-­efficacy except when dealing with stressful, painful, or conflictual circumstances. They have some sense of purpose and meaning, along with periods of uncertainty and doubt. Looking outside the self requires effor...
chunks.text: 732 Adulthood 3. At this level, individuals generally show a sense of agency and self-­efficacy except when dealing with stressful, painful, or conflictual circumstances. They have some sense of purpose and meaning, along with periods of uncertainty and doubt. Looking outside the self requires effort for them. They may have some sense of connectedness with other people, broader communities, and the world, despite limitations in experiencing a deep sense of belonging. They grasp the broader implications of attitudes and beliefs, and can accept alternative perspectives in certain conflict-­free domains. 1. Individuals at this level show a deficient sense of agency and self-­efficacy, along with lack of direction, aimlessness, and little or no sense of purpose. They appear to experience the past as a series of disconnected events and have difficulty developing a coherent and meaningful account of their life story. In the most severe cases, functioning is dominated by the sense that internally generated thoughts and actions are controlled by external events. Some may find some sense of purpose by adhering to external sources of control. When asked, such individuals are unable to articulate a personal philosophy or set of life goals. Subjectivity and intersubjectivity have little or no importance to them. Whether or not they are aware of their lack of direction, they experience pervasive isolation, meaninglessness, alienation, and anomie. Summary of Basic Mental Functioning We recommend that clinicians summarize basic mental functioning quantitatively (see Table 13.1a and 13.1b, pp. 739–740). Empirically Grounded Assessment of M‐Axis Capacities As mentioned in the introduction to this chapter, a growing body of evidence is available on assessment measures that, in addition to the Psychodiagnostic Charts–3 (see Chapter 21, pp. 1023–1031), can lead to a more comprehensive evaluation of these 13 components of mental functioning. In order to obtain reliable and valid information for a clinically useful diagnosis and case conceptualization, assessment of M-Axis capacities must yield practically applicable results with utility for treatment planning and implementation, usable by clinicians across different theoretical orientations. Even though we have opted for leaving the clinician or researcher to choose the most appropriate, validated, and clinically useful measure for the specific domain of mental functioning, in this last paragraph we provide a list of well-­validated clinical tools that may enhance clinicians’ use of the M-Axis for both clinical and research purposes. Some of these instruments (e.g., the Shedler–­Westen Assessment Procedure, described below) have also influenced the development of the PDM-based approach to personality and mental functioning. Assessment measures for the M-Axis capacities should be chosen also according to the point of view from which data are derived: (1) the clinician’s report; (2) the patient’s self-­report; (3) significant others’ reports of the patient; (4) reports by independent judges or observers of the patient; and (5) observations of the patients’ performance, often on unstructured or minimally structured tasks that are also described as “projective.” Both assessment validity and clinical utility are improved by using a

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 733
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 733 battery of assessment instruments from different points of view (i.e., a multi-­informant perspective). In general, in this paragraph we favor clinician-­rated and self-­report measures, but to obtain a more nuanced and detailed picture of a patient’s functioning i...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 733 battery of assessment instruments from different points of view (i.e., a multi-­informant perspective). In general, in this paragraph we favor clinician-­rated and self-­report measures, but to obtain a more nuanced and detailed picture of a patient’s functioning in each capacity, these empirically validated assessment instruments can and should be integrated with information obtained in other ways (e.g., clinical interview, life history records, reports from individuals who are familiar with the patient). To enhance the clinical utility of the assessment of M-Axis capacities, evaluators may use some of the following measures to tap the patient’s and the therapist’s contributions to the psychotherapeutic process. Shedler–Westen Assessment Procedure (SWAP) The Shedler–Westen Assessment Procedure-200 (SWAP-200; Westen & Shedler, 1999a, 1999b), (updated version SWAP-II; Westen, Waller, Shedler, & Blagov, 2014), is a well-­established, widely used psychometric procedure designed to provide a comprehensive assessment of personality functioning and psychological capacities. It comprises 200 statements, written in straightforward, experience-­near language. It can be used by raters of diverse theoretical orientations and experience levels. The instrument utilizes a Q-sort method, which requires the rater to sort items into eight categories, ranging from 0 (not descriptive) to 7 (most descriptive). The SWAP-200 scoring algorithms generate a dimensional score for each of the 10 personality disorders in Section II of the DSM-5 and DSM-5-TR (PD Scales), as well as for factor-­analytically derived traits for an alternative set of personality styles/pathologies that have been empirically identified (Q-­factors) (for a detailed description, see Westen & Shedler, 1999a, 1999b). Dimensional PD and Q-­Factor scores place each personality style on a continuum rather than construing them categorically as present–absent. Low scores indicate that the individual does not resemble or match the PD prototype/Q-­factor; high scores indicate that the person matches it well; intermediate scores indicate varying degrees of similarity. The SWAP-200 can also yield categorical diagnoses if desired or needed. It has been shown to have very good validity and reliability, with raters both trained and untrained in using the instrument. The 200 items of the SWAP, and the seven degrees of salience assigned to each, generate nearly innumerable permutations that capture of complexity of both personality and mental functioning. For example, it provides descriptors of positive mental health, represented in 24 of the 200 items (e.g., “Is able to use his/her talents, abilities, and energy effectively and productively”; “Enjoys challenges; takes pleasure in accomplishing things”; “Is able to assert him- or herself effectively and appropriately when necessary”; “Appears to have come to terms with painful experiences from the past; has found meaning in, and grown from such experiences”), and the resulting healthy/ high-­functioning score might be a valuable support in evaluating the M-Axis capacity for adaptation and resiliency. Further on, the SWAP Insight Scale (SIS; ­Lehmann & Hilsenroth, 2011) includes six SWAP-200 items to assess patients’ insight, thus offering a useful complement to evaluating the M-Axis capacity to explore one’s inner life (e.g., “Is capable of hearing information that is emotionally threatening [i.e., that challenges cherished beliefs, perceptions, and self-­perceptions] and can use and benefit from it”; “Has the capacity to recognize alternative viewpoints, even in matters that stir up strong feelings”; “Has little psychological insight into own motives, behavior, etc.”; “Is unable to consider alternate interpretations of his/her experiences [reverse]”; “Is psychologically insightful; is able to understand self and others in subtle and sophisticated ways”).

chunks.heading: 734 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 734 Adulthood Other SWAP items may be of interest for several M-Axis capacities, such as the capacity for affect regulation and expression (e.g., “Emotions tend to spiral out of control, leading to extremes of anxiety, sadness, rage, excitement, etc.”; “Tends to express affect appropriate in quality...
chunks.text: 734 Adulthood Other SWAP items may be of interest for several M-Axis capacities, such as the capacity for affect regulation and expression (e.g., “Emotions tend to spiral out of control, leading to extremes of anxiety, sadness, rage, excitement, etc.”; “Tends to express affect appropriate in quality and intensity to the situation at hand”), the capacity for mentalization (“Has difficulty making sense of other people’s behavior; often misunderstands, misinterprets, or is confused by others’ actions and reactions”); the capacity for trust, empathy, and intimacy (e.g., “Is capable of sustaining a meaningful love relationship characterized by genuine intimacy and caring”; “Is empathic; is sensitive and responsive to other people’s needs and feelings”), or the capacity for impulse regulation (“Tends to oscillate between undercontrol and overcontrol of needs and impulses; i.e., needs and wishes are expressed impulsively and with little regard for consequences, or else disavowed and permitted virtually no expression”). Adult Attachment Interview The Adult Attachment Interview (AAI; George, Kaplan, & Main, 1985) is a semistructured interview assessing attachment “states of mind.” Sharing a “state of mind” means narrating a history of internal working models (IWMs) not only in their conscious aspects but also in connection with deeper experiences not in full awareness. Central tasks include producing and reflecting on relationships in one’s attachment history, while maintaining a coherent, cooperative conversation with the interviewer. Relevant interview questions include “I’d like you to try to describe your relationship with your parents as a young child, if you could start from as far as you can remember”; “In general, how do you think your overall experiences with your parents have affected your adult personality?”; “Were there many changes in your relationship with your parents after childhood?”; “What is your relationship with your parents like for you now as an adult? Here, I am asking about your current relationship.” The coding system (Main, Goldwyn, & Hesse, 2002) includes scales germane to some M-Axis capacities, such as the capacity for differentiation and integration: Indices of “coherence of transcript” and “coherence of mind” assess the ability to organize thoughts and feelings about attachment experiences without resorting to splitting, idealization, and denial, and test the coherence of internal representations of childhood memories. The AAI is the “gold standard” in the study of the IWMs of attachment, which contain implicit rules for how one “lives in relationships.” The IWMs are “maps” (mental representations) of self and others that enable members of an attachment dyad (e.g., parent–­child, romantic couple) to foresee, interpret, and engage appropriately in mutual interactions. The AAI might therefore be a valuable tool for assessing the capacity for trust, empathy, and intimacy. Reflective Functioning Scale The Reflective Functioning Scale (RFS; Fonagy, Target, Steele, & Steele, 1998) is a quantified index of the ability to mentalize, applied to transcripts of the AAI. It is a central measure for the M-Axis capacity for mentalization, as well as, albeit to a lesser extent, for the capacity for affect regulation and expression; the capacity for trust, empathy, and intimacy; and the capacity for bodily experiences and representations. AAI questions are divided into two groups: (1) “permit questions,” which allow the speaker to demonstrate reflective capacities, and (2) “demand questions,” which require a reflective response. Answers are evaluated by whether they contain markers

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 735
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 735 of an appreciation of mental functioning. Statements must be specific (not generic), refer to identifiable mental states, involve an example that relates to the attachment, and refer to self–other interactions. Passages in the interview are scored on an 11-point sc...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 735 of an appreciation of mental functioning. Statements must be specific (not generic), refer to identifiable mental states, involve an example that relates to the attachment, and refer to self–other interactions. Passages in the interview are scored on an 11-point scale ranging from negative or antireflective RF (–1 = low RF) to full or exceptional RF (9 = high RF). The RFS can also be used to measure reflective functioning in parents, via the Parent Development Interview (PDI; Slade, 2005), a semistructured clinical interview examining parents’ representations of their children, of themselves as parents, and of their relationships with their children. Defense Mechanism Rating Scales The Defense Mechanism Rating Scales (DMRS; Perry, 1990) is an observer-­rated method to identify and assess (qualitatively and quantitatively) defensive patterns that occur in the clinical session (or other verbatim sources). The Defense Mechanisms Rating Scales–­Q -Sort (DMRS-Q; Di Giuseppe, Perry, Petraglia, Janzen, & Lingiardi, 2014), a computerized observer-­rated Q-sort method, and the 30-item Defense Mechanism Rating Scales Self-­Report-30 (DMRS-SR-30; Di Giuseppe et al., 2020; Prout, Di Giuseppe, Zilcha-­Mano, Perry, & Conversano, 2022), were developed based on the original DMRS. While the original DMRS is the “gold standard” assessment tool for defensive functioning, as it allows identification of each defense as it occurs, the DMRS-Q and DMRS-SR-30 provide similar scores with some methodological differences. All the three DMRS measures provide key information for the M-Axis defensive functioning capacity and are also useful in assessing level of personality organization (P Axis). Clinical and research literatures have since shown that defenses are relevant for understanding ego strengths, subjective reactions to stress, several mental health conditions, quality of relationships, and therapeutic process and outcome. Then, defensive functioning may be related to other M-Axis capacities, such as the capacity for differentiation and integration, the capacity to regulate self-­esteem, the capacity for impulse control and regulation, or the capacity for adaptation and resiliency. The DMRS defines 30 defenses, noting how to discriminate each from others that are similar. Some authors have suggested an additional psychotic level of defenses (level 0) (e.g., Berney, de Roten, Beretta, Kramer, & Despland, 2014; Constantinides & Beck, 2010). The DMRS model arranges defenses hierarchically in seven levels, in ascending order of maturity, adaptation, and health. These are: Level 1—Action defenses; Level 2—Major image-­distorting defenses; Level 3—Disavowal defenses and autistic fantasy; Level 4—Minor image-­distorting defenses; Level 5b—Other neurotic defenses and Level 5a—­Hysterical defenses; Level 6—Obsessional defenses; and Level 7—Highly adaptive defenses (previously called mature defenses). The DMRS and DMRS-based measures provide four quantitative scores (i.e., individual defense score, defense-­level score, defense category score, and overall defensive functioning [ODF]). For an additional qualitative description of an individual’s defensive profile, six psychotic defenses have been added, scored at level 0, making the ODF scale 0–7. Structured Interview of Personality Organization The Structured Interview of Personality Organization (STIPO; Clarkin, Caligor, Stern, & Kernberg, 2004; Stern et al., 2010) and its revision, the STIPO-R (Clarkin, Caligor,

chunks.heading: 736 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 736 Adulthood Stern, & Kernberg, 2019), operationalize the interview developed by Kernberg (1984) to evaluate the key structural domains of personality functioning. The current STIPOR comprises 55 questions that are used to assess the following domains, relevant for the related M-Axis capacities: (1...
chunks.text: 736 Adulthood Stern, & Kernberg, 2019), operationalize the interview developed by Kernberg (1984) to evaluate the key structural domains of personality functioning. The current STIPOR comprises 55 questions that are used to assess the following domains, relevant for the related M-Axis capacities: (1) identity (i.e., an integrated concept of the self and an integrated concept of significant others), (2) defenses, including both more mature defenses and more primitive defenses, such as splitting; (3) a capacity for a broad spectrum of affect dispositions that are complex and well-­modulated, (4) an integrated and mature system of internalized values, and (5) an appropriate management of sexual, dependent, and aggressive motivations that are experienced subjectively as needs, impulses, wishes, and fears. Furthermore, compared to the original STIPO, the STIPO-R has enhanced the items on narcissistic pathology into a full narcissism scale. The patient’s reply to each questions is scored on a 3-point Likert scale, from 0 (absence of pathology) to 3 (presence of pathology), with descriptive scoring anchors for each item. The rater makes an overall estimation for each domain on a 5-point scale from 1 (healthy) to 5 (pathological). Personality Assessment Inventory The Personality Assessment Inventory (PAI; Morey, 1996), a 344-item self-­report measure of psychopathology, was developed to provide measures of constructs that are central in treatment planning, implementation, and evaluation. Items are rated on a 4-point Likert scale (from false to very true). Twenty-­t wo non-­overlapping scales measure a broad range of psychological constructs, including four validity scales. Eleven are clinical scales that may be divided into three broad classes of disorders: those within the neurotic spectrum, those within the psychotic spectrum, and those associated with behavior disorder or impulse control problems (i.e., somatization, anxiety, anxiety-­related disorders, depression, mania, paranoia, schizophrenia, borderline features, antisocial features, alcohol use, and drug use). The Borderline Features scale (PAI-BOR) contains four subscales that tap relevant indices for some M-Axis capacities: affective instability (capacity for affect regulation and expression), identity problems (capacity for differentiation and integration), negative relationships (capacity for trust, empathy, and intimacy), and self-harm (capacity for impulse control and regulation; bodily experiences and representations). The Antisocial Features scale (PAI-ANT) includes three subscales that may be relevant for the M-Axis capacity to construct and use internal standards and ideals. The PAI also includes two interpersonal scales that provide valuable information about the M-Axis capacity for trust, empathy, and intimacy. Interpersonal style is assessed along two dimensions: a warmly affiliative versus a cold rejecting style, and a dominating/ controlling versus a meekly submissive style. Finally, five scales provide information about potential complications in treatment (i.e., aggression, suicidal ideation, stress, nonsupport, and treatment rejection). Rorschach Inkblot Test The Rorschach Inkblot Test involves the serial presentation of a set of 10 inkblot cards showing ambiguous and evocative stimuli. Persons being tested are seated beside the examiner and instructed to describe what they see in each card. Each response is scored after appropriate inquiry, according to features the subject views as relevant.

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 737
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 737 These main features are related to perceptual organization and integration (location, developmental quality, and integrative process); characteristics that are actually contained in the card (e.g., form, color, and shading); or characteristics that are subjectively...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 737 These main features are related to perceptual organization and integration (location, developmental quality, and integrative process); characteristics that are actually contained in the card (e.g., form, color, and shading); or characteristics that are subjectively added to it (e.g., perception of movement). Evaluations are also made of the perceptual adequacy of the responses given to the stimuli (form quality); contents of the representation (animal, human figure, anatomical perception, sexual aspects, etc.); and ideation (thought processes in giving the response). Reliable scoring criteria are provided for coding each separate aspect. The range of constructs measured by the Rorschach is wide and, given its long history, it is unsurprising that it has come to be seen in multiple ways—as a perceptual task, a problem-­solving task, an index of associational patterns, an interpersonal task, and a measure of personality dynamics. Multiple scoring and interpretation systems exist. The Comprehensive System (CS) developed by Exner (2003) is among the most widely used scoring and interpretation system; it has had a far-­reaching impact on the assessment of personality and psychopathology and has become a standard part of psychological test batteries (Bornstein, 2010). The Rorschach Performance Assessment System (RPAS; Meyer & Eblin, 2012) is another influential omnibus scoring and interpretation system. Despite the relevance of the Rorschach for assessing most of the 13 M-Axis capacities, we have opted not to include descriptions of its most relevant categories for each mental capacity, because differing individual scores, ratios, percentages, and other derivations are used in different Rorschach interpretations. The mental domain of greatest interest in a given assessment situation is determined by the patient’s characteristics, the purpose of the assessment, and the referral question. Optimal use of the Rorschach for M-Axis assessment often requires that scores be interpreted in the context of a patient’s performance on other tests. Measures on Psychotherapy Process (Working Alliance Inventory, Therapist Response Questionnaire, Psychotherapy Relationship Questionnaire) The following instruments have utility for determining how a patient is functioning with a particular therapist, the therapist’s contributions to the therapeutic process, and the functioning of the therapeutic dyad, which reflects the skill and relational capacity of the therapist in general, and with that individual patient in particular (Norcross & Lambert, 2019). Whereas DSM and ICD systems were not designed to evaluate the type of relationship that may be healing to a particular kind of client, the PDM-3 emphasizes interactive elements (i.e., the therapeutic alliance and transference–­ countertransference phenomena). A comprehensive assessment of M-Axis capacities can provide useful insight into the development of the therapeutic alliance—­one of the best predictors of treatment success—­and of transference–­countertransference phenomena, and can be applied to both patients’ and therapists’ functioning in psychotherapy (Lingiardi, Colli, & Muzi, 2018). The Working Alliance Inventory (WAI; Horvath & Greenberg, 1989) is a widely used, 36-item, self-­report questionnaire developed from Bordin’s (1979) pantheoretical conceptualization of the therapeutic alliance. It comprises three subscales: Bond (e.g., “I believe is genuinely concerned for my welfare,” “ and I trust one another”), Task (e.g., “ and I agree about the things I will need to do in therapy to help improve my situation”), and Goal (e.g., “The goals of these sessions are important for me”). Each item is rated on a 7-point Likert scale (ranging

chunks.heading: 738 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 738 Adulthood from 1 [never] to 7 [always]). Horvath and Greenberg developed three versions of the WAI: a client–­patient form (WAI-P), a therapist form (WAI-T), and an observer form (WAI-O). A short form, the WAI-SF (Tracey & Kokotovic, 1989), comprises 12 items and is available in two versions (cl...
chunks.text: 738 Adulthood from 1 [never] to 7 [always]). Horvath and Greenberg developed three versions of the WAI: a client–­patient form (WAI-P), a therapist form (WAI-T), and an observer form (WAI-O). A short form, the WAI-SF (Tracey & Kokotovic, 1989), comprises 12 items and is available in two versions (client–­patient and therapist). The Therapist Response Questionnaire (TRQ; Betan, Heim, Zittel Conklin, & Westen, 2005; Zittel Conklin & Westen, 2003; see also Tanzilli, Colli, Del Corno, & Lingiardi, 2016) is a clinician-­report instrument that measures countertransference patterns (or therapist emotional responses) in psychotherapy. It consists of 79 items that describe a wide range of cognitive, affective, and behavioral responses of therapists toward patients, ranging from relatively specific feelings to complex constructs, such as projective identification. These statements are written without jargon, to ensure that clinicians of any theoretical orientation can use the instrument without bias. Clinicians assess each item on a 5-point Likert scale ranging from not true (1) to very true (5). The TRQ’s factor structure comprises eight emotional dimensions that are clinically sensitive and conceptually coherent: overwhelmed/disorganized (e.g., “I feel pushed to set very firm limits with him/her”), helpless/inadequate (e.g., “I think s/ he might do better with another therapist or in a different kind of therapy”), positive (e.g., “I feel pleased or satisfied after sessions with him/her”), special/overinvolved (e.g., “I find myself discussing him/her more with colleagues or supervisors than my other patients”), sexualized (e.g., “I feel sexual tension in the room”), disengaged (e.g., “My mind often wanders to things other than what s/he is talking about”), parental/ protective (e.g., “I wish I could give him/her what others never could”), and criticized/ mistreated (e.g., “I feel criticized by him/her”). The Psychotherapy Relationship Questionnaire (PRQ; Bradley, Heim, & Westen, 2005; see also Tanzilli, Colli, Gualco, & Lingiardi, 2018) is a clinician-­report instrument of 90 items that measure a wide range of thoughts, feelings, motives, conflicts, and behaviors expressed by patients toward their therapists. Similar to the TRQ, its items were written in everyday language. Clinicians assess each item on a 5-point Likert scale, ranging from 1 (not true) to 5 (very true). The PRQ’s factor structure comprises five transference patterns: angry/entitled (e.g., “Is provocative; tends to set up situations in which the therapist feels angry, attacked, or provoked”), anxious/preoccupied (e.g., “Feels, or fears, doing ‘something wrong’ in therapy”), secure/engaged (e.g., “Is attuned to the verbal and nonverbal meanings of the therapist’s communications; is adept at reading subtle social cues”), avoidant/counterdependent (e.g., “Tries hard not to be, or to feel, needy or dependent in therapy”), and sexualized (e.g., “Tries hard not to feel, or admit feeling, sexually attracted to the therapist”). Summary of Basic Mental Functioning: M Axis To obtain a rating of a patient’s overall mental functioning, a clinician should add up the 1- to 5-point ratings assigned to each capacity (Table 13.1a), yielding a single numerical index of overall function ranging from 13 to 65. This total permits provisionally assigning a patient to one of the categories outlined in Table 13.1b, which provides brief qualitative descriptions of seven levels of mental functioning. Schematically, healthy mental functioning corresponds to M1; neurotic to M2 and M3; borderline to M4, M5, and M6 (from high to low, from moderate impairments to significant defects); and psychotic to M7.

APPENDIX 13.1 Assessing Mental Functioning in Parenting C O N S U LTA N T Nicola Carone, PhD Caregiving is a unique context in which the M-Axis domains of mental functioning can influence daily practices and parent–­child interactions. While research and clinical work have addressed the influence on parenting of caregiver personalities (e.g., Steele, Townsend, & Grenyer, 2019) and mental health (e.g., Dix & Moed, 2019), much less attention has been devoted to parents’ mental functioning. Appendix 13.1 illustrates how M-Axis capacities can be assessed in relation to the parental role, while keeping in mind that the evaluation of caregivers’ mental functioning cannot be detached from their levels of personality organization, types of personality, and subjective experiences of symptoms. Where relevant, mental capacities are framed in terms of the child’s developmental level. Clinicians assessing parents with infants and young children should also refer to the Axis IV, “Relational patterns and disorders” of the Infancy and Early Childhood (IEC) Section (see Chapter 1, pp. 21–142). Parenting is the context in which caregivers’ psychological processes both express themselves and are transmitted, more or less implicitly, to the child, thereby influencing the child’s mental functioning. Irrespective of an individual’s parental status, we note that most M-Axis capacities (e.g., mentalization, bodily experiences and representations, regulation of self-­esteem, impulse regulation, defensive functioning, construction and use of internal standards and ideals) are rooted in early life, when dyadic interactions with caregivers are encoded and internalized.

chunks.heading: 746 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 746 Adulthood Cognitive and Affective Processes in Parenting 1. Parental capacity for attention and learning (executive functioning) is an internal mental function that precedes a parent’s regulation of interactions with a child. Parents with impaired cognitive self-­regulatory capacities may have d...
chunks.text: 746 Adulthood Cognitive and Affective Processes in Parenting 1. Parental capacity for attention and learning (executive functioning) is an internal mental function that precedes a parent’s regulation of interactions with a child. Parents with impaired cognitive self-­regulatory capacities may have difficulty controlling their own thoughts, actions, and behaviors. As a result, they may be subject to negative and/or controlling parenting practices. Executive functioning is a fundamental component of responsive and sensitive parenting. Parents need to learn to inhibit some responses, use their working memory skills, and flexibly move between tasks to create a positive environment and to engage in collaborative parent–­child interactions. Parents with poorer executive function may have difficulty inhibiting reactions such as yelling in response to the behavior of a frustrated child. Similarly, when engaging their child in a learning task, they may have to call upon their executive function to stay on task, remember the rules, and not get distracted in a demanding environment (Bridgett, Burt, Edwards, & Deater-­Deckard, 2015; Crandall, Deater-­ Deckard, & Riley, 2015). Parental executive functioning may affect how parents construct the home learning environment, including their engagement in activities specific to executive functioning with their children (e.g., playing with puzzles, game-like concentration tasks, memory games). Assessment of this capacity should therefore consider a parent’s ability to rely on inhibitory control, working memory, and attentional flexibility. Assessment may reveal deficits in working memory that are linked to greater negative and reactive parenting behaviors; parents with lower performance on executive functioning tasks are more likely to engage in harsh parenting (Deater-­Deckard & Bell, 2017; Deater-­Deckard, Wang, Chen, & Bell, 2012). Caregiver inhibitory control is associated positively with sensitive and responsive parenting behaviors (Shaffer & Obradovic, 2017) and negatively with negative parenting behaviors such as intrusiveness (Musser, Kaiser-­Laurent, & Ablow, 2012). 2. The capacity for affect regulation and expression is relevant to parenting especially when caregivers are distressed or angry (Zimmer-­G embeck, Rudolph, Kerin, & Bohadana-­ Brown, 2022). Parental affect regulation and expression facilitate sensitive responding, irrespective of the affective state of the child. Assessment should focus on the extent to which parents have a sense of control over their own affect and whether their affect-­management efforts are appropriate to goals and situational demands and are expressed in a regulated fashion during parent–­child interactions. Parents with greater difficulty in affect regulation and expression may be more hostile and less warm toward their children. They may engage more in negative caregiving practices and respond to their children with nonsupportive behaviors, such as minimizing or punishing their negative feelings (Crandall et al., 2015; Zimmer-­G embeck et al., 2022). Parents with better affect regulation are more likely to show positive parenting behaviors (Crandall et al., 2015; Zimmer-­G embeck et al., 2022). Because children’s affective regulation and expression develops through interaction with caregivers, a parent’s capacity for affect regulation and expression can transmit self-­regulation abilities to the next generation (Bridgett et al., 2015; Zimmer-­G embeck et al., 2022). 3. The capacity for mentalization has significant implications for parenting. Mentalization involves the parent’s ability to represent and hold in mind a child’s internal states, such as emotions, thoughts, desires, and intentions (Sharp & Fonagy, 2008). A parental attitude of taking seriously the child’s internal states fosters secure infant–­parent attachment (Zeegers, Colonnesi, Stams, & Meins, 2017). Children learn through parental

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 747
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 747 mirroring of their affect that they are recognized and that their emotional states can be contained by the parent (Fonagy & Target, 1997). Low parental mentalization can take either of two forms: (a) lack of awareness or disregard of the mental world of the child,...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 747 mirroring of their affect that they are recognized and that their emotional states can be contained by the parent (Fonagy & Target, 1997). Low parental mentalization can take either of two forms: (a) lack of awareness or disregard of the mental world of the child, or (b) inaccuracy in interpreting the child’s internal states. Assessment of this function involves the degree to which a parent shows frequent, coherent, appropriate mentalizing in relation to their child (for a meta-­analysis, see Zeegers et al., 2017). Assessment of parental mentalization typically focuses on the analysis of the frequency and content of mind-­related speech during an interview or parent–­child interaction (Meins & Fernyhough, 2015). It may involve asking parents to describe their child or the parent–­child relationship. Observational assessment focuses on how appropriately caregivers attribute internal states to their child during parent–­child interactions. Although these approaches assess parental mentalization under varying conditions and yield different measurements, they all characterize mentalization in terms of explicit verbal expressions, indexing processes that are accessible to awareness, introspection, and flexible control (Van Overwalle & Vanderkerckhove, 2013). Mentalization can also refer to processes that are implicit and inaccessible to awareness and control (also referred to as parental embodied mentalization; Shai & Belsky, 2017). For example, when a parent interacts with their child, the parent may not talk about what is going on in the child’s mind but may nonetheless act in ways that indicate an appreciation of the child’s mental state (e.g., turn-­taking, mirroring facial expressions). Explicit forms of parental mentalization are indexed by three concepts (for a detailed overview, see Zeegers et al., 2017)—parental mind-­mindedness, parental insightfulness, and parental reflective functioning. Mind-­mindedness, originally defined as a tendency to treat one’s child as an individual with a mind of their own, is operationalized in different ways depending on the child’s age. In infancy, mind-­mindedness is assessed in terms of the parents’ appropriate mind-­related versus nonattuned comments on their infant’s internal states during parent–­infant interaction. From preschool years onward, it is assessed by the extent to which the parent talks about mental and emotional characteristics when given an open-ended invitation to describe the child (Meins, 2013). Oppenheim and Koren-Karie (2013, p. 551) define parental insightfulness as the capacity “to ‘see things from the child’s point of view’ and the thought processes that can impede or derail this capacity.” The assessment of this capacity should therefore focus on a parent’s tendency to invoke the motives underlying the child’s behavior (i.e., the parent takes a mentalizing stance), to accept the mental states of the child, to hold an open-to-­ change attitude toward the child’s behaviors and mind, as well as a multidimensional and balanced view of the positive and negative features of the child (Oppenheim & KorenKarie, 2013). Parental reflective functioning involves a parent’s ability to reflect thoughtfully when talking about a child and the parent–­child relationship (Slade, Grienenberger, Bernbach, Levy, & Locker, 2005); it is considered to be at the heart of sensitive caregiving. During infancy and toddlerhood, parental reflective functioning is usually assessed using the Parent Development Interview (PDI; Aber, Slade, Berger, Bresgi, & Kaplan, 1985; Slade et al., 2005). This procedure can be used to assess parental reflective functioning in later childhood and adolescence as well. Level of reflective functioning can also be scored on the AAI (George et al., 1985), although PDI questions focus on current parenting of the child, whereas the AAI focuses on the childhood experiences. Therefore, parental reflective functioning as measured by the PDI and parental reflective functioning as measured by the AAI should be considered partly distinct constructs of a broader parental mentalizing capacity. Such a conceptualization has important implications for interventions.

chunks.heading: 748 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 748 Adulthood A more recently developed instrument is the Parental Reflective Functioning Questionnaire (PRFQ; Fonagy et al., 2016), which assesses mentalization in parents of children under age 3, a period during which children’s verbal communication skills are not fully developed. The PRFQ is an 1...
chunks.text: 748 Adulthood A more recently developed instrument is the Parental Reflective Functioning Questionnaire (PRFQ; Fonagy et al., 2016), which assesses mentalization in parents of children under age 3, a period during which children’s verbal communication skills are not fully developed. The PRFQ is an 18-item questionnaire comprised of questions on three scales: (1) prementalizing modes, capturing a possible lack of mentalizing (e.g., “When my child is fussy, she does that just to annoy me”); (2) certainty about the child’s mental states (“I always know why my child acts as he does”); and (3) interest and curiosity in the child’s mental states (e.g., “I am often curious to find out how my child feels”). Identity and Relationships in Parenting 4. The bodily experiences and representations capacity may play a key role in the postpartum and infancy periods, particularly for primary caregivers, when the parent needs to rapidly detect and respond to the infant’s physical needs and emotional states. This ability depends on a fine balance between awareness of bodily signals originating in the parent’s body and those belonging to the infant (Feldman, 2015; Lambert, 2012), which influences the parental function identified by Winnicott (1965) as “handling.” This ability relies on specific neural circuits, endocrine systems, and behavioral patterns that undergo significant reorganization with the birth of an infant (Numan & Young, 2016). Clinicians should assess caregivers’ capacities to detect changes in their own bodily states and to use them adaptively to support maturation of the child’s social skills, empathic abilities, body awareness, and sense of well-being. Closely linked to parents’ somatic experience is their internal representation of their own bodily milieu (interoception). The capacity to detect, understand, and empathize with an infant’s physical and affective states requires a sense of one’s own body. This is linked to the parent’s capacity for mentalization (especially in the embodied stance) and provides the foundation for both the parent’s sensitive caregiving and the infant’s lifelong ability to engage interoception in the service of emotion regulation, sensory modulation, and self-­ awareness (Bowlby, 1958; Fotopoulou & Tsakiris, 2017). Clinicians should assess the parent’s awareness of internal bodily signals: proprioception, heart rate, arousal, temperature, pain, air supply, energy level, and hunger and thirst signals. Because interoception plays a central role in a parent’s moment-­by-­moment adaptation to the environment, including the parent–­infant relationship, this is a key task. 5. The capacity for differentiation and integration may need to be assessed in circumstances in which parents look to a child to meet their own needs. Multiple terms have been suggested for this inverted parent–­child relationship, including parentification, role reversal, boundary dissolution, parent as child, parent as peer, and parent as spouse. They all denote a shift in roles such that the child performs either psychological and instrumental functions usually carried out by the parent for the child, or functions usually performed by another adult for the parent. In these situations, the capacity for differentiation and integration may be an index of a parent’s projections into the child or, more broadly, of scenarios in which the parent’s unconscious expectations and defenses are psychically transmitted to the child. Such transmission occurs mainly through nonverbal communication and is perpetuated by deep unconscious narcissistic alliances between desires of the parent and needs of the child; to preserve the bond, children in this predicament align their wishes and needs to those of the parent. When a parent has a low capacity for differentiation and integration, it is likely that a primary, authentic aspect related to the affective core of the child’s self cannot fully come

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 749
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 749 into existence and is lost in the sense of being undefined, suppressed, unrepresented, and split. Said differently, children suffering this role reversal attempt to hide their true feelings and conform to the parent’s needs and expectations. They develop what Winni...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 749 into existence and is lost in the sense of being undefined, suppressed, unrepresented, and split. Said differently, children suffering this role reversal attempt to hide their true feelings and conform to the parent’s needs and expectations. They develop what Winnicott (1962) called a “false self.” In response to the child’s exuberance, for example, the parent may become critical or controlling, or, conversely, may identify uncritically with the child’s grandiosity. Some parents seem unable to balance their response to the child, sometimes supporting the child’s omnipotence and at other times differentiating themselves and the child to experience their relationship in a more realistic manner. This phenomenon may be observed with “negative” emotions such as shame, sadness, and guilt, which may be handled with systematic blindness and be acted out in relationships. A mother, for example, may be narcissistically invested in denying negative affects within herself and may enact with the child what she cannot tolerate internally. She may transmit disavowed affects through projective identification, unmediated by words, conveying direct affect devoid of symbolic transformation. Under such circumstances, an assessment of parental defenses may be extremely informative (as detailed below). The specific ability to differentiate oneself from one’s child is germane to Bowen’s (1978) construct of “differentiation of self.” It indicates the capacity of a system and its members to manage emotional reactivity, act thoughtfully under stress, and allow for both intimacy and autonomy in relationships without fusion. Although differentiation of self has been defined as an individual (intrapsychic) trait-like (stable) construct, it is thought to be the result of emotional patterns in the family, both in the family of origin and across generations, with consequences for the family system’s patterns of interaction in general and parental functioning in particular. A properly “differentiated parent” (Bowen, 1978) is characterized by low emotional reactivity, ability to regulate affect, capacity to think and make decisions under stress, and ability to be at ease with one’s child while supporting the child’s autonomy. A less differentiated parent, by contrast, is more anxious and bases their parenting style on intrusiveness, control, and overprotection, often adopting critical attitudes and emotional distancing that undermine their children’s self-­esteem and confidence in their abilities. 6. The parent’s capacity to regulate self-­esteem is linked to emotional stability and, consequently, to the parent’s ability to handle stress effectively, discipline the child consistently, set clear and predictable boundaries, and demonstrate adaptive coping and affect regulation. Assessment of parents’ self-­esteem regulation should consider the extent to which parents rely on the child to satisfy their own needs and accomplish their own goals. As in the case of the capacity for differentiation and integration, clinicians should consider how the parent, more or less implicitly, pushes the child to realize excessive and unrealistic ambitions to compensate for the parent’s own feelings of inadequacy, unworthiness, and shame. Caregivers who can regulate their self-­esteem adequately are more likely to be warm and responsive with their children, to feel confident in their parenting abilities, to give consistent guidance and support, and to adapt their parenting practices as their children grow and their needs change. Those who regulate their self-­esteem inadequately, in contrast, may compensate for insecurity about their parenting skills by setting strict rules, making excessive demands, and being insufficiently responsive. This parenting style can lead to low self-­esteem, anxiety, and poor social skills in children. Or parents, in an attempt to be liked by their child, may become overly permissive and fail to set boundaries. Or they may become disengaged or uninvolved, providing minimal emotional support or guidance. Insofar as children mirror their parents’ attitudes and behaviors, parental capacity to regulate self-­esteem can directly affect a child’s self-­esteem regulation.

chunks.heading: 750 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 750 Adulthood 7. The capacity for trust, empathy, and intimacy is relevant to parenting because of its relationship to the ability to rely on others for help that can contribute to establishing an intimate, authentic affective connection with one’s child. Assessment of the capacity for trust should...
chunks.text: 750 Adulthood 7. The capacity for trust, empathy, and intimacy is relevant to parenting because of its relationship to the ability to rely on others for help that can contribute to establishing an intimate, authentic affective connection with one’s child. Assessment of the capacity for trust should focus on the parent’s ability to rely on multiple sources of guidance (e.g., partners or other parents for shared experiences, pediatricians for medical counsel, educators, and psychologists for insights into learning and development, digital platforms and social media for a wider range of parenting perspectives). Parents who can trust such sources of information, especially when they are new to parenthood, are more likely to feel confident in their actions and decisions. This confidence may reduce stress and contribute to more rewarding parenting experiences. The capacity for epistemic trust may help parents navigate the vast sea of available information, empowering them to make well-­informed choices that align with their own values and with the best interests of their children. Conversely, when parents are unable to feel reasonable trust and must sort through conflicting advice with no sense of which sources are reliable, they may feel inundated and confused. Their ongoing need to make decisions, with no sense of which decision is preferable, may result in second-­guessing. Every decision, large or small, may become a stressor, adding to mental fatigue and detracting from parental enjoyment. Profound skepticism about the veracity and usefulness of information received from others (mistrust) may become a barrier to effective communication, creating chasms between parent and child, and between parent and potential support systems, setting the stage for stress and burnout. Excessive credulity, a different expression of problems with epistemic trust, poses its own challenges. Caregivers who uncritically accept harmful or misleading advice may jeopardize their ability to balance parenting demands and resources. Assessment of the parent’s capacity for empathy should focus on its two components: the capacity to experience concern, tenderness, and desire for one’s child’s well-being (“empathic concern”), and the capacity to identify and understand the child’s emotions (“perspective taking” or “empathic awareness”). Taken together, these sensibilities indicate the parent’s overall capacity to resonate with, and attune to, a child’s internal states. Assessment of this capacity should consider the parent’s ability to adjust emotional distance and closeness to the child in response to situational demands and to share genuine affect in their relationship. This facility may depend on the parent’s capacities for affect regulation and expression, and for differentiation and integration. Defense and Coping in Parenting 8. The capacity for impulse control and regulation is fundamental to successful adaptation to parenthood. In moment-­to-­moment interactions, effective parents maintain awareness of their own behavior, of the needs and behaviors of their child, and of exigencies in the immediate social context, responding according to their values and goals for their child and family. The capacity for impulse control and regulation is closely linked to the capacity for affect regulation and expression. This capacity is highly relevant to disciplinary contexts given that shaping and responding to children’s behavior requires persisting at difficult goals, delaying gratification, and inhibiting impulses that could harm the child. A common way that deficits in impulse regulation appear in parenting is harsh discipline; for example, struggling with anger and failing to inhibit a harsh behavioral impulse (e.g., yelling or hitting) when responding to the child’s difficult behavior, a common precursor to physical abuse (Gershoff, Lee, & Durrant, 2017). In assessing parental capacity for impulse control and regulation, clinicians should consider how the parent and the child mutually regulate one another’s emotions, cognitions,

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning 751
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning 751 biology, and behavior. Parent–­child coregulation is a maturational process through which children internalize regulatory skills (Lunkenheimer, Sturge-­ Apple, & Kelm, 2017). Because parental responses are a function of dynamic relations among intraindividual, inte...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning 751 biology, and behavior. Parent–­child coregulation is a maturational process through which children internalize regulatory skills (Lunkenheimer, Sturge-­ Apple, & Kelm, 2017). Because parental responses are a function of dynamic relations among intraindividual, interindividual, environmental, and temporal processes, clinicians should consider multiple factors, including the parent’s own history, neurobiology, and cognition, as well as the context, the child’s temperament, and the parent’s relationship with the child. 9. Defensive functioning applies to how a parent manages motives, affects, urges, conflicts, memories, thoughts, and other potentially anxiety-­producing inner experiences while carrying out the caregiving role. It includes the parent’s response to anxiety, shame, and other painful states evoked by interactions with a child and by resonances from parents’ childhood experiences with their own parents. A parent’s defensive functioning shapes the parent–­child interaction and can be transmitted across generations (Carone, Tracchegiani, & Lingiardi, 2025; Cramer, 2015). Assessment of a parent’s defensive functioning should consider the extent to which the parent’s defenses influence parental perceptions, attitudes, and behaviors toward a child in areas such as discipline, emotional support, and communication. Parents who use projection, for example, may unconsciously attribute their own undesirable thoughts or feelings to their child, leading to misinterpretations of the child’s behavior or needs. Parents who use denial may minimize or ignore signs of distress or dysfunction in their child, avoiding the need to confront uncomfortable realities and exerting an authoritarian or neglectful parenting style. Parents who rely heavily on repression may struggle to acknowledge and express their own emotions, leading to difficulties in providing empathic and supportive responses to their child’s emotional needs. Parental defenses may affect the consistency and effectiveness of disciplinary strategies. Caregivers who rationalize or intellectualize, for example, may justify or minimize disciplinary actions, potentially undermining their ability to set limits and enforce consistent consequences for unacceptable behavior. Parents who use sublimation may channel their frustrations into positive activities with their children, fostering an authoritative relationship characterized by warmth and high expectations. Under psychological stress, parents may revert to more primitive defenses that tilt their parenting style in a more reactive or overprotective direction. Table 13.2 (pp. 754–759) reports some examples of defenses in their specific relationship to parenting and suggests how they can be identified in both transcripts and parent–­child interactions. Defenses can be identified on interview transcripts (e.g., on AAI transcripts) to assess how parents may defend against recalling personal attachment experiences from the past, or on PDI transcripts to assess how they use defenses when describing their child, themselves as parents, their parent–­child relationship, and specific parent–­child interactions. Table 13.2 is not exhaustive and follows the DMRS-based hierarchical organization of defenses (Di Giuseppe & Perry, 2021; Perry & Bond, 2012); it emphasizes specific indicators of parenting behavior that clinicians can note during assessment. 10. The capacity for adaptation and resilience is closely linked to defensive functioning but focuses specifically on caregivers’ ability to cope adaptively when confronted with significant risk, stress, or trauma, whether current or related to childhood experiences. Assessment of this capacity requires clinicians to consider how parents use their own resources (strengths, skills, knowledge) to deal with problems or setbacks to their parenting and to anticipate, perceive, and respond to changing personal, family, and social circumstances. One aspect of this capacity involves accepting the imperfections of one’s parenting while maintaining an overall positive self-­evaluation.

chunks.heading: 752 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 752 Adulthood Insight and Self‐Direction in Parenting 11. The capacity to construct and use internal standards and ideals is relevant to parenting insofar as such standards guide parents in setting expectations for their child that are reasonable and achievable, thus building the child’s self-­estee...
chunks.text: 752 Adulthood Insight and Self‐Direction in Parenting 11. The capacity to construct and use internal standards and ideals is relevant to parenting insofar as such standards guide parents in setting expectations for their child that are reasonable and achievable, thus building the child’s self-­esteem and confidence. Assessment of this capacity should focus on several areas, including a caregiver’s beliefs about what constitutes good parenting. These ideas may reflect their own experiences, societal expectations, cultural norms, and understanding of child development and awareness of the impact of their behavior on the child. Assessment should consider a parent’s ability to maintain internal standards and ideals under stress, specifically, the extent to which a parent can translate inner ideals into consistent and effective parenting practices and can adapt in the face of contradictory advice and conflicting societal pressures that can sow confusion and doubt. Clinicians should note a parent’s capacity to filter such information and make decisions that align with their core values and beliefs rather than being swayed by external pressures. Finally, they should observe parents’ ability to recognize and address their own biases, weaknesses, and areas needing improvement in the face of discrepancies between stated ideals and actual behavior or when the priorities and values of co-­parents differ. 12. The capacity to explore one’s inner life in relation to parenting involves caregivers’ ability to understand and reflect upon their own psychological processes and those of their child. This capacity is closely linked to the capacity for mentalization (parental insightfulness, in particular) and for affect regulation and expression. It enables a parent to be attuned to a child’s emotional and psychological needs and to foster a nurturing, supportive environment. Clinicians should assess parents’ ability to manage their emotions and reactions and to recognize and empathize with their child’s feelings and experiences. Assessment should also focus on a parent’s ability to think critically about caregiving decisions and behaviors, considering their impact on the child. This reflective capacity allows parents to learn from their experiences, adjust to the evolving and unique needs of the child, and seek alternative methods that are more effective and supportive. Finally, clinicians should consider parental abilities to reflect upon and make sense of their own experiences as children and recognize the impact of such experiences on their role and behavior as parents. 13. The capacity for agency and purpose in relation to parenting involves the ability to make intentional, informed, and proactive decisions about one’s child’s upbringing and to have a clear direction in one’s parenting approach. Assessment of this capacity should consider parental feelings of self-­efficacy and ability to tolerate potential conflicts between one’s individual parenting goals and those of the co-­parent or those deriving from societal expectations. Like the capacity to construct and use internal standards and ideals, assessment of this capacity should focus on a parent’s ability to reflect on and eventually adjust parenting practices to meet a child’s needs better. It should also consider the parent’s ability to learn from past and present experiences, to seek out new knowledge, and to adapt parental approaches as a child matures. Courtois, PhD Brian Koehler, PhD MD Michael Garrett, MD Matthia...
chunks.text: CHAPTER 14 Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience S Axis C H A P T E R E D IT O R S Emanuela Mundo, MD Laura Muzi, PhD Vittorio Lingiardi, MD CONSULTANTS Richard Chefetz, MD Richard Kluft, MD Babak Roshanaei‐Moghaddam, Christine A. Courtois, PhD Brian Koehler, PhD MD Michael Garrett, MD Matthias Michal, MD Golan Shahar, PhD William H. Gottdiener, PhD Marta Mirabella, PhD Tom Wooldridge, PhD Mads Gram Henriksen, PhD John A. O’Neil, MD Marvin Hurvich, PhD Humberto Persano, MD, PhD Introduction This chapter refers mostly to the categories of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, fifth edition, text revision (DSM-5-TR; American Psychiatric Association, 2022) and the International Classification of Diseases, 11th revision (ICD-11; World Health Organization [WHO], 2019). We sometimes mention earlier versions of both systems (particularly earlier DSMs; American Psychiatric Association, 1968, 1980, 1994, 2000, 2013) as well. In recent years, other taxonomies have been gaining popularity as potential alternatives to the DSM and ICD. For example, the Hierarchical Taxonomy of Psychopathology (HiTOP) is an empirically based, quantitative, dimensional system that organizes psychopathology into a multilevel hierarchy, in which phenomena are arranged by levels of specificity (i.e., a broader dimension at one level can be parsed into more specific dimensions at lower levels) (Kotov et al.,

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 761
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 761 2021). Despite their clinical utility, we chose to not include contributions of the HiTOP model in the S Axis. It is still a work in progress, and despite some notable exceptions (see Kotov et al., 2020; Watson et al., 2022) there are still relatively few studies of its concept...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 761 2021). Despite their clinical utility, we chose to not include contributions of the HiTOP model in the S Axis. It is still a work in progress, and despite some notable exceptions (see Kotov et al., 2020; Watson et al., 2022) there are still relatively few studies of its concepts and applications in clinical settings. The DSM and ICD are deliberately categorical and descriptive, as opposed to dimensional and inferential. Their clinical usefulness is limited by the fact that lists of the symptoms of disorders are insufficient for understanding the lived experience of people diagnosed with them. Methodologies based on descriptive and categorical concepts do not adequately capture the complex human experiences of conditions that may need professional attention. Individuals in the same diagnostic category may vary widely in their subjective experience, and these variations have implications for treatment. It was the limitations of categorical, descriptive, “neo-­K raepelinian” nosology that inspired Stanley Greenspan to spearhead the creation of the original Psychodynamic Diagnostic Manual (PDM Task Force, 2006). Yet given the widespread use of the DSM and ICD, Greenspan felt the PDM should take each diagnostic entity as represented in those taxonomies, and describe how patients tend to experience each symptom pattern. Accordingly, we depict subjective states associated with discrete psychiatric diagnoses in terms of affective, cognitive, somatic, and relational patterns. Table 14.1 provides an overview of the S Axis, listing the sections and subsections of the various conditions we address here. International and North American Taxonomies We provide both ICD-11 and DSM-5-TR listings for a number of reasons. Like PDM2, PDM-3 is a collaborative effort of North American and international associations; DSM-5-TR has adopted a number of ICD-11’s innovations; the United States has finally moved from ICD-9 (WHO, 1977) codes to ICD-10 (WHO, 1992) and ICD11 codes for recording DSM diagnoses. When ICD changed its first coding character from a number to a letter, it greatly expanded the number of available codes. This expansion is reflected in the replacement of the all-­purpose “not otherwise specified” (NOS) of DSM-III and -IV by the “other specified” (last digit 8) and “unspecified” (last digit 9) listings of DSM-5-TR. Like DSM-5, DSM-5-TR has followed a number of ICD-10 reclusterings, such as separating out obsessive–­compulsive disorder and posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) from the anxiety disorders. PDM-3’s S Axis adopts most of this reclustering, with one major modification: A second level of grouping is introduced. The lower-level groups correspond largely to those of DSM-5-TR. The second level corresponds to what is implicit but unstated in DSM-5-TR and explicit in ICD-11. We regard the ICD and DSM characterizations of psychological problems as useful starting points for elucidating aspects of mental and emotional suffering. A deeper exploration might merge some diagnostic categories and differentiate others. We have placed this chapter third in our diagnostic profile for adulthood because symptom patterns are best understood in the context of a patient’s overall personality structure, personality styles/disorders, and profile of mental functioning. Symptoms such as anxiety, depression, and/or impulse-­control problems may be part of a larger emotional challenge. For example, problems with impulse control and mood regulation are common in patients with the higher-­order problem of inability to represent

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 763
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 763 (symbolize) a wide range of affects and wishes. When significant psychoanalytic writing exist about a particular symptom pattern, we offer ideas about its origins, meanings, and implications for transference, countertransference, and treatment. Differential Diagnosis of Certain...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 763 (symbolize) a wide range of affects and wishes. When significant psychoanalytic writing exist about a particular symptom pattern, we offer ideas about its origins, meanings, and implications for transference, countertransference, and treatment. Differential Diagnosis of Certain Subjective Experiences Some symptoms, such as fear, anxiety, and sadness, are universal and, consequently, also common in most psychiatric disorders and nonpsychopathological conditions. Others (e.g., tactile hallucinations) are rarer and more specific, so a given symptom may engender a very long or very short differential diagnostic process. Symptoms may have specific interactions, such as those delusions that derive from hallucinations. These anomalous subjective experiences are often the result of complex interactions between psychological factors and the biological background. Substance-­mediated symptoms and symptoms caused by another medical condition should consequently always be considered. Some other examples include the following: • Anxiety and depression. These are the most common unpleasant subjective states and may appear in almost any disorder. When they are relatively monosymptomatic, pronounced, or specific, an anxiety or depressive disorder can be diagnosed. When they are curiously absent, the clinicians should search for a “primary gain” or for a specific mental function (e.g., emotional blunting, isolation of affect, la belle indifference, dissociation of affect). • Somatoform symptoms. These may be direct bodily expressions of emotional pain, especially in persons with impaired psychological mindedness and insight. Symptoms may include tactile posttraumatic flashbacks of real past events, whose origins are murky because autobiographical memory and context are missing. They may be somatic “betrayals” of unacceptable repressed impulses, as in classic conversion disorders.
