Negative somatoform symptoms, such as conversion anesthesia for sharp pain, commonly accompany self-­mutilation and worsen its prognosis. • Hallucinations. These symptoms carry a wide differential diagnosis. These may be (in decreasing order of frequency) auditory, tactile, visual, olfactory, or gustatory. Auditory hallucinations may occur in toxic or epileptic psychosis, schizophrenia, brief psychotic disorder, mania, melancholia, PTSD, dissociative identity disorder, or very severe personality disorders, without calling for another diagnosis. Visual hallucinations may also occur in many of these disorders and in depersonalization disorder (as in out-of-body experiences). Tactile hallucinations (negative and positive) are especially common as components of posttraumatic and dissociative psychopathology. Olfactory and gustatory hallucinations are likewise often posttraumatic or dissociative but may be organic. • Delusions. These may occur in toxic or epileptic psychosis, schizophrenia, brief psychotic disorder, mania, melancholia, delusional disorder, or very severe personality disorders (transiently), without calling for another diagnosis. Some delusions come with hallucinations. Hallucinations of persecutory voices may lead to persecutory delusions. Hallucinating one’s own thoughts aloud may lead to the delusion of thought broadcasting. The negative hallucination of feeling unreal or alien may set in motion the delusion of being an extraterrestrial. Somatic hallucinations may lead to delusions of infestation. And so on.

chunks.heading: 764 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 764 Adulthood • Amnesia. This may occur in organic conditions, but also commonly in PTSD, dissociative disorders, and borderline personality disorder (transiently). • Depersonalization– ­derealization. These occur, of course, in the disorder itself, but may also complicate schizophrenia, severe depr...
chunks.text: 764 Adulthood • Amnesia. This may occur in organic conditions, but also commonly in PTSD, dissociative disorders, and borderline personality disorder (transiently). • Depersonalization– ­derealization. These occur, of course, in the disorder itself, but may also complicate schizophrenia, severe depression, PTSD, panic attacks, dissociative identity disorder, and severe personality disorders (transiently). • Sleep–wake symptoms and disorders. Sleep–wake disorders involve difficulty initiating or maintaining sleep (insomnia disorders), excessive sleepiness (hypersomnolence disorders), respiratory disturbance during sleep (sleep-­related breathing disorders), disorders of the sleep–wake schedule (circadian rhythm sleep–wake disorders), abnormal movements during sleep (sleep-­related movement disorders), or problematic behavioral or physiological events that occur while falling asleep, during sleep, or upon awakening (parasomnia disorders). In ICD-11, all these disorders have been unified in a new and separate chapter, independent from mental, behavioral or neurodevelopmental disorders. Accordingly, we have chosen not to include a specific description of the subjective experience of these conditions, but to understand them as transdiagnostic features across other disorders. Early morning awakening is especially indicative of major depression. Difficulty in falling asleep is common in anxiety disorders (e.g., generalized anxiety disorder) but may also be motivated by a posttraumatic dread of flashbacks while drifting off or of subsequent nightmares. Persistent sleep disturbances (both insomnia and excessive sleepiness) are risk factors for the subsequent development of other mental illnesses (including substance use disorders and behavioral addictions) (American Psychiatric Association, 2022). Suicidality We consider suicidal intent, behavior, and attempts as serious challenges for the clinical assessment of patients’ subjective experience. Suicide is a leading cause of death worldwide, accounting for over 700,000 deaths each year (WHO, 2021). DSM-5-TR now includes freestanding symptom codes for reporting suicidal and nonsuicidal selfharm and history of suicidal behavior that can be given either in addition to a psychiatric diagnosis or in its absence. Further on, it includes a section on “Association with Suicidal Thoughts or Behavior” for each mental disorder. ICD-11 includes suicidal behaviors within “symptoms or signs involving appearance or behavior,” and suicidal ideation in the wider category of “symptoms or signs involving content of thought.” Both systems recognize that a wide range of psychopathological symptoms can have a relevant impact on suicide risk. Suicidal thoughts and intent are considered as mind-brain disturbances that need to be identified and managed in clinical practice and have a specific subjective experience within and beyond the diagnosis (Siegel, 2012). Suicidal symptoms are typical “transdiagnostic” attitudes and behaviors; thus, as most of the psychodynamic and biological literature points out, they may be present in many disorders at different times. Suicidal risk should be carefully assessed for any patient, regardless of the “primary diagnosis” or the patient’s primary treatment request. The subjective experience of suicidal thoughts or behavior may vary widely within the same patient during the person’s life course or treatment; such thoughts should always be considered a primary risk factor for suicide attempts. Most practice guidelines for assessing suicidal

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 765
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 765 risk and suicidality assume that clinicians should assess suicidal risk in every patient, irrespective of symptoms and diagnosis. The assessment requires a complex process of collecting information on both risk and protective factors that can be inferred from the patient’s hist...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 765 risk and suicidality assume that clinicians should assess suicidal risk in every patient, irrespective of symptoms and diagnosis. The assessment requires a complex process of collecting information on both risk and protective factors that can be inferred from the patient’s history. A keystone of suicidal risk assessment is the accurate investigation of motivation, intent, and planning. Previous suicide attempts and their circumstances, along with family history of suicide, should be assessed from the medical record. Self-­admission of suicidal ideation or action is another vital factor for detecting and managing the suicide risk (Hallford et al., 2023). Several sociodemographic, psychological, and clinical variables may affect the likelihood of such disclosure. Along with other factors (e.g., presence of a psychiatric disorder, interpersonal functioning, health-­related stigma, mental health literacy), public and private disclosure may be affected by lack of relational and social support, fear of negative consequences for oneself and others, or even a fatalistic attitude that nothing can be done to improve one’s condition. Both older and recent literatures suggest that even though suicide is a multifactorial phenomenon, genetics may play a role in increasing suicidal risk. An international consortium of scientists has disentangled the shared genetic architecture of suicide attempts, psychiatric disorders, and nonpsychiatric risk factors, showing that genetic underpinnings are not driven by psychiatric disorders (Mullins et al., 2022). Genetics do not, however, completely explain suicidal risk. Rather, genetic predispositions interact with environmental (psychosocial and cultural) factors, such as the following: • Previous suicide attempts • Presence of any psychiatric disorders, especially depressive and bipolar disorders, anxiety disorders, schizophrenia spectrum disorders, and certain personality pathologies (e.g., borderline personality disorder) • Substance-­related and addictive disorders • History of trauma, abuse, or neglect • Impulsive and/or aggressive tendencies • Some major physical illnesses • Job and/or financial loss • Relational and/or social loss (e.g., bereavements, separations, etc.) • Easy access to lethal means • Stigma associated with help-­seeking behaviors • Barriers to accessing health care, especially for mental and substance use disorders • Cultural and religious beliefs (e.g., that suicide is a noble act) • Influence of others who have died by suicide, including media and social-­ network exposure Clinicians should be careful in questioning the patient about suicidal risk: A recent meta-­analytic study on this topic (Hallford et al., 2023) found that in-­person disclosure of suicidal ideations and behaviors may be harder for patients, and therefore less frequent than online or written disclosure. In evaluating suicide risk, providing options for disclosure such as “prefer not to say” in assessment measures, or ways of deferring answering in face-to-face conversation (e.g., “It’s OK if you don’t want to answer that right now”), may foster further opportunities to identify risks in patients who deny suicide-­related thoughts or actions. Clinicians should ask questions gently

chunks.heading: 766 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 766 Adulthood and calmly, taking the time necessary to create a trusting, safe atmosphere. Excessive reserve during the assessment, however, can lead to a serious underestimation of the risk. Therapists should pay special attention to their negative countertransferences. Clinical management of suici...
chunks.text: 766 Adulthood and calmly, taking the time necessary to create a trusting, safe atmosphere. Excessive reserve during the assessment, however, can lead to a serious underestimation of the risk. Therapists should pay special attention to their negative countertransferences. Clinical management of suicidal risk is challenging. After assessing risk factors, clinicians should adhere to Peters’s (2000) standard of care. This requires: (1) carefully, comprehensively assessing suicide risk; (2) planning and implementing clinical interventions that can decrease such risk; (3) engaging in clinical case discussions (also useful also in cases of self-­harming and impulsive patients). Building a strong alliance with people at risk of suicide or self-harm is imperative, irrespective of comorbid diagnoses (Dunster-­Page, Haddock, Wainwright, & Berry, 2017). The “contract-­based approach” of Yeomans, Selzer, and Clarkin (1992, 1993), originally developed for patients with severe personality disorders and impulsivity, implements both general and patient-­specific prescriptions based on the person’s history, previous suicidal attempts or self-­injuries, and individual risk factors. The contract provides detailed instructions that patients should follow when experiencing suicidal or self-­harming impulses, for example, to seek help from their family doctor or call emergency services. Although they are expected to talk about such events in treatment, it is not the therapist to whom suicidal patients should appeal in acute circumstances. Such contracts are designed to prevent manipulations and to deal with the alternating idealization and devaluation that tends to characterize treatments of patients with severe personality disorders. Some studies have found that this contract-­based approach significantly reduces the severity of self-harm and suicidal risk. Comorbidity On both scientific and clinical grounds, there is solid evidence that symptoms are continuous rather than categorical, and that overlap between disorders is the rule rather than the exception (Krueger & Markon, 2006). We do not assume that the presence of multiple symptoms and experiences inevitably constitutes “comorbidity”; instead, we see them as expressions of a basic complex disturbance of mental functioning. Each person’s symptoms, while sharing features with similar patterns in others, have a unique signature. Nonetheless, because so much research on psychopathology has been conducted under the assumption that disorder categories are discrete and that combinations of symptoms constitute “comorbidities” (see Aftab, Banicki, Ruffalo, & Frances, 2024), when we report important scientific findings in this chapter, we have to convey them in the language of models that privilege descriptive, operationalized criteria. The clinical illustrations in each section of this chapter provide examples of specific patterns of patients’ internal experiences. They are not definitive or exhaustive. We encourage clinicians to capture each patient’s unique subjective experience in an individualized case formulation. In some instances, research findings support the observations that follow; in others, in the absence of empirical work on the topic, we have drawn on the clinical experience of therapists with expertise in each area we cover. For example, extensive clinical experience has led us to include the “diagnosis” of complex PTSD. We include consideration of the biological contributions to some of these patterns. We see mind and brain as in a dialogic relationship, and genes and environment in constant interaction.

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 767
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 767 Context of Subjective Experience: Time Psychodynamic practitioners are interested in the role symptoms play in a patient’s overall life history and experience. Symptoms often indicate a diagnosis, but they always also tell a story. They express how someone characteristically co...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 767 Context of Subjective Experience: Time Psychodynamic practitioners are interested in the role symptoms play in a patient’s overall life history and experience. Symptoms often indicate a diagnosis, but they always also tell a story. They express how someone characteristically copes. They have a temporality: Why now? And how does this kind of suffering relate to this person’s past and future? Developmental Context Developmentally relevant aspects of symptom patterns interact with personality variables. An elderly woman may experience a depression quite differently from a woman in her 30s, and may thus require a different therapeutic approach. Formulation and treatment plan should recognize age-­related differences along with the patient’s history, individual life events, and familial, social, economic and cultural context. Temporal Aspects of the Current Condition Why are these symptoms occurring now, and what do they mean? Clinicians can wonder about the “first and worst”: If a man is depressed, when does he remember being depressed like this for the first time? Is this the most depressed he has ever felt? The first and worst may be extended to thoughts and relationships. Does he recall when he was first concerned with this question? Was he once even more worried about it than he is now? When does he recall being in this sort of interpersonal conflict for the first time? Was it ever more serious than it is now? Bimodal Symptoms Some symptoms from the past may reappear today. What was the trigger? It may have been any item of “unfinished business” from a person’s history, which becomes reactivated under either stressful conditions or specific life events—­regression to a point of fixation—­especially if the trigger has some thematic affinity to the original item. For example, a man’s sexual molestation in earlier childhood may have been perplexing but may become more overtly traumatic retrospectively when puberty arrives, and the sexual intent suddenly becomes experientially clear (Freud’s Nachträglichkeit); or a woman may have successfully repressed an early trauma until her child reaches the age at which she was first abused. Interpersonal Functioning The family of origin is the original crucible in which relational patterns originate not only in “objective fact” but also, and especially, in the patient’s personal experience. A simple way to elicit the subjective experience of relationship patterns with historical pertinence may be to wonder how patients relate or related to, and take or took after, their parents. Contrasts may emerge first, but it is worth pursuing identifications, as the most clinically pertinent ones are the ones most regretted: “I hate the fact that mother always put herself first, and I find myself doing the same thing. . . . ” Counterintuitive questions may also bear fruit: “Are there ways in which your boyfriend is like

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 769
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 769 Henriksen, 2023). It also does not permit recognition of atypical, transient, milder, or subclinical psychotic manifestations (recognized as latent, borderline, or pseudoneurotic schizophrenia in the pre-DSM-III era), which thus are likely to remain untreated. The last 25 years...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 769 Henriksen, 2023). It also does not permit recognition of atypical, transient, milder, or subclinical psychotic manifestations (recognized as latent, borderline, or pseudoneurotic schizophrenia in the pre-DSM-III era), which thus are likely to remain untreated. The last 25 years have seen two critical developments relevant to the diagnosis and treatment of schizophrenia spectrum disorders, each of which is in some respects a rediscovery of prior clinical knowledge. First, with the aim of limiting the prevalence, damage, and social burden of schizophrenia spectrum disorders, scholars have recognized transient or subclinical psychotic symptoms as “at-risk” conditions needing clinical care before they develop into full-blown psychotic disorders—­see also the “at-Risk Mental State for Psychosis” section in the SA Axis (Chapter 9, pp. 523–626). Preventive interventions for individuals with a high risk of developing psychosis are associated with significantly improved clinical outcomes (Fusar-Poli et al., 2020). This finding supports Harry Stack Sullivan’s (1927, pp. 106–107) belief that “many incipient [schizophrenia] cases might be arrested before the efficient contact with reality is completely suspended, and a long stay in institutions made necessary.” Early interventions reduce the “duration of untreated psychosis”: the time between the appearance of the first psychotic symptom and the first appropriate intervention (Marshall et al., 2005). A longer duration of untreated psychosis is associated with more severe clinical outcomes in both the short and long term (Penttilä, Jääskeläainen, Hirvonen, Isohanni, & Miettunen, 2014). Second, phenomenological inquiry into core symptoms of schizophrenia (i.e., Henriksen, Raballo, & Nordgaard, 2021; Sass & Parnas, 2003) has provided finer depictions of the subjective experience of psychosis, including the altered self-state of a disturbance of “ipseity” (experience of the subjective presence of the self), which may occur in no other psychiatric disorder. These phenomenological discoveries support a more holistic, gestalt approach to the diagnosis of psychotic conditions. Noting signs and symptoms listed in major taxonomies is relatively simple, but recognizing and interpreting disturbances of subjective experience requires clinicians, as participant observers, to immerse themselves in the seemingly odd and puzzling experience of the patient. At the end of this section, we give illustrations of the applicability and value of this more dimensional, more inferential, and less categorical diagnostic sensibility. S1.1 Brief Psychotic Disorder The S1.1 diagnosis unifies a wide range of psychotic conditions that in the past have been called, among other terms, benign schizophreniform psychosis, hysterical psychosis, psychogenic psychosis, bouffée délirante, polymorphe aigüe, benign stupor, and acute reactive psychosis. Brief psychotic disorder is currently identified by the presence of transient symptoms of schizophrenia followed by a return to premorbid levels of functioning. ICD-11’s “acute and transient psychotic disorder” (6A23) emphasizes the acuteness of onset of psychotic symptoms, that emerge without a prodrome, progressing from a nonpsychotic state to a clearly psychotic state within 2 weeks. By definition, symptoms change rapidly (both in nature and intensity), there is an absence of negative symptoms, and the duration of the symptoms does not exceed 3 months. An episode of acute stress may precede onset but is not a diagnostic requirement. ICD-11 includes a specifier to rate positive, negative, depressive, manic, psychomotor, and cognitive symptoms (Fusar-Poli et al., 2022; WHO, 2019).

chunks.heading: 770 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 770 Adulthood DSM-5-TR’s “brief psychotic disorder” denotes a similar condition, while also requiring an indication of whether the disorder manifests in the presence of significant stressors, in their absence, during the peripartum period, or with catatonic features. In the context of early detectio...
chunks.text: 770 Adulthood DSM-5-TR’s “brief psychotic disorder” denotes a similar condition, while also requiring an indication of whether the disorder manifests in the presence of significant stressors, in their absence, during the peripartum period, or with catatonic features. In the context of early detection and intervention efforts (see “At-Risk Mental State for Psychosis” section in the SA Axis, Chapter 9, pp. 523–626), two additional (research-­based) depictions of short-lived psychotic symptoms have been developed: the “brief limited intermittent psychotic symptoms” (BLIPS; assessed with the Comprehensive Assessment of At-Risk Mental States; Yung & McGorry, 1996; Yung et al., 2005) and “brief intermittent psychotic symptoms” (BIPS; assessed with the Structured Interview for Psychosis-­R isk Syndromes; McGlashan et al., 2001; Miller et al., 2003). The former remits in 7 days without antipsychotic medications; the latter may last up to 3 months, may require antipsychotic medications, and for patients with seriously disorganizing or dangerous symptoms may involve diagnosing other clinical syndromes (Fusar-Poli et al., 2022). Brief psychotic disorder and acute and transient psychotic disorder are frank psychotic disorders, whereas, in accordance with epidemiological evidence, BLIPS and BIPS are markers of an increased risk for developing a subsequent psychotic disorder. What all these conditions have in common is a relatively brief and self-­limited clinical course (i.e., from 1 month [DSM] to 3 months [ICD]), which is critical for differential diagnosis, though even this apparently simple characteristic may be difficult to ascertain exactly. The peak age of onset is 18.5 years, with 20.6% of cases before 25 years (Solmi et al., 2022). Onset of symptoms is generally inferred from personal and family narratives, which can be biased by many factors. Their apparent remission can also be influenced by several factors, including a patient’s wish to seal over the psychotic episode and the family’s collusion in regarding it as over. Clinician flexibility, openness of mind, and professional caution remain essential in using this diagnosis. Personality disorders at the low borderline level of organization may predispose an individual to short-lived psychotic disorders. Clinicians need to be attentive to possible underlying dynamics: Do the symptoms seem to be obscuring or modifying some affective state? Does the transient departure from reality seem to be motivated by denial of some painful truth from which the patient flees, or by wishful fantasies to which the patient flees? Epidemiological studies report a 33% incidence over 3 years of psychotic relapse, and a 49% incidence over 4 years of retaining a schizophrenia spectrum diagnosis. Robust evidence on prognostic factors is currently lacking (Provenzani, Salazar de Pablo, Arribas, Pillman, & Fusar-Poli, 2021). The literature highlights the importance of prompt and comprehensive therapeutic interventions that include both pharmacological and psychotherapeutic support. The subjective experience of psychotic symptoms is in itself highly traumatic; suicide is among the leading causes of death in patients diagnosed with acute and transient psychotic disorder (Castagnini, Foldager, & Bertelsen, 2013). Unfortunately, individuals with short-lived psychotic episodes go mostly undetected at clinical centers and often receive heterogeneous and nonspecific interventions, with insufficient follow-­up duration (Fusar-Poli et al., 2022). S1.2 Delusional Disorder (“Pure Paranoia”) On the basis of their clinical course, Emil Kraepelin divided the major psychoses into two groups, “manic–­depressive insanity” (mania and melancholia) and “dementia praecox” (catatonia, hebephrenia, paranoia). Individuals with dementia praecox had

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 771
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 771 a chronic deteriorating course, and those with manic–­depressive insanity could return to a normal state between episodes. But there were some paranoid “leftover” individuals, with clinical courses more like those with manic–­depressive insanity than those with “precocious deme...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 771 a chronic deteriorating course, and those with manic–­depressive insanity could return to a normal state between episodes. But there were some paranoid “leftover” individuals, with clinical courses more like those with manic–­depressive insanity than those with “precocious dementia.” Individuals with delusional disorder, or “pure paranoia,” have a delusion or a structured system of delusions, but rarely hallucinations (if present, they connect to the primary delusion). They do not show formal thought disorder, catatonia, wild mood swings, or chronic deteriorating course. ICD-7, -8, and -9 and DSM-II included “paranoid states”: (pure) paranoia and an “involutional paranoid state” (also called involutional paraphrenia), a term for a syndrome of later middle age that has fallen into disuse. DSM-5-TR and ICD-11 have retained delusional disorder, which is characterized by the persistent presence of one or more delusions for at least 3 months, in the absence of other psychotic symptoms typically seen in schizophrenia. These delusions may range from seemingly plausible to entirely implausible beliefs but remain circumscribed, allowing individuals to maintain relatively intact functioning outside the delusional domain. The delusions are variable in content across individuals, while showing remarkable stability within individuals, although they may evolve over time. Common forms of delusions include persecutory, somatic (e.g., a belief that organs are rotting or malfunctioning despite normal medical examination), grandiose (e.g., a belief that one has discovered an elixir that gives eternal life), jealous (e.g., the unjustified belief that one’s spouse is unfaithful) and erotomania (i.e., the belief that another person, usually a famous or high-­status stranger, is in love with the person experiencing the delusion) (WHO, 2019). Persecutory themes are the most prevalent (Lemonde et al., 2021) and are associated with severe psychological stress and high levels of suicidal ideation (Freeman et al., 2019). Delusional disorder is more common among individuals with personality disorders at the lower borderline level of organization. Research evidence suggests that patients with delusional disorder are usually older, with a higher level of functioning than individuals diagnosed with schizophrenia (Muñoz-Negro et al., 2018). Other symptoms commonly noted in those diagnosed with delusional disorders are ideas of reference, irritable or dysphoric mood, and actions linked to the content of the delusion (e.g., stalking a loved one or filing lawsuits against supposed persecutors). Individuals with jealous, persecutory, and erotomanic delusions may behave angrily and even violently (Whiting, Gulati, Geddes, & Fazel, 2022); past criminality and comorbid substance disorders are the best predictors of violent crime. But because severe violence is rare among people with mental disorders (Whiting, Lichtenstein, & Fazel, 2021), clinicians should try not to contribute to social stigma. The rare phenomenon of shared delusional disorder (folie à deux) occurs when two closely related individuals have the same delusions. Typically, they originate in one person and are adopted by the more submissive and/or dependent individual. The delusions tend to disappear when the relationship is interrupted. The course of a delusional disorder ranges from full remission to relapse to conversion to other psychotic disorders (American Psychiatric Association, 2022; WHO, 2019). Psychotherapy should aim at establishing a relationship of trust, improving general functioning, and monitoring risk. Pharmacological interventions are generally similar to those used for other psychotic disorders, with the exception that antidepressants are prescribed more frequently in delusional disorder and, for acute treatment, effective antipsychotic doses are lower in delusional disorder than in schizophrenia.

chunks.heading: 772 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 772 Adulthood S1.3 Schizotypal (Personality) Disorder The term “schizotypy” refers to latent personality traits that putatively harbor vulnerability to schizophrenia and can give rise to a variety of schizophrenia-­related phenotypic outcomes. Rado (1953) coined the term “schizotype” as an abbreviat...
chunks.text: 772 Adulthood S1.3 Schizotypal (Personality) Disorder The term “schizotypy” refers to latent personality traits that putatively harbor vulnerability to schizophrenia and can give rise to a variety of schizophrenia-­related phenotypic outcomes. Rado (1953) coined the term “schizotype” as an abbreviation of “schizophrenic phenotype,” influencing the etiological model of schizophrenia developed by Meehl (1990), who proposed the seminal concepts of “schizotaxia” and “schizotypy.” The former referred to an underlying genetic predisposition to develop schizophrenia, whereas the latter indicated a trait-like manifestation (i.e., a psychological and personality organization) of the developmental interplay between such a genetic liability and environmental stress factors (Lenzenweger, Maher, & Manschreck, 2005). Currently, schizotypy is operationalized by at least three dimensions: positive (e.g., inclination to give meaning to the objectively irrelevant), negative (e.g., inclination to feel overwhelmed by stimuli), and disorganized (e.g., inclination to dissociate between and within mental functions). Manifestations range from subtle, subclinical psychotic-­like phenomena in high-­functioning individuals (e.g., perceptual aberrations, magical ideation, referential thinking, interpersonal aversiveness), through pathological personality manifestations (e.g., schizotypal, paranoid, avoidant, and schizoid personality disorders), to diagnosable psychotic disorders (Flückiger et al., 2019; Mohr & Claridge, 2015). In other words, schizotypal traits do not necessarily involve clinically significant distress, whereas schizotypal (personality) disorders are construed as psychopathology in international diagnostic systems. Some historical context is relevant here. The influential Danish Adoption Study (a research program investigating nonpsychotic family members of patients diagnosed with schizophrenia who showed chronic peculiarities of thought and communication and impoverished social function) proposed the term “schizophrenia spectrum,” to denote psychopathological conditions genetically linked to schizophrenia manifesting along a clinical continuum (Kety et al., 1968, 1971, 1976). This research program introduced diagnostic criteria for subclinical forms of schizophrenia (i.e., “borderline schizophrenia” or “latent schizophrenia”) with symptomatology in the areas of thinking (e.g., vague speech), experience (e.g., transient delusional ideas), affect (e.g., anhedonia), interpersonal behavior (e.g., sexual maladjustment), and psychopathology (e.g., severe widespread anxiety). Such clinical criteria strongly influenced the first description of schizotypal personality disorder in DSM-III, which was mostly operationalized in terms of cognitive and perceptual symptoms rather than clinical manifestations related to anxiety, sexuality, and neurotic symptomatology (Lingiardi & Boldrini, 2019; Parnas, Licht, & Bovet, 2005a). Schizotypal (personality) disorder is currently considered as an intermediate schizophrenia spectrum phenotype with a relatively high lifetime prevalence (4% in the United States) compared to other primarily psychotic disorders (Rosell, Futterman, McMaster, & Siever, 2014). Overall, this condition is characterized by both trait features (i.e., enduring patterns occurring in multiple contexts) and state features (i.e., denoting clinical manifestations waxing and waning); however, certain symptoms (e.g., odd speech) encompass both (Parnas et al., 2005a). In the current DSM-5-TR, schizotypal (personality) disorder is listed in both “Schizophrenia Spectrum and Other Psychotic Disorders” and “Personality Disorders” sections. Schizotypal (personality) disorder has onset in early adulthood and manifests itself in several contexts. ICD-11 lists schizotypal disorder among the “schizophrenia or other primarily psychotic disorders.” It stresses its enduring pattern

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 773
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 773 (i.e., characteristic of the person’s functioning over a period of at least several years) of eccentricities in behavior, appearance and speech, accompanied by cognitive and perceptual distortions, unusual beliefs, and discomfort with—and often reduced capacity for—­interperson...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 773 (i.e., characteristic of the person’s functioning over a period of at least several years) of eccentricities in behavior, appearance and speech, accompanied by cognitive and perceptual distortions, unusual beliefs, and discomfort with—and often reduced capacity for—­interpersonal relationships. Transient delusions, hallucinations, formal thought disorder, or experiences of influence, passivity or control may occur, but do not last for more than 1 month. Empirical investigations suggest a link between schizotypal (personality) disorder and both childhood trauma and parental factors, even though further research is needed to establish the cumulative effects of early life risk factors (Dong, Liu, Hodgson, & Medoff-­Cooper, 2021). Cognitive difficulties (e.g., working memory) and interpersonal and affective processing deficits that impair general functioning are central clinical manifestations of schizotypal personality disorder. Perceptual abnormalities may be experienced in association with magical ideation. Schizotypal (personality) disorder is commonly underdetected, as its clinical manifestations may be either attributed to other psychiatric conditions or, unfortunately, dismissed without deeper investigation. In clinical settings, patients may report attentional impairments, social anxiety, and lasting difficulties in building connections with other individuals. Clinical assessment should focus on the person’s capacity to engage in a therapeutic alliance and agree on treatment goals. Suspiciousness, limited insight, and poor capacity to reflect on mental states may be obstacles to clinical care (Rosell et al., 2014). Psychotherapy should work toward ameliorating overt symptomatology (i.e., schizotypal personality disorder as a “syndrome”) and also promote change in enduring personality patterns (i.e., schizotypal personality disorder as “personality pathology”). We have insufficient evidence as to whether antipsychotic medication is useful to most patients diagnosed with schizotypy (Jakobsen et al., 2017). For further considerations, we refer readers to the discussion of schizoid (and schizotypal) personalities in the P Axis (see Chapter 12, pp. 651–708). S1.4 Substance‐Induced Psychotic Disorder The relationship between psychotropic substance use and onset of psychotic symptoms has strong scientific support (Fiorentini et al., 2021). Some substances (mainly cannabinoids, cocaine, amphetamines, and hallucinogens—­but not opioids) have “psychotomimetic” properties. Acute intoxication with these substances can not only induce transient psychotic symptoms, but also result in a syndrome that closely resembles a primary psychotic disorder, namely a substance-­induced psychotic disorder. Given the spread of a wide range of new psychoactive substances, including synthetic cannabinoids, cathinone derivatives, psychedelic phenethylamines, novel stimulants, tryptamine derivatives, phencyclidine-­ like dissociatives, piperazines, and gamma-­ aminobutyric (GABA) A/B receptor agonists (Schifano, Orsolini, Duccio Papanti, & Corkery, 2015), studies on substance-­induced psychotic disorder have become even more challenging in recent decades. The essential features of substance/drug-­induced psychotic disorder are delusions and/or hallucinations that seem to be the physiological effects of a substance/drug/ toxin. Individuals who realize that the hallucinations are induced by a substance do not meet criteria for this diagnosis; they are diagnosed as having “substance intoxication” or “substance withdrawal.” The proportion of people suffering a substance-­ induced psychotic disorder who refer themselves to emergency services is currently unknown; it is possible that most remain undetected.

chunks.heading: 774 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 774 Adulthood Acute episodes of substance-­induced psychosis can last from a few days to months; duration varies across studies (Fiorentini et al., 2021). They are commonly transient, resolving as the psychoactive substance is metabolized and excreted. There is growing recognition, however, that ind...
chunks.text: 774 Adulthood Acute episodes of substance-­induced psychosis can last from a few days to months; duration varies across studies (Fiorentini et al., 2021). They are commonly transient, resolving as the psychoactive substance is metabolized and excreted. There is growing recognition, however, that individuals with a substance-­induced psychotic disorder are at higher risk for later developing a “primary” schizophrenia spectrum disorder. Current evidence suggests that about 25% of individuals with any substance-­induced psychotic disorder and roughly 50% of those with a cannabis-­induced psychotic disorder will later develop schizophrenia. Higher rates of conversion are associated with younger age at occurrence (16–25 years) (Starzer, Nordentoft, & Hjorthøj, 2018). As this heightened risk seems to be reduced in patients who abstain from the substance after the first substance-­induced psychotic episode (Shah, Chand, Bandawar, Benegal, & Murthy, 2017), clinicians should inform patients about their risks and support in stopping them using the substance permanently after they experience psychotic symptoms. Contacts with clinical services should be maintained. We encourage clinicians to maintain a “watch and wait” attitude. S1.5 Schizophrenia and Schizoaffective Disorder Eugen Bleuler (1950) originated the concept of “schizophrenia” to designate a group of conditions with a varying array of psychotic symptoms leading to dysfunction in the context of an observed clinical course. Bleuler’s characterization of schizophrenia can be summarized in his “four A’s”: loosening of associations (which he saw as the core symptom of the disorder), disturbances of affect, ambivalence (impairment of decision making), and autism (absorption in a private internal world detached from consensual reality). Although its lifetime prevalence is lower than that of most mental disorders (approximately 0.7%; Moreno-­Küstner, Martin, & Pastor, 2018), schizophrenia is among the top 20 causes of disability worldwide (GBD 2017 Disease and Injury Incidence and Prevalence Collaborators, 2018). Signs and symptoms are both “positive” (something present that should not be there) and “negative” (something missing that should be present). Positive signs and symptoms include delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, and disorganized or catatonic behavior. Negative signs and symptoms include diminished emotional expression, avolition apathy, asociality, and anhedonia. Beyond positive and negative symptoms, patients experience subtle distortions in an array of mental processes that include cognition, affect, stress tolerance, drive, attention, and speech (e.g., a sense of disconnection with the environment, disturbances in grasping common words and sentences, problems in generating accurate words, and intrusive thoughts that disturb concentration). Such phenomena (termed “basic symptoms”—see also the “At-Risk Mental State of Psychosis” section in the SA Axis, Chapter 9, pp. 539–542) can precede onset of the first diagnosed psychosis and have been conceptualized as the earliest manifestation of the neurobiological processes underlying psychosis (Schultze-­Lutter & Theodoridou, 2017). Other clinical conditions associated with schizophrenia include depression, anxiety, anger, phobias, depersonalization, derealization, obsessions and compulsions, unusual bodily experiences, sleep–wake pattern disturbances, and substance use disorders (Plana-­R ipoll et al., 2019). There has been controversy over the years as to whether to construe these phenomena as inherent to the schizophrenic condition (the earlier view) or to consider them as categorically different coexisting disorders (Nordgaard et al., 2023).

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 775
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 775 Some scholars regard anomalous self-­experiences in several domains (i.e., cognition, stream of consciousness, self-­awareness, presence, bodily experiences, demarcation between self and others, existential reorientation) as the core symptoms of the disorder; they precede the p...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 775 Some scholars regard anomalous self-­experiences in several domains (i.e., cognition, stream of consciousness, self-­awareness, presence, bodily experiences, demarcation between self and others, existential reorientation) as the core symptoms of the disorder; they precede the psychotic episode, sometimes by years, and persist after remission, showing a trait-like status. Others consider the defining feature to be a striking fusion of perception with emotional experience (Marcus, 2017). Suicide is serious concern: Schizophrenia carries a 9.7-fold risk of mortality by suicide compared to the general population (Correll et al., 2022). Schizophreniform disorder is a DSM-5-TR diagnosis intended to minimize the premature diagnosis of schizophrenia and schizoaffective disorder. Descriptively, it is identical to schizophrenia except for its short course (less than 6 months) and less severe impairment of social and occupational functioning. Roughly two-­thirds of patients diagnosed with schizophreniform disorder progress to a final diagnosis of schizophrenia or schizoaffective disorder. Because it is hard to control for the effects of good, timely treatment approaches, these patterns should be interpreted cautiously. Schizoaffective disorder is “located” between schizophrenia and bipolar disorder, and is arbitrarily classified with schizophrenia rather than bipolar disorder. For diagnosis, symptoms meeting the criteria for a mood disorder episode (manic, depressive, or mixed) should be present for the majority of the active or residual phase of the disorder, together with symptoms of schizophrenia. Since mania and depression may have psychotic features (hallucinations, delusions), most often mood-­congruent but sometimes mood-­incongruent, to “qualify” for the schizophrenic component of the disorder, the patient must experience hallucinations or delusions without mood symptoms for at least 2 weeks. Onset may occur between adolescence and late in life, but schizoaffective disorder most commonly begins in early adulthood. The condition appears to be less common than schizophrenia. As with schizophrenia, there are indications of a genetic component that predisposes a person to develop a schizoaffective disorder. Schizophrenia is currently considered a complex biopsychosocial developmental disorder with multiple etiological factors (e.g., genes and environmental stressors, including childhood trauma; Varese et al., 2012), progressively altering the development of the brain and the personality from early childhood, even from gestation (Lieberman, Small, & Girgis, 2019). Estimates of lifetime risk to develop the illness are 20% in dizygotic twins, 45% in monozygotic twins, and higher in first-­degree relatives of patients diagnosed with schizophrenia compared to controls (Besteher, Brambilla, & Nenadi?, 2020). Nonetheless, 95% of individuals diagnosed with schizophrenia do not have an immediate family history of the illness (Lichtenstein et al., 2009; Lu et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2010). In other words, genes are only part of the story. Neuroscientific research underscores the role of brain plasticity: Brain areas change in structure and function, depending on context and life events, including psychotherapeutic treatment. Thus, genetic inheritance is a factor, but whether and how this influences psychosis will depend on what happens in a person’s life. Known lifetime risk factors include minor physical anomalies, trait anhedonia, being in an ethnic minority in a low ethnic-­density area, childhood social withdrawal, childhood trauma, living in urban environments, and being first-­generation immigrants (Radua et al., 2018). Unlike other neurodevelopmental disorders (e.g., autism spectrum disorder), the schizophrenia phenotype manifests itself after a latency period (i.e., presyndromal stages) ranging from one to three decades before onset of the first psychosis (i.e., syndromal stage)—peak age at onset is 20.5 years, with 47.4% cases before age 25 (Solmi

chunks.heading: 776 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 776 Adulthood et al., 2022). In the presyndromal stages, few cognitive and perceptual symptoms have been observed during childhood (i.e., premorbid stage), but attenuated positive psychotic symptoms (lower in frequency, duration, and intensity) have been noted during adolescence and young adulthood...
chunks.text: 776 Adulthood et al., 2022). In the presyndromal stages, few cognitive and perceptual symptoms have been observed during childhood (i.e., premorbid stage), but attenuated positive psychotic symptoms (lower in frequency, duration, and intensity) have been noted during adolescence and young adulthood several years before the first psychotic episode (i.e., prodromal stage). Immediately after onset (i.e., onset/progressive stage), without timely clinical interventions, progressive and clinical deterioration may occur, leading ultimately to a chronic, residual stage. This “natural history of the illness” (progressing through premorbid, prodromal, onset/progressive, and chronic residual stages) has provided the rationale for early detection and intervention strategies that we noted in the “Predominantly Psychotic Disorders” in the SA Axis (Chapter 9, pp. 523–626; see also, Lieberman et al. (2019). Interventions recommended by international guidelines encompass coordinated specialty care programs for first-­episode psychosis, cognitive-­behavioral therapy for psychosis, psychoeducation, supported employment services, and community treatment for those with poor adherence history who suffer frequent social disruption or relapse. Psychosocial treatments include family intervention for individuals with ongoing family ties, acceptance and commitment therapy (Hayes, Strosahl, & Wilson, 2016), interventions aimed at improving self-­management abilities and promoting person-­oriented recovery, cognitive remediation, social skills training for those seeking improved social functioning, and supportive psychotherapy (Keepers et al., 2020). Although psychodynamic treatments for psychotic disorders have been understudied, adding mentalization-­based treatment for psychotic disorder to “treatment as usual” may achieve better social functioning, particularly for patients with recent psychotic onset (Weijers et al., 2021). International practice guidelines (Keepers et al., 2020; NICE, 2014) recommend that patients with schizophrenia be treated with an antipsychotic medication and monitored for effectiveness and side effects (Huhn et al., 2019). Clinicians should discuss the treatment plan with patients in a shared decision-­making process and should avoid exclusively pharmacological treatment without concomitant psychosocial treatments. Long-­standing convictions that schizophrenia is a chronic and deteriorating illness of the brain has resulted in unwarranted pessimism about psychotherapy for psychosis (Garrett, 2019). Contrary to the common misconception that the prognosis of schizophrenia is invariably poor, outcome is highly variable. Meta-­analytic outcome research on studies that included 20 or more years of follow-­up with patients diagnosed with schizophrenia found that 59.7% had either a “moderate” outcome, a “good” outcome, or even “recovery” (24.2%) (Molstrom et al., 2022). In conclusion, the treatment of schizophrenia should be ambitious. Outreach programs can promote early detection, reducing the duration of untreated psychosis (in the onset/progressive stage) and promoting preventive interventions for at-risk individuals (in the prodromal stage). Specialty care programs should include psychotherapy and multidisciplinary interventions to recover and restore the patient’s interpersonal and social network. The Subjective Experience of Psychosis While the previous depictions of the various nonaffective psychotic disorders identify qualities that are observable in those afflicted, they do not capture the internal world of individuals suffering from any of these conditions. Psychological treatments assume sufficient overlap between ordinary mental life and psychopathological states to allow

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 777
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 777 the clinician some empathic understanding of the subjective experience of psychosis. Melanie Klein’s (1946) formulation of the paranoid–­schizoid position that dominates early mental life has been found useful in understanding the primitive mental states expressed in both psych...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 777 the clinician some empathic understanding of the subjective experience of psychosis. Melanie Klein’s (1946) formulation of the paranoid–­schizoid position that dominates early mental life has been found useful in understanding the primitive mental states expressed in both psychotic and nonpsychotic conditions. The subjective experience of psychosis is such that the usual four subcategories—­affect, cognition, somatic states, and interpersonal relations—­do not suffice. Based on phenomenological clinical descriptions, the “ipseity disturbance model” ascribes the pathogenetic core of schizophrenia to disruptions of self-­experience (Sass & Parnas, 2003). Such a fundamental distortion of the relation between the self and the world is grounded on the distinction between the “narrative self” and the “minimal self.” The “narrative self” refers to all characteristics (e.g., biological, cognitive, and characterological features) that depict any given individual and that, through language, may be shared. In contrast, the “minimal self” refers to a prereflective sense of subjectivity and familiarity with oneself, articulating the structure for the narrative level of the experience (Zandersen & Parnas, 2019). While nonpsychotic mental disorders may be attributed to impairments at the level of the narrative self, psychotic disorders have been ascribed to distortion of the minimal self. Within this framework, anomalous self-­experiences refer to nonpsychotic trait-like symptoms that precede the onset of schizophrenia spectrum disorders and show a high degree of temporal stability (Henriksen et al., 2021). There is convergence between this research paradigm and psychodynamic theories. Psychoanalytic concepts of “nameless dread” (Bion, 1957), “disintegration anxiety” (Kohut, 1971), “falling forever” (Winnicott, 1974), and “ontological insecurity” (Laing, 1965), for example, overlap significantly with the phenomenological experience of “ontological anxiety”: a pervasive feeling of weakness, inferiority, indecisiveness, poor tolerance for anxiety, and a sense that something ominous is going to unfold (Parnas et al., 2005b). Other contact points are observable between anomalous self-­experiences described in phenomenological models and accounts of psychoanalysts that focus on self-­experience, including those of Jung (1942, 1944), Kohut (1977, 1984), Bollas (1995), Auerbach and Blatt (1996), and Stern (1995). Ego Boundaries and Experience of Self Individuals generally experiences themselves as a first-­personal “I,” an agent with a personal point of view, embedded in an ongoing experience of the real world. Thoughts, feelings, and perceptions are tacitly felt to be one’s own, with no need for metacognitive self-­conscious reflection to establish a link between ongoing experience and oneself. One’s sense of self and experience of the world are seamlessly intertwined. In psychosis, this natural un-self-­conscious relationship between one’s internal world and the perceived world is disturbed. The full-­bodied first-­personal “I” fades and is replaced by an uncomfortably insubstantial “lightness of being.” Individuals in both the prodromal and acute phases of their illness may report a variety of anomalous subjective disturbances in their first-­person experience of the (minimal) self. These occur along a continuum in multiple domains, may come and go, or may remain relatively stable over extended periods. A classic expression is the blurring of ego boundaries. Such boundaries include differences among thoughts, feelings, and perceptions, and the boundaries between self and environment, self and others—­ the appreciation of where one begins and ends, of what is “me” versus “not me,” and

chunks.heading: 778 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 778 Adulthood so on. The boundary of the self may be so permeable that one’s identity merges with someone else’s, or one may come to feel transparent, vulnerable, an open book, easily invaded or read by others. Loss of ego boundary and perceptualization of inner speech (i.e., hearing one’s own thoug...
chunks.text: 778 Adulthood so on. The boundary of the self may be so permeable that one’s identity merges with someone else’s, or one may come to feel transparent, vulnerable, an open book, easily invaded or read by others. Loss of ego boundary and perceptualization of inner speech (i.e., hearing one’s own thoughts aloud internally) invites the delusion that others can hear one’s thoughts (i.e., thought broadcasting) or read and control one’s mind. Individuals in psychotic states may lose their sense of personal agency, doubting that they are the originators and authors of their thoughts. In extreme cases, they may believe that their thoughts are being inserted into their mind by an outside agency. They may say, “I feel like a ghost,” or “Do I really exist? Am I really here?” or “It feels like someone else is thinking my thoughts.” As the sense of self become frail or fades, a pathological hyperreflexive self-­awareness takes hold. Instead of a life where much in daily experience is familiar and can be comfortably taken for granted, little now is automatic or goes unquestioned. All life experience is simultaneously accompanied by a constant referral to a fragmented internal experience. For example, instead of simply enjoying the redness of a rose, one might reflexively ask, “Why is red called ‘red’?” When the ordinary automaticity of the familiar erodes, the psychological basis for opinion, motivation, and action is lost. Nothing can be assumed; everything must be considered. The person may feel flooded by choices, unable to make even mundane decisions, such as whether to have coffee or tea with breakfast. In this state of hypertrophied self-­awareness, instead of thinking thoughts and feeling feelings, mental activity takes on the quality of perceived objects. Thoughts may take on an uncanny auditory quality (as in the case of verbal hallucinations) and seem to have a spatial location not ordinarily associated with the ephemeral experience of thinking a thought: “I hear my thoughts as I am thinking them,” or “My voice and my thoughts feel alien to me,” or “All my thoughts seem to pass through a certain channel in my mind.” Whereas individuals suffering from nonpsychotic mental conditions might have the repetitive thought “I am a loser,” persons with a fundamental distortion at the level of the basic self might perceive that thought as a hallucinated “voice” saying, “You are a loser!” When experienced to a minor degree, this slipping away of the foundation of the self can be accompanied by a pervasive sense of anxiety, or anxiety that intensifies in the context of interpersonal contact; when felt to a marked degree, it may result in an overwhelming feeling of dread that, as we noted earlier, psychoanalysts have termed “annihilation anxiety.” People in psychotic states are preoccupied with the survival of the self rather than worried about the fate of the real people in their lives. They may feel they are fundamentally different from other human beings owing to some failing in themselves that excludes them from the human community. It is against this background of a globally altered change in self-­experience that the particulars of affect, perception, cognition, and somatic states ought to be interpreted. These disturbances of the basic (minimal) self have been extensively documented in schizophrenia spectrum disorders, at-risk mental states for psychosis, and schizotypal personality disorders, but not in nonpsychotic mental disorders and healthy controls (Henriksen et al., 2021). As we have noted, evidence indicates that anomalous self-­experiences may be core symptoms of schizophrenia spectrum disorders. This understanding allows for a holistic approach to the diagnosis that can refine the differential diagnosis, with implications for both prognosis (as anomalous self-­experiences have long-term temporal stability), and treatment (e.g., helping the patient verbalize anomalous self-­experiences that are hard to describe).

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 779
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 779 Affective States Affect may appear blunted to observers, or may be obscured internally such that people suffering psychosis have trouble naming their feelings. Yet it can at times be intense and overwhelming. What in a nonpsychotic person might be “signal anxiety” that triggers...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 779 Affective States Affect may appear blunted to observers, or may be obscured internally such that people suffering psychosis have trouble naming their feelings. Yet it can at times be intense and overwhelming. What in a nonpsychotic person might be “signal anxiety” that triggers a defense may in psychosis flood the person in a tidal wave of intense anxiety and consequent wishes to withdraw from the world into sleep, to fail to respond to stimuli while awake (catatonia), or to end one’s suffering with suicide. Terror is a core affective experience, both of the disintegration of the self (Bion’s [1962] “nameless dread,”) and of persecutors believed to intend harm. Overt rage at imagined enemies and suppressed rage over developmental impingements are common, as are despair, intense shame, deep sadness, and terrible loneliness. Perceptual Changes Perceptual changes in psychotic subjects may affect various sensory modalities and their subjective interpretation. People in psychotic states may have a derealized experience of the environment, in which the world is apprehended through a veil or fog. At the other extreme, there may be heightened sensory awareness of the outside world and of sensory changes. Colors may appear more vivid, and spatial relationships distorted, such that stepping off a curb into the street may feel like leaping off a cliff. Sounds may be more intense, too high or too low in pitch, or strange. Sounds from the environment may mix with speech perception, such that one is uncertain whether one is hearing the wind or voices whispering. The person’s attention may be drawn to fragments of the perceptual frame, such that ambient stimuli that would ordinarily go unnoted are now the focus of attention. At first these sensory changes may not be related to the self, but they inevitably take on personal meaning for the psychotic individual. Hallucinations occur when a person perceives something that does not exist. A hallucination is a “perception in the absence of a percept.” Although hallucinations in all modalities may occur, the most common are auditory verbal hallucinations (“voices”) perceived as originating outside the boundary of the self. These voices may resemble what happens in dissociative states. In psychosis, however, voices are better understood as arising from a noncohesive self, and not necessarily as voices of alternative self-­states. They are commonly critical, threatening, or commanding, causing great distress. Critical voices mock, degrade, and insult; threatening ones predict harm or punishment; command hallucinations often urge self-harm or suicide, and, less often, destruction or harm of others. Locutions are typically in the second person (insulting or commanding), or third-­person comments about the patient. Voices are typically felt as omnipotent and omniscient, and frighteningly beyond the patient’s control. In a minority of cases, voices depart from these patterns, at which point differential diagnosis becomes more important. Voices that are positive and supportive, telling individuals that they are special or have religious significance, may be more indicative of mania (see p. 485). Voices capable of conversation (such that talking back alters a voice’s message) may be more indicative of dissociative identity disorder (see p. 600), where helpful messages supplying information or advice are sometimes conveyed, though they may also include the insulting, threatening, and commanding

chunks.heading: 780 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 780 Adulthood voice of a self-state identified with a perpetrator of earlier abuse (identification with the aggressor). Cognitive Dysfunctions Formal Thought Disorder People suffering from psychosis may have trouble maintaining a focused, organized, goal-­directed train of thought. They may have dif...
chunks.text: 780 Adulthood voice of a self-state identified with a perpetrator of earlier abuse (identification with the aggressor). Cognitive Dysfunctions Formal Thought Disorder People suffering from psychosis may have trouble maintaining a focused, organized, goal-­directed train of thought. They may have difficulty accessing words when attempting to express thoughts or feelings, and may lose track of the main idea they wish to express. A range of disturbances may impair clear thinking, including reduced short-term memory, thought blocking (when no thought seems to come to mind), feeling swamped by a flood of pressured thoughts, and concrete or idiosyncratic phrases, images, and metaphors not easily understood by others. For example, the answer to the question “What do a bus and a bicycle have in common?” may be concrete, such as “Wheels,” or else bizarrely idiosyncratic, such as “They both achieve forward locomotion by an impetus imparted to the rear axle.” Such disorders of thought form are indicative of what was previously called hebephrenia or disorganized schizophrenia. Global Reality Sense, Memory, Orientation Episodic autobiographical memories may be fragmented or overly generalized, and working memory (necessary for problem solving) may be impaired. Time may appear to speed up, slow down, or lose its continuity. A patient may report never feeling fully awake, and complain of mental fatigue, clouded thinking, and lack of pleasure. People in psychotic states may feel less easily affected by and engaged in life events. Existential orientation to the world may shift. To compensate for this, a new interest in metaphysical, philosophical, or religious themes may take hold, at times accompanied by the belief that one is seeing more deeply into events than the average person. These themes are usually idiosyncratic and unshared with others. Positive Cognitive Symptoms Disorders of Thought Content There is a wide spectrum of severity. At the mild end are idiosyncratic beliefs, preoccupations, and magical thinking, which may occur in the prodromal period leading up to frank psychosis. Delusions often arise in the context of the psychotic person’s attempts to find an explanation for anomalous experiences, such as hallucinations, or to express sentiments too powerful and conflicted to be easily put into socially consensual language. Early on, there may be a state of “delusional mood,” a vague sense or anxious expectation that something is about to happen, that significant change is afoot. This may be followed by a delusion, which brings some cognitive “order” to the emotional chaos by bringing some closure or meaning to particular intrapsychic themes of importance to the patient. In ideas of reference, an otherwise normal perception is interpreted as having personal significance (e.g., “A television commentator looked right at me when he said the word ‘pathetic’ and I know he was talking about me”). The possibility of benign coincidence dissolves. Nothing is thought to happen by chance. A person might

chunks.heading: 782 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 782 Adulthood such as toothbrushing. The erosion of an integrated, central, first-­person “I” may result in splits in internal experience that might be described as a fragmentation of the self into a variety of internal psychological objects. A person might report, “The right side of my body is male...
chunks.text: 782 Adulthood such as toothbrushing. The erosion of an integrated, central, first-­person “I” may result in splits in internal experience that might be described as a fragmentation of the self into a variety of internal psychological objects. A person might report, “The right side of my body is male, and the left side of my body is female,” or “There is a cat in my stomach that controls my thoughts.” Hallucinations and delusions are elaborated into a delusional narrative, which often features a beleaguered self who is being assailed by a persecutor. Once a stable, systematized delusion has been established, various feelings of conflict and social alienation arise from the contrast between the person’s beliefs and consensual reality. Some psychotic individuals fight to impose their personal reality on the world, in which case they may feel anger toward society for opposing the outcomes they think their beliefs require. Others withdraw into a psychotic world that they struggle to hide from their family and the mental health establishment, in which case they may regard others with suspicion lest they intrude upon their private delusional world. Delusions of omnipotence (feelings of great power and importance that have gone unnoticed by other people or feelings of special powers of prophecy, mind reading, and mind control) and ideas of reference (feelings that everything that happens pertains to oneself) may be both comforting and terrifying, as such power is expected to evoke attacks by envious others. Relational Patterns Owing to their psychological history and current state of mind, persons with primarily psychotic disorders have experienced a succession of interpersonal disappointments and failures that leave them feeling socially incompetent. Feelings of being disoriented or “out of sync” with others may be pervasive. Because individuals suffering psychosis have difficulty accurately intuiting what other people are thinking and feeling (a problem often framed as a limitation or aberration in their “theory of mind”), they have problems negotiating social situations. They may have deep convictions of being unlikable or toxic and may on that basis be suicidal. Intense self-­disgust may produce the belief that one will inevitably be rejected or abandoned. People in psychotic states assume that their relational needs cannot and will not be met. They may give up hope of expressing their individuality and try to relate to others with a needy, clinging surrender (e.g., to a family member, therapist, or friend), or they may remain avoidant and suspicious. People close to a psychotic person may feel frightened, helpless, or exasperated, as their efforts to calm the individual fail, and their attempts to persuade the person of their own version of reality are rejected. Clinicians can understand that the patient is caught in a dire version of Schopenhauer’s “porcupine dilemma,” in which too much closeness with other human beings brings intense pain, and too much distance brings loneliness and isolation that impoverishes the spirit. Having found that past attempts to relate to others have occasioned intense anxiety and repeated failure, some psychotic persons withdraw from consensual reality and ordinary social contact into a solitary delusional world. Subjective Experience of Taking Antipsychotic Medication Although first- and second-­generation antipsychotic medications can ameliorate psychotic symptoms in some patients, these drugs may induce subjective changes. Effects

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 783
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 783 are especially pronounced for first-­generation antipsychotics; they are generally milder and more variable, and thus less predictable, for second-­generation antipsychotics, but these medications may cause a metabolic syndrome characterized by weight gain and high cholesterol....
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 783 are especially pronounced for first-­generation antipsychotics; they are generally milder and more variable, and thus less predictable, for second-­generation antipsychotics, but these medications may cause a metabolic syndrome characterized by weight gain and high cholesterol. Single doses of first-­generation antipsychotic medication in nonpsychotic volunteers induce flattened mental states akin to the negative symptoms of schizophrenia. These medications may also exert an emotional numbing effect that induces a relative state of indifference (a state that may be welcomed by people suffering intense emotional pain). Rather than changing a delusional belief, a person might report being less bothered by the delusional idea: “I still believe it, but I don’t think about it as much anymore.” This state of relative indifference to psychotic symptoms may be accompanied by a more generally diminished capacity to experience pleasure and may contribute to social withdrawal. Subjective Experience of Recovery Over the last few decades, some first-­person accounts of people diagnosed with schizophrenia have shed light on the subjective experience of “recovery” (e.g., Greenberg, 1964; Lauveng, 2012; Reichbart, 2022; Saks, 2008). This term does not connote the traditional meaning of full clinical remission, but a subjective experience of being relieved of symptoms and severe impairments in social, occupational, and relational areas. Subjective Experience of the Therapist Clinicians’ understanding of the inner experience of persons suffering from psychosis emerges from a complex process of emotional and verbal exchanges. At the individual level, countertransference experience may vary along a continuum that runs from immediate and irrational aversion to dedicated and affectionate interest. Early in the last century, Karl Jaspers opined that delusional perception is not psychologically reducible—­that is, it cannot be understood as emerging from the content of previous mental states. Henricus Cornelius Rümke (1941) termed the praecox Gefühl or “praecox feeling” referring to the rejecting, distancing reaction of the clinician to a seemingly alien quality in the patient. The opposite reaction has been described by the term “empathism” (as opposed to the more balanced, spontaneous feeling of empathy) and conceptualized as the result of a reciprocal “narcissistic seduction” within the patient–­therapist dyad, or as a reaction formation to the “hate” that may arise in countertransference toward psychotic individuals. Bertram Karon (2003) underscored the difficulty a therapist may have empathizing with the intense suffering experienced by psychotic patients. Similar extreme reactions can be observed at the group level. Individuals with psychosis are often treated in a group therapy format, most often by a treatment team affiliated with an inpatient facility. If not attended to, “basic-­assumption group” functioning may divert the clinicians from the primary patient care task to the institutional group itself, resulting in a collective loss of purpose, the feeling that the group’s work is meaningless, discouragement, and staff burnout. To understand the emotional swings that may occur in reaction to psychosis, and how or to what extent these swings may affect clinical judgment, clinicians should consider not only individual transference and countertransference processes but also collective group attitudes that may result in messianic, dependent, or belligerent dynamics (where the patient group serves as the

chunks.heading: 784 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 784 Adulthood target for the team’s projections of deficits and failures, thus functioning as the team’s scapegoat). The basic emotional and affective disturbances that characterize the inner experience of a patient with psychosis may resonate at a deep level with a clinician’s mental functioning. T...
chunks.text: 784 Adulthood target for the team’s projections of deficits and failures, thus functioning as the team’s scapegoat). The basic emotional and affective disturbances that characterize the inner experience of a patient with psychosis may resonate at a deep level with a clinician’s mental functioning. The resulting intense reactions and primitive emotions can be either a precious occasion for learning or a source of personal distress and professional dysfunction. Countertransference reactions, along that continuum between polar extremes, may include the following: 1. Interplay between the patient’s psychotic and nonpsychotic partial components of the self may blur the clinician’s own distinction between “normal” and “psychotic,” and stir up a terror of becoming crazy. Conversely, it may evoke a better understanding of the overlap of psychotic and nonpsychotic elements in our common humanity. 2. The patient’s “need–fear” dilemma, which deeply interferes with their ability to be involved in close relationships (the “porcupine dilemma”), may amplify the clinician’s ambivalence about intimacy and trigger loathsome or disdainful withdrawal from the patient. Conversely, it may enhance the clinician’s ability to feel comfortable with balancing closeness and distance with the patient. 3. The ambivalent intertwining in psychosis between love and hate may frighten a clinician and keep the person at a distance. Conversely, it may become a field of relational experience in which unforeseen opportunities of communication may be created. 4. Difficulties in reaching the patient within the person’s internal retreat may evoke a dysfunctional and overconfident feeling that progress and symptom recovery can be readily achieved. Conversely, it may prompt the clinician’s insight into the magnitude of the work needed to engage the patient in individual treatment and in rehabilitation programs. Patients’ lack of insight may present clinicians with a peculiar countertransference challenge. They may feel confounded by a person’s seeming inability to grasp a reality that appears perfectly obvious to everyone else. Patients often do not realize they are suffering from a mental illness, and delusional beliefs that are patently absurd to others appear literally true to them. Faced with these patients’ inability to affirm the obvious, clinicians may take on the role of attempting to represent “consensual reality,” but at the cost of dispensing with efforts to understand primary gain (e.g., the psychodynamic etiology underlying a delusion that makes it psychologically preferable to the alternative, whatever that might be). One ought to avoid becoming that English speaker who, in trying to communicate with a nonspeaker of English, simply raises one’s voice, speaks in a more insistent tone, and dramatically gesticulates. In addition to the previously described reactions, other countertransference feelings or attitudes may arise in a relationship with a patient suffering from psychosis. For example, when asked to certify the patient’s eligibility for government-­sponsored support or care programs, the clinician may have difficulty sorting out the patient’s primary psychological inability (which would elicit sympathy, though this is not equivalent to primary gain) from secondary gain (which might elicit a negative countertransference reaction). Secondary gain is often overemphasized, and primary gain is

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 785
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 785 often neglected entirely. The therapist may feel deeply gratified by the intense work with the patient, to the point of having difficulties in recognizing and encouraging the patient’s progress, and also in encouraging a reduced frequency of sessions and termination of treatmen...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 785 often neglected entirely. The therapist may feel deeply gratified by the intense work with the patient, to the point of having difficulties in recognizing and encouraging the patient’s progress, and also in encouraging a reduced frequency of sessions and termination of treatment. The clinician may notice a diminished interest in the patient as the dramatically fascinating flare of the psychosis fades and a more deeply defended “normality,” dominated by less interesting negative symptoms rather than dramatic positive ones, ensues. Finally, a quite common response of clinicians working with psychotic ennui is a deep sense of boredom, sleepiness, difficulty thinking, and tendency toward dream-like thoughts and images that come up but are difficult to remember and put together, even after a few moments. Clinical Illustration A 21-year-old college student became undone when a man she had been dating broke up with her. She felt devastated, anxious, unable to sleep, and then oppressed by a mounting sense of impending doom. She began to notice strangers looking at her in a way she believed indicated that they knew intimate details of her relationship with her former boyfriend. She feared that other students were in league with him and talking about her, and she began hearing a “voice” telling her she was a loser. Her friends found her suddenly uncommunicative. She felt little need to explain her feelings; she thought people could read her mind. Her roommate escorted her to the emergency room, where she was admitted with a diagnosis of schizophreniform disorder. S2 Mood Disorders Like DSM-5, DSM-5-TR divides mood disorders into “depressive disorders” and “bipolar and related disorders.” Depressive disorders are further subdivided into disruptive mood dysregulation disorder, major depressive disorder, persistent depressive disorder (which now includes both dysthymic disorder and chronic major depressive disorder), premenstrual dysphoric disorder, substance/medication-­induced depressive disorder, and other specified/unspecified depressive disorder. The bipolar disorders include bipolar I, bipolar II, cyclothymic disorders, and other specified/unspecified bipolar and related disorders.
