Despite this clear delineation, the clinical presentation of virtually all depressive and bipolar disorders is markedly heterogeneous; “textbook cases” appear to be quite rare in clinical practice. Unlike DSM-5-TR, ICD-11 includes within the mood disorders category both bipolar disorders (i.e., bipolar type I disorder, bipolar type II disorder, cyclothymic disorder, and other specified–­unspecified bipolar or related disorders) and depressive disorders (i.e., single-­episode depressive disorder, recurrent depressive disorder, dysthymic disorder, mixed depressive and anxiety disorder, premenstrual dysphoric disorder, and other specified–­unspecified depressive disorders). Cyclothymia, which was in a separate grouping in ICD-10, is now incorporated in the core bipolar or related disorders group. Further on, the addition of a “mixed depressive and anxiety disorder” is of special interest, because the presence of a significant anxiety component in a depressive episode has been found to be associated with higher suicide risk, longer duration of illness, and a greater likelihood of nonresponse to treatment (Stein et al., 2020).

chunks.heading: 786 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 786 Adulthood S2.1 Persistent Depressive Disorder (Dysthymia) DSM-II’s depressive neurosis became DSM-III’s and DSM-IV’s dysthymic disorder; in DSM-5 and DSM-5-TR, it has been relabeled as persistent depressive disorder, a broader category that implies more significant clinical impact than former la...
chunks.text: 786 Adulthood S2.1 Persistent Depressive Disorder (Dysthymia) DSM-II’s depressive neurosis became DSM-III’s and DSM-IV’s dysthymic disorder; in DSM-5 and DSM-5-TR, it has been relabeled as persistent depressive disorder, a broader category that implies more significant clinical impact than former labels. As we noted previously, although DSM-5-TR’s persistent depressive disorder is a relatively homogeneous category, patients with this diagnosis are a heterogeneous population whose common denominator is the persistence of symptoms beyond the time that defines an “episode.” In ICD-11, this condition is characterized by a persistent depressive mood (i.e., lasting 2 years or more) accompanied by additional symptoms such as markedly diminished interest or pleasure in activities, reduced concentration and attention or indecisiveness, low self-worth or excessive or inappropriate guilt, or hopelessness about the future. Chronic depression may be masked by physical symptoms such as sleep and appetite disturbance (decreased or increased), fatigue, headaches, or chronic pain—­ complaints often brought to family doctors. The depressive symptoms may gradually accumulate and coalesce into persistent depressive disorder. Characterological pessimism may predispose a person to a low-grade, persistent depressive disorder. Life events, stressors, early caregiving deficits, and problematic family functioning may influence its onset. Acute symptoms are often precipitated by later events and may significantly impair social, academic, or occupational activities and health. The research literature highlights correlations among brain development, stressful environmental factors, and depressive conditions. Given this multifactorial pathogenesis, evaluation of depressed patients should always include an assessment of psychological attitudes and/or personality traits, together with family and environmental factors involved in both the precipitation and perpetuation of these long-­lasting depressive states. From a psychodynamic perspective, these factors are broadly related to loss or neglect (and thus link with anaclitic depression), which causes feelings of loneliness, helplessness, hopelessness, emptiness, and boredom, as well as abandonment anxiety. Self-­criticism and guilt may arise from unconscious conclusions that it was one’s badness that caused the loss or neglect. Concomitant defenses are introjection and turning hostility against the self. In the clinical literature, cases of long-­standing depressive symptoms are characterized by low self-­ esteem and disturbances in affect regulation. See also the discussions in the P Axis of depressive (and hypomanic) personalities and of masochistic (self-­defeating) personalities (Chapter 12, pp. 651–708). Given the complex causes of persistent depressive disorder, patients who seek help with continuing symptoms are not likely to respond adequately to pharmacotherapy; most of them need integrated psychotherapeutic treatment. S2.2 Major Depressive Disorder Serious depression is a common problem, affecting about 185 million people globally. The estimated prevalence of diagnosed major depressive disorder varies markedly: A recent review by the World Mental Health Survey reported that lifetime prevalence was ~10%, but because it was based on retrospective assessments, it underestimated true prevalence. Prospective studies have suggested a lifetime prevalence of >30%. Studies of shorter time frames have found an estimated 12-month prevalence of 4–5%

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 787
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 787 globally (Marx et al., 2023). It is commonly supposed that major depressive disorder begins during adolescence, but epidemiological evidence suggests that its onset may be as early as the preschool years. In any case, recent studies indicate that the prevalence of this conditio...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 787 globally (Marx et al., 2023). It is commonly supposed that major depressive disorder begins during adolescence, but epidemiological evidence suggests that its onset may be as early as the preschool years. In any case, recent studies indicate that the prevalence of this condition increases markedly during adolescence and young adulthood. Symptoms develop over days to weeks. Some people have only a single episode, with a full return to premorbid functioning. More than 50% of those who initially suffer a single major depressive episode, however, eventually develop another. Major depressive disorder is not just a form of extreme sadness. It is a medical condition that affects both brain and body, including cognition, behavior, the immune system, and the peripheral nervous system. Unlike transient sadness, it is considered a disorder because it interferes with ordinary functioning in work, school, or relationships. Loss of a loved one is a common precipitant, although major depressive disorder should not be confused with pathological bereavement. DSM-IV-TR had an exclusion criterion for a major depressive episode if symptoms lasted less than 2 months following a major bereavement. DSM-5 has somewhat controversially omitted this “bereavement exclusion,” as the omission could lead to an artifactual increase in the measured prevalence of depression. DSM-5 argues, however, that bereavement is a major life stressor that may precipitate major depressive disorder, and that the resulting clinical syndrome takes precedence over the purported etiology. In DSM-5-TR, the category of “prolonged grief disorder” has been added within “trauma- and stressor-­related disorders.” Conversely, in ICD-11, the diagnosis of major depressive disorder is not excluded in a person who is bereaved, but the threshold for that diagnosis is higher. From a psychodynamic point of view, the presumed causal effect of bereavement on major depressive disorder makes sense, especially among individuals prone to depression prior to losing loved ones. Although depression may deal a devastating blow to one’s functioning, many high-­functioning people are severely depressed; they often hide their plight behind behaviors such as overworking, substance-­related or behavioral addictions, and aggressiveness. Such “masked depression,” more common among men, suggests psychodynamic notions such as “manic defenses” and “narcissistic rage.” The medical definition of depression is a sustained abnormality in a person’s mood, or feelings of despair, hopelessness, and negative cognitions about the self and world. A major depressive episode is defined as a period lasting at least 2 weeks, in which a person feels depressed mood or becomes unable to experience any pleasure, accompanied by some of the following: changes in sleep patterns, changes in appetite, changes in sexual desire, loss of interest in things that were previously interesting, loss of pleasure in life (anhedonia), loss of energy, inability to concentrate, slowing of reflexes and bodily movements (psychomotor retardation), feelings of guilt, and suicidal ideation or planning. Overt behaviors and symptoms include crying spells, loss of interest in previously enjoyable activities, indifference to social interaction, neglect of personal care and physical appearance, passive or withdrawn behavior, restlessness, and slowed movement, thought, and/or speech. The quality, intensity, and disruptive nature of the symptoms are most relevant clinically. Depression is a condition that can vary in intensity from relatively mild to extremely severe, from a subtle experience to a severely disabling disorder. Depression also appears to be a leading cause of suicidality. A relatively rare “psychotic

chunks.heading: 788 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 788 Adulthood depression,” complicated by delusions (e.g., the belief that one is someone else, or that one must kill oneself to purge the world of evil) and/or hallucinations (e.g., hearing voices that savagely criticize the self or command one to die), is not unknown to clinicians. Thus, the reali...
chunks.text: 788 Adulthood depression,” complicated by delusions (e.g., the belief that one is someone else, or that one must kill oneself to purge the world of evil) and/or hallucinations (e.g., hearing voices that savagely criticize the self or command one to die), is not unknown to clinicians. Thus, the reality distortions accompanying depression may vary along a very broad continuum. A careful assessment for major depressive disorder includes screening for medical problems and asking questions about what else may be going on in a person’s life that might produce depression-­like symptoms. Several medical conditions (including cancer, diabetes, hypo- or hyperthyroidism, multiple sclerosis, Parkinson’s disease, head trauma, chronic pain, hepatitis, HIV-­related conditions, and other infectious diseases) may cause symptoms that resemble depression. Use of steroids or withdrawal from cocaine, alcohol, or amphetamines may also produce depressive reactions. Finally, normal bereavement may resemble depression, except that in normal grief, the painful mood state tends to come in waves, between which there is normal functioning; also, the source of pain is subjectively located in a failure of one’s world (loss or disappointment in an external reality) rather than in a failure or inadequacy of oneself. There are various relatively elusive forms of this disorder. “Seasonal affective disorder syndrome” denotes depression that recurs annually, usually during the winter months. “Agitated depression” pertains to depression accompanied by nervousness and agitation, as opposed to a prototypical “lethargic depression,” and may be better classified as belonging to the bipolar disorder spectrum. “Double depression” involves repeated occurrences of major depressive episodes superimposed upon persistent depressive disorder. Finally, “depression with atypical features” is characterized by mood reactivity (mood brightens in response to actual or potential positive events), interpersonal rejection sensitivity resulting in significant social or occupational impairment, and hypercortisolism. While the first of these conditions might lead a professional to overlook depression, the last three might persuade a clinician that a patient is “beyond cure.” This is particularly the case in patients with double depression, where an acute depressive episode abates but the patients return to a baseline dysthymic state, feeling demoralized and thinking, “Is this all the treatment can do for me?” Such demoralization may evoke hostile countertransference reactions. Subjective Experience of Depressive Disorders The subjective experience of individuals with depressive disorders is complex. Because the clinical picture is often “masked” by somatic states, physical symptoms, or anxiety, a clear distinction among affective, cognitive, somatic, and relational patterns is hard to draw. Affective states of depressed individuals include two general psychic orientations that have been termed, respectively, “anaclitic” and “introjective” (Blatt, 2004). Anaclitic depressive patterns, frequently associated with neglect or a disrupted relationship with a primary caregiver, are characterized by feelings of helplessness, weakness, inadequacy, guilt, and depletion; fears of being abandoned, isolated, and unloved; struggles to maintain physical contact with a need-­gratifying person; wishes to be soothed, held, fed, and protected; difficulty tolerating delay and postponement; difficulty expressing anger (for fear of destroying the other as a source of satisfaction); and valuing caregivers only for their capacity to provide needed gratification.

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 789
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 789 Individuals with strong anaclitic tendencies appear to experience others as unreliable and unstable: Their defenses tend to shut anger down as they struggle to maintain a sort of interpersonal harmony. Introjective depressive patterns are characterized by harsh, punitive, unrel...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 789 Individuals with strong anaclitic tendencies appear to experience others as unreliable and unstable: Their defenses tend to shut anger down as they struggle to maintain a sort of interpersonal harmony. Introjective depressive patterns are characterized by harsh, punitive, unrelenting self-­criticism; feelings of inferiority, worthlessness, and guilt; a sense of having failed to live up to expectations and standards; fears of loss of approval, recognition, and love from important others; and fears of the loss of acceptance of one’s assertive strivings. Self-­esteem suffers badly under such onslaughts. Cognitive patterns may include rationalized conviction of guilt; fantasies of loss of approval, recognition, and love; inability to make decisions; low self-­regard; suicidal ideas; and impaired memory. Individuals with strong introjective tendencies appear to experience others as punitive and judgmental, and to employ “counteractive” defenses that deny dependency and favor autonomy and separation. While both anaclitic and introjective tendencies have been implicated in psychopathology in general and depression in particular, self-­criticism—­what Huprich has called “malignant self-­regard” (Huprich & Malone, 2022)—confers increased risk of numerous psychopathologies and for suicidality. Research indicates that self-­criticism specifically increases “active vulnerability.” Individuals with strong self-­critical tendencies tend to generate negative interpersonal conditions (e.g., stressful events, lack of positive events, and absence of social support), which in turn lead to depression. The manifestation of this process in therapy, consistent with psychodynamic notions of “projective identification” and “externalization,” has been empirically tested and corroborated. Thus, the treatment of self-­critical, depressed individuals is likely to be tumultuous, characterized by therapeutic ruptures and rifts. It is likely to involve strong countertransference reactions, including therapists’ doubting and criticizing themselves. Another development in research on depression is the line of inquiry about its scarring effects. Formulated within the cognitive perspective but consistent with psychodynamic theory, the scarring hypothesis posits that depression adversely affects personality similarly to a scar that forms around a wound. Although initial research failed to confirm this supposition, the past decades have seen numerous studies attesting to the adverse effect of depression on the self-­concept. Thus, not only does pathology in the self set the groundwork for depression, but depression, arguably acting as a persecutory object, also erodes the self. Clinical Illustration A woman in her late 40s, previously an avid reader, described apathy and feelings of guilt, unreliability, and failure for having given up her job as a nurse in order to mother her twin sons, now 13 years old. “I know that I had a lot to enjoy in life, and that I’ve been a good mother for my babies all along. My sons are doing well at school; my relationship with my husband and family is really good; but I still don’t feel well within myself, for no good reason.” She adds, “I can’t read a single page of any of my books. Sometimes I try, but I can’t concentrate. And my children—­I know I love them, but sometimes I feel as if I have no feelings for them anymore.” She ruminated about finding a job, but felt she would not be able to handle both home and work. She said, “I have failed as a woman and as a mother. I should probably accept this and admit that I am only a burden on everyone; I have lost my self-­respect.”

chunks.heading: 790 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 790 Adulthood S2.3 Cyclothymic Disorder Cyclothymic disorder is diagnosed when a patient has suffered for at least 2 years from recurrent mood fluctuations (i.e., hypomanic and/or depressive symptoms) that have been insufficiently severe or persistent to be classified as bipolar disorder or major de...
chunks.text: 790 Adulthood S2.3 Cyclothymic Disorder Cyclothymic disorder is diagnosed when a patient has suffered for at least 2 years from recurrent mood fluctuations (i.e., hypomanic and/or depressive symptoms) that have been insufficiently severe or persistent to be classified as bipolar disorder or major depressive episodes. It can appear as early as adolescence, with an average age of onset similar to that of bipolar disorder (early 20s), with no observed significant assigned sex-­related difference. In ICD-11, the cyclothymia rubric includes cycloid and cyclothymic personalities. This addition echoes the Research Diagnostic Criteria that influenced DSM-III and earlier literature on the effect of temperament, suggesting a bridge between the core bipolar and unipolar mood disorders and the mood instability so commonly seen in borderline and other personality disorders. The subjective experience of patients with cyclothymic disorder may include being out of control of their mood variations and subsequent behavior. An additional subjective aspect is the experience of an unstable sense of self. People with cyclothymic disorder oscillate between feeling weak and strong, competent and incompetent, and so on. Once they understand that this oscillation stems from the effect of fluctuating moods on their self-state, the confusion is often abated. Even though symptom and behavior severity do not appear at first glance as severe as in bipolar disorder, social roles and relational functioning may be affected quite seriously. S2.4 Bipolar Disorders Bipolar disorders involve fluctuating mood states, generally characterized as hypomanic, manic, depressive, or mixed. While diagnostic criteria for bipolar depression are the same as for major depressive disorder, the depressive episodes of patients with bipolar disorder more often include agitated or atypical aspects, which may be understood as the simultaneous presence of some manic features. In both DSM-5-TR and ICD-11, diagnosis requires manic, hypomanic, and major depressive episodes or symptoms. Patients with bipolar disorder are said to be “rapid cycling” if there has been a high frequency of mood episodes (at least four) over the past 12 months. There may be a switch from one polarity of mood to the other, or the mood episodes may be demarcated by a period of remission (WHO, 2019). Faster cycling suggests another diagnosis (e.g., switching in a patient with dissociative identity disorder), or else mixed states in which depressive and manic symptoms co-occur. Patients falling outside these narrow constraints should be treated according to the main presenting pathology. “Pure” mania is more common in the early years of the illness; mixed states are more common in later years, when euphoria may be replaced with oppressive hostility. Bipolar disorders are characterized by the presence of at least one episode with manic features (full mania, mixed features, or hypomania), usually in addition to a depressive episode. Manic episodes involve an extreme mood state lasting at least 1 week unless shortened by a treatment intervention characterized by euphoria, irritability, or expansiveness, and by increased activity or a subjective experience of increased energy, accompanied by other characteristic symptoms such as rapid or pressured speech, flight of ideas, increased self-­esteem or grandiosity, decreased need for sleep, distractibility, impulsive or reckless behavior, and rapid changes among different mood states (i.e., mood lability) (WHO, 2019). In full manic episodes, psychotic symptoms may be present, including delusions and hallucinations that are usually congruent with the mood states. Formal thought disorders may also be present.

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 791
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 791 Diagnosis of bipolar disorder requires longitudinal observation, as the disorder may begin with a major depressive episode. Bipolar I disorder involves both a full manic episode and major depressive episode, whereas bipolar II disorder requires the presence and/or history of at...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 791 Diagnosis of bipolar disorder requires longitudinal observation, as the disorder may begin with a major depressive episode. Bipolar I disorder involves both a full manic episode and major depressive episode, whereas bipolar II disorder requires the presence and/or history of at least one major depressive episode and the existence and/ or history of hypomanic episodes. Average age at onset is in the early 20s, with no gender-­related differences. Family studies suggest a strong genetic influence. Perhaps the greatest challenge in diagnosing bipolar disorders is their similarity symptomatically and phenomenologically to some expressions of low-level borderline personality organizations (including psychopathic, narcissistic, and histrionic personalities, as well as emotionally dysregulated personalities; see the P Axis, Chapter 12 [pp. 651–708]). These personality styles/disorders, characterized by mood instability, are highly prevalent in patients suffering from all forms of bipolar disorders and consequently may complicate differential diagnosis. A common diagnostic error is to determine that a narcissistic or histrionic personality organization exists when a patient’s uncontrolled behavior is driven by hypomanic mood states. Subjective Experience of Bipolar Disorders Affective States In manic states, a person may feel intense euphoria or intense irritability, often with transient agitation and hypersensitivity to real and expected insult and rejection. Mania is characterized by excessive energy that may be experienced positively or negatively, as a disruptive and distracting internal pressure, or as infinite power, ability, and creativity. In either case, the internal overreaction may be accompanied by impulsive, risky behavior. Patients experiencing manic states often report increased desire—­ even desperate cravings—­for others, frequently accompanied by intense sexual desire and loss of social inhibition. Individuals with mania may have feelings of being strained, fragmented, and restless, as well as feelings of utter elation and absolute well-being. Their rapid mood fluctuations are attended by equally rapid fluctuations in the experience of self. Thus, an individual may feel sullen, useless, and agitated for a time—then, immediately afterward, like a hero, a prophet, or a master of the universe. These changes may be sudden, unpredictable, and uncontrollable. There can be feelings of loss when the mania dissipates—­a yearning for the emotional intensity, ecstasy, and productivity. As a consequence, many individuals with manic tendencies seek out “upper” drugs (i.e., cocaine or methamphetamines) to intensify or maintain the manic euphoria. Cognitive Patterns Cognitive patterns may include fantasies of invincibility and exceptional, unrecognized talent; a sense of proficiency at any task; and wishes for fame or adoration. Individuals in severe manic or mixed states also have difficulties in thinking clearly, logically, and linearly. They may be concerned about not being able to hold onto their flighty and pressured thoughts. Sometimes they may feel disoriented, unable to identify which of their racing thoughts should take precedence; at other times, they may experience the turmoil of thoughts as highly pleasant. They frequently express the contents of their mind without self-­censorship or inhibition. Psychoanalytic theory and research have construed these processes in terms of denial and use of narcissistic defenses. This

chunks.heading: 792 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 792 Adulthood interpretation is consistent with the aforementioned findings that hypomanic mood states may be associated with narcissistic (and histrionic) features. Somatic States Somatic states may include restlessness, sleeplessness, and increased energy. Some patients in a manic state insist tha...
chunks.text: 792 Adulthood interpretation is consistent with the aforementioned findings that hypomanic mood states may be associated with narcissistic (and histrionic) features. Somatic States Somatic states may include restlessness, sleeplessness, and increased energy. Some patients in a manic state insist that sleep is literally a waste of time. They may feel full of energy and describe a need to “Never stop!” Sexual desire is frequent and intense. Physical exhaustion following manic states of any intensity may come with serious risk of suicidal depression. Relational Patterns Relationships are often unpredictable, chaotic, impulsive, and highly sexualized. Some people with manic features inspire followers and protégés, whose moods are elevated by sharing in the manic person’s grand schemes. In this context, we note that the psychodynamic treatment of bipolar states is likely to be daunting. Therapists may have trouble keeping up with manic and hypomanic phases, and may be bewildered by rapid shifts between these phases and depressed ones. The resulting confusion may spiral into countertransference hostility. Clinical Illustration An energetic 28-year-old woman with a previous diagnosis of major depressive disorder began feeling “low” as an intimate relationship was ending. Coffee and “herbal remedies” increased her energies and elevated her mood, but she also needed alcohol to quell excessive agitation. After a sleepless night, she was suddenly irritable and then elated. She became hyperactive and impulsively accepted several dates, on which she felt high sexual arousal. At huge expense, she immediately changed her whole wardrobe. Thinking she had a “special ability” at prophecy, she approached friends and colleagues to predict their futures. Her work functioning decreased significantly, as her hyperactivity compromised her focus. Her family helped her to enter an inpatient program and get medication. At the time, she was unable to sustain a psychotherapeutic relationship. S2.5 Maternal Mood Disorders During pregnancy, a mother commonly enters a mental state of “primary maternal preoccupation” (Winnicott, 1957) that involves heightened sensitivity toward, and intense identification with, her developing baby. With the baby’s birth, she develops a new mental organization that has been called the “motherhood constellation” (Stern, 1995). The psychic organization that emerges may be permanent, transient, or (most frequently) permanently evocable. The puerperium is a well-­recognized time of increased vulnerability to depression. Sociocultural evolution has led to a relative disappearance of the functional extended family that traditionally aided mothers; this support has not been replaced by any other social unit or health structure. Understanding this phase, and finding ways of supporting mothers in it, is crucial, because difficulties during pregnancy and in the postpartum period have a profound impact on the mother–­baby dyad and on the

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 793
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 793 baby’s developing mental organization. Recent research indicates, for example, that home visits to teenage mothers enhance parenting, reduce maternal risks, and improve child developmental outcomes. In order of severity, three relevant conditions within the category of maternal...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 793 baby’s developing mental organization. Recent research indicates, for example, that home visits to teenage mothers enhance parenting, reduce maternal risks, and improve child developmental outcomes. In order of severity, three relevant conditions within the category of maternal mood disorders are maternity blues, postpartum depression, and puerperal psychosis. Professional consultation is recommended for maternity blues and considered mandatory for postpartum depression and puerperal psychosis. Maternity Blues The term “maternity blues” (or “baby blues”) denotes a common, relatively mild, transient mood disturbance. Dysphoria or mood swings peak between the third and fifth day after childbirth, with some cultural variation (e.g., symptoms may appear as late as the 10th day after labor). Prevalence varies between 26 and 80%, depending on culture and context (e.g., immigrants vs. nonimmigrants, socioeconomic stressors, high vs. low social support, wanted vs. unwanted pregnancy, good vs. poor vs. nonexistent marital relationship). Family histories of depression and personal histories of depression or premenstrual dysphoric disorder are common. Risk factors include stressful events, poor social adjustment, ambivalence in families, economic problems, and unplanned pregnancies. Symptoms may include tearful episodes, anxiety, fatigue, headache, emotional lability, irritability, depersonalization–­derealization, and depressed mood. Maternity blues is a common enough condition to be seen as within the normal spectrum. Both DSM-5-TR and ICD-11 state that because of its short duration, minimal functional impairment, and impact of physical changes after delivery, it should not be considered a mental disorder. Maternity blues may have serious consequences, however, including compromise of emotional closeness and maternal competence. Mothers can be fragile in the postpartum period and generally benefit from family ties, especially if these are not compromised by prior conflicts. Postpartum Depression Postpartum depression is a significant health problem affecting approximately 10–20% of new mothers yearly. DSM-5-TR represents it as a specifier for major depressive disorder: “with peripartum onset,” whereas ICD-11 lists postpartum depression within the “mental or behavioral disorders associated with pregnancy, childbirth or the puerperium” category, with or without co-­occurrent psychotic symptoms. It involves excessive crying; difficulty bonding with the baby; withdrawing from family and friends; loss of appetite or eating much more than usual; inability to sleep (insomnia) or sleeping too much (hypersomnia); overwhelming fatigue or loss of energy; reduced interest and pleasure in usually enjoyable activities, intense irritability and anger; fears of not being a good mother, feelings of worthlessness, shame, guilt or inadequacy; diminished ability to think clearly, concentrate or make decisions; thoughts of harming oneself or the baby (WHO, 2019). Thoughts of death or suicide are common, continuing beyond 2 weeks after delivery. Symptoms may occur right after birth and persist up to 1 year afterward. Women vulnerable to depression prior to delivery tend to have depressions that extend into the postpartum period. Undiagnosed major depressive disorder during pregnancy is the primary risk factor. Postpartum depression affects not only mothers but also their children, with immediate and long-term adverse consequences. It leads to insecure attachment patterns and

chunks.heading: 794 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 794 Adulthood increased risk of emotional problems in children. Clinical decisions must weigh the relative risks and benefits of antidepressant medication and breastfeeding; emotional and practical support to the family should be considered, as well as psychotherapy. Puerperal Psychosis Puerperal ps...
chunks.text: 794 Adulthood increased risk of emotional problems in children. Clinical decisions must weigh the relative risks and benefits of antidepressant medication and breastfeeding; emotional and practical support to the family should be considered, as well as psychotherapy. Puerperal Psychosis Puerperal psychosis (postpartum psychosis) is characterized by polymorphic delusions, alteration of consciousness, and affective symptoms of acute onset. Delusional themes generally relate to the child: Was this baby exchanged for another child? Was the baby’s gender changed? The delusion may be limited to denial: denial of the marriage or relationship, of motherhood, or of the child’s existence. Pan-­anxiety and ecstasy are also observed, sometimes followed by stupor and other catatonic symptoms. Altered states of consciousness may also occur: Psychotic symptoms may have dream-like features; there may be agitated somnambulistic features at night. Mother–­baby contact is invariably impaired, and breastfeeding is usually interrupted. In 80% of cases, puerperal psychosis involves manic or depressive symptoms. DSM-5-TR classifies the disorder under bipolar disorders with psychotic symptoms and peripartum onset, whereas ICD-11 listed mental or behavioral disorders associated with pregnancy, childbirth or the puerperium, with psychotic symptoms. A family history of mood disorder is quite common. Suicide risk is low, but risk for infanticide is elevated, often reflecting delusions about the child. The mother’s despair may spread to her family, and when family support is insufficient, hospitalization is commonly required. Subjective Experience of Maternal Mood Disorders Mental health professionals, as well as obstetricians and pediatricians, should ask about sadness and dysphoria in recent mothers. Although maternity blues are common and only mildly detrimental, postpartum depression and puerperal psychosis carry serious risks for both mother and child. Affective States Affective states may range, on the depressive spectrum, from transient episodes of tearfulness in maternity blues to frank depressed mood, mixed states, or manic states in postpartum depression and puerperal psychosis. Anxiety symptoms run from fears and mild somatic symptoms in maternity blues to frankly manic agitation in puerperal psychosis. Common feelings include sadness; loss of interest, especially in the baby; weakness and fatigue; guilt (“I think I will not be able to be a good mother”); extreme anxiety (“I need to rest, but I can’t”); and feelings of being overwhelmed and incapable of taking care of the baby or of doing usual activities (“I’m too exhausted to do anything well”). Cognitive Patterns Cognitive patterns may include identity disturbances, depersonalization, or obsessions and fears about the baby. Puerperal psychosis involves a more severe thought disturbance, including delusions, hallucinations, identity disorganization, stupor. Mothers may say things like “It’s a great responsibility for me . . . If I don’t feed him, he could

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 795
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 795 die,” or “Delivery was such a strange experience, I felt myself as not me, I felt like an animal.” In one case, a new mother was smiling into space; beside her a baby was crying inconsolably, but she did not react. Another mother had this delusion: “She isn’t my baby; my baby w...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 795 die,” or “Delivery was such a strange experience, I felt myself as not me, I felt like an animal.” In one case, a new mother was smiling into space; beside her a baby was crying inconsolably, but she did not react. Another mother had this delusion: “She isn’t my baby; my baby was stolen.” Somatic States Somatic states may include headaches, chest pains, heart palpitations, numbness, and hyperventilation. Depressive or manic states may induce insomnia or sleep disturbances, dysfunctional eating attitudes (e.g., restrictive behaviors or binge-­eating episodes), and psychomotor retardation or agitation, especially at night. Relational Patterns Relating to a newborn requires empathy. Mothers who suffer from maternal mood disorders have difficulty making contact with their babies, and this difficulty often precludes genuine emotional intimacy. New mothers need the support of partners and extended families to cope with their new role. The “motherhood constellation” is based primarily on identification with one’s own mother and the quality of one’s own mothering in early childhood. The following difficulties in the immediate mother–­ baby relationship are common, in increasing severity: difficulty in breastfeeding, awkward mother–­baby relationships, difficulty calming and soothing the baby, rejecting contact with the baby, and denying the baby’s reality. A new mother may also have difficulties with her close relations, including stress on the parental couple, as the baby’s needs come first, the mother’s second, and the father’s or partner’s are felt to come last. In second and subsequent births, there may be conflict with or problems posed by the other children because of their relative neglect. The mother’s work relationships may suffer as her career is interrupted, while her partner’s work may be barely affected. Subjective Experience of the Therapist All health care professionals start life as babies with variably adequate mothers; many have parented their own babies. Countertransference feelings are inevitably mediated by similarities and differences between the clinical challenges a patient poses and a clinician’s own history. Any clinician should be alarmed by an infant at risk. Against this background is the specific countertransference arising from the patient’s transference and, in severe cases, the patient’s use of primitive defenses. Some clinicians find themselves playing the role of surrogate caregiver, others, the role of surrogate partner. Clinicians need insight into what “belongs” to the patient and what “belongs” to themselves in order to maintain professional identity and provide effective care. Clinical Illustration A young mother was referred for difficulties in breastfeeding and care of her newborn. She had become pregnant after unprotected sex with her boyfriend, who left her on learning of the pregnancy. She was now living with her widowed mother in a small suburban apartment. Difficulties arose as soon as the baby came home. She did not know how to feed him, and he cried every night. The mental health team found the

chunks.heading: 796 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 796 Adulthood mother suffering from sadness, irritability, and threats of self-harm, and the grandmother suffering anxiety and insomnia. The home environment was chaotic, and the baby was always crying. The mother stated, “I’m not ready to be a mother; it’s all my fault. I can’t manage the situation...
chunks.text: 796 Adulthood mother suffering from sadness, irritability, and threats of self-harm, and the grandmother suffering anxiety and insomnia. The home environment was chaotic, and the baby was always crying. The mother stated, “I’m not ready to be a mother; it’s all my fault. I can’t manage the situation at home. . . . Sometimes I’m ready to explode, and I’ve actually had outbursts.” The team referred her to individual and group therapy. These improved the mother–­baby relationship and mitigated the grandmother’s anxiety and insomnia. S.3 Disorders Related to Anxiety This large grouping of anxiety-­related problems draws on ICD-10 and ICD-11 (“anxiety or fear-­related disorders”) and appreciates the contiguity of a number of DSM-5 and DSM-5-TR diagnoses and their corresponding ICD-11 codes. A major change in DSM-5 and DSM-5-TR is the “promotion” of obsessive–­compulsive and related disorders to a stand-alone diagnostic category, now independent of anxiety disorders. In contrast, ICD-11 has added a new grouping of “obsessive-­compulsive or related disorders.” For this reason and others, better explained in the S4 introductory paragraph, we have grouped these disorders in a separate category in PDM-3. Under the rubric of anxiety disorders, DSM-5 and DSM-5-TR include specific phobia, social anxiety disorder, panic disorder, agoraphobia, generalized anxiety disorder, substance/medication-­induced anxiety disorder, anxiety disorder due to another medical condition, and other specified and unspecified anxiety disorder. ICD-11 also listed selective mutism under this heading. Posttraumatic disorders (PTSD, acute stress disorder) now have their own grouping, again following ICD-10, ICD-11, and DSM5-TR. We follow this convention. Anxiety is fear in the absence of obvious danger. Psychodynamically oriented clinicians, who have found anxiety lurking behind virtually all psychopathological symptoms, have distinguished carefully between potential danger and present danger, between the evaluation of danger and the response to danger, and between an adaptive response to actual danger (which may or may not include fear/anxiety) and an anxiety response, in which the fight–­flight–­freeze system is activated in the expectation of disaster. Because some individuals suffer pervasive, chronic, and disabling anxieties with unclear onset, we regard many individuals currently diagnosed with generalized anxiety disorder as having a personality disorder rather than a clinical condition based on transient symptoms (see the discussion of anxious–­avoidant and phobic personalities in the P Axis, Chapter 12, pp. 651–708). The psychodynamic literature identifies different kinds of anxiety, any one of which may be conscious or unconscious. Separation anxiety is the fear of losing a love object. Castration anxiety, a Freudian term used generally by some and specifically by others, is fear of damage to the body and sometimes specifically to the genitals. Moral anxiety is fear of the consequences of transgressing one’s values. Annihilation anxiety (see below) includes the apprehension of being catastrophically overwhelmed, invaded, and destroyed. Fragmentation anxiety is fear of self-­disintegration. Persecutory anxiety and irrational fears of harm to loved ones are understood psychoanalytically as the outcome of denial, projection, and projective identification of one’s own hostile feelings in the former case, and of omnipotent destructive unconscious fantasies in the latter. In certain contexts, and in bearable amounts, some of these anxieties are

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 797
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 797 relatively common, but when they are unremitting or disproportionate, they may lead to psychopathology. Gender-­related differences in prevalence may vary, but is a consistent finding that women suffer about twice as many anxiety disorders as men do. Psychosocial variables (e.g...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 797 relatively common, but when they are unremitting or disproportionate, they may lead to psychopathology. Gender-­related differences in prevalence may vary, but is a consistent finding that women suffer about twice as many anxiety disorders as men do. Psychosocial variables (e.g., childhood sexual abuse, chronic stressors, more willingness to report anxiety to physicians and therapists), and genetic and neurobiological factors (e.g., hormones), have been seen as possible causes for this robust finding (Bandelow & Michaelis, 2015). Anxiety disorders tend to start in childhood, adolescence, or early adulthood, peak in middle age, then decrease with older age. During the maturation process, anxiety evolves from diffuse somatic excitation to pervasive psychic anxiety, to a more mature signaling function. “Signal anxiety,” a concept similar to “learned expectations” in contemporary learning theory, is a state of arousal signaling that past experience has identified an object or situation as a danger. It involves both anticipation and an attenuated level of affect. Subjective Experience of Anxiety In this section, we address anxiety as the core experience of the different anxiety-­ related disorders and also as a subjective, transdiagnostic experience and possible epiphenomenon of other clinical conditions. We emphasize that the affective states, cognitive patterns, somatic states, and relational patterns associated with anxiety overlap different disorders. Affective States Psychoanalytic thinkers often relate affective states in anxiety to Freud’s (1909a, 1909b) basic danger situations: fear of (1) loss of the object; (2) loss of the object’s love; (3) castration; and (4) loss of the approval of one’s superego. In less technical terms, these situations translate into fear of (1) loss of a significant other, resulting in feelings of abandonment expressed as anger, anxiety, depression, and/or guilt; (2) loss of love, experienced as rejection, and usually accompanied by rage, anxiety, depression, guilt, and feelings of being unworthy and even unlovable; (3) loss of bodily integrity and functioning; and (4) loss of affirmation and approval by one’s own conscience, resulting in anxiety, guilt, shame, or depression. In addition, there may be fear of loss of self-­regulation (e.g., loss of control of one’s feelings, thoughts, sensations, movements, actions). Anxiety associated with anticipating these dangers may be controlled or uncontrolled. When out of control, it may spark annihilation concerns. Annihilation anxiety originates very early but can be triggered at any point in life by threats to survival; it may arise without warning, in presymbolic form or in fantasies involving conflict and compromise formation, and may involve particularly recalcitrant resistance. The study of annihilation anxiety in relation to the basic danger series has both theoretical and clinical advantages, especially for understanding panic, generalized anxiety, and phobias. Fears typical of annihilation anxiety are of being overwhelmed, trapped, invaded, fragmented, merged, and destroyed; many of these relate to conscious and unconscious memories of early childhood adverse experiences. At its most severe, annihilation anxiety is felt as intolerable and may be associated with a breakdown of functioning and sense of loss of self.

chunks.heading: 798 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 798 Adulthood Cognitive Patterns Anxiety may come with a range of cognitive symptoms: poor concentration, difficulty focusing, distractibility, and poor memory. Basic fears (e.g., of separation, abandonment, rejection) are common; more severe and complex fears and related thoughts, such as of being...
chunks.text: 798 Adulthood Cognitive Patterns Anxiety may come with a range of cognitive symptoms: poor concentration, difficulty focusing, distractibility, and poor memory. Basic fears (e.g., of separation, abandonment, rejection) are common; more severe and complex fears and related thoughts, such as of being devoured or engulfed, of losing mental or bodily control, of falling in space, or of being injured and dying, are less frequent. Some patients have overwhelming catastrophic anxiety (e.g., during a panic attack) that compromises their sense of body boundaries and sense of identity. There may be intense worry over losing someone they depend on or of losing financial security. Fear of fear, also referred to as anticipatory anxiety, is common in those with panic attacks; they avoid certain objects, situations, or places for fear they will have another attack. Somatic States Somatic states include tension, sweaty palms, the sense of butterflies in one’s stomach or a tight band around one’s head, bladder and bowel urgency, and breathing difficulties. Anxiety disorders may be complicated by depersonalization–­derealization—­ feelings of disconnection from one’s body or from external reality. Anxiety can arise with varying degrees of autonomic or physiological arousal. Most individuals afflicted by anxiety experience hyperarousal. Relational Patterns Patterns of relationship may include expressions of fear of rejection, such as clinging and seeking reassurance; expressions of guilt, such as blaming oneself or others, or blame avoidance and expressions of conflicts and ambivalence about dependency, such as feelings that one is being smothered or suffocated, or that one is drowning or choking, and vacillation between pulling others closer and pushing them away. Subjective Experience of the Therapist Countertransference experiences vary widely. Clinicians may feel empathic involvement, parental concern, and the need to reassure their anxious patients. Or they may feel overwhelmed by the anxiety as they become objects of projective identification, especially in patients with severe personality disorder or psychosis. In response, they are at risk of defensively disengaging themselves from the relationship. It is common to feel frustrated while treating anxious or phobic patients, as reflected in the adage that “Any symptom, behavior pattern, or ideational content that serves in part as a defense against annihilation anxiety will be highly resistant to change.” Clinical Illustrations In the following, we discriminate among conditions that may involve the transdiagnostic experience of anxiety. Vignettes on specific anxiety disorders as listed at the beginning of this section will follow the specific diagnostic subsections. Here are some examples of anxiety as experienced by individuals at the neurotic or borderline level of organization:

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 799
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 799 “My mind is deluged with all sorts of frightening thoughts and images. My body is all nerves. I can’t sit down for any period of time; I’m constantly up and down. I feel like I’m going to crack up. At my job, I can’t do a thing; I just feel I can’t go on.” “I can’t stand the fe...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 799 “My mind is deluged with all sorts of frightening thoughts and images. My body is all nerves. I can’t sit down for any period of time; I’m constantly up and down. I feel like I’m going to crack up. At my job, I can’t do a thing; I just feel I can’t go on.” “I can’t stand the fear; I need comfort. Once fear steps in, I can’t see anything or do anything. There are so many things I do out of fear. I’m afraid I’ll fall apart, lose control, be taken over, be shot down with a machine gun. . . . The fear is always of something extreme happening. I won’t get smacked; I’ll get bombed. Some people fight or run away. When I’m cornered, I crumble.” “When I get involved [in a relationship], I feel I have no rights, no prerogatives. The other person can make decisions; I can’t. They can do what they want; I can’t. I feel like I can’t keep my separate existence; I can’t be a person when I’m in a relationship. It makes me feel panicky.” Examples of anxiety experiences in individuals at a psychotic level of organization, or with delusional thinking, include the following: “My brain and body are being eaten away by parasites. That is why I can’t think or move my bowels.” “I have faded into nothingness. I am no longer alive.” “They have been blowing poison gas through the keyhole. It’s destroying me and obliterating my thoughts.” Preliminary Note on Phobias Although phobias vary, psychoanalytic scholars see all phobic experiences as having the same core mechanism: Free-­floating anxiety is transformed via repression, displacement, and projection into a more specific symptom symbolizing the underlying psychological conflict. The primary gain of a phobia is thus that it carries less anxiety than full consciousness of the conflict. Phobias have been recognized for centuries, dating back to Hippocrates. The term is derived from the Greek φόβος = phobos = “fear.” In phobias, the emotion is an irrational fear of a specific object (e.g., blood, insects, heights, flying) or situation (e.g., giving a talk, being blocked in a crowded place), exposure to which elicits anxiety reactions or the idea of which elicits active avoidance. Clinical assessment of patients suffering from phobias includes (1) whether the person has one or many phobias, (2) whether the phobic concern is specific or general, (3) the content of the phobia, (4) the presence of panic attacks associated with the phobic experience and behavior, and (5) the level of psychic functioning reflected in the phobia. In some individuals, one sees counterphobic behavior: Instead of avoiding a frightening object or situation, they actively pursue it, evidently in an unconscious effort to master the fear rather than endure it passively. Counterphobic repetitions rarely eventuate in mastery of phobic apprehensions but instead tend to lead to the compulsive repetition of the counterphobic activity. Although this phenomenon has been reliably observed clinically, it has received little empirical investigation, perhaps because it is not a problem for which treatment is ordinarily sought. These factors may explain the absence of counterphobia from all editions of DSM, despite its being potentially self-­ destructive and thus warranting clinical attention.

chunks.heading: 800 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 800 Adulthood As a psychopathological manifestation, phobia occupies a prominent place in the psychodynamic literature. From the dynamic–­genetic perspective, much literature on the topic has explored the symbolic meaning and underlying (unconscious) fantasy behind particular phobic symptoms, as a p...
chunks.text: 800 Adulthood As a psychopathological manifestation, phobia occupies a prominent place in the psychodynamic literature. From the dynamic–­genetic perspective, much literature on the topic has explored the symbolic meaning and underlying (unconscious) fantasy behind particular phobic symptoms, as a parallel to what one finds in the manifest content of a dream. A number of dynamics have been identified, reflecting diverse theoretical emphases. From the structural perspective, phobic symptoms are interpreted with reference to the ego and superego. Some authors have construed phobias as projections of bad internal objects or identifications or as superego failures. Others have understood them as reflecting an underlying conflict over dependency or separation. Still others interpret them in terms of conflicts over aggression. In the formation of a phobic symptom, preexisting developmental arrests expedite a regression of various ego functions, with a related increase in primary-­process functioning, leading to a confusion between potential danger and actual danger. Fantasy, thought, and action are equated, while internal (instinctual drive) danger and external danger become muddled. This process results in the reversion of controlled anxiety to uncontrolled anxiety, and it reflects the failure of the ego to limit these responses to a signal level. Phobic symptoms involve less suffering and better overall adaptation than intense states of free-­floating anxiety (i.e., panic attacks). In phobias, defensive measures restrict and focus what is feared, allowing afflicted individuals to remain free of anxiety as long as they avoid specific objects or situations. Clinically, phobias have been found to derive from attempts to moderate anxieties over competition and feared retaliation (oedipal issues), as well as efforts to deal with fears of loss of control (autonomy issues). At a more basic level, they may defend against fears of abandonment by caregivers, represent concerns over body integrity, and involve loss of ego functions. At a still more primitive level, phobias may entail efforts to restore a lost sense of connection with others and to reestablish a lost sense of identity. S3.1 Specific Phobias Onset of specific phobias is usually early (5–9 years) but may occur later (20–25 years), when the phobia more often involves situations (e.g., heights, flying, storms) rather than an object (e.g., blood, scars, needles) or animal (e.g., spiders, snakes, insects, dogs, horses). Younger age of onset has been found to be associated with an increased number of feared stimuli. The phobic objects or situations are either avoided or else endured with intense anxiety. The general symptom-­oriented definition of a phobia provided by the psychiatric tradition (namely, fear-­driven avoidance) is insufficient to capture the complexity of the psychoanalytic understanding of the phenomenon. From this perspective, a phobic symptom functions as a symbolic representation of an underlying conflict. The adaptive gain of phobia over anxiety neurosis lies in this advantage: While raw anxiety offers no escape, the phobic individual can attempt to avoid anxiety by avoiding the feared object or situation. The phobic person, however, can sometimes escape the anxiety by avoiding the feared object or situation. Some individuals with phobias report having observed another person (e.g., a caregiver) react with fear or anxiety when confronted by an object or situation, resulting in vicarious learning of a fear response to the object or situation. Others may have had direct negative experience with an object or situation (e.g., they were bitten by

chunks.heading: 802 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 802 Adulthood prolongs the alarms and potentiates learning. It is probably interference or conflict with this survival-­based behavior that is the most important factor in “convincing” the organism not to let this happen again at any cost. A current view is that panic attacks in phobic conditions (e...
chunks.text: 802 Adulthood prolongs the alarms and potentiates learning. It is probably interference or conflict with this survival-­based behavior that is the most important factor in “convincing” the organism not to let this happen again at any cost. A current view is that panic attacks in phobic conditions (especially agoraphobia) are “spontaneous”—unrelated to psychic events—­and based on pure physiology. Psychodynamically oriented therapists, in contrast, assume that agoraphobia and susceptibility to panic attacks may reflect ego weakness, and are interested in what specific underlying conflicts and deficits, possibly related to past events, predispose a given patient to develop agoraphobia, and what specific stressors might trigger a panic attack. Several authors have made connections between agoraphobia and claustrophobia. For Weiss (1964), they are both attempts to deal with “specific threats to the patient’s ego unity.” These are generally considered to be the basic phobias, expressing in extreme form two major annihilation-­related dangers for young children as they go through separation–­individuation processes. Too much closeness can lead to fear of loss of the self through merger or engulfment, whereas too much distance can lead to feelings of abandonment and its attendant dangers. The word “panic” owes its origin to the Greek god Pan, the god of woods and streams and patron of shepherds. When disturbed from his nap in a thicket or cave near the road, this apparently benign sprite is alleged to have let out a yell so intense and terrifying that a passerby would be frightened to death. A panic attack is an unpredictable acute anxiety reaction with no known significant stressor or trigger. The attack is frequently described by the person as involving somatic symptoms that resemble those of a heart attack. For this reason, many patients seek help at hospital emergency rooms and may develop secondary illness anxiety disorder (hypochondriasis). The attack typically lasts for 5–20 minutes and is almost always characterized by both somatic and psychic symptoms (e.g., shortness of breath, tachycardia, sweating, dizziness, nausea, the feeling of losing control, fear of dying, derealization, depersonalization). Panic disorder is diagnosed when panic attacks are recurrent. Usually, they are followed by anticipatory anxiety (fear of having another panic attack) and active avoidance of certain situations in which it would be difficult or embarrassing to seek help if a panic attack were to occur. The first attack is usually vividly remembered by the sufferer. Age at onset is typically early adulthood (20–24 years), with a lifetime prevalence twice as high in women. Agoraphobia and panic disorder are frequently complicated by depressive symptoms secondary to the primary anxiety disorder, and by substance misuse for self-­medication (e.g., alcohol, benzodiazepines). It is plausible that a panic attack in the background of phobic symptom onset reflects a traumatic factor in the etiology of phobias. S3.4 Generalized Anxiety Disorder For both DSM-5-TR and ICD-11, generalized anxiety disorder is a discrete clinical condition requiring a specific diagnostic label, even though symptoms of this condition do not appear to be specific. Rather, they appear to constitute the “generalization” of an anxiety state experienced by sufferers as both psychic and somatic (e.g., restlessness, fatigue, muscle tension), extended over time (i.e., at least several months, for more days than not; WHO, 2019). Patients suffering from this condition usually report hyperarousal and constant exaggerated apprehension in dealing with most everyday activities. Diagnosed generalized anxiety disorder has a lifetime prevalence as high as 9%

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 803
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 803 in the general population of the United States, while worldwide 12-month prevalence is about 3% in adulthood. The prevalence is higher in assigned females than in males (2:1). As we stated earlier, considering the fact that anxious individuals suffer pervasive, disabling, and c...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 803 in the general population of the United States, while worldwide 12-month prevalence is about 3% in adulthood. The prevalence is higher in assigned females than in males (2:1). As we stated earlier, considering the fact that anxious individuals suffer pervasive, disabling, and chronic anxieties, we may also regard generalized anxiety disorder as a personality pattern–­disorder rather than a condition based on transient symptoms (see the discussion in the P Axis, Chapter 12, pp. 651–708). Clinical Illustration A 31-year-old corporate lawyer was referred by his CEO for feeling “stressed” at work and for multiple somatic complaints. He began by saying that what he had been going through was “pretty scary.” He described a recent work experience: “I went to the meeting, and shortly after being there, I felt a wave go through my head. I began to feel dizzy, like I was going to pass out—and I started to breathe fast and feel nauseated.” He emphasized that it was a terrible experience, yet added that his recent medical tests had all produced normal results. He began to feel calmer as the day progressed, especially after going home. Several better days followed, marred only by stomach cramps. He still did not feel quite right, describing ongoing discomfort in social situations, in which he would feel lightheaded and dizzy in conversation. He speculated about having an “anxiety condition,” and noted a “phobia of public speaking.” He wondered whether his condition was mental or physical, and seemed to be asking for reassurance. S4 Obsessive–Compulsive and Related Disorders Obsessive–­compulsive disorder began as one of the ICD-9/DSM-II neuroses. DSMIII, -IV, and -IV-TR grouped it with the anxiety disorders. ICD-10 elevated it to a specific group of disorders, obsessive–­compulsive syndromes, embedded in the group “neurotic, stress-­related, and somatoform syndromes.” DSM-5 has followed ICD-10’s lead in “promoting” obsessive–­compulsive disorder to its own category, together with other disorders hypothesized to be related to it. Thus, DSM-5 “imports” trichotillomania from DSM-IV’s impulse-­control disorders and body dysmorphic disorder from DSM-IV’s somatoform disorders; it adds two new diagnoses, hoarding disorder and excoriation disorder. In this way, the obsessive–­compulsive and related disorders have become one of the major groupings in DSM-5. ICD-11 follows DSM-5-TR in moving body dysmorphic disorder and trichotillomania to the obsessive–­compulsive grouping and adding hoarding disorder and excoriation disorder. Both DSM-5-TR and ICD11 recommend assessing individuals with obsessive–­compulsive disorder and related disorders for comorbid tic disorders, as these conditions have different prognoses and may require different treatments. The core of the spectrum of obsessive–­compulsive disorders is well known and easily described. “Obsessions” are recurrent thoughts, impulses, or images that impinge upon the affected person’s mental functioning; “compulsions” are actions, mental activities, and repetitive behaviors that patients experience as “compulsory” and that significantly affect their functioning. Severity varies widely across patients and within the life of a given person. Compulsions may become more and more inflexible; the subject may not only be forced to perform some action or mental activity, but also required to do so in a rigidly organized sequence (compulsive rituals).

chunks.heading: 804 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 804 Adulthood DSM-5-TR’s obsessive–­compulsive disorder and related disorders include body dysmorphic disorder, in which obsessive thinking is focused on imagined or exaggerated body imperfections; hoarding disorder, in which the content of the obsessive thinking and consequent compulsive behavior a...
chunks.text: 804 Adulthood DSM-5-TR’s obsessive–­compulsive disorder and related disorders include body dysmorphic disorder, in which obsessive thinking is focused on imagined or exaggerated body imperfections; hoarding disorder, in which the content of the obsessive thinking and consequent compulsive behavior are related to the urge to accumulate and retain objects of no objective or sentimental value; and trichotillomania and excoriation disorder, the compulsive urge to pull hair or pick at skin, respectively, with a frequency that causes hair loss or observable scars and sometimes bleeding. The psychodynamics underlying these last two diagnostic entities may be different from those underlying obsessive–­compulsive disorders. The subjective experience of obsessive–­compulsive problems and related symptoms and disorders is the result of many factors, including age at onset and level of insight. Children between 6 and 9 years old (see the SC Axis, Chapter 5, pp. 256–308) frequently develop minor rituals, accompanied by magical thinking, to avoid dire consequences (e.g., “Step on a crack and break your mother’s back!”—often implying an unconscious fantasy of causing harm or being punished). Ordinarily, such rituals are short-lived and not incapacitating. Although more serious obsessions, compulsions, and rituals are rare, they may persist for years and extend to other contexts (e.g., contamination, order, and symmetry). From a psychodynamic perspective, obsessive–­ compulsive symptom formation results from unconscious conflicts, usually between drive and conscience, desire and repulsion, appetite and prohibition, or initiative and guilt. They cause much distress, inclining the sufferer to translate them into some compromise symptom or behavior (e.g., to undo sexual impulses that they see as repulsive, individuals may wash their hands repeatedly). Patients may recognize that their symptoms are a problem (i.e., their obsessions and compulsions may be completely ego-­dystonic) and struggle actively against them. Or they may experience them as mostly or completely ego-­syntonic, and passively accept what they consider to be their ordinary mental functioning. In cases of poor insight, clinicians should consider the diagnosis of obsessive–­compulsive personality disorder (as defined in DSM) or obsessive–­compulsive personality (as defined in the P Axis, Chapter 12, pp. 678–680). The disorder and the personality style, though sharing some terminology, are significantly different. While patients with obsessive–­ compulsive disorders may experience specific, ego-­syntonic obsessions and compulsions, those with obsessive–­compulsive personalities typically do not. Rather, they have traits, attitudes, and general behavior patterns characterized by perfectionism, moral inflexibility, and difficulty delegating tasks to others. Recent literature suggests that co-­occurrence of DSM-­diagnosed obsessive–­compulsive disorder and obsessive–­ compulsive personality disorder is infrequent. Other factors help shape the clinical presentation and subjective experience of obsessive–­compulsive disorder and related symptoms: level of personality organization, maturity of defenses, and level of cognitive functioning. Probably more than any other class of symptoms, obsessions and compulsions are present in various diagnostic categories and should be considered somewhat transdiagnostic. They may affect persons with a high level of relational and social functioning, as well as patients with frankly psychotic structure and functioning. It is valuable to investigate recent losses, stresses, and other possible precipitants of obsessive–­compulsive disorder and related disorders, especially those that may have affected a patient’s fantasies of control. Conveying the message that hostile and selfish thoughts are normal and not inherently dangerous may also be of help. Although notably challenging clinically, some obsessions and compulsions remit when the afflicted

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 805
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 805 person can express the feelings connected with difficult experiences—­especially normal disappointment, anger, and grief. S4.1 Obsessive–Compulsive Disorder Obsessive–­compulsive disorder is the historical prototype for the other diagnoses within its grouping. It may involve pr...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 805 person can express the feelings connected with difficult experiences—­especially normal disappointment, anger, and grief. S4.1 Obsessive–Compulsive Disorder Obsessive–­compulsive disorder is the historical prototype for the other diagnoses within its grouping. It may involve prevalent obsessions (including ruminations and aggressive or punitive fantasies), compulsions (including rituals), or both. The subject unsuccessfully resists either one, resulting in unpleasant repetition. Onset is generally in adolescence or early adulthood and tends to be earlier in males and in those with a tic disorder. To fulfill the DSM-based diagnosis, symptoms must have been present for at least 2 weeks. Patients often have intact reality testing, but there is a spectrum of insight that at its lower end approaches zero, associated with patients who are quasi- or frankly delusional (approximately 4% of patients with diagnosed obsessive–­ compulsive disorder). Depression with recurrent and sometimes intrusive thoughts about death, failure, or inadequacy may mimic obsessive–­compulsive disorder. Subjective Experience of Obsessive–Compulsive Disorder Affective States Obsessive and compulsive patients, being heterogeneous in severity, mental functioning, and insight, experience a range of affective states. Depression is not uncommon, including suicidal ideation and plans related to the sense of being unable to deal with symptoms in everyday life. This complication is more frequent in patients with good insight. Those diagnosed with obsessive–­compulsive disorder and poor insight, contrastingly, may experience pain and isolation at being considered weird and unacceptable by others, even significant others. When insight is largely absent and close to delusional thinking, a person is likely to project aggression onto others, thereby feeling actively isolated by them or in danger from them. When individuals with this disorder are prevented from carrying out their compulsions or rituals, they may become diffusely terrified, irritable, or overtly aggressive. Behind such symptoms, psychoanalytic clinical experience points to a range of unconscious concerns, including potential loss of control (especially with respect to contamination, aggression, and shame). Cognitive Patterns Insight is a critical dimension in assessing individuals with obsessions and compulsions. An obsession may be recognized as a mental dysfunction, something that interferes with ordinary cognitive functioning and flow of thinking, and thus as highly ego-­ dystonic, disturbing, and intrusive. Ego-­syntonic obsessions are usually related to poor or absent insight (i.e., delusional beliefs). In the absence of insight, magical thinking may appear as delusional; patients with intact insight tend to find it absurd and shameful. Compulsive activity is often a remnant of the magical thinking of early childhood, when impulses and actions were incompletely differentiated. Thus, individuals with obsessive–­compulsive symptoms may be understood as having convicted themselves unconsciously of thought crimes (hostile, selfish cognitions), which continue to haunt them in the form of obsessive images and ideas, motivating attempts to expiate their guilt through rituals that represent the defenses of reaction formation and undoing.

chunks.heading: 806 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 806 Adulthood Somatic States Patients with obsessive–­compulsive disorder often suffer hyperarousal and symptoms of somatic anxiety. In those with contamination obsessions and severe cleaning compulsions, it is common to see health problems (e.g., infections, skin excoriations) caused by excessive w...
chunks.text: 806 Adulthood Somatic States Patients with obsessive–­compulsive disorder often suffer hyperarousal and symptoms of somatic anxiety. In those with contamination obsessions and severe cleaning compulsions, it is common to see health problems (e.g., infections, skin excoriations) caused by excessive washing. Some obsessive–­compulsive patients complain of fatigue or restlessness. Some have co-­occurring motor tics (approximately 30% over a lifetime, according to recent epidemiological studies). Relational Patterns Obsessions and compulsions may severely impair social and relational functioning. In extreme cases, they may lead to social isolation. In general, afflicted individuals tend to remain in relationships if they can control the partner. They may choose significant others who actively reassure them in their symptoms or even participate in their compulsive rituals. Subjective Experience of the Therapist The internal experience of a person with an obsession has often been intuited to be shaped by a struggle between besieging and besieged parts of the mind. Clinicians treating patients with obsessive–­compulsive disorder may oscillate between an optimistic, positive, empathic attitude that frames such behavior as normal defenses of daily life, and astonished and pessimistic discouragement and doubt in their ability to be of any help in the face of such a “cold, metallic” organization of defenses. Countertransference may be dominated by themes of control, as patients try to control their lives and behaviors—­efforts often deemed as tragically defeated. Whatever strength is expended in efforts to control inner life by throwing rejected thoughts out the window is sabotaged by their quickly and triumphantly reentering via the front door as uncontrolled compulsions. A beleaguered clinician is often left feeling impotent. Clinical Illustration A 33-year-old man seeks therapy after being fired for delays in completing work assignments. He is very anxious about his future and pessimistic about not only finding another job but also his marital relationship: His wife is becoming less and less tolerant of what she calls his “manias.” When asked about these, he says, “Nothing really serious. I think many people should behave like I do. There are too many contaminants outside, and so I take particular care of my hands and clothes. I wash my hands up to 30 times a day, and clean my desk and computer quite often with special products to remove the dust and to kill all the germs. I know that this is time-­consuming, but it is necessary to prevent contamination and diseases.” S4.2 Body Dysmorphic Disorder (Dysmorphophobia) Body dysmorphic disorder, also known as “body dysmorphia,” “dysmorphic syndrome,” or “dysmorphophobia,” involves recurrent intrusive thoughts about one’s appearance, considered defective. Sufferers want to hide such defects or fix them medically or surgically. Many spend hours each day attempting to conceal or correct their perceived flaws, which may be really present though not as severe as the afflicted

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 807
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 807 person believes. These patients may compulsively check their physical appearance (e.g., at the mirror), repetitively seek reassurance, and incessantly compare their own appearance to that of others. Both DSM-5-TR and ICD-11 include several specifiers on degree of insight into t...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 807 person believes. These patients may compulsively check their physical appearance (e.g., at the mirror), repetitively seek reassurance, and incessantly compare their own appearance to that of others. Both DSM-5-TR and ICD-11 include several specifiers on degree of insight into the accuracy of the beliefs about the body, ranging from good to absent insight–­delusional beliefs. Another specifier denotes a subtype in which the dysmorphic thinking focuses on muscles (i.e., the person’s concern is mostly on the inadequate development of muscular body mass). This subtype is seen mostly in male patients, and it has defined as “muscle dysmorphia.” Despite the fact that most clinicians and researchers have construed muscle dysmorphia as a stand-alone diagnosis, we need further evidence to distinguish it from body dysmorphic disorder and to clarify the link between muscle dysmorphia and eating disorders (see S7 below). Notwithstanding such controversy, the clinical picture of body dysmorphia includes preoccupation with leanness, muscularity, and body shape/size; behavioral components such as the use of ergogenic substances, unhealthy dietary practices, or excessive exercise; and cognitive symptoms such as body dissatisfaction, dysfunctional body change behaviors, mental acts, and checking behaviors. ICD-10 included body dysmorphic disorder and nondelusional dysmorphophobia among somatoform disorders, but moved it to the “obsessive–­compulsive or related disorders” section in ICD-11. Body dysmorphic disorder is common. Despite a high degree of heterogeneity, prevalence across settings (e.g., cosmetic/dermatology, mental health patients, student samples) is approximately 11%. No significant differences between assigned males and females have emerged (2.2: 2.5%). Earlier literature reported gender-­based discrepancies in prevalence, which likely arose from inconsistent views among researchers before the development of standardized questionnaires and better clinical assessment. Age at onset is usually adolescence (prompted by the somatic changes of puberty) or early adulthood. Subclinical symptoms may appear during early adolescence (at 12 or 13 years of age), however.
