Such individuals may discover, to their chagrin, something about their dissociated acts from evidence around the house or from those who witnessed them during the amnestic interval. The “host” role is just that; over time, it may be taken on by different alters. If the alternate subject, or alter, present during the host’s “amnestic interval,” appears in a therapy session, that version of the person may be remarkably without subjective symptoms and may give a coherent account of experience during the interval in question. While the host may be ignorant of other selves, most of the others are usually cognizant of the host, and often of each other, or at least of some subset of the others. More commonly, especially in the clinic, the host does have symptoms, which may include amnesia, depersonalization, derealization, and any variety of “intrusion” symptoms from any of the other self-­states. Intrusions (and withdrawals) include hallucinations of others’ voices or flashbacks, as well as the intrusion and withdrawal of emotions, thoughts, impulses, fears, sensations, and so on. The default subject, with time, may come to appreciate that the “host” position is a role that others can play. Especially with therapy, different alters may take turns playing the “host” role as the system evolves. Therapy makes use of the inherently autohypnotic elements of dissociative identity disorder. Some patients have elaborated autohypnotic solutions to managing symptoms and multiplicity itself in what has been called the “third reality.” Indeed, patients with marked dissociative symptoms commonly experience three “realities.” The first is “objective” reality—­the reality that “everyone” agrees on. The second is “subjective” reality, or what is real for a given individual consciousness—­the locus of subjective experience. The third reality is generally experienced by alters, not always by the host: an internal virtual or phantom landscape, sometimes called the “inscape,” wherein the various alters experience each other as cohabiting this space, much as different real people experience each other in the first reality. In patients with dissociative identity disorder and related disorders, there is generally a multiplicity of second realities. For an overwhelmed person, the alters serve as vehicles for endorsing and inhabiting more tolerable alternative realities. The primary gain is the sequestering of the burden of an intolerable reality, at the cost of the confusing and often chaotic flux of alters’ subjective experiences, perceptions, and understandings of themselves, their lives, and their relationships. Paradoxically, this confusion may be minimal when dissociative barriers among alters are strong; with less severe presentations, or with the weakening of barriers that generally occurs in therapy, co-­presence and “symptom leakage” may increase among self-­states to such an extent that a patient may complain, “I don’t really know who I am right now!” The third reality is a feature of the subjective experiences of many patients. Alters appear with phantom bodies appropriate to their sex, age, putative personal history, temperamental characteristics, and other characteristics of “real” reality. The clinical advantage of working in the third reality is that it guarantees a certain level of co-­presence and immediate sharing across self-­states. Such “face-to-face” encounters have proven to be therapeutically useful for negotiation and compromise among host and alters; for the expression, containment, and sharing of posttraumatic memories; and for the integration of self-­states.

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 835
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 835 Specific Subjective Experiences in Severe Dissociative Disorders Amnesia As noted, patients typically show dissociative amnesia, often with minifugues. “Lost time” occurs most commonly because of an executive switch to another self-state. Subsequent recall may be absent, or els...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 835 Specific Subjective Experiences in Severe Dissociative Disorders Amnesia As noted, patients typically show dissociative amnesia, often with minifugues. “Lost time” occurs most commonly because of an executive switch to another self-state. Subsequent recall may be absent, or else delayed, with sketchy recall, or as if recalling a dream; alternatively, there may be no sensory recall per se, but “secondhand” knowledge of the period in question through a “verbal report” from another self-state. This process may be very upsetting. Further dissociative defenses may then lead to indifference, distracting idle chatter, or amnesia for what has just happened or for what has just been verbally shared. Affective States Certain affects may “belong” to a given self-state, and may be relatively absent from all others. Rage may be the prerogative of one; lust of another; fastidious housecleaning yet another. Incomplete switches to another self-state may leave the subject in the role of passive observer of another’s perception of reality and behavior, possibly experienced as if it were a nightmare. The inner confusion occasioned by the clash of such simultaneously experienced alternate realities may cause many forms of distress and withdrawal, anxiety and panic, with an intense preoccupation with what is happening, terrifying fear of loss of control, and paralyzing despair. The loss of “I,” a sense of grounding in “me,” may be accompanied by sharp anxiety, even to the point of panic—or by its opposite, a strange indifference or calm. It is often helpful to encourage individuals with dissociative disorders to describe their affective experiences in detail and/or to reflect upon their apparent absence. Exploration of such reports—­even if fragmentary, seemingly contradictory, or full of numbness or nothingness—­may prove a positive step in reintegrating the different states that constitute a sense of self. As therapy proceeds, it is usually valuable to study the relationship of these accounts to the stressful experiences that may have been their antecedents and the triggers for the onset or mobilization of dissociative defenses. Perception Unlike a patient with a schizophrenia spectrum disorder, a patient with a significant dissociative disorder may often give a description of the hallucinatory voices: name, gender, age, appearance, how and of what they speak, their “function” or purpose in the system, and so on. In addition, the interaction between the person and the voices is typically closer to a normal conversation than one finds in schizophrenic hallucinations. Cognitive Patterns Patients vary widely in their insight. Interpretation of the presence of intact or defective insight hinges, of course, on what the clinician believes to be the case. If dissociative identity disorder and OSDD-1 are regarded as “true” diagnoses, then a patient who claims that the alters are all imaginary and don’t really exist will be regarded as being in denial by a clinician who “believes in” dissociative identity disorder. Conversely, the same statement may be interpreted as an indication of intact reality testing

chunks.heading: 836 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 836 Adulthood by a clinician who does not “believe” in dissociative disorders. There are some common false beliefs that arise in specific self-­states (e.g., belief of one or more alters that they do not share the body with others, and that they may commit suicide, or else kill one of the other self...
chunks.text: 836 Adulthood by a clinician who does not “believe” in dissociative disorders. There are some common false beliefs that arise in specific self-­states (e.g., belief of one or more alters that they do not share the body with others, and that they may commit suicide, or else kill one of the other self-­states, without killing “everyone”; that if the body dies, they can migrate to someone else’s body; or that therapy ought to be terminated because this disorder does not exist). Somatic States Somatic symptoms are common and may be dominated by intrusions of posttraumatic reexperiencing. The source may be the patient in the consulting room, but such symptoms often constitute “leakage” from another self-state. Partial tactile flashbacks are especially common, resulting in body symptoms that involve pain, unpleasant sensations, or numbness. Somatic intrusions may also be present. For instance, a woman is walking down the department store aisle on her way to the exit. Her left hand reaches out and grabs a brightly colored scarf and stuffs it into her pocket. Alarmed, the woman tries to put the scarf back. Her left arm will not move. She reaches over with her right hand, and has a minor struggle to free the scarf from the left hand. She replaces it on the display table and exits the store immediately. Later on, she discovers that a child alter had seen the scarf, found it pretty, and wanted it. Relational Patterns The relationships of dissociative patients vary widely. They may show a wide range of interpersonal abilities. Certain self-­states may look after home, spouse/partner, and children; others may look after work. Adaptation may be more apparent than real, as competent functioning may be carried out by a self-state with no life outside the workplace. Disturbed relationships are common and often mimic or recreate some aspect of the traumatic history: Alters may be perceived as perpetrators, exploiters, or rescuers. Sexual trauma often impairs sexual intimacy, which triggers overt flashbacks. The trauma bond may be repeated with subsequent partners, with patterns of masochistic submissiveness and revictimization. The traumatic history that engenders this condition, usually involving abuse by a primary caregiver, creates in its victims a disposition to placate authorities—­including therapists, from whom grave mistreatment is unconsciously feared. Patients have little reason to trust clinicians, and clinicians who convey skepticism about amnesia and altered states of consciousness unwittingly discourage them from revealing the dissociative symptoms from which they suffer. Subjective Experience of the Therapist The overall encounter with a dissociative patient may leave the average therapist feeling completely deskilled and at a loss. Without specific training on the condition, it is common to feel blamed for not being or knowing or doing enough. In response to a vague, disjointed, or contradictory narrative, history taking may beget confusion and a sense of futility. The dissociative structure (self-­states and their attributes and relationships) may be so interesting that the clinician becomes immersed in it to the detriment of other therapeutic needs. The trauma history may excite rescue fantasies in the therapist. Patience is needed to tolerate the patient’s deep mistrust. Perceived sensitivity

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 837
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 837 to closeness may lead to distancing, which may reenact the negligent, non-­abusing parent whose obliviousness allowed the abuse to happen. Limits and boundaries may be tested by the patient who presents as “the exception” (Freud, 1925) in many different ways. Child alters may e...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 837 to closeness may lead to distancing, which may reenact the negligent, non-­abusing parent whose obliviousness allowed the abuse to happen. Limits and boundaries may be tested by the patient who presents as “the exception” (Freud, 1925) in many different ways. Child alters may excite the desire to assume the role of a new parental figure for the patient. Claims of childhood traumatization may come with insistent demands to be believed or else with insistent demands that the clinician collude with the patient’s denial. The patient may be rocked by seemingly interminable flashbacks or else frozen into protracted affective numbing and amnesia. Therapists may suffer secondary traumatization to the extent that they are unprepared to confront pervasive emotional and physical neglect in early childhood, together with the early, severe, repeated emotional, physical, and sexual traumatization that this neglect permitted. Countertransference reactions may range from outrage to denial. Supervision with a trusted colleague is often critical. Clinical Illustrations Case 1 A woman in her 30s began having panic attacks, severe abdominal pain, and moments of confusion with no organic origin. Evaluators noted that the symptoms had begun after she gave birth to her first child. Then she mentioned traumatic nightmares of abuse when she and her daughter had returned home, and panic after a family visit that included an uncle with whom she had always felt uncomfortable. “These dreams must be crazy,” she said. “I can’t believe things like that could have happened to me.” Her husband called her sister, who said that this uncle had abused both siblings, but that her sister had refused to believe it. When the sisters talked, with the husband listening, she spoke as if she had always remembered the abuse, but when her husband tried to discuss what he had heard her say, she had no recall of it. Case 2 A middle-­aged woman went to bed at night and woke to find herself in a bank teller booth serving a client. She had no memory of applying for the job or of the 2-week training. She retreated to the washroom. The following day she woke up at home; this happened a few times. She eventually earned a supervisory role for exemplary performance. She remained largely amnestic and feared she would be exposed any moment as a fraud. At the bank, she had two nicknames: “Mother Hen” when working inhouse, and “The Shark” when dealing with other financial institutions. Her husband and children knew only the devoted mother. Years later, she was sexually assaulted, and her dissociative structure collapsed. Posttraumatic child alters surfaced, along with hallucinations and flashbacks. She never returned to work but had 4 years of hospital admissions and heavy antipsychotic medication, all accompanied by a worsening clinical picture, before finally beginning therapy for dissociative identity disorder. S5.3 Conversion Disorder Medical diagnosis of conversion disorder requires that it be pseudoneurological and psychogenic. The concept of “conversion” has had a checkered history. Freud (1894, p. 48) coined the term: “In hysteria, the incompatible idea is rendered innocuous by

chunks.heading: 838 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 838 Adulthood its sum of excitation being transformed into something somatic. For this I should like to propose the name of conversion.” Not long afterward, conversion was seen as the paradigmatic compromise formation between internal drive (urge, intent, wish, motive) and defense (repression), and...
chunks.text: 838 Adulthood its sum of excitation being transformed into something somatic. For this I should like to propose the name of conversion.” Not long afterward, conversion was seen as the paradigmatic compromise formation between internal drive (urge, intent, wish, motive) and defense (repression), and only secondarily between external trauma and defense. It also became a paradigmatic example of primary gain: Better to suffer the symptom than be conscious of the forbidden wish or impulse. The psychiatric sense of conversion—­pseudoneurological—­would then be considered as a mere surface phenomenon of a more specific underlying dynamic, repressed drive. Since then, there has been something of a return of the repressed: Repressed wish remains, but repressed trauma has been reinstated. Hysterical neurosis was present in both ICD-9 and DSM-II, with two major variants: dissociative type and conversion type. ICD-10 dropped the word “hysterical,” but maintained the grouping of the two types in a single category, dissociative (conversion) disorders, despite having its own somatoform disorders category. DSM-III and -IV relabeled conversion hysteria as conversion disorder and moved it to the new category of somatoform disorders. DSM-5 maintains the link to dissociation, suggesting that conversion disorder may be associated with depersonalization, derealization, or dissociative amnesia. DSM-5-TR renamed conversion disorder with the new label “functional neurological symptom disorder,” while ICD-11 listed it as “dissociative neurological symptom disorder,” emphasizing the main feature of conversion phenomena. These days, repression and conversion continue to be invoked for repressed wishes with somatic expression. Repressed trauma has to share the field with dissociated trauma. Some authors regard all unremembered trauma as dissociated rather than repressed; others maintain the distinction. The difficulty derives from the lexical ambiguity of both repression and dissociation: Classically, repression stood for all defenses before other defenses were distinguished, and in recent decades, dissociation has expanded to cover myriad distinct psychic operations. The psychiatric definition of a conversion symptom requires neurological competence to judge its pseudoneurological status. Faced with such a symptom, one may wonder whether it is evidence of a repressed wish or a partial posttraumatic reexperiencing (or blunting), or neither. In the course of the psychotherapeutic working through of a conversion symptom, there is the opportunity to witness the simultaneity of diagnosis and treatment. This occurs when the patient is able to enunciate the symbolic significance of the symptom, and it then vanishes. Rarely, a clear example of repression and conversion is seen in clinical practice. A negative pseudoneurological symptom (e.g., local anesthesia or analgesia or paralysis) may also be a reliving of one’s dissociative state at the time of a trauma, and so have the quality of a “negative” PTSD symptom. For example, a patient in an eating disorders inpatient unit claims to be anesthetic from umbilicus to midthigh. She seems surprised when told that most women have sensation “down there.” This is a variant on what has been called la belle indifference—a puzzling emotional minimization of the seriousness of conditions as disabling as blindness, paralysis, or anesthesia. This should not be confused with the more generic alexithymia or pensée opératoire associated with somatizing personalities or somatic symptom and related disorders (see the P Axis, Chapter 12, pp. 651–708, and section S6 below). Pseudoneurological symptoms may occur also in the context of dissociative identity disorder. If this is the case, the “source” (i.e., the person or the alter) needs to be ascertained before the nature of symptoms can be clarified. Because intrusion

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 839
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 839 phenomena are more commonly partial and undisguised revivifications from a traumatized child alter, psychoanalytic interpretation of symbolic significance may support denial rather than insight. In conclusion, the classic Freudian concept of repression continues to apply to the...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 839 phenomena are more commonly partial and undisguised revivifications from a traumatized child alter, psychoanalytic interpretation of symbolic significance may support denial rather than insight. In conclusion, the classic Freudian concept of repression continues to apply to the history of the conversion concept—­both the repression of a trauma that gains somatic expression in a partial posttraumatic flashback (body memory) and the repression of a wish that gains somatic expression in a compromise formation (conversion proper). S6 Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders As we noted earlier, ICD-11 and DSM-5-TR cluster these conditions differently. ICD11’s “disorders of bodily distress or bodily experience” omits conversion disorder, which remains within the “dissociative disorders” as “dissociative neurological symptom disorder,” defined by the presentation of motor, sensory, or cognitive symptoms that imply an involuntary discontinuity in the normal integration of motor, sensory, or cognitive functions (WHO, 2019). This section includes only two disorders: “bodily distress disorder,” similar to the DSM-5-TR “somatic symptom disorder,” and “body integrity dysphoria,” a disturbance in the experience of the body manifested by an intense and persistent desire to become physically disabled in a significant way (e.g., major limb amputee, paraplegic, blind), accompanied by persistent discomfort, or intense feelings of inappropriateness concerning current nondisabled body configuration. In contrast, as we have noted, DSM-5-TR retains conversion disorder within the somatic symptom and related disorders section as “functional neurological symptom disorder (conversion disorder).” Somatization disorder, present from DSM-III to DSMIV-TR, has been dropped. This condition, once called “Briquet’s syndrome,” followed a tradition of requiring in DSM-IV an elevated somatic symptom count—for example, at least eight from four groups (pain, gastrointestinal, sexual, pseudoneurological). It was the multiplicity of symptoms that had differentiated Briquet’s syndrome from conversion hysteria. Conversely, the DSM-5-TR somatic symptom disorder requires only one somatic symptom that is distressing or disruptive. It includes roughly three out of every four patients previously diagnosed with hypochondriasis (all those with actual symptoms). Illness anxiety disorder is now reserved for those with illness anxiety who lack actual symptoms. Compared to previous DSM editions, the criterion for medically unexplained symptoms has been dropped except for conversion disorder. Given the former focus on lack of medical explanation, individuals tended to regard these diagnoses as pejorative and demeaning, as implying that their physical symptoms were not “real.” To date, more central are the subjective reactions to the somatic symptom: abnormal thoughts, feelings, and behavior. This grouping with such minimal requirements necessarily puts together conditions that are psychologically quite disparate, as least one of which can be viewed as characterological (see the discussion of somatizing personalities in the P Axis, Chapter 12, pp. 651–708). These disorders are encountered, in decreasing frequency, in physicians’ offices, hospital psychiatric consultation–­liaison services (for patients admitted to medical or surgical floors), psychiatrists’ offices, and, rarely, in the offices of psychologists and other nonmedical therapists. With a somatic symptom, clinicians should wonder whether it is consciously produced or feigned, as in Munchausen’s syndrome or malingering, or unconsciously

chunks.heading: 840 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 840 Adulthood produced. Evidence suggests that, along with early childhood trauma or insecure attachment, several psychological factors (e.g., catastrophizing, negative affectivity, rumination, avoidance, health anxiety, negative physical self-­concept) may have a substantial influence on severely i...
chunks.text: 840 Adulthood produced. Evidence suggests that, along with early childhood trauma or insecure attachment, several psychological factors (e.g., catastrophizing, negative affectivity, rumination, avoidance, health anxiety, negative physical self-­concept) may have a substantial influence on severely impairing complaints and somatic symptom disorders. Beyond these conditions, there are some that remain on the border between psychiatry and certain medical specialties because of severity of complaints, paucity of clear pathophysiology, and lack of effective biological treatments. These include, among others, fibromyalgia, chronic fatigue syndrome, irritable bowel syndrome, and psychogenic and nonepileptic seizures. Medical and psychiatric comorbidity is highest in those patients reporting more than three somatic symptoms plus health/illness concerns, especially anxiety, depression, and personality disorders. S6.1 Somatic Symptom Disorder The diagnosis of somatic symptom disorder is given when there are somatic symptoms about which a patient is highly distressed, most often about prognosis, and for which the patient spends inordinate time and energy pursuing medical treatments. It includes what was formerly called “psychalgia” (pain disorder, in earlier DSMs). ICD-11 adds that bodily distress disorder typically involves multiple bodily symptoms that may vary over time; occasionally there is a single symptom—­usually pain or fatigue—­that is associated with the other features of the disorder (WHO, 2019). Somatic symptom disorder may arise from a heightened awareness of various bodily sensations, combined with an inclination to interpret them as indicative of medical illness (see also the M-Axis capacity for “Bodily Experiences and Representations,” Chapter 13, pp. 709–759). While the etiology of the disorder is still unclear, the estimated prevalence ranges from 5 to 7% in the general population, and increases to approximately 17% within the primary care patient population. There is always the possibility of an underlying organic condition, and the somatic presentation may be a functional exaggeration of this. Dynamically, we assume that dysphoria is expressed somatically and that the primary field of play is the body-self. As we have noted, this label applies to the majority of patients formerly diagnosed with hypochondriasis. The tendency to experience, conceptualize, and communicate psychological distress as somatic symptoms, and to seek medical attention for them, is universal. Somatizing patients appear to feel most comfortable providing details of symptoms in physical or somatic terms. As severity increases, affected individuals are more likely to reject psychological explanations for their symptoms. Cultural, social, and economic factors are important in the expression and subjective experience of the disorder. For example, individual differences may reflect the organizational culture of the health care system, with somatic complaints more likely where clinical encounters are brief and the delivery of services is less person-­centered. Focus on secondary gain (hypothesized attempts to “profit” from a condition) should not distract from the deeper task of determining primary gain (ways in which somatizing may spare the patient from dysphoria). An early example of close attention to primary gain is found in Deutsch’s “The Associative Anamnesis” (1939), in which gentle, focused, nonconfrontational exploration of the symptom generally leads to significant underlying interpersonal issues (e.g., trauma, losses, perceived failures, conflicts) that may be dynamically relevant to the onset and clinical course of somatic symptoms. Symptoms should be carefully tracked, even as a patient continues to deny a connection between probable underlying issues and bodily symptoms.

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 841
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 841 Secondary gain is often also present and, if severe, may interfere with a therapist’s efforts to appreciate primary gain. Secondary gain may eventually be interpreted as a “legitimate” way to get dependency needs met. When secondary gain motives are conscious (e.g., to get off...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 841 Secondary gain is often also present and, if severe, may interfere with a therapist’s efforts to appreciate primary gain. Secondary gain may eventually be interpreted as a “legitimate” way to get dependency needs met. When secondary gain motives are conscious (e.g., to get off work, to maximize insurance payments), they are legitimately considered as malingering. But primary and secondary gain motives are generally unconscious. If the focus is too narrowly on secondary gain, patients may feel accused of malingering. Subjective Experience of Somatic Symptom Disorder Affective States Patients with somatic symptom disorder typically show deficits in identifying emotions and alterations in the selection and implementation of strategies for regulating feelings. They may be unable to recognize, name, and express their own affects (alexithymia). Individuals who are not aware of, or able to clarify, their emotions may not be aware of the need to manage them or able to select an effective strategy to regulate their intensity. This may then lead to the amplification of the somatic symptom they experience. Feelings of vulnerability, helplessness, and sadness, together with excessive somatic concerns and hyperarousal, are common. Cognitive Patterns Accompanying alexithymia is a common cognitive deficit called la pensée opératoire. This “operational thinking” involves concrete thinking, which is comfortable with things but not with feelings, symbolic expression, imagination, or fantasy. In accordance with their varying degrees of insight, patients may or may not continue to attribute symptoms to an undiagnosed medical disorder despite an adequate or repeated medical workup. Such attribution leads to rumination about bodily symptoms, selective attention to or frequent checking of symptoms, hypervigilance about bodily sensations, and catastrophic thinking. Such preoccupation then leads to frequent health care visits, care seeking from multiple providers for the same symptoms, requests for repeated unnecessary medical tests, and medical procedures with some risk of iatrogenic complications. Patients’ health concerns lead to disproportionate and persistent thoughts about the seriousness of their somatic symptoms and to excessive time or energy devoted to them. Somatic States These patients suffer “somatosensory amplification,” the tendency to experience somatic and visceral sensations as unusually intense, noxious, and disturbing. As the problem progresses, in addition to the somatic symptoms themselves, there may be physiological accompaniments of anxiety, such as rapid heartbeat, blood pressure increase, and muscle tensions that give rise to pain. These responses of the autonomic nervous system are part of the “fight-or-­f light” reaction. Relational Patterns Preoccupation with the body supplants in-depth relationships with other individuals, including relationships with health and mental health professionals, such that others

chunks.heading: 842 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 842 Adulthood are reduced to potential sources of reassurance (which fail to reassure), or else are alienated and distanced. Encounters of these patients with their clinicians generally evoke frustration and disengagement on both sides. Patients may seem desperate to connect with clinicians through...
chunks.text: 842 Adulthood are reduced to potential sources of reassurance (which fail to reassure), or else are alienated and distanced. Encounters of these patients with their clinicians generally evoke frustration and disengagement on both sides. Patients may seem desperate to connect with clinicians through their symptoms, perhaps as a repetition of some insecure attachment pattern in childhood. In psychotherapy, patients are often poised to see the therapist as yet another authority who does not really listen, understand, and/ or care. They may feel disbelieved or blamed, or even become paranoid when the therapist tries to explore meanings beyond the purely medical. Subjective Experience of the Therapist Countertransference feelings often mirror the alexithymia or operatory thinking of the patient: inner deadness and disengagement, leading to boredom, irritation, and a sense of futility, especially as the patient proves to be immune to efforts to steer the focus off the worry about somatic symptoms and toward other issues. S6.2 Illness Anxiety Disorder (Hypochondriasis) Illness anxiety disorder in DSM-5-TR supersedes the diagnosis of hypochondriasis in earlier DSMs. Hypochondriasis literally means “under” (hypo) “the ribs” (chondria), as a patient would typically enter a doctor’s office with a hand cradling the right or left side, the source of some feared or imagined illness. As we have noted, DSM-5-TR now reserves this diagnosis for patients with illness anxiety but without actual symptoms. This choice overstates matters to some extent: There may well be mild symptoms, often a nonpathological physiological sign or symptom, whose meaning, significance, or cause the patient misinterprets. ICD-11 includes hypochondriasis within the “obsessive-­compulsive and related disorders” as a condition characterized by persistent preoccupation or fear about the possibility of having one or more serious, progressive, or life-­threatening illnesses. The preoccupation is typically accompanied by either repetitive and excessive health-­ related behaviors, such as repeatedly checking of the body for evidence of illness or seeking reassurance (e.g., arranging multiple medical consultations); or maladaptive avoidance behaviors related to health (e.g., avoiding medical appointments). It can be accompanied by panic attacks, and it can vary in degree of insight (i.e., fair to good insight, or poor to absent) (WHO, 2019). Subjective Experience of Illness Anxiety Disorder Affective States There is general anxiety about one’s health, and about illness in general, with particular anxiety about prognosis—­feeling vulnerable and fearing death. Such fears may be accompanied by depressed affect and a sense of doom. Specifically, death anxiety (conscious or unconscious fear of death, based on the awareness that death can occur at any time) can lead to intense feelings of vulnerability. Subjective state varies from preoccupation to intense anxiety in reaction to what are probably normal variations in internal sensations. Severe health anxieties are often associated with a tendency to be hypervigilant about bodily sensations and symptoms, and to become easily alarmed about one’s own health status.

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 843
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 843 Cognitive Patterns There is a general cognitive absorption with one’s body. Thoughts may include the terrifying belief that each pain or symptom is life-­threatening, that this time it is serious. There may be a vague sense that “something is wrong with my body,” even in the ab...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 843 Cognitive Patterns There is a general cognitive absorption with one’s body. Thoughts may include the terrifying belief that each pain or symptom is life-­threatening, that this time it is serious. There may be a vague sense that “something is wrong with my body,” even in the absence of specific complaints. This preoccupation can have a paranoid-­like intensity and can include a deep reluctance to relinquish physical symptoms or divert attention to other aspects of life. Rumination may also play a role in the development and maintenance of hypochondriasis. Many explanations have been suggested for this, including that the symptoms represent a way of taking care of conscious or unconscious feelings of guilt; that they are ways of dealing with early, hostile caregivers who have been internalized and equated with the ill body organs; and that they justify the patient’s being cared for. Insight level varies widely across patients suffering from this disorder, with some cases of almost delusional beliefs about having severe illness. Somatic States Somatic states are central to the diagnosis. Patients with illness anxiety disorder are preoccupied with their body, sometimes to the exclusion of all else. They often have difficulty differentiating somatic sensations from mental states, and make catastrophic misinterpretations of bodily signs or symptoms, including normal or commonplace sensations. Symptoms of increased arousal are frequent. Relational Patterns Health-­related preoccupations are often a central topic in the interpersonal relations of these patients. Others may be perceived as powerful, healthy, and indifferent. The persistence of symptoms is often felt as evidence of a physician’s ineffectiveness. Conscious or unconscious hostility toward health care professionals is typical. Excessive health-­related reassurance seeking leads to health-­related alienation and beliefs that others are unconcerned about one’s perceived health problems. Feeling alienated from others fuels further health-­related worry, resulting in continued self-­defeating attempts at health-­related reassurance-­seeking. Subjective Experience of the Therapist The subjective experience of therapists, and of health care professionals who encounter these patients, may include parallel feelings of hostility, a sense of being useless to such patients, or feeling bothered and irritated by them. S6.3 Factitious Disorders In DSM-IV and DSM-IV-TR, factitious disorders had their own category. DSM-5-TR incorporates them into “somatic symptom and related disorders,” because a factitious disorder often embodies persistent problems related to illness perception and identity, and often co-­occurs with somatic symptoms and conviction of medical disease. Conversely, ICD-11 retains factitious disorders as a stand-alone diagnostic category defined by intentionally feigning, falsifying, inducing, or aggravating medical,

chunks.heading: 844 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 844 Adulthood psychological, or behavioral signs and symptoms or injury in oneself or in another person (most commonly, a dependent child) (WHO, 2019). Sometimes called “Munchausen’s syndrome,” factitious disorders are conditions in which a patient intentionally produces or simulates physical or psy...
chunks.text: 844 Adulthood psychological, or behavioral signs and symptoms or injury in oneself or in another person (most commonly, a dependent child) (WHO, 2019). Sometimes called “Munchausen’s syndrome,” factitious disorders are conditions in which a patient intentionally produces or simulates physical or psychological symptoms. In contrast to malingering, there is no obvious secondary gain. Compared with other psychiatric disorders, diagnosis of factitious disorders is rare, with identification largely dependent on the systematic collection of relevant information, including a detailed chronology and scrutiny of the patient’s medical record. Additionally, while malingering is commonly assessed and takes place within specific legal contexts, factitious disorders are generally encountered in clinical settings. Little is known about the etiology, family patterns, or predisposing factors, except that individuals with factitious disorders typically have a prior history of early (and possibly traumatizing) medical treatment. Psychoanalytic experience suggests that, not infrequently, factitious phenomena may be understood as variants of PTSD, in which patients reenact painful illnesses or surgeries in an effort to feel that this time, they are in control of what in childhood was inflicted on them; that is, a form of repetition compulsion motivated by the desire for mastery. Such patients may also have defined their sense of self in terms of their physical suffering. Individuals with factitious disorders are famously difficult to treat medically and are highly refractory to psychotherapy. DSM-5-TR’s and ICD-11’s factitious disorder imposed on another, also called “Munchausen’s syndrome by proxy,” applies to individuals (usually parents, but sometimes nurses or other caregivers) who repeatedly cause illness or physical harm to persons in their care. Perpetrators of these acts deny their role in the damage—­sometimes even in the face of video-­recorded evidence of their destructive activity—­and present themselves to medical personnel in states of great distress over the health crises of the persons they have harmed. The simulation or induction of illness or injury may be quite dramatic, resulting in numerous medical investigations and interventions in spite of negative or inconclusive findings. Subjective Experience of Factitious Disorders Affective States Individuals with factitious disorders express a wide range of affective states, from anxiety over alleged physical symptoms to dysphoria and hostile expectations that medical professionals will not believe them. The overriding affective tone, however, is one of superficiality rather than emotional depth. A manipulative, opportunistic quality is often present. Despite such a patient’s seemingly sincere insistence on the seriousness of a physical or psychological ailment, it may be difficult for a clinician to feel empathy or concern. Cognitive Patterns Cognitive patterns involve the physical or psychological complaints of the moment, coupled with ruminations about how to get medical professionals to take the complaints seriously. Patients with factitious disorders may be chronically preoccupied with persuading themselves and others of the reality of their suffering. They may be

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 845
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 845 more or less obsessed with their connections to physicians and others on whom they feel medically dependent. Somatic States Somatic states may include chronic tension and anxiety when desired care is not forthcoming. Some with factitious disorders, in their quest for attention...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 845 more or less obsessed with their connections to physicians and others on whom they feel medically dependent. Somatic States Somatic states may include chronic tension and anxiety when desired care is not forthcoming. Some with factitious disorders, in their quest for attention from doctors or hospital personnel, inflict serious injury upon themselves. Others have persuaded previous physicians to do unnecessary surgeries; in such instances, their bodies may be permanently harmed. In Munchausen’s syndrome by proxy, individuals deliberately damage the bodies of others, usually their children, evidently out of an unconscious wish to enact a personal drama and to reinforce the strength of relationships with medical professionals who figure in their fantasy lives. Relational Patterns Relationships tend to be needy and dependent, with apparent compliance masking deep negativism and dissatisfaction. To get their complaints taken seriously, patients may exaggerate symptom descriptions, eliciting irritation from others. It is common for clinicians to experience individuals with these disorders as unreliable, manipulative, self-­dramatizing, and difficult. Subjective Experience of the Therapist The most specific, and difficult, countertransference experience is anger, which is likely to affect all clinicians involved: physicians, psychiatrists, therapists, nurses, and others. Clinicians are devoted to curing real illness, not fake illness. It is tempting to dismiss patients with factitious disorders as a waste of time and resources. Frustration is also common to the extent that such patients are resistant to recognizing what they do and to entering into therapy. Clinical Illustration A 42-year-old woman with a history of multiple surgeries for congenital abnormalities was convinced she needed gall bladder surgery “because I have pain in my stomach all the time.” Multiple medical evaluations, including full gastrointestinal workups, were negative. During psychotherapy, she accessed feelings of anger, fear, and helplessness about her childhood surgeries, complaining poignantly about her parents’ “handling me like a fragile piece of glass.” She gradually explored her potential for assertiveness. Eventually she confessed, “I ate spicy foods that gave me pain because I felt like my stomach pain was a friend.” S7 Feeding and Eating Disorders Feeding and eating disorders (hereinafter shortened to eating disorders), including anorexia nervosa, bulimia nervosa, binge-­eating disorder, avoidant/restrictive food intake disorder, and other specified and unspecified feeding and eating disorders, are

chunks.heading: 846 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 846 Adulthood broadly defined as complex conditions that involve dysfunctional eating behaviors and a preoccupation with food, typically accompanied by prominent concerns with body weight and shape to a degree that is subjectively distressing and interferes with psychosocial functioning. Etiological...
chunks.text: 846 Adulthood broadly defined as complex conditions that involve dysfunctional eating behaviors and a preoccupation with food, typically accompanied by prominent concerns with body weight and shape to a degree that is subjectively distressing and interferes with psychosocial functioning. Etiological models of eating disorders are multifactorial and individually variable, with risk conferred from personality pathology, family history, developmental issues, sociocultural factors, comorbid disorders, and genetic endowment. From a sociocultural standpoint, these conditions appear to be more prevalent in industrialized societies, where there is an abundance of food, and where conceptions of attractiveness or acceptance often involve being thin. Even in cultures with food shortages, however, people may aspire to cultural patterns of contemporary Western societies. Body size ideals, eating norms, and eating-­related behaviors are all defined or shaped by culture and ethnicity. Cross-­cultural variations have been observed in the occurrence and clinical presentations of eating disorders. Historically, the vast majority of reported cases have been cisgender women, but since the removal of assigned sex-­related criteria from the main taxonomies, there is increasing evidence that eating disorders are growing among cisgender men. Binge-­ eating disorder, in particular, has been found to be common among males. Being in a sexual minoritized group—­including but not limited to lesbian women, gay men, and bisexual individuals—­also appears to be a risk factor for developing disordered eating, with individual differences mainly grounded in sociocultural and objectification theories. Some evidence has highlighted the risk of eating disorders in those who report a gender identity different from birth-­assigned gender, a phenomenon related, in part, to a desire to make changes to body shape in support of gender expression. Some eating disorders, such as anorexia, usually begin in early adolescence, whereas bulimia and binge-­eating disorder may begin in late adolescence or early adulthood. Onset may be associated with a stressful life event, such as a family disturbance, a loss, or bullying by peers. Both anorexia and bulimia may arise from difficulties handling the progressive emergence of sexuality. In all eating disorders, eating symptoms often arise during or following a diet, or with sporadic episodes of loss-of-­control eating. Minor transient episodes may occur in middle adolescence, and may come to clinical attention only in passing when one is reviewing the history of someone coming for treatment of another condition. Eating disorders, especially anorexia and bulimia, range from minor to life-­ threatening, depending on both intrinsic severity and comorbidity. Binge-­eating disorder may be associated with obesity and related conditions, such as diabetes. Comorbidity often complicates the clinical picture and has relevant implications for onset, presentation, treatment, and prognosis. Because disorders of mood, anxiety, and personality are among the most common comorbid diagnoses, clinicians should ask about eating patterns when evaluating patients with these disorders. Course and outcome are highly variable: Some conditions are severe, with relevant medical complications, elevated mortality rates, suicide risk, and significant recidivism. There is often a “diagnostic crossover” or a lifetime co-­occurrence between one disorder and the other. Among first-­degree relatives there is an elevated family history of anorexia and bulimia and also of mood disorders, suggesting some common genetic predisposition. Binge-­eating disorder commonly overlaps with anxiety, mood, impulse-­control disorders, and obesity. Eating disorders may be best approached as falling on a psychopathological continuum: They may begin with a problem with food or disordered eating behaviors but

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 847
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 847 extend to body discomfort and, in severe cases, a severely distorted body image. Disturbed affect regulation, low self-­esteem, difficulties in mentalization, and impaired self-­direction and identity are also common. Some eating disorders arise from the spectrum of development...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 847 extend to body discomfort and, in severe cases, a severely distorted body image. Disturbed affect regulation, low self-­esteem, difficulties in mentalization, and impaired self-­direction and identity are also common. Some eating disorders arise from the spectrum of developmental trauma, beginning with conflictual family dynamics and proceeding through disordered attachment to clear emotional and physical neglect, and to overt emotional, physical, and sexual abuse. In the face of unprocessed trauma and emotions, the body may become the tool with which individuals try to attain mastery and control over their feelings. Thus, eating disorders are complex psychological phenomena with aspects that are often not self-­reported or discernible to the outside observer. Subjective Experience of Feeding and Eating Disorders Eating disorders appear in a heterogeneous group of individuals who differ significantly in terms of overall personality functioning and average personality characteristics. Those with anorexia and/or primarily restricting symptoms generally tend to be more obsessive–­compulsive, avoidant, and narcissistic. They may appear perfectionistic, and may refuse not only food but also any new experience, including any psychological change, by using mental “no entry” defenses. Certain symptoms, such as the need for control over body weight and eating patterns, excessive concern over body shape, and feelings of inadequacy, may lead to withdrawal from others. Those with bulimia and/or with relevant purging symptoms tend to be more emotionally dysregulated (often diagnosable with borderline personality functioning or traits), with subjective feelings of emptiness and emotional hunger that they try to assuage with food. After eating, they continue to feel empty, but feel disgusted in two senses: with anger and with self-­loathing. Patients with binge-­eating disorder and/or binge symptoms may also show impulsivity and emotional lability, in which binge episodes may function as a strategy to modulate unthinkable negative emotions. Borderline, avoidant, and paranoid personality features commonly co-occur. Empirical data suggest three main personality profiles associated with eating disorders: (1) a high-­functioning, perfectionistic style, with marked self-­criticism and negative affectivity but also some psychological strengths; (2) an emotionally dysregulated style, with affective lability, impulsivity, and relationship craving; and (3) an overconstricted style with general inhibition and passivity, interpersonal avoidance, and deficits in mentalization. Researchers found anorexia more associated with overconstricted characteristics, whereas the dysregulated group included mostly patients with bulimia. Despite a relative lack of personality studies of patients with binge-­ eating disorder, current evidence suggests high levels of neuroticism, impulsivity (especially when negative emotions are involved), and novelty seeking, and low levels of self-­directedness. Practitioners should begin their assessments by trying to understand the meaning and function of the symptoms within the larger context of patients’ personality dynamics, developmental histories, and unique self-­experiences. Patients with eating disorders often try to render their symptoms ego-­syntonic by claiming them to be a “lifestyle,” ignoring their ultimate destructiveness to the body. Paradoxically, patients with anorexia often feel better as their health worsens, because as they approach their ideal of thinness, often to the point of starvation, their anxiety may attenuate. Self-­ report screening/evaluation tools and symptom-­oriented clinical interviews should be enriched by multi-­informant and multimethod assessment procedures.

chunks.heading: 848 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 848 Adulthood Affective States Eating disorders may be construed as paradoxical expressions of overwhelming emotional pain under conditions in which the ability to think about painful mind states is missing. Impairments in affective functioning and regulation may arise from failure in the primary pa...
chunks.text: 848 Adulthood Affective States Eating disorders may be construed as paradoxical expressions of overwhelming emotional pain under conditions in which the ability to think about painful mind states is missing. Impairments in affective functioning and regulation may arise from failure in the primary parental holding system, making the subjective experience of the individual unbearable and overwhelming, and creating “indigestible” affective states. Food restriction might be conceptualized as a defense against myriad concealed and unacceptable feelings, while binge eating and compensatory behaviors may serve as a channel of expression for the affects that remain dissociated from patients’ conscious minds in everyday life. Common affective states include symptoms of depression, self-­ criticism, self-­ hatred, anxiety, alexithymia, impulsivity, guilt, and shame. Depressive symptoms may be both anaclitic and introjective, and include dejected mood, social withdrawal, and low self-­esteem. Experiences of anxiety are often intense, persistent, and overwhelming, with an intense fear of weight gain. Alexithymia and emotional avoidance may act as maintaining factors. In all eating disorders, eating alone, while feeling shame, guilt, self-­disgust, and sadness, is common. Symptoms are generally kept hidden for long periods. Shame, embarrassment, and self-­denigration are often part of the clinical picture. Anorexic patients feel shame about the shape of their bodies, while bulimic and binge-­eating patients feel shame about their lack of behavioral control, which may extend beyond food to self-harm and addiction. Rage and anger are common as well, and can be related to fantasied or real abandonment. Parasuicidal gestures and suicide attempts are frequent in this population, prompted by both depressed mood and failure to attain one’s ideal body. The prevalent emotional concerns in eating disorders are summarized below: • Feelings of being starved or “hungry” for care and affection, longings to be protected and cherished, and/or a frustrated rage at the lack of emotional nourishment. • Feelings of failure, weakness, embarrassment, guilt, and shame. • Low self-­esteem and feelings of being unworthy, ineffective, and not “good enough.” • Fear of abandonment or rejection by others, along with an inability to soothe oneself. • Anger, hostility, and aggressiveness, which (because they are frightening, dangerous, and intolerable) are denied, muted, projected, dissociated, or expressed through the body. • Fear that emotional expression will lead to a general loss of control. • Depressive symptoms, anhedonia, self-­punishment, and unhappiness, along with little or no pleasure, satisfaction, or enjoyment in life’s activities. • Feelings of emptiness or loss of control, leading to extreme anxiety, sadness, rage, excitement, and intense states. Cognitive Patterns Eating disorders may involve rigid thought processes, perfectionism, and perceptual distortions of one’s body. In minor or emerging symptoms, there may be dissatisfaction with the body or part of it (e.g., stomach, hips). In more severe conditions,

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 849
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 849 patients’ reality testing may be impaired, leading to extreme rigid thinking and severe perceptual body distortions. Eating disorders span the spectrum from neurosis to psychosis, though most are more closely related to personality disorders. Common cognitive patterns include f...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 849 patients’ reality testing may be impaired, leading to extreme rigid thinking and severe perceptual body distortions. Eating disorders span the spectrum from neurosis to psychosis, though most are more closely related to personality disorders. Common cognitive patterns include fears of maturity, unconscious wishes to avoid puberty and adulthood, and high levels of perfectionism. Some patients (especially those with anorexia) display a perfectionistic drive to achieve and a tendency to conform to external standards of success, which may trigger an intense pursuit of societal standards of thinness. Others (especially those with bulimia or binge eating) may also be vulnerable to socially prescribed perfectionism, or the perception that others are demanding perfection, which may increase cognitive discrepancies between the “ideal self” and the “actual self” and trigger dysfunctional eating behaviors. Anorexic patients are frequently preoccupied with narcissistic issues, perfectionism, self-­criticism, and asceticism. Omnipotence and idealization are common. Patients with severe eating disorders may use the defenses of splitting, withdrawal from external reality, and autistic fantasy. Patients with bulimia tend to be more dysregulated, with identity diffusion and immature defenses that impair cognitive apperception of self and body. Body checking (i.e., scrutinizing one’s body in a mirror, checking the fit of clothes, measuring body parts), compulsive self-­monitoring, and self-­referential ruminations about body shape and size are core features of all eating disorders. Cognitive patterns may include the following: • Preoccupation with being devalued, inadequate, incompetent, or unloved, together with coping strategies and defenses to deal with these issues. • A tendency to set high standards and employ overly critical self-­evaluations, and to see oneself as unattractive, grotesque, disgusting, and so forth. • A focus on being young, “little,” child-like, and innocent. • Excessive involvement with body image and excessive dependence on viewing one’s body in a mirror to track its shape and changes; sometimes distortions of body image resulting from impaired reality testing and overwhelming emotions. • An obsessive, rigid, inflexible cognitive style, with pervasive difficulties shifting attention and excessive focus on details; alternatively, an impressionistic cognitive style, with a tendency to talk in generalities because of an inability to offer specific details. Somatic States Somatic states involve the effect of mental conflict on the body. Patients with eating disorders have trouble distinguishing between mental and somatic states. Hunger may express feelings of emptiness or desire for emotional bonds. Identity diffusion is common during binges or purges, again reflecting a difficulty in distinguishing between mental and somatic states. These patients may have experienced critical failures in early parental responsivity and parental impingement, resulting in a mind–body split and what has been called a “disembodied self.” They then may develop interoceptive and exteroceptive problems: difficulties distinguishing between stimuli originating inside (e.g., hunger) and outside the body (e.g., touch). There may be disturbances in the perception or interpretation of body-based sensations that ordinarily accompany eating and elimination, such as an inability to detect or respond to signals evidencing hunger and satiety. Tactile perceptions (e.g.,

chunks.heading: 850 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 850 Adulthood feeling thighs touch or clothes tight on the body) are also often altered. To understand their somatic experiences, individuals with eating disorders may overrely on external information. For example, to assess satiety, they may try to gain insight into their body via visual or tactile...
chunks.text: 850 Adulthood feeling thighs touch or clothes tight on the body) are also often altered. To understand their somatic experiences, individuals with eating disorders may overrely on external information. For example, to assess satiety, they may try to gain insight into their body via visual or tactile information (e.g., looking in the mirror, pinching skin). Difficulties interpreting body signals may extend to deficits in somatosensory perception, including distortions in visualizing the body (thinking and/or imagining themselves as fat). Relevant somatic states may include the following: • Feelings of emptiness in the stomach, which may symbolize an empty, depleted self. • Bingeing and purging, often associated with underlying identity diffusion or dissociation. • Confusion of mental and somatic states. • Interoceptive disturbance (inability to identify and respond to internal sensations, including hunger, fullness, and affective states). • Exteroceptive disturbance (altered external sensations; e.g., feeling tight clothes on the skin, thighs spreading out, thighs touching, body fat jiggling, skin folds or rolls in stomach, skin jiggling). • Somatic sensations and bodily characteristics that are expressed literally and that lack experiential meaning. Relational Patterns Patients with eating disorders often have difficulties in relationships and in social contexts. They may relate superficially and in ways that seem immature. They may go to great lengths to keep their problems secret, making genuine emotional intimacy impossible. An intense fear of others’ negative evaluation and an “interpersonal paradox” between dependency needs and fear of abandonment are common. Family and other relationships may be characterized by lack of emotional support, disengagement, poor quality of communications, and lack of reciprocity. Patients with anorexia tend to approach relationships with a controlling, perfectionistic attitude. They need to be admired, but from a distance that poses no threat. They may handle their wish to be loved by compliance, politeness, and ingratiating behavior. They may be rigid, anxious, and introverted in social relations, and prioritize the feelings of others over their own. They may have trouble accepting positive gestures from others and feel significant levels of social discomfort. Patients with bulimia are also hungry for love, but their relationships tend to be unstable. Difficulties in managing interpersonal boundaries are common. In close relationships, they may suffer from frequent anxieties about both abandonment and engulfment. When they lose relationships, they may feel themselves unworthy or useless, and they may rage or act out self-­destructively. Patients with binge eating tend to be insecure in interpersonal relationships and unable to express emotions and affect. They may have a deep need for relationships along with a frustrated anger that causes them to deny dependency on others. Because of their fear of negative evaluation, they often experience shame and embarrassment in social interactions. Common relationship patterns include the following: • A tendency to be passive, unassertive, and submissive (mainly due to low self-­ esteem, self-­blaming, feelings of inadequacy and shame).

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 851
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 851 • Shyness, avoidant behaviors, and discomfort in social situations, along with a tendency to feel like an outsider or outcast. • Overt or more subtle dependency needs coexisting with an intense fear of being rejected or abandoned by emotionally significant others. • Anger, rese...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 851 • Shyness, avoidant behaviors, and discomfort in social situations, along with a tendency to feel like an outsider or outcast. • Overt or more subtle dependency needs coexisting with an intense fear of being rejected or abandoned by emotionally significant others. • Anger, resentment, and hostility toward others. Subjective Experience of the Therapist Clinical and empirical evidence suggests that many patients with eating disorders are difficult to treat because they tend to evoke from therapists strong negative emotional reactions such as rage, hate, hopelessness, frustration, disorganization, pity, and sorrow. Some elicit warm, protective, positive feelings as well. Such responses tend to affect staff performance, treatment process, and outcome; several clinicians have suggested that shifting focus from patients’ to therapists’ emotional responses can improve work with individuals with eating disorders. Countertransference issues often reflect a patient’s level of personality functioning, with the prominent use of splitting and the resultant diffusion of identity, polarized and intense affective states, and, in patients with a borderline personality organization, a tendency to act out. Clinical Illustration Case 1 A 22-year-old cisgender woman, a former athlete, was the youngest child and only girl in a family that was very strict about nutrition, exercise, and studies. After graduating from college, she became worried about “being thin enough to be a high-­fashion model.” She embarked on a series of diets and then began feeling anxious and nauseated whenever she went near food. She noted that “even when I force myself to eat, so I won’t get sick, I can’t tell if I’m hungry or not. I can never tell if I’ve eaten enough.” Her worries about being too fat and her regimen of exercise, nutrition, and study became ever more severe. She feared she looked pregnant and justified her avoidance of dating by claiming, “I don’t want to get pregnant.” Eventually, her symptoms became severe enough to require specialized treatment. Case 2 A 21-year-old cisgender women was admitted in an intensive inpatient treatment for a severe binge-­eating disorder and obesity. She reported a history of dysfunctional eating since early adolescence. At age 11, she was sexually abused by an adult family friend. She tried to talk to her parents about the molestation: She wanted to report the man to the police. They strongly disagreed, adding that a report would be humiliating for the entire family. She described a constant home atmosphere of intense fear and violence, and perceived her parents as incapable of responding to her distress, pain, and requests for care: “For instance, if I didn’t get the unrealistically high grades that my father wished, he would beat me.” She described long-­standing wishes to die to avoid the intense relational pain: “When I feel so depressed and empty, I can’t even force myself to throw up anymore. I just eat everything, and then I lie in bed. Every night I want to die in that bed. But every morning when I woke up, I am still there. And then I feel the urge to eat everything again.”

chunks.heading: 852 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 852 Adulthood S8 Disorders Related to Sexuality S8.1 Paraphilic Disorders Paraphilic disorders, also called “paraphilias” (Greek root) or “sexual perversions” (Latin root), are recurrent, intense sexual urges, fantasies, or behaviors that involve unusual objects, activities, or situations. To be cla...
chunks.text: 852 Adulthood S8 Disorders Related to Sexuality S8.1 Paraphilic Disorders Paraphilic disorders, also called “paraphilias” (Greek root) or “sexual perversions” (Latin root), are recurrent, intense sexual urges, fantasies, or behaviors that involve unusual objects, activities, or situations. To be classified as disorders, paraphilias must be a person’s most frequent fantasies or behaviors that induce sexual interest or pleasure, often involving rigidly ritualized sexual encounters. They must also cause clinically significant distress or impairment in social, occupational, or other important areas of functioning. Atypical sexual interests have historically been classified as psychiatric disorders, most notably in von Krafft-­Ebing’s (1939) 19th-­century work Psychopathia Sexualis. Sexologist Friedrich Salomon Krauss (1903) is credited with coining the term “paraphilia” in 1903. Freud (1905) called them “perversions,” defining them as deviations from the sexual object and aim. Some analysts still use this term for fantasies and activities, sexual or otherwise, characterized by compulsivity, rigidity, repetitiveness, and strong hostile and aggressive components. Early editions of DSM and ICD referred to paraphilias as “sexual deviations.” DSM-5 includes a diagnosis of transvestic disorder for persons aroused by cross-­ dressing. In contrast to DSM-IV and -IV-TR’s transvestic fetishism, limited to heterosexual men sexually aroused by cross-­dressing, in DSM-5 and DSM-5-TR, this diagnosis can be applied to anyone who cross-­dresses. ICD-11, in contrast, deleted the categories of fetishism and transvestitism, and included the following disorders: exhibitionistic, voyeuristic, pedophilic, coercive sexual sadistic, frotteuristic, other paraphilic disorder involving nonconsenting adults, and paraphilic disorder involving solitary behavior or consenting individuals. It is not unusual for a person to have more than one paraphilic interest. Paraphilic disorders are much more common in men than women. So far, however, paraphilic interests and acts in women have been little studied, with most research based solely on samples of male sexual offenders recruited in forensic settings. Paraphilic diagnoses have been criticized for their highly subjective nature and their use of scientific and medical language to disguise moral opprobrium. They have been referred to as an odd collection of “disorders,” since they include criminal behavior (voyeurism, exhibitionism, frotteurism, and child sexual abuse) combined with what appear to be victimless behaviors (fetishism and cross-­dressing), plus two intermediate noncriminal disorders (sexual sadism and sexual masochism) (Ross, 2015). They are often seen as an effort to control sexuality, much as masturbation, oral sex, anal sex, and homosexuality were once considered mental disorders or symptoms of mental disorders. In response to these criticisms, recent DSM editions note the possibility for persons to engage in consensual atypical sexual behavior without a diagnosis of mental disorder. They distinguish individuals with atypical sexual fantasies, urges, and behaviors (paraphilias) from those with diagnosable psychopathology (paraphilic disorders). A paraphilic disorder is diagnosed when an individual either suffers substantial personal distress due to their sexual interests, independent of societal judgment, or engages in sexual desires or behaviors that cause psychological harm, physical injury, or involve individuals who are unwilling or legally incapable of giving consent.

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 853
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 853 Although the causes of paraphilias remain unknown, many psychodynamic explanations have been offered. Some analysts object to replacing the concept of “perversions” with the concept of “paraphilias,” which, while helpful in reducing stigma, may excessively shift the diagnostic...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 853 Although the causes of paraphilias remain unknown, many psychodynamic explanations have been offered. Some analysts object to replacing the concept of “perversions” with the concept of “paraphilias,” which, while helpful in reducing stigma, may excessively shift the diagnostic focus onto the behavior or manifest symptom rather than the underlying personality difficulties. They consider the term “perversion” more appropriate, because it indicates a shift of internal content onto objects, based on disturbances of intra- and interpersonal functioning that impede the attainment of intimacy, creativity, and self-worth. The psychodynamic literature also notes the relevance of early traumatic experiences or losses. “Perverse functioning” is conceived as a defense against the pain associated with these experiences (Wood, 2013). In some cases, therapeutic insight-­oriented interventions may reduce paraphilic desires and behaviors, but these changes are not easily obtained because of the reinforcing nature of orgasm from paraphilic sexual activity. Subjective Experience of Paraphilic Disorders The subjective experience of individuals with a paraphilic disorder varies according to its specific nature (not all paraphilias are experienced equally). It also varies with the patient’s acceptance or rejection of paraphilic interests (due to factors such as religious beliefs, culture of origin, social class) and degree of openness (from total secrecy, to sharing with a partner, to involvement in a community with similar interests, to humiliating arrest for illegal activity), and varies with the degrf social opprobrium attached to a particular paraphilic interest. Affective States Affective states vary. Some individuals regard their interests in a matter-­of-fact, self-­ accepting way and express no conscious emotions about them. Other feelings may include pleasure when engaged in or thinking about the paraphilic interest; anxiety, depression, guilt, and self-­loathing about that interest; anxiety and fear of being discovered; and grandiose feelings of superiority to those who gage in conventional (“vanilla”) sexual practices. Cognitive Patterns Cognitive patterns can include obsessing about (but not acting upon) a paraphilic interest or making elaborate plans to act upon a specific desire. Some individuals immerse themselves in sex research literature, sometimes compulsively, acquiring technical knowledge about their paraphilia that is equal to or greater than that of recognized experts. Some rationalize satisfying their own desires at another’s expense. Others seek to inhibit any open expressions of their paraphilic interests. Somatic States Somatic states may include high levels of arousal and vigilance when one is engaged in sexual pursuits or behaviors, and numbness and emptiness when one is not. Some individuals use drugs or alcohol as a necessary precondition for engaging in a paraphilic activity.

chunks.heading: 854 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 854 Adulthood Relational Patterns Some individuals dissociate their paraphilic interests from the rest of their life; others belong to a like-­minded community. Some paraphilias involve exploitation of nonconsenting others (or, in the case of pedophilia, individuals who are not legally allowed to gi...
chunks.text: 854 Adulthood Relational Patterns Some individuals dissociate their paraphilic interests from the rest of their life; others belong to a like-­minded community. Some paraphilias involve exploitation of nonconsenting others (or, in the case of pedophilia, individuals who are not legally allowed to give consent). Some persons with pedophilic disorders enter into adult, heterosexual marriages while secretly acting on their paraphilic interests. Some “virtuous pedophiles” have formed online communities to help one another refrain from acting upon their sexual interests in children. Subjective Experience of the Therapist Subjective experiences of clinicians may include feelings of anxiety, concern, pity, judgment, revulsion, or voyeuristic curiosity. Legal reporting requirements may tap into authoritarian impulses to control or punish patient behavior or evoke antiauthoritarian responses to shield a patient (and the “sanctity” of the therapeutic encounter) from outside scrutiny. Clinical Illustration A 25-year-old man worked a midnight shift. On nights off, he would get drunk or high on cannabis and go to an area with some nightlife. There he would fall on the ground in a way that would “inadvertently” cause his pants to drop and expose his underwear to passing women. This evolved into making it appear as if his trousers were accidentally hooked on a fence, and torn as he tried to get free (he bought pants in large quantities at thrift shops). He would later masturbate to orgasm while thinking about the looks on the women’s faces. S8.2 Sexual Dysfunctions Sexual dysfunctions were subdivided in DSM-IV-TR, but DSM-5 and DSM-5-TR collapsed these into a single list: delayed ejaculation, erectile disorder, female orgasmic disorder, female sexual interest/arousal disorder, genito–­pelvic pain/penetration disorder, male hypoactive sexual desire disorder, premature (early) ejaculation, substance/ medication-­induced sexual dysfunction, and other specified–­unspecified sexual dysfunctions. In contrast, ICD-11 provides the new chapter 17, “Conditions Related to Sexual Health,” in which sexual response is defined as a complex interaction of psychological, interpersonal, social, cultural, and physiological processes. In ICD-11, to be considered a sexual dysfunction, the problem must (1) occur frequently, although it may be absent on some occasions; (2) have been present for at least several months; and (3) be associated with clinically significant distress (WHO, 2019). Subjective Experience of Sexual Dysfunctions Affective States Affective states vary in relation to particular sexual dysfunctions and, within the same diagnostic labels, across patients meeting criteria for the same diagnosis. Subjectivity is perhaps even more marked in sexual life than in other realms. Some patterns are

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 857
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 857 Subjective Experience of the Therapist Therapists encountering or treating individuals with impulse-­control disorders may find it difficult to empathize and may feel a reluctance to engage that parallels that of the patient. They may also experience intense fear and worry, alo...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 857 Subjective Experience of the Therapist Therapists encountering or treating individuals with impulse-­control disorders may find it difficult to empathize and may feel a reluctance to engage that parallels that of the patient. They may also experience intense fear and worry, along with feeling of helplessness and impotence. S10 Disorders Related to Addiction S10.1 Addictions S10.1.1 Substance‐Related Disorders Substance use disorders are defined by a pattern of cognitive, behavioral, and physiological dysfunction, in which an individual persistently consumes a substance despite experiencing substantial negative consequences directly related to its use. The more neutral term “substance use disorder” has replaced the phrase “drug addiction” because of its uncertain definition and stigmatizing connotations. Substance use disorders encompass 10 classes of substances, albeit not fully distinct: alcohol; caffeine; cannabis; hallucinogens; inhalants; opioids, sedatives, hypnotics, or anxiolytics; stimulants (amphetamine-­type substances, cocaine, and other stimulants); tobacco; and other (or unknown) substances. Three levels of severity are distinguished, based on the number of symptom criteria met: mild (2 or 3), moderate (4 or 5), and severe (6 or more). Similarly, ICD-11’s “disorders due to substance use” include conditions that result from a single occasion or repeated use of substances that have psychoactive properties, including certain medications. Disorders due to harmful nonmedical use of nonpsychoactive substances are also included in this grouping. “Addiction” is defined by tolerance, withdrawal symptoms, and intense craving for the substance of choice. “Tolerance” is a neuroadaptive response of the central nervous system, necessitating progressively higher doses of the substance to achieve desired effects. “Withdrawal” denotes the emergence of physical and psychological symptoms following the reduction or cessation of substance use. “Craving,” defined as an overwhelming and uncontrollable desire for the substance, is driven by a pursuit of its psychoactive effects. Historically, persons suffering from a substance use disorder have been viewed as individuals with a character flaw or moral deficiency, and stigmatized with labels such as “addict” or worse.
