Despite the fact that this condition is often progressive, chronic, and relapsing, however, it is amenable to treatment and recovery. It affects millions of people worldwide, with nicotine-­related disorders being most common. The next most frequently misused substance is alcohol, followed by marijuana, cocaine, heroin, prescription pain relievers, and designer drugs such as methamphetamines. Prevalence rates vary across countries and type of drugs used, as well as by demographic and socioeconomic characteristics of populations. Mortality rates are high, with increases in drug-­related overdose deaths over the past decade that were further accelerated during the COVID-19 pandemic. Rates of substance use disorders tend to be higher for assigned males and younger people. Psychodynamic views of addictive disorders tend to emphasize traumatic experiences and unresolved conflicts that arouse intolerably dysphoric affects. An influential psychodynamic model interprets them as self- ­medication against dysphoric feelings or

chunks.heading: 858 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 858 Adulthood disordered mood. It posits that suffering associated with substance use stems from the complex interaction among various dimensions of functioning: inability to regulate emotions, problems in self-­esteem, and problems in relationships. Childhood trauma, mistreatment, and neglect can h...
chunks.text: 858 Adulthood disordered mood. It posits that suffering associated with substance use stems from the complex interaction among various dimensions of functioning: inability to regulate emotions, problems in self-­esteem, and problems in relationships. Childhood trauma, mistreatment, and neglect can hinder the development of self-­regulation skills, especially in dealing with intense emotional states, leading individuals to turn to substances as maladaptive coping strategies. Beyond emotional dysregulation, those with substance use disorders may also experience alexithymia, anhedonia, and states of nonfeeling. The choice of a specific substance may be influenced by the level of personality organizations and by the dysphoric emotional state that the person seeks to mitigate or regulate. Both empirical studies and clinical reports suggest that appreciating these underlying dynamics can help sufferers understand and modify them and overcome their addictive behaviors. This formulation has influenced cognitive-­behavioral views of addiction as well. The self-­medication hypothesis posits common biopsychosocial pathological processes underlying all addictions. The complexity of interactions among these factors explains why not everyone who is exposed to drugs develops a substance use disorder, and why some individuals recover, while others progress into greater chronicity and associated negative outcomes. Because addictions are physiologically based and serve defensive function, they are very hard to stop. They ameliorate suffering, but because relief is only temporary, the person needs to repeat the behavior once the relief wears off. Thus, these disorders are compulsive in their nature. Although substance use disorders co-occur in approximately 50% of all people who seek mental health treatment, they may not feature in a patient’s presenting complaint. Comorbidities between psychiatric disorders and substance use disorders are common and often bidirectional: Psychiatric disorders (especially mood disorders, anxiety, psychotic and personality disorders, PTSD, and ADHD) are associated with an increased risk for substance use and misuse. In turn, substance use disorders are associated with increased risk for a mental disorder. Comorbidity between substance use disorders and personality disorders varies across different substances, with rates ranging from 10 to 100%, depending on the disorder. Highest correlations are between excessive substance use and psychopathic, emotionally dysregulated, and narcissistic personalities. Some studies have explored dimensional personality factors relating to substance use, converging on a profile characterized by high levels of impulsivity, disinhibition, and negative emotionality. These features have been linked to severity of dependence, age of onset, and etiology. Temperamental factors such as impulsivity, sensation seeking, and novelty seeking may act as mediators between personality and substance use disorders. In general, the presence of a personality disorder increases severity of substance use disorders and weakens cooperation in treatment. Personality disorders seem higher in patients who misuse multiple substances—­a clinical population that has been growing in recent years. In intakes, especially with patients with personality disorders, it is critical to ask about addictions when patients do not mention them spontaneously. This should be done in a matter-­of-fact, nonjudgmental manner. Because denial of substance use is common, clinicians must tolerate the fact that ameliorating problems with substances may take time given the important defensive function that substance use disorders may play in a person’s psychic life. Psychodynamic psychotherapy helps to reduce emotional suffering. It can be useful to tell patients early in treatment that as they feel better, they will likely become less interested in engaging in their addictions. This may help them realize that relieving

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 859
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 859 emotional pain is the motive for their substance misuse and that healing that pain will improve their substance-­related problems. There is a wide range of psychodynamic supportive and expressive techniques for treating people diagnosed with addictions, including empirically su...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 859 emotional pain is the motive for their substance misuse and that healing that pain will improve their substance-­related problems. There is a wide range of psychodynamic supportive and expressive techniques for treating people diagnosed with addictions, including empirically supported therapies, such as supportive–­expressive therapy and dynamic deconstructive psychotherapy. Some therapists make treatment contingent on a patient’s participating in a program such as Alcoholics Anonymous because of the risk that the person will never get the courage to try to stop or reduce the substance use. The addition of a community that supports sobriety can be critical to recovery. Subjective Experience of Substance‐Related Disorders Affective States Psychoactive drugs help people to tolerate intolerable feelings, especially those involving terror, rage, and aggression. Affective life, at one extreme, may be perplexing, elusive, cut off, or absent; at the other extreme, feelings may be experienced as overwhelming and unbearable. The effects of substances, over and above their class, are specific and temporary; the main emotions a person feels will generally determine which type of substance is preferred. Opiates help users to feel “calm,” “mellow,” or “normal.” Stimulants counter low energy, feelings of weakness, and feelings of being unloved; high-­energy individuals may use them to augment a hypomanic adaptation or to combat depression. In low doses, sedatives (e.g., alcohol, benzodiazepines, barbiturates) can overcome feelings of isolation and promote closeness and warmth (e.g., “I can feel like one of the guys . . . I can join the human race”); in high doses, they drown out negative, unwelcome feelings and lead to social isolation. The following are common affective states in individuals with substance use disorders: • Boredom and depression, sense of unworthiness alternating with demandingness. • Pervasive anhedonia that may underlie the often “magical” relief often first experienced with the drug of choice. Such euphoria is interpreted as pleasure but in fact may result from relief of the underlying anhedonia. • Intense rage or anger, which can fester or erupt, sometimes associated with agitation or a sense of falling apart. • Feelings of helplessness, emptiness, fragmentation, impoverishment, shame, and low self-­esteem that may lead to defensive attitudes of omnipotence and bravado. • Physical addiction and the desperate need not to be deprived of the substance. Patients report feeling awful without their substance of choice; they may feel panic if they cannot obtain it. As physical dependence develops, many of the feelings relieved by the drugs are made more intense and intolerable when the substances are abruptly withdrawn. Cognitive Patterns Cognitions tend to center on rationalizations and denial that support addiction (e.g., “My drinking isn’t as bad as my father’s. He was a real drunk”). The dangers of addiction are denied, as in comments such as “I never thought about it.” The fact

chunks.heading: 862 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 862 Adulthood created by the addiction. Problems related to compulsive gambling include maladaptive behavior, interpersonal difficulties, and damage to professional and/or educational activities. Gambling often leads to financial difficulties and, in extreme cases, legal consequences of criminal beh...
chunks.text: 862 Adulthood created by the addiction. Problems related to compulsive gambling include maladaptive behavior, interpersonal difficulties, and damage to professional and/or educational activities. Gambling often leads to financial difficulties and, in extreme cases, legal consequences of criminal behaviors (e.g., theft). Individuals with a gambling disorder may deceive family members, partners, and/or therapists to conceal the extent of their gambling. Most people with this addiction have one or more co-­occurring psychiatric disorders, including depression, anxiety, and suicidal ideation. As in other addictions, alexithymia is common. Additional risk factors include traumatic and adverse experiences, which have been found to be related to earlier onset of gambling and increasing severity of related problems. The gambling population is not homogenous. Some individuals reliably experience high levels of arousal, whereas others perceive their arousal as too low. Therefore, while gambling serves to increase arousal and seek euphoric states for some, it may have a calming effect on others. “Gaming disorder” refers to excessive and prolonged involvement in video games, leading to behavioral and cognitive symptoms, tolerance, withdrawal, and significant impairment in several areas (relationships, school, work). Excessive gaming impairs social functioning and interferes with meeting basic needs such as eating, sleeping, or maintaining adequate personal hygiene. Absence of gaming opportunities may trigger negative emotional states, including irritability, sadness, and boredom. Individuals with game-­related problems may show high levels of aggression, depression, anxiety, impulsivity, substance abuse, ADHD, obsessions and compulsions. Self-­esteem, quality of life, and academic success can be severely affected. The concept of internet addiction (or “problematic internet use”) is so heterogeneous that the phrase is often used as an umbrella term for various compulsive activities enacted online, including excessive use of social networks, problematic cybersex, online gambling and shopping, and excessive gaming. Despite not being yet recognized as a distinct diagnostic entity, the potentially harmful consequences of internet addiction are receiving increasing attention. High levels of internet use may adversely affect individuals’ well-being. Related symptoms may include apathy, anhedonia, social withdrawal, anxiety, depression, concentration difficulties, sleep disorders, impairments in self-­esteem, and dissociative experiences. This form of addiction often co-­occurs with mood disorders, obsessive–­compulsive disorders, PTSD, ADHD, and anxiety (especially social anxiety). A core factor is the feeling of loneliness and interpersonal incompetence, which can make online interactions more appealing than face-to-face ones. While problematic internet use stems from a desire for interpersonal connection, it can paradoxically lead to prioritizing fleeting online encounters and the development of a fictitious identity. This, in turn, may further reduce the sense of self-worth. Sex addiction has clear psychopathological affinities with substance and gambling addictions. Although neither taxonomy includes sex addiction within this group, some authors have argued that this clinical phenomenon fits best within an addictive model because of behavioral escalation, withdrawal symptoms, and the dysphoric feelings arising from attempts to stop or reduce the behavior. The closest ICD-11 designation would be compulsive sexual behavior disorder; this would be consistent with writings that note the presence of sexually exciting fantasies (whether or not paraphilic) and recurrent, intense sexual urges and behaviors that cause distress in social, occupational, and other areas. But this rather misses the point: The problematic domain is the addictive behavior, not simply impaired impulse regulation.

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns 863
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns 863 Subjective Experience of the Therapist Negative countertransferences are common in work with patients diagnosed with either substance use disorders or behavioral addictions, and they can interfere with effective treatment. They may reflect the negative attitudes many have towar...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns 863 Subjective Experience of the Therapist Negative countertransferences are common in work with patients diagnosed with either substance use disorders or behavioral addictions, and they can interfere with effective treatment. They may reflect the negative attitudes many have toward those with addictive disorders. It is not unusual for therapists to cease trying to understand the mental lives of patients whose problems include substance use or addiction. At worst, they may berate them or convey a belief that all people with a substance use disorder are liars; at best, they may become frustrated with their slow progress. Negative emotional responses in clinicians may also be evoked by the attitude of deep mistrust with which these patients may view them. Even though it can be challenging, an empathic stance is crucial to understanding and mitigating patients’ suffering. Patients who are also diagnosable with a personality disorder are prone to angry and hostile outbursts. In these cases, therapists need to tolerate intense countertransferences while also discouraging self-­destructive behaviors. Supervision, consultation, and continued personal therapy are valuable. Setting up a therapeutic contract stipulating that a patient undergo medical detoxification (as necessary for substance use disorders) and rehabilitation before starting treatment may help the clinician and patient build a solid therapeutic frame and maintain the alliance.
