chunks.heading: 876 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 876 Adulthood pathways that are pockmarked by the experience of environmental crisis, highlighting interindividual differences in resilience and vulnerability factors, and testing innovative techniques and therapies designed to respond contingently to a global need. At the same time, to account for...
chunks.text: 876 Adulthood pathways that are pockmarked by the experience of environmental crisis, highlighting interindividual differences in resilience and vulnerability factors, and testing innovative techniques and therapies designed to respond contingently to a global need. At the same time, to account for the specific experience that patients who are especially sensitive to climate change report, the term “ecoanxiety,” an affective response to an environmental circumstance (Clayton, 2020), has become widespread and has been operationalized as a measure of stress. In 2017, the American Psychological Association introduced the term as a specific type of generalized anxiety. Ecoanxiety can be conceived as a pretrauma type of symptomatology, as it develops as a feeling of anticipation toward an impending catastrophe. It also can involve depressive responses, in which grief and loss are central. In relation to this complexity, there has been a proliferation of increasingly specific terms that try to capture different nuances of the new eco-­affectivity. Researchers are invoking constructs variously termed “eco-guilt” (Mallet et al., 2012), “eco-anger” (Stanley, Hogg, Leviston, Walker, 2021), “eco-grief” (Cunsolo & Ellis, 2018), and “solastalgia” (Albrecht, 2005), a term used to condense the sense of defeat felt toward the place one inhabits while witnessing its devastation. Many of these topics are driven by the subjective experience of indigenous people, who have participated in qualitative research conducted in their independent communities. Individuals who are directly concerned with climate change seem to be subject to the experience of eco-­burnout. Others show “ecophobia,” a term implying not only a paralyzing state of ecoanxiety but also a patent contempt for the natural environment. This experience suggests a lack of connection with the physical context that hosts human life and has been hypothesized to underlie behaviors of environmental depredation. It can thus be traced to the need to reverse the condition of human helplessness toward a superordinate natural entity. Disputes between nations are rooted in conflicts over controlling territories and resources. Climate change imposes geopolitical changes that translate into intense migratory flows (a consequence of frequent border wars, to be added to the spontaneous exodus of “climate refugees” fleeing territories that have become unlivable), and into alterations of already precarious economic balances that affect the international community. Economic and housing insecurity, unavailability of necessary medical care, traumatic exposure to violence, imprisonment, torture, and combat are all elements that increase psychosocial risk for civilians living in war territories. Often, even political and spiritual guides fail, contributing to a collective sense of alienation. The United Nations reports that for 1 in 10 people, the chaotic experience of war brings a subsequent deterioration of mental health (noting in particular anxiety, depression, posttraumatic stress disorder, schizophrenia, and bipolar disorders). In researching the consequences of the recent Russian–­U krainian conflict (RUW-22), several studies have been dedicated to investigating “nuclear anxiety,” fear of the use of nuclear weapons. With the alteration of climates and landscapes, a radical change in the biome also occurs, whereby predominant life forms are established while others perish. This leads to an impoverishment of biodiversity, and, at the same time, new fears emerge for humans relating to the dangers of the consequences of these mutations. Even the COVID-19 pandemic, an event sufficient to disturb the sense of continuity in daily experience itself, has been linked to the environmental vulnerabilities resulting from climate change. The last 5 years have seen substantial research into the traumatic impact on worldwide mental health of the restrictions adopted to contain

chunks.heading: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 877
chunks.preview: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 877 the Sars-Cov-2 pandemic. Even though the international health emergency can now be considered over (Cénat et al., 2022), investigators have concluded that anxious, depressive, and posttraumatic symptoms attributable to the pandemic r...
chunks.text: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 877 the Sars-Cov-2 pandemic. Even though the international health emergency can now be considered over (Cénat et al., 2022), investigators have concluded that anxious, depressive, and posttraumatic symptoms attributable to the pandemic remain clinically relevant. Protecting a sense of personal security in contexts affected by war and during states of national emergency is a challenge, especially for those with preexisting psychological vulnerabilities. Scholars expect that other global epidemics may develop in the future, facilitated partly by the dynamism of the globalized world and partly by the alteration of biological balances. Many have consequently emphasized the need to implement public policies that can help to contain what they anticipate to be a true psychiatric emergency of global magnitude (Royal College of Psychiatrists, 2021). Environmental uncertainty affects not only the psychological experience of the present but also the ability to envision a desirable future. Some researchers have found that economic insecurity and unsustainability attributed to current overpopulation have influenced many people to choose not to reproduce (Helm, Kemper, & White, 2021). And with the free circulation of unverified information, many of us lack the tools necessary to interpret immediate and distant events. The confusion between legitimate and arbitrary sources of information that are made accessible by digital platforms, the creation of echo chambers in which partial information circulates, and the intolerance of scientific dissemination that cannot yet provide definitive answers to complex current questions have all increased an attitude of epistemic mistrust toward institutional bodies—­an attitude that has worsened notably in the pandemic years (Roozenbeek et al., 2020). Feeling suspicion of institutions that should offer protection contributes to the sense of insecurity and fuels the impression of living in an unreliable, unintelligible social environment. In the clinical field, at the personal level, the same epistemic mistrust has been linked to the experience of instability that characterizes internal working models of insecure and disorganized attachments. In this new millennium characterized by so much unpredictability in the environment, we need to consider a new existential dimension, which develops online and involves incursions of artificial intelligence and apprehension about cyber threats. The copious technological advances of our era imply profound transformations for the professional world. On the one hand, these advances create new perspectives and new applications (especially for the young); on the other, they raise concerns about the future of one’s professional identity. Subjective Experience of Climate Change, War, and Pandemic Affective States Affective states may include anxiety, depression, nostalgia, melancholy, guilt, shame, fear of annihilation, impotence, indifference, anger, frustration, disorientation, and desperation. People affected by catastrophes that threaten their physical and mental integrity may experience states of shock and difficulties in adaptation. Feelings of hope and determination that push us toward proactive efforts to improve living conditions for ourselves and others are also possible. Researchers have found that during critical periods of lockdown associated with the COVID-19 pandemic, and following traumatic experiences related to war, terrorism, or climatic events, there are alterations in sleep patterns and dream life, including increased insomnia, parasomnias, and nightmares featuring catastrophic scenarios.

chunks.heading: 878 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 878 Adulthood Cognitive Patterns Cognitive patterns associated with an uncertain environment include obsessive strategies to keep anxiety under control, including intrusive, recurrent ruminations. Fatalism and pessimism are common. Some individuals search compulsively for information to feel adequat...
chunks.text: 878 Adulthood Cognitive Patterns Cognitive patterns associated with an uncertain environment include obsessive strategies to keep anxiety under control, including intrusive, recurrent ruminations. Fatalism and pessimism are common. Some individuals search compulsively for information to feel adequately updated on current events. The following cognitive strategies are also possible: denial of the severity of the environmental crisis; propagation of conspiratorial explanations for disturbing phenomena; minimization of troubling phenomena as too distant in time or space to be actual threats; distrust of conventional media and a turn toward independent sources of information. Limitations in controlling potentially catastrophic events can engender learned helplessness, leaving individuals feeling a profound isolation and a belief that no one can help them. Somatic States Substance abuse to cope with emotional distress is common. Somatic states affected by the environmental crisis may include sleep disorders, eating disorders, apathy, exhaustion, burnout, stress responses, and a generic deterioration of health resulting from physical phenomena that affect human life directly, for example, warmer temperatures, droughts, floods, hurricanes, fires, pollution). Preoccupation with climate change, war, or pandemic can be manifested as physiological symptoms such as heat strokes, tachycardia, and labored breathing, particularly during warm seasons. Relational Patterns Apprehension about an imminent scarcity of resources and fear for one’s survival can lead to social withdrawal or can motivate aggregation into rigidly ideological subcultures. Identification with extreme political positions in polarized conflicts with antagonistic groups is common. New communication technologies allow for connections between individuals and communities that are geographically far apart; although this reality offers intercultural exchange, it may also exacerbate the need for cognitive closure. Relationships may be affected by both cognitive rigidity and emotional distress. Issues that animate debates on social injustice often ignite civil conflicts that fuel a climate of collective tension. Emotional dysregulation, resulting in resorts to aggression and violence becomes more frequent. Subjective Experience of the Therapist Clinicians dealing with the complex emotions evoked by environmental stress need to face their own resistance to painful knowledge and be mentally prepared for annoyances that come with the issues that concern them as a citizen. Because patient and therapist often confront the same realities, clinicians need to take care to avoid fusional dynamics that can interfere with professional objectivity. Or they may feel tensions between honoring their ethical commitment to disseminate valid scientific information and respecting the patient’s opinion. The online psychological services that have been widely implemented since the pandemic pose new questions about the limits of the psychotherapeutic setting and may also challenge the adaptability of therapists unfamiliar with digital tools, leading

chunks.heading: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 879
chunks.preview: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 879 to unease, fatigue, nostalgia, and feelings of incompetence. Others may find in online counseling and psychotherapy new ways to extend their practice to those who have been previously difficult to reach. Less predictable environments...
chunks.text: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 879 to unease, fatigue, nostalgia, and feelings of incompetence. Others may find in online counseling and psychotherapy new ways to extend their practice to those who have been previously difficult to reach. Less predictable environments pose greater risks of dropout and sudden treatment interruption, with discouraging effects on clinicians. 15.2. Racial, Ethnic, and Cultural Minoritized Groups Subjective Experiences of Racial, Ethnic, and Cultural Minoritized Groups Affective States Affects can vary widely depending on the person and the situation. Individuals from racial, ethnic, and cultural minoritized groups may feel anxious or concerned with not being treated with respect and dignity or with not feeling adequate due to stereotyping and discrimination. They may feel sad, isolated, lonely, or angry. In challenging circumstances (e.g., at work or when starting new relationships), they may feel dysphoric and experience mood swings. Cognitive Patterns Cognitive patterns vary, depending on the patient’s background and on how authority is culturally perceived. Individuals from hierarchical or autocratic/theocratic societies may expect those in roles of authority to be more active and directive and may become, by the standards of more egalitarian societies, overly compliant to fulfill the role of “good patient.” Depending on cultural expectations and norms, and on language proficiency and comfort, they may hesitate to disagree with the therapist (a reasonable reaction in the face of societal power differences). Individuals who have been hurt within their family or community may distance themselves from people in their racial or ethnic group. Wishes for mainstream acceptance may impel them to minimize or deny differences between themselves and others. Different cultural experiences may be expressed in diverse ways when patients navigate racial, ethnic, and cultural differences and similarities between themselves and their therapists. Somatic States If somatic complaints are present, they are usually related to anxiety and hyperarousal. Individuals from some ethnic, cultural, religious and linguistic minorities may communicate their distress more via somatic complaints than via explicitly psychic symptoms (see section SA61 on somatic symptom disorder, pp. 578–579). Within a person’s racial, ethnic, or religious community, bodily expressions of distress may be more acceptable than verbal expressions. Relational Patterns Relationships can be affected by mild feelings of concern and wariness, or more explicit ambivalence (i.e., attachment coexisting with suspicion), or severe mistrust and resentment toward those from other backgrounds (including the therapist). Based on experiences of discrimination and/or collective trauma, a person may be reluctant to trust a

chunks.heading: 880 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 880 Adulthood therapist from a different racial or ethnic background. For some minoritized people working with a majority-­status therapist, the inherent power differential in a therapeutic relationship can invite projections of a wished-­for omniscient parent, which may generalize to others of majo...
chunks.text: 880 Adulthood therapist from a different racial or ethnic background. For some minoritized people working with a majority-­status therapist, the inherent power differential in a therapeutic relationship can invite projections of a wished-­for omniscient parent, which may generalize to others of majority status. Individuals from minoritized backgrounds may fear they will get suboptimal care and may defend against these expectations by devaluing or idealizing their therapist. Form of intercultural transference include overcompliance and friendliness to please the therapist, denial about the relevance of culture in the encounter, mistrust or hostility, and ambivalence about wanting help but feeling vulnerable (Comas-Díaz & Jacobsen, 1991). Subjective Experience of the Therapist Therapists may question their ability to connect with and deeply understand patients who comes from backgrounds or identities that are different, a worry that may create estrangement: “We are miles apart from each other.” Depending on preconscious prejudices and unconscious conflicts about their own race, ethnicity, culture, or religion vis-à-vis certain other communities, they may project internal issues onto patients. Therapists who themselves identify with a minority may feel unremitting pressure to prove their competence and worth according to assumed majority norms. Therapists who find it hard to empathize with certain patients may find themselves feeling angry, anxious, envious, or bored. Or they may become inhibited by guilt for belonging to an ethnicity that currently or historically exploited or abused communities with which the patient identifies. Therapists’ ambivalence about their own sociocultural background may lead to ambivalence toward patients of that background. Overidentification and reaction formation may lead majority-­status therapists to pity and infantilize minority patients. These defenses may incline a therapist to collude with a patient’s maladaptive behavior, potentially compromising treatment and endangering aspects of the patient’s life outside treatment. Clinicians may also have stereotypic beliefs that narrow and distort their perception of certain patients. Or they may show excessive curiosity about nuances of their patients’ culture that may strike patients as voyeuristic and self-­satisfying rather than therapeutically motivated; for example, non-­I ndians who are curious about the caste system in India may offend a South Asian patient by asking about the person’s caste. Ethnic and cultural differences may lead to misinterpretations of nonverbal communications. Relative to the norm in a therapist’s culture, higher or lower levels of verbal and emotional expressiveness can be interpreted respectively as a patient’s being indiscreet and alarmingly histrionic or else withdrawn and inhibited. In cases of racial, ethnic, cultural, or religious similarity between therapist and patient, patients may find relief in not having to explain their cultural or religious background and may find it easier to develop trust. Patients who have been hurt by family or community may find it challenging to form a trusting alliance with a therapist of a similar background. At the same time, the therapist may make assumptions of sameness that hinder a more complete exploration of the patient’s unique experience of race, ethnicity, culture, and religion. Countertransference reactions in these instances can span the range of overidentification. Treatment difficulties may arise from a sense of “the two of us against the world,” anger that the patient may not have the same psychological or social resources to cope, ambivalence, and excessive hope (ComasDíaz & Jacobsen, 1991).

chunks.heading: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 881
chunks.preview: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 881 15.3. Minoritized Linguistic Groups Subjective Experience of Minoritized Linguistic Groups Affective States People who are multilingual may experience their affective selves differently depending on which language they are speaking....
chunks.text: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 881 15.3. Minoritized Linguistic Groups Subjective Experience of Minoritized Linguistic Groups Affective States People who are multilingual may experience their affective selves differently depending on which language they are speaking. They may feel more comfortable expressing certain feelings in one language, either because it feels more expressive or because it distances emotion. Affective states may include anxiety about being misunderstood and shame at making linguistic mistakes. Depressive feelings may occur, with accompanying worries about unacceptability to others. Language barriers may create frustration about finding appropriate words to convey needs and feelings, making the person feel like a child with inadequate words for self-­expression. Fear and shame about mispronunciation may impede fluid free association. The therapist may easily misinterpret the resulting silence or affective dissonance. Cognitive Patterns Cognitive patterns may include thoughts such as, “I don’t think people can understand me.” Patients with perceived linguistic limitations may ruminate about how to talk with others. Their need to rehearse wording and phrasing to ensure effective communication may jeopardize the spontaneity vital to authentic communication. Patients with particular accents or with less proficiency in the language of the dominant society may worry (sometimes rightly) that others see them as less competent or intelligent. They may consequently feel disempowered socially. Somatic States and Relational Patterns Both somatic states and relationsips largely overlap with those of persons from racial, ethnic, and cultural minoritized groups (see earlier discussion), though the sense of being regressed into the position of an inarticulate child is notable for individuals in linguistic minorities. Subjective Experience of the Therapist Therapists may feel apprehensive that language will be a barrier to effective communication. If they often have to ask patients to repeat certain words or phrases, they may find themselves irritated by having to intervene so often instead of maintaining their customary reserve. Yet if they remain relatively quiet and avoid interrupting, they may fail to take in much of the patients’ material. A therapist may conclude, “Only a therapist who speaks this client’s language could possibly treat them.” The patient may be thinking, “What a relief that this therapist doesn’t speak my language. These therapists all know each other, and they all gossip. All my secrets would become public!” Or the patient may feel profoundly unheard. When difficulties seem formidable, it would be valuable to include an interpreter in the linguistic and cultural translation for the patient. Therapists should pay attention when a patient switches from one language to another, as the shift may reflect either a defensive avoidance of difficult emotional

chunks.heading: 882 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 882 Adulthood material or a need to express oneself fully in the language that makes such expression emotionally possible. 15.4. Minoritized Religious Groups Subjective Experience of Minoritized Religious Groups Affective States Affective states may include anxiety, anger, resentment, mistrust, and...
chunks.text: 882 Adulthood material or a need to express oneself fully in the language that makes such expression emotionally possible. 15.4. Minoritized Religious Groups Subjective Experience of Minoritized Religious Groups Affective States Affective states may include anxiety, anger, resentment, mistrust, and hostility. Given the potential divisiveness of religious differences, patients may find it difficult or impossible to trust those of other faiths or to have confidence in a therapist from a different religious background, especially if that background involves a historical or current conflict with theirs. Yet some patients seek a therapist of any religion other than their own, especially when hoping to escape trauma or pain that they attribute to the religion in question. Cognitive Patterns Cognitive patterns may include a sense of moral inferiority with attendant misperceptions arising from the projection of self-­attacking mental states onto others, including the therapist. Either patient or therapist may assume that the other’s religious convictions are primitive or repressive or dangerously latitudinarian; either assumption undermines the working alliance and the potential significance of religion and spirituality for each individual. Somatic States Somatic states related to differences of religion can include a general state of tension and/or physical sensations of anxiety. Certain somatic states may be a part of a person’s religious or spiritual experiences and practices; these can be explored in psychotherapy. Relational Patterns Relational patterns can range from virtual absence of any contacts with similar religious backgrounds in one’s social circle (with consequent uncomfortable feelings of isolation) to a need to associate exclusively with those in the same religious community. Subjective Experience of the Therapist Internal experiences of therapists may include curiosity, anxiety, anger, resentment, contempt, or even hatred. Therapists with a history of personal or collective misunderstandings and maltreatments based on religion may find it difficult to empathize with a patient of another religion. Agnostic, atheistic, and secular therapists may have trouble empathizing with any religious or spiritual sensibility, leading to feelings of mistrust, hostility, or moral superiority. Conversely, via reaction formation to counteract their own biases, therapists may become overly gratifying toward patients of religions they see as problematic. Therapists unfamiliar with certain religious or spiritual practices

chunks.heading: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 883
chunks.preview: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 883 may inappropriately label them as pathological. Countertransference reactions can span the same range as those discussed earlier with other groups in society that are minoritized based on their demographic identities. 15.5. Minoritiz...
chunks.text: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 883 may inappropriately label them as pathological. Countertransference reactions can span the same range as those discussed earlier with other groups in society that are minoritized based on their demographic identities. 15.5. Minoritized Political Groups Subjective Experience of Minoritized Political Groups Affective States Affective states may include mistrust and hostility, dysphoria, mood instability, and anxiety. Feelings of hostility may objectively correspond to the political reality of external circumstances or social injustice, or may be projections of the patient’s conflicts and prejudices, or may represent some combination of these. Cognitive Patterns Cognitive patterns may be dominated by suspicion or fear. Individuals in politically marginalized groups may consequently avoid political statements in their social networks, thereby censoring their ideas. Patients may assume that someone whose politics differ (including the therapist) could not possibly understand their values and involvements with consequent damage to the therapeutic alliance. This issue is amplified when there is greater political polarization within a community or country. Somatic States Somatic states largely overlap with those of individuals from other minoritized groups (see earlier discussion). Relational Patterns Although patterns may resemble those of individuals in other minorities, the fact that one’s politics is chosen and potentially changeable can evoke more hostility than fixed aspects of identity. Many individuals find it hard to have genuinely intimate relationships with people who disagree politically in significant ways. Subjective Experience of the Therapist Therapists working with patients of differing political persuasions may encounter some inherent difficulties in being respectful toward people of significantly different political convictions. Their inner reactions may include feelings of entitlement and righteousness: “What I believe is common sense.” As with patients in racial, ethnic, and cultural minorities, clinicians may project disowned negative aspects of self onto politically dissimilar patients. Patients may sense the therapist’s suppressed criticism, leading to negative feelings that the therapist defensively interprets as originating in the patient. Some therapists may feel bored and detached rather than angry and critical, perhaps to defend against unresolved aggression. Therapist and patient may collude, consciously or unconsciously, in declining to address politics at all. Countertransference factors may incline the therapist to misinterpret the patient’s politics as intrapsychic phenomena. A

chunks.heading: 884 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 884 Adulthood therapist or a patient who has suffered politically based trauma may approach the ideas of the other in both helpful and potentially challenging ways. 15.6. Lesbian, Gay, and Bisexual Populations Our understanding of lesbian, gay, and bisexual (LGB) populations has matured rapidly, as...
chunks.text: 884 Adulthood therapist or a patient who has suffered politically based trauma may approach the ideas of the other in both helpful and potentially challenging ways. 15.6. Lesbian, Gay, and Bisexual Populations Our understanding of lesbian, gay, and bisexual (LGB) populations has matured rapidly, as has our nomenclature for sexual orientations. The term “homosexuality” has been almost completely replaced with once offensive terms such as “gay” and “queer.” “Gay,” “lesbian,” and “bisexual” have become both positive social identities and acceptable adjectives for sexual orientations. “Queer” is now an umbrella term for sexual orientations and gender expressions that may not “match” one’s biological sex. Sexual orientation should not be confused with gender expression, as many combinations are possible. For example, a gay man might be “masculine” in gender expression, a heterosexual man might be “feminine” in gender expression, a lesbian might be “feminine” in gender expression, and a heterosexual woman might be “masculine” in gender expression. Although the term “gay” can refer to all lesbian, gay, and bisexual individuals, we use it here only in referencing gay men. While historically a psychoanalytic understanding of lesbian, gay, and bisexual individuals posited an intrapsychic pathological etiology for sexual orientation, such as unresolved oedipal issues, current psychoanalytic understanding is informed by science supporting a biologically based, nonpathological etiology. Scientific inquiry has documented same-­gender sexual behavior in over 1,500 nonhuman species. A preponderance of evidence suggests genetic factors and early uterine environment as significant for sexual orientation. The evidence does not exclude psychodynamic etiologies (which need not equate with pathology), but does focus more on “nature” than “nurture.” Of course, this strict dichotomy is obsolete with the rise of epigenetics, the science of how nurture may directly affect gene expression. Lesbian, gay, and bisexual populations as a group are as psychologically robust as heterosexual populations; they report mental health problems in no greater frequency than the population as a whole. Some evidence suggests that when lesbian, gay, or bisexual individuals report a mental health problem, it may reflect the kinds of distress to which they are subjected as sexual minorities. Lesbian, gay, and bisexual populations do not have common affective states, typical mental content, or known accompanying somatic states. Instead, they parallel the heterosexual population in diversity and range along these continua. Their relationship patterns may involve “families of choice”—nonbiologically related groups that self-­select and organize to celebrate holidays together, support one another in times of need, and otherwise play roles typical of a nuclear or extended biological families. LGB populations are subjected to significant minority stress. Unlike persons in other marginalized groups, they cannot always rely on the recognition and support of their families; in fact, family members may be an additional source of stress. It is not unusual for homonegative dynamics to occur in a sociocultural context that is indifferent or even collusive. Episodes of discrimination and violence can have a profound emotional impact transcending their direct effects; that is, they may affect not only the direct victims but also those who cannot avoid thinking that such disasters could happen to them, too. Many studies have confirmed the impact of minority stress on lesbian women and gay men. Meyer (1995, 2003), who tested a minority stress model in a community

chunks.heading: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 885
chunks.preview: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 885 sample of 741 gay men, described a continuum ranging from distal stressors—­objective events and conditions—­to proximal personal processes based on individual perceptions and appraisals. We can therefore construct a continuum extend...
chunks.text: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 885 sample of 741 gay men, described a continuum ranging from distal stressors—­objective events and conditions—­to proximal personal processes based on individual perceptions and appraisals. We can therefore construct a continuum extending from objective to subjective on three dimensions: (1) actual experiences of discrimination and violence; (2) perceived stigma, or expectations of rejection and discrimination; and (3) internalized homonegative prejudice, the directing of negative societal attitudes toward the self. Recent research has also focused on the “microaggressions” that gender and sexual minorities experience on a daily basis. This term refers to messages of hostility and contempt, verbal or nonverbal, that may be expressed less explicitly. Initially identified by the psychiatrist Chester Pierce to depict ubiquitous communications, often in the form of “criticism” or “jokes,” received by people who are victims of racism, microaggressions aimed at other stigmatized groups (e.g., women or sexual and gender minorities) have since been studied and divided into (1) microassaults: explicitly denigrating messages, usually consciously, with the specific aim of offending those to whom they are directed; (2) microinsults: offensive messages that convey, often unconsciously, subtle criticism, stereotyping, and disrespect; and (3) microinvalidations: communications, usually unintentional, that exclude, deny, or tend to nullify the thoughts, feelings, and experiences of those to whom they are directed (Sue & Spanierman, 2020). Microaggressions are by no means harmless (as the prefix micro- might suggest). On the contrary, their insidious, undetectable, often automatic nature contributes to minimizing the possibility that their victims can evade them or defend themselves. Like all ongoing and cumulative stressors, microaggressions can give rise to a wide range of effects on physical and psychological well-being (for a review, see Nadal, Whitman, Davis, Erazo, & Davidoff, 2016). Some lesbian, gay, and bisexual persons who seek clinical help may show internalized homophobia and the sequelae of homophobia-­related affects, cognitions, somatic states, and relational patterns. “Internalized homophobia” is the mental condition of believing same-­gender sexual orientation to be wrong, sick, or inferior, while simultaneously experiencing oneself as having that orientation. Understanding internalized homophobia improves psychotherapeutic and psychoanalytic treatment. Although we describe the phenomenon in general in what follows, a spectrum of experience exists between robust, joyous self-­acceptance and thorny, miserable self-­rejection. The process by which LGB people acknowledge their sexual orientation and come to feel comfortable being open about it is widely referred to as “coming out.” The phrase originated colloquially as “coming out of the closet”; the prior state of mind is referred to as “being in the closet” or “closetedness.” Given the invisibility of sexual orientation, LGB people are constantly faced with the choice of revealing or concealing their sexual orientation. This reality has a psychological cost; sometimes the decision to reveal or not must be made quickly and requires disconfirming standard heteronormative communication. Subjective Experience of Internalized Homophobia Affective States Internalized homophobia among LBG individuals generate feelings of guilt and shame. In severe cases, it involves self-­hatred and, in extreme cases, nearly constant self-­loathing. Denial of same-­gender attraction can engender feelings of emptiness or

chunks.heading: 886 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 886 Adulthood recurrent dissociative states. Splitting off one’s experience of same-­gender orientation from one’s homophobic beliefs may manifest itself in externalizing paranoid fears and aggression toward lesbian, gay, and bisexual people who are perceived as being seductive. At a higher level of...
chunks.text: 886 Adulthood recurrent dissociative states. Splitting off one’s experience of same-­gender orientation from one’s homophobic beliefs may manifest itself in externalizing paranoid fears and aggression toward lesbian, gay, and bisexual people who are perceived as being seductive. At a higher level of ego development, splitting off one’s sexual orientation from one’s homophobia may be expressed as moralization against homosexuality that lends itself to feelings of righteous indignation and pious anger. Repression of such intrapsychic conflicts results in depression and anxiety. Compartmentalization leads to separate types of feeling states such as a daily “normal” asexual self-­perception and an occasional “gay” hypersexual self-state, as clearly described in Freud’s concept of splitting of the ego. For example, a 30-year-old man feels ashamed of his attraction to a male colleague, tries to push these feelings out of his mind, and then acts them out homophobically. He writes on the walls of the office bathroom “Kill the faggot” and repeatedly asks colleagues, “Hey, are you a faggot?” Cognitive Patterns LBG individuals with internalized homophobia experience intrusive sexual fantasies and images about same-­gender partners and/or body parts. At lower levels of developmental organization, they report paranoid ideation such as “The world is dangerous,” and may personalize societal stigma in thoughts such as “I’m not acceptable.” At borderline levels, they may wish to be different, good, healthy, or noninferior with respect to their sexuality. In severe cases, they may have suicidal ideation. Particularly in novel social contexts, they scan the environment for signs of aggression and ask themselves, “Is it safe?” Even those at higher levels of development, with significant self-­acceptance, may experience passing confusion and momentary self-doubt when confronted with negative societal messages. For example, a 27-year-old woman worries that she is “broken” because of her love for a woman and wonders who can see her secret damage. At home, at work, and with friends, she pretends to be looking for a boyfriend and is careful to hide any behaviors considered “masculine.” Somatic States LBG individuals with internalized homophobia endure hypervigilance and hyperarousal. Compartmentalization and dissociated states can lead to risk taking by these patients. They may suffer poor long-term physical health outcomes, such as stress-­ related symptoms and the effects of substances used to manage internal conflicts. For example, a 25-year-old gay man has specific abuse memories related to homophobic bullying episodes in the bathrooms at school; these are encoded in a specific elimination disorder. Relational Patterns Minority stress can complicate the relationships of gay, lesbian, and bisexual people, and obstruct the resolution of difficulties. An understanding of its role in psychological and relational development in these populations can be helpful to professionals. In many places, marriage is still denied to same-­gender couples; lack of civil rights may involve not only practical problems but also feelings of delegitimization and inadequacy.

chunks.heading: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 887
chunks.preview: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 887 Splitting off one’s experience of same-­gender sexual orientation from one’s homophobic beliefs can result in sexual or aggressive acting out or in unconscious moralizing punitiveness. Discrepancies between partners in the outness–­c...
chunks.text: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 887 Splitting off one’s experience of same-­gender sexual orientation from one’s homophobic beliefs can result in sexual or aggressive acting out or in unconscious moralizing punitiveness. Discrepancies between partners in the outness–­closetedeness dimension may be a source of stress. For example, one partner may want to hold hands in public, while the other prefers to avoid behaviors that make “visible” their sexual orientation. Some bisexuals may be challenged with the difficulties inherent in “passing,” meaning pairing themselves with other gender partners and thereby obscuring their sexual orientation. We should note that bisexuality exists on a continuum of being more attracted to one gender relative to the other. Subjective Experience of the Therapist Clinicians’ prejudices—­or simple lack of knowledge about sexual minoritized groups—­ can seriously obstruct their understanding and bias their clinical work with lesbian, gay, and bisexual clients. We encourage those who feel uncertain in this area to consult a supervisor. Respectful listening, awareness of one’s own prejudices, and adequate knowledge are vital for creating a nonharmful and effective mental health practice. The following vignette exemplifies such issues. Clinical Illustration Antonio, a 32-year-old Italian bank clerk, consulted a male psychiatrist at the public mental health services for increasing difficulties in managing feelings of anxiety and irritability. When the psychiatrist asked for more details about the origin of his anxiety, Antonio reluctantly stated that it probably had to do with his “private life.” At that point, the psychiatrist awkwardly hazarded a guess: “Problems with your wife?” Antonio, becoming visibly more anxious and irritated, turned his eyes away. “I have a boyfriend,” he muttered. The psychiatrist realized how inappropriate his question had been and tried to restart: “Oh . . . I see. Well, that is not a problem. Go ahead.” This new beginning turned out to be even more awkward, as it inadvertently revealed the doctor’s discomfort with gay or lesbian clients. His difficulty appeared to stem more from a lack of knowledge and clinical experience than from overt prejudice or hostility; he had rarely considered homosexuality in relation to relational or family issues, although he implicitly associated it with sexual promiscuity, loneliness, and shame. In a flash, he became aware of how much he did not know about the lives of gay individuals. He felt professionally inadequate, yet intellectually and ethically challenged. Consequently, he sought supervision, which helped him to understand how unacknowledged biases could interfere with clinical listening. 15.7. Gender Incongruence DSM-5 (and DSM-5-TR) included the diagnosis of “gender dysphoria” in adolescents and adults, which replaced the DSM-IV diagnosis “gender identity disorder in adolescents and adults.” ICD-11 went further, removing the diagnosis of “gender incongruence” from mental disorders and moving it to a new section, “Conditions Related to Sexual Health.” The reasons for this choice include the need to maintain a diagnosis that is required for treatment in many countries, conveying at the same time a strong

chunks.heading: 888 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 888 Adulthood signal of depathologizing and destigmatization: “Incongruence,” unlike “dysphoria,” does not automatically imply distress; not all transgender or nonbinary people experience distress over gender incongruence (Drescher, Cohen-­Kettenis, & Reed, 2016). In this spirit, in PDM-3, as in the...
chunks.text: 888 Adulthood signal of depathologizing and destigmatization: “Incongruence,” unlike “dysphoria,” does not automatically imply distress; not all transgender or nonbinary people experience distress over gender incongruence (Drescher, Cohen-­Kettenis, & Reed, 2016). In this spirit, in PDM-3, as in the case of sexual orientations, we do not treat gender incongruence as a mental disorder. Western psychiatric and medical theorizing about transsexualism and transgender phenomena began in the 19th century. Until the middle of the 20th century, with rare exceptions, transgender presentations were classified as psychopathological. Early psychoanalytic theories conflated gender identity with sexual orientation, which is why Freud, for example, attributed some cases of homosexuality to identifications with the parent of the other gender. Modern sex researchers separate the desires of homosexuality (to have partners of the same gender) from those of transgender individuals (to live as another gender). Usually, one’s gender identity provides little or no information about one’s sexual orientation. The placement of gender diagnoses has shifted in various iterations of both ICD and DSM. The diagnosis “transsexualism” first appeared in ICD-9 (1975), where it was classified as a “sexual deviation.” ICD-11’s new gender incongruence category includes three diagnoses: gender incongruence of adolescence or adulthood (HA60), gender incongruence of childhood (HA61), and gender incongruence, unspecified (HA6Z). Neither DSM-I (1952) nor DSM-II (1968) had gender diagnoses. DSM-III (1980) added gender identity disorder of childhood and transsexualism. DSM-IV (1994) and DSM-IV-TR (2000) combined these two diagnoses into one overarching diagnosis, gender identity disorder, with transsexualism renamed as gender identity disorder in adolescents or adults. The diagnoses changed categories over time: psychosexual disorders (DSM-III); disorders usually first evident in infancy, childhood, or adolescence (DSM-III-R); sexual and gender identity disorders (DSM-IV, DSM-IV-TR); and, finally, gender dysphoria (DSM-5). Clinical presentations of gender incongruence that first appear in childhood (early onset) differ from those that arise in adolescence and adulthood (see SC Axis, Chapter 5, pp. 256–380), on the subjective experience of children). The majority of children with gender incongruence do not grow up to be transgender. Most of these “desisters” become cisgender as adults, with a gay, lesbian, or bisexual orientation. A few may become cisgender and heterosexual. When childhood gender incongruence continues into adolescence (“persisters”), individuals may undergo puberty suppression and later seek gender-­affirming hormones (e.g., testosterone, estrogen) and surgical treatment. Because gender incongruence that first appears in adolescence and adulthood (late onset) is highly unlikely to desist, gender affirmation interventions are often the treatment of choice. Prior to DSM-5, having a disorder of sexual development (intersex condition) was an exclusionary criterion for any diagnosis that was a forerunner of gender incongruence. DSM-5 recognizes that some intersex patients develop gender incongruence and adds a fifth-digit specifier for those who both are dysphoric and have a disorder of sexual development. The field of gender identities has expanded markedly. According to a study by Herman, Flores, and O’Neill (2022), more than 1.3 million individuals in the United States age 18 and older (0.5% of the general adult population) identify as transgender; younger subjects ages 13–17 years are significantly more likely to identify as transgender (1.4% of the general population). Internationally, many specialized gender services reported a substantial increase in individuals identifying as gender diverse and seeking

chunks.heading: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 889
chunks.preview: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 889 assessment, support, and treatment. A recurring finding is an increase in patients assigned female at birth (AFAB). Previously, more assigned males at birth (AMAB) had consistently been referred to specialized centers. In recent year...
chunks.text: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 889 assessment, support, and treatment. A recurring finding is an increase in patients assigned female at birth (AFAB). Previously, more assigned males at birth (AMAB) had consistently been referred to specialized centers. In recent years, this trend has reversed, especially for adolescents, with AMAB:AFAB ratios ranging between 1:2 and 1:6 (e.g., Aitken et al., 2015; de Graaf, Giovanardi, Zitz, & Carmichael, 2018). For children under 12, the ratio has flattened from 5:1 in earlier studies (e.g., Zucker, Bradley, & Sanikhani, 1997) to substantial 1:1 parity (Wood et al., 2013). Recent years have seen increasing heterogeneity in those who decline binary gender identities, reflected in the multiplication of terms describing subjective identifications. More and more people are adopting nonbinary gender identities, beyond the mutually exclusive categories of male and female. “Nonbinary” should be understood not only as a discrete category but also as an umbrella term encompassing different experiences of gender: from the perceived absence of gender, typical of agender, gender neutral and genderless people, to the stable mutability of genderfluid and bigender identities; from the feeling of pangender multiplicity, to the gender mixing of androgynous people (Richards et al., 2016). Nonbinary is a very recent phenomenon. Those who identify with it reject assignment to either male or female gender. They perceive and express themselves in nonbinary ways, often through clothing and behavior that do not reflect social norms associated with gender. They may adopt nongendered pronouns (e.g., “they,” “their,” and “them”), in reference to themselves. Contemporary research (which often relies on samples recruited in specialized medical services), seems to have so far neglected the specific characteristics and needs of the nonbinary population, which remain essentially unexplored (Cheung et al., 2020). The small body of research on nonbinary individuals has highlighted the variability of trajectories and developmental outcomes across the population. The acquisition of awareness about one’s identity often occurs only in the later stages of adolescence or early adulthood, when the achievement of greater autonomy allows a freer exploration of expressive, esthetic, and semantic possibilities. Compared to the transgender binary population, whose path is generally linear, nonbinary individuals tend to be more flexible in their development, with less demand for medical interventions and, where desired, an inclination to opt for partial interventions. Many people report childhood experiences of continuous “identification and disidentification” with one gender or another. So far, research suggests that this is mainly due to the absence of fixed social references (non-­binary identity representations available at the societal level), leading to more individualized developmental trajectories and greater variability of outcomes. A common finding is that the experience of one’s identity as nonbinary follows the discovery of the category, unknown in childhood, which then permits the recontextualization of one’s experience and the initiation of a meaning-­making process in relation to feelings of confusion and incongruence. Individual narratives often highlight the unavailability of words to describe their internal experience, resulting in difficulty communicating it to others (Losty & O’Connor, 2018; Barsigian et al., 2020). For nonbinary people, gender experiences seem to vary between fixed and fluid states, giving rise in some circumstances to feelings of dysphoria. The experience of fluidity as a stressor can be understood in light of the hegemonic expectation that identity, cis or trans, should be stable, integrated, and unambiguous, resulting in the induction of self-­understandings as chaotic, unnatural, or disturbing (Bradford et al., 2018). The late acquisition of nonbinary identity seems to imply a resignification of one’s deep-­seated perceptions under new lenses, acquired through new political and social awareness. In light of current male–­female stereotypes, and of the apparent prevalence

chunks.heading: 890 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 890 Adulthood in recent years of atypical gender identity for people assigned female at birth, it may be a common experience of AFAB nonbinary people to perceive a gender incongruence that initially directs them toward a male identity; then, following a problematic elaboration of the male gender rol...
chunks.text: 890 Adulthood in recent years of atypical gender identity for people assigned female at birth, it may be a common experience of AFAB nonbinary people to perceive a gender incongruence that initially directs them toward a male identity; then, following a problematic elaboration of the male gender role in society, they are led to embrace a nonbinary identity, placing themselves at varying distances between the two poles M and F. Compared with the general population, both binary and nonbinary adults with gender incongruence suffer more psychopathology, especially a higher prevalence of mood and anxiety disorders (for a review, see Zucker, Lawrence, & Kreukels, 2016). The origin of this suffering may be traced to two main sources: (1) gender dysphoria, linked to a strong dissatisfaction with one’s body, especially with primary and secondary sexual characteristics, on which hormonal therapies might intervene; and (2) minority stress; that is, the consequences of belonging to a group that is subject to mistreatment that must be countered on different levels: clinical, social, and cultural. Although many transgender and nonbinary individuals report no mental suffering, clinicians working with those who do suffer need to consider these two elements, periodically weighing their respective contributions. Patients facing these problems need mirroring and validation of their feelings and perceptions, attention to their relationship with their body (with its rejected parts and its transformations in the transition paths), and a focus on mentalization and the processing of perceived incongruence (Lemma, 2013; Saketopoulou, 2014). It is often useful to work with family members and partners, to increase social support and counteract the effects of minority stress. One helpful resource is the American Psychological Association’s (2015) “Guidelines for Psychological Practice with Transgender and Gender Nonconforming People.” Also of value is the Standards of Care document from the World Professional Association for Transgender Health (WPATH; formerly the Harry Benjamin International Gender Dysphoria Association), now in its 8th version (Coleman et al., 2022) and available in several languages. Subjective Experience of Gender Incongruence Gender incongruence is characterized by marked, persistent lack of alignment between an individual’s experienced (subjective sense of) gender and birth-­assigned gender. This incongruence often leads to a desire to transition to live and be accepted as a person of the subjectively experienced gender. Although not all gender-­incongruent individuals experience distress, many do, especially when hormonal and/or surgical treatments are unavailable. Most individuals prefer being referred to by names that suit their experienced gender and with appropriately gendered pronouns. Thanks to the internet, today’s young adults know about puberty suppression, hormone treatment, and gender affirmation surgery (often before their parents do). They may expect to be treated as persons of their experienced gender. Some adults may seek hormone treatment, others may choose surgery, and still others may seek both. Where treatment is not readily accessible or covered by insurance, individuals may pay privately or seek out hormones on the black market. Inability to access care may lead to suicidal feelings and, in extreme cases, genital self-­mutilation. Recent literature has highlighted the difference between body dysphoria, which transgender and nonbinary individuals may experience, and social dysphoria, giving increasing weight and relevance to the latter. “Body dysphoria” is distress related to the incongruence between one’s experienced gender and one’s somatic characteristics. This can affect the body as a whole or be confined to specific areas or experiences perceived as incongruent, with

chunks.heading: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 891
chunks.preview: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 891 varying degrees of intensity. Dysphoria can also include feelings of estrangement and disconnection from the body, particularly toward primary and secondary sexual characteristics and bodily regions most associated with male and fema...
chunks.text: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 891 varying degrees of intensity. Dysphoria can also include feelings of estrangement and disconnection from the body, particularly toward primary and secondary sexual characteristics and bodily regions most associated with male and female identities. “Social dysphoria” is discomfort that arises when there is a discrepancy between an individual’s experienced gender and how it is perceived within a social context. It may or may not involve a bodily dimension. Social dysphoria can be set off by external factors, social interactions, or linguistic triggers, which include behaviors or communications that invalidate the individual’s gender identity, such as the use of unwanted pronouns or names (misgendering), heavily gendered language, and the gendered organization of social and institutional spaces. Affective States Affective states include anxious and depressed moods, sometimes with despair and suicidal ideation. Anxiety or depression may be of sufficient severity to warrant its own diagnosis. Self-­medication of anxiety with drugs and alcohol is not uncommon. Inability to express one’s experienced gender may lead to rage directed at one’s body or at external factors or individuals seen as obstructing gender expression. In adolescence, panic may be induced by onset of menses or breast development in AFAB individuals or by facial hair emergence and penis growth in AMAB individuals. There may be a strong yearning for the primary and secondary sex characteristics of the other gender. Individuals report satisfaction and pleasure when permitted, either privately or publicly, to express their inner-­experienced gender. Cognitive Patterns Cognitive patterns include preoccupations with gender, bordering on the obsessional, in individuals who have not made a transition. These usually abate when transition services are available and provided. The experience of misgendering and lack of recognition can easily activate intrusive thoughts, rumination, and recurring concerns about how other people will rceive one’s gender, leading some individuals to avoid others or limit exchanges with them. Somatic States Somatic states in nontransitioned individuals involve extreme discomfort with the natal body. In adolescents, this may involve negative responses to anticipating or experiencing physical changes at puberty. Starting in adolescence, natal men may shave legs or bind genitals to make erections less visible. Natal women may bind breasts or disguise them with nonrevealing clothing. Efforts at body modification (e.g., cross-sex hormones, electrolysis) are common. Relational Patterns Relationships vary. Like cisgender people, adults with gender incongruence may identify with a broad range of sexual orientations: Some are androphilic (attracted to men), others gynephilic (attracted to women); still others are attracted to more than one gender, to all genders, or to none. Some live in ostensibly homosexual relationships before transition, others in heterosexual marriages, often with biological children.

chunks.heading: 892 Adulthood
chunks.preview: 892 Adulthood Some individuals may be socially isolated. It is not uncommon for a transitioning individual to lose many long-term relationships through lack of acceptance. Some couples, whether in heterosexual or same-­gender marriages, have remained together after one partner’s transition. Subjecti...
chunks.text: 892 Adulthood Some individuals may be socially isolated. It is not uncommon for a transitioning individual to lose many long-term relationships through lack of acceptance. Some couples, whether in heterosexual or same-­gender marriages, have remained together after one partner’s transition. Subjective Experience of the Therapist Cisgender clinicians may feel anxiety, concern, revulsion, or voyeuristic curiosity. Their feelings may range from rejection of a person’s experienced or expressed gender (sometimes labeled “transphobia”) to uncritical acceptance of the patient’s gender preference, without considering alternative explanations or diagnoses (e.g., a dissociative condition). Attachment to their own cisgender identity may make it difficult for a clinician to enter into the subjective world of those wishing to make a gender transition either socially or anatomically. Therapists who themselves are transgender or nonbinary need to take care not to assume that their trans or nonbinary patient’s experience is similar to theirs, as there is so much diversity in this area. Case 1 A 20-year-old patient, AMAB and brought by parents for evaluation of gender incongruence, insisted on being addressed by a female name and referred to with female pronouns. She had recently taken a leave of absence from college because of overwhelming anxiety and distress. She had had social difficulties since childhood and has been diagnosed with an autism spectrum disorder of low severity. She did not recall childhood memories of gender incongruence, although she acknowledged gender-­atypical interests, including playing “dress-up” with an older sister at age 4. Although she had seen herself as a woman, she recently began thinking of herself as “trans.” She began masturbating to orgasm at age 13 and continues to do so at present; an ongoing source of sexual excitement involves wearing articles of female clothing while masturbating. She occasionally cross-­dresses in private, but not in public. She once experimented sexually with a male school friend but has had no sexual experiences with women. She is made anxious by her sexual attraction to women (gynephilia), as she does not want to be a lesbian and believes she should be attracted to men. She occasionally desires to be rid of her penis and to have a vagina, although the thought of having surgery seems “disgusting.” Nevertheless, she wishes to be treated as a woman. She wishes to be rid of her beard, to have a woman’s smooth face, and to have no Adam’s apple. Psychological testing shows no evidence of a thought disorder but attests to an ongoing struggle with gender issues. After treatment of her anxiety and depression, the patient returned to school. Her gender incongruence persisted, but she was not as desperate to have an immediate transition (which her parents opposed). Instead, she has chosen a gender-­neutral name as a way to ease into transition. She has started to seek out other transgender individuals online and at school, and has gone out publicly with her nails done with colored polish. She plucks her beard and is thinking about getting electrolysis, but does not currently wish to take hormones or have surgery. Case 2 A 29-year-old nonbinary person, AFAB, sought transition services. They have a history of eating disorders (anorexia and bulimia). They spent most of their adolescence with

chunks.heading: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 893
chunks.preview: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 893 a cisgender identity, a homosexual orientation, and tomboy traits in gender expression (in clothing choices, hairstyles, and posture), while also going through phases of increased femininity. They have always felt dysphoria about cer...
chunks.text: Psychological Experiences That May Require Clinical Attention 893 a cisgender identity, a homosexual orientation, and tomboy traits in gender expression (in clothing choices, hairstyles, and posture), while also going through phases of increased femininity. They have always felt dysphoria about certain feminine features, particularly their breasts, which were reportedly insensitive and rarely included in sexual exchanges. Around age 17, amid many doubts, they began a social transition toward the male gender, changing name and pronouns, and intending to start hormone therapy upon reaching adulthood. Subsequently, however, they adopted a nonbinary identity, which has led to their feeling more centered and authentic. The eating problems have decreased, but the new identity has created relational challenges: They have had bad experiences with people who refuse to accept their nonbinary identity, and they find it hard to let anyone get close. The therapist, a cisgender woman, recounts a difficult beginning in which she realized she had enormous problems accepting the patient’s nonbinary identity. But over time, she came to see it as viable identity and even as a way of resolving various conflicts. The patient has developed a free, flexible relationship with their own identity, a greater ability to share their story with others, and increased tolerance in the face of misgendering experiences. chunks.heading: CHAPTER 16
chunks.preview: CHAPTER 16 When Does Later Life Begin? C H A P T E R E D IT O R S Franco Del Corno, MPhyl, DPsych Daniel Plotkin, MD, MPH, PhD Over time, the boundary of “later life” changes. As people live longer, with lengthier periods of relatively good health, the already uncertain cutoff age gets extended. Lat...
chunks.text: CHAPTER 16 When Does Later Life Begin? C H A P T E R E D IT O R S Franco Del Corno, MPhyl, DPsych Daniel Plotkin, MD, MPH, PhD Over time, the boundary of “later life” changes. As people live longer, with lengthier periods of relatively good health, the already uncertain cutoff age gets extended. Later life may have once been defined as 60 years of age, but it now may be 70, and compelling arguments can be made to extend it further. Yet there are still individuals who suffer from age-­related conditions in their 50s (e.g., early-onset Alzheimer’s disease). One remedy for this complexity, which we noted in PDM-2, has been to divide later life into four subcategories (i.e., early, middle, old, and old-old). This approach acknowledges an important feature of this life stage: the heterogeneity and diversity of older adults. Indeed, older adults differ from one another more than members of any other age group, making generalizations particularly challenging. We attempt here to describe aspects of older adults based on clinical observation, as well as empirical investigation. Largely because this life stage has been historically neglected in research, the empirical foundation for such descriptions may be less robust than for other age-­related sections. And at the same time, clinical information, though representing a century of experience, is still generally relegated to comparatively infrequent reports. One reason for this relative dearth of information is collective bias and discrimination against older adults—­what Robert Butler, the father of geriatrics in the United States, more than half a century ago termed “ageism.” We note that ageism continues to receive little attention today, even in the context of recent gains in understanding the pernicious effects of other kinds of prejudice. Ageism is manifested in different ways. Sometimes it is relatively subtle, such as the patronizing appreciation of “cuteness” in older people or the overly simplified

chunks.heading: 900 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 900 Older Adults explanations often offered to older adults, as if they were young children. Sometimes a simple hearing impairment is mistaken for a cognitive deficit, leading to a patronizing attitude. Older adults feel invisible much of the time, a feeling that younger people might have only in th...
chunks.text: 900 Older Adults explanations often offered to older adults, as if they were young children. Sometimes a simple hearing impairment is mistaken for a cognitive deficit, leading to a patronizing attitude. Older adults feel invisible much of the time, a feeling that younger people might have only in the grocery store or shopping mall. An entire anti-aging industry flourishes in the United States and increasingly in other countries. Even sympathetic authors encourage older adults to think of themselves as “elders” rather than “elderly” or “older adults.” There is also the “soft bigotry of low expectations,” as George W. Bush once observed about racism. Easily applied to older adults, this attitude creates self-­fulfilling prophecies. Becca Levy (2022) has demonstrated that negative age stereotypes, such as the belief that dementia (“senility”) and depression are normal and inevitable in the elderly, correlate with an increased risk of developing psychopathological conditions. Such assumptions also discourage older adults from pursuing treatments that may help them to ameliorate their suffering. The second edition of the Psychodynamic Diagnostic Manual was the first major international taxonomy that addressed issues specific to aging. As in PDM-2, the choice to devote an entire chapter to older adults in PDM-3 represents our conviction that older adults are worthy of inclusion and of attention, and that they still have minds and personalities worth understanding. We continue here to try to shed light on the psychological aspects of older adulthood and on mental health problems as they appear in older individuals. As we wrote in PDM-2: “The prevailing attitude in most societies is that older adults are rigid and incapable of change . . . Another prevalent negative assumption about the elderly is either that they do not have a psychodynamic dimension, or that if they do, it is not relevant.” In 2017, when that manual was published, and even more so today, clinical experience suggests otherwise. Although this chapter cannot offer the final word on the matter, it can serve as a basis for inspiring future endeavors. With the aging of modern societies and further advances in health care delivery (Wang & Nussbaum, 2017), we can expect a gradual shift toward more capability and functionality among older adults, making our efforts to understand aspects of later life even more relevant and important. Since the release of PDM-2, the world has faced the COVID pandemic, whose effects on older adults we discuss in what follows. In this context, we will draw on the concept of existential maturity of Linda Emanuel et al. (2020). To address directly how individuals experience illness in the context of aging minds and brains, as well as in the context of daily life demands, social networks, cultural expectations, and general societal considerations, we have built the following chapters on the basic PDM formulation of multiple axes, choosing to retain separate yet interconnecting axes on mental functioning, personality, and subjective experiences of symptoms, with specific adaptations for older adults. We should note that we considered certain dimensions for additional axes or subaxes in this section (e.g., cognitive status and level of engagement), but ultimately, we decided to include these aspects in relevant sections of existing axes, rather than to add further complexity to an already complex system. We try here to include empirical research that informs more operationalized descriptions of various conditions and disorders. Unfortunately, there is a paucity of empirical studies of older adults in general and, specifically, of psychodynamic aspects of aging. This is due at least in part to the fact that older adults have often been excluded from clinical research studies. We have consequently made suggestions for further empirical investigations, whenever possible.

chunks.heading: When Does Later Life Begin? 901
chunks.preview: When Does Later Life Begin? 901 Some basic observations and concerns underlie the contents of the following chapters: 1. Heterogeneity. A literature review on research in aging must begin with recognition that one of the most robust findings is heterogeneity: There is tremendous variability associa...
chunks.text: When Does Later Life Begin? 901 Some basic observations and concerns underlie the contents of the following chapters: 1. Heterogeneity. A literature review on research in aging must begin with recognition that one of the most robust findings is heterogeneity: There is tremendous variability associated with aging. Some older adults function well as heads of state, while others cannot recognize their own family members or even get out of bed. 2. Classification of older adults. While there is no biological or psychological parameter that defines “old,” it has been generally accepted that old age begins at 65 years. For clinical purposes, an accepted cutoff has been even younger (i.e., 55 years of age). A recent epidemiological study of mental disorders in older adults in the United States (Reynolds, Pietrzak, El-­Gabalawy, Mackenzie, & Sareen, 2015) divided the elderly into four groups: “young-old” (55–64 years of age), “middle-­old” (65–74 years of age), “old-old” (75–84 years of age), and “oldest-­old” (85 years of age and older). 3. Aging as positive. Contrary to popular belief, aging is commonly associated with positive emotional and mental health benefits, including increased life satisfaction and enhanced capacity to find meaning in life. 4. Chronicity and comorbidity. Aging is associated with chronicity and with comorbidity; that is, medical and emotional conditions tend to be chronic and to coexist and interact in complex ways. 5. Change is possible. Neuroscientists have documented neuroplasticity in the brain, indicating that change in the brain (and presumably the mind) is possible even in old age. 6. Cultural views and attitudes. As we noted earlier, cultural views and attitudes on aging are important considerations in any effort to design a classification of mental health conditions, especially one that focuses on a psychodynamic dimension. In general, the older population is becoming increasingly diverse. Ethnic and racial factors have been shown to play roles in physiological/medical dimensions such as blood pressure and sleep, and in attitudes and expectations about aging, dependency, and living arrangements. Ethnic groups differ in decision-­making processes, disclosure of personal information, and end-of-life care. From an international perspective, there is a wide divergence in people’s confidence that they will have an adequate standard of living in their old age (Wickrama, Mancini, Kwag, & Kwon, 2013). Confidence in one’s eventual standard of living appears to be related to the rate at which a country is aging and to its economic vitality.
