chunks.heading: CHAPTER 17
chunks.preview: CHAPTER 17 Profile of Mental Functioning in Older Adults MOA Axis C H A P T E R E D IT O R S Franco Del Corno, MPhyl, DPsych Daniel Plotkin, MD, MPH, PhD CONSULTANTS Emanuela Brusadelli, PhD Salvatore Gullo, PhD Giuseppe Cafforio, Dpsych Alessia Zoppi, PhD Introduction There is a general tendency in...
chunks.text: CHAPTER 17 Profile of Mental Functioning in Older Adults MOA Axis C H A P T E R E D IT O R S Franco Del Corno, MPhyl, DPsych Daniel Plotkin, MD, MPH, PhD CONSULTANTS Emanuela Brusadelli, PhD Salvatore Gullo, PhD Giuseppe Cafforio, Dpsych Alessia Zoppi, PhD Introduction There is a general tendency in the literature to denote a psychological and physical process of development in later life as “typical (normal) aging.” This process involves a gradual decline in psychophysical and social capabilities, following a cumulative pattern of age-­specific involution (Allen, Morris, Stark, Fortin, & Stark, 2015; Moran, 2013; Palmore, 1974). Many have viewed “old age” as a spectrum encompassing markedly different experiences. Accordingly, the literature distinguishes between “younger old” individuals who are in an “active aging” phase that coincides with the initial years postretirement, and “older old” individuals, whose frailty and dependency significantly affect their quality of life (Gilleard & Higgs, 2010; Grenier, 2012). In normal aging, processes of enrichment, integration, and completion of identity and autobiographical narratives can compensate for such decline. These processes can enable an elderly person to assume the role of the “wise elder” (the positive aspect of the Jungian archetypical “Senex,” as well as a classic literary figure). Thus, when discussing the natural development of “senile personality” in older adults, we should

chunks.heading: 904 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 904 Older Adults consider not only the decrease in psychophysical resources and related changes in behavior, emotions, and attitudes, but also the emergence of a new way of life, characterized by changed habits, roles (e.g., grandparent, expert, wise person), and relationships. This emergence is bes...
chunks.text: 904 Older Adults consider not only the decrease in psychophysical resources and related changes in behavior, emotions, and attitudes, but also the emergence of a new way of life, characterized by changed habits, roles (e.g., grandparent, expert, wise person), and relationships. This emergence is best encapsulated by the concept of “successful aging” (Rowe & Kahn, 1987, 1997), achieved through experiences that protect older adults from the melancholy, anxiety, and social isolation commonly associated with old age. Older individuals may approach the subject of death in various ways, depending on whether they have achieved a healthy self-­integration or are feeling a terrifying sense of dispersion and loss of hope. They often show an integrative capacity, an ability to apply old skills in new contexts and reorganize old relationships in more beneficial ways, based on current needs (e.g., seeking help from their children). In general, it is normal for elderly people to modify their behaviors, with gradual changes in how they experience and express emotions, social interests, and cognitive abilities. Although older adults may lose some roles and opportunities for interaction and influence in broader social contexts, they may concurrently acquire new roles and identities in more intimate social settings, particularly within their families. An older relative may become the “memory” of a family or community group, serving as a bearer of wisdom and knowledge—­conveying a sense of continuity, coherence, integration, and awareness of having been the protagonist in a life story in which a true self was formed amid a multiplicity of possible selves. Elderly people can share this narrative with others, with the understanding that they have completed their life journey and no longer look to the future with longing or expectations of change. Older people may thus develop a meditative and spiritual outlook that allows them to attain a deeper understanding of themselves and the world around them. All these aspects have been proposed as potential explanations for what has been called the “aging paradox”: the fact that subjective well-being remains stable or even increases in later life, a phenomenon noted by both clinical writers (e.g., ­McWilliams, 2017) and empirical researchers (Wettstein, Schilling, & Wahl, 2016; Hansen & Blekesaune, 2022). Research on this paradox indicates that older adults frequently employ accommodative strategies, such as rescaling goals and adjusting aspirations, which help maintain positive self-­evaluations and overall well-being even in the face of social losses and declining health. Assessing mental functioning in individuals over 70 requires attention to the interplay of biological, psychological, and social factors. Although aging does not inherently imply illness, the likelihood of developing health issues is greater after 70 than during other life stages. It is particularly important to recognize that “patients experiencing major medical illnesses may have a reduced capacity for symbolic thought due to the physical demands of their illness and the stress associated with mortality” (McClintock Greenberg, 2009, p. 37). This aspect is further reinforced by the necessity for ongoing medical treatment. Physical illness in older adults is approached differently than in previous life stages, in which illness is often seen as an unpredictable event that individuals attempt to manage and from which they can often seek complete recovery. For older adults, every illness may be perceived as part of an inevitable decline; the most common attitude tends to be resignation, similar to attitudes surrounding death, which may be envisioned as relatively imminent. Another consideration for clinicians assessing an older individual’s mental functioning is the balance between mental abilities and the demands of the person’s environment. An elderly person who lives in a nursing home needs different skills than

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning in Older Adults 905
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning in Older Adults 905 someone the same age who continues to live independently. Quality and quantity of environmental stimuli can lead to variations in brain aging, resulting in changes in functions and mental abilities (Churchill et al., 2002; Fratiglioni, Paillard-­Bor...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning in Older Adults 905 someone the same age who continues to live independently. Quality and quantity of environmental stimuli can lead to variations in brain aging, resulting in changes in functions and mental abilities (Churchill et al., 2002; Fratiglioni, Paillard-­Borg, & Winblad, 2004). A stimulating environment can contribute to maintaining—­and sometimes even improving—­mental functioning, while an understimulating environment may accelerate decline. Assessment of older adults should consider the possibility of neurocognitive disorders, manifested by cognitive impairments in one or more domains, as outlined in the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (5th ed., text revised; DSM5-TR). Individuals with neurocognitive disorders may struggle to comprehend events that affect them and may also have difficulty responding appropriately to stimuli. Accurate diagnosis of neurocognitive disorders is critical in old age, as these global, pervasive conditions (at varying levels of impairment) can profoundly impact emotions and behaviors. The aging of the brain and the state of mental functions in older adults are influenced by both genetic factors (species-­specific and individual) (Caleb, Finch, & Tanzi, 1997; Rodríguez-­Rodero, Fernández-­Morera, Menéndez-Torre, Calvanese, & Fraga, 2011) and by past and present environmental conditions (Ryff & Singer, 2005). Clinicians should keep in mind, however, that “the brain is not the mind. The physical organ of the brain is not the same as one’s mind . . . a person’s mind may remain sharp, even if the speed at which his or her brain functions declines” (Ilardo, 1998, p. 10). The construct of “cognitive aging,” introduced in a recent report by the Institute of Medicine (2015) of the U.S. National Academies of Sciences, is useful here. Cognitive aging is inherent in humans and other animals, occurring across the spectrum of elderly individuals, regardless of their initial levels of cognitive functioning. Mental processes in older adults can improve in some areas (e.g., those related to wisdom and experience) while declining in others (e.g., memory, attention, and processing speed). In essence, cognitive aging represents a lifelong process of gradual, ongoing, yet highly variable changes in cognitive functions over time; it is not a disease or a quantifiable level of functioning, as its implications for internal mental processes in older adults are not yet fully understood. Assessment of mental functioning in older adults thus requires a differential diagnosis of (1) optimal mental abilities that have been preserved despite aging, (2) mental abilities impaired by aging, and (3) impaired or low-level mental abilities that are aspects of an individual’s developmental history, which aging has not notably influenced. Aging itself can be a stressor (Cesari, Vellas, & Gambassi, 2013), to which an individual may respond either optimally—­preserving capacities to the fullest extent possible—­or with further deterioration of mental functioning. Some authors (e.g., Cohen-Sachs, 2006; Desjardins & Warnke, 2012) argue that the ability to cope with stress is enhanced by a significant investment in the pursuit of self-­realization, which is cultivated throughout life and contributes to assigning meaning and purpose to the physical and emotional challenges encountered in the later life. Our discussion of the capacity for meaning and purpose, later in this chapter, is relevant here. In summary, as with personality functioning (see Chapter 18, pp. 926–956), normal age-­related decline can be a highly stressful experience for some individuals and not particularly stressful for others. In the following, we describe the basic mental functions of the M Axis as they pertain to older adults. We encourage clinicians to use these categories to capture the strengths and assets of older patients while considering any difficulties and impairments they may have. Although no profile can encompass

chunks.heading: 906 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 906 Older Adults the full breadth of an elderly person’s mental life, the 13 M-Axis capacities highlight several key areas. As with the other M-Axis chapters, we recommend for assessment the Summary of Basic Mental Functioning (see Table 17.1) for the MOA Axis, in which clinicians indicate on a 5-po...
chunks.text: 906 Older Adults the full breadth of an elderly person’s mental life, the 13 M-Axis capacities highlight several key areas. As with the other M-Axis chapters, we recommend for assessment the Summary of Basic Mental Functioning (see Table 17.1) for the MOA Axis, in which clinicians indicate on a 5-point scale the level at which each mental function is best depicted. As anchor points, we provide descriptors of key characteristics at levels 5, 3, and 1 for each function. For a broader definition of each capacity, see Chapter 13, M Axis (pp. 709–759). Neuropsychological Tests for General Use with Older Adults The following neuropsychological tests are helpful in assessing general cognition and other areas of neuropsychological functioning in older adults (Tsoi, Chan, Hirai, Wong, & Kwok, 2015). Definitions and Assessment of the 13 MOA-Axis Capacities 1. Capacity for Attention and Learning (Executive Functioning) When rating this capacity in individuals ages 70 and older, clinicians should consider potential changes in the neurobiological substrate, such as neurocognitive disorders (see previous discussion). Problems in memory are often the leading symptoms of these disorders, particularly impairments in immediate and recent memory, which may affect daily functioning in diverse ways. In older adults, difficulties in learning new tasks appear normative, often the result of a progressive “tightening” of overall mental functioning. Whereas younger individuals can readily receive and process new stimuli, elderly adults may often process only those that are similar to one another, making it challenging to handle novel inputs. Aging negatively affects mental flexibility, leading to more concrete thinking, as well as inhibiting the ability to suppress automatic responses in favor of novel ones (Harada, Natelson Love, & Triebel, 2013). Yet many people can maintain learning abilities even in advanced age. Emerging evidence indicates a positive effect on selective attention and memory in healthy elderly individuals; in fact, older adults seem to focus more on positive information (relevant to the self) while ignoring negative stimuli (Sasse, Gamer, Buchel, & Brassen, 2014). This research may lead to strategies (teaching methods and training techniques) that can enhance attention (Chaffin & Harlow, 2005; Saczinski, Willis, & Schaie, 2002; Wrenn & Maurer, 2004) and help preserve cognitive functioning. Rating Scale 5. At this level, the individual’s ability to express thoughts, feelings, and other inner experiences is unaffected by the aging process. Memory, attention, executive functions, and learning capacities are well-­maintained and integrated, even under stress, despite the normal age-­related decline in overall psychological and physical functioning.

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning in Older Adults 907
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning in Older Adults 907 3. The individual may express thoughts, feelings, and other inner experiences only for relatively short periods and when specifically motivated. In other conditions and/or under stress, age-­related impairments may become more evident, leading to di...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning in Older Adults 907 3. The individual may express thoughts, feelings, and other inner experiences only for relatively short periods and when specifically motivated. In other conditions and/or under stress, age-­related impairments may become more evident, leading to difficulties in language, information processing, and other cognitive abilities. 1. The aging process has severely compromised the individual’s ability to remain focused, organized, and attentive. This interference affects the capacity to learn and to maintain active and appropriate behaviors in response to external stimuli. Mini‐Mental State Examination The Mini-­Mental State Examination (MMSE; Folstein, Folstein, & McHugh, 1975) is a brief screening for potential mental impairment, particularly in older adults. The test comprises items that assess orientation to time and place, attention/concentration, language, constructional ability, and immediate and delayed recall. The MMSE can be administered in 5–10 minutes. Alzheimer’s Disease Assessment Scale The Alzheimer’s Disease Assessment Scale (ADAS; Rosen, Mohs, & Davis, 1984) was developed to assess the severity of cognitive and noncognitive behavioral dysfunctions typical of persons with Alzheimer’s disease. The scale comprises 11 cognitive items (ADAS-Cog) and 10 noncognitive items (ADAS-Non-Cog), which can be used separately. ADAS-Cog includes performance-­based short neuropsychological tests; ADASNon-Cog includes observation-­based behavioral rating items. Cambridge Mental Disorders of the Elderly Examination The standard form of the Cambridge Mental Disorders of the Elderly Examination (CAMDEX; Roth et al., 1986) includes 340 items; the short form (Revised, or CAMDEX-­R ; Roth, Huppert, Mountjoy, & Tym, 1998) includes 67 items. The time required for administration is approximately 60–90 minutes for the standard form and 20–40 minutes for the short form. Frontal Assessment Battery The Frontal Assessment Battery (FAB; Dubois, Slachevsky, Litvan, & Pillon, 2000) is a cognitive and behavioral six-­subtest battery for a bedside mental status examination (particularly useful in cases of frontal and subcortical dementia). It takes about 10 minutes to administer. Dementia Rating Scale The Dementia Rating Scale (DRS; Mattis, 1988) provides an index of cognitive functioning in individuals with known or suspected dementia. A global measure of dementia is derived from subscores for specific cognitive capacities: attention; initiation and perseveration; construction; conceptualization; and verbal and nonverbal short-term memory. The time required is about 10–15 minutes for normal elderly persons or 30–45 minutes for patients with dementia.

chunks.heading: 908 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 908 Older Adults MicroCog: Assessment of Cognitive Functioning The MicroCog (Powell, Kaplan, Whitla, Catlin, & Funkenstein, 1993) is a computer-­ administered and -scored test to screen for mental impairment in individuals ages 18 to 89. The standard form comprises 18 subtests; the short form contai...
chunks.text: 908 Older Adults MicroCog: Assessment of Cognitive Functioning The MicroCog (Powell, Kaplan, Whitla, Catlin, & Funkenstein, 1993) is a computer-­ administered and -scored test to screen for mental impairment in individuals ages 18 to 89. The standard form comprises 18 subtests; the short form contains 12 of these subtests, organized into five domains (attention/mental control, memory, reasoning/ calculation, spatial processing, and reaction times). The MicroCog is essentially self-­ administered. The standard form can be administered in 60 minutes; the short form requires about 30 minutes. Montreal Cognitive Assessment The Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA; Nasreddine et al., 2005) is a rapid screening instrument for mild cognitive dysfunction. It assesses attention/concentration, executive functions, memory, language, visual–­constructional skills, conceptual thinking, calculations, and orientation. Administration takes aproximately 10 minutes. 2. Capacity for Affective Regulation and Expression The nature and direction of emotional changes affecting older individuals depend on “the level of emotional adjustment each person reached during his/her younger years” (Ilardo, 1998, p. 13). This level is influenced by culture, social norms, and past experiences. Type and valence of previous experiences can have a powerful impact; the ability to understand and express affective states and emotions may diminish or become constricted if earlier experiences have been predominantly negative. It appears, however, that affect regulation follows a different trajectory than the decline observed in other capacities. For example, older adults are more rather than less likely to report positive feelings (Hansen & Blekesaune, 2022). Less rigid adherence to social norms, a tendency that characterizes some older individuals, combined with a greater sense of freedom, may enhance their willingness to evaluate and share both positive and negative feelings with others (Gross et al., 1997). And some older persons show higher levels of gratitude, compassion, forgiveness, and tolerance—­character strengths that promote healthy relational and emotional outcomes (Beadle & De la Vega, 2019; Chopik, Newton, Ryan, Kashdan, & Jarden, 2019). Many variations of these patterns can be reflected in an individual’s life narrative. Rating Scale 5. At this level, the individual’s ability to understand, express, and regulate a wide range of emotions remains unaffected by the aging process; the person’s resources in this area are consistent with the level of this capacity achieved in earlier life phases. 3. The individual’s range of emotional states is limited, with some difficulty in modulating and regulating specific affects. The normal age-­related decline is experienced as a stress, constricting and diminishing the expression of emotions and making them less appropriate to the situation and social expectations. 1. Emotional expression is chaotic; there is patent incongruence between environmental stimuli and affective responses. It may be difficult to distinguish between

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning in Older Adults 909
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning in Older Adults 909 the impact of a more or less severe cognitive decline and a previously low level of resources in this mental area. 3. Capacity for Mentalization Age-­related reduction in social contacts and diminished opportunities for new experiences may influence...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning in Older Adults 909 the impact of a more or less severe cognitive decline and a previously low level of resources in this mental area. 3. Capacity for Mentalization Age-­related reduction in social contacts and diminished opportunities for new experiences may influence the capacity to reflect on one’s internal life and to infer the mental states of others. Older adults sometimes respond to difficulties or unpleasant events through somatic impairments, which may be linked to age-­related physical vulnerability, thereby serving as an alternative to reflective functioning. As McClintock Greenberg (2009, p. 56) notes, “the imposing threat to the integrity and cohesion of the self, as well as the distressing fear of death, [can] result in reduced access to symbolic thought.” Some individuals who have achieved good mentalization and reflective functioning in earlier periods of life may experience enhancements in their ability to understand both their own reactions to various experiences and the mental states of others, an improvement that can be attributed to the knowledge and experience they have acquired (Happé, Winner, & Brownell, 1998; Melehin, 2015). Some evidence suggests that old age may be associated with differences in the ability to comprehend one’s own and others’ mental states, with potentially greater self-­reflective abilities (Peters & Schulz, 2022). An understanding of age-­ related changes in mentalizing capacity is vital for assessing reflective functioning impairments that result from neurocognitive decline or mental disorders (see Chapters 18 and 19 on the POA and SOA Axes, respectively; see pp. 926–956 and pp. 957–1008). Rating Scale 5. At this level, individuals are capable of understanding their own mental states and constructing representations of the internal experiences that underlie the behaviors of others. This capacity remains intact even in challenging or distressing situations. The aging process does not significantly impair the individual’s psychological insight into self or ability to identify the mental states of others. The person uses mentalization and reflective functioning to modulate impulses and differentiate between inner and outer realities. 3. In the face of conflict or strong emotion, individuals at this level show notable decline in their mentalization and reflective functioning skills. Symptoms of somatization and/or increased impulsivity may be evident. The decreased availability of physical and psychological resources associated with aging may further impair their ability to reflect on their behaviors and feelings and to attend to those of others. 1. The individual’s age-­related decline in cognitive flexibility and/or need to cope with physical challenges may lead to an inability to pay attention to inner affective states or to distinguish between internal and external experiences. Reality testing may become compromised, and confusion may make it difficult for individuals to articulate their emotions. Both of these challenges diminish the capacity to reflect and consequently to act appropriately.

chunks.heading: 910 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 910 Older Adults 4. Bodily Experiences and Representations Implicit awareness of one’s body as a complex entity with defined boundaries is not initially acquired in old age. But older adults inevitably encounter physical limitations that lead to changes over time in perceptions, sensations, feelings...
chunks.text: 910 Older Adults 4. Bodily Experiences and Representations Implicit awareness of one’s body as a complex entity with defined boundaries is not initially acquired in old age. But older adults inevitably encounter physical limitations that lead to changes over time in perceptions, sensations, feelings, and thoughts, both conscious and unconscious. Illnesses and treatments for them may heighten older adults’ attention to their bodies. Thus, the aging process influences both implicit and explicit bodily experiences and representations. Appearance-­ related anxieties are prevalent among older adults, particularly among women, given the pressures in many cultures to look youthful (Schoemann & Branscombe, 2011). Physical changes can lead to a distorted body image, triggering feelings of worthlessness, incapacity, and inadequacy (Barnett, Moore, & Edzards, 2020). Among older adults, lower body image satisfaction is associated with age-­ related stereotypes and stigma consciousness, which in turn can lead to isolation and social disengagement (Sabik, 2017). Rating Scale 5. Despite the physical changes associated with aging, individuals at this level maintain both prereflective and active awareness of their bodies as complex entities, enhancing their sense of self-­integrity. They can reflect on both implicit and explicit bodily experiences (old and new) and show flexibility and the ability to integrate these relatively new bodily representations into their overall self-­experience. They can differentiate inner experiences (e.g., affects and emotions) from internal bodily states, even under stress or in conflictual circumstances, resulting in adaptive responses within their social environments. 3. In stressful or conflictual situations, individuals at this level show a diminished ability to differentiate bodily states from psychic inner experiences affecting their integration of self-­experience. This may stem from a previously decreased capacity or from the impact of age-­related physical changes on an already vulnerable ability to process bodily experiences and representations. This condition may increase somatization or somatic complaints. 1. Older adults at this level experience severe difficulties in perceiving self–body unity and in distinguishing bodily experiences from mental states. The unpleasant somatic features associated with aging adversely affect self-­perception, leading to alterations in body experience or detachment. They may struggle to differentiate between stimuli inside and outside their bodies, as well as to express unprocessed inner experiences. 5. Capacity for Differentiation and Integration A sense of identity and of an integrated self are not acquired in old age; individuals function well in this area only if these qualities were developed during earlier life stages. A background of diverse positive experiences can imbue an older person with stronger self-­confidence, founded on established values, beliefs, and habits. Nevertheless, the aging process can weaken the sense of identity because of reduced physical resources and the typical age-­related decline in cognitive capacities, which may increase reliance on others.

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning in Older Adults 911
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning in Older Adults 911 Rating Scale 5. The flexibility and spontaneity characteristic of a healthy sense of identity are preserved from earlier life into old age. Individuals’ understanding of their motives, wishes, and affects enables them to cope effectively with the ch...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning in Older Adults 911 Rating Scale 5. The flexibility and spontaneity characteristic of a healthy sense of identity are preserved from earlier life into old age. Individuals’ understanding of their motives, wishes, and affects enables them to cope effectively with the challenges and opportunities associated with aging. 3. In the face of stressful experiences, normal age-­related impairments can disturb the sense of identity. The individual may be confused about wishes and needs and may struggle to cope with varying conditions or to maintain an integrated, stable sense of self. 1. Diminished cognitive, psychological, and physical resources significantly affect the individual’s capacity for differentiation and integration. The older person may be unable to perceive the continuity between earlier life stages and the present, resulting in a fragmented or rigidly compartmentalized sense of identity. This can lead to maladaptive defenses, including splitting and projective identification, and a decline in reality testing. 6. Capacity to Regulate Self‐Esteem Aging is often accompanied by a sense of mourning, linked to an older person’s need to reshape self-image and find new ways to establish relationships based on capacities and personal characteristics different from those of the past, while simultaneously preserving a sense of continuity. This process can be framed as a progressive identity crisis with which one learns to cope (Goswami, 2013; Ploton, 2001). Self-­esteem in older adults is associated with a favorable perception of aging and a strong sense of self-­efficacy, motivating them to be more active and to address age-­related challenges effectively (Marquet, Chasteen, Plaks, & Balasubramaniam, 2019; Velaithan et al., 2023). While this process may sometimes come at some expense to self-­esteem, it can also enhance it, depending partly on previous sources of confidence. Yet age-­related physical impairments, reductions in sensory capacities, and increasing dependence on children, relatives, and professional caregivers can interfere with aspects of this capacity. Rating Scale 5. At this level, the individual maintains a stable sense of confidence, vitality, and realistic self-­esteem, even when faced with age-­related declines or impairments. Decreases in sensory capacities and motor skills do not reduce self-­reliance, and the person can sustain a positive self-view, with good effects on their relationships with others and their environment. 3. The individual’s confidence and self-­esteem are generally adequate but may be frequently disrupted by stressful situations and age-­related impairments. Feelings of inadequacy stemming from diminished confidence in sensory and motor resources may be evident. Willingness to engage in relationships may be hampered by perceived decline in these abilities. 1. The individual’s perception of vulnerability caused by declines in sensory and motor skills leads to feelings of emptiness, incompleteness, and emotional withdrawal. Resilience in response to age-­related challenges is limited or even absent. Self-­ confidence may be excessively low or, defensively inflated, or may alternate between

chunks.heading: 912 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 912 Older Adults these extremes. The individual may react to age-­related changes by transforming objective weaknesses into an illusory sense of strength, which can lead to inappropriate behaviors (e.g., underestimating danger). 7. Capacity for Trust, Empathy, and Intimacy In evaluating this capacit...
chunks.text: 912 Older Adults these extremes. The individual may react to age-­related changes by transforming objective weaknesses into an illusory sense of strength, which can lead to inappropriate behaviors (e.g., underestimating danger). 7. Capacity for Trust, Empathy, and Intimacy In evaluating this capacity in older adults, clinicians need to consider the number and types of social contacts available to them, and their ongoing need to share feelings with people who are significant in their lives. Sensory impairments common in later life (e.g., hearing and vision deficits) can restrict social interactions and may sometimes increase isolation, but the capacity for relatedness may be preserved or even enhanced. Issues of dependence require attention in older adults, as their needs and circumstances change. Zarit (1980, p. 47) notes that “older people’s relationships with family members who must care for them can suffer, as both the caregiver and the patient may feel torn between anger and guilt over their new roles.” As Kring, Davison, Neale, and Johnson (2007, p. 523) noted, when changes in health or cognitive capacity become too daunting, many older individuals move to retirement communities, assisted living facilities, or nursing homes. Needs for help from relatives, friends, and professionals (e.g., health care providers and institutional staff) may alter the nature of an elderly person’s relationships with significant others. There is growing evidence that in later life, the capacity for empathy can diminish or, more specifically, that older adults can lose some cognitive empathy while preserving or even increasing emotional empathy. This shift may have important consequences, as reduced empathy has been associated with loneliness, depression, and relationship dissatisfaction, especially for older adults involved in helping roles, such as physicians or family caregivers. Given that many older adults take on the role of caregiver for a spouse or parent with dementia or other chronic illness, this lower empathy can exacerbate caregiver burnout and compassion fatigue, already significant and pervasive issues, and contribute to poor patient care. Issues related to sexuality in old age also require consideration. Reduced sexual desire and less sharing of sexual fantasies and longings, a feature of normal aging, constitute a loss that may be particularly hard for individuals whose sexuality was previously central to their identity. Depressive reactions may arise from decreased sexual interest or sexual dysfunction; conversely, there may be a defensive increase in sexually disinhibited behaviors (not associated with a manic episode or a neurocognitive disorder; see Chapter 19 on the SOA Axis, pp. 957–1008). A balance in earlier life between sexuality, other interests, and nonsexual relationships provides a useful framework for addressing the changes that aging brings here. This issue has long been neglected. There is now substantial evidence of the importance of sexuality in late life: Sexual activity contributes to physical and mental health, feelings of well-being, reduction of stress, and relief from anxiety. Rating Scale 5. Despite potential relationship difficulties related to aging (e.g., sensory impairments or mobility challenges), the individual at this level retains a desire for contact with others and can maintain deep and meaningful relationships, including possible romantic involvement.

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning in Older Adults 913
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning in Older Adults 913 3. The individual retains the capacity for meaningful interactions with friends, relatives, and possibly romantic partners. The person may withdraw and become isolated, however, in the presence of physical or emotional difficulties or, conversely, m...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning in Older Adults 913 3. The individual retains the capacity for meaningful interactions with friends, relatives, and possibly romantic partners. The person may withdraw and become isolated, however, in the presence of physical or emotional difficulties or, conversely, may make excessive and unrealistic demands for care and relationships. 1. The individual cannot maintain meaningful relationships and shows little interest in sharing personal experiences with others. Indifference and withdrawal may stem from previous life experiences or be linked to physical impairments and/or psychological challenges associated with aging. The capacity to provide support to others or to accept help is largely absent. 8. Capacity for Impulse Regulation The aging process may adversely affect the capacity to regulate impulses; individual characteristics from earlier life stages may predict specific types of dysregulation. Patterns of overcontrol or undercontrol may increase over time, making deficiencies here more evident. This capacity can be severely impaired in the presence of a neurocognitive disorder. Rating Scale 5. Individuals at this level can control and regulate impulses effectively. They express urges and needs in a modulated, adaptive manner which age-­related changes have not impaired. 3. Impulse control and regulation may diminish in the presence of conflicts and tensions. Reduced availability of physical, cognitive, and emotional resources may hinder the person’s ability to manage impulses such as anger or may lead to defensive withdrawal, accompanied by denial of needs and feelings. 1. The individual’s experiences and feelings related to aging (e.g., intolerance of dependence, decreased self-­esteem linked to diminished sensory and motor skills and/or lack of cognitive resources) significantly interfere with the ability to modulate needs, motives, and feelings. In those who previously showed a limited capacity to control and regulate impulses, this pattern may persist into old age or become more pronounced and severe. 9. Defensive Functioning The aging process, with its accompanying decline in physical resources and cognitive abilities, may affect defensive capacities. Flexibility of reactions to events and relationships may decrease. Older adults may rely more on familiar or more costly defensive patterns, even when these are inappropriate to their situation. Objective life events such as retirement, bereavement, illness and death of relatives and friends, personal illness, and needs for assistance from professionals such as doctors, nurses, and social workers, along with general age-­related physical and emotional vulnerability, can instigate more primitive defenses; splitting may take precedence over more adaptive reactions. But individuals in favorable circumstances with positive past experiences may show higher-­level defensive functioning and an increased capacity to reflect on events and on their own feelings.

chunks.heading: 914 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 914 Older Adults Rating Scale 5. Increasing age-­related impairments have not weakened the individual’s defensive structure, which is characterized by neurotic-­level adaptive defenses (e.g., repression, isolation of affect, intellectualization, sublimation). The person can feel a wide range of affe...
chunks.text: 914 Older Adults Rating Scale 5. Increasing age-­related impairments have not weakened the individual’s defensive structure, which is characterized by neurotic-­level adaptive defenses (e.g., repression, isolation of affect, intellectualization, sublimation). The person can feel a wide range of affects, engage in satisfying interactions, and express creativity. The individual is aware of the challenges associated with aging and responds accordingly, without resorting to more costly defenses. 3. The experience of age-­related physical and psychological limitations prompts the use of lower-level defenses (e.g., denial, extreme idealization or devaluation of self and others, splitting) particularly when the individual feels threatened or anxious. Reality testing may then become less effective, leading the person to overlook significant phenomena. 1. The experience of physical difficulties (especially severe illnesses or impairment) may leave the individual overwhelmed by anxiety. Defenses based on splitting may evolve into distortions of reality (delusional projection). The use of more mature and adaptive defenses becomes increasingly difficult and may become impossible under heightened stress. 10. Capacity for Adaptation and Resilience The different stages of aging involve events and circumstances that may challenge an individual’s capacity to cope effectively with experiences such as loss, change in living conditions, awareness of limited time, and general decline in physical and/or cognitive resources. In facing these experiences, older adults must rely on whatever capacity they have for adaptation. “Resilience” in aging is commonly defined as the ability to thrive in the face of adversity or to respond positively (beyond what is expected) to high-­intensity stressors. A recent review has noted that “resilient response” is an umbrella term encompassing diverse ways that older adults may react to stress. These include maintaining equilibrium in response to low-­intensity and frequent stressors, or functioning at a relatively good (but lower) level after exposure to a stressor (Cosco, Kok, Wister, & Howse, 2019; Angevaare et al., 2020). Specific strengths, such as the ability to manage a range of adverse conditions, should be considered in the diagnostic evaluation (Cyrulnik & Ploton, 2014). Clinicians need to assess whether impairments in this capacity are due to a neurocognitive disorder. A special case arises when an older adult must enter a nursing home or extended care facility. As Ilardo notes (1998, p. 198), “We are territorial creatures, and our environment is our territory.” When older adults lose their familiar reference points, they naturally feel anxious and angry. In such instances, it remains essential to assess the individual’s capacity for resilience and adaptation, as well as to plan appropriate supportive interventions. Rating Scale 5. Even in the presence of considerable stress, complex social-­emotional contexts, and novel events, individuals at this level can manage their emotional states, maintain a good level of interpersonal functioning, and function well cognitively. Their reality

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning in Older Adults 915
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning in Older Adults 915 testing is preserved, allowing for accurate understanding of themselves and others. They are sensitive to environmental cues, can be assertive, and can promote positive interactions. 3. Moderate levels of impairment are present in thought processes...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning in Older Adults 915 testing is preserved, allowing for accurate understanding of themselves and others. They are sensitive to environmental cues, can be assertive, and can promote positive interactions. 3. Moderate levels of impairment are present in thought processes and interpersonal functioning. When confronted with particularly complex and/or stressful situations, normal age-­related physical and emotional impairments become more evident, influencing the individual’s feelings and behavior. Nevertheless, the person remains able to maintain a degree of adaptation. 1. The need to cope with new situations, difficult emotional states, and unfamiliar relationships leads the individual to rely on primitive defenses and to suffer uncontrolled affects. Decline in cognitive, emotional, and relational capacities becomes pronounced, with weakness and vulnerability rapidly increasing. Capacity for adaptation and resilience is greatly impaired, necessitating continual support for appropriate engagement with the environment. 11. Capacity to Construct and Use Internal Standards and Ideals The aging process sometimes leads individuals to develop more rigid moral standards. Such rigidity may be a defense against rapid changes, such as widespread modifications in social norms, sexual habits, and intergenerational relationships, or extremely rapid technological advancements that older adults may find foreign and daunting. Or moral rigidity may be a reaction to declining physical and cognitive resources, a diminishing network of friends and relatives, and losses or changes in social roles, such as those resulting from retirement. Conversely, some older adults may feel entitled to express a degree of independence from social and cultural customs given their diminished expectations for social rewards. Rating Scale 5. Individuals at this level are aware of their behavioral standards and moral beliefs, expressing them flexibly while considering social contexts and the capacities of others. They strive to understand the reasons behind others’ behaviors, even when these reasons are not explicit. This approach allows them to base moral judgments not on an ethic of guilt and punishment but on a balance of compassion and empathy toward both self and others. 3. Individuals may struggle to adapt their internal standards and ideals to current norms and values across different social contexts. They show moderate rigidity and inflexibility, and some deficit in understanding of the capacities, values, and ideals of others. Their self-­esteem is often driven more by feelings of guilt and/or shame than by a sense of responsibility or a desire to improve their behavior. 1. Individuals at this level cannot cope with behaviors that deviate significantly from their moral standards, even when such behaviors are acceptable within current social contexts. They frequently express criticism and condemnation. Their comparisons between the putatively more ethical standards of earlier times and current moral habits are unyielding. They make little room for empathy or constructive criticism, making genuine dialogue with others virtually impossible.

chunks.heading: 916 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 916 Older Adults 12. Capacity to Explore One’s Inner Life? As we have detailed throughout this chapter, in the aging process, diverse uncomfortable experiences often occur. All these require a considerable degree of psychological mindedness, which, like other capacities, an individual may have devel...
chunks.text: 916 Older Adults 12. Capacity to Explore One’s Inner Life? As we have detailed throughout this chapter, in the aging process, diverse uncomfortable experiences often occur. All these require a considerable degree of psychological mindedness, which, like other capacities, an individual may have developed to varying degrees earlier in life, and which may be influenced by the aging process. Self-­ observational capacities can increase during old age, thanks to an elderly person’s prior experiences and positive reactions to similar events. Individuals may develop a deeper awareness of their capacities for functional behaviors and appropriate emotional responses in challenging situations. Rating Scale 5. At this level, individuals have high motivation and capacity to observe and understand their feelings, thoughts, and behaviors, and how they vary in different situations. Even under pressure, their capacity to explore their inner life is not significantly impaired by the aging process. Their ability to reflect on previous experiences plays a critical role in helping them evaluate and organize affective responses and behavioral reactions. 3. The aging process and its typical features partially impair insight into one’s own and others’ feelings and behaviors. Under pressure, self-­observational and understanding capacities may become unavailable but can potentially be regained when the stress decreases. 1. Self-­awareness is lacking; individuals at this level appear unable to reflect on their feelings, thoughts, and behaviors. Their affective states may polarize, and they are unable to observe mindfully the physical and emotional aspects of aging, which may be either over- or underestimated, sometimes resulting in significant impairments in reality testing. 13. Capacity for Agency and Purpose The term “engagement” applies to this capacity. In older adults, the capacity for agency and purpose reflects a sense of belonging and participation in a family, society, and community. It encompasses the ability to accept change and also to work with ongoing processes of change—­adapting old projects or creating new ones—­according to an individual’s evolving personal status and roles. This capacity provides meaning to the current stage of life, independent of evaluations based solely on efficiency and productivity. It also aids in coping with issues such as death, mourning, age-­related declines in psychological and physical resources, and various life transitions (e.g., increasing needs for assistance or entry into a care facility). Agentic capacity is considered a distinguishing feature of movement from younger-­ old to older-old ages. When the sense of agency begins to wane and the person needs to depend more on others’ help, withdrawal rather than struggle may become the primary strategy for preserving an acceptable self-­perception. This ties into the theme of “staying me,” that is, continuity with earlier life. Capacity for agency can be an antidote to loneliness, defined as “a discrepancy between one’s desired and achieved levels of social relations” (Perlman & Peplau, 1981, p. 32). Loneliness in old age has

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning in Older Adults 917
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning in Older Adults 917 adverse consequences for well-being, physical and mental health, and even mortality (Leigh-Hunt et al., 2017; Rico-Uribe et al., 2018; Dahlberg, McKee, Frank, & Naseer, 2022). Yet as an edited book by Dimitrijević and Buchholz (2022) attests, loneli...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning in Older Adults 917 adverse consequences for well-being, physical and mental health, and even mortality (Leigh-Hunt et al., 2017; Rico-Uribe et al., 2018; Dahlberg, McKee, Frank, & Naseer, 2022). Yet as an edited book by Dimitrijević and Buchholz (2022) attests, loneliness can also provide a motivation to seek and create meaning, deepen relationships, foster personal growth, and explore new opportunities. Older adults functioning at a high level of agency and purpose can face loneliness from this perspective and may demonstrate the following: • Ability and willingness to identify and pursue new interests and activities (e.g., reading, travel, volunteering, hobbies). • Interest in developing new relationships (while, if possible, maintaining previous ones) and ability to give up maladaptive behaviors and conflictual relationships. • Ability to accept and adapt to the realignment of roles in various reference groups. • Capacity to share acquired knowledge and wisdom with others, especially younger people. • Ability to appreciate scientific and technological advances and willingness to learn how to use new devices. • Capacity to contemplate the future of loved ones after one’s death. Rating Scale 5. At this level, the individual acknowledges finiteness and engages in reasonable behaviors aimed at preserving health, maintaining physical functions, and prolonging quality of life. The person’s motto might be “I prefer not to live at any cost” or “I accept not being young forever.” Individuals with these qualities may find value in the role of “wise elder” within the family and community. When reflecting on previous life stages, they can remember and evaluate mistakes and achievements, successes and failures, without descending into guilt, self-­attack, or denigration of the present. Those who cultivate spirituality or adhere to a specific doctrine express and apply their beliefs adaptively rather than with all-or-­nothing behaviors and attitudes. 3. The individual experiences some difficulty maintaining a sense of agency and purpose. Awareness of limited time and decreased efficiency have led to anxious and depressive feelings. The person has clear discomfort with physical and aesthetic manifestations of aging, sometimes prompting a compulsive search for remedies. Spirituality or adherence to specific dogma serves a compensatory and utilitarian purpose. Negative memories from previous life stages become more frequent, evoking sadness and regret, while positive past events provide scant comfort. 1. Individuals at this level have lost their sense of agency and purpose. They may have reacted with feelings of alienation, meaninglessness, and isolation to their experiences of decreased energy, increased susceptibility to illness, unwanted bodily changes, restrictions in social roles, and other negative age-­related events. Or they may resort to primitive defenses such as manic denial rather than adapt to the challenges of aging. Spirituality and the desire to transcend the present moment may be absent or may take rigid and excessive forms that verge on delusional beliefs or compulsive behaviors.

chunks.heading: 918 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 918 Older Adults Summary of Basic Mental Functioning Following the same approach detailed in the other M Axes, we recommend that clinicians summarize basic mental functioning quantitatively (see Table 17.1). Summary of Basic Mental Functioning: MOA Axis To obtain a rating of a patient’s overall ment...
chunks.text: 918 Older Adults Summary of Basic Mental Functioning Following the same approach detailed in the other M Axes, we recommend that clinicians summarize basic mental functioning quantitatively (see Table 17.1). Summary of Basic Mental Functioning: MOA Axis To obtain a rating of a patient’s overall mental functioning, a clinician should add up the 1–5-point ratings assigned to each capacity (Table 17.1a), yielding a single numerical index of overall functioning ranging from 13 to 65. This total permits provisionally assigning a patient to one of the categories outlined in Table 17.1b, which provides brief qualitative descriptions of seven levels of mental functioning. Schematically, healthy mental functioning corresponds to M1; neurotic to M2 and M3; borderline to M4, M5, and M6 (from high to low, from moderate impairments to significant defects); and psychotic to M7. TABLE 17.1A. MOA-Axis Functioning: Total Score MC-Axis Capacities Rating scale 1. Capacity for attention and learning (executive functioning) 5 4 3 2 1 2. Capacity for affect regulation and expression 5 4 3 2 1 3. Capacity for mentalization 5 4 3 2 1 4. Bodily experiences and representations 5 4 3 2 1 5. Capacity for differentiation and integration 5 4 3 2 1 6. Capacity to regulate self-esteem 5 4 3 2 1 7. Capacity for trust, empathy, and intimacy 5 4 3 2 1 8. Capacity for impulse regulation 5 4 3 2 1 9. Defensive functioning 5 4 3 2 1 10. Capacity for adaptation and resilience 5 4 3 2 1 11. Capacity to construct and use internal standards and ideals 5 4 3 2 1 12. Capacity to explore one’s inner life 5 4 3 2 1 13. Capacity for agency and purpose 5 4 3 2 1 Total score =

chunks.heading: Profile of Mental Functioning in Older Adults 919
chunks.preview: Profile of Mental Functioning in Older Adults 919 TABLE 17.1B. Levels of Mental Functioning Level; range Heading Description Healthy M1; 58–65 Healthy/optimal mental Optimal or very good functioning in all or most mental capacities, with functioning modest, expectable variations in flexibility and...
chunks.text: Profile of Mental Functioning in Older Adults 919 TABLE 17.1B. Levels of Mental Functioning Level; range Heading Description Healthy M1; 58–65 Healthy/optimal mental Optimal or very good functioning in all or most mental capacities, with functioning modest, expectable variations in flexibility and adaptation across life contexts. Neurotic M2; 51–57 Good/adequate mental Adequate level of mental functioning, with some specific areas of difficulty functioning with some (e.g., in three or four capacities). These difficulties can reflect conflicts or areas of difficulty challenges related to specific situations or events. M3; 43–50 Mild impairments in Mild constrictions and inflexibility in some domains, implying rigidities and mental functioning impairments in areas such as self-esteem regulation, impulse and affect regulation, defensive functioning, and self-observing capacity. Borderline M4; 36–42 Moderate impairments Moderate constrictions and inflexibility in most domains, affecting in mental functioning quality and stability of relationships, sense of identity, and range of affects tolerated. Functioning begins to reflect the significantly impaired adaptations that are described as “borderline-level” in many psychodynamic writings, and that are found, in increasing severity, in the next two levels. M5; 22–35 Major impairments in Major constrictions and alterations in most domains (e.g., tendencies mental functioning toward fragmentation and difficulties in self–object differentiation), along with limited experience of feelings and/or thoughts in major life areas (i.e., love, work, play). M6; 21–27 Significant defects in Significant defects in most domains of mental functioning, along with basic mental functions problems in the organization and/or integration and differentiation of self and others. Psychotic M7; 13–20 Major/severe defects in Severe defects in almost all domains, with impaired reality testing; basic mental functions fragmentation and/or difficulties in self–object differentiation; disturbed perception, integration, and regulation of affect and thought; and severe defects in one or more basic functions (e.g., perception, integration, motor, memory, regulation, judgment).

chunks.heading: CHAPTER 18
chunks.preview: CHAPTER 18 Personality Styles and Disorders in Older Adults POA Axis C H A P T E R E D IT O R S Franco Del Corno, MPhyl, DPsych Daniel Plotkin, MD, MPH, PhD CONSULTANTS Emanuela Brusadelli, PhD Salvatore Gullo, PhD Alessia Zoppi, PhD Introduction Like the adult P Axis (see Chapter 12, pp. 651–708),...
chunks.text: CHAPTER 18 Personality Styles and Disorders in Older Adults POA Axis C H A P T E R E D IT O R S Franco Del Corno, MPhyl, DPsych Daniel Plotkin, MD, MPH, PhD CONSULTANTS Emanuela Brusadelli, PhD Salvatore Gullo, PhD Alessia Zoppi, PhD Introduction Like the adult P Axis (see Chapter 12, pp. 651–708), the POA Axis represents a “map” of personality styles and disorders in older adulthood that we hope will facilitate clinical understanding and treatment planning. Because older adults have traditionally been excluded from clinical and empirical studies of personality, we are trying to map relatively uncharted territory (Brudey, 2021). Relevant questions are still unanswered: Do personality styles and disorders remain stable or change over the life course? How do personality styles interact with the physical, cognitive, biological, and other changes common in old age? Do maladaptive traits and personality disorders emerge in older age, or are they exacerbated manifestations of former personality traits? Are certain personalities more likely to be associated with “successful aging” and/or longevity? Luckily, as Oldham and Skodol (2013, p. 709) note, “There is increasing interest in, and an accumulating body of knowledge about, the longitudinal course of personality traits and personality disorders . . . and the interaction of these uniquely defining features of who we are with cognitive functioning, mood states, and patterns of behavior in later life.” Prevalence of personality disorders among older adults varies by setting, with estimates of 2.8–13% in community samples, 5–33% in outpatients, and 7–61.5% in

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 927
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 927 inpatients (van Alphen, Engelen, Kuin, & Derksen, 2006; van Alphen, Derksen, Sadavoy, & Rosowsky, 2012; Newton-­Howes, Clark, & Chanen, 2015). Findings from the U.S. National Epidemiologic Survey on Alcohol and Related Conditions indicate that approximately 14.5...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 927 inpatients (van Alphen, Engelen, Kuin, & Derksen, 2006; van Alphen, Derksen, Sadavoy, & Rosowsky, 2012; Newton-­Howes, Clark, & Chanen, 2015). Findings from the U.S. National Epidemiologic Survey on Alcohol and Related Conditions indicate that approximately 14.5% of American adults age 65 and older have at least one personality disorder. The most common is obsessive–­compulsive disorder (7.6%), followed by narcissistic disorder (3.9%) (Reynolds, Pietrzak, El-­Gabalawy, Mackenzie, & Sareen, 2015). Other studies have found that 12% of elderly patients met criteria for avoidant personality disorder, as opposed to only 5% who reached the threshold for the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM) diagnosis of borderline personality disorder (Mordekar & Spence, 2008). Interestingly, personality disorders in older adults seem to be significantly more common in men (16.8%) than in women (12.7%), although paranoid, avoidant, and dependent personality disorders have been found to be more prevalent in women (Volkert, Gablonski, & Rabung, 2018). The POA Axis differs from classifications in the DSM and International Classification of Diseases (ICD) in its focus on subjective individual experience, level of personality organization, and type of personality style or disorder. Some concepts related to personality organization may be particularly relevant to older adults. For example, virtues such as wisdom, resilience, and integrity, as per Erikson’s (1950) concept of integrity versus despair, should be considered in this age group. We view a dimensional perspective on personality styles and disorders as more useful than categorical taxonomies, but few studies have yet explored the reliability and validity in later life of dimensional classifications of personality (e.g., the DSM-5-TR Alternative Model of Personality Disorder, AMPD). One recent study provided some support for using the AMPD with older adults, though the authors noted that this system does not fully resolve the age-­related issues associated with the categorical approach; use of the AMPD did not show any incremental validity beyond pathological personality traits (Stone & Segal, 2021), possibly because even though it is dimensional, it is a trait-based system. Other studies of older populations are based on the Five-­Factor Model (Costa & McRae, 1992). Some personality traits, such as neuroticism and extraversion, have been associated with depression, anxiety, and cognitive impairment in older adults (Abrams & Bromberg, 2007; Dar-­Nimrod et al., 2012; Debast et al., 2014; Graham & Lachman, 2012; Lautenschlager & Förstl, 2007; Seivewright, Tyrer, & Johnson, 2002; van den Broeck, 2012). Low neuroticism and high extraversion and consciousness have been associated with longevity (Andersen et al., 2013; Hill, Turiano, Hurd, Mroczek, & Roberts, 2011), but these findings require further investigation. With respect to whether personality disorders remain relatively stable or are subject to change in older people, the concept of “heterotypic continuity” may apply: Diagnosed mental disorders predict future episodes of other mental disorders (Gutiérrez et al., 2012; Karaklic & Bungener, 2010; Rosowsky, Abrams, & Zweig, 1999). In other words, a person may have a stable core pathology, but its behavioral expression may change over time. In elderly persons, some traits, often in response to changes such as retirement or loss of a partner, may become exaggerated or rigid, while others may diminish. This finding suggests a latent continuity expressed through behavioral discontinuity, following a logic of “functional equivalence” (Debast et al., 2014). Most experts now agree that personality disorders may appear in old age without having been identified earlier in life. Clinicians have observed as well that life events may selectively contribute to the expression of late-onset personality pathologies (Rosowsky, Lodish, Ellison, & van Alphen, 2019).

chunks.heading: 928 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 928 Older Adults The presence and intensity of certain traits can evolve over the life course. In old age, traits such as extraversion and conscientiousness tend to decline, while harm avoidance increases. DSM-5-TR Cluster A personality disorders, especially paranoid and schizoid disorders, may incr...
chunks.text: 928 Older Adults The presence and intensity of certain traits can evolve over the life course. In old age, traits such as extraversion and conscientiousness tend to decline, while harm avoidance increases. DSM-5-TR Cluster A personality disorders, especially paranoid and schizoid disorders, may increase or become exacerbated in older adults (Bangash, 2020; Gutiérrez et al., 2012; Rosowsky et al., 1999). Cluster B disorders, such as borderline personality disorder, generally decrease with age (Abrams & Horowitz, 1999; Coolidge, Segal, Hook, & Stewart, 2000; Oltmanns & Balsis, 2010, 2011; Seivewright et al., 2002; Singleton, Meltzer, Gatward, Coid, & Deasy, 1998; Stevenson, Meares, & Comerford, 2003; van Alphen et al., 2006). DSM-5-TR Cluster C personality disorders, in contrast, tend to worsen in old age. With loss of predictability and control, and less flexibility in adapting to physical changes and impairments, obsessive–­compulsive and anxious traits can become more pronounced. It remains a matter of debate whether diagnostic criteria and instruments designed for younger adults are appropriate for older adults. In fact, there is growing evidence that they may be somewhat problematic (Stone & Segal, 2021) and should be scrutinized more closely and adapted better to use with older adults. Differentiating “normative” aspects of aging from potentially pathological ones is a key challenge in the mental health field. An older man who reduces his social life and contacts may appear to have schizoid traits but may not have the subjective dynamics of schizoid personality. He may prefer to stay alone because of diminished energy, physical limitations, or specific life events (retirement, widowhood). In such cases, it would be incorrect to diagnose a schizoid personality disorder. The DSM effort to diagnose personality based on traits rather than underlying themes may be especially problematic when assessing personality in older people (see McWilliams, 2012). When an older person loses someone of emotional importance, we need to consider the role that this individual may have played in masking or compensating for personality dysfunctions. The partner or relative or caregiver may have buffered the person’s relationships, or reduced the expression of maladaptive traits while reinforcing adaptive ones, or inhibited maladaptive behaviors (Bangash, 2020; Gleason, Weinstein, Balsis, & Oltmanns, 2014). Personality develops not in a vacuum but within a social context. Cultural issues are critically relevant to personality and aging. Western cultures tend to reward youth, beauty, independence, and productivity. In Eastern cultures, there is a greater sense of duty, harmony, and respect for elders, and dependency is less stigmatized. Eastern societies, which regard cognitive decline as less pathological than Western societies do, have greater tolerance for cognitively impaired elders (Helman, 2005). Personal growth is more valued by older adults in Eastern than in Western cultures, but a sense of purpose tends to be lower in older adults in both (Karasawa et al., 2011). Across cultures, but more in Western than Eastern societies, older people are pressed to maximize positivity (Grossmann, Karasawa, Kan, & Kitayama, 2014). Urbanization and modernity have deprived older adults of the higher status they traditionally had in smaller, rural societies. We now describe each personality style and/or disorder, in parallel with the Adult P Axis (see Chapter 12, pp. 651–708), while also addressing the following issues: • Age-­related behavioral features that may complicate the diagnosis of a personality style or disorder. • Specific expressions of a personality style or disorder in older adults.

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 929
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 929 • Relationship between a particular personality syndrome or disorder and the aging process. • Suggestions for treatment. The different levels of personality organization, as described in detail in Chapter 12, can also be applied to older adults and are useful fo...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 929 • Relationship between a particular personality syndrome or disorder and the aging process. • Suggestions for treatment. The different levels of personality organization, as described in detail in Chapter 12, can also be applied to older adults and are useful for clinical assessment. The aging process may increase the severity of certain symptoms and behaviors typical of various pathological personalities or, in some cases, reduce the extent of the subjective pain felt or the objective symptoms observed. This means that even older adults may shift from more impaired to more adaptive levels of personality organization (or vice versa), influenced by life events specific to old age, as well as by age-­related physical, psychological, and cognitive changes. In most cases, however, these changes are connected to the level of personality organization that was typical of the patient in earlier adulthood. Aging brings numerous changes to an individual’s life—often more impactful than those occurring during the middle age—and these changes may worsen the level of personality organization. Conversely, age brings opportunities for disengagement (from social relations, professional commitments, or intimate relationships) that, for certain individuals, encourage better adaptation. This complex interplay between external factors and personality organization highlights the value of reflecting on the clinical features of different personality styles and disorders in old age. Personality Styles and Disorders in Older Adults Primarily Internalizing Personalities Depressive (and Hypomanic) Personalities Potential Diagnostic Biases Related to Age Depressive personalities should not be confused with major depressive disorder or depression associated with dementia (see also Chapter 19, SOA Axis, pp. 957–1008). Diagnosing this personality style or disorder in older adults is complicated by the prevalence of depressive disorders and the frequency of stressful events that can lead to overlapping depressive symptoms. These include personal losses, declining functional and health status, retirement, moving, and social isolation. Some older adults may have a genetic predisposition to depression (Alexopoulos, 2005). Depressive issues may be overlooked in older adults because of assumptions that depression is “normal” for this age group. Accordingly, it is crucial to identify any previous episodes of subclinical mood disorders in the person’s history. Distinguishing between chronic depressive illness and depressive personality can be challenging. Older adults may reduce their social networks through “relational pruning,” a process that is not necessarily indicative of depressive symptoms or disorders. Individuals with a history of depressive personality features, however, are at increased risk of depressive reactions to various age-­specific events. In such cases, a spouse or other significant attachment object may have played a compensatory role by buffering pathological traits, which may emerge in that person’s absence (e.g., when the spouse dies or when children leave home). Not infrequently,

chunks.heading: 930 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 930 Older Adults retirement triggers a depressive response, as work often provides a protective context. The counterpart to depressive personality traits is hypomanic behavior (see below). Depressive (and Hypomanic) Personalities in Older Adults The central issue for depressive personalities, both o...
chunks.text: 930 Older Adults retirement triggers a depressive response, as work often provides a protective context. The counterpart to depressive personality traits is hypomanic behavior (see below). Depressive (and Hypomanic) Personalities in Older Adults The central issue for depressive personalities, both older and younger, is the sense of the self as defective. This perception is accompanied by fear of judgments from others, whose rejection is strongly feared. A self-­fulfilling prophecy may lead to social isolation and feelings of rejection, though some individuals maintain an active social life as compensation for underlying fears. In older adults, negative self-­attributions (often connected to a self-­defeating attitude) emerge in relation to regrets, guilt, or missed opportunities from the past. Such individuals often express recriminations (e.g., “I have not been able to . . . ”). They feel that their current loneliness results from past malfeasance. Compensatory behaviors may include increased religiosity or efforts to help others not to feel worthless. If these compensatory efforts fail, depressive individuals may experience a deep and catastrophic loneliness, which can develop into major depression. Age-­related physical, emotional, and cognitive impairments, though often minor, may be difficult to accept and may trigger feelings of shame and sadness. Psychosomatic symptoms or deep anguish may surface, as well as obsessive thoughts of abandonment, death, or inevitable loneliness (Costa, Weiss, Duberstein, Friedman, & Siegler, 2014; Wongpakaran, Wongpakaran, Boonyanaruthee, Pinyopornpanish, & Intaprasert, 2015). Anger and resentment toward others may also arise. Although these feelings seem directed at the environment, they fundamentally stem from inflexible beliefs about personal errors, limits, and past mistakes. Depressive personality features are strongly linked to increased suicidal ideation and lowered resilience in older adults (Segal, Gottschling, Marty, Meyer, & Coolidge, 2015). Even in old age, it is clinically relevant to distinguish between introjective and anaclitic depressive experience (Blatt, 2004, 2008). Their respective characteristics resemble those found in depressive personalities in adulthood (see Chapter 12 on the P Axis, pp. 651–708), and they have different effects on the aging process. Introjectively depressive individuals, who are typically characterized by guilt, self-­criticism, and perfectionism, struggle with age-­related decline in physical, psychological, and cognitive resources. Their feelings of inadequacy and emptiness may be heightened as they age, reinforcing their depressive tendencies. Anaclitically depressive individuals, who are instead inclined to feelings of inadequacy, emptiness, and longings for attachment, show high sensitivity to life events such as loss, separation, and grief, which are particularly difficult for them to process and accept. Their depressive feelings are characterized primarily by despair and complaints of hopelessness and loneliness. It is useful to assess whether a patient’s predominant psychological orientation—­independent of personality style—is anaclitic or introjective, because this difference in subjective experience has implications for treatment. Comorbid mental conditions, such as generalized anxiety and depression, may arise and significantly affect cognitive functioning in older people. They can lead to marked slowing and loss of residual abilities (Alexopoulos, 2005; Fiske, Wetherell, & Gatz, 2009; Vink, Aartsen, & Schoevers, 2008). The individual may perceive adaptation to the environment and relationships, despite physical or cognitive impairments, as false and shameful. Fear of exposing one’s fragility and facing rejection may be persistent. Others are often perceived as intolerant and judgmental.

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 931
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 931 If there is a history of suicidality, the combined condition of aging and depressive personality may heighten the risk of recurrence, particularly if social support is inadequate or there is a co-­occurring depressive disorder (Alexopoulos, Bruce, Hull, Sirey, &...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 931 If there is a history of suicidality, the combined condition of aging and depressive personality may heighten the risk of recurrence, particularly if social support is inadequate or there is a co-­occurring depressive disorder (Alexopoulos, Bruce, Hull, Sirey, & Kakuma, 1999; Chan, Chiu, Lam, Wong, & Conwell, 2014; Harwood, Hawton, Hope, & Jacoby, 2001). The empirical literature reports concomitant alterations in the immune, endocrinological, and cardiac systems (Alexopoulos, 2005). Anxiety and depression may also accelerate the development of markedly disabling neurocognitive disorders. Sometimes, an attitude of self-­ absolution triggers feelings of euphoria, unrelated to toxicological causes or other medical conditions, leading to an acceleration of activities, intensification of emotions, and increase in the speed and superficiality of thoughts and plans (hypomania at a subclinical level). Exaggerated behaviors may emerge, placing the individual in vulnerable situations and dangerous relational patterns (Segal, Coolidge, & Rosowsky, 2006). These behaviors should not be confused with disorders linked to neurocognitive impairments. They may represent a relational style rather than an actual mood disorder; the alternation between a depressive/self-­ defeating approach and hypomanic behavior differs from the cycling seen in bipolar disorder (see Chapter 19 on the SOA Axis, pp. 957–1008). When hypomanic reactions do occur, they are typically short-lived and do not alleviate the underlying suffering. Suggestions for Treatment Individuals with depressive (and hypomanic) personalities are often inadequately diagnosed and treated because of the subclinical nature of their symptoms (their depressive and hypomanic qualities may be less evident and disabling than those in major depressive or bipolar disorder). The treatment of introjectively depressive patients must account for the overwhelming presence of negative feelings, which can also affect the clinician. These patients tend either to isolate themselves or to associate with others who share their pessimism. If the clinician attempts to counteract their negativity, the patient may react with irritation or withdrawal. Every therapeutic intervention with these individuals must balance the effort to inspire hope for change with an acceptance of the patients’ difficulty in altering negative self-­percepts. As noted earlier, anaclitically depressive patients often react painfully to separation or abandonment, whether real or imagined. Their idealization of others and devaluation of themselves are heightened by the age-­related decline in physical and cognitive abilities. Clinicians must develop a therapeutic relationship marked by attention and acceptance, with the aim of fostering recovery of personal resources and reduction of dependency needs. Finally, the main challenge in treating hypomanic patients is addressing their reliance on denial and reaction formation. Their pervasive fear of experiencing depressive feelings should be both respected and addressed to help them develop more adaptive coping strategies, more mature defenses, and better emotional capacities. Masochistic (Self‐Defeating) Personalities Potential Diagnostic Biases Related to Age Masochistic personalities are characterized by a persistent unconscious attachment to suffering, which may continue into old age. They seem less numerous among older adults, however. Self-­sabotaging behaviors are more commonly expressed in settings

chunks.heading: 932 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 932 Older Adults like the workplace or within romantic relationships. With retirement and reduced opportunities to form new romantic partnerships, the incidence of expression of this personality style tends to decrease. Masochistic (Self‐Defeating) Personalities in Older Adults With older adults, cl...
chunks.text: 932 Older Adults like the workplace or within romantic relationships. With retirement and reduced opportunities to form new romantic partnerships, the incidence of expression of this personality style tends to decrease.
