Masochistic (Self‐Defeating) Personalities in Older Adults With older adults, clinicians should distinguish between different variants of masochistic personality. Some individuals repetitively subordinate their own needs to those of others, for fear of losing them. Others seek to affirm a sense of moral superiority by showcasing their endurance of deprivation and stress, which they see themselves as facing with courage and self-­sacrifice. Here, there is an evident narcissistic component. Other manifestations of masochistic patterns involve intense anxiety, particularly related to fears that others might take advantage of one’s perceived weakness. Masochism is thus not a singular construct, but rather a range of phenomena with different origins and functions in the individual’s psychology. Masochism often leads older adults to emphasize their weaknesses and frailties in an effort to highlight the seriousness of their suffering and thereby gain some form of compensation. This behavior tends to provoke irritation and intolerance from clinicians and caregivers, especially in institutional settings. As a result, some of the patient’s legitimate difficulties may be underestimated or dismissed as mere attention seeking. Suggestions for Treatment As we noted previously, many patients believe that the attention and care of clinicians are guaranteed only when their suffering is made obvious. The primary task for those caring for these patients, including their relatives, is to help them realize that attention, affection, and concern do not depend on their being in pain. This is often difficult, particularly when the self-­defeating attitude has been a lifelong pattern. Such individuals have learned to view suffering as the price they must pay to feel valued and considered by others. Dependent and Dependent‐Victimized Personalities Potential Diagnostic Biases Related to Age In older adults, dependency in its various behavioral and emotional forms is central not only when clinicians consider the possibility of a preexisting dependent personality pattern but also for understanding “functional and structural dependency” as an essential expression of the elderly adult’s needs (Townsend, 1981). Several factors contribute to the need for support and assistance, as well as the accompanying subjective reactions: loss of significant relationships (e.g., spousal death or other causes of grief); “empty nest syndrome” (children leaving the household); outbreaks of illness, cognitive decline, and reduced autonomy; and the natural, age-­related reduction in emotional investment in daily life and personal responsibilities. The tendency to respond with dependency to age-­related problems appears to correlate with various personality traits: prior sense of autonomy, emotional reactivity, coping skills, and more (Gignac, Cott, & Badley, 2000). Under some conditions (e.g., during periods of stress, health difficulties, or environmental challenges) elderly adults

chunks.heading: 934 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 934 Older Adults The dependent personality style or disorder in older adults may coexist with symptoms of depression or anxiety (Lawton & Oltmanns, 2013). Similarities between dependent personality disorder and depression (e.g., lack of initiative, self-­confidence, autonomy, and capacity to make de...
chunks.text: 934 Older Adults The dependent personality style or disorder in older adults may coexist with symptoms of depression or anxiety (Lawton & Oltmanns, 2013). Similarities between dependent personality disorder and depression (e.g., lack of initiative, self-­confidence, autonomy, and capacity to make decisions) often blur the distinction between these diagnoses (Heintz, Freedberg, & Harper, 2021). People with dependent styles or disorders may have specific fears (e.g., of falling) or suffer separation anxiety, particularly related to children or grandchildren (Donald & Bulpitt, 1999; Wijeratne & ­Manicavasagar, 2003; Yur’yev et al., 2010). Their separation anxiety may impel demands for frequent contact (daily visits, phone calls for reassurance, support with decisions) and discussions about fears or doubts—­symptoms that may be exacerbated by losses of family members or friends (Wijeratne & Manicavasagar, 2003). Chronic anxiety and dependency on familiar figures are common in their histories. Dependent individuals tend to be submissive, to prioritize others’ needs over their own, and to neglect their own well-being. Sometimes there is a passive–­aggressive, oppositional tone to their dependency, leading to angry isolation, grumpiness, refusal of help, or the extreme self-­neglect of “Diogenes syndrome,” characterized by domestic squalor, social withdrawal, apathy, compulsive hoarding of garbage or animals, and lack of shame. Under these circumstances, substance abuse is a possibility (Saka, Kaya, Ozturn, Erten, & Karan, 2010; Tomstad, Söderhamn, Espnes, & Söderhamn, 2012). When dependency in old age reaches the threshold of a personality disorder, it has significant consequences for families and caregivers. Some individuals use their physical and psychological difficulties as entitlements to closeness and support, while fear of abandonment leads them to present themselves as weaker than they actually are. Such behavior can increase the risk of neglect or abuse in supposedly “fragile” elderly individuals (Agüero-­Torres et al., 1998). Special conditions arise when elderly individuals are institutionalized. Some older adults openly express to staff members their need for affection and closeness, while others initially show submissiveness and acquiescence. In some cases, requests for attention become repetitive and petulant, leading to caregiver fatigue, boredom, and burnout (Baltes et al., 1983; Cobo, 2014; Segal et al., 2006). Managing older individuals who are pathologically defended against their dependency needs and insistent on preserving their autonomy can be difficult because of their demands and the ambivalence behind them. Their denial of impairments and refusal of help (e.g., in washing, eating, or using a wheelchair), and the gruff, irritable behavior that may result, may be a problem for both the counterdependent individual and the staff (Caljouw, Cools, & Gussekloo, 2014). Suggestions for Treatment Accurate diagnosis of dependent personality style or disorder, especially when a person is also depressed, can significantly improve treatment outcomes, quality of life, and overall functioning in older adults (Heintz et al., 2021). Case reports suggest that recognizing dependent personality disorder reduces the risk of iatrogenic harm from overuse of therapies that address depression but not dependency (Morse, Pilkonsis, Houck, Frank, & Reynolds, 2005). In treating these patients, clinicians should be aware that attitudes of dependency and passive–­aggressive counterdependency may alternate. In elderly adults, life events can make these fluctuations more frequent. For example, worsening disease symptoms or diminished physical and cognitive resources may increase the need for support,

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 935
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 935 while conflicts with family members or care staff can exacerbate irritability and withdrawal. The challenge for clinicians and caregivers lies in balancing sensitivity to the patient’s demands with preserving the patient’s realistic areas of autonomy. Anxious–Av...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 935 while conflicts with family members or care staff can exacerbate irritability and withdrawal. The challenge for clinicians and caregivers lies in balancing sensitivity to the patient’s demands with preserving the patient’s realistic areas of autonomy. Anxious–Avoidant and Phobic Personalities Potential Diagnostic Biases Related to Age Anxiety is common in older adults (Alwahhabi, 2003) and is a frequent component of several personality styles (Shedler & Westen, 2004). It may stem from earlier life experiences, including childhood separation anxiety (Milrod et al., 2014). Distinguishing pathological anxiety from the emotional strains related to the cognitive and physical challenges of aging is usually easy, but in the case of anxious–­avoidant and phobic personalities, differential diagnosis may be more complex. Avoidant personalities should not be confused with the natural reduction in social networks commonly seen in aging. Furthermore, when faced with new situations, experiences, or social interactions, elderly persons may develop anxious or phobic tendencies that do not typically define their entire relationship with the environment or all their everyday activities. For example, cautious behaviors toward situations that could worsen their health or deplete resources (e.g., avoiding certain foods, activities, or places) are common. Similarly, difficulties with new technologies may result in avoidance in older adults who are in careers that require new technical skills. Withdrawal in such cases does not necessarily indicate avoidant personality disorder. Similarly, fears of being criticized or marginalized in social situations and embarrassment over decreased sensory or motor abilities are not evidence of personality dysfunction but rather of typical difficulties of aging. A differential diagnosis must also distinguish anxious–­avoidant behavior from schizoid personality style and social phobia (Gretarsdottir, Woodruff-­Borden, Meeks, & Depp, 2004). People with schizoid personalities are deeply conflicted about their attachment needs, while avoidant persons desire social contact but have fears related to it. In anxious–­avoidant personalities, feelings of shyness and embarrassment about social interactions are more pervasive and persistent than in cases of social phobia, extending to people, places, situations, and more. We should also mention that some older adults enjoy their isolation and choose it deliberately. “Clinicians should assess whether the isolation is due to adventitious circumstances (e.g., losses, illness), choice, or life-long traits of shyness, timidity, and inhibition (the latter being indicative of personality pathology)” (Segal et al., 2006, p. 107). Anxious–Avoidant and Phobic Personalities in Older Adults In adults, the features of an anxious–­avoidant personality include social inhibition, shyness, and conflict between a desire for and fear of relationships. Clinical histories often reveal anxious temperaments and a tendency toward withdrawal. In old age, these traits may intensify because of the diminished self-­confidence caused by reduced physical, cognitive, and emotional capacities (Coolidge et al., 2000). Anxiety itself can become unbearable, leading the individual to try to control it by withdrawing from relationships and avoiding unfamiliar situations. This tendency toward isolation complicates the ability of older adults to receive necessary support, especially when medical conditions worsen or when hospitalization

chunks.heading: 936 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 936 Older Adults or institutionalization becomes necessary. Old age is often perceived as a threat to one’s integrity and ability to cope. The reactions of anxious–­avoidant individuals are not realistic attempts to seek help or enhance their physical and psychological resources. Instead, avoidant b...
chunks.text: 936 Older Adults or institutionalization becomes necessary. Old age is often perceived as a threat to one’s integrity and ability to cope. The reactions of anxious–­avoidant individuals are not realistic attempts to seek help or enhance their physical and psychological resources. Instead, avoidant behaviors take precedence, even when the costs (e.g., isolation, loneliness, depression) are significant. Social isolation and withdrawal may increase the risk of developing anxiety and depression. When events typical of later years occur, such as loss, retirement, or widowhood, avoidant attitudes make it more difficult for older adults to seek or receive help and support. Avoidant individuals may also have fewer opportunities to form new relationships that could at least partially replace those they have lost. They may feel ashamed of many aspects of aging, both physical and psychological, and fear others’ judgments, assuming them to be negative and humiliating. As Segal and colleagues (2006, p. 107) write, these older adults are “isolated but desperately lonely, too shy to seek warmth and comfort from others.” Suggestions for Treatment Even in situations where help is needed, whether for physical or psychological challenges, anxiety may lead to withdrawal rather than a willingness to engage in relationships. Embarrassment and fear of judgment can be significant obstacles in interactions with health care professionals. An attitude of assertiveness combined with empathy for fears can help build a therapeutic relationship, which can be a starting point for fostering broader social connections (van Alphen, 2011). In a therapeutic setting, another dynamic can emerge: Anxious older adults may develop excessive dependency on the clinician or health care provider. Sometimes, submission is used as a way to ward off anxiety. This phenomenon highlights the underlying need for contact and relationships hidden behind the avoidance. In other cases, avoidant behaviors and phobias are rationalized to justify withdrawal. Denial and other primitive defenses may also be employed. Obsessive–Compulsive Personalities Potential Diagnostic Biases Related to Age Obsessive–­compulsive personality style or disorder is characterized by a preoccupation with orderliness, control, details, and rules. Its association with rigidity, a quality often linked to aging, poses complications for diagnosing older adults. This stereotype of rigidity in old age is not only widespread but also internalized by many older people themselves (Levy, 2008). A key factor in aging is the decrease in control over oneself and one’s environment, a decrease that creates problems that need to be addressed. In response, older individuals may engage in behaviors meant to reassure themselves about their ability to manage various situations. Persistent concerns and repetitive behaviors can emerge, especially in unfamiliar settings like hospitals and nursing homes. Healthy older adults remain capable of flexible adaptations to new circumstances, accepting help and adjusting their behavior when necessary. In individuals without personality disorder, defenses such as reaction formation or isolation of affect may appear in stressful situations but are generally context-­specific. The need for control is appropriate to the situation, and compulsive behaviors are rare

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 937
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 937 or sporadic. Intellectualization and moralization, often seen in obsessive–­compulsive personalities, may remain stable and can even be reinforced by the societal roles that older individuals assume. Diagnosing obsessive personality style or disorder in older ad...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 937 or sporadic. Intellectualization and moralization, often seen in obsessive–­compulsive personalities, may remain stable and can even be reinforced by the societal roles that older individuals assume. Diagnosing obsessive personality style or disorder in older adults is further complicated by the fact that many obsessive–­compulsive personality traits tend to increase with age. This personality constellation is typically chronic and stable, often occurring alongside obsessive–­compulsive disorder, other anxiety conditions, or even psychotic disorders (Barton, Karner, Salih, Baldwin, & Edwards, 2014; Calamari et al., 2014). Older adults with obsessive–­compulsive features may also have neurocognitive symptoms or disorders, such as dementia, frontotemporal syndromes, or hoarding disorders (Cervellati et al., 2013; Low, Brodaty, & Draper, 2002; Sabbe & Vandenbulcke, 2014). Patients with Parkinson’s disease show a higher prevalence of diagnosed obsessive–­compulsive personality disorder (Nicoletti et al., 2013), and obsessive and compulsive traits may reappear in old age even after a long period of remission (Shprecher, Rubenstein, Gannon, Frank, & Kurlan, 2014). Some diagnostic criteria for obsessive–­compulsive personality disorder frequently reported in the literature can be problematic when applied to older adults. For example, perfectionism and solipsistic isolation at work may not be observable in retired individuals. Diagnostic criteria related to miserliness and hoarding may misdiagnose older adults who are financially conservative because their families endured the Great Depression of the 1930s; their frugality should not be mistaken for obsessive–­ compulsive personality traits. A history of greed and hoarding in wealthy individuals, however, may be more indicative of the disorder (Segal et al., 2006). Obsessive–Compulsive Personalities in Older Adults An individual’s clinical history can provide valuable insights for diagnosing obsessive–­ compulsive personality in old age. Many characteristics of the personality style, such as irritability, meticulousness, perfectionism, and a preoccupation with orderliness and control, may remain stable throughout life. The aging process, with its associated physical, emotional, and cognitive changes, often intensifies these concerns, leading to increased rigidity in behaviors and thought patterns. Control is a central issue in obsessive–­compulsive personalities. As the ability to exert control diminishes with age, older individuals may resort to ruminative thoughts or controlling actions to cope with feelings of fear, shame, and anger. They often use such defenses to avoid awareness of uncomfortable emotions or urges, and the need for certainty and precision can distort their thoughts and actions. In contrast, an older adult without personality pathology can bear the loss of some past capabilities, relinquish needs for control, and adapt to new circumstances. Level of personality organization is a critical consideration. Obsessive–­compulsive traits look different depending on the underlying personality structure. Individuals whose personality is structured at a borderline or psychotic level may have intense obsessive and compulsive symptoms, ego-­syntonic fears, and severe annihilation anxiety that leans toward paranoia, while those with a neurotic-­level organization may show more ego-alien anxiety or depressive symptoms. Obsessive thoughts and compulsive behaviors tend to remain consistent in meaning and structure over time, but their content may evolve. In old age, they may be manifested in specific control-­related behaviors, such as rigidly managing personal spaces or objects and refusing to discard even worthless items (e.g., old newspapers or

chunks.heading: 938 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 938 Older Adults boxes), potentially leading to compulsive hoarding (Ayers et al., 2014; Turner, Steketee, & Nauth, 2010). Additionally, older adults with obsessive–­compulsive personalities may resist being compared to or controlled by others, with rigidity of thought, refusal to recognize their im...
chunks.text: 938 Older Adults boxes), potentially leading to compulsive hoarding (Ayers et al., 2014; Turner, Steketee, & Nauth, 2010). Additionally, older adults with obsessive–­compulsive personalities may resist being compared to or controlled by others, with rigidity of thought, refusal to recognize their impairments, and sometimes delusional thinking (e.g., fears of being robbed or cheated). Often, their histories show a significant intellectual path that has managed their need for control, which resurfaces in old age as a “stiffening of attitudes.” These individuals may become highly dependent on family or caregivers, but their increased reliance on others is often accompanied by intensified control over their caregivers’ actions. Judgments of caregivers’ reliability tend to be negative, leading to further attempts to control them. Resistance to change is a hallmark, as obsessive–­ compulsive individuals deeply dislike any external demands that disrupt their routines. If they need drug therapy, they seek total control over prescription and doses (Bloch et al., 2013; Grant, Mancebob, & Weinhandlc, 2013). When hospitalized or placed in a care facility, their rigid behaviors often intensify in reaction to their increased dependency on others. They struggle to adapt to new routines or settings, and they feel great distress if required to leave their familiar surroundings. Suggestions for Treatment Treatment of older adults with obsessive–­compulsive personalities can be stymied by their tendency to be resistant to treatment. Their symptoms often become chronic, elevating the risk of their developing dementia, functional disability, or social marginalization (Barton et al., 2014). Anxiety, depression, and delusions may emerge (Ayers et al., 2014; Kichuk et al., 2013). Considerable time and effort are required to alleviate even a portion of these patients’ anxieties about control. Their perceptions of age-­ related weakness further complicate the therapeutic process, as offers of help may be interpreted as an affront to their autonomy. Interventions aimed at improving the environment, such as practical assistance with organizing objects or compensating for sensory deficits, can encourage a therapeutic alliance and reduce agitation and compulsivity. Caregivers and health care professionals are at high risk of burnout when dealing with obsessive–­compulsive patients, as their demeaning and devaluing behaviors can be exhausting. Older adults with obsessive personalities may dismiss the advice and assistance of younger health care staff or family members, claiming superior knowledge or experience. This attitude, combined with their rigidity, may leave them facing dangerous situations alone. Schizoid Personalities Potential Diagnostic Biases Related to Age The process of aging may foster or strengthen personality traits similar to those found in individuals with schizoid personalities. Older adults may reduce the number and range of their relationships, possibly expressing a propensity for emotional detachment (Lang, 2001), but more likely as a way to maximize emotional rewards (Carstensen, Isaacowitz, & Charles, 1999). Sensory impairments common in later life, such as hearing and vision deficits, can restrict social contact. Life events typical of old age, including retirement, widowhood, and other losses, also contribute to a reduction in social

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 939
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 939 interactions. There is often diminishing interest in sexual experiences, which may stem from a lack of suitable partners or a normal, age-­related decrease in libido. Thus, the challenge for clinicians is to distinguish between the isolating processes often asso...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 939 interactions. There is often diminishing interest in sexual experiences, which may stem from a lack of suitable partners or a normal, age-­related decrease in libido. Thus, the challenge for clinicians is to distinguish between the isolating processes often associated with aging and the expressions of schizoid dynamics. This task becomes easier with a sensitive assessment of the inner emotional life of the individual, which in a genuinely schizoid person has involved a lifelong preoccupation with issues of closeness and distance. A focus on the person’s constitutional and maturational patterns is essential for identifying preexisting tendencies toward withdrawal and hypersensitivity to stimuli. Schizoid Personalities in Older Adults Individuals with schizoid personalities can range from high-­functioning to severely disordered (Badcock & Dragović, 2006; Bora & Arabaci, 2009). They are often described by others as aloof, distant, isolated, and emotionally cold. Detachment from social relationships can vary: in mild cases, it reflects a personal lifestyle preference that allows for intermittent close connections, whereas in severe cases, it represents a pervasive pattern of retreat that significantly impairs the ability to form and maintain social bonds. These difficulties can resemble those seen in diagnosed schizophrenia, other psychotic disorders, and autism. Withdrawal from relationships and lack of emotional investment in others are central to this personality style. Schizoid individuals experience a constant tension between the fear of closeness and a longing for it, which results in emotional suppression. Their withdrawal, efforts to be sure of sufficient space, and rigid behavior are maintained by idiosyncratic reasons and preoccupations, and they react rigidly to many stimuli and conditions. Older schizoid individuals are bothered by the presence of others and tend to avoid interactions. Although often privately interested in others’ thoughts, emotions, or behaviors, they do not show these aspects of their inner life. They often replace the communication of emotions with intellectualization, metaphors, or references to art and literature. Their need to keep others at a distance may result in behavior that jeopardizes their health or safety. The attention of others is often perceived as an annoyance, even when assistance is necessary to address age-­related challenges. Refusing help can increase their vulnerability to neglect and isolation. In contrast, older adults without a schizoid personality may experience occasional periods of withdrawal in reaction to specific stressful events or circumstances, but such moments are generally motivated by understandable conditions, and their desire for positive social interactions remains intact. They typically have a broader, more flexible range of defenses against relational anxiety. The natural decrease in sexual needs that occurs with age does not diminish their desire for emotional intimacy. Once temporary challenges are overcome, their motivation for social engagement tends to recover. Periods of withdrawal may still arise, usually in response to reductions in mental or physical resources or to changes in activity levels and quality of life, but their relational skills are often well-­preserved, as evidenced by their personal history. Some changes associated with aging, such as retirement or bereavement, may be better tolerated by older schizoid individuals because of their reduced need for social contact (Stevens, 1995; van Tilburg, 1992). In some individuals, however, there may be vulnerability to late-onset psychosis (see SOA Axis, Chapter 19, pp. 957–1008).

chunks.heading: 940 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 940 Older Adults Schizoid individuals may have a particularly hard time with common events in old age, such as hospitalization or moving to a retirement home. Because their self-­ esteem may depend on near-total self-­sufficiency, offers of help can provoke anxiety, discomfort, and irritation. They...
chunks.text: 940 Older Adults Schizoid individuals may have a particularly hard time with common events in old age, such as hospitalization or moving to a retirement home. Because their self-­ esteem may depend on near-total self-­sufficiency, offers of help can provoke anxiety, discomfort, and irritation. They find their increased dependency needs, which usually precede such placements, difficult to tolerate. Sensory deficits associated with age that heighten their need for assistance and decrease their independence are hard for them to bear. In contrast to nonschizoid individuals, who come to accept age-­related limitations despite valuing their autonomy, persons with schizoid psychologies may struggle to adjust to hospitals or long-term care settings, especially when sharing rooms (Bangash, 2020). Suggestions for Treatment Treating schizoid older adults requires a delicate balance between encouraging more emotional intimacy and respecting their need for space. Some clinicians may find it challenging to empathize with their suffering. For example, they may express grief over the loss of a significant other in purely practical terms, such as missing someone to help with household chores, rather than framing the loss as an emotional one. This apparent coldness is often a defense against a severity of grief that feels too painful and disorganizing to express. When working with severely disturbed patients with schizoid tendencies, clinicians should expect frequent periods of withdrawal. They should not force intimacy, instead maintaining an attitude of availability. Caregivers and clinicians working with schizoid individuals may feel rejected and unappreciated. They may risk overestimating the patient’s capacity for autonomy or, conversely, imposing excessive control in response to the patient’s habitual negative reactions. Such responses can exacerbate the patient’s sense of already feeling overwhelmed and provoke dysfunctional protective strategies. Somatizing Personalities Potential Diagnostic Biases Related to Age DSM-5-TR does not include a diagnosis of somatizing personality disorder. It remains in the P Axis in PDM-3, with the caveat that “some scholars see somatizing presen­ tations as outside the personality spectrum” (see Chapter 12 on the P Axis, pp. 651– 708). Older adults often have multiple chronic medical conditions. Distinguishing normal concern about symptoms of those conditions from abnormal preoccupation with somatic difficulties can be challenging. The latter, characterized by excessive preoccupation with somatic issues, is typical of individuals with somatizing personality style or disorder. Further complicating diagnosis, many medical conditions are comorbid with psychological symptoms (Parmelee, Harralson, McPherron, & Ralph Schumacher, 2013). In older adults, somatic expression may be influenced by co-­occurring illnesses, leading to a perception of poor health, low self-­esteem, and diminished self-­ efficacy (Nützel et al., 2014). A well-­functioning older adult is aware of age-­related physical difficulties and manages them with the help of family, friends, and health care professionals. Requests for help may be frequent but are appropriate to the severity of the discomfort, and

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 941
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 941 available resources are realistically assessed. Most older people can recognize improvements in their health and do not experience excessive anxiety or depressive reactions that might produce additional somatic symptoms. They are aware of stress and discomfort r...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 941 available resources are realistically assessed. Most older people can recognize improvements in their health and do not experience excessive anxiety or depressive reactions that might produce additional somatic symptoms. They are aware of stress and discomfort related to aging but can appreciate their strengths and use a range of adaptive defenses. Somatizing Personalities in Older Adults The starting point for diagnosing somatic elements of personality is the individual’s medical history, coupled with an observation of current behavior. The history may reveal previous physical fragility, early sickliness, or prior episodes of somatization. Several indicators may suggest a possible somatizing personality (Mailis-­Gagnon, Nicholson, Yegneswaran, & Zurowski, 2008): • Bodily complaints that are not associated with an identifiable disease or that seem linked to multiple diseases. • Avoidance or social isolation, justified by physical difficulties. • Bizarre behavior incongruent with the actual disease. • Fluctuation of symptom expression and severity in response to specific situations (e.g., unwanted relationships) or individuals (e.g., children, spouse, health care professionals). • Correlation of physical difficulties with significant life events (e.g., retirement, loss). • Suggestion of secondary gain from physical difficulties (e.g., more attention from family members). • History of multiple medical treatments for poorly defined ailments. A second key aspect of the differential diagnosis is to rule out syndromes with somatic components, such as the “masked depression” common in older adults, in which somatization defends against depression. Both generalized anxiety and depression may exacerbate the severity of somatizing patterns (Calleo et al., 2009; Hoedeman, Blankenstein, Krol, Koopmans, & Groothoff, 2010; Maharaj et al., 2013). At the core of a somatizing personality pattern is an altered representation of illness in which somatic symptoms express psychological pain. This “illness representation” is strongly influenced by anxiety and depressive states, which are expressed physically rather than verbally. Alexithymic individuals, who are unaware of their emotions or unable to articulate them, often experience physical discomfort instead of expressing their distress verbally. Feelings like anger and suffering related to prolonged stress are translated into somatic symptoms, with repeated requests for help with bodily impairments. Thoughts of death and fear of physical or cognitive decline, often accompanied by excessive anxiety or reactive depression, are central concerns for somatizing patients. Their focus on bodily issues becomes repetitive, spirals out of control, and dominates daily life (Letamendi et al., 2013), sometimes leading to substance use disorders and unnecessary medical diagnostic procedures. The continuous preoccupation with bodily illness creates stress that may exacerbate existing diseases or contribute to the development of new symptoms. Individuals who somatize often rely heavily on regressive defenses when age-­related physical decline has undermined an already fragile sense of self and self-­esteem. These defenses,

chunks.heading: 942 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 942 Older Adults in turn, damage their relationships with others. Repeated requests for help with bodily symptoms often stem from a belief that others possess inexhaustible resources of health, strength, and vitality. When such requests are not met, the somatizing individual may perceive others as h...
chunks.text: 942 Older Adults in turn, damage their relationships with others. Repeated requests for help with bodily symptoms often stem from a belief that others possess inexhaustible resources of health, strength, and vitality. When such requests are not met, the somatizing individual may perceive others as hostile or indifferent. Suggestions for Treatment When addressing somatic concerns of older patients, clinicians must first consider whether previous diagnostic or therapeutic interventions failed to identify an underlying medical condition (Mailis-­Gagnon et al., 2008; Parmelee et al., 2013). Discovery of a previously unsuspected physical problem may allow medical intervention that relieves not only the patient but also the families, friends, health care professionals, and caregivers involved. This personality pattern exacerbates both physical and psychological age-­related difficulties. Older individuals who somatize often respond poorly to treatments; may be repeatedly hospitalized (the “revolving door” phenomenon); burden their friends, families, and caregivers; and typically experience a reduced quality of life. Treating older adults with somatizing patterns is daunting because of their difficulty in recognizing and acknowledging the emotional roots of their somatic symptoms. They may regard all efforts to explore the feelings behind their physical suffering as accusations of malingering. An inability to understand and express their inner worlds has often characterized their entire lives, leading to withdrawal, disinvestment, and refuge in physical ailments that persist into old age. The strains they put on relationships make them more likely to be isolated or marginalized in old age, intensifying their tendency to express emotional distress through physical complaints rather than verbal communication. Clinicians should resist the temptation to disengage in the face of persistent somatic complaints. Regardless of its psychological origins, the patient’s suffering is real. Primarily Externalizing Personalities Hysteric–Histrionic Personalities Potential Diagnostic Biases Related to Age The following configuration applies mostly to individuals with histrionic personalities organized at the borderline-­to-­psychotic level (in fact, the word “histrionic” was once differentiated from “hysterical” as denoting more primitive dynamics), and applies more to people in Western societies than in cultures where sexual conservatism, modesty, and compliance with authority are valued. In many religious and collectivist subcultures, individuals with this personality type are likely to be reclusive and inhibited rather than dramatizing and exhibitionistic. Traits that have been associated with hysteric–­histrionic personalities in contemporary Western cultures include neuroticism and attention seeking. “Neuroticism” has been defined as intense and unstable emotions, including anxiety, depression, impulsiveness, vulnerability, suggestibility, affective lability, separation anxiety, hostility, suspiciousness, and perseveration. Attention seeking may involve theatrical and eccentric behavior. In terms of inferred inner processes, patients with hysterical personality styles may rely on regression and dissociation, especially in stressful situations, which may trigger conversion symptoms or hypochondriacal concerns. Historically, their

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 943
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 943 tendency to sexualize relationships has been understood as a defense against feelings of inadequacy and humiliation (McWilliams, 2011; see also Chapter 12 on the P Axis, pp. 651–708). In normal personality maturation, in contrast, fears of old age (death, illnes...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 943 tendency to sexualize relationships has been understood as a defense against feelings of inadequacy and humiliation (McWilliams, 2011; see also Chapter 12 on the P Axis, pp. 651–708). In normal personality maturation, in contrast, fears of old age (death, illness, loss of autonomy) may lead to increased self-­concern, potentially at the expense of attending to others. Some regressive behavior may occur but is relatively temporary. Needs for attention, complaints about physical and psychological age-­related difficulties, and regrets about the past may emerge but are often fleeting. They may produce exaggerated emotional expressions, and they do not dominate the usual behavior of the person, who remains in touch with emotions and can communicate them, even though occasionally subject to conversion and somatization. Hysteric–Histrionic Personalities in Older Adults In older adults, some personality traits remain stable, while others diminish with age. Traits that have been found to persist include emotional intensity and instability, as well as conversion and somatization tendencies. The aging process often involves fear and shame, which amplify and exaggerate related emotions. A low threshold for responsiveness to stimuli is typically seen in the clinical histories of histrionic patients, along with a demonstrated need for attention. These individuals seek care from others while simultaneously fearing overstimulation in relationships, leading to their self-­ dramatizing as a way to maintain control. With aging, the use of sexuality to express conflict often diminishes, likely because of physical changes. Reduction in physical attractiveness can have devastating consequences for a histrionic individual’s self-­esteem, leading to a sense of weakness and low self-worth. As a result, the need for reassurance may increase, and the individual may resort to excessive use of anti-aging remedies. Attempts at seduction may still occur, sometimes through eroticism but more often through dramatizing needs or referencing past successes. These concerns are handled by conversion and regression, manifesting themselves as medically unexplainable physical symptoms. Somatic and hypochondriacal complaints may replace the physical charms once used to attract attention. Dissociative experiences may recede, though in clinical evaluations focused on cognitive decline, dissociative states may go unnoticed. It is often challenging to differentiate real physical illnesses from psychological amplifications, especially when both are present, as with symptoms like physical weakness, lack of energy, vision problems, apathy, gastrointestinal issues, and autonomic disturbances (Wongpakaran & Wongpakaran, 2014). In older adults, somatic symptoms and social isolation often increase, while histrionic expressiveness tends to decrease. Aging may temper histrionic tendencies, leading to a different expression of this personality pattern in response to life changes. The trait of “infantilizing egocentrism” generally remains intact, with special reference to the body and hypochondriacal fears, whether or not real health problems exist. Reactive depression and anxiety may also be part of the clinical picture (Dewsbury, 1973; Dols, Rhebergen, Eikelenboom, & Stek, 2012; Hilderink et al., 2009; Reynolds, 2012). Older adults with histrionic personalities are often plaintive, child-like, and prone to self-­victimization. Especially in institutions, they seek attention from relatives, friends, and caregivers through physical complaints and difficulties adjusting to life’s challenges. They may feel abandoned if not adequately cared for and may respond with self-pity or anger to real or imagined neglect. The aging process is particularly

chunks.heading: 944 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 944 Older Adults hard for them to accept, especially its effects on physical appearance, propelling them toward plastic surgery or anti-aging treatments. The stress they feel over bodily limitations and dependency needs can lead to symptoms of depression or anxiety. These individuals may experience...
chunks.text: 944 Older Adults hard for them to accept, especially its effects on physical appearance, propelling them toward plastic surgery or anti-aging treatments. The stress they feel over bodily limitations and dependency needs can lead to symptoms of depression or anxiety. These individuals may experience faster cognitive decline, primarily due to depression (reactive to their environment, aging-­related limitations, or perceived neglect), anxiety, and difficulty coping with new stimuli. Suggestions for Treatment Hysteric–­histrionic personalities in old age pose many difficulties for clinicians. They often resist treatment, including hospitalization or institutionalization. If admitted to a care facility, their incessant demands (frequent use of call bells if bedridden, persistent requests for anxiety medications, repetitive complaints) may strain their relationships with staff. Even in old age, excessive emotionality and attention-­seeking behavior may persist. Theatricality may extend to discussions about current illnesses or past medical treatments, or even to accounts of their past successes and life events. In psychotherapy, histrionic patients may attempt to erotize the therapeutic relationship (see Segal et al., 2006). They tend to perceive their relationships, including with the therapist, as more intimate than they actually are, and often expect equal emotional investment in return. Therapists should remember that behind these behaviors—­and often behind stories of past social achievements—­lie failed or superficial relationships. The patient’s seductiveness should not be rejected but understood and interpreted, with the goal of addressing the underlying conflicts and fears. Narcissistic Personalities Potential Diagnostic Biases Related to Age Some older adults may demand attention and respect from others, expressing their needs with somewhat arrogant attitudes. For example, they may seek admiration by proudly communicating their age or boasting of the persistence of their intellectual skills (e.g., memory) or physical abilities (e.g., strength and endurance). Such behaviors do not necessarily indicate a narcissistic personality disorder, especially if such tendencies were not present earlier in life. Some features of aging may naturally increase realistic needs for reassurance about one’s value, identity, and relationships (Balsis, Eaton, Uke, Cooper, & Oltmanns, 2011). A positive outcome of the need for attention may be enhanced attachments and reduced feelings of loneliness. Narcissistic Personalities in Older Adults DSM criteria for diagnosing narcissistic personality disorder are focused primarily on the grandiose or arrogant presentation of that psychology. This bias overlooks individuals who feel a vague sense of failure and shame, avoid relationships, and hide their grandiose fantasies. Particularly in older adults, the realities of aging may cause individuals to avoid overtly arrogant behavior and instead demand that a therapist help them achieve the attention and relationships that more fortunate individuals have. Clinicians should remain aware of the presence of grandiose fantasies, which may appear in the form of hypochondriacal symptoms or somatic complaints.

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 945
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 945 If they were already present earlier in life, narcissistic traits such as grandiosity, need for admiration, lack of empathy, arrogance, entitlement, and interpersonal exploitation tend to persist in old age, though their intensity may decrease (Foster, Campbell,...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 945 If they were already present earlier in life, narcissistic traits such as grandiosity, need for admiration, lack of empathy, arrogance, entitlement, and interpersonal exploitation tend to persist in old age, though their intensity may decrease (Foster, Campbell, & Twenge, 2003; Twenge, Konrath, Foster, Keith Campbell, & Bushman, 2008). And some of these traits may diminish in later life (Segal et al., 2006). When narcissistic traits do persist into old age, they may be manifested somewhat differently than in earlier developmental phases. For example, older individuals may experience a reduction in power and influence, limiting their opportunities to exploit others narcissistically, whether in family or professional settings. Fantasies of success may decrease, and reduced opportunities for social interactions may reduce narcissistic enactments (Bressler-­Feiner, 1981). Despite these changes, certain narcissistic traits may take new forms, which should not escape diagnostic attention. Arrogance may turn into stubbornness, the tendency to exploit others may provoke isolation that exacerbates depressive symptoms, or the need for admiration may lead to imprudent behavior. A defining aspect of the narcissistic personality is the protection of one’s image (physical and social) and fear of its devaluation. Thus, the difficulties associated with aging—such as physical illness and body deterioration (hair loss, wrinkles, diminished sensory and motor skills)—can significantly affect how narcissistic individuals view themselves. They may struggle to tolerate their changing appearance. During old age, they are often isolated and may begin to regret their past successes. Their lack of empathy and inability to maintain deep relationships can lead to a reduction in supportive relationships, contributing to Eriksonian “despair” rather than “integrity” in old age. Increased dependency on others is particularly difficult for older people with narcissistic personalities, as current realities may contrast sharply with their previous sense of independence, power, and control (Clark, 1993; Heisel, Links, Conn, van Reekum, & Flett, 2007). As a result, they may shift to the more depressed-­depleted version of narcissism and report fears of criticism, rejection, or dislike (Stone, Segal, & Krus, 2020). Comparisons between their earlier life and the increasing difficulties of aging can lead to feelings of bitterness, shame, and regret, while relationships with younger people may be marked by envy, sometimes expressed in a rancorous manner. These factors may contribute to depression and an increased risk of suicide (Jahn, Poindexter, & Cukrowicz, 2015). Narcissistic personality disorder has been associated with suicidal ideation and is considered a suicide risk factor in older adults, particularly when comorbid with depression (Szücs, Szanto, Aubry, & Dombrovski, 2018). Suggestions for Treatment Providing therapeutic support to older patients with narcissism can be difficult for clinicians and health care staff. Clinicians need to assess how central the need for attention and praise has been to the individual’s psychological and medical history. Such individuals often link physical, psychological, and cognitive age-­related difficulties to the deterioration of their image in the eyes of others rather than to a normal sense of loss over diminishing resources. Establishing a therapeutic alliance and encouraging compliance with medical treatments may be hard. Bitterness and envy often predominate in people with narcissism, limiting their ability to seek and accept help. Their demands tend to be excessive, and they frequently express regret over their lost autonomy and power. Devaluation and idealization of both self and others preoccupies them. They may buttress such attitudes with primitive defenses such as denial, projection, and splitting.

chunks.heading: 946 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 946 Older Adults In institutional settings, their entitled or otherwise problematic behavior can result in frequent changes of nursing staff, sometimes requested by the patient’s relatives. Health care professionals trained to recognize narcissistic traits in older patients may find some opportuniti...
chunks.text: 946 Older Adults In institutional settings, their entitled or otherwise problematic behavior can result in frequent changes of nursing staff, sometimes requested by the patient’s relatives. Health care professionals trained to recognize narcissistic traits in older patients may find some opportunities for collaboration and support (Garner, 2002). Paranoid Personalities Potential Diagnostic Biases Related to Age With aging, a certain degree of distrust and suspicion is common. Such attitudes may result from the weakening of sensory, motor, and cognitive capacities, diminishing the sense of control and mastery. As a result, older adults may feel less able to react adaptively to their environment and to cope with potential dangers or difficulties. When evaluating an older adult for paranoid tendencies, clinicians should consider the possibility of current or prior maltreatment (Wolf, 1998). Because of their physical fragility and/or cognitive confusion, and sometimes because of certain personality traits, older adults may become victims of abuse, which can exacerbate feelings of distrust and suspicion (Akaza et al., 2003). Paranoid feelings often intensify isolation and withdrawal from intimate and social contact. Unlike schizoid individuals, whose behavior may be similar based on a deep ambivalence about intimacy, paranoid individuals resist closeness because they expect malice or deceit from others. Paranoid Personalities in Older Adults The foundational belief of individuals with paranoid personalities is that they cannot openly and genuinely relate to others, because anything they share can and will be used against them (Segal et al., 2006, p. 28). A pattern of irritability, hostility toward others, and aggressive behaviors often appears in the clinical history, stemming from feelings of shame and humiliation. These individuals are preoccupied with fears of being attacked or mistreated, concerns that become heightened by age-­related physical, cognitive, and emotional difficulties. Their fears are not always verbalized but may be expressed in irritability, isolation, and discomfort. Although expressions of these traits may diminish as a result of the reduced physical energy in later life, the effects of aging can still be problematic for individuals with paranoid personalities. Physical illnesses, sensory decline, and increased dependence can exacerbate suspiciousness and worsen pathological adaptations. The subjective perception of cognitive decline often heightens concerns about being attacked or humiliated by others (Forsell & Henderson, 1998). In some cases, paranoid attitudes may develop later in life, sometimes as a response to the experienced humiliations attendant on aging, and sometimes as a symptom of a neurocognitive disorder. Feelings of distrust and fears of being exploited make it difficult for paranoid individuals to manage age-­related challenges. They often respond with resistance to offers of help from friends, family members, and health care personnel. Increased dependence may provoke fears of losing autonomy, fueling a need for control and heightened caution. If they require hospitalization or institutional care, their agitation and suspicion may intensify, as they perceive doctors, nurses, other staff members, and fellow patients as intrusive and dubiously trustworthy.

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 947
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 947 Suggestions for Treatment Older adults can experience actual abuse, whether in their families or in institutions. When they report difficult relationships in their environment, clinicians should assess their living conditions carefully and avoid prematurely attr...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 947 Suggestions for Treatment Older adults can experience actual abuse, whether in their families or in institutions. When they report difficult relationships in their environment, clinicians should assess their living conditions carefully and avoid prematurely attributing their discomfort solely to paranoid fears. A thorough examination of the person’s clinical history is helpful for differential diagnosis. Even when a patient’s fears are paranoid rather than realistic, an empathic approach to their concerns is essential for establishing even a minimal therapeutic alliance. Though such an alliance may be fragile and prone to rupture, it is the foundation for any effective therapeutic intervention (Bateman & Fonagy, 2000). Psychopathic Personalities Potential Diagnostic Biases Related to Age Psychopathic personalities are less common among older adults than in younger individuals (Engels, Duijsens, Haringsma, & van Putten, 2003; Grant et al., 2008; Oltmanns & Balsis, 2011). Some common features of psychopathic behavior in younger adults, such as deriving pleasure from deceiving and manipulating others, are naturally less frequent (and less feasible) in old age, probably because of declines in social interactions and in physical, cognitive, and emotional capacities. Yet the need to assert power for its own sake—or to compensate for a perceived loss of personal power—can persist into old age. In certain contexts, such as business or finance, some psychopathic older individuals may continue to manipulate and control others, disregarding social norms. Normal aging brings a greater sense of freedom from societal and occupational pressures, an awareness of one’s limited lifespan, diminished concern for others’ opinions, and an increased focus on the self. These should not be confused with psychopathy. In addition, some characteristics of antisocial behavior, such as impulsivity, aggression, and disregard for others, may also be seen in individuals in the early stages of neurocognitive disorders. Psychopathic Personalities in Older Adults Older adults who prioritize personal needs over those of others may increase their antisocial behaviors in later life. This may take the form of treatment nonadherence, misuse of prescribed medications, or abuse of recreational substances. A preoccupation with being exploited or manipulated may drive some individuals into transgressive behavior and attempts to dominate or subjugate others. This tendency can be particularly evident in institutional settings, where they may isolate themselves from support staff or fellow residents, seeking control over their environment to feel reassured or even to derive sadistic pleasure. A review of the individual’s clinical history often reveals that these behaviors have been consistent from childhood on. Psychopathy can significantly affect the aging process (van Alphen, Nijhuis, & Oei, 2007). Disregard for others’ needs and feelings, combined with a challenging and often impulsive or aggressive attitude, can cause others, especially family members, to avoid contact with the psychopathic elder, leading to further isolation. Older adults with psychopathic traits may justify their transgressions by believing that they were wronged earlier in life or by claiming special merits that have never been recognized.

chunks.heading: 948 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 948 Older Adults They may see old age as a form of permission to act without regard for societal rules. In such cases, clinical histories often reveal that this sense of entitlement and the antisocial behavior that accompanies it have been long-­standing features of such individuals, with old age pr...
chunks.text: 948 Older Adults They may see old age as a form of permission to act without regard for societal rules. In such cases, clinical histories often reveal that this sense of entitlement and the antisocial behavior that accompanies it have been long-­standing features of such individuals, with old age providing a new rationale for their actions. Suggestions for Treatment As with younger individuals with psychopathic tendencies, therapeutic interventions are extremely challenging because of the difficulty in establishing a shared purpose or therapeutic alliance. Domination, control, and contempt for fellow human beings remain central features of psychopathic individuals. In old age, they may perceive others as selfish and manipulative, using this belief to justify exploiting others’ weaknesses related to aging. When an older adult with psychopathic traits enters a care facility, management difficulties are likely to arise, both with other residents and with health care staff. Frequent conflicts may occur over the person’s appropriating others’ belongings or refusing to follow institutional rules. Clear, consistent rules and expectations can help manage such behaviors. The treatment team needs to recognize that behind the adversarial stance and frequent transgressions lies the individual’s need to assert a sense of identity and relative autonomy, which may have been undermined by the aging process and its associated difficulties. Sadistic Personalities Potential Diagnostic Biases Related to Age The nature and frequency of sadistic patterns in older adults are unknown, as there is almost no empirical literature available to shed light on these behaviors. Sadistic personalities are often associated with psychopathy and criminal behavior, and because of lack of data are not explicitly recognized in the DSM. Given diminishing social networks, physical functioning, and cognitive abilities in older adults, it is unlikely that they would engage in the overt sadistic behaviors found in younger adults, such as physical violence or cruelty. Older adults with sadistic tendencies may therefore be overlooked by traditional diagnostic classifications. Yet difficulties associated with aging, such as increased personal dependency and exposure to unfamiliar environments (e.g., hospitals or other facilities), can trigger anger that may be expressed through acts of aggression or humiliation. This behavior may serve the purpose of helping the person maintain a sense of power and control. If there is no history of manifest sadistic behaviors, such episodes tend to be brief and are not a defining characteristic of the individual’s relationships with others. Sadistic Personalities in Older Adults As Segal and colleagues (2006, p. 142) note, “Emotional cruelty seems to know no age limits.” Thus, sadistic personality traits may persist into old age, evidenced in humiliating attitudes or a sadistic pleasure derived from others’ unhappiness, often justified by a belief in one’s own superiority. Fears of being humiliated may lead to anticipatory defenses, such as devaluing others and acting with a conspicuous lack of empathy.

chunks.heading: Personality Styles and Disorders 949
chunks.preview: Personality Styles and Disorders 949 Omnipotent control and emotional detachment are notable attitudes here. Older adults with sadistic tendencies may express contempt or frequently reproach people on whom they depend, including family members, nurses, and caregivers. In some families, when an olde...
chunks.text: Personality Styles and Disorders 949 Omnipotent control and emotional detachment are notable attitudes here. Older adults with sadistic tendencies may express contempt or frequently reproach people on whom they depend, including family members, nurses, and caregivers. In some families, when an older adult who had been the primary decision maker in the household develops cognitive or physical impairments, the family may allow that person to retain control, even when no longer capable of handling such responsibilities. Sadistic elements may emerge in this context, as the older adult continues to exert authority while disregarding other family members. Hatred and contempt are central emotions in sadistic personalities, and because the aging process diminishes the ability to express superiority and increases dependency, it is often met with great resentment. When hospitalization or placement in a care facility becomes necessary, relationships with doctors, nurses, and other residents may become opportunities for the older adult to express efforts at sadistic domination. Suggestions for Treatment Individuals with sadistic personalities often have limited insight into their own feelings and behaviors, and minimize their impact on others. They typically believe their feelings and actions are completely normal and acceptable. As a result, treating them is challenging, often of necessity focusing on containing their most extreme behaviors. When possible, and especially in institutional settings, it is crucial to protect those who may become targets of their sadism. Emotionally Dysregulated Personalities Potential Diagnostic Biases Related to Age Given the heterogeneity of this pattern and the frequent presence of multiple comorbidities, recognizing emotionally dysregulated personalities in older adults is not easy. Significant age-­related differences in the expression of dysregulated personality symptoms have been identified. For example, suicidal and self-­harming actions and unstable/intense relationships are less common in later life, while other traits (e.g., impulsivity, labile and difficult-­to-­control emotions, identity instability, and feelings of an inner “void”) appear less frequently and with less intensity than in earlier life stages, making diagnosis more difficult (Videler, Hutsebaut, Schulkens, Sobczak, & van Alphen, 2019; Winsper, 2021). Emotionally Dysregulated Personalities in Older Adults A persistent inability to regulate emotional responses to environmental stressors remains a core feature of emotionally dysregulated older adults (Stevenson et al., 2003). The behavioral expressions of this dysregulation may change with age, however (Powers, Gleason, & Oltmanns, 2013). For example, instead of self-harm and suicidal gestures in response to anxiety and feelings of emptiness, older adults may sabotage medical treatments, misuse substances, express intense and conflicted emotions, and suffer somatic syndromes, dissociative phenomena, or even slips into psychotic states (McMahon et al., 2019; Epstein, Fischer-­Elber, & Al-­Otaiba, 2007; Galione & ­Oltmanns, 2013; Landesman, 2003; Sadavoy, 1987).

chunks.heading: 950 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 950 Older Adults Expressions of feelings such as anger, fear, and shame may be less noticeable given the reduction in significant relationships in older age. But the intensity of emotion and the centrality of anger remain core characteristics of this personality style (Peckham et al., 2020; D’Agosti...
chunks.text: 950 Older Adults Expressions of feelings such as anger, fear, and shame may be less noticeable given the reduction in significant relationships in older age. But the intensity of emotion and the centrality of anger remain core characteristics of this personality style (Peckham et al., 2020; D’Agostino, Pepi, & Starcevic, 2022). Dissociative episodes may indicate a discontinuity of self and identity, accompanied by other primitive defenses and rigidities. The individual’s clinical history often includes marked reactivity to stressors and symptoms of mood disorders or psychotic episodes. Zanarini, Frankenburg, Hennen, and Silk (2003) report that only about one-­ fourth of individuals with borderline personality traits maintain the same features of the disorder 20 years later. The tumultuous and chaotic behavior linked to impulsivity and acting out tends to diminish in elderly individuals, possibly due to physical limitations rather than to a genuine change in personality. Other core traits, such as defensive styles, emotional responses to stress, feelings of emptiness, and fear of abandonment, remain stable throughout life. It is unclear whether changes in behavior represent actual improvement in the underlying condition. Older adults with emotionally dysregulated personalities often struggle with relationships, sometimes finding themselves socially isolated (Landesman, 2003). These personality features affect their responses to age-­related stressors and the aging process itself (Powers et al., 2013). A diagnosis of emotionally dysregulated personality at a younger age is associated with greater severity of physical, psychological, and cognitive symptoms in later life (Ruocco, Lam, & McMain, 2014; Salzbrenner et al., 2009). Previous chronic use of drugs, including alcohol, can increase the risk of developing neurocognitive disorders. Overall, emotionally dysregulated personalities in older adults are linked to a poor quality of life, poor response to medical or psychological treatments, increased use of psychotropic medications, more frequent psychiatric hospitalizations, and difficulty adjusting to transitions, such as moving to an assisted living or skilled nursing facility. Suggestions for Treatment As we have noted, the treatment of older adults with emotionally dysregulated personalities is less likely to involve symptoms such as self-­injury, suicidal gestures, or sexual acting out, which are more common in younger individuals (Kasho et al., 2019). Instead, older adults with dysregulated personalities tend to report feelings of emptiness and vague discomfort during treatment, along with difficulties related to identity—­possibly exacerbated by vulnerabilities associated with aging. Dependency needs may be particularly intense, especially when compounded by the social isolation that often results from a lifetime of chaotic and unsatisfactory relationships. It may be helpful to enlist a treatment team to provide tangible resources and services for socially isolated older individuals with emotionally dysregulated personalities, addressing both their emotional and practical needs. chunks.heading: 960 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 960 Older Adults fear, which reinforce each other. Clinicians should help relatives understand that it is ineffective to challenge the patient’s delusional beliefs. This simple intervention often reduces pressure on the patient, potentially leading to some reduction in symptoms or better adaptation...
chunks.text: 960 Older Adults fear, which reinforce each other. Clinicians should help relatives understand that it is ineffective to challenge the patient’s delusional beliefs. This simple intervention often reduces pressure on the patient, potentially leading to some reduction in symptoms or better adaptation to the disorder. Clinical Illustration An 83-year-old married woman, who had previously enjoyed considerable business success, developed a delusional disorder associated with her husband’s illness and aging, and their declining functional abilities. When her husband became bedridden and required home nursing care, her symptoms worsened. Although her general cognitive functioning remained intact, her delusional beliefs about cleanliness and hygiene led her to interfere with her husband’s medical care, reinforcing her sense of being different and being victimized by others. When a geriatric psychiatrist was consulted, she was initially reluctant to engage in treatment. Over time, however, she established a good relationship with the doctor and eventually responded fairly well to psychotherapy and antipsychotic medication. SOA2 Mood Disorders SOA2.1 Depressive Disorders With the exception of premenstrual dysphoric disorder, all depressive disorder diagnoses (for a general description, see Chapter 14, pp. 760–874) can be applied to older adults. A useful principle is that most individuals who suffer a psychological disorder in old age, particularly depression, are experiencing a recurrence of a condition that began earlier in life (Gallo & Lebowitz, 1999). Meta-­analytic data indicate that the average prevalence of depression among older adults is 31.74%, with higher rates observed in developing countries (40.78%) compared to developed countries (17.05%) (Zenebe, Akele, Selassie, & Necho, 2021). Depressive disorders account for nearly half the mental health admissions of older adults, with those identifying as female more often affected. Women tend to experience more depressive episodes than men over their lifespan (Gallo & Lebowitz, 1999). Depression in late life often coexists with alcohol use disorders and cognitive symptoms, particularly those related to memory and concentration (Lockwood, Alexopoulos, Kakuma, & van Gorp, 2000). Distinguishing between depression and dementia in older adults is a critical but challenging task (Kring, Davison, Neale, & Johnson, 2007). Kiloh (1961) introduced the term “pseudodementia” to describe cases in which cognitive impairment appears to resolve with the treatment of depression. More recent terms, such as “depression-­ related cognitive dysfunction” and “dementia syndrome of depression” have been proposed (Nagar, Boazak, & Hermida, 2021). Approximately 20% of older adults with depression show cognitive deficits justifying such labels (LaRue, D’Elia, Clark, Spar, & Jarvik, 1986; Storandt & VandenBos, 1994). In some cases, even neuropsychological tests may fail to clearly distinguish between neurocognitive impairment and depression (Swainson et al., 2001). Yet treatment of their depression often produces significant improvements in the cognitive abilities of elderly adults (LaRue, 1992). Depression and neurocognitive disorders can coexist. An older adult’s awareness of progressive cognitive decline may exacerbate depressive symptoms, while depression in

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 961
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 961 middle age increases the risk of developing Alzheimer’s disease later in life (Rosenblatt, Mehta, Romanoski, Eaton, & Lyketsos, 2003; Teri & Reifler, 1987; Sáiz-Vázquez et al., 2021). Depression is also a predictor of worse outcomes in those with card...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 961 middle age increases the risk of developing Alzheimer’s disease later in life (Rosenblatt, Mehta, Romanoski, Eaton, & Lyketsos, 2003; Teri & Reifler, 1987; Sáiz-Vázquez et al., 2021). Depression is also a predictor of worse outcomes in those with cardiovascular disease (Camus, Kraehenbuhl, Preisig, Bula, & Waeber, 2004). Depression at any age is a major risk factor for suicide. Among those over age 65, the suicide rate is three times higher than that in younger adults; their attempts are rarely unsuccessful (Butler, Lewis, & Sunderland, 1998). Older adults may also engage in “passive” suicidal behaviors, such as neglecting their medication or diet, or engaging in risky behaviors (for a brief overview of suicide risk assessment, see Chapter 14, pp. 760–874). The causes of depression in older adults generally mirror those in younger individuals, but certain issues become more prominent in late life. Stroke and other vascular diseases are common in those experiencing a first episode of late-life depression. Other causes of depression in older adults include the following: • Medical illnesses: Age-­related disorders increase vulnerability to depression and are frequently associated with it (Marengo & Westermeyer, 1996). • Physical disabilities: Conditions such as walking difficulties (Gallo & Lebowitz, 1999) or hearing loss (Cosh, Helme, Delcourt, Robins, & Tully, 2019). • Widowhood and other losses: Major life changes such as the loss of a spouse can trigger depression (Wilcox et al., 2003; Holm, Berland, & Severinsson, 2019). • Social isolation: The actual condition of not having relationships, contact with, or support from others. • Retirement: If accompanied by financial hardship or a loss of a sense of purpose, retirement can lead to depression (Dang, Ananthasubramaniam, & Mezuk, 2022). Several protective factors can shield older adults from the potential depressive effects of such events. Kring et al. (2007) found that older adults practice “social selectivity”: they prioritize fewer but more satisfying relationships. If retirement does not reduce income significantly, it may offer opportunities for personal enrichment, such as pursuing hobbies, gaining free time, or learning new skills. Kim and Moen (2001) posit that high self-­efficacy, self-­esteem, education, and supportive marital relationships protect individuals from depression postretirement. Thus, “adaptation rather than depression is the common reaction to loss and stress in late life” (Kring et al., 2007, p. 516). Some authors (e.g., Sheikh, 1996) recommend paying attention to the specific ways depression is expressed. Older adults may focus on physical symptoms (e.g., disrupted sleep–wake cycles, appetite changes, constipation, psychomotor agitation, or retardation) or on interpersonal and financial difficulties. Physical problems may mask persistent depression or dysthymia, which may be overlooked when more obvious symptoms are lacking, as in major depressive disorder. Older patients may be reluctant to discuss depressive thoughts and may resist medications, hospitalization, or changes to their routines. In such cases, the primary care physician, social workers, or family members may need to help identify and address the underlying depressive symptoms. In older adults with depressive disorders, self-­esteem problems are less frequent than in younger individuals. Feelings of emptiness, hopelessness, and helplessness

chunks.heading: 962 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 962 Older Adults about the future often dominate. Many older adults describe situations that evoke feelings of uselessness, stemming from normal age-­related declines in concentration, movement, and reaction time, which may be experienced with frustration or shame. Clinicians should assess the gener...
chunks.text: 962 Older Adults about the future often dominate. Many older adults describe situations that evoke feelings of uselessness, stemming from normal age-­related declines in concentration, movement, and reaction time, which may be experienced with frustration or shame. Clinicians should assess the general environmental conditions of an older adult with depression. In some cases, the condition may arise from caregiving that is negligent, overinvolved, or micromanaged. Stress and conflict with adult children or the witnessing of family disputes—­especially over inheritance—­can also trigger depressive episodes. Subjective Experience of Depressive Disorders in Older Adults Physical, psychological, and cognitive changes, as well as changes in an individual’s environment, can trigger depressive reactions at any age. Each change has the potential to create stress between the affected person and the altered situation. This is particularly true for older adults, where events such as loss, role changes within the family and society, and reduced physical resources compound age-­related frailty. As Sheikh (1996) has noted, “Depression may be a by-­product of a person’s inability to manage these changes” (p. 6). Affective States Ineffective coping strategies in response to age-­related changes or painful life events can intensify negative feelings and lead to maladaptive reactions. These may occur even with mild age-­related impairments, such as slight declines in attention, concentration, and memory. In older adults, depressive reactions may be accompanied by irritability and agitation. Perceived loss of efficiency and control may evoke suspicions or feelings of being exploited. Paranoid worries (e.g., “People are following me”) may develop as a defense against the unbearable passivity associated with diminished physical resources and autonomy. Interventions that promote acceptance of the aging process can often resolve or reduce paranoid ideation. For individuals with a history of depression, the challenges of aging can trigger new depressive episodes. A comprehensive assessment should explore feelings and beliefs about aging, disability, illness, and death. Cultural factors influence these views, shaping both concerns and attitudes toward seeking psychological and practical help. Cognitive Patterns Even in the absence of neurocognitive disorders, depression can slow cognitive processes, impairing memory, concentration, and processing of new information. As we have noted, distinguishing dementia from depression is critical but challenging, as complaints about memory are common in both normal aging and depression. Many depressed older adults complain about memory difficulties, even when no performance deficits are detectable (see, e.g., Storandt & VandenBos, 1997). Memory complaints tend to decrease as depressive symptoms improve. Conversely, individuals with genuine neurocognitive disorders (e.g., Alzheimer’s disease or vascular dementia) may overestimate their memory abilities, despite patent deficits in memory performance. Assessment of their cognitive abilities and differentiation between depressive and neurocognitive disorders are essential for determining the current and future care needs of older adults. They often struggle with instrumental activities of daily living

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 963
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 963 (e.g., managing money, shopping, cooking) and basic self-care (bathing, eating). As their depressive symptoms alleviate, however, their ability to cope typically improves. In dementia, in contrast, such deficits tend to worsen and require continued su...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 963 (e.g., managing money, shopping, cooking) and basic self-care (bathing, eating). As their depressive symptoms alleviate, however, their ability to cope typically improves. In dementia, in contrast, such deficits tend to worsen and require continued support.
