Somatic States Certain illnesses (e.g., cancer, thyroid hypofunction) produce symptoms that can mimic depression. Conversely, depression can lead to physical issues such as malnutrition, medication overdose, or addiction (e.g., to antidepressants, tranquilizers, alcohol). Depressed older adults consequently require a thorough medical evaluation to identify and address any comorbid physical conditions and to assess their medications and dosages. Physical illness is the most common trigger of depression in older adults. The severity and duration of depression often reflect the characteristics of the disease, which may cause impairments such as immobility, incontinence, or sexual dysfunction—­factors that can further exacerbate depression. Managing both somatic illness and concurrent depression requires support for older adults’ cognitive and emotional capacities, and attention to their quality of life. For many, even nonserious illnesses evoke dread of impending death. The attitudes of people in the individual’s social and cultural environment can either bolster resilience or become an obstacle to managing the symptoms of both physical illness and depression. Relational Patterns Isolation and detachment from friends and social activities are common in depression. Older adults may be reluctant to let clinicians contact family members or friends, yet these people can be valuable informants, especially if cognitive function is impaired. Therapists may feel frustrated or uncertain about the diagnosis, especially when trying to distinguish between depression and adjustment disorders. Interviews with the patient’s spouse or adult children can help identify the causes and precipitants of depression, highlight comorbid conditions, and provide insight into the patient’s diagnosis and history of treatment. Tools for Assessment For differential diagnosis between dementia and depression in older adults, Storandt and VandenBos (1997) provide a thorough guide, with detailed psychometric data from the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) and the Geriatric Depression Scale (GDS; Krishnamoorthy, Rajaa, & Rehman, 2020), They review the strengths and limitations of other tools as well (see also Korczyn & Halperin, 2009; Dias, Barbosa, Kuang, & Teixeira, 2020). Suggestions for Treatment The scholarly literature on late-life depression includes a wide range of treatment approaches, including pharmacotherapy, psychotherapy, and psychosocial interventions. There is currently no professional consensus on the most effective treatments, particularly pharmacological interventions (see Kring et al., 2007; Sheikh, 1996). But one nearly universal conclusion is that while aging brings many physical and

chunks.heading: 964 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 964 Older Adults psychological challenges, depression is not a normal consequence of growing old (Sheikh, 1996). Clinical Illustration Maura’s son contacted a psychologist about his 76-year-old mother’s behavior following her diagnosis of breast cancer, which required surgery and chemotherapy. Maura...
chunks.text: 964 Older Adults psychological challenges, depression is not a normal consequence of growing old (Sheikh, 1996). Clinical Illustration Maura’s son contacted a psychologist about his 76-year-old mother’s behavior following her diagnosis of breast cancer, which required surgery and chemotherapy. Maura, who lived with her husband, had become increasingly reclusive, developed phobias, such as a fear of falling, and was eating less and losing weight. She struggled with sleep, cried often, and frequently called her son for help with simple tasks, expressing concerns about her memory. Her son described her as markedly different from her former self. The psychologist recommended a neurological evaluation to rule out cognitive decline. The neurologist ruled out neurological issues and diagnosed Maura with major depressive disorder with anxiety, secondary to her cancer. A course of psychological support was initiated. SOA2.2 Cyclothymic Disorder Although less common than bipolar disorder, cyclothymic disorder may be found in older adults. It involves persistent mood fluctuations that are not severe or consistent enough to be classified as bipolar disorder, yet differ from the normal recurrence of sadness and relief often seen in old age. For older adults, the subjective experience of cyclothymic disorder can be particularly distressing. The mood variations may heighten fears of losing self-­control and mental stability. Cyclothymic disorder can significantly affect behavior and social relationships. It can evoke countertransferences similar to those induced by patients with bipolar disorder. SOA2.3 Bipolar Disorders Bipolar disorders typically emerge in young adulthood but can persist into old age, where the clinical presentation is similar to that of younger adults. Mania is often less severe, however, and cognitive impairment is more likely (for further discussion of diagnosis, see Chapter 14, pp. 760–874). Generally, bipolar disorder in older adults evolves into a more complex illness, characterized by somatic comorbidities and polypharmacy issues that may contribute to cognitive decline. Mania can appear for the first time in people over 50, in which case, they are diagnosed with “late-onset bipolar disorder.” This condition is more often associated with medical or neurological conditions, or more often triggered by medications, than early-onset bipolar disorder. Individuals with late-onset bipolar disorder tend to have better premorbid functioning and less family history of bipolar disorder than those with early onset. Previously, late-onset manic episodes tied to neurological conditions or medications were referred to as “secondary mania.” Newer theories suggest that late-onset bipolar disorder may be the expression of a long-­standing brain condition, potentially a multisystem inflammatory disease. Late-onset mania might thus be considered a form of “neuroprogression,” triggered by medical or neurological conditions in susceptible individuals. As with many geriatric conditions, there is limited research on which to base definitive conclusions.

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 965
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 965 Most epidemiological studies have focused on psychiatric inpatients, where bipolar disorder accounts for 8–10% of late-life psychiatric admissions. The overall prevalence of late-life mania is estimated at 6%. On average, individuals with bipolar diso...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 965 Most epidemiological studies have focused on psychiatric inpatients, where bipolar disorder accounts for 8–10% of late-life psychiatric admissions. The overall prevalence of late-life mania is estimated at 6%. On average, individuals with bipolar disorder have a reduced life expectancy due to cardiovascular disease, cancer, suicide, and lifestyle factors (e.g., smoking, poor diet, substance abuse). But older bipolar patients tend to report fewer unmet needs compared to those with depression, schizophrenia, or dementia. Subjective Experience of Bipolar Disorders in Older Adults Affective States Affective states correspond to the mania, hypomania, or depression observed in other age groups. Older adults with bipolar disorder tend to be more passive than healthy peers. Cognitive Patterns Cross-­sectional studies show an association between bipolar disorder and cognitive impairments (e.g., in attention, working memory, executive function, verbal memory, and processing speed). These have a significant impact on disability. We need longitudinal studies to determine whether the cognitive impairments are causes or effects of bipolar disorder. Somatic States Older adults with bipolar disorder often have multiple chronic medical conditions, including metabolic syndromes, which can be precipitated by long-term use of psychotropic medications. Consequently, it is difficult to determine whether medical problems are inherent to bipolar disorder in older adults or result from prolonged use of psychotropic medication. Relational Patterns As with most serious mental health conditions, bipolar disorder causes disruptions in significant relationships. Its episodic, cyclical nature can be stressful for both affected persons and their loved ones. There is often a sense of “waiting for the other shoe to drop,” anticipating the next manic or depressive episode that may disrupt life and functioning. Suggestions for Treatment In older adults, manic or hypomanic symptoms may defend against deep fears of experiencing depressive feelings. Age-­related physical, psychological, and cognitive changes can exacerbate anxiety, which may, in turn, worsen manic defenses. Treatment requires clinicians to understand the underlying dynamics of bipolar disorder in older patients and work through these primitive defenses. The goal is to help them remain connected to their emotions and to reality, while fostering more adaptive attitudes and behaviors.

chunks.heading: 966 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 966 Older Adults Clinical Illustration A 74-year-old married woman with a history of bipolar disorder had been relatively stable and high-­functioning for 20 years. After developing a serious respiratory illness that required intensive care, she had a slow and difficult recovery, followed by mild de...
chunks.text: 966 Older Adults Clinical Illustration A 74-year-old married woman with a history of bipolar disorder had been relatively stable and high-­functioning for 20 years. After developing a serious respiratory illness that required intensive care, she had a slow and difficult recovery, followed by mild depression. A year later, she developed hypomanic symptoms: racing thoughts, preoccupation with early life events, paranoia, and mild insomnia. In spite of psychotherapy and medication, her symptoms worsened. She was eventually hospitalized for psychiatric treatment, which gradually resolved her depression after she addressed chronic marital difficulties and made changes that improved her quality of life. SOA3 Disorders Related Primarily to Anxiety SOA3.1 Anxiety Disorders Converging evidence suggests that anxiety disorders in older adults are experienced and expressed in many different ways (Bryant et al., 2013; Welzel et al., 2021). They are strongly affected by medical comorbidity, cognitive decline, and age-­related physiological and psychological changes. A distinctive aspect of late-life anxiety is its high comorbidity with depression. The diagnosis of mixed anxiety–­depression (or, in DSM5-TR, depressive disorder “with anxious distress”) may be particularly apt in these cases. Older adults with anxiety may also have symptoms that are not assessed or may describe their symptoms differently. Their anxiety symptoms often overlap with medical conditions (e.g., hyperthyroidism), and they may express anxiety through somatic complaints such as pain, making it difficult to distinguish between psychological and physical symptoms (Wolitzky-­Taylor et al., 2010). When a medical illness is present, anxiety can exacerbate functional decline, leading to increased use of health services. Comorbid cognitive impairment can complicate matters: The relationship between anxiety and dementia is complex. Anxiety symptoms and cognitive difficulties can appear concurrently, with anxiety potentially contributing to cognitive decline. Alternatively, anxiety may arise as a reaction to worsening cognitive difficulties. If anxiety symptoms precede cognitive decline, the patient may be diagnosed with a separate anxiety disorder, or the anxiety may be seen as prodromal of dementia (Kogan, Edelstein, & McKee, 2008). The DSM-5-TR criterion of impairment in work or social relationships may not be evident in retired or socially isolated older adults, who find it easier to avoid anxiety-­provoking situations. Social stereotypes about aging make it more acceptable for older people to withdraw from social activities (Lenze & Loebach Wetherell, 2011). Late-life anxiety disorders are underrecognized and underestimated by both clinicians and researchers. Diagnosing anxiety in older adults is challenging because they tend to underreport psychological symptoms and overreport somatic ones. The estimated prevalence of anxiety disorders in adults over 65 ranges from 1.5 to 15% in community samples and 1 to 28% in clinical samples (Balsamo, Cataldi, Carlucci, & Fairfield, 2018). Although anxiety disorders are slightly more common in younger adults, they remain prevalent in older individuals (Gum, King-­Kallimanis, & Kohn, 2009). Anxiety about aging tends to be higher in females, possibly influenced by societal stereotypes about beauty and youth (especially in Western cultures). Other social factors such as chronic medical conditions, education level, socioeconomic status, and physical limitations also influence the experience of aging.

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 967
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 967 Some evidence suggests that anxiety prevalence and severity vary across subgroups within the elderly population, decreasing from middle to old age, and then increasing again after age 80. Anxiety disorders in older adults are often linked to traumatic...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 967 Some evidence suggests that anxiety prevalence and severity vary across subgroups within the elderly population, decreasing from middle to old age, and then increasing again after age 80. Anxiety disorders in older adults are often linked to traumatic events, such as acute physical illness, falls, or muggings. Anxiety symptoms in elderly patients are more common among those with a history of any affective disorder. Age of onset is a significant factor in the expression and severity of symptoms and in responsiveness to treatment (Bryant, Jackson, & Ames, 2008). Late-onset generalized anxiety disorder is associated with less symptom severity, higher rates of comorbid mood and other psychiatric disorders, and worse health-­related quality of life. Late-onset panic disorder tends to involve fewer distressing panic attacks than early-onset panic disorder (Le Roux, Gatz, & Wetherell, 2005). Those who have experienced panic attacks or phobias earlier in life, especially if inadequately treated, often report a recurrence of these symptoms in later life. Lateonset panic disorder occurs relatively infrequently and is characterized by “fewer panic symptoms, less avoidance, and lower scores on somatization measures compared to early-onset panic disorder” (Sheikh, 1996, p. 80). In older adults, it can be difficult to determine whether avoidance behaviors are due to a phobia or physical impairments, as phobias and somatic issues are often mutually reinforcing. From a psychodynamic perspective, anxiety serves as an alerting mechanism that arises when unconscious motives conflict with conscious constraints. Older individuals may be more vulnerable to specific forms of anxiety, such as loneliness, separation, and grief. Sources of anxiety and exposure to (or impact of) risk factors vary with age (Beekman, 1998). Life changes associated with aging, such as losses in mental abilities, physical competence, relationships, and independence, along with thoughts about the approach of death, increase the likelihood of anxiety disorders in late life. Older adults tend to experience anxiety as agitation, hoarding, dread of impending doom, fear of falling, and health-­related worries, whereas younger patients report concerns about work, physical appearance, and general apprehension. Fear of falling and worries about dying are common forms of anxiety in older adults, often intensified by feelings of uncontrollability (Gagnon, Flint, Naglie, & Devins, 2005). They also suffer from separation anxiety, expressed as concern about loved ones’ health and safety, and avoidance of being alone at home (Wijeratne & Manicavasagar, 2003). Late-life anxiety can be triggered by reminders of past trauma or by intense feelings of frustration related to unwanted changes. Older adults with anxiety disorders are more likely than younger ones to reduce their activity levels, leading to a diminished sense of accomplishment. They may focus on minor worries to avoid confronting more significant concerns. Age-­related stereotypes can lead to avoidance of previously enjoyed activities, as older adults may view the loss of these activities as a natural part of aging rather than as a consequence of their anxiety (Diefenbach, Stanley, & Beck, 2001). The ability to adapt to changing circumstances across the lifespan has been found to reduce anxiety in later life by mitigating the effects of aging stereotypes (Lynch, 2000). Old age is marked by stresses such as bereavement, health decline, and disability, which can make older people more vulnerable to emotional disturbances. Bereavement is a significant cause of loneliness and distress, as it often requires changes in relationships and coping strategies. Death anxiety must be included in any discussion of anxiety in older adults, and not only because of the proximity of death in old age. Menzies and Menzies (2023) regard death anxiety as a transdiagnostic variable that contributes to many mental

chunks.heading: 968 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 968 Older Adults health conditions. Ernest Becker’s (1973) seminal exploration of death anxiety as the primary human terror inspired confirmatory studies that led to the development of Terror Management Theory (TMT). According to TMT, humans create “cultural world views”—shared systems of values and...
chunks.text: 968 Older Adults health conditions. Ernest Becker’s (1973) seminal exploration of death anxiety as the primary human terror inspired confirmatory studies that led to the development of Terror Management Theory (TMT). According to TMT, humans create “cultural world views”—shared systems of values and beliefs—­to give life meaning and purpose, which ward off death anxiety. “Self-­esteem,” the sense of meaning and purpose, is seen as the primary defense against death anxiety. When self-­esteem is challenged by reminders of mortality, “mortality salience” increases, triggering two kinds of defenses against death anxiety: “proximal” defenses (conscious strategies; e.g., intellectualization and rationalization) and “distal” defenses (which operate subconsciously to protect self-­esteem, keeping death anxiety out of awareness). It is interesting that although we might expect proximity to death in old age to increase death anxiety, research shows that anxiety about death tends to decrease with age (Rasmussen & Brems, 1996). Older adults with “existential maturity” tend to be more accepting of death and to feel less death-­related anxiety (Emanuel, Reddy, Hauser, & Sonnefeld, 2017). As we have noted, older people tend to minimize psychiatric symptoms and emphasize somatic complaints. Their symptoms can be distressing and disabling, and are sometimes misdiagnosed or treated inappropriately. A clinician’s first task should be to assess whether the patient’s current level of functioning warrants a diagnosis of anxiety. Then the clinician should take a thorough psychological history, identifying any medical conditions or medications that could mimic anxiety symptoms. To tailor the treatment appropriately, the clinician should explore the specific content of the patient’s worries. Subjective Experience of Anxiety Disorders in Older Adults Affective States Older adults with anxiety often report decreased life satisfaction and increased loneliness. They may not always acknowledge feeling depressed. While depression without sadness is sometimes considered characteristic of older adults, depression is not a normal part of aging. Compared to younger individuals, older adults experience anxiety more as agitation and a sense of impending doom. Some evidence suggests that older adults experience fewer negative emotional states and a lower level of negative affect (e.g., depression, anxiety, guilt, hostility, and shame) relative to younger individuals. Cognitive Patterns Cognitive patterns in older adults with anxiety include irrational worries, fear of catastrophic events, memory problems, negative perceptions of health, and fear of physical symptoms. Elderly individuals may cope with death anxiety by mentally immobilizing time, creating a monotonous routine to prevent new developments or changes (Quinodoz, 2009). Cognitive patterns in late life tend to reflect an external locus of control. Somatic States In later life, anxiety is often expressed through somatic symptoms, such as motor tension and a reduced ability to perform daily activities. Older adults tend to report other

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 969
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 969 issues more frequently, such as pain and sleep disturbances. Other prominent physical symptoms of anxiety include sweating, restlessness, muscle tension, pacing, tachycardia, poor concentration, fatigue, dizziness, paresthesia, dry mouth, gastrointest...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 969 issues more frequently, such as pain and sleep disturbances. Other prominent physical symptoms of anxiety include sweating, restlessness, muscle tension, pacing, tachycardia, poor concentration, fatigue, dizziness, paresthesia, dry mouth, gastrointestinal disturbances, facial grimacing and flushing, palpitations, tremor, and general body aches and pains. Relational Patterns Older adults with anxiety may become overly dependent on family and caregivers, creating additional burdens and conflicts. To alleviate separation anxiety, they may become overly warm and nurturant, sometimes to the point of intrusiveness. Anxiety symptoms often require family members to be responsive, leading to feelings of resentment or guilt. Family conflicts can arise, particularly when making decisions about whether an elderly relative should move to a care facility. The therapist’s experience of treating an older adult with anxiety varies depending on whether the anxiety is linked to aging-­related issues or is more free-­floating. Anxiety tied to specific life changes may be more responsive to reassurance and supportive interventions, while more generalized anxiety often requires pharmacological support. Tools for Assessment Few anxiety assessment tools are specifically designed for older adults. The Adult Manifest Anxiety Scale—­Elderly Version (Lowe & Reynolds, 2006) includes subscales for worry/stress, fear of aging, and physiological symptoms. Other assessment tools include the Geriatric Anxiety Scale (GAS; Segal, June, Payne, Coolidge, & Yochim, 2010) and the Geriatric Anxiety Inventory (GAI; Byrne & Pachana, 2011). Clinical Illustrations A 72-year-old woman, recently widowed and now living alone, was a reserved person who struggled to express her emotions despite her evident grief. She described herself as having been an anxious child, often returning home soon after being dropped off at school by her mother. In her early 20s, she had panic attacks when leaving for college but never sought treatment. At age 56, after one of her three daughters was involved in a car accident, she began to experience constant and severe ruminations about the safety of her other daughters and grandchildren, whom she contacted daily. She reported significant physical symptoms of anxiety, such as palpitations and abdominal discomfort, especially after family visits ended. Another patient, a 69-year-old married man, did not recall being particularly anxious as a child, though he occasionally experienced mild dread about attending school or being left alone at home. At age 53, after suffering heart attacks, he began worrying excessively about his health. He described himself as a chronic worrier, suffering physical symptoms of anxiety and catastrophic thoughts whenever he had to leave the house or when his wife was away. His fear of falling while outside led him to avoid going out unless accompanied by his wife. This avoidance further contributed to his feelings of isolation and loneliness.

chunks.heading: 970 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 970 Older Adults SOA4 Obsessive–Compulsive and Related Disorders ICD-11 has introduced a new grouping for “Obsessive–­Compulsive and Related Disorders,” aligning with the DSM-5 and DSM-5-TR classifications. We organize topics here in compliance with this “promotion” of obsessive–­compulsive disorder...
chunks.text: 970 Older Adults SOA4 Obsessive–Compulsive and Related Disorders ICD-11 has introduced a new grouping for “Obsessive–­Compulsive and Related Disorders,” aligning with the DSM-5 and DSM-5-TR classifications. We organize topics here in compliance with this “promotion” of obsessive–­compulsive disorder from one of the anxiety disorders to its own group (For further discussion of diagnosis see Chapter 14, pp. 760–874). This category now includes obsessive–­compulsive disorder, body dysmorphic disorder, hoarding disorder, trichotillomania, excoriation (skin-­picking) disorder, substance/medication-­induced obsessive–­compulsive and related disorders, obsessive–­compulsive and related disorders due to another medical condition, and other specified or unspecified obsessive–­compulsive and related disorders. Anxiety and mood disorders often coexist with these conditions. Some who suffer them also have an obsessive–­compulsive personality style or disorder. Hoarding disorder is frequently a symptom of more serious psychiatric conditions such as major depression and schizophrenia. There is very little research on obsessive–­compulsive and related disorders in older adults. If left untreated, they tend to become chronic. Even if they appear to remit, they may recur more frequently and for longer duration, often triggered by stressful events. Age-­related losses (retirement, bereavement, separation from children, or loss of a family home) and neurocognitive impairments can exacerbate obsessive–­compulsive pathology, especially if the disorder was untreated in earlier life stages. Older people with obsessive–­compulsive disorder report its typical preoccupations: concerns about cleanliness and contamination, control over taboo or forbidden thoughts, and fears of causing harm to oneself or others. Severity, expression, and internal experiences of these symptoms can vary, depending on prior clinical history and treatment. Biopsychosocial factors related to aging can exacerbate the disorder. The limited research we do have suggests that if obsessive–­compulsive disorder is not treated at its first occurrence or within the early years of its full manifestation, it tends to become chronic. SOA4.1 Obsessive–Compulsive Disorder Onset of obsessive–­ compulsive disorder after age 35 is uncommon (Fernandes, Vilaverde, Freitas, Pereira, & Morgado, 2021), and onset in old age is virtually unheard of (Ayers, 2010; Carmin, 2002; Kumar, 2000). But in older adults who were previously susceptible to obsessive and compulsive symptoms, stressful events can trigger fluctuating preoccupations. Without treatment, recovery rates in adults, especially the elderly, are low. Limited research on obsessive and compulsive symptoms in older adults suggests that a history of negative emotionality and behavioral inhibition are risk factors, while stressful and traumatic events can exacerbate the disorder. A first-­ degree family relationship with someone with obsessive–­compulsive disorder increases the likelihood of developing it. Integrated pharmacological and psychological therapies can improve the quality of life in older adults with this problem, though the rates of complete symptom remission are generally low (Colvin, 1997; Bourin, 2003). SOA4.2 Body Dysmorphic Disorder Older adults with a history of body dysmorphic disorder may continue to focus obsessively on their bodies. The unavoidable aesthetic changes that come with aging often

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 971
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 971 amplify these concerns. In some higher-­functioning individuals, the disorder may lessen or diversify; ruminations about the body and criticism of perceived defects may diminish in frequency or severity. Yet other obsessive priorities may emerge, or d...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 971 amplify these concerns. In some higher-­functioning individuals, the disorder may lessen or diversify; ruminations about the body and criticism of perceived defects may diminish in frequency or severity. Yet other obsessive priorities may emerge, or depressive symptoms may partially replace the overinvestment in body image. Body dysmorphic disorder tends to persist in elderly individuals with a narcissistic personality structure. Like younger adults with this condition (see Chapter 14, pp. 760–874), elderly individuals may seek cosmetic surgery. It can be difficult to differentiate people with body dysmorphic disorder, who seek such treatment because of a profoundly distorted sense of their bodies, from people who want simply to look better, and from those who seek it for compelling, dynamic reasons rooted in personality and exacerbated by the changes brought by aging. Countertransferences of therapists treating an older adult with body dysmorphic disorder are comparable those of therapists treating a similarly afflicted younger person. SOA4.3 Hoarding Disorder Hoarding disorder is characterized by a persistent difficulty in parting with possessions, regardless of their actual value. It differs fundamentally from normal collecting, which involves specific objects or categories (see also Chapter 14, pp. 760–874). Hoarding disorder typically begins between ages 20 and 30 and, if left untreated, worsens in old age, especially in response to age-­related stressful events (Ayers, 2010; Kim, Steketee, & Frost, 2001). In recent years, researchers have paid increasing attention to hoarding disorder in older adults, examining its social, political, and health care implications (Tinlin-­Dixon & Purvis, 2022). The consequences of pathological hoarding include the obstruction of living spaces (house, office, car) to a point where they cannot be used as intended, pose serious personal hygiene problems and create conflicts with neighbors. In older adults, the social impacts of hoarding disorder are often more significant than the health implications. Individuals living alone or with weak family ties are particularly vulnerable to hoarding. Social and psychiatric service professionals need to implement integrated interventions to help the patient remove hoarded materials. Because hoarding behaviors such as rummaging through garbage, stealing, or hiding small items are also common symptoms of Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson’s disease, Huntington’s disease, and frontotemporal dementia, differential diagnosis is critical. Clinical Illustration Loretta, a 65-year-old widow, had no children and lived alone in a small tenement flat. A few months after her husband’s death, Loretta’s neighbors complained to social services that bad odors were coming from her apartment and that she was failing to follow communal rules. She was often seen in shabby, smelly clothing, rummaging through garbage bins. When social workers approached her, she yelled at them, insisting she did not need help. When her behavior subsequently worsened, social service personnel entered her home forcibly. They found it full of plastic bags, pizza boxes, and discarded clothing from garbage bins. The smell was overwhelming. Loretta’s personal hygiene was appalling. Medical evaluation found no signs of cognitive impairment; although alert, Loretta showed thought disturbance, dysphoria, and paranoia.

chunks.heading: 972 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 972 Older Adults She was diagnosed with compulsive hoarding disorder, or Diogenes syndrome (“senior squalor syndrome”), in the context of a pathological personality pattern. Hospitalization in a specialized facility was recommended. SOA4.4 Trichotillomania and Excoriation Disorder DSM-5-TR identifie...
chunks.text: 972 Older Adults She was diagnosed with compulsive hoarding disorder, or Diogenes syndrome (“senior squalor syndrome”), in the context of a pathological personality pattern. Hospitalization in a specialized facility was recommended. SOA4.4 Trichotillomania and Excoriation Disorder DSM-5-TR identifies two disorders involving body-­ focused repetitive behaviors: trichotillomania (hair-­pulling disorder) and excoriation (skin-­picking) disorder. These are often preceded or accompanied by specific emotional states, such as boredom, anxiety, or a heightened sense of tension. Engaging in these behaviors produces temporary relief or satisfaction. The degree of awareness of them can vary. Currently, there is insufficient research to highlight differences between older adults and younger individuals affected by these conditions (Sutar, Rajan, & Siddiqui, 2022). Subjective Experience of Obsessive–Compulsive and Related Disorders in Older Adults Affective States The emotional states of older adults with obsessive–­compulsive or related disorders are similar to those of younger individuals. Anxiety is the dominant emotion, often coupled with impatience over the invasiveness of unwanted thoughts (Beekman, 1998). There may be unremitting self-­criticism over the inability to rid oneself of obsessive thoughts and fears, and occasionally social shame for being unable to change. Anxiety is often manifested as restlessness, which decreases only after carrying out compulsive acts aimed at controlling what is perceived as intolerable (e.g., untidiness) (Kirmizioglu, 2009). Older adults may show distinctive characteristics. Physical fragility can lead to a “relentless tenacity” in their fear of losing control. The intensity of control mechanisms may diminish or change as physical health declines, limiting the energy available to fuel compulsions (e.g., reduced physical agility may hinder ritualistic behaviors). Anxiety may arise in new ways that are more compatible with the individual’s changing physical, social, and economic circumstances, and depressive symptoms may emerge. The evolution of obsessive–­compulsive and related disorders in older adults is influenced by personality factors, family relationships, and environmental conditions. Cognitive Patterns The content of obsessions in older adults can change in response to personal life events and circumstances. Neurocognitive decline may influence the content of obsessions and compulsions. Specific neurocognitive disorders can significantly affect the cognitive patterns associated with obsessive–­compulsive and related disorders. Somatic States Somatic symptoms in obsessive–­compulsive and related disorders are typically linked to anxiety expressed in the musculoskeletal and neurovegetative systems. High levels of physical tension can activate obsessional concerns. Because the physical health

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 973
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 973 of older adults significantly influences anxiety-­related impairments, clinicians should evaluate this. Relational Patterns Obsessions and compulsions often lead to social withdrawal, particularly in situations that trigger obsessive thoughts, such as...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 973 of older adults significantly influences anxiety-­related impairments, clinicians should evaluate this. Relational Patterns Obsessions and compulsions often lead to social withdrawal, particularly in situations that trigger obsessive thoughts, such as fears of contamination or aggression. This isolation can aggravate the marginalization that often accompanies old age even in the absence of psychopathology. Older adults who need assistance may disclose their symptoms to family members or caregivers. Tensions may arise when the older adult, now in a less influential position, tries to impose personal obsessions and compulsions on the family (Cullen, 2008). In older adults, the subjective experiences of body dysmorphic disorder and hoarding disorder share more similarities than one sees in younger adults and adolescents. The key subjective issue is loss—­either the loss of beauty (in body dysmorphic disorder) or of possessions (in hoarding disorder). In body dysmorphic disorder, the painful perception of a deteriorating body can lead to obsessive and scrupulous monitoring, with attempts to regain some form of control. In hoarding disorder, the accumulation of objects serves as a defense against the feeling of loss; the objects themselves, through their sheer presence, create an illusion of power. The refusal to discard hoarded items often represents a form of defiance or strength, particularly toward family members, clinicians, or social workers. Common conditions in aging, for example, increased isolation and the impact of events such as retirement or bereavement, are shared features of both body dysmorphic disorder and hoarding disorder. Clinical Illustrations A 75-year-old retired hotel owner had never married and had no children. Despite suffering from obsessive–­compulsive disorder from a young age, he sought help from a psychologist only at age 45, after taking over the management of the family’s hotel and properties. He was tormented by fears that he might blurt out offensive remarks, accusing villagers or politicians of dishonesty, theft, or misuse of public funds. Although these individuals were friendly, his doubts persisted. He developed fears that he might have accidentally hit someone with his car. He would repeatedly check the rearview mirror and return to the scene to reassure himself that nothing bad had happened. At age 45, he underwent two years of psychotherapy, during which he refused pharmacological treatment for fear of side effects. At age 70, he returned to therapy in response to two new stressors: a diagnosis of skin cancer requiring minor surgery and the decision of his nephews to renovate the hotel without consulting him. Feeling excluded from their decisions and unable to control the situation, he resumed worrying about potentially offensive remarks and car accidents. And he developed new concerns that he might have inadvertently hurt his nephews’ feelings. The new treatment took several months, during which he gradually regained the insights he had gained during his previous therapy. A 72-year-old woman was referred to social services by her sisters, who were concerned about her neglect of her home and herself since retiring. Her apartment was in a state of disarray, with newspapers and books scattered everywhere, rotten or expired

chunks.heading: 974 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 974 Older Adults food left out, boxes of cookies in the bedroom, and clothes piled haphazardly. Initially, she approached therapy as an intellectual diversion, a stance that seemed to help her feel more comfortable in the therapeutic relationship. Over time, she expressed shame, acknowledged her lim...
chunks.text: 974 Older Adults food left out, boxes of cookies in the bedroom, and clothes piled haphazardly. Initially, she approached therapy as an intellectual diversion, a stance that seemed to help her feel more comfortable in the therapeutic relationship. Over time, she expressed shame, acknowledged her limitations, and accepted help from a psychiatrist and a home attendant. With their support, she managed to keep her house cleaner and more organized. In response to combined pharmacological and psychological treatment, her symptoms diminished and her quality of life improved. SOA5 Event‐ and Stressor‐Related Disorders DSM-5-TR’s classification of trauma- and stressor-­related disorders encompasses reactive attachment disorder, disinhibited social engagement disorder, posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD), acute stress disorder, adjustment disorders, and prolonged grief disorder. Before DSM-5, PTSD was categorized as an anxiety disorder. It has also been viewed as a dissociative, affective, or personality disorder. This issue dates back at least to Freud’s Introductory Lectures (1915–1917) and centers on a fundamental question: Why do some individuals develop mental health problems as a result of trauma, while others do not? This question applies to both physical and mental health conditions. Although there is no definitive answer, it has been explored in terms of personality factors that may increase vulnerability (Mills, 2008; Verhaeghe & Vanheule, 2005) and through efforts to identify positive, protective factors (i.e., resilience) in adults in later life (Pietrzak & Cook, 2013). Most of the relatively few studies on these disorders in older adults focus on PTSD, a condition that is often underestimated in elderly adults, as many do not seek care. There is also some literature on “complicated grief” in older adults, a kind of anguish that prompted the official diagnosis of “prolonged grief disorder” in the DSM-5-TR. SOA5.1 Trauma‐ and Stressor‐Related Disorders SOA5.1.1 Acute and Posttraumatic Stress Disorders For discussion of diagnosis of acute stress disorder, PTSD, and related concepts, see Chapter 14 (pp. 760–874). Acute stress disorder and PTSD in older adults are more complex than in younger individuals. One reason involves the amount of time since the traumatic event. It can be helpful to subcategorize PTSD in older adults as “de novo,” “chronic,” “delayed-­ onset,” or “complex” (Charles, Garand, Ducrocq, & Clément, 2005). Advanced age brings additional stressors, including declining health, fear of pain and disability, retirement, cognitive impairment, dependency, financial problems, relocation, elder abuse, neglect, and the loss of loved ones. These factors heighten sensitivity to trauma: Ostensibly, less severe stressors can have a significant impact. Some studies suggest that PTSD plays a role in the development of dementia (Yaffe et al., 2010; Meziab et al., 2014; Quereshi et al., 2010) and that its behavioral and biological manifestations can accelerate cellular aging, contributing to premature cardiometabolic health decline (Wolf & Schnurr, 2016). Medical diagnoses and treatments can themselves be traumatic for older adults (Moye & Rouse, 2015). The longer a person lives, the more opportunities there are for

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 975
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 975 new trauma. PTSD symptoms can emerge or reemerge late in life (e.g., the so-­called après coup effect), and cumulative trauma may also play a role (Cloitre et al., 2009). Alternatively, previous trauma may have an “inoculating” effect, enhancing subse...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 975 new trauma. PTSD symptoms can emerge or reemerge late in life (e.g., the so-­called après coup effect), and cumulative trauma may also play a role (Cloitre et al., 2009). Alternatively, previous trauma may have an “inoculating” effect, enhancing subsequent coping (Palgi, Gelkopf, & Berger, 2015). PTSD can impair an older adult’s ability to manage stress and navigate late life successfully (Weintraub & Ruskin, 1999). Symptom comorbidity is another challenge in this population, with certain aspects of older age (e.g., social withdrawal and memory impairment) potentially masking PTSD symptoms. Older adults may seek care for somatic complaints, which can obscure the underlying psychological causes. Konnert and Wong (2014) report age differences in the experiencing and processing of emotion, autobiographical memory, and combat experiences. A study by Wittekind et al. (2017) suggests, however, that in older adults, PTSD may not necessarily be associated with autobiographical memory impairment. There is evidence that the age at which trauma occurs (early vs. late life) can affect the development of PTSD symptoms (Böttche, Kuwert, & Knaevelsrud, 2012). Epidemiological studies indicate that 50–90% of older adults have been exposed to at least one potentially traumatic event (Forman-­Hoffman et al., 2012; Monson, Lonergan, Caron, & Brunet, 2016; Norris, 1992). Because of their impact across the life span, some studies suggest examining childhood adversities (e.g., neglect, abuse), which are known to result in poor mental and physical outcomes in older adults, including depressive symptoms, suicide attempts, sleep problems, and interpersonal difficulties (Lee et al., 2017; Sachs-­Ericsson, Rushing, Stanley, & Sheffler, 2016; Krammer, Kleim, Simmen-­Janevska, & Maercker, 2016; Poon & Knight, 2011). Gender differences in trauma exposure have also been noted, possibly resulting from differences in combat exposure. Creamer and Parslow (2008) found that approximately 70% of older men and only 41% of older women reported trauma exposure during their lifetime. Compared to the general population, older veterans have higher rates of both trauma exposure and PTSD symptoms. Intimate partner violence, however, a type of interpersonal trauma, is especially relevant for older women; middle-­aged and older women (ages 45–70) are more likely to have experienced it than younger women (Wilke & Vinton, 2005). Studies of PTSD in adults over 60 find prevalence from 1.5 to 4%, reaching up to 4.5% in ethnic/minority and nonminority groups (Pietrzak, Goldstein, Southwick, & Grant, 2011; Jimenez, Alegria, Chen, Chan, & Laderman, 2010; Acierno et al., 2007). A recent study in primary care settings in Québec reported a 6-month prevalence of 11%, while lifetime prevalence estimates of PTSD and partial PTSD are 4.5 and 5.5%, respectively (Lamoureux-­Lamarche et al., 2016; Pietrzak, Goldstein, Southwick, & Grant, 2012). Although many older adults do not meet full criteria for a PTSD diagnosis, subclinical PTSD symptoms are present in 7–15% of older adults (Glaesmer, Gunzelmann, Braehler, Forstmeier, & Maercker, 2010; Schnurr, Spiro, Vielhauer, Findler, & Hamblen, 2002; van Zelst, de Beurs, Beekman, Deeg, & van Dyck, 2003). These data may underestimate the actual prevalence of PTSD symptoms in later life given the scarcity of studies and the tendency of older adults to underreport emotional symptoms (Cook & Niederehe, 2007). The après coup effect, also known as Nachträglichkeit (deferred retroactive impact) may also affect older people (see Chapter 14, pp. 760–874). This is the phenomenon in which an experience that was not initially felt as traumatic takes on new

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 979
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 979 Losing someone with whom one had a close relationship can be particularly difficult if the bereaved person experienced a challenging upbringing or faces unusually stressful consequences after the death, such as inadequate social support, conflicts wit...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 979 Losing someone with whom one had a close relationship can be particularly difficult if the bereaved person experienced a challenging upbringing or faces unusually stressful consequences after the death, such as inadequate social support, conflicts with friends or relatives, or major financial problems. Complicated grief is associated with other health issues, such as sleep disturbances (which can contribute to other health problems), substance use disorders, suicidal ideation, and impairments in immune function. It poses an increased risk of cardiovascular disease and cancer (Buckley et al., 2012). It can also interfere with adherence to prescribed treatments for various medical conditions. Subjective Experience of Complicated Grief in Older Adults Affective States Feelings of sadness and longing for the deceased dominate the minds of older sufferers of complicated grief. Guilt is not uncommon, generally focused on regrets toward the lost person. Anxiety may also be present, often stemming from insecurity in life without the deceased. There may be rumination over troubling aspects of the death. ­A nhedonia and feelings of worthlessness are not typically part of the emotional experience. Cognitive Patterns Cognitive patterns are typically dominated by recurring thoughts, images, and memories of the deceased. Somatic States Somatic states include disturbed sleep, although nightmares are not commonly reported. Disturbed eating behaviors may also occur, such as avoiding certain foods or mealtimes that evoke memories of the deceased. Relational Patterns Relationships are often affected by the surviving person’s avoidance of individuals and situations that remind the mourner of the lost person. Clinical Illustration Four years after her husband’s death, which ended a long, successful marriage, a 78-year-old woman saw her primary physician for difficulty sleeping. She had taken to sleeping on a couch in her den, because she could not bear to be in the bed she had shared with her husband. She had given up many activities she used to enjoy, because they reminded her of him and made her miss him too much. She declined most social invitations and had become socially isolated. She frequently ruminated on how unfair it was that her husband had died, how she had not expected him to go before her, and how she wished she could “be with him.” She fluctuated among anger, sadness, and self-blame for not recognizing her husband’s illness earlier, though realistically, this would not have altered the outcome.

chunks.heading: 980 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 980 Older Adults SOA6 Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders Some authors (e.g., Frances, 2013), argue that the DSM category of somatic symptom and related disorders is overly broad and should be applied cautiously. This caution is especially pertinent to older adults, but additional considerations a...
chunks.text: 980 Older Adults SOA6 Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders Some authors (e.g., Frances, 2013), argue that the DSM category of somatic symptom and related disorders is overly broad and should be applied cautiously. This caution is especially pertinent to older adults, but additional considerations apply as well. Old age in itself is not a disease: What we call “successful aging” denotes an elderly person who remains active and socially engaged, and serves as a valued point of reference for friends and younger family members. But the aging process does involve coping with new stressors, including an increased likelihood of illness (McClintock Greenberg, 2009). The incidence of heart disease is 13.6% in adults ages 45–64, 26.8% in adults 65–74, and 36.6% in those 75 and older (U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, 2006). Older adults are more acutely aware of the finiteness of time and the inevitability of death. Many develop multiple somatic symptoms and chronic conditions. In industrialized societies, however, many individuals can live a long life with only minor medical issues. Individualized assessments are essential for this heterogeneous population. SOA6 Somatic Symptom Disorder In a rare large-scale cross-­sectional study of Chinese adults, Wu and colleagues (2022) found the prevalence of somatic symptom disorder to be higher in older than in younger individuals (63.2 vs. 45.3%). Older people were 1.56 times more likely to be diagnosed with the disorder, had more severe symptoms, and were almost four times as likely to have coexisting depressive or anxiety disorders. Older age, female gender, and changes in marital status were significant predictors of higher symptom rates in the elderly population. Frances (2013, p. 175) provides a diagnostic prototype for somatic symptom disorder: frequent doctor visits, resistance to realistic reassurance about health, and a life centered around health concerns. These behaviors can occur throughout adulthood but may increase in old age. Professionals and family members of older patients may react unhelpfully to these concerns. Medical personnel may underestimate an older person’s complaints, assume illness is inevitable with age, and/or fail to assess a patient’s health in depth, including mental health conditions (e.g., ruling out bipolar disorder, depression, anxiety, or psychosis). Or they may order unnecessary diagnostic evaluations and treatments without considering whether the person’s condition involves unrealistic health concerns. In either scenario, the true problem may remain unrecognized and untreated. Pain presents a special challenge in older adults. Regardless of the cause (e.g., arthritis, cancer) pain is usually accompanied by a degree of depression. Patients may feel that life is no longer worth living if it means enduring chronic pain (Sheikh, 1996). Untreated or poorly managed pain is a serious risk for older persons; doctors may fear overprescribing painkillers or may focus on the depressive reaction, mistakenly assuming it to be the cause of the pain. Proper pain management can improve mood, reduce anxiety, and enhance quality of life. Finally, clinicians should consider the cultural context in which somatic symptom disorder is expressed. Frances (2013, p. 177) notes that “different cultures have highly varying thresholds of acceptance of somatic symptoms as being normal and everyday versus being a sign of mental disorder.” This is especially true for older adults. Clinicians may be tempted to either underestimate or overestimate a patient’s symptoms based on the views expressed by the elderly person’s family and friends.

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 981
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 981 SOA6.2 Illness Anxiety Disorder (Hypochondriasis) Nussbaum (2013) offers critical criteria for differentiating somatic symptom disorder from illness anxiety disorder. When worry about ongoing somatic symptoms predominates, it is likely somatic symptom...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 981 SOA6.2 Illness Anxiety Disorder (Hypochondriasis) Nussbaum (2013) offers critical criteria for differentiating somatic symptom disorder from illness anxiety disorder. When worry about ongoing somatic symptoms predominates, it is likely somatic symptom disorder. But if the worry centers on having a serious illness without significant symptoms, or with only mild symptoms, it is more likely illness anxiety disorder. This second scenario is common in older adults, as they may fear that developing a serious illness could further diminish their autonomy, increase dependency, or lead to hospitalization or placement in a nursing home. The distinction between “care-­seeking” and “care-­avoidant” individuals is particularly germane to older adults. Those in the former group require frequent reassurance and often demand repeated diagnostic and medical interventions. Those in the latter group feel such intense anxiety that they avoid taking preventive measures, fearing they will become seriously ill. These differing attitudes may reflect personality patterns. For example, individuals with dependent or hysteric–­histrionic personalities may be more prone to care-­seeking behavior, while those with schizoid psychologies may avoid care (for further discussion see Chapters 12 and 18 on the P and POA Axes (pp. 651–709 and pp. 926–956, respectively). Barnett, Moore, and Archuleta (2019) recently proposed a loneliness model of hypochondriasis in older adults. They found that loneliness plays an indirect but crucial role in illness anxiety by increasing intolerance of uncertainty and symptoms of anxiety. As in young adults and adolescents (Stickley et al., 2016; Brink & Niemeyer, 1993), hypochondriasis in older adults may stem from a lack of social contact, which leads to an inward focus that may include being overly worried about bodily symptoms (El-­Gabalawy et al., 2013; Monopoli, 2005). The COVID-19 pandemic intensified these concerns, as both the virus itself and the containment measures increased isolation, anxiety, and awareness of somatic symptoms. Keshtkar and Bahrami (2021), who investigated relationships between COVID-19-related anxiety and hypochondriasis in Iranian retirees, found that 32% of their sample experienced mild hypochondriasis, with no significant differences based on sex or marital status. SOA6.3 Factitious Disorders Factitious disorders are uncommon in old age. In the absence of other serious mental disorders, elderly individuals rarely falsify psychological or physical symptoms, or induce injury or illness with the intent to deceive. Some, however, may emphasize their suffering as a way to seek attention and support. For example, individuals with hysteric–­ histrionic dynamics may dramatize absent or insignificant symptoms; the line between conscious and unconscious symptom exaggeration can be unclear to clinicians. SOA6.4 Psychological Factors Affecting Other Medical Conditions Among DSM-5-TR’s diagnoses, “psychological factors affecting other medical conditions” is one of the most significant for older adults. This is assigned when “a person has a documented medical condition other than a mental disorder, but behavioral or psychological factors adversely affect the course of the medical condition by delaying recovery, decreasing adherence, significantly increasing health risks, or influencing the underlying pathophysiology” (Nussbaum, 2013, p. 99). In general, and especially in older adults, the onset of a disease can set off psychological reactions that exacerbate

chunks.heading: 982 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 982 Older Adults discomfort and hinder recovery, depending on stress levels and underlying personality traits, styles, and disorders. For example, in cases involving symptoms that cause embarrassment (e.g., elimination problems), feelings of shame may prevent individuals from seeking help. Frances (...
chunks.text: 982 Older Adults discomfort and hinder recovery, depending on stress levels and underlying personality traits, styles, and disorders. For example, in cases involving symptoms that cause embarrassment (e.g., elimination problems), feelings of shame may prevent individuals from seeking help. Frances (2013) gives examples of how individual differences can influence medical outcomes at various ages. After a heart attack, depression may increase the risk of a second attack; a passive–­aggressive individual may not adhere to medical prescriptions; a person in denial may refuse necessary surgery. In old age, such situations may occur more frequently. Each illness poses a threat to the autonomy that is already diminished by the aging process; each symptom serves as a reminder of mortality; family members, friends, and professionals may respond to illness by increasing control over the elderly patient through medication regimens, diets, restrictions, and other well-­intentioned but potentially intrusive interventions. Subjective Experience of Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders in Older Adults McClintock Greenberg (2009, pp. 45–46) calls medical illness “adult-onset trauma,” noting that “the conceptualization of PTSD and PTSD symptoms can help us begin to make sense of the kind of experiences people with medical illness have. The diagnosis of PTSD is common in medical patients.” This perspective helps explain why physical illness can trigger severe emotional reactions, which may be even more intense and pervasive in older adults. They are already dealing with the minor challenges of the aging process, which may feel like attacks on their integrity and identity. While most cope well with these troubles, the discovery of a disease—­or even the fear of getting sick—can threaten their psychological equilibrium. Affective States Anxiety often takes center stage, but shame can also play a significant role (McClintock Greenberg, 2009). Guilt may arise, centering on the belief that one caused the illness or failed to take preventive measures. An older person may question long-­standing habits such as diet, smoking, and inactivity. Denial and dissociation may emerge, along with manic defenses that interfere with compliance with medical prescriptions (Jerant, Chapman, Duberstein, Robbins, & Franks, 2011). For individuals with a history of trauma, illness and aging may trigger the recall of painful past events, reactivating feelings of vulnerability, weakness, and powerlessness. Cognitive Patterns Pervasive anxiety can impair cognitive functioning, preventing effective coping. Faced with somatic symptoms, or even the possibility of illness, an older person may struggle to take action or seek information and help. Combined with the stresses of aging, the emotional burden of illness may inhibit the person’s ability to manage physical problems. Somatic States Anxiety and alarm can exacerbate any ongoing illness, particularly age-­related ailments, heightening concern about health and physical symptoms.

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 983
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 983 Relational Patterns Disproportionate, persistent thoughts about health and symptom severity can undermine an older person’s autonomy and increase burdens on family members and caregivers. Poor treatment adherence, excessive dependency, or counterdepen...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 983 Relational Patterns Disproportionate, persistent thoughts about health and symptom severity can undermine an older person’s autonomy and increase burdens on family members and caregivers. Poor treatment adherence, excessive dependency, or counterdependency can strain relationships with relatives, friends, and health care professionals. Suggestions for Treatment The broad scope of this diagnosis makes general treatment guidance difficult. One consideration is the need to integrate the inevitable physical changes of aging (e.g., changes in appearance, sensory and cognitive decline, and increased vulnerability to illness) into an evolving sense of self. Only by accepting these unwelcome changes can older adults enjoy the positive aspects of aging, such as stable self-­esteem, the acquisition of wisdom, and the pleasure of witnessing the growth of the next generation. When possible, professional interventions should aim to help older individuals reduce denial and omnipotence, minimize narcissistic concerns, and learn to rely on others when necessary. Aging can also bring favorable changes, including the weakening of previously rigid defenses (Zinberg, 1964). Aware of their limited time, older adults often feel strongly motivated to change. Therapists should recognize not only their vulnerabilities but also their strengths and potential resilience. Clinical Illustration A 75-year-old widow was referred by her general practitioner, who had grown frustrated with her repeated demands for lab tests and specialist visits, which she then would avoid for various improbable reasons. Despite a lack of symptoms, she worried constantly about having a serious illness (cancer, heart disease, diabetes, etc.). Her psychologist suggested that her excessive preoccupation with her health made it too frightening to face painful realities, leading her to avoid further investigation. She was in a vicious cycle: heightened anxiety, more demands for tests, more avoidance, and so on. She eventually asked for help in going for the tests, and her sister-­in-law agreed to accompany her. She completed most of them and submitted the results to her primary care physician. Discovering that she was in good health seemed to ease her anxiety somewhat, but she later requested more tests. SOA7 Feeding and Eating Disorders Although feeding and eating disorders have traditionally been considered problems of childhood, emerging evidence suggests that they can occur throughout the lifespan. The exact prevalence of eating disorders in older adults is unknown, as there is no consensus across studies; reported prevalence rates range from approximately 2 to 4% of this population (Mangweth-­Matzek et al., 2006; Smink, van Hoeken, & Hoek, 2012). Eating disorders affect older men as well as older women, but societal responses to aging tend to place women in a more vulnerable position (Peat, Peyerl, & Muehlenkamp, 2008). Perhaps this is why women represent 90% of reported cases of eating disorders in older adults. There is some evidence that eating disorders are increasing among older men. Eating disorders may occur in older women who have never recovered from an early-onset disorder or in those with a remitting and relapsing pattern throughout

chunks.heading: 984 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 984 Older Adults life (Scholtz, Hill, & Lacey, 2010). A relatively small percentage develop eating disorders for the first time in their 50s, 60s, or 70s. Prevalence rates in older women are estimated to be as high as 4.6% for a formal diagnosis and between 4.8 and 13% for subthreshold eating disord...
chunks.text: 984 Older Adults life (Scholtz, Hill, & Lacey, 2010). A relatively small percentage develop eating disorders for the first time in their 50s, 60s, or 70s. Prevalence rates in older women are estimated to be as high as 4.6% for a formal diagnosis and between 4.8 and 13% for subthreshold eating disorders (Conceição, Gomes, Vaz, Pinto-­Bastos, & Machado, 2017; Mangweth-­Matzek, Hoek, & Pope, 2014; Gagne et al., 2012). Anorexia nervosa appears to be the most prevalent diagnosis (Mulchandani, Shetty, Conrad, Muir, & Mah, 2021; Lapid et al., 2010). Changes in diagnostic criteria since DSM-5 may have implications for older people. For example, DSM-IV-TR Criterion D for anorexia nervosa, which required amenorrhea, has been deleted. Criterion B now permits a diagnosis even in the absence of a fear of gaining weight, as long as there are behaviors that interfere with weight gain, and criterion C requires only a lack of recognition of the seriousness of low body weight. Given case reports of late-life eating disorders involving fear of food and body image concerns, these changes may lead to an increase in the number of older patients diagnosed with anorexia nervosa. No definitive data are currently available on the prevalence of bulimia nervosa or binge-­eating disorder in older women. Some evidence suggests that nearly half of those with binge-­eating disorder begin bingeing as adults, and that compared to other eating disorders, this problem is more common in both males and older persons (Smink et al., 2012). A study on picking/nibbling behaviors in older women (Conceição et al., 2017) found a prevalence rate of 18.9%. Although most clinical features of eating disorders in older adults resemble those of younger populations, there are differences in causes, drives, and underlying psychological mechanisms. In later life, a decline in food intake and loss of motivation to eat may be influenced by environmental, social, physiological, or psychological factors, or a combination of these (Podfigurna-­Stopa et al., 2015; Huffmann, 2002). In a 2010 review, Lapid and colleagues found that in a sample of mostly women, the commonest precipitating event for developing an eating disorder was bereavement. Factors such as poverty, loneliness, and social isolation also contribute to dysfunctional eating and attitudes in older adults. Weight loss or gain may be related to medical illnesses or treatments, disposing vulnerable older individuals toward unhealthy eating behaviors. Depression, which is often associated with deterioration of social networks, is a prime cause of appetite loss in older adults. Age-­related bodily changes, along with changes in family relationships, are linked to concerns about weight, dieting, and exercise (Lewis & Cachelin, 2001). Typical features of feeding and eating disorders in older adults include lack of self-­ control around food, distorted body image, and unhealthy eating attitudes. Some may use eating as a means of regaining a sense of power over their lives—a consequence of the loss of control that often accompanies old age (Harris & Cumella, 2006). Others may use an eating disorder as a way to exert control over loved ones, as a form of protest, or as a way to draw attention to their dissatisfaction with their life circumstances (Duggal & Lawrence, 2001). Subjective Experience of Feeding and Eating Disorders in Older Adults Personal losses and life changes can amplify the sense of lack of control in older adults; eating disorders may emerge as a response to such events. Restricting, bingeing, and/ or purging can become powerful ways to regulate mood and emotion. Postmenopausal women, in particular, are often urged to stay “fit” and appear youthful, despite the

chunks.heading: 992 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 992 Older Adults fear of disapproval from family members discourage them from addressing the issue. This reluctance combines two culturally driven taboos—­sexuality and aging—which are often perceived as unspeakable, even when the individual’s suffering is evident. SOA9.2.3 Internet Addiction Use of...
chunks.text: 992 Older Adults fear of disapproval from family members discourage them from addressing the issue. This reluctance combines two culturally driven taboos—­sexuality and aging—which are often perceived as unspeakable, even when the individual’s suffering is evident. SOA9.2.3 Internet Addiction Use of the internet has become increasingly common among older adults. It provides not only information, entertainment, communication, and health care support but has also led to problematic uses, likely exacerbated by the effects of pandemic-­related isolation and the increase in technical skills among older populations. Their physical decline makes excessive internet use more harmful to the elderly, potentially causing eye problems, insomnia, cervical spondylosis, and cardiovascular issues. The internet can expose them to false information and scams, leading to feelings of insecurity, worries about imminent threats, and depression (Dahl & Bergmark, 2020; Lopez-­ Fernandez et al., 2023). Anxiety or uneasiness when smartphones cannot connect to the network may be aggravated in older adults, especially those who are concerned with health, safety, and self-­identity. Compared to the “oldest old,” younger old adults are more adept at using smartphones and apps, increasing their risk of excessive use. Women report higher levels of social media addiction than men. A lack of real-life social support is likely a major cause of internet addiction among older individuals. As with other behavioral addictions, internet use may serve to relieve loneliness, boredom, or dissatisfaction with life. It can fulfill needs like shopping, socializing, managing administrative tasks, and leisure, especially for those who are isolated. Older adults are more likely to use the internet to alleviate depressive symptoms, which can lead to adverse effects on daily life in the form of a “vicious circle” of addiction. SOA10 Neurocognitive Disorders SOA10.1 Delirium In scholarly literature, “delirium” is an acute, transient, usually reversible and fluctuating disturbance of attention, cognitive status, and level of consciousness. Generally, it is caused by acute illness or by the toxicity of a drug. The overall prevalence of delirium in medical patients ranges from 19 to 31%, while incidence rates vary depending on the setting: 15% of older adults develop delirium after certain types of elective surgery, and up to 80% of patients in intensive care units show symptoms of delirium (Gibb et al., 2020; Krewulak et al., 2018). Although delirium was not extensively researched in the past, there has been a recent increase in studies of it. Previously, it was thought to be self-­limiting, with patients expected to return to their baseline functioning. It is now understood as having long-term effects and risking poor health outcomes. Subjective Experience of Delirium in Older Adults Affective States Delirium may involve feelings of fear, anxiety, dread, and loss of control. It can sometimes resemble a dream-like state and may even involve pleasurable emotions. Sometimes, these different affective states coexist or alternate.

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 993
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 993 Cognitive Patterns In individuals with preexisting cognitive impairment, delirium appears to accelerate cognitive decline. In those without demonstrable cognitive impairment, it is associated with an increased risk of future cognitive decline. Differe...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 993 Cognitive Patterns In individuals with preexisting cognitive impairment, delirium appears to accelerate cognitive decline. In those without demonstrable cognitive impairment, it is associated with an increased risk of future cognitive decline. Differentiating cognitive impairment caused by dementia from damage caused by delirium can be challenging for clinicians, making the selection of appropriate assessment tools crucial (Marra et al., 2018). Delirium is often superimposed on dementia (in about one-third of cases), and both conditions can show similar impairments in cognitive functions like attention (Morandi et al., 2014). Somatic States By definition, delirium is associated with coexisting medical or surgical illness. Older adults are thought to have fewer physiological resources and, therefore, less capacity to maintain homeostasis under stress than younger adults. Neuroinflammation may play a role in the delirium syndrome, potentially due to changes in the blood–brain barrier. Relational Patterns Relationships are generally strained and disrupted by delirium. Family members and friends usually find it distressing to see loved ones in such an altered state, particularly when they express ideas based on delusions or misidentifications. Clinical Illustration An 85-year-old widowed man with mild cognitive impairment underwent elective knee surgery. During postoperative recovery, he developed delirium, in the form of agitation and an inability to cooperate with hospital staff. His subjective experience oscillated dramatically from appearing frightened and irritable to seeming relatively calm and comfortable. With frequent visits from his adult children, reassurance and orienting conversations from the hospital staff, and low-dose antipsychotic medication, his delirium resolved. He was able to recover both psychologically and physically. SOA10.2 Mild Neurocognitive Disorder (Mild Cognitive Impairment) Mild neurocognitive disorder (or impairment) refers to subtle changes that are distinct from normal aging. These do not interfere with functional status and do not meet criteria for dementia. It is common for many older adults, from “young-old” to “oldold,” to experience subjective and/or objective mild cognitive impairment; many fear progressing to major neurocognitive disorder (dementia due to Alzheimer’s disease or other causes). Prevalence estimates are high but variable, ranging from 6 to 45%, with an average of 19% (McGrattan et al., 2022). The course of mild neurocognitive disorder is unpredictable: It may stabilize, remit, fluctuate, or progress to major neurocognitive disorder. In primary care settings, approximately one-­fourth of those diagnosed with mild neurocognitive disorder develop major neurocognitive disorder (dementia) within 3 years. There is currently no widely accepted treatment for mild neurocognitive disorder. Individuals are often advised to stay mentally and physically active, and are sometimes prescribed medications used to treat major neurocognitive disorder.

chunks.heading: 994 Older Adults
chunks.preview: 994 Older Adults Previous classifications of mild cognitive impairment into amnestic (primarily affecting memory) and nonamnestic (affecting executive function, language, or visual–­spatial abilities) have been of limited value. Perception of one’s own memory can be influenced by personality, level...
chunks.text: 994 Older Adults Previous classifications of mild cognitive impairment into amnestic (primarily affecting memory) and nonamnestic (affecting executive function, language, or visual–­spatial abilities) have been of limited value. Perception of one’s own memory can be influenced by personality, level of insight, and mood symptoms such as anxiety and depression. There is a complex relationship between subjective memory complaints, assessments by observers (e.g., family members), and performance on objective memory and cognitive tests. While changes in subjective memory perceptions are associated with changes in memory performance, the strength of this relationship is modulated by personality traits and depressive symptoms. Factors beyond memory, such as overall health status, also influence memory perceptions. Some authors have raised concerns about the psychological impact that the uncertainty surrounding a diagnosis of mild cognitive impairment can have on patients (Collier, Kayes, Reay, Hayes, & Bill, 2020). Subjective Experience of Mild Neurocognitive Disorder in Older Adults Affective States Symptoms of anxiety, worry, obsessive fears, and dread may accompany mild neurocognitive disorder (Carter, James, Higgs, Cooper, & Rapaport, 2013). Subjective experience often reflects how a person copes with uncertainty and confusion. Denial is common. Once considered a reliable indicator of an impending dementing disorder, denial is no longer seen as a dependable sign but is still worth noting. Some individuals with mild cognitive disorder, in contrast, regard themselves as having more severe impairment than they actually have, a tendency often seen by others as exaggeration. Such individuals may experience stigma, fear, and reluctance to engage in activities that highlight their insecurities (Renn, Wool, & Belza, 2021). Cognitive Patterns Cognitive patterns as described earlier are inherent to the disorder. Somatic States Because this disorder afflicts older adults who typically have multiple chronic medical conditions, clinicians should consider interactions between these conditions and cognition. Relational Patterns Mild neurocognitive disorder can strain relationships with family and friends, leading to frustration on both sides. This is especially true for those who feel responsible for the person’s well-being or worry about the individual’s ability to perform tasks such as driving. Clinical Illustration An 82-year-old married woman with multiple chronic medical conditions expressed concerns about forgetting people’s names and having difficulty finding words during

chunks.heading: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 995
chunks.preview: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 995 casual conversations. She recalled one of her grandparents becoming “senile” and worried that the same might happen to her. Despite these concerns, she could manage her daily activities, although they required more effort than before, and she occasion...
chunks.text: Symptom Patterns: The Subjective Experience 995 casual conversations. She recalled one of her grandparents becoming “senile” and worried that the same might happen to her. Despite these concerns, she could manage her daily activities, although they required more effort than before, and she occasionally needed help from a caregiver. She was taking several medications that can affect cognition, including a benzodiazepine for anxiety and sleep, and an anticholinergic medication for bladder control.
